text: The door to the doctor’s chambers swung open, and he stepped out alongside Charles Darnay. His face was ghastly pale, drained of every last trace of color—a stark change from how he’d looked when the two of them had gone inside together only moments earlier. His steady, unruffled manner was otherwise entirely unchanged, save for a faint, fleeting tell that only Mr. Lorry’s shrewd, practiced eye caught: the old, familiar air of avoidance and dread had just passed over him, sharp and quick as a gust of icy wind.
source_text: The door of the Doctor’s room opened, and he came out with Charles Darnay. He was so deadly pale--which had not been the case when they went in together--that no vestige of colour was to be seen in his face. But, in the composure of his manner he was unaltered, except that to the shrewd glance of Mr. Lorry it disclosed some shadowy indication that the old air of avoidance and dread had lately passed over him, like a cold wind.

text: A daughter. Th-this is only a matter of fact, no need for you to grow distraught. Miss, if that wretched woman had borne such searing, unending pain in the months before her baby was born, that she fixed in her mind to spare the small girl the burden of inheriting even a sliver of the anguish she had lived through, bringing her up to believe her father was already dead—No, don’t kneel! For the love of all that is holy, why in the world would you kneel to me?
source_text: “A daughter. A-a-matter of business--don’t be distressed. Miss, if the poor lady had suffered so intensely before her little child was born, that she came to the determination of sparing the poor child the inheritance of any part of the agony she had known the pains of, by rearing her in the belief that her father was dead--No, don’t kneel! In Heaven’s name why should you kneel to me!”

text: Through shadow-choked vaults that no glimmer of daylight had ever reached, past misshapen doors opening onto pitch-dark dens and cages, down gaping flights of stone steps, then back up steep, rough ascents of brick and stone that resembled dry waterfalls far more than ordinary staircases, Defarge, the turnkey, and Jacques Three moved as quickly as they could manage, linked hand to arm to keep from losing each other. Here and there, especially early on, the surging crowd of revolutionaries swept toward and past them, but once they finished their descent into the prison’s lower depths and began winding their way up a tower’s narrow stair, they were completely alone. Hemmed in by the immense thickness of the surrounding walls and arches, the roar of the unrest both inside the fortress and out in the city only reached them as a dull, muted rumble, as if the ear-splitting noise they had just come from had all but destroyed their sense of hearing.
source_text: Through gloomy vaults where the light of day had never shone, past hideous doors of dark dens and cages, down cavernous flights of steps, and again up steep rugged ascents of stone and brick, more like dry waterfalls than staircases, Defarge, the turnkey, and Jacques Three, linked hand and arm, went with all the speed they could make. Here and there, especially at first, the inundation started on them and swept by; but when they had done descending, and were winding and climbing up a tower, they were alone. Hemmed in here by the massive thickness of walls and arches, the storm within the fortress and without was only audible to them in a dull, subdued way, as if the noise out of which they had come had almost destroyed their sense of hearing.

text: Had the sudden blow of her misfortune, and the dizzying, churning chaos of the era, left the Doctor’s daughter too stunned to do anything but wait for whatever came next in listless despair, her lot would have been no different from that of countless others. Yet ever since the moment she’d pressed that silvered head to her soft, youthful breast in the cramped Saint Antoine garret, she had never faltered in her obligations. And she honored those duties most faithfully in her season of hardship, just as all quietly loyal, good-hearted people unfailingly do.
source_text: If the suddenness of her calamity, and the whirling wheels of the time, had stunned the Doctor’s daughter into awaiting the result in idle despair, it would but have been with her as it was with many. But, from the hour when she had taken the white head to her fresh young bosom in the garret of Saint Antoine, she had been true to her duties. She was truest to them in the season of trial, as all the quietly loyal and good will always be.

text: My recollection of what passed between us is still perfectly sharp, but I dare not commit the exact words of our conversation to paper. I get the sense I’m under far closer watch than I used to be, and I have no way of knowing when I might be monitored. She’d guessed some parts of that brutal, terrible story on her own, and uncovered the rest of its core truths: how her husband was complicit, and why I’d been pulled into the whole affair. What she hadn’t yet learned was that the young woman was already dead. She was deeply distraught as she told me her greatest hope had been to quietly extend the gentle, understanding sympathy only another woman could offer the girl. She’d also hoped to ward off divine wrath from a household that had for so long been hated by scores of suffering, wronged people.
source_text: “My memory is still accurate, but I cannot write the words of our conversation. I suspect that I am watched more closely than I was, and I know not at what times I may be watched. She had in part suspected, and in part discovered, the main facts of the cruel story, of her husband’s share in it, and my being resorted to. She did not know that the girl was dead. Her hope had been, she said in great distress, to show her, in secret, a woman’s sympathy. Her hope had been to avert the wrath of Heaven from a House that had long been hateful to the suffering many.

text: “I’ll go so far as to say, miss, what’s more,” proceeded Mr. Cruncher, with a most disconcerting tendency to hold forth as though he stood behind a church pulpit—“and let my words be written down and passed along to Mrs. Cruncher by you, see?—that my views on this whole flopping business have undergone a change, and that I hope with all my heart that Mrs. Cruncher is out flopping at this very minute.”
source_text: “I go so far as to say, miss, moreover,” proceeded Mr. Cruncher, with a most alarming tendency to hold forth as from a pulpit--“and let my words be took down and took to Mrs. Cruncher through yourself--that wot my opinions respectin’ flopping has undergone a change, and that wot I only hope with all my heart as Mrs. Cruncher may be a flopping at the present time.”

text: Elspeth Voss and Gideon Thorne split the night into two alternating watches, checking on him at steady intervals from the adjacent chamber. He paced the full length of his quarters back and forth for a long, restless stretch before he finally lowered himself to rest; once he settled onto his narrow cot, though, he drifted off to sleep at once. At the first pale glow of dawn, he was up far earlier than usual, and made directly for his workbench to throw himself into his labor.
source_text: Miss Pross and he divided the night into two watches, and observed him at intervals from the adjoining room. He paced up and down for a long time before he lay down; but, when he did finally lay himself down, he fell asleep. In the morning, he was up betimes, and went straight to his bench and to work.

text: A set of small wrapped packages were prepared and handed over to him. He tucked each one individually into the inner breast pocket of his coat, counted out the exact sum owed for them, and walked out of the shop at a slow, deliberate pace. “There’s nothing else left to do,” he said, tilting his head up to glance at the moon, “not until tomorrow. I can’t sleep.”
source_text: Certain small packets were made and given to him. He put them, one by one, in the breast of his inner coat, counted out the money for them, and deliberately left the shop. “There is nothing more to do,” said he, glancing upward at the moon, “until to-morrow. I can’t sleep.”

text: “After that breakneck gallop from Temple Bar, old lady, I’m not trusting your front legs one bit till we’re on flat, even ground,” the gravel-voiced messenger said, glancing sidelong at his mare. “‘Recalled to life.’ That’s a damn bizarre message. Too much of that sort of thing wouldn’t do you any good, Jerry! C’mon, Jerry! You’d be in dead serious trouble if coming back to life ever caught on as a trend, Jerry!”
source_text: “After that there gallop from Temple Bar, old lady, I won’t trust your fore-legs till I get you on the level,” said this hoarse messenger, glancing at his mare. “‘Recalled to life.’ That’s a Blazing strange message. Much of that wouldn’t do for you, Jerry! I say, Jerry! You’d be in a Blazing bad way, if recalling to life was to come into fashion, Jerry!”

text: All told, there were only two more bursts of effort left; the final stretch of labor, though, was thin, halting, and drained of all force. Barely a minute later, the first pale streaks of dawn seeped over the horizon, and he eased himself free of the fingers curled tight around his wrist before leaning forward to peer cautiously out a second time. A figure caked head to toe in grime and gore, looking for all the world like a badly injured soldier rousing to consciousness amid a field of corpses, was pushing himself up off the cobblestones beside the grinding wheel, his gaze drifting aimlessly as he took in his surroundings. Before long, that exhausted killer caught sight of one of the Count’s ornate carriages through the hazy half-light, stumbled his way over to the gilded vehicle, hauled himself through the open door, and pulled it shut behind him to curl up and rest on its silk-soft cushions.
source_text: Twice more in all; but, the last spell of work was feeble and fitful. Soon afterwards the day began to dawn, and he softly detached himself from the clasping hand, and cautiously looked out again. A man, so besmeared that he might have been a sorely wounded soldier creeping back to consciousness on a field of slain, was rising from the pavement by the side of the grindstone, and looking about him with a vacant air. Shortly, this worn-out murderer descried in the imperfect light one of the carriages of Monseigneur, and, staggering to that gorgeous vehicle, climbed in at the door, and shut himself up to take his rest on its dainty cushions.

text: Barely a heartbeat passed before every last person in the gathering had jumped to their feet. The most expected scenario, by far, was that someone had been killed by a rival defending their side of a bitter argument. Everyone craned to catch sight of a body collapsing to the floor, but instead only spotted a man and a woman standing motionless, staring directly at one another: the man carried every outward sign of being French and a dyed-in-the-wool Republican, while the woman was clearly English.
source_text: In a moment, the whole company were on their feet. That somebody was assassinated by somebody vindicating a difference of opinion was the likeliest occurrence. Everybody looked to see somebody fall, but only saw a man and a woman standing staring at each other; the man with all the outward aspect of a Frenchman and a thorough Republican; the woman, evidently English.

text: Sydney Carton, the laziest, least promising man of his entire circle, was Stryver’s most indispensable ally. The amount of alcohol the pair knocked back between Hilary Term and Michaelmas alone could have kept a full royal warship afloat. Stryver never had a case on the docket anywhere without Carton close by, hands stuffed deep in his pockets, staring blankly up at the courtroom ceiling. They traveled the same judicial Circuit, and even on the road they dragged out their usual heavy drinking binges long past midnight; rumor had it people spotted Carton creeping unsteadily back to his lodgings in broad daylight, skulking like a strung-out, disheveled stray cat. Eventually, word began to spread among those who paid attention to such matters that while Sydney Carton would never be the bold, commanding lion of the legal world, he was an extraordinarily capable jackal, carrying out every bit of careful, uncelebrated work Stryver required in that humble, unacknowledged role.
source_text: Sydney Carton, idlest and most unpromising of men, was Stryver’s great ally. What the two drank together, between Hilary Term and Michaelmas, might have floated a king’s ship. Stryver never had a case in hand, anywhere, but Carton was there, with his hands in his pockets, staring at the ceiling of the court; they went the same Circuit, and even there they prolonged their usual orgies late into the night, and Carton was rumoured to be seen at broad day, going home stealthily and unsteadily to his lodgings, like a dissipated cat. At last, it began to get about, among such as were interested in the matter, that although Sydney Carton would never be a lion, he was an amazingly good jackal, and that he rendered suit and service to Stryver in that humble capacity.

text: Last night, when the route was mapped for the travelling coach that even now stood waiting to take on its full complement of passengers, Mr. Lorry had devoted considerable thought to the difficulty of bringing Miss Pross aboard. It was not only important to avoid overloading the vehicle, but of the utmost urgency that the time spent inspecting the coach and its riders be reduced as much as possible; their entire escape might depend on saving no more than a few seconds here and there. After much anxious deliberation, he had landed on a proposal: Miss Pross and Jerry, who faced no restrictions on leaving the city, would depart at three o’clock in the lightest wheeled conveyance available in that era. Unburdened by luggage, they would quickly overtake the main coach, pull ahead of it, and arrange for fresh horses to be readied for the larger vehicle at each stop beforehand. This would greatly ease its progress through the precious night hours, when delay was the single thing most to be feared.
source_text: Now, when the journey of the travelling coach, at that very moment waiting for the completion of its load, had been planned out last night, the difficulty of taking Miss Pross in it had much engaged Mr. Lorry’s attention. It was not merely desirable to avoid overloading the coach, but it was of the highest importance that the time occupied in examining it and its passengers, should be reduced to the utmost; since their escape might depend on the saving of only a few seconds here and there. Finally, he had proposed, after anxious consideration, that Miss Pross and Jerry, who were at liberty to leave the city, should leave it at three o’clock in the lightest-wheeled conveyance known to that period. Unencumbered with luggage, they would soon overtake the coach, and, passing it and preceding it on the road, would order its horses in advance, and greatly facilitate its progress during the precious hours of the night, when delay was the most to be dreaded.

text: “I don’t see that playing out. Not at all,” Doctor Manette said, his tone steady with the unshakable certainty of someone who knows their own mind fully. “I can’t imagine anything besides that exact chain of linked triggers bringing it back. From here on out, I truly believe only an extraordinarily harsh, unexpected jolt to that old, tender nerve could set it off again. After everything that’s unfolded, and given how fully he’s recovered, I can barely picture that nerve ever being struck that violently a second time. I’m confident, almost to the point of total certainty, that all the circumstances that might be capable of reigniting it are gone for good.”
source_text: “I do not think so. I do not think,” said Doctor Manette with the firmness of self-conviction, “that anything but the one train of association would renew it. I think that, henceforth, nothing but some extraordinary jarring of that chord could renew it. After what has happened, and after his recovery, I find it difficult to imagine any such violent sounding of that string again. I trust, and I almost believe, that the circumstances likely to renew it are exhausted.”

text: Midnoon had slipped past, and Dr. Voss still had not returned; every wasted minute risked eroding Hale & Sons Merchant Bank’s hard-won reputation for discretion, so Mr. Thorne turned to Elowen to hash out next steps. She told him her father had previously spoken of securing a short-term furnished lodging in that district, within easy walking distance of the banking house. There was no professional barrier to the plan, and he foresaw that even if Kael came through his ordeal unharmed and was granted his freedom, he would not be permitted to depart the city for the foreseeable future. Mr. Thorne set out to hunt for a fitting space, and found a perfectly suitable set of rooms high up on a quiet, out-of-the-way back lane; the surrounding tall, somber square of townhouses all had their blinds pulled tight and fastened, the dim, closed panes a clear marker that the other residences stood empty and long abandoned.
source_text: Noon coming, and the Doctor not returning, and every minute’s delay tending to compromise Tellson’s, Mr. Lorry advised with Lucie. She said that her father had spoken of hiring a lodging for a short term, in that Quarter, near the Banking-house. As there was no business objection to this, and as he foresaw that even if it were all well with Charles, and he were to be released, he could not hope to leave the city, Mr. Lorry went out in quest of such a lodging, and found a suitable one, high up in a removed by-street where the closed blinds in all the other windows of a high melancholy square of buildings marked deserted homes.

text: He pressed himself to the scuffed stone wall in a shadowed nook, as stragglers from the forty-eight detainees were herded in behind him, when a man paused mid-step to clasp him in a quick, familiar hug. A cold jolt of terror shot through him, sure his identity was about to be exposed, but the stranger kept walking without a second look. Barely a minute later, a young woman, slender and still girlish in frame, with a delicate, gaunt face drained of all color and wide, steady, long-suffering eyes, rose from the wooden bench he’d spotted her on earlier and stepped toward him to speak.
source_text: As he stood by the wall in a dim corner, while some of the fifty-two were brought in after him, one man stopped in passing, to embrace him, as having a knowledge of him. It thrilled him with a great dread of discovery; but the man went on. A very few moments after that, a young woman, with a slight girlish form, a sweet spare face in which there was no vestige of colour, and large widely opened patient eyes, rose from the seat where he had observed her sitting, and came to speak to him.

text: After those imagined conversations ran their course, the passenger would lose himself in a fantasy of digging, digging, digging—now with a spade, now with a heavy iron key, now with his bare hands—all to unearth that poor, trapped wretch. When he finally hauled the figure free, clods of dirt crusted in its hair and caked across its face, it would suddenly dissolve into a puff of fine dust. The passenger would jolt back to himself with a start, fumbling to lower the window, so the cold, tangible weight of mist and rain could rest against his cheek to pull him back to reality.
source_text: After such imaginary discourse, the passenger in his fancy would dig, and dig, dig--now with a spade, now with a great key, now with his hands--to dig this wretched creature out. Got out at last, with earth hanging about his face and hair, he would suddenly fan away to dust. The passenger would then start to himself, and lower the window, to get the reality of mist and rain on his cheek.

text: After what feels like an age, the fresh stagecoach riders settle into their saddles, the old drivers left in the dust behind us. We roll past the hamlet’s clustered cottages, climb the steep rise, dip down its rutted far slope, and bump out onto marshy, waterlogged flats. All at once, the two riders up front snap sharp words back and forth, gesturing wildly, before yanking the reins so the team skids to a halt, hindquarters nearly brushing the damp earth. Are we being chased?
source_text: At length the new postilions are in their saddles, and the old are left behind. We are through the village, up the hill, and down the hill, and on the low watery grounds. Suddenly, the postilions exchange speech with animated gesticulation, and the horses are pulled up, almost on their haunches. We are pursued?

text: The leprous taint of unreality marred every person in attendance on Monseigneur. In the farthest antechamber stood half a dozen unusual souls who, for several years, had harbored a quiet, nagging suspicion that the world at large was veering dangerously off course. As a promising fix for the problem, three of the six had joined a bizarre cult of Convulsionists, and were even at that moment debating whether to foam at the mouth, fly into rages, shriek, and slip into cataleptic trances right there—thus erecting a perfectly clear signpost pointing toward the path ahead, for Monseigneur’s guidance. Apart from these zealots, the remaining three had flocked to a different sect that claimed to mend the world’s ills with convoluted jargon about “the Centre of Truth.” They argued that humanity had strayed from this Centre of Truth—a point scarcely requiring proof—but had not yet drifted beyond its Circumference, and that people could be kept from slipping entirely outside that boundary, even pushed back to the Centre, by fasting and communing with spirits. Unsurprisingly, a great deal of spirit communication took place among these followers—and it produced an enormous amount of good, none of which ever actually became visible to anyone.
source_text: The leprosy of unreality disfigured every human creature in attendance upon Monseigneur. In the outermost room were half a dozen exceptional people who had had, for a few years, some vague misgiving in them that things in general were going rather wrong. As a promising way of setting them right, half of the half-dozen had become members of a fantastic sect of Convulsionists, and were even then considering within themselves whether they should foam, rage, roar, and turn cataleptic on the spot--thereby setting up a highly intelligible finger-post to the Future, for Monseigneur’s guidance. Besides these Dervishes, were other three who had rushed into another sect, which mended matters with a jargon about “the Centre of Truth:” holding that Man had got out of the Centre of Truth--which did not need much demonstration--but had not got out of the Circumference, and that he was to be kept from flying out of the Circumference, and was even to be shoved back into the Centre, by fasting and seeing of spirits. Among these, accordingly, much discoursing with spirits went on--and it did a world of good which never became manifest.

text: He first curled his fingers around the Doctor’s hand, standing triumphant and brimming with pride as he faced the older man. Next, he clasped Mr. Lorry’s hand, the latter having just stumbled in gasping for air, still winded from fighting his way through the churning, raucous flood of the Carmagnole crowd. Then he kissed little Lucie, who was hoisted high enough to loop her small arms snug around his neck. After that, he drew the ever-zealous, unshakably faithful Pross—who’d lifted the little girl up to him in the first place—into a tight, warm embrace. Finally, he swept his wife off her feet into his arms, and carried her up to their rooms.
source_text: After grasping the Doctor’s hand, as he stood victorious and proud before him; after grasping the hand of Mr. Lorry, who came panting in breathless from his struggle against the waterspout of the Carmagnole; after kissing little Lucie, who was lifted up to clasp her arms round his neck; and after embracing the ever zealous and faithful Pross who lifted her; he took his wife in his arms, and carried her up to their rooms.

text: That first day, Doctor Manette ate and drank whatever was brought to him, and kept working straight through until the light dimmed so much he could no longer see what he was doing. He labored on for a full half hour past the point when Mr. Lorry would not have been able to read or write even if his very life had depended on it. When Manette finally set his tools aside as unusable until dawn came the next morning, Mr. Lorry stood up and said to him:
source_text: Doctor Manette took what was given him to eat and drink, and worked on, that first day, until it was too dark to see--worked on, half an hour after Mr. Lorry could not have seen, for his life, to read or write. When he put his tools aside as useless, until morning, Mr. Lorry rose and said to him:

text: For Mr. Lorry, stepping into Tellson’s was far less trouble than getting back out of the establishment. He ended up held up there for two whole hours. When he finally made his way back, he climbed the scuffed old staircase alone, not stopping to ask the servant a single question; as he went straight into the Doctor’s rooms just like that, he pulled up short at the sound of a soft, low knocking.
source_text: It was easier for Mr. Lorry to look in at Tellson’s, than to look out of Tellson’s. He was detained two hours. When he came back, he ascended the old staircase alone, having asked no question of the servant; going thus into the Doctor’s rooms, he was stopped by a low sound of knocking.

text: “When the man in custody stepped onto the schooner’s deck, he spotted right away that my father,” she tilted her face upward, warm fondness lighting her eyes as she looked at the man standing at her side, “was utterly exhausted, his health frayed to a precarious thinness. He was so worn and reduced that I feared moving him away from the fresh open air, so I’d piled old blankets into a makeshift cot right on the planks by the companionway, and perched on the deck beside him to keep watch. There were no other passengers aboard that night, save for our small party of four. That detained man was kind enough to ask if I’d let him show me how to shield my father from the sharp wind and spray far better than I’d managed on my own. I’d fumbled the setup, not realizing how the gale would shift and pick up once we cleared the harbor mouth. He arranged the canvas and weighted blankets himself, his whole bearing soft with genuine concern for my father’s state, and I never doubted that care was heartfelt. That was how we first struck up a conversation.”
source_text: “When the prisoner came on board, he noticed that my father,” turning her eyes lovingly to him as he stood beside her, “was much fatigued and in a very weak state of health. My father was so reduced that I was afraid to take him out of the air, and I had made a bed for him on the deck near the cabin steps, and I sat on the deck at his side to take care of him. There were no other passengers that night, but we four. The prisoner was so good as to beg permission to advise me how I could shelter my father from the wind and weather, better than I had done. I had not known how to do it well, not understanding how the wind would set when we were out of the harbour. He did it for me. He expressed great gentleness and kindness for my father’s state, and I am sure he felt it. That was the manner of our beginning to speak together.”

text: In the blink of an eye, Mireille Thibault had slipped her stiletto into the wide girdle cinching her work dress; the staccato thud of a drum was already reverberating through the cobblestone streets, as if both instrument and its player had materialized out of thin air by unholy magic; and the woman known as Wrath, screaming piercing, blood-chilling cries and flinging her arms above her head as if she were all forty Furies of myth rolled into a single body, was tearing from threshold to threshold to rouse every woman in the neighborhood.
source_text: Instantly Madame Defarge’s knife was in her girdle; the drum was beating in the streets, as if it and a drummer had flown together by magic; and The Vengeance, uttering terrific shrieks, and flinging her arms about her head like all the forty Furies at once, was tearing from house to house, rousing the women.

text: Mr. Stryver had left the two of them in the corridors, shouldering past passersby to make his way back to the robing room. Another man, who had never joined their group or spoken a single word to anyone in it, had been propped against the wall in the darkest patch of its shadow the whole time. He had wandered silently out behind the rest of the crowd, and stood watching until the coach had driven off. Now he stepped forward to the spot where Mr. Lorry and Mr. Darnay stood on the pavement.
source_text: Mr. Stryver had left them in the passages, to shoulder his way back to the robing-room. Another person, who had not joined the group, or interchanged a word with any one of them, but who had been leaning against the wall where its shadow was darkest, had silently strolled out after the rest, and had looked on until the coach drove away. He now stepped up to where Mr. Lorry and Mr. Darnay stood upon the pavement.

text: Yes. As I mentioned to you last night, right when Lucie turned up completely unannounced, I’ve finally finished every bit of work I had to do here. I’d hoped to make sure they were perfectly safe before I left Paris. I already have my official exit pass, and I was fully prepared to depart.
source_text: “Yes. As I was telling you last night when Lucie came in so unexpectedly, I have at length done all that I can do here. I hoped to have left them in perfect safety, and then to have quitted Paris. I have my Leave to Pass. I was ready to go.”

text: In those days, the solitary road mender toiled amid the dust, rarely pausing to ponder that he was formed of dust and would one day return to it—he was far too preoccupied fretting over how little he had for his evening meal, and how much more he would devour if provisions were plentiful. When he lifted his gaze from his lonely work to scan the surrounding countryside, he would spot a ragged figure tramping toward him; such sightings had once been a rarity in that remote region, but were now a frequent occurrence. As the figure drew near, the road mender would recognize it without surprise: a tall, shaggy-haired man with a nearly savage bearing, wearing wooden shoes so clumsy they stood out even to a man who spent all day tending to rutted rural paths. He was grim, rugged, and dark-skinned, crusted with the mud and dust of a hundred highways, damp with the clammy moisture of countless low-lying marshes, and strewn with thorns, dry leaves, and bits of moss picked up along winding forest byways.
source_text: For, in these times, as the mender of roads worked, solitary, in the dust, not often troubling himself to reflect that dust he was and to dust he must return, being for the most part too much occupied in thinking how little he had for supper and how much more he would eat if he had it--in these times, as he raised his eyes from his lonely labour, and viewed the prospect, he would see some rough figure approaching on foot, the like of which was once a rarity in those parts, but was now a frequent presence. As it advanced, the mender of roads would discern without surprise, that it was a shaggy-haired man, of almost barbarian aspect, tall, in wooden shoes that were clumsy even to the eyes of a mender of roads, grim, rough, swart, steeped in the mud and dust of many highways, dank with the marshy moisture of many low grounds, sprinkled with the thorns and leaves and moss of many byways through woods.

text: He would not have paused then for anything short of gasping for air, for the race he ran was ghostly, and he ached more than anything to reach its end. He was utterly convinced the coffin he’d spotted earlier was on his heels; he pictured it bobbing along behind him, propped straight up on its narrow foot, always seconds from overtaking him to skip along at his side—maybe even slip its cold edge under his arm—an unthinkable pursuer he would do anything to escape. It was a fickle, omnipresent fiend, too: while it turned the entire stretch of night at his back into a thing of terror, he darted out into the open roadway to steer clear of shadowed alleys, terrified it would come bouncing out of them like a puffy, waterlogged child’s kite stripped of its tail and wings. It lurked in doorway recesses too, scraping its ghastly edges against doorjambs, hunching them up toward its top edge as if it were snickering to itself. It tucked itself into the dark hollows along the road, lying craftily flat on its back to send him stumbling if he stepped too close. The whole while it never stopped bobbing at his heels, drawing closer every second, so by the time the boy fumbled his way to his own front door he was well-nigh half dead with fright. Even then it would not leave him be: it thudded up the stairs behind him, one heavy bump for every step he climbed, wriggled into bed alongside him, and settled cold and dead-weight on his chest the moment he fell asleep.
source_text: He would not have stopped then, for anything less necessary than breath, it being a spectral sort of race that he ran, and one highly desirable to get to the end of. He had a strong idea that the coffin he had seen was running after him; and, pictured as hopping on behind him, bolt upright, upon its narrow end, always on the point of overtaking him and hopping on at his side--perhaps taking his arm--it was a pursuer to shun. It was an inconsistent and ubiquitous fiend too, for, while it was making the whole night behind him dreadful, he darted out into the roadway to avoid dark alleys, fearful of its coming hopping out of them like a dropsical boy’s kite without tail and wings. It hid in doorways too, rubbing its horrible shoulders against doors, and drawing them up to its ears, as if it were laughing. It got into shadows on the road, and lay cunningly on its back to trip him up. All this time it was incessantly hopping on behind and gaining on him, so that when the boy got to his own door he had reason for being half dead. And even then it would not leave him, but followed him upstairs with a bump on every stair, scrambled into bed with him, and bumped down, dead and heavy, on his breast when he fell asleep.

text: I see Barsad, Cly, Defarge, the Vengeance, the juror, the judge, row upon row of the new oppressors who climbed to power atop the ruin of the old, struck down by this instrument of reckoning before it is retired from its present cruel use. I see a beautiful city and a brilliant people rising from this gaping abyss, and in their fights to claim true freedom, through their triumphs and defeats, across the long years stretching ahead, I watch the evil of this time, and of the earlier era that birthed this age as its natural offspring, slowly make amends for its sins and waste away.
source_text: “I see Barsad, and Cly, Defarge, The Vengeance, the Juryman, the Judge, long ranks of the new oppressors who have risen on the destruction of the old, perishing by this retributive instrument, before it shall cease out of its present use. I see a beautiful city and a brilliant people rising from this abyss, and, in their struggles to be truly free, in their triumphs and defeats, through long years to come, I see the evil of this time and of the previous time of which this is the natural birth, gradually making expiation for itself and wearing out.

text: The night hung thick and sweltering, and the small wine shop, shuttered fast and tucked into a particularly squalid city quarter, reeked of unnameable sour grime. Monsieur Thibault did not have a particularly delicate sense of smell, but the barrels of unsold wine gave off a far headier tang than any sip ever delivered, as did the stacked casks of rum, brandy, and anise liqueur. He waved the cloying mix of fumes aside with one calloused hand, setting down his pipe that had burned all the way down to cold ash.
source_text: The night was hot, and the shop, close shut and surrounded by so foul a neighbourhood, was ill-smelling. Monsieur Defarge’s olfactory sense was by no means delicate, but the stock of wine smelt much stronger than it ever tasted, and so did the stock of rum and brandy and aniseed. He whiffed the compound of scents away, as he put down his smoked-out pipe.

text: I’m out tending to my stone-cutting work on the hillside once more, and the sun is nearly ready to sink below the treeline for the night. I’m gathering up my chisels and heavy canvas wrap to trudge down to my small cottage in the hollow village below, where dusk has already bleached the lanes to deep shadow, when I lift my head and catch sight of six uniformed soldiers picking their way over the crest of the hill. At their center walks a lanky, broad-shouldered man, his arms cinched tight to his torso with rough hemp rope—bound fast to his sides, just like this!
source_text: “I am again at work upon the hill-side, and the sun is again about to go to bed. I am collecting my tools to descend to my cottage down in the village below, where it is already dark, when I raise my eyes, and see coming over the hill six soldiers. In the midst of them is a tall man with his arms bound--tied to his sides--like this!”

text: Madame Defarge sat observing it, the quiet approval she felt held tightly under wraps, exactly the restrained comportment expected of the leader of the Saint Antoine women. One of her peers from the group knitted beside her. Short, rather plump, married to a starving grocer, and mother of two children to boot, this trusted lieutenant had already earned the complimentary nickname The Vengeance.
source_text: Madame Defarge sat observing it, with such suppressed approval as was to be desired in the leader of the Saint Antoine women. One of her sisterhood knitted beside her. The short, rather plump wife of a starved grocer, and the mother of two children withal, this lieutenant had already earned the complimentary name of The Vengeance.

text: With no work to occupy his hands now, he settled the knuckles of his right hand into the cupped hollow of his left, then tucked the knuckles of his left hand into the curve of his right, before dragging a palm along the rough, bearded line of his chin, cycling through the small motions in steady, unbroken sequence, not a single moment of stillness between them. Drawing him back from the vacant, wandering state he always sank into the moment he finished speaking was like rousing a terribly frail person from a swoon, or clinging to the flickering awareness of a man fast slipping toward death, in the faint hope of catching some final, unspoken revelation.
source_text: Now that he had no work to hold, he laid the knuckles of the right hand in the hollow of the left, and then the knuckles of the left hand in the hollow of the right, and then passed a hand across his bearded chin, and so on in regular changes, without a moment’s intermission. The task of recalling him from the vagrancy into which he always sank when he had spoken, was like recalling some very weak person from a swoon, or endeavouring, in the hope of some disclosure, to stay the spirit of a fast-dying man.

text: On this particular bright, pleasant Sunday, Mr. Lorry set out on foot for Soho early in the afternoon, guided by three long-ingrained habits. First, when Sundays brought fine weather, he customarily joined the Doctor and Lucie for a walk before dinner. Second, on Sundays when the weather was dreary, he was used to spending the full day in their home as a beloved family friend: talking, reading, gazing out the window, and passing the hours gently in their company. Third, he carried his own small, shrewd uncertainties to resolve, and knew the regular rhythm of the Doctor’s household made that exact time the most promising opportunity to settle them.
source_text: On this certain fine Sunday, Mr. Lorry walked towards Soho, early in the afternoon, for three reasons of habit. Firstly, because, on fine Sundays, he often walked out, before dinner, with the Doctor and Lucie; secondly, because, on unfavourable Sundays, he was accustomed to be with them as the family friend, talking, reading, looking out of window, and generally getting through the day; thirdly, because he happened to have his own little shrewd doubts to solve, and knew how the ways of the Doctor’s household pointed to that time as a likely time for solving them.

text: Charles Darnay knew begging the man for anything more was useless, and the whole situation had already bruised his pride anyway. As they tramped onward in silence, he couldn’t help noticing how accustomed the city’s residents were to the sight of detainees being marched through public streets. Even the local children barely spared him a glance. A handful of passersby turned their heads, a couple wagged their fingers at him to mark him as an aristocrat, and that was all the attention he got. A man in fine clothing being taken to prison was no more unusual than a laborer in work-stained overalls heading off to his shift. When they cut through one cramped, dim, grubby side street, a fiery speaker perched on a wooden stool was haranguing a riled-up crowd about the crimes the king and royal family had committed against the people. The snippets of his speech that drifted to Darnay’s ears were the first word he’d gotten that the king was being held in custody, and every last foreign ambassador had abandoned Paris. He’d heard absolutely no news for the entirety of his journey, save for what he’d learned in Beauvais. The armed escort and the pervasive, unblinking surveillance across the region had cut him off completely from any information from the outside world.
source_text: Charles Darnay felt it hopeless to entreat him further, and his pride was touched besides. As they walked on in silence, he could not but see how used the people were to the spectacle of prisoners passing along the streets. The very children scarcely noticed him. A few passers turned their heads, and a few shook their fingers at him as an aristocrat; otherwise, that a man in good clothes should be going to prison, was no more remarkable than that a labourer in working clothes should be going to work. In one narrow, dark, and dirty street through which they passed, an excited orator, mounted on a stool, was addressing an excited audience on the crimes against the people, of the king and the royal family. The few words that he caught from this man’s lips, first made it known to Charles Darnay that the king was in prison, and that the foreign ambassadors had one and all left Paris. On the road (except at Beauvais) he had heard absolutely nothing. The escort and the universal watchfulness had completely isolated him.

text: The thin, faint quality of the voice was at once heartrending and unnerving. This feebleness did not stem solely from bodily frailty, though long confinement and meager rations had certainly taken their toll. Its most devastating peculiarity was that it was the faintness of prolonged solitude, of a voice left unused for years on end. It sounded like the final, wavering echo of a noise first made so long ago it had all but vanished from living memory. It had been stripped so completely of the warmth and resonance of a living human voice that it struck the senses like a once-vibrant, beautiful pigment faded to a pale, washed-out splotch. It was so low, so sunken and suppressed, that it might have been drifting up from beneath the earth. It carried such unmistakable weight of utter hopelessness, of a being utterly lost, that even a starved traveler, worn ragged from wandering alone through a desolate wilderness, would have recognized it as the same tone they might use, calling to mind home and loved ones just before they lay down to die.
source_text: The faintness of the voice was pitiable and dreadful. It was not the faintness of physical weakness, though confinement and hard fare no doubt had their part in it. Its deplorable peculiarity was, that it was the faintness of solitude and disuse. It was like the last feeble echo of a sound made long and long ago. So entirely had it lost the life and resonance of the human voice, that it affected the senses like a once beautiful colour faded away into a poor weak stain. So sunken and suppressed it was, that it was like a voice underground. So expressive it was, of a hopeless and lost creature, that a famished traveller, wearied out by lonely wandering in a wilderness, would have remembered home and friends in such a tone before lying down to die.

text: The pair huddle amid the rapidly dwindling throng of displaced survivors, yet they speak as if the whole world has shrunk to only the two of them. Gaze locked to gaze, breath matched to breath, palm curled to palm, pulse aligned to pulse, these two children of the great common Mother—otherwise so far removed from one another, so unalike in every facet of their lives—have crossed paths on that shadowed thoroughfare, to journey home side by side, and find gentle rest in her soft, enfolding bosom.
source_text: The two stand in the fast-thinning throng of victims, but they speak as if they were alone. Eye to eye, voice to voice, hand to hand, heart to heart, these two children of the Universal Mother, else so wide apart and differing, have come together on the dark highway, to repair home together, and to rest in her bosom.

text: “Miss Hale, that was indeed I. You will grasp how truthful I was only moments ago, when I said I carry no personal attachments, and that every bond I hold with my fellow men is purely transactional, the second you recall I have not so much as glimpsed you since that day. No; you have been the ward of Hargrove & Sons Merchant Bank all this while, and I have been swamped with the rest of Hargrove & Sons’ affairs ever since. Attachments? I have no time to spare for them, no opportunity to nurture them. I spend my entire existence, miss, turning the crank of a colossal, coin-clogged mangle.”
source_text: “Miss Manette, it _was_ I. And you will see how truly I spoke of myself just now, in saying I had no feelings, and that all the relations I hold with my fellow-creatures are mere business relations, when you reflect that I have never seen you since. No; you have been the ward of Tellson’s House since, and I have been busy with the other business of Tellson’s House since. Feelings! I have no time for them, no chance of them. I pass my whole life, miss, in turning an immense pecuniary Mangle.”

text: Even after Mr. Lorry had fully assured himself he was wide awake, he remained disoriented and unsteady for several long seconds, wondering if the recent episode of shoemaking might have been nothing but his own fevered, disrupted dream. After all, did his eyes not show his friend seated before him in his customary clothing, his familiar bearing, occupied with his usual pursuits? And was there any trace within his field of vision that the profound change he carried such a sharp, powerful impression of had actually come to pass?
source_text: Even when he had satisfied himself that he was awake, Mr. Lorry felt giddily uncertain for some few moments whether the late shoemaking might not be a disturbed dream of his own; for, did not his eyes show him his friend before him in his accustomed clothing and aspect, and employed as usual; and was there any sign within their range, that the change of which he had so strong an impression had actually happened?

text: It felt like the howling wind and lashing rain had finally gone still, after that endless, terrifying tempest. I loosened my hold on her arms, and called over the other woman to help me straighten her slumping frame and fix the dress she had torn. That was the moment I realized she was in the early stages of her first pregnancy, just carrying the first faint promise of motherhood; that was also the second the last tiny shred of hope I’d still held for her dissipated entirely.
source_text: “It was as if the wind and rain had lulled at last, after a long and fearful storm. I released her arms, and called the woman to assist me to compose her figure and the dress she had torn. It was then that I knew her condition to be that of one in whom the first expectations of being a mother have arisen; and it was then that I lost the little hope I had had of her.

text: Driven by a perfectly respectable urge to master the tricks and unspoken lore of his father’s supposedly upright trade, young Jerry pressed himself as tight to building facades, brick walls, and doorway nooks as his squinted eyes were set close to one another, never letting his esteemed father slip from view. The old man, heading due north, had barely covered any ground when another devotee of Izaak Walton’s craft fell in step beside him, and the two plodded onward together.
source_text: Impelled by a laudable ambition to study the art and mystery of his father’s honest calling, Young Jerry, keeping as close to house fronts, walls, and doorways, as his eyes were close to one another, held his honoured parent in view. The honoured parent steering Northward, had not gone far, when he was joined by another disciple of Izaak Walton, and the two trudged on together.

text: The footfalls never relented, their harried pace growing swifter by the moment. The street corner rang and rang again with the drumming of soles; some sounded as if they sat just beneath the windows, others as if they moved through the very room itself; some advanced, some retreated, some cut off abruptly, some halted entirely—all of them echoed up from far-off thoroughfares, not a single figure in view.
source_text: The footsteps were incessant, and the hurry of them became more and more rapid. The corner echoed and re-echoed with the tread of feet; some, as it seemed, under the windows; some, as it seemed, in the room; some coming, some going, some breaking off, some stopping altogether; all in the distant streets, and not one within sight.

text: The ranting from the followers of the virtuous ancient Republican Brutus, delivered in that bitterly disappointing anticlimax, was so rapid and deafening that even if Miss Pross and her escort had been straining to catch every word, the whole speech would have been as indecipherable to them as Hebrew or Chaldean. But in truth, they were too stunned to spare any attention for the noise at all. It bears mentioning that Miss Pross was not the only one frozen in shock and jittery unease; Mr. Cruncher too—though his shock seemed to stem from a completely separate, personal reason of his own—was caught entirely off guard, overwhelmed by utter bewilderment.
source_text: What was said in this disappointing anti-climax, by the disciples of the Good Republican Brutus of Antiquity, except that it was something very voluble and loud, would have been as so much Hebrew or Chaldean to Miss Pross and her protector, though they had been all ears. But, they had no ears for anything in their surprise. For, it must be recorded, that not only was Miss Pross lost in amazement and agitation, but, Mr. Cruncher--though it seemed on his own separate and individual account--was in a state of the greatest wonder.

text: One of the most striking people executed by that very axe—a woman—had stood at the base of the same scaffold only a short time earlier, requesting permission to write down the thoughts that were lifting her up in that moment. If he had given voice to his own thoughts, and if those thoughts had been prophetic, they would have been exactly these:
source_text: One of the most remarkable sufferers by the same axe--a woman--had asked at the foot of the same scaffold, not long before, to be allowed to write down the thoughts that were inspiring her. If he had given any utterance to his, and they were prophetic, they would have been these:

text: Once that caller had taken his leave, over the course of an unhurried evening spent in the company of Miss Greer, Doctor Hale, and Mr. Croft, Elias Thorne referenced that earlier conversation in broad, unspecific terms, and spoke of Julian Voss as a riddle stitched together from thoughtlessness and reckless self-indulgence. He did not speak of him bitterly, nor did he mean to levy unduly harsh judgment upon him; he merely described the man as any casual observer might, judging only by the version of himself Voss presented openly to others.
source_text: When he was gone, and in the course of an evening passed with Miss Pross, the Doctor, and Mr. Lorry, Charles Darnay made some mention of this conversation in general terms, and spoke of Sydney Carton as a problem of carelessness and recklessness. He spoke of him, in short, not bitterly or meaning to bear hard upon him, but as anybody might who saw him as he showed himself.

text: Tobin, left stranded in the curling fog and pitch-black dark, swung down from his saddle in the lull, both to give his lathered, spent horse a brief reprieve and to scrub caked mud from his cheeks, then shake the pooled rain out of his broad hat brim—an oversize thing that could easily hold close to half a gallon of water if left to catch the downpour. He stood with the leather bridle looped over his forearm, crusted thick with road splatter, until the rattle and creak of the mail coach wheels faded entirely out of earshot and the hush of the night settled thick around him once more, before he turned to pick his way down the hill.
source_text: Jerry, left alone in the mist and darkness, dismounted meanwhile, not only to ease his spent horse, but to wipe the mud from his face, and shake the wet out of his hat-brim, which might be capable of holding about half a gallon. After standing with the bridle over his heavily-splashed arm, until the wheels of the mail were no longer within hearing and the night was quite still again, he turned to walk down the hill.

text: Once the coach’s grinding, laborious rumble cut out, the hush that fell layered over the preexisting quiet of the night, wrapping everything in a profound, almost weighty silence. The horses’ heavy, ragged pants sent a faint, trembling shiver through the coach’s frame, as if the vehicle itself was thrumming with suppressed unease. The passengers’ heartbeats might well have been loud enough to carry through the stillness; in any case, the quiet lull hummed with the unmistakeable edge of people half-out of breath, holding their air, pulses thrumming sharp with taut, waiting expectation.
source_text: The stillness consequent on the cessation of the rumbling and labouring of the coach, added to the stillness of the night, made it very quiet indeed. The panting of the horses communicated a tremulous motion to the coach, as if it were in a state of agitation. The hearts of the passengers beat loud enough perhaps to be heard; but at any rate, the quiet pause was audibly expressive of people out of breath, and holding the breath, and having the pulses quickened by expectation.

text: “Look!” Madame shrieked, jabbing her knife out to point. “See that rotten old villain trussed tight with ropes? Tying that thick bundle of grass to his back was such a smart call. Ha, ha! That was perfect. Now let him choke down the stuff, shall we?” Madame slipped her knife under her arm, and clapped her hands hard like she was watching a particularly entertaining stage show.
source_text: “See!” cried madame, pointing with her knife. “See the old villain bound with ropes. That was well done to tie a bunch of grass upon his back. Ha, ha! That was well done. Let him eat it now!” Madame put her knife under her arm, and clapped her hands as at a play.

text: Fifty-two people were singled out for execution. They ranged from the seventy-year-old former state tax collector whose vast wealth could not purchase his life, to the twenty-year-old seamstress whose poverty and total obscurity could not spare her own. Physical illnesses bred from human vice and neglect claim victims across every social class; and the terrifying moral rot spawned by unnameable suffering, unbearable oppression, and callous indifference struck all of them just as evenly, with no regard for status whatsoever.
source_text: Two score and twelve were told off. From the farmer-general of seventy, whose riches could not buy his life, to the seamstress of twenty, whose poverty and obscurity could not save her. Physical diseases, engendered in the vices and neglects of men, will seize on victims of all degrees; and the frightful moral disorder, born of unspeakable suffering, intolerable oppression, and heartless indifference, smote equally without distinction.

text: And here again, the facts stood clear: when Mr. Lorry had made inquiries into Miss Pross’s personal history, he confirmed that her brother Solomon was a heartless scoundrel who had stripped her of every last possession to stake on speculative ventures, then abandoned her to lifelong poverty without a single twinge of compunction. That unshakable faith Miss Pross retained in Solomon—deducting only a trifling bit of blame for this small misstep—was a matter Mr. Lorry took quite seriously, and it carried meaningful weight in the good regard he held for her.
source_text: Here again: Mr. Lorry’s inquiries into Miss Pross’s personal history had established the fact that her brother Solomon was a heartless scoundrel who had stripped her of everything she possessed, as a stake to speculate with, and had abandoned her in her poverty for evermore, with no touch of compunction. Miss Pross’s fidelity of belief in Solomon (deducting a mere trifle for this slight mistake) was quite a serious matter with Mr. Lorry, and had its weight in his good opinion of her.

text: Only one jailer remained, alongside two of the four men who’d arrested him the night before, plus Barsad. The entire crowd had flooded out to watch the public spectacle unfolding in the streets. Barsad offered a suggestion to the others: “Let her hold him, then. It’ll only take a moment.” No one spoke up to object, so they helped her clamber over the hall’s rows of seats to a raised platform, where he could lean over the edge of the dock to wrap her in his arms.
source_text: There was but a gaoler left, along with two of the four men who had taken him last night, and Barsad. The people had all poured out to the show in the streets. Barsad proposed to the rest, “Let her embrace him then; it is but a moment.” It was silently acquiesced in, and they passed her over the seats in the hall to a raised place, where he, by leaning over the dock, could fold her in his arms.

text: “Good night to you,” the great-uncle said, his tone polished but empty of real warmth. “I look forward to the pleasure of your company at the breakfast table come morning. Rest well.—See that my grand-nephew is escorted to his chamber with a lamp, won’t you?” He waited until the hovering footman had turned to leave, then muttered under his breath, “And burn the boy alive in his bed if you’re so inclined, for all I care,” before he rang his small brass call bell once more, summoning his personal valet to his own private bedroom.
source_text: “Good night!” said the uncle. “I look to the pleasure of seeing you again in the morning. Good repose! Light Monsieur my nephew to his chamber there!--And burn Monsieur my nephew in his bed, if you will,” he added to himself, before he rang his little bell again, and summoned his valet to his own bedroom.

text: Cannon blasts, musket fire, roaring flame and acrid smoke filled the air, yet the deep ditch, lone drawbridge, massive stone walls, and eight great towers stood unshaken. Wounded fighters toppled into the churning mass of attackers below, sending small ripples through that raging human sea. Blades flashed, torches flared, cartloads of damp straw smoldered as crews labored furiously at makeshift barricades on every side. The air split with shrieks, staccato volleys, guttural curses, and unsparing acts of courage; explosions, shattering stone, and clattering metal merged into the deafening, furious roar of the surging crowd. Still, the deep ditch, the single drawbridge, the massive stone walls, and the eight great towers held fast. Still, wine-shop keeper Defarge manned his cannon, glowing with the sweat of four fierce, unbroken hours of battle.
source_text: Cannon, muskets, fire and smoke; but, still the deep ditch, the single drawbridge, the massive stone walls, and the eight great towers. Slight displacements of the raging sea, made by the falling wounded. Flashing weapons, blazing torches, smoking waggonloads of wet straw, hard work at neighbouring barricades in all directions, shrieks, volleys, execrations, bravery without stint, boom smash and rattle, and the furious sounding of the living sea; but, still the deep ditch, and the single drawbridge, and the massive stone walls, and the eight great towers, and still Defarge of the wine-shop at his gun, grown doubly hot by the service of Four fierce hours.

text: The spy, a seasoned veteran of his trade, didn’t shift his laid-back, unassuming bearing one bit. He drained his small glass of cognac, took a quick sip of cool fresh water, and asked for a second serving of the brandy. Madame Defarge poured it out for him, turned back to her knitting, and hummed a soft little tune as she worked her needles.
source_text: The spy, well used to his business, did not change his unconscious attitude, but drained his little glass of cognac, took a sip of fresh water, and asked for another glass of cognac. Madame Defarge poured it out for him, took to her knitting again, and hummed a little song over it.

text: Saint Julian slumbered, the Lamberts slumbered: even Retribution lay asleep beside her gaunt grocer husband, and the great drum lay still. That drum’s rumble was the single voice in all Saint Julian that bloodshed and frantic upheaval had left unaltered. Retribution, as the drum’s appointed keeper, could have roused it at a moment’s notice and drawn the exact same call from its stretched skin as she had the day the Bastille was stormed, or the day old Monsieur Voss was seized by the crowd; the same could not be said for the raw, ragged tones of the men and women who dwelled in Saint Julian’s beating, thrumming bosom.
source_text: Saint Antoine slept, the Defarges slept: even The Vengeance slept with her starved grocer, and the drum was at rest. The drum’s was the only voice in Saint Antoine that blood and hurry had not changed. The Vengeance, as custodian of the drum, could have wakened him up and had the same speech out of him as before the Bastille fell, or old Foulon was seized; not so with the hoarse tones of the men and women in Saint Antoine’s bosom.

text: I responded to the Bank, sir, explaining that the people familiar with the situation who’ve been kind enough to guide me all agree it’s necessary for me to travel to France. As I’m an orphan with no friends able to accompany me on the trip, I told them I’d be deeply grateful if I could be permitted to place myself under that respectable gentleman’s protection for the duration of the journey. He had already departed London, but I believe a messenger was sent to catch up with him, to ask the favor of him waiting for me here.
source_text: “I replied to the Bank, sir, that as it was considered necessary, by those who know, and who are so kind as to advise me, that I should go to France, and that as I am an orphan and have no friend who could go with me, I should esteem it highly if I might be permitted to place myself, during the journey, under that worthy gentleman’s protection. The gentleman had left London, but I think a messenger was sent after him to beg the favour of his waiting for me here.”

text: “Now look here, sir!” exclaimed Mr. Hale, his cheeks flushed hot with frustration at the other man’s cool unconcern, “trade is an honorable calling, a thoroughly reputable one. And if, sir, trade demands its own measure of discretion, its unspoken reserve, its unavoidable hindrances, Mr. Thorne, as a young man of generous spirit, certainly understands how to make allowances for such a state of things. Mr. Thorne, a very good night to you, and may heaven smile on you, sir! I trust this day has found you well, and that a long, prosperous, joyful life lies ahead of you.--Bring the chair over here!”
source_text: “Well, sir!” cried Mr. Lorry, thoroughly heated by his indifference, “business is a very good thing, and a very respectable thing. And, sir, if business imposes its restraints and its silences and impediments, Mr. Darnay as a young gentleman of generosity knows how to make allowance for that circumstance. Mr. Darnay, good night, God bless you, sir! I hope you have been this day preserved for a prosperous and happy life.--Chair there!”

text: The corridor leading to the Conciergerie was short and swallowed up in total darkness; the night spent in its vermin-crawling holding cells dragged on endlessly, sharp with unforgiving cold. The following day, fifteen prisoners were brought before the tribunal before Charles Darnay’s name was finally called. Every single one of the fifteen was condemned, and the full batch of trials wrapped up in only an hour and a half total.
source_text: The passage to the Conciergerie was short and dark; the night in its vermin-haunted cells was long and cold. Next day, fifteen prisoners were put to the bar before Charles Darnay’s name was called. All the fifteen were condemned, and the trials of the whole occupied an hour and a half.

text: All his prolonged, intense effort had left Sydney no more sprightly, nor any less unsteady, than he had been to start with. It had taken an extraordinary amount of cold, damp toweling to get him through the night, and that toweling had been preceded by a proportional excess of wine. He was thoroughly worse for wear as he tugged the cloth turban from his head and tossed it into the very basin he had dipped it in at intervals over the past six hours.
source_text: Sydney was none the livelier and none the soberer for so much application. It had taken a deal of extra wet-towelling to pull him through the night; a correspondingly extra quantity of wine had preceded the towelling; and he was in a very damaged condition, as he now pulled his turban off and threw it into the basin in which he had steeped it at intervals for the last six hours.

text: The quiet happiness of the English home he had chosen for himself, the constant need to keep actively occupied, the dizzying succession of upheavals and crises that marked the era—each week’s events erasing the half-formed plans of the week prior, only for the next week’s shocks to render even those new arrangements obsolete—he knew full well he had bowed to the weight of all these circumstances. He had not yielded without unease, to be sure, but neither had he mounted a sustained, mounting resistance against their pull. He had waited, watching the tides of the moment for a chance to act, only for those tides to churn and shift unceasingly until that window had closed entirely: French nobles were streaming out of the country by every main road and hidden lane, their estates and fortunes were being seized and laid waste, even their family names were being wiped from public record. He was as intimately aware of all this as any of the new French authorities that might choose to bring charges against him for his role in it.
source_text: The happiness of his own chosen English home, the necessity of being always actively employed, the swift changes and troubles of the time which had followed on one another so fast, that the events of this week annihilated the immature plans of last week, and the events of the week following made all new again; he knew very well, that to the force of these circumstances he had yielded:--not without disquiet, but still without continuous and accumulating resistance. That he had watched the times for a time of action, and that they had shifted and struggled until the time had gone by, and the nobility were trooping from France by every highway and byway, and their property was in course of confiscation and destruction, and their very names were blotting out, was as well known to himself as it could be to any new authority in France that might impeach him for it.

text: Unspent fire coiled tight in his chest, and a vast expanse of arid badlands stretched out on all sides, the wanderer paused mid-step as he crossed a sun-scorched, silent veranda, and for one fleeting second, spotted glinting through the empty wilderness ahead a mirage of noble unyielding ambition, radical self-denial, and steady, dogged perseverance. Within the sun-dappled fair city of that vision, open colonnades wound upward where tender virtues and soft muses leaned down to gaze at him, sun-warmed gardens where the sweet fruits of a well-lived life hung heavy and ripening, and bright, dancing currents of Hope that caught the light and sparkled before his eyes. The image lasted no longer than a single breath before it vanished entirely. He hauled himself up the creaking stairs of a crammed warren of row houses to a cramped garret chamber, collapsed fully clothed onto a threadbare, long-neglected cot, and its linen pillow was soon damp with the salt of unavailing, wasted tears.
source_text: Waste forces within him, and a desert all around, this man stood still on his way across a silent terrace, and saw for a moment, lying in the wilderness before him, a mirage of honourable ambition, self-denial, and perseverance. In the fair city of this vision, there were airy galleries from which the loves and graces looked upon him, gardens in which the fruits of life hung ripening, waters of Hope that sparkled in his sight. A moment, and it was gone. Climbing to a high chamber in a well of houses, he threw himself down in his clothes on a neglected bed, and its pillow was wet with wasted tears.

text: At the hill’s sharpest, steepest incline sat a small graveyard, marked by a cross topped with a large, newly completed figure of Our Saviour. The wooden statue was crude and unpolished, carved by some inexperienced rural artisan, yet he had clearly modeled the form after a living subject—perhaps his own self, for the figure was painfully thin and gaunt.
source_text: At the steepest point of the hill there was a little burial-ground, with a Cross and a new large figure of Our Saviour on it; it was a poor figure in wood, done by some inexperienced rustic carver, but he had studied the figure from the life--his own life, maybe--for it was dreadfully spare and thin.

text: Doctor Manette’s quiet, unassuming lodgings sat on a hushed street corner a short distance from Soho Square. It was a crisp, pleasant Sunday afternoon, four full months since the treason trial that had long since faded from public interest and collective memory, swept far out to sea by the slow roll of passing weeks. Mr. Jarvis Lorry strolled along the sun-warmed streets from his home in Clerkenwell, bound for dinner with the doctor. Though he’d slipped back into being fully consumed by his work on several occasions, Lorry had grown into a close friend of the doctor’s, and that quiet street corner had become the brightest, most welcoming part of his daily life.
source_text: The quiet lodgings of Doctor Manette were in a quiet street-corner not far from Soho-square. On the afternoon of a certain fine Sunday when the waves of four months had rolled over the trial for treason, and carried it, as to the public interest and memory, far out to sea, Mr. Jarvis Lorry walked along the sunny streets from Clerkenwell where he lived, on his way to dine with the Doctor. After several relapses into business-absorption, Mr. Lorry had become the Doctor’s friend, and the quiet street-corner was the sunny part of his life.

text: Madame Vignon, arms crossed tight across her chest, perched on a splintered wooden stool in the thick, glowing morning heat, her gaze drifting between her own cider shop front and the rutted lane outside. Both spaces held clusters of loiterers, ragged and hollow-eyed with hunger, yet every face bore the unmistakeable glow of new power, seated squarely atop a lifetime of grinding suffering. Even the tattered wool nightcap, lopsided on the gaunt, unwashed head of the poorest wretch there, carried that same twisted unspoken message: “I know how impossible it has become for me, the man wearing this, to keep myself fed and breathing another week. But do you have any idea how simple it would be for me, the man wearing this, to snuff out your life before the day is done?” Every thin, bare arm that had gone months without paid work now had one task always close at hand: the ability to lash out and strike down an enemy. The calloused fingers of the women clicking their knitting needles by the door were sharp and cruel, honed by the knowledge that they could rip flesh as easily as they pulled loose yarn. The whole face of the Saint Marceau quarter had shifted; this visage had been beaten into shape over hundreds of years of oppression, and those final, brutal, recent blows had carved its final, fearsome expression deep into every stone and every resident’s face.
source_text: Madame Defarge, with her arms folded, sat in the morning light and heat, contemplating the wine-shop and the street. In both, there were several knots of loungers, squalid and miserable, but now with a manifest sense of power enthroned on their distress. The raggedest nightcap, awry on the wretchedest head, had this crooked significance in it: “I know how hard it has grown for me, the wearer of this, to support life in myself; but do you know how easy it has grown for me, the wearer of this, to destroy life in you?” Every lean bare arm, that had been without work before, had this work always ready for it now, that it could strike. The fingers of the knitting women were vicious, with the experience that they could tear. There was a change in the appearance of Saint Antoine; the image had been hammering into this for hundreds of years, and the last finishing blows had told mightily on the expression.

text: “I’ve lived with my darling— or rather, the darling has lived under my roof, and paid me for it, which she never once would have been permitted to do, you can take that as sworn fact, if I’d had the means to support either myself or her for free— ever since she turned ten years old. And it really is so very hard,” said Miss Pross.
source_text: “I have lived with the darling--or the darling has lived with me, and paid me for it; which she certainly should never have done, you may take your affidavit, if I could have afforded to keep either myself or her for nothing--since she was ten years old. And it’s really very hard,” said Miss Pross.

text: “Let me state this plainly again, Mr. Verrall, I am deeply aggrieved by the whole affair. I am frankly stunned you would pose such an absurd, out-of-line question. The man in question is corrupted by the most pestilent, godless doctrine of evil ever devised, who signed over all his lands and holdings to the very dregs of society, the same murderous hordes that carry out mass slaughter at a whim, and you dare ask why I am distressed that a man charged with educating young people knows him? Very well, I will give you your answer. I am distressed because I know a scoundrel like that carries corruption in every fiber of his being, ready to taint anyone who associates with him. That is why.”
source_text: “Then I tell you again, Mr. Darnay, I am sorry for it. I am sorry to hear you putting any such extraordinary questions. Here is a fellow, who, infected by the most pestilent and blasphemous code of devilry that ever was known, abandoned his property to the vilest scum of the earth that ever did murder by wholesale, and you ask me why I am sorry that a man who instructs youth knows him? Well, but I’ll answer you. I am sorry because I believe there is contamination in such a scoundrel. That’s why.”

text: I’d long noticed that the Voss brothers’ fierce family pride burned sharp at the fact that the younger of the two, as I privately referred to him, had crossed blades with a common farmer’s son, no more than a scrawny adolescent. The only thought that seemed to occupy either man was that the incident was a humiliating, absurd stain on their household’s honor. Every time our gazes locked, the younger brother’s cold stare made plain how thoroughly he loathed me, for I’d heard the full truth of the fight straight from the boy’s own mouth. He laid on charm and formal courtesy far thicker than his older sibling did, but I was not fooled for an instant. I could tell just as clearly that the older brother, for his part, considered me a tedious, unwelcome burden to be endured.
source_text: “I always observed that their pride bitterly resented the younger brother’s (as I call him) having crossed swords with a peasant, and that peasant a boy. The only consideration that appeared to affect the mind of either of them was the consideration that this was highly degrading to the family, and was ridiculous. As often as I caught the younger brother’s eyes, their expression reminded me that he disliked me deeply, for knowing what I knew from the boy. He was smoother and more polite to me than the elder; but I saw this. I also saw that I was an incumbrance in the mind of the elder, too.

text: He stays confined in that iron cage perched high on the bluff for days on end. The villagers steal glances at him only when they think no one notices, too cowed to stare openly. Their eyes always drift upward, from a safe distance, to the rough stone prison jutted from the crag; come evening, when the day’s labor is done and the whole hamlet gathers to swap gossip at the stone spring, every face tilts toward that high, cold fortress. Once, those same faces would all angle toward the stagecoach post down the lane, hungry for word of travelers and distant goings-on; now they fix only on the prison. They murmur soft by the spring that even though he is condemned to death, he will never be executed: petitions have been sent all the way to Paris, they say, laying out how he was driven wild with rage and grief by the death of his small child, that one petition even found its way to the King’s own hands. What would I know of such far-off matters? It is possible. Perhaps yes, perhaps no.
source_text: “He remains up there in his iron cage some days. The village looks at him by stealth, for it is afraid. But it always looks up, from a distance, at the prison on the crag; and in the evening, when the work of the day is achieved and it assembles to gossip at the fountain, all faces are turned towards the prison. Formerly, they were turned towards the posting-house; now, they are turned towards the prison. They whisper at the fountain, that although condemned to death he will not be executed; they say that petitions have been presented in Paris, showing that he was enraged and made mad by the death of his child; they say that a petition has been presented to the King himself. What do I know? It is possible. Perhaps yes, perhaps no.”
