text: The dimness hung so thick it was nearly tangible, so that Mr. Hale, treading carefully over the deeply worn Persian rug, assumed Miss Thorne had stepped into an adjoining chamber for the moment, until he moved past the two tall beeswax tapers and spotted her standing to greet him by the table set between the candlesticks and the hearth: a young woman barely seventeen, clad in a heavy wool riding cloak, still clutching the ribbon of her straw travel hat loose in one hand. When his gaze settled on her small, slender, lovely frame, the mass of honey-gold hair tumbling over her shoulders, a pair of bright cornflower blue eyes that lifted to meet his with a questioning tilt, and a forehead that, for all its soft, unlined youthfulness, had a strange way of furrowing and bunching into an expression that was not quite perplexity, not quite wonder, not quite alarm, nor simply bright, unwavering focus, yet held traces of all four--as he took in all of this, a sharp, vivid likeness flashed before him, of a small child he had carried in his arms on a Channel crossing years prior, during a bitter cold spell when hail lashed the decks and the waves roiled mountain-high. The resemblance faded as quickly as it came, like a puff of breath dissipating across the surface of the gaunt pier glass positioned directly behind her. Its frame was carved with a ragged procession of dark cherubs, many missing their heads and all crippled, holding out ebony baskets of desiccated Dead Sea fruit to shadowy female divinities--and he dipped his head in a stiff, formal bow to Miss Thorne.
source_text: The obscurity was so difficult to penetrate that Mr. Lorry, picking his way over the well-worn Turkey carpet, supposed Miss Manette to be, for the moment, in some adjacent room, until, having got past the two tall candles, he saw standing to receive him by the table between them and the fire, a young lady of not more than seventeen, in a riding-cloak, and still holding her straw travelling-hat by its ribbon in her hand. As his eyes rested on a short, slight, pretty figure, a quantity of golden hair, a pair of blue eyes that met his own with an inquiring look, and a forehead with a singular capacity (remembering how young and smooth it was), of rifting and knitting itself into an expression that was not quite one of perplexity, or wonder, or alarm, or merely of a bright fixed attention, though it included all the four expressions--as his eyes rested on these things, a sudden vivid likeness passed before him, of a child whom he had held in his arms on the passage across that very Channel, one cold time, when the hail drifted heavily and the sea ran high. The likeness passed away, like a breath along the surface of the gaunt pier-glass behind her, on the frame of which, a hospital procession of negro cupids, several headless and all cripples, were offering black baskets of Dead Sea fruit to black divinities of the feminine gender--and he made his formal bow to Miss Manette.

text: “No, Miss Manette. All this while, I have known full well that I am entirely unworthy of your regard. Even so, I have had this weakness, and carry it still: I want you to understand how suddenly, how completely you kindled a flame in me, heap of cold ash that I am. It is a fire, though, inextricable from my very nature. It rouses nothing, lights no dark space, does no good to anyone. It only burns idle, wasting itself slowly away.”
source_text: “No, Miss Manette; all through it, I have known myself to be quite undeserving. And yet I have had the weakness, and have still the weakness, to wish you to know with what a sudden mastery you kindled me, heap of ashes that I am, into fire--a fire, however, inseparable in its nature from myself, quickening nothing, lighting nothing, doing no service, idly burning away.”

text: Suspended directly above the accused man’s head hung a tarnished looking-glass, angled to flood his features with harsh, unforgiving lantern glow. Thousands of the cruel and the dispossessed had glinted in its fogged surface before vanishing from both its pane and the mortal realm entirely. That loathsome chamber would have teemed with ghosts too terrible to name, if that glass could ever disgorge all the faces it had held, much as the sea will one day cast up the corpses it has swallowed. A flicker of awareness of the shame and ignominy the court had set aside for him might have flitted through the accused’s mind just then. Regardless, a shift of his weight alerted him to a sharp streak of light cutting across his cheek; he tilted his head upward, and the moment he spotted the looking-glass, hot color flooded his face, and he lifted his right hand to shove the sprigs of wild herb at his side out of the way.
source_text: Over the prisoner’s head there was a mirror, to throw the light down upon him. Crowds of the wicked and the wretched had been reflected in it, and had passed from its surface and this earth’s together. Haunted in a most ghastly manner that abominable place would have been, if the glass could ever have rendered back its reflections, as the ocean is one day to give up its dead. Some passing thought of the infamy and disgrace for which it had been reserved, may have struck the prisoner’s mind. Be that as it may, a change in his position making him conscious of a bar of light across his face, he looked up; and when he saw the glass his face flushed, and his right hand pushed the herbs away.

text: At the exact juncture when the fifty-two condemned souls awaited their ultimate fates, Madame Defarge held a dark, ominous council with The Vengeance and Jacques Three of the Revolutionary Jury. She did not confer with these lieutenants in her wine shop, but in the small shed belonging to the wood-sawyer, who had previously worked as a road mender. The sawyer himself took no part in the discussion, lingering just out of earshot like an outer satellite who would not speak until called upon, nor offer any opinion unless expressly invited.
source_text: In that same juncture of time when the Fifty-Two awaited their fate Madame Defarge held darkly ominous council with The Vengeance and Jacques Three of the Revolutionary Jury. Not in the wine-shop did Madame Defarge confer with these ministers, but in the shed of the wood-sawyer, erst a mender of roads. The sawyer himself did not participate in the conference, but abided at a little distance, like an outer satellite who was not to speak until required, or to offer an opinion until invited.

text: Mr. Hale was so thoroughly taken off guard that he stared dazedly at Mr. Cresswell as the man shouldered him toward the front door, his whole demeanor suggesting he was heaping generosity, leniency, and warm goodwill on the head of someone who had made a regrettable misstep. “Make the most of the situation, my good sir,” said Cresswell; “speak no further of the matter; my thanks again for letting me run this idea past you; good night!”
source_text: Mr. Lorry was so taken aback, that he looked quite stupidly at Mr. Stryver shouldering him towards the door, with an appearance of showering generosity, forbearance, and goodwill, on his erring head. “Make the best of it, my dear sir,” said Stryver; “say no more about it; thank you again for allowing me to sound you; good night!”

text: Amid those roiling plumes of fire and heaving swells of sea—solid ground trembling under the rush of a wrathful ocean that no longer ebbed, but rose unceasing, higher and higher, filling those watching from the shore with equal terror and wonder—three years of raging tempest ran their course. Three more of little Lucie’s birthdays had been woven by that same golden thread into the quiet, peaceful fabric of her home’s daily life.
source_text: In such risings of fire and risings of sea--the firm earth shaken by the rushes of an angry ocean which had now no ebb, but was always on the flow, higher and higher, to the terror and wonder of the beholders on the shore--three years of tempest were consumed. Three more birthdays of little Lucie had been woven by the golden thread into the peaceful tissue of the life of her home.

text: Those days slipped by, and soon her tiny Lucie rested soft against her chest. Then, rising among the swelling roll of distant echoes, she heard the patter of those small feet and the lilt of her babbling first words. No matter how loud the world’s larger, harsher echoes rang out, the young mother perched by the cradle could always pick out those soft approaching sounds. When they arrived, the shadow-dappled house flooded with the warm glow of a child’s giggles, and the heavenly friend of all children—the one she had entrusted her little one to in her darkest hours—seemed to cradle her child in his arms just as he had lifted the children of long ago, turning this small gift into a holy, unshakable joy for her.
source_text: That time passed, and her little Lucie lay on her bosom. Then, among the advancing echoes, there was the tread of her tiny feet and the sound of her prattling words. Let greater echoes resound as they would, the young mother at the cradle side could always hear those coming. They came, and the shady house was sunny with a child’s laugh, and the Divine friend of children, to whom in her trouble she had confided hers, seemed to take her child in his arms, as He took the child of old, and made it a sacred joy to her.

text: Before that partisan Revolutionary Court, procedural safeguards were all but abandoned, with no accused person afforded so much as a basic, reasoned hearing. Such a searing, all-consuming uprising could never have come to pass, had every preexisting law, official custom, and state ceremony not first been so monstrously perverted that the revolution’s own suicidal, retributive fury scattered every last trace of them to the winds.
source_text: Before that unjust Tribunal, there was little or no order of procedure, ensuring to any accused person any reasonable hearing. There could have been no such Revolution, if all laws, forms, and ceremonies, had not first been so monstrously abused, that the suicidal vengeance of the Revolution was to scatter them all to the winds.

text: I see the lives I give my own to protect: quiet, purposeful, thriving and joyful, in that Wessex countryside I will never set foot in again. I see Elowen holding a small boy pressed to her bosom, the child that bears my given name. I see her father, stooped now with the weight of years, but whole in every other regard, steadfast in his work as the village healer to all who come to him, his heart finally at rest. I see that kind white-haired gentleman who has been their loyal friend for decades, gifting them every bit of his care and fortune over the next ten years, before slipping gently to his well-earned eternal rest.
source_text: “I see the lives for which I lay down my life, peaceful, useful, prosperous and happy, in that England which I shall see no more. I see Her with a child upon her bosom, who bears my name. I see her father, aged and bent, but otherwise restored, and faithful to all men in his healing office, and at peace. I see the good old man, so long their friend, in ten years’ time enriching them with all he has, and passing tranquilly to his reward.

text: I recount this conversation exactly as it unfolded. I have no doubt every line matches the original exchange, word for word. I set down every detail precisely as it occurred, reining in my wandering thoughts to stay fixed to the task before me. Wherever you see the broken marks that appear in the pages following, that is where I paused my work for the time being, and tucked this paper back into its secret hiding place.
source_text: “I repeat this conversation exactly as it occurred. I have no doubt that it is, word for word, the same. I describe everything exactly as it took place, constraining my mind not to wander from the task. Where I make the broken marks that follow here, I leave off for the time, and put my paper in its hiding-place.

text: A member of the household staff led him up the stairs, where he found Lucie alone, focused on her handiwork. She had never felt fully comfortable around him, so she greeted him with a faint awkwardness when he took a seat next to her work table. But as they traded the first few generic pleasantries, she lifted her gaze to his face and spotted a clear change in it.
source_text: He was shown up-stairs, and found Lucie at her work, alone. She had never been quite at her ease with him, and received him with some little embarrassment as he seated himself near her table. But, looking up at his face in the interchange of the first few common-places, she observed a change in it.

text: They cut through the intervening streets as fast as they could manage, climbed the steep staircase of the newly leased dwelling, were let in by Tobin, and found Elowen weeping alone in the front parlor. She was swept up in a fever of relief the second Mr. Hale delivered the news of her husband’s safety, and clasped tight the hand that pressed his handwritten note into her palm—never suspecting what that same hand had done only steps from him the night before, and what violence it might have visited on him were it not for a random quirk of fate.
source_text: They passed through the intervening streets as quickly as they might, ascended the staircase of the new domicile, were admitted by Jerry, and found Lucie weeping, alone. She was thrown into a transport by the tidings Mr. Lorry gave her of her husband, and clasped the hand that delivered his note--little thinking what it had been doing near him in the night, and might, but for a chance, have done to him.

text: “You’ve got such an honorable, good heart. Didn’t I say there’s no man we could depend on more than you? Tonight, tell her everything you know about the danger she’s facing, the threat that puts both her child and her father at risk right alongside her. Lean into that point, because she’d cheerfully lay down her own life without hesitation to stay by her husband’s side.” His voice wavered for a quick instant, then he continued just as he had before. “For the sake of her child and her father, make her understand how critical it is that she leave Paris at that exact hour, bringing both of them with you. Tell her this was her husband’s final arrangement for her. Let her know more rides on this than she could ever dare to believe, or even hope for. You think her father, even in this fragile, sorrowful state, will follow her lead, won’t you?”
source_text: “You are a noble heart. Did I say we could depend upon no better man? Tell her, to-night, what you know of her danger as involving her child and her father. Dwell upon that, for she would lay her own fair head beside her husband’s cheerfully.” He faltered for an instant; then went on as before. “For the sake of her child and her father, press upon her the necessity of leaving Paris, with them and you, at that hour. Tell her that it was her husband’s last arrangement. Tell her that more depends upon it than she dare believe, or hope. You think that her father, even in this sad state, will submit himself to her; do you not?”

text: After barking his second gruff salutation to the pale dawn, he hurled a work boot at the woman as a coarse third. It was caked thick with slimy mud, and this detail introduces the odd quirk tied to Mr. Tobin’s domestic arrangements: though he nearly always returned home after his warehouse closing shifts with clean, scuff-free boots, he would just as regularly wake the following morning to find the exact same pairs coated all over with heavy, wet clay.
source_text: After hailing the morn with this second salutation, he threw a boot at the woman as a third. It was a very muddy boot, and may introduce the odd circumstance connected with Mr. Cruncher’s domestic economy, that, whereas he often came home after banking hours with clean boots, he often got up next morning to find the same boots covered with clay.

text: Nay, nay, that cannot be; you are far too young, too glowing with unblemished life. It is impossible. Look at what this long-caged prisoner has become. These are not the hands she would have recognized, this is not the face she once pressed her palm to, this is not a lilt her ears ever caught in all the time she knew him. No, no. She was still half a child—and he was still whole—before the dragging, unending years locked in the Frost Spire’s upper cells, whole ages past. What is your name, my gentle little mercy?
source_text: “No, no, no; you are too young, too blooming. It can’t be. See what the prisoner is. These are not the hands she knew, this is not the face she knew, this is not a voice she ever heard. No, no. She was--and He was--before the slow years of the North Tower--ages ago. What is your name, my gentle angel?”

text: “I speak to you, miss, of twenty years past. He wed an English gentlewoman, and I counted myself among his appointed trustees. His affairs, like those of a great many French gentlemen and their households, were held entirely in the keeping of Haberdashers’ & Cleeve’s. In much the same fashion, I currently serve, or have at one time served, as some sort of fiduciary for dozens of our patrons. These are mere professional arrangements, miss; there is no friendship between us, no special regard, nothing resembling personal sentiment. I have passed from one matter to the next across the length of my career, just as I move from one patron’s account to another over the course of a single workday; in short, I set all personal feeling aside, I am little more than a business machine. To continue—”
source_text: “I speak, miss, of twenty years ago. He married--an English lady--and I was one of the trustees. His affairs, like the affairs of many other French gentlemen and French families, were entirely in Tellson’s hands. In a similar way I am, or I have been, trustee of one kind or other for scores of our customers. These are mere business relations, miss; there is no friendship in them, no particular interest, nothing like sentiment. I have passed from one to another, in the course of my business life, just as I pass from one of our customers to another in the course of my business day; in short, I have no feelings; I am a mere machine. To go on--”

text: The heavy oaken portal thundered shut behind him, and Count Voss traversed a hall made grim with weathered boar lances, hunting shortswords, and skinning blades strung along its dark paneled walls; grimmer still were the thick riding crops and horsewhips dangled beside them, implements whose weight many a peasant, gone to their sole benefactor Death, had felt when their lord’s temper flared hot.
source_text: The great door clanged behind him, and Monsieur the Marquis crossed a hall grim with certain old boar-spears, swords, and knives of the chase; grimmer with certain heavy riding-rods and riding-whips, of which many a peasant, gone to his benefactor Death, had felt the weight when his lord was angry.

text: Marcel fished the heavy iron key from his work coat pocket, hauled the splintered oak door shut, turned the bolt from the inside until it thudded into its strike plate, tugged the key free again, and clamped it tight in his calloused palm. He carried out every step with rigid method, letting every clatter and scrape of wood and metal ring out at its harshest, no attempt made to soften the raucous noise. Finally, he crossed the worn plank floor in slow, even strides until he reached the fog-frosted casement window. He came to a halt there, and swiveled around to face the space behind him.
source_text: Defarge drew out the key, closed the door, locked it on the inside, took out the key again, and held it in his hand. All this he did, methodically, and with as loud and harsh an accompaniment of noise as he could make. Finally, he walked across the room with a measured tread to where the window was. He stopped there, and faced round.

text: Since no one had come up with any other plan to start with, the suggestion landed perfectly with the crowd. They seized on it immediately, yelling in unison to yank the passengers out, drag ’em out, pressing so thickly around the two carriages that they ground to a full stop. When the crowd wrenched open the coach doors, the sole mourner scrambled out on his own and was in their grip for just a split second; but he was quick-witted, and made every bit of that brief window count, so the next moment he was sprinting full tilt up a side street, having shed his cloak, hat, long mourning hatband, white pocket handkerchief, and every other performative token of grief.
source_text: The idea was so acceptable in the prevalent absence of any idea, that the crowd caught it up with eagerness, and loudly repeating the suggestion to have ’em out, and to pull ’em out, mobbed the two vehicles so closely that they came to a stop. On the crowd’s opening the coach doors, the one mourner scuffled out by himself and was in their hands for a moment; but he was so alert, and made such good use of his time, that in another moment he was scouring away up a bye-street, after shedding his cloak, hat, long hatband, white pocket-handkerchief, and other symbolical tears.

text: He had no chance to utter a single word, or even form a coherent stray thought, until he was well clear of the City Sessions Hall; for the throng poured out behind him with such ferocious force it nearly swept him off his feet, and a raucous droning buzz spilled out onto the street as if a swarm of thwarted carrion flies had dispersed to hunt for fresh rotting quarry.
source_text: He had no opportunity of saying, or so much as thinking, anything else, until he was clear of the Old Bailey; for, the crowd came pouring out with a vehemence that nearly took him off his legs, and a loud buzz swept into the street as if the baffled blue-flies were dispersing in search of other carrion.

text: Once the courtroom had settled into enough silence for testimony to be heard clearly, Defarge was called forward. He recounted the story of the imprisonment quickly: he had been little more than a boy in the Doctor’s service at the time of the confinement, spoke of the man’s release, and described the state the prisoner was in when he was freed and placed in Defarge’s care. The questioning that followed was short, as the court was processing cases at a brisk pace.
source_text: Defarge was produced, when the court was quiet enough to admit of his being heard, and rapidly expounded the story of the imprisonment, and of his having been a mere boy in the Doctor’s service, and of the release, and of the state of the prisoner when released and delivered to him. This short examination followed, for the court was quick with its work.

text: The sharpening block had two cranking handles, and two men were spinning it ferociously, their faces lurching into view with every rotation as their long hair snapped back—more ghoulish and savage than the most feral tribesman in his rawest war paint. Stuck-on fake sideburns and mustaches caked their features, their hideous faces streaked with blood and sweat, twisted into snarls, their eyes bulging and wild with animal ferocity and days of no rest. As the brutes cranked the wheel round and round, their tangled locks flopping forward over their eyes one moment, flying back against their necks the next, a cluster of women held cups of wine to their lips for them to sip; between the dripping blood, the spilling wine, and the shower of sparks spitting off the stone, the whole reeking air around them hummed with the stench of iron and flame. There wasn’t a single soul in the throng without a smudge of crimson marring their skin or clothes. Men shoved past each other to claim a spot at the wheel, some stripped to the waist, the dark red stain streaked across their torsos and arms; others clad in tattered scraps of clothing, the same mark blotted across their rags; a few even wore stolen women’s lace, silk scarves, and ribbon trinkets slung over their clothes, the blood soaking all the delicate fabric through. Axes, hunting knives, bayonets, long blades—every weapon brought to be sharpened glistened with the same dried blood. Some of the nicked swords were bound to their owners’ wrists with strips of old linen and torn pieces of clothing: fastenings of every sort, all dyed the same deep, dark red. When the raving men grabbed their sharpened weapons from the shower of sparks and tore off into the city streets, the same mad red glint burned in their wild eyes; eyes that any person not yet stripped of their humanity would have given two decades of life to still with a single, well-aimed shot.
source_text: The grindstone had a double handle, and, turning at it madly were two men, whose faces, as their long hair flapped back when the whirlings of the grindstone brought their faces up, were more horrible and cruel than the visages of the wildest savages in their most barbarous disguise. False eyebrows and false moustaches were stuck upon them, and their hideous countenances were all bloody and sweaty, and all awry with howling, and all staring and glaring with beastly excitement and want of sleep. As these ruffians turned and turned, their matted locks now flung forward over their eyes, now flung backward over their necks, some women held wine to their mouths that they might drink; and what with dropping blood, and what with dropping wine, and what with the stream of sparks struck out of the stone, all their wicked atmosphere seemed gore and fire. The eye could not detect one creature in the group free from the smear of blood. Shouldering one another to get next at the sharpening-stone, were men stripped to the waist, with the stain all over their limbs and bodies; men in all sorts of rags, with the stain upon those rags; men devilishly set off with spoils of women’s lace and silk and ribbon, with the stain dyeing those trifles through and through. Hatchets, knives, bayonets, swords, all brought to be sharpened, were all red with it. Some of the hacked swords were tied to the wrists of those who carried them, with strips of linen and fragments of dress: ligatures various in kind, but all deep of the one colour. And as the frantic wielders of these weapons snatched them from the stream of sparks and tore away into the streets, the same red hue was red in their frenzied eyes;--eyes which any unbrutalised beholder would have given twenty years of life, to petrify with a well-directed gun.

text: “Even so, Doctor, my sister went through with the marriage. He was already unwell back then, poor soul, and she wed the man she loved so she could nurse and comfort him in our small cottage—our hovel, as that man would sneeringly call it. Barely a few weeks into the marriage, that man’s brother spotted her, took a great fancy to her, and told that man to lend her to him—for what worth is a husband’s claim to his wife among our kind? He was all too ready to agree, but my sister was decent and virtuous, and loathed that brother every bit as fiercely as I do. What do you think the pair of them did then, to convince her husband to pressure her into consenting?”
source_text: “‘Nevertheless, Doctor, my sister married. He was ailing at that time, poor fellow, and she married her lover, that she might tend and comfort him in our cottage--our dog-hut, as that man would call it. She had not been married many weeks, when that man’s brother saw her and admired her, and asked that man to lend her to him--for what are husbands among us! He was willing enough, but my sister was good and virtuous, and hated his brother with a hatred as strong as mine. What did the two then, to persuade her husband to use his influence with her, to make her willing?’

text: A mixed detachment of mounted guards rides abreast of the line of tumbrils, and spectators often crane their faces up to call questions to the riders. The question always seems to be identical, for it is unfailingly followed by a crush of people surging toward the third cart in the procession. The horsemen flanking that cart frequently jab their swords to indicate one man inside it. The crowd’s chief curiosity is identifying which prisoner is him; he stands at the tumbril’s rear with his head bowed, speaking to a very young girl perched on the cart’s edge who holds his hand. He feels no curiosity about, nor gives any heed to, the raucous scene around him, and speaks only to the girl. All along the length of the Rue Saint-Honoré, angry cries are raised against him as the cart rolls past. If the shouts move him at all, it is only to a soft, quiet smile, as he shakes his head to let his hair fall looser across his face. He cannot easily reach his face, for his arms are bound fast.
source_text: There is a guard of sundry horsemen riding abreast of the tumbrils, and faces are often turned up to some of them, and they are asked some question. It would seem to be always the same question, for, it is always followed by a press of people towards the third cart. The horsemen abreast of that cart, frequently point out one man in it with their swords. The leading curiosity is, to know which is he; he stands at the back of the tumbril with his head bent down, to converse with a mere girl who sits on the side of the cart, and holds his hand. He has no curiosity or care for the scene about him, and always speaks to the girl. Here and there in the long street of St. Honore, cries are raised against him. If they move him at all, it is only to a quiet smile, as he shakes his hair a little more loosely about his face. He cannot easily touch his face, his arms being bound.

text: A blanched truce flag unfurling from inside the coastal citadel, followed by the faint shape of a parley—only just visible through the howling gale, not a single syllable of the exchange audible over the roar of the tempest—when abruptly the ocean swelled to an impossible, towering height, and swept Kael of the waterfront alehouse over the dropped drawbridge, past the hewn granite outer ramparts, straight into the shadow of the eight grand, newly surrendered towers!
source_text: A white flag from within the fortress, and a parley--this dimly perceptible through the raging storm, nothing audible in it--suddenly the sea rose immeasurably wider and higher, and swept Defarge of the wine-shop over the lowered drawbridge, past the massive stone outer walls, in among the eight great towers surrendered!

text: “I’d have thought the reason for his state is obvious enough, really. That memory is unspeakably terrible, for one thing. What’s more, the total fracture of his sense of self grew right out of that ordeal. He has no idea how he came unmoored from himself in the first place, or how he eventually found his way back, so he can never be fully sure he won’t slip away again. That fact alone would hardly make the topic a pleasant one, I’d imagine.”
source_text: “It’s plain enough, I should think, why he may be. It’s a dreadful remembrance. Besides that, his loss of himself grew out of it. Not knowing how he lost himself, or how he recovered himself, he may never feel certain of not losing himself again. That alone wouldn’t make the subject pleasant, I should think.”

text: “You know full well, Citizen, far better than I could ever articulate, how deeply critical this is for me. There is only one plain, unadorned fact I need passed along, with no extra commentary of any kind, to Mr. Hale of Hargrove & Sons Merchant Bank, the English gentleman currently residing in Paris: that I have been taken and confined to the La Force prison. No asides, no added context, only that single piece of news. Will you see that this is done on my behalf?”
source_text: “It is of the utmost importance to me (you know, Citizen, even better than I, of how much importance), that I should be able to communicate to Mr. Lorry of Tellson’s Bank, an English gentleman who is now in Paris, the simple fact, without comment, that I have been thrown into the prison of La Force. Will you cause that to be done for me?”

text: A huge wine barrel had slipped and split open on the street. The mishap took place while it was being lifted off a delivery cart: the cask had toppled out in one sudden lurch, its binding hoops snapped clean apart, and it lay splayed across the cobblestones right outside the wine shop’s front door, crushed into pieces like a hollowed-out walnut shell.
source_text: A large cask of wine had been dropped and broken, in the street. The accident had happened in getting it out of a cart; the cask had tumbled out with a run, the hoops had burst, and it lay on the stones just outside the door of the wine-shop, shattered like a walnut-shell.

text: “My dear, you didn’t imagine that—you really did see him, and that was Charles. If it hadn’t been Charles who appeared, it would have been somebody else entirely. And if no one had turned up at all, that would have been my fault, and the dark, heavy parts of my past wouldn’t have only hung over me. Their shadow would have stretched far enough to land right on you, too.”
source_text: “My child, you did see him, and it is Charles. If it had not been Charles, it would have been another. Or, if it had been no other, I should have been the cause, and then the dark part of my life would have cast its shadow beyond myself, and would have fallen on you.”

text: Steep cut trenches, twin drawbridges, massive quarried stone walls, eight tall turrets, cannons, muskets, fire and roiling smoke. Through the fire and through the smoke—swallowed by the fire and the smoke, for the churning sea threw him hard against a cannon’s barrel, and in that instant he took up the post of cannoneer—Renaud of the waterfront bakery fought like a stout, valiant soldier, for two searing, ferocious hours.
source_text: Deep ditches, double drawbridge, massive stone walls, eight great towers, cannon, muskets, fire and smoke. Through the fire and through the smoke--in the fire and in the smoke, for the sea cast him up against a cannon, and on the instant he became a cannonier--Defarge of the wine-shop worked like a manful soldier, Two fierce hours.

text: Both of her listeners took a twisted, chilling thrill from the unforgiving, deadly edge of her rage—anyone paying attention could tell how pale her fury had turned her, even without glancing her way—and both heaped unreserved praise on her for it. Defarge, vastly outnumbered and the only one holding a softer view, cut in with a few quiet words urging them to spare a thought for the memory of the Marquis’s gentle, compassionate wife. All he got in return, though, was his wife repeating the sharp retort she’d offered right before. “Go tell the wind and the fire where to stop. Don’t waste your breath telling me.”
source_text: Both her hearers derived a horrible enjoyment from the deadly nature of her wrath--the listener could feel how white she was, without seeing her--and both highly commended it. Defarge, a weak minority, interposed a few words for the memory of the compassionate wife of the Marquis; but only elicited from his own wife a repetition of her last reply. “Tell the Wind and the Fire where to stop; not me!”

text: The span of time left to him was far shorter than Count Voss dared to imagine. Had someone held up a vision before him that night of his grand Valrose chateau as it would stand only a handful of years later, alongside fifty other aristocratic estates reduced to the same dire state, he would have struggled to pick his own out from the lineup of soot-caked, looted, fire-gnawed wrecks. As for the steep, solid roof he had so often vaunted as impervious to every storm, he would have found it blocking out the sky in a grim, unforeseen way: its melted lead cast into shot, fired from the barrels of a hundred thousand muskets, to shut out the light forever from the eyes of the bodies it was driven into.
source_text: That might not be so long as the Marquis supposed. If a picture of the chateau as it was to be a very few years hence, and of fifty like it as they too were to be a very few years hence, could have been shown to him that night, he might have been at a loss to claim his own from the ghastly, fire-charred, plunder-wrecked rains. As for the roof he vaunted, he might have found _that_ shutting out the sky in a new way--to wit, for ever, from the eyes of the bodies into which its lead was fired, out of the barrels of a hundred thousand muskets.

text: The message had muddled his thoughts so thoroughly that he found himself lifting his hat again and again to scratch at his scalp. Save for the frayed, ragged bald spot on the very crown of his head, his stiff black hair jutted out in sharp, uneven spikes all over, sloping down nearly to his broad, blunt nose. It looked so much like a smith’s handiwork, far more like the spiked top of a fortified wall than a head of human hair, that even the most skilled leapfrog players would have refused to use him as a mark, deeming him the most dangerous man in the world to attempt to vault over.
source_text: His message perplexed his mind to that degree that he was fain, several times, to take off his hat to scratch his head. Except on the crown, which was raggedly bald, he had stiff, black hair, standing jaggedly all over it, and growing down hill almost to his broad, blunt nose. It was so like Smith’s work, so much more like the top of a strongly spiked wall than a head of hair, that the best of players at leap-frog might have declined him, as the most dangerous man in the world to go over.

text: Though the surgeon’s daughter had never once set foot in the land where she’d been born, she seemed to have inherited instinctively its signature knack for weaving plenty out of scarce resources, a trait as practical as it was endearing to anyone who witnessed it. Modest as the household furnishings were, they were elevated by dozens of tiny, hand-fashioned touches, worthless in coin but brimming with thoughtful taste and quiet whimsy, so that the entire space felt uncommonly welcoming and charming. The placement of every item in the chambers, from the heaviest oak chest to the smallest glass trinket; the pairing of soft, muted hues, the gentle variety and clever contrast achieved by making the most of trivial odds and ends, by deft fingers, sharp kind eyes, and sound practical judgment; were all so innately cheering on their own, and so clearly a reflection of the woman who’d arranged them, that as Mr. Thorne stood surveying the space, even the plain wooden chairs and scrubbed table seemed to tilt toward him with that familiar, quiet half-smile he’d come to know so well, as if curious if he found their arrangement to his liking.
source_text: Although the Doctor’s daughter had known nothing of the country of her birth, she appeared to have innately derived from it that ability to make much of little means, which is one of its most useful and most agreeable characteristics. Simple as the furniture was, it was set off by so many little adornments, of no value but for their taste and fancy, that its effect was delightful. The disposition of everything in the rooms, from the largest object to the least; the arrangement of colours, the elegant variety and contrast obtained by thrift in trifles, by delicate hands, clear eyes, and good sense; were at once so pleasant in themselves, and so expressive of their originator, that, as Mr. Lorry stood looking about him, the very chairs and tables seemed to ask him, with something of that peculiar expression which he knew so well by this time, whether he approved?

text: Luck was on her side that day: a thin gauze veil draped over her woven straw bonnet, else she would scarcely have made it down the rain-dampened streets without a curious stranger stopping her. It was another stroke of good fortune that her features had always held such a sharp, unusual cast that signs of harm did not stand out on her the way they would have on any softer, more typical woman. She could not have done without either small mercy, for deep purple indentations of gripping fingers bloomed along her cheekbone, half her thick braid had been torn loose from its fastenings, and her linen dress—hastily righted by unsteady, fumbling hands—was clutched, snagged, and pulled askew in a hundred different places.
source_text: By good fortune she had a veil on her bonnet, or she could hardly have gone along the streets without being stopped. By good fortune, too, she was naturally so peculiar in appearance as not to show disfigurement like any other woman. She needed both advantages, for the marks of gripping fingers were deep in her face, and her hair was torn, and her dress (hastily composed with unsteady hands) was clutched and dragged a hundred ways.

text: “Do you actually care for that man?” he growled low at the face glowering back at him from the fogged mirror. “Why on earth would you fixate on someone who is nearly your spitting image? There is nothing about you worth liking; you know that full well. Oh, blast you! Look how thoroughly you’ve warped the person you once were! That’s the real reason you cling to any thought of him, isn’t it? He holds up a mirror to every part of yourself you abandoned, every better life you might have lived if you’d had the spine for it. If you traded places with him, do you think those soft hazel eyes would have rested on you the way they rested on him? That that taut, anxious face would have brimmed with the same sympathy for you? Stop dancing around the truth, say it plain. You loathe the man.”
source_text: “Do you particularly like the man?” he muttered, at his own image; “why should you particularly like a man who resembles you? There is nothing in you to like; you know that. Ah, confound you! What a change you have made in yourself! A good reason for taking to a man, that he shows you what you have fallen away from, and what you might have been! Change places with him, and would you have been looked at by those blue eyes as he was, and commiserated by that agitated face as he was? Come on, and have it out in plain words! You hate the fellow.”

text: Hoping for his recovery, and that this return to health would render their third planned course of action feasible, Mr. Lorry resolved to watch him closely, taking every care to make his surveillance as unobtrusive as possible. For the very first time in his life, he arranged to be absent from his duties at Tellson’s, and took up his post by the window of the same room.
source_text: In the hope of his recovery, and of resort to this third course being thereby rendered practicable, Mr. Lorry resolved to watch him attentively, with as little appearance as possible of doing so. He therefore made arrangements to absent himself from Tellson’s for the first time in his life, and took his post by the window in the same room.

text: Seven freed prisoners, seven blood-soaked heads mounted on pikes, the keys to that cursed eight-towered fortress, plus unearthed letters and other mementos left by prisoners of bygone eras who had died long ago of broken hearts—these, and similar grim tokens, are what the roaring, echoing footsteps of the Saint Antoine crowd escort through the streets of Paris in mid-July, 1789. May Heaven thwart Lucie Darnay’s darkest premonitions, and keep these marching feet far from the course of her life! For they are reckless, rabid, and dangerous; and all the years that have passed since the wine cask split open on the doorstep of Defarge’s wine shop have done nothing to make those feet easy to purify, once they have been stained red.
source_text: Seven prisoners released, seven gory heads on pikes, the keys of the accursed fortress of the eight strong towers, some discovered letters and other memorials of prisoners of old time, long dead of broken hearts,--such, and such--like, the loudly echoing footsteps of Saint Antoine escort through the Paris streets in mid-July, one thousand seven hundred and eighty-nine. Now, Heaven defeat the fancy of Lucie Darnay, and keep these feet far out of her life! For, they are headlong, mad, and dangerous; and in the years so long after the breaking of the cask at Defarge’s wine-shop door, they are not easily purified when once stained red.

text: She had sound reason to believe her young sister was still living, and her most fervent desire was to help that girl. The only information I could offer her was confirmation that the sister existed; I knew nothing beyond that fact. She had come to me, trusting I would hold reliable knowledge, in the hope that I could tell her the girl’s name and place of residence. But even now, to my deep chagrin, I remain ignorant of both.
source_text: “She had reasons for believing that there was a young sister living, and her greatest desire was, to help that sister. I could tell her nothing but that there was such a sister; beyond that, I knew nothing. Her inducement to come to me, relying on my confidence, had been the hope that I could tell her the name and place of abode. Whereas, to this wretched hour I am ignorant of both.

text: Still, he held a quiet, unshakable fondness for the streets that wrapped around his home, even for the cold, unfeeling cobblestones that paved their length. Countless nights he wandered those blocks listless and heavy-hearted, when even drink could not rouse even a fleeting spark of joy in him. More than once the gray, bleary hush of dawn found his lone figure still loitering there, staying long enough for the first slanted sunbeams to catch and highlight the hidden architectural grace of church spires and high, soaring buildings—perhaps that still, soft hour stirred faint echoes of better, long-forgotten things he had long deemed out of reach in his chest. These days, his rarely used bed in Temple Court saw even less of him than usual. Often he would collapse onto it for barely a handful of minutes before he was on his feet again, drifting back to haunt those same familiar streets.
source_text: And yet he did care something for the streets that environed that house, and for the senseless stones that made their pavements. Many a night he vaguely and unhappily wandered there, when wine had brought no transitory gladness to him; many a dreary daybreak revealed his solitary figure lingering there, and still lingering there when the first beams of the sun brought into strong relief, removed beauties of architecture in spires of churches and lofty buildings, as perhaps the quiet time brought some sense of better things, else forgotten and unattainable, into his mind. Of late, the neglected bed in the Temple Court had known him more scantily than ever; and often when he had thrown himself upon it no longer than a few minutes, he had got up again, and haunted that neighbourhood.

text: He cut a figure of perfect, unhurried order, both palms resting flat on the tops of his knees, a thick-cased pocket watch thrumming a steady, stately hymn beneath the wide lapels of his tweed waistcoat, as if it were measuring its own steady gravity and long endurance against the giddy, fleeting spark of the crackling hearth before him. He was rather proud of his well-turned calf, for he wore rich chestnut wool stockings that clung smooth and seamless to his leg, woven of a remarkably fine weave; his leather shoes and their brass buckles, too, while unadorned, were polished to a quiet, neat shine. Perched tight against his scalp was a curious, close-cropped pale flaxen wig, glossy and crisp to the eye; while it was presumably fashioned from human hair, it looked for all the world as if it had been spun from fine strands of polished silk or spun glass. His shirt linen, though not nearly as fine a weave as his stockings, was as bright white as the crests of the surf that crashed on the shingle beach half a mile away, or the tiny flecks of canvas that winked in the sun far out on the horizon of the bay. His features were usually held in a soft, restrained set, but beneath the odd little wig, a pair of damp, bright eyes still glowed; it must have taken their owner years of careful practice in his youth to train them to hold the calm, guarded expression expected of all staff at Hale & Croft Merchant Bank. His cheeks held a warm, robust flush, and while his face bore a few fine lines, there were almost no marks of lingering worry to be seen. It might well have been that the trusted unmarried clerks of Hale & Croft spent most of their time tending to other people’s troubles; and perhaps borrowed worries, much like secondhand coats, slip on and off far easier than those that are one’s own.
source_text: Very orderly and methodical he looked, with a hand on each knee, and a loud watch ticking a sonorous sermon under his flapped waist-coat, as though it pitted its gravity and longevity against the levity and evanescence of the brisk fire. He had a good leg, and was a little vain of it, for his brown stockings fitted sleek and close, and were of a fine texture; his shoes and buckles, too, though plain, were trim. He wore an odd little sleek crisp flaxen wig, setting very close to his head: which wig, it is to be presumed, was made of hair, but which looked far more as though it were spun from filaments of silk or glass. His linen, though not of a fineness in accordance with his stockings, was as white as the tops of the waves that broke upon the neighbouring beach, or the specks of sail that glinted in the sunlight far at sea. A face habitually suppressed and quieted, was still lighted up under the quaint wig by a pair of moist bright eyes that it must have cost their owner, in years gone by, some pains to drill to the composed and reserved expression of Tellson’s Bank. He had a healthy colour in his cheeks, and his face, though lined, bore few traces of anxiety. But, perhaps the confidential bachelor clerks in Tellson’s Bank were principally occupied with the cares of other people; and perhaps second-hand cares, like second-hand clothes, come easily off and on.

text: But the stillness did not hold. Elowen let go of his forearm, resting her palm light on the curve of his shoulder. Kael blinked at her hand two or three times, a faint confused furrow in his brow as if he could not trust it was not a trick of dim lamplight, then set down the half-carved wooden spool he’d been working on, reached up to the nape of his neck, and unclasped a grimy leather cord strung with a crumpled scrap of stained linen. He unfolded the scrap with painstaking care atop his work-worn knee, and inside rested only a tiny clutch of hair: no more than two long honey-blonde strands, which he’d wound around his finger and tucked away in some long-forgotten sunlit stretch of years past.
source_text: But not for long. Releasing his arm, she laid her hand upon his shoulder. After looking doubtfully at it, two or three times, as if to be sure that it was really there, he laid down his work, put his hand to his neck, and took off a blackened string with a scrap of folded rag attached to it. He opened this, carefully, on his knee, and it contained a very little quantity of hair: not more than one or two long golden hairs, which he had, in some old day, wound off upon his finger.

text: Hale & Croft’s Bank, tucked beside St. Paul’s Churchyard, was a stubbornly antiquated spot, even in the year 1782. It was remarkably cramped, perpetually dim, unapologetically drab, and deeply inconvenient for anyone trying to conduct business at speed. What made it even more of a relic, though, was the institutional ethos held by its senior partners: they took fierce pride in its cramped quarters, its dim corners, its drab facade, its total lack of modern comfort. They even bragged openly about how singularly unaccommodating it was, clinging to the fervent, unshakable belief that if the premises were less off-putting, it would be far less reputable. This was no quiet, unspoken conviction, but a sharp rhetorical weapon they brandished whenever anyone mentioned newer, more user-friendly banking houses. Hale & Croft’s, they insisted, needed no extra space to stretch, no bright lamps to light its halls, no gilded trim or fresh paint to spruce it up. Prewett & Sons might crave such frills, or Hargrove Brothers might beg for them; but Hale & Croft’s, thank the good Lord--!
source_text: Tellson’s Bank by Temple Bar was an old-fashioned place, even in the year one thousand seven hundred and eighty. It was very small, very dark, very ugly, very incommodious. It was an old-fashioned place, moreover, in the moral attribute that the partners in the House were proud of its smallness, proud of its darkness, proud of its ugliness, proud of its incommodiousness. They were even boastful of its eminence in those particulars, and were fired by an express conviction that, if it were less objectionable, it would be less respectable. This was no passive belief, but an active weapon which they flashed at more convenient places of business. Tellson’s (they said) wanted no elbow-room, Tellson’s wanted no light, Tellson’s wanted no embellishment. Noakes and Co.’s might, or Snooks Brothers’ might; but Tellson’s, thank Heaven--!

text: Veterans of the Bar had once observed that while Mr. Thorne Gresham was a glib, unprincipled, quick-thinking, and bold barrister, he lacked the specific faculty of distilling the core essence from a jumble of conflicting statements, a skill counted among the most striking and necessary of an advocate’s credentials. A remarkable improvement in this area soon began to show, however. The more work he took on, the stronger his gift for cutting straight to a case’s pith and marrow seemed to become; no matter how late he sat carousing into the night with Elias Vey, he always had every critical point of his argument at his fingertips when morning came.
source_text: It had once been noted at the Bar, that while Mr. Stryver was a glib man, and an unscrupulous, and a ready, and a bold, he had not that faculty of extracting the essence from a heap of statements, which is among the most striking and necessary of the advocate’s accomplishments. But, a remarkable improvement came upon him as to this. The more business he got, the greater his power seemed to grow of getting at its pith and marrow; and however late at night he sat carousing with Sydney Carton, he always had his points at his fingers’ ends in the morning.

text: Elias Thorne had already clambered into the hired coach, his daughter Clara close on his heels, when Mr. Hale’s boot froze halfway up the step: the gaunt old prisoner was begging, voice thin and wretched, for his cobbler’s tools and the half-finished pair of boots he’d left in his cell. Margot Voss called out at once to her husband Klaus that she would fetch them, and still clicking her knitting needles as she went, stepped out of the pool of lantern light across the cobblestone yard. She returned moments later with the worn bundle, passing it through the coach window; as soon as her hand pulled back, she rested her shoulder against the gatepost, needles moving steadily again, her gaze fixed on nothing at all.
source_text: The prisoner had got into a coach, and his daughter had followed him, when Mr. Lorry’s feet were arrested on the step by his asking, miserably, for his shoemaking tools and the unfinished shoes. Madame Defarge immediately called to her husband that she would get them, and went, knitting, out of the lamplight, through the courtyard. She quickly brought them down and handed them in;--and immediately afterwards leaned against the door-post, knitting, and saw nothing.

text: Beyond the fleeting tears that glistened through the grins of the small gathering once the ceremony concluded, a handful of dazzling, glittering diamonds winked on the bride’s left hand, newly freed from the shadowed lining of Mr. Thorne’s waistcoat pocket. They made their way back to the house for breakfast, every detail unfolding without a hitch, and in good time, the honey-gold hair that had tangled with the destitute cobbler’s wispy white locks in that drafty Paris garret years before, twisted together with them again in the soft morning sunlight, on the front stoop as they paused to say their goodbyes.
source_text: Besides the glancing tears that shone among the smiles of the little group when it was done, some diamonds, very bright and sparkling, glanced on the bride’s hand, which were newly released from the dark obscurity of one of Mr. Lorry’s pockets. They returned home to breakfast, and all went well, and in due course the golden hair that had mingled with the poor shoemaker’s white locks in the Paris garret, were mingled with them again in the morning sunlight, on the threshold of the door at parting.

text: Monsieur and Madame Defarge trundled beneath the starlight in their public conveyance, making for the Paris gate that was the natural destination of their journey. As always, they were halted at the barrier guardhouse, and familiar lanterns glided out to conduct the routine inspection and questioning. Monsieur Defarge stepped down from the vehicle; he knew a pair of the soldiers posted there, and one of the police officers. He was intimate with the latter, and greeted him with a warm, affectionate embrace.
source_text: The Defarges, husband and wife, came lumbering under the starlight, in their public vehicle, to that gate of Paris whereunto their journey naturally tended. There was the usual stoppage at the barrier guardhouse, and the usual lanterns came glancing forth for the usual examination and inquiry. Monsieur Defarge alighted; knowing one or two of the soldiery there, and one of the police. The latter he was intimate with, and affectionately embraced.

text: Just as a roiling whirlpool of boiling water has a fixed central point, all that ferocious, surging chaos circled tight around Defarge’s wine shop. Every person bobbing like a drop in that seething, cauldron-like crowd was pulled steadily toward the vortex, where Defarge himself stood already caked with gunpowder and glistening with sweat, calling out orders, distributing arms, shoving one man back, tugging another forward, disarming one person to equip another, laboring relentlessly right in the thick of the deafening uproar.
source_text: As a whirlpool of boiling waters has a centre point, so, all this raging circled round Defarge’s wine-shop, and every human drop in the caldron had a tendency to be sucked towards the vortex where Defarge himself, already begrimed with gunpowder and sweat, issued orders, issued arms, thrust this man back, dragged this man forward, disarmed one to arm another, laboured and strove in the thickest of the uproar.

text: “Six paces by four, six paces by four, six paces by four.” Kael paced back and forth across his cell, muttering the dimensions under his breath on every loop, while the distant roar of the capital drifted through the stone walls like padded war drums, tangled with the rough swell of a thousand unknown voices. “He carved toys, he carved toys, he carved toys.” Kael reeled off the measurements again, picking up his pace to yank his thoughts away from that looping phrase before it swallowed him whole. “The phantoms that faded the moment the cell wicket clanged shut. There was one among them, the shape of a woman in deep indigo robes, leaning in the stone recess of a casement, silver moonlight spilling over her honey-blonde hair, and she looked like * * * * Let us ride on again, for God’s sake, through the lantern-strung hamlets where the people are all awake and cheering! * * * * He carved toys, he carved toys, he carved toys. * * * * Six paces by four.” With such disjointed scraps churning and bubbling upward from the murk of his fractured mind, the prisoner walked faster and faster, obstinately counting and counting; and the roar of the city changed to this extent—that it still rolled in like muffled drums, but with the thin, keening wail of voices he knew by heart, in the swell that rose above them.
source_text: “Five paces by four and a half, five paces by four and a half, five paces by four and a half.” The prisoner walked to and fro in his cell, counting its measurement, and the roar of the city arose like muffled drums with a wild swell of voices added to them. “He made shoes, he made shoes, he made shoes.” The prisoner counted the measurement again, and paced faster, to draw his mind with him from that latter repetition. “The ghosts that vanished when the wicket closed. There was one among them, the appearance of a lady dressed in black, who was leaning in the embrasure of a window, and she had a light shining upon her golden hair, and she looked like * * * * Let us ride on again, for God’s sake, through the illuminated villages with the people all awake! * * * * He made shoes, he made shoes, he made shoes. * * * * Five paces by four and a half.” With such scraps tossing and rolling upward from the depths of his mind, the prisoner walked faster and faster, obstinately counting and counting; and the roar of the city changed to this extent--that it still rolled in like muffled drums, but with the wail of voices that he knew, in the swell that rose above them.

text: Laurent roused himself with a swallow of rough red wine—he took far less than he’d served the unknown traveler, for it was no scarce luxury to a man who lived above the tavern’s cellar—and stood waiting while the hill country farmer finished his morning meal. He cast no glance at any person present, and not a soul in the taproom paid him any mind now; not even his wife Elodie, who had picked up her knitting and was working stitches in steady, unhurried motions.
source_text: Defarge refreshed himself with a draught of wine--but, he took less than was given to the stranger, as being himself a man to whom it was no rarity--and stood waiting until the countryman had made his breakfast. He looked at no one present, and no one now looked at him; not even Madame Defarge, who had taken up her knitting, and was at work.

text: He’d shed his coat and waistcoat, his shirt gaping open at the throat just as it always was when he did this particular work; even that old haggard, faded cast to his features had settled back across his face. He labored fiercely, sharp with impatience, as if he carried the quiet sense that he’d been pulled away from the task before he could finish it.
source_text: He had laid aside his coat and waistcoat; his shirt was open at the throat, as it used to be when he did that work; and even the old haggard, faded surface of face had come back to him. He worked hard--impatiently--as if in some sense of having been interrupted.

text: Oh! Most gracious former Marquis, where on earth could that emigrant be? I cry out asking after him even in my sleep. I plead with the heavens—won’t he come to rescue me? I never get a single reply. Oh, former Marquis, I send my hollow, lonely cry out across the sea, hoping it might reach your ears somehow, passed along through that well-known Tilson bank everyone in Paris recognizes by name!
source_text: “Ah! most gracious Monsieur heretofore the Marquis, where is that emigrant? I cry in my sleep where is he? I demand of Heaven, will he not come to deliver me? No answer. Ah Monsieur heretofore the Marquis, I send my desolate cry across the sea, hoping it may perhaps reach your ears through the great bank of Tilson known at Paris!

text: To Lila’s father, Mr. Voss, Kael wrote in the same quiet, earnest vein; but he took special care to explicitly entrust his wife and young child to the older man’s keeping. He emphasized this charge with sharp, unmistakeable weight, all in the hope of rousing Mr. Voss from the dull despondency and harmful preoccupation with the past that he suspected the grieving man was already beginning to slip into.
source_text: To her father himself, he wrote in the same strain; but, he told her father that he expressly confided his wife and child to his care. And he told him this, very strongly, with the hope of rousing him from any despondency or dangerous retrospect towards which he foresaw he might be tending.

text: Word spread instantly, all the way to the very edges of the throng. Defarge had barely vaulted a railing and a table, locking the miserable wretch in a deadly, unbreakable hold. Madame Defarge had only just followed in his wake, twisting her hand into one of the ropes binding the man. The Vengeance and Jacques Three hadn’t even caught up to them yet, and the men perched at the windows hadn’t yet swooped into the hall like birds of prey diving from their high perches, when the cry went up, as if resounding across every corner of the entire city: “Bring him out! Bring him to the lamppost!”
source_text: It was known directly, to the furthest confines of the crowd. Defarge had but sprung over a railing and a table, and folded the miserable wretch in a deadly embrace--Madame Defarge had but followed and turned her hand in one of the ropes with which he was tied--The Vengeance and Jacques Three were not yet up with them, and the men at the windows had not yet swooped into the Hall, like birds of prey from their high perches--when the cry seemed to go up, all over the city, “Bring him out! Bring him to the lamp!”

text: Those quiet trappings of genteel refinement were so warped and clouded over by the prison’s harsh unspoken rules and its thick, suffocating gloom, so eerie and spectral when set against the wildly mismatched filth and crushing misery all around them, that Charles Darnay might as well have been standing in a throng of the dead. Every last one of them a ghost! The ghost of soft, polished beauty, the ghost of rigid, unyielding stateliness, the ghost of effortless elegance, the ghost of sharp, unapologetic pride, the ghost of giddy frivolity, the ghost of quick, biting wit, the ghost of bright, untested youth, the ghost of lined, weary age, all lingering on that desolate stretch of ground waiting for their order to depart, all turning to fix on him eyes that had been irrevocably changed by the small, cold death they’d already died just to arrive there.
source_text: So strangely clouded were these refinements by the prison manners and gloom, so spectral did they become in the inappropriate squalor and misery through which they were seen, that Charles Darnay seemed to stand in a company of the dead. Ghosts all! The ghost of beauty, the ghost of stateliness, the ghost of elegance, the ghost of pride, the ghost of frivolity, the ghost of wit, the ghost of youth, the ghost of age, all waiting their dismissal from the desolate shore, all turning on him eyes that were changed by the death they had died in coming there.

text: “I’m not afraid to die, Citizen Evrémonde, but I’ve done nothing wrong. I don’t even mind losing my life, if the Republic that’s supposed to bring so much good to us poor folks actually benefits from my death; but I have no idea how that could possibly be the case, Citizen Evrémonde. I’m just such a poor, weak little creature, after all.”
source_text: “I am not afraid to die, Citizen Evrémonde, but I have done nothing. I am not unwilling to die, if the Republic which is to do so much good to us poor, will profit by my death; but I do not know how that can be, Citizen Evrémonde. Such a poor weak little creature!”

text: The ailing woman brought before me was breathtakingly lovely, barely out of her girlhood and certainly no more than twenty-one. Her hair hung in matted, tangled clumps, torn loose from whatever coiffure it had been arranged in earlier, and her arms were pinned tight to her torso with knotted sashes and crumpled handkerchiefs. I noted immediately that every one of these restraints had been cut or ripped from a gentleman’s wardrobe. One, a fringed silk scarf meant for formal ceremonial wear, bore the stitched heraldic crest of a noble house, along with the crisply embroidered initial E.
source_text: “The patient was a woman of great beauty, and young; assuredly not much past twenty. Her hair was torn and ragged, and her arms were bound to her sides with sashes and handkerchiefs. I noticed that these bonds were all portions of a gentleman’s dress. On one of them, which was a fringed scarf for a dress of ceremony, I saw the armorial bearings of a Noble, and the letter E.

text: “We will have lent our hands to its rise,” returned Señora Calderón, her outstretched fist held rigid with unshakable force. “No deed of ours is done for nothing. I believe, with every fiber of my being, that we will live to see the victory. But even if we do not, even if I knew beyond all doubt that we never would, hold out the neck of a wealthy lord and cruel oppressor before me, and I would still--”
source_text: “We shall have helped it,” returned madame, with her extended hand in strong action. “Nothing that we do, is done in vain. I believe, with all my soul, that we shall see the triumph. But even if not, even if I knew certainly not, show me the neck of an aristocrat and tyrant, and still I would--”

text: Mr. Stryver then called his small handful of witnesses, and Mr. Cruncher was forced to sit through what came next: the Attorney-General turned the entire rhetorical suit Mr. Stryver had tailored for the jury fully inside out. He argued that Barsad and Cly were a hundred times more virtuous than Stryver had made them out to be, and the prisoner a hundred times more villainous. Finally, the presiding judge himself took up that same garment, twisting it now inside out, now right side in, but on the whole clearly cutting and shaping it to be the prisoner’s own grave-clothes.
source_text: Mr. Stryver then called his few witnesses, and Mr. Cruncher had next to attend while Mr. Attorney-General turned the whole suit of clothes Mr. Stryver had fitted on the jury, inside out; showing how Barsad and Cly were even a hundred times better than he had thought them, and the prisoner a hundred times worse. Lastly, came my Lord himself, turning the suit of clothes, now inside out, now outside in, but on the whole decidedly trimming and shaping them into grave-clothes for the prisoner.

text: He told me he was traveling for work of an exceptionally delicate, high-stakes nature that could get other people caught up in trouble, so he was going by an assumed name for the duration of his trip. The work, he added, had already taken him to France within the past few days, and would likely require him to shuttle back and forth between France and England at irregular intervals for a long stretch of time ahead.
source_text: “He told me that he was travelling on business of a delicate and difficult nature, which might get people into trouble, and that he was therefore travelling under an assumed name. He said that this business had, within a few days, taken him to France, and might, at intervals, take him backwards and forwards between France and England for a long time to come.”

text: His streaming white hair, his striking, unforgettable face, and the impetuous, unshakable confidence of his bearing as he brushed weapons aside as effortlessly as one swipes through water, carried him in an instant to the heart of the concourse clustered at the stone. For a few moments there was a lull, a flurry of hurried movement, a low murmur rippling through the crowd, and the muffled, unintelligible sound of his voice; then Mr. Lorry spotted him, surrounded on all sides, at the head of a line of twenty men, all linked shoulder to shoulder and hand to shoulder, swept outward as cries rang out: “Long live the Bastille prisoner! Help for the Bastille prisoner’s kindred in La Force! Room for the Bastille prisoner in front there! Save the prisoner Evrémonde at La Force!” answered by a thousand roaring shouts.
source_text: His streaming white hair, his remarkable face, and the impetuous confidence of his manner, as he put the weapons aside like water, carried him in an instant to the heart of the concourse at the stone. For a few moments there was a pause, and a hurry, and a murmur, and the unintelligible sound of his voice; and then Mr. Lorry saw him, surrounded by all, and in the midst of a line of twenty men long, all linked shoulder to shoulder, and hand to shoulder, hurried out with cries of--“Live the Bastille prisoner! Help for the Bastille prisoner’s kindred in La Force! Room for the Bastille prisoner in front there! Save the prisoner Evrémonde at La Force!” and a thousand answering shouts.

text: No plea, no gentle coaxing could draw another word from his lips. When prompted to respond, he would lift his gaze for a split second at a time, but no amount of persuasion could pry a single syllable loose. He labored on, hour after hour, in unbroken silence, every word directed at him bounced off as though it had struck an echoless stone wall, or simply vanished into the open air. The only glimmer of hope Mr. Thorne could discern was that he sometimes snuck a quick look upward without being prompted first. In that small, unbidden gesture, there hung a faint air of curiosity or bewilderment—as though he was struggling to resolve some unspoken doubt lingering at the edge of his thoughts.
source_text: Nothing would induce him to speak more. He looked up, for an instant at a time, when he was requested to do so; but, no persuasion would extract a word from him. He worked, and worked, and worked, in silence, and words fell on him as they would have fallen on an echoless wall, or on the air. The only ray of hope that Mr. Lorry could discover, was, that he sometimes furtively looked up without being asked. In that, there seemed a faint expression of curiosity or perplexity--as though he were trying to reconcile some doubts in his mind.

text: Clamping a frayed wheat stalk between his teeth, Mr. Rook sat gazing at the two churning currents, much like the pagan goatherd of local lore who has stood watch over a single moorland stream for near a thousand years—except that Tom Rook held no expectation those waters would ever run dry. That would hardly have been a hopeful wish anyway, for a tidy slice of his income came from escorting skittish gentlewomen (most of them plump, well past middle age) across the slippery crossing from Calthorpe & Sons Bank’s side of the tide to the opposite embankment. Brief as each of these little journeys was, Mr. Rook never failed to work up such a show of fond regard for his passenger that he would profess a fervent wish to have the honor of toasting her excellent health. And the small gratuities pressed upon him to fund this kindly act were exactly the contributions that padded his earnings, as just observed.
source_text: With his straw in his mouth, Mr. Cruncher sat watching the two streams, like the heathen rustic who has for several centuries been on duty watching one stream--saving that Jerry had no expectation of their ever running dry. Nor would it have been an expectation of a hopeful kind, since a small part of his income was derived from the pilotage of timid women (mostly of a full habit and past the middle term of life) from Tellson’s side of the tides to the opposite shore. Brief as such companionship was in every separate instance, Mr. Cruncher never failed to become so interested in the lady as to express a strong desire to have the honour of drinking her very good health. And it was from the gifts bestowed upon him towards the execution of this benevolent purpose, that he recruited his finances, as just now observed.

text: He had no doubt he faced the strong likelihood, if not absolute certainty, of unjust abuse while in custody, unforgiving hardship, and the brutal, rending separation from his wife and young child. Yet outside that grim set of fates, no other distinct dread settled in his bones. That heavy, unshakable thought stayed with him as he stepped into the desolate, gray prison courtyard, arriving at last at the heavy iron doors of Saint-Lazare Prison.
source_text: Of unjust treatment in detention and hardship, and in cruel separation from his wife and child, he foreshadowed the likelihood, or the certainty; but, beyond this, he dreaded nothing distinctly. With this on his mind, which was enough to carry into a dreary prison courtyard, he arrived at the prison of La Force.

text: “To put it plainly,” said Sydney, “these are desperate times, where reckless gambles are waged for life-or-death stakes. Let the Doctor play for the winning hand; I will take the losing one. No person’s life here carries any guarantee of safety. Whoever the crowd carries home as a favorite today might be condemned to die tomorrow. The stake I have decided to play for, should the worst come to pass, is an ally within the Conciergerie. And the ally I intend to win over is Mr. Barsad.”
source_text: “In short,” said Sydney, “this is a desperate time, when desperate games are played for desperate stakes. Let the Doctor play the winning game; I will play the losing one. No man’s life here is worth purchase. Any one carried home by the people to-day, may be condemned tomorrow. Now, the stake I have resolved to play for, in case of the worst, is a friend in the Conciergerie. And the friend I purpose to myself to win, is Mr. Barsad.”

text: Once he’d finished his pipe and sat mulling things over a little longer, he turned to head out, intent on reporting to his post at Tellson’s before closing time. The exact reason he stopped briefly to visit his personal physician—a celebrated surgeon—on his return trip is far less relevant than the fact of the visit itself: it might have been that his quiet meditations on mortality had irritated his liver, that he’d already been feeling generally unwell, or that he simply wanted to pay a small courtesy to a prominent public figure.
source_text: Having smoked his pipe out, and ruminated a little longer, he turned himself about, that he might appear, before the hour of closing, on his station at Tellson’s. Whether his meditations on mortality had touched his liver, or whether his general health had been previously at all amiss, or whether he desired to show a little attention to an eminent man, is not so much to the purpose, as that he made a short call upon his medical adviser--a distinguished surgeon--on his way back.

text: “Listen up,” said Madame, “I don’t care at all about this Doctor, not me. Whether he keeps his head or loses it means absolutely nothing to me; it’s all the same. But the entire Evrémonde line is to be wiped out entirely, so the wife and child must follow their husband and father.”
source_text: “See you,” said madame, “I care nothing for this Doctor, I. He may wear his head or lose it, for any interest I have in him; it is all one to me. But, the Evrémonde people are to be exterminated, and the wife and child must follow the husband and father.”

text: The hungry fixation with which the crowd gaped and held their breath staring at this man was no feeling that honored the better parts of human nature. If he had stood to face a less unthinkably gruesome sentence, if even one of its savage, gory particulars had been set to be spared, his hold over the onlookers’ rapt attention would have weakened by exactly that measure. What drew every eye was the living frame fated to be maimed and desecrated so shamefully; what sparked the sharp, giddy thrill in every onlooker was the knowledge that this being, possessed of an immortal soul, would soon be butchered and torn limb from limb. No matter what pretty justifications the various spectators layered over their interest, drawing on their own varied tricks and talent for self-deception, that fascination was, at its very root, unapologetically ogreish.
source_text: The sort of interest with which this man was stared and breathed at, was not a sort that elevated humanity.
