Had he stood in peril of a less horrible sentence--had there been a chance of any one of its savage details being spared--by just so much would he have lost in his fascination. The form that was to be doomed to be so shamefully mangled, was the sight; the immortal creature that was to be so butchered and torn asunder, yielded the sensation. Whatever gloss the various spectators put upon the interest, according to their several arts and powers of self-deceit, the interest was, at the root of it, Ogreish.

text: Every gaze in the room turned as one to the five judges and the public prosecutor. There was no glimmer of leniency to be found in that cohort that day, only a grim, unyielding, ruthless intent to see their brutal business through. Every eye then sought another in the crowd, glinting with quiet approval when they met; heads nodded in mutual accord before everyone leaned forward, taut with strained, rapt attention.
source_text: Every eye then turned to the five judges and the public prosecutor. No favourable leaning in that quarter to-day. A fell, uncompromising, murderous business-meaning there. Every eye then sought some other eye in the crowd, and gleamed at it approvingly; and heads nodded at one another, before bending forward with a strained attention.

text: “Dear Doctor Manette, I’ve always known this about the two of you, always seen that sacred, gentle light hovering around you and her every moment you’re together. I’ve held myself back, again and again, for as long as any human being could possibly bear to. I’ve always felt, and I still feel even right now, that pushing my love—even this love of mine, as true as it is—between the two of you would be brushing against the story of your past with something that doesn’t measure up to its own quiet goodness. But I love her. Heaven is my witness that I love her!”
source_text: “Dear Doctor Manette, always knowing this, always seeing her and you with this hallowed light about you, I have forborne, and forborne, as long as it was in the nature of man to do it. I have felt, and do even now feel, that to bring my love--even mine--between you, is to touch your history with something not quite so good as itself. But I love her. Heaven is my witness that I love her!”

text: Young Jerry had only put on a show of undressing when he crawled into bed, so he was barely a minute behind his father when the man stepped out. Tucked safely under the cover of darkness, he followed him out of their room, down the stairs, across the courtyard, and out onto the city streets. He didn’t have a single worry about getting back inside later, either; the building was packed full of lodgers, and the front door stayed propped ajar all night long.
source_text: Young Jerry, who had only made a feint of undressing when he went to bed, was not long after his father. Under cover of the darkness he followed out of the room, followed down the stairs, followed down the court, followed out into the streets. He was in no uneasiness concerning his getting into the house again, for it was full of lodgers, and the door stood ajar all night.

text: Faint sounds drifted to his ears, sounds he held no fear of, for he knew instinctively what they portended. One door after another swung open in succession, until at last the lock on his own cell snicked apart. A gaoler clutching a written list poked his head inside, saying only, “Follow me, Evrémonde!” He trailed a short distance behind the man into a large, shadow-choked chamber. It was a bleak winter day, and between the dimness within the room and the gray, lightless fog outside, he could only faintly make out the other prisoners gathered there to have their arms bound. Some stood shifting on their feet, others sat hunched against the walls. A small number wept and moved restlessly about, but these were few. The great majority were completely silent and still, their eyes fixed unwaveringly on the ground at their feet.
source_text: Sounds that he was not afraid of, for he divined their meaning, then began to be audible. Several doors were opened in succession, and finally his own. A gaoler, with a list in his hand, looked in, merely saying, “Follow me, Evrémonde!” and he followed into a large dark room, at a distance. It was a dark winter day, and what with the shadows within, and what with the shadows without, he could but dimly discern the others who were brought there to have their arms bound. Some were standing; some seated. Some were lamenting, and in restless motion; but, these were few. The great majority were silent and still, looking fixedly at the ground.

text: This dragged on for twenty-six hours total, from the very first moment I laid eyes on Maren. I’d left and returned twice already, and was settled once more at her side when her breath and half-slurred speech first began to falter. I did whatever small, gentle acts I could to ease that quiet progression, and before long she sank into a heavy, unfeeling lethargy, lying as still as one already dead.
source_text: “This lasted twenty-six hours from the time when I first saw her. I had come and gone twice, and was again sitting by her, when she began to falter. I did what little could be done to assist that opportunity, and by-and-bye she sank into a lethargy, and lay like the dead.

text: My dear Manette, this is a case of a long-buried, prolonged shock that cut brutally sharp and deep to his affections, his feelings, his—the term you use, yes—his mind. His very psyche. It’s the sort of blow that crushed the sufferer entirely, and no one can say how long he was laid low by it: I’m quite sure he can’t calculate that span for himself, and there’s no other way to pin down the timeline. He did eventually recover, though he can’t trace the steps of that healing process himself— I heard him speak to that gap once in public, in a remarkably striking way. He’d bounced back so fully, in fact, that he’s now a sharp, highly intelligent man, capable of intense, sustained mental focus, heavy physical exertion, and constant new additions to what was already an exceptionally vast store of knowledge. But unfortunately, there’s been”—he paused, drawing a deep breath—“a mild relapse.”
source_text: “My dear Manette, it is the case of an old and a prolonged shock, of great acuteness and severity to the affections, the feelings, the--the--as you express it--the mind. The mind. It is the case of a shock under which the sufferer was borne down, one cannot say for how long, because I believe he cannot calculate the time himself, and there are no other means of getting at it. It is the case of a shock from which the sufferer recovered, by a process that he cannot trace himself--as I once heard him publicly relate in a striking manner. It is the case of a shock from which he has recovered, so completely, as to be a highly intelligent man, capable of close application of mind, and great exertion of body, and of constantly making fresh additions to his stock of knowledge, which was already very large. But, unfortunately, there has been,” he paused and took a deep breath--“a slight relapse.”

text: Hardwired to associate all people held in custody with vile transgression and irrevocable social disgrace, the newly arrived man flinched from the thought of joining this company. But the most staggeringly unreal turn of his already surreal, long journey came when every last one of them rose to receive him, bearing every polished mannerism considered proper to the age, and all the warm, winning grace and courtesy of ordinary well-bred life.
source_text: In the instinctive association of prisoners with shameful crime and disgrace, the new-comer recoiled from this company. But the crowning unreality of his long unreal ride, was, their all at once rising to receive him, with every refinement of manner known to the time, and with all the engaging graces and courtesies of life.

text: This family of ours—our so-called honorable family, the one whose good name matters so deeply to both of us, even if we care about it for entirely opposite reasons. Even back when my father was at the head of things, we committed no end of harm, hurting every single person who stood between us and whatever whim or pleasure we were chasing at the time. What’s the point of even speaking of my father’s era as if it’s separate from yours? How could I ever distinguish my father from his identical twin, his co-heir, the man who stepped directly into his role as successor the moment he was gone?
source_text: “Our family; our honourable family, whose honour is of so much account to both of us, in such different ways. Even in my father’s time, we did a world of wrong, injuring every human creature who came between us and our pleasure, whatever it was. Why need I speak of my father’s time, when it is equally yours? Can I separate my father’s twin-brother, joint inheritor, and next successor, from himself?”

text: The two moved upward slowly, barely a sound stirring from their boots on the worn treads. The stairwell was short, and they gained the landing in moments. A sharp, unforeseen bend in the passage just beyond put them abruptly in view of three men, all huddled with their heads pressed tight together beside a closed door, their attention fixed entirely on the room behind it as they peered through narrow chinks and gaps bored into the wall. At the faint scrape of footsteps right at their backs, the three twisted around and pushed to their feet, revealing themselves as the trio of Marette brothers who had been drinking heavily in the village wine shop earlier that afternoon.
source_text: They went up slowly and softly. The staircase was short, and they were soon at the top. There, as it had an abrupt turn in it, they came all at once in sight of three men, whose heads were bent down close together at the side of a door, and who were intently looking into the room to which the door belonged, through some chinks or holes in the wall. On hearing footsteps close at hand, these three turned, and rose, and showed themselves to be the three of one name who had been drinking in the wine-shop.

text: “‘Me, think?’ the alehouse mistress retorted, her voice pitched sharp over the clink of tankards by the door. ‘My husband and I have more than enough work just keeping this establishment open, no energy left for idle ruminations. The only subject we occupy our minds with here is staying afloat. That single worry fills every hour from dawn till well after dark, more than enough to occupy us without cluttering our heads with the affairs of strangers. I think for other people? No, not a bit of it.’”
source_text: “_I_ think?” returned madame, in a high voice. “I and my husband have enough to do to keep this wine-shop open, without thinking. All we think, here, is how to live. That is the subject _we_ think of, and it gives us, from morning to night, enough to think about, without embarrassing our heads concerning others. _I_ think for others? No, no.”

text: As his host followed him out onto the staircase, candle in hand to light his descent, chill pale daylight seeped through the grime-caked windowpanes. When he stepped outside the house, the air was cold and weighted with quiet melancholy, the dull, washed-out sky hung fully overcast, the river glinted dark and indistinct, and the whole landscape stretched before him like a lifeless, barren desert. Coils of dust whirled round and round ahead of the sharp morning gust, as if desert sand had stirred far in the distance, and its first drifting spray, carried forward on the wind, had already begun to engulf the city.
source_text: When his host followed him out on the staircase with a candle, to light him down the stairs, the day was coldly looking in through its grimy windows. When he got out of the house, the air was cold and sad, the dull sky overcast, the river dark and dim, the whole scene like a lifeless desert. And wreaths of dust were spinning round and round before the morning blast, as if the desert-sand had risen far away, and the first spray of it in its advance had begun to overwhelm the city.

text: Mr. Lorry did as instructed, and the two descended the stairs and stepped out into the street. A few minutes’ walk brought them to Mr. Lorry’s intended destination. Carton parted from him at the gate, but lingered a short distance away; once the entrance had swung shut behind Lorry, he circled back to it and brushed his fingers against its surface. He had heard she made the trip to the prison every day. “She came out through this very gate,” he said, glancing around at the pavement underfoot, “turned down this same path, must have trod these stones time and again. Let me follow in her footsteps.”
source_text: Mr. Lorry did so, and they went down-stairs and out in the streets. A few minutes brought them to Mr. Lorry’s destination. Carton left him there; but lingered at a little distance, and turned back to the gate again when it was shut, and touched it. He had heard of her going to the prison every day. “She came out here,” he said, looking about him, “turned this way, must have trod on these stones often. Let me follow in her steps.”

text: La Force! Lucie, my child, if you’ve ever been brave and dependable a day in your life—and you’ve always been both—you need to steady yourself right now and follow every single thing I tell you to the letter. Far more hangs in the balance here than you could ever guess, or than I can put into words right now. There is no good that can come of you trying to take action tonight; you cannot step foot outside for any reason. I’m telling you this because the favor I need you to do for Charles’s sake is the hardest thing you will ever have to do. You have to obey me immediately, stay perfectly still, and make no noise. You have to let me lead you to one of the back rooms here, and leave your father and me alone for two full minutes. This is a matter of life and death, so do not delay even for a second.
source_text: “La Force! Lucie, my child, if ever you were brave and serviceable in your life--and you were always both--you will compose yourself now, to do exactly as I bid you; for more depends upon it than you can think, or I can say. There is no help for you in any action on your part to-night; you cannot possibly stir out. I say this, because what I must bid you to do for Charles’s sake, is the hardest thing to do of all. You must instantly be obedient, still, and quiet. You must let me put you in a room at the back here. You must leave your father and me alone for two minutes, and as there are Life and Death in the world you must not delay.”

text: Flames raged through the chateau. The nearest trees, caught in the fire’s greedy grip, scorched and shrank into crisp, blackened husks; trees further afield, set alight by the four fierce figures, wreathed the blazing building in a thick new forest of churning smoke. Molten lead and iron bubbled in the fountain’s marble basin; the water had boiled away entirely, the extinguisher-shaped caps atop the towers vanishing like ice under the searing heat, trickling down into four rough, churning wells of flame. Deep gashes and jagged cracks branched across the solid stone walls, like spreading crystal formations; dazed, stupefied birds wheeled through the hot air before dropping straight into the furnace. The four fierce figures tramped off, one east, one west, one north, one south, down the roads wrapped in night, guided by the beacon they had lit toward their next destination. The lit-up village below had seized control of the alarm bell, thrown out the official ringer, and was pealing the bell in wild, unbridled joy.
source_text: The chateau burned; the nearest trees, laid hold of by the fire, scorched and shrivelled; trees at a distance, fired by the four fierce figures, begirt the blazing edifice with a new forest of smoke. Molten lead and iron boiled in the marble basin of the fountain; the water ran dry; the extinguisher tops of the towers vanished like ice before the heat, and trickled down into four rugged wells of flame. Great rents and splits branched out in the solid walls, like crystallisation; stupefied birds wheeled about and dropped into the furnace; four fierce figures trudged away, East, West, North, and South, along the night-enshrouded roads, guided by the beacon they had lighted, towards their next destination. The illuminated village had seized hold of the tocsin, and, abolishing the lawful ringer, rang for joy.

text: “Nah, hold on—before I go any further,” Stryver said, shaking his head in that overbearing, pushy way of his, “we’re settling this right now. You’ve been hanging around Doctor Manette’s house just as much as I have, if not more. Hell, I’ve been straight-up embarrassed by how glum and unresponsive you are there. You’re always dead quiet, surly, and slouching around like you’ve done something wrong—swear on my life, Sydney, I’ve been mortified to be seen with you when we’re over there.”
source_text: “No; but before I go on,” said Stryver, shaking his head in his bullying way, “I’ll have this out with you. You’ve been at Doctor Manette’s house as much as I have, or more than I have. Why, I have been ashamed of your moroseness there! Your manners have been of that silent and sullen and hangdog kind, that, upon my life and soul, I have been ashamed of you, Sydney!”

text: A whole host of prospects have been suggested to me, but I haven’t the slightest desire to entertain any of them. I’ve decided on Barnaby. Barnaby has served as my personal escort on Sunday nights for years now, and I am perfectly used to his presence. No one would ever suspect Barnaby of being anything other than a regular English bulldog, or of having any thought in his skull besides launching himself at anyone who lays so much as a finger on his master.
source_text: “All sorts of people have been proposed to me, but I will have nothing to say to any of them. I intend to take Jerry. Jerry has been my bodyguard on Sunday nights for a long time past and I am used to him. Nobody will suspect Jerry of being anything but an English bull-dog, or of having any design in his head but to fly at anybody who touches his master.”

text: “My husband—no, wait, just a moment!” she cried, as he strained to pull free from her hold. “We will not be separated for long,” he said. “I can feel this will break my heart sooner or later, but I will do my duty while I am able. When I leave her, God will raise up friends to stand by her, just as He did for me.”
source_text: “My husband. No! A moment!” He was tearing himself apart from her. “We shall not be separated long. I feel that this will break my heart by-and-bye; but I will do my duty while I can, and when I leave her, God will raise up friends for her, as He did for me.”

text: Mr. Darnay pressed a fervent, grateful kiss to her hand before turning to Mr. Stryver, whom he thanked warmly. Barely over thirty, Stryver looked a full twenty years older than his actual age: stocky, loud, red-faced, and blunt, with not a scrap of overly fussy delicacy to hold him back. He had a habit of shouldering his way both literally and figuratively into social groups and ongoing conversations, a tendency that made it obvious he would carve the same aggressive path to advancement through life.
source_text: Mr. Darnay had kissed her hand fervently and gratefully, and had turned to Mr. Stryver, whom he warmly thanked. Mr. Stryver, a man of little more than thirty, but looking twenty years older than he was, stout, loud, red, bluff, and free from any drawback of delicacy, had a pushing way of shouldering himself (morally and physically) into companies and conversations, that argued well for his shouldering his way up in life.

text: Even when cries of grief rose from those around them, those sounds carried no harshness, no cruelty. Even when a little boy with hair as golden as hers lay with that bright hair fanned like a halo across the pillow around his worn, thin face, and he smiled radiantly as he said, “Dear Papa and Mama, I’m so sorry to leave both of you, and my pretty sister too; but I’ve been called, and I have to go,” the tears that dampened his young mother’s cheeks as the soul entrusted to her care slipped out of her embrace were not tears of unmitigated agony. “Let the little children come to me, and do not hinder them.” They see my Father’s face. Oh Father, what blessed, sacred words those are.
source_text: Even when there were sounds of sorrow among the rest, they were not harsh nor cruel. Even when golden hair, like her own, lay in a halo on a pillow round the worn face of a little boy, and he said, with a radiant smile, “Dear papa and mamma, I am very sorry to leave you both, and to leave my pretty sister; but I am called, and I must go!” those were not tears all of agony that wetted his young mother’s cheek, as the spirit departed from her embrace that had been entrusted to it. Suffer them and forbid them not. They see my Father’s face. O Father, blessed words!

text: The crowd pressed in tight, all their eyes fixed on the Comte de Séverin. No trace of threat or fury shone in those dozens of upturned gazes; only a sharp, waiting alertness was plain to see. No one among the crowd spoke a word, either; they had fallen quiet the moment that first shout faded, and they held their peace still. The meek laborer who had spoken earlier had a dull, pliant tone, softened to the point of utter servility. The Comte swept his gaze over the whole throng, as if they were nothing more than scurrying vermin spilling from their burrows.
source_text: The people closed round, and looked at Monsieur the Marquis. There was nothing revealed by the many eyes that looked at him but watchfulness and eagerness; there was no visible menacing or anger. Neither did the people say anything; after the first cry, they had been silent, and they remained so. The voice of the submissive man who had spoken, was flat and tame in its extreme submission. Monsieur the Marquis ran his eyes over them all, as if they had been mere rats come out of their holes.

text: Like I was just saying: suppose Monsieur Manette never actually died. Suppose he vanished abruptly and without a sound, spirited away by unseen hands. Suppose everyone had a pretty clear hunch what dreadful place he’d been taken to, even if there was no possible way to track him or confirm the guess. Suppose he had an enemy among his own countrymen, someone who wielded a power I’ve personally seen even the boldest people dare not speak of even in quiet whispers, over there across the channel. That power, for example, was the right to fill out blank pre-signed warrants to lock anyone away in a prison where they’d be completely forgotten, for however long the person holding the warrant saw fit. Suppose his wife begged the king, the queen, every figure at court, every high-ranking member of the clergy for any scrap of news about him, and every last plea went completely unanswered. If all of that were the case, then the story of your father would be the exact same story as this poor, wronged gentleman, the Doctor of Beauvais.
source_text: “As I was saying; if Monsieur Manette had not died; if he had suddenly and silently disappeared; if he had been spirited away; if it had not been difficult to guess to what dreadful place, though no art could trace him; if he had an enemy in some compatriot who could exercise a privilege that I in my own time have known the boldest people afraid to speak of in a whisper, across the water there; for instance, the privilege of filling up blank forms for the consignment of any one to the oblivion of a prison for any length of time; if his wife had implored the king, the queen, the court, the clergy, for any tidings of him, and all quite in vain;--then the history of your father would have been the history of this unfortunate gentleman, the Doctor of Beauvais.”

text: Their exchange was brief, but unmistakeably resolute. Barely had the first syllable landed when Etienne Thibault flinched, then locked into sharp, unwavering focus. The whole conversation ran shorter than sixty seconds before he gave a sharp, confirming nod and slipped through the door. The well-dressed stranger who’d spoken to him then crooked a finger at the young woman at his side, and the pair followed him out. Elodie Thibault never paused her knitting, her fingers flying deft and her brow smooth and unchanging, as if she had witnessed nothing at all.
source_text: Their conference was very short, but very decided. Almost at the first word, Monsieur Defarge started and became deeply attentive. It had not lasted a minute, when he nodded and went out. The gentleman then beckoned to the young lady, and they, too, went out. Madame Defarge knitted with nimble fingers and steady eyebrows, and saw nothing.

text: “Carton,” his friend said, squaring up to him with a hectoring air, as if the fire grate were the very furnace where sustained effort was forged, and the only small favour to do for old Sydney Carton of their old Shrewsbury School days was to shoulder him straight into its heat, “your way of going through life is, and always has been, limp and useless. You rouse not a scrap of energy or clear purpose. Look at me.”
source_text: “Carton,” said his friend, squaring himself at him with a bullying air, as if the fire-grate had been the furnace in which sustained endeavour was forged, and the one delicate thing to be done for the old Sydney Carton of old Shrewsbury School was to shoulder him into it, “your way is, and always was, a lame way. You summon no energy and purpose. Look at me.”

text: Hardly any private carriages plied the streets that evening; anyone riding in one risked drawing hostile, unwarranted suspicion, so even the well-to-do tucked their fine coiffures under frayed wool nightcaps, laced into scuffed heavy boots, and plodded along on foot. Still, every playhouse in the district was packed to the rafters, and crowds spilled gaily onto the pavement as he walked past, chattering as they drifted homeward. Outside the entrance of one playhouse, a small girl huddled beside her mother, peering uncertainly at the mud-caked crosswalk, unsure how to cross without soaking her stockings. He lifted the little one easily and carried her to the opposite curb, and before her shy, small arms had loosened from around his throat, he asked her for a kiss.
source_text: Few coaches were abroad, for riders in coaches were liable to be suspected, and gentility hid its head in red nightcaps, and put on heavy shoes, and trudged. But, the theatres were all well filled, and the people poured cheerfully out as he passed, and went chatting home. At one of the theatre doors, there was a little girl with a mother, looking for a way across the street through the mud. He carried the child over, and before the timid arm was loosed from his neck asked her for a kiss.

text: It was obvious to the group that he had no memory whatsoever of being transported from his prison cell to this house. They caught him muttering under his breath, “One Hundred and Five, North Tower;” when he glanced around the space, he was clearly searching for the heavy, unyielding fortress walls that had enclosed him for decades. When they stepped out into the courtyard, he adjusted his step on instinct, bracing to find a drawbridge ahead; when no drawbridge appeared, and he spotted the carriage waiting at the edge of the open street, he let go of his daughter’s hand and clutched at his own head once more.
source_text: That he had no recollection whatever of his having been brought from his prison to that house, was apparent to them. They heard him mutter, “One Hundred and Five, North Tower;” and when he looked about him, it evidently was for the strong fortress-walls which had long encompassed him. On their reaching the courtyard he instinctively altered his tread, as being in expectation of a drawbridge; and when there was no drawbridge, and he saw the carriage waiting in the open street, he dropped his daughter’s hand and clasped his head again.

text: The second that ragged cry tore from his lips, he released his hold on her, raising both hands to yank at his white hair in a fit of wild frenzy. That fervor died away as fast as it had come, just as every trace of his old self but his shoemaking craft had long since faded from him; he refolded his small, worn packet, fumbling to tuck it safely against his chest beneath his clothes. He still kept his eyes fixed on her the whole while, and shook his head in quiet, heavy gloom.
source_text: His hands released her as he uttered this cry, and went up to his white hair, which they tore in a frenzy. It died out, as everything but his shoemaking did die out of him, and he refolded his little packet and tried to secure it in his breast; but he still looked at her, and gloomily shook his head.

text: The devastated wife of the innocent man newly condemned to die crumpled the moment the sentence was read, as though she had been struck by a mortal blow. Yet she uttered not a sound; the quiet voice deep within her was too firm, insisting that of all people on earth, she was the one who must uphold him in his misery rather than add to it, and that unshakable conviction lifted her almost at once, even out of the throes of that devastating shock.
source_text: The wretched wife of the innocent man thus doomed to die, fell under the sentence, as if she had been mortally stricken. But, she uttered no sound; and so strong was the voice within her, representing that it was she of all the world who must uphold him in his misery and not augment it, that it quickly raised her, even from that shock.

text: Still too unsteady to trust her voice not to waver, she lowered herself onto the bench next to him. He flinched back at her sudden nearness, but she rested her hand lightly on his upper arm. A strange, thrumming jolt shot through him the second her skin made contact, so sharp it visibly rippled across his entire body; he set the knife down softly, sitting frozen as he stared right at her.
source_text: Not yet trusting the tones of her voice, she sat down on the bench beside him. He recoiled, but she laid her hand upon his arm. A strange thrill struck him when she did so, and visibly passed over his frame; he laid the knife down softly, as he sat staring at her.

text: A wide rolling landscape, with sun-ripened sorghum glowing vividly across its folds, but far too sparse to bring in a plentiful harvest. Stunted, sallow rye poked up in plots where sorghum ought to have grown, next to straggling clumps of lentils and fava beans, and wide stretches of tough, unpalatable coarse vegetables planted in place of wheat. The lifeless land bore the same worn, listless cast as the men and women who tended its soils: a pervasive air of half-heartedly clinging to growth against their will, a downcast, defeated inclination to surrender entirely and crumble into dry, dead dust.
source_text: A beautiful landscape, with the corn bright in it, but not abundant. Patches of poor rye where corn should have been, patches of poor peas and beans, patches of most coarse vegetable substitutes for wheat. On inanimate nature, as on the men and women who cultivated it, a prevalent tendency towards an appearance of vegetating unwillingly--a dejected disposition to give up, and wither away.

text: Nearly everyone in the room had their eyes locked on him, save for the one powdered-wig gentleman who was staring blankly up at the ceiling. Every exhalation in the space seemed to surge toward him, as unrelenting as a tide, a gale, or a wildfire. Faces craned around pillars and sharp corners to catch even a sliver of a look at him; spectators in the back rows jumped to their feet, unwilling to miss so much as a single strand of his hair. People standing on the court floor pressed their palms to the shoulders of the people in front of them, willing to inconvenience anyone at all just to secure a better view—they balanced on tiptoes, clambered onto low ledges, perched on whatever flimsy surface they could scrabble onto, all to take in every inch of him. Standing out among this latter group was Jerry, looking as sharp and unyielding as a living fragment of Newgate Prison’s spiked perimeter wall. He huffed beer-tinged breath from the quick drink he’d grabbed on his way in, adding it to the roiling cloud of other beer fumes, gin, tea, coffee, and a dozen other unidentifiable scents washing toward the prisoner. That thick, stale mix already fogged the large windows behind the man, beading them with a grimy, damp haze.
source_text: Everybody present, except the one wigged gentleman who looked at the ceiling, stared at him. All the human breath in the place, rolled at him, like a sea, or a wind, or a fire. Eager faces strained round pillars and corners, to get a sight of him; spectators in back rows stood up, not to miss a hair of him; people on the floor of the court, laid their hands on the shoulders of the people before them, to help themselves, at anybody’s cost, to a view of him--stood a-tiptoe, got upon ledges, stood upon next to nothing, to see every inch of him. Conspicuous among these latter, like an animated bit of the spiked wall of Newgate, Jerry stood: aiming at the prisoner the beery breath of a whet he had taken as he came along, and discharging it to mingle with the waves of other beer, and gin, and tea, and coffee, and what not, that flowed at him, and already broke upon the great windows behind him in an impure mist and rain.

text: When he first came to London, he never held the naive expectation that the sidewalks would be paved with gold, or that he’d get to live a cushy, effortless life full of nothing but comfort. If he’d clung to any such absurdly lofty hopes, he never would have thrived the way he did. He’d arrived anticipating hard work, and that’s exactly what he found: he put his head down, got every task done, and made the most of every part of the experience. That steady, uncomplaining commitment was the entire foundation of his success.
source_text: In London, he had expected neither to walk on pavements of gold, nor to lie on beds of roses; if he had had any such exalted expectation, he would not have prospered. He had expected labour, and he found it, and did it and made the best of it. In this, his prosperity consisted.

text: “Do not weep for me,” he murmured, “I am not deserving of this grief, Miss Thorne. In an hour or two, the sordid company and shameful vices I despise yet cannot resist will make me far less worthy of these tears than any ragged beggar crawling through the gutter. Hush, now. In my soul, I will always hold for you the same regard I bear in this moment, even if my outward self sinks back to the wretched state you have known before. The second-to-last request I will ever ask of you is that you hold this truth in your heart, and believe it of me.”
source_text: “Be comforted!” he said, “I am not worth such feeling, Miss Manette. An hour or two hence, and the low companions and low habits that I scorn but yield to, will render me less worth such tears as those, than any wretch who creeps along the streets. Be comforted! But, within myself, I shall always be, towards you, what I am now, though outwardly I shall be what you have heretofore seen me. The last supplication but one I make to you, is, that you will believe this of me.”

text: Even in his flustered, agitated state, Mr. Lorry couldn’t help but notice the feral, unkempt woman barrelling toward him. She was flushed bright red all over, had vivid fiery hair, wore some odd, extremely tight-fitting outfit, and perched on her head was the most outlandish bonnet imaginable—it looked just like a full grenadier’s wooden measuring jug, or a giant wheel of Stilton cheese. She burst into the room ahead of all the inn’s servants, and immediately resolved the question of separating him from the poor young lady: she planted a thick, brawny hand flat against his chest, and sent him flying backward to slam into the closest wall.
source_text: A wild-looking woman, whom even in his agitation, Mr. Lorry observed to be all of a red colour, and to have red hair, and to be dressed in some extraordinary tight-fitting fashion, and to have on her head a most wonderful bonnet like a Grenadier wooden measure, and good measure too, or a great Stilton cheese, came running into the room in advance of the inn servants, and soon settled the question of his detachment from the poor young lady, by laying a brawny hand upon his chest, and sending him flying back against the nearest wall.

text: Devising a protective measure that was already being carried out had lifted a great weight from Miss Elspeth Rowe’s shoulders. The need to smooth out her disheveled appearance so she would draw no unusual stares while out on the public streets offered a second, smaller comfort. She flicked a glance at her scuffed brass pocket watch: it was twenty minutes past two. There was not a moment to spare, and she had to make her final preparations at once.
source_text: The having originated a precaution which was already in course of execution, was a great relief to Miss Pross. The necessity of composing her appearance so that it should attract no special notice in the streets, was another relief. She looked at her watch, and it was twenty minutes past two. She had no time to lose, but must get ready at once.

text: He left both his armed escort and the man they’d been accompanying without a single syllable of explanation, stepping straight into the cramped guard post; the rest of the party remained mounted just beyond the gate, shifting restlessly in their saddles. Strung taut between dread and uncertainty as he waited, Elias Thorne scanned the gate perimeter, noting the sentry detail was a jumble of regular army troops and citizen patriots, the latter far outstripping the former in number. He also saw that while entry into the city was nearly unimpeded for peasant carts hauling grain and produce, and for other small-scale traders and their wares, exiting the walls was brutally difficult, even for the most unassuming, harmless-looking residents. A chaotic throng of men, women, even children, not to mention draft animals and rickety carts of every description, had clustered at the barrier waiting to leave; the required pre-exit identity checks were so rigorous that only a handful of people slipped through each minute. Dozens in the crowd knew their turn for questioning was still hours off, so they’d stretched out on the hard packed dirt to nap or roll rough tobacco, while others clustered in chattering knots or wandered aimlessly along the edge of the throng. The red liberty cap and tricolor cockade were near-universal, pinned to hats, coats, and frayed neck scarves alike on every man and woman in the press of people.
source_text: He left escort and escorted without saying a word, however, and went into the guard-room; meanwhile, they sat upon their horses outside the gate. Looking about him while in this state of suspense, Charles Darnay observed that the gate was held by a mixed guard of soldiers and patriots, the latter far outnumbering the former; and that while ingress into the city for peasants’ carts bringing in supplies, and for similar traffic and traffickers, was easy enough, egress, even for the homeliest people, was very difficult. A numerous medley of men and women, not to mention beasts and vehicles of various sorts, was waiting to issue forth; but, the previous identification was so strict, that they filtered through the barrier very slowly. Some of these people knew their turn for examination to be so far off, that they lay down on the ground to sleep or smoke, while others talked together, or loitered about. The red cap and tri-colour cockade were universal, both among men and women.

text: Still, le Comte de Valroux had gradually come to realize that crass, unseemly misfortunes were seeping into every corner of his dealings, both personal and state-related; and he had, of necessity, struck a partnership with a Tax-Farmer for both spheres of his life. Where public finances were concerned, the comte was entirely incapable of untangling their knotted ledgers, and so had no choice but to contract the work out to someone with the skill to make them function; as for his personal accounts, Tax-Farmers accumulated fortunes faster than any other class in the realm, and the comte, after generations of his line’s unbridled luxury and reckless spending, was sliding rapidly into crippling debt. For this reason, the comte had pulled his younger sister from the abbey while there was still time to spare her the impending nun’s veil—the only garment in the world that would cost him nothing to clothe her in—and offered her hand as a reward to a phenomenally wealthy Tax-Farmer of exceedingly common lineage. This same Tax-Farmer, who carried a polished ebony cane capped with a gilded pomegranate, was now lingering among the crowd in the outer antechambers, bowed and scraped to by nearly everyone in attendance—always excepting those of the comte’s own noble bloodline, who, his new wife included, regarded him with the most unassailable, icy scorn.
source_text: Yet, Monseigneur had slowly found that vulgar embarrassments crept into his affairs, both private and public; and he had, as to both classes of affairs, allied himself perforce with a Farmer-General. As to finances public, because Monseigneur could not make anything at all of them, and must consequently let them out to somebody who could; as to finances private, because Farmer-Generals were rich, and Monseigneur, after generations of great luxury and expense, was growing poor. Hence Monseigneur had taken his sister from a convent, while there was yet time to ward off the impending veil, the cheapest garment she could wear, and had bestowed her as a prize upon a very rich Farmer-General, poor in family. Which Farmer-General, carrying an appropriate cane with a golden apple on the top of it, was now among the company in the outer rooms, much prostrated before by mankind--always excepting superior mankind of the blood of Monseigneur, who, his own wife included, looked down upon him with the loftiest contempt.

text: Even so, not a single moment was wasted—none at all. That despised Foulon was being held at the Hôtel de Ville, and there was every risk he could be turned loose. That would never happen, not if the people of Saint Antoine remembered all the suffering, humiliation, and injustice he had inflicted on them. Armed men and women flooded out of the quarter so quickly, and pulled even the last remaining stragglers along in their wake with the unyielding force of a suction current, that within fifteen minutes, the very heart of Saint Antoine held no living soul save a few frail old women and their wailing young children.
source_text: Nevertheless, not a moment was lost; not a moment! This Foulon was at the Hotel de Ville, and might be loosed. Never, if Saint Antoine knew his own sufferings, insults, and wrongs! Armed men and women flocked out of the Quarter so fast, and drew even these last dregs after them with such a force of suction, that within a quarter of an hour there was not a human creature in Saint Antoine’s bosom but a few old crones and the wailing children.

text: What followed was one of those uncanny, all-too-familiar public displays: the crowd would indulge these bursts sometimes to feed their own fickleness, sometimes to act on the better, more generous and merciful parts of their nature, and sometimes to score what felt like a moral offset against the long, ugly tally of cruel violence they’d racked up elsewhere. There’s no way to know for certain which of these drives was at work this time, though it’s likely a mix of all three, with that kinder, more compassionate impulse coming out on top. The second the not-guilty verdict was read, people cried as hard and unreservedly as they’d spilled blood on other, uglier days. Every man and woman who could shove their way to the front wrapped the newly freed man in warm, eager hugs, and after weeks of cramped, unhygienic confinement, he came dangerously close to passing out from the sudden overstimulation and exertion. His disorientation wasn’t helped by the quiet, bitter certainty he carried: that if mob sentiment had swung the other direction, that exact same crowd would have surged toward him just as fiercely, ready to tear him limb from limb and leave his remains scattered across the pavement.
source_text: Then, began one of those extraordinary scenes with which the populace sometimes gratified their fickleness, or their better impulses towards generosity and mercy, or which they regarded as some set-off against their swollen account of cruel rage. No man can decide now to which of these motives such extraordinary scenes were referable; it is probable, to a blending of all the three, with the second predominating. No sooner was the acquittal pronounced, than tears were shed as freely as blood at another time, and such fraternal embraces were bestowed upon the prisoner by as many of both sexes as could rush at him, that after his long and unwholesome confinement he was in danger of fainting from exhaustion; none the less because he knew very well, that the very same people, carried by another current, would have rushed at him with the very same intensity, to rend him to pieces and strew him over the streets.

text: “Hold on, you’re being far too hasty here, sir,” Barsad said, a smirk tugging his sharp hooked nose even further lopsided to one side; “that really puts me a step ahead of you, doesn’t it? Cly—who I’ll freely admit, this many years down the line, was a former associate of mine—has been dead for years. I looked after him through the entirety of his final illness. He was buried in London, at the Saint Pancras-in-the-Fields church. He was so reviled by the rabble of rough, lawless thugs back then that I couldn’t walk behind his casket in the procession, but I did help lay him inside his coffin before it was closed.”
source_text: “Now, there you are hasty, sir,” said Barsad, with a smile that gave his aquiline nose an extra inclination to one side; “there you really give me an advantage over you. Cly (who I will unreservedly admit, at this distance of time, was a partner of mine) has been dead several years. I attended him in his last illness. He was buried in London, at the church of Saint Pancras-in-the-Fields. His unpopularity with the blackguard multitude at the moment prevented my following his remains, but I helped to lay him in his coffin.”

text: A shift had come over the village clustered where the fountain spilled over its stone rim, the same spot where the road mender set out each day to chip at highway stones for the scraps of bread that might hold his unlearned, neglected soul and his gaunt, half-starved body together for another stretch. The stone prison perched on the crag loomed far less imposingly than it had in days gone by; there were soldiers stationed to guard it, but not nearly enough, and officers tasked with overseeing those soldiers, but not one of those officers had any clue what their men would do next—except for this: whatever action they took would almost certainly not follow the orders they’d been given.
source_text: There was a change on the village where the fountain fell, and where the mender of roads went forth daily to hammer out of the stones on the highway such morsels of bread as might serve for patches to hold his poor ignorant soul and his poor reduced body together. The prison on the crag was not so dominant as of yore; there were soldiers to guard it, but not many; there were officers to guard the soldiers, but not one of them knew what his men would do--beyond this: that it would probably not be what he was ordered.

text: My love, I’m asking you to always be as generous as you can with him, and to go easy on his faults when he isn’t around to speak for himself. I need you to trust that he carries a heart he almost never lets anyone see, one marked with deep, old, unhealed wounds. Darling, I’ve watched that very heart bleed firsthand.
source_text: “I would ask you, dearest, to be very generous with him always, and very lenient on his faults when he is not by. I would ask you to believe that he has a heart he very, very seldom reveals, and that there are deep wounds in it. My dear, I have seen it bleeding.”

text: Kael fished a charred cherrywood pipe from his wool coat pocket, tamped crumbs of dried sage tobacco into its bowl, struck a spark with flint and a folded steel striker to catch the loose leaves, and drew slow, deep pulls until the embers inside burned a fierce, steady gold: then he abruptly held the pipe out at arm’s length, flicking a tiny pinched pellet between his forefinger and thumb into its glowing center, which flared bright for an instant before fizzling out in a thin, curling puff of acrid smoke.
source_text: He took out a blackened pipe, filled it, lighted it with flint and steel, pulled at it until it was in a bright glow: then, suddenly held it from him and dropped something into it from between his finger and thumb, that blazed and went out in a puff of smoke.

text: He paced steadily back and forth, arms folded across his chest, a vastly different man from the frantic prisoner who had once tramped ceaseless circuits at La Force. When the clock struck one, the note fading away unheeded, he felt no flicker of surprise. The hour had run its course just like nearly every other that had come before it. He offered a quiet, devout thanks to Heaven for the regained self-possession he held now, thought to himself, There is only one more left, and turned to resume his walking.
source_text: Walking regularly to and fro with his arms folded on his breast, a very different man from the prisoner, who had walked to and fro at La Force, he heard One struck away from him, without surprise. The hour had measured like most other hours. Devoutly thankful to Heaven for his recovered self-possession, he thought, “There is but another now,” and turned to walk again.

text: “You curs!” said Count Valerius, his tone silken, his pale, sharp face set save for the faint throbbing flush that pricked at the edge of his high cheekbone: “I would gallop over every last one of you without a flicker of remorse, and wipe you all from the face of this earth. If I knew which scoundrel hurled that object at my coach, and if that cutthroat were close enough to reach, he would be crushed to pulp beneath my horses’ hooves before he could draw another breath.”
source_text: “You dogs!” said the Marquis, but smoothly, and with an unchanged front, except as to the spots on his nose: “I would ride over any of you very willingly, and exterminate you from the earth. If I knew which rascal threw at the carriage, and if that brigand were sufficiently near it, he should be crushed under the wheels.”

text: It is a staggering truth to ponder, that every single human being is inherently a deep, unknowable secret and mystery to every other person. It strikes me as deeply somber, when I wander into a large city after dark, that each of those huddled, shadowed buildings holds its own secret; that every room inside each of those buildings guards its own hidden truth; that every beating heart among the hundreds of thousands of people there harbors fancies even the closest person to it will never know. There is a thread of the same terror that clings to Death itself woven into this truth. I will never again turn the pages of that beloved book I once cherished, fooling myself that I might one day read every word of it. I will never again gaze down into the depths of that unplumbed water, where fleeting glimmers of light once let me catch faint sights of buried treasure and other sunken things. It was fated that the book would snap shut with a sharp click, forever and always, after I had only read a single page. It was ordained that the water would freeze over into permanent ice, just as light danced across its surface, while I stood unknowing on the bank. My friend is gone, my neighbor has passed, the love that was the very darling of my soul is dead; this is the unyielding solidifying and preservation of the secret that always lived at the core of their unique self, a secret I will carry in my own heart until the day I die. Among all the graves in this city I walk through, is there a single resting soul more unknowable than the bustling people going about their days are to me, in their deepest private selves, or than I am to them?
source_text: A wonderful fact to reflect upon, that every human creature is constituted to be that profound secret and mystery to every other. A solemn consideration, when I enter a great city by night, that every one of those darkly clustered houses encloses its own secret; that every room in every one of them encloses its own secret; that every beating heart in the hundreds of thousands of breasts there, is, in some of its imaginings, a secret to the heart nearest it! Something of the awfulness, even of Death itself, is referable to this. No more can I turn the leaves of this dear book that I loved, and vainly hope in time to read it all. No more can I look into the depths of this unfathomable water, wherein, as momentary lights glanced into it, I have had glimpses of buried treasure and other things submerged. It was appointed that the book should shut with a spring, for ever and for ever, when I had read but a page. It was appointed that the water should be locked in an eternal frost, when the light was playing on its surface, and I stood in ignorance on the shore. My friend is dead, my neighbour is dead, my love, the darling of my soul, is dead; it is the inexorable consolidation and perpetuation of the secret that was always in that individuality, and which I shall carry in mine to my life’s end. In any of the burial-places of this city through which I pass, is there a sleeper more inscrutable than its busy inhabitants are, in their innermost personality, to me, or than I am to them?

text: These were the brightest of times, these were the grimmest of times; this was an age of careful, earned wisdom, this was an age of reckless, unthinking foolishness; this was an epoch of unshakable collective belief, this was an epoch of pervasive, unsparing cynicism; this was the season of illuminating Light, this was the season of smothering Darkness; this was the spring overflowing with unbridled hope, this was the winter hollowed out by total despair. Every possible blessing lay within our grasp, and nothing of worth was ours to claim at all; we were all bound straight for the gates of Heaven, and we were all marching directly down the opposite road. To put it plainly, the era was so strikingly similar to our current moment that its loudest, most self-assured authorities insisted it be assessed, for better or for worse, exclusively in the most extreme, unnuanced superlative terms.
source_text: It was the best of times, it was the worst of times, it was the age of wisdom, it was the age of foolishness, it was the epoch of belief, it was the epoch of incredulity, it was the season of Light, it was the season of Darkness, it was the spring of hope, it was the winter of despair, we had everything before us, we had nothing before us, we were all going direct to Heaven, we were all going direct the other way--in short, the period was so far like the present period, that some of its noisiest authorities insisted on its being received, for good or for evil, in the superlative degree of comparison only.

text: I make sure the soldiers don’t catch that I know the tall man, and he hides that he knows me too—we pass the acknowledgement only through our eyes, both of us fully aware of the unspoken exchange. “Hurry up!” barks the company commander, jabbing a finger toward the village, “get him to his grave quick!” They yank the man along at a faster pace, and I trail after them. His arms are puffed and swollen from the too-tight ropes binding them, his wooden clogs are oversized and awkward, and he walks with a limp. Since his limp slows him down, the soldiers jab at him with their rifles to force him onward—just like this!
source_text: “I do not show the soldiers that I recognise the tall man; he does not show the soldiers that he recognises me; we do it, and we know it, with our eyes. ‘Come on!’ says the chief of that company, pointing to the village, ‘bring him fast to his tomb!’ and they bring him faster. I follow. His arms are swelled because of being bound so tight, his wooden shoes are large and clumsy, and he is lame. Because he is lame, and consequently slow, they drive him with their guns--like this!”

text: “That was all I had it in me to do,” said Thorne. “To press for any more concessions would be to lay that man’s neck directly beneath the headsman’s axe, and as he himself put it, no worse harm could befall him if he were denounced to the authorities. The fragility was inherent to the whole position, obvious from the first. There is no way to remedy it.”
source_text: “It is all I could do,” said Carton. “To propose too much, would be to put this man’s head under the axe, and, as he himself said, nothing worse could happen to him if he were denounced. It was obviously the weakness of the position. There is no help for it.”

text: Mr. Lorry and Monsieur Defarge had finished all preparations for their journey, and brought along more than just traveling cloaks and warm wraps: they had also packed bread, meat, wine, and steaming hot coffee. Monsieur Defarge set this stock of provisions, along with the lamp he’d been carrying, on the shoemaker’s workbench—there was nothing else in the cramped garret save for a thin pallet bed—before he and Mr. Lorry roused the captive and helped him to his feet.
source_text: Mr. Lorry and Monsieur Defarge had made all ready for the journey, and had brought with them, besides travelling cloaks and wrappers, bread and meat, wine, and hot coffee. Monsieur Defarge put this provender, and the lamp he carried, on the shoemaker’s bench (there was nothing else in the garret but a pallet bed), and he and Mr. Lorry roused the captive, and assisted him to his feet.

text: ‘That man standing over there robbed us blind, the same way every last one of us lowly common dogs are crushed under the boots of those so-called superior beings—he taxes us without a scrap of mercy, forces us to work for him for no pay, makes us grind all our grain at his mill, compels us to feed dozens of his pet birds off our scrawny, meager crops, and bans us on pain of death from keeping even one single caged bird of our own. We’ve been picked clean and looted so thoroughly that if we ever got lucky enough to have a small bit of meat, we’d eat it shaking with fear, barring the door and latching the shutters tight so none of his goons would spot it and yank it right out of our hands. I’m telling you, we were so thoroughly cheated, hounded, and ground into abject poverty that our father told us bringing a child into this kind of world was a dreadful curse, and the only thing we ought to pray for most of all was that our women would be barren, so our miserable, suffering line could die out entirely!’
source_text: “‘We were so robbed by that man who stands there, as all we common dogs are by those superior Beings--taxed by him without mercy, obliged to work for him without pay, obliged to grind our corn at his mill, obliged to feed scores of his tame birds on our wretched crops, and forbidden for our lives to keep a single tame bird of our own, pillaged and plundered to that degree that when we chanced to have a bit of meat, we ate it in fear, with the door barred and the shutters closed, that his people should not see it and take it from us--I say, we were so robbed, and hunted, and were made so poor, that our father told us it was a dreadful thing to bring a child into the world, and that what we should most pray for, was, that our women might be barren and our miserable race die out!’

text: It was so clearly hopeless to reason with him, or try to restore his old self, that—as if by unspoken accord—they each laid a hand upon his shoulder, and gently persuaded him to sit down before the fire, promising he could return to his work shortly. He sank into the chair, brooding over the smoldering embers, and let tears slip down his cheeks. As if everything that had happened since they pulled him from the garret were nothing but a fleeting fancy, or a wisp of a dream, Mr. Lorry watched him shrink into the exact gaunt figure that Defarge had once kept locked away.
source_text: It was so clearly beyond hope to reason with him, or try to restore him, that--as if by agreement--they each put a hand upon his shoulder, and soothed him to sit down before the fire, with a promise that he should have his work presently. He sank into the chair, and brooded over the embers, and shed tears. As if all that had happened since the garret time were a momentary fancy, or a dream, Mr. Lorry saw him shrink into the exact figure that Defarge had had in keeping.

text: I know I hold a sacred, untouchable space in their hearts, and in the hearts of every generation of their descendants that follows. I can picture her, years from now, a stooped elderly woman, crying for me every year on the anniversary of this very day. I can see her and her husband too, when their lives have run their full course, laid side by side in their final resting place, and I know without a doubt that neither of them held the other in higher, more reverent regard than they both held me, deep in their souls.
source_text: “I see that I hold a sanctuary in their hearts, and in the hearts of their descendants, generations hence. I see her, an old woman, weeping for me on the anniversary of this day. I see her and her husband, their course done, lying side by side in their last earthly bed, and I know that each was not more honoured and held sacred in the other’s soul, than I was in the souls of both.

text: “You’ll learn what this is about right away. Keep writing; there are only a few more words left.” He began dictating again. “‘I am grateful the moment has finally arrived when I can prove them true. That I do so is no cause for regret or sorrow.’” His eyes remained locked on the writer as he spoke the words, and his hand moved slowly, gently downward until it hovered just beside the writer’s face.
source_text: “You shall know directly. Write on; there are but a few words more.” He dictated again. “‘I am thankful that the time has come, when I can prove them. That I do so is no subject for regret or grief.’” As he said these words with his eyes fixed on the writer, his hand slowly and softly moved down close to the writer’s face.

text: It was a cloudy, moon-fogged night in the third week of December 1757—the twenty-second, if my memory serves right. I was out walking a quiet, little-trafficked stretch of the Seine’s quayside, drinking in the sharp frosty air to clear my head, my lodgings on School of Medicine Street a full hour’s walk away, when a carriage came speeding up behind me at a reckless pace. Worried it would run me over if I stayed in its path, I stepped to the edge of the quay to let it pass, but just then a person stuck their head out of the carriage window and shouted for the driver to pull over.
source_text: “One cloudy moonlight night, in the third week of December (I think the twenty-second of the month) in the year 1757, I was walking on a retired part of the quay by the Seine for the refreshment of the frosty air, at an hour’s distance from my place of residence in the Street of the School of Medicine, when a carriage came along behind me, driven very fast. As I stood aside to let that carriage pass, apprehensive that it might otherwise run me down, a head was put out at the window, and a voice called to the driver to stop.

text: Stretching far and wide lay a blighted country, yielding nothing but utter desolation. Every green leaf, every blade of grass and stalk of grain, was as shrivelled and meager as the miserable, gaunt people who inhabited it. All things were bowed low, dispirited, ground down by hardship, and broken. Dwellings, fences, domesticated animals, men, women, children, and the very soil that bore them—every last one was thoroughly worn out.
source_text: Far and wide lay a ruined country, yielding nothing but desolation. Every green leaf, every blade of grass and blade of grain, was as shrivelled and poor as the miserable people. Everything was bowed down, dejected, oppressed, and broken. Habitations, fences, domesticated animals, men, women, children, and the soil that bore them--all worn out.

text: That’s the whole of it, messieurs. I left as the sun sank, just as I’d been warned to do, and walked straight through that night and half the following day, until I ran into this comrade, exactly as I’d been told I would. With him, I pushed on the rest of yesterday and all last night, sometimes riding, sometimes on foot. And here I am, just as you see!
source_text: “That’s all, messieurs. I left at sunset (as I had been warned to do), and I walked on, that night and half next day, until I met (as I was warned I should) this comrade. With him, I came on, now riding and now walking, through the rest of yesterday and through last night. And here you see me!”

text: As he lifted his daughter’s spirits, her father carried himself with a gentle, magnanimous poise that rose entirely above what he viewed as feminine frailty, and it was a striking thing to behold. The days of the cramped garret, the mind-numbing hours cobbling shoes, the hollow, cold cell of One Hundred and Five, North Tower—none of that had any hold on him now. He had followed through on every goal he’d set for himself, made good on his every promise, and he had saved Charles. Every single one of them could lean on him for whatever they needed.
source_text: Her father, cheering her, showed a compassionate superiority to this woman’s weakness, which was wonderful to see. No garret, no shoemaking, no One Hundred and Five, North Tower, now! He had accomplished the task he had set himself, his promise was redeemed, he had saved Charles. Let them all lean upon him.

text: No crowd milled about the doorway; not a single figure could be spotted at any of the numerous windows lining the street, not even a stray passerby wandered the empty thoroughfare. An uncanny stillness and sense of abandonment reigned over the whole space. The only living soul in sight was Madame Defarge, leaning against the doorpost, her needles moving steadily as she knitted, and she saw nothing of the desolation around her.
source_text: No crowd was about the door; no people were discernible at any of the many windows; not even a chance passerby was in the street. An unnatural silence and desertion reigned there. Only one soul was to be seen, and that was Madame Defarge--who leaned against the door-post, knitting, and saw nothing.

text: A roar split the air, as though every breath held in every pair of lungs across France had been forged into that single detested word. The living tide of people swelled, crest after crest, layer upon layer, surging through the city’s streets until it flooded all the way to that targeted spot. Alarm bells pealed, drums rattled, and as that churning human sea raged and thundered against its new shore, the attack began.
source_text: With a roar that sounded as if all the breath in France had been shaped into the detested word, the living sea rose, wave on wave, depth on depth, and overflowed the city to that point. Alarm-bells ringing, drums beating, the sea raging and thundering on its new beach, the attack began.

text: He penned a long letter to Lucie, making clear he’d had no inkling of her father’s imprisonment until she herself told him, and that he’d been just as ignorant as she was that his own father and uncle bore responsibility for that suffering, until the hidden document was read aloud. He had already explained to her previously that concealing the family name he’d renounced from her was the sole condition her father had attached to their betrothal—a condition that made full sense now—and the one promise he had pressed him to keep again on the morning of their wedding. He begged her, for her father’s sake, never to seek to learn whether her father had forgotten the document existed entirely, or if the story of the Tower they’d discussed that long-ago Sunday beneath the beloved old plane tree in the garden had brought it back to his mind, whether for a passing moment or for good. If he did retain any clear memory of it, there was no doubt he would have assumed it was destroyed when the Bastille fell, since no mention of it had turned up among the prisoners’ relics the populace uncovered there, relics whose contents had been shared widely with the whole world. He entreated her—though he added he knew the request was unnecessary—to comfort her father, using every gentle, tender means she could think of to impress upon him the truth that he had done nothing he could justly reproach himself for, and had consistently set his own needs aside for the sake of Lucie and their family. Next to her holding fast to his own last, grateful love and blessing, and working through her grief to devote herself to their dear child, he implored her, as they would one day meet again in Heaven, to tend to her father’s peace.
source_text: He wrote a long letter to Lucie, showing her that he had known nothing of her father’s imprisonment, until he had heard of it from herself, and that he had been as ignorant as she of his father’s and uncle’s responsibility for that misery, until the paper had been read. He had already explained to her that his concealment from herself of the name he had relinquished, was the one condition--fully intelligible now--that her father had attached to their betrothal, and was the one promise he had still exacted on the morning of their marriage. He entreated her, for her father’s sake, never to seek to know whether her father had become oblivious of the existence of the paper, or had had it recalled to him (for the moment, or for good), by the story of the Tower, on that old Sunday under the dear old plane-tree in the garden. If he had preserved any definite remembrance of it, there could be no doubt that he had supposed it destroyed with the Bastille, when he had found no mention of it among the relics of prisoners which the populace had discovered there, and which had been described to all the world. He besought her--though he added that he knew it was needless--to console her father, by impressing him through every tender means she could think of, with the truth that he had done nothing for which he could justly reproach himself, but had uniformly forgotten himself for their joint sakes. Next to her preservation of his own last grateful love and blessing, and her overcoming of her sorrow, to devote herself to their dear child, he adjured her, as they would meet in Heaven, to comfort her father.

text: They journeyed only under cover of darkness, halting for an hour or two shortly after daybreak, then lying low out of sight until twilight fell once more. The men escorting him were so wretchedly clothed that they twisted straw around their bare legs, and patched the tattered shoulders of their ragged garments with rushes to keep the rain off. Aside from the personal discomfort of being attended by such a bedraggled party, and the immediate danger posed by one chronically drunk patriot who carried his musket with reckless carelessness, Charles Darnay did not let the restraint placed upon him stir any serious fears in his breast. He reasoned to himself that the detention could have no connection to the merits of his individual case, which he had not yet been allowed to state, nor to the corroborating testimony he could secure from the prisoner held in the Abbaye, which he had not yet had the chance to present.
source_text: They travelled in the night, halting an hour or two after daybreak, and lying by until the twilight fell. The escort were so wretchedly clothed, that they twisted straw round their bare legs, and thatched their ragged shoulders to keep the wet off. Apart from the personal discomfort of being so attended, and apart from such considerations of present danger as arose from one of the patriots being chronically drunk, and carrying his musket very recklessly, Charles Darnay did not allow the restraint that was laid upon him to awaken any serious fears in his breast; for, he reasoned with himself that it could have no reference to the merits of an individual case that was not yet stated, and of representations, confirmable by the prisoner in the Abbaye, that were not yet made.

text: Yet her courage was so deeply rooted in raw, overwhelming feeling that it drew unbidden, unstoppable tears to her eyes. It was a brand of courage Madame Defarge could not fathom at all, so she misread it entirely as weakness. “Ha, ha!” she laughed harshly, “you poor, pathetic wretch! What good are you even worth? I’m here to speak to the Doctor.” Then she lifted her voice to shout across the space: “Citizen Doctor! Wife of Evrémonde! Child of Evrémonde! Anyone besides this miserable fool, answer Citizeness Defarge!”
source_text: But, her courage was of that emotional nature that it brought the irrepressible tears into her eyes. This was a courage that Madame Defarge so little comprehended as to mistake for weakness. “Ha, ha!” she laughed, “you poor wretch! What are you worth! I address myself to that Doctor.” Then she raised her voice and called out, “Citizen Doctor! Wife of Evrémonde! Child of Evrémonde! Any person but this miserable fool, answer the Citizeness Defarge!”

text: When he dipped his head in his most polished, courtly bow, a secretive edge lingered beneath his smile, and the words he spoke carried such a distinct air of mystery that they struck his nephew forcibly, sharp to both eye and ear. At the same moment, the crisp, straight lines framing his eyes, his thin, stiff lips, and the faint creases along his nose all curved into a note of sarcasm that looked handsomely, almost alluringly diabolic.
source_text: As he bent his head in his most courtly manner, there was a secrecy in his smiling face, and he conveyed an air of mystery to those words, which struck the eyes and ears of his nephew forcibly. At the same time, the thin straight lines of the setting of the eyes, and the thin straight lips, and the markings in the nose, curved with a sarcasm that looked handsomely diabolic.

text: “I’m telling you,” madame said, holding out her right hand to drive her point home, “even if it’s been plodding along its path for ages, it’s well on its way and closing in. I’m telling you it never turns back, never stops for anything. I’m telling you it’s always pushing forward. Look around you—think about the lives of every person we know across this whole world, look at the faces of all the people we recognize, consider the rage and discontent the Jacquerie tends to, growing more sure of its footing with every single hour. Do you really think a state of affairs like this can last? Bah! I’m mocking you for even imagining it could.”
source_text: “I tell thee,” said madame, extending her right hand, for emphasis, “that although it is a long time on the road, it is on the road and coming. I tell thee it never retreats, and never stops. I tell thee it is always advancing. Look around and consider the lives of all the world that we know, consider the faces of all the world that we know, consider the rage and discontent to which the Jacquerie addresses itself with more and more of certainty every hour. Can such things last? Bah! I mock you.”

text: Then Mr. Stryver turned on his heel and stormed out of the bank, churning up such a sharp gust of air in his wake that the two elderly clerks, bowing behind their respective counters, had to draw on the very last of their strength just to stay upright against the draft. Those frail, venerable old men were always seen by members of the public in the act of bowing, and a popular local belief held that once they had bowed a customer out the door, they kept right on bowing in the empty office until the next patron stepped through the threshold, to be greeted by the same steady, deferential dip of their heads.
source_text: Then Mr. Stryver turned and burst out of the Bank, causing such a concussion of air on his passage through, that to stand up against it bowing behind the two counters, required the utmost remaining strength of the two ancient clerks. Those venerable and feeble persons were always seen by the public in the act of bowing, and were popularly believed, when they had bowed a customer out, still to keep on bowing in the empty office until they bowed another customer in.

text: Now the cloud that a fleeting glimmer had briefly chased from the sacred face of Saint Antoine settled once more, wrapping the district in thick, heavy darkness. Cold, filth, sickness, ignorance, and want were the courtiers attending this saintly domain—all nobles of immense power, none more formidable than the last. The people here had been ground and re-ground in the jaws of a mill, certainly not the fabled one that restored youth to the aged. They shivered in every corner, slipped in and out of every doorway, stared hollow-eyed from every window, their tattered garments fluttering in the wind like faint, ragged markers of their suffering. The mill that wore them down was the kind that ages the young before their time: children bore ancient, lined faces and hollow, grave voices. The sigh of Hunger was etched into every child’s expression, every grown person’s countenance, carved deep into every wrinkle of the old, and surfaced again in every ragged breath they drew. It was omnipresent, seeping into every cranny of the district. Hunger spilled out of the tall tenement houses, woven into the rags strung up on poles and washing lines; Hunger was stitched into those scraps with straw, sacking, wood splinters, and paper. It marked every splinter of the meager firewood a man sawed for his hearth; it stared down from smokeless chimneys, and rose from the filth-caked streets where even the refuse held no scrap of offal fit to eat. Hunger was scrawled across the baker’s shelves, in every small, stale loaf of his sparse stock of poor-quality bread; it lurked in the sausage shop, in every preparation made from dead dog put up for sale. It rattled its parched bones among the chestnuts turning in the roasting cylinder; it was chopped into tiny fragments in every farthing porringer of tough potato scraps, fried with a few grudging drops of oil.
source_text: And now that the cloud settled on Saint Antoine, which a momentary gleam had driven from his sacred countenance, the darkness of it was heavy--cold, dirt, sickness, ignorance, and want, were the lords in waiting on the saintly presence--nobles of great power all of them; but, most especially the last. Samples of a people that had undergone a terrible grinding and regrinding in the mill, and certainly not in the fabulous mill which ground old people young, shivered at every corner, passed in and out at every doorway, looked from every window, fluttered in every vestige of a garment that the wind shook. The mill which had worked them down, was the mill that grinds young people old; the children had ancient faces and grave voices; and upon them, and upon the grown faces, and ploughed into every furrow of age and coming up afresh, was the sigh, Hunger. It was prevalent everywhere.
