Hunger was pushed out of the tall houses, in the wretched clothing that hung upon poles and lines; Hunger was patched into them with straw and rag and wood and paper; Hunger was repeated in every fragment of the small modicum of firewood that the man sawed off; Hunger stared down from the smokeless chimneys, and started up from the filthy street that had no offal, among its refuse, of anything to eat. Hunger was the inscription on the baker’s shelves, written in every small loaf of his scanty stock of bad bread; at the sausage-shop, in every dead-dog preparation that was offered for sale. Hunger rattled its dry bones among the roasting chestnuts in the turned cylinder; Hunger was shred into atomics in every farthing porringer of husky chips of potato, fried with some reluctant drops of oil.

text: As the droning, tar-black wheels of the six prison wagons grind forward, they carve a long, meandering furrow through the throng packed tight in the cobblestone streets. Waves of upturned faces are shoved left and right as the heavy wagons roll steadily onward, unheeding. The area’s long-term residents are so inured to the grim pageant that many windows stand empty of onlookers, and in others, people’s hands never pause in their mending, kneading, or sorting even as their eyes flick to the haggard faces huddled in the open backs of the tumbrils. Every so often, a household has guests come specifically to watch the procession; then the resident will jab a finger, with the quiet self-satisfaction of a museum steward or official guide, at one wagon then another, as if explaining which condemned soul rode this spot yesterday, and which occupied the same rough bench two days prior.
source_text: As the sombre wheels of the six carts go round, they seem to plough up a long crooked furrow among the populace in the streets. Ridges of faces are thrown to this side and to that, and the ploughs go steadily onward. So used are the regular inhabitants of the houses to the spectacle, that in many windows there are no people, and in some the occupation of the hands is not so much as suspended, while the eyes survey the faces in the tumbrils. Here and there, the inmate has visitors to see the sight; then he points his finger, with something of the complacency of a curator or authorised exponent, to this cart and to this, and seems to tell who sat here yesterday, and who there the day before.

text: It lay beneath his hand, so I spoke softly to calm him enough that I could gently shift it out of the way. The gash was a sword stab, inflicted somewhere between 20 and 24 hours earlier, but even the most skilled healer couldn’t have saved him if he’d received care immediately after the injury. He was fading fast by that point. When I lifted my eyes to the older brother, I saw him staring down at the handsome young man whose life was ebbing away, as if he were nothing more than a wounded bird, hare, or rabbit—there was no trace of recognition that he was looking at a fellow human being.
source_text: “It was under his hand, and I soothed him to let me move his hand away. The wound was a sword-thrust, received from twenty to twenty-four hours before, but no skill could have saved him if it had been looked to without delay. He was then dying fast. As I turned my eyes to the elder brother, I saw him looking down at this handsome boy whose life was ebbing out, as if he were a wounded bird, or hare, or rabbit; not at all as if he were a fellow-creature.

text: This exchange unfolded at Mr. Lorry’s regular work desk, with a crowd of refugee aristocrats clustered barely three feet away, loudly bragging about how they would soon take brutal revenge on the lowborn rabble they blamed for their troubles. That kind of talk was par for the course for these deposed nobles navigating their exile, and just as common among rigid, conventional British thinkers of the time: they spoke of the horrific Revolution as if it were the only harvest in human history that had sprung up entirely unseeded. They acted as if no choice, no failure to act, had paved the way for the uprising; as if the people who’d watched France’s millions of destitute citizens suffer, who’d seen the resources that should have made them thrive hoarded and misused for the elite, hadn’t seen the collapse coming for years, and hadn’t documented their observations in clear, unambiguous writing. All this empty posturing, paired with the aristocrats’ absurd schemes to restore a regime that had already completely devoured itself, alienating every last person on earth and even defying all sense of divine justice in the process, was almost impossible for any sensible person who knew the full truth to tolerate without speaking up. And it was this endless stream of loud, foolish rhetoric swirling right around him, buzzing in his ears like the disorienting thud of blood rushing to his own skull, layered over the quiet, underlying anxiety he already held in his chest, that had first left Charles Darnay restless – and that still kept him that way.
source_text: This dialogue had taken place at Mr. Lorry’s usual desk, with Monseigneur swarming within a yard or two of it, boastful of what he would do to avenge himself on the rascal-people before long. It was too much the way of Monseigneur under his reverses as a refugee, and it was much too much the way of native British orthodoxy, to talk of this terrible Revolution as if it were the only harvest ever known under the skies that had not been sown--as if nothing had ever been done, or omitted to be done, that had led to it--as if observers of the wretched millions in France, and of the misused and perverted resources that should have made them prosperous, had not seen it inevitably coming, years before, and had not in plain words recorded what they saw. Such vapouring, combined with the extravagant plots of Monseigneur for the restoration of a state of things that had utterly exhausted itself, and worn out Heaven and earth as well as itself, was hard to be endured without some remonstrance by any sane man who knew the truth. And it was such vapouring all about his ears, like a troublesome confusion of blood in his own head, added to a latent uneasiness in his mind, which had already made Charles Darnay restless, and which still kept him so.

text: “Listen to me again, Jacques!” said the kneeling Number Three: his fingers drifted ceaselessly over those fine nerves, with a startlingly ravenous edge to his bearing, as if he hungered for something that was neither food nor drink; “the guard, mounted and on foot, closed in around the petitioner and rained blows upon him. You hear?”
source_text: “And once again listen, Jacques!” said the kneeling Number Three: his fingers ever wandering over and over those fine nerves, with a strikingly greedy air, as if he hungered for something--that was neither food nor drink; “the guard, horse and foot, surrounded the petitioner, and struck him blows. You hear?”

text: If you could speak unflinchingly honest tonight, to the lonely, closed recesses of your own heart, “I have never earned the love and devotion, the gratitude or respect, of a single living soul; no one holds a soft, tender spot for me in their affections; I have never done a single good or useful deed that anyone would remember me for!” those seventy-eight years of yours would hang over you as seventy-eight heavy, bitter curses, would they not?
source_text: “If you could say, with truth, to your own solitary heart, to-night, ‘I have secured to myself the love and attachment, the gratitude or respect, of no human creature; I have won myself a tender place in no regard; I have done nothing good or serviceable to be remembered by!’ your seventy-eight years would be seventy-eight heavy curses; would they not?”

text: The jittery dread coiled tight in Elias’s bones stoked that faint, gnawing unease about the Central Custodial Vault, the kind of restless anxiety any sudden, seismic shift would naturally rouse when one’s nerves were already frayed to snapping. The facility was heavily fortified, and he pushed to his feet to join the cadre of vetted sentries monitoring its grounds, when his office door slammed open without preamble. Two shadowed figures surged across the threshold, and the sight of them sent him reeling backward, frozen in astonishment.
source_text: The nervousness and dread that were upon him inspired that vague uneasiness respecting the Bank, which a great change would naturally awaken, with such feelings roused. It was well guarded, and he got up to go among the trusty people who were watching it, when his door suddenly opened, and two figures rushed in, at sight of which he fell back in amazement.

text: Madame Valois’s inky dark eyes tracked her through that sharp, hasty motion, and settled unblinking on her the second it stilled. Mrs. Thorne bore no trace of conventional soft beauty; the passing decades had not tamed the unruly brashness of her bearing, or blunted the hard, unyielding grimness of her face; but she too was a woman of unshakable resolve, forged in her own distinct mold, and she swept her gaze up and down Madame Valois’s full frame, measuring every last inch of her.
source_text: Madame Defarge’s dark eyes followed her through this rapid movement, and rested on her when it was finished. Miss Pross had nothing beautiful about her; years had not tamed the wildness, or softened the grimness, of her appearance; but, she too was a determined woman in her different way, and she measured Madame Defarge with her eyes, every inch.

text: But the curiosity he’d nursed for so long about exactly what was going on here didn’t just bring his flight to a sudden halt—it tugged him right back toward the gate, too. When he peeked through the slats a second time, they were still at their stubborn, steady fishing, but it looked like they’d finally gotten a bite. A creaking, groaning noise drifted up from the dark hollow below, their hunched frames pulled taut as if they were straining against a massive weight. Little by little, that weight dislodged the packed earth covering it and broke through to the open air. Young Jerry had a pretty clear idea what it would turn out to be, but when he laid eyes on it, and watched his supposed honoured father get ready to pry it open, he was so unmoored by the unfamiliar, grisly sight that he turned and ran again, not pausing for breath until he’d put a full mile or more between himself and the spot.
source_text: But, his long-cherished desire to know more about these matters, not only stopped him in his running away, but lured him back again. They were still fishing perseveringly, when he peeped in at the gate for the second time; but, now they seemed to have got a bite. There was a screwing and complaining sound down below, and their bent figures were strained, as if by a weight. By slow degrees the weight broke away the earth upon it, and came to the surface. Young Jerry very well knew what it would be; but, when he saw it, and saw his honoured parent about to wrench it open, he was so frightened, being new to the sight, that he made off again, and never stopped until he had run a mile or more.

text: The horrors he witnessed during those days, broken only by fleeting scraps of sustenance and snatched minutes of sleep, will never be spoken aloud. The wild, unhinged elation for prisoners pulled free from death stunned him nearly as much as the rabid, bloodthirsty cruelty meted out to those hacked apart mid-scream. There was one captive, he recalled, who had been formally freed and sent stumbling into the open road, only for a confused, blood-maddened militiaman to drive a spear through his side as he crossed the threshold. When begged to rush out and tend the man’s injury, Doctor Thorne stepped through that same gate, only to find the wounded man cradled in the arms of a band of self-appointed good samaritans, who were perched directly atop the heaped corpses of the people they’d killed only minutes earlier. In a contradiction as grotesque as any other terror of that living nightmare, these same men aided the doctor, fussed over the injured captive with the softest, most earnest care—fashioned a makeshift stretcher for him and walked him safely out of the fray—then snatched up their axes and pikes and threw themselves back into a slaughter so unrelentingly horrific that Doctor Thorne pressed both palms over his eyes and fainted where he stood.
source_text: The sights he had seen there, with brief snatches of food and sleep by intervals, shall remain untold. The mad joy over the prisoners who were saved, had astounded him scarcely less than the mad ferocity against those who were cut to pieces. One prisoner there was, he said, who had been discharged into the street free, but at whom a mistaken savage had thrust a pike as he passed out. Being besought to go to him and dress the wound, the Doctor had passed out at the same gate, and had found him in the arms of a company of Samaritans, who were seated on the bodies of their victims. With an inconsistency as monstrous as anything in this awful nightmare, they had helped the healer, and tended the wounded man with the gentlest solicitude--had made a litter for him and escorted him carefully from the spot--had then caught up their weapons and plunged anew into a butchery so dreadful, that the Doctor had covered his eyes with his hands, and swooned away in the midst of it.

text: If you catch in my voice—I cannot be sure it is there, but I pray that it might be—if you catch any echo of the voice that was once sweet as song to your ears, weep for it, weep for it! If as you run your fingers through my hair, you feel any flicker of memory of the beloved head that rested against your chest back when you were young and unshackled, weep for it, weep for it! If when I speak softly of the home that waits for us ahead, where I will be loyal to you in every duty, every act of devoted care, you are reminded of the home that stood desolate for so long while your poor heart pined and wasted away, weep for it, weep for it!
source_text: “If you hear in my voice--I don’t know that it is so, but I hope it is--if you hear in my voice any resemblance to a voice that once was sweet music in your ears, weep for it, weep for it! If you touch, in touching my hair, anything that recalls a beloved head that lay on your breast when you were young and free, weep for it, weep for it! If, when I hint to you of a Home that is before us, where I will be true to you with all my duty and with all my faithful service, I bring back the remembrance of a Home long desolate, while your poor heart pined away, weep for it, weep for it!”

text: The ragged youth’s gaze, locked tight on mine a moment before, drifted slowly toward the huddled bystander by the cell bars, and the flicker of unspoken accord across both their faces told me every word he’d spoken was wholly true. Two clashing strains of pride confront one another even here, in this damp, stone Fleet Prison holding cell: the gentleman’s, all careless, dismissive indifference; the farm laborer’s, all trodden-down bitterness and smoldering, fierce hunger for revenge.
source_text: “The boy’s eyes, which had been fixed on mine, slowly turned to the looker-on, and I saw in the two faces that all he said was true. The two opposing kinds of pride confronting one another, I can see, even in this Bastille; the gentleman’s, all negligent indifference; the peasant’s, all trodden-down sentiment, and passionate revenge.

text: Midway through his heated protest, he let his less soiled hand fall—perhaps by accident, perhaps not—square against the trickster’s sternum. The trickster tapped it away sharply with his own palm, bounded lightly upward, and landed in a whimsical, swaying dance stance, one of his mud-streaked shoes having jerked free of his foot mid-leap to land in his grasp, which he held out toward the other man. In that moment, he cut the figure of a trickster of an exceedingly, not to say viciously, practical turn of mind.
source_text: In his expostulation he dropped his cleaner hand (perhaps accidentally, perhaps not) upon the joker’s heart. The joker rapped it with his own, took a nimble spring upward, and came down in a fantastic dancing attitude, with one of his stained shoes jerked off his foot into his hand, and held out. A joker of an extremely, not to say wolfishly practical character, he looked, under those circumstances.

text: Mr. Cruncher had calmed down, though he still shook his head in a doubtful, morally censorious fashion. “It all comes down to how you cultivate your talents. Be careful to nurture those gifts well, and never speak more than you absolutely must to anyone, and there’s no telling right now what you might end up suited for.” As Young Jerry, buoyed by this encouragement, walked a few yards ahead to set his stool in the shadow of the Bar, Mr. Cruncher muttered under his breath to himself: “Jerry, you honest tradesman, there’s every hope that boy will yet be a blessing to you, and make up for every bit of grief his mother has caused you!”
source_text: Mr. Cruncher was soothed, but shook his head in a dubious and moral way. “It depends upon how you dewelop your talents. Be careful to dewelop your talents, and never to say no more than you can help to nobody, and there’s no telling at the present time what you may not come to be fit for.” As Young Jerry, thus encouraged, went on a few yards in advance, to plant the stool in the shadow of the Bar, Mr. Cruncher added to himself: “Jerry, you honest tradesman, there’s hopes wot that boy will yet be a blessing to you, and a recompense to you for his mother!”

text: Sydney had been pulling double shifts that night, the night before, the night before that, and a long stretch of consecutive nights total, powering through a complete sweep of Mr. Stryver’s paperwork before the long vacation kicked off. The sweep was finished at last; all of Stryver’s outstanding backlogs were neatly tied up, every last loose end sorted out, until November rolled around with its thick, hanging atmospheric fog and its equally tangled piles of legal casework, to bring fresh work to keep the practice busy once more.
source_text: Sydney had been working double tides that night, and the night before, and the night before that, and a good many nights in succession, making a grand clearance among Mr. Stryver’s papers before the setting in of the long vacation. The clearance was effected at last; the Stryver arrears were handsomely fetched up; everything was got rid of until November should come with its fogs atmospheric, and fogs legal, and bring grist to the mill again.

text: “Worth no more than that?” Mr. Cruncher repeated. “Then they’re not worth much of anything, are they? Either way, I’m telling you plain, I won’t have people praying against me. I can’t afford that kind of bad luck. I won’t have your sneaking behavior cursing me with misfortune. If you’re determined to flop down on your knees to pray, pray for your husband and your child, not against them. If I’d had any wife but an unnatural one, and this poor boy had any mother but an unnatural one, I might have made a tidy sum last week, instead of being crossed with prayers, undermined, and religiously double-crossed into the worst run of luck I’ve ever had. Blast me!” said Mr. Cruncher, who had been pulling on his clothes this entire time, “If I haven’t been swindled out of every good chance last week, what with all this piety rubbish and one blasted thing after another, worse luck than any poor honest tradesman ever stumbled into! Young Jerry, get dressed, my boy, and while I clean my boots, keep an eye on your mother now and then. If you see any signs of her getting ready to flop down again, call for me. Because I’m telling you,” here he turned to address his wife once more, “I won’t have you working against me like this again. I’m as rickety as a hackney coach, as drowsy as if I’d dosed on laudanum, my muscles are strung so tight I wouldn’t know which was me and which was someone else if it wasn’t for the ache in them, and I haven’t got a single extra penny to show for all that trouble. I suspect you’ve been at it from morning to night trying to keep me from earning that extra penny, and I won’t put up with it, Aggerawayter. What have you got to say for yourself now, eh?”
source_text: “Worth no more than that,” repeated Mr. Cruncher. “They ain’t worth much, then. Whether or no, I won’t be prayed agin, I tell you. I can’t afford it. I’m not a going to be made unlucky by _your_ sneaking. If you must go flopping yourself down, flop in favour of your husband and child, and not in opposition to ’em. If I had had any but a unnat’ral wife, and this poor boy had had any but a unnat’ral mother, I might have made some money last week instead of being counter-prayed and countermined and religiously circumwented into the worst of luck. B-u-u-ust me!” said Mr. Cruncher, who all this time had been putting on his clothes, “if I ain’t, what with piety and one blowed thing and another, been choused this last week into as bad luck as ever a poor devil of a honest tradesman met with! Young Jerry, dress yourself, my boy, and while I clean my boots keep a eye upon your mother now and then, and if you see any signs of more flopping, give me a call. For, I tell you,” here he addressed his wife once more, “I won’t be gone agin, in this manner. I am as rickety as a hackney-coach, I’m as sleepy as laudanum, my lines is strained to that degree that I shouldn’t know, if it wasn’t for the pain in ’em, which was me and which somebody else, yet I’m none the better for it in pocket; and it’s my suspicion that you’ve been at it from morning to night to prevent me from being the better for it in pocket, and I won’t put up with it, Aggerawayter, and what do you say now!”

text: Just above her dainty, faintly upturned nose, whose delicate contour was as fine and precisely etched as any artist’s sketch, the faint furrow between her brows deepened as she settled thoughtfully into the armchair she’d been standing beside up until that moment. He watched quietly as she drifted through her own musings, and the instant her eyes lifted back to his face, he picked up where he had left off:
source_text: Between the eyebrows and just over the little feminine nose, the line of which was as delicate and fine as it was possible to be, the expression deepened itself as she took her seat thoughtfully in the chair by which she had hitherto remained standing. He watched her as she mused, and the moment she raised her eyes again, went on:

text: “Now I’ll tell you straight how it is!” Mr. Cruncher said to his wife the moment he entered. “If, as an honest tradesman, my ventures go awry tonight, I’ll know for sure you’ve been praying against me, and I’ll punish you for it just the same as if I’d seen you do it with my own eyes.”
source_text: “Now, I tell you where it is!” said Mr. Cruncher to his wife, on entering. “If, as a honest tradesman, my wenturs goes wrong to-night, I shall make sure that you’ve been praying again me, and I shall work you for it just the same as if I seen you do it.”

text: “Wait, there’s another tale going round at the village well,” the crofter went on, leaning in a little, “that they’re dragging him down to these parts to execute him on the spot, and there’s no question he’ll be put to death. Folks mutter too that since he killed the Seigneur, and the Seigneur was master of all his tenants—bondsmen, whatever you want to call the lot—he’ll be put to death as a kin-killer, as if he’d struck his own father. One gray-haired old fellow lingering by the well said they’ll burn his right hand, the one that held the knife, right before his eyes; that they’ll cut open gashes in his arms, his chest, his legs, and pour boiling oil, molten lead, hot pine resin, beeswax, and sulfur into the wounds; last of all, four heavy plow horses will tear him apart limb from limb. That old man swears every last bit of that was done to a prisoner who tried to kill the late King, Louis the Fourteenth, years back. But how would I know if he’s spinning lies? I never learned my letters, after all.”
source_text: “Again; on the other hand, they whisper at the fountain,” resumed the countryman, “that he is brought down into our country to be executed on the spot, and that he will very certainly be executed. They even whisper that because he has slain Monseigneur, and because Monseigneur was the father of his tenants--serfs--what you will--he will be executed as a parricide. One old man says at the fountain, that his right hand, armed with the knife, will be burnt off before his face; that, into wounds which will be made in his arms, his breast, and his legs, there will be poured boiling oil, melted lead, hot resin, wax, and sulphur; finally, that he will be torn limb from limb by four strong horses. That old man says, all this was actually done to a prisoner who made an attempt on the life of the late King, Louis Fifteen. But how do I know if he lies? I am not a scholar.”

text: I turned her slowly onto her back, rested my palms light but firm against her chest to soothe her and hold her still, then studied her face. Her eyes were blown wide and wild, and she let out constant, piercing shrieks, crying over and over, “My husband, my father, and my brother!” before counting up to twelve, and murmuring, “Hush.” For no more than a single instant she would fall quiet, straining to listen, then the rending shrieks would start anew, the same cycle spilling from her: the cry for her three loved ones, the count to twelve, the soft, urgent hush. There was no change to the order, no shift in her delivery. The sounds never ceased entirely, broken only by that regular, brief pause between each iteration.
source_text: “I turned her gently over, placed my hands upon her breast to calm her and keep her down, and looked into her face. Her eyes were dilated and wild, and she constantly uttered piercing shrieks, and repeated the words, ‘My husband, my father, and my brother!’ and then counted up to twelve, and said, ‘Hush!’ For an instant, and no more, she would pause to listen, and then the piercing shrieks would begin again, and she would repeat the cry, ‘My husband, my father, and my brother!’ and would count up to twelve, and say, ‘Hush!’ There was no variation in the order, or the manner. There was no cessation, but the regular moment’s pause, in the utterance of these sounds.

text: So there they went, the raucous, unruly procession, chugging beer, puffing on pipes, belting out songs at the top of their lungs and hamming up their grief to absurd, cartoonish extremes. They picked up more joiners with every step they took, every shop along their route shuttering its doors well before they passed. Their destination was the old Saint Pancras Church, tucked far out in the open fields beyond the town. They arrived in due course, demanded to be let into the burial ground, forced their way inside when they met pushback, and finally managed to inter the late Roger Cly entirely on their own terms, leaving them thoroughly pleased with the whole affair.
source_text: Thus, with beer-drinking, pipe-smoking, song-roaring, and infinite caricaturing of woe, the disorderly procession went its way, recruiting at every step, and all the shops shutting up before it. Its destination was the old church of Saint Pancras, far off in the fields. It got there in course of time; insisted on pouring into the burial-ground; finally, accomplished the interment of the deceased Roger Cly in its own way, and highly to its own satisfaction.

text: When they made it to the entryway where he’d lingered in the dark only a few hours prior, imagining which of the street’s rough, uneven paving stones her feet had stepped on as she walked by, he lifted her into his arms again and carried her up the staircase to their rooms. There, he set her down softly on a couch, where her young child and Miss Pross wept as they leaned over her.
source_text: When they arrived at the gateway where he had paused in the dark not many hours before, to picture to himself on which of the rough stones of the street her feet had trodden, he lifted her again, and carried her up the staircase to their rooms. There, he laid her down on a couch, where her child and Miss Pross wept over her.

text: That’s good to hear. I’ve seen people with the same spark and drive you carry pull off incredible things in the past—though none of them,” he added, a smile crossing his face at the same time as a soft, weary sigh, “ever managed something as monumental as this. But give it your best shot. As empty and meaningless as life feels when we squander it, it’s still worth putting in that work. If that weren’t the case, letting go of it entirely would cost us nothing at all.
source_text: “That’s well. I have known such energy as yours do great things before now--though never,” he added, with a smile and a sigh together, “such great things as this. But try! Of little worth as life is when we misuse it, it is worth that effort. It would cost nothing to lay down if it were not.”

text: The deep, resonant bell of Saint Paul’s was striking one o’clock through the clear, unobscured air when Mr. Lorry set off on his return journey to Clerkenwell, escorted by Jerry, who wore tall, thick boots and carried a lit lantern. The route between Soho and Clerkenwell had its share of isolated, empty road patches, and Mr. Lorry, always wary of footpads, regularly retained Jerry for this escort service, even though the trip was typically carried out a full two hours earlier on most occasions.
source_text: The great bell of Saint Paul’s was striking one in the cleared air, when Mr. Lorry, escorted by Jerry, high-booted and bearing a lantern, set forth on his return-passage to Clerkenwell. There were solitary patches of road on the way between Soho and Clerkenwell, and Mr. Lorry, mindful of foot-pads, always retained Jerry for this service: though it was usually performed a good two hours earlier.

text: From that bittersweet evening onward, no matter the forecast, Mara waited at the edge of the old coach stop for two full hours. When the village clock struck two, she was already perched on its weathered wooden step, and when it tolled four, she turned away quiet and resigned, steps slow as she headed back toward her cottage. On days when the rain held off and the air wasn’t sharp enough to sting her five-year-old’s raw winter cough, little Kael came with her, tracing cracks in the cobblestones while she stared down the empty road; on harsher, inclement days, she made the trip alone, wool scarf wrapped tight to her chin. She never missed a single one of those daily waits.
source_text: From that time, in all weathers, she waited there two hours. As the clock struck two, she was there, and at four she turned resignedly away. When it was not too wet or inclement for her child to be with her, they went together; at other times she was alone; but, she never missed a single day.

text: Responses to that question were wildly varied and deeply contradictory. Sometimes the halting, cracked reply came: “Wait! It would kill me if I saw her too soon.” Other times, it poured out through a soft, tender rain of tears: “Take me to her.” Now and then, the person would stare blankly, disoriented and confused, and say, “I don’t know her. I don’t understand.”
source_text: The answers to this question were various and contradictory. Sometimes the broken reply was, “Wait! It would kill me if I saw her too soon.” Sometimes, it was given in a tender rain of tears, and then it was, “Take me to her.” Sometimes it was staring and bewildered, and then it was, “I don’t know her. I don’t understand.”

text: “I take them into my keeping!” Carton announced. “I ask no questions and set no terms. An immense crowd is bearing down on us, Miss Manette, and I see them—by the lightning.” He added those final words right after a bright, vivid flash had revealed him lounging casually in the window.
source_text: “I take them into mine!” said Carton. “_I_ ask no questions and make no stipulations. There is a great crowd bearing down upon us, Miss Manette, and I see them--by the Lightning.” He added the last words, after there had been a vivid flash which had shown him lounging in the window.

text: When I am in that calmer, gentler frame of mind, I have pictured her coming to me through the moonlight, leading me out to show me that the home she built in her marriage was stitched full of warm, loving memories of her late father. My photograph sat in her bedroom, and I was always held in her prayers. Her days were active, cheerful, and devoted to doing good for those around her, but every part of her life was threaded through with my own sad, troubled history.
source_text: “In that more peaceful state, I have imagined her, in the moonlight, coming to me and taking me out to show me that the home of her married life was full of her loving remembrance of her lost father. My picture was in her room, and I was in her prayers. Her life was active, cheerful, useful; but my poor history pervaded it all.”

text: Six tumbrils rumble along the city streets. Reverse these to their prior shapes, O mighty enchanter Time, and you would recognize them as the gilded carriages of absolute monarchs, the lavish equipages of feudal nobles, the gaudy travelling toilettes of flaunting Jezebels, the churches that pervert my Father’s house to become dens of thieves, and the squalid huts of millions of starving peasants! No; the great magician, who carries out the Creator’s preordained order with solemn majesty, never undoes his transformations. “If you have been changed to this form by God’s own will,” the seers tell the enchanted in the wise old Arabian tales, “then remain as you are! But if you wear this shape only through a fleeting, trivial conjuration, then resume your former aspect!” Unchanging, unmoored from all hope, the tumbrils roll onward.
source_text: Six tumbrils roll along the streets. Change these back again to what they were, thou powerful enchanter, Time, and they shall be seen to be the carriages of absolute monarchs, the equipages of feudal nobles, the toilettes of flaring Jezebels, the churches that are not my father’s house but dens of thieves, the huts of millions of starving peasants! No; the great magician who majestically works out the appointed order of the Creator, never reverses his transformations. “If thou be changed into this shape by the will of God,” say the seers to the enchanted, in the wise Arabian stories, “then remain so! But, if thou wear this form through mere passing conjuration, then resume thy former aspect!” Changeless and hopeless, the tumbrils roll along.

text: Bearing a solemn, quiet fascination for the warm lit windows behind which city dwellers readied for rest, set to spend a few calm hours forgetting the horrors pressing in on every side; for the church towers where no prayers were uttered, for public revulsion had curdled so deep after decades of lying, predatory, dissolute priests that the population had rejected all worship entirely in that act of collective self-sabotage; for the distant burial grounds whose gates bore inscriptions marking them as reserved for Eternal Sleep; for the overflowing, crammed gaols; for the streets where batches of sixty condemned people rolled toward execution, death so common and unremarkable that no sorrowful tales of haunting spirits ever arose among the populace from all the guillotine’s bloody work; wrapped in this grave preoccupation with the whole city’s cycle of life and death as it settled into its short nightly pause from raging fury, Sydney Carton crossed the Seine once more, bound for the lighter, less grim streets.
source_text: With a solemn interest in the lighted windows where the people were going to rest, forgetful through a few calm hours of the horrors surrounding them; in the towers of the churches, where no prayers were said, for the popular revulsion had even travelled that length of self-destruction from years of priestly impostors, plunderers, and profligates; in the distant burial-places, reserved, as they wrote upon the gates, for Eternal Sleep; in the abounding gaols; and in the streets along which the sixties rolled to a death which had become so common and material, that no sorrowful story of a haunting Spirit ever arose among the people out of all the working of the Guillotine; with a solemn interest in the whole life and death of the city settling down to its short nightly pause in fury; Sydney Carton crossed the Seine again for the lighter streets.

text: All these small, unremarkable occurrences were just the familiar rhythm of daily life, the soft unfurling of a new dawn. Surely that steady pattern did not account for the deep, reverberating peal of the countryside manor’s great cast bell, nor the clatter of footsteps racing up and down the timber staircases; nor the wild, haggard figures darting across the flagstone terrace; nor the thud of boots and loud tramping echoing down every hall and passage, nor the fast strapping of saddle girths and riders galloping off down the lane?
source_text: All these trivial incidents belonged to the routine of life, and the return of morning. Surely, not so the ringing of the great bell of the chateau, nor the running up and down the stairs; nor the hurried figures on the terrace; nor the booting and tramping here and there and everywhere, nor the quick saddling of horses and riding away?

text: Even as the Doctor poured every ounce of his effort into securing Charles Darnay’s release, or at the very least getting him a formal court hearing, and never once let up in that pursuit, the roaring tide of public sentiment in that era moved far too quickly and powerfully for him to push against. A new age had dawned: the king was put on trial, condemned to death, and publicly beheaded. The Republic, whose rallying cry was Liberty, Equality, Fraternity, or Death, had declared that it would accept nothing short of total victory or utter destruction in its fight against the armed monarchies of the rest of the world. A pitch-black flag flew around the clock from the towering spires of Notre Dame Cathedral. Three hundred thousand men, called to rise up against the tyrants ruling every corner of the globe, had sprung up across every inch of French soil, as if the mythic dragon’s teeth had been scattered far and wide, bearing fruit just as readily on craggy hilltops and flat lowlands, in bare rock, gravel beds, and soft alluvial silt; under the blazing southern sun and the mist-shrouded northern clouds; across windswept moors and dense old forests, through sun-warmed vineyards, gnarled olive groves, pastures of clipped grass and golden corn stubble, along the lush banks of wide, winding rivers, and in the fine sand of the coastline. What small, personal worry could possibly hold its ground against the overwhelming flood of the first year of Liberty – a flood that swelled upward from the ground below rather than pouring down from the sky, with the windows of Heaven sealed shut rather than thrown open to unleash the waters?
source_text: But, though the Doctor tried hard, and never ceased trying, to get Charles Darnay set at liberty, or at least to get him brought to trial, the public current of the time set too strong and fast for him. The new era began; the king was tried, doomed, and beheaded; the Republic of Liberty, Equality, Fraternity, or Death, declared for victory or death against the world in arms; the black flag waved night and day from the great towers of Notre Dame; three hundred thousand men, summoned to rise against the tyrants of the earth, rose from all the varying soils of France, as if the dragon’s teeth had been sown broadcast, and had yielded fruit equally on hill and plain, on rock, in gravel, and alluvial mud, under the bright sky of the South and under the clouds of the North, in fell and forest, in the vineyards and the olive-grounds and among the cropped grass and the stubble of the corn, along the fruitful banks of the broad rivers, and in the sand of the sea-shore. What private solicitude could rear itself against the deluge of the Year One of Liberty--the deluge rising from below, not falling from above, and with the windows of Heaven shut, not opened!

text: It would be utterly unbefitting a humble writer to leave so exalted a figure as a parish beadle waiting, his back to the hearth, the tails of his coat bunched under his arms, until it suited the author’s fancy to turn his attention to him. It would be even more of a slight to his station, and a failure of gallantry, to extend that same neglect to the lady upon whom that beadle has cast a fond, tender gaze, and into whose ear he has breathed soft sentiments that, coming from such a source, would send a thrill through the breast of any maid or matron, no matter her rank. So the chronicler setting down these words—confident he understands his proper station, and that he holds a fitting reverence for those on Earth entrusted with lofty, vital authority—hastes to render them the respect their positions require, and to treat them with all the obedient deference their exalted status, and (as a natural consequence) profound virtue, rightfully demand of him. To that end, he had in fact planned to include here a treatise on the divine right of beadles, laying out the principle that a beadle can never act amiss: a work that would no doubt have been both edifying and enjoyable for all right-thinking readers, but which he is unfortunately forced to delay to a more convenient, appropriate occasion for lack of time and page space. When that occasion arrives, he will be ready to demonstrate that a properly appointed beadle—that is to say, a parish beadle, assigned to a parish workhouse, and serving in his official role at the parish church—possesses, by the very virtue of his office, every human excellence and finest quality; and that ordinary company beadles, court-of-law beadles, or even chapel-of-ease beadles (save for the last, and they only in the most meager, subordinate degree) can lay not the faintest valid claim to any of these virtues.
source_text: As it would be, by no means, seemly in a humble author to keep so mighty a personage as a beadle waiting, with his back to the fire, and the skirts of his coat gathered up under his arms, until such time as it might suit his pleasure to relieve him; and as it would still less become his station, or his gallantry to involve in the same neglect a lady on whom that beadle had looked with an eye of tenderness and affection, and in whose ear he had whispered sweet words, which, coming from such a quarter, might well thrill the bosom of maid or matron of whatsoever degree; the historian whose pen traces these words—trusting that he knows his place, and that he entertains a becoming reverence for those upon earth to whom high and important authority is delegated—hastens to pay them that respect which their position demands, and to treat them with all that duteous ceremony which their exalted rank, and (by consequence) great virtues, imperatively claim at his hands. Towards this end, indeed, he had purposed to introduce, in this place, a dissertation touching the divine right of beadles, and elucidative of the position, that a beadle can do no wrong: which could not fail to have been both pleasurable and profitable to the right-minded reader but which he is unfortunately compelled, by want of time and space, to postpone to some more convenient and fitting opportunity; on the arrival of which, he will be prepared to show, that a beadle properly constituted: that is to say, a parochial beadle, attached to a parochial workhouse, and attending in his official capacity the parochial church: is, in right and virtue of his office, possessed of all the excellences and best qualities of humanity; and that to none of those excellences, can mere companies’ beadles, or court-of-law beadles, or even chapel-of-ease beadles (save the last, and they in a very lowly and inferior degree), lay the remotest sustainable claim.

text: “We only got word of that family the night before last,” the beadle said. “We wouldn’t have known a thing about them at all, mind you, if a woman lodging in their same house hadn’t put in a request to the parish committee to send the parish surgeon to look after a woman who was terribly unwell. The surgeon was out at dinner, but his apprentice—very sharp young lad, that one—slung some medicine into a blacking bottle and sent it over to them straight off, no messing about.”
source_text: “We only heard of the family the night before last,” said the beadle; “and we shouldn’t have known anything about them, then, only a woman who lodges in the same house made an application to the porochial committee for them to send the porochial surgeon to see a woman as was very bad. He had gone out to dinner; but his ’prentice (which is a very clever lad) sent ’em some medicine in a blacking-bottle, offhand.”

text: “A being,” the young man went on, his voice thick with raw, unbridled feeling, “a being as gentle and free of deceit as any of heaven’s own seraphs, hung wavering in the thin, sharp space between life and death. Oh, who could have dared to believe, when the far realm she was born to belong to drifted half into her line of sight, that she would ever choose to turn back to the grief and hardship of this one? Lila, Lila, to know you were slipping away like some faint mist that a sunbeam from the heavens gilds as it brushes the grass; to hold no scrap of hope that you might be spared to the ones who lingered here mourning you; to scarce find a single reason you ought to stay; to feel you already belonged to that glowing sphere where so many of the kindest and most beloved have taken their early flight; and yet to plead, even amid all these quiet comforts, that you might be given back to the souls who loved you—these were torments almost too sharp to endure. They were mine, every hour of every day and every long, restless night; and with them came a roaring flood of dread, and foreboding, and selfish grief, that you might die and never learn how fiercely, how entirely I adored you, a current so heavy it all but swept my sense and sanity away in its wake. You recovered. Day by day, almost minute by minute, a little spark of strength seeped back into you, mixing with the thin, sluggish trickle of life that moved slow through your veins, until it swelled again into a full, quick, racing current. I watched you shift almost from the edge of the grave back to the land of the living, with eyes that grew blurred and burning from how fiercely I clung to every small sign of improvement, how deep my affection ran. Do not tell me that you wish I had missed this; for it has softened every edge of my heart, made me gentler to every living soul I meet.”
source_text: “A creature,” continued the young man, passionately, “a creature as fair and innocent of guile as one of God’s own angels, fluttered between life and death. Oh! who could hope, when the distant world to which she was akin, half opened to her view, that she would return to the sorrow and calamity of this! Rose, Rose, to know that you were passing away like some soft shadow, which a light from above, casts upon the earth; to have no hope that you would be spared to those who linger here; hardly to know a reason why you should be; to feel that you belonged to that bright sphere whither so many of the fairest and the best have winged their early flight; and yet to pray, amid all these consolations, that you might be restored to those who loved you—these were distractions almost too great to bear. They were mine, by day and night; and with them, came such a rushing torrent of fears, and apprehensions, and selfish regrets, lest you should die, and never know how devotedly I loved you, as almost bore down sense and reason in its course. You recovered. Day by day, and almost hour by hour, some drop of health came back, and mingling with the spent and feeble stream of life which circulated languidly within you, swelled it again to a high and rushing tide. I have watched you change almost from death, to life, with eyes that turned blind with their eagerness and deep affection. Do not tell me that you wish I had lost this; for it has softened my heart to all mankind.”

text: “Pshaw! pshaw! absolute rubbish!” said the stout functionary in the starched off-white waistcoat. “He’d be a bargain even if you paid not a farthing in premium. Take him, you daft fellow! He’s just the lad for you. He needs a good strike of the cane every so often: it’ll do him nothing but good; and his board won’t run you much at all, for he’s never been overfed a day since he was born. Ho! ho! ho!”
source_text: “Pooh! pooh! nonsense!” said the gentleman in the white waistcoat. “He’d be cheap with nothing at all, as a premium. Take him, you silly fellow! He’s just the boy for you. He wants the stick, now and then: it’ll do him good; and his board needn’t come very expensive, for he hasn’t been overfed since he was born. Ha! ha! ha!”

text: Oliver had endured the exact mistreatment that the blunt, expressive monosyllable just uttered referred to far too many times to entertain even the faintest doubt that the owner of that voice, whoever he might be, would make good on his pledge as scrupulously as any gentleman honoring a formal vow. He drew back the bolts with a trembling hand, and pulled the door open.
source_text: Oliver had been too often subjected to the process to which the very expressive monosyllable just recorded bears reference, to entertain the smallest doubt that the owner of the voice, whoever he might be, would redeem his pledge, most honourably. He drew back the bolts with a trembling hand, and opened the door.

text: The command was followed by a sharp kick that sent the animal sprawling to the far end of the room. He was clearly well accustomed to such treatment, however; he coiled himself quietly into a corner without uttering a single noise, his deeply unappealing eyes winking twenty times each minute as he appeared to busy himself with scanning the entire apartment.
source_text: This command was accompanied with a kick, which sent the animal to the other end of the room. He appeared well used to it, however; for he coiled himself up in a corner very quietly, without uttering a sound, and winking his very ill-looking eyes twenty times in a minute, appeared to occupy himself in taking a survey of the apartment.

text: The plaza fronting the county gaol had been fully cleared, and a handful of heavy, black-painted barricades had already been set across the adjacent thoroughfare to blunt the crush of the expected crowd, when Mr. Halewood and Toby appeared at the wicket gate, holding out an order granting them access to the inmate, signed by one of the appointed sheriffs. They were ushered straight into the guard lodge without delay.
source_text: The space before the prison was cleared, and a few strong barriers, painted black, had been already thrown across the road to break the pressure of the expected crowd, when Mr. Brownlow and Oliver appeared at the wicket, and presented an order of admission to the prisoner, signed by one of the sheriffs. They were immediately admitted into the lodge.

text: Elias’s bellows drew an immediate response: a shrill shriek from Lila, and an even louder, ragged wail from Mrs. Hale. The former came bursting into the kitchen through a side entrance, while the latter hovered on the staircase landing, refusing to descend another step until she was fully certain moving closer would not pose any threat to her own life and limb.
source_text: Noah’s shouts were responded to, by a loud scream from Charlotte, and a louder from Mrs. Sowerberry; the former of whom rushed into the kitchen by a side-door, while the latter paused on the staircase till she was quite certain that it was consistent with the preservation of human life, to come further down.

text: “There’s something I wish I could do,” the small child said, “send all my fondest love to poor Oliver Twist, and let him know how often I’ve sat by myself crying, thinking of him wandering out in the dark nights with no one there to look after him. And I want to tell him too,” the child went on, clasping his tiny hands tight together, his voice bright with deep, earnest fervor, “that I’m glad I died when I was so young. Because if I’d lived to be a man, to grow old, my little sister who waits up in Heaven might forget me, or we might no longer be alike. It will be so much happier for us both if we get to stay little children together there, always.”
source_text: “I should like,” said the child, “to leave my dear love to poor Oliver Twist; and to let him know how often I have sat by myself and cried to think of his wandering about in the dark nights with nobody to help him. And I should like to tell him,” said the child pressing his small hands together, and speaking with great fervour, “that I was glad to die when I was very young; for, perhaps, if I had lived to be a man, and had grown old, my little sister who is in Heaven, might forget me, or be unlike me; and it would be so much happier if we were both children there together.”

text: Fagin rested his hand on the wrapped bundle, stowed it in the cupboard and turned the key in the lock, then settled back into his seat without uttering a sound. Not once during this whole sequence did his gaze stray from the robber’s face, and now that they sat directly across from each other, face to face, he pinned the other man with a fixed stare. His lips quivered so violently, and his features were so warped by the overwhelming emotions that had seized him, that the housebreaker pulled his chair back without thinking, eyeing him with a look of unfeigned, sharp fright.
source_text: Fagin laid his hand upon the bundle, and locking it in the cupboard, sat down again without speaking. But he did not take his eyes off the robber, for an instant, during this action; and now that they sat over against each other, face to face, he looked fixedly at him, with his lips quivering so violently, and his face so altered by the emotions which had mastered him, that the housebreaker involuntarily drew back his chair, and surveyed him with a look of real affright.

text: “You think you will?” the unsightly, hunched cripple jeered. “If you ever have need of me, I’m right here. I haven’t spent twenty-five years holed up in this place, half mad and entirely alone, just to be frightened off by the likes of you. You’ll pay for this. You will pay for this.” No sooner had the words left his mouth than the gnarled little fiend threw back his head and howled, bouncing on his heels where he stood as if seized by unbridled rage.
source_text: “Will you?” sneered the ill-favoured cripple. “If you ever want me, I’m here. I haven’t lived here mad and all alone, for five-and-twenty years, to be scared by you. You shall pay for this; you shall pay for this.” And so saying, the mis-shapen little demon set up a yell, and danced upon the ground, as if wild with rage.

text: Noah Claypole lay stretched out on a mattress laid flat on the floor, fast asleep. Now and then the old man flicked a quick glance his way before turning his eyes back to the candle on the table before him. Its wick, burned down so far it drooped nearly double, sent thick clots of hot grease plopping onto the wooden surface, making it plain his thoughts were occupied far from the present moment.
source_text: Stretched upon a mattress on the floor, lay Noah Claypole, fast asleep. Towards him the old man sometimes directed his eyes for an instant, and then brought them back again to the candle; which with a long-burnt wick drooping almost double, and hot grease falling down in clots upon the table, plainly showed that his thoughts were busy elsewhere.

text: “She was my daughter,” the old woman said, tipping her head toward the corpse. She spoke through a foolish, glassy leer that was far more horrifying than the stark presence of death even in that grim, unforgiving space. “Lord, Lord! Man, is that not the wildest thing? I’m the one that carried her and pushed her into the world, back when I was still a young woman myself, and now here I am, alive and laughing and getting on just fine, while she’s laid out there, ice-cold and stiff as a board. Lord, Lord—just think of that! It’s as good as any stage play, ain’t it? Just as good as a play!”
source_text: “She was my daughter,” said the old woman, nodding her head in the direction of the corpse; and speaking with an idiotic leer, more ghastly than even the presence of death in such a place. “Lord, Lord! Well, it _is_ strange that I who gave birth to her, and was a woman then, should be alive and merry now, and she lying there: so cold and stiff! Lord, Lord!—to think of it; it’s as good as a play—as good as a play!”

text: Every rear window of the house had been bricked up years before, save for one tiny vent in the locked room where the boy was held, an opening so small he could not even fit his body through it. Still, he had never ceased calling through that gap to the people outside, urging them to guard the back of the property. As a result, when the murderer finally stepped out onto the rooftop through the access door set into the roof, a loud shout announced his appearance to the crowd gathered out front, who immediately began surging around the sides of the house, pressing against one another in an unbroken, rushing stream.
source_text: All the windows in the rear of the house had been long ago bricked up, except one small trap in the room where the boy was locked, and that was too small even for the passage of his body. But, from this aperture, he had never ceased to call on those without, to guard the back; and thus, when the murderer emerged at last on the house-top by the door in the roof, a loud shout proclaimed the fact to those in front, who immediately began to pour round, pressing upon each other in an unbroken stream.

text: The man who hadn’t shifted an inch since he’d taken up his post at the graveside jolted, lifted his head, stared hard at the person who’d addressed him, walked a few paces forward, and collapsed in a dead faint. The addled old woman was far too wrapped up in bemoaning the loss of her cloak—which the undertaker had taken off her—to pay him any attention, so the others doused him with a can of cold water. Once he came to, they saw him safely out of the churchyard, turned the key in the gate lock, and headed off their separate ways.
source_text: The man who had never once moved, since he had taken his station by the grave side, started, raised his head, stared at the person who had addressed him, walked forward for a few paces; and fell down in a swoon. The crazy old woman was too much occupied in bewailing the loss of her cloak (which the undertaker had taken off), to pay him any attention; so they threw a can of cold water over him; and when he came to, saw him safely out of the churchyard, locked the gate, and departed on their different ways.

text: What a stark, unmistakeable lesson in the tyranny of clothing young Toby Hale offered! Swaddled in the coarse wool blanket that had been his sole garment since birth, he could as easily have been the scion of a titled lord as the whelp of a starving street vendor; even the proudest, most discerning visitor would have struggled to place him on society’s rigid ladder. But the moment he was slipped into the faded, yellowed calico smock worn thin by decades of service to other parentless parish children, he was branded and catalogued, slotted immediately into his preordained niche: a parish ward, a workhouse foundling, a ragged, underfed drudge fated to be shoved and struck at every turn, scorned by all who crossed his path and granted not a single spark of pity.
source_text: What an excellent example of the power of dress, young Oliver Twist was! Wrapped in the blanket which had hitherto formed his only covering, he might have been the child of a nobleman or a beggar; it would have been hard for the haughtiest stranger to have assigned him his proper station in society. But now that he was enveloped in the old calico robes which had grown yellow in the same service, he was badged and ticketed, and fell into his place at once—a parish child—the orphan of a workhouse—the humble, half-starved drudge—to be cuffed and buffeted through the world—despised by all, and pitied by none.

text: Dusk was just starting to fall when Mr. Brownlow climbed out of a hired carriage at his own front door and rapped softly on the wood. The moment the door swung open, a sturdy, broad-shouldered man stepped down from the carriage and planted himself on one side of the entry steps, while the driver, who’d been perched on the box seat up front, climbed down too and took his place on the opposite side. At a quiet signal from Mr. Brownlow, the two men pulled a third man out of the carriage, held him tight between them, and hurried him straight into the house. That man was Monks.
source_text: The twilight was beginning to close in, when Mr. Brownlow alighted from a hackney-coach at his own door, and knocked softly. The door being opened, a sturdy man got out of the coach and stationed himself on one side of the steps, while another man, who had been seated on the box, dismounted too, and stood upon the other side. At a sign from Mr. Brownlow, they helped out a third man, and taking him between them, hurried him into the house. This man was Monks.

text: The bay mare, whose soundness had been toasted round the taproom while her owner was gone, stood hitched just outside the tavern, already strapped into the light dray’s traces. Toby and Rook clambered into the dray’s bed without so much as a nod of acknowledgement to anyone loitering nearby. The man who owned her hung back a minute or two, swearing he’d “back her against any beast alive” and daring the stable hand and every onlooker to produce a sturdier, faster mount, before hauling himself up onto the driver’s bench. The stable hand was ordered to let her have her head, and the moment he slackened his hold, she put it to remarkably uncooperative use: flinging it skyward with unmistakeable contempt, then lurching straight for the front parlor windows of the boarding house across the street. After pulling that stunt, balancing on her hind hooves for a few lurching seconds, she bolted at a full gallop, wheels clattering over the cobblestones as she tore out of the town limits in fine, feisty form.
source_text: The horse, whose health had been drunk in his absence, was standing outside: ready harnessed to the cart. Oliver and Sikes got in without any further ceremony; and the man to whom he belonged, having lingered for a minute or two “to bear him up,” and to defy the hostler and the world to produce his equal, mounted also. Then, the hostler was told to give the horse his head; and, his head being given him, he made a very unpleasant use of it: tossing it into the air with great disdain, and running into the parlour windows over the way; after performing those feats, and supporting himself for a short time on his hind-legs, he started off at great speed, and rattled out of the town right gallantly.

text: A pair of scuffed, unpolished feet shuffled hurriedly across the bare, splintered floor of the room the moment the question was posed; and from a door set in the right-hand wall emerged first a sputtering, feeble tallow candle, then the stooped form of the same man previously introduced as afflicted with the infirmity of speaking entirely through his nose, and employed as a waiter at the public taphouse on Bramble Row.
source_text: A pair of slipshod feet shuffled, hastily, across the bare floor of the room, as this interrogatory was put; and there issued, from a door on the right hand; first, a feeble candle: and next, the form of the same individual who has been heretofore described as labouring under the infirmity of speaking through his nose, and officiating as waiter at the public-house on Saffron Hill.

text: “Easy now, easy,” the gaunt old fence crooned, coaxing. “That was only my usual caution, nothing more. Now, my dear, about that country house out at Brampton; when is the job to go off, Jake, eh? When is it to be done? Such fine silver, my love, such splendid silver!” The old man rubbed his palms together, lifting his eyebrows high as he fairly glowed with ravenous anticipation.
source_text: “There, there,” said the Jew, coaxingly. “It was only my caution, nothing more. Now, my dear, about that crib at Chertsey; when is it to be done, Bill, eh? When is it to be done? Such plate, my dear, such plate!” said the Jew: rubbing his hands, and elevating his eyebrows in a rapture of anticipation.

text: “There’s no way you’re doing any such thing,” Mr. Sikes cut in. “The Artful’s far too shifty for that. If you ask him to handle it, he’ll either forget to turn up, claim he got lost, say cops were tailing him so he couldn’t follow through, or make up any other flimsy excuse to get out of it. Nancy’s the one who’ll go to the safe house and grab it, that way we know it’ll actually get done. I’ll stretch out here and catch a quick nap while she’s gone.”
source_text: “You won’t do nothing of the kind,” rejoined Mr. Sikes. “The Artful’s a deal too artful, and would forget to come, or lose his way, or get dodged by traps and so be perwented, or anything for an excuse, if you put him up to it. Nancy shall go to the ken and fetch it, to make all sure; and I’ll lie down and have a snooze while she’s gone.”

text: Rook gave a sharp, confirming nod, then cupped his palm around the candle’s guttering flame to keep its light from spilling far, and flicked a quick, wordless warning to Jip Coombs that it was no time for his usual silly quips. Once that small, thoughtful favor was done, he settled his gaze firmly on the old fence’s weathered face, holding perfectly still as he awaited his directions.
source_text: The Dodger nodded in the affirmative, and, shading the flame of the candle with his hand, gave Charley Bates a private intimation, in dumb show, that he had better not be funny just then. Having performed this friendly office, he fixed his eyes on the Jew’s face, and awaited his directions.

text: “Nurse, you needn’t hold back from sending for me if the child starts crying,” said the surgeon, drawing on his gloves with slow, deliberate care. “It’s quite likely it will be fussy, after all. If it grows restless, give it a little gruel to soothe it.” He settled his hat on his head, and pausing beside the bed as he made for the door, added, “She was a lovely young woman, too—where did she come from?”
source_text: “You needn’t mind sending up to me, if the child cries, nurse,” said the surgeon, putting on his gloves with great deliberation. “It’s very likely it _will_ be troublesome. Give it a little gruel if it is.” He put on his hat, and, pausing by the bed-side on his way to the door, added, “She was a good-looking girl, too; where did she come from?”

text: “You hear me, Workhouse brat?” Noah went on, emboldened by Oliver’s silence, his voice oozing that sneering, performative pity that’s more infuriating than any outright insult: “You know there’s nothing to be done about it now, right? Obviously you couldn’t have changed any of it back then, I’m really sorry for you, I swear all of us are, we pity you so bad. But you’ve got to know, Workhouse brat, your mom was a total, unvarnished, no-good lowlife.”
source_text: “Yer know, Work’us,” continued Noah, emboldened by Oliver’s silence, and speaking in a jeering tone of affected pity: of all tones the most annoying: “Yer know, Work’us, it can’t be helped now; and of course yer couldn’t help it then; and I am very sorry for it; and I’m sure we all are, and pity yer very much. But yer must know, Work’us, yer mother was a regular right-down bad ’un.”

text:
Come morning, Oliver was up and out walking by six o’clock, wandering the fields and snagging bright blooms and sprigs from hedgerows far and wide to make wildflower bouquets, hauling whole armfuls back home with him. He’d spend ages fussing over how to arrange them just right, so they’d look their prettiest sprucing up the breakfast table.
He also brought back fresh groundsel for Miss Maylie’s pet birds, and after studying bird care closely under the village clerk’s expert guidance, he’d trim and arrange the foliage around their cages to fit the most widely agreed-upon standards of good taste. Once the birds’ enclosures were neat and cheerful for the day, he’d usually run a small charitable errand somewhere in the village first. If there were no errands to tend to, he’d sometimes join a casual cricket match on the village green. And if there was no cricket to be had, there was always work waiting in the garden, tending to the potted plants and flower beds—Oliver had picked up gardening skills from that same village clerk, who worked as a professional gardener by trade, and he threw himself into those tasks with real enthusiasm, right up until Miss Rose stepped outside. The second she appeared, she’d heap a hundred little compliments on every single thing he’d gotten done that morning.
source_text: In the morning, Oliver would be a-foot by six o’clock, roaming the fields, and plundering the hedges, far and wide, for nosegays of wild flowers, with which he would return laden, home; and which it took great care and consideration to arrange, to the best advantage, for the embellishment of the breakfast-table. There was fresh groundsel, too, for Miss Maylie’s birds, with which Oliver, who had been studying the subject under the able tuition of the village clerk, would decorate the cages, in the most approved taste. When the birds were made all spruce and smart for the day, there was usually some little commission of charity to execute in the village; or, failing that, there was rare cricket-playing, sometimes, on the green; or, failing that, there was always something to do in the garden, or about the plants, to which Oliver (who had studied this science also, under the same master, who was a gardener by trade,) applied himself with hearty good-will, until Miss Rose made her appearance: when there were a thousand commendations to be bestowed on all he had done.

text: “I got you out of being mistreated once, I’ll do it again, and I’m doing it right now,” the girl said loudly. “If I hadn’t come for you, the people who were supposed to fetch you would’ve been way rougher than I am. I already gave my word you’d stay calm and keep quiet. If you don’t, you’ll only hurt both yourself and me, and you might even be the reason I end up dead. Look here! I’ve already put up with all of this for you, and God as my witness, every bit of what I’m saying is true.”
source_text: “I have saved you from being ill-used once, and I will again, and I do now,” continued the girl aloud; “for those who would have fetched you, if I had not, would have been far more rough than me. I have promised for your being quiet and silent; if you are not, you will only do harm to yourself and me too, and perhaps be my death. See here! I have borne all this for you already, as true as God sees me show it.”

text: When he roused himself the following morning, he was cold and stiff all over, and so ravenous that he had to trade his only penny for a small loaf the moment he passed through the first village along his route. He had barely walked twelve miles when night closed in again. His feet were sore and raw, his legs so weak with fatigue that they trembled beneath him. Spending another night exposed to the bleak, damp air left him even more feeble; when he set out on his journey the next morning, he could scarcely drag himself forward.
source_text: He felt cold and stiff, when he got up next morning, and so hungry that he was obliged to exchange the penny for a small loaf, in the very first village through which he passed. He had walked no more than twelve miles, when night closed in again. His feet were sore, and his legs so weak that they trembled beneath him. Another night passed in the bleak damp air, made him worse; when he set forward on his journey next morning he could hardly crawl along.

text: I wish some overstuffed armchair moralist, whose roasted dinners and spiced ales curdle to bitter bile in his gut, whose veins run frost instead of blood, whose chest holds a lump of cold wrought iron where his heart ought to be, could have laid eyes on Toby Creech clutching at the dainty, half-eaten scraps the household hound had turned his snout up at. I wish he could have witnessed the gnarled, ravenous urgency with which Toby tore those fragments apart, every motion sharp with the raw, gnawing ferocity of prolonged famine. There is only one sight I should like better still; and that would be to see that very same moralist making the exact same sort of meal himself, with that exact same desperate, unhinged relish.
source_text: I wish some well-fed philosopher, whose meat and drink turn to gall within him; whose blood is ice, whose heart is iron; could have seen Oliver Twist clutching at the dainty viands that the dog had neglected. I wish he could have witnessed the horrible avidity with which Oliver tore the bits asunder with all the ferocity of famine. There is only one thing I should like better; and that would be to see the Philosopher making the same sort of meal himself, with the same relish.

text: Those were uncommonly happy days. The hours unfolded soft and unhurried, no flicker of fear or gnawing worry darkened the nights; there was no wasting away in a cold, miserable jail cell, no forced companionship with bitter, desperate men, only gentle, hopeful thoughts filling every waking moment. Each morning, Elias called on the silver-haired old scholar who lived a short walk from the village chapel; the man taught him to read with greater fluency and to form his letters neatly, his tone always warm, his patience endless, so that Elias pushed himself to the very limit of his abilities to make the old man proud. Afterwards, he would wander the lanes with Mrs. Hale and her niece Lila, listening as they discussed the novels and poetry they’d recently finished; sometimes he would perch on a mossy stone beside them in a dappled, shaded copse, drinking in every line Lila read aloud, content to sit there until the sun dipped low enough that the print blurred on the page. When those walks ended, he had his next day’s lessons to prepare, and he would bend over his books diligently in a small back room that looked out over the cottage’s rose garden, until the first pale streaks of twilight streaked the sky, when the two women would head out again for an evening stroll, Elias trailing happily at their heels. He hung on every word they exchanged, and was so eager to help when they wanted a wild bloom growing high on a hedgerow, or a small item left back on the garden bench, that he always thought he moved too slowly to fulfill their requests fast enough. When full dark fell and they returned to the cottage, Lila would seat herself at the parlor piano, playing a lilting, cheerful melody, or singing in a soft, honeyed voice the old ballads her aunt loved best. They never lit the tapers during those quiet hours, and Elias would curl up on the windowsill, drinking in the sweet, drifting music, completely lost in perfect rapture.
source_text: It was a happy time. The days were peaceful and serene; the nights brought with them neither fear nor care; no languishing in a wretched prison, or associating with wretched men; nothing but pleasant and happy thoughts. Every morning he went to a white-headed old gentleman, who lived near the little church: who taught him to read better, and to write: and who spoke so kindly, and took such pains, that Oliver could never try enough to please him. Then, he would walk with Mrs. Maylie and Rose, and hear them talk of books; or perhaps sit near them, in some shady place, and listen whilst the young lady read: which he could have done, until it grew too dark to see the letters. Then, he had his own lesson for the next day to prepare; and at this, he would work hard, in a little room which looked into the garden, till evening came slowly on, when the ladies would walk out again, and he with them: listening with such pleasure to all they said: and so happy if they wanted a flower that he could climb to reach, or had forgotten anything he could run to fetch: that he could never be quick enough about it. When it became quite dark, and they returned home, the young lady would sit down to the piano, and play some pleasant air, or sing, in a low and gentle voice, some old song which it pleased her aunt to hear. There would be no candles lighted at such times as these; and Oliver would sit by one of the windows, listening to the sweet music, in a perfect rapture.

text: Mr. Bumble did not pause to chat with the small shopkeepers and other townsfolk who addressed him with clear deference as he walked past. He merely returned their greetings with a flick of his hand, never slackening his dignified, lofty pace, until he reached the farm where Mrs. Mann cared for the parish’s infant paupers under official parochial oversight.
source_text: Mr. Bumble stopped not to converse with the small shopkeepers and others who spoke to him, deferentially, as he passed along. He merely returned their salutations with a wave of his hand, and relaxed not in his dignified pace, until he reached the farm where Mrs. Mann tended the infant paupers with parochial care.

text: Right at the moment he’d slipped the loop over his head, just before drawing it down to fit beneath his armpits, and the aforementioned elderly gentleman—who’d clung so firmly to the bridge railing he’d fended off the press of the crowd and kept his place—was urgently warning everyone nearby that the man was about to lower himself over the side, that very same instant the murderer glanced behind him from the roof, flung both arms above his head, and let out a raw yell of terror.
source_text: At the very instant when he brought the loop over his head previous to slipping it beneath his arm-pits, and when the old gentleman before-mentioned (who had clung so tight to the railing of the bridge as to resist the force of the crowd, and retain his position) earnestly warned those about him that the man was about to lower himself down—at that very instant the murderer, looking behind him on the roof, threw his arms above his head, and uttered a yell of terror.

text: He sought me out, and left in my keeping, alongside a handful of other small effects, a painting: a portrait he had made himself, the likeness of that unfortunate young woman. He did not want to abandon it, but could not bring it with him on his hurried journey. Worn thin by gnawing anxiety and remorse to little more than a shadow of himself, he spoke in wild, disjointed bursts of the ruin and dishonor he had brought down with his own hands. He confided that he planned to liquidate all his property, no matter the loss, to turn it into ready cash; after setting aside a share of his recently acquired fortune for his wife and for you, he intended to flee the country. I knew all too well he would not be traveling alone, and that he never planned to return. Even to me, his oldest and earliest friend, whose fierce devotion to him had taken root above the grave of a person we had both held dearest—even to me he would not share any more specific details of his plan.
