He promised he would write to tell me everything, and then see me one final time, for the last meeting we would ever have on this earth. Alas! That meeting was already the last. No letter ever came, and I never laid eyes on him again.
source_text: “He came to me, and left with me, among some other things, a picture—a portrait painted by himself—a likeness of this poor girl—which he did not wish to leave behind, and could not carry forward on his hasty journey. He was worn by anxiety and remorse almost to a shadow; talked in a wild, distracted way, of ruin and dishonour worked by himself; confided to me his intention to convert his whole property, at any loss, into money, and, having settled on his wife and you a portion of his recent acquisition, to fly the country—I guessed too well he would not fly alone—and never see it more. Even from me, his old and early friend, whose strong attachment had taken root in the earth that covered one most dear to both—even from me he withheld any more particular confession, promising to write and tell me all, and after that to see me once again, for the last time on earth. Alas! _That_ was the last time. I had no letter, and I never saw him more.”

text: The surgeon laid the tiny newborn in her arms. She pressed her cold, pale lips fervently to its forehead, dragged a shaking hand over her own face, cast a wild, disoriented gaze around the room, shuddered from head to toe, slumped backward—and died. They rubbed vigorously at her chest, her hands, her temples to rouse her, but her blood had stopped pumping forever. They murmured soft words of hope and comfort, but those gentle things had been strangers to her for far too long.
source_text: The surgeon deposited it in her arms. She imprinted her cold white lips passionately on its forehead; passed her hands over her face; gazed wildly round; shuddered; fell back—and died. They chafed her breast, hands, and temples; but the blood had stopped forever. They talked of hope and comfort. They had been strangers too long.

text: “Now then,” the man said, “I saw three boys loitering on the opposite side of the road while this gentleman was reading: the prisoner here, and two other lads. The robbery was committed by an entirely different boy. I watched the act unfold, and I saw that this boy was utterly astonished and stupefied by what had taken place.” By this point the upright book-stall keeper had recovered his breath somewhat, and proceeded to recount the exact circumstances of the theft in a far more coherent manner.
source_text: “This,” said the man: “I saw three boys: two others and the prisoner here: loitering on the opposite side of the way, when this gentleman was reading. The robbery was committed by another boy. I saw it done; and I saw that this boy was perfectly amazed and stupified by it.” Having by this time recovered a little breath, the worthy book-stall keeper proceeded to relate, in a more coherent manner the exact circumstances of the robbery.

text: The work took almost no effort at all: the plot was so packed with caskets that the lid of the topmost one sat barely three feet below the grassy lip of the hole. Old Tom, the cemetery groundskeeper, hefted clods of loose earth into the gap, tamped them down only loosely with the heel of his boot, slung his shovel over one broad shoulder, and turned toward the tool shed. A small cluster of local urchins trailed at his heels, grousing loudly that the whole grim show had wrapped up far sooner than they’d hoped.
source_text: It was no very difficult task, for the grave was so full, that the uppermost coffin was within a few feet of the surface. The grave-digger shovelled in the earth; stamped it loosely down with his feet: shouldered his spade; and walked off, followed by the boys, who murmured very loud complaints at the fun being over so soon.

text: But warring with these kinder, better impulses was pride— a vice that clings to the lowest, most debased people every bit as tightly as it does to the privileged, self-assured elite. This miserable woman ran with thieves and brutal ruffians, a castoff from the filthiest, most forgotten slum haunts, an acquaintance to the dregs of jails and rotting prison ships, living her whole life under the cold, looming shadow of the gallows itself. Even this degraded, discarded soul was too proud to let a single faint glimmer of that soft, womanly feeling slip out. She wrote that feeling off as a weakness, but it was the only link she still had to the shared humanity almost every trace of which had been scrubbed away by her harsh, wasting life, all the way back when she was just a small child.
source_text: But struggling with these better feelings was pride,—the vice of the lowest and most debased creatures no less than of the high and self-assured. The miserable companion of thieves and ruffians, the fallen outcast of low haunts, the associate of the scourings of the jails and hulks, living within the shadow of the gallows itself,—even this degraded being felt too proud to betray a feeble gleam of the womanly feeling which she thought a weakness, but which alone connected her with that humanity, of which her wasting life had obliterated so many, many traces when a very child.

text: This brought precious little comfort to the young boy. Still, for all his tender age, he was sharp enough to pretend he was heartbroken to leave. Summoning tears was no hard task for him: hunger and the harsh treatment he had endured of late make excellent allies when you need to weep, and Oliver’s sobs were thoroughly, unmistakeably natural. Mrs. Mann showered him with cloying, endless embraces, then pressed into his hand what he wanted far more: a slice of bread and butter, so he would not appear overly ravenous when he reached the workhouse. Clutching the bread in one hand, the small rough brown parish cap perched on his head, Oliver was led off by Mr. Bumble, leaving the squalid hovel where not a single kind word or gentle glance had ever cut through the cold gloom of his earliest childhood. Even so, he dissolved into a fit of raw, childish agony the moment the cottage gate clicked shut behind him. As miserable as his fellow suffering little companions were, they were the only friends he had ever known; and for the first time, the cold weight of how alone he was in the vast, unforgiving world settled deep into his small, tender heart.
source_text: This was no very great consolation to the child. Young as he was, however, he had sense enough to make a feint of feeling great regret at going away. It was no very difficult matter for the boy to call tears into his eyes. Hunger and recent ill-usage are great assistants if you want to cry; and Oliver cried very naturally indeed. Mrs. Mann gave him a thousand embraces, and what Oliver wanted a great deal more, a piece of bread and butter, lest he should seem too hungry when he got to the workhouse. With the slice of bread in his hand, and the little brown-cloth parish cap on his head, Oliver was then led away by Mr. Bumble from the wretched home where one kind word or look had never lighted the gloom of his infant years. And yet he burst into an agony of childish grief, as the cottage-gate closed after him. Wretched as were the little companions in misery he was leaving behind, they were the only friends he had ever known; and a sense of his loneliness in the great wide world, sank into the child’s heart for the first time.

text: Not a word left the Dodger’s lips at first; he only brushed Oliver’s hair down over his eyes, and commented that he would wisen up to how things worked soon enough. The older gentleman noticed the color rushing to Oliver’s cheeks then, and shifted the subject to ask whether the execution held that morning had drawn a particularly large crowd. That left Oliver more bewildered than he’d been already; the two boys’ responses made it clear both had been in attendance, and Oliver couldn’t help but wonder how they ever managed to find the time to be as remarkably hardworking as they seemed to be.
source_text: The Dodger said nothing, but he smoothed Oliver’s hair over his eyes, and said he’d know better, by and by; upon which the old gentleman, observing Oliver’s colour mounting, changed the subject by asking whether there had been much of a crowd at the execution that morning? This made him wonder more and more; for it was plain from the replies of the two boys that they had both been there; and Oliver naturally wondered how they could possibly have found time to be so very industrious.

text: “Punctual as clockwork, no less!” retorted the parish verger. “But what comes of it, eh? What thankless conduct do these unruly folks show us, sir? Why, the husband sends back word that the tonic won’t suit his wife’s ailment, so she won’t take a drop of it—says she won’t take it, sir! Good, strong, wholesome tonic, that worked with great success for two Welsh farmhands and a chimney sweep barely a week prior—sent out for nothing, poured into an old ink bottle to boot—and he has the nerve to send back word that she won’t take it, sir!”
source_text: “Promptness, indeed!” replied the beadle. “But what’s the consequence; what’s the ungrateful behaviour of these rebels, sir? Why, the husband sends back word that the medicine won’t suit his wife’s complaint, and so she shan’t take it—says she shan’t take it, sir! Good, strong, wholesome medicine, as was given with great success to two Irish labourers and a coal-heaver, only a week before—sent ’em for nothing, with a blackin’-bottle in,—and he sends back word that she shan’t take it, sir!”

text: Then the night descended: thick, dreary, hush-stilled night. For every other soul keeping vigil through the dark, the toll of the village steeple clock is a comfort, counting down the hours until dawn and the warm stir of waking life. For him, each peal dragged only despair in its wake. The low, resonant clang of each cast-iron bell carried only a single, echoing, hollow note: Death. What good was the bright, raucous bustle of cheerful morning, which seeped through even the shadowed walls of his small room, to a man in his state? It was just another kind of death knell, sharp with mockery layered over its cold, unspoken warning.
source_text: Then came the night—dark, dismal, silent night. Other watchers are glad to hear this church-clock strike, for they tell of life and coming day. To him they brought despair. The boom of every iron bell came laden with the one, deep, hollow sound—Death. What availed the noise and bustle of cheerful morning, which penetrated even there, to him? It was another form of knell, with mockery added to the warning.

text: Kael pressed onward, jaw set; but as the last glow of the village’s gabled roofs faded behind him and he stepped into the unbroken quiet and inky black of the country lane, a cold, crawling dread coiled so deep in his bones it made his teeth ache. Every shape ahead of him, solid form or wispy shade, motionless or skittering across the dirt, took on the cast of some nightmarish thing; yet these fleeting frights paled next to the unshakable conviction that the gaunt, ghostly figure he’d seen at dawn was dogging his steps. He could make out its shadow pooling in the murk, fill in every tiny detail of its sharp, hunched outline, and picture the rigid, funereal slowness with which it glided behind him. He swore he heard the scrape of its tattered robes against the underbrush, and every puff of cool night air carried the echo of that final, guttural whimper it had uttered hours before. If he froze mid-step, it halted too. If he broke into a sprint, it kept pace—not running alongside him, which would almost have been a comfort; it moved instead like a corpse animated by no more than the barest spark of unholy motion, drifting on a single slow, mournful draft that never picked up speed or died away.
source_text: He went on doggedly; but as he left the town behind him, and plunged into the solitude and darkness of the road, he felt a dread and awe creeping upon him which shook him to the core. Every object before him, substance or shadow, still or moving, took the semblance of some fearful thing; but these fears were nothing compared to the sense that haunted him of that morning’s ghastly figure following at his heels. He could trace its shadow in the gloom, supply the smallest item of the outline, and note how stiff and solemn it seemed to stalk along. He could hear its garments rustling in the leaves, and every breath of wind came laden with that last low cry. If he stopped it did the same. If he ran, it followed—not running too: that would have been a relief: but like a corpse endowed with the mere machinery of life, and borne on one slow melancholy wind that never rose or fell.

text: “Hah!” cackled the grizzled old fence, hunching his shoulders nearly to his ears and twisting every feature into a grotesque, sneering grin. “Sharp little curs! Sharp little curs! Staunch right to the very end! Never breathed a word to the parish constable where their crew had holed up. Never ratted on old Silas, not once! And why would they? It wouldn’t have slackened the noose a single thread, or held the gallows drop from falling a minute longer. No, no, no! Fine lads, that lot! Fine lads!”
source_text: “Aha!” said the Jew, shrugging up his shoulders, and distorting every feature with a hideous grin. “Clever dogs! Clever dogs! Staunch to the last! Never told the old parson where they were. Never poached upon old Fagin! And why should they? It wouldn’t have loosened the knot, or kept the drop up, a minute longer. No, no, no! Fine fellows! Fine fellows!”

text: “Because, my cheeky little cross-examiner,” the doctor replied, “when you look at the whole situation through their eyes, there’s a whole pile of damning, ugly details stacked against him. He can only corroborate the parts that make him look guilty, and none of the stuff that would paint him in a good light. Damn those people, they’re dead set on prying out every last why and wherefore, they won’t accept a single thing on faith. Just going off his own story, see? He’s been running with a crew of thieves for a while now, he got dragged to a police officer on a charge of picking a gentleman’s pocket, then he was taken by force from that same gentleman’s house to some spot he can’t even describe or point to—he hasn’t got the faintest clue where it even is. Some men who’ve apparently taken a weird, obsessive shine to him cart him down to Chertsey whether he wants to go or not, and push him through a window to rob the house. Then, right at the exact second he’s about to yell to warn the people inside, the one thing that would’ve cleared his whole name, some bumbling, oafish half-breed butler who acts like a feral mutt comes barrelling out of nowhere and shoots him! It’s like he did it on purpose just to stop the kid from doing anything to help himself. Do you follow all that?”
source_text: “Because, my pretty cross-examiner,” replied the doctor: “because, viewed with their eyes, there are many ugly points about it; he can only prove the parts that look ill, and none of those that look well. Confound the fellows, they _will_ have the why and the wherefore, and will take nothing for granted. On his own showing, you see, he has been the companion of thieves for some time past; he has been carried to a police-officer, on a charge of picking a gentleman’s pocket; he has been taken away, forcibly, from that gentleman’s house, to a place which he cannot describe or point out, and of the situation of which he has not the remotest idea. He is brought down to Chertsey, by men who seem to have taken a violent fancy to him, whether he will or no; and is put through a window to rob a house; and then, just at the very moment when he is going to alarm the inmates, and so do the very thing that would set him all to rights, there rushes into the way, a blundering dog of a half-bred butler, and shoots him! As if on purpose to prevent his doing any good for himself! Don’t you see all this?”

text: “Some upstanding citizen you are, hauling a penniless, ragged boy up on charges!” scoffed Mr. Croft, his features twisting into a laughably unconvincing show of compassion. “As I see it, sir, you came to possess that book under deeply suspicious, unsavory circumstances, and you ought to count yourself extraordinarily fortunate the property’s rightful owner has declined to press charges. Let this be a warning to you, fellow, or the law will catch up to you soon enough. The boy is discharged. Clear the office!”
source_text: “A nice person to prefer a charge against a poor boy!” said Fang, with a comical effort to look humane. “I consider, sir, that you have obtained possession of that book, under very suspicious and disreputable circumstances; and you may think yourself very fortunate that the owner of the property declines to prosecute. Let this be a lesson to you, my man, or the law will overtake you yet. The boy is discharged. Clear the office!”

text: “Somethin’ along those lines, old feller,” said Charley. Hardly had he finished speaking when Master Bates caught hold of one end of his neckerchief, held it upright in the air, let his head drop onto his shoulder, and jerked a strange, tight sound through his teeth. That lively, wordless little performance made it plain that strangling and hanging were exactly the same thing.
source_text: “Something in this way, old feller,” said Charley. As he said it, Master Bates caught up an end of his neckerchief; and, holding it erect in the air, dropped his head on his shoulder, and jerked a curious sound through his teeth; thereby indicating, by a lively pantomimic representation, that scragging and hanging were one and the same thing.

text: “Now,” the doctor murmured, as he turned the bedroom door handle with deliberate softness, “let’s hear what you make of him. He hasn’t had a shave in a little while, but he doesn’t look even remotely ferocious for all that. Hold on, though—let me make sure he’s decent enough for visitors first.”
source_text: “Now,” said the doctor, in a whisper, as he softly turned the handle of a bedroom-door, “let us hear what you think of him. He has not been shaved very recently, but he don’t look at all ferocious notwithstanding. Stop, though! Let me first see that he is in visiting order.”

text: “I’m not even sure what it is,” the girl answered. “I wish I could put a name to it. All day I’ve been hounded by awful thoughts of death, blood-stained burial shrouds, and a terror so sharp it’s felt like I’m burning alive from the inside out. I tried to pass the time tonight by reading a book, and those same horrifying images seemed to swim right into the text on the page.”
source_text: “I scarcely know of what,” replied the girl. “I wish I did. Horrible thoughts of death, and shrouds with blood upon them, and a fear that has made me burn as if I was on fire, have been upon me all day. I was reading a book tonight, to wile the time away, and the same things came into the print.”

text: Bates was just as focused on the card game, but he had a far more high-strung, impulsive temperament than his suave, quick-witted companion, so it was impossible to miss how often he reached for the gin and water, not to mention the constant stream of jokes and off-topic asides that were entirely inappropriate for a proper, skill-focused rubber of cards. The Artful Dodger, counting on their long, tight friendship, pulled him aside more than once to reason seriously with him about how unseemly those little outbursts were. Bates took every single one of those lectures entirely in stride: he just told his friend to go blow himself, or stick his head in a burlap sack, or fired back some other equally sharp, silly wisecrack of the same stripe, all of which landed so well that Mr. Chitling was thoroughly impressed by his wit. It was hard to overlook the fact that Chitling and his partner lost every single hand, too; and far from annoying Bates, that state of affairs seemed to give him more unbridled joy than anything else. He cackled so loud he was practically roaring at the end of every deal, swearing up and down that he’d never come across such a delightfully chaotic, fun game in his entire life.
source_text: Master Bates was also attentive to the play; but being of a more excitable nature than his accomplished friend, it was observable that he more frequently applied himself to the gin-and-water, and moreover indulged in many jests and irrelevant remarks, all highly unbecoming a scientific rubber. Indeed, the Artful, presuming upon their close attachment, more than once took occasion to reason gravely with his companion upon these improprieties; all of which remonstrances, Master Bates received in extremely good part; merely requesting his friend to be “blowed,” or to insert his head in a sack, or replying with some other neatly-turned witticism of a similar kind, the happy application of which, excited considerable admiration in the mind of Mr. Chitling. It was remarkable that the latter gentleman and his partner invariably lost; and that the circumstance, so far from angering Master Bates, appeared to afford him the highest amusement, inasmuch as he laughed most uproariously at the end of every deal, and protested that he had never seen such a jolly game in all his born days.

text: “I say again, I hold this to be unshakably true. To prove that I am ready to put my full trust in you, I will speak without reserve: we intend to wring the secret, whatever it may be, out of that man Rook by preying on his fear. But if—if—” the gentleman said, pausing just long enough to tap a finger against his oak desk, “we cannot take him into custody, or once secured, he cannot be made to comply with our wishes, you must deliver up the Jew.”
source_text: “I repeat that I firmly believe it. To prove to you that I am disposed to trust you, I tell you without reserve, that we propose to extort the secret, whatever it may be, from the fear of this man Monks. But if—if—” said the gentleman, “he cannot be secured, or, if secured, cannot be acted upon as we wish, you must deliver up the Jew.”

text: She stumbled and crumpled to the ground, her vision nearly completely obscured by blood pouring in thick streams from a deep gash across her forehead. Fighting through waves of sharp, disorienting pain, she strained to push herself up onto her knees, fumbling inside her bodice to draw out a white handkerchief—Rose Maylie’s very own. Clasping it tight in her folded hands, she lifted it as high toward the heavens as her waning strength would permit, then breathed out a quiet, shaking prayer begging her Creator for mercy.
source_text: She staggered and fell: nearly blinded with the blood that rained down from a deep gash in her forehead; but raising herself, with difficulty, on her knees, drew from her bosom a white handkerchief—Rose Maylie’s own—and holding it up, in her folded hands, as high towards Heaven as her feeble strength would allow, breathed one prayer for mercy to her Maker.

text: “That gentleman and lady she went to entirely of her own accord earlier? They told her to ditch every last one of her friends, Monks first of all—and she did it. They asked her to describe what he looked like, and she did. Asked her what house we all meet at, the one we’re always coming and going from, and she gave that up. Asked where the best vantage point to watch it from was, she told them that too. Asked what time people usually turned up there, and she handed that over as well. She did every last bit of it, spilled every single word, no one held a threat over her, not a single murmur of pushback from her—she did, didn’t she?” Fagin shrieked, half-deranged with boiling fury.
source_text: “A gentleman and a lady that she had gone to of her own accord before, who asked her to give up all her pals, and Monks first, which she did—and to describe him, which she did—and to tell her what house it was that we meet at, and go to, which she did—and where it could be best watched from, which she did—and what time the people went there, which she did. She did all this. She told it all every word without a threat, without a murmur—she did—did she not?” cried Fagin, half mad with fury.

text: “Right here,” the woman replied. As if relieved to finally be free of the burden, she hastily tossed a small goatskin pouch barely large enough for a lady’s pendant watch onto the scarred tabletop. Elias pounced on it at once, ripping its stitched seam open with trembling, eager fingers. Inside rested a tiny gold locket holding two fine locks of hair, and a simple, unadorned gold wedding ring.
source_text: “_There_,” replied the woman. And, as if glad to be relieved of it, she hastily threw upon the table a small kid bag scarcely large enough for a French watch, which Monks pouncing upon, tore open with trembling hands. It contained a little gold locket: in which were two locks of hair, and a plain gold wedding-ring.

text: Dawn advanced apace. The air grew sharper and more biting, as its first murky cast—less the soft blush of day’s birth than the final fading gasp of night’s death—glimmered faintly across the horizon. The shapes that had loomed dim and terrifying in the darkness sharpened by degrees, slowly resolving into their well-known, ordinary forms. The rain came down thick and relentless, drumming loudly among the bare, leafless hedgerows. But Elias did not feel the cold drops beating against him; he still lay stretched out, helpless and unresponsive, atop his bed of packed clay.
source_text: Morning drew on apace. The air became more sharp and piercing, as its first dull hue—the death of night, rather than the birth of day—glimmered faintly in the sky. The objects which had looked dim and terrible in the darkness, grew more and more defined, and gradually resolved into their familiar shapes. The rain came down, thick and fast, and pattered noisily among the leafless bushes. But, Oliver felt it not, as it beat against him; for he still lay stretched, helpless and unconscious, on his bed of clay.

text: Mr. Hale, the recipient of that wordless entreaty, and perhaps conscious that his personal authority and reputation rested on bringing Lila Mae to heel without delay, loosed some twenty-odd curses and threats in quick succession; the speed with which he produced them spoke volumes to the ingenuity of his foul tongue. As none left any discernible mark on the target they’d been hurled at, however, he turned to far more tangible, forceful measures.
source_text: Mr. Sikes, thus mutely appealed to; and possibly feeling his personal pride and influence interested in the immediate reduction of Miss Nancy to reason; gave utterance to about a couple of score of curses and threats, the rapid production of which reflected great credit on the fertility of his invention. As they produced no visible effect on the object against whom they were discharged, however, he resorted to more tangible arguments.

text: “I hold very firm opinions on this matter, sir,” the short-tempered old gentleman remarked as he tugged off his gloves. “Our street’s pavement is always littered with orange peel, to varying degrees, and I know for a fact it’s left there by the surgeon’s apprentice who works at the end of the road. Just last night a young woman tripped over a piece and crashed against my garden railings. The second she got to her feet, I saw her glance toward that accursed red lamp of his, glowing like some gaudy pantomime light. ‘Don’t you go near him,’ I shouted out my window, ‘he’s an assassin! A regular man-trap!’ And that’s exactly what he is. If he isn’t—” At that, the cantankerous old man rapped his cane hard against the floor; his friends all knew this gesture stood in for the customary wager he offered whenever he didn’t state it outright. He kept hold of his cane as he settled back into his seat, then flipped open the double lorgnette he wore strung on a wide black ribbon to peer at Oliver. Noticing he was the object of this scrutiny, Oliver flushed and bowed again.
source_text: “I feel strongly on this subject, sir,” said the irritable old gentleman, drawing off his gloves. “There’s always more or less orange-peel on the pavement in our street; and I _know_ it’s put there by the surgeon’s boy at the corner. A young woman stumbled over a bit last night, and fell against my garden-railings; directly she got up I saw her look towards his infernal red lamp with the pantomime-light. ‘Don’t go to him,’ I called out of the window, ‘he’s an assassin! A man-trap!’ So he is. If he is not—” Here the irascible old gentleman gave a great knock on the ground with his stick; which was always understood, by his friends, to imply the customary offer, whenever it was not expressed in words. Then, still keeping his stick in his hand, he sat down; and, opening a double eye-glass, which he wore attached to a broad black riband, took a view of Oliver: who, seeing that he was the object of inspection, coloured, and bowed again.

text: “Every single word!” the gentleman cried out, “every word exchanged between you and that loathsome scoundrel is fully known to me. The shadows stretched across the wall caught every one of your hushed whispers and carried them straight to my ears; the sight of that persecuted, wronged child has moved even vice itself to turn course, gifting it the nerve and nearly all the trappings of virtue. A murder has been committed, and you are complicit in it morally, even if you took no direct part in the act.”
source_text: “Every word!” cried the gentleman, “every word that has passed between you and this detested villain, is known to me. Shadows on the wall have caught your whispers, and brought them to my ear; the sight of the persecuted child has turned vice itself, and given it the courage and almost the attributes of virtue. Murder has been done, to which you were morally if not really a party.”

text: “Let me tell you straight, ma’am. He turned up again last night. The pair of them headed upstairs once more, and I wrapped myself up tight so my shadow wouldn’t give me away, then pressed close to the door to listen in again. The first words I caught from Monks were these: ‘The only proof of that boy’s identity is lying at the bottom of the river, and the old hag who took them from his mother is rotting in her coffin.’ They laughed, and chattered about how well he’d pulled that off; then Monks kept ranting about the boy, growing more and more unruly, saying that even though he’d already locked the little devil’s money away safely for himself, he’d rather have taken the other path. What a game it would have been, he said, to dash the whole point of his father’s will by running the boy through every jail in town, then framing him for some hanging offense Fagin could easily arrange, after squeezing a healthy profit out of him first on top of it all.”
source_text: “I’ll tell you, lady. Last night he came again. Again they went upstairs, and I, wrapping myself up so that my shadow would not betray me, again listened at the door. The first words I heard Monks say were these: ‘So the only proofs of the boy’s identity lie at the bottom of the river, and the old hag that received them from the mother is rotting in her coffin.’ They laughed, and talked of his success in doing this; and Monks, talking on about the boy, and getting very wild, said that though he had got the young devil’s money safely now, he’d rather have had it the other way; for, what a game it would have been to have brought down the boast of the father’s will, by driving him through every jail in town, and then hauling him up for some capital felony which Fagin could easily manage, after having made a good profit of him besides.”

text: “These sorts of things hold perfectly well, and like fine wine, their worth often doubles as the years drag on,” the matron answered, still clinging to the deliberate, unflappable indifference she had adopted. “As for lying dead? For all you or I can know, there are those who will lie cold in the earth for twelve thousand years yet, or even twelve million, who will tell the strangest of tales when the time finally comes!”
source_text: “Such matters keep well, and, like good wine, often double their value in course of time,” answered the matron, still preserving the resolute indifference she had assumed. “As to lying dead, there are those who will lie dead for twelve thousand years to come, or twelve million, for anything you or I know, who will tell strange tales at last!”

text: Though Mr. Crackit spoke in a whisper so faint it was nearly inaudible, and laughed without making a sound, Sikes cut him off with an imperious command to fall silent and get to work. Toby obeyed at once: he first drew out his lantern and set it on the ground, then braced himself firmly with his head pressed to the wall beneath the window, hands resting on his knees to turn his back into a step. The moment he was in position, Sikes clambered onto him, guided Oliver gently through the window feet first, and, without releasing his grip on the boy’s collar, set him safely on the floor inside.
source_text: Although Mr. Crackit spoke in a scarcely audible whisper, and laughed without noise, Sikes imperiously commanded him to be silent, and to get to work. Toby complied, by first producing his lantern, and placing it on the ground; then by planting himself firmly with his head against the wall beneath the window, and his hands upon his knees, so as to make a step of his back. This was no sooner done, than Sikes, mounting upon him, put Oliver gently through the window with his feet first; and, without leaving hold of his collar, planted him safely on the floor inside.

text: The truth was, this well-regarded doctor had lived his entire life guided almost entirely by gut instinct, and the quality of those instincts spoke for itself: far from landing him in any unusual scrapes or hardships, he was held in the highest affection and regard by every person who knew him. To be perfectly frank, he was mildly irritable for a few moments when he failed to secure supporting proof for Oliver’s account the very first time he’d had an opportunity to do so. He shook off the mood quickly, though; when he saw that Oliver’s responses to his questions remained just as direct and unwavering, and were delivered with the same obvious openness and honesty as they always had been, he resolved to take everything the boy told him as fact from that point forward.
source_text: Now, the fact was that the excellent doctor had never acted upon anything but impulse all through his life, and it was no bad compliment to the nature of the impulses which governed him, that so far from being involved in any peculiar troubles or misfortunes, he had the warmest respect and esteem of all who knew him. If the truth must be told, he was a little out of temper, for a minute or two, at being disappointed in procuring corroborative evidence of Oliver’s story on the very first occasion on which he had a chance of obtaining any. He soon came round again, however; and finding that Oliver’s replies to his questions, were still as straightforward and consistent, and still delivered with as much apparent sincerity and truth, as they had ever been, he made up his mind to attach full credence to them, from that time forth.

text: Roughly around this same hour, Mr. Hawthorne, Marmaduke, and the roving tinsmith were replenishing their strength after the frights and bone-deep exhaustion of the previous night, huddled over tea and assorted small savories in the estate kitchen. It was hardly Mr. Hawthorne’s usual custom to permit overmuch casual intimacy with the lower household staff, after all: he typically carried himself with a courteous but lofty amiability around them, a demeanor that flattered their sense of being seen even as it never let them forget his far higher station in the household hierarchy. But calamities—sudden deaths, raging house fires, bold break-ins—level all social lines, and so Mr. Hawthorne sat with his long legs propped before the kitchen’s cast-iron fender, his left elbow braced on the scuffed pine table, as he gestured widely with his right hand to lay out a granular, blow-by-blow retelling of the burglary. His audience (and most of all the head cook and under-housemaid, who had drifted over to join the little group) hung on every word, their breaths held in rapt, unblinking attention.
source_text: It happened that about this time, Mr. Giles, Brittles, and the tinker, were recruiting themselves, after the fatigues and terrors of the night, with tea and sundries, in the kitchen. Not that it was Mr. Giles’s habit to admit to too great familiarity the humbler servants: towards whom it was rather his wont to deport himself with a lofty affability, which, while it gratified, could not fail to remind them of his superior position in society. But, death, fires, and burglary, make all men equals; so Mr. Giles sat with his legs stretched out before the kitchen fender, leaning his left arm on the table, while, with his right, he illustrated a circumstantial and minute account of the robbery, to which his hearers (but especially the cook and housemaid, who were of the party) listened with breathless interest.

text: “Now this,” the hawker said, holding up a small cake of the product, “is the foolproof, priceless formula that strips away every last stain, rust, grime, mildew, smudge, fleck, splotch, or splatter from silk, satin, linen, cambric, broadcloth, crape, woven goods, carpet, merino, muslin, bombazeen, or any woolen textile. Wine marks, fruit stains, beer spots, water rings, paint blots, pitch smears—any stain at all, every last one wipes off with a single rub of this infallible, invaluable mixture. If a lady has so much as tarnished her good name, she only needs to swallow one cake and she’ll be rid of all trouble instantly—because it’s poison. If a gentleman wants to put its claims to the test, he just has to bolt down one little square, and he’ll have irrefutable proof of its potency—works every bit as reliably as a pistol bullet, tastes a great deal worse, so all the more honor in taking it. One penny a square. For all these miraculous virtues, just one penny a square!”
source_text: “This,” said the fellow, producing one, “this is the infallible and invaluable composition for removing all sorts of stain, rust, dirt, mildew, spick, speck, spot, or spatter, from silk, satin, linen, cambric, cloth, crape, stuff, carpet, merino, muslin, bombazeen, or woollen stuff. Wine-stains, fruit-stains, beer-stains, water-stains, paint-stains, pitch-stains, any stains, all come out at one rub with the infallible and invaluable composition. If a lady stains her honour, she has only need to swallow one cake and she’s cured at once—for it’s poison. If a gentleman wants to prove this, he has only need to bolt one little square, and he has put it beyond question—for it’s quite as satisfactory as a pistol-bullet, and a great deal nastier in the flavour, consequently the more credit in taking it. One penny a square. With all these virtues, one penny a square!”

text: His palm settled on the pine chair standing dead center of the small sitting room, but a sharp shudder wracked his frame the second he bent to lower himself into the seat. Darting a skittish glance over his right shoulder, he hauled the heavy furniture backward as far as it would go, grinding its rough wooden legs harshly against the plaster wall before he finally dropped down onto its hard, worn seat.
source_text: He laid his hand upon a chair which stood in the middle of the room, but shuddering as he was about to drop into it, and seeming to glance over his shoulder, dragged it back close to the wall—as close as it would go—and ground it against it—and sat down.

text: “There’s one reason,” the girl answered firmly, “first, that the lady already knows what it is, and she’ll back me up on it, I know she will—she gave me her word. And there’s another reason on top of that, too: as rough and misguided as his life has been, I’ve lived just as messy a life myself. Plenty of us ran in the same circles, made all the same bad choices together. I’m not gonna betray them, not when any one of them could’ve turned on me first, but they never did, no matter how deeply flawed they all are.”
source_text: “For one reason,” rejoined the girl firmly, “for one reason, that the lady knows and will stand by me in, I know she will, for I have her promise: and for this other reason, besides, that, bad life as he has led, I have led a bad life too; there are many of us who have kept the same courses together, and I’ll not turn upon them, who might—any of them—have turned upon me, but didn’t, bad as they are.”

text: By that point, a swarm of disoriented, jumbled thoughts pressed thick against the edges of his consciousness. It felt as if he still tramped between Rook and Harker, bickering furiously at either elbow—he could make out each sharp, snapped phrase as clearly as if they spoke right against his cheek; and only when he wrenched his focus back with a sharp, desperate lurch to keep from pitching face-first into the mud did he realize he was answering their taunts out loud. Next he was tramping alone at Rook’s side, slow and heavy-footed as they’d been the day before; every time half-seen figures drifted past their path, he could feel the thief’s cold, iron grip clamped tight around his forearm. Without warning he flinched hard at the crack of gunfire; wild shouts and wails split the air; torches flared blindingly bright in front of his face; the whole world dissolved into roar and chaos, some invisible force yanking him fast away from the din. Threading through every one of these flashing, disjointed visions was a vague, gnawing awareness of ache, a dull, persistent throb that wore at him and nagged without pause.
source_text: And now, hosts of bewildering and confused ideas came crowding on his mind. He seemed to be still walking between Sikes and Crackit, who were angrily disputing—for the very words they said, sounded in his ears; and when he caught his own attention, as it were, by making some violent effort to save himself from falling, he found that he was talking to them. Then, he was alone with Sikes, plodding on as on the previous day; and as shadowy people passed them, he felt the robber’s grasp upon his wrist. Suddenly, he started back at the report of firearms; there rose into the air, loud cries and shouts; lights gleamed before his eyes; all was noise and tumult, as some unseen hand bore him hurriedly away. Through all these rapid visions, there ran an undefined, uneasy consciousness of pain, which wearied and tormented him incessantly.

text: “If you don’t grab the pocket handkerchiefs and watches yourself,” the Dodger said, simplifying his argument to suit Oliver’s limited understanding, “some other bloke will. That leaves the folks who lost ’em just as bad off, you worse off for passing them up, and not a soul a halfpenny’s worth richer except the bloke who nabs them first—and you’ve got every bit as much right to those things as he does.”
source_text: “If you don’t take pocket-handkechers and watches,” said the Dodger, reducing his conversation to the level of Oliver’s capacity, “some other cove will; so that the coves that lose ’em will be all the worse, and you’ll be all the worse, too, and nobody half a ha’p’orth the better, except the chaps wot gets them—and you’ve just as good a right to them as they have.”

text: The moment he finished speaking, the Jew bent low to set the candle on a higher step of the staircase, positioned directly across from the room’s entrance. Once that was done, he led the way into the apartment, which held scarcely any furnishings at all: only a splintered broken armchair, and a tattered, uncovered old sofa tucked behind the door. The stranger sank onto that ragged piece of seating with the heavy slump of a thoroughly worn out man, while the Jew dragged the armchair across to face him, until they sat directly opposite one another. The room was not fully shrouded in dark: the door stood slightly ajar, and the candle burning out on the stairs cast a faint, wavering glimmer onto the wall across from them.
source_text: With those words, the Jew, stooping down, placed the candle on an upper flight of stairs, exactly opposite to the room door. This done, he led the way into the apartment; which was destitute of all movables save a broken arm-chair, and an old couch or sofa without covering, which stood behind the door. Upon this piece of furniture, the stranger sat himself with the air of a weary man; and the Jew, drawing up the arm-chair opposite, they sat face to face. It was not quite dark; the door was partially open; and the candle outside, threw a feeble reflection on the opposite wall.

text: The gentleman wandered slowly and deliberately into the bar to settle his bill, a process that dragged on far longer than it should have. Once the tab was finally finalized and paid, they still had to saddle a horse and get the messenger suited up, which ate up another full ten minutes. All the while, Oliver was thrumming with such unbearable impatience and anxiety that he swore he could have clambered onto the horse himself and torn off at a full gallop straight to the next stop. Finally, every last thing was ready. The small package was passed up to the rider, paired with a litany of urgent reminders and pleas to deliver it as quickly as possible. The man dug his spurs into his horse’s flank, hooves clattering noisily over the marketplace’s uneven paving stones, and he was out of the town limits, thundering down the toll road, in barely two minutes.
source_text: This gentleman walked with much deliberation into the bar to make out the bill: which took a long time making out: and after it was ready, and paid, a horse had to be saddled, and a man to be dressed, which took up ten good minutes more. Meanwhile Oliver was in such a desperate state of impatience and anxiety, that he felt as if he could have jumped upon the horse himself, and galloped away, full tear, to the next stage. At length, all was ready; and the little parcel having been handed up, with many injunctions and entreaties for its speedy delivery, the man set spurs to his horse, and rattling over the uneven paving of the market-place, was out of the town, and galloping along the turnpike-road, in a couple of minutes.

text: Mr. Thaddeus Croft was a tall, gaunt man with prominent, knobbly joints, clad in a suit of frayed faded black, paired with darned cotton stockings of the exact same dull hue, and scuffed leather shoes that matched the rest of his worn attire. His sharp, angular features were not naturally shaped to hold a smile, but he was generally inclined to the cheerful, practiced affability his trade required. His step was light and springy, and the faint crinkle at the corners of his eyes bespoke quiet inner good humor as he stepped forward to greet Mr. Gideon Wren, shaking his hand warmly and firmly.
source_text: Mr. Sowerberry was a tall gaunt, large-jointed man, attired in a suit of threadbare black, with darned cotton stockings of the same colour, and shoes to answer. His features were not naturally intended to wear a smiling aspect, but he was in general rather given to professional jocosity. His step was elastic, and his face betokened inward pleasantry, as he advanced to Mr. Bumble, and shook him cordially by the hand.

text: The winding alleyways and cramped courtyards finally spilled out onto a wide, unenclosed lot, where wooden livestock pens jutted up at random intervals, along with all the other telltale markers of a weekly cattle market. Caleb slowed his steps the second they crossed onto the lot: Lila could barely keep her feet under her anymore, worn thin by the breakneck speed they had maintained since leaving the warren of side streets. Twisting sharply to face Toby, he barked a rough order for the boy to take firm hold of Lila’s hand.
source_text: The narrow streets and courts, at length, terminated in a large open space; scattered about which, were pens for beasts, and other indications of a cattle-market. Sikes slackened his pace when they reached this spot: the girl being quite unable to support any longer, the rapid rate at which they had hitherto walked. Turning to Oliver, he roughly commanded him to take hold of Nancy’s hand.

text: “May heaven reward you for your kindness,” the girl replied. “If you had any idea what a state I’m in some days, you’d truly take pity on me, I promise. I snuck away from people who would absolutely kill me if they learned I’d come here, all to pass along what I overheard. Do you know a man named Monks?”
source_text: “Heaven bless you for your goodness!” rejoined the girl. “If you knew what I am sometimes, you would pity me, indeed. But I have stolen away from those who would surely murder me, if they knew I had been here, to tell you what I have overheard. Do you know a man named Monks?”

text: “I reckon that’s why,” Elias answered softly, “since the kingdom of heaven’s so far removed, and everyone there’s far too full of joy to travel down to the cot of a ragged, sick boy. But if she knew I was laid up and unwell, she’d feel for me even there—she was wracked with terrible sickness herself, right before she passed, after all.” He fell quiet for a long, slow beat before speaking again. “I don’t think she knows any of what’s befallen me, though. If she’d ever seen me hurt or suffering, it would have cut her to the core. Every time she comes to me in dreams, her face is only ever sweet, and peaceful, and bright.”
source_text: “I suppose it was,” replied Oliver, “because heaven is a long way off; and they are too happy there, to come down to the bedside of a poor boy. But if she knew I was ill, she must have pitied me, even there; for she was very ill herself before she died. She can’t know anything about me though,” added Oliver after a moment’s silence. “If she had seen me hurt, it would have made her sorrowful; and her face has always looked sweet and happy, when I have dreamed of her.”

text: “It is this,” Rose replied, “that you must strive to forget me—not as your old, deeply cherished companion, for that would cut me to the quick—but as the object of your romantic love. Look out into the world, and think of how many hearts there are whose regard you would be proud to earn. If you wish to confide any other feelings to me, I will be the truest, warmest, most faithful friend you could ever have.”
source_text: “It is,” replied Rose, “that you must endeavour to forget me; not as your old and dearly-attached companion, for that would wound me deeply; but, as the object of your love. Look into the world; think how many hearts you would be proud to gain, are there. Confide some other passion to me, if you will; I will be the truest, warmest, and most faithful friend you have.”

text: “We shall see,” the old gentleman replied, his gaze sharp and searching. “I lost the boy, and no effort on my part could turn up any trace of him. With your mother dead, I knew you alone could untangle this mystery if anyone could. The last I’d heard, you were living on your private estate in the West Indies—you know full well you retreated there after your mother’s passing to escape the fallout from the vicious, reckless choices you’d made here—so I booked passage across the sea. You’d already left the island months prior, though; word was you’d come to London, but no one could say where exactly. I sailed back home. Your agents had no clue to your residence. They told me you came and went as erratically as you ever had: gone for days on end sometimes, other times not appearing for months, seemingly still frequenting the same seedy low haunts and mixing with the same disreputable crowd you’d run with when you were a fierce, ungovernable young boy. I hounded them with repeated requests for information until they grew sick of hearing from me. I paced the city’s streets at all hours, day and night, but until two hours ago, every last one of my efforts was fruitless, and I’d never once caught so much as a fleeting glimpse of you.”
source_text: “We shall see,” returned the old gentleman with a searching glance. “I lost the boy, and no efforts of mine could recover him. Your mother being dead, I knew that you alone could solve the mystery if anybody could, and as when I had last heard of you you were on your own estate in the West Indies—whither, as you well know, you retired upon your mother’s death to escape the consequences of vicious courses here—I made the voyage. You had left it, months before, and were supposed to be in London, but no one could tell where. I returned. Your agents had no clue to your residence. You came and went, they said, as strangely as you had ever done: sometimes for days together and sometimes not for months: keeping to all appearance the same low haunts and mingling with the same infamous herd who had been your associates when a fierce ungovernable boy. I wearied them with new applications. I paced the streets by night and day, but until two hours ago, all my efforts were fruitless, and I never saw you for an instant.”

text: The two housemaids sprinted up the stairs to pass along the news that Mr. Giles had caught a robber; the tinker, meanwhile, poured all his energy into reviving Oliver, terrified the boy might die before he could be hanged. In the thick of all that racket and chaos, a soft, sweet woman’s voice rang out, silencing the entire commotion immediately.
source_text: The two women-servants ran upstairs to carry the intelligence that Mr. Giles had captured a robber; and the tinker busied himself in endeavouring to restore Oliver, lest he should die before he could be hanged. In the midst of all this noise and commotion, there was heard a sweet female voice, which quelled it in an instant.

text: “Wide open,” Toby replied, after poking his head inside to confirm for himself. “How that works is they always leave it propped on the latch, not fully shut, so the dog that’s got a bed in here can wander up and down the hallway whenever he’s awake and restless. Ha! Ha! Barney lured him off tonight, though. That’s such a slick little trick.”
source_text: “Wide,” replied Toby, after peeping in to satisfy himself. “The game of that is, that they always leave it open with a catch, so that the dog, who’s got a bed in here, may walk up and down the passage when he feels wakeful. Ha! ha! Barney ’ticed him away tonight. So neat!”

text: It was impossible to tell whether the spark of freedom and independence any rational being might feel had taken hold of him: perched on a table in a relaxed, easy pose, smoking a pipe, one leg swinging lazily back and forth while his boots were polished without him ever having to take them off—no irksome memory of fumbling with them earlier nor the dreary future chore of pulling them on to disrupt his quiet musings. Or perhaps it was the fine, smooth tobacco that soothed the Dodger’s spirits, or the mild, mellow beer that softened his usual sharp thoughts. Either way, for the time being, he was clearly suffused with a faint streak of romance and earnest enthusiasm entirely alien to his typical streetwise nature. He gazed down at Oliver with a pensive expression for a short moment, then lifted his head, let out a gentle sigh, and spoke half lost in his own reverie, half addressed to Master Bates:
source_text: Whether it was the sense of freedom and independence which a rational animal may be supposed to feel when he sits on a table in an easy attitude smoking a pipe, swinging one leg carelessly to and fro, and having his boots cleaned all the time, without even the past trouble of having taken them off, or the prospective misery of putting them on, to disturb his reflections; or whether it was the goodness of the tobacco that soothed the feelings of the Dodger, or the mildness of the beer that mollified his thoughts; he was evidently tinctured, for the nonce, with a spice of romance and enthusiasm, foreign to his general nature. He looked down on Oliver, with a thoughtful countenance, for a brief space; and then, raising his head, and heaving a gentle sigh, said, half in abstraction, and half to Master Bates:

text: He lowered himself onto the rough stone bench opposite the door, a slab that doubled as both seat and sleeping cot, and dropped his bloodshot gaze to the ground, fighting to pull his scattered thoughts into order. After a few minutes, disjointed snippets of the judge’s address began to surface, even though he’d been convinced at the time he could not make out a single word. Bit by bit, the fragments slotted into their proper places, each detail jogging loose the next, until in little time he could recall nearly the entire sentence exactly as it had been delivered. To be hanged by the neck until he was dead—that was the end of it. To be hanged by the neck until he was dead.
source_text: He sat down on a stone bench opposite the door, which served for seat and bedstead; and casting his blood-shot eyes upon the ground, tried to collect his thoughts. After awhile, he began to remember a few disjointed fragments of what the judge had said: though it had seemed to him, at the time, that he could not hear a word. These gradually fell into their proper places, and by degrees suggested more: so that in a little time he had the whole, almost as it was delivered. To be hanged by the neck, till he was dead—that was the end. To be hanged by the neck till he was dead.

text: Only a minute prior, the boy had looked every bit the quiet, mild, dispirited child that unforgiving harsh treatment had molded him into. But his spirit had been roused at last; the cruel slight against his dead mother had set his blood thrumming with white-hot rage. His chest heaved with ragged breaths, his posture pulled rigid and upright, his eyes glowed sharp and vivid with fury; his entire bearing transformed as he stood glowering down at the cowardly tormentor now huddled cringing at his feet, defying the other boy with a fierce, untested energy he had never known himself capable of before.
source_text: A minute ago, the boy had looked the quiet child, mild, dejected creature that harsh treatment had made him. But his spirit was roused at last; the cruel insult to his dead mother had set his blood on fire. His breast heaved; his attitude was erect; his eye bright and vivid; his whole person changed, as he stood glaring over the cowardly tormentor who now lay crouching at his feet; and defied him with an energy he had never known before.

text: “That’s where she died,” Monks said, “wasting away after a long, drawn-out illness. On her deathbed, she passed all those secrets down to me, along with her ravenous, poisonous hatred for everyone tangled up in them—though she didn’t even need to leave that part to me. I’d inherited that same hatred deep in my bones years before she was even dying. She refused to believe the girl had killed herself and taken the baby with her, not for a second. She was completely convinced a baby boy had been born, and that he was still alive out there somewhere. I swore to her if that boy ever crossed my path, I’d hunt him down without mercy. I’d never let him rest, hound him with the most bitter, unforgiving malice I possessed. I’d pour every drop of my deep-seated hatred out on him, and tear down that empty, arrogant brag in that offensive will by dragging him straight to the gallows steps if I could manage it. She was right. He finally turned up in my life. I got off to a perfect start, too, and if it wasn’t for those chattering, loose-lipped whores running their mouths, I’d have finished the job just as clean as I started it!”
source_text: “There she died,” said Monks, “after a lingering illness; and, on her death-bed, she bequeathed these secrets to me, together with her unquenchable and deadly hatred of all whom they involved—though she need not have left me that, for I had inherited it long before. She would not believe that the girl had destroyed herself, and the child too, but was filled with the impression that a male child had been born, and was alive. I swore to her, if ever it crossed my path, to hunt it down; never to let it rest; to pursue it with the bitterest and most unrelenting animosity; to vent upon it the hatred that I deeply felt, and to spit upon the empty vaunt of that insulting will by dragging it, if I could, to the very gallows-foot. She was right. He came in my way at last. I began well; and, but for babbling drabs, I would have finished as I began!”

text: The truth of the matter was, deep in the most private corners of his heart, Mr. Grimwig was very much inclined to acknowledge that Oliver’s looks and bearing were remarkably charming; but he had a fierce fondness for being contrary, a tendency sharpened that day by his discovery of a stray piece of orange peel. Having privately decided that no man could dictate to him whether a boy was handsome or not, he had made up his mind from the very start to contradict his friend. When Mr. Brownlow conceded that he could not yet give a satisfactory answer to any question about the boy’s background, and that he had put off looking into Oliver’s past until the boy was well enough to bear hearing the details, Mr. Grimwig cackled with ill-natured amusement. He then asked, with a sneer, if the housekeeper made a habit of counting the household silver each night; for if she did not wake up some bright, sunny morning to find a tablespoon or two missing, why, he would be perfectly willing to—and so on.
source_text: Now, the fact was, that in the inmost recesses of his own heart, Mr. Grimwig was strongly disposed to admit that Oliver’s appearance and manner were unusually prepossessing; but he had a strong appetite for contradiction, sharpened on this occasion by the finding of the orange-peel; and, inwardly determining that no man should dictate to him whether a boy was well-looking or not, he had resolved, from the first, to oppose his friend. When Mr. Brownlow admitted that on no one point of inquiry could he yet return a satisfactory answer; and that he had postponed any investigation into Oliver’s previous history until he thought the boy was strong enough to hear it; Mr. Grimwig chuckled maliciously. And he demanded, with a sneer, whether the housekeeper was in the habit of counting the plate at night; because if she didn’t find a table-spoon or two missing some sunshiny morning, why, he would be content to—and so forth.

text: This struck Mr. Sowerberry as delightfully funny, as well it ought to have, and he laughed on for quite some time without stopping. When he finally regained his composure, he said, “There now, Mr. Bumble, there’s no disputing that fact. Ever since the new feeding system was put in place, our coffins do run somewhat narrower and shallower than they used to. But we have to make some profit, Mr. Bumble. Properly seasoned timber is a costly commodity, sir, and all the iron handles are shipped up by canal all the way from Birmingham, too.”
source_text: Mr. Sowerberry was much tickled at this: as of course he ought to be; and laughed a long time without cessation. “Well, well, Mr. Bumble,” he said at length, “there’s no denying that, since the new system of feeding has come in, the coffins are something narrower and more shallow than they used to be; but we must have some profit, Mr. Bumble. Well-seasoned timber is an expensive article, sir; and all the iron handles come, by canal, from Birmingham.”

text: Right then, a sturdy older gentleman hobbled into the room, propping his weight on a thick walking stick, one leg noticeably lame. He wore a navy blue coat, striped waistcoat, pale nankeen cotton breeches paired with matching gaiters, and a wide-brimmed white hat with its edges turned up and lined with green. A tiny, tightly braided shirt ruffle peeked out over the top of his waistcoat, and below it, an extra-long steel watch chain swung loose, the only trinket on its end a single plain key. The ends of his white neck scarf were twisted tight into a ball roughly the size of an orange, and the wild, shifting array of contortions playing across his face was impossible to put into words. When he spoke, he had a habit of cocking his head to one side and glancing out from the corners of his eyes at the same time—a quirk that irresistibly made anyone looking at him think of a parrot. He locked into that exact pose the second he stepped through the door, held a tiny scrap of orange peel out straight at arm’s length, and barked in a gruff, dissatisfied tone,
source_text: At this moment, there walked into the room: supporting himself by a thick stick: a stout old gentleman, rather lame in one leg, who was dressed in a blue coat, striped waistcoat, nankeen breeches and gaiters, and a broad-brimmed white hat, with the sides turned up with green. A very small-plaited shirt frill stuck out from his waistcoat; and a very long steel watch-chain, with nothing but a key at the end, dangled loosely below it. The ends of his white neckerchief were twisted into a ball about the size of an orange; the variety of shapes into which his countenance was twisted, defy description. He had a manner of screwing his head on one side when he spoke; and of looking out of the corners of his eyes at the same time: which irresistibly reminded the beholder of a parrot. In this attitude, he fixed himself, the moment he made his appearance; and, holding out a small piece of orange-peel at arm’s length, exclaimed, in a growling, discontented voice,

text: “Right then,” Sikes said, lowering himself back into his seat, “if you’ll rustle up something to eat and drink while we wait, you’ll put a bit of heart back in the lot of us—me, most of all, anyway. Settle down by the fire, younker, and rest your legs; you’ve got to come out with us again tonight, though we won’t be going far.”
source_text: “Now,” said Sikes, as he resumed his seat, “if you’ll give us something to eat and drink while we’re waiting, you’ll put some heart in us; or in me, at all events. Sit down by the fire, younker, and rest yourself; for you’ll have to go out with us again tonight, though not very far off.”

text: And so Oliver remained there all that first day, and for the greater part of many days that came after, seeing not a single soul between the break of dawn and the deep of midnight, left to occupy the long, empty hours in conversation with his own thoughts. Those thoughts never strayed long from his kind old friends, and the poor opinion they must surely have formed of him by now, which made them bitter with sadness indeed.
source_text: And so Oliver remained all that day, and for the greater part of many subsequent days, seeing nobody, between early morning and midnight, and left during the long hours to commune with his own thoughts. Which, never failing to revert to his kind friends, and the opinion they must long ago have formed of him, were sad indeed.

text: “Pray think on it, madam,” said Elias, hot tears stinging the corners of his eyes no matter how hard he blinked them back. “Oh! only think how gentle and young she is, how she brings such light and ease to every soul about her. I know it—swear it, fully swear it—that for your sake, who are so good and kind yourself; and for her own; and for the sake of all she fills with such quiet happiness; she will not die. The heavens will never let her pass from this world so early in her days.”
source_text: “And consider, ma’am,” said Oliver, as the tears forced themselves into his eyes, despite of his efforts to the contrary. “Oh! consider how young and good she is, and what pleasure and comfort she gives to all about her. I am sure—certain—quite certain—that, for your sake, who are so good yourself; and for her own; and for the sake of all she makes so happy; she will not die. Heaven will never let her die so young.”

text: The scuffle, though fierce, was far too lopsided to drag on. Rook had already pinned his opponent flat, his braced knee digging into the man’s windpipe, when Hodge wrenched him backward, face tight with unmistakeable panic, and jabbed a trembling finger toward the front casement. Lantern light flickered up from the lane below, raised voices tangled in heated, urgent exchange, and the thud of rushing feet—what looked like dozens of them, endless in their stream—pounded across the rickety wooden bridge at the end of the road. A rider on horseback was clearly part of the mob, too; sharp, staccato clatters of hooves rang out against the cracked, lumpy cobblestones under the bridge. The glow of lanterns swelled brighter by the second, footfalls growing denser and louder as they surged toward the cottage. A thunderous banging shook the front door a heartbeat later, followed by the low, guttural roar of so many furious people that even the steadiest, most unflappable nerve would have frayed.
source_text: The contest, however, was too unequal to last long. Sikes had him down, and his knee was on his throat, when Crackit pulled him back with a look of alarm, and pointed to the window. There were lights gleaming below, voices in loud and earnest conversation, the tramp of hurried footsteps—endless they seemed in number—crossing the nearest wooden bridge. One man on horseback seemed to be among the crowd; for there was the noise of hoofs rattling on the uneven pavement. The gleam of lights increased; the footsteps came more thickly and noisily on. Then, came a loud knocking at the door, and then a hoarse murmur from such a multitude of angry voices as would have made the boldest quail.

text: He arrived at the scene just as the chaos peaked. Half-clothed figures darted frantically to and fro: some strained to drag spooked horses free of the stables, others herded cattle out of the yard and outbuildings, while more staggered out of the burning structure loaded with salvaged goods, weaving through a shower of falling sparks and tumbling red-hot support beams. Where doors and windows had stood barely an hour before, gaping openings now poured forth roiling, raging fire. Walls swayed before crumbling into the fiery chasm below, and molten lead and iron, searing white-hot, streamed down to splash against the ground. Women and children shrieked and wailed, while men called encouragement to one another in loud, rough shouts and cheers. The clanking of fire engine pumps, and the sharp sputter and hiss of water as it hit blazing wood, swelled the already deafening roar of the blaze. He shouted too, until his throat went raw, and fleeing both his memories and his own self, threw himself headlong into the thickest part of the crowd. All night he wove back and forth through the chaos: now manning the pumps, now racing through plumes of smoke and licks of flame, never pausing, always seeking out where the noise was loudest and the crowd most dense. He clambered up and down ladders, trod across building roofs, stepped over floorboards that quivered and buckled under his weight, ducked clear of falling bricks and rubble, turning up in every corner of the massive fire. By some charm he escaped entirely unharmed: not a scratch or bruise marked his skin, he felt no weariness and held no thought beyond the work at hand, until dawn broke at last, and all that remained of the blaze was curling smoke and blackened, gutted ruins.
source_text: He came upon the spot. There were half-dressed figures tearing to and fro, some endeavouring to drag the frightened horses from the stables, others driving the cattle from the yard and out-houses, and others coming laden from the burning pile, amidst a shower of falling sparks, and the tumbling down of red-hot beams. The apertures, where doors and windows stood an hour ago, disclosed a mass of raging fire; walls rocked and crumbled into the burning well; the molten lead and iron poured down, white hot, upon the ground. Women and children shrieked, and men encouraged each other with noisy shouts and cheers. The clanking of the engine-pumps, and the spirting and hissing of the water as it fell upon the blazing wood, added to the tremendous roar. He shouted, too, till he was hoarse; and flying from memory and himself, plunged into the thickest of the throng. Hither and thither he dived that night: now working at the pumps, and now hurrying through the smoke and flame, but never ceasing to engage himself wherever noise and men were thickest. Up and down the ladders, upon the roofs of buildings, over floors that quaked and trembled with his weight, under the lee of falling bricks and stones, in every part of that great fire was he; but he bore a charmed life, and had neither scratch nor bruise, nor weariness nor thought, till morning dawned again, and only smoke and blackened ruins remained.

text: “There we go,” Monks said, slamming the trapdoor shut so it dropped heavily back into its original position with a dull thud. “If the sea ever spits out all its dead like those books claim it will, it’s going to hoard every bit of its gold and silver for itself, and that garbage we stowed down there right along with it. We’ve got nothing left to discuss now. Might as well call this nice little get-together of ours over.”
source_text: “There!” said Monks, closing the trap-door, which fell heavily back into its former position. “If the sea ever gives up its dead, as books say it will, it will keep its gold and silver to itself, and that trash among it. We have nothing more to say, and may break up our pleasant party.”

text: “There’s more we know besides that,” the first speaker picked back up, “because a long time ago, she used to tell us all the time that the young mother had confided in her: convinced she would never get over what had happened, she’d been on her way to die beside the grave of her child’s father the very moment she fell ill.”
source_text: “And we know more than that,” resumed the first, “for she told us often, long ago, that the young mother had told her that, feeling she should never get over it, she was on her way, at the time that she was taken ill, to die near the grave of the father of the child.”

text: As that thought crossed his mind, the old man turned to pull the door shut behind him, and the soft scrape of the wood roused the dozing girl. She studied his cunning, shifty face closely as she asked him to recount the full story of Toby Crackit. When his recital ended, she slumped back into the posture she had held before, and said not a word. She shoved the candle away from her with clear impatience; once or twice she shifted restlessly, her movements fevered, and scuffed her feet against the floor, but she made no other sign of disturbance.
source_text: The old man turned to close the door, as he made this reflection; the noise thus occasioned, roused the girl. She eyed his crafty face narrowly, as she inquired to his recital of Toby Crackit’s story. When it was concluded, she sank into her former attitude, but spoke not a word. She pushed the candle impatiently away; and once or twice as she feverishly changed her position, shuffled her feet upon the ground; but this was all.

text: “Feels just like being snatched by the devil,” Sikes retorted. “There never was another man with a face as wretched as yours, save for your father, and I reckon he’s singeing that grizzled red beard of his over hell’s fires by now. Unless you came straight from the old devil himself with no father between you two at all—and I wouldn’t be the least bit surprised if that’s the truth.”
source_text: “Reminds me of being nabbed by the devil,” returned Sikes. “There never was another man with such a face as yours, unless it was your father, and I suppose _he_ is singeing his grizzled red beard by this time, unless you came straight from the old ’un without any father at all betwixt you; which I shouldn’t wonder at, a bit.”

text: Lord love her sweet soft heart, sir, when she’s lived as many hard years as I have, and borne thirteen bairns of her own, every last one of ’em laid in the churchyard but two, and those two right in the parish poorhouse with me, she’ll know better than to wail and carry on that way, bless her sweet soft heart! Think what it is to be a mother, there’s a dear little lamb, do.
source_text: “Lor bless her dear heart, when she has lived as long as I have, sir, and had thirteen children of her own, and all on ’em dead except two, and them in the wurkus with me, she’ll know better than to take on in that way, bless her dear heart! Think what it is to be a mother, there’s a dear young lamb, do.”

text: Mr. Bumble, who’d been eyeing the building with thoroughly glum, regretful looks, was clearly on the verge of voicing his doubts about whether pushing forward with their plan right then was even advisable, when he was stopped short by Monks showing up: the man pulled open a small door right near where they were standing, and beckoned them inside.
source_text: Mr. Bumble, who had eyed the building with very rueful looks, was apparently about to express some doubts relative to the advisability of proceeding any further with the enterprise just then, when he was prevented by the appearance of Monks: who opened a small door, near which they stood, and beckoned them inwards.

text: Little by little, the shutters swung open, window blinds were hauled up, and people began coming and going along the pavement. A few stopped to glance at Oliver for a moment or two, or twisted around to stare as they rushed by, but no one offered him any help, or even bothered to ask how he’d ended up there. He couldn’t bring himself to beg. And there he sat.
source_text: By degrees, the shutters were opened; the window-blinds were drawn up; and people began passing to and fro. Some few stopped to gaze at Oliver for a moment or two, or turned round to stare at him as they hurried by; but none relieved him, or troubled themselves to inquire how he came there. He had no heart to beg. And there he sat.

text: “Oh, it’s just the stuff I have to keep a little of on hand around the house, to mix into the dear little babies’ Daffy tonic when they’re feeling unwell, Mr. Bumble,” Mrs. Mann replied, as she pulled open the corner cupboard and lifted down a bottle and a glass. “It’s gin. I’m not going to lie to you, Mr. B. It’s gin.”
source_text: “Why, it’s what I’m obliged to keep a little of in the house, to put into the blessed infants’ Daffy, when they ain’t well, Mr. Bumble,” replied Mrs. Mann as she opened a corner cupboard, and took down a bottle and glass. “It’s gin. I’ll not deceive you, Mr. B. It’s gin.”

text: A dog that had remained concealed out of view up until that moment darted back and forth along the parapet, keening dismal, ragged howls. It coiled every muscle in its body to gather momentum for a leap, then launched itself straight for the dead man’s shoulders. It missed its mark entirely, flipping end over end as it plummeted into the ditch below. When its head struck a jagged stone at the bottom, its skull split open and its brains were dashed out.
source_text: A dog, which had lain concealed till now, ran backwards and forwards on the parapet with a dismal howl, and collecting himself for a spring, jumped for the dead man’s shoulders. Missing his aim, he fell into the ditch, turning completely over as he went; and striking his head against a stone, dashed out his brains.

text: “Of course, lad, of course,” said the gentleman in the white waistcoat, smiling gently and patting Noah’s head, even though Noah stood a full three inches taller than he did. “You’re a good boy—an awfully good boy, really.
