Here’s a penny for you. Bumble, grab your cane and head up to Sowerberry’s place, would you? Work out what the best call is for what needs doing there, and don’t go easy on him, Bumble.”
source_text: “Certainly, my boy; certainly,” said the gentleman in the white waistcoat: smiling benignly, and patting Noah’s head, which was about three inches higher than his own. “You’re a good boy—a very good boy. Here’s a penny for you. Bumble, just step up to Sowerberry’s with your cane, and see what’s best to be done. Don’t spare him, Bumble.”

text: That short order given to Charley Bates and the boy he’d just been tussling with was followed immediately, without a single sound. Not a peep gave away where the two had gone by the time the Dodger stepped down the stairs holding the lit lamp, a man in a rough, coarse work smock trailing close behind him. He darted a harried glance all around the room first, then tugged off the large swathe of fabric that had hidden the lower half of his face, baring his features: gaunt, grimy, crusted with days of uncut stubble, they belonged to none other than flash Toby Crackit.
source_text: This brief direction to Charley Bates, and his recent antagonist, was softly and immediately obeyed. There was no sound of their whereabout, when the Dodger descended the stairs, bearing the light in his hand, and followed by a man in a coarse smock-frock; who, after casting a hurried glance round the room, pulled off a large wrapper which had concealed the lower portion of his face, and disclosed: all haggard, unwashed, and unshorn: the features of flash Toby Crackit.

text: “I had no hold over him to drag him deeper into wrongdoing,” the Jew pressed on, his gaze fixed anxiously on his companion’s face. “He’d never taken part in any of our jobs yet. I had nothing to hold over him to cow him into line, and we both know we always need that sort of leverage right at the start, or all our efforts count for nothing. What was I meant to do? Send him out on the pull with the Dodger and Charley? We had more than enough of that mess at the beginning, my dear— I was quaking for all our sakes.”
source_text: “I had no hold upon him to make him worse,” pursued the Jew, anxiously watching the countenance of his companion. “His hand was not in. I had nothing to frighten him with; which we always must have in the beginning, or we labour in vain. What could I do? Send him out with the Dodger and Charley? We had enough of that, at first, my dear; I trembled for us all.”

text: “Oh, you’re such a strange little thing!” Charlotte exclaimed, bursting into a loud, hearty laugh that Noah quickly joined in on. Once their amusement faded just enough for them to focus again, the pair cast sharp, scornful looks at poor Oliver Twist, who sat huddled and shivering on the wooden box in the coldest nook of the room, eating the stale, dry scraps they had specifically reserved just for him.
source_text: “Oh, you queer soul!” said Charlotte, bursting into a hearty laugh, in which she was joined by Noah; after which they both looked scornfully at poor Oliver Twist, as he sat shivering on the box in the coldest corner of the room, and ate the stale pieces which had been specially reserved for him.

text: After a long stretch of unbroken quiet, a thin, pained whimper cut through the prevailing stillness, and the boy jolted awake. His left arm, crudely wrapped in a shawl, dangled heavy and useless at his side, the bandage soaked completely through with blood. He was so weak he could barely push himself up into a sitting position; once he’d managed the effort, he glanced dazedly around for help, groaning with sharp, throbbing pain. Every joint trembled with cold and total exhaustion, and he strained to pull himself upright, only to shudder violently from head to foot and collapse fully prone onto the ground.
source_text: At length, a low cry of pain broke the stillness that prevailed; and uttering it, the boy awoke. His left arm, rudely bandaged in a shawl, hung heavy and useless at his side; the bandage was saturated with blood. He was so weak, that he could scarcely raise himself into a sitting posture; when he had done so, he looked feebly round for help, and groaned with pain. Trembling in every joint, from cold and exhaustion, he made an effort to stand upright; but, shuddering from head to foot, fell prostrate on the ground.

text: Rose Maylie found herself caught in a truly uncommon, agonizing predicament. Though she burned with fierce, unrelenting curiosity to pierce the thick veil of mystery shrouding Oliver’s history, she could not bring herself to violate the sacred trust the despairing woman she had just spoken with had placed in her, trusting her as a guileless, innocent young girl. The woman’s words and quiet, wretched bearing had touched Rose to her core; tangled together with her deep, devoted love for her young charge, and nearly equal in its unshakable sincerity and fervor, was her tender, earnest wish to draw the outcast back to remorse and the warmth of renewed hope.
source_text: Her situation was, indeed, one of no common trial and difficulty. While she felt the most eager and burning desire to penetrate the mystery in which Oliver’s history was enveloped, she could not but hold sacred the confidence which the miserable woman with whom she had just conversed, had reposed in her, as a young and guileless girl. Her words and manner had touched Rose Maylie’s heart; and, mingled with her love for her young charge, and scarcely less intense in its truth and fervour, was her fond wish to win the outcast back to repentance and hope.

text: “Quiet,” the girl breathed, bending low over him, her finger prodding toward the door as her gaze swept warily around the dim room. “You can’t wriggle out of this on your own right now. I’ve fought tooth and nail to help you escape, but every attempt fell flat. You’re boxed in on every side. If you ever stand a chance of getting loose from this place, now is not the moment to make a move.”
source_text: “Hush!” said the girl, stooping over him, and pointing to the door as she looked cautiously round. “You can’t help yourself. I have tried hard for you, but all to no purpose. You are hedged round and round. If ever you are to get loose from here, this is not the time.”

text: Mr. Bumble, though, was far too shrewd to miss the opportunity laid out in front of him: a perfect chance to profit off a confidential secret his wife held close to her chest. He had no trouble recalling the night old Sally died, either—events from earlier that same day had burned the memory into his mind, as that was the very occasion he’d proposed to Mrs. Corney. Though his wife had never confided the full details of the revelation she’d been the sole witness to, he’d picked up enough scattered clues over time to know it tied back to something that happened while the old woman was working as a workhouse nurse, caring for Oliver Twist’s young mother in her final hours. Snapping that context to the front of his mind immediately, he adopted a heavy, mysterious air as he spoke to the stranger: he told him one woman had been shut up alone with the sharp-tongued old hag just before she passed, and that he had good reason to believe that woman could shed light on exactly what the stranger was asking after.
source_text: But Mr. Bumble was cunning enough; and he at once saw that an opportunity was opened, for the lucrative disposal of some secret in the possession of his better half. He well remembered the night of old Sally’s death, which the occurrences of that day had given him good reason to recollect, as the occasion on which he had proposed to Mrs. Corney; and although that lady had never confided to him the disclosure of which she had been the solitary witness, he had heard enough to know that it related to something that had occurred in the old woman’s attendance, as workhouse nurse, upon the young mother of Oliver Twist. Hastily calling this circumstance to mind, he informed the stranger, with an air of mystery, that one woman had been closeted with the old harridan shortly before she died; and that she could, as he had reason to believe, throw some light on the subject of his inquiry.

text: All the while, Oliver Twist had not the faintest inkling he was mere steps away from the jovial old gentleman, and was making his steady way toward the bookstall. When he reached Clerkenwell, he veered accidentally down a side lane that was not quite on his intended route; he only caught his mistake when he was already halfway along it, but since he knew the street would eventually lead in the correct direction, he saw no point in doubling back. So he pressed onward at as brisk a pace as he could manage, the stack of books tucked firmly under his arm.
source_text: Meanwhile, Oliver Twist, little dreaming that he was within so very short a distance of the merry old gentleman, was on his way to the book-stall. When he got into Clerkenwell, he accidently turned down a by-street which was not exactly in his way; but not discovering his mistake until he had got half-way down it, and knowing it must lead in the right direction, he did not think it worth while to turn back; and so marched on, as quickly as he could, with the books under his arm.

text: Mr. Grimes stared at Toby’s trembling, wretched face for several long seconds, openly taken aback. He cleared his throat three or four times in a rough, thick rasp, grumbled something offhand about “that nagging chest tickle” before ordering Toby to wipe his tears and be a well-behaved lad. Then he curled his thick fingers around the boy’s hand once more, and tramped onward beside him without a word.
source_text: Mr. Bumble regarded Oliver’s piteous and helpless look, with some astonishment, for a few seconds; hemmed three or four times in a husky manner; and after muttering something about “that troublesome cough,” bade Oliver dry his eyes and be a good boy. Then once more taking his hand, he walked on with him in silence.

text: He flipped idly through the book’s frayed pages at first, his gaze wandering, until a single passage snagged his attention and he leaned forward, utterly absorbed by the tattered volume. It was a chronicle of the lives and court trials of infamous criminals, its leaves smudged and worn thin from years of rough handling. Here he read of atrocities that curdled the blood in his veins: secret slayings committed along remote, overgrown roads, corpses stuffed into deep quarries and abandoned wells that, no matter how far down the bodies were hidden, gave up their dead after long, forgotten years, the sight driving the killers mad with terror until they screamed their full confessions, begging for the gallows to end their agony. Here too he read of men who, lying awake in their beds in the dead hush of midnight, claimed they had been goaded and led astray by their own dark, festering thoughts into acts of butchery so horrific that just the thought of it made the skin prickle and the limbs tremble. The terrible descriptions felt so vivid and real that the sallow, stained pages seemed to flush crimson with gore before his eyes, and the words printed on them sounded in his ears as if they were whispered in low, hollow murmurs by the spirits of the long-dead.
source_text: He turned over the leaves. Carelessly at first; but, lighting on a passage which attracted his attention, he soon became intent upon the volume. It was a history of the lives and trials of great criminals; and the pages were soiled and thumbed with use. Here, he read of dreadful crimes that made the blood run cold; of secret murders that had been committed by the lonely wayside; of bodies hidden from the eye of man in deep pits and wells: which would not keep them down, deep as they were, but had yielded them up at last, after many years, and so maddened the murderers with the sight, that in their horror they had confessed their guilt, and yelled for the gibbet to end their agony. Here, too, he read of men who, lying in their beds at dead of night, had been tempted (so they said) and led on, by their own bad thoughts, to such dreadful bloodshed as it made the flesh creep, and the limbs quail, to think of. The terrible descriptions were so real and vivid, that the sallow pages seemed to turn red with gore; and the words upon them, to be sounded in his ears, as if they were whispered, in hollow murmurs, by the spirits of the dead.

text: Mr. Gamfield cast a sly, knowing glance at the faces circled around the table, and when he noticed smiles on every last one of them, he gradually broke into a grin of his own. The deal was settled right then. Mr. Bumble was immediately instructed to bring Oliver Twist and his indenture papers before the magistrate that very afternoon, so the documents could be signed and formally approved.
source_text: Mr. Gamfield gave an arch look at the faces round the table, and, observing a smile on all of them, gradually broke into a smile himself. The bargain was made. Mr. Bumble, was at once instructed that Oliver Twist and his indentures were to be conveyed before the magistrate, for signature and approval, that very afternoon.

text: They exchanged very few words along the way. Oliver was caught in a flurry of restlessness and unresolvable doubt that left him unable to collect his thoughts, barely even able to speak at all, and that same frayed unease seemed to weigh just as heavily on his two female traveling companions, if not more. Mr. Brownlow had already carefully walked all three of them through the full substance of the confessions Monks had been pressured into making, and while they understood their present journey was meant to see through the final steps of the work that had already been so successfully launched, the whole affair was still shrouded in enough ambiguity and secrecy to leave them caught in a state of nearly unbearable, taut suspense.
source_text: They had not talked much upon the way; for Oliver was in a flutter of agitation and uncertainty which deprived him of the power of collecting his thoughts, and almost of speech, and appeared to have scarcely less effect on his companions, who shared it, in at least an equal degree. He and the two ladies had been very carefully made acquainted by Mr. Brownlow with the nature of the admissions which had been forced from Monks; and although they knew that the object of their present journey was to complete the work which had been so well begun, still the whole matter was enveloped in enough of doubt and mystery to leave them in endurance of the most intense suspense.

text: Oliver covered twenty miles on foot that day, and the entire stretch he ate nothing but dry, crumbly bread crusts, washed down with a few sips of water he’d begged from cottage doors strung along the roadside. When dark fell, he slipped into a grassy meadow, huddled tight against the side of a hayrick, and resolved to stay curled there until morning came. He was jittery at first: the wind moaned drearily across the empty fields, he was shivering with cold and sharp with hunger, and more completely alone than he had ever felt in his life. The long walk had left him thoroughly worn out, though, and he fell asleep almost right away, all his troubles slipping from his mind as he drifted off.
source_text: Oliver walked twenty miles that day; and all that time tasted nothing but the crust of dry bread, and a few draughts of water, which he begged at the cottage-doors by the road-side. When the night came, he turned into a meadow; and, creeping close under a hay-rick, determined to lie there, till morning. He felt frightened at first, for the wind moaned dismally over the empty fields, and he was cold and hungry, and more alone than he had ever felt before. Being very tired with his walk, however, he soon fell asleep and forgot his troubles.

text: After what felt like an eternity, when his knuckles and palms were rubbed raw from slamming his fists against the heavy door and unyielding walls, two men stepped into view: one carried a guttering candle, which he wedged tightly into an iron candlestick fixed to the wall, the other hauled in a thin mattress to sleep on through the night, for the prisoner would not be left unattended for even a moment going forward.
source_text: At length, when his hands were raw with beating against the heavy door and walls, two men appeared: one bearing a candle, which he thrust into an iron candlestick fixed against the wall: the other dragging in a mattress on which to pass the night; for the prisoner was to be left alone no more.

text: Yet for weeks on end, Elias registered nothing of the gentle care his new companions lavished on him. The sun climbed the sky and dipped below the horizon, repeated the cycle again, and again, a dozen more times after that; still the boy lay sprawled across his restlessly lumpy pallet, wasting away under the parching, devouring burn of the fever that had seized him. No maggot eats through a corpse more steadily than this slow, creeping flame gnaws at a living, breathing body.
source_text: But, for many days, Oliver remained insensible to all the goodness of his new friends. The sun rose and sank, and rose and sank again, and many times after that; and still the boy lay stretched on his uneasy bed, dwindling away beneath the dry and wasting heat of fever. The worm does not work more surely on the dead body, than does this slow creeping fire upon the living frame.

text: Not long after that, Oliver drifted back off to sleep. When he woke again, it was almost twelve o’clock. The gentle elderly woman who’d been with him wished him a soft good night a few minutes later, and left him in the care of a plump older woman who’d just arrived, carrying a small bundle holding a tiny prayer book and an oversized nightcap. She pulled the nightcap over her own head and set the prayer book down on the table, told Oliver she’d come to sit up through the night with him, then dragged her chair right up to the fireplace and slipped into a pattern of quick little naps. These dozes were interrupted every few minutes by jolts forward as she nodded off, plus assorted grunts, moans and faint choking sounds. None of these little mishaps had any worse consequences, though: each time they happened, she’d just scrub at her nose very roughly, then drift right back to sleep.
source_text: Oliver dozed off again, soon after this; when he awoke, it was nearly twelve o’clock. The old lady tenderly bade him good-night shortly afterwards, and left him in charge of a fat old woman who had just come: bringing with her, in a little bundle, a small Prayer Book and a large nightcap. Putting the latter on her head and the former on the table, the old woman, after telling Oliver that she had come to sit up with him, drew her chair close to the fire and went off into a series of short naps, chequered at frequent intervals with sundry tumblings forward, and divers moans and chokings. These, however, had no worse effect than causing her to rub her nose very hard, and then fall asleep again.

text: He hunkered low over a dead, cold hearth, swaddled in a frayed, tattered old coverlet, his face turned toward a guttering candle propped on the small table at his elbow. His right hand was lifted to his mouth; lost entirely in his own thoughts as he gnawed at his overgrown, blackened nails, he revealed between his mostly toothless gums a handful of sharp, jagged fangs that belonged far more properly to a stray dog or a scavenging rat.
source_text: He sat crouching over a cold hearth, wrapped in an old torn coverlet, with his face turned towards a wasting candle that stood upon a table by his side. His right hand was raised to his lips, and as, absorbed in thought, he bit his long black nails, he disclosed among his toothless gums a few such fangs as should have been a dog’s or rat’s.

text: Toby darted a dubious glance at Mr. Hale; Mr. Hale stared back at Toby with the same wavering uncertainty. The local constable pressed a palm behind his ear, straining to catch the coming reply; the two house maids and the wandering tinker craned their necks forward, holding their breath to listen. The physician cast a sharp, sweeping look across the small crowd, when suddenly a sharp ring sounded from the gate, and the low rumble of approaching wheels hit their ears at the very same moment.
source_text: Brittles looked doubtfully at Mr. Giles; Mr. Giles looked doubtfully at Brittles; the constable put his hand behind his ear, to catch the reply; the two women and the tinker leaned forward to listen; the doctor glanced keenly round; when a ring was heard at the gate, and at the same moment, the sound of wheels.

text: He addressed a courier boy nodding off under the entry arch, who, after grasping the gist of his request, sent him to speak to the stable groom; the groom, after listening to his full query a second time, directed him to the inn’s landlord, a lanky gentleman wearing a navy cravat, a crisp white hat, taupe breeches, and boots with tops cut to the same neutral hue, propped against a water pump beside the stable threshold, working a silver toothpick between his teeth.
source_text: He spoke to a postboy who was dozing under the gateway; and who, after hearing what he wanted, referred him to the ostler; who after hearing all he had to say again, referred him to the landlord; who was a tall gentleman in a blue neckcloth, a white hat, drab breeches, and boots with tops to match, leaning against a pump by the stable-door, picking his teeth with a silver toothpick.

text: On Jacob’s Island, the warehouses stand roofless and hollowed out. Their walls are slowly crumbling away, gaps where windows used to be gape wide, door frames sag half-fallen into the streets, and soot-blackened chimneys give off no wisp of smoke at all. Thirty or forty years ago, before financial losses and drawn-out chancery suits gutted the area, it had been a thriving, busy neighborhood. Now, it is truly a desolate, abandoned island on paper. None of the homes have registered owners; people brave enough to force their way past the ruined entrances break in, settle down, and spend the rest of their lives there, dying on the premises without fanfare. Anyone who seeks out Jacob’s Island as a refuge must either have very strong reasons to keep their residence a secret, or be so utterly destitute that they have no other options left.
source_text: In Jacob’s Island, the warehouses are roofless and empty; the walls are crumbling down; the windows are windows no more; the doors are falling into the streets; the chimneys are blackened, but they yield no smoke. Thirty or forty years ago, before losses and chancery suits came upon it, it was a thriving place; but now it is a desolate island indeed. The houses have no owners; they are broken open, and entered upon by those who have the courage; and there they live, and there they die. They must have powerful motives for a secret residence, or be reduced to a destitute condition indeed, who seek a refuge in Jacob’s Island.

text: “Jesse,” the girl sobbed, straining to press her forehead to his chest, “the gentle old gentleman and that warm, kind lady I spoke with tonight told me of a home across the sea, where I can live out the rest of my days in quiet, unbroken peace. Let me seek them out again, fall to my knees before them if I must, and beg them to show that same mercy and generosity to you too. Then we can both flee this dreadful place, go our separate ways to lead better lives, and put all the ugliness of our past behind us—we will only ever call it to mind in our prayers, and we will never see one another again. It is never too late to repent. They said that to me, and I feel it deep in my soul now, but we need time—just a little, small bit of time!”
source_text: “Bill,” cried the girl, striving to lay her head upon his breast, “the gentleman and that dear lady, told me tonight of a home in some foreign country where I could end my days in solitude and peace. Let me see them again, and beg them, on my knees, to show the same mercy and goodness to you; and let us both leave this dreadful place, and far apart lead better lives, and forget how we have lived, except in prayers, and never see each other more. It is never too late to repent. They told me so—I feel it now—but we must have time—a little, little time!”

text: “The gallows,” Fagin went on, “the gallows, my dear, is one hideous, unforgiving signpost. It points to a harsh, abrupt dead-end turn that’s cut short the path of so many bold lads out making their way on the wide open highway. Your number one priority right now is to stick to the easy, low-risk road, and keep that dreadful thing as far out of your orbit as you possibly can.”
source_text: “The gallows,” continued Fagin, “the gallows, my dear, is an ugly finger-post, which points out a very short and sharp turning that has stopped many a bold fellow’s career on the broad highway. To keep in the easy road, and keep it at a distance, is object number one with you.”

text: There was no question the dog had heard—Mr. Sikes had spoken in the absolute harshest register of his already gruff, deeply unkind voice. But for some unfathomable reason, the animal seemed to take sharp objection to the prospect of having his throat slit, so he stayed rooted exactly where he stood, growling far more fiercely than he had only moments before. All the while, he clamped the end of the fire poker between his teeth, worrying at it like a feral, cornered beast.
source_text: The dog no doubt heard; because Mr. Sikes spoke in the very harshest key of a very harsh voice; but, appearing to entertain some unaccountable objection to having his throat cut, he remained where he was, and growled more fiercely than before: at the same time grasping the end of the poker between his teeth, and biting at it like a wild beast.

text: “I only ended up here because I was seized by the most agonizing, gut-deep terror I’ve ever known,” the young man said. “I was terrified I’d lose the only beloved person every single one of my wishes and hopes is anchored to. You’d been at death’s door, trembling between this world and whatever comes after. We all know that when the young, beautiful, and kind fall ill, their pure, unspoiled spirits slowly turn toward that bright, permanent home of eternal rest. We know, heaven help us, that the best and loveliest among us far too often fade away when they’re still in the full bloom of life.”
source_text: “I was brought here, by the most dreadful and agonising of all apprehensions,” said the young man; “the fear of losing the one dear being on whom my every wish and hope are fixed. You had been dying; trembling between earth and heaven. We know that when the young, the beautiful, and good, are visited with sickness, their pure spirits insensibly turn towards their bright home of lasting rest; we know, Heaven help us! that the best and fairest of our kind, too often fade in blooming.”

text: Jangling with harness bells and clattering over rutted ground, the vehicle wound along the road, nearly swallowed whole by the thick cloud of dust churning up in its wake. As it pulled farther away, its clamor faded to total silence, only its headlong speed still perceptible to the watching eye. It vanished entirely for long stretches, then blinked back into view as gaps between roadside obstructions or gentler curves in the winding track permitted. It was not until even the faint, hazy smudge of its dust cloud had vanished entirely from the horizon that the group of gazers finally turned and dispersed.
source_text: Jingling and clattering, till distance rendered its noise inaudible, and its rapid progress only perceptible to the eye, the vehicle wound its way along the road, almost hidden in a cloud of dust: now wholly disappearing, and now becoming visible again, as intervening objects, or the intricacies of the way, permitted. It was not until even the dusty cloud was no longer to be seen, that the gazers dispersed.

text: Crowley, who still answered exclusively to the false alias he had long hidden behind, took his cut of the spoils and retreated to a remote corner of the Americas. He burned through every last penny in mere weeks, slipping effortlessly back into the dishonest, predatory patterns of his youth. After serving a lengthy prison sentence for a fresh spree of swindling and grifting, he finally succumbed to a severe flare-up of the chronic ailment that had hounded him for decades, dying alone in his cell. Thousands of miles from the cobblestone streets they once prowled, the last remaining core members of his accomplice Silas’s criminal gang met equally ignominious ends, far from any home they had ever known.
source_text: Monks, still bearing that assumed name, retired with his portion to a distant part of the New World; where, having quickly squandered it, he once more fell into his old courses, and, after undergoing a long confinement for some fresh act of fraud and knavery, at length sunk under an attack of his old disorder, and died in prison. As far from home, died the chief remaining members of his friend Fagin’s gang.

text: The days of Oliver’s recovery were unreservedly happy. Every part of the house was quiet, neat, and perfectly ordered; everyone around him was so kind and gentle that, after a lifetime spent amid constant noise and turmoil, the place felt like nothing short of heaven. As soon as he was strong enough to dress himself properly, Mr. Brownlow arranged for a full new suit, a new cap, and a new pair of shoes to be made for him. When Oliver was told he could do whatever he liked with his old clothes, he gave them to a servant who had been particularly good to him, and asked her to sell them to the Jewish peddler who frequented the area and keep the money for herself. She did so gladly, and as Oliver watched from the parlor window while the peddler rolled the clothes into his bag and walked off, he felt thoroughly delighted to know they were gone for good, and there was no possible chance he would ever have to wear them again. To tell the truth, those clothes were little more than tattered rags, and Oliver had never owned a new suit in his entire life.
source_text: They were happy days, those of Oliver’s recovery. Everything was so quiet, and neat, and orderly; everybody so kind and gentle; that after the noise and turbulence in the midst of which he had always lived, it seemed like Heaven itself. He was no sooner strong enough to put his clothes on, properly, than Mr. Brownlow caused a complete new suit, and a new cap, and a new pair of shoes, to be provided for him. As Oliver was told that he might do what he liked with the old clothes, he gave them to a servant who had been very kind to him, and asked her to sell them to a Jew, and keep the money for herself. This she very readily did; and, as Oliver looked out of the parlour window, and saw the Jew roll them up in his bag and walk away, he felt quite delighted to think that they were safely gone, and that there was now no possible danger of his ever being able to wear them again. They were sad rags, to tell the truth; and Oliver had never had a new suit before.

text: “She didn’t say another word,” the woman said, outwardly completely unmoved by the strange man’s violent outburst (a reaction Mr. Bumble was very far from sharing); “she instead grabbed my gown roughly with one half-curled hand, her grip so tight the fabric pulled. When I realized she was dead and pried her grip loose by force, I found she was clutching a grimy, crumpled scrap of paper clamped tight in that fist.”
source_text: “She didn’t utter another word,” said the woman, to all appearance unmoved (as Mr. Bumble was very far from being) by the strange man’s violence; “but she clutched my gown, violently, with one hand, which was partly closed; and when I saw that she was dead, and so removed the hand by force, I found it clasped a scrap of dirty paper.”

text: “Hah!” the young woman snapped again. “Cast your eyes straight ahead, madam. Gaze on that inky, glass-dark water. How often have you read of girls just like me, who hurl themselves into the tide, and leave not a single living soul to care for them, or mourn their passing? It may be years down the line, or it may be only a few short months, but that is the end I am bound for, one way or another.”
source_text: “What!” repeated the girl. “Look before you, lady. Look at that dark water. How many times do you read of such as I who spring into the tide, and leave no living thing, to care for, or bewail them. It may be years hence, or it may be only months, but I shall come to that at last.”

text: As the conversation played out, Oliver flicked his gaze back and forth between the two speakers, stupefied as though in a trance, barely able to grasp what was unfolding around him. The second Bill Sikes stopped speaking, however, he bolted abruptly to his feet, tore wildly out of the room, and shrieked for help so loud his cries bounced off the walls of the bare, aging house and rang all the way up to its roof.
source_text: Oliver had looked from one to the other, while these words were being spoken, as if he were bewildered, and could scarecely understand what passed; but when Bill Sikes concluded, he jumped suddenly to his feet, and tore wildly from the room: uttering shrieks for help, which made the bare old house echo to the roof.

text: “Mr. Grimes! Mr. Grimes!” shouted Tobias, his dismay perfectly counterfeited, his voice so loud and frayed with manufactured panic that it not only reached Mr. Grimes himself, who was lingering just around the corner, but jolted him so thoroughly he bolted into the courtyard without his tall ceremonial tricorn—an exceedingly odd and notable detail, as it proves that even a parish beadle, driven by a sharp, unanticipated impulse, can suffer a fleeting lapse of self-possession, and forget entirely the weight of his own personal dignity.
source_text: “Mr. Bumble! Mr. Bumble!” cried Noah, with well-affected dismay, and in tones so loud and agitated, that they not only caught the ear of Mr. Bumble himself, who happened to be hard by, but alarmed him so much that he rushed into the yard without his cocked hat,—which is a very curious and remarkable circumstance, as showing that even a beadle, acted upon a sudden and powerful impulse, may be afflicted with a momentary visitation of loss of self-possession, and forgetfulness of personal dignity.

text: Oliver obeyed instantly, swiping the back of his free hand briskly across his eyes, but a tear still lingered when he lifted his face to look at the man guiding him. When Mr. Bumble fixed him with a stern, unforgiving stare, that lone tear slipped down his cheek. Another followed, then a third. The child fought hard to stem the flow, but his effort was fruitless. He wrenched his other hand from Mr. Bumble’s grip, covering his face with both palms, and wept until tears spilled out between his chin and his thin, bony fingers.
source_text: Although Oliver did as he was desired, at once; and passed the back of his unoccupied hand briskly across his eyes, he left a tear in them when he looked up at his conductor. As Mr. Bumble gazed sternly upon him, it rolled down his cheek. It was followed by another, and another. The child made a strong effort, but it was an unsuccessful one. Withdrawing his other hand from Mr. Bumble’s he covered his face with both; and wept until the tears sprung out from between his chin and bony fingers.

text: Mr. Bumble had been tasked with carrying out a round of preliminary enquiries, in the hopes of locating some sea captain in need of a friendless cabin boy, and was just making his way back to the workhouse to report the outcome of his errand when he crossed paths at the gate with none other than Mr. Sowerberry, the parish undertaker.
source_text: Mr. Bumble had been despatched to make various preliminary inquiries, with the view of finding out some captain or other who wanted a cabin-boy without any friends; and was returning to the workhouse to communicate the result of his mission; when he encountered at the gate, no less a person than Mr. Sowerberry, the parochial undertaker.

text: “Then spare my life for the love of Heaven, just as I spared yours,” the girl begged, clinging fast to his frame. “Bill, dear Bill, you cannot have the cruelty to kill me. Oh! Think of all I have given up, this single night alone, for you. You *shall* have time to think things over, and spare yourself this terrible crime; I will not loosen my hold, you cannot throw me off. Bill, Bill, for dear God’s sake, for your own sake, for mine, stop before you spill my blood! I have been true to you, upon my guilty soul I have!”
source_text: “Then spare my life for the love of Heaven, as I spared yours,” rejoined the girl, clinging to him. “Bill, dear Bill, you cannot have the heart to kill me. Oh! think of all I have given up, only this one night, for you. You _shall_ have time to think, and save yourself this crime; I will not loose my hold, you cannot throw me off. Bill, Bill, for dear God’s sake, for your own, for mine, stop before you spill my blood! I have been true to you, upon my guilty soul I have!”

text: “Me, madam?” the girl retorted, voice rough with unapologetic candor. “I’m that disreputable wretch you’ve no doubt heard tales of, the one who lives among thieves. From the very first moment I can remember opening my eyes and making sense of the London streets around me, I’ve never known a better life, or heard a kinder word, than what those people have given me—so help me God. Don’t trouble yourself to hide that you want to shrink away from me, either. I’m younger than I look, I’ll have you know, but I’m well used to that sort of reaction. Even the poorest, raggedest women step back and out of my way when I walk down the crowded pavement.”
source_text: “I, lady!” replied the girl. “I am the infamous creature you have heard of, that lives among the thieves, and that never from the first moment I can recollect my eyes and senses opening on London streets have known any better life, or kinder words than they have given me, so help me God! Do not mind shrinking openly from me, lady. I am younger than you would think, to look at me, but I am well used to it. The poorest women fall back, as I make my way along the crowded pavement.”

text: The very next day, a new round of searches launched, and local inquiries picked back up, though they yielded no better results than before. The day after that, Oliver and Mr. Maylie traveled to the nearby market town, holding out hope they might spot the men in question or pick up even a scrap of news about them there, but that effort ended up just as useless. After a few more days, the whole affair began to fade from public memory, as most such incidents do: when curiosity has no new details to feed on, it burns out all on its own.
source_text: On the next day, fresh search was made, and the inquiries renewed; but with no better success. On the day following, Oliver and Mr. Maylie repaired to the market-town, in the hope of seeing or hearing something of the men there; but this effort was equally fruitless. After a few days, the affair began to be forgotten, as most affairs are, when wonder, having no fresh food to support it, dies away of itself.

text: “Nah,” Fagin replied, “not all that bad. He was booked for attempted pickpocketing, and they turned up a silver snuff box on him—his own, though, dear, his very own, ‘cause he took snuff himself and was really fond of the stuff. They kept him locked up till today, since they thought they knew who the box really belonged to. That boy was worth fifty of those boxes, easy. I’d pay the full price for that many just to get him back. You ought to have known the Dodger, love; you really should have known the Dodger.”
source_text: “No,” replied Fagin, “not very. He was charged with attempting to pick a pocket, and they found a silver snuff-box on him,—his own, my dear, his own, for he took snuff himself, and was very fond of it. They remanded him till today, for they thought they knew the owner. Ah! he was worth fifty boxes, and I’d give the price of as many to have him back. You should have known the Dodger, my dear; you should have known the Dodger.”

text: Oliver had no idea what had sparked that abrupt, loud exclamation; the startled jolt it sent through him was more than his fragile strength could withstand, and he fainted dead away. This passing weakness on his part, however, gives the tale a welcome chance to ease the reader out of their mounting suspense, for the sake of the Merry Old Gentleman’s two young wards, and to set down—
source_text: Oliver knew not the cause of this sudden exclamation; for, not being strong enough to bear the start it gave him, he fainted away. A weakness on his part, which affords the narrative an opportunity of relieving the reader from suspense, in behalf of the two young pupils of the Merry Old Gentleman; and of recording—

text: London! That sprawling, endless city! No one could ever track him down there, not even Mr. Bumble himself. He had often heard the elderly men in the workhouse say, too, that no plucky lad need ever go hungry in London, that the vast metropolis held all sorts of ways to get by that people raised in the countryside could never even fathom. It was exactly the place for a homeless boy, one who would surely die out on the roads if no one stopped to help him. As these thoughts ran through his mind, he leaped to his feet and kept moving forward.
source_text: London!—that great place!—nobody—not even Mr. Bumble—could ever find him there! He had often heard the old men in the workhouse, too, say that no lad of spirit need want in London; and that there were ways of living in that vast city, which those who had been bred up in country parts had no idea of. It was the very place for a homeless boy, who must die in the streets unless some one helped him. As these things passed through his thoughts, he jumped upon his feet, and again walked forward.

text: “You are looking squarely at me now, are you not?” said Voss, rising to his feet with unflinching boldness, “What of it? Fraud and robbery are lofty, damning words—you imagine you are justified in hurling them merely because some scrawny young urchin bears a passing likeness to a careless daub of a dead man’s sibling? You do not even know if that maudlin, soft-hearted pair ever had a child at all; you have not a single scrap of proof for that claim.”
source_text: “And now you do see me,” said Monks, rising boldly, “what then? Fraud and robbery are high-sounding words—justified, you think, by a fancied resemblance in some young imp to an idle daub of a dead man’s Brother! You don’t even know that a child was born of this maudlin pair; you don’t even know that.”

text: He made his way up to the house. It was still so early that none of the household’s residents showed the slightest sign of stirring. Oliver came to a stop and peered into the garden. A small boy was pulling weeds from one of the tiny planting beds, and when he caught sight of Oliver’s figure, he lifted his pale face, revealing the features of one of Oliver’s old companions. A soft rush of gladness washed over Oliver at getting to see him before he left: though the boy was a few years younger than him, he had once been his dearest little friend and playmate. More times than they could keep track of, the two of them had been beaten, half-starved, and shut up together in the same cramped, cold space.
source_text: He reached the house. There was no appearance of its inmates stirring at that early hour. Oliver stopped, and peeped into the garden. A child was weeding one of the little beds; as he stopped, he raised his pale face and disclosed the features of one of his former companions. Oliver felt glad to see him, before he went; for, though younger than himself, he had been his little friend and playmate. They had been beaten, and starved, and shut up together, many and many a time.

text: When young Oliver was brought before the assembled “gentlemen” that evening, he received word that he would be dispatched that very night to serve as an all-around house lad for a local coffin-maker. He was further warned that if he complained about his lot at all, or ever set foot back in the parish for any reason, he would be shipped out to sea, where he would either drown or be struck over the head and killed, as chance would have it. He displayed such a striking lack of emotion at the news that the men unanimously pronounced him a hardened young rascal, and ordered Mr. Bumble to escort him away immediately.
source_text: When little Oliver was taken before “the gentlemen” that evening; and informed that he was to go, that night, as general house-lad to a coffin-maker’s; and that if he complained of his situation, or ever came back to the parish again, he would be sent to sea, there to be drowned, or knocked on the head, as the case might be, he evinced so little emotion, that they by common consent pronounced him a hardened young rascal, and ordered Mr. Bumble to remove him forthwith.

text: “How dare you press me to go through with this, young man?” Mr. Brownlow retorted, facing him down with a steady, unwavering stare. “Are you so far lost to sense that you think you can walk out of this house? Let go of him at once. There, sir. You are free to go, and we are free to follow right behind. But I warn you, by every belief and vow I hold most solemn and sacred, the very second you cross the threshold you will be taken into custody on charges of fraud and robbery. I am resolute and will not be moved. If you are equally set on this course, whatever harm befalls you will be entirely your own fault.”
source_text: “How dare you urge me to it, young man?” replied Mr. Brownlow, confronting him with a steady look. “Are you mad enough to leave this house? Unhand him. There, sir. You are free to go, and we to follow. But I warn you, by all I hold most solemn and most sacred, that instant will have you apprehended on a charge of fraud and robbery. I am resolute and immoveable. If you are determined to be the same, your blood be upon your own head!”

text: The girl sat at the table with her arms folded, appearing entirely uninterested in the ongoing arrival, indifferent to whether the newcomer, whoever he was, came or went—until the low murmur of a man’s voice drifted to her ears. The instant she caught the sound, she tore off her bonnet and shawl with lightning speed, shoving them roughly under the table. The Jew turned around immediately afterward, and she muttered a complaint about the stifling heat in a languid tone that stood in sharp contrast to the extreme haste and roughness of her recent action. That movement, however, had gone unseen by Fagin, who had his back to her the entire time the action unfolded.
source_text: The girl, who was sitting at the table with her arms folded, appeared in no way interested in the arrival: or to care whether the person, whoever he was, came or went: until the murmur of a man’s voice reached her ears. The instant she caught the sound, she tore off her bonnet and shawl, with the rapidity of lightning, and thrust them under the table. The Jew, turning round immediately afterwards, she muttered a complaint of the heat: in a tone of languor that contrasted, very remarkably, with the extreme haste and violence of this action: which, however, had been unobserved by Fagin, who had his back towards her at the time.

text: The second Sikes exited the room, the Jew poked his head out past the doorframe. He kept his eyes fixed on Sikes as the man tramped up the shadowy passage, shook his tightly clenched fist, and muttered a thick, bitter curse under his breath. Then, pulling his mouth into a horrid, sneering grin, he settled back into his seat at the table, and within moments was completely engrossed in the vivid, attention-grabbing pages of the *Hue-and-Cry*.
source_text: The Jew thrust his head out of the room door when Sikes had left it; looked after him as he walked up the dark passage; shook his clenched fist; muttered a deep curse; and then, with a horrible grin, reseated himself at the table; where he was soon deeply absorbed in the interesting pages of the Hue-and-Cry.

text: “Ah, don’t fret, Toby,” old Mordecai murmured, soft and wheedling; “it’ll all work out, there’s no question of that. Every last one of ’em will learn just how sharp a lad he is; he’ll prove it himself, and never bring shame on his old cronies and the folks that taught him everything he knows. And think how green he still is, too! What an honor, Toby, to get sent down at his age!”
source_text: “Never mind, Charley,” said Fagin soothingly; “it’ll come out, it’ll be sure to come out. They’ll all know what a clever fellow he was; he’ll show it himself, and not disgrace his old pals and teachers. Think how young he is too! What a distinction, Charley, to be lagged at his time of life!”

text: “Pay attention, Oliver—pay real close attention!” the old man said, shaking his right hand sharply in front of him as a warning. “That man’s a vicious brute, and he doesn’t bat an eye at spilling blood when his temper’s fully riled. No matter what happens, don’t utter a single word, and do exactly what he tells you. Got it?” He laid heavy stress on that final word, let his features slowly twist into a ghastly, cold grin, gave a short nod of his head, and walked out of the room.
source_text: “Take heed, Oliver! take heed!” said the old man, shaking his right hand before him in a warning manner. “He’s a rough man, and thinks nothing of blood when his own is up. Whatever falls out, say nothing; and do what he bids you. Mind!” Placing a strong emphasis on the last word, he suffered his features gradually to resolve themselves into a ghastly grin, and, nodding his head, left the room.

text: The boy shifted slightly, a faint smile playing across his face as he slept, as if those small, soft acts of pity and care had stirred up a warm dream of the gentle, unwavering affection he had never known in his waking life. It’s the same quiet, fleeting magic that catches you off guard sometimes: a snippet of soft, lulling music, the ripple of water in a silent, undisturbed spot, the sweet scent of a blooming flower, or even a single familiar word spoken in passing, can spark sudden, hazy recollections of scenes that never actually existed in this lifetime. They vanish as fast as a breath, these half-formed memories, as if they were roused by some faint leftover trace of a far happier existence long faded into the past; the kind you can never deliberately call back to mind, no matter how hard you strain to reach for them.
source_text: The boy stirred, and smiled in his sleep, as though these marks of pity and compassion had awakened some pleasant dream of a love and affection he had never known. Thus, a strain of gentle music, or the rippling of water in a silent place, or the odour of a flower, or the mention of a familiar word, will sometimes call up sudden dim remembrances of scenes that never were, in this life; which vanish like a breath; which some brief memory of a happier existence, long gone by, would seem to have awakened; which no voluntary exertion of the mind can ever recall.

text: The moneylender cast a sneering glance at his companion’s ashen face, grunted that he was welcome to trail behind if he saw fit, then turned and climbed the creaking wooden steps. They peered into every chamber they passed, each one drafty, stripped of all furnishings, and devoid of any trace of recent habitation. They trundled back down to the ground-floor corridor, then pressed further down the rickety stone steps that led to the underground cellars. Thick, verdant mildew clung to the low, pitted stone walls; slimy snail and slug trails glinted silver in the wavering glow of their tallow candle, yet the whole space held a silence as deep and unbroken as a tomb.
source_text: The Jew glanced contemptuously at the pale face of his associate, and, telling him he could follow, if he pleased, ascended the stairs. They looked into all the rooms; they were cold, bare, and empty. They descended into the passage, and thence into the cellars below. The green damp hung upon the low walls; the tracks of the snail and slug glistened in the light of the candle; but all was still as death.

text: There was little about Mr. Dawkins’ appearance to suggest the comforts his patron claimed to secure for those under his care were anything close to what was promised, but he spoke in a flighty, unapologetically dissolute tone, and further volunteered that his closest friends knew him exclusively by the nickname “The Artful Dodger.” Oliver reasoned that the boy was so wild and heedless by nature, all the moral precepts his benefactor had tried to impart had thus far been entirely wasted on him. With this thought in mind, he privately resolved to win the old gentleman’s good opinion as quickly as he could; if the Dodger proved as incorrigible as he more than half suspected he would, he would decline the supposed honor of keeping his company any longer.
source_text: Mr. Dawkins’s appearance did not say a vast deal in favour of the comforts which his patron’s interest obtained for those whom he took under his protection; but, as he had a rather flightly and dissolute mode of conversing, and furthermore avowed that among his intimate friends he was better known by the _sobriquet_ of “The Artful Dodger,” Oliver concluded that, being of a dissipated and careless turn, the moral precepts of his benefactor had hitherto been thrown away upon him. Under this impression, he secretly resolved to cultivate the good opinion of the old gentleman as quickly as possible; and, if he found the Dodger incorrigible, as he more than half suspected he should, to decline the honour of his farther acquaintance.

text: It was this very lane the Jewish man turned into. The pale, sallow regulars who lived and did business along its length all knew him well; every one of them loitering to buy or sell goods gave him a familiar nod as he walked past. He returned their greetings the same way, but didn’t stop for any further chat or closer recognition until he reached the far end of the alley. There, he paused to speak to a short salesman who’d crammed as much of his body as he possibly could into a child-sized chair, smoking a pipe right outside the door of his warehouse.
source_text: It was into this place that the Jew turned. He was well known to the sallow denizens of the lane; for such of them as were on the look-out to buy or sell, nodded, familiarly, as he passed along. He replied to their salutations in the same way; but bestowed no closer recognition until he reached the further end of the alley; when he stopped, to address a salesman of small stature, who had squeezed as much of his person into a child’s chair as the chair would hold, and was smoking a pipe at his warehouse door.

text: Following that suggestion, they moved into the adjacent room, where they called Mr. Brittles in. He promptly tangled both himself and his well-respected superior up in such a bewildering web of fresh contradictions and implausible claims that no one gained any new clarity on the situation at all, aside from the clear fact that Brittles himself was thoroughly mystified. He did make a few definitive statements, though: he would not recognize the actual boy in question even if he were placed directly in front of him that very moment; he’d only assumed Oliver was the boy in the first place because Mr. Giles had told him so; and five minutes earlier, back in the kitchen, Mr. Giles had already admitted he was starting to fear he’d been far too hasty in jumping to that conclusion.
source_text: Acting upon this suggestion, they adjourned to a neighbouring apartment, where Mr. Brittles, being called in, involved himself and his respected superior in such a wonderful maze of fresh contradictions and impossibilities, as tended to throw no particular light on anything, but the fact of his own strong mystification; except, indeed, his declarations that he shouldn’t know the real boy, if he were put before him that instant; that he had only taken Oliver to be he, because Mr. Giles had said he was; and that Mr. Giles had, five minutes previously, admitted in the kitchen, that he began to be very much afraid he had been a little too hasty.

text: Noah Claypole tore down the streets at his top speed, never pausing to draw breath until he stood right at the workhouse gate. He rested there for a minute or two to work up a heaving fit of sobs and a convincing, dramatic display of tear-streaked fear, before pounding loudly on the small wicket door. The aged pauper who answered took one look at his wretched, twisted face, and even he—who was surrounded by nothing but mournful, haggard faces even on his best days—jumped back in astonishment.
source_text: Noah Claypole ran along the streets at his swiftest pace, and paused not once for breath, until he reached the workhouse-gate. Having rested here, for a minute or so, to collect a good burst of sobs and an imposing show of tears and terror, he knocked loudly at the wicket; and presented such a rueful face to the aged pauper who opened it, that even he, who saw nothing but rueful faces about him at the best of times, started back in astonishment.

text: “Mark my words, he could turn out far worse,” Mr. Grimwig repeated. “Where does he even come from? Who is he, really? What’s his whole background? So he had a fever—what of it? Fevers aren’t some special affliction that only hits decent people, are they? Terrible folks get fevers too, don’t they, eh? I knew a man who was hanged in Jamaica for killing his master. He’d come down with fever six separate times, and nobody offered him mercy on account of that, either. Pooh! What a load of rubbish!”
source_text: “He may have worse, I say,” repeated Mr. Grimwig. “Where does he come from! Who is he? What is he? He has had a fever. What of that? Fevers are not peculiar to good people; are they? Bad people have fevers sometimes; haven’t they, eh? I knew a man who was hung in Jamaica for murdering his master. He had had a fever six times; he wasn’t recommended to mercy on that account. Pooh! nonsense!”

text: The upright, gentle Mr. Hale curled the bed curtain loosely in his fist, lingering by the frame for a long minute without a sound. As he stood watching the sleeping boy’s shallow breaths, Elara slipped soundlessly past his elbow, lowering herself into the worn wicker seat tucked at the bedside, and brushed a stray strand of Toby’s matted brown hair away from his face. When she leaned over to tuck the lock behind his ear, a warm tear slipped down her cheek and landed soft on his forehead.
source_text: The honest gentleman held the curtain in his hand, and looked on, for a minute or so, in silence. Whilst he was watching the patient thus, the younger lady glided softly past, and seating herself in a chair by the bedside, gathered Oliver’s hair from his face. As she stooped over him, her tears fell upon his forehead.

text: “He’s tall,” the girl said, “solidly built, but not heavy. He walks like he’s trying not to draw attention, always glancing over his shoulder as he moves, first one side, then the other. Don’t miss that detail—his eyes are set so much deeper in his skull than most people’s, you could practically spot him by that feature alone. He’s dark-skinned, same as his hair and eyes, and even though he can’t be more than twenty-six or twenty-eight, his face looks worn and gaunt. His lips are usually discolored and chewed raw with tooth marks, too; he gets these terrible, desperate fits, and sometimes he even bites his own hands so hard they’re covered in wounds—why did you start?” the girl asked, cutting herself off abruptly.
source_text: “He is tall,” said the girl, “and a strongly made man, but not stout; he has a lurking walk; and as he walks, constantly looks over his shoulder, first on one side, and then on the other. Don’t forget that, for his eyes are sunk in his head so much deeper than any other man’s, that you might almost tell him by that alone. His face is dark, like his hair and eyes; and, although he can’t be more than six or eight and twenty, withered and haggard. His lips are often discoloured and disfigured with the marks of teeth; for he has desperate fits, and sometimes even bites his hands and covers them with wounds—why did you start?” said the girl, stopping suddenly.

text: “What in the nine hells is going on now?” rumbled a gravel-throated growl. “Who slung that mess at my head? Lucky for ’em it was just the beer, not the mug, that connected, or I’d have cracked some fool’s skull open. I oughta guessed, only a cursed, hoarding, swindling, roaring old grifter like you could afford to toss good drink away when even water’s too precious unless you cheat the water company out of their dues every quarter. What’s the big idea, Ezra? Blimey, my whole neckerchief’s soaked through with suds! Get in here, you skulking little rat—what’re you loitering out there for, like you’re ashamed to be seen with your master? Get in!”
source_text: “Why, what the blazes is in the wind now!” growled a deep voice. “Who pitched that ’ere at me? It’s well it’s the beer, and not the pot, as hit me, or I’d have settled somebody. I might have know’d, as nobody but an infernal, rich, plundering, thundering old Jew could afford to throw away any drink but water—and not that, unless he done the River Company every quarter. Wot’s it all about, Fagin? D—me, if my neck-handkercher an’t lined with beer! Come in, you sneaking warmint; wot are you stopping outside for, as if you was ashamed of your master! Come in!”

text: “My mother,” Monks said, raising his voice, “did what any woman in her position ought to have done. She burned that will. The letter never reached the person it was meant for, but she held onto that letter and other bits of proof, just in case anyone ever tried to lie their way out from under the stain of that secret. She laid the full truth before the girl’s father, laced with every cruel twist her searing, violent hatred could conjure—though I love her for that very fury now. Goaded half-mad by shame and disgrace, he fled with his children to a far-flung corner of Wales, even changing his name so none of his old friends could ever find where he’d hidden. He was found dead in his bed not long after he settled there. The girl had snuck away from home in secret a few weeks prior; he’d searched for her on foot, combing every nearby town and village for any sign of her. It was the night he returned home, convinced she’d killed herself to bury both her shame and his, that his worn old heart finally broke.”
source_text: “My mother,” said Monks, in a louder tone, “did what a woman should have done. She burnt this will. The letter never reached its destination; but that, and other proofs, she kept, in case they ever tried to lie away the blot. The girl’s father had the truth from her with every aggravation that her violent hate—I love her for it now—could add. Goaded by shame and dishonour he fled with his children into a remote corner of Wales, changing his very name that his friends might never know of his retreat; and here, no great while afterwards, he was found dead in his bed. The girl had left her home, in secret, some weeks before; he had searched for her, on foot, in every town and village near; it was on the night when he returned home, assured that she had destroyed herself, to hide her shame and his, that his old heart broke.”

text: Look here, will you! Do you see this mess? Isn’t it the most absurd, unheard-of thing? I can’t stop by a man’s house for a call without finding a scrap of that wretched surgeon’s friend strewn across the staircase! I was left lame for weeks after slipping on orange peel once, and I know full well orange peel will be the end of me, or I’d be perfectly content to eat my own head, sir!
source_text: “Look here! do you see this! Isn’t it a most wonderful and extraordinary thing that I can’t call at a man’s house but I find a piece of this poor surgeon’s friend on the staircase? I’ve been lamed with orange-peel once, and I know orange-peel will be my death, or I’ll be content to eat my own head, sir!”

text: “So many nights lost to this,” the young woman said, that soft, innate feminine tenderness wrapping around her words, gilding even her usual sharp voice with a faint, honeyed warmth: “all those evenings I stayed patient by your side, nursing you through every low, tending to you like you were a small, helpless child: and tonight’s the first time I’ve seen you act like the person I know. You wouldn’t have spoken to me the way you did just now, not if you’d paused to remember all that, would you? Come on, now. Say you wouldn’t.”
source_text: “Such a number of nights,” said the girl, with a touch of woman’s tenderness, which communicated something like sweetness of tone, even to her voice: “such a number of nights as I’ve been patient with you, nursing and caring for you, as if you had been a child: and this the first that I’ve seen you like yourself; you wouldn’t have served me as you did just now, if you’d thought of that, would you? Come, come; say you wouldn’t.”

text: He got the words out in a thin, reedy voice, so drained and lightheaded he could barely push the sounds past his lips, but someone caught every syllable immediately. The curtain strung across the head of the bed was yanked back in a rush, and a gentle, motherly older woman, dressed with neat, precise care, rose as she pulled it to the side – she’d been settled in the armchair right beside the bed, working on a sewing project.
source_text: He uttered these words in a feeble voice, being very faint and weak; but they were overheard at once. The curtain at the bed’s head was hastily drawn back, and a motherly old lady, very neatly and precisely dressed, rose as she undrew it, from an arm-chair close by, in which she had been sitting at needle-work.

text: Elias Thorne’s jaw tightened as if he might follow that snappy exchange with a handful of comments that would have left Dr. Voss thoroughly taken aback; but he held his tongue, only saying, “Time will show,” and let the matter rest entirely. The post carriage rumbled up to the entryway only moments later; when Tobin poked his head in to collect the luggage, the kind, brisk doctor hurried out to oversee that every item was stowed correctly.
source_text: Harry Maylie looked as if he could have followed up this short dialogue by one or two remarks that would have staggered the doctor not a little; but he contented himself with saying, “We shall see,” and pursued the subject no farther. The post-chaise drove up to the door shortly afterwards; and Giles coming in for the luggage, the good doctor bustled out, to see it packed.

text: After a flurry of farewells and earnest well wishes, Mr. Fagin took his leave. Noah Claypole, first securing his good lady’s full attention, proceeded to explain the arrangement he had struck, carrying himself with all the haughty, self-important swagger befitting not only a member of the sterner sex, but a gentleman who well appreciated the dignity of a special appointment working the kinchin lay in London and its neighboring districts.
source_text: With many adieus and good wishes, Mr. Fagin went his way. Noah Claypole, bespeaking his good lady’s attention, proceeded to enlighten her relative to the arrangement he had made, with all that haughtiness and air of superiority, becoming, not only a member of the sterner sex, but a gentleman who appreciated the dignity of a special appointment on the kinchin lay, in London and its vicinity.

text: “Court’s officially in session,” said Jax, “if they wrap the inquest up quick, and Weasel turns King’s evidence—he’s certain to, from every remark he’s let slip so far—they can pin Old Harker as an accessory before the fact, get the trial listed for Friday, and he’ll swing six days from this day, by G—!”
source_text: “The sessions are on,” said Kags: “if they get the inquest over, and Bolter turns King’s evidence: as of course he will, from what he’s said already: they can prove Fagin an accessory before the fact, and get the trial on on Friday, and he’ll swing in six days from this, by G—!”

text: Helping Oliver to his feet, the young gentleman led him to a nearby chandler’s shop, where he bought a generous portion of pre-cooked ham and a half-quartern loaf—or, as he phrased it himself, “a fourpenny bran!” To keep the ham clean and protected from dust, he used a clever little trick: he tore out a chunk of the loaf’s soft inner crumb to hollow out a space, then stuffed the ham inside. Tucking the loaf under his arm, the young gentleman turned into a small public house, and led the way to a taproom at the rear of the premises. A pot of beer was brought over at the mysterious youth’s request; at his new friend’s urging, Oliver dug in, making a long, hearty meal of it, throughout which the strange boy glanced at him every so often with great attentiveness.
source_text: Assisting Oliver to rise, the young gentleman took him to an adjacent chandler’s shop, where he purchased a sufficiency of ready-dressed ham and a half-quartern loaf, or, as he himself expressed it, “a fourpenny bran!” the ham being kept clean and preserved from dust, by the ingenious expedient of making a hole in the loaf by pulling out a portion of the crumb, and stuffing it therein. Taking the bread under his arm, the young gentleman turned into a small public-house, and led the way to a tap-room in the rear of the premises. Here, a pot of beer was brought in, by direction of the mysterious youth; and Oliver, falling to, at his new friend’s bidding, made a long and hearty meal, during the progress of which the strange boy eyed him from time to time with great attention.

text: Even as it grew clearer their hunt was going nowhere, they refused to call it off until dusk draped the lanes so thick that pushing on would have been utterly fruitless; even at that point, abandoning the search felt like a defeat they resented deeply. Tompkins was dispatched to every tavern and public house scattered through the hamlet, armed with the most detailed account Luka could muster of the unknown men’s features and clothing. Of the group, the haggard, silver-bearded ringleader was distinct enough that anyone who’d spotted him lingering over a pint or loitering on the tavern thresholds would have surely recalled him; yet Tompkins returned with not a single scrap of intelligence that might cut through or even soften the edges of the unyielding mystery.
source_text: Notwithstanding the evidently useless nature of their search, they did not desist until the coming on of night rendered its further prosecution hopeless; and even then, they gave it up with reluctance. Giles was dispatched to the different ale-houses in the village, furnished with the best description Oliver could give of the appearance and dress of the strangers. Of these, the Jew was, at all events, sufficiently remarkable to be remembered, supposing he had been seen drinking, or loitering about; but Giles returned without any intelligence, calculated to dispel or lessen the mystery.

text: Mr. Sikes didn’t bother with anything more elaborate than miming the act of tying a tight knot just beneath his left ear, then tipping his head sharply to rest against his right shoulder; that bit of wordless pantomime was one the Jew appeared to grasp perfectly well. He then requested a glass of liquor in the dense, niche street slang he laced through every part of his conversation, terms that would be completely unintelligible if we transcribed them directly here.
source_text: Mr. Sikes contented himself with tying an imaginary knot under his left ear, and jerking his head over on the right shoulder; a piece of dumb show which the Jew appeared to understand perfectly. He then, in cant terms, with which his whole conversation was plentifully besprinkled, but which would be quite unintelligible if they were recorded here, demanded a glass of liquor.

text: This was the make-or-break moment of Oliver Twist’s fate. Had the inkstand been where the elderly gentleman believed it was kept, he would have dipped his pen into its well, signed the indenture papers, and Oliver would have been hurried off at once. But as the inkstand happened to sit directly beneath his nose, he naturally rummaged every inch of his desk in search of it to no avail; mid-way through his hunt, he chanced to glance straight ahead, and his gaze fell on Oliver’s ashen, terrified face. For all Bumble’s warning glares and sharp, admonitory pinches, Oliver was gazing at his repulsive future master with a tangled mix of horror and fear so unmistakeable that even the half-blind magistrate could not overlook it.
source_text: It was the critical moment of Oliver’s fate. If the inkstand had been where the old gentleman thought it was, he would have dipped his pen into it, and signed the indentures, and Oliver would have been straightway hurried off. But, as it chanced to be immediately under his nose, it followed, as a matter of course, that he looked all over his desk for it, without finding it; and happening in the course of his search to look straight before him, his gaze encountered the pale and terrified face of Oliver Twist: who, despite all the admonitory looks and pinches of Bumble, was regarding the repulsive countenance of his future master, with a mingled expression of horror and fear, too palpable to be mistaken, even by a half-blind magistrate.

text: Their midday meal was plain cold cured meat, and they lingered at the scuffed wooden table for so long afterward, while Mr. Hale worked his way through three or four chipped clay pipes, that Luka grew thoroughly convinced they had no intention of setting out again that day. Worn ragged from the miles they’d tramped and the hour he’d risen before dawn, he drifted in fitful half-sleep at first; then, fully overpowered by bone-deep fatigue and the thick, acrid curl of tobacco smoke hanging heavy in the stuffy cottage air, he slipped completely into unconsciousness.
source_text: They had some cold meat for dinner, and sat so long after it, while Mr. Sikes indulged himself with three or four pipes, that Oliver began to feel quite certain they were not going any further. Being much tired with the walk, and getting up so early, he dozed a little at first; then, quite overpowered by fatigue and the fumes of the tobacco, fell asleep.

text: Even amid the fragile, newly won happiness he’d only just dared to embrace, this searing disappointment left Elias heavy with unspoken grief. All through the long days and fevered nights of his illness, he’d amused himself time and again with visions of what Mr. Hale and Mrs. Thorne would say when they were reunited, how bright it would feel to tell them how many waking hours he’d spent lingering on their kindness to him, and mourning the cruel twist of fate that had torn him away from their care. The quiet hope of one day clearing his name in their eyes, of explaining how he’d been dragged off against his will, had lifted his spirits and carried him through so many of his recent misfortunes. Now, the thought that they had departed entirely, clinging to the belief that he was an imposter and a robber—a belief that might go unchallenged for as long as he lived—was nearly more than he could stand.
source_text: This bitter disappointment caused Oliver much sorrow and grief, even in the midst of his happiness; for he had pleased himself, many times during his illness, with thinking of all that Mr. Brownlow and Mrs. Bedwin would say to him: and what delight it would be to tell them how many long days and nights he had passed in reflecting on what they had done for him, and in bewailing his cruel separation from them. The hope of eventually clearing himself with them, too, and explaining how he had been forced away, had buoyed him up, and sustained him, under many of his recent trials; and now, the idea that they should have gone so far, and carried with them the belief that he was an impostor and a robber—a belief which might remain uncontradicted to his dying day—was almost more than he could bear.

text: Before he moved to the quiet country cottage on the village outskirts, Tobias had struck up a fierce, warm friendship with Mr. Thaddeus Grigg, and that cantankerous, eccentric gentleman returned the affection wholeheartedly. As a result, Mr. Grigg turns up on his doorstep dozens of times each year, unannounced more often than not. Every time he visits, he throws himself into gardening, fishing, and odd carpentry jobs with frantic energy; every task is carried out in a bizarre, unheard-of fashion, yet he always insists, with his favorite gruff declaration, that his way is the only correct one. On Sundays, he never misses a chance to pick apart the week’s sermon directly to the young curate’s face, only to lean in and whisper to Dr. Elwood Finch later, in strictest confidence, that he thought the homily was masterfully done, but sees no sense in letting the boy get a big head about it. It is a long-running, much-loved joke for Mr. Arthur Weatherly to tease him about his old dire prediction about Tobias, and to bring up the night the two of them sat with a pocket watch set on the table between them, waiting for the boy to come back; but Mr. Grigg maintains he was mostly correct, offering as proof the fact that Tobias never did return to town that night as they’d expected, a quip that always makes him cackle and puts him in an even brighter mood.
source_text: Before his removal, he had managed to contract a strong friendship for Mr. Grimwig, which that eccentric gentleman cordially reciprocated. He is accordingly visited by Mr. Grimwig a great many times in the course of the year. On all such occasions, Mr. Grimwig plants, fishes, and carpenters, with great ardour; doing everything in a very singular and unprecedented manner, but always maintaining with his favourite asseveration, that his mode is the right one. On Sundays, he never fails to criticise the sermon to the young clergyman’s face: always informing Mr. Losberne, in strict confidence afterwards, that he considers it an excellent performance, but deems it as well not to say so. It is a standing and very favourite joke, for Mr. Brownlow to rally him on his old prophecy concerning Oliver, and to remind him of the night on which they sat with the watch between them, waiting his return; but Mr. Grimwig contends that he was right in the main, and, in proof thereof, remarks that Oliver did not come back after all; which always calls forth a laugh on his side, and increases his good humour.

text: He guided them into a cold stone kitchen fitted with large copper vats for preparing prison rations, then pointed to a closed wooden door. An open iron grating set just above the door’s frame carried a jumble of sounds: the rough murmur of men’s voices, the sharp crack of hammer blows, and the heavy thud of planks being tossed down. They were erecting the scaffold.
source_text: He led them into a stone kitchen, fitted with coppers for dressing the prison food, and pointed to a door. There was an open grating above it, through which came the sound of men’s voices, mingled with the noise of hammering, and the throwing down of boards. They were putting up the scaffold.

text: There is no way to know whether that remark referred to her husband, or the teapot set on the table before her.
