The latter was the far more likely subject, for Mrs. Corney’s gaze rested on it the entire time she spoke, and she lifted it immediately after finishing her sentence. She had barely sampled the first sip of her poured cup when a faint, gentle tap at the room door pulled her attention away.
source_text: Whether this remark bore reference to the husband, or the teapot, is uncertain. It might have been the latter; for Mrs. Corney looked at it as she spoke; and took it up afterwards. She had just tasted her first cup, when she was disturbed by a soft tap at the room-door.

text: “Alright, that’s enough now—don’t get all mushy on me, yeah? Don’t make me snap at you,” Noah said, pulling away with a deadpan, serious air. “What I want is to run my own crew, y’know? Get to knock the lads around when they step out of line, trail ’em where they go without them catching on. That’s the life for me, so long as the money’s good. And if we can get in good with some bloke who’s already running that kind of operation? I say handing over that twenty-pound note you’ve got is a steal, especially seeing as we haven’t got a clue how to offload it ourselves anyway.”
source_text: “There, that’ll do: don’t yer be too affectionate, in case I’m cross with yer,” said Noah, disengaging himself with great gravity. “I should like to be the captain of some band, and have the whopping of ’em, and follering ’em about, unbeknown to themselves. That would suit me, if there was good profit; and if we could only get in with some gentleman of this sort, I say it would be cheap at that twenty-pound note you’ve got,—especially as we don’t very well know how to get rid of it ourselves.”

text: Gathered in that informal lower chamber of the household’s own domestic parliament were the female servants, Mr. Brittles, Mr. Giles, the tinker (who’d received a special invitation to feast and make himself at home for the rest of the day as a reward for his work), and the local constable. This last gentleman carried a thick truncheon, had a broad, heavy head, oversized facial features, and bulky half-boots; he looked as if he’d drunk a perfectly proportional share of ale—which, as it turned out, he most certainly had.
source_text: There were assembled, in that lower house of the domestic parliament, the women-servants, Mr. Brittles, Mr. Giles, the tinker (who had received a special invitation to regale himself for the remainder of the day, in consideration of his services), and the constable. The latter gentleman had a large staff, a large head, large features, and large half-boots; and he looked as if he had been taking a proportionate allowance of ale—as indeed he had.

text: “Let me make this perfectly clear, Mr. Bumble,” his wife shot back. “We’ve got no use at all for your meddling here. You’re far too keen on sticking your nose into matters that are none of your business, making everyone in the house laugh at you the second your back is turned, and making a total fool of yourself every single hour of the day. Get moving, now!”
source_text: “I’ll tell you what, Mr. Bumble,” returned his lady. “We don’t want any of your interference. You’re a great deal too fond of poking your nose into things that don’t concern you, making everybody in the house laugh, the moment your back is turned, and making yourself look like a fool every hour in the day. Be off; come!”

text: Yet while this bright, happy shift had left a visible mark on their tight-knit little group, and the cottage once more rang with lighthearted voices and merry laughter, an unaccustomed, odd reserve hung over some members of the household at odd moments—even Rose herself—and Oliver could not fail to notice it. Mrs. Maylie and her son would frequently shut themselves away together for long stretches, and more than once Rose appeared with faint, fresh traces of tears on her face. Once Mr. Losberne settled on a firm date for his upcoming departure for Chertsey, these small, strange signs grew more pronounced; it became plainly evident that some unspoken matter was unfolding, one that disturbed not just the young lady’s peace of mind, but that of someone else besides.
source_text: But, although this happy change had a visible effect on the little circle; and although cheerful voices and merry laughter were once more heard in the cottage; there was at times, an unwonted restraint upon some there: even upon Rose herself: which Oliver could not fail to remark. Mrs. Maylie and her son were often closeted together for a long time; and more than once Rose appeared with traces of tears upon her face. After Mr. Losberne had fixed a day for his departure to Chertsey, these symptoms increased; and it became evident that something was in progress which affected the peace of the young lady, and of somebody else besides.

text: Elias moved quickly to carry out the small favor she’d asked of him. The dark-haired girl, pushing hard to summon back her lighthearted mood, fumbled to start a brighter, bouncier melody; but her fingers went limp and slid uselessly off the ivory keys. Pressing both palms to her cheeks, she crumpled onto the plush velvet sofa, letting loose the hot tears she’d fought so hard to hold back until that moment.
source_text: Oliver hastened to comply with her request. The young lady, making an effort to recover her cheerfulness, strove to play some livelier tune; but her fingers dropped powerless over the keys. Covering her face with her hands, she sank upon a sofa, and gave vent to the tears which she was now unable to repress.

text: “Fair enough,” the doctor said. “Seeing you here just jogged my memory, Mr. Giles. The day before I got that urgent call that had me rushing off at a moment’s notice, I carried out a small errand on your behalf at your kind mistress’s request. Would you step over to this corner with me for a second?”
source_text: “That’s well,” said the doctor. “Seeing you here, reminds me, Mr. Giles, that on the day before that on which I was called away so hurriedly, I executed, at the request of your good mistress, a small commission in your favour. Just step into this corner a moment, will you?”

text: “The set of your features, every bit as much as your recent actions, makes plain you have never wiped that time from your mind, nor ceased to regard it with sharp bitterness,” Mr. Hale returned. “I speak of fifteen years past, when you were barely eleven years old, and your father only thirty-one—for he was scarcely more than a boy himself, I remind you, when his own father ordered him into that arranged marriage. Must I dredge up events that would cast a permanent shadow over your father’s memory in your eyes, or will you spare me that unkindness, and reveal the full truth to me now?”
source_text: “Your manner, no less than your actions, assures me that you have never forgotten it, or ceased to think of it with bitterness,” returned Mr. Brownlow. “I speak of fifteen years ago, when you were not more than eleven years old, and your father but one-and-thirty—for he was, I repeat, a boy, when _his_ father ordered him to marry. Must I go back to events which cast a shade upon the memory of your parent, or will you spare it, and disclose to me the truth?”

text: Elias’s maladies were no trivial, short-lived affair. Beyond the throbbing ache and slow, cumbersome healing of his fractured leg, the hours he’d spent exposed to lashing rain and bitter frost had stirred up a raging fever and shaking ague that clung to his system for weeks, wasting his small frame to a gaunt, listless shadow. But at length, he began to mend by almost imperceptible degrees, now and then able to murmur a few wobbly, tear-thick words about how deeply he cherished the kindness of the two gentle women who’d taken him in, and how ardently he hoped that once he regained his full strength and health, he might find some small way to show his gratitude. It only needed to be some small act, something that would lay bare the overflowing devotion and sense of duty swelling in his breast; no matter how slight the effort, it would prove that their soft, unhesitating goodness had not been cast aside for nothing, that the ragged, destitute boy their charity had pulled from misery, and almost certain death, ached to devote every beat of his heart and every scrap of his strength to their care.
source_text: Oliver’s ailings were neither slight nor few. In addition to the pain and delay attendant on a broken limb, his exposure to the wet and cold had brought on fever and ague: which hung about him for many weeks, and reduced him sadly. But, at length, he began, by slow degrees, to get better, and to be able to say sometimes, in a few tearful words, how deeply he felt the goodness of the two sweet ladies, and how ardently he hoped that when he grew strong and well again, he could do something to show his gratitude; only something, which would let them see the love and duty with which his breast was full; something, however slight, which would prove to them that their gentle kindness had not been cast away; but that the poor boy whom their charity had rescued from misery, or death, was eager to serve them with his whole heart and soul.

text: “We’re absolutely delighted to see you, Oliver, truly we are,” the Jew said. “Dodger, take those sausages off the fire, and pull that tub up close to the hearth for Oliver. Ah, you’re staring at the pocket handkerchiefs, aren’t you, my dear? There’s quite a lot of them, I know. We’ve just sorted them all out to go to the wash, that’s all, Oliver—nothing more to it. Ha! ha! ha!”
source_text: “We are very glad to see you, Oliver, very,” said the Jew. “Dodger, take off the sausages; and draw a tub near the fire for Oliver. Ah, you’re a-staring at the pocket-handkerchiefs! eh, my dear? There are a good many of ’em, ain’t there? We’ve just looked ’em out, ready for the wash; that’s all, Oliver; that’s all. Ha! ha! ha!”

text: The dapper young gentleman did not pause to offer Elias any other sign of recognition beyond a roguish, amused grin; instead, he turned on his heel and beckoned the small party of visitors to trail after him down a rickety flight of stairs. They crossed through an empty, draughty kitchen, then pushed open the door of a low, damp-earth-scented room that appeared to have been built in a cramped rear yard, and were greeted at once with a boisterous peal of laughter.
source_text: The young gentleman did not stop to bestow any other mark of recognition upon Oliver than a humourous grin; but, turning away, beckoned the visitors to follow him down a flight of stairs. They crossed an empty kitchen; and, opening the door of a low earthy-smelling room, which seemed to have been built in a small back-yard, were received with a shout of laughter.

text: The stone steps the young woman had nodded toward sat on the Kentish riverbank, on the same side of the old stone bridge as Saint Thomas’s Church, forming a rickety public landing from the Thames. The man, who carried the rough, sun-browned look of a farmer from the outlying Weald, hurried to the spot without drawing any eyes; after a quick, sweeping glance at the empty riverfront around him, he began to pick his way down the slippery, algae-slick treads.
source_text: The steps to which the girl had pointed, were those which, on the Surrey bank, and on the same side of the bridge as Saint Saviour’s Church, form a landing-stairs from the river. To this spot, the man bearing the appearance of a countryman, hastened unobserved; and after a moment’s survey of the place, he began to descend.

text: “Whether it takes long or not,” the matron snapped, “she won’t find me still hanging around here when she does wake up. Both of you watch yourselves before you waste my time over nothing again. It’s no part of my job to hover around waiting for every old woman in this house to die, and I won’t do it—plain and simple. Don’t you forget that, you cheeky old harpies. If you make a fool out of me again, I’ll sort you out right quick, you can count on that!”
source_text: “Long or short,” said the matron, snappishly, “she won’t find me here when she does wake; take care, both of you, how you worry me again for nothing. It’s no part of my duty to see all the old women in the house die, and I won’t—that’s more. Mind that, you impudent old harridans. If you make a fool of me again, I’ll soon cure you, I warrant you!”

text: As Oliver Twist’s biographer, I can’t claim with any real confidence that the example of these virtuous people prompted Oliver to resign himself to his circumstances; but I can state unequivocally that for months on end he meekly endured the domination and abuse of Noah Claypole. Noah treated him far worse than he had before, his jealousy sparked when he saw the new boy promoted to the black walking stick and official hatband, while he, the longer-serving staff member, was stuck wearing the same old muffin cap and leather work gear he’d had for ages. Charlotte picked on him too, just because Noah did, and Mrs. Sowerberry made no secret of being his sworn enemy, all because Mr. Sowerberry was inclined to be kind to him. Caught between these three ganging up on him on one side, and a constant glut of funerals keeping him overworked on the other, Oliver was quite a bit less comfortable than that hungry pig that once got accidentally locked in a brewery’s grain storage room.
source_text: That Oliver Twist was moved to resignation by the example of these good people, I cannot, although I am his biographer, undertake to affirm with any degree of confidence; but I can most distinctly say, that for many months he continued meekly to submit to the domination and ill-treatment of Noah Claypole: who used him far worse than before, now that his jealousy was roused by seeing the new boy promoted to the black stick and hat-band, while he, the old one, remained stationary in the muffin-cap and leathers. Charlotte treated him ill, because Noah did; and Mrs. Sowerberry was his decided enemy, because Mr. Sowerberry was disposed to be his friend; so, between these three on one side, and a glut of funerals on the other, Oliver was not altogether as comfortable as the hungry pig was, when he was shut up, by mistake, in the grain department of a brewery.

text: Her demeanor held such an uncanny, unsettling edge that the hidden listener’s skin crawled as the girl uttered those words, and his blood ran cold in his veins. He had never known a greater surge of relief than when the young lady’s sweet, warm voice cut in, begging her to calm herself and not fall prey to such terrifying, dark fancies.
source_text: There was something so uncommon in her manner, that the flesh of the concealed listener crept as he heard the girl utter these words, and the blood chilled within him. He had never experienced a greater relief than in hearing the sweet voice of the young lady as she begged her to be calm, and not allow herself to become the prey of such fearful fancies.

text: “Come along with me, then,” said Mrs. Sowerberry, picking up a dim, grimy lamp and leading the way up the stairs. “Your bed’s stowed under the counter. I suppose you don’t mind sleeping surrounded by coffins, do you? But it hardly makes a difference whether you mind or not, because there’s nowhere else for you to sleep. Hurry up now—don’t make me hang around here all night!”
source_text: “Then come with me,” said Mrs. Sowerberry: taking up a dim and dirty lamp, and leading the way upstairs; “your bed’s under the counter. You don’t mind sleeping among the coffins, I suppose? But it doesn’t much matter whether you do or don’t, for you can’t sleep anywhere else. Come; don’t keep me here all night!”

text: “Wait, are you actually trying to tell me you couldn’t have pulled this off if you’d wanted to?” Monks demanded sharply. “You’ve done the exact same thing with dozens of other boys loads of times before, haven’t you? If you’d just had the patience to wait a year at most, wouldn’t you have been able to get him convicted, shipped clean out of the country? You could’ve even gotten him sent away for life, for that matter?”
source_text: “Why, do you mean to say you couldn’t have done it, if you had chosen?” demanded Monks, sternly. “Haven’t you done it, with other boys, scores of times? If you had had patience for a twelvemonth, at most, couldn’t you have got him convicted, and sent safely out of the kingdom; perhaps for life?”

text: For a single fleeting instant, Oliver darted a harried glance down the empty street, a cry for help balanced precariously on the edge of his lips. But the girl’s voice curled in his ear, beseeching him with such raw, agonized urgency to remember her that he could not find the heart to call out. In the span of his hesitation, the chance slipped away entirely; he was already across the threshold inside the house, and the door clicked shut behind him.
source_text: For one brief moment, Oliver cast a hurried glance along the empty street, and a cry for help hung upon his lips. But the girl’s voice was in his ear, beseeching him in such tones of agony to remember her, that he had not the heart to utter it. While he hesitated, the opportunity was gone; he was already in the house, and the door was shut.

text: As he busied himself with his task, the other two men sat nearby in silence, their gazes fixed to the floor, when a pattering scuff of paws sounded on the stairs, and Sikes’s dog bounded into the room. They darted to the window, raced down the stairs, and poured out into the street. The dog had leapt in through an open window; he made no attempt to follow them, and his master was nowhere to be seen.
source_text: While he was thus engaged, and the two men sat by in silence with their eyes fixed upon the floor, a pattering noise was heard upon the stairs, and Sikes’s dog bounded into the room. They ran to the window, downstairs, and into the street. The dog had jumped in at an open window; he made no attempt to follow them, nor was his master to be seen.

text: Oliver noticed a small, notable shift right away: he no longer set off on his morning flower-gathering expeditions alone. The very first time Harry Maylie had spotted him trundling home weighed down with armfuls of blossoms, he had developed such an intense fondness for flowers, and such a deft hand at arranging them, that he easily outstripped his young companion in that regard. What Oliver lacked in floral styling skill, though, he made up for in knowing exactly where the finest wild blooms grew; morning after morning, the two scoured the countryside side by side, returning with the most delicate, vivid flowers in bloom that season. Rose’s bedroom window now stood propped open, for she loved to let the thick, warm summer air drift in, rousing her with its sweet, fresh scent; and tucked just inside the lattice, in a small vase of water, there always sat one particular tiny bunch, carefully assembled fresh each morning. Oliver could not help but notice that even as the vase was regularly replenished with new blooms, the wilted old ones were never discarded; he also saw that every time the doctor stepped into the garden to start his morning walk, he always glanced up at that exact spot, nodding his head in a most knowing, expressive way. As these quiet observations unfolded, the days slipped past swiftly, and Rose recovered faster by the hour.
source_text: It is worthy of remark, and Oliver did not fail to note it at the time, that his morning expeditions were no longer made alone. Harry Maylie, after the very first morning when he met Oliver coming laden home, was seized with such a passion for flowers, and displayed such a taste in their arrangement, as left his young companion far behind. If Oliver were behindhand in these respects, he knew where the best were to be found; and morning after morning they scoured the country together, and brought home the fairest that blossomed. The window of the young lady’s chamber was opened now; for she loved to feel the rich summer air stream in, and revive her with its freshness; but there always stood in water, just inside the lattice, one particular little bunch, which was made up with great care, every morning. Oliver could not help noticing that the withered flowers were never thrown away, although the little vase was regularly replenished; nor, could he help observing, that whenever the doctor came into the garden, he invariably cast his eyes up to that particular corner, and nodded his head most expressively, as he set forth on his morning’s walk. Pending these observations, the days were flying by; and Rose was rapidly recovering.

text: “Name’s Noah Claypole—Mister Claypole, to you,” the charity ward said, “and you answer directly to me. Get those shutters pulled down, you lazy little lout!” With that, Claypole landed a sharp kick to Oliver’s leg, then marched into the shop with a lofty, dignified bearing he clearly thought suited him perfectly. It’s hard enough for a big-headed, beady-eyed, clumsily built, thick-featured teen to pull off dignity at the best of times, but the task becomes outright impossible when you add a blotchy red nose and scuffed yellow knee-breeches to that already unflattering list of traits.
source_text: “I’m Mister Noah Claypole,” said the charity-boy, “and you’re under me. Take down the shutters, yer idle young ruffian!” With this, Mr. Claypole administered a kick to Oliver, and entered the shop with a dignified air, which did him great credit. It is difficult for a large-headed, small-eyed youth, of lumbering make and heavy countenance, to look dignified under any circumstances; but it is more especially so, when superadded to these personal attractions are a red nose and yellow smalls.

text: The walls and low ceiling of the garret were glossy black with decades of smoke and accumulated grime. A scuffed pine table hunkered before the sputtering hearth: atop it rested a tallow candle wedged into an empty seltzer bottle, three dented tin mugs, a crusty sourdough loaf slathered with half-eaten lard, and a chipped earthenware plate. A cast-iron frying pan perched on the fire grate, lashed to the mantel ledge with frayed twine to keep it from tipping, held a heap of sputtering pork links; leaning over it, a brass toasting fork in his grip, was a gaunt, wrinkled old Romany man whose scowling, uninviting face was half-hidden under a nest of tangled auburn curls. He wore an oil-stained flannel robe slung open over his bare throat, and his gaze darted constantly between the spitting sausages and the wooden drying rack strung across the far corner, draped with dozens of pilfered silk neckerchiefs. Half a dozen lumpy pallets stuffed with old sacking were pushed in a ragged huddle side by side on the floor, heaped with tattered wool scraps for warmth. Huddled around the pine table were four or five ragged urchins, none older than Rook, the gang’s quickest pickpocket, sucking on long clay pipes and swigging stolen gin with the swaggering ease of middle-aged men. They all pressed close to their newly returned companion as he muttered a quick update in the old man’s ear, then turned as one to leer at Toby, perched wide-eyed on the hearth stone. The old man grinned right along with them, his toasting fork still held aloft over the sizzling pan.
source_text: The walls and ceiling of the room were perfectly black with age and dirt. There was a deal table before the fire: upon which were a candle, stuck in a ginger-beer bottle, two or three pewter pots, a loaf and butter, and a plate. In a frying-pan, which was on the fire, and which was secured to the mantelshelf by a string, some sausages were cooking; and standing over them, with a toasting-fork in his hand, was a very old shrivelled Jew, whose villainous-looking and repulsive face was obscured by a quantity of matted red hair. He was dressed in a greasy flannel gown, with his throat bare; and seemed to be dividing his attention between the frying-pan and the clothes-horse, over which a great number of silk handkerchiefs were hanging. Several rough beds made of old sacks, were huddled side by side on the floor. Seated round the table were four or five boys, none older than the Dodger, smoking long clay pipes, and drinking spirits with the air of middle-aged men. These all crowded about their associate as he whispered a few words to the Jew; and then turned round and grinned at Oliver. So did the Jew himself, toasting-fork in hand.

text: Of the two women present, one was well into her senior years, yet she carried herself every bit as straight and unyielding as the tall-backed oak chair she sat in. Her clothing was assembled with meticulous care, a charming blend of vintage silhouettes and small, subtle nods to current fashion that only emphasized the pleasant charm of the older styles rather than weakening their effect. She sat with quiet stateliness, her hands folded neatly on the table in front of her. Her eyes, which age had barely dimmed at all, were fixed attentively on her younger companion.
source_text: Of the two ladies, one was well advanced in years; but the high-backed oaken chair in which she sat, was not more upright than she. Dressed with the utmost nicety and precision, in a quaint mixture of by-gone costume, with some slight concessions to the prevailing taste, which rather served to point the old style pleasantly than to impair its effect, she sat, in a stately manner, with her hands folded on the table before her. Her eyes (and age had dimmed but little of their brightness) were attentively upon her young companion.

text: The whole building was thick with grime and long neglect. Upstairs, the chambers boasted towering carved wooden mantels, wide solid doorframes, wood-panelled walls, and decorative plaster cornices running along the ceiling’s edge. Though every surface was stained black by years of unaddressed dust and disuse, faint traces of carvings and ornamentation still peeked through the grime. From these faded clues, Luka guessed that long before old Silas, the ragged fence who’d taken him in, had been born, the house had belonged to far more refined people. It must once have been bright and handsome, he thought, hard as that was to believe beneath its current shroud of drear and decay.
source_text: It was a very dirty place. The rooms upstairs had great high wooden chimney-pieces and large doors, with panelled walls and cornices to the ceiling; which, although they were black with neglect and dust, were ornamented in various ways. From all of these tokens Oliver concluded that a long time ago, before the old Jew was born, it had belonged to better people, and had perhaps been quite gay and handsome: dismal and dreary as it looked now.

text: He fumbled for a match, lit the hearth fire, and rammed the cudgel straight into the flames. Strands of hair clung to its tip, which seared and crumpled into fine ash before a draft caught the charred bits and sent them swirling up the flue. For all his usual toughness, even that small, fleeting movement made his skin crawl; he kept a grip on the weapon until it split apart, then shoveled the broken pieces onto the embers to burn through and smolder down to nothing. He scrubbed his skin raw and brushed frantically at his clothes; when some dark marks refused to fade no matter how hard he rubbed, he cut those patches out entirely and tossed them into the fire too. He couldn’t believe how far the stains had spread across every surface of the room. Even the dog’s paws were caked with blood.
source_text: He struck a light, kindled a fire, and thrust the club into it. There was hair upon the end, which blazed and shrunk into a light cinder, and, caught by the air, whirled up the chimney. Even that frightened him, sturdy as he was; but he held the weapon till it broke, and then piled it on the coals to burn away, and smoulder into ashes. He washed himself, and rubbed his clothes; there were spots that would not be removed, but he cut the pieces out, and burnt them. How those stains were dispersed about the room! The very feet of the dog were bloody.

text: The first indication he had of what was coming arrived as a low, hollow thud, followed immediately by his hat flying clean off his head to land at the far end of the room. That opening gambit left his head bare, and the quick, capable woman locked one arm tight around his throat before raining a flurry of blows on his exposed scalp with her other hand, each strike landing with surprising strength and skill. When she tired of that, she mixed things up a little, scratching at his face and yanking hard at his hair; once she’d dished out as much punishment as she thought his offence warranted, she shoved him backward over a chair that just happened to be perfectly positioned for the task, then dared him to ever spout off about his prerogative again if he had the guts.
source_text: The first proof he experienced of the fact, was conveyed in a hollow sound, immediately succeeded by the sudden flying off of his hat to the opposite end of the room. This preliminary proceeding laying bare his head, the expert lady, clasping him tightly round the throat with one hand, inflicted a shower of blows (dealt with singular vigour and dexterity) upon it with the other. This done, she created a little variety by scratching his face, and tearing his hair; and, having, by this time, inflicted as much punishment as she deemed necessary for the offence, she pushed him over a chair, which was luckily well situated for the purpose: and defied him to talk about his prerogative again, if he dared.

text: The tavern landlord laughed right back along with the old man, then turned to rejoin his waiting guests. The second the Jew was left by himself, his face slipped back into the same anxious, preoccupied expression it had carried earlier. After a few moments of quiet deliberation, he flagged down a hack cabriolet and ordered the driver to make for Bethnal Green. He sent the driver off when they were still roughly a quarter of a mile out from Mr. Sikes’s residence, and covered the short remaining stretch of the journey on foot.
source_text: The landlord reciprocated the old man’s laugh; and returned to his guests. The Jew was no sooner alone, than his countenance resumed its former expression of anxiety and thought. After a brief reflection, he called a hack-cabriolet, and bade the man drive towards Bethnal Green. He dismissed him within some quarter of a mile of Mr. Sikes’s residence, and performed the short remainder of the distance on foot.

text: The dark had settled so thick it could almost be touched. Fog clung far heavier than it had in the first hours of the night, and the air hummed with dampness; though no rain fell, Luka’s hair and eyebrows crusted stiff with half-frozen drifting vapor within minutes of stepping outside the cottage door. They crossed the weathered stone bridge, making straight for the faint warm lights he had spied through the mist earlier. The glow was not far off, and as they moved at a sharp, steady clip, they covered the remaining ground quickly, pulling up on the edge of Harlow’s Ford before Luka could fully register how fast they’d walked.
source_text: It was now intensely dark. The fog was much heavier than it had been in the early part of the night; and the atmosphere was so damp, that, although no rain fell, Oliver’s hair and eyebrows, within a few minutes after leaving the house, had become stiff with the half-frozen moisture that was floating about. They crossed the bridge, and kept on towards the lights which he had seen before. They were at no great distance off; and, as they walked pretty briskly, they soon arrived at Chertsey.

text: “Oh, my brother! My poor, sweet, innocent little brother!” Nancy cried out, dissolving into tears as she twisted her tiny basket and front door key in her hands, nearly crumpling both in her searing, agonized distress. “What’s happened to him? Where did they take him? Oh please, have a heart, tell me what they’ve done with my dear boy, gentlemen—please, I’m begging you, gentlemen, won’t you, gentlemen?”
source_text: “Oh, my brother! My poor, dear, sweet, innocent little brother!” exclaimed Nancy, bursting into tears, and wringing the little basket and the street-door key in an agony of distress. “What has become of him! Where have they taken him to! Oh, do have pity, and tell me what’s been done with the dear boy, gentlemen; do, gentlemen, if you please, gentlemen!”

text: “So I *do* do exactly what she tells me to,” Mr. Chitling answered. “I never would’ve gotten locked up if I hadn’t taken her advice, that’s for sure. But the whole mess worked out pretty well for you, didn’t it, Fagin? And what’s six weeks of that time, anyway? Everyone’s got to serve a little stretch sooner or later, right? Why not get it over with in the winter, when you don’t even feel like going out wandering around all that much? Eh, Fagin?”
source_text: “So I _do_ do as she bids me,” replied Mr. Chitling; “I shouldn’t have been milled, if it hadn’t been for her advice. But it turned out a good job for you; didn’t it, Fagin! And what’s six weeks of it? It must come, some time or another, and why not in the winter time when you don’t want to go out a-walking so much; eh, Fagin?”

text: That night, the one after, and the third as well, the spy waited fully booted and outfitted in his carter’s clothes, ready to set off the instant Fagin gave the word. Six long, dreary nights dragged by, each ending with Fagin tramping home with a disappointed frown, muttering briefly that the time had not yet come. On the seventh, he returned far earlier than usual, thrumming with an exultation he could not conceal. It was Sunday.
source_text: That night, and the next, and the next again, the spy sat booted and equipped in his carter’s dress: ready to turn out at a word from Fagin. Six nights passed—six long weary nights—and on each, Fagin came home with a disappointed face, and briefly intimated that it was not yet time. On the seventh, he returned earlier, and with an exultation he could not conceal. It was Sunday.

text: “But even if he has acted wickedly,” Rose went on earnestly, “think of how young he still is. He may never have known a mother’s tender love, or the quiet comfort of a steady home; harsh mistreatment and blows, or the pangs of hunger when there was no bread to eat, may have driven him to fall in with men who forced him into wrongdoing. Aunt, dear aunt, for mercy’s sake, stop to consider this before you let them drag this ailing child off to a prison that will, no matter what, be the grave of every chance he has to mend his ways. Oh! As you love me, and know that I never felt the loss of parents thanks to your goodness and affection, but that I might well have been in his position, just as helpless and unprotected as this poor boy, have pity on him before it is too late!”
source_text: “But even if he has been wicked,” pursued Rose, “think how young he is; think that he may never have known a mother’s love, or the comfort of a home; that ill-usage and blows, or the want of bread, may have driven him to herd with men who have forced him to guilt. Aunt, dear aunt, for mercy’s sake, think of this, before you let them drag this sick child to a prison, which in any case must be the grave of all his chances of amendment. Oh! as you love me, and know that I have never felt the want of parents in your goodness and affection, but that I might have done so, and might have been equally helpless and unprotected with this poor child, have pity upon him before it is too late!”

text: Whether an exceedingly faint widening of the eye is enough to subdue pauper inmates, who, kept on thin rations, are rarely in any state to put up resistance; or whether the widowed Mrs. Hale was particularly impervious to sharp, eagle-eyed glares, is a matter of personal opinion. What is unassailable fact, however, is that the workhouse matron was not at all cowed by Mr. Harlow’s thunderous scowl. On the contrary, she greeted the expression with frank disdain, and even tossed out a laugh in response, a sound that carried all the ring of genuine amusement.
source_text: Whether an exceedingly small expansion of eye be sufficient to quell paupers, who, being lightly fed, are in no very high condition; or whether the late Mrs. Corney was particularly proof against eagle glances; are matters of opinion. The matter of fact, is, that the matron was in no way overpowered by Mr. Bumble’s scowl, but, on the contrary, treated it with great disdain, and even raised a laugh thereat, which sounded as though it were genuine.

text: Most of the shops lining the back lanes and side avenues she threaded through were already shuttering for the night, as she made her way from Spitalfields toward London’s West End. The clock struck ten, sharpening her already frayed impatience. She tore down the narrow pavement, elbowing passersby left and right, and darted almost beneath the heads of cart horses to cross crowded thoroughfares, where clusters of pedestrians lingered, eyes fixed on the flow of traffic for their own chance to make the same crossing.
source_text: Many of the shops were already closing in the back lanes and avenues through which she tracked her way, in making from Spitalfields towards the West-End of London. The clock struck ten, increasing her impatience. She tore along the narrow pavement: elbowing the passengers from side to side; and darting almost under the horses’ heads, crossed crowded streets, where clusters of persons were eagerly watching their opportunity to do the like.

text: This exchange took place between the two men who’d stumbled on the burglars mid-intrusion, and an itinerant tinsmith who’d been dozing in a disused outbuilding, roused along with his two scruffy mixed-breed hounds to join the manhunt. Mr. Hale pulled double duty as butler and estate steward for the manor’s elderly mistress; Tobin was a general handyman who’d entered her service as a small, wide-eyed child, and was still treated as a promising young lad even though he was already well past thirty.
source_text: This dialogue was held between the two men who had surprised the burglars, and a travelling tinker who had been sleeping in an outhouse, and who had been roused, together with his two mongrel curs, to join in the pursuit. Mr. Giles acted in the double capacity of butler and steward to the old lady of the mansion; Brittles was a lad of all-work: who, having entered her service a mere child, was treated as a promising young boy still, though he was something past thirty.

text: “I’ve got more to tell you,” Fagin said, once he’d calmed the girl down with a string of warm, friendly nods and soft, mumbled words of encouragement. “I have a friend I’m certain can grant that dearest wish of yours, and get you set up exactly where you need to be. You can pick whichever part of the work feels like the best fit for you to start out with, and you’ll be taught how to do every other part of the business too.”
source_text: “I’ll tell you more,” said Fagin, after he had reassured the girl, by dint of friendly nods and muttered encouragements. “I have got a friend that I think can gratify your darling wish, and put you in the right way, where you can take whatever department of the business you think will suit you best at first, and be taught all the others.”

text: “What mill? C’mon, you know the mill—the one so tiny it can run perfectly fine inside a jail cell, for crying out loud. It always works better when folks are down on their luck than when times are good, too—you can’t find workers to hire when everyone’s already got enough money to pass on the gig. But hey,” the young man said, “you’re hungry, and I’ll get you something to eat. I’m pretty strapped myself right now—only got a pound and a bit of change on me, but whatever I’ve got, I’ll pony up and cover it. C’mon, get on your feet. There we go! Alright, Morrice, let’s move!”
source_text: “What mill! Why, _the_ mill—the mill as takes up so little room that it’ll work inside a Stone Jug; and always goes better when the wind’s low with people, than when it’s high; acos then they can’t get workmen. But come,” said the young gentleman; “you want grub, and you shall have it. I’m at low-water-mark myself—only one bob and a magpie; but, as far as it goes, I’ll fork out and stump. Up with you on your pins. There! Now then! Morrice!”

text: As it chanced, Mr. Hale was at that very moment poring over the lead editorial of that day’s morning broadsheet, which referenced a recent ruling he had handed down and, for the three hundred and fiftieth time, urged that he be afforded special, dedicated consideration by the Secretary of State for the Home Department. He was already in a sour mood, and he lifted his gaze with a sharp, simmering scowl.
source_text: Now, it so happened that Mr. Fang was at that moment perusing a leading article in a newspaper of the morning, adverting to some recent decision of his, and commending him, for the three hundred and fiftieth time, to the special and particular notice of the Secretary of State for the Home Department. He was out of temper; and he looked up with an angry scowl.

text: When the Jew saw that Mr. Chitling was thoroughly riled up, he hurried to reassure him that no one was laughing at him; to prove the whole company was keeping a solemn demeanor, he called on Master Bates, the main culprit of the teasing. Unfortunately, the second Charley opened his mouth to insist he had never been more serious in his life, he could not hold back a loud, raucous peal of laughter. The wronged Mr. Chitling skipped all formalities entirely, charged across the room, and swung a blow at the offender. Charley, skilled at dodging pursuit, ducked just in time, so the punch landed square on the chest of the merry old gentleman instead. He staggered backward until he hit the wall, where he stood panting for breath, as a shocked Mr. Chitling looked on in intense dismay.
source_text: The Jew, perceiving that Mr. Chitling was considerably roused, hastened to assure him that nobody was laughing; and to prove the gravity of the company, appealed to Master Bates, the principal offender. But, unfortunately, Charley, in opening his mouth to reply that he was never more serious in his life, was unable to prevent the escape of such a violent roar, that the abused Mr. Chitling, without any preliminary ceremonies, rushed across the room and aimed a blow at the offender; who, being skilful in evading pursuit, ducked to avoid it, and chose his time so well that it lighted on the chest of the merry old gentleman, and caused him to stagger to the wall, where he stood panting for breath, while Mr. Chitling looked on in intense dismay.

text: “No, you’re quite right, it isn’t, my friend,” Mr. Brownlow answered. “But the business I have with this man is deeply tied to the boy, after all. Since this child witnessed him at the peak of both his success and his vicious misdeeds, I believe it’s only proper—even if it means subjecting him to a bit of pain and fear in the process—that he see the man now.”
source_text: “It is not indeed, my friend,” rejoined Mr. Brownlow; “but my business with this man is intimately connected with him; and as this child has seen him in the full career of his success and villainy, I think it as well—even at the cost of some pain and fear—that he should see him now.”

text: It wasn’t as if, the entire time, his mind ever stopped being hounded by the heavy, suffocating awareness of an open grave waiting right at his feet. That dread hung over him constantly, but it felt fuzzy and unfocused, like he couldn’t pin his mind down to really grapple with it. So even as he shook all over, his skin prickling with feverish heat at the thought of how soon he would die, he found himself counting the iron spikes set before him, puzzling over how the tip of one had snapped clean off, and if someone would eventually fix it or just leave it broken like that. Next, his mind drifted to every grisly detail of the gallows and the execution scaffold—until he paused to stare at a man sprinkling water across the floor to bring down the temperature—before his wandering thoughts pulled him right back to his grim ruminations.
source_text: Not that, all this time, his mind was, for an instant, free from one oppressive overwhelming sense of the grave that opened at his feet; it was ever present to him, but in a vague and general way, and he could not fix his thoughts upon it. Thus, even while he trembled, and turned burning hot at the idea of speedy death, he fell to counting the iron spikes before him, and wondering how the head of one had been broken off, and whether they would mend it, or leave it as it was. Then, he thought of all the horrors of the gallows and the scaffold—and stopped to watch a man sprinkling the floor to cool it—and then went on to think again.

text: “Listen up, you!” Monks snapped back. “His father fell gravely ill in Rome, so his wife—my mother, who’d been estranged from him for years—traveled over from Paris with me in tow. For all I know, she only came to sort out his property; she felt no great fondness for him, and the feeling was entirely mutual. He had no idea we were even there, for his senses had already failed him. He slumbered fitfully until the next day, when he died. Among the papers in his desk were two documents, dated the very first night his illness set in, addressed to you”—he cut his gaze sharply to Mr. Brownlow—“enclosed with a short note to you, with a clear marking on the package’s cover that it was not to be delivered until after his death. One of those papers was a letter to that girl Agnes; the other was a will.”
source_text: “Listen then! You!” returned Monks. “His father being taken ill at Rome, was joined by his wife, my mother, from whom he had been long separated, who went from Paris and took me with her—to look after his property, for what I know, for she had no great affection for him, nor he for her. He knew nothing of us, for his senses were gone, and he slumbered on till next day, when he died. Among the papers in his desk, were two, dated on the night his illness first came on, directed to yourself”; he addressed himself to Mr. Brownlow; “and enclosed in a few short lines to you, with an intimation on the cover of the package that it was not to be forwarded till after he was dead. One of these papers was a letter to this girl Agnes; the other a will.”

text: “The note?—A crumpled sheet scrawled over front and back, ink smudged where tears had bled through the page, holding a broken, repentant confession and frantic pleas for God to have mercy on her. He’d fed the young woman a lie about a private, unspoken trouble he would reveal when the time was right, which kept him from wedding her right away; so she’d held on, waiting and trusting him completely, until she’d given more than she could ever take back, the one thing no apology or promise could ever restore. When she found the note, she was barely three months from giving birth. He’d written of all the plans he’d made to shield her from disgrace if he’d survived, begging her, if he perished, never to curse his memory, or fear that the weight of their mistake would fall on her or their unborn child; every last bit of the wrong, he said, was his alone. He recalled the afternoon he’d pressed the tiny enamel locket and thin gold band into her palm, the band carved with her given name, a space left empty for his surname that he’d dreamed of adding one day—he begged her to still hold onto it, to keep it resting against her breast just as she always had—then the words devolved into frantic repetition, the same phrases looping again and again, like he’d lost his mind. I think he truly had.”
source_text: “The letter?—A sheet of paper crossed and crossed again, with a penitent confession, and prayers to God to help her. He had palmed a tale on the girl that some secret mystery—to be explained one day—prevented his marrying her just then; and so she had gone on, trusting patiently to him, until she trusted too far, and lost what none could ever give her back. She was, at that time, within a few months of her confinement. He told her all he had meant to do, to hide her shame, if he had lived, and prayed her, if he died, not to curse his memory, or think the consequences of their sin would be visited on her or their young child; for all the guilt was his. He reminded her of the day he had given her the little locket and the ring with her christian name engraved upon it, and a blank left for that which he hoped one day to have bestowed upon her—prayed her yet to keep it, and wear it next her heart, as she had done before—and then ran on, wildly, in the same words, over and over again, as if he had gone distracted. I believe he had.”

text: “No,” Mr. Bumble remarked approvingly, “no, you could not. You are a deeply humane woman, Mrs. Mann.” At that, she set her glass down on the surface between them. “I shall mention your thoughtfulness to the board at the earliest opportunity, Mrs. Mann.” He drew the tumbler of gin and water toward his own side as he spoke. “You feel as any tender mother would, Mrs. Mann.” He stirred the drink slowly, the spoon clinking soft against the glass. “I—I drink to your health with great cheer, Mrs. Mann.” With that, he tipped the glass back and swallowed half its contents in one go.
source_text: “No”; said Mr. Bumble approvingly; “no, you could not. You are a humane woman, Mrs. Mann.” (Here she set down the glass.) “I shall take a early opportunity of mentioning it to the board, Mrs. Mann.” (He drew it towards him.) “You feel as a mother, Mrs. Mann.” (He stirred the gin-and-water.) “I—I drink your health with cheerfulness, Mrs. Mann”; and he swallowed half of it.

text: Before long, the group reached Silas Veck’s cramped, candlelit lodgings, where they found Joss Rooke and Ned Pim deep in their fifteenth round of cribbage. It hardly needs noting that Pim, the greener and more hapless of the two, came out the loser, surrendering his very last sixpence in the process—to the great amusement of the younger lads loitering by the hearth. Rooke, clearly flustered to be caught passing the time with a man so far beneath him in both street reputation and plain wits, gave a slow, lazy yawn, asked after Griff Harker’s whereabouts, then snatched his hat from the side table and made to leave.
source_text: In due course, they arrived at Fagin’s abode, where they found Toby Crackit and Mr. Chitling intent upon their fifteenth game at cribbage, which it is scarcely necessary to say the latter gentleman lost, and with it, his fifteenth and last sixpence: much to the amusement of his young friends. Mr. Crackit, apparently somewhat ashamed at being found relaxing himself with a gentleman so much his inferior in station and mental endowments, yawned, and inquiring after Sikes, took up his hat to go.

text: Good God! What sharp, lurching shock sent blood thrumming so hard through his heart it numbed his tongue and rooted his feet to the floor? There—there—at the window, so close he could have reached out and brushed his sleeve before he stumbled back: eyes fixed deep into the room, locking directly with his, stood the old moneylender! And hovering just at his elbow, his face drained white with rage, fear, or some tangled mix of both, were the scowling, sharp-jawed features of the very man who had accosted him in the tavern courtyard two days prior.
source_text: Good Heaven! what was that, which sent the blood tingling to his heart, and deprived him of his voice, and of power to move! There—there—at the window—close before him—so close, that he could have almost touched him before he started back: with his eyes peering into the room, and meeting his: there stood the Jew! And beside him, white with rage or fear, or both, were the scowling features of the man who had accosted him in the inn-yard.

text: The room where Mr. Sikes posed this question wasn’t the same space he’d rented before the Chertsey expedition, though it sat in the same part of town, only a short distance from his former lodgings. It wasn’t nearly as pleasant a place to live as his old quarters, either: it was a shabby, poorly furnished, very cramped apartment, lit only by one tiny window set into the sloped roof, and backed directly onto a narrow, filthy lane. There were plenty of other signs that the self-styled gentleman had fallen on hard times recently, too: the severe lack of furniture, complete absence of any small comforts, and the disappearance of every small portable belonging, from extra clothes to bed linens, all spoke to a state of dire poverty. If those signs had needed any corroboration at all, the gaunt, wasted condition of Mr. Sikes himself would have confirmed them fully.
source_text: The room in which Mr. Sikes propounded this question, was not one of those he had tenanted, previous to the Chertsey expedition, although it was in the same quarter of the town, and was situated at no great distance from his former lodgings. It was not, in appearance, so desirable a habitation as his old quarters: being a mean and badly-furnished apartment, of very limited size; lighted only by one small window in the shelving roof, and abutting on a close and dirty lane. Nor were there wanting other indications of the good gentleman’s having gone down in the world of late: for a great scarcity of furniture, and total absence of comfort, together with the disappearance of all such small moveables as spare clothes and linen, bespoke a state of extreme poverty; while the meagre and attenuated condition of Mr. Sikes himself would have fully confirmed these symptoms, if they had stood in any need of corroboration.

text: When Brittles nodded in confirmation and pointed out the correct building, the stout man walked back to the garden gate to help his companion stow their small gig. Brittles held a lantern aloft to light their path the entire time, utterly caught up in wide-eyed admiration. Once that task was wrapped up, the pair headed back into the house, were escorted into a parlor, and peeled off their thick overcoats and hats, finally revealing their true, unobscured appearances.
source_text: Brittles replying in the affirmative, and pointing out the building, the portly man stepped back to the garden-gate, and helped his companion to put up the gig: while Brittles lighted them, in a state of great admiration. This done, they returned to the house, and, being shown into a parlour, took off their great-coats and hats, and showed like what they were.

text: Jasper stepped across the rutted front walk, glancing up at the cottage window to confirm no sign of what lay below could be glimpsed from the country road beyond the oak fence. The linen curtain was still drawn tight, the same one Lila would have thrown wide at this hour to welcome the warm daylight she would never see again. It rested nearly straight beneath that sill. He knew that better than anyone. God, the summer sun was beating down so fiercely it seemed to set that very patch of ground glowing.
source_text: He crossed over, and glanced up at the window, to be sure that nothing was visible from the outside. There was the curtain still drawn, which she would have opened to admit the light she never saw again. It lay nearly under there. _He_ knew that. God, how the sun poured down upon the very spot!

text: “So these new friends we’re discussing,” Mr. Brownlow said, “were a retired naval officer who’d wrapped up his time on active duty. His wife had passed almost six months back, leaving him to raise two children—they’d had more kids over the years, but happily, only those two had survived out of their entire family. Both were daughters: the elder was a lovely, vibrant nineteen-year-old, and the younger was little more than a toddler, barely two or three years old.”
source_text: “These new friends, then,” said Mr. Brownlow, “were a naval officer retired from active service, whose wife had died some half-a-year before, and left him with two children—there had been more, but, of all their family, happily but two survived. They were both daughters; one a beautiful creature of nineteen, and the other a mere child of two or three years old.”

text: He traced over each memory again. He had dragged them unbidden to the surface, and tucking back the heavy veil that had hidden them for decades felt all but impossible. Faces of old companions, bitter rivals, and dozens of near-strangers pressed forward, peering uninvited from the throng; there were the soft, dewy features of girls once in the first flush of youth, now lined and weathered by decades of life; there were faces the grave had claimed and warped, yet which memory, outstripping death’s cold hold, still clothed in their first bright bloom: the glint of a laughing eye, the curve of a quick, warm smile, the glow of a spirit shining through its mortal shell, murmuring of a loveliness that outlasts the grave, altered only to burn brighter, snatched from earth to hang as a quiet lantern, spilling soft, golden light over the road that leads to the skies.
source_text: He wandered over them again. He had called them into view, and it was not easy to replace the shroud that had so long concealed them. There were the faces of friends, and foes, and of many that had been almost strangers peering intrusively from the crowd; there were the faces of young and blooming girls that were now old women; there were faces that the grave had changed and closed upon, but which the mind, superior to its power, still dressed in their old freshness and beauty, calling back the lustre of the eyes, the brightness of the smile, the beaming of the soul through its mask of clay, and whispering of beauty beyond the tomb, changed but to be heightened, and taken from earth only to be set up as a light, to shed a soft and gentle glow upon the path to Heaven.

text: “Ah,” the country physician said, shaking with hearty, unreserved laughter, “that’s hardly a tricky matter at all. But let us circle back to the boy in the next room. The most critical term of our arrangement still hangs unaddressed. He’ll wake in an hour or so, I’d wager; even though I told that thick-skulled constable posted downstairs he must not be moved or spoken to on pain of worsening his injuries to fatal degrees, I believe we may converse with him without risk now. Here is my condition: I will question him while you are present, and if, from his testimony, we decide, and I can demonstrate to your cool, practical satisfaction that he is a truly unrepentant, thoroughgoing miscreant (which is far more than possible), he will be left to face whatever fate awaits him, without any further intervention on my part, under any circumstances.”
source_text: “Well,” said the doctor, laughing heartily, “that is no very difficult matter. But to return to this boy. The great point of our agreement is yet to come. He will wake in an hour or so, I dare say; and although I have told that thick-headed constable-fellow downstairs that he musn’t be moved or spoken to, on peril of his life, I think we may converse with him without danger. Now I make this stipulation—that I shall examine him in your presence, and that, if, from what he says, we judge, and I can show to the satisfaction of your cool reason, that he is a real and thorough bad one (which is more than possible), he shall be left to his fate, without any farther interference on my part, at all events.”

text: He scanned the area around him and spotted a house not too far off, close enough that he might still be able to stumble his way to it. The people inside would surely see how awful his condition was and take pity on him, he reasoned; even if they turned him away without a second thought, dying within reach of other people was still far better than drawing his last breath all alone in the empty, isolated fields. He pulled together every last shred of strength he had left for one final effort, and directed his unsteady, faltering steps toward the building.
source_text: He looked about, and saw that at no great distance there was a house, which perhaps he could reach. Pitying his condition, they might have compassion on him; and if they did not, it would be better, he thought, to die near human beings, than in the lonely open fields. He summoned up all his strength for one last trial, and bent his faltering steps towards it.

text: “Meat, madam, meat,” Bumble replied, his tone sharp and stern with deliberate emphasis. “You’ve overfed that boy, madam. You’ve nurtured a false sense of self and spirit in him, something entirely unfit for someone of his low station. The board, Mrs. Sowerberry—they’re practical, levelheaded philosophers, they’ll tell you the same. What do paupers need with souls or spirit, anyway? It’s more than generous of us to let them keep their living bodies as it is. If you’d kept that boy on nothing but gruel, madam, this whole mess never would have happened.”
source_text: “Meat, ma’am, meat,” replied Bumble, with stern emphasis. “You’ve overfed him, ma’am. You’ve raised a artificial soul and spirit in him, ma’am unbecoming a person of his condition: as the board, Mrs. Sowerberry, who are practical philosophers, will tell you. What have paupers to do with soul or spirit? It’s quite enough that we let ’em have live bodies. If you had kept the boy on gruel, ma’am, this would never have happened.”

text: “Oh God!” the man cried, tears bursting from him as he crumpled to his knees before the body of his dead wife, “Kneel, all of you—kneel down around her, every last one, and mark what I say. I tell you she was starved to death. I never knew how worn thin she was till the fever took her, and then her bones jutted sharp through her paper-thin skin. There was no fire in the hearth, no tallow candle lit on the shelf; she died in total dark—total dark! She couldn’t even make out her children’s faces, even as we heard her gasping their names between ragged breaths. I begged for scraps for her in the streets, and they locked me up for vagrancy. When I came back, she was already breathing her last, and all the blood in my heart has turned to dust, for they starved her to death. I swear it before the God that saw every moment of it! They starved her!” He tangled his fingers deep in his matted hair, let out a raw, guttural scream, and collapsed grovelling on the floorboards: his eyes fixed and unseeing, white foam flecking his lips.
source_text: “Ah!” said the man: bursting into tears, and sinking on his knees at the feet of the dead woman; “kneel down, kneel down—kneel round her, every one of you, and mark my words! I say she was starved to death. I never knew how bad she was, till the fever came upon her; and then her bones were starting through the skin. There was neither fire nor candle; she died in the dark—in the dark! She couldn’t even see her children’s faces, though we heard her gasping out their names. I begged for her in the streets: and they sent me to prison. When I came back, she was dying; and all the blood in my heart has dried up, for they starved her to death. I swear it before the God that saw it! They starved her!” He twined his hands in his hair; and, with a loud scream, rolled grovelling upon the floor: his eyes fixed, and the foam covering his lips.

text: “He’s a boy with a noble core and such a warm, open heart,” Rose said, a faint flush coloring her cheeks. “Whatever higher power saw fit to put him through trials far heavier than any child his years should bear also planted such deep, gentle affection and thoughtful, generous feeling in his chest that many people who’ve lived six times as long as he has would be proud to call those traits their own.”
source_text: “He is a child of a noble nature and a warm heart,” said Rose, colouring; “and that Power which has thought fit to try him beyond his years, has planted in his breast affections and feelings which would do honour to many who have numbered his days six times over.”

text: The crowd surged forward from the front line, pushing onward, onward, onward in a roiling, straining tide of furious faces. Harsh, blazing torches dotted the throng here and there, throwing their rage and raw, boiling passion into sharp, unflinching relief. The mob had already forced their way into the buildings lining the far side of the ditch: window sashes were flung open, or ripped right out of their frames entirely. Every window held rows upon rows of staring faces, while clump after clump of people clung tight to every rooftop, packed shoulder to shoulder. Every small bridge in view—all three of them—sagged under the heft of the crowd crammed across its surface. Still the tide of people kept pouring forward, all scrambling to find some nook or vantage point where they could hurl their shouts, and catch even a split-second glimpse of the hated wretch.
source_text: On pressed the people from the front—on, on, on, in a strong struggling current of angry faces, with here and there a glaring torch to lighten them up, and show them out in all their wrath and passion. The houses on the opposite side of the ditch had been entered by the mob; sashes were thrown up, or torn bodily out; there were tiers and tiers of faces in every window; cluster upon cluster of people clinging to every house-top. Each little bridge (and there were three in sight) bent beneath the weight of the crowd upon it. Still the current poured on to find some nook or hole from which to vent their shouts, and only for an instant see the wretch.

text: The Jew flashed a hideous, sneering smile, patted Oliver lightly on the top of his head, and told him that if he kept his head down, stayed out of trouble, and applied himself properly to the work they assigned him, he was certain the two of them would end up being very good friends before long. After that, he picked up his hat, pulled on a tattered, patch-riddled old overcoat, stepped out of the room, and turned the key in the lock to fasten the door shut behind him.
source_text: The Jew, smiling hideously, patted Oliver on the head, and said, that if he kept himself quiet, and applied himself to business, he saw they would be very good friends yet. Then, taking his hat, and covering himself with an old patched great-coat, he went out, and locked the room-door behind him.

text: Yet it came to pass, as it so often does when people are thrown into each other’s company under such circumstances, that Mr. Bumble found himself regularly struck by an irresistible urge to steal a glance at the stranger beside him. Every time he gave in to that impulse, he would dart his eyes away in flustered confusion, only to find the other man had been sneaking a look right back at him in that same moment. Bumble’s awkwardness deepened when he noted the remarkable expression in the stranger’s gaze: it was sharp and bright, yet shadowed by a scowl of cold distrust and suspicion, unlike anything he had ever observed before, and deeply repulsive to look at.
source_text: It so happened, however: as it will happen very often, when men fall into company under such circumstances: that Mr. Bumble felt, every now and then, a powerful inducement, which he could not resist, to steal a look at the stranger: and that whenever he did so, he withdrew his eyes, in some confusion, to find that the stranger was at that moment stealing a look at him. Mr. Bumble’s awkwardness was enhanced by the very remarkable expression of the stranger’s eye, which was keen and bright, but shadowed by a scowl of distrust and suspicion, unlike anything he had ever observed before, and repulsive to behold.

text: The night was raw, damp, and wind-scoured when the Jew emerged from his den, buttoning his thick greatcoat tight around his shrivelled frame and yanking the collar up over his ears to fully obscure the lower half of his face. He paused on the step as the door was locked and chained behind him, listening closely while the boys finished fastening everything securely, and waited until the patter of their retreating footsteps was no longer audible before slinking down the street as fast as he could go.
source_text: It was a chill, damp, windy night, when the Jew: buttoning his great-coat tight round his shrivelled body, and pulling the collar up over his ears so as completely to obscure the lower part of his face: emerged from his den. He paused on the step as the door was locked and chained behind him; and having listened while the boys made all secure, and until their retreating footsteps were no longer audible, slunk down the street as quickly as he could.

text: “Wait and see what the Home Secretary has to say to you lot on the bench if I’ve got anything to do with it, won’t we?” retorted Mr. Dawkins. “Come on now! What’s all this holdup? I’d be much obliged if the magistrates would wrap this little business up sharpish, instead of making me loiter here while they flick through their newspapers. I’ve got an appointment with a gentleman down in the City, see, and I’m a man of my word, dead punctual when it comes to business matters. He’ll up and leave if I don’t show up when I’m supposed to, and then I reckon there’ll be a damages claim against the folks that kept me here, won’t there? Oh no, certainly not!”
source_text: “We’ll see wot the Secretary of State for the Home Affairs has got to say to the beaks, if I don’t,” replied Mr. Dawkins. “Now then! Wot is this here business? I shall thank the madg’strates to dispose of this here little affair, and not to keep me while they read the paper, for I’ve got an appointment with a genelman in the City, and as I am a man of my word and wery punctual in business matters, he’ll go away if I ain’t there to my time, and then pr’aps ther won’t be an action for damage against them as kep me away. Oh no, certainly not!”

text: Stopped at last! That was a deft, well-timed blow. He falls hard onto the pavement, and the crowd surges eagerly around him, each new arrival jostling and struggling past the others to catch a glimpse. “Stand aside!” “Give him a little air!” “Nonsense! He doesn’t deserve it.” “Where’s the gentleman?” “There he is, coming down the street now.” “Make room there for the gentleman!” “Is this the boy, sir?” “Yes.”
source_text: Stopped at last! A clever blow. He is down upon the pavement; and the crowd eagerly gather round him: each new comer, jostling and struggling with the others to catch a glimpse. “Stand aside!” “Give him a little air!” “Nonsense! he don’t deserve it.” “Where’s the gentleman?” “Here he is, coming down the street.” “Make room there for the gentleman!” “Is this the boy, sir!” “Yes.”

text: As he spoke, Mr. Hale glanced over at Toby, but that soft-spoken young man, naturally modest to a fault, likely considered himself far too unimportant to be the intended recipient of the question, so he assumed the inquiry could not possibly be meant for him; in any case, he offered no reply whatsoever. Mr. Hale then turned an imploring look toward the itinerant tinker, but the man had suddenly fallen fast asleep. Asking any of the women in the room was entirely out of the question.
source_text: Mr. Giles, as he spoke, looked at Brittles; but that young man, being naturally modest, probably considered himself nobody, and so held that the inquiry could not have any application to him; at all events, he tendered no reply. Mr. Giles directed an appealing glance at the tinker; but he had suddenly fallen asleep. The women were out of the question.

text: The second the room door clicked shut behind the departing guest, Mr. Grimwig lifted his head, propped one of his chair’s hind legs as a pivot, and spun three distinct, even circles, bracing himself with his walking stick and the table edge the entire time without ever rising from his seat. Once he’d finished that odd little maneuver, he pushed himself to his feet and limped as fast as he could back and forth across the room at least a dozen times, then halted abruptly right in front of Rose and kissed her with no advance warning whatsoever.
source_text: When the room-door closed behind him, Mr. Grimwig lifted up his head, and converting one of the hind legs of his chair into a pivot, described three distinct circles with the assistance of his stick and the table; sitting in it all the time. After performing this evolution, he rose and limped as fast as he could up and down the room at least a dozen times, and then stopping suddenly before Rose, kissed her without the slightest preface.

text: Even though every one of her internal tussles always landed her on the same final conclusion, those fights kept creeping back unbidden, over and over, and left clear marks of their presence. In the span of just a handful of days, she’d already grown gaunt and ashen-faced. Sometimes she paid no attention at all to the things unfolding right in front of her, or sat silent through discussions where she would have been the most boisterous, eager participant not long before. Other times, she’d laugh with no real joy behind the sound, or chatter loudly out of nowhere, only to drop quiet a second later: slumped, dejected, head resting in her palms as she spiraled into her own thoughts. The obvious strain it took for her to pull herself back to the present when spoken to spoke louder than any of these little cues could: she was deeply unsettled, her mind fixed on concerns entirely separate, far removed from whatever trivial topics her friends were hashing out around her.
source_text: Though all her mental struggles terminated in this conclusion, they forced themselves upon her, again and again, and left their traces too. She grew pale and thin, even within a few days. At times, she took no heed of what was passing before her, or no part in conversations where once, she would have been the loudest. At other times, she laughed without merriment, and was noisy without a moment afterwards—she sat silent and dejected, brooding with her head upon her hands, while the very effort by which she roused herself, told, more forcibly than even these indications, that she was ill at ease, and that her thoughts were occupied with matters very different and distant from those in the course of discussion by her companions.

text:
“Shhh, now,” Mrs. Maylie murmured, resting her hand lightly on top of Oliver’s head. “You see the world like the child you are, poor little thing. But even so, you’re reminding me of my own duty. I’d lost sight of it for a moment, Oliver, and I hope I can be forgiven for that. I’m old now, and I’ve witnessed enough sickness and death to know how searing the pain is when you’re separated from the people you love most.
I’ve also seen enough to know it’s not always the youngest, best among us who get to stay behind with the folks that adore them. But that truth should bring us comfort even in our grief, you see. Heaven is just, and hard moments like these remind us powerfully that there’s a far brighter world waiting beyond this one, and that the path to it is short. God’s will be done. I love her, and He knows exactly how much.”
source_text: “Hush!” said Mrs. Maylie, laying her hand on Oliver’s head. “You think like a child, poor boy. But you teach me my duty, notwithstanding. I had forgotten it for a moment, Oliver, but I hope I may be pardoned, for I am old, and have seen enough of illness and death to know the agony of separation from the objects of our love. I have seen enough, too, to know that it is not always the youngest and best who are spared to those that love them; but this should give us comfort in our sorrow; for Heaven is just; and such things teach us, impressively, that there is a brighter world than this; and that the passage to it is speedy. God’s will be done! I love her; and He knows how well!”

text: When the courier finished relaying the news, the broad-shouldered man held his gaze unblinking, the sharp focus of his stare softening only slowly as the seconds stretched on, until it went hollow and distant, as if his mind had wandered far from the small, drafty room they stood in. For a long beat he looked caught, unsure if the update should leave him relieved or let down; at last his chest rose and fell in a slow, unclenched breath, he dropped his eyes to the scuffed floorboards beneath his boots, and muttered that the development hardly mattered. He pushed himself up from the splintered oak bench as he spoke, already angling toward the door as if ready to take his leave.
source_text: The man looked fixedly at him when he had given this information, and although he did not withdraw his eyes for some time afterwards, his gaze gradually became vacant and abstracted, and he seemed lost in thought. For some time, he appeared doubtful whether he ought to be relieved or disappointed by the intelligence; but at length he breathed more freely; and withdrawing his eyes, observed that it was no great matter. With that he rose, as if to depart.

text: Oliver mulled it over for a short while, then finally said he thought being a bookseller would be a far better choice. At that, the old gentleman laughed heartily, his shoulders shaking with the force of it, and announced that Oliver had come out with an extremely sharp, clever line. Oliver was quietly pleased to have earned that reaction, even though he had no clue at all what was so noteworthy about the thing he’d just said.
source_text: Oliver considered a little while; and at last said, he should think it would be a much better thing to be a book-seller; upon which the old gentleman laughed heartily, and declared he had said a very good thing. Which Oliver felt glad to have done, though he by no means knew what it was.

text: Whatever Mr. Bumble had intended—and there’s no doubt his motives were perfectly honorable—he ran into the same unlucky quirk we’ve mentioned twice before: the table was round. So as Mr. Bumble shifted his chair a tiny fraction of an inch at a time, he quickly started closing the gap between himself and the matron. He kept inching his way around the outer edge of the table’s circle, and before long, his chair was pulled right up next to the one the matron was sitting in.
source_text: Whatever were Mr. Bumble’s intentions, however (and no doubt they were of the best): it unfortunately happened, as has been twice before remarked, that the table was a round one; consequently Mr. Bumble, moving his chair by little and little, soon began to diminish the distance between himself and the matron; and, continuing to travel round the outer edge of the circle, brought his chair, in time, close to that in which the matron was seated.

text: Mrs. Sowerberry, who had a sharp, practiced knack for all the ins and outs of the undertaking trade, was genuinely impressed by how novel and clever this idea was. But admitting that outright in the moment would have undermined her carefully maintained dignity, so she gave no outward sign of her approval. Instead, she snapped the question sharply, as if chiding him for a careless oversight: why had such an obvious, common-sense suggestion never crossed her husband’s mind any earlier? Mr. Sowerberry correctly read this sharp retort as her full acquiescence to his proposal. They settled the matter quickly: Oliver would be immediately initiated into all the closely guarded nuances of the trade, and to that end, he would accompany his master on the very next job that required his employer’s services.
