source_text: Mrs. Sowerberry, who had a good deal of taste in the undertaking way, was much struck by the novelty of this idea; but, as it would have been compromising her dignity to have said so, under existing circumstances, she merely inquired, with much sharpness, why such an obvious suggestion had not presented itself to her husband’s mind before? Mr. Sowerberry rightly construed this, as an acquiescence in his proposition; it was speedily determined, therefore, that Oliver should be at once initiated into the mysteries of the trade; and, with this view, that he should accompany his master on the very next occasion of his services being required.

text: The supper cloth was spread ready for the meal, the table loaded with bread and butter, plates and glasses, a stout porter pot and a bottle of wine. At the head of the table, Mr. Noah Claypole slouched lazily in an easy chair, his legs slung carelessly over one armrest: an open pocket knife in one hand, a thick chunk of buttered bread in the other. Standing just beside him was Charlotte, prying oysters open from a barrel: morsels Mr. Claypole deigned to swallow with sharp, greedy enthusiasm. An unusual bright redness across the young gentleman’s nose, and a sort of fixed, half-winking twitch in his right eye, marked that he was slightly intoxicated; these symptoms were confirmed by the fierce relish with which he ate his oysters, a preference that could only be adequately explained by a strong awareness of their cooling qualities, perfect for soothing the internal fever brought on by drink.
source_text: The cloth was laid for supper; the table was covered with bread and butter, plates and glasses; a porter-pot and a wine-bottle. At the upper end of the table, Mr. Noah Claypole lolled negligently in an easy-chair, with his legs thrown over one of the arms: an open clasp-knife in one hand, and a mass of buttered bread in the other. Close beside him stood Charlotte, opening oysters from a barrel: which Mr. Claypole condescended to swallow, with remarkable avidity. A more than ordinary redness in the region of the young gentleman’s nose, and a kind of fixed wink in his right eye, denoted that he was in a slight degree intoxicated; these symptoms were confirmed by the intense relish with which he took his oysters, for which nothing but a strong appreciation of their cooling properties, in cases of internal fever, could have sufficiently accounted.

text: The burglar lay sprawled across the bed, swaddled in his white greatcoat as a makeshift dressing gown, his features none the more appealing for the sickly, corpse-pale cast of illness over his skin, a grubby nightcap perched crookedly on his head, and a coarse, week-old black beard bristling along his jaw. His dog hunkered at the bedside, one moment fixing his master with a soft, longing gaze, the next pricking his ears and letting out a low, rumbling growl whenever a clatter from the street or the lower floors of the house caught his notice. Seated beside the window, her hands busy darning a tattered waistcoat that formed part of the robber’s regular attire, was a woman so gaunt and ashen from sleepless nights and hardship that there would have been great difficulty recognizing her as the same Nancy who had appeared earlier in this tale, were it not for the familiar tone of her voice as she answered Mr. Sikes’s question.
source_text: The housebreaker was lying on the bed, wrapped in his white great-coat, by way of dressing-gown, and displaying a set of features in no degree improved by the cadaverous hue of illness, and the addition of a soiled nightcap, and a stiff, black beard of a week’s growth. The dog sat at the bedside: now eyeing his master with a wistful look, and now pricking his ears, and uttering a low growl as some noise in the street, or in the lower part of the house, attracted his attention. Seated by the window, busily engaged in patching an old waistcoat which formed a portion of the robber’s ordinary dress, was a female: so pale and reduced with watching and privation, that there would have been considerable difficulty in recognising her as the same Nancy who has already figured in this tale, but for the voice in which she replied to Mr. Sikes’s question.

text: Oliver made it to the stile marking the end of the narrow side path, and stepped back onto the main public road. It was already eight o’clock by that point. Even though he was nearly five miles outside the town limits, he alternated between sprinting as fast as he could and ducking out of sight behind the thick hedgerows all the way until noon, frozen with fear that someone was on his trail and would overtake him any moment. Only when he finally sank down to rest beside the weathered milestone did he start to think, for the very first time, where he ought to head next to try and build some kind of life for himself.
source_text: Oliver reached the stile at which the by-path terminated; and once more gained the high-road. It was eight o’clock now. Though he was nearly five miles away from the town, he ran, and hid behind the hedges, by turns, till noon, fearing that he might be pursued and overtaken. Then he sat down to rest by the side of the milestone, and began to think, for the first time, where he had better go and try to live.

text: The crook-backed man gaped, as if flooded by an overwhelming rush of astonishment and sharp indignation; then, squirming nimbly free of the physician’s grip, he snarled a ragged volley of brutal curses before turning to storm back inside the house. Before he could slam the door shut behind him, though, the physician had stepped straight over the threshold into the front parlour, not bothering to utter a single word of negotiation.
source_text: The hump-backed man stared, as if in excess of amazement and indignation; then, twisting himself, dexterously, from the doctor’s grasp, growled forth a volley of horrid oaths, and retired into the house. Before he could shut the door, however, the doctor had passed into the parlour, without a word of parley.

text: “Lord Almighty help me, it’s the truth!” the girl cried, hot with passion; “I would rather have been struck dead in the street, or swapped places with those we passed so close to tonight, than ever have lent a hand to bring him here. He’s a thief, a liar, a devil, every sort of wicked thing there is, as of this night. Isn’t that more than enough for the old wretch, without adding blows?”
source_text: “God Almighty help me, I am!” cried the girl passionately; “and I wish I had been struck dead in the street, or had changed places with them we passed so near tonight, before I had lent a hand in bringing him here. He’s a thief, a liar, a devil, all that’s bad, from this night forth. Isn’t that enough for the old wretch, without blows?”

text: Even so, a heavy, aching weight settled in his chest; as he slipped into his cramped, narrow bed, he wished it were his coffin instead. He longed to lie in quiet, unending rest beneath the churchyard dirt, tall grass swaying softly above his head, the deep, resonant chime of the old church bell lulling him gently through his sleep.
source_text: But his heart was heavy, notwithstanding; and he wished, as he crept into his narrow bed, that that were his coffin, and that he could be lain in a calm and lasting sleep in the churchyard ground, with the tall grass waving gently above his head, and the sound of the old deep bell to soothe him in his sleep.

text: “Pay heed to the truths I hold that you do not,” said Mr. Haleworth. “You will be wholly caught up in what I have to say soon enough. I know you are the sole, deeply aberrant product of that wretched marriage your unhappy father was coerced into as a barely grown boy, spurred by your family’s overweening pride and the pettiest, most sordid strain of narrow ambition.”
source_text: “Attend to what I do know, and you may not,” said Mr. Brownlow. “I shall interest you by and by. I know that of the wretched marriage, into which family pride, and the most sordid and narrowest of all ambition, forced your unhappy father when a mere boy, you were the sole and most unnatural issue.”

text: The conference dragged on interminably. Oliver laid out the plain, unvarnished story of his life for them, forced to pause again and again by searing pain and the limits of his frail strength. There was a terrible solemnity to sitting in that dimmed room, listening to the sick boy’s thin, reedy voice run through the long, exhausting list of cruelties and misfortunes unfeeling, harsh men had heaped on his small shoulders. Oh! if only those of us who grind down and oppress our fellow humans would spare a single thought to the heavy, damning record of our wrongs, rising like dense, weighty clouds toward heaven—slowly, it is true, but no less surely, destined to break one day in delayed retribution over our own heads; if only we could pause for a single instant to imagine the unshakable testimony of the voices of the dead, testimony no power can stifle, no pride can shut out; how much of the injury and injustice, the suffering, misery, cruelty, and wrong that marks every ordinary day would cease to exist entirely!
source_text: The conference was a long one. Oliver told them all his simple history, and was often compelled to stop, by pain and want of strength. It was a solemn thing, to hear, in the darkened room, the feeble voice of the sick child recounting a weary catalogue of evils and calamities which hard men had brought upon him. Oh! if when we oppress and grind our fellow-creatures, we bestowed but one thought on the dark evidences of human error, which, like dense and heavy clouds, are rising, slowly it is true, but not less surely, to Heaven, to pour their after-vengeance on our heads; if we heard but one instant, in imagination, the deep testimony of dead men’s voices, which no power can stifle, and no pride shut out; where would be the injury and injustice, the suffering, misery, cruelty, and wrong, that each day’s life brings with it!

text: “He’s laying low to avoid showing up here before dark, but he’ll turn up soon enough,” Chitling replied. “There’s nowhere else he can go right now: everyone at the Cripples is in custody, and the bar of the den—I went up there and saw it for myself—is crammed full of traps.”
source_text: “He hung about, not to come over here afore dark, but he’ll be here soon,” replied Chitling. “There’s nowhere else to go to now, for the people at the Cripples are all in custody, and the bar of the ken—I went up there and see it with my own eyes—is filled with traps.”

text: The small room he always retreated to when buried in his books sat on the ground floor, tucked at the very rear of the house. It was a cozy, classic cottage space, fitted with a lattice window ringed with thick clusters of jessamine and honeysuckle that wove their way over the casement, suffusing every corner of the room with their soft, heady fragrance. The window looked out over a garden, where a small wooden wicket gate opened onto a compact grazing paddock; past that stretched rolling, lush meadowland and dense stretches of woodland. There were no other dwellings anywhere nearby in that direction, so the view the room offered was remarkably broad and unbroken.
source_text: The little room in which he was accustomed to sit, when busy at his books, was on the ground-floor, at the back of the house. It was quite a cottage-room, with a lattice-window: around which were clusters of jessamine and honeysuckle, that crept over the casement, and filled the place with their delicious perfume. It looked into a garden, whence a wicket-gate opened into a small paddock; all beyond, was fine meadow-land and wood. There was no other dwelling near, in that direction; and the prospect it commanded was very extensive.

text: Monks paced back and forth across the room, his face set in a dark, menacing grimace as he mulled over the proposal laid out before him, turning over every possible angle to dodge its terms. He was pulled sharply between two warring impulses: crippling, icy fear on one side, and searing, unbridled hatred on the other, when the lock on the door turned with a rough, hurried clack. A gentleman—Mr. Losberne—stepped over the threshold, his whole frame thrumming with raw, frantic agitation.
source_text: While Monks was pacing up and down, meditating with dark and evil looks on this proposal and the possibilities of evading it: torn by his fears on the one hand and his hatred on the other: the door was hurriedly unlocked, and a gentleman (Mr. Losberne) entered the room in violent agitation.

text: Mr. Dawkins took these flattering compliments in his usual unflappable stride, then offered to cut cards against any gentleman in the room for the first face card, at a shilling per round. When no one stepped forward to accept his challenge, and his pipe had burned all the way down by that point, he turned to amusing himself by sketching a ground plan of Newgate Prison on the tabletop with the same scrap of chalk he had used as gaming counters earlier, whistling all the while at a peculiarly shrill, piercing pitch.
source_text: Mr. Dawkins received these handsome compliments with much philosophy, and offered to cut any gentleman in company, for the first picture-card, at a shilling at a time. Nobody accepting the challenge, and his pipe being by this time smoked out, he proceeded to amuse himself by sketching a ground-plan of Newgate on the table with the piece of chalk which had served him in lieu of counters; whistling, meantime, with peculiar shrillness.

text: Lila’s arrival pulled the conversation in an entirely new direction; the ragged street boys, catching a sly side wink from shrewd old Silas the fence, started pressing her to help herself to the gin set out on the splintered table. She only sipped a tiny mouthful, though, turning down nearly all their offers, while Silas put on an uncharacteristically giddy air, slowly drawing the gruff Mr. Hale out of his foul temper by framing his snappish threats as playful, lighthearted ribbing. He even roared with genuine-sounding laughter at a couple of coarse jokes Hale deigned to crack once he’d tipped back the spirit bottle enough times to loosen his usual tight jaw.
source_text: Nancy’s appearance gave a new turn to the conversation; for the boys, receiving a sly wink from the wary old Jew, began to ply her with liquor: of which, however, she took very sparingly; while Fagin, assuming an unusual flow of spirits, gradually brought Mr. Sikes into a better temper, by affecting to regard his threats as a little pleasant banter; and, moreover, by laughing very heartily at one or two rough jokes, which, after repeated applications to the spirit-bottle, he condescended to make.

text: Nancy ducked low to stay beneath the edge of the window shutters, and Oliver picked up the faint tinkle of a bell. They crossed over to the opposite side of the street, and lingered for a few seconds under the glow of a streetlamp. A soft, quiet noise drifted through the air, like someone lifting a sash window as gently as possible to avoid making a sound; moments later, the front door eased open without a creak. Mr. Sikes skipped all pleasantries entirely, snatching the terrified boy by the collar with rough, unapologetic haste, and all three were inside the house in seconds.
source_text: Nancy stooped below the shutters, and Oliver heard the sound of a bell. They crossed to the opposite side of the street, and stood for a few moments under a lamp. A noise, as if a sash window were gently raised, was heard; and soon afterwards the door softly opened. Mr. Sikes then seized the terrified boy by the collar with very little ceremony; and all three were quickly inside the house.

text: As these events unfolded in the country workhouse, Mr. Fagin sat in his grubby old den—the very same space Oliver had been taken from by the girl—brooding over a dim, smoke-choked fire. A pair of bellows rested across his knees, which he had plainly been using only moments earlier to try and rouse the flames into a warmer, cheerier blaze; but he had sunk into deep, unmoored thought now, arms folded over the bellows, chin propped on his thumbs, his gaze fixed absently on the hearth’s rusted iron bars.
source_text: While these things were passing in the country workhouse, Mr. Fagin sat in the old den—the same from which Oliver had been removed by the girl—brooding over a dull, smoky fire. He held a pair of bellows upon his knee, with which he had apparently been endeavouring to rouse it into more cheerful action; but he had fallen into deep thought; and with his arms folded on them, and his chin resting on his thumbs, fixed his eyes, abstractedly, on the rusty bars.

text: Elias was stuck exactly in that disorienting, half-conscious limbo. He could make out the old pawnbroker’s slitted, half-lidded eyes, catch his low, reedy whistle, and pick out the gritty scrape of a tin spoon against the chipped enamel stew pot’s sides—yet those very same senses were simultaneously tangled up in busy, unspooling communion with nearly every person he had ever known.
source_text: Oliver was precisely in this condition. He saw the Jew with his half-closed eyes; heard his low whistling; and recognised the sound of the spoon grating against the saucepan’s sides: and yet the self-same senses were mentally engaged, at the same time, in busy action with almost everybody he had ever known.

text: The group of visitors stayed for quite a long while. Bottles of hard liquor were brought out after one of the young women complained she felt a persistent chill deep in her core, and the conversation shifted to a lively, convivial pitch that everyone in the room seemed to find surprisingly edifying. Eventually, Charley Bates announced that it was time to hit the bricks. Oliver assumed the weird turn of phrase must be French for leaving, because right after he spoke, the Dodger, Charley, and the two young women all headed out together, the good-natured old Jew having kindly pressed a bit of spending money on each of them before they departed.
source_text: The visitors stopped a long time. Spirits were produced, in consequence of one of the young ladies complaining of a coldness in her inside; and the conversation took a very convivial and improving turn. At length, Charley Bates expressed his opinion that it was time to pad the hoof. This, it occurred to Oliver, must be French for going out; for directly afterwards, the Dodger, and Charley, and the two young ladies, went away together, having been kindly furnished by the amiable old Jew with money to spend.

text: The coach rattled off, covering nearly the exact stretch of road Oliver had traveled when he first arrived in London in the Dodger’s company; upon reaching the Angel at Islington, it veered down an alternate route, finally pulling to a stop outside a neat house on a quiet, leaf-shaded street close to Pentonville. A bed was readied without a moment’s delay here, and Mr. Brownlow watched as his young charge was settled carefully and comfortably into it; he was tended to with a warmth and thoughtfulness that knew no limits.
source_text: The coach rattled away, over nearly the same ground as that which Oliver had traversed when he first entered London in company with the Dodger; and, turning a different way when it reached the Angel at Islington, stopped at length before a neat house, in a quiet shady street near Pentonville. Here, a bed was prepared, without loss of time, in which Mr. Brownlow saw his young charge carefully and comfortably deposited; and here, he was tended with a kindness and solicitude that knew no bounds.

text: Relieved to know for certain that help had been sent for without a single moment wasted, Oliver hurried across the inn yard, his heart somewhat lighter than it had been moments prior. He had just turned toward the exit gateway when he accidentally stumbled into a tall man wrapped in a thick cloak, who was stepping out of the inn’s front door at exactly that instant.
source_text: As it was something to feel certain that assistance was sent for, and that no time had been lost, Oliver hurried up the inn-yard, with a somewhat lighter heart. He was turning out of the gateway when he accidently stumbled against a tall man wrapped in a cloak, who was at that moment coming out of the inn door.

text: At a scrubbed pine table just over his shoulder sat Cunning Jem, Master Toby Hackett, and Mr. Griggs, all wholly absorbed in a game of whist; Jem held the dummy hand, pitted against the paired play of Hackett and Griggs. The face of the first-named young rascal, sharp and quick-witted even on the slowest of days, took on an extra air of keen absorption as he tracked every turn of the game, his gaze drifting repeatedly to study Mr. Griggs’ fanned-out hand. Whenever the moment suited, he darted a series of shrewd sidelong looks at those exposed cards, carefully calibrating his own moves to what he’d gleaned from his neighbor’s hold. The night carried a sharp bite, so Jem kept his cap pulled down on his head, a habit he indulged even indoors more often than not. A dented clay pipe jutted from between his teeth, which he only removed for a few seconds at a time when he wanted a sip of refreshment from the quart pot resting on the table, brimming full of gin-and-water for the whole party’s use.
source_text: At a table behind him sat the Artful Dodger, Master Charles Bates, and Mr. Chitling: all intent upon a game of whist; the Artful taking dummy against Master Bates and Mr. Chitling. The countenance of the first-named gentleman, peculiarly intelligent at all times, acquired great additional interest from his close observance of the game, and his attentive perusal of Mr. Chitling’s hand; upon which, from time to time, as occasion served, he bestowed a variety of earnest glances: wisely regulating his own play by the result of his observations upon his neighbour’s cards. It being a cold night, the Dodger wore his hat, as, indeed, was often his custom within doors. He also sustained a clay pipe between his teeth, which he only removed for a brief space when he deemed it necessary to apply for refreshment to a quart pot upon the table, which stood ready filled with gin-and-water for the accommodation of the company.

text: They were just emerging from a cramped alley no more than a hundred yards from the open market square in Ancoats, which is still called, by some odd quirk of local custom, “The Lea”: when Midge came to an abrupt halt; and, pressing his index finger to his lips, drew his two companions back out of sight, with the utmost care and quiet deliberation.
source_text: They were just emerging from a narrow court not far from the open square in Clerkenwell, which is yet called, by some strange perversion of terms, “The Green”: when the Dodger made a sudden stop; and, laying his finger on his lip, drew his companions back again, with the greatest caution and circumspection.

text: “No, no,” Mrs. Sowerberry cut in, her mind flicking to Oliver’s old friend just as she spoke. “Noah, bolt over to Mr. Bumble and tell him to get here immediately, no dragging your feet. Don’t even bother grabbing your cap! Hurry! You can press a knife to that black eye of yours while you run, that’ll stop the swelling from getting any bigger.”
source_text: “No, no,” said Mrs. Sowerberry: bethinking herself of Oliver’s old friend. “Run to Mr. Bumble, Noah, and tell him to come here directly, and not to lose a minute; never mind your cap! Make haste! You can hold a knife to that black eye, as you run along. It’ll keep the swelling down.”

text: Every man and woman on the property was fully occupied with the urgent work of saving the hay crop—even Liddy had stepped out of the house to pitch in—so Bathsheba decided she would hive the bees herself, if she could pull it off. She’d coated the inside of the empty hive with herbs and honey to draw the swarm in, fetched a ladder, soft bristle brush, and beekeeping crook, then armored herself against stings with thick leather gloves, a wide-brimmed straw hat, and an oversized gauze veil that had once been a vibrant green, now faded to a murky snuff shade. She’d just climbed a dozen rungs up the ladder when she heard a voice no more than ten yards away, one that was starting to hold a strange, unsettling power to rattle her completely.
source_text: The men and women being all busily engaged in saving the hay—even Liddy had left the house for the purpose of lending a hand—Bathsheba resolved to hive the bees herself, if possible. She had dressed the hive with herbs and honey, fetched a ladder, brush, and crook, made herself impregnable with armour of leather gloves, straw hat, and large gauze veil—once green but now faded to snuff colour—and ascended a dozen rungs of the ladder. At once she heard, not ten yards off, a voice that was beginning to have a strange power in agitating her.

text: The heavy stone building comprised a main central block and two flanking wings, with a handful of slim chimneys perched atop them like quiet sentinels, now letting out low, mournful gurgles as the slow wind drifted through. Set into its surrounding wall was a gate, and fastened right next to the gate was a bell pull strung from a single length of hanging wire. The woman pushed herself up as high as she could manage while remaining on her knees, and could just barely stretch far enough to grip the handle. She gave it a tug, then pitched forward into a hunched, bowed position, her face dropping to rest against her bosom.
source_text: This stone edifice consisted of a central mass and two wings, whereon stood as sentinels a few slim chimneys, now gurgling sorrowfully to the slow wind. In the wall was a gate, and by the gate a bellpull formed of a hanging wire. The woman raised herself as high as possible upon her knees, and could just reach the handle. She moved it and fell forwards in a bowed attitude, her face upon her bosom.

text: “Honest to God,” Troy said, his voice muffled through the veil’s fine mesh, “holding this beehive up makes my arm throb way worse than a full week of sword drills ever did.” Once he’d wrapped up the delicate maneuver entirely, he stepped close to her. “Would you do me a favor and untie this so I can get out? I’m nearly suffocating stuck inside this silly silk cage.”
source_text: “Upon my life,” said Troy, through the veil, “holding up this hive makes one’s arm ache worse than a week of sword-exercise.” When the manœuvre was complete he approached her. “Would you be good enough to untie me and let me out? I am nearly stifled inside this silk cage.”

text: The night lay windless and clammy, the air thick with cool dew. Just as the first pale smudge of dawn bled over the moor’s eastern ridge, Elias Thorne was roused from shallow sleep by a warped echo of a sound he knew better than his own heartbeat. For a shepherd, the tinkle of the lead ewe’s bell is as constant a background hum as a cottage clock’s tick is to lowland townsfolk—you never truly register it unless it cuts off entirely, or shifts away from the lazy, familiar chime that tells a practiced ear, even when carried miles on faint breezes, that every animal in the flock is safe and unharried. Beneath the hush of that waking, starlit quiet, the bell reached Elias, clanging sharp and fast, far more urgent than its usual soft cadence. There were only two reasons for such an unusual ring: either the bell-bearer was hurrying to graze as the flock burst onto fresh, sweet pasture, which made the sound skip in uneven, stuttering bursts, or the entire flock was fleeing at a full run, which gave the ring a steady, thrumming, regular pace. Thorne’s decades tending sheep on these uplands let him parse the sound at once: the noise he heard now meant the flock was sprinting at full velocity.
source_text: It was a still, moist night. Just before dawn he was assisted in waking by the abnormal reverberation of familiar music. To the shepherd, the note of the sheep-bell, like the ticking of the clock to other people, is a chronic sound that only makes itself noticed by ceasing or altering in some unusual manner from the well-known idle twinkle which signifies to the accustomed ear, however distant, that all is well in the fold. In the solemn calm of the awakening morn that note was heard by Gabriel, beating with unusual violence and rapidity. This exceptional ringing may be caused in two ways—by the rapid feeding of the sheep bearing the bell, as when the flock breaks into new pasture, which gives it an intermittent rapidity, or by the sheep starting off in a run, when the sound has a regular palpitation. The experienced ear of Oak knew the sound he now heard to be caused by the running of the flock with great velocity.

text: Bathsheba was so utterly desolate that evening, ravenous for any measure of pity and sympathy, and wretched at the thought that she seemed to have outlived the only true friendship she had ever known, that she pulled on her bonnet and cloak and set off for Oak’s house just after sunset, her path guided by the pale, primrose-toned rays of a crescent moon barely a few days old.
source_text: So desolate was Bathsheba this evening, that in an absolute hunger for pity and sympathy, and miserable in that she appeared to have outlived the only true friendship she had ever owned, she put on her bonnet and cloak and went down to Oak’s house just after sunset, guided on her way by the pale primrose rays of a crescent moon a few days old.

text: Each separate light dotting Casterbridge was now distinct to the naked eye. The hour was creeping toward dawn, and while she couldn’t reasonably expect a passing cart or carriage any minute now, there was still reason to hope one might come along before much longer. She held her breath and listened. Not a single other sound of living movement broke the stillness, save for what might be the peak of all wretched, desolate noises: the bark of a wild fox. It let out three hollow, echoing notes at exact one-minute intervals, as unwaveringly regular and somber as the toll of a funeral bell.
source_text: The Casterbridge lights were now individually visible. It was getting towards morning, and vehicles might be hoped for, if not expected soon. She listened. There was not a sound of life save that acme and sublimation of all dismal sounds, the bark of a fox, its three hollow notes being rendered at intervals of a minute with the precision of a funeral bell.

text: The morning was crisp and clear, sunlight gilding the deep burgundy coat she wore until it flamed the hue of crushed poppies, spilling warm, honeyed light over her sharp, lively cheeks and ink-black braids. The potted jasmine, pelargoniums, and prickly pear cacti clustered at her feet glowed vivid, dewy green, and in that stretch of late winter when every bare branch stood sharp against the sky, they wrapped the whole ramshackle assemblage—draft horses, canvas-covered cart, stacked household goods, and the young woman perched on top—in an unexpected, sweet air of spring. What drove her to put on that little show, with only chattering house sparrows, trilling blackbirds, and a farmer hidden behind the hedgerow for an audience—whether the smile first tugged at her lips as a deliberate exercise, to test if she could summon the expression even when her spirits felt heavy—no one could say; what was certain was that it softened into something unfeigned before long. Heat flooded her cheeks when she realized what she’d done, and when she caught sight of her own flushed face in the smudged polished side panel of the cart, the pink deepened to a vivid burn that crept all the way to the tips of her ears.
source_text: It was a fine morning, and the sun lighted up to a scarlet glow the crimson jacket she wore, and painted a soft lustre upon her bright face and dark hair. The myrtles, geraniums, and cactuses packed around her were fresh and green, and at such a leafless season they invested the whole concern of horses, waggon, furniture, and girl with a peculiar vernal charm. What possessed her to indulge in such a performance in the sight of the sparrows, blackbirds, and unperceived farmer who were alone its spectators,—whether the smile began as a factitious one, to test her capacity in that art,—nobody knows; it ended certainly in a real smile. She blushed at herself, and seeing her reflection blush, blushed the more.

text: That was all it took to snap him back to the convictions he’d held from the start. He knew now he was right, and Troy was wrong. Every small signal from the natural world aligned to confirm a shift was on its way. But these wordless, quiet cues carried two very separate interpretations. It was clear a thunderstorm would roll through first, followed by an unbroken stretch of cold, steady rain. The small crawling creatures seemed to sense every detail of the coming rain, but had no idea the thunderstorm would strike first; the sheep, by contrast, knew full well the thunderstorm was imminent, but had no notion of the lingering cold rain that would come after it.
source_text: This was enough to re-establish him in his original opinion. He knew now that he was right, and that Troy was wrong. Every voice in nature was unanimous in bespeaking change. But two distinct translations attached to these dumb expressions. Apparently there was to be a thunder-storm, and afterwards a cold continuous rain. The creeping things seemed to know all about the later rain, but little of the interpolated thunder-storm; whilst the sheep knew all about the thunder-storm and nothing of the later rain.

text: She leaned against the rough stone to rest for a few short moments, then roused herself and continued on her way. She pushed on gamely for a short stretch, only to flag with weariness again, just as she had before. She was walking alongside an isolated stand of coppiced wood now; piles of pale wood shavings scattered across the leaf-strewn ground told her woodcutters had been bundling faggots and weaving hurdles there earlier that day. Right then, there was not a single rustle, not a puff of breeze, not even the softest clink of twigs knocking together to keep her company. The woman peered over the gate, lifted its latch, and stepped inside. Just inside the entrance stood a stack of faggots, some bound tight and others loose, alongside wooden stakes of every size.
source_text: She leant against the stone as a means of rest for a short interval, then bestirred herself, and again pursued her way. For a slight distance she bore up bravely, afterwards flagging as before. This was beside a lone copsewood, wherein heaps of white chips strewn upon the leafy ground showed that woodmen had been faggoting and making hurdles during the day. Now there was not a rustle, not a breeze, not the faintest clash of twigs to keep her company. The woman looked over the gate, opened it, and went in. Close to the entrance stood a row of faggots, bound and unbound, together with stakes of all sizes.

text: Elspeth adored Torin with the fierce, unmoored devotion that only fiercely independent women know when they cast aside their own hard-won self-sufficiency. When a woman accustomed to holding her own carelessly discards that well-earned fortitude, she fares far worse than the soft, untested woman who never possessed such strength to squander in the first place. A core root of her helplessness lies in how entirely unacquainted she is with this state of being. She has never had cause to practice navigating, let alone making the most of, such vulnerable disarray. Fragility is doubly feeble when it is still new, unrooted in long habit.
source_text: Bathsheba loved Troy in the way that only self-reliant women love when they abandon their self-reliance. When a strong woman recklessly throws away her strength she is worse than a weak woman who has never had any strength to throw away. One source of her inadequacy is the novelty of the occasion. She has never had practice in making the best of such a condition. Weakness is doubly weak by being new.

text: In the meantime, Elowen remained a voluntary captive in the garret. The door stayed bolted at all hours, save for the comings and goings of Maren, whose cot had been arranged in a cramped antechamber just next door. The glow of Kael’s lantern bobbing through the churchyard caught the maidservant’s eye a little after ten, when she glanced idly out the window in that direction mid-supper, and she drew Elowen’s attention to the odd sight. The pair peered curiously at the unaccountable light for several minutes, before Maren was sent off to bed for the night.
source_text: Meanwhile, Bathsheba remained a voluntary prisoner in the attic. The door was kept locked, except during the entries and exits of Liddy, for whom a bed had been arranged in a small adjoining room. The light of Troy’s lantern in the churchyard was noticed about ten o’clock by the maid-servant, who casually glanced from the window in that direction whilst taking her supper, and she called Bathsheba’s attention to it. They looked curiously at the phenomenon for a time, until Liddy was sent to bed.

text: Bathsheba retreated into the shade. She could scarcely tell if she was more amused by the strangeness of this unexpected meeting, or unsettled by its clumsy, stilted energy. There was space in her chest for a small thread of pity, too, and an even slighter flicker of quiet triumph: the former for his current circumstances, the latter for her own. She felt no embarrassment at all, and when she recalled Gabriel’s declaration of love to her back at Norcombe, the thought came accompanied only by the quiet realization that she had nearly forgotten it entirely.
source_text: Bathsheba withdrew into the shade. She scarcely knew whether most to be amused at the singularity of the meeting, or to be concerned at its awkwardness. There was room for a little pity, also for a very little exultation: the former at his position, the latter at her own. Embarrassed she was not, and she remembered Gabriel’s declaration of love to her at Norcombe only to think she had nearly forgotten it.

text: “Wait, wait, Marnie—you mustn’t leave!” said Elowen, her earlier sharp haughtiness melting into urgent entreaty with the same abrupt, fickle impulsiveness she was known for. “Pay no mind to how ruffled and snappish I was a minute ago. You’re no ordinary servant to me—you’re my closest companion, the only one I can speak plainly to. Oh, gracious, I don’t know what I’m doing half the time these days, not since this wretched, gnawing ache in my chest settled in and weighed me down to the bone! What is going to become of me? I suppose I’ll sink deeper and deeper into misfortune, won’t I? Sometimes I wonder if I’m fated to die penniless in the parish workhouse. I’m as friendless as a stray caught in the rain, God knows that’s true!”
source_text: “No, no, Liddy; you must stay!” said Bathsheba, dropping from haughtiness to entreaty with capricious inconsequence. “You must not notice my being in a taking just now. You are not as a servant—you are a companion to me. Dear, dear—I don’t know what I am doing since this miserable ache of my heart has weighted and worn upon me so! What shall I come to! I suppose I shall get further and further into troubles. I wonder sometimes if I am doomed to die in the Union. I am friendless enough, God knows!”

text: At the back of the big main tent were two small, private changing tents. The one set aside for male performers was split into two equal sections by a hanging piece of cloth, and seated on the grass in one of those sections, pulling a pair of stiff jackboots up his legs, was a young man we recognized immediately as Sergeant Troy.
source_text: At the rear of the large tent there were two small dressing-tents. One of these, alloted to the male performers, was partitioned into halves by a cloth; and in one of the divisions there was sitting on the grass, pulling on a pair of jack-boots, a young man whom we instantly recognise as Sergeant Troy.

text: That night at Coggan’s, Gabriel Oak lay with his eyelids firmly shut, his mind teeming with bright fancies and as restless as a fast-flowing river rushing beneath a sheath of ice. Night had always been the time when Bathsheba appeared to him most vividly, and as the slow, shadowed hours stretched on, he turned her image over in his mind with quiet tenderness. The pleasures of the imagination rarely make up for the sharp ache of sleeplessness, but they likely did for Oak that night: the sheer delight of merely seeing her in his mind’s eye erased, for the time being, any recognition of the enormous gulf between picturing her and actually having her in his life.
source_text: That night at Coggan’s, Gabriel Oak, beneath the screen of closed eyelids, was busy with fancies, and full of movement, like a river flowing rapidly under its ice. Night had always been the time at which he saw Bathsheba most vividly, and through the slow hours of shadow he tenderly regarded her image now. It is rarely that the pleasures of the imagination will compensate for the pain of sleeplessness, but they possibly did with Oak to-night, for the delight of merely seeing her effaced for the time his perception of the great difference between seeing and possessing.

text: Only in recent months had folks taken to calling Elias Thorne “Farmer” Thorne. Over the twelve months preceding this shift, steady unflagging industry and his customary unflappable good humor had allowed him to lease the small sheep farm that included a stretch of Bramblecrest Hill, and stock it with two hundred sheep. Before that he had served a short term as a farm steward, and earlier still worked only as a shepherd, having helped his father tend the flocks of large local landowners from childhood on, until old Elias Thorne senior passed quietly to his rest.
source_text: It was only latterly that people had begun to call Gabriel “Farmer” Oak. During the twelvemonth preceding this time he had been enabled by sustained efforts of industry and chronic good spirits to lease the small sheep-farm of which Norcombe Hill was a portion, and stock it with two hundred sheep. Previously he had been a bailiff for a short time, and earlier still a shepherd only, having from his childhood assisted his father in tending the flocks of large proprietors, till old Gabriel sank to rest.

text: “The finest set I’ve got in stock is this one,” the stone-cutter said, stepping into a nearby shed. “This is a marble headstone with lovely carved crockets along the top edge, and standard themed medallions set just beneath the carvings. The footstone follows the exact same pattern, and that piece over there is the coping to line the whole grave enclosure. Polishing this full set alone cost me eleven pounds—these slabs are the highest quality of their kind, and I guarantee they’ll hold up to rain and frost for a full hundred years without chipping or flaking apart.”
source_text: “The best I have in stock is this one,” said the stone-cutter, going into a shed. “Here’s a marble headstone beautifully crocketed, with medallions beneath of typical subjects; here’s the footstone after the same pattern, and here’s the coping to enclose the grave. The polishing alone of the set cost me eleven pounds—the slabs are the best of their kind, and I can warrant them to resist rain and frost for a hundred years without flying.”

text: The evening still held its bright, warm glow, even as night crept in quiet and unassuming low to the ground. Slanted western rays skimmed the earth’s surface but barely settled there, doing nothing at all to illuminate the flat, featureless stretches of plain. The sun had slid around the tree trunk in one final, fading push before dipping out of sight, and as it sank lower, the shearers’ legs and lower torsos were swallowed up by deepening, bronze-tinged twilight. Their heads and shoulders, though, still basked in the last of the daylight, gilded with a bright honey-yellow glow that looked as if it emanated from their own skin rather than being cast down by the fading sun.
source_text: It was still the beaming time of evening, though night was stealthily making itself visible low down upon the ground, the western lines of light raking the earth without alighting upon it to any extent, or illuminating the dead levels at all. The sun had crept round the tree as a last effort before death, and then began to sink, the shearers’ lower parts becoming steeped in embrowning twilight, whilst their heads and shoulders were still enjoying day, touched with a yellow of self-sustained brilliancy that seemed inherent rather than acquired.

text: As the minutes dragged on with no one else arriving and not a soul stirring, the silence swelled until it was impossible to overlook. The quarter-jack clattered again from its carved recess, struck the three-quarter hour, then retreated with its customary fussy clink; the sound was so abruptly jarring it verged on painful, and many in the congregation flinched noticeably.
source_text: The silence grew to be a noticeable thing as the minutes went on, and nobody else appeared, and not a soul moved. The rattle of the quarter-jack again from its niche, its blows for three-quarters, its fussy retreat, were almost painfully abrupt, and caused many of the congregation to start palpably.

text: “Hmph, right. I’d wager I won’t be the most welcome visitor if she’s staying with him,” the sergeant remarked, huffing a small, dry laugh. “Bit like that old tale of Alonzo the Brave, isn’t it? The moment I step through the door, every guest will freeze stiff with dread, all merriment and lighthearted chatter will die right out, the chamber lamps will flicker cold blue, and the worms—ugh, revolting thought! Ring for another serving of brandy, Pennyways, that just sent an awful shudder down my spine. Now, what else do I need? A stick— I absolutely must have a walking-stick.”
source_text: “H’m, yes. I expect I shall not be a very welcome guest if he has her there,” said the sergeant, with a slight laugh. “A sort of Alonzo the Brave; and when I go in the guests will sit in silence and fear, and all laughter and pleasure will be hushed, and the lights in the chamber burn blue, and the worms—Ugh, horrible!—Ring for some more brandy, Pennyways, I felt an awful shudder just then! Well, what is there besides? A stick—I must have a walking-stick.”

text: Gabriel could not make out a word of their exchange, too proud to draw near yet too preoccupied to look away. Their conversation concluded when the courteous farmer offered her his hand to steady her as she stepped over the wide shearing board, out into the vivid, glowing June sunlight beyond. Once they stood beside the freshly shorn sheep, they resumed talking. Was their subject the flock? It did not appear so. Gabriel’s theory carried no small measure of truth: when two people speak quietly of a matter within their line of sight, their gaze almost always fixes upon that very thing. Bathsheba’s eyes were lowered demurely to a flimsy, worthless straw lying on the dirt at her feet, a bearing that suggested far less critical consideration of the sheep than womanly embarrassment. Color rose and fell unsteadily in her cheeks, the blood shifting in uncertain waves across that sensitive skin, wavering midway between pale ebb and bright flush. Gabriel kept shearing, stiff with constraint and heavy with quiet sorrow.
source_text: What they conversed about was not audible to Gabriel, who was too independent to get near, though too concerned to disregard. The issue of their dialogue was the taking of her hand by the courteous farmer to help her over the spreading-board into the bright June sunlight outside. Standing beside the sheep already shorn, they went on talking again. Concerning the flock? Apparently not. Gabriel theorized, not without truth, that in quiet discussion of any matter within reach of the speakers’ eyes, these are usually fixed upon it. Bathsheba demurely regarded a contemptible straw lying upon the ground, in a way which suggested less ovine criticism than womanly embarrassment. She became more or less red in the cheek, the blood wavering in uncertain flux and reflux over the sensitive space between ebb and flood. Gabriel sheared on, constrained and sad.

text: He left the room, and once he judged she would have had time to steady her nerves enough, sent one of the maids to attend her. Bathsheba masked the lingering traces of the recent upset as best she could, followed the girl, and in only a few minutes appeared downstairs, hat and cloak already fastened, ready to leave. The only route to the door ran through the main hall, and before entering it, she paused at the foot of the staircase, which descended into one far corner of the space, to steal one last look at the assembled group.
source_text: He left the room, and when he thought she might be sufficiently composed sent one of the maids to her. Bathsheba cloaked the effects of the late scene as she best could, followed the girl, and in a few moments came downstairs with her hat and cloak on, ready to go. To get to the door it was necessary to pass through the hall, and before doing so she paused on the bottom of the staircase which descended into one corner, to take a last look at the gathering.

text: A caterpillar had apparently crawled out of the nearby fern to settle on the front of her bodice. She watched its glistening pointed tip drift toward her bosom, seeming to sink straight into her skin. Bathsheba squeezed her eyes shut, fully convinced she had been fatally struck at last. When she registered that she felt exactly as well as she had a moment prior, however, she eased her eyes open again.
source_text: It appeared that a caterpillar had come from the fern and chosen the front of her bodice as his resting place. She saw the point glisten towards her bosom, and seemingly enter it. Bathsheba closed her eyes in the full persuasion that she was killed at last. However, feeling just as usual, she opened them again.

text: Once the wagon had rolled onward, Gabriel pulled back from his hidden vantage point, climbed down to the road, and followed the vehicle to the turnpike gate a short way past the hill’s foot, where the figure he’d been intently observing had now stopped to pay toll. He was still roughly twenty paces from the gate when the sound of a dispute reached his ears. The disagreement, over a mere twopence, was between the people accompanying the wagon and the man manning the toll bar.
source_text: When the waggon had passed on, Gabriel withdrew from his point of espial, and descending into the road, followed the vehicle to the turnpike-gate some way beyond the bottom of the hill, where the object of his contemplation now halted for the payment of toll. About twenty steps still remained between him and the gate, when he heard a dispute. It was a difference concerning twopence between the persons with the waggon and the man at the toll-bar.

text: Joseph felt a tight weight lift off his chest the second the warm, welcoming tavern sign came into view, and he reined his horse to a halt right beneath it, finally ready to follow through on a plan he’d been nursing for ages. His spirits had been draining steadily the whole ride, leaving him hollow and frayed at the edges. He guided his horse toward the soft green bank out front to tie it up, then pushed through the hostel’s door, headed straight for a cold, frothy mug of ale.
source_text: It was a relief to Joseph’s heart when the friendly signboard came in view, and, stopping his horse immediately beneath it, he proceeded to fulfil an intention made a long time before. His spirits were oozing out of him quite. He turned the horse’s head to the green bank, and entered the hostel for a mug of ale.

text: Kael reached the canvas tent flap, lingering at the edge of the huddle of revelers clustered there as he scanned the crowd anxiously for Rook, clearly keen to avoid drawing attention to himself by asking after the man out loud. A pair of farm hands nearby were murmuring about a bold snatch-and-grab that had just unfolded: a thief had sliced through the tent wall beside a young woman to rob her. They guessed the scoundrel had mistaken the scrap of paper clutched in her fist for a banknote, because he’d wrenched it free and bolted straight off, leaving her full leather purse untouched on the bench beside her. The men snickered that his bitter disappointment when he realized the paper was nothing but a scribbled shopping list would make for a fine, silly pub story later. The incident had barely registered with most of the crowd, anyway; it hadn’t even made the fiddler who’d just struck up a tune by the tent entrance miss a note, nor thrown off the four stooped, grizzled old men gripping gnarled walking sticks as they stomped through “Mulligan’s Reel” in time to the music. Rook was leaning against a tent pole just behind the dancers. Kael slipped across the packed dirt to his side, flicked his chin in a quick beckoning gesture, and breathed a handful of quiet words into his ear; after a single shared, knowing look, the pair slipped away together into the dark beyond the tent’s glow.
source_text: Troy reached the tent door, and standing among the groups there gathered, looked anxiously for Pennyways, evidently not wishing to make himself prominent by inquiring for him. One or two men were speaking of a daring attempt that had just been made to rob a young lady by lifting the canvas of the tent beside her. It was supposed that the rogue had imagined a slip of paper which she held in her hand to be a bank note, for he had seized it, and made off with it, leaving her purse behind. His chagrin and disappointment at discovering its worthlessness would be a good joke, it was said. However, the occurrence seemed to have become known to few, for it had not interrupted a fiddler, who had lately begun playing by the door of the tent, nor the four bowed old men with grim countenances and walking-sticks in hand, who were dancing “Major Malley’s Reel” to the tune. Behind these stood Pennyways. Troy glided up to him, beckoned, and whispered a few words; and with a mutual glance of concurrence the two men went into the night together.

text:
The moment Troy was out of sight, Bathsheba was wracked by heaving sobs: dry, tearing gasps that raked her throat, unsoothed by a single tear. Yet she forced herself to clamp down on every outward sign of her distress. She had been broken, but she would never confess it to a single soul for as long as she drew breath. Her pride had been utterly crushed by the horrifying, despairing realization that marrying a man of far baser character than her own had left her irrevocably tarnished. She paced restlessly back and forth, bristling with rebellion like a leopard trapped in a cage; every fiber of her being seethed with rage, and hot blood burned high in her cheeks.

Before she crossed paths with Troy, Bathsheba had taken fierce pride in her status as an unwed, untouched woman. It had been a point of quiet glory to her that no man on earth had ever pressed his lips to hers, or wrapped his arm around her waist in a lover’s embrace. Now she loathed the very sight of herself. In those younger, freer days, she’d harbored a quiet, unspoken scorn for girls who fell head over heels for the first handsome young man who bothered to pay them attention. Unlike most of the women she knew, she’d never felt any soft, instinctive fondness for the idea of marriage as an inherent good. She’d agreed to marry him in the chaotic, fevered rush of her infatuation and worry for him, and even her happiest moments in that courtship had carried the quiet sense that she was making a sacrifice, not stepping into a higher, more honoured station in life.

Though she could barely have put a name to the goddess, Bathsheba had always worshipped Diana by instinct. The memories cut her to the bone now: how she’d never once encouraged a man’s advance with a glance, a word, or a small gesture, how she’d felt entirely whole on her own, and how in the bright independence of her girlhood she’d believed there was something demeaning in abandoning the simple purity of maidenhood to become the lesser, secondary partner in a lukewarm, unremarkable marriage. Oh, if only she had never stooped to this foolhardy mistake, for all it wore the respectable face of marriage! If only she could stand once more where she’d stood on the hill at Norcombe, daring Troy or any other man alive to so much as touch a single hair on her head and sully her with his unwanted attention.
source_text: Directly he had gone, Bathsheba burst into great sobs—dry-eyed sobs, which cut as they came, without any softening by tears. But she determined to repress all evidences of feeling. She was conquered; but she would never own it as long as she lived. Her pride was indeed brought low by despairing discoveries of her spoliation by marriage with a less pure nature than her own. She chafed to and fro in rebelliousness, like a caged leopard; her whole soul was in arms, and the blood fired her face. Until she had met Troy, Bathsheba had been proud of her position as a woman; it had been a glory to her to know that her lips had been touched by no man’s on earth—that her waist had never been encircled by a lover’s arm. She hated herself now. In those earlier days she had always nourished a secret contempt for girls who were the slaves of the first good-looking young fellow who should choose to salute them. She had never taken kindly to the idea of marriage in the abstract as did the majority of women she saw about her. In the turmoil of her anxiety for her lover she had agreed to marry him; but the perception that had accompanied her happiest hours on this account was rather that of self-sacrifice than of promotion and honour. Although she scarcely knew the divinity’s name, Diana was the goddess whom Bathsheba instinctively adored. That she had never, by look, word, or sign, encouraged a man to approach her—that she had felt herself sufficient to herself, and had in the independence of her girlish heart fancied there was a certain degradation in renouncing the simplicity of a maiden existence to become the humbler half of an indifferent matrimonial whole—were facts now bitterly remembered. Oh, if she had never stooped to folly of this kind, respectable as it was, and could only stand again, as she had stood on the hill at Norcombe, and dare Troy or any other man to pollute a hair of her head by his interference!

text: “—But the plain truth of it is, despite what some folks imagine, she never jilted me at all. There was never any engagement between Miss Everdene and myself. The rumors going around are entirely false: she never gave me any sort of promise!” Boldwood stopped short now, twisting his wild, haggard face toward Oak. “Oh, Gabriel,” he continued, his voice raw with misery, “I am weak, foolish, barely even know what I am anymore, and I can’t fight off this crushing, wretched grief! I held some faint belief in God’s mercy right up until I lost that woman. Yes, He raised a gourd to shade me, and just like the prophet I thanked Him and was full of gladness. But the very next day He sent a worm to strike the gourd and make it wither away; and now I know it is better to die than to keep on living!”
source_text: “—But the real truth of the matter is that there was not, as some fancy, any jilting on—her part. No engagement ever existed between me and Miss Everdene. People say so, but it is untrue: she never promised me!” Boldwood stood still now and turned his wild face to Oak. “Oh, Gabriel,” he continued, “I am weak and foolish, and I don’t know what, and I can’t fend off my miserable grief!... I had some faint belief in the mercy of God till I lost that woman. Yes, He prepared a gourd to shade me, and like the prophet I thanked Him and was glad. But the next day He prepared a worm to smite the gourd and wither it; and I feel it is better to die than to live!”

text: “We’ve got to follow their trail now that we can’t pick up any sound of them,” Jan said, fumbling through his pockets. He struck a match and held its wavering flame low to the earth. The rain had fallen harder in this spot, so every footprint and horse hoof print left before the storm had been eroded and blurred by the falling drops, reduced to tiny shallow pools of water that glinted back the match’s flame like watching eyes. Only one set of tracks was fresh, with no water pooled inside its indentations; a single pair of wagon ruts was also dry, rather than the narrow little water channels the older ruts had turned into. Those recent footprints held clear clues about the pace of the person who’d left them: they fell in evenly spaced pairs, three or four feet between each set, the right and left foot of each pair perfectly aligned side by side.
source_text: “We must try to track ’em, since we can’t hear ’em,” said Jan, fumbling in his pockets. He struck a light, and held the match to the ground. The rain had been heavier here, and all foot and horse tracks made previous to the storm had been abraded and blurred by the drops, and they were now so many little scoops of water, which reflected the flame of the match like eyes. One set of tracks was fresh and had no water in them; one pair of ruts was also empty, and not small canals, like the others. The footprints forming this recent impression were full of information as to pace; they were in equidistant pairs, three or four feet apart, the right and left foot of each pair being exactly opposite one another.

text: I suppose I ought to press on, now that I’ve started. It can’t do any harm, after all—you’re under no false impression that I ever cared for you, or that I’m speaking for any reason beyond the one I already laid out. I was stranded alone in an unfamiliar town, my carriage horse gone lame. I had no clue what to do next, and realized far too late that gossip might cling to me if anyone saw me meeting him unchaperoned like that. I was just turning to leave when he told me he’d crossed paths with a woman lovelier than me that very day, and that he couldn’t promise to stay constant if I didn’t agree to be his right then. I was so hurt and flustered—” She cleared her throat, pausing a beat as if to draw in a steadying breath. “And half out of jealousy, half out of utter disarray, I married him!” she burst out in a sharp, desperate whisper.
source_text: “I must, I suppose, say more, now that I have begun. And perhaps it’s no harm, for you are certainly under no delusion that I ever loved you, or that I can have any object in speaking, more than that object I have mentioned. Well, I was alone in a strange city, and the horse was lame. And at last I didn’t know what to do. I saw, when it was too late, that scandal might seize hold of me for meeting him alone in that way. But I was coming away, when he suddenly said he had that day seen a woman more beautiful than I, and that his constancy could not be counted on unless I at once became his.... And I was grieved and troubled—” She cleared her voice, and waited a moment, as if to gather breath. “And then, between jealousy and distraction, I married him!” she whispered with desperate impetuosity.

text: His chance arrived when a ewe died, leaving a living lamb behind her. It was a day that wore a summer’s face but held a winter’s bite: a bright, crisp January morning, where just enough snippets of blue sky peeked through the cloud cover to make upbeat people long for more of the same, paired with the occasional flash of silvery sunlight cutting through the chill. Oak tucked the lamb into a proper, good-quality basket reserved for Sunday use, then tramped across the open fields toward the home of his aunt, Mrs. Hurst. His dog George walked a few paces behind him, his face set in an expression of deep worry over the grim turn the farm’s pastoral affairs appeared to have taken.
source_text: He found his opportunity in the death of a ewe, mother of a living lamb. On a day which had a summer face and a winter constitution—a fine January morning, when there was just enough blue sky visible to make cheerfully-disposed people wish for more, and an occasional gleam of silvery sunshine, Oak put the lamb into a respectable Sunday basket, and stalked across the fields to the house of Mrs. Hurst, the aunt—George, the dog walking behind, with a countenance of great concern at the serious turn pastoral affairs seemed to be taking.

text: Fair enough. If she doesn’t come back tomorrow, make sure you head over there, figure out who that man is, and meet with him. I bear far more responsibility for her than I would if she had any living friends or relatives to look out for her. I just hope she hasn’t come to any harm at the hands of some guy like that… And then there’s that whole disgraceful mess with the bailiff, too—but I can’t bring myself to talk about him right now.
source_text: “Very well; if she doesn’t return to-morrow, mind you go there and try to discover which man it is, and see him. I feel more responsible than I should if she had had any friends or relations alive. I do hope she has come to no harm through a man of that kind.... And then there’s this disgraceful affair of the bailiff—but I can’t speak of him now.”

text: The smooth, spotless creature lifted itself free of its fleece—you would have to see the sight to fully grasp how perfectly it mirrored Aphrodite rising from the seafoam—looking startled and timid now that its covering was gone, the garment spread across the floor in a single, soft, unbroken cloud. Only the inner layer of the fleece was visible, a surface that had never been uncovered before, as white as fresh fallen snow, with not even the tiniest speck, scratch, or blemish to be found anywhere.
source_text: The clean, sleek creature arose from its fleece—how perfectly like Aphrodite rising from the foam should have been seen to be realized—looking startled and shy at the loss of its garment, which lay on the floor in one soft cloud, united throughout, the portion visible being the inner surface only, which, never before exposed, was white as snow, and without flaw or blemish of the minutest kind.

text: Bathsheba stopped short, turning the thought over slowly and thoroughly in her head. Boldwood had started to feel like a persistent, annoying thorn in her side—some Daniel figure in her little domain, dead set on kneeling east to pray even when every bit of logic and common sense said he could just go along with everyone else, and toss her that standard, harmless look of admiration that didn’t cost him a single thing. It’s not like she was actually all that bothered by his refusal to play along with the unspoken rules. Even so, it stung faintly that the most dignified, upstanding man in the whole parish refused to so much as glance her way, and that a girl like Liddy had picked up on the oddity and started chattering about it. So Liddy’s suggestion, at first, felt more grating than titillating.
source_text: Bathsheba paused to regard the idea at full length. Boldwood’s had begun to be a troublesome image—a species of Daniel in her kingdom who persisted in kneeling eastward when reason and common sense said that he might just as well follow suit with the rest, and afford her the official glance of admiration which cost nothing at all. She was far from being seriously concerned about his nonconformity. Still, it was faintly depressing that the most dignified and valuable man in the parish should withhold his eyes, and that a girl like Liddy should talk about it. So Liddy’s idea was at first rather harassing than piquant.

text: Oak tilted his chin to gaze up at her. She hadn’t said a single word since he’d stepped out of the barn. The faint, steady shimmer of the fading lightning illuminated her pale, marble-smooth face, set stark against the pitch-black sky on the far opposite side. Bathsheba was perched nearly at the very peak of the haystack, her feet tucked tight beneath her, resting on the ladder’s top rung.
source_text: Oak looked up at her. She had not spoken since he left the barn. The soft and continual shimmer of the dying lightning showed a marble face high against the black sky of the opposite quarter. Bathsheba was sitting almost on the apex of the stack, her feet gathered up beneath her, and resting on the top round of the ladder.

text: Tucked in Roy-Town, the small roadside hamlet just beyond this stretch of woods, stood the aging Buck’s Head inn. Roughly a mile and a half from Weatherbury, the establishment had been a busy stagecoach stop during the golden age of horse-drawn travel, where countless lines swapped out exhausted teams and kept their relay horses stabled on site. All that old stabling has long since been torn down, however, and little remains besides the still-habitable inn itself, set a short distance back from the road. It signals its presence to passersby far up and down the highway via a sign hung from a thick horizontal bough of an elm tree growing directly across the way.
source_text: At the roadside hamlet called Roy-Town, just beyond this wood, was the old inn Buck’s Head. It was about a mile and a half from Weatherbury, and in the meridian times of stage-coach travelling had been the place where many coaches changed and kept their relays of horses. All the old stabling was now pulled down, and little remained besides the habitable inn itself, which, standing a little way back from the road, signified its existence to people far up and down the highway by a sign hanging from the horizontal bough of an elm on the opposite side of the way.

text: Everywhere else you looked, snow had bleached the fields and sky to nearly identical pale tones, so a hasty glance could barely distinguish where the horizon lay between them. The whole landscape also carried that strange, previously noted uncanny inversion of light and shadow that marks such wintry prospects: the harsh, garish brightness that usually resides in the sky was instead splayed across the frozen ground, while the deep, dusky shades that normally cling to the earth hung heavy in the air overhead. Low over the western horizon hung the fading moon, its surface dull and tinged a murky greenish yellow, like a disk of old, tarnished brass.
source_text: In other directions, the fields and sky were so much of one colour by the snow, that it was difficult in a hasty glance to tell whereabouts the horizon occurred; and in general there was here, too, that before-mentioned preternatural inversion of light and shade which attends the prospect when the garish brightness commonly in the sky is found on the earth, and the shades of earth are in the sky. Over the west hung the wasting moon, now dull and greenish-yellow, like tarnished brass.

text: Gabriel took the churchyard path on his way home. As he passed the tower, he recalled what she had told him of the sergeant’s unobtrusive habit of slipping into the church unseen just as services began. Convinced the small gallery door she had alluded to was entirely disused, he climbed the external flight of stone steps leading up to its threshold to examine it. The pale, lingering glow still hanging low in the northwestern sky was bright enough to show that a sprig of ivy had grown out from the wall, stretching more than a foot across the door’s face to tie its panel delicately to the stone jamb. It was unambiguous proof that the door had not been opened at least since Troy returned to Weatherbury.
source_text: Gabriel went home by way of the churchyard. In passing the tower he thought of what she had said about the sergeant’s virtuous habit of entering the church unperceived at the beginning of service. Believing that the little gallery door alluded to was quite disused, he ascended the external flight of steps at the top of which it stood, and examined it. The pale lustre yet hanging in the north-western heaven was sufficient to show that a sprig of ivy had grown from the wall across the door to a length of more than a foot, delicately tying the panel to the stone jamb. It was a decisive proof that the door had not been opened at least since Troy came back to Weatherbury.

text: Today the wide side doors stood thrown wide toward the sun, letting bountiful light flood the shearers’ work area: the central wooden threshing floor, hewn from thick oak planks turned black with age, buffed smooth by the beat of flails over countless generations until it grew as slippery and rich in tone as the state-room floors of an Elizabethan manor. The shearers knelt here, slanted sunlight catching their bleached work shirts, tanned arms, and the polished shears they lifted, sending up a thousand sharp glints bright enough to blind a person with weak eyes. Beneath them a held sheep lay panting, its breaths quickening as faint misgiving melted into full-blown terror, until it quivered like the heat-wavered landscape stretching outside.
source_text: To-day the large side doors were thrown open towards the sun to admit a bountiful light to the immediate spot of the shearers’ operations, which was the wood threshing-floor in the centre, formed of thick oak, black with age and polished by the beating of flails for many generations, till it had grown as slippery and as rich in hue as the state-room floors of an Elizabethan mansion. Here the shearers knelt, the sun slanting in upon their bleached shirts, tanned arms, and the polished shears they flourished, causing these to bristle with a thousand rays strong enough to blind a weak-eyed man. Beneath them a captive sheep lay panting, quickening its pants as misgiving merged in terror, till it quivered like the hot landscape outside.

text: “Let me think a second,” Mrs. Hurst said, prodding at the fire for no real purpose.... “Oh right—bless your heart, there are loads of young men interested in her. Look, Farmer Oak, she’s so pretty, and a really sharp student on top of that—she was going to become a governess once, you know? Only she was way too wild for that kind of rigid, quiet work. It’s not like any of those suitors ever turn up around here, mind you—but come on, that’s just how women are, isn’t it? She must have a whole dozen of them on the line!”
source_text: “Let me think,” said Mrs. Hurst, poking the fire superfluously.... “Yes—bless you, ever so many young men. You see, Farmer Oak, she’s so good-looking, and an excellent scholar besides—she was going to be a governess once, you know, only she was too wild. Not that her young men ever come here—but, Lord, in the nature of women, she must have a dozen!”

text: “You won’t ever get the chance to speak to me again, anyway; I won’t be staying here much longer. I’m due back any day now to that miserable, endless grind of military drills, and there’s even talk our battalion might get deployed out on assignment soon. Even so, you’re going to snatch away the only tiny, tender scrap of joy I’ve managed to hold onto in this whole drab, lifeless existence of mine. Oh well, I suppose generosity never was a woman’s most distinguishing characteristic, was it?”
source_text: “You will never speak to me; for I shall not be here long. I am soon going back again to the miserable monotony of drill—and perhaps our regiment will be ordered out soon. And yet you take away the one little ewe-lamb of pleasure that I have in this dull life of mine. Well, perhaps generosity is not a woman’s most marked characteristic.”

text: Marwenna held her composure masterfully, but when the words rose unbidden to her throat, she could barely shape them into a steady tone: “There is scarce any dignity owed to a woman in words like those.” She spoke no louder than a breath, for there was something both unspeakably mournful and gut-wrenching about the sight of a man so wholly adrift at the mercy of his own ardor, turned every which way like a weather vane caught in a squall, that it sapped the usual feminine impulse to stand on petty, precise points of etiquette.
source_text: Bathsheba commanded herself well. But she could hardly get a clear voice for what came instinctively to her lips: “There is little honour to the woman in that speech.” It was only whispered, for something unutterably mournful no less than distressing in this spectacle of a man showing himself to be so entirely the vane of a passion enervated the feminine instinct for punctilios.

text: They stood in unbroken silence then, each man occupied with his own quiet ruminations, while faint sounds of revelry drifted out to them from the interior of the house. The front door swung open a moment later, warm lamplight spilling outward, and Boldwood’s instantly recognizable form was framed in the sharp oblong of cast light. The door snicked shut behind him, and he moved at a deliberate, unhurried pace down the path.
source_text: They stood silent then, every man busied with his own thoughts, during which interval sounds of merriment could be heard within. Then the front door again opened, the rays streamed out, the well-known form of Boldwood was seen in the rectangular area of light, the door closed, and Boldwood walked slowly down the path.

text: “I couldn’t say—at the very least, I cannot put it to words for you. It is so hard for a woman to articulate her feelings using language that was crafted chiefly by men, to give voice to their own experiences first. The way I treated you was thoughtless, unforgivable, cruel! I will regret it for as long as I draw breath. If there had been any single thing I could do to make this right, I would have done it gladly—there was nothing on this earth I ached for more than repairing that terrible error. But it was never possible, not even for a moment.”
source_text: “I don’t know—at least, I cannot tell you. It is difficult for a woman to define her feelings in language which is chiefly made by men to express theirs. My treatment of you was thoughtless, inexcusable, wicked! I shall eternally regret it. If there had been anything I could have done to make amends I would most gladly have done it—there was nothing on earth I so longed to do as to repair the error. But that was not possible.”

text: “I hold no such hope now, else I would have acted differently,” she said softly. “At first, I truly felt a strange, unaccountable conviction that he could not have perished, but I have since been able to explain that feeling in several ways. Even though I am fully persuaded I shall never see him again, I am far from open to the thought of marrying another. I would consider myself utterly contemptible to even indulge such an idea.”
source_text: “I have none now, or I should have acted differently,” she said, gently. “I certainly, at first, had a strange unaccountable feeling that he could not have perished, but I have been able to explain that in several ways since. But though I am fully persuaded that I shall see him no more, I am far from thinking of marriage with another. I should be very contemptible to indulge in such a thought.”

text: Marwenna carried an impulsive, fiery streak beneath a cool, deliberative outward bearing. Endowed with the shrewd, strategic mind of Eleanor of Aquitaine and the reckless, romantic spirit of Isabella of France, she often pulled off acts of staggering boldness with a demeanor of quiet, unflappable discretion. Most of her ruminations were perfectly constructed, logically sound syllogisms; unluckily, they never progressed beyond the realm of idle thought.
