Only a tiny fraction were ungrounded, impulsive leaps of assumption; yet those, unfortunately, were the notions that most often took root and grew into tangible action.
source_text: Bathsheba’s was an impulsive nature under a deliberative aspect. An Elizabeth in brain and a Mary Stuart in spirit, she often performed actions of the greatest temerity with a manner of extreme discretion. Many of her thoughts were perfect syllogisms; unluckily they always remained thoughts. Only a few were irrational assumptions; but, unfortunately, they were the ones which most frequently grew into deeds.

text: Worry had scored deep lines and tight puckers across Joseph’s face. Fray’s forehead was crisscrossed with vertical and horizontal furrows, patterned exactly like a portcullis, a clear marker of his twofold despair. Laban Tall’s lips were pressed into a thin, taut line, his features held rigid and unyielding. Matthew’s jaw had gone slack and sunken, his eyes drifting whichever way the strongest pull of his facial muscles happened to guide them.
source_text: Joseph’s countenance was drawn into lines and puckers by his concern. Fray’s forehead was wrinkled both perpendicularly and crosswise, after the pattern of a portcullis, expressive of a double despair. Laban Tall’s lips were thin, and his face was rigid. Matthew’s jaws sank, and his eyes turned whichever way the strongest muscle happened to pull them.

text: Bathsheba understood him far better these days; he’d laid his whole heart bare to her, to the point that he struck her as looking almost pitiful, like a majestic bird stripped of the plumage that made it so imposing in the first place. She’d been stunned to realize how recklessly bold she’d been back then, and was fumbling to make things right, even though she hadn’t stopped to consider if her mistake actually warranted the heavy penance she was forcing herself to carry out. Having kicked up this whole mess that now crashed down around her felt awful at first; but as time passed, she couldn’t deny the quiet, giddy thrill tangled up in the terror of it all. It’s astonishing how easily even the most shy, cautious women can learn to savor something awful, just as long as there’s a small spark of victory mixed in with the chaos.
source_text: Bathsheba knew more of him now; he had entirely bared his heart before her, even until he had almost worn in her eyes the sorry look of a grand bird without the feathers that make it grand. She had been awe-struck at her past temerity, and was struggling to make amends without thinking whether the sin quite deserved the penalty she was schooling herself to pay. To have brought all this about her ears was terrible; but after a while the situation was not without a fearful joy. The facility with which even the most timid women sometimes acquire a relish for the dreadful when that is amalgamated with a little triumph, is marvellous.

text: Boldwood found her stunning, but he questioned if his own judgment was sound. Surely this walking, breathing romantic ideal, if she was even half as lovely as he imagined, would have sparked waves of giddy excitement among men and drawn far more curiosity than Bathsheba had already managed—and she’d already turned more than her fair share of heads, at that. As far as he could tell, there was nothing nature or even the most skilled human craft could tweak to make her any more flawless, a rare perfect exception amid a world of flawed, ordinary people. Something soft and unsteady began to shift in his chest. It’s worth remembering that, at forty years old, Boldwood had never before fixed his full, unfiltered attention on a woman; prior to this, women had only ever brushed the edges of his senses, never registering fully, as if he’d always been looking at them from the corner of his eye.
source_text: Boldwood thought her beautiful, but wondered whether he was right in his thought, for it seemed impossible that this romance in the flesh, if so sweet as he imagined, could have been going on long without creating a commotion of delight among men, and provoking more inquiry than Bathsheba had done, even though that was not a little. To the best of his judgement neither nature nor art could improve this perfect one of an imperfect many. His heart began to move within him. Boldwood, it must be remembered, though forty years of age, had never before inspected a woman with the very centre and force of his glance; they had struck upon all his senses at wide angles.

text: Right as he pulled himself out of a soft, wistful stare into empty space, Henry didn’t turn the offer down. He was well past middle age, with eyebrows set high on his forehead, and he held firm to the belief that the entire governing order of the world was rotten. When he spoke on the subject, he’d fix a weary, long-suffering gaze right through whoever he was talking to, as if he was staring directly at the flawed world he described, the version of it that played out so clearly in his imagination. He always signed his name “Henery,” and would strenuously defend that spelling whenever it was questioned. If some passing schoolteacher dared to remark that the second “e” was unnecessary and hopelessly old-fashioned, he’d retort that “H-e-n-e-r-y” was the name he’d been given at his christening, and the only one he’d ever answer to. He spoke in the tone of someone who considered differences in spelling to be matters deeply tied to a person’s core character.
source_text: Having at this moment reached the end of a wistful gaze into mid-air, Henry did not refuse. He was a man of more than middle age, with eyebrows high up in his forehead, who laid it down that the law of the world was bad, with a long-suffering look through his listeners at the world alluded to, as it presented itself to his imagination. He always signed his name “Henery”—strenuously insisting upon that spelling, and if any passing schoolmaster ventured to remark that the second “e” was superfluous and old-fashioned, he received the reply that “H-e-n-e-r-y” was the name he was christened and the name he would stick to—in the tone of one to whom orthographical differences were matters which had a great deal to do with personal character.

text: As Joseph descended into the inn’s kitchen—its floor a step lower than the entry passage, which in turn lay a step beneath the public road outside—he was greeted by a sight that cheered him immediately: two warm, copper-hued faces, belonging to none other than Mr. Jan Coggan and Mr. Mark Clark. The pair held the local reputation for having the two most appreciative throats in the district, all while remaining firmly within the bounds of respectability, and they were now seated across from one another at a three-legged round table fitted with an iron rim to stop mugs and pots from being accidentally elbowed to the floor. You could well have compared them to a setting sun and a full moon, shining face to face across the curve of the world.
source_text: Going down into the kitchen of the inn, the floor of which was a step below the passage, which in its turn was a step below the road outside, what should Joseph see to gladden his eyes but two copper-coloured discs, in the form of the countenances of Mr. Jan Coggan and Mr. Mark Clark. These owners of the two most appreciative throats in the neighbourhood, within the pale of respectability, were now sitting face to face over a three-legged circular table, having an iron rim to keep cups and pots from being accidentally elbowed off; they might have been said to resemble the setting sun and the full moon shining _vis-à-vis_ across the globe.

text: Alongside the mellow piping of Gabriel’s flute, Boldwood contributed a bass line in his customary deep, rumbling tone, though he voiced his notes so softly that the performance never took the shape of an ordinary duet. Instead, his vocals formed a rich, unobtrusive undercurrent that threw her own tones into even sharper, sweeter relief. The shearers leaned against one another as attendees at communal feasts had in the earliest ages of the world, so silent and absorbed in the music that the soft huff of her breath was nearly audible between the verses. When the ballad drew to its end, its final note lingering into an inexpressible, gentle fade, a low, warm buzz of pleasure rose around them: the concentrated, unfiltered essence of genuine applause.
source_text: In addition to the dulcet piping of Gabriel’s flute, Boldwood supplied a bass in his customary profound voice, uttering his notes so softly, however, as to abstain entirely from making anything like an ordinary duet of the song; they rather formed a rich unexplored shadow, which threw her tones into relief. The shearers reclined against each other as at suppers in the early ages of the world, and so silent and absorbed were they that her breathing could almost be heard between the bars; and at the end of the ballad, when the last tone loitered on to an inexpressible close, there arose that buzz of pleasure which is the attar of applause.

text: It seemed he had crossed that unmarked threshold of midlife some years prior, the point at which a man’s countenance naturally stops shifting for a span of a dozen years or more; with a little deliberate upkeep, the same holds true for women. Thirty-five and fifty marked the outer bounds of any plausible guess at his age—he could have passed for either, or any age tucked neatly between the two.
source_text: Apparently he had some time ago reached that entrance to middle age at which a man’s aspect naturally ceases to alter for the term of a dozen years or so; and, artificially, a woman’s does likewise. Thirty-five and fifty were his limits of variation—he might have been either, or anywhere between the two.

text: “Alright then, let me think. I’ve got the perfect way we can make this work,” the other man said, likely well aware that crossing his leading actor right now would be deeply awkward. “I won’t say a single thing to anyone about you staying silent. Just carry on with the performance as usual, don’t utter a line, toss in a well-timed wink every now and again, and a few resolute, grand nods during the heroic beats, you know what I mean. They’ll never catch on that all your speeches are cut.”
source_text: “Very well, then let me see. I tell you how we’ll manage,” said the other, who perhaps felt it would be extremely awkward to offend his leading man just at this time. “I won’t tell ’em anything about your keeping silence; go on with the piece and say nothing, doing what you can by a judicious wink now and then, and a few indomitable nods in the heroic places, you know. They’ll never find out that the speeches are omitted.”

text: For the first time, Boldwood grasped that women felt fully entitled to reverse their stated commitments on a whim, even when that choice could leave another person’s life irrevocably damaged. He had rested his entire hope on the belief that Bathsheba was a steady, decisive young woman, far less prone to flighty, thoughtless choices than her peers. That unshakable reliability, he’d reasoned, would lead her to follow through on the path she’d hinted at for the sake of consistency, and agree to be with him even if she didn’t regard him with that shimmering, uncritical adoration that comes with giddy, untested love. But now that entire line of thinking came back to him as faint, warped glints off a shattered mirror, useless and distorted beyond recognition. This revelation was as much a stinging, cutting blow as it was a total surprise.
source_text: Boldwood had for the first time been awakened to woman’s privileges in tergiversation even when it involves another person’s possible blight. That Bathsheba was a firm and positive girl, far less inconsequent than her fellows, had been the very lung of his hope; for he had held that these qualities would lead her to adhere to a straight course for consistency’s sake, and accept him, though her fancy might not flood him with the iridescent hues of uncritical love. But the argument now came back as sorry gleams from a broken mirror. The discovery was no less a scourge than a surprise.

text: “Well, someone definitely did—and listen up, neighbors,” Gabriel, who was normally one of the quietest, gentlest men you’d ever meet, rose to the occasion with all the brisk, unyielding fire of a soldier jumping into action. “This right here is my fist.” He set his knuckles down, only slightly smaller than an average loaf of bread, exactly dead center of the maltster’s small table, rapping it twice against the worn wood as if to make sure every pair of eyes in the room got the full picture of exactly what that fist could do before he kept speaking. “Now—the first man in this parish I hear badmouthing our mistress, well” —he lifted the fist and slammed it back down the way Thor might have tested the weight of his hammer—“he’s gonna taste and feel every bit of it, and that’s a promise. If I don’t follow through, you can call me a liar.”
source_text: “Well, somebody has—and look here, neighbours,” Gabriel, though one of the quietest and most gentle men on earth, rose to the occasion, with martial promptness and vigour. “That’s my fist.” Here he placed his fist, rather smaller in size than a common loaf, in the mathematical centre of the maltster’s little table, and with it gave a bump or two thereon, as if to ensure that their eyes all thoroughly took in the idea of fistiness before he went further. “Now—the first man in the parish that I hear prophesying bad of our mistress, why” (here the fist was raised and let fall as Thor might have done with his hammer in assaying it)—“he’ll smell and taste that—or I’m a Dutchman.”

text: Bathsheba did, in fact, have a small, eager interest in seeing the show, and had only refrained from stepping up the ladder because she dreaded going in entirely alone. She had been hoping Oak would show up, after all—his aid in these kinds of small, awkward situations had long been treated by her as an unassailable, inherent right. But Oak was nowhere to be spotted, and so it was that she said, “Well, if you’ll just peek inside first to make sure there’s room, I think I’ll come in for a minute or two.”
source_text: Now Bathsheba did care a little to see the show, and had only withheld her feet from the ladder because she feared to go in alone. She had been hoping that Oak might appear, whose assistance in such cases was always accepted as an inalienable right, but Oak was nowhere to be seen; and hence it was that she said, “Then if you will just look in first, to see if there’s room, I think I will go in for a minute or two.”

text: His budding friendship with Elowen’s aunt had been cut short when his courtship of the younger woman ended in blunt rejection, and all Elias learned of Elowen’s comings and goings reached him through roundabout, secondhand channels. Word had it she’d traveled to a small village named Brambleward, more than twenty miles from the hamlet they’d both long called home, but he could never confirm the nature of her stay there—whether she was merely a passing visitor, or had put down roots for good.
source_text: His incipient friendship with her aunt had been nipped by the failure of his suit, and all that Oak learnt of Bathsheba’s movements was done indirectly. It appeared that she had gone to a place called Weatherbury, more than twenty miles off, but in what capacity—whether as a visitor, or permanently, he could not discover.

text:
An opening cut through the landscape facing east, and the soft, spreading glow of the still-unrisen sun drew her gaze that way. Starting right at her feet, sloping down between the lovely gold-tinged ferns with their lacy, outstretched fronds, the land dipped into a low hollow holding a small marsh, dotted all over with wild fungi. A thin morning mist draped over it just then, a lush yet striking silvery veil flooded with the sun’s incoming light but still half-opaque, so the hedgerow behind was partially hidden beneath its hazy, luminous sheen. Clumps of common rush climbed the sides of that depression, dotted here and there with an unusual species of flag, their narrow blades glinting in the newly breaking sunlight like sharpened scythes.

For all that soft light, the marsh as a whole felt menacing. Its damp, toxic-seeming surface seemed to breathe out the very essence of every terrible thing that festers in soil, or in the dark waters hidden beneath the earth. Fungi sprouted from every cranny of rotting leaf litter and weathered tree stumps, some turning their clammy caps up to her half-lidded gaze, others showing off their sticky, oozing gills. Some were splotched with thick, vivid patches as bright red as fresh arterial blood, others a deep saffron yellow, others tall and spindly with stems as pale and ridged as macaroni. A few had tough, leathery flesh in the deepest, richest shades of brown. That hollow felt like a breeding ground for every minor and terrible pestilence, sitting so close to the space where she’d found comfort and safety just hours before. Bathsheba pushed herself to her feet with a little shiver, horrified to realize she’d spent the entire night sleeping right on the edge of such a dread, unwholesome place.
source_text: There was an opening towards the east, and the glow from the as yet unrisen sun attracted her eyes thither. From her feet, and between the beautiful yellowing ferns with their feathery arms, the ground sloped downwards to a hollow, in which was a species of swamp, dotted with fungi. A morning mist hung over it now—a fulsome yet magnificent silvery veil, full of light from the sun, yet semi-opaque—the hedge behind it being in some measure hidden by its hazy luminousness. Up the sides of this depression grew sheaves of the common rush, and here and there a peculiar species of flag, the blades of which glistened in the emerging sun, like scythes. But the general aspect of the swamp was malignant. From its moist and poisonous coat seemed to be exhaled the essences of evil things in the earth, and in the waters under the earth. The fungi grew in all manner of positions from rotting leaves and tree stumps, some exhibiting to her listless gaze their clammy tops, others their oozing gills. Some were marked with great splotches, red as arterial blood, others were saffron yellow, and others tall and attenuated, with stems like macaroni. Some were leathery and of richest browns. The hollow seemed a nursery of pestilences small and great, in the immediate neighbourhood of comfort and health, and Bathsheba arose with a tremor at the thought of having passed the night on the brink of so dismal a place.

text: Boldwood stepped out of the room. He paused for a moment in the hallway, trying to steady his reeling, overwrought senses. The whole encounter had unfolded too quickly for his mind to keep pace, and just as he’d finally wrapped his head around what was happening, it had already slipped through his fingers. For those few perfect, golden, heaven-sent seconds, she’d been held tight in his arms. Did it even matter that she had no clue it was him holding her? She’d been pressed flush to his chest, and he’d been just as close, right up against the soft, beating thud of her own heart.
source_text: Boldwood left the room. He stood for a moment in the passage to recover his senses. The experience had been too much for his consciousness to keep up with, and now that he had grasped it it had gone again. For those few heavenly, golden moments she had been in his arms. What did it matter about her not knowing it? She had been close to his breast; he had been close to hers.

text: She stood in the open window frame, facing the group of men gathered outside, warm candlelight spilling out behind her. Gabriel was positioned at her right, just beyond the edge of the sash, while Boldwood had drawn close to her left, remaining inside the room. Her singing was soft and a little shaky when she first started, but it quickly swelled to a steady, unwavering clarity. The events that unfolded afterward ensured one of the song’s verses would stay lodged in the memory of more than one person in that assembly, lingering for many months, and even years, after that moment:
source_text: She stood up in the window-opening, facing the men, the candles behind her, Gabriel on her right hand, immediately outside the sash-frame. Boldwood had drawn up on her left, within the room. Her singing was soft and rather tremulous at first, but it soon swelled to a steady clearness. Subsequent events caused one of the verses to be remembered for many months, and even years, by more than one of those who were gathered there:—

text: Carved with a five-pointed coronet, it bears the inscription beneath *Cedit amor rebus*—“Love yields to circumstance.” That is the ancestral motto of the Earls of Severn. This watch belonged to the last holder of that title, and was entrusted to my mother’s husband, a physician, to use as his own until I came of age, at which point it was to be passed down to me. It was the entire inheritance I ever received. In its day, this watch kept time for affairs of imperial weight: grand state ceremonies, quiet court rendezvous, opulent official travels, and the unrushed slumbers of the peerage. Now it is yours.
source_text: “A coronet with five points, and beneath, _Cedit amor rebus_—‘Love yields to circumstance.’ It’s the motto of the Earls of Severn. That watch belonged to the last lord, and was given to my mother’s husband, a medical man, for his use till I came of age, when it was to be given to me. It was all the fortune that ever I inherited. That watch has regulated imperial interests in its time—the stately ceremonial, the courtly assignation, pompous travels, and lordly sleeps. Now it is yours.”

text: You spot the cleverness of it? Why, the whole chat was really about me taking the farm bailiff role, but I spoke so roundabout she couldn’t grasp my true meaning, which let me lay on the persuasion all the thicker without rousing suspicion. That’s the depth of my smarts, see! ... Be that as it may, let her marry whomever she wishes. It’s likely high time she did, if you ask me. I’d lay my best pocket knife that Farmer Greer kissed her behind the spear-thistle bed at the sheep washing the other day—truly, I would.
source_text: “You see the artfulness? Why, ’twas about being baily really; but I didn’t put it so plain that she could understand my meaning, so I could lay it on all the stronger. That was my depth!... However, let her marry an she will. Perhaps ’tis high time. I believe Farmer Boldwood kissed her behind the spear-bed at the sheep-washing t’other day—that I do.”

text: Thaddeus Voss was the first to head out after the gathering broke. Next, Silas Hale pushed himself to his feet and departed alongside Tobin Mears, who had offered him a place to lodge for the night. A handful of minutes later, just as the last remaining guests had risen and were preparing to leave, Voss came hurrying back again. Wagging a single finger in a foreboding arc, he cast a gaze brimming with unspoken news straight at the first face his eye happened to land on by chance, which belonged to Mosey Tuttle.
source_text: Henery Fray was the first to follow. Then Gabriel arose and went off with Jan Coggan, who had offered him a lodging. A few minutes later, when the remaining ones were on their legs and about to depart, Fray came back again in a hurry. Flourishing his finger ominously he threw a gaze teeming with tidings just where his eye alighted by accident, which happened to be in Joseph Poorgrass’s face.

text: The unrelenting downpour spewing from the gargoyle’s mouth poured all its unbridled force straight down into the open grave. The deep, warm brown loam churned and roiled, bubbling thick as melted chocolate. Water kept piling up, carving deeper into the earth as it settled, and the low, thunderous roar of the growing pool cut through the dark, louder than any other sound the drenching storm had stirred up across the grounds. The blooms Fanny’s regretful former lover had tended so gently started to twist and thrash in their planting bed. The winter violets flipped slowly root-over-bloom, dissolving into nothing but a sodden, muck-covered mat. Before long, snowdrops and other bulb flowers bounced and tossed in the churning mire like bits of seasoning tossed into a bubbling cauldron. Clumping, tufted ground cover plants were torn free from the soil, bobbed up to the top of the pool, and drifted away on the murky water.
source_text: The persistent torrent from the gurgoyle’s jaws directed all its vengeance into the grave. The rich tawny mould was stirred into motion, and boiled like chocolate. The water accumulated and washed deeper down, and the roar of the pool thus formed spread into the night as the head and chief among other noises of the kind created by the deluging rain. The flowers so carefully planted by Fanny’s repentant lover began to move and writhe in their bed. The winter-violets turned slowly upside down, and became a mere mat of mud. Soon the snowdrop and other bulbs danced in the boiling mass like ingredients in a cauldron. Plants of the tufted species were loosened, rose to the surface, and floated off.

text: “Aye,” said Coggan. “We know full well that if any folks make it to heaven, they will. They’ve worked their fingers to the bone for it, and they deserve every bit of it, for whatever it’s worth. I ain’t fool enough to pretend that we who stick by the Church have the same chance they do, because we know we don’t. But I hate a man who’d cast off his old, long-held doctrines just to weasel his way into heaven. I’d as soon turn king’s evidence for the paltry few pounds it pays. Look here, neighbours, when every last one of my potatoes got frostbitten, it was Parson Thirdly who gave me a sack of seed, even when he barely had enough for his own use, and no money to buy more. If it hadn’t been for him, I wouldn’t have had a single potato to plant in my garden. D’ye think I’d turn coat after that? Not on your life. I’ll stick to my own side; and if we’re in the wrong, so be it: I’ll fall with the fallen.”
source_text: “Yes,” said Coggan. “We know very well that if anybody do go to heaven, they will. They’ve worked hard for it, and they deserve to have it, such as ’tis. I bain’t such a fool as to pretend that we who stick to the Church have the same chance as they, because we know we have not. But I hate a feller who’ll change his old ancient doctrines for the sake of getting to heaven. I’d as soon turn king’s-evidence for the few pounds you get. Why, neighbours, when every one of my taties were frosted, our Parson Thirdly were the man who gave me a sack for seed, though he hardly had one for his own use, and no money to buy ’em. If it hadn’t been for him, I shouldn’t hae had a tatie to put in my garden. D’ye think I’d turn after that? No, I’ll stick to my side; and if we be in the wrong, so be it: I’ll fall with the fallen!”

text: Elias Thorne’s upland farm had been fully stocked by a regional supply merchant—who’d agreed to the arrangement solely on the weight of Thorne’s earnest bearing and solid local reputation—who collected a fixed percentage of the farm’s monthly earnings until the full sum of the initial advance was repaid. When Thorne tallied all his holdings, he discovered the total worth of livestock, crop stores, and tools that rightfully belonged to him was barely enough to settle all his outstanding debts, leaving him unburdened by obligations but owning nothing beyond the scuffed work clothes he had on his back.
source_text: Gabriel’s farm had been stocked by a dealer—on the strength of Oak’s promising look and character—who was receiving a percentage from the farmer till such time as the advance should be cleared off. Oak found that the value of stock, plant, and implements which were really his own would be about sufficient to pay his debts, leaving himself a free man with the clothes he stood up in, and nothing more.

text: He was an enormous, bulky, silent animal, looming dark against the flat, low skyline, his head sitting at least two feet above the level of her gaze where she stood. There was no way to tell if he was a Newfoundland, mastiff, bloodhound, or some uncategorized mix entirely. His bearing was so odd, so unplaceable, that he did not fit any of the common breed labels most people would recognize. Since he could not be slotted into any existing breed type, he felt like the perfect physical manifestation of all that is noble about dogs: a distillation of every good trait shared across the entire species. He seemed to be the living embodiment of night’s softer qualities—its quiet sorrow, its weighty solemnity, its gentle kindness—stripped of all the furtive, vicious edges that darkness can carry. Darkness has a way of gifting even the smallest, most unremarkable people a spark of poetic imagination, and so even this woman, worn thin by her troubles, wove her own quiet meaning out of the shape before her.
source_text: He was a huge, heavy, and quiet creature, standing darkly against the low horizon, and at least two feet higher than the present position of her eyes. Whether Newfoundland, mastiff, bloodhound, or what not, it was impossible to say. He seemed to be of too strange and mysterious a nature to belong to any variety among those of popular nomenclature. Being thus assignable to no breed, he was the ideal embodiment of canine greatness—a generalization from what was common to all. Night, in its sad, solemn, and benevolent aspect, apart from its stealthy and cruel side, was personified in this form. Darkness endows the small and ordinary ones among mankind with poetical power, and even the suffering woman threw her idea into figure.

text: Reclined where she rested, she tipped her face up to him just as she’d once lifted her gaze to a man back when she could stand steady on her own two feet. The creature, as homeless and unmoored as she was, drew back respectfully a pace or two when she shifted, and when he saw she did not repel him, he licked her hand again.
source_text: In her reclining position she looked up to him just as in earlier times she had, when standing, looked up to a man. The animal, who was as homeless as she, respectfully withdrew a step or two when the woman moved, and, seeing that she did not repulse him, he licked her hand again.

text: The field he wandered through that dawn sloped gently up to a wind-scoured ridge known as Bramblelow Rise. A paved turnpike linking the market towns of Hazelhurst and Stony Cleeve cut right through one of the ridge’s stony spurs. Glancing idly over the thick hawthorn hedge, Elias Thorne spotted a brightly painted spring cart trundling down the slope ahead of him: buttercup yellow, stenciled with cheery floral motifs, pulled by a pair of stout bays, the driver plodding at its side holding his lash straight up as a signal to passersby. The cart was piled high with crockery, woven rugs, and potted houseplants, and perched atop the very peak of the load was a young, strikingly lovely woman. Elias had barely watched the procession for thirty seconds when the cart rumbled to a complete halt directly below where he stood.
source_text: The field he was in this morning sloped to a ridge called Norcombe Hill. Through a spur of this hill ran the highway between Emminster and Chalk-Newton. Casually glancing over the hedge, Oak saw coming down the incline before him an ornamental spring waggon, painted yellow and gaily marked, drawn by two horses, a waggoner walking alongside bearing a whip perpendicularly. The waggon was laden with household goods and window plants, and on the apex of the whole sat a woman, young and attractive. Gabriel had not beheld the sight for more than half a minute, when the vehicle was brought to a standstill just beneath his eyes.

text: She slung a thick woolen shawl tight around her shoulders, crossed the creaky front hall floorboards to the door, and tugged it open. Every grass blade, every bare twig hung perfectly motionless. The air still hummed with damp, though it had thinned somewhat from the cloying, muggy weight of the afternoon, and a steady, soft plop of raindrops hitting crumpled fallen leaves under the overhanging boughs carried a near-musical, lulling regularity. The quiet outside felt far kinder than the suffocating hush of the house behind her, so Elowen pulled the door shut behind her, walking slowly down the rutted lane until she stopped across from Kael’s small cottage. He lived there alone now, having left Merrick’s crowded farmhouse after growing too pinched for space to keep his tools and personal belongings. A single light glowed in one window, tucked on the ground floor. The shutters had been left flung wide, no blind or linen curtain pulled across the glass; neither robbery nor prying observation were contingencies that could do much harm to the cottage’s occupant, who owned little of value and cared even less for strangers’ gazes. Sure enough, it was Kael himself sitting up late, bent over a book. From her spot in the shadow of the hedge on the road she could see him plainly, perched motionless at his wooden table, one hand propping up his mop of sun-bleached curly hair, only glancing up every few minutes to snuff the guttering wick of the tallow candle at his elbow. At last he lifted his gaze to the wooden clock hung on the wall above his mantel, his eyebrows lifting in faint surprise at how late the hour had grown. He snapped his book shut, pushed his chair back, and rose to his feet. He was heading to bed, she knew, and if she meant to tap on his door, she had to do so at once.
source_text: She flung a cloak round her, went to the door and opened it. Every blade, every twig was still. The air was yet thick with moisture, though somewhat less dense than during the afternoon, and a steady smack of drops upon the fallen leaves under the boughs was almost musical in its soothing regularity. It seemed better to be out of the house than within it, and Bathsheba closed the door, and walked slowly down the lane till she came opposite to Gabriel’s cottage, where he now lived alone, having left Coggan’s house through being pinched for room. There was a light in one window only, and that was downstairs. The shutters were not closed, nor was any blind or curtain drawn over the window, neither robbery nor observation being a contingency which could do much injury to the occupant of the domicile. Yes, it was Gabriel himself who was sitting up: he was reading. From her standing-place in the road she could see him plainly, sitting quite still, his light curly head upon his hand, and only occasionally looking up to snuff the candle which stood beside him. At length he looked at the clock, seemed surprised at the lateness of the hour, closed his book, and arose. He was going to bed, she knew, and if she tapped it must be done at once.

text: For several long beats Elias Thorne stood so motionless that it seemed his very spirit had drained out of him along with the ragged, fervent phrases he’d just spoken. He averted his face and turned to leave, his broad silhouette quickly swallowed by the gathering dusk as the soft crunch of his steps blurred into the faint, sibilant rustle of the leafy boughs swaying nearby.
source_text: For a moment Boldwood stood so inertly after this that his soul seemed to have been entirely exhaled with the breath of his passionate words. He turned his face away, and withdrew, and his form was soon covered over by the twilight as his footsteps mixed in with the low hiss of the leafy trees.

text: As a woman of sound good sense when reasoning through matters unclouded by her own emotional investments, Bathsheba genuinely regretted ever following through on that frivolous lark, which had been as much Liddy’s idea as her own, and thus disturbing the quiet, steady calm of a man she held in such high esteem she would never have intentionally set out to tease him.
source_text: Being a woman with some good sense in reasoning on subjects wherein her heart was not involved, Bathsheba genuinely repented that a freak which had owed its existence as much to Liddy as to herself, should ever have been undertaken, to disturb the placidity of a man she respected too highly to deliberately tease.

text: “But Mara,” Tobin went on—“Mara would never suffer a single word of that ilk, nor the smallest offhand phrase spoken without the respect it was owed.... Ah, poor Mara, I often wonder if she had the luck to win her way into Heaven when she passed! She never had much favor from fortune her whole life through, though, so maybe she turned down the other path after all, poor dear.”
source_text: “But Charlotte,” continued Coggan—“not a word of the sort would Charlotte allow, nor the smallest item of taking in vain.... Ay, poor Charlotte, I wonder if she had the good fortune to get into Heaven when ’a died! But ’a was never much in luck’s way, and perhaps ’a went downwards after all, poor soul.”

text: “I ain’t planning to emigrate, mind you; I had no idea you’d be opposed to the thought when I told you of it, else I’d never have entertained it at all,” he said, plain and earnest. “I’ve finalized all the arrangements for Small Bramblecroft Farm, and it’ll be fully in my charge come Lady Day. You know I’ve held a partial stake in the place for years now. Even so, that wouldn’t have stopped me tending to your affairs just the same as I always have, if folks hadn’t started whispering gossip about the two of us.”
source_text: “I am not going to emigrate, you know; I wasn’t aware that you would wish me not to when I told ’ee or I shouldn’t ha’ thought of doing it,” he said, simply. “I have arranged for Little Weatherbury Farm and shall have it in my own hands at Lady-day. You know I’ve had a share in it for some time. Still, that wouldn’t prevent my attending to your business as before, hadn’t it been that things have been said about us.”

text: Elias swiveled to face Elowen. The young woman’s anguish in that moment was too raw, too searing, for any imagination to capture or words to adequately convey. She had crumpled onto the lowest step of the staircase, huddled there motionless: her lips were a parched, dusky blue, her dark eyes locked emptily on his face, as if she could not fathom whether the entire unfolding nightmare was no more than a terrible, unshakable illusion.
source_text: Troy turned to Bathsheba. The poor girl’s wretchedness at this time was beyond all fancy or narration. She had sunk down on the lowest stair; and there she sat, her mouth blue and dry, and her dark eyes fixed vacantly upon him, as if she wondered whether it were not all a terrible illusion.

text: In the space of a heartbeat, he registered the other cruel detail of the disaster. The flock had no insurance. Every penny he had scraped together over a lifetime of frugality was wiped out in a single stroke; his dream of working as an independent farmer was shattered, likely for good. Gabriel had stretched his energy, patience, and relentless industry to their absolute limits over the decade between his eighteenth and twenty-eighth birthdays to reach his current standing, and he had no more reserves left to draw on. He sagged against a fence rail, and buried his face in his hands.
source_text: It was a second to remember another phase of the matter. The sheep were not insured. All the savings of a frugal life had been dispersed at a blow; his hopes of being an independent farmer were laid low—possibly for ever. Gabriel’s energies, patience, and industry had been so severely taxed during the years of his life between eighteen and eight-and-twenty, to reach his present stage of progress that no more seemed to be left in him. He leant down upon a rail, and covered his face with his hands.

text: “I let them slip,” Elias Thorne murmured low to himself. There is no easy way to capture the sharp, jolting weight that quiet admission settled on Thorne right then. He had spent every hour of the dark convinced the oversight he was scrambling to fix was a bizarre, unheard-of lapse—something no other land manager for miles around would ever have let happen. And yet, here in the same parish, an even costlier dereliction had been unfolding for weeks, unnoted, unprotested, unremarked by anyone. Six months prior, the thought of Silas Hale letting his husbandry fall by the wayside would have seemed as absurd as a lighthouse keeper forgetting to tend his beacon. Thorne was just turning over the thought that for all his own hurt over Elowen’s wedding, this man had borne a far deeper, sharper grief, when Hale spoke in a raw, unsteady tone—that of someone desperate to unburden himself, to let the weight he carried spill out at last.
source_text: “Overlooked them,” repeated Gabriel slowly to himself. It is difficult to describe the intensely dramatic effect that announcement had upon Oak at such a moment. All the night he had been feeling that the neglect he was labouring to repair was abnormal and isolated—the only instance of the kind within the circuit of the county. Yet at this very time, within the same parish, a greater waste had been going on, uncomplained of and disregarded. A few months earlier Boldwood’s forgetting his husbandry would have been as preposterous an idea as a sailor forgetting he was in a ship. Oak was just thinking that whatever he himself might have suffered from Bathsheba’s marriage, here was a man who had suffered more, when Boldwood spoke in a changed voice—that of one who yearned to make a confidence and relieve his heart by an outpouring.

text: Sirius the Dog Star and Aldebaran, pointing toward the restless Pleiades cluster, had climbed halfway up the southern sky. Slung between them hung Orion, that resplendent constellation never burning more vividly than it did now, as it soared clear above the low rim of the surrounding landscape. The twin stars Castor and Pollux glowed with their soft, steady light, nearly aligned with the sky’s central meridian; the stark, somber Square of Pegasus crept slowly toward the northwest. Far beyond the tree plantation, Vega sparkled like a suspended lamp strung amid the leafless branches, and Cassiopeia’s Chair perched daintily, balanced right on the very topmost boughs.
source_text: The Dog-star and Aldebaran, pointing to the restless Pleiades, were half-way up the Southern sky, and between them hung Orion, which gorgeous constellation never burnt more vividly than now, as it soared forth above the rim of the landscape. Castor and Pollux with their quiet shine were almost on the meridian: the barren and gloomy Square of Pegasus was creeping round to the north-west; far away through the plantation Vega sparkled like a lamp suspended amid the leafless trees, and Cassiopeia’s chair stood daintily poised on the uppermost boughs.

text: When he drew near, he saw no horses hitched to the wagon, and the whole area around it appeared entirely deserted. Judging by its placement, the vehicle looked as if it had been left there for the night; save for half a truss of hay piled loosely across its floor, it was completely empty. Gabriel lowered himself onto the wagon’s shafts and turned his current circumstances over in his mind. He reckoned he had already covered a good portion of his journey, and after tramping on foot since daybreak, he was sorely tempted to stretch out on the hay inside rather than push on to the village of Weatherbury, where he would have to pay for a night’s lodging.
source_text: On coming close, he found there were no horses attached to it, the spot being apparently quite deserted. The waggon, from its position, seemed to have been left there for the night, for beyond about half a truss of hay which was heaped in the bottom, it was quite empty. Gabriel sat down on the shafts of the vehicle and considered his position. He calculated that he had walked a very fair proportion of the journey; and having been on foot since daybreak, he felt tempted to lie down upon the hay in the waggon instead of pushing on to the village of Weatherbury, and having to pay for a lodging.

text: The air felt like an eye struck blind mid-blink. The heavy wagon and its crated cargo no longer balanced on the thin line between clear air and mist, but was swallowed whole by a springy, uniform haze that stretched pale and unbroken in every direction. Not a breath of wind stirred the stillness, not a single glistening drop of moisture fell onto the leaves of the firs, birches, and beeches that made up the dense wood crowding both sides of the track. The trees held themselves rigid with a quiet intensity, as if they had been waiting for hours, hungry for a gust of wind to sway their heavy boughs. A jarring, thick silence draped over all the surrounding landscape—so complete that the crunch of the wagon’s wheels over packed earth sounded like a great roar, and faint rustles that would never have caught a listener’s ear except in the deepest dark of night stood out sharp and distinctly individual.
source_text: The air was as an eye suddenly struck blind. The waggon and its load rolled no longer on the horizontal division between clearness and opacity, but were imbedded in an elastic body of a monotonous pallor throughout. There was no perceptible motion in the air, not a visible drop of water fell upon a leaf of the beeches, birches, and firs composing the wood on either side. The trees stood in an attitude of intentness, as if they waited longingly for a wind to come and rock them. A startling quiet overhung all surrounding things—so completely, that the crunching of the waggon-wheels was as a great noise, and small rustles, which had never obtained a hearing except by night, were distinctly individualized.

text: “Ah, I mean to do right by all of this,” said Silas Thrift, in a warm, half-munching cadence of bright anticipation. “Aye; meat and drink are a cheering sort of thing, putting spine into those who lack it, if you’ll pardon the turn of phrase. It’s the gospel of the flesh, so to speak—we’d perish outright without it.”
source_text: “Well, I hope to do my duty by it all,” said Joseph Poorgrass, in a pleasant, masticating manner of anticipation. “Yes; victuals and drink is a cheerful thing, and gives nerves to the nerveless, if the form of words may be used. ’Tis the gospel of the body, without which we perish, so to speak it.”

text: The small flock huddled shoulder to shoulder on the far side of the split-rail fence, clustered tight around a stand of gorse thickets, and the first odd detail Elias noticed when he poked his head over the weathered planks was that they did not startle or bolt at the sight of him. Some deeper, sharper dread had curdled in their bones, more urgent than their usual instinct to flee human presence. But that was not the most striking quirk: every single ewe and lamb was positioned so their tails pointed unswervingly toward the roiling, charcoal-gray edge of the sky where the tempest was building. A tight inner knot of sheep pressed chest to flank, while those further out fanned wider and wider, the whole arrangement bearing a striking resemblance to a scalloped lace collar, with the gorse clump standing exactly where the wearer’s neck would rest beneath the fabric.
source_text: They were crowded close together on the other side around some furze bushes, and the first peculiarity observable was that, on the sudden appearance of Oak’s head over the fence, they did not stir or run away. They had now a terror of something greater than their terror of man. But this was not the most noteworthy feature: they were all grouped in such a way that their tails, without a single exception, were towards that half of the horizon from which the storm threatened. There was an inner circle closely huddled, and outside these they radiated wider apart, the pattern formed by the flock as a whole not being unlike a vandyked lace collar, to which the clump of furze-bushes stood in the position of a wearer’s neck.

text: I’ll say it once more: you are an utterly captivating woman. There is nothing at all unusual about me stating that, is there? The truth of it is plain enough to anyone with eyes, I’m sure. Miss Everdene, I may have spoken my mind too bluntly to please you, and for that matter, my opinion is likely too trivial to convince you of anything. But it is perfectly honest, all the same—why can’t that be enough to excuse it?
source_text: “I again say you are a most fascinating woman. There’s nothing remarkable in my saying so, is there? I’m sure the fact is evident enough. Miss Everdene, my opinion may be too forcibly let out to please you, and, for the matter of that, too insignificant to convince you, but surely it is honest, and why can’t it be excused?”

text: “Just a shepherd,” Gabriel repeated, his tone flat and heavy with unmistakeable finality. His mind was wandering back through old memories, his gaze fixed on the girl’s feet, and it was only then that he spotted a bundle of some kind lying on the ground right next to them. She must have caught the direction his eyes had drifted, because she spoke up in a soft, coaxing lilt.
source_text: “Only a shepherd,” Gabriel repeated, in a dull cadence of finality. His thoughts were directed to the past, his eyes to the feet of the girl; and for the first time he saw lying there a bundle of some sort. She may have perceived the direction of his face, for she said coaxingly,—

text: “I don’t have much to say for myself, and I don’t care to ramble on about it,” Coggan continued, already leaning into that preachy, principle-spouting streak people get when they’ve had a few drinks. “But I’ve never flipped on a single one of my beliefs, not once. I’ve stuck like a band-aid to the old faith I was born and raised in, no exceptions. Yeah, and that’s the best thing about the regular old Church, if you ask me. You can be a fully committed member, still hang around your warm, familiar old pub every evening, and never waste a second fretting over nitpicky religious rules at all. But if you’re one of those chapel goers? You’ve got to drag yourself to services no matter how brutal the weather is, and work yourself into such a frenzy you look like you’re acting out a silly skit half the time. Not that chapel folks aren’t clever enough in their own way, mind you. They can pull the most beautiful, off-the-cuff prayers right out of their heads, all about their families and whatever shipwreck they caught a story about in the latest newspaper.”
source_text: “I won’t say much for myself; I don’t wish to,” Coggan continued, with that tendency to talk on principles which is characteristic of the barley-corn. “But I’ve never changed a single doctrine: I’ve stuck like a plaster to the old faith I was born in. Yes; there’s this to be said for the Church, a man can belong to the Church and bide in his cheerful old inn, and never trouble or worry his mind about doctrines at all. But to be a meetinger, you must go to chapel in all winds and weathers, and make yerself as frantic as a skit. Not but that chapel members be clever chaps enough in their way. They can lift up beautiful prayers out of their own heads, all about their families and shipwrecks in the newspaper.”

text: “I wish I knew how to say all the polite, flattering lines you deserve,” the farmer continued, his tone growing more relaxed, “and wrap these rough, unruly feelings of mine in something graceful and polished. But I don’t have the knack for that sort of thing, nor the patience to learn how to pull it off. I want you to be my wife—so badly that there’s no space left in me for any other feeling to take root. I never would have spoken up like this, though, if I hadn’t been led to hold out hope first.”
source_text: “I wish I could say courteous flatteries to you,” the farmer continued in an easier tone, “and put my rugged feeling into a graceful shape: but I have neither power nor patience to learn such things. I want you for my wife—so wildly that no other feeling can abide in me; but I should not have spoken out had I not been led to hope.”

text: Everyone in his small circle of confidants had noticed he’d been uncharacteristically restless and high-strung ever since Bramblewood Fair, right up to the disastrous Christmas Eve where everything unspooled; but not a single one of them had picked up on the unambiguous markers of mental unravelling that only Elowen Voss and Kael Rowe, entirely separately and in passing, had once briefly wondered about. When a locked closet off his bedchamber was forced open, a bizarre, carefully hoarded collection of items came to light. There were multiple uncut lengths of women’s fine clothing fabric, ranging from glossy embroidered silks and buttery satin to thick wool poplin and plush velvet, all in the exact deep, rich hues Elowen consistently favored in her own gowns. Two luxe fur muffs, one sable and one snowy ermine, sat atop the fabric stack, and above them rested a velvet-lined jewelry case holding four weighty gold bracelets, half a dozen intricately worked lockets, and several gem-set rings, all crafted to the highest standard. He had purchased these pieces intermittently over months in Bristol and surrounding market towns, smuggling them back to his cottage under cover of dark to avoid notice. Each item was individually wrapped in crisp tissue paper, and every package bore the handwritten label “Elowen Thorne,” paired with a date six full years in the future, scrawled in the same tight, slanted script across every tag.
source_text: That he had been from the time of Greenhill Fair until the fatal Christmas Eve in excited and unusual moods was known to those who had been intimate with him; but nobody imagined that there had shown in him unequivocal symptoms of the mental derangement which Bathsheba and Oak, alone of all others and at different times, had momentarily suspected. In a locked closet was now discovered an extraordinary collection of articles. There were several sets of ladies’ dresses in the piece, of sundry expensive materials; silks and satins, poplins and velvets, all of colours which from Bathsheba’s style of dress might have been judged to be her favourites. There were two muffs, sable and ermine. Above all there was a case of jewellery, containing four heavy gold bracelets and several lockets and rings, all of fine quality and manufacture. These things had been bought in Bath and other towns from time to time, and brought home by stealth. They were all carefully packed in paper, and each package was labelled “Bathsheba Boldwood,” a date being subjoined six years in advance in every instance.

text: She stepped to the edge of the thicket, craning her neck first one way, then peering over to the far side of the hawthorn hedge. Elias was just preparing to step out and hand back the linen handkerchief she’d dropped moments earlier, when an unanticipated bit of whimsy made him suspend his movement for the time being. The dirt track, once it curved past the weathered stone cowshed, cut straight through the middle of the old pine plantation. It was no wide bridle road—only a narrow footpath worn soft by decades of walkers, and the pine boughs stretched out level barely seven feet above the packed earth, making it impossible for anyone on horseback to sit upright and pass beneath without getting swatted by needles. The young woman, who wore a simple cotton frock instead of a formal riding habit, glanced over her shoulders twice, as if confirming no other soul was nearby to see her, then flipped nimbly backward until she lay flat across the pony’s broad back, her head lolling near its tail, her boots propped against its shoulders, her gaze fixed straight up at the patchy blue sky between the boughs. She shifted into the position as fast as a kingfisher diving for minnows, as quiet as a hawk gliding on a thermal. Elias could barely track the movement, quick as it was. The tall, rangy pony seemed perfectly accustomed to such antics, and plodded along unconcerned without so much as a twitch of its ear. Thus she slipped unhindered beneath the low-hanging boughs.
source_text: She came up and looked around—then on the other side of the hedge. Gabriel was about to advance and restore the missing article when an unexpected performance induced him to suspend the action for the present. The path, after passing the cowshed, bisected the plantation. It was not a bridle-path—merely a pedestrian’s track, and the boughs spread horizontally at a height not greater than seven feet above the ground, which made it impossible to ride erect beneath them. The girl, who wore no riding-habit, looked around for a moment, as if to assure herself that all humanity was out of view, then dexterously dropped backwards flat upon the pony’s back, her head over its tail, her feet against its shoulders, and her eyes to the sky. The rapidity of her glide into this position was that of a kingfisher—its noiselessness that of a hawk. Gabriel’s eyes had scarcely been able to follow her. The tall lank pony seemed used to such doings, and ambled along unconcerned. Thus she passed under the level boughs.

text: “Barely a mile left!” the woman murmured under her breath. “No, farther than that,” she amended after a beat of quiet. “That mile measurement only leads to the county hall, and my resting place is on the far side of Casterbridge. Just a little over a mile, and I’ll be there.” After another lull in the silence, she spoke again, her voice thin with fatigue. “Five or six steps to a yard—six, most likely. I’ve still got seventeen hundred yards to cover. A hundred times six is six hundred. Seventeen times that much… Oh, pity me, Lord!”
source_text: “Less than a mile!” the woman murmured. “No; more,” she added, after a pause. “The mile is to the county hall, and my resting-place is on the other side Casterbridge. A little over a mile, and there I am!” After an interval she again spoke. “Five or six steps to a yard—six perhaps. I have to go seventeen hundred yards. A hundred times six, six hundred. Seventeen times that. O pity me, Lord!”

text: “I haven’t fallen for you, Mr. Boldwood—I have to make that perfectly clear.” As she spoke, a tiny smile tugged across her usually solemn face for the very first time. The flash of her even white upper teeth, paired with the sharply defined lips everyone had already taken note of, gave a fleeting impression of cold heartlessness, an impression that was immediately erased by the warm, pleasant glow of her eyes.
source_text: “I have not fallen in love with you, Mr. Boldwood—certainly I must say that.” She allowed a very small smile to creep for the first time over her serious face in saying this, and the white row of upper teeth, and keenly-cut lips already noticed, suggested an idea of heartlessness, which was immediately contradicted by the pleasant eyes.

text: “Is that really what you are saying, Frank?” Bathsheba asked, sorrow ringing in every word—her voice was soft and strained now, stripped of the bright, buoyant fullness it had held the summer prior. “You have gambled away more than a hundred pounds in a single month on that dreadful horse racing? Oh Frank, this is unforgivably cruel. How can you be so reckless, to take my money and throw it away like that? We shall have to leave the farm. That is the only end this can come to.”
source_text: “And you mean, Frank,” said Bathsheba, sadly—her voice was painfully lowered from the fulness and vivacity of the previous summer—“that you have lost more than a hundred pounds in a month by this dreadful horse-racing? O, Frank, it is cruel; it is foolish of you to take away my money so. We shall have to leave the farm; that will be the end of it!”

text: By that point, the dazed stupor she’d been trapped in had given way to quiet anxiety, and that anxiety soon made room for sharp, insistent pangs of hunger and thirst. Just then, a figure appeared on the low rise on the far side of the swamp, half-shrouded in mist, and began moving toward Bathsheba. It was a woman, approaching with her head tilted askance, as if she was carefully scanning every part of the area around her. When she veered a little further left and drew closer still, Bathsheba could make out the newcomer’s profile outlined against the bright, sunlit sky. She knew that soft, flowing curve stretching from forehead to chin, with no sharp angles or defined harsh lines anywhere along it, instantly: it was the familiar contour of Liddy Smallbury.
source_text: By this time stupor had given place to anxiety, and anxiety began to make room for hunger and thirst. A form now appeared upon the rise on the other side of the swamp, half-hidden by the mist, and came towards Bathsheba. The woman—for it was a woman—approached with her face askance, as if looking earnestly on all sides of her. When she got a little further round to the left, and drew nearer, Bathsheba could see the newcomer’s profile against the sunny sky, and knew the wavy sweep from forehead to chin, with neither angle nor decisive line anywhere about it, to be the familiar contour of Liddy Smallbury.

text: The sight before her was steeped in unvarnished misery, yet she held her gaze steady for long moments, indulging, as young women are prone to do, in the soft, bittersweet fantasy of the life she might have lived had Kael been Elias, and the giddy, uneven path of her infatuation run straight along the line of her duty. She heaped unnecessary torment upon herself by conjuring images of him wooing another woman, her own face and name wiped entirely from his mind; she had seen far enough into Kael’s flighty nature to gauge his tendencies with painful accuracy, and yet the thought that he might soon cease to care for her did not weaken her love – if anything, it fanned that reckless devotion into an even hotter, more unshakable flame.
source_text: It was a picture full of misery, but for a while she contemplated it firmly, allowing herself, nevertheless, as girls will, to dwell upon the happy life she would have enjoyed had Troy been Boldwood, and the path of love the path of duty—inflicting upon herself gratuitous tortures by imagining him the lover of another woman after forgetting her; for she had penetrated Troy’s nature so far as to estimate his tendencies pretty accurately, but unfortunately loved him no less in thinking that he might soon cease to love her—indeed, considerably more.

text: Boldwood made no haste to return home. It was well after ten o’clock when, walking at a deliberate pace through the lower end of Weatherbury, he caught the rattle of the carrier’s spring van as it entered the village. The vehicle ran regular routes to and from a town situated to the north, owned and operated by a local Weatherbury man, and it now pulled up directly at the threshold of his home. The lamp fixed to the front of its hood cast light over a figure clad in scarlet and gold, who was the first to step down from the van.
source_text: Boldwood did not hurry homeward. It was ten o’clock at least, when, walking deliberately through the lower part of Weatherbury, he heard the carrier’s spring van entering the village. The van ran to and from a town in a northern direction, and it was owned and driven by a Weatherbury man, at the door of whose house it now pulled up. The lamp fixed to the head of the hood illuminated a scarlet and gilded form, who was the first to alight.

text: Bathsheba slept very lightly that night, never sinking into any kind of deep, unbroken rest. Her attendant had long since drifted off, soft, steady breaths drifting through from the next room, but the mistress of the house remained fixed at her window, gazing at the faint glimmer seeping out from between the tree boughs. It did not burn with a steady glow, but blinked on and off like a rotating coastal lighthouse beacon, though she never connected that pulsing pattern to the fact that someone was moving back and forth directly in front of the light. She sat there until the first raindrops began to fall, snuffing the glimmer out entirely. Only then did she pull herself away to climb into bed, where she tossed and turned restlessly, her frayed, worn-out mind replaying the ugly, jarring scene from the previous night on a loop.
source_text: Bathsheba did not sleep very heavily that night. When her attendant was unconscious and softly breathing in the next room, the mistress of the house was still looking out of the window at the faint gleam spreading from among the trees—not in a steady shine, but blinking like a revolving coast-light, though this appearance failed to suggest to her that a person was passing and repassing in front of it. Bathsheba sat here till it began to rain, and the light vanished, when she withdrew to lie restlessly in her bed and re-enact in a worn mind the lurid scene of yesternight.

text: The inside of the tent was dim, steeped in a strange, distinctive soft shadow. The odd, glowing half-translucence of crisp autumn late afternoons and early evenings turned the handful of pale gold sunbeams slipping through tears and gaps in the canvas into dramatic Rembrandt-style light effects: they streaked like sprays of fine gold dust across the hazy, dusky blue air that filled the entire space, before landing on the opposite inner canvas lining, where they glowed like tiny, suspended lamps.
source_text: The interior was shadowy with a peculiar shade. The strange luminous semi-opacities of fine autumn afternoons and eves intensified into Rembrandt effects the few yellow sunbeams which came through holes and divisions in the canvas, and spirted like jets of gold-dust across the dusky blue atmosphere of haze pervading the tent, until they alighted on inner surfaces of cloth opposite, and shone like little lamps suspended there.

text: Coggan gave a fitting response. Oak kept shearing, and didn’t speak another word. Sour, gnawing restlessness had settled over him, chasing off his usual quiet calm. Bathsheba had dropped hints that she might elevate him above the rest of the workers, naming him the bailiff the farm so desperately needed. The position itself, as just a role managing the farm, didn’t appeal to him at all: what he’d coveted was the closeness it would bring to her, the woman he loved, who was not yet promised to anyone else. All his previous guesses about her feelings and intentions felt foggy and untrustworthy now. That sharp lecture he’d given her, he thought, was one of the most idiotic mistakes he’d ever made. She hadn’t been flirting with Boldwood at all; instead, she’d been toying with him, putting on a whole show of leading another man on just to rattle him. He was convinced deep down that his laid-back, less educated coworkers had been right all along—by the end of the day, Boldwood would be Miss Everdene’s accepted fiancé. At this point in his life, Gabriel had outgrown the automatic, instinctive dislike of reading the Bible that nearly every Christian boy carries through childhood, and he pored over its pages fairly often now. In his head, he bit out a verse he knew well: “‘I find more bitter than death the woman whose heart is snares and nets!’” That was just a burst of angry frustration, nothing more—froth floating on the surface of a roiling storm. He adored Bathsheba just as fiercely as he always had.
source_text: Coggan furnished a suitable reply. Oak went on with his shearing, and said not another word. Pestilent moods had come, and teased away his quiet. Bathsheba had shown indications of anointing him above his fellows by installing him as the bailiff that the farm imperatively required. He did not covet the post relatively to the farm: in relation to herself, as beloved by him and unmarried to another, he had coveted it. His readings of her seemed now to be vapoury and indistinct. His lecture to her was, he thought, one of the absurdest mistakes. Far from coquetting with Boldwood, she had trifled with himself in thus feigning that she had trifled with another. He was inwardly convinced that, in accordance with the anticipations of his easy-going and worse-educated comrades, that day would see Boldwood the accepted husband of Miss Everdene. Gabriel at this time of his life had out-grown the instinctive dislike which every Christian boy has for reading the Bible, perusing it now quite frequently, and he inwardly said, “‘I find more bitter than death the woman whose heart is snares and nets!’” This was mere exclamation—the froth of the storm. He adored Bathsheba just the same.

text: Shifts between the seasons make far less of a loud, obvious entrance on tracts of this sort than they do in the dappled hush of old-growth woodlands. Even so, they are no less visible to anyone who pays careful, lingering attention; the only distinction is that the signs they use to announce themselves are far less commonplace and widely recognized than the unfurling of spring leaf buds or the crisp drift of autumn foliage to the forest floor. Many of these signals are not nearly as slow and furtive as one might assume, given the quiet, almost dormant air that hangs over a stretch of open heath and barren upland waste. When winter arrives to this stretch of countryside, it rolls in in clear, distinct phases, each bringing a new marker one could track in turn: the slither of adders and grass snakes retreating to their underground dens, the bracken fronds bleaching and curling at the edges, rain-swollen hollows filling to become dark, still pools, banks of clammy mist clinging low to the ground, the first hard frost turning all remaining greenery to deep russet, puffball and toadstool colonies shrivelling into dark smudges, and finally a thick fall of snow blanking out every last uneven edge of the landscape.
source_text: The changes of the seasons are less obtrusive on spots of this kind than amid woodland scenery. Still, to a close observer, they are just as perceptible; the difference is that their media of manifestation are less trite and familiar than such well-known ones as the bursting of the buds or the fall of the leaf. Many are not so stealthy and gradual as we may be apt to imagine in considering the general torpidity of a moor or waste. Winter, in coming to the country hereabout, advanced in well-marked stages, wherein might have been successively observed the retreat of the snakes, the transformation of the ferns, the filling of the pools, a rising of fogs, the embrowning by frost, the collapse of the fungi, and an obliteration by snow.

text: “That’s a perfectly reasonable question,” he returned—and there was a strange dissonance to the grave, unyielding earnestness he brought to debating the merits of a handwritten valentine. “You know it’s always taken for granted that quiet, private inquiries will be made: that’s where the—fun of it lies.” If he had swapped the word “fun” for “torture,” it could not have been spoken with a more taut, fidgety countenance than the one Hale wore in that exact instant.
source_text: “The question was perfectly fair,” he returned—and there was something incongruous in the serious earnestness with which he applied himself to an argument on a valentine. “You know it is always expected that privy inquiries will be made: that’s where the—fun lies.” If the word “fun” had been “torture,” it could not have been uttered with a more constrained and restless countenance than was Boldwood’s then.

text: It was late May when the farmer made up his mind he wouldn’t let small, trivial hurdles turn him away any longer, or let the gnawing, unresolvable suspense keep him frozen in place. By this point, he’d grown fully accustomed to being in love; even when the feeling ached sharper and twisted tighter than it ever had before, it no longer caught him off guard, and he was sure he had what it took to handle whatever came of the moment. When he stopped by her house to ask for her, the people there told him she was out at the sheep washing, so he set off immediately for that spot to track her down.
source_text: It was the end of May when the farmer determined to be no longer repulsed by trivialities or distracted by suspense. He had by this time grown used to being in love; the passion now startled him less even when it tortured him more, and he felt himself adequate to the situation. On inquiring for her at her house they had told him she was at the sheep-washing, and he went off to seek her there.

text: For Bathsheba, any choice made in a rush was always a reckless one; and unusually, whenever she gave herself time to deliberate, she was guaranteed to act sensibly. The catch, though, was that she almost never carved out that extra time for herself. Right then, the only person in the whole parish whose take on her and her choices she trusted more than her own judgment was Gabriel Oak. His character was so unflinchingly, bluntly honest that she could count on him to give a fully unbiased take no matter the topic, even if it was about her having feelings for another man, or marrying someone else—all she had to do was ask. He was fully convinced there was no chance she’d ever return his own romantic feelings, so he held himself to a strict, noble code not to sabotage any other suitor’s shot with her. That’s the most stoic, unselfish virtue a person in love can have, just as failing to live up to that standard is the most forgivable fault a lover can commit. She knew he’d give her a truthful answer, so she asked him the question anyway, even though she had to have realized how much that topic would hurt him. That’s the kind of casual selfishness some utterly captivating women carry with them. To be fair, there was a small excuse for her leaning on his honesty for her own gain even when it pained him: she truly didn’t have anyone else whose good sense she could easily access to weigh in on the matter.
source_text: With Bathsheba a hastened act was a rash act; but, as does not always happen, time gained was prudence insured. It must be added, however, that time was very seldom gained. At this period the single opinion in the parish on herself and her doings that she valued as sounder than her own was Gabriel Oak’s. And the outspoken honesty of his character was such that on any subject, even that of her love for, or marriage with, another man, the same disinterestedness of opinion might be calculated on, and be had for the asking. Thoroughly convinced of the impossibility of his own suit, a high resolve constrained him not to injure that of another. This is a lover’s most stoical virtue, as the lack of it is a lover’s most venial sin. Knowing he would reply truly she asked the question, painful as she must have known the subject would be. Such is the selfishness of some charming women. Perhaps it was some excuse for her thus torturing honesty to her own advantage, that she had absolutely no other sound judgment within easy reach.

text: She turned sharply away from him, so he would not spot the mood written so openly across her face, and stepped inside dogged by a miserable faintness and a throbbing brow. Nearly an hour later, she heard the rattle of the waggon and went back out, still acutely conscious that her expression held all her muddled, troubled feelings. Joseph, dressed in his very best suit, was hitching the horse to the waggon to set off. The shrubs and flowers were piled high in the back exactly as she had ordered, though Bathsheba barely registered their presence now.
source_text: She turned herself away from him, that he might no longer notice the mood which had set its sign so visibly upon her, and went indoors with a distressing sense of faintness and a beating brow. About an hour after, she heard the noise of the waggon and went out, still with a painful consciousness of her bewildered and troubled look. Joseph, dressed in his best suit of clothes, was putting in the horse to start. The shrubs and flowers were all piled in the waggon, as she had directed; Bathsheba hardly saw them now.

text: Bathsheba held unwaveringly to her conviction until Monday, when two coinciding circumstances shattered her certainty. The first was a short item in the local newspaper, which, set down in the precise, methodical prose of a careful writer, laid out formidable, highly credible evidence that Troy had drowned, alongside the critical testimony of young Dr. Barker of Budmouth, who wrote to the editor to state he had witnessed the accident firsthand. In his account, he noted he had been traversing the cliffs on the cove’s far side just as the sun was sinking below the horizon. At that moment, he spotted a bather caught in the current just outside the cove’s mouth, and knew instantly the man had precious little chance of survival unless he possessed uncommon physical strength. The swimmer drifted behind a rocky outcrop of the coastline, and Barker followed along the shore in the same direction. But by the time he managed to reach a vantage point high enough to see the stretch of sea beyond the rock, dusk had settled, and no further sign of the man could be found.
source_text: Bathsheba remained firm in this opinion till Monday, when two circumstances conjoined to shake it. The first was a short paragraph in the local newspaper, which, beyond making by a methodizing pen formidable presumptive evidence of Troy’s death by drowning, contained the important testimony of a young Mr. Barker, M.D., of Budmouth, who spoke to being an eyewitness of the accident, in a letter to the editor. In this he stated that he was passing over the cliff on the remoter side of the cove just as the sun was setting. At that time he saw a bather carried along in the current outside the mouth of the cove, and guessed in an instant that there was but a poor chance for him unless he should be possessed of unusual muscular powers. He drifted behind a projection of the coast, and Mr. Barker followed along the shore in the same direction. But by the time that he could reach an elevation sufficiently great to command a view of the sea beyond, dusk had set in, and nothing further was to be seen.

text: Dawn crept steadily up the horizon. Even the full pale glow of the unobscured early sky held less brightness than an overcast midday by the time Boldwood roused himself and pulled on his clothes. He descended the staircase, stepped outside, and made his way toward the gate of an eastward pasture. Leaning his weight against its wooden slats, he paused and let his eyes wander across the surrounding stretch of land.
source_text: Then the dawn drew on. The full power of the clear heaven was not equal to that of a cloudy sky at noon, when Boldwood arose and dressed himself. He descended the stairs and went out towards the gate of a field to the east, leaning over which he paused and looked around.

text: He cut in, his voice thrumming with urgent feeling. “Don’t misread me like that, okay? I’m just being upfront about what any man in my shoes would’ve thought, and now you’re flushing bright red and snapping at me for it. That whole line about you not being good enough for me is total nonsense. Everyone in the parish notices how poised and well-spoken you are, like a proper gentlewoman, and I’ve heard your uncle over in Weatherbury runs a massive farm—way bigger than anything I’ll ever own. Can I stop by to visit on evenings, or would you come for a walk with me on Sundays? You don’t have to make your mind up right away, if you’d rather take more time to think it over.”
source_text: He broke in passionately. “But don’t mistake me like that! Because I am open enough to own what every man in my shoes would have thought of, you make your colours come up your face, and get crabbed with me.
