That about your not being good enough for me is nonsense. You speak like a lady—all the parish notice it, and your uncle at Weatherbury is, I have heerd, a large farmer—much larger than ever I shall be. May I call in the evening, or will you walk along with me o’ Sundays? I don’t want you to make-up your mind at once, if you’d rather not.”

text: The striking young woman loitered idly in her place for some time, the only sound cutting through the stillness the soft patter of the canary hopping up and down the perches of its cage. Then she directed her gaze downward with careful intent. Her focus was not on the bird, nor the cat curled near it; it settled on a rectangular paper-wrapped parcel tucked between the two. She craned her neck to check if the waggoner was approaching. He was still nowhere in sight, and her eyes drifted back to the package, her thoughts clearly fixed on what might lie inside. At length she pulled the item into her lap and untied the knotted paper covering; a small hanging hand mirror was revealed, and she leaned in to survey her reflection attentively. She parted her lips and smiled.
source_text: The handsome girl waited for some time idly in her place, and the only sound heard in the stillness was the hopping of the canary up and down the perches of its prison. Then she looked attentively downwards. It was not at the bird, nor at the cat; it was at an oblong package tied in paper, and lying between them. She turned her head to learn if the waggoner were coming. He was not yet in sight; and her eyes crept back to the package, her thoughts seeming to run upon what was inside it. At length she drew the article into her lap, and untied the paper covering; a small swing looking-glass was disclosed, in which she proceeded to survey herself attentively. She parted her lips and smiled.

text: “Oh no, ma’am,” said Matthew. “Two of his store ewes wandered into a patch of vetches the other day, and they looked exactly like these ones. He sent a man on horseback over here posthaste to fetch Gable, and Gable went out and saved both of ’em. Farmer Boldwood’s got the tool they use for that job, you know. It’s a hollow pipe with a sharp pricker fitted inside it. That’s right, isn’t it, Joseph?”
source_text: “O no, ma’am” said Matthew. “Two of his store ewes got into some vetches t’other day, and were just like these. He sent a man on horseback here post-haste for Gable, and Gable went and saved ’em. Farmer Boldwood hev got the thing they do it with. ’Tis a holler pipe, with a sharp pricker inside. Isn’t it, Joseph?”

text: She rose again nearly before the first faint hint of dawn touched the edge of the sky, pushing open the window to draw deep breaths of the crisp new morning air. The panes were beaded with trembling, teardrop-shaped remnants of the night’s rain, each rounded glistening bead catching a pale soft luster from primrose-hued streaks cutting through low-hanging clouds in the slowly awakening sky. A steady drip pattered down from tree boughs onto the drifts of fallen leaves heaped beneath them, and from the direction of the church drifted a distinct, unusual sound: constant, not stop-start and intermittent like the tree drip, the soft purl of running water spilling into a still pool.
source_text: Almost before the first faint sign of dawn appeared she arose again, and opened the window to obtain a full breathing of the new morning air, the panes being now wet with trembling tears left by the night rain, each one rounded with a pale lustre caught from primrose-hued slashes through a cloud low down in the awakening sky. From the trees came the sound of steady dripping upon the drifted leaves under them, and from the direction of the church she could hear another noise—peculiar, and not intermittent like the rest, the purl of water falling into a pool.

text: Kael stepped onto the gravel track that curved behind the old stone belfry. The track, far rougher and crunchier underfoot when he’d hiked it after dark the previous evening, was now sheathed in a thin, slick layer of tawny mud that muted its sharp stone edges. Halfway along its length, he spotted a clump of gnarled, stringy roots, scrubbed pale and clean as a handful of stripped sinew. He bent to pluck it—surely this couldn’t be a fragment of the cowslips he’d tucked into the bank last spring? As he walked further, he spotted one bulb, then a second, then a third peeking half-buried from the mud. There was no mistaking them: they were the crocus corms he’d set out in neat rows two weeks prior. His face tight with confused, hollow dismay, Kael rounded the bend and came face to face with the full extent of the flood damage the swollen creek had left in its wake.
source_text: He entered the gravel path which would take him behind the tower. The path, instead of being stony as it had been the night before, was browned over with a thin coating of mud. At one place in the path he saw a tuft of stringy roots washed white and clean as a bundle of tendons. He picked it up—surely it could not be one of the primroses he had planted? He saw a bulb, another, and another as he advanced. Beyond doubt they were the crocuses. With a face of perplexed dismay Troy turned the corner and then beheld the wreck the stream had made.

text: Until just a few weeks prior, the gully’s surging runoff had been caught by a jumble of loose field stones strewn across the bank, which buffered the underlying soil from the full brunt of the current. Those stones had been hauled away over the warm summer months, leaving nothing but unprotected packed earth to hold back the cascade. The creek hadn’t sprayed so far past the old watchtower’s foundation in close to a decade, and no one had thought to account for such an unlikely turn. This tucked-away hollow often sat empty for two or three years at a stretch, and the rare soul who settled there was almost always a destitute vagrant, a deer poacher, or some other petty offender whose misdeeds carried little public weight.
source_text: The force of the stream had, until very lately, been received upon some loose stones spread thereabout, which had acted as a shield to the soil under the onset. These during the summer had been cleared from the ground, and there was now nothing to resist the down-fall but the bare earth. For several years the stream had not spouted so far from the tower as it was doing on this night, and such a contingency had been over-looked. Sometimes this obscure corner received no inhabitant for the space of two or three years, and then it was usually but a pauper, a poacher, or other sinner of undignified sins.

text: The pair stood there wrapped in thick, awkward silence for a few long seconds, Bathsheba dabbing at her thoroughly soaked, flushed, stinging face in a way she hoped would fly under his radar. Finally, Oak spoke. “I haven’t seen you—wait, I mean, I haven’t spoken to you—in so long, have I?” But he was afraid he would dredge up distressing old memories, so he cut himself off abruptly to ask, “Were you heading into the church?”
source_text: Then they stood in a state of some embarrassment, Bathsheba trying to wipe her dreadfully drenched and inflamed face without his noticing her. At length Oak said, “I’ve not seen you—I mean spoken to you—since ever so long, have I?” But he feared to bring distressing memories back, and interrupted himself with: “Were you going into church?”

text: The most grievous, singular apex of Elara’s desperate ingenuity arrived when, chest rising and falling fast, she pushed herself up into a hunched stance, rested her two slight forearms on the hound’s broad shoulders, leaned her weight steadily against him, and murmured soft, urging words under her breath. Though her chest ached with unspoken grief, her tone stayed bright and buoyant, and there was no stranger paradox than that a creature so sturdy should draw courage from one so frail, or that warm hope could be kindled by a spirit so crushed by despair. Her companion paced forward at a crawl, and she picked her way beside him in tiny, shuffling steps, half her mass bearing down on his broad frame. Every now and then her knees buckled, just as they had when she’d tried walking unassisted, when she’d relied on crutches, when she’d clung to roadside fence posts for support. The hound, who now fully grasped what she needed and how little she could manage on her own, grew nearly frantic with worry when she stumbled; he would yank at the hem of her skirt and dart a few paces ahead, as if trying to pull her onward. She always called him back to her side, and anyone watching might have noticed she now strained to catch the sound of other people only to veer out of their way. It was clear she was determined to keep her presence on the lonely lane and her wretched condition hidden from all passersby.
source_text: The ultimate and saddest singularity of woman’s effort and invention was reached when, with a quickened breathing, she rose to a stooping posture, and, resting her two little arms upon the shoulders of the dog, leant firmly thereon, and murmured stimulating words. Whilst she sorrowed in her heart she cheered with her voice, and what was stranger than that the strong should need encouragement from the weak was that cheerfulness should be so well stimulated by such utter dejection. Her friend moved forward slowly, and she with small mincing steps moved forward beside him, half her weight being thrown upon the animal. Sometimes she sank as she had sunk from walking erect, from the crutches, from the rails. The dog, who now thoroughly understood her desire and her incapacity, was frantic in his distress on these occasions; he would tug at her dress and run forward. She always called him back, and it was now to be observed that the woman listened for human sounds only to avoid them. It was evident that she had an object in keeping her presence on the road and her forlorn state unknown.

text: Mr. Jan Coggan, who’d just passed the cup across to Henery, was a florid, crimson-complexioned man with a broad, expansive face and a faint, secret glint in his eye. Over the twenty years prior, his name had appeared again and again on the marriage registers of Weatherbury and neighboring parishes, listed as best man and chief witness for countless unions. He also very often filled the post of head godfather at those quietly, understatedly jovial baptism ceremonies.
source_text: Mr. Jan Coggan, who had passed the cup to Henery, was a crimson man with a spacious countenance and private glimmer in his eye, whose name had appeared on the marriage register of Weatherbury and neighbouring parishes as best man and chief witness in countless unions of the previous twenty years; he also very frequently filled the post of head godfather in baptisms of the subtly-jovial kind.

text: Tobin Mear spoke, his heavy, red-rimmed eyes glistening with quiet wretchedness as he mumbled: “I can’t fathom why a lass would take a husband when she’s stout enough to fight her own battles, and has no use for a ready-made home; all it does is shut some other poor woman out of a match. But let it be, for ’tis a pity he and she would go on to trouble two separate households.”
source_text: Henery Fray spoke, exhibiting miserable eyes at the same time: “I don’t see why a maid should take a husband when she’s bold enough to fight her own battles, and don’t want a home; for ’tis keeping another woman out. But let it be, for ’tis a pity he and she should trouble two houses.”

text: Soft, indistinct noises drifted from the barn, so he turned his gaze that way. Figures emerged one by one and in pairs through its doors, all moving stiffly and sheepishly, except the man at their head, who wore a bright red jacket, strolling forward with his hands shoved in his pockets, whistling casually. The rest stumbled along behind him, looking clearly guilt-ridden; the entire procession bore a striking resemblance to Flaxman’s illustrated group of suitors shuffling toward the underworld, led onward by Mercury. Those gnarled, hunched shapes filtered into the village, their leader Troy breaking off to enter the farmhouse. Not a single one of them had so much as glanced toward the ricks, nor appeared to spare even a passing thought for their condition.
source_text: Faint sounds came from the barn, and he looked that way. Figures stepped singly and in pairs through the doors—all walking awkwardly, and abashed, save the foremost, who wore a red jacket, and advanced with his hands in his pockets, whistling. The others shambled after with a conscience-stricken air: the whole procession was not unlike Flaxman’s group of the suitors tottering on towards the infernal regions under the conduct of Mercury. The gnarled shapes passed into the village, Troy, their leader, entering the farmhouse. Not a single one of them had turned his face to the ricks, or apparently bestowed one thought upon their condition.

text:
Boldwood drifted, half-distracted, as he took in how the frost had crusted and glazed the snow’s surface until it glowed beneath the faint red eastern dawn with the smooth, luminous sheen of polished marble; how, on patches of the sloping ground, dried grass stems sheathed in thin icicles jutted through the pale, smooth blanket of snow, twisted and curled into the strange, looping shapes of antique Venetian glassware; and how the tiny footprints left by a handful of birds, which had hopped across the snow while it was still a soft, downy fleece, were now frozen solid, preserved temporarily in the hard crust.

A muffled, soft clatter of light wheels cut through his reverie. Boldwood turned back toward the road. It was the mail cart: a rickety two-wheeled contraption, so flimsy it seemed a single strong gust of wind could knock it off course. The driver held out a letter. Boldwood grabbed it and tore it open, fully expecting another anonymous note—such is the nature of human assumptions about probability, that we default entirely to the belief that what has happened before will repeat itself.
source_text: Boldwood was listlessly noting how the frost had hardened and glazed the surface of the snow, till it shone in the red eastern light with the polish of marble; how, in some portions of the slope, withered grass-bents, encased in icicles, bristled through the smooth wan coverlet in the twisted and curved shapes of old Venetian glass; and how the footprints of a few birds, which had hopped over the snow whilst it lay in the state of a soft fleece, were now frozen to a short permanency. A half-muffled noise of light wheels interrupted him. Boldwood turned back into the road. It was the mail-cart—a crazy, two-wheeled vehicle, hardly heavy enough to resist a puff of wind. The driver held out a letter. Boldwood seized it and opened it, expecting another anonymous one—so greatly are people’s ideas of probability a mere sense that precedent will repeat itself.

text: Bathsheba only knew two or three of the local farmers personally, and those were the first people she’d sought out to talk business. But if she was going to be the practical, capable woman she’d promised herself she’d be, she couldn’t let a lack of formal introductions grind her operations to a halt. Eventually, she built up enough confidence to speak and reply boldly to men she’d only ever heard about through word of mouth. She carried her own sample bags, too, and over time mastered the standard professional move of pouring grain straight into her palm: she’d hold the kernels up in her narrow hand to inspect them, pulling off the gesture exactly the way every seasoned trader in Casterbridge did.
source_text: Two or three only of the farmers were personally known to Bathsheba, and to these she had made her way. But if she was to be the practical woman she had intended to show herself, business must be carried on, introductions or none, and she ultimately acquired confidence enough to speak and reply boldly to men merely known to her by hearsay. Bathsheba too had her sample-bags, and by degrees adopted the professional pour into the hand—holding up the grains in her narrow palm for inspection, in perfect Casterbridge manner.

text: She tilted her head to glance over her shoulder, the gateman’s flickering candle catching the quick, bright clarity of her eyes as she moved, stepped through the gateway, and was soon swallowed whole by the dense, enfolding shadows of the quiet, mysterious summer boughs overhead. Coggan and Gabriel wheeled their horses around, the soft, velvety July night air fanning gently at their cheeks, and headed back down the exact stretch of road they had traveled to get there.
source_text: She turned her head—the gateman’s candle shimmering upon her quick, clear eyes as she did so—passed through the gate, and was soon wrapped in the embowering shades of mysterious summer boughs. Coggan and Gabriel put about their horses, and, fanned by the velvety air of this July night, retraced the road by which they had come.

text: Boldwood stepped close to wish her good morning, his bearing so stiff and constrained that she instantly knew he’d only crossed over to the washing area hoping she wouldn’t be there. She also noticed the sharp set of his brow and the dismissive flicker of his gaze as he looked at her. Bathsheba immediately made to leave, slipping quietly along the riverbank until she was a full stone’s throw away. She heard the soft scrape of footsteps through the grass, and a sudden awareness washed over her that affection was curling around her as tangibly and warmly as a fine perfume. Instead of stopping or turning back, Bathsheba moved deeper into the tall, dense sedges, but Boldwood seemed unshakably determined, pressing onward until the two of them were fully around the river’s curve. Hidden from sight here, they could still make out the splashing of water and the loud, lively shouts of the washers upstream.
source_text: Boldwood came close and bade her good morning, with such constraint that she could not but think he had stepped across to the washing for its own sake, hoping not to find her there; more, she fancied his brow severe and his eye slighting. Bathsheba immediately contrived to withdraw, and glided along by the river till she was a stone’s throw off. She heard footsteps brushing the grass, and had a consciousness that love was encircling her like a perfume. Instead of turning or waiting, Bathsheba went further among the high sedges, but Boldwood seemed determined, and pressed on till they were completely past the bend of the river. Here, without being seen, they could hear the splashing and shouts of the washers above.

text: “There is absolutely no way I will let Mr. Boldwood attend to this—*I* will see it done. Fanny was my uncle’s servant, and though I only knew her for a handful of days, she falls to my care. It is so unbearably sad, the very thought of Fanny ending up in a workhouse.” Bathsheba had come to know the sharp weight of suffering herself by this point, and she spoke with unfeigned, earnest feeling. “Send someone over to Mr. Boldwood’s to say that Mrs. Troy will take full responsibility for collecting the family’s old servant. We have no business moving her in a common waggon; we will arrange for a hearse instead.”
source_text: “Indeed I shall not let Mr. Boldwood do any such thing—I shall do it! Fanny was my uncle’s servant, and, although I only knew her for a couple of days, she belongs to me. How very, very sad this is!—the idea of Fanny being in a workhouse.” Bathsheba had begun to know what suffering was, and she spoke with real feeling.... “Send across to Mr. Boldwood’s, and say that Mrs. Troy will take upon herself the duty of fetching an old servant of the family.... We ought not to put her in a waggon; we’ll get a hearse.”

text: “Well,” Troy continued, “I’ll admit there’s such a thing as praise that lands as rudeness, and I’ll own that might be my misstep here. All the same, there’s a kind of treatment that’s pure injustice, and that’s what you’re guilty of right now. Just because a plain, blunt man who never learned to mask his thoughts speaks his mind without meaning any offense, he’s to be sharply cut off and dressed down like he’s no better than a good-for-nothing scoundrel?”
source_text: “Well,” continued Troy, “I suppose there is a praise which is rudeness, and that may be mine. At the same time there is a treatment which is injustice, and that may be yours. Because a plain blunt man, who has never been taught concealment, speaks out his mind without exactly intending it, he’s to be snapped off like the son of a sinner.”

text: “I’ll do anything at all!” she said, her tone unshakable. Right away she hefted a sheaf of straw onto her shoulder, scrambled up the rick right on his heels, set it down behind the measuring rod, and climbed back down to grab the next one. When she was halfway up for the third time, the entire rick flared abruptly, glowing with the brassy, vivid sheen of polished majolica—every tiny knotted bump on each individual straw stood out sharp and clear. Two human silhouettes, as dark as polished jet, popped into view on the sloped ground directly in front of him. Then the glow faded from the rick, and the silhouettes were gone in an instant. Gabriel twisted to look over his shoulder. That had been the sixth lightning burst, coming from the eastern sky right behind him, and the two dark shapes he’d spotted on the slope were just his and Bathsheba’s own shadows.
source_text: “I’ll do anything!” she said, resolutely. She instantly took a sheaf upon her shoulder, clambered up close to his heels, placed it behind the rod, and descended for another. At her third ascent the rick suddenly brightened with the brazen glare of shining majolica—every knot in every straw was visible. On the slope in front of him appeared two human shapes, black as jet. The rick lost its sheen—the shapes vanished. Gabriel turned his head. It had been the sixth flash which had come from the east behind him, and the two dark forms on the slope had been the shadows of himself and Bathsheba.

text: He stepped forward, reaching his arm out once more. Bathsheba had just caught up to him at the spot where a short, stunted holly bush stood, heavy with clusters of bright red berries. When she realized his posture suggested he might try to pull her into his arms, if not squeeze her tight against him, she edged away and circled around the bush.
source_text: He went forward and stretched out his arm again. Bathsheba had overtaken him at a point beside which stood a low stunted holly bush, now laden with red berries. Seeing his advance take the form of an attitude threatening a possible enclosure, if not compression, of her person, she edged off round the bush.

text: Bramble’s End was the Leipzig of the Welsh Marches; the most bustling, joyous, raucous day in the entire calendar of chartered market gatherings was the day of the annual sheep fair. This yearly convocation took place atop a broad hill where the remains of an ancient earthwork stood in remarkably good repair: a massive earthen rampart and ditch, oval in shape, that girdled the hill’s entire crown, though it had crumbled and worn away in scattered spots. Winding cart tracks climbed the slope to each of the two main openings cut into the earthwork on opposite sides, and the flat, grassy expanse of ten to fifteen acres enclosed by the old bank served as the fairgrounds. A handful of permanent wooden sheds dotted the site, but the majority of visitors relied solely on canvas shelters for resting and eating during the length of their stay.
source_text: Greenhill was the Nijni Novgorod of South Wessex; and the busiest, merriest, noisiest day of the whole statute number was the day of the sheep fair. This yearly gathering was upon the summit of a hill which retained in good preservation the remains of an ancient earthwork, consisting of a huge rampart and entrenchment of an oval form encircling the top of the hill, though somewhat broken down here and there. To each of the two chief openings on opposite sides a winding road ascended, and the level green space of ten or fifteen acres enclosed by the bank was the site of the fair. A few permanent erections dotted the spot, but the majority of visitors patronized canvas alone for resting and feeding under during the time of their sojourn here.

text: Troy’s forehead crumpled into a deep, tight furrow. He ground his jaw firmly together, his pressed lips twitching like those of a man wracked with searing, unspoken agony. Against the tangled knot of conflicting emotions already churning inside him, this bizarre, unforeseen accident landed like the sharpest, most cruel sting he had ever known. Troy wore every feeling plainly on his features, and any onlooker who caught sight of him now would barely have recognized the man who had once laughed until his sides ached, sung through sunlit afternoons, and murmured soft, silly love-trifles into a lover’s ear. His first instinct was to rail against his wretched, rotten lot in life, but even that small, base act of rebellion required a spark of energy he no longer possessed, hollowed out as he was by the sick, throbbing misery that held him fast in its grip. This sight, stacked on top of all the grim, soul-heavy events of the days prior, formed a brutal climax to the whole grim panorama of recent misfortunes, and it was more than he could endure. Sanguine by nature, Troy had long been able to elude grief by simply hitting pause on it: he could set the thought of any particular haunting spectre aside, leave it untouched long enough for time to soften its sharp, cutting edges. Planting flowers on Fanny’s grave had likely been just another version of that evasion, a way to avoid confronting the rawness of his primary grief head-on, and now it felt as if that quiet intention had been seen through and deliberately circumvented.
source_text: Troy’s brow became heavily contracted. He set his teeth closely, and his compressed lips moved as those of one in great pain. This singular accident, by a strange confluence of emotions in him, was felt as the sharpest sting of all. Troy’s face was very expressive, and any observer who had seen him now would hardly have believed him to be a man who had laughed, and sung, and poured love-trifles into a woman’s ear. To curse his miserable lot was at first his impulse, but even that lowest stage of rebellion needed an activity whose absence was necessarily antecedent to the existence of the morbid misery which wrung him. The sight, coming as it did, superimposed upon the other dark scenery of the previous days, formed a sort of climax to the whole panorama, and it was more than he could endure. Sanguine by nature, Troy had a power of eluding grief by simply adjourning it. He could put off the consideration of any particular spectre till the matter had become old and softened by time. The planting of flowers on Fanny’s grave had been perhaps but a species of elusion of the primary grief, and now it was as if his intention had been known and circumvented.

text: For anyone perched alone on a moorland rise on a crisp midnight like this, the world’s slow eastward roll feels nearly tangible enough to touch. The feeling might stem from the slow panoramic drift of stars past terrestrial landmarks, a shift noticeable after just a few minutes of unbroken stillness, or from the unobstructed view of the cosmos a high vantage point grants, or the tug of the wind on your sleeves, or the quiet weight of being entirely alone; no matter its source, the sense of gliding steadily forward is sharp and lingers long after you first notice it. We often speak of the poetry of motion as a trivial, everyday delight, but to experience its grand, sweeping epic iteration, you need to stand on that high rise in the deep small hours of the night, first let yourself unfurl with the quiet thrill of being set apart from the bulk of civilized humanity, all of them swaddled in dreams and utterly oblivious to these cosmic goings-on right now, then linger long and silent to observe your own stately drift through the constellations. After that kind of nocturnal survey of the skies, it takes a real effort to sink back down into the mundane rhythms of earth, and to accept that the awareness of such grand, sweeping velocity springs from a single, minuscule human frame.
source_text: To persons standing alone on a hill during a clear midnight such as this, the roll of the world eastward is almost a palpable movement. The sensation may be caused by the panoramic glide of the stars past earthly objects, which is perceptible in a few minutes of stillness, or by the better outlook upon space that a hill affords, or by the wind, or by the solitude; but whatever be its origin, the impression of riding along is vivid and abiding. The poetry of motion is a phrase much in use, and to enjoy the epic form of that gratification it is necessary to stand on a hill at a small hour of the night, and, having first expanded with a sense of difference from the mass of civilised mankind, who are dreamwrapt and disregardful of all such proceedings at this time, long and quietly watch your stately progress through the stars. After such a nocturnal reconnoitre it is hard to get back to earth, and to believe that the consciousness of such majestic speeding is derived from a tiny human frame.

text: The world shifted completely before Bathsheba’s eyes in the span of a heartbeat. Golden shafts plucked from the dipping sun glinted above, beside, and straight ahead of her, so bright they nearly blotted out ground and sky alike, all cast by the masterful, dazzling arcs of Troy’s polished blade. It seemed to occupy every space at once, never lingering long enough to be pinned to a single spot. The looping flashes came paired with a sharp, high-pitched rush, nearly a whistle, that also seemed to pour in from every direction around her at once. All told, she was enclosed in a swirling dome of glinting light and sharp, piercing whirs, as if a whole sky of streaking meteors had descended to hover right within arm’s reach.
source_text: In an instant the atmosphere was transformed to Bathsheba’s eyes. Beams of light caught from the low sun’s rays, above, around, in front of her, well-nigh shut out earth and heaven—all emitted in the marvellous evolutions of Troy’s reflecting blade, which seemed everywhere at once, and yet nowhere specially. These circling gleams were accompanied by a keen rush that was almost a whistling—also springing from all sides of her at once. In short, she was enclosed in a firmament of light, and of sharp hisses, resembling a sky-full of meteors close at hand.

text: The edge of wild excitement in his tone made her almost afraid of him in that moment, even as she felt a sharp tug of sympathy for him. It was no more than a simple, instinctive physical fear—the wariness of the vulnerable in the face of someone far stronger; there was no emotional dislike or inner revulsion tangled up in the feeling. When she spoke, a note of distress softened her voice, for she could still vividly recall the furious outburst he’d had on Yalbury Road, and shrank from the thought of witnessing another fit of his rage.
source_text: His tone was so excited that she almost feared him at this moment, even whilst she sympathized. It was a simple physical fear—the weak of the strong; there was no emotional aversion or inner repugnance. She said, with some distress in her voice, for she remembered vividly his outburst on the Yalbury Road, and shrank from a repetition of his anger:—

text: Bathsheba’s heart leaped with gratitude at the realization she was not entirely abandoned, and she sprang to her feet. “Oh, Liddy!” she tried to cry out, but the words only took shape silently against her lips, no sound issuing from her throat at all. Hours of breathing that thick, stagnant air through the long stretch of the night had robbed her of her voice entirely.
source_text: Bathsheba’s heart bounded with gratitude in the thought that she was not altogether deserted, and she jumped up. “Oh, Liddy!” she said, or attempted to say; but the words had only been framed by her lips; there came no sound. She had lost her voice by exposure to the clogged atmosphere all these hours of night.

text: “If you’d never turned up, I would certainly—yes, certainly—have won her approval by now. If you’d never laid eyes on her, you might well be married to Fanny already. All said, the gap between your social station and Miss Everdene’s is far too wide for this flirtation of yours to ever lead to the favorable outcome of a marriage between you. So all I ask is this: leave her be, no more pestering. Marry Fanny, and I’ll make it well worth your while.”
source_text: “If you had not come I should certainly—yes, _certainly_—have been accepted by this time. If you had not seen her you might have been married to Fanny. Well, there’s too much difference between Miss Everdene’s station and your own for this flirtation with her ever to benefit you by ending in marriage. So all I ask is, don’t molest her any more. Marry Fanny. I’ll make it worth your while.”

text: Troy was always on the move, but his constant activity was less purposeful than a steam engine and more like the unplanned spread of a climbing plant; he never chose a clear core aim or direction for his efforts, so he simply poured energy into whatever random thing chance dropped in front of him. That’s why, while he could occasionally turn out brilliantly sharp, off-the-cuff lines when speaking, his actions were far worse than unremarkable—he simply couldn’t guide the first flickers of effort into anything coherent. He had a quick, perceptive mind and no shortage of inherent force of character, but he lacked the ability to make those two strengths work in tandem. His sharp perception would fixate on meaningless small details while it waited for his will to give it a clear goal, and all that inherent grit burned away on pointless, repetitive dead ends because he never let his good judgment steer it.
source_text: Troy was full of activity, but his activities were less of a locomotive than a vegetative nature; and, never being based upon any original choice of foundation or direction, they were exercised on whatever object chance might place in their way. Hence, whilst he sometimes reached the brilliant in speech because that was spontaneous, he fell below the commonplace in action, from inability to guide incipient effort. He had a quick comprehension and considerable force of character; but, being without the power to combine them, the comprehension became engaged with trivialities whilst waiting for the will to direct it, and the force wasted itself in useless grooves through unheeding the comprehension.

text: Elias hesitated, his gaze darting away for half a second before he settled it back on her face. He did not care for her deeply enough these days to let her headstrong whims pull him further than he was willing to bend. Yet good manners required he keep his tone gentle, not sharp. “You wrong me with that constant, suspicious edge,” he said. “Boxing me into this tiny, restrictive set of expectations you’ve laid out hardly becomes you, especially when our courtship is still in its earliest days.”
source_text: Troy hesitated. He did not now love her enough to allow himself to be carried too far by her ways. Yet it was necessary to be civil. “You wrong me by such a suspicious manner,” he said. “Such strait-waistcoating as you treat me to is not becoming in you at so early a date.”

text: Set in the open meadows stood the sheep-washing pool, a perfectly round brick basin brimming with water so clear it seemed to hold nothing but light. For soaring birds miles overhead, its glassy surface caught the pale sky so sharply it must have looked like a gleaming Cyclops’ eye set in the wide green face of the fields. The grass edging its rim at this time of year was a small, unmissable sight that stuck in the memory long after; you could almost watch it drinking up moisture from the damp, nutrient-rich sod beneath it, so vigorous was its growth. Beyond the flat water-meadow, the land rose and fell into rolling, dimpled pastures where every bloom popping up right now was either a bright buttercup or a soft white daisy. The river slipped past as silently as a shadow, its damp banks lined with plump, swaying reeds and sedges that formed a supple, living fence along its edge. To the north of the meadow stood a cluster of trees, their leaves newly unfurled, soft and dewy, not yet turned rigid and deep green under the baking summer sun and dry spells. Their hue shifted between pale yellow and tender green, one bleeding into the other wherever you looked. From deep within the shelter of those leafy boughs, the loud, clear calls of three cuckoos rang out, cutting through the still, quiet air.
source_text: The sheep-washing pool was a perfectly circular basin of brickwork in the meadows, full of the clearest water. To birds on the wing its glassy surface, reflecting the light sky, must have been visible for miles around as a glistening Cyclops’ eye in a green face. The grass about the margin at this season was a sight to remember long—in a minor sort of way. Its activity in sucking the moisture from the rich damp sod was almost a process observable by the eye. The outskirts of this level water-meadow were diversified by rounded and hollow pastures, where just now every flower that was not a buttercup was a daisy. The river slid along noiselessly as a shade, the swelling reeds and sedge forming a flexible palisade upon its moist brink. To the north of the mead were trees, the leaves of which were new, soft, and moist, not yet having stiffened and darkened under summer sun and drought, their colour being yellow beside a green—green beside a yellow. From the recesses of this knot of foliage the loud notes of three cuckoos were resounding through the still air.

text: Perched right on the very top of the stacked moving load, the girl sat perfectly still. Upside-down tables and chairs ringed her on all sides, with a heavy oak settle propped up behind her back. Lined up in front of her were potted geraniums, myrtles, and cacti, alongside a canary in a small cage—every last one of these items had almost certainly been pulled straight from the windows of the house they’d just vacated. There was a cat tucked into a woven willow basket too, peeking out through its half-opened lid with half-closed, drowsy eyes, as it gazed fondly at the small birds gathered nearby.
source_text: The girl on the summit of the load sat motionless, surrounded by tables and chairs with their legs upwards, backed by an oak settle, and ornamented in front by pots of geraniums, myrtles, and cactuses, together with a caged canary—all probably from the windows of the house just vacated. There was also a cat in a willow basket, from the partly-opened lid of which she gazed with half-closed eyes, and affectionately surveyed the small birds around.

text: A woman can receive harsh treatment that tastes sweet to her, and bluntness that carries no real offense. Bathsheba would have meekly submitted to a sharp, indignant rebuke for her frivolity, so long as Gabriel paired it with a declaration that he loved her all the while. The hot, headlong rush of unrequited passion is bearable even when it stings and hurls bitter accusations—there is a quiet triumph in that kind of humiliation, and a soft, hidden tenderness tangled up in the strife. That was exactly what she had been anticipating, and what she had not gotten. Being lectured by a man who saw her only in the cold, unsparing morning light of disillusion, shutters thrown wide to strip away every last trace of romance, was unbearably exasperating. Nor was he finished speaking. He went on, his voice more agitated than before:—
source_text: A woman may be treated with a bitterness which is sweet to her, and with a rudeness which is not offensive. Bathsheba would have submitted to an indignant chastisement for her levity had Gabriel protested that he was loving her at the same time; the impetuosity of passion unrequited is bearable, even if it stings and anathematizes—there is a triumph in the humiliation, and a tenderness in the strife. This was what she had been expecting, and what she had not got. To be lectured because the lecturer saw her in the cold morning light of open-shuttered disillusion was exasperating. He had not finished, either. He continued in a more agitated voice:—

text: “I do, I do; but no steward’s place for me—I earned that post fair and square,” wailed Tobit, marking his squandered gifts by staring blankly at the ghost of a grand destiny he seemed to see stretched across Jeb Merton’s frayed smock-frock. “Ah well, ’twas fated, I reckon. Your lot is your lot, and the Good Book’s nothing but empty words; for if you do good every day of your life, you don’t get rewarded according to your labors, just cheated out of your rightful due in some low, shabby trick.”
source_text: “I do, I do; but no baily—I deserved that place,” wailed Henery, signifying wasted genius by gazing blankly at visions of a high destiny apparently visible to him on Billy Smallbury’s smock-frock. “There, ’twas to be, I suppose. Your lot is your lot, and Scripture is nothing; for if you do good you don’t get rewarded according to your works, but be cheated in some mean way out of your recompense.”

text: Bathsheba hesitated. She had heard wondrous occasional accounts from Weatherbury locals who’d happened to stay a while in Casterbridge near the barracks, of that strange, glorious display: the sword exercise. Men and boys who’d peeped through cracks or over walls into the barrack yard returned calling it the most dazzling affair imaginable; their gear and blades glistened like stars, darting here, there, all around, yet every movement followed strict, methodical order. So she spoke gently what she felt intensely.
source_text: Bathsheba hesitated. She had heard wondrous reports from time to time by dwellers in Weatherbury, who had by chance sojourned awhile in Casterbridge, near the barracks, of this strange and glorious performance, the sword-exercise. Men and boys who had peeped through chinks or over walls into the barrack-yard returned with accounts of its being the most flashing affair conceivable; accoutrements and weapons glistening like stars—here, there, around—yet all by rule and compass. So she said mildly what she felt strongly.

text: Cainy hurries forward now, tar pot in hand. The letters “B. E.” are freshly stamped into the sheep’s newly shorn hide, and the uncomplicated ewe leaps away huffing, over the wooden board to join the rest of the bare-skinned, newly shorn flock waiting outside. Next comes Maryann, who tucks loose stray tufts into the center of the full fleece, rolls it tight, and carries it off to the storage area at the rear: three and a half pounds of pure, unadulterated warmth, destined to bring winter comfort to unknown strangers who live far away. Those strangers will never experience the singular soft warmth of the wool in this exact moment, though—fresh and unspoiled, before the natural oily coating it bore when it grew on the living animal has dried, stiffened, and been washed out during processing. Right now, it is as far superior to any manufactured woollen item as thick, rich cream is to watered-down milk.
source_text: Cainy now runs forward with the tar-pot. “B. E.” is newly stamped upon the shorn skin, and away the simple dam leaps, panting, over the board into the shirtless flock outside. Then up comes Maryann; throws the loose locks into the middle of the fleece, rolls it up, and carries it into the background as three-and-a-half pounds of unadulterated warmth for the winter enjoyment of persons unknown and far away, who will, however, never experience the superlative comfort derivable from the wool as it here exists, new and pure—before the unctuousness of its nature whilst in a living state has dried, stiffened, and been washed out—rendering it just now as superior to anything _woollen_ as cream is superior to milk-and-water.

text: Farmer Boldwood made his way steadily toward Miss Everdene’s house. When he reached the front of the property and stepped near the entrance, he caught sight of a lit lamp glowing in the parlor. The blind had not been pulled down, and Bathsheba was visible inside, leaning over a pile of letters or documents to read through them. Her back was turned entirely to Boldwood. He walked up to the door, knocked firmly, and waited, every muscle drawn taut, a dull ache thrumming across his forehead.
source_text: Farmer Boldwood went on towards Miss Everdene’s house. He reached the front, and approaching the entrance, saw a light in the parlour. The blind was not drawn down, and inside the room was Bathsheba, looking over some papers or letters. Her back was towards Boldwood. He went to the door, knocked, and waited with tense muscles and an aching brow.

text: The scheme felt entirely doable, for Harker had barely a dozen lines across the whole production, all of them clipped and brisk; the play’s entire draw rested on its stunts, not its dialogue. So the performance kicked off right on schedule, and at the prearranged beat, Red Molly bounded into the sun-dappled ring to raucous cheers from the crowd. When the turnpike sequence unfolded, with Harker and Molly sprinting through the midnight dark, constables hard on their heels, and the groggy tollkeeper in a frayed flannel nightcap swearing no rider had come through his gate, Gideon Hale let loose a deep, chest-thumping “Good on him!” that cut clear over the fairground’s lowing and chatter for every soul to hear. Toby Prewitt’s face split into a giddy, wide grin, tickled by the sharp dramatic irony: their hero cleared the gate in one effortless leap, while the bumbling forces of the law had to yank their own mounts to a clumsy halt and wait for the tollkeeper to fumble the lock open. When Jem Roach fell dead mid-scene, Toby couldn’t stop himself from clutching Gideon’s wrist, eyes glistening as he breathed, “He ain’t actually hit, Gid—just putting on a show, right?” And when the final, somber sequence played out, and the body of the brave, loyal Molly was meant to be borne off on a wooden shutter by twelve volunteer spectators, Toby couldn’t hold himself back from darting forward to help, calling over his shoulder for Gideon to come with him: “Think of the stories we’ll tell down at Merton’s for years, Gid! We’ll pass this down to the little ones, mark my words.” For decades after, folks in Bramble End would listen to Toby hold forth like a man with a lifetime of grand adventures under his belt, recounting how he’d rested the shutter on his own shoulder, and felt the hard curve of Molly’s hoof under his palm as he carried her out. If, as certain philosophers claim, immortality is nothing more than being held fast in the memories of other people, then Red Molly earned eternal life that afternoon, even if she’d never come close to it before.
source_text: This seemed feasible enough, for Turpin’s speeches were not many or long, the fascination of the piece lying entirely in the action; and accordingly the play began, and at the appointed time Black Bess leapt into the grassy circle amid the plaudits of the spectators. At the turnpike scene, where Bess and Turpin are hotly pursued at midnight by the officers, and the half-awake gatekeeper in his tasselled nightcap denies that any horseman has passed, Coggan uttered a broad-chested “Well done!” which could be heard all over the fair above the bleating, and Poorgrass smiled delightedly with a nice sense of dramatic contrast between our hero, who coolly leaps the gate, and halting justice in the form of his enemies, who must needs pull up cumbersomely and wait to be let through. At the death of Tom King, he could not refrain from seizing Coggan by the hand, and whispering, with tears in his eyes, “Of course he’s not really shot, Jan—only seemingly!” And when the last sad scene came on, and the body of the gallant and faithful Bess had to be carried out on a shutter by twelve volunteers from among the spectators, nothing could restrain Poorgrass from lending a hand, exclaiming, as he asked Jan to join him, “’Twill be something to tell of at Warren’s in future years, Jan, and hand down to our children.” For many a year in Weatherbury, Joseph told, with the air of a man who had had experiences in his time, that he touched with his own hand the hoof of Bess as she lay upon the board upon his shoulder. If, as some thinkers hold, immortality consists in being enshrined in others’ memories, then did Black Bess become immortal that day if she never had done so before.

text: Bathsheba was flooded with flustered, dazed confusion, and she spoke in a trembling, half-suspicious tone thick with raw feeling: “Can this be true? Oh, how could it possibly be that you care for me, and so out of the blue? You’ve seen so little of who I really am: I might not be nearly as— as warm, as pretty as I look to you right now. Please, take it back; oh, won’t you? I cannot, and will not, keep it. You have to believe me, your generosity overwhelms me. I’ve never done you even the smallest kindness, so why on earth would you be so good to me?”
source_text: Bathsheba was brimming with agitated bewilderment, and she said, in half-suspicious accents of feeling, “Can it be! Oh, how can it be, that you care for me, and so suddenly! You have seen so little of me: I may not be really so—so nice-looking as I seem to you. Please, do take it; Oh, do! I cannot and will not have it. Believe me, your generosity is too great. I have never done you a single kindness, and why should you be so kind to me?”

text: It was true. For well-mannered drinking sprees of the better sort, the kind that left you no closer to the devil than you’d been before you took your first sip, there was nothing to rival those hosted in Farmer Everdene’s kitchen. Not a single curse was permitted; no, not even a faint, harmless one, even at the most cheerfully rowdy moments when everyone was too far gone in drink to think straight, though dropping that good old sinful word every now and again at such times is a great relief to a soul bent on merrymaking.
source_text: “’Twas. But for a wet of the better class, that brought you no nearer to the horned man than you were afore you begun, there was none like those in Farmer Everdene’s kitchen. Not a single damn allowed; no, not a bare poor one, even at the most cheerful moment when all were blindest, though the good old word of sin thrown in here and there at such times is a great relief to a merry soul.”

text: Troy stepped into Weatherbury churchyard at roughly ten o’clock, and made straight for the corner where he’d marked out the empty grave plot first thing that morning. It sat on the unobtrusive side of the tower, almost entirely hidden from the view of people passing along the adjacent road. Until just a short while before, that spot had been left forgotten, heaped with piles of loose stone and thick alder bushes, but it had recently been cleared and straightened out to accommodate new burials, as the rest of the cemetery grounds were filling up at a rapid clip.
source_text: Troy entered Weatherbury churchyard about ten o’clock and went immediately to the corner where he had marked the vacant grave early in the morning. It was on the obscure side of the tower, screened to a great extent from the view of passers along the road—a spot which until lately had been abandoned to heaps of stones and bushes of alder, but now it was cleared and made orderly for interments, by reason of the rapid filling of the ground elsewhere.

text: Flames poured from a tall straw stack, already so far consumed that there was no possibility of saving it. A rick burns very differently from a house. When wind drives the fire inward, the flaming portions dissolve entirely like melting sugar, and the stack’s original outline disappears completely from view. A tightly packed hay or wheat rick, however, will hold out against burning for a long stretch of time if the fire first takes hold on its outer surface.
source_text: The fire was issuing from a long straw-stack, which was so far gone as to preclude a possibility of saving it. A rick burns differently from a house. As the wind blows the fire inwards, the portion in flames completely disappears like melting sugar, and the outline is lost to the eye. However, a hay or a wheat-rick, well put together, will resist combustion for a length of time, if it begins on the outside.

text: “Got a hunk of sourdough and smoked ham the mistress sent over, herdsman. That hard cider’ll sit a whole lot easier with a little something to soak it up, see? Don’t bite down too hard now, mind – I dropped the ham right in the rutted lane out front when I was hauling it over, so it might be a touch sandy. Aw, it’s just plain clean dirt anyway, we all know that’s no real harm, like you’ve said before, and we can tell you’re not the sort to kick up a fuss over a little grit, herdsman.”
source_text: “And here’s a mouthful of bread and bacon that mis’ess have sent, shepherd. The cider will go down better with a bit of victuals. Don’t ye chaw quite close, shepherd, for I let the bacon fall in the road outside as I was bringing it along, and may be ’tis rather gritty. There, ’tis clane dirt; and we all know what that is, as you say, and you bain’t a particular man we see, shepherd.”

text: To any outside onlooker, there was barely anything objectionable about that comment. But for Oak, who knew Bathsheba was fully conscious that she herself was the real reason the poor ewe had been wounded—because she’d hurt the ewe’s shearer in a far more vital, vulnerable part—it carried a sharp sting that his persistent sense of being beneath both her and Boldwood was never going to soften. Still, his steady, unflinching resolve to openly acknowledge he no longer felt any romantic interest in her helped him hide that hurt from time to time.
source_text: To an outsider there was not much to complain of in this remark; but to Oak, who knew Bathsheba to be well aware that she herself was the cause of the poor ewe’s wound, because she had wounded the ewe’s shearer in a still more vital part, it had a sting which the abiding sense of his inferiority to both herself and Boldwood was not calculated to heal. But a manly resolve to recognize boldly that he had no longer a lover’s interest in her, helped him occasionally to conceal a feeling.

text: He mumbled the words half to himself, but every person loitering on the porch caught them clear as a bell. Elias Thorne held his tongue after that, and the faint clink of glass and hum of conversation from inside the house drifted out to them again, soft enough to nearly blend with the rustle of maple leaves. The quiet stretched only a few minutes, before the faint rattle of light buggy wheels cut through, winding down the grassy slope above the lane. The sound grew steadily louder, crunching over loose gravel before cutting off abruptly right at the property gate. Elias turned and hurried back to the front door, yanking it wide; the warm golden lamplight from the entry spilled across the walk, gilding Elowen Voss as she climbed the flagstone path toward them.
source_text: He said this to himself, and they all distinctly heard it. Boldwood remained silent after that, and the noise from indoors was again just audible, until, a few minutes later, light wheels could be distinguished coming down the hill. They drew nearer, and ceased at the gate. Boldwood hastened back to the door, and opened it; and the light shone upon Bathsheba coming up the path.

text: Elowen never could shake the faint, fleeting image of Marnie picking her way across the fen toward her through the pale gold of early morning. Shimmering, opalescent pockets of cold marsh gas seeped up from the dewy, damp turf alongside the housemaid’s scuffed boots with each step she took, popping with a soft sibilant fizz before drifting upward to meld with the wispy fog that hung low over the wetland. Marnie never slipped beneath the muck, as Elowen had quietly feared she would.
source_text: Bathsheba never forgot that transient little picture of Liddy crossing the swamp to her there in the morning light. Iridescent bubbles of dank subterranean breath rose from the sweating sod beside the waiting-maid’s feet as she trod, hissing as they burst and expanded away to join the vapoury firmament above. Liddy did not sink, as Bathsheba had anticipated.

text: The annual sheep shearing took place inside the sprawling outbuilding known that season only as the Clipping Shed, whose floor plan mirrored a Gothic parish church complete with projecting transepts. It not only echoed the silhouette of the small stone church that served the surrounding hamlet, but rivaled it in centuries of weathered history. No local could say for certain if the barn had once stood among a cluster of medieval convent outbuildings; every trace of those earlier grounds had long eroded away. The deep overhanging porches on either flank, tall enough to fit a farm cart piled high with unthreshed wheat sheaves, were framed by heavy pointed stone arches cut in broad, unadorned strokes, their very simplicity lending a stately grandeur absent in far more ornate later constructions. Its deep nut-brown roof, dusted with a fine age-born film and reinforced by massive timber collars, curved braces, and crosswise diagonal beams, boasted a far more dignified design, owing to its lavish old-growth materials, than fully nine out of ten contemporary church roofs. Running the length of each side wall stood a row of sturdy, projecting buttresses that cast long, deep shadows across the recesses between them, each recess pierced by a narrow lancet opening whose balanced dimensions met the exact dual needs of aesthetic grace and steady cross-ventilation.
source_text: They sheared in the great barn, called for the nonce the Shearing-barn, which on ground-plan resembled a church with transepts. It not only emulated the form of the neighbouring church of the parish, but vied with it in antiquity. Whether the barn had ever formed one of a group of conventual buildings nobody seemed to be aware; no trace of such surroundings remained. The vast porches at the sides, lofty enough to admit a waggon laden to its highest with corn in the sheaf, were spanned by heavy-pointed arches of stone, broadly and boldly cut, whose very simplicity was the origin of a grandeur not apparent in erections where more ornament has been attempted. The dusky, filmed, chestnut roof, braced and tied in by huge collars, curves, and diagonals, was far nobler in design, because more wealthy in material, than nine-tenths of those in our modern churches. Along each side wall was a range of striding buttresses, throwing deep shadows on the spaces between them, which were perforated by lancet openings, combining in their proportions the precise requirements both of beauty and ventilation.

text: Overseeing the entire operation from the back of the space was Bathsheba, watching the shearers carefully to ensure no careless strokes cut or wounded the sheep, and that every fleece was clipped as close as possible. Gabriel, who flitted and hovered in the glow of her bright, attentive gaze like a moth, did not shear nonstop; half his time was devoted to checking on the other workers and selecting which sheep each would tend to next. At this very moment, he was passing around a mug of mild liquor drawn from the barrel in the corner, alongside cut portions of bread and cheese.
source_text: Behind all was Bathsheba, carefully watching the men to see that there was no cutting or wounding through carelessness, and that the animals were shorn close. Gabriel, who flitted and hovered under her bright eyes like a moth, did not shear continuously, half his time being spent in attending to the others and selecting the sheep for them. At the present moment he was engaged in handing round a mug of mild liquor, supplied from a barrel in the corner, and cut pieces of bread and cheese.

text: When Bathsheba learned of this proposed arrangement—since Oak had no choice but to run it by her first—she objected halfheartedly at the outset. She figured the combined acreage of the two farms was far too large for one person to monitor effectively. Boldwood, who was obviously motivated by personal rather than financial concerns, suggested Oak be given a horse reserved exclusively for his use, which would erase that obstacle entirely, as the two farms sat directly adjacent to one another. Boldwood never spoke to Bathsheba directly over the course of these negotiations, only addressing Oak, who served as the go-between the entire time. Everything was worked out amicably in the end, and now Oak can be seen mounted on a sturdy cob, trotting the full length and breadth of nearly two thousand acres each day, keeping watch in a bright, satisfied mood, as if every last crop on the land belonged to him—while the actual mistress of one half and the master of the other sat shut away in their separate homes, mired in dreary, lonely sorrow.
source_text: Bathsheba, when she learnt of this proposal—for Oak was obliged to consult her—at first languidly objected. She considered that the two farms together were too extensive for the observation of one man. Boldwood, who was apparently determined by personal rather than commercial reasons, suggested that Oak should be furnished with a horse for his sole use, when the plan would present no difficulty, the two farms lying side by side. Boldwood did not directly communicate with her during these negotiations, only speaking to Oak, who was the go-between throughout. All was harmoniously arranged at last, and we now see Oak mounted on a strong cob, and daily trotting the length and breadth of about two thousand acres in a cheerful spirit of surveillance, as if the crops all belonged to him—the actual mistress of the one-half and the master of the other, sitting in their respective homes in gloomy and sad seclusion.

text: Harlow wandered thoughtfully down the grassy incline, gaze fixed on his work boots, which pale gold buttercup pollen had dusted into soft, graduated bands of warm amber. A small offshoot of the county’s main river cut through the pool’s sunken basin, flowing in through an inlet on one side and out through a discharge opening directly across its width. Herdsman Elias, Tom Rigg, Jude Hale, Mark Thorne, Toby Pye, and half a dozen other farm hands were gathered here, every last one soaked through to the roots of their hair, while Elowen stood a little apart in a brand-new riding habit—the finest she had ever owned—her horse’s reins looped loosely over one forearm. Earthen flagons of spiced cider rolled haphazardly across the lush turf. The docile sheep were herded into the pool by Rigg and Hale, who stood at the lower sluice waist-deep in cold water; Elias, perched on the pool’s edge, pushed their heads under as they paddled past with a crutch-shaped tool crafted specifically for the task, which also served to yank up exhausted animals whose fleeces had grown heavy with water and begun to slip beneath the surface. The sheep were released against the current through the upper opening, all grime and loose fleece washing away downstream. Toby Pye and Mark Thorne, who handled this final step, were somehow even more sodden than the rest; they looked like river otters beneath a cascade, every seam and fold of their work clothes leaking a steady trickle of water.
source_text: Boldwood went meditating down the slopes with his eyes on his boots, which the yellow pollen from the buttercups had bronzed in artistic gradations. A tributary of the main stream flowed through the basin of the pool by an inlet and outlet at opposite points of its diameter. Shepherd Oak, Jan Coggan, Moon, Poorgrass, Cain Ball, and several others were assembled here, all dripping wet to the very roots of their hair, and Bathsheba was standing by in a new riding-habit—the most elegant she had ever worn—the reins of her horse being looped over her arm. Flagons of cider were rolling about upon the green. The meek sheep were pushed into the pool by Coggan and Matthew Moon, who stood by the lower hatch, immersed to their waists; then Gabriel, who stood on the brink, thrust them under as they swam along, with an instrument like a crutch, formed for the purpose, and also for assisting the exhausted animals when the wool became saturated and they began to sink. They were let out against the stream, and through the upper opening, all impurities flowing away below. Cainy Ball and Joseph, who performed this latter operation, were if possible wetter than the rest; they resembled dolphins under a fountain, every protuberance and angle of their clothes dribbling forth a small rill.

text: Warmed back to full awareness, the lamb let out a soft bleat, and the sound landed in Gabriel’s ears and mind with immediate, unmistakeable meaning, the way sounds you’ve been waiting for always do. He moved from the deepest sleep to sharp wakefulness just as easily as he’d drifted off not long before, glanced at his watch to find the hour hand had shifted once more, tugged on his hat, scooped the lamb into his arms, and carried it out into the dark. After tucking the small creature down next to its mother, he stood still and studied the sky carefully, trying to figure out the exact time of night from the height of the stars above.
source_text: The lamb, revived by the warmth, began to bleat, and the sound entered Gabriel’s ears and brain with an instant meaning, as expected sounds will. Passing from the profoundest sleep to the most alert wakefulness with the same ease that had accompanied the reverse operation, he looked at his watch, found that the hour-hand had shifted again, put on his hat, took the lamb in his arms, and carried it into the darkness. After placing the little creature with its mother, he stood and carefully examined the sky, to ascertain the time of night from the altitudes of the stars.

text: The first Saturday after Troy’s departure, she made the trip to Casterbridge alone—a journey she hadn’t taken on her own at any point since her wedding. That same day, Bathsheba was ambling slowly on foot through the crowd of rural tradespeople and farmers gathered as usual outside the market house, while the town’s longstanding residents watched the group with their typical quiet belief that those hearty, outdoorsy lives came at the steep cost of being barred from any chance at holding local municipal office. Just then, a man who’d clearly been trailing her murmured a few words to another man standing to her left. Bathsheba’s hearing was as sharp as any wild animal’s, and she caught every single word the speaker said distinctly, even though her back was fully turned to him.
source_text: The first Saturday after Troy’s departure she went to Casterbridge alone, a journey she had not before taken since her marriage. On this Saturday Bathsheba was passing slowly on foot through the crowd of rural business-men gathered as usual in front of the market-house, who were as usual gazed upon by the burghers with feelings that those healthy lives were dearly paid for by exclusion from possible aldermanship, when a man, who had apparently been following her, said some words to another on her left hand. Bathsheba’s ears were keen as those of any wild animal, and she distinctly heard what the speaker said, though her back was towards him.

text: Perched on this highly sought-after plot just beyond the town limits stood a strikingly scenic building. At its inception, it had been designed as nothing more than a bare enclosure to hold human occupants. Its outer shell was so thin, so stripped of any superfluous ornamentation, and so tightly molded to the utilitarian space contained within, that the stark, unforgiving nature of its core purpose seeped plainly through the facade, much as the outline of a corpse shows clearly beneath a burial shroud.
source_text: On this much-desired spot outside the town rose a picturesque building. Originally it had been a mere case to hold people. The shell had been so thin, so devoid of excrescence, and so closely drawn over the accommodation granted, that the grim character of what was beneath showed through it, as the shape of a body is visible under a winding-sheet.

text: Such a tangled, volatile mix of weather patterns was rare enough to feel doubly menacing. Oak turned and made his way back to the stack yard. No sound disturbed the quiet there, the sharp conical peaks of the ricks jabbing dark against the dim expanse of sky. The yard held five full wheat ricks, alongside three tall stacks of barley. Once threshed, each wheat stack would yield roughly thirty quarters on average, while the barley stacks would produce no less than forty apiece. Oak calculated their total value to Bathsheba, and to any other owner for that matter, running through the simple arithmetic in his head:
source_text: This complication of weathers being uncommon, was all the more to be feared. Oak returned to the stack-yard. All was silent here, and the conical tips of the ricks jutted darkly into the sky. There were five wheat-ricks in this yard, and three stacks of barley. The wheat when threshed would average about thirty quarters to each stack; the barley, at least forty. Their value to Bathsheba, and indeed to anybody, Oak mentally estimated by the following simple calculation:—

text:
Well before this moment, the whole of Weatherbury had been jolted wide awake, and the shocking act that had cut short Boldwood’s festive gathering was common knowledge across the village. Gabriel Oak was among the first people not inside the house to get word of the disaster, and when he stepped into the room roughly five minutes after Boldwood had fled, the sight inside was gut-wrenching. Every woman guest was pressed tight against the walls, wide-eyed with terror like sheep caught in a gale, while the men stood frozen, completely at a loss for next steps.

Bathsheba, for her part, was unrecognizable from her earlier self. She sat on the floor next to Troy’s body, his head resting in her lap where she had lifted it herself. One hand pressed her handkerchief to the wound on his chest, though barely a drop of blood had seeped through, and her other hand clung fast to one of his own. The chaos that had torn through the house had snapped her back to her true self. Her earlier numb stupor had lifted, and purposeful action had risen to meet the urgent need of the moment.

Acts of steady fortitude that sound unremarkable in theory are remarkably rare in practice, and Bathsheba was astounding everyone around her now, for she lived her beliefs instead of just holding them; she almost never deemed a thing doable unless she was willing to do it herself. She was cut from the same cloth as the women who raise great men. She was the backbone of noble lineage, a nuisance at prim tea parties, intimidating to shopkeepers, and utterly beloved when disaster struck. Troy, still and reclining in his wife’s lap, was now the sole focal point in the middle of the spacious room.
source_text: Long before this time Weatherbury had been thoroughly aroused, and the wild deed which had terminated Boldwood’s merrymaking became known to all. Of those out of the house Oak was one of the first to hear of the catastrophe, and when he entered the room, which was about five minutes after Boldwood’s exit, the scene was terrible. All the female guests were huddled aghast against the walls like sheep in a storm, and the men were bewildered as to what to do. As for Bathsheba, she had changed. She was sitting on the floor beside the body of Troy, his head pillowed in her lap, where she had herself lifted it. With one hand she held her handkerchief to his breast and covered the wound, though scarcely a single drop of blood had flowed, and with the other she tightly clasped one of his. The household convulsion had made her herself again. The temporary coma had ceased, and activity had come with the necessity for it. Deeds of endurance, which seem ordinary in philosophy, are rare in conduct, and Bathsheba was astonishing all around her now, for her philosophy was her conduct, and she seldom thought practicable what she did not practise. She was of the stuff of which great men’s mothers are made. She was indispensable to high generation, hated at tea parties, feared in shops, and loved at crises. Troy recumbent in his wife’s lap formed now the sole spectacle in the middle of the spacious room.

text: “What on earth is the matter with you men?” she asked, coming face to face with them in the doorway just as she was stepping out to head to church. She’d been yanking a tight glove onto her hand only a second before, her full red lips pressed hard together with the effort, and that tight, focused line of her mouth softened the instant she registered their presence.
source_text: “Whatever _is_ the matter, men?” she said, meeting them at the door just as she was coming out on her way to church, and ceasing in a moment from the close compression of her two red lips, with which she had accompanied the exertion of pulling on a tight glove.

text: “Us workfolk are in for a proper grand feast tonight,” said Cainy Ball, veering his thoughts to an entirely new topic. “I caught ’em mixing up great big puddings in the milking pails first thing this morning—lumps of fat as big as your thumb, Mister Oak! I’ve never laid eyes on such splendid large chunks of fat in all my born days; before now, they were never any bigger than a horse bean. And there was a great big black crock perched on the cooking stand with its legs sticking out, though I’ve no notion what was inside it.”
source_text: “We workfolk shall have some lordly junketing to-night,” said Cainy Ball, casting forth his thoughts in a new direction. “This morning I see ’em making the great puddens in the milking-pails—lumps of fat as big as yer thumb, Mister Oak! I’ve never seed such splendid large knobs of fat before in the days of my life—they never used to be bigger then a horse-bean. And there was a great black crock upon the brandish with his legs a-sticking out, but I don’t know what was in within.”

text: Fanny Robin, Miss Everdene’s youngest servant, is nowhere to be found. The household has been waiting two full hours to lock up for the night, but she still hasn’t come in. They daren’t retire to bed yet, for fear of turning the lock and shutting her out entirely. They wouldn’t be half so anxious if she hadn’t been so visibly sunk in low spirits these last few days, and Maryann is convinced the poor lass might already have met with an end bad enough to warrant a coroner’s inquest.
source_text: “Fanny Robin—Miss Everdene’s youngest servant—can’t be found. They’ve been wanting to lock up the door these two hours, but she isn’t come in. And they don’t know what to do about going to bed for fear of locking her out. They wouldn’t be so concerned if she hadn’t been noticed in such low spirits these last few days, and Maryann d’ think the beginning of a crowner’s inquest has happened to the poor girl.”

text: Elias stepped through the door of the stagecoach operator’s cottage, the same space he’d rented a room in during his last trip back to his childhood valley. Gideon was seized by a sharp, unplanned resolve. He rushed back to his own farmstead, and returned barely ten minutes later, feigning an errand to call on Elias at the cottage. But just as he neared the porch, the door swung open and a figure stepped out. He heard the man bid the household “Good-night,” and the timbre was unmistakably Elias’s. It was a bizarrely quick departure, given he’d only just arrived minutes earlier. Even so, Gideon quickened his pace to catch up. Elias clutched what looked like a carpet satchel in his fist—the very same one he’d carried when he arrived. It seemed for all the world as if he intended to leave town again that very evening.
source_text: Troy entered the carrier’s house, which had been the place of his lodging on his last visit to his native place. Boldwood was moved by a sudden determination. He hastened home. In ten minutes he was back again, and made as if he were going to call upon Troy at the carrier’s. But as he approached, some one opened the door and came out. He heard this person say “Good-night” to the inmates, and the voice was Troy’s. This was strange, coming so immediately after his arrival. Boldwood, however, hastened up to him. Troy had what appeared to be a carpet-bag in his hand—the same that he had brought with him. It seemed as if he were going to leave again this very night.

text: No wanderer had ever strained their cleverness as thin as Elowen did that day. Every plausible trick, tool, ruse, or makeshift she could imagine for slipping across those final, desperate eight hundred yards without being spotted cycled through her racing mind, only to be discarded as unworkable. She considered propping herself on scavenged branches, rigging a low rolling frame, creeping on hands and knees—she even entertained the thought of tucking into a tight ball and rolling the whole stretch. But both crawling and rolling would demand far more physical exertion than simply striding upright, conspicuous as that would be. Every last spark of resourcefulness had burned down to ash. Hopelessness settled over her at last, cold and unyielding.
source_text: Never was ingenuity exercised so sorely as the traveller here exercised hers. Every conceivable aid, method, stratagem, mechanism, by which these last desperate eight hundred yards could be overpassed by a human being unperceived, was revolved in her busy brain, and dismissed as impracticable. She thought of sticks, wheels, crawling—she even thought of rolling. But the exertion demanded by either of these latter two was greater than to walk erect. The faculty of contrivance was worn out. Hopelessness had come at last.

text: “It hardly matters at all,” said Hale, flicking a fleck of road dust from his uniform cuff with a lazy, dismissive grin. “I’ll be out joining you lads in the lower fields one day this week, mind – but there’s a handful of loose ends I need to tie up before I can get away. So I bid you good day for now. We’ll keep on as friendly terms as we always have, obviously. I’ve never been one for putting on airs: no soul alive could ever claim Corporal Hale thinks too highly of himself. But what’s fated is fated, eh? Here’s half a crown for you lot to buy a round and toast my good health, lads.”
source_text: “It is of no importance,” said Troy, lightly. “Well, I shall be down in the fields with you some time this week; but I have a few matters to attend to first. So good-day to you. We shall, of course, keep on just as friendly terms as usual. I’m not a proud man: nobody is ever able to say that of Sergeant Troy. However, what is must be, and here’s half-a-crown to drink my health, men.”

text: The parson’s words drifted through the thick, heavy air in a sorrowful yet unshaken cadence, and Gabriel shed a genuine, unfeigned tear. Bathsheba gave no outward sign of being moved. After Mr. Thirdly took his leave of them, Gabriel lit a lantern, fetched three other men to assist, and the group carried the unresponsive truant indoors. They set the coffin atop two wooden benches in the middle of the small sitting room adjacent to the hall, exactly as Bathsheba had directed.
