source_text: The parson’s words spread into the heavy air with a sad yet unperturbed cadence, and Gabriel shed an honest tear. Bathsheba seemed unmoved. Mr. Thirdly then left them, and Gabriel lighted a lantern. Fetching three other men to assist him, they bore the unconscious truant indoors, placing the coffin on two benches in the middle of a little sitting-room next the hall, as Bathsheba directed.

text: “You had no business sending me that harsh letter first thing this morning,” she interrupted, her voice tight with unspilled tears and sharp resentment. “It makes plain you don’t care a scrap for me, that you’d toss me aside just like all the rest of them have! It was horribly cruel of you, especially when I was your very first sweetheart, and you were mine too. I’m never going to let this go, and I won’t ever forget it.”
source_text: “You ought not to have sent me that harsh letter this morning,” she interrupted. “It shows you didn’t care a bit about me, and were ready to desert me like all the rest of them! It was very cruel of you, considering I was the first sweetheart that you ever had, and you were the first I ever had; and I shall not forget it!”

text: “Nope,” said Tall, stepping over to join the group. “And honestly, I think we’d better keep this to ourselves. If it isn’t true, repeating it will just rattle her, do her real harm for no good reason. If it is true, there’s no point in dumping all that trouble on her before she has to face it anyway. God, I hope it’s all a lie. Even if Henery Fray and some of the others run their mouths against her, she’s never been anything but fair to me. She’s quick to temper and acts impulsively, sure, but she’s a brave girl who’d never tell a lie, no matter how badly the truth might hurt her. I’ve got no cause to wish her any ill will.”
source_text: “No,” said Tall, joining the group. “And I’m inclined to think we’d better keep quiet. If so be ’tis not true, ’twill flurry her, and do her much harm to repeat it; and if so be ’tis true, ’twill do no good to forestall her time o’ trouble. God send that it mid be a lie, for though Henery Fray and some of ’em do speak against her, she’s never been anything but fair to me. She’s hot and hasty, but she’s a brave girl who’ll never tell a lie however much the truth may harm her, and I’ve no cause to wish her evil.”

text: A sickening, unshakable certainty shot through Hale like a shard of ice. His whole body flooded with a cold, swimming faintness as he stumbled forward; a stretch of the split-rail fence had been smashed clean through, and the cloven prints of his flock pressed deep into the soft mud there, impossible to miss. His old sheepdog trotted to his side, nuzzled his wrist and licked his palm, tail flicking high in proud expectation of a generous reward for what it clearly deemed a job exceptionally well done. Hale leaned forward to peer over the edge of the sharp precipice. His ewes lay crumpled at its foot, some already cold and still, some twitching and bleating in quiet agony— a jagged heap of two hundred mangled, broken carcasses, each heavy with an unborn lamb, their condition adding at least two hundred more lost lives to the toll.
source_text: A horrible conviction darted through Oak. With a sensation of bodily faintness he advanced: at one point the rails were broken through, and there he saw the footprints of his ewes. The dog came up, licked his hand, and made signs implying that he expected some great reward for signal services rendered. Oak looked over the precipice. The ewes lay dead and dying at its foot—a heap of two hundred mangled carcasses, representing in their condition just now at least two hundred more.

text: Dropping lightly over the fence into Harlow’s water-meadows, each man stuffed his rope halter into his coat pocket so the grazing horses would see no tools in their hands. The skittish creatures, fooled into thinking the pair posed no threat, let themselves be caught by the thick tufts of their manes without a fuss, at which point the halters were slipped deftly over their heads. With no bits or bridles on hand, Elias Thorne and Toby Hargrove rigged a makeshift alternative, threading each halter’s free end through the horse’s mouth and knotting it tight on the opposite cheek. Elias swung himself astride his mount in one fluid motion, while Toby hauled himself up using the rise of the earthen bank for leverage; once they’d navigated the meadow gate, they pounded off at a gallop, following the track left by Elowen Voss’s stolen horse and the thief who’d taken it. Just what sort of cart or carriage the animal had been hitched to, though, was still anyone’s guess.
source_text: Springing down into Boldwood’s pastures, each pocketed his halter to hide it from the horses, who, seeing the men empty-handed, docilely allowed themselves to be seized by the mane, when the halters were dexterously slipped on. Having neither bit nor bridle, Oak and Coggan extemporized the former by passing the rope in each case through the animal’s mouth and looping it on the other side. Oak vaulted astride, and Coggan clambered up by aid of the bank, when they ascended to the gate and galloped off in the direction taken by Bathsheba’s horse and the robber. Whose vehicle the horse had been harnessed to was a matter of some uncertainty.

text: As the summer stretched long and golden-dusted, she spent more of her days beneath the open sky, and took to sorting out the farm’s needs out of sheer, unignorable necessity, though she no longer rode out at dawn or walked the fields to oversee work as she had done in years past. On a soft, hazy Friday evening in late August, she wandered a short stretch of the rutted lane and stepped into the village for the first time since the grim, life-shifting incident that had unfolded the previous Christmas. No flush had yet returned to her cheeks; their stark, unbroken pallor was thrown into even sharper relief by the inky black of her dress, making her look almost unearthly against the warm, sun-bleached wood and stone of the village. When she reached the small general store at the far end of the hamlet, standing almost directly across from the iron gates of the churchyard, Elspeth caught the sound of hymn-singing drifting from the church interior, and knew the village choir was holding their weekly practice. She stepped off the verge, crossed the empty road, lifted the creaking latch of the graveyard gate, and slipped inside; the tall, deep sills of the church windows blocked her completely from the view of the people gathered inside the nave. She moved quietly, almost stealthily, toward the shaded corner where Gideon had knelt to plant crocus bulbs and wild roses on Lila Mae Hargrove’s grave months earlier, and stopped before the smooth marble headstone that marked the spot.
source_text: As the summer drew on she passed more of her time in the open air, and began to examine into farming matters from sheer necessity, though she never rode out or personally superintended as at former times. One Friday evening in August she walked a little way along the road and entered the village for the first time since the sombre event of the preceding Christmas. None of the old colour had as yet come to her cheek, and its absolute paleness was heightened by the jet black of her gown, till it appeared preternatural. When she reached a little shop at the other end of the place, which stood nearly opposite to the churchyard, Bathsheba heard singing inside the church, and she knew that the singers were practising. She crossed the road, opened the gate, and entered the graveyard, the high sills of the church windows effectually screening her from the eyes of those gathered within. Her stealthy walk was to the nook wherein Troy had worked at planting flowers upon Fanny Robin’s grave, and she came to the marble tombstone.

text: “I’d trudge over to his farm regular back when I was courting my first wife, Elspeth, who worked as his dairymaid. Oh, Farmer Thorne was a right good-hearted man, and since I was a respectable young chap back then, I was permitted to call and see her and drink as much homebrewed ale as I fancied, just not to carry any off with me—outside of what’s sunk under my skin, that is, I mean, of course.”
source_text: “I used to go to his house a-courting my first wife, Charlotte, who was his dairymaid. Well, a very good-hearted man were Farmer Everdene, and I being a respectable young fellow was allowed to call and see her and drink as much ale as I liked, but not to carry away any—outside my skin I mane of course.”

text: “Ah, I knew you’d come,” she trilled, warm with playful teasing; “and Elias, isn’t that the loveliest bit of luck? There’s not a single soul in the house but me tonight. I sent everyone off so nobody on earth will know you stopped by your sweetheart’s little bower. Marnie was dying to run over to her grandfather’s to tell him all about her holiday, so I told her she could stay with them straight through till tomorrow—long after you’ve headed off again.”
source_text: “Well, I thought you would,” she said, playfully; “and, Frank, it is so lucky! There’s not a soul in my house but me to-night. I’ve packed them all off so nobody on earth will know of your visit to your lady’s bower. Liddy wanted to go to her grandfather’s to tell him about her holiday, and I said she might stay with them till to-morrow—when you’ll be gone again.”

text: In full daylight, the home of Oak’s newfound mistress Bathsheba Everdene appeared as a weathered, age-faded structure, its architecture hailing from the earliest phase of the Classical Renaissance, and its dimensions making it instantly clear that, as is so frequently the case, it had once functioned as the memorial hall for the small independent estate that once encircled it, a holding now entirely erased as a separate property and absorbed into the sprawling acreage of an absentee landlord whose vast estate included several of these modest, formerly standalone demesnes.
source_text: By daylight, the bower of Oak’s new-found mistress, Bathsheba Everdene, presented itself as a hoary building, of the early stage of Classic Renaissance as regards its architecture, and of a proportion which told at a glance that, as is so frequently the case, it had once been the memorial hall upon a small estate around it, now altogether effaced as a distinct property, and merged in the vast tract of a non-resident landlord, which comprised several such modest demesnes.

text: The sky truly split open right then. The burst of light was so startlingly unfamiliar that they didn’t immediately register its unspeakable peril, all they could grasp first was the staggering, unearthly beauty of it. It arced out from every point of the compass—east, west, north, south—a flawless, terrible dance of death. Skeleton shapes flickered into view in the open air, their bones forged from searing blue flame: dancing, leaping, striding, darting in circles, all blending together into a chaos no one could have imagined. Wriggling, coiling streaks of green lightning wove through them, and behind the whole swirling display loomed a wide, dimmer bank of diffused light. At the exact same moment, a sound tore out of every corner of the roiling sky that could only be called a shout; no human yell could ever match its power, but it aligned more with the raw, feral energy of a shout than any other earthly phenomenon. Right as that sound hit, one of those ghoulish, glowing shapes landed on the tip of Gabriel’s rod, snaking invisibly down its length, along the chain at its base, and straight into the soil. Gabriel was nearly blinded, and he could feel Bathsheba’s warm arm trembling where he clutched it, a jolt of something new and sharp and thrilling shooting through him at the contact; but love, life, every small, human concern felt trivial and tiny, pressed up so close against the unbridled rage of the universe itself.
source_text: Heaven opened then, indeed. The flash was almost too novel for its inexpressibly dangerous nature to be at once realized, and they could only comprehend the magnificence of its beauty. It sprang from east, west, north, south, and was a perfect dance of death. The forms of skeletons appeared in the air, shaped with blue fire for bones—dancing, leaping, striding, racing around, and mingling altogether in unparalleled confusion. With these were intertwined undulating snakes of green, and behind these was a broad mass of lesser light. Simultaneously came from every part of the tumbling sky what may be called a shout; since, though no shout ever came near it, it was more of the nature of a shout than of anything else earthly. In the meantime one of the grisly forms had alighted upon the point of Gabriel’s rod, to run invisibly down it, down the chain, and into the earth. Gabriel was almost blinded, and he could feel Bathsheba’s warm arm tremble in his hand—a sensation novel and thrilling enough; but love, life, everything human, seemed small and trifling in such close juxtaposition with an infuriated universe.

text: “A strange old piece, you say?” the maltster cut in, his voice peevish and rough-edged. “All the same, you’re no proper old man worth the name—not an elder of any account at all. Your teeth aren’t even half gone yet, and what standing has an old man if his teeth haven’t first fallen out? Wasn’t I long stale in wedlock before you were even out of your nurse’s arms? It’s a paltry thing to boast of being sixty when there are folks well past fourscore about—such a brag is as weak and insubstantial as water.”
source_text: “A strange old piece, ye say!” interposed the maltster, in a querulous voice. “At the same time ye be no old man worth naming—no old man at all. Yer teeth bain’t half gone yet; and what’s a old man’s standing if so be his teeth bain’t gone? Weren’t I stale in wedlock afore ye were out of arms? ’Tis a poor thing to be sixty, when there’s people far past four-score—a boast weak as water.”

text: “I’ll give you every last detail. When I showed up at Casterbridge Barracks, the staff there told me the Eleventh Dragoon Guards had already deployed out, and a fresh set of troops had taken their place. The Eleventh left last week bound for Melchester and points further afield. The official movement order dropped from the government completely unannounced, just like a thief in the night, which is par for the course for those kinds of sudden directives. Before the regiment barely even had time to process the news, they were already on the march. Their route passed right close to this area, too.”
source_text: “I’ll tell ye all particulars. When I got to Casterbridge Barracks, they said, ‘The Eleventh Dragoon-Guards be gone away, and new troops have come.’ The Eleventh left last week for Melchester and onwards. The Route came from Government like a thief in the night, as is his nature to, and afore the Eleventh knew it almost, they were on the march. They passed near here.”

text: Warren’s Malthouse sat ringed by a weathered old wall thickly tangled with ivy. While barely any of its facade was visible at this hour, the building’s character and core function came through clearly enough in the sharp silhouette it cut against the night sky. A deep overhang of thatch stretched out past the wall line, sloping upward to a sharp central peak where a small wooden lantern perched, fitted with slatted louvers on all four sides. A thin haze of mist could be faintly seen drifting out through those openings, dissolving into the dark air above. The front of the malthouse had no proper windows, but a square cutout set into the door held a single pane of glass, warm ruddy light spilling out through it to stretch across the ivy-draped wall directly outside. Faint, muffled voices drifted out from the building’s interior, too.
source_text: Warren’s Malthouse was enclosed by an old wall inwrapped with ivy, and though not much of the exterior was visible at this hour, the character and purposes of the building were clearly enough shown by its outline upon the sky. From the walls an overhanging thatched roof sloped up to a point in the centre, upon which rose a small wooden lantern, fitted with louvre-boards on all the four sides, and from these openings a mist was dimly perceived to be escaping into the night air. There was no window in front; but a square hole in the door was glazed with a single pane, through which red, comfortable rays now stretched out upon the ivied wall in front. Voices were to be heard inside.

text: Nah. First she moved to a garrison town on the far side of Wessex, then she spent several months earning her keep doing seamstress work in Melchester, boarding with a very respectable widow who takes in that kind of piece work. She only turned up at the Union house Sunday morning, as far as I know, and folks around here figure she tramped every last step of the way from Melchester. I can’t tell you why she left her position, because I truly don’t know, and I’d never lie just to make up an answer. That’s all there is to the story, ma’am.
source_text: “No. She first went to live in a garrison-town t’other side o’ Wessex, and since then she’s been picking up a living at seampstering in Melchester for several months, at the house of a very respectable widow-woman who takes in work of that sort. She only got handy the Union-house on Sunday morning ’a b’lieve, and ’tis supposed here and there that she had traipsed every step of the way from Melchester. Why she left her place, I can’t say, for I don’t know; and as to a lie, why, I wouldn’t tell it. That’s the short of the story, ma’am.”

text: Elias Thorne finally turned up at her cottage door. She was nowhere to be found. “Naturally she isn’t,” he muttered under his breath. He had been so wrapped up in thinking of Elspeth Voss as a woman to long for that he’d entirely overlooked the plain facts of her livelihood as a landowner—that she ran a farm every bit as large and demanding as his own, and at this point in the growing season, she was far more likely to be out in the fields or the barnyard than tucked inside her house. This lapse, and the other small oversights Elias was prone to lately, were unavoidable in the grip of his infatuation, and even more logical given how little they’d spoken. All the perfect conditions for loving idealization were aligned here: he only ever saw her in passing, from across a field or a market crowd, and they had never shared an unhurried conversation—he knew the line of her jaw and the way her hair caught the sun by heart, but had no idea what her laugh sounded like when she was at ease, or what small irritations ruffled her day. The messy, unglamorous small parts of being human were kept entirely out of his view; the trivial annoyances and unremarkable routines that make up most of ordinary life were hidden by the simple fact that he and Elspeth were barely acquainted, not on terms to drop by unannounced. It had barely crossed Elias’ mind that she dealt with chipped crockery and drafty windows and days where nothing went right, or that she, like every other person alive, had dull, unremarkable hours when being only half-remembered was far kinder than being seen up close. And so a quiet, gentle apotheosis unfolded in his imagination, even as she went about her days only a few miles from his own farm, a person just as harried, flawed, and uncertain as he was.
source_text: Boldwood did eventually call upon her. She was not at home. “Of course not,” he murmured. In contemplating Bathsheba as a woman, he had forgotten the accidents of her position as an agriculturist—that being as much of a farmer, and as extensive a farmer, as himself, her probable whereabouts was out-of-doors at this time of the year. This, and the other oversights Boldwood was guilty of, were natural to the mood, and still more natural to the circumstances. The great aids to idealization in love were present here: occasional observation of her from a distance, and the absence of social intercourse with her—visual familiarity, oral strangeness. The smaller human elements were kept out of sight; the pettinesses that enter so largely into all earthly living and doing were disguised by the accident of lover and loved-one not being on visiting terms; and there was hardly awakened a thought in Boldwood that sorry household realities appertained to her, or that she, like all others, had moments of commonplace, when to be least plainly seen was to be most prettily remembered. Thus a mild sort of apotheosis took place in his fancy, whilst she still lived and breathed within his own horizon, a troubled creature like himself.

text: The only hints of the crippling grief Boldwood had fought all night, and was still fighting in that moment, showed in the drained, pale cast of his sharp-featured face, the bulging veins standing out across his forehead and temples, and the newly harsh edges of the lines framing his mouth. The horse carried him off, and even the creature’s plodding gait felt heavy with the same stubborn, unyielding despair clinging to its rider. For a few seconds, Gabriel set his own heartache aside to watch Boldwood’s distress unfold. He tracked the broad, square-shouldered form sitting rigidly upright in the saddle, head held straight without glancing left or right, elbows fixed firmly at his sides, the brim of his hat sitting perfectly even as he glided forward, until the sharp, distinct outline of Boldwood’s figure slowly dipped out of sight over the crest of the hill. For anyone who knew Boldwood and what he’d been through, this rigid stillness hit far harder than any public breakdown would have. The jarring mismatch between his outward composure and inner turmoil cut straight to the chest with a sharp, painful clarity; just as there are strains of laughter far more terrible than sobs, the unshakable calm of this man raw with agony held a weight of emotion far more profound than any screamed outburst.
source_text: The only signs of the terrible sorrow Boldwood had been combating through the night, and was combating now, were the want of colour in his well-defined face, the enlarged appearance of the veins in his forehead and temples, and the sharper lines about his mouth. The horse bore him away, and the very step of the animal seemed significant of dogged despair. Gabriel, for a minute, rose above his own grief in noticing Boldwood’s. He saw the square figure sitting erect upon the horse, the head turned to neither side, the elbows steady by the hips, the brim of the hat level and undisturbed in its onward glide, until the keen edges of Boldwood’s shape sank by degrees over the hill. To one who knew the man and his story there was something more striking in this immobility than in a collapse. The clash of discord between mood and matter here was forced painfully home to the heart; and, as in laughter there are more dreadful phases than in tears, so was there in the steadiness of this agonized man an expression deeper than a cry.

text: Oak’s face was streaked with grime, rendered completely unrecognizable by billowing smoke and scorching heat. His work smock was pocked with burn holes and still dripping with water, the solid ash handle of his sheep crook charred down a full six inches shorter than its original length. Carrying the quiet, unassuming humility that brutal, unyielding adversity had thrust upon him, he moved toward the slim female figure seated in the saddle. He lifted his hat with deliberate respect, and no lack of understated gallantry, before stepping close to her dangling feet. He spoke in a soft, hesitant tone,—
source_text: Oak, his features smudged, grimy, and undiscoverable from the smoke and heat, his smock-frock burnt into holes and dripping with water, the ash stem of his sheep-crook charred six inches shorter, advanced with the humility stern adversity had thrust upon him up to the slight female form in the saddle. He lifted his hat with respect, and not without gallantry: stepping close to her hanging feet he said in a hesitating voice,—

text: Boldwood’s deep, all-consuming attachment had become a source of endless fascination for everyone around him; yet after being held up for so many years as the very epitome of a thriving, contented bachelor, his sudden lapse from that status felt like a stark anticlimax, closely echoing the irony of St. John Long dying of consumption while he was still compiling his proofs that the disease was not fatal.
source_text: Boldwood’s deep attachment was a matter of great interest among all around him; but, after having been pointed out for so many years as the perfect exemplar of thriving bachelorship, his lapse was an anticlimax somewhat resembling that of St. John Long’s death by consumption in the midst of his proofs that it was not a fatal disease.

text: “Yep,” said Joseph, emboldened by the fire’s cozy warmth, the heady cider he’d been sipping, and the clear sense that the story he was teasing had everyone in the room hooked. “I swear my heart turned to stone in my chest that day, I was that terrified. But I dropped to my knees and said the Lord’s Prayer slow, like I meant every single word, then ran all the way through the Apostles’ Creed, then the Ten Commandments, praying as hard as I ever have in my life. But it didn’t do a lick of good – that gate still wouldn’t budge an inch. So I moved on to the ‘Dearly Beloved Brethren’ opening from the church service, and I thought to myself, that’s four whole passages I know off by heart from the good book, that’s every last one I’ve got memorized. If this doesn’t work, nothing will, and I’m a goner for sure. Well, right when I got to the ‘Repeat After Me’ line, I pushed myself up off my knees and found the gate gave way easy – you hear that, folks? That gate swung open just the same as it ever did, no fuss at all.”
source_text: “Ay,” said Joseph, acquiring confidence from the warmth of the fire, the cider, and a perception of the narrative capabilities of the experience alluded to. “My heart died within me, that time; but I kneeled down and said the Lord’s Prayer, and then the Belief right through, and then the Ten Commandments, in earnest prayer. But no, the gate wouldn’t open; and then I went on with Dearly Beloved Brethren, and, thinks I, this makes four, and ’tis all I know out of book, and if this don’t do it nothing will, and I’m a lost man. Well, when I got to Saying After Me, I rose from my knees and found the gate would open—yes, neighbours, the gate opened the same as ever.”

text: Because I wasn’t about to hold it in, anyway. Half the joy of feeling something strongly is getting to voice it right the second it hits you, and that’s exactly what I did with mine. I would’ve reacted the exact same way even if you were the total opposite of who you are right now—old and unattractive, even—I’d have gushed about it just the same.
source_text: “Because I wasn’t going to. Half the pleasure of a feeling lies in being able to express it on the spur of the moment, and I let out mine. It would have been just the same if you had been the reverse person—ugly and old—I should have exclaimed about it in the same way.”

text: “That doesn’t hold much weight, either,” Elspeth went on. “Any suitor of hers could have called straight at the house if he was a respectable young fellow. The most puzzling detail of her disappearance—truly, the only thing that fills me with real unease—is that Maren spotted her leave the house wearing nothing but her everyday indoor work frock, not even a bonnet on her head.”
source_text: “It is hardly likely, either,” continued Bathsheba. “For any lover of hers might have come to the house if he had been a respectable lad. The most mysterious matter connected with her absence—indeed, the only thing which gives me serious alarm—is that she was seen to go out of the house by Maryann with only her indoor working gown on—not even a bonnet.”

text: “I loved my father, that much is true. But the truth is I love you more. That’s how I’m able to go through with this,” the sergeant said, his tone so unfeigned and rooted in real feeling that it was clear he wasn’t just putting on a performance anymore. He’d tossed off playful compliments about her looks before, when she was quiet and her energy was muted, but when she lit up and her beauty hummed with life, that casual teasing softened into genuine feeling. While the depth of his sincerity was nowhere near as great as she imagined it to be, it almost certainly ran deeper than he was willing to admit to himself.
source_text: “I loved my father: good; but better, I love you more. That’s how I can do it,” said the sergeant, with an intonation of such exquisite fidelity to nature that it was evidently not all acted now. Her beauty, which, whilst it had been quiescent, he had praised in jest, had in its animated phases moved him to earnest; and though his seriousness was less than she imagined, it was probably more than he imagined himself.

text: The wall-mounted timepiece ticked on unceasingly. The gathered women had shaken off their initial jitters by then, peals of snickering and breathless giggles rising louder by the minute. Abruptly, a dead hush fell over the room. Every last person held their breath, waiting for the final strike. Most folks know the strange trick quarter-hour chimes play, making time feel like it slips by far faster than it ought. It felt impossible that the gears hadn’t slipped and misaligned the minutes when the familiar rattle of the mechanism kicked off, the gnarled wooden jack popped out of its niche, and the four quarter chimes clanged stutteringly just as they always did. You could almost swear the grotesque little figure wore a sneer of petty malice on its carved face, that its jerky little movements hummed with unkind glee at the group’s tension. A moment later, the deep, thrumming boom of twelve heavy bell strokes rolled down from the stone church tower looming above the cottage. The women went rigid with quiet awe, and not a single giggle broke the heavy silence that followed.
source_text: The clock ticked on. The women threw off their nervousness, and titters and giggling became more frequent. Then came a dead silence. Every one was waiting for the end. Some persons may have noticed how extraordinarily the striking of quarters seems to quicken the flight of time. It was hardly credible that the jack had not got wrong with the minutes when the rattle began again, the puppet emerged, and the four quarters were struck fitfully as before. One could almost be positive that there was a malicious leer upon the hideous creature’s face, and a mischievous delight in its twitchings. Then followed the dull and remote resonance of the twelve heavy strokes in the tower above. The women were impressed, and there was no giggle this time.

text: She had tramped nearly two miles of her route, tracking the last streaks of daylight bleed slow over the hedgerows, and musing on how the hours of small, urgent doing were softening into the stretch of quiet rumination, soon to make way for the hush of evening prayer and deep rest, when she glanced up to see the very man she’d been desperate to avoid picking his way over Bramblecrest Rise. Elias Hawthorne was moving forward without the steady, measured tread of contained power that was his customary gait, the bearing that always made it look as though he was turning two conflicting thoughts over side by side in his mind. Right now, his whole demeanor was stunned, sluggish, unmoored.
source_text: She had walked nearly two miles of her journey, watching how the day was retreating, and thinking how the time of deeds was quietly melting into the time of thought, to give place in its turn to the time of prayer and sleep, when she beheld advancing over Yalbury hill the very man she sought so anxiously to elude. Boldwood was stepping on, not with that quiet tread of reserved strength which was his customary gait, in which he always seemed to be balancing two thoughts. His manner was stunned and sluggish now.

text: Elias was the sort of man who often caught himself noticing the quiet magic of the life he’d carved out, so after glancing up at the sky first to gauge the hour, as one might consult a well-worn pocket watch, he paused entirely, drinking in the stretch of blue and wispy cloud like a superlatively crafted masterpiece. For a breath he was struck by the vivid, speaking quiet of the landscape around him, or more precisely, by how thoroughly every trace of human sight and sound had been erased from the full sweep of the world laid out before him. Human forms, petty interferences, small sorrows and giddy delights might never have existed at all, and it felt as if the entire shadow-draped hemisphere of the globe held no other sentient being but him; he could easily fancy every other person on Earth had drifted over to the sunlit side, leaving this stretch of cool dark entirely to him.
source_text: Being a man not without a frequent consciousness that there was some charm in this life he led, he stood still after looking at the sky as a useful instrument, and regarded it in an appreciative spirit, as a work of art superlatively beautiful. For a moment he seemed impressed with the speaking loneliness of the scene, or rather with the complete abstraction from all its compass of the sights and sounds of man. Human shapes, interferences, troubles, and joys were all as if they were not, and there seemed to be on the shaded hemisphere of the globe no sentient being save himself; he could fancy them all gone round to the sunny side.

text: For a man of middle-class background, he was reasonably well educated—exceptionally learned, even, for a common rank-and-file soldier. He spoke smoothly, fluently, and almost without pause. This gift of gab let him embody one thing while appearing to be another: he could gush about the tender thrills of love while his thoughts drifted to his next meal, drop by a husband’s home for a supposed visit when his real target was the man’s wife, or act impatient to settle a tab when he fully intended to leave the debt unpaid.
source_text: He was a fairly well-educated man for one of middle class—exceptionally well educated for a common soldier. He spoke fluently and unceasingly. He could in this way be one thing and seem another: for instance, he could speak of love and think of dinner; call on the husband to look at the wife; be eager to pay and intend to owe.

text: “Mr. Hale,” she said, with unblinking clarity and unvarnished good sense, “you are far more comfortably situated than I am. I have scarcely a shilling to my name— I am staying with my aunt only for the bare necessities of food and shelter. I have received a more thorough education than you, and I do not love you in the slightest: that is the full account of my position in this matter. Now for yours: you are a farmer only just establishing your operation, and basic prudence holds that if you marry at all (which you certainly ought not to be entertaining the thought of right now), you would do best to wed a woman with personal wealth, who could help you stock a far larger farm than the small holding you currently manage.”
source_text: “Mr. Oak,” she said, with luminous distinctness and common sense, “you are better off than I. I have hardly a penny in the world—I am staying with my aunt for my bare sustenance. I am better educated than you—and I don’t love you a bit: that’s my side of the case. Now yours: you are a farmer just beginning; and you ought in common prudence, if you marry at all (which you should certainly not think of doing at present), to marry a woman with money, who would stock a larger farm for you than you have now.”

text: Oh heavens, not mannish, no—so unyieldingly, stoutly feminine that it brushes that line now and then, that’s all. Ah, lass,” she said, after drawing a grief-thick breath deep into her lungs and pushing it out slow and heavy with sorrow, “I wish I had half that supposed little failing of yours. It’s the greatest protection a poor young maid could ask for in these unruly, unprincipled times!”
source_text: “Oh no, not mannish; but so almighty womanish that ’tis getting on that way sometimes. Ah! miss,” she said, after having drawn her breath very sadly in and sent it very sadly out, “I wish I had half your failing that way. ’Tis a great protection to a poor maid in these illegit’mate days!”

text: She rounded the pasture edge, a dented tin pail slung from one hand, knocking softly against her calf as she walked. Her left arm was stretched out to steady her uneven steps, the flannel sleeve pushed up just far enough to expose a warm, freckled strip of forearm that made Elias Thorne wish this moment had fallen in mid-summer, when she would have rolled the fabric all the way to her shoulder. There was a bright, unapologetic lilt to her bearing now, as if she took it as given that no one could question how welcome her presence was; this rather bold, saucy presumption never came off as offensive, because any onlooker could see it was rooted in plain, unassailable fact. Much like the rich, unmissable cadence of a master poet’s recitation, what would have made a plain, ordinary person seem absurd only added to the charm of someone who already earned every bit of that confidence. It took her completely by surprise when she glanced up and spotted Elias’s face peeking over the gorse hedge, rising slow and pale as a full moon creeping above the horizon.
source_text: She came, the pail in one hand, hanging against her knee. The left arm was extended as a balance, enough of it being shown bare to make Oak wish that the event had happened in the summer, when the whole would have been revealed. There was a bright air and manner about her now, by which she seemed to imply that the desirability of her existence could not be questioned; and this rather saucy assumption failed in being offensive because a beholder felt it to be, upon the whole, true. Like exceptional emphasis in the tone of a genius, that which would have made mediocrity ridiculous was an addition to recognised power. It was with some surprise that she saw Gabriel’s face rising like the moon behind the hedge.

text: The dusk had thickened to full dark by the time Kael stepped out of Hollowford. A weighty wicker basket slung over one forearm, he tramped sullenly along the rutted road, pausing every so often at stone bridges or splintered field gates to set his load down and work the ache from his stiff muscles. Halfway to his destination, he crossed paths with the work crew and their rickety wagon, trundling back through the dimness after hauling the grave monument. He only stopped long enough to ask if the job had been finished properly, and when they assured him it had, he nodded and pressed onward down the empty road.
source_text: It was quite dark when Troy came out of Casterbridge. He carried rather a heavy basket upon his arm, with which he strode moodily along the road, resting occasionally at bridges and gates, whereon he deposited his burden for a time. Midway on his journey he met, returning in the darkness, the men and the waggon which had conveyed the tomb. He merely inquired if the work was done, and, on being assured that it was, passed on again.

text: “That was just a joke?” she said, heavy with grief and stunned disbelief. “How can you crack jokes right now when I’m being so wretchedly, unmistakeably serious? Tell me the truth, Frank. I’m not a fool, you know that. Sure, I’m a woman, and I have my flighty, overemotional feminine moments like any other, but that doesn’t make me stupid. Come on, be fair with me,” she said, locking eyes with him honestly, no trace of fear in her face. “I don’t ask for much. Just basic fairness, that’s all. God, there was a time I thought I’d settle for nothing less than the most devoted, adoring respect from the husband I chose for myself. Now? I’d be happy with literally anything that stops short of outright cruelty. That’s right. The independent, sharp-spirited Bathsheba everyone used to know? I’ve really fallen this far.”
source_text: “A mere jest!” she said, in mournful astonishment. “Can you jest when I am so wretchedly in earnest? Tell me the truth, Frank. I am not a fool, you know, although I am a woman, and have my woman’s moments. Come! treat me fairly,” she said, looking honestly and fearlessly into his face. “I don’t want much; bare justice—that’s all! Ah! once I felt I could be content with nothing less than the highest homage from the husband I should choose. Now, anything short of cruelty will content me. Yes! the independent and spirited Bathsheba is come to this!”

text: She sank to her knees at the edge of the casket, pressing her palms tight to her face, and for long minutes the air in the chamber hung as still and quiet as a sealed grave. Whether it was some unthinking, bodily reflex working its effect, or something far more intangible, when Elowen pushed herself to her feet her spirit felt soft and quieted, and she was stung with sharp remorse for the bitter, contrary impulses that had flared up in her only moments before.
source_text: She knelt beside the coffin, covered her face with her hands, and for a time the room was silent as a tomb. Whether from a purely mechanical, or from any other cause, when Bathsheba arose it was with a quieted spirit, and a regret for the antagonistic instincts which had seized upon her just before.

text: “Does anyone round here know of a stooped man, or a gimpy-legged one, any secondhand sort of fellow at all that’d suit poor old me?” said Elspeth. “I’ve no hope of landing a perfect one at my time of life. If I heard tell of such a soul, it’d do me more good than hot buttered crumpets and cold hard cider.”
source_text: “Do anybody know of a crooked man, or a lame, or any second-hand fellow at all that would do for poor me?” said Maryann. “A perfect one I don’t expect to get at my time of life. If I could hear of such a thing ’twould do me more good than toast and ale.”

text: Shivering from head to toe, she set her hand lightly on his forearm, her voice a wavering mix of raw misery and soft cajolery as she spoke: “I only regret any of it if you don’t love me more than every other woman on this earth, Luka. If you do, I don’t feel a flicker of remorse. You aren’t carrying regret over this because you already care for someone more than you care for me, are you?”
source_text: Trembling now, she put her hand upon his arm, saying, in mingled tones of wretchedness and coaxing, “I only repent it if you don’t love me better than any woman in the world! I don’t otherwise, Frank. You don’t repent because you already love somebody better than you love me, do you?”

text: He moved onward again, first sending a woman to attend her, then stepping outside to confirm every detail of the situation. The facts proved to be no more extensive than what he had already heard. He then ordered her horse to be harnessed to the gig, and when all preparations were complete, returned to tell her it was ready. He found that, though she was still pale and feeling unwell, she had in the meantime sent for the Budmouth man who had brought the initial tidings, and learned every last detail he had to offer.
source_text: He started onward again, and sending a woman to her, went out to ascertain all the facts of the case. These appeared to be limited to what he had already heard. He then ordered her horse to be put into the gig, and when all was ready returned to inform her. He found that, though still pale and unwell, she had in the meantime sent for the Budmouth man who brought the tidings, and learnt from him all there was to know.

text: “Thorne,” said Hale, “hold on a moment before you go—there’s something I’ve been turning over in my head these past weeks, that old agreement we struck about your share of the farm, I mean. That cut is far too small, all things considered: I barely tend to any of the business these days, while you pour so much of your time and careful judgment into keeping this place afloat. Well, things are looking brighter for me lately, and I want to show how much I value that work by raising your stake in the partnership. I’ve jotted down a rough draft of the terms that seemed most sensible to me, since I don’t have time to hash through every detail right now; we can sit and go over it all at our leisure later. My long-term plan is to step back from management entirely, and until you can cover all the operating costs on your own, I’ll stay on as a silent partner with a claim only to the stock. Then, if I marry her—and I hope, I truly think I shall—why—”
source_text: “Oak,” said Boldwood, “before you go I want to mention what has been passing in my mind lately—that little arrangement we made about your share in the farm I mean. That share is small, too small, considering how little I attend to business now, and how much time and thought you give to it. Well, since the world is brightening for me, I want to show my sense of it by increasing your proportion in the partnership. I’ll make a memorandum of the arrangement which struck me as likely to be convenient, for I haven’t time to talk about it now; and then we’ll discuss it at our leisure. My intention is ultimately to retire from the management altogether, and until you can take all the expenditure upon your shoulders, I’ll be a sleeping partner in the stock. Then, if I marry her—and I hope—I feel I shall, why—”

text: “What am I going to do without you? Oh, Gabriel, I truly don’t think you ought to leave. We’ve stood at each other’s sides for so long—through every bright, warm stretch and every dark, heavy stretch, such dear old friends we are—that this feels almost unkind, somehow. I’d always fancied that even if you leased the other farm as its master, you’d still spare a helping glance across at my place every now and then. And now you’re just going away entirely?”
source_text: “And what shall I do without you? Oh, Gabriel, I don’t think you ought to go away. You’ve been with me so long—through bright times and dark times—such old friends as we are—that it seems unkind almost. I had fancied that if you leased the other farm as master, you might still give a helping look across at mine. And now going away!”

text: He stormed off furious at his own choices, mortified that for the first time in his entire life he’d done something anyone could label sneaky or underhanded. Boldwood was never any good at subtle maneuvering, after all—he had as much tact as a sledgehammer. He squirmed under the conviction that he’d come off not just foolish, but far worse: petty and small. Still, he’d stumbled on one small truth as a sort of consolation prize. It was such a vivid, thrilling little detail, and even if it carried a twinge of melancholy, it was unshakably concrete and relevant. A little over six years from that day, Bathsheba could very well agree to marry him. That hope had a real, tangible weight to it; even if she hadn’t put much careful thought into what she’d told Liddy about her marriage plans, those offhand words still revealed her core beliefs on the subject.
source_text: He went away vexed with himself, and ashamed of having for this one time in his life done anything which could be called underhand. Poor Boldwood had no more skill in finesse than a battering-ram, and he was uneasy with a sense of having made himself to appear stupid and, what was worse, mean. But he had, after all, lighted upon one fact by way of repayment. It was a singularly fresh and fascinating fact, and though not without its sadness it was pertinent and real. In little more than six years from this time Bathsheba might certainly marry him. There was something definite in that hope, for admitting that there might have been no deep thought in her words to Liddy about marriage, they showed at least her creed on the matter.

text: The air that hit Elias’s nostrils was thick with the honeyed tang of freshly cured malt. The chatter that had been buzzing only moments before—apparently centered on where the recent blaze had first sparked—cut off abruptly. Every person in the room turned to appraise him, foreheads knit tight and eyelids slitted as if he radiated a light far too searing for their dark-adjusted gaze. Once they had finished this quiet assessment, a handful of people let out low, meditative exclamations:
source_text: Gabriel’s nose was greeted by an atmosphere laden with the sweet smell of new malt. The conversation (which seemed to have been concerning the origin of the fire) immediately ceased, and every one ocularly criticised him to the degree expressed by contracting the flesh of their foreheads and looking at him with narrowed eyelids, as if he had been a light too strong for their sight. Several exclaimed meditatively, after this operation had been completed:—

text: Left to its own devices, that casual acquaintanceship would likely have dwindled away into slow, unremarked forgetting, if not for a small incident that unfolded at the end of that very week. One afternoon the first sharp freeze set in, and the cold deepened as dusk fell, the dark creeping in like a set of bonds drawn taut by unseen, stealthy hands. It was the kind of frigid night when, in cramped country cottages, sleepers’ exhaled breath freezes crisp to the bedsheets above them; when even in the thick-walled drawing rooms of grand manors, those huddled close to the crackling fire have icy backs, even as their cheeks flush bright with the heat off the flames. Dozens of tiny, shivering birds crept to roost that night among the naked, leafless boughs, their bellies empty of any supper.
source_text: The acquaintanceship might, however, have ended in a slow forgetting, but for an incident which occurred at the end of the same week. One afternoon it began to freeze, and the frost increased with evening, which drew on like a stealthy tightening of bonds. It was a time when in cottages the breath of the sleepers freezes to the sheets; when round the drawing-room fire of a thick-walled mansion the sitters’ backs are cold, even whilst their faces are all aglow. Many a small bird went to bed supperless that night among the bare boughs.

text: Once the day’s marketing was finished, she hurried straight to Liddy, who was waiting for her beside the bright yellow gig they had driven into town. The horse was already harnessed, and they set off at a brisk trot—Bathsheba’s parcels of sugar, tea, and dress fabric were stowed in the rear, and there was some indescribable quality to their hues, shapes, and general appearance that made plain they now belonged to the young lady-farmer, and were no longer the property of the local grocer or draper.
source_text: When marketing was over, she rushed off to Liddy, who was waiting for her beside the yellow gig in which they had driven to town. The horse was put in, and on they trotted—Bathsheba’s sugar, tea, and drapery parcels being packed behind, and expressing in some indescribable manner, by their colour, shape, and general lineaments, that they were that young lady-farmer’s property, and the grocer’s and draper’s no more.

text: Up with him on the second floor, sir. When they carried him home and brought him up to the bedroom, she sent all the day laborers away straight off, said she didn’t need any more of their help. Then she called for me, had me run a full hot bath, and once that was done she told me I ought to go lie down, said I was as pale as chalk and looked ready to keel over. After that she turned the lock on the bedroom door, kept only herself and him inside, wouldn’t let the hired nurse or anyone else cross the threshold. I didn’t have the heart to leave, though, so I sat in the dressing room next door just in case she called for something. I could hear her moving about and shifting things in there for well over an hour, and she only stepped out once, to ask for more candles—hers had burned all the way down to the socket and guttered out. She told us we were to alert her the second you or Mr. Hale arrived, sir.
source_text: “Upstairs with him, sir. When he was brought home and taken upstairs, she said she wanted no further help from the men. And then she called me, and made me fill the bath, and after that told me I had better go and lie down because I looked so ill. Then she locked herself into the room alone with him, and would not let a nurse come in, or anybody at all. But I thought I’d wait in the next room in case she should want me. I heard her moving about inside for more than an hour, but she only came out once, and that was for more candles, because hers had burnt down into the socket. She said we were to let her know when you or Mr. Thirdly came, sir.”

text: “Good heavens, Elspeth, have you ever known a soul so sorely tested?” he said, chuckling warmly. “You know full well it all came down to this: I’m an unmarried man tending to your business, and you’re such a fetching young woman, I had a right tricky line to walk—all the more so when every local soul knew I carried a soft spot for you; I reckoned, from the way folks kept chattering about us being paired off, that it might sully your good name. No one has the faintest idea how much fluster and vexation this whole business has put me through.”
source_text: “Now, Bathsheba, was ever anybody so provoking,” he said, laughing. “You know it was purely that I, as an unmarried man, carrying on a business for you as a very taking young woman, had a proper hard part to play—more particular that people knew I had a sort of feeling for ’ee; and I fancied, from the way we were mentioned together, that it might injure your good name. Nobody knows the heat and fret I have been caused by it.”

text: That letter had to have some source, some driving reason behind it. Boldwood, naturally, had no inkling that this motive was the tiniest possible one that could have spawned the letter in the first place. The thought never even crossed his mind as a plausible explanation. When you are thoroughly mystified by a situation, you can hardly grasp that the person who confounded you might have simply gone along with a path suggested by random circumstance rather than striking out on that path from deliberate inner impulse—and that both processes would produce exactly the same outward result. The vast gulf between intentionally setting a whole chain of events in motion, and simply nudging a sequence that is already unfolding down a specific track, almost never reveals itself to someone left confounded by the final outcome.
source_text: The letter must have had an origin and a motive. That the latter was of the smallest magnitude compatible with its existence at all, Boldwood, of course, did not know. And such an explanation did not strike him as a possibility even. It is foreign to a mystified condition of mind to realize of the mystifier that the processes of approving a course suggested by circumstance, and of striking out a course from inner impulse, would look the same in the result. The vast difference between starting a train of events, and directing into a particular groove a series already started, is rarely apparent to the person confounded by the issue.

text: Gabriel was already moving among the swollen, prostrate sheep. He’d flung his coat aside, rolled his shirtsleeves up to his elbows, and drawn the life-saving implement from his pocket. It was a small trocar: a hollow tube fitted with a sharp lance running through its center, and Gabriel wielded it with the precise dexterity that would do a seasoned hospital surgeon proud. He ran his hand over the sheep’s left flank to find the correct spot, then drove the lance, still housed in the tube, through the skin and distended rumen. He withdrew the lance in a single sharp movement, leaving the tube fixed in place. A forceful stream of air rushed up through the tube, powerful enough to snuff out a candle held directly at its opening.
source_text: Gabriel was already among the turgid, prostrate forms. He had flung off his coat, rolled up his shirt-sleeves, and taken from his pocket the instrument of salvation. It was a small tube or trochar, with a lance passing down the inside; and Gabriel began to use it with a dexterity that would have graced a hospital surgeon. Passing his hand over the sheep’s left flank, and selecting the proper point, he punctured the skin and rumen with the lance as it stood in the tube; then he suddenly withdrew the lance, retaining the tube in its place. A current of air rushed up the tube, forcible enough to have extinguished a candle held at the orifice.

text: He clambered down the jagged rock face until he reached a small, sheltered sea cove cupped between sheer bluffs. The briny breeze roused Kael from his fog of fatigue; he decided he would pause to rest and swim here before pressing on with his journey. He stripped off his dust-streaked clothes and dove headfirst into the crisp water. The cove’s surf was as smooth as a farm pond, too dull for a confident swimmer, so to chase the thrum of open ocean swells, Kael soon paddled between the two jutting rock spurs that acted as gateposts to this tiny, landlocked patch of sea. Unluckily for Kael, an unmarked rip current churned just beyond those rocks, negligible for any weighted vessel, but treacherous for a swimmer caught unawares in its pull. Kael felt himself dragged sharply to the left, then whipped in a wide, dizzying arc straight out into the open sea.
source_text: He descended and came to a small basin of sea enclosed by the cliffs. Troy’s nature freshened within him; he thought he would rest and bathe here before going farther. He undressed and plunged in. Inside the cove the water was uninteresting to a swimmer, being smooth as a pond, and to get a little of the ocean swell, Troy presently swam between the two projecting spurs of rock which formed the pillars of Hercules to this miniature Mediterranean. Unfortunately for Troy a current unknown to him existed outside, which, unimportant to craft of any burden, was awkward for a swimmer who might be taken in it unawares. Troy found himself carried to the left and then round in a swoop out to sea.

text: The low, sprawling hall, propped up by heavy timber beams and thick pillars, only recently elevated to the formal title of Corn Exchange, was packed full of overheated men clustered in pairs and small trios. Whoever was speaking at any given moment would glance sidelong at his listener, squinting one eye tight to drive the force of his argument home as he spoke. Most carried young ground-ash saplings in their hands, using them partly as walking sticks and partly to prod at pigs, sheep, neighbors who had their backs turned, and any generally stationary object that seemed to demand such attention as they wandered through the space. As they talked, each man put his sapling through all manner of rough handling: bending it around his back, curving it into an arch between both palms, bearing down on its end against the floor until it bowed nearly into a semicircle; sometimes he would tuck it hastily under his arm to yank out his sample sack, pour a handful of grain into his palm, and after inspecting it closely, fling the kernels onto the floor. This outcome was perfectly familiar to half a dozen sharp, town-raised fowls that had slipped into the building unnoticed as they always did, necks craned high and heads tilted to fix the scene with a sidelong gaze, waiting patiently for their expectations to be fulfilled.
source_text: The low though extensive hall, supported by beams and pillars, and latterly dignified by the name of Corn Exchange, was thronged with hot men who talked among each other in twos and threes, the speaker of the minute looking sideways into his auditor’s face and concentrating his argument by a contraction of one eyelid during delivery. The greater number carried in their hands ground-ash saplings, using them partly as walking-sticks and partly for poking up pigs, sheep, neighbours with their backs turned, and restful things in general, which seemed to require such treatment in the course of their peregrinations. During conversations each subjected his sapling to great varieties of usage—bending it round his back, forming an arch of it between his two hands, overweighting it on the ground till it reached nearly a semicircle; or perhaps it was hastily tucked under the arm whilst the sample-bag was pulled forth and a handful of corn poured into the palm, which, after criticism, was flung upon the floor, an issue of events perfectly well known to half-a-dozen acute town-bred fowls which had as usual crept into the building unobserved, and waited the fulfilment of their anticipations with a high-stretched neck and oblique eye.

text: Elowen’s drafty, mildew-furred manor halls held only Maren tonight; Lila was, as previously noted, staying with her sister, the very woman Elowen had set out to visit earlier that evening. A few minutes after the grandfather clock struck eleven, Maren twisted restlessly in her bed, pricked by a faint sense of disruption. She had no awareness at all of what had pulled her from the edge of deep sleep. The feeling bled into a half-formed dream, and the dream jolted her fully awake, a cold prickle of unease coiling in her chest that something was amiss. She swung her legs over the mattress and crossed to the window to peer out. The small grazing paddock butted right up against this end of the house, and in the dim, shifting gray of the late night, she could just make out a moving figure creeping toward the horse grazing loose there. The figure caught the horse by its forelock, and led it to the far corner of the field. A shape she could not initially place stood there, which events soon proved to be a small cart: after a few minutes of silent fumbling that looked clearly like harnessing, she heard the sharp, steady trot of the horse down the lane, tangled with the soft rattle of light wooden wheels.
source_text: Bathsheba’s crannied and mouldy halls were to-night occupied only by Maryann, Liddy being, as was stated, with her sister, whom Bathsheba had set out to visit. A few minutes after eleven had struck, Maryann turned in her bed with a sense of being disturbed. She was totally unconscious of the nature of the interruption to her sleep. It led to a dream, and the dream to an awakening, with an uneasy sensation that something had happened. She left her bed and looked out of the window. The paddock abutted on this end of the building, and in the paddock she could just discern by the uncertain gray a moving figure approaching the horse that was feeding there. The figure seized the horse by the forelock, and led it to the corner of the field. Here she could see some object which circumstances proved to be a vehicle, for after a few minutes spent apparently in harnessing, she heard the trot of the horse down the road, mingled with the sound of light wheels.

text: Every soul in the room filed out save for Elias Thorne. He lingered uncertain at the edge of the sheet-draped body, hesitant to depart. A cold, heavy unease coiled in his chest at the brutally ironic shape events had taken where Kael Hale’s widow was concerned, and his own complete powerlessness to turn them from their grim course. For all his careful, quiet maneuvering over the course of the day, the single worst catastrophe that could have befallen the burial arrangements had come to pass. Thorne pictured a devastating revelation rising from that afternoon’s work, one that would drape Elspeth Hale’s life in a shadow the passage of dozens of slow, rolling years would barely soften, and no force on earth could erase entirely.
source_text: Every one except Gabriel Oak then left the room. He still indecisively lingered beside the body. He was deeply troubled at the wretchedly ironical aspect that circumstances were putting on with regard to Troy’s wife, and at his own powerlessness to counteract them. In spite of his careful manœuvering all this day, the very worst event that could in any way have happened in connection with the burial had happened now. Oak imagined a terrible discovery resulting from this afternoon’s work that might cast over Bathsheba’s life a shade which the interposition of many lapsing years might but indifferently lighten, and which nothing at all might altogether remove.

text: Bathsheba was turning the question over in her mind: could she make a bold, desperate lunge to break free, even if it meant leaving her entire skirt behind her? The thought was too dreadful to countenance. That gown—she had put it on specifically to cut a stately, impressive figure at the supper—was the crowning piece of her wardrobe; no other garment she owned flattered her nearly as well. What woman in Bathsheba’s position, not naturally faint of heart, and with her retainers within shouting distance, would have purchased escape from a dashing soldier at such a dear, unthinkable price?
source_text: Bathsheba was revolving in her mind whether by a bold and desperate rush she could free herself at the risk of leaving her skirt bodily behind her. The thought was too dreadful. The dress—which she had put on to appear stately at the supper—was the head and front of her wardrobe; not another in her stock became her so well. What woman in Bathsheba’s position, not naturally timid, and within call of her retainers, would have bought escape from a dashing soldier at so dear a price?

text: This all took place in July, two months before the September day we find the troupe gathered at Greenhill Fair: he came across a traveling circus performing on the outskirts of a northern town. Troy introduced himself to the manager by taming one of the troupe’s restive horses, hitting a suspended apple with a pistol bullet fired from the animal’s back mid-full gallop, and pulling off a handful of other impressive feats. All these skills drew directly from his time serving as a dragoon-guardsman, and on the strength of these displays, Troy was taken into the company. They immediately began preparing a production of *Turpin*, with the explicit plan that Troy would portray the lead character. Though the troupe treated him with obvious, sincere appreciation, Troy felt no great elation at the warm reception; he only saw the engagement as a chance to buy himself a few weeks to consider his next move. It was in this casual, unplanned way, with no definite roadmap for his future, that Troy found himself at Greenhill Fair alongside the rest of the company that day.
source_text: At this time—the July preceding the September in which we find at Greenhill Fair—he fell in with a travelling circus which was performing in the outskirts of a northern town. Troy introduced himself to the manager by taming a restive horse of the troupe, hitting a suspended apple with a pistol-bullet fired from the animal’s back when in full gallop, and other feats. For his merits in these—all more or less based upon his experiences as a dragoon-guardsman—Troy was taken into the company, and the play of Turpin was prepared with a view to his personation of the chief character. Troy was not greatly elated by the appreciative spirit in which he was undoubtedly treated, but he thought the engagement might afford him a few weeks for consideration. It was thus carelessly, and without having formed any definite plan for the future, that Troy found himself at Greenhill Fair with the rest of the company on this day.

text: He veered toward a gap in the thorny hedge, and found it was a rickety wooden gate; he hoisted himself up onto its upper rail, and sat mulling over whether to hunt for an affordable room in the village just down the lane, or save what little coin he had left by curling up for the night under a hay or rick stack. The gritty, jangling clatter of the passing wagon faded slowly from his ears. He was just about to climb down and keep moving, when an odd, flickering light snagged his attention on his left side, glowing roughly half a mile away across the fields. Thorne watched as the golden glow brightened, seeping higher over the treeline. Something was on fire.
source_text: He turned to an opening in the hedge, which he found to be a gate, and mounting thereon, he sat meditating whether to seek a cheap lodging in the village, or to ensure a cheaper one by lying under some hay or corn-stack. The crunching jangle of the waggon died upon his ear. He was about to walk on, when he noticed on his left hand an unusual light—appearing about half a mile distant. Oak watched it, and the glow increased. Something was on fire.

text: Three weeks dragged by, and further proof of Oak’s total lack of interest in her kept emerging. She noticed he no longer entered the small parlour that served as the farm’s account office to wait for her, or leave a memorandum as he had reliably done while she had kept to her rooms. Instead, Oak never appeared at all during hours she was likely to be there, only slipping in at odd, unplanned times when her presence in that part of the house was least probable. Whenever he needed direction, he sent a messenger with a verbal request, or a scrap of note with no greeting and no signature, forcing her to reply in the same casual, impersonal tone. Poor Bathsheba now writhed under the most torturing sting she had ever felt: the cold, sinking sensation that she was thoroughly despised.
source_text: Three weeks went on, and more evidence of his want of interest in her was forthcoming. She noticed that instead of entering the small parlour or office where the farm accounts were kept, and waiting, or leaving a memorandum as he had hitherto done during her seclusion, Oak never came at all when she was likely to be there, only entering at unseasonable hours when her presence in that part of the house was least to be expected. Whenever he wanted directions he sent a message, or note with neither heading nor signature, to which she was obliged to reply in the same offhand style. Poor Bathsheba began to suffer now from the most torturing sting of all—a sensation that she was despised.

text: “Has anything ever been that strange?” she finally muttered to herself, once she’d shut the door to her own room behind her. “And have I ever done anything so lousily petty? Sneaking off like that from a guy who was only ever polite and kind to me?” It was obvious she didn’t see his unashamed praise of her appearance as an insult anymore, not in the slightest.
source_text: “Was ever anything so odd!” she at last exclaimed to herself, in her own room. “And was ever anything so meanly done as what I did—to skulk away like that from a man who was only civil and kind!” Clearly she did not think his barefaced praise of her person an insult now.

text: Surrender—that was the unstated gist of the quiet, guarded response—surrender, even if she had no idea she had offered it. No half-finished, fragile line had ever carried so unmistakeable a weight. The easygoing corporal smiled to himself, and no doubt the Adversary grinned from a crack in the Pit’s outer wall, for this instant marked the turning point of an entire life. Her bearing and the lilt of her voice made it clear beyond doubt that the seed meant to pry the whole foundation loose had already sunk roots into that narrow crevice: all that was left was a matter of time and slow, natural unfolding.
source_text: Capitulation—that was the purport of the simple reply, guarded as it was—capitulation, unknown to herself. Never did a fragile tailless sentence convey a more perfect meaning. The careless sergeant smiled within himself, and probably too the devil smiled from a loop-hole in Tophet, for the moment was the turning-point of a career. Her tone and mien signified beyond mistake that the seed which was to lift the foundation had taken root in the chink: the remainder was a mere question of time and natural changes.

text: The three of them sat their horses, holding perfectly still to listen. Not a sound drifted to their ears but the thick, gurgling seep of millpond water through a sluice gate, a noise that carried faint, grim echoes of what might happen if someone slipped or leaped into its dark depths. Elias swung down from his saddle when the party reached a fork in the rutted path. The hoof prints they’d been trailing were their only remaining guide to the correct direction, and extreme care was required to avoid muddling them with a set of unrelated tracks that had appeared along the route only recently.
source_text: They rode three, and listened. No sound was to be heard save a millpond trickling hoarsely through a hatch, and suggesting gloomy possibilities of drowning by jumping in. Gabriel dismounted when they came to a turning. The tracks were absolutely the only guide as to the direction that they now had, and great caution was necessary to avoid confusing them with some others which had made their appearance lately.

text: The shared belief that Boldwood had not been morally accountable for his actions in the period leading up to his arrest was now nearly universal. Details uncovered prior to his trial had leaned heavily toward this same conclusion, but they had not been compelling enough to secure a court mandate for a formal evaluation of Boldwood’s mental state. It was striking, once the possibility of insanity was formally put forward, how many related, long-overlooked circumstances were recalled that only made sense in the context of an underlying mental illness—including, for example, the completely unheard-of neglect of his corn stacks the previous summer.
source_text: The conviction that Boldwood had not been morally responsible for his later acts now became general. Facts elicited previous to the trial had pointed strongly in the same direction, but they had not been of sufficient weight to lead to an order for an examination into the state of Boldwood’s mind. It was astonishing, now that a presumption of insanity was raised, how many collateral circumstances were remembered to which a condition of mental disease seemed to afford the only explanation—among others, the unprecedented neglect of his corn stacks in the previous summer.

text: “Neighbors, tonight we are not only gathered to toast the safe conclusion of our harvest and the bringing in of every last sheaf; this is also our long-awaited wedding feast. Barely three weeks have passed since I had the great happiness of leading your mistress, Mara, to the church altar to be wed, and we’ve not had the chance to hold a proper public celebration of the match for all of Thornbrook until now. To make sure the night goes as well as it possibly can, and that every man among you returns home to bed warm and merry, I’ve had a dozen bottles of fine aged brandy and kettles of steaming hot water brought out to the green. A full, generously strong goblet will be passed around to each and every guest tonight.”
source_text: “Friends, it is not only the harvest home that we are celebrating to-night; but this is also a Wedding Feast. A short time ago I had the happiness to lead to the altar this lady, your mistress, and not until now have we been able to give any public flourish to the event in Weatherbury. That it may be thoroughly well done, and that every man may go happy to bed, I have ordered to be brought here some bottles of brandy and kettles of hot water. A treble-strong goblet will be handed round to each guest.”

text: There was not a single bleat to be heard. He walked over to the hedgerow, where a gap had been torn through the thick, tangled growth, and sheep hoof prints were clearly pressed into the soft earth at the opening. He was rather surprised that the flock would break through fencing at this time of year, but he instantly chalked the behavior up to their well-documented fondness for ivy in the cold winter months, and a great deal of the vine grew thick in the plantation just beyond the hedge. He clambered through the gap. The sheep were nowhere to be seen in the plantation. He called out for them again, his voice bouncing off the valley walls and the farthest hilltops, ringing bright as the cries of sailors invoking the lost Hylas on the Mysian shore; still there was no sign of the flock, no answering bleat. He pushed through the tree line and tramped along the hill’s ridge. At the very summit, where the two converging hedges we have spoken of stop short where they meet the steep brow of the chalk pit, he spotted the younger dog silhouetted against the open sky—dark, utterly motionless, as stoic and still as Napoleon stranded on St. Helena.
source_text: Not a single bleat. He went to the hedge; a gap had been broken through it, and in the gap were the footprints of the sheep. Rather surprised to find them break fence at this season, yet putting it down instantly to their great fondness for ivy in winter-time, of which a great deal grew in the plantation, he followed through the hedge. They were not in the plantation. He called again: the valleys and farthest hills resounded as when the sailors invoked the lost Hylas on the Mysian shore; but no sheep. He passed through the trees and along the ridge of the hill. On the extreme summit, where the ends of the two converging hedges of which we have spoken were stopped short by meeting the brow of the chalk-pit, he saw the younger dog standing against the sky—dark and motionless as Napoleon at St. Helena.

text: “Aren’t ye dearer to me than all my own concerns, even my very life?” he pressed on. “Come now, listen to me! I’m six years older than you, and Mr. Boldwood is a full ten years older than I am. Just consider— I do beg of ’ee to think carefully before it is too late— how completely safe you would be in his care!”
source_text: “Are ye not more to me than my own affairs, and even life!” he went on. “Come, listen to me! I am six years older than you, and Mr. Boldwood is ten years older than I, and consider—I do beg of ’ee to consider before it is too late—how safe you would be in his hands!”

text: The grounds of the Casterbridge Union-house were ringed by a solid wall, save for a small stretch at one end of the property. A tall, sharply peaked gable jutted out noticeably here, its surface draped in the same dense mat of ivy that covered the building’s front facade. This gable face was completely unadorned, with no windows, chimneys, decorative carvings, or even the smallest protrusion to break its smooth line. Aside from the wide swathe of deep green ivy leaves covering most of its surface, the only feature belonging to the gable was a narrow, unassuming door.
source_text: A wall bounded the site of Casterbridge Union-house, except along a portion of the end. Here a high gable stood prominent, and it was covered like the front with a mat of ivy. In this gable was no window, chimney, ornament, or protuberance of any kind. The single feature appertaining to it, beyond the expanse of dark green leaves, was a small door.

text: Oh, you’d scarce believe it, but that man—our Miss Everdene’s own father—turned out to be one of the most fickle husbands walking the earth, after a while. Mark this: he never meant to be unsteady, he just couldn’t help himself. Poor fellow, every part of him wanted to be loyal and true to his wife, but his heart would wander no matter how hard he tried to rein it in.
