He poured out his trouble to me once, proper wretched over the whole mess. “Coggan,” he said, “I could never wish for a handsomer woman than the one I’ve got, but the second I remember she’s my lawfully wedded wife, my wicked heart drifts off, no matter what I do to stop it.” But I think he cured himself of it in the end, you see: he had her take off her wedding ring, and he’d call her by her maiden name every night once the shop was shut and they were sitting together alone. That way, he could fool himself into thinking she was only his sweetheart, not his spouse at all. The minute he could fully believe he was doing wrong and breaking the seventh commandment, he grew as fond of her as he’d ever been, and they went on to live as perfect a picture of quiet, devoted love as you could ever find.
source_text: “Well, now, you’d hardly believe it, but that man—our Miss Everdene’s father—was one of the ficklest husbands alive, after a while. Understand? ’a didn’t want to be fickle, but he couldn’t help it. The pore feller were faithful and true enough to her in his wish, but his heart would rove, do what he would. He spoke to me in real tribulation about it once. ‘Coggan,’ he said, ‘I could never wish for a handsomer woman than I’ve got, but feeling she’s ticketed as my lawful wife, I can’t help my wicked heart wandering, do what I will.’ But at last I believe he cured it by making her take off her wedding-ring and calling her by her maiden name as they sat together after the shop was shut, and so ’a would get to fancy she was only his sweetheart, and not married to him at all. And as soon as he could thoroughly fancy he was doing wrong and committing the seventh, ’a got to like her as well as ever, and they lived on a perfect picture of mutel love.”

text: The two of them lingered by the gate for a few minutes, Gabriel’s gaze drifting blankly down at the dirt under his feet. His mind raced ahead to the future, where he could picture all the scenes of regret playing out across slow, empty years, all springing from this single hasty choice. He’d known for certain the second he heard that they’d gotten married, but he couldn’t wrap his head around why the whole thing had been arranged so oddly, in total secrecy. Word had gotten around that her trip to Bath had been a nightmare: she’d misjudged how far the town was, her horse had gone lame halfway, and the whole journey had taken her over two days total. Sneaking around and hiding things was never Bathsheba’s style. For all her flaws, she was the most open, straightforward person he knew. Could she have been tricked into it? This marriage wasn’t just an unbearable grief to him—it left him stunned, even though he’d spent the whole previous week suspecting this might be exactly what came of Troy meeting up with her while she was out of town. When she’d come home quietly with Liddy, those fears had faded a little, at least for a time. It was like those tiny, imperceptible movements that look exactly like stillness, but are fundamentally, completely different from actual stillness: the faint flicker of hope he’d held onto, which had felt indistinguishable from despair, had been a world away from total hopelessness after all.
source_text: They stood by the gate awhile, Gabriel listlessly staring at the ground. His mind sped into the future, and saw there enacted in years of leisure the scenes of repentance that would ensue from this work of haste. That they were married he had instantly decided. Why had it been so mysteriously managed? It had become known that she had had a fearful journey to Bath, owing to her miscalculating the distance: that the horse had broken down, and that she had been more than two days getting there. It was not Bathsheba’s way to do things furtively. With all her faults, she was candour itself. Could she have been entrapped? The union was not only an unutterable grief to him: it amazed him, notwithstanding that he had passed the preceding week in a suspicion that such might be the issue of Troy’s meeting her away from home. Her quiet return with Liddy had to some extent dispersed the dread. Just as that imperceptible motion which appears like stillness is infinitely divided in its properties from stillness itself, so had his hope undistinguishable from despair differed from despair indeed.

text: “Oh, Frank!” Bathsheba replied, those two words thrumming with so much raw, earnest pleading. “It was only a few weeks ago that you told me I was far sweeter than every other little joy you had, all added up, and that you’d give every last one of them up just for me. And now you won’t set this one thing aside? It’s more of a headache than a pleasure anyway, isn’t it? Please, Frank. Come on—let me pull every trick I have to convince you to stay home: I’ll say all the soft, sweet little lines you like, I’ll give you all my prettiest, warmest looks, anything I can possibly think of, just to make you stay. Say yes to your wife, won’t you? Please say yes!”
source_text: “Oh, Frank!” Bathsheba replied, and there was such a volume of entreaty in the words. “Only such a few weeks ago you said that I was far sweeter than all your other pleasures put together, and that you would give them all up for me; and now, won’t you give up this one, which is more a worry than a pleasure? Do, Frank. Come, let me fascinate you by all I can do—by pretty words and pretty looks, and everything I can think of—to stay at home. Say yes to your wife—say yes!”

text: Elowen Voss’s aunt was indoors when the visitor spoke. “Would you let Miss Voss know someone would be glad to have a word with her?” said Mr. Briar. (Referring to oneself only as “somebody” without offering a name is not to be taken as a mark of rural bad manners: it grows from a quiet, refined modesty that townspeople, with their printed calling cards and formal arrival announcements, have no conception of whatsoever.)
source_text: Bathsheba’s aunt was indoors. “Will you tell Miss Everdene that somebody would be glad to speak to her?” said Mr. Oak. (Calling one’s self merely Somebody, without giving a name, is not to be taken as an example of the ill-breeding of the rural world: it springs from a refined modesty, of which townspeople, with their cards and announcements, have no notion whatever.)

text: Well, that wraps up that discussion. We’ll push the funeral to tomorrow morning. The body can either be brought over to the church right now, or kept here at the farm for the bearers to pick up when they come in the morning. They hung around for more than an hour, and have already headed home.
source_text: “Then that settles the matter. We’ll put off the funeral till to-morrow morning. The body may be brought on to the church, or it may be left here at the farm and fetched by the bearers in the morning. They waited more than an hour, and have now gone home.”

text: Where would she make her home? The obvious answer was Blooms-End. It was true the old rooms had barely more headroom than the between-decks of a frigate; the tall new clock case she’d brought from the inn could only fit after they’d sunk a small patch of the floor beneath it and removed the handsome brass knobs that crowned its top to make enough vertical space. But for all their minor inconveniences, the house had plenty of rooms to spare, and every inch of it was dear to her, woven through with her warmest early recollections. Clym welcomed her as a tenant gladly, restricting his own living quarters to two small rooms at the top of the back staircase. He lived quietly there, fully separated from Thomasin and the three servants she’d chosen to employ now that her fortune let her indulge such comforts, going about his own business and tending to his own private thoughts, undisturbed.
source_text: Where should she live? The obvious place was Blooms-End. The old rooms, it is true, were not much higher than the between-decks of a frigate, necessitating a sinking in the floor under the new clock-case she brought from the inn, and the removal of the handsome brass knobs on its head, before there was height for it to stand; but, such as the rooms were, there were plenty of them, and the place was endeared to her by every early recollection. Clym very gladly admitted her as a tenant, confining his own existence to two rooms at the top of the back staircase, where he lived on quietly, shut off from Thomasin and the three servants she had thought fit to indulge in now that she was a mistress of money, going his own ways, and thinking his own thoughts.

text: The first tall, lurching flame from Rainbarrow burst upward into the dark sky, yanking every gaze that had been locked on the far-off fires back to their own group’s small effort at the same ritual. The warm, crackling blaze gilded the faces of everyone huddled in the ring around it—their numbers now swollen by a handful of stray passersby, men and women both—painting every cheek and coat sleeve with its bright golden hue, even spilling a vivid, dancing glow over the dark peat surrounding them that faded slowly into shadow as the barrow curved gently downward past the edge of the firelight. The glow made clear just how perfectly the barrow preserved its original rounded, half-sphere shape, exactly as intact as the day it was first piled high, even the small narrow trench from which its dirt had been dug still visible. No plow had ever turned so much as a single crumb of that tough, unyielding earth. What made the heath useless, barren land for a farmer was exactly what made it such a rich, unspoiled resource for a historian: no one had ever tended or cultivated it, so none of its ancient traces had ever been erased or worn away.
source_text: The first tall flame from Rainbarrow sprang into the sky, attracting all eyes that had been fixed on the distant conflagrations back to their own attempt in the same kind. The cheerful blaze streaked the inner surface of the human circle—now increased by other stragglers, male and female—with its own gold livery, and even overlaid the dark turf around with a lively luminousness, which softened off into obscurity where the barrow rounded downwards out of sight. It showed the barrow to be the segment of a globe, as perfect as on the day when it was thrown up, even the little ditch remaining from which the earth was dug. Not a plough had ever disturbed a grain of that stubborn soil. In the heath’s barrenness to the farmer lay its fertility to the historian. There had been no obliteration, because there had been no tending.

text: “I know, I know—I won’t deny it,” she breathed, her voice thrumming with that slow, burning intensity that belonged only to her. “Don’t lecture me the way you did last time, Elias. You’ll push me to spit out things I’ll regret, words I never want to let fall between us. I’d already written you off, made up my mind to scrub every last thought of you from my head. Then I heard what happened, so I slipped out here and stoked the fire to wait—because I’d let myself believe you’d stayed true to me.”
source_text: “Yes, yes—I own it,” she cried under her breath, with a drowsy fervour of manner and tone which was quite peculiar to her. “Don’t begin speaking to me as you did, Damon; you will drive me to say words I would not wish to say to you. I had given you up, and resolved not to think of you any more; and then I heard the news, and I came out and got the fire ready because I thought that you had been faithful to me.”

text: Every now and then she came upon a spot where entire self-contained worlds of ephemerons whiled away their short lives in giddy, frantic revelry: some darted through the open air, some scurried over sunbaked earth and foliage, others wriggled through the lukewarm, slimy water of a half-evaporated pool. All the shallower ponds in the area had shrunk to little more than steamy, mist-wreathed mud, where the maggot-like forms of countless unidentifiable small creatures drifted in and out of view, squirming and rolling contentedly in the muck. As a woman given to quiet philosophical musing, she would sometimes settle beneath her umbrella to rest and observe their small, uncomplicated joy. A soft, steady hopefulness about the outcome of her visit had lightened her mind entirely, so that in the lulls between weightier ruminations, she was free to fix her attention on whatever tiny, trivial thing happened to catch her eye.
source_text: Occasionally she came to a spot where independent worlds of ephemerons were passing their time in mad carousal, some in the air, some on the hot ground and vegetation, some in the tepid and stringy water of a nearly dried pool. All the shallower ponds had decreased to a vaporous mud amid which the maggoty shapes of innumerable obscure creatures could be indistinctly seen, heaving and wallowing with enjoyment. Being a woman not disinclined to philosophize she sometimes sat down under her umbrella to rest and to watch their happiness, for a certain hopefulness as to the result of her visit gave ease to her mind, and between important thoughts left it free to dwell on any infinitesimal matter which caught her eyes.

text: Lior’s mother, Mara, was at that moment treading a lane blocked from Elowen’s view by a sloped spur of the heath. Her trudge from the cottage garden gate had been sharp and resolute, the gait of a woman every bit as desperate to flee the place now as she had been eager to cross its threshold only minutes before. Her gaze stayed pinned to the mossy dirt under her boots, two sharp images seared behind her lids: the scuffed edge of Lior’s woodcutting axe tangled in wild blackberry brambles propped by the front step, and the cold, guarded curve of Elowen’s cheek pressed to the parlor window pane. Her lips quivered, pulling tight into a pale, unnaturally thin line as she whispered under her breath, “’Tis far too much—Lior, how can he bear to do this? He’s right there at home, and still he lets her shut the door against me!”
source_text: Clym’s mother was at this time following a path which lay hidden from Eustacia by a shoulder of the hill. Her walk thither from the garden gate had been hasty and determined, as of a woman who was now no less anxious to escape from the scene than she had previously been to enter it. Her eyes were fixed on the ground; within her two sights were graven—that of Clym’s hook and brambles at the door, and that of a woman’s face at a window. Her lips trembled, becoming unnaturally thin as she murmured, “’Tis too much—Clym, how can he bear to do it! He is at home; and yet he lets her shut the door against me!”

text: Eustacia jolted upright. That was the exact signal she’d known by heart years earlier, back when Wildeve used to sneak to Mistover to court her in secret. She realized right away that Wildeve was waiting just outside, but before she could even work out what to do next, her husband came down the stairs and into the room. Eustacia’s face burned bright crimson at this unplanned, jarring collision of circumstances, the flush gifting her features a sharp liveliness that far too rarely graced them.
source_text: Eustacia started up. This had been a well-known signal in old times when Wildeve had used to come secretly wooing to Mistover. She at once knew that Wildeve was outside, but before she could consider what to do her husband came in from upstairs. Eustacia’s face burnt crimson at the unexpected collision of incidents, and filled it with an animation that it too frequently lacked.

text: “Even if they were feeling ashamed, I still don’t see why they couldn’t have held the whole thing right here,” said the broad, ample-framed woman, whose stiff stays creaked like well-worn shoes every time she stooped or turned. “It’s a fine thing to gather all the neighbors together and raise a good, lively ruckus now and again; a wedding’s just as fitting an occasion for it as any seasonal holiday, if you ask me. I’ve never had any patience for stuffy, close-mouthed ways of doing things.”
source_text: “Supposing they were ashamed, I don’t see why they shouldn’t have done it here-right,” said a wide-spread woman whose stays creaked like shoes whenever she stooped or turned. “’Tis well to call the neighbours together and to hae a good racket once now and then; and it may as well be when there’s a wedding as at tide-times. I don’t care for close ways.”

text: Had Eustacia chosen to shadow the reddleman, she would have quickly found her suspicions fully confirmed. The moment Clym offered her his arm and escorted her away from the spot, Diggory turned off the worn footpath toward East Egdon—he had only been strolling that direction to accompany Clym in the first place, his van once again parked in the surrounding area. Lengthening his stride, he cut across the untamed, pathless stretch of heath, heading roughly the same way Wildeve had departed. Only a man well-versed in wandering the moors after dark could have navigated those scrubby, uneven slopes at Venn’s speed without tumbling headfirst into a hollow, or catching his foot in a rabbit burrow and snapping his leg clean. But Venn pressed on with almost no difficulty, his scrambling course set directly for the Quiet Woman Inn. He arrived at the spot in barely half an hour, and he knew with absolute certainty that no other person who had been near Throope Corner when he set out could have reached the inn ahead of him.
source_text: If Eustacia had been able to follow the reddleman she would soon have found striking confirmation of her thought. No sooner had Clym given her his arm and led her off the scene than the reddleman turned back from the beaten track towards East Egdon, whither he had been strolling merely to accompany Clym in his walk, Diggory’s van being again in the neighbourhood. Stretching out his long legs, he crossed the pathless portion of the heath somewhat in the direction which Wildeve had taken. Only a man accustomed to nocturnal rambles could at this hour have descended those shaggy slopes with Venn’s velocity without falling headlong into a pit, or snapping off his leg by jamming his foot into some rabbit burrow. But Venn went on without much inconvenience to himself, and the course of his scamper was towards the Quiet Woman Inn. This place he reached in about half an hour, and he was well aware that no person who had been near Throope Corner when he started could have got down here before him.

text: This man from Paris was now so thoroughly disguised beneath his leather work gear and the goggles he was forced to wear over his eyes that even his closest friend might have walked straight past without recognizing him. He was little more than a small brown smudge tucked amid the wide expanse of olive-green gorse, and nothing else. Though he often felt low and dispirited when he wasn’t actually working, weighed down by thoughts of Eustacia’s situation and the bitter estrangement from his mother, he grew cheerful and steady as soon as he was fully immersed in the flow of his labor.
source_text: This man from Paris was now so disguised by his leather accoutrements, and by the goggles he was obliged to wear over his eyes, that his closest friend might have passed by without recognizing him. He was a brown spot in the midst of an expanse of olive-green gorse, and nothing more. Though frequently depressed in spirit when not actually at work, owing to thoughts of Eustacia’s position and his mother’s estrangement, when in the full swing of labour he was cheerfully disposed and calm.

text: Once Kael Voss had shut the garden gate behind him, Elspeth Thorne lingered in the porch, turning over the best way to handle the stitched pouch of guineas she had pointedly refused to hand over to him. It strained all credibility that Lila, his wife, had sent him to ask for the coins, after all—the very need for the funds had sprung from Kael’s stubborn refusal to release any household money to her in the first place. At the same time, Lila truly did need the sum, and would be unable to make the trip out to Hollowthorn Cottage for another seven days at the earliest. Bringing or dispatching the money to her at the Rusty Harrow inn would be unforgivably awkward; Kael would almost certainly be present, or would catch wind of the exchange soon after, and if he treated Lila as poorly as her aunt feared, he would pry every last guinea from her yielding, unresisting palms. But this very evening, Lila was visiting Bramble Rise, and any small parcel could be slipped to her there without her husband ever catching wind of it. All things considered, it was far too good an opportunity to waste.
source_text: When Wildeve was gone Mrs. Yeobright stood and considered what would be the best course to adopt with regard to the guineas, which she had not liked to entrust to Wildeve. It was hardly credible that Thomasin had told him to ask for them, when the necessity for them had arisen from the difficulty of obtaining money at his hands. At the same time Thomasin really wanted them, and might be unable to come to Blooms-End for another week at least. To take or send the money to her at the inn would be impolite, since Wildeve would pretty surely be present, or would discover the transaction; and if, as her aunt suspected, he treated her less kindly than she deserved to be treated, he might then get the whole sum out of her gentle hands. But on this particular evening Thomasin was at Mistover, and anything might be conveyed to her there without the knowledge of her husband. Upon the whole the opportunity was worth taking advantage of.

text: As she stood transfixed, weighed down by her grim premonitions, the old clock inside the house whirred out twelve sharp strokes. Moments later, faint sounds drifted to her from across the distant hills. The breeze blew in from that direction, carrying the clear notes of far-off bells, gaily bursting into a peal: one, two, three, four, five. The ringers at East Egdon were announcing the wedding of Eustacia and her son.
source_text: While she remained thus, overcome by her forebodings, the old clock indoors whizzed forth twelve strokes. Soon after, faint sounds floated to her ear from afar over the hills. The breeze came from that quarter, and it had brought with it the notes of distant bells, gaily starting off in a peal: one, two, three, four, five. The ringers at East Egdon were announcing the nuptials of Eustacia and her son.

text: “Paris, once again,” she breathed, the words curling out almost like a sigh. She had never mentioned to Elias the quiet craving for the city that Lucien’s glowing accounts had sown in her years before; yet now, entirely by chance, he was unintentionally poised to fulfill that very longing. “Do you care for Paris a great deal, then?” she added.
source_text: “Back to Paris again,” she murmured in a voice that was nearly a sigh. She had never once told Wildeve of the Parisian desires which Clym’s description had sown in her; yet here was he involuntarily in a position to gratify them. “You think a good deal of Paris?” she added.

text: She set out at two o’clock, and her meeting with Eustacia came sooner than anticipated, for the young woman was already standing beside the pool and sloped bank marking the edge of her grandfather’s property, taking in the surrounding view and perhaps musing on the romantic episodes the spot had witnessed in years past. When Mrs. Yeobright drew near, Eustacia regarded her with the cool, unblinking stare of a total stranger.
source_text: She started at two o’clock, and her meeting with Eustacia was hastened by the appearance of the young lady beside the pool and bank which bordered her grandfather’s premises, where she stood surveying the scene, and perhaps thinking of the romantic enactments it had witnessed in past days. When Mrs. Yeobright approached, Eustacia surveyed her with the calm stare of a stranger.

text: The match tugged back and forth, tipping first to one opponent, then the next, with neither ever pulling far enough ahead to claim a clear upper hand. Close to twenty minutes slipped by in this even, tense rhythm. By now, the guttering candle glow had lured moor gnats, miller moths, and all manner of small winged night creatures, which drifted in lazy loops around the glass lantern, dove straight into the licking flame, or bumped and skittered across the faces of the two competitors.
source_text: The game fluctuated, now in favour of one, now in favour of the other, without any great advantage on the side of either. Nearly twenty minutes were passed thus. The light of the candle had by this time attracted heath-flies, moths, and other winged creatures of night, which floated round the lantern, flew into the flame, or beat about the faces of the two players.

text: Wildeve had only ten guineas left at that point, so each man staked five apiece. Wildeve rolled three points; Venn rolled two, and raked the coins into his own pile. Wildeve snatched up the die, clamping his teeth down on it in sheer, unthinking rage, as if he would bite it straight in two. “Never give in—here are my last five!” he cried, slamming the coins down hard. “Blast these glowworms, they’re fading out. Why won’t you keep burning, you little fools? Stir them up with a thorn.”
source_text: Wildeve had now ten guineas left; and each laid five. Wildeve threw three points; Venn two, and raked in the coins. The other seized the die, and clenched his teeth upon it in sheer rage, as if he would bite it in pieces. “Never give in—here are my last five!” he cried, throwing them down. “Hang the glowworms—they are going out. Why don’t you burn, you little fools? Stir them up with a thorn.”

text: “Look, Mrs. Yeobright, let’s not fall out with each other over this. Just give me a reasonable amount of time to work through things. I have no intention of standing in the way of any better chance that might come her way; I only wish you’d let me know about this earlier. I’ll either write to you or stop by to visit in a day or two. Does that work for you?”
source_text: “Now, Mrs. Yeobright, don’t let us disagree. Give me a fair time. I don’t want to stand in the way of any better chance she may have; only I wish you had let me know earlier. I will write to you or call in a day or two. Will that suffice?”

text: People of real, substantial character carry their own atmospheres with them as they move through the world, the same way planets drag their atmospheric layers along their orbital paths; and the matron who’d just stepped into the gathering had always been able to shift the entire mood of a crowd to match her own energy, and she almost always did just that. When she was around other heath residents, her default demeanor was quiet and reserved, a restraint rooted in the quiet knowledge that she held far more skill with communication than most of them. But after wandering alone through the dark, stepping into a lit, social space had pulled an openness and eagerness to connect out of her that ran far higher than her usual baseline, and that warmth showed far more clearly in her expression than it did in anything she said.
source_text: Persons with any weight of character carry, like planets, their atmospheres along with them in their orbits; and the matron who entered now upon the scene could, and usually did, bring her own tone into a company. Her normal manner among the heathfolk had that reticence which results from the consciousness of superior communicative power. But the effect of coming into society and light after lonely wandering in darkness is a sociability in the comer above its usual pitch, expressed in the features even more than in words.

text: She climbed the stairs to her bedroom and got dressed with painstaking, deliberate care. To anyone watching, her sheer, unmissable beauty would have made the weight of her feelings feel almost entirely reasonable. The bleak, dead-end situation this woman had landed in, brought on as much by random misfortune as her own past bad calls, would have convinced even a casual, half-hearted supporter that she had perfectly valid grounds to ask whatever higher power ruled the world, by what right a being so exquisitely crafted had been placed in circumstances intentionally designed to turn her most dazzling charms into a curse rather than a blessing.
source_text: She ascended to her bedroom and dressed herself with scrupulous care. To an onlooker her beauty would have made her feelings almost seem reasonable. The gloomy corner into which accident as much as indiscretion had brought this woman might have led even a moderate partisan to feel that she had cogent reasons for asking the Supreme Power by what right a being of such exquisite finish had been placed in circumstances calculated to make of her charms a curse rather than a blessing.

text: At this hour, the isolated cottage was shuttered tight, its windows fully sealed to shut out the biting chill and pitch-black dark outside. Wildeve had worked out a secret arrangement with her: he would bring a small handful of fine gravel, hold it to the narrow crevice at the top of the exterior window shutter, and let the pebbles trickle slowly through. The soft, faint rustle they made slipping between the shutter and the glass would sound exactly like a scurrying mouse. This careful, low-key way of catching her attention was intended to avoid rousing her grandfather’s suspicions.
source_text: At this hour the lonely dwelling was closely blinded and shuttered from the chill and darkness without. Wildeve’s clandestine plan with her was to take a little gravel in his hand and hold it to the crevice at the top of the window shutter, which was on the outside, so that it should fall with a gentle rustle, resembling that of a mouse, between shutter and glass. This precaution in attracting her attention was to avoid arousing the suspicions of her grandfather.

text: But as a mother, it was only natural that she would soon stop fixating on her own struggles. If the course of her very next thought had left a bright, visible streak through the air, just like the tail of a passing meteor, it would have pointed directly opposite the heron’s flight path, curving down east to settle atop the roof of Clym’s house.
source_text: But, being a mother, it was inevitable that she should soon cease to ruminate upon her own condition. Had the track of her next thought been marked by a streak in the air, like the path of a meteor, it would have shown a direction contrary to the heron’s, and have descended to the eastward upon the roof of Clym’s house.

text: He made his way back across the open heath to his van. The fire had not yet burned out, and every item sat exactly where he had left it. Venn suddenly thought of his clothes, which were so soaked through with rain they felt as heavy as lead. He changed out of the damp garments, spread them flat in front of the fire to dry, and stretched out to sleep. But rest was out of the question: his mind buzzed with sharp, vivid images of the chaos unfolding at the house he had just left. Chiding himself for departing in the first place, he pulled on a different suit, locked the van door, and hurried back across the heath toward the inn. Rain was still pouring heavily when he stepped into the kitchen. A bright, warm fire blazed in the hearth, and two women bustled about the space, one of whom was Olly Dowden.
source_text: He returned across the heath to his van. The fire was not yet out, and everything remained as he had left it. Venn now bethought himself of his clothes, which were saturated with water to the weight of lead. He changed them, spread them before the fire, and lay down to sleep. But it was more than he could do to rest here while excited by a vivid imagination of the turmoil they were in at the house he had quitted, and, blaming himself for coming away, he dressed in another suit, locked up the door, and again hastened across to the inn. Rain was still falling heavily when he entered the kitchen. A bright fire was shining from the hearth, and two women were bustling about, one of whom was Olly Dowden.

text: I can’t stand talking about Paris! That said, there’s one sunlit room in the Louvre that would be such a perfect place for you to live: the Galerie d’Apollon. Most of its windows face east, so when the sun cuts bright first thing in the morning, the entire space is flooded with unbroken, breathtaking splendor. Sunbeams dart and glint first off the thick crusted gilding, then bounce to the elaborate inlaid ceiling coffers, leap from the coffers to the stacks of gold and silver serving ware, flash from the metal pieces to the loose jewels and precious stones, and skip from those to the glossy enamel works, weaving an intricate web of light so bright it stings the eye. But anyway, back to our marriage—
source_text: “I hate talking of Paris! Well, I remember one sunny room in the Louvre which would make a fitting place for you to live in—the Galerie d’Apollon. Its windows are mainly east; and in the early morning, when the sun is bright, the whole apartment is in a perfect blaze of splendour. The rays bristle and dart from the encrustations of gilding to the magnificent inlaid coffers, from the coffers to the gold and silver plate, from the plate to the jewels and precious stones, from these to the enamels, till there is a perfect network of light which quite dazzles the eye. But now, about our marriage——”

text: “There was no one else to ask. She’d stepped into the church just ahead of me, not heading for the gallery at all. The parson glanced about the congregation right before starting the service, and since she was the only person close at hand, he beckoned her forward, so she walked up to the communion rails. Later, when it came time to sign the register, she pushed her veil up from her face and signed her name; Tamsin seemed to be thanking her for the favor.” The reddleman told the story slowly, thoughtfully, for the sharp shift in Wildeve’s coloring still lingered in his mind, from the moment Eustacia lifted that thick veil that had kept her unrecognizable and looked calmly, unblinking, straight into his face. “And then,” Diggory said, his voice heavy with sadness, “I left. The life she’d lived as Tamsin Yeobright was over.”
source_text: “Because there was nobody else. She had gone into the church just before me, not into the gallery. The parson looked round before beginning, and as she was the only one near he beckoned to her, and she went up to the rails. After that, when it came to signing the book, she pushed up her veil and signed; and Tamsin seemed to thank her for her kindness.” The reddleman told the tale thoughtfully for there lingered upon his vision the changing colour of Wildeve, when Eustacia lifted the thick veil which had concealed her from recognition and looked calmly into his face. “And then,” said Diggory sadly, “I came away, for her history as Tamsin Yeobright was over.”

text: Two matched pistols hung within easy reach of her grandfather’s bedhead; he kept them perpetually loaded as a safeguard against potential burglars, given how thoroughly isolated the house sat. Eustacia stared at the pair for minutes on end, as if they were a single page torn from a book that laid out a bizarre, entirely unforeseen truth she was only just parsing for the first time. Moving fast, as if she was frightened of her own impulses, she hurried back down the stairs and stood motionless, lost in heavy, roiling thought.
source_text: It was a brace of pistols, hanging near the head of her grandfather’s bed, which he always kept there loaded, as a precaution against possible burglars, the house being very lonely. Eustacia regarded them long, as if they were the page of a book in which she read a new and a strange matter. Quickly, like one afraid of herself, she returned downstairs and stood in deep thought.

text: For those who cherished it, this place lingered in memory with a peculiar, gentle sense of perfect fit. Bright, sunlit fields heavy with blossoms and ripe fruit rarely carry that same quality, for they only feel permanently harmonious with a life that promises far happier outcomes than most can claim in the present. When twilight draped over Egdon Heath, it wove the landscape into something majestic without harshness, striking without showiness, clear in its quiet admonitions, and grand in its unadorned simplicity. That same intangible quality that often lends a prison’s facade far more dignity than the front of a palace twice its size draped this heath in a sublimity entirely absent from spots renowned for their conventional, widely celebrated beauty. Cheerful, pretty landscapes pair happily with bright, fortunate days, but how cruel they feel when times are unkind! People more often suffer under the mockery of a setting far too blithe for their sorrows than they do under the weight of surroundings steeped in overmuch sadness. Worn, haggard Egdon spoke to a subtler, rarer instinct, a more recently forged emotion, than the surface impulse that responds to the kind of easy beauty we call charming and fair.
source_text: It was a spot which returned upon the memory of those who loved it with an aspect of peculiar and kindly congruity. Smiling champaigns of flowers and fruit hardly do this, for they are permanently harmonious only with an existence of better reputation as to its issues than the present. Twilight combined with the scenery of Egdon Heath to evolve a thing majestic without severity, impressive without showiness, emphatic in its admonitions, grand in its simplicity. The qualifications which frequently invest the façade of a prison with far more dignity than is found in the façade of a palace double its size lent to this heath a sublimity in which spots renowned for beauty of the accepted kind are utterly wanting. Fair prospects wed happily with fair times; but alas, if times be not fair! Men have oftener suffered from, the mockery of a place too smiling for their reason than from the oppression of surroundings oversadly tinged. Haggard Egdon appealed to a subtler and scarcer instinct, to a more recently learnt emotion, than that which responds to the sort of beauty called charming and fair.

text: But I don’t even deserve to be given any more proof that heaven itself has cast me off. Thomasin, do you think she truly knew me— that she didn’t die clinging to that awful, wrongheaded idea that I’d never forgiven her? I have no idea how she ever came to hold that stupid misconception in the first place. I wish more than anything you could reassure me that’s not what happened. Do you think that’s how it was, Eustacia? Please, just say something to me.
source_text: “But I am not worth receiving further proof even of Heaven’s reprobation. Do you think, Thomasin, that she knew me—that she did not die in that horrid mistaken notion about my not forgiving her, which I can’t tell you how she acquired? If you could only assure me of that! Do you think so, Eustacia? Do speak to me.”

text: While Wildeve was not particularly prone to physical cowardice, this underhanded, cheap blow from a person he knew all too well gnawed at his peace of mind. Still, it did nothing to alter his intended movements. A couple of nights later, he set off again through the vale toward Alderworth, taking the precaution of avoiding all established paths. The quiet certainty that he was being watched, that others were employing trickery to curb his roving desires, only lent a sharp, thrilling edge to this deeply sentimental journey, so long as the danger never rose to anything truly menacing. He guessed Venn and Mrs. Yeobright were working in league, and felt there was a certain legitimacy in pushing back against such a coordinated alliance.
source_text: Although his weaknesses were not specially those akin to physical fear, this species of coup-de-Jarnac from one he knew too well troubled the mind of Wildeve. But his movements were unaltered thereby. A night or two later he again went along the vale to Alderworth, taking the precaution of keeping out of any path. The sense that he was watched, that craft was employed to circumvent his errant tastes, added piquancy to a journey so entirely sentimental, so long as the danger was of no fearful sort. He imagined that Venn and Mrs. Yeobright were in league, and felt that there was a certain legitimacy in combating such a coalition.

text: “The spirit moved them.” A specific reading of that phrase pushed unbidden to the forefront of awareness; an emotionally receptive listener might well have seen their primitive, fetishistic frame of mind soften into something far more elevated. It was not, after all, the broad stretch of long-rooted blooms to the left that spoke, nor those to the right, nor the blossoms covering the slope ahead; rather, it was the single, unified presence of some other entity that voiced itself through each and every one of them simultaneously.
source_text: “The spirit moved them.” A meaning of the phrase forced itself upon the attention; and an emotional listener’s fetichistic mood might have ended in one of more advanced quality. It was not, after all, that the left-hand expanse of old blooms spoke, or the right-hand, or those of the slope in front; but it was the single person of something else speaking through each at once.

text: Clym Yeobright was at Blooms-End just then, clinging to the quiet hope that Eustacia would return to his side. All the household furniture had only been moved into the cottage that very day, though Clym had already been living in the old family home for more than a week. He had filled those days tending to the grounds: sweeping fallen leaves from the garden paths, snipping dead stalks out of the flower beds, and nailing back into place the creepers torn loose by sharp autumn winds. These small labors brought him no particular joy, but they served as a soft screen between himself and the despair that threatened to overwhelm him. What’s more, tending to every part of the property that had passed from his mother’s hands to his own had become something of a sacred duty to him.
source_text: Yeobright was at this time at Blooms-End, hoping that Eustacia would return to him. The removal of furniture had been accomplished only that day, though Clym had lived in the old house for more than a week. He had spent the time in working about the premises, sweeping leaves from the garden paths, cutting dead stalks from the flower beds, and nailing up creepers which had been displaced by the autumn winds. He took no particular pleasure in these deeds, but they formed a screen between himself and despair. Moreover, it had become a religion with him to preserve in good condition all that had lapsed from his mother’s hands to his own.

text: Elowen clutched a bulky, wrapped bundle pressed tight to her chest; once she’d settled herself into a seat at last, she began picking at the knotted layers of wool and oilcloth, until a small infant peeked out from the folded husk of fabric—dry, warm to the touch, and blissfully unaware of the rough journey she’d just made through lashing wind and cold rain. Elowen pressed a fleeting kiss to the baby’s downy forehead, before a sob bubbled up unbidden, her voice cracking as she spoke: “I brought her with me, I couldn’t bear to guess what might befall her if I left her behind. I know this harsh trip could end up being the death of her, but I would never have left her in Mara’s care!”
source_text: Thomasin carried in her arms a large bundle; and having by this time seated herself she began to unroll it, when a baby appeared as the kernel to the husks—dry, warm, and unconscious of travel or rough weather. Thomasin briefly kissed the baby, and then found time to begin crying as she said, “I brought baby, for I was afraid what might happen to her. I suppose it will be her death, but I couldn’t leave her with Rachel!”

text: Once every last member of the Egdon gathering had left the bonfire site to its usual quiet seclusion, a woman swathed in thick, close-wrapped layers made her way toward the barrow from the stretch of heath where the smaller, separate fire burned. If the reddleman had still been keeping watch, he would have recognized her instantly as the woman who’d first stood there so oddly some time prior, slipping away the moment strangers drew near. She climbed back up to the familiar spot she’d occupied earlier at the crest, where the glowing red coals of the dying fire seemed to peer up at her like living eyes set in the cold corpse of the fading day. She stood motionless there, the vast expanse of night air stretching out all around her; its half-lit dimness, measured against the pitch-black darkness blanketing the heath far below, was as a venial sin might be next to an unforgivable mortal one.
source_text: When the whole Egdon concourse had left the site of the bonfire to its accustomed loneliness, a closely wrapped female figure approached the barrow from that quarter of the heath in which the little fire lay. Had the reddleman been watching he might have recognized her as the woman who had first stood there so singularly, and vanished at the approach of strangers. She ascended to her old position at the top, where the red coals of the perishing fire greeted her like living eyes in the corpse of day. There she stood still around her stretching the vast night atmosphere, whose incomplete darkness in comparison with the total darkness of the heath below it might have represented a venial beside a mortal sin.

text: As the grown men and teenaged boys stacked kindling and peat for their own bonfire, a shift rippled through the heavy shadow that cloaked the far-off ridgeline. Crimson pinpricks and smudges of flame bloomed one after another, dappling the dark expanse of countryside stretching on all sides. They were the bonfires of outlying villages and homesteads holding the same annual remembrance rite. A few glowed so far off, filtered through layers of mist, that pale, straw-hued rays fanned outward from their edges like the spokes of a half-turned wheel. Others loomed large and close, blazing a vivid, blood-red against the dark, like raw gashes cut into a pelt of ink-black fleece. The brightest burned like maenads mid-frenzy, their faces flushed the deep purple of spiced wine, their tresses of flame tossing wild in the wind. These leaping flames stained the quiet underbellies of the clouds overhead, gilding their fleeting, hollowed recesses until those soft pockets of air looked like bubbling, scalding cauldrons. All told, nearly thirty bonfires could be picked out across the whole stretch of the valley; and just as one can tell the time on a clock face even when its numerals have worn away, the men knew exactly which village each fire belonged to by its angle and bearing, even when the surrounding landscape was swallowed entirely by dark.
source_text: While the men and lads were building the pile, a change took place in the mass of shade which denoted the distant landscape. Red suns and tufts of fire one by one began to arise, flecking the whole country round. They were the bonfires of other parishes and hamlets that were engaged in the same sort of commemoration. Some were distant, and stood in a dense atmosphere, so that bundles of pale straw-like beams radiated around them in the shape of a fan. Some were large and near, glowing scarlet-red from the shade, like wounds in a black hide. Some were Mænades, with winy faces and blown hair. These tinctured the silent bosom of the clouds above them and lit up their ephemeral caves, which seemed thenceforth to become scalding caldrons. Perhaps as many as thirty bonfires could be counted within the whole bounds of the district; and as the hour may be told on a clock-face when the figures themselves are invisible, so did the men recognize the locality of each fire by its angle and direction, though nothing of the scenery could be viewed.

text: The second the door clicked shut behind them and they were fully alone, Thomasin tilted her pale, tear-streaked face up to Damon. “This whole mess is eating me alive, Damon! I never wanted to leave you on bad terms when we were at Anglebury this morning. I was just so shaken I barely knew what was coming out of my mouth. I haven’t told Aunt a thing about how much I’ve suffered today, either. It’s so hard to keep my expression and voice steady, to smile like this is just some trivial annoyance that doesn’t bother me at all, but I’m forcing myself to do it so she doesn’t get even angrier with you. I know you couldn’t help what happened, dear, no matter what Aunt thinks.”
source_text: As soon as they were alone, and the door closed, Thomasin said, turning up her pale, tearful face to him, “It is killing me, this, Damon! I did not mean to part from you in anger at Anglebury this morning; but I was frightened and hardly knew what I said. I’ve not let Aunt know how much I suffered today; and it is so hard to command my face and voice, and to smile as if it were a slight thing to me; but I try to do so, that she may not be still more indignant with you. I know you could not help it, dear, whatever Aunt may think.”

text: “We’ll see about that,” Mrs. Yeobright said, and the pair turned toward the local inn, known around the area as the Quiet Woman, whose hanging sign showed the image of a matron carrying her own head tucked under her arm. The inn’s front faced the heath and Rainbarrow, the landform’s dark shadow looming as if threatening the building from the edge of the sky. A neglected, tarnished brass plate was fixed to the door, bearing the unexpected engraving: “Mr. Wildeve, Engineer.” It was a useless yet carefully kept relic from the period when he had started out in that profession at a Budmouth office, supported by people who’d held high hopes for his success, only to be deeply disappointed. A small garden sat at the back of the inn, and behind the garden ran a still, deep stream that formed the heath’s boundary in that spot, with wide meadowlands visible across the water.
source_text: “I shall see about that,” said Mrs. Yeobright; and they turned towards the inn, known in the neighbourhood as the Quiet Woman, the sign of which represented the figure of a matron carrying her head under her arm. The front of the house was towards the heath and Rainbarrow, whose dark shape seemed to threaten it from the sky. Upon the door was a neglected brass plate, bearing the unexpected inscription, “Mr. Wildeve, Engineer”—a useless yet cherished relic from the time when he had been started in that profession in an office at Budmouth by those who had hoped much from him, and had been disappointed. The garden was at the back, and behind this ran a still deep stream, forming the margin of the heath in that direction, meadow-land appearing beyond the stream.

text: “I’ll wager he sailed on some rickety old trading tub at one point, that’s for certain. Why on earth can’t he keep an eye on his wife? No proper gentlewoman would go traipsing across the moor at all hours, sunrise or midnight, the way she does. And that’s hardly the half of it. There was something mighty off between her and Elspeth’s husband a few years back— I’m as sure of that as I am that my feet are planted on this very turf right now.”
source_text: “No doubt he has been to sea in some tub or other. Why doesn’t he look after her? No lady would rove about the heath at all hours of the day and night as she does. But that’s not all of it. There was something queer between her and Thomasin’s husband at one time—I am as sure of it as that I stand here.”

text: “No, sir,” Christian said, pulling back, a quick, doubtful glance darting across his face. “I’m just a regular poor guy here to watch, if that’s alright with you, sir. I don’t even know the first thing about how you pull this off. If I was totally sure I’d get what I’m supposed to, I’d lay that shilling down right now, but I can’t do it otherwise.”
source_text: “No, sir,” said Christian, drawing back, with a quick gaze of misgiving. “I am only a poor chap come to look on, an it please ye, sir. I don’t so much as know how you do it. If so be I was sure of getting it I would put down the shilling; but I couldn’t otherwise.”

text: Yeobright and Eustacia held each other’s gaze for one fleeting instant, as though both were turning over the memory of that short, shared moonlit interlude they had experienced together. In that single glance, the calm, fixed set of her features softened and deepened into an expression of gentle refinement and warmth; it was as if bright, garish midday had lifted into the quiet dignity of sunset in the space of just two seconds.
source_text: Yeobright and Eustacia looked at each other for one instant, as if each had in mind those few moments during which a certain moonlight scene was common to both. With the glance the calm fixity of her features sublimed itself to an expression of refinement and warmth; it was like garish noon rising to the dignity of sunset in a couple of seconds.

text: “Honestly,” Venn said earnestly, “she has no idea this request is even being made. I’m the only one asking you to let him go. This will work out better for both you and her, at the end of the day. If folks find out a woman of your station is meeting in secret with a man who’s already mistreated another woman, there’s going to be all kinds of ugly gossip flying around about you.”
source_text: “Indeed,” said Venn earnestly, “she knows nothing whatever about it. It is only I who ask you to give him up. It will be better for her and you both. People will say bad things if they find out that a lady secretly meets a man who has ill-used another woman.”

text: Rachel looked dreadfully upset, and at last burst into tears. “If you please, ma’am, I didn’t have anything proper to wear on Maypole day. I spotted yours sitting on the table, and I thought I’d borrow them for a little while. I never meant to harm them at all, but one of them went missing. Someone gave me money to buy you a new pair, but I haven’t been able to get out anywhere to pick them up yet.”
source_text: Rachel appeared as one dreadfully troubled, and at last began to cry. “Please, ma’am, on the day of the Maypole I had none to wear, and I seed yours on the table, and I thought I would borrow ’em. I did not mean to hurt ’em at all, but one of them got lost. Somebody gave me some money to buy another pair for you, but I have not been able to go anywhere to get ’em.”

text: Clym Yeobright hovered between sending for his mother and deciding against the idea entirely. He knew word of his condition would only deepen her distress, and the isolated rhythm of their lives out there meant she’d almost certainly never hear what had happened unless he sent a messenger to tell her specifically. Doing his best to bear the weight of the situation as calmly as he could, he waited out the days until the third week of his illness arrived, when he stepped into the open air for the first time since the attack first hit him. The surgeon stopped by to check on him again at this stage, and Clym pressed him to share a clear, definitive opinion on his case. The young man was hit with fresh surprise when he learned there was still no fixed timeline for when he might be able to resume his work: his eyes were in that odd, precarious state where, while he had enough vision to walk around without trouble, straining them to focus on any detailed, fixed task would run the serious risk of triggering a sharp, acute flare-up of his ophthalmia.
source_text: Yeobright thought he would send for his mother; and then he thought he would not. Knowledge of his state could only make her the more unhappy; and the seclusion of their life was such that she would hardly be likely to learn the news except through a special messenger. Endeavouring to take the trouble as philosophically as possible, he waited on till the third week had arrived, when he went into the open air for the first time since the attack. The surgeon visited him again at this stage, and Clym urged him to express a distinct opinion. The young man learnt with added surprise that the date at which he might expect to resume his labours was as uncertain as ever, his eyes being in that peculiar state which, though affording him sight enough for walking about, would not admit of their being strained upon any definite object without incurring the risk of reproducing ophthalmia in its acute form.

text: He held perfectly still for what felt like hours, until he began to be certain the midnight hour must have already struck. A heavy, persistent doubt had taken root in his head that Eustacia would ever venture down the hillside in weather this harsh; yet he knew her temperament too well to rule it out entirely. “Poor thing, this is just the sort of terrible luck she always seems stuck with,” he murmured.
source_text: He lingered on in perfect stillness till he began to fancy that the midnight hour must have struck. A very strong doubt had arisen in his mind if Eustacia would venture down the hill in such weather; yet knowing her nature he felt that she might. “Poor thing! ’tis like her ill-luck,” he murmured.

text: The words were a guttural, uncanny patois: the Lord’s Prayer intoned end to beginning, the standard cantrip called upon to summon unholy aid to strike down a foe. Elspeth droned the dreary incantation three times at a glacial pace, and by the time she fell silent, the small wax effigy had shrunk noticeably. Each drop of melting wax that fell into the hearth spawned a tall, lashing flame, which coiled its snaking tip around the figure and gnawed ever deeper into its soft, malleable bulk. Now and then a pin embedded in the effigy clattered down alongside the dripping wax, and the glowing coals seared it to a bright, molten crimson where it rested.
source_text: It was a strange jargon—the Lord’s Prayer repeated backwards—the incantation usual in proceedings for obtaining unhallowed assistance against an enemy. Susan uttered the lugubrious discourse three times slowly, and when it was completed the image had considerably diminished. As the wax dropped into the fire a long flame arose from the spot, and curling its tongue round the figure ate still further into its substance. A pin occasionally dropped with the wax, and the embers heated it red as it lay.

text: Elowen’s gaze settled on the solitary man. Kael had told her at their last secret meeting that Rian had been put forward by Morwenna as a suitor eager and ready to take his place as Liora’s betrothed. His frame was perfectly proportioned, his face youthful with clean, defined features, his eye bright, his wit keen, and his social standing one he could easily improve if he so chose. Yet for all these promising traits, there was little likelihood Liora would accept this rootless, outcast figure when she had a cousin like Celyn close at hand, and Kael himself still far from entirely indifferent to her. It took Elowen no time at all to guess that poor, anxious Morwenna, fretting over her niece’s future, had named this suitor only to stir the flagging zeal of the other two. Elowen was firmly aligned with Morwenna’s family now, and she fully embraced the spirit of the aunt’s quiet gambit.
source_text: Eustacia looked at the lonely man. Wildeve had told her at their last meeting that Venn had been thrust forward by Mrs. Yeobright as one ready and anxious to take his place as Thomasin’s betrothed. His figure was perfect, his face young and well outlined, his eye bright, his intelligence keen, and his position one which he could readily better if he chose. But in spite of possibilities it was not likely that Thomasin would accept this Ishmaelitish creature while she had a cousin like Yeobright at her elbow, and Wildeve at the same time not absolutely indifferent. Eustacia was not long in guessing that poor Mrs. Yeobright, in her anxiety for her niece’s future, had mentioned this lover to stimulate the zeal of the other. Eustacia was on the side of the Yeobrights now, and entered into the spirit of the aunt’s desire.

text: “I’d give up every last thing I own, my sweet wife. Go, do whatever your heart desires. Who would ever bar you from indulging even the smallest whim? You still hold my entire heart, I know that much; and for putting up with me, when I am truly nothing but a burden dragging you down, I owe you more gratitude than I can say. Yes, go on your own and shine bright. As for me, I will accept the fate I am bound to. At events like that, everyone would avoid me. My hook and gloves are just like the rattle Saint Lazarus’s lepers carried, a warning to the world to step out of the path of a sight that would only bring them sorrow.” He kissed her softly, pulled on his leggings, and walked out.
source_text: “I would rather lose all my own, my sweet wife. Go and do whatever you like. Who can forbid your indulgence in any whim? You have all my heart yet, I believe; and because you bear with me, who am in truth a drag upon you, I owe you thanks. Yes, go alone and shine. As for me, I will stick to my doom. At that kind of meeting people would shun me. My hook and gloves are like the St. Lazarus rattle of the leper, warning the world to get out of the way of a sight that would sadden them.” He kissed her, put on his leggings, and went out.

text: “I can’t say for certain, other than that there are so many things other people hold dear that I don’t care for at all; that’s part of why I feel I ought to go through with this. For one thing, I have very few physical needs. Fancy delicacies hold no pleasure for me; all those fine, costly indulgences would be wasted on someone who can’t appreciate them. Well, I ought to turn that quirk of mine to good use: since I can get by perfectly well without all the things other people rely on, I can put the money those things would cost toward anyone who actually needs it.”
source_text: “I don’t know, except that there are many things other people care for which I don’t; and that’s partly why I think I ought to do this. For one thing, my body does not require much of me. I cannot enjoy delicacies; good things are wasted upon me. Well, I ought to turn that defect to advantage, and by being able to do without what other people require I can spend what such things cost upon anybody else.”

text: “I’ll let you know how you can get a look at her, Mr. Yeobright,” said Sam. “We’re heading over to her house at six o’clock tonight to haul the dropped bucket up from the well, and you could lend a hand if you’re willing. Five or six others are coming, but the well is terribly deep, so an extra pair of arms would be a real help, if you don’t mind being seen doing that sort of rough work. She’s bound to be wandering about the grounds the whole time.”
source_text: “I’ll tell you how you mid see her, Mr. Yeobright,” said Sam. “We are going to grapple for the bucket at six o’clock tonight at her house, and you could lend a hand. There’s five or six coming, but the well is deep, and another might be useful, if you don’t mind appearing in that shape. She’s sure to be walking round.”

text: “Of course, this is all about her future. You might not realize that another man has already made it plain he’s eager to marry Thomasin. While I haven’t given him any encouragement so far, I can’t in good conscience keep denying him a fair shot any longer. I don’t want to come off as curt with you, but I have to be fair to both him and her.”
source_text: “It is, of course, in reference to her future. You may not be aware that another man has shown himself anxious to marry Thomasin. Now, though I have not encouraged him yet, I cannot conscientiously refuse him a chance any longer. I don’t wish to be short with you; but I must be fair to him and to her.”

text: Her grandfather had turned to trudge back the way he came, too preoccupied to pick up the boy’s mumbled reply. The moment the silver-haired old man was out of view, she snapped at the child, thick with pique, “Ungrateful little thing, how dare you contradict me? You’ll never get to have another bonfire again unless you tend to this one right now. Go on, tell me you like doing little things for me, and don’t you dare lie about it.”
source_text: Her grandfather had turned back again, and did not hear the boy’s reply. As soon as the white-haired man had vanished she said in a tone of pique to the child, “Ungrateful little boy, how can you contradict me? Never shall you have a bonfire again unless you keep it up now. Come, tell me you like to do things for me, and don’t deny it.”

text: “Still, she never came to call on me, even when I pressed her to visit months before I took my wedding vows. Had she made the journey, or had I traveled back to her small cottage, she never would have breathed her last murmuring, ‘I am a woman with a shattered heart, discarded by my own son.’ My door has never been barred to her—warmth and a place at my table have always waited, whenever she might choose to arrive. But she never came to claim that invitation.”
source_text: “But she didn’t come to see me, though I asked her, before I married, if she would come. Had she come, or had I gone there, she would never have died saying, ‘I am a broken-hearted woman, cast off by my son.’ My door has always been open to her—a welcome here has always awaited her. But that she never came to see.”

text: Another time when she was scanning the area the same way, she spotted two women walking along the valley floor. The day was bright and completely clear; the pair were no more than half a mile away, so she could make out every tiny detail through her telescope. The woman walking in front held a white bundle tucked in her arms, with a long swathe of fabric hanging loose from one end. When the two turned so the sun hit them full on, Eustacia could see plainly that the bundle was a baby. She called Charley over to ask if he knew who they were, even though she already had a pretty good guess.
source_text: On another occasion when reconnoitring thus she beheld two female figures walking in the vale. The day was fine and clear; and the persons not being more than half a mile off she could see their every detail with the telescope. The woman walking in front carried a white bundle in her arms, from one end of which hung a long appendage of drapery; and when the walkers turned, so that the sun fell more directly upon them, Eustacia could see that the object was a baby. She called Charley, and asked him if he knew who they were, though she well guessed.

text: Elspeth gave a small shake of her head, and Kael hauled her gently into his arms, then set off at a plodding pace with his weighty charge. The evening air had turned crisp all around them, but every time he crossed a stretch of bare, vegetation-free sand, the warmth the earth had soaked up through the daylight hours billowed up to sear his cheeks. When he’d first lifted her, he’d given barely any thought to how far they still had to go to reach Thistle Corner; even though he’d caught a long nap that very afternoon, the heft of her in his arms soon began to make his muscles burn. He pressed onward, like Aeneas bearing Anchises from the ruins of Troy; bats looped lazy arcs around his head, nightjars beat their soft wings barely a foot from his face, and not a single human soul was within shouting distance.
source_text: She shook her head, and he lifted her up; then, at a slow pace, went onward with his load. The air was now completely cool; but whenever he passed over a sandy patch of ground uncarpeted with vegetation there was reflected from its surface into his face the heat which it had imbibed during the day. At the beginning of his undertaking he had thought but little of the distance which yet would have to be traversed before Blooms-End could be reached; but though he had slept that afternoon he soon began to feel the weight of his burden. Thus he proceeded, like Æneas with his father; the bats circling round his head, nightjars flapping their wings within a yard of his face, and not a human being within call.

text: “He doesn’t take the blessing of winning her for granted,” Eustacia whispered, “and in that regard, he is a good man. Many women would go to great lengths for a husband like him. But am I craving something unreasonably excessive when I long for what is called true life—music, poetry, burning passion, the fire of grand struggle, all the thrumming, surging pulse that beats in the great arteries of the wider world? That was the exact shape of my youthful dream; I never got to hold it, yet I once thought I saw the path to it in my Clym.”
source_text: “He is not ungrateful for winning her,” whispered Eustacia, “and in that respect he is a good man. Many women would go far for such a husband. But do I desire unreasonably much in wanting what is called life—music, poetry, passion, war, and all the beating and pulsing that are going on in the great arteries of the world? That was the shape of my youthful dream; but I did not get it. Yet I thought I saw the way to it in my Clym.”

text: Eustacia stayed quiet once more, her mind foggy and unmoored. What was this strange, unnameable sensation creeping through her? Could her entire fixation on Wildeve have actually been nothing more than a product of rivalry, so that the magic and fantasy around him vanished the second she heard he was no longer her rival’s most desired prize? So she’d finally won him, no strings attached. Thomasin didn’t want him anymore. What a cheap, embarrassing win that felt like. He cared for her more than anyone, she told herself; and yet—could she even admit this quiet, disloyal thought to herself, let alone say it out loud?—what good was a man that someone she considered beneath her didn’t even think was worth keeping? That impulse that hums in almost every living thing, the reflex to not want what no one else is fighting for, burned as bright as any full-blown passion in Eustacia’s sharp, pleasure-seeking, over-sensitive soul. The gap between her social standing and his, which she’d barely ever noticed before, now pressed on her unpleasantly, impossible to ignore, and for the first time it hit her that she’d lowered herself by caring for him at all.
source_text: Eustacia again remained in a sort of stupefied silence. What curious feeling was this coming over her? Was it really possible that her interest in Wildeve had been so entirely the result of antagonism that the glory and the dream departed from the man with the first sound that he was no longer coveted by her rival? She was, then, secure of him at last. Thomasin no longer required him. What a humiliating victory! He loved her best, she thought; and yet—dared she to murmur such treacherous criticism ever so softly?—what was the man worth whom a woman inferior to herself did not value? The sentiment which lurks more or less in all animate nature—that of not desiring the undesired of others—was lively as a passion in the supersubtle, epicurean heart of Eustacia. Her social superiority over him, which hitherto had scarcely ever impressed her, became unpleasantly insistent, and for the first time she felt that she had stooped in loving him.

text: He made his way to the stair landing and stood waiting for close to five minutes. She still did not return. He turned back to grab a light, ready to go after her, but first he peered into her bedroom. There, pressed into the outer surface of the quilt, was the faint impression of her form, proof that the bed had not been turned down at all; even more telling, she had not brought her candlestick downstairs with her. Thoroughly alarmed now, he pulled on his clothes in a rush and hurried down to the front door, which he distinctly remembered bolting and locking himself. It was now unlatched. There was no longer any doubt that Eustacia had slipped out of the house in the dead of midnight, and where she could have gone he could not fathom. Following her was all but impossible. If the dwelling had stood on an ordinary road, two people setting out in opposite directions might well have been sure to overtake her; but searching for anyone out on the heath after dark was a hopeless task, the number of passable paths leading away from any point as numerous as the meridians radiating from the pole. Perplexed as to what to do next, he glanced into the parlour, and was vexed to find the letter still lying there completely untouched.
source_text: He went on to the landing, and stood waiting nearly five minutes. Still she did not return. He went back for a light, and prepared to follow her; but first he looked into her bedroom. There, on the outside of the quilt, was the impression of her form, showing that the bed had not been opened; and, what was more significant, she had not taken her candlestick downstairs. He was now thoroughly alarmed; and hastily putting on his clothes he descended to the front door, which he himself had bolted and locked. It was now unfastened. There was no longer any doubt that Eustacia had left the house at this midnight hour; and whither could she have gone? To follow her was almost impossible. Had the dwelling stood in an ordinary road, two persons setting out, one in each direction, might have made sure of overtaking her; but it was a hopeless task to seek for anybody on a heath in the dark, the practicable directions for flight across it from any point being as numerous as the meridians radiating from the pole. Perplexed what to do, he looked into the parlour, and was vexed to find that the letter still lay there untouched.

text: After a long stretch of quiet, he turned toward the lamp and glanced at his watch. To his astonishment, it was almost a quarter past midnight. He now wished he’d elected to drive the winding road up to Mistover, a plan he’d discarded earlier because that route was vastly longer than the footpath leading down the open hillside, and would have added significantly to the horse’s burden.
source_text: At length he turned to the lamp and looked at his watch. To his surprise it was nearly a quarter past midnight. He now wished that he had driven up the circuitous road to Mistover, a plan not adopted because of the enormous length of the route in proportion to that of the pedestrian’s path down the open hillside, and the consequent increase of labour for the horse.

text: “Ah, God, ah!” she wailed, the last of her resolve shattering all at once; wracked by gasping sobs that closed up her throat, she crumpled to her knees on the scuffed floorboards. “Oh, can’t you just stop? You’re so unyielding—even wild beasts don’t torment their prey this endlessly! I held out for so long, but you’re grinding me to dust. I beg you for mercy—I can’t take another minute of this, it’s cruel beyond all decency to keep pressing! Even if I’d slit your sister’s throat with my own two hands, I wouldn’t deserve to be flayed raw like this. Ah, ah! Lord have pity on a wretched, broken woman!... You’ve won, you’ve beaten me at this whole ugly game—I beg you, just stay your hand, for pity’s sake!... I admit it—I purposely didn’t unbolt the door the first time she rapped on it—but I would’ve undone it the second time, if I hadn’t assumed you’d already gotten up to let her in. When I realized you hadn’t moved, I wrenched it open, but she was already gone. That’s all I did wrong to her. Even the kindest people slip up and do terrible things sometimes, don’t they? I know they do. Now I’ll go—leave you for ever and ever, I swear!”
source_text: “O, O, O!” she cried, breaking down at last; and, shaking with sobs which choked her, she sank upon her knees. “O, will you have done! O, you are too relentless—there’s a limit to the cruelty of savages! I have held out long—but you crush me down. I beg for mercy—I cannot bear this any longer—it is inhuman to go further with this! If I had—killed your—mother with my own hand—I should not deserve such a scourging to the bone as this. O, O! God have mercy upon a miserable woman!... You have beaten me in this game—I beg you to stay your hand in pity!... I confess that I—wilfully did not undo the door the first time she knocked—but—I should have unfastened it the second—if I had not thought you had gone to do it yourself. When I found you had not I opened it, but she was gone. That’s the extent of my crime—towards _her_. Best natures commit bad faults sometimes, don’t they?—I think they do. Now I will leave you—for ever and ever!”

text: At least one thing was unmistakeable: she was never meant to be gawked at like this, unguarded and half-asleep. The reddleman seemed keenly aware of that fact, too; as Mrs. Yeobright peered in at her, he averted his eyes, a small act of quiet tact that suited him perfectly. The sleeping woman apparently shared that unspoken feeling, because the very next moment, her eyes fluttered open.
source_text: One thing at least was obvious: she was not made to be looked at thus. The reddleman had appeared conscious of as much, and, while Mrs. Yeobright looked in upon her, he cast his eyes aside with a delicacy which well became him. The sleeper apparently thought so too, for the next moment she opened her own.

text: The production in question was the beloved, widely known staging of Saint George, and everyone working backstage pitched in with the preparations, including the women from every local household. If sisters and sweethearts hadn’t lent their support, the costumes would almost certainly have turned out poorly; that said, this particular brand of assistance was not without its downsides. No one could ever get these young women to honor longstanding tradition when designing and decorating the armor; they insisted on sewing silk and velvet loops and bows anywhere that suited their personal taste. Gorget, gusset, basinet, cuirass, gauntlet, sleeve—every last one of these components, in these women’s eyes, was a perfectly usable surface to stitch on scraps of bright, fluttering color.
source_text: The piece was the well-known play of Saint George, and all who were behind the scenes assisted in the preparations, including the women of each household. Without the co-operation of sisters and sweethearts the dresses were likely to be a failure; but on the other hand, this class of assistance was not without its drawbacks. The girls could never be brought to respect tradition in designing and decorating the armour; they insisted on attaching loops and bows of silk and velvet in any situation pleasing to their taste. Gorget, gusset, basinet, cuirass, gauntlet, sleeve, all alike in the view of these feminine eyes were practicable spaces whereon to sew scraps of fluttering colour.

text: Thorne, when he made his way back to the mill pond’s mossy edge, noticed the narrow upper buoy hatches had been pulled free from their slots. One of the light wooden floats lay discarded in the clover, and he tucked it under one arm, his brass lantern swinging from his opposite hand, before wading into the pond’s shallow lower end just as Elias had done before him. The moment the water rose past his chest he stretched himself flat across the hatch; buoyed by the dense wood, he could stay adrift as long as he liked, holding the lantern high above the ripples with his free hand to keep the flame from sputtering out. Kicking his feet to propel himself, he circled the pond again and again, each loop carrying him up one of the narrow feeder creeks at the back before drifting back down with the main current through the pond’s center.
source_text: Diggory, having returned to the brink of the pool, observed that the small upper hatches or floats were withdrawn.
