He found one of these lying upon the grass, and taking it under one arm, and with his lantern in his hand, entered at the bottom of the pool as Clym had done. As soon as he began to be in deep water he flung himself across the hatch; thus supported he was able to keep afloat as long as he chose, holding the lantern aloft with his disengaged hand. Propelled by his feet, he steered round and round the pool, ascending each time by one of the back streams and descending in the middle of the current.

text: What had brought a woman of her temperament to settle on wind-scoured Bramble Moor? Brineport was her birthplace, a gilded coastal holiday spot wildly popular with fashionable visitors at the time. Her father had been the bandmaster of an infantry regiment posted to the town, a Sardinian by birth and a remarkably gifted musician, who first met his future wife when the young woman traveled there on holiday with her own father, a decorated naval captain from a well-respected gentry line. The match hardly aligned with the old man’s wishes, for the bandmaster’s pockets were as empty as his profession was deemed unfit for a gentleman’s daughter. But the musician did everything in his power to prove his worth: he adopted his wife’s family name, made England his permanent home, poured endless care into his daughter’s education (expenses the grandfather covered in full), and thrived as the town’s most in-demand local musician, until his wife’s sudden death broke his spirit. He abandoned his work, turned to drink, and passed away not long after. The girl was left to her grandfather’s care, and three broken ribs sustained in a violent shipwreck had led the old seaman to retire to this airy, elevated cottage on Bramble Moor. He’d settled on the spot because the house could be rented for next to nothing, and the faint blue smudge straddling the horizon between the distant hills, visible straight from the cottage door, was long rumored to be the distant stretch of the English Channel. She hated the shift, felt as if she’d been cast into exile from every part of the world she knew, but she had no choice but to abide there.
source_text: Why did a woman of this sort live on Egdon Heath? Budmouth was her native place, a fashionable seaside resort at that date. She was the daughter of the bandmaster of a regiment which had been quartered there—a Corfiote by birth, and a fine musician—who met his future wife during her trip thither with her father the captain, a man of good family. The marriage was scarcely in accord with the old man’s wishes, for the bandmaster’s pockets were as light as his occupation. But the musician did his best; adopted his wife’s name, made England permanently his home, took great trouble with his child’s education, the expenses of which were defrayed by the grandfather, and throve as the chief local musician till her mother’s death, when he left off thriving, drank, and died also. The girl was left to the care of her grandfather, who, since three of his ribs became broken in a shipwreck, had lived in this airy perch on Egdon, a spot which had taken his fancy because the house was to be had for next to nothing, and because a remote blue tinge on the horizon between the hills, visible from the cottage door, was traditionally believed to be the English Channel. She hated the change; she felt like one banished; but here she was forced to abide.

text: And so, when his name cropped up unprompted among nearby smallholders, whoever was listening would ask, “Oh, Kael Thorne—what’s he gotten up to these days?” When the first question that springs to mind about someone is what he’s been up to, there’s an unspoken assumption he’s likely drifting through life the same as most of us, with no grand purpose to speak of. There’s a vague hunch he’s wandered into some out-of-the-ordinary path, for better or for worse. Everyone outwardly wishes he’s thriving. Privately, most are convinced he’s making a thorough hash of things. Half a dozen well-off peddlers who regularly stopped at the Crooked Hearth on their cart routes loved to chew over this very topic. Even though they were not native to Bramble Moor, they could scarce keep from returning to it as they puffed on their long clay pipes and stared out the window at the rolling moorland. Kael had been so tied to the moor through all his boyhood that scarcely anyone could set eyes on its rolling slopes without him coming to mind. And so the conversation circled back time and again: if he was building a fortune and a reputation for himself, all the better for his sake; if he was cutting a tragic, ruined figure out in the wider world, all the better for a good story.
source_text: Hence, when his name was casually mentioned by neighbouring yeomen, the listener said, “Ah, Clym Yeobright—what is he doing now?” When the instinctive question about a person is, What is he doing? it is felt that he will be found to be, like most of us, doing nothing in particular. There is an indefinite sense that he must be invading some region of singularity, good or bad. The devout hope is that he is doing well. The secret faith is that he is making a mess of it. Half a dozen comfortable market-men, who were habitual callers at the Quiet Woman as they passed by in their carts, were partial to the topic. In fact, though they were not Egdon men, they could hardly avoid it while they sucked their long clay tubes and regarded the heath through the window. Clym had been so inwoven with the heath in his boyhood that hardly anybody could look upon it without thinking of him. So the subject recurred: if he were making a fortune and a name, so much the better for him; if he were making a tragical figure in the world, so much the better for a narrative.

text: That was the end of their conversation, and Eustacia turned to leave him. Clym stood watching as she retreated toward the sinking sun, its luminous rays wrapping tighter around her figure the farther she drifted, until the soft rustle of her skirts brushing through newly sprouted sedge and grass faded completely. Even as he gazed, the dead flatness of the landscape pressed down on him, though he was still sharply aware of the beauty of that unspoiled early summer green that clothed even the scrawniest grass blade for the time being. There was an oppressive weight to its unbroken horizontality that called too vividly to mind the rough arena of ordinary life; it left him with the bare, unshakable sense that he held no higher place, no advantage, over any single living thing beneath the wide sun.
source_text: This was the end of their talk, and Eustacia left him. Clym watched her as she retired towards the sun. The luminous rays wrapped her up with her increasing distance, and the rustle of her dress over the sprouting sedge and grass died away. As he watched, the dead flat of the scenery overpowered him, though he was fully alive to the beauty of that untarnished early summer green which was worn for the nonce by the poorest blade. There was something in its oppressive horizontality which too much reminded him of the arena of life; it gave him a sense of bare equality with, and no superiority to, a single living thing under the sun.

text: Kael curled his fingers around the hand she had already slipped free of its glove to offer him—bare palm to bare palm was their preferred way of strolling, after all—and guided her through the thick drifts of ferns. They made a strikingly tender portrait of love in its full, glowing bloom as they picked their way down the valley that soft late afternoon, the sun sinking low on their right side, stretching their thin, ghostly silhouettes as high as white poplars to reach far across the gorse and fern fronds at their feet. Liora held her head tilted back in a dreamy, unburdened arc, a bright, giddy, almost luxurious edge of triumph lighting her eyes: she had won this man entirely on her own merit, a partner who matched her perfectly in intellect, bearing, and years. As for the young man, the faint pallor he had carried home with him from Lyon, and the first faint lines of weariness and deep thinking etched into his face, were far less visible than they had been in the first weeks of his return; the hearty, vigorous ruggedness that was his birthright had slowly reclaimed much of its old hold on his features. They meandered further on until they came to the lowermost edge of the heath, where the ground grew soft and waterlogged, bleeding seamlessly into the wide, boggy moor beyond.
source_text: Clym took the hand which was already bared for him—it was a favourite way with them to walk bare hand in bare hand—and led her through the ferns. They formed a very comely picture of love at full flush, as they walked along the valley that late afternoon, the sun sloping down on their right, and throwing their thin spectral shadows, tall as poplar trees, far out across the furze and fern. Eustacia went with her head thrown back fancifully, a certain glad and voluptuous air of triumph pervading her eyes at having won by her own unaided self a man who was her perfect complement in attainment, appearance, and age. On the young man’s part, the paleness of face which he had brought with him from Paris, and the incipient marks of time and thought, were less perceptible than when he returned, the healthful and energetic sturdiness which was his by nature having partially recovered its original proportions. They wandered onward till they reached the nether margin of the heath, where it became marshy and merged in moorland.

text: I won’t lie, there’s a little bit you’d have to do to keep her entertained, but folks who laze around all day might call that work, while anyone who actually earns their keep would say it’s more like fun. Just think about the company you’d keep and the kind of life you’d lead, miss! All the bright, exciting goings-on you’d get to witness, and the fine gentleman you could end up marrying. My uncle’s the one tasked with finding a reliable young woman from the countryside for the role, since she can’t stand girls who grew up in town.
source_text: “I confess there would be a trifle to do in the way of amusing her; but though idle people might call it work, working people would call it play. Think of the company and the life you’d lead, miss; the gaiety you’d see, and the gentleman you’d marry. My uncle is to inquire for a trustworthy young lady from the country, as she don’t like town girls.”

text: The next day, she went about her routine exactly as usual, keeping up her habit of taking walks across the heath with no companion but little Eustacia. The toddler was at that wobbly, uncoordinated age where it feels like even they can’t decide if they’re meant to move through the world on their hands or their feet, so they wind up in all sorts of awkward, sometimes mildly painful tangles trying to do both. Thomasin found it deeply lovely, once she’d carried the child to some quiet, isolated spot, to let her get in a little unobserved practice on the soft green turf and mounded shepherd’s thyme, which made for a gentle, springy mat to catch her every time she lost her balance and went tumbling headfirst.
source_text: Next day she went her ways as usual, and continued her custom of walking in the heath with no other companion than little Eustacia, now of the age when it is a matter of doubt with such characters whether they are intended to walk through the world on their hands or on their feet; so that they get into painful complications by trying both. It was very pleasant to Thomasin, when she had carried the child to some lonely place, to give her a little private practice on the green turf and shepherd’s-thyme, which formed a soft mat to fall headlong upon them when equilibrium was lost.

text: She stepped over the threshold indoors, her cheeks flushed bright, and faint traces of her recent flurry of excitement still glinting in her eyes. Clym Yeobright looked up, astonished; he had never caught even a hint of this kind of unguarded, heightened state in her before. She slipped past him, ready to climb the stairs without drawing notice, but Clym was so thoroughly concerned that he rose immediately and followed close at her heels.
source_text: She came indoors with her face flushed, and her eyes still showing traces of her recent excitement. Yeobright looked up astonished; he had never seen her in any way approaching to that state before. She passed him by, and would have gone upstairs unnoticed, but Clym was so concerned that he immediately followed her.

text: I was perched on the churchyard boundary wall when the pair of them turned up, one coming from one end of the lane, the other from the opposite side; Miss Vye was strolling around the grounds not far off, pausing to study the inscriptions on the headstones. As soon as the two had stepped inside the church, I made my way over to the door, struck by a sudden urge to see what was happening inside, given how well I knew her. I pulled off my boots first because their hard soles would make far too much noise on the old floorboards, then crept up the stairs to the gallery. When I reached the top, I saw the parson and the parish clerk were already there.
source_text: “I was sitting on the churchyard wall when they came up, one from one way, the other from the other; and Miss Vye was walking thereabouts, looking at the headstones. As soon as they had gone in I went to the door, feeling I should like to see it, as I knew her so well. I pulled off my boots because they were so noisy, and went up into the gallery. I saw then that the parson and clerk were already there.”

text: “Oh, I was properly riled at her right at that moment. She struck me as stubborn to a fault, and once I realized you didn’t cross her thoughts at all, I swore she’d never cross yours either, not if I could help it. I reminded myself she was only my niece, at the end of the day; I told her she could marry whomever she liked, but that I’d take no interest in the match, and certainly wouldn’t pester you about it, either.”
source_text: “Well, I felt vexed with her just then. She seemed to me to be obstinate; and when I found that you were nothing in her mind I vowed that she should be nothing in yours. I felt that she was only my niece after all; I told her she might marry, but that I should take no interest in it, and should not bother you about it either.”

text: The window that had cast candlelight drifting up the valley to the group huddled around the bonfire was completely uncurtained, but its sill sat so high that anyone passing by on foot outside couldn’t crane far enough to peer over it into the room. A sprawling shadow, within which faint traces of a man’s figure could be just barely made out, blotted out fully half the ceiling.
source_text: The window, whence the candlelight had shone up the vale to the eyes of the bonfire group, was uncurtained, but the sill lay too high for a pedestrian on the outside to look over it into the room. A vast shadow, in which could be dimly traced portions of a masculine contour, blotted half the ceiling.

text: She pressed further down the path, which was barely a sliver cutting through the heath’s thick, unruly mass of vegetation, so the reddleman had to follow directly in the prints her boots left behind. When she spotted the captain standing on the raised earth bank up ahead, scanning the entire horizon with his telescope from far off, she told Venn to wait right where he was before heading into the house on her own.
source_text: She went on, and as the path was an infinitely small parting in the shaggy locks of the heath, the reddleman followed exactly in her trail. She saw from a distance that the captain was on the bank sweeping the horizon with his telescope; and bidding Venn to wait where he stood she entered the house alone.

text: Bring her to memory—hold the thought of her, see how all goodness shone through every contour of her face! Most women, even at the smallest slight, let a spark of cruelty peek through a twist of the lip or a hard set to the jaw, but her? Even when she was furious enough to shake, there was never a trace of spite in her expression. Her temper flared fast, but it faded just as quickly, and for all her stiff pride, there was a child’s soft humility beneath it all. What good did that do her? What did you ever care? You scorned her just as she was finally learning to care for you. Oh! Could you not see what was right in front of you, that you had to lay a blight on my life, and wring pain and death out of her, with that cruel, senseless act? What was the name of the man you were sneaking around with, the one who made you add slighting her to all the wrongs you’ve done me? Was it Kael? Was it poor Mara’s husband? Good God, what rot you’ve sunk to. Got nothing to say now, do you? It’s only natural, after that fine little scheme of yours was laid bare…. Elowen, did no soft memory of your own mother ever cross your mind, to make you treat mine gently when she was so worn and weary? Did not a single shred of mercy move you when she turned and walked away? Think of what a priceless chance you threw away then, to start over with kindness and truth. Why did you not throw him out the door, let her come in, and say I will be a faithful wife, a better woman from this day on? If I had ordered you to snuff out our last tiny, guttering chance at joy in this life, you could not have followed the order more perfectly. Well, she’s at rest now; even if you have a hundred suitors lining your threshold, neither you nor any of them can ever wound her again.
source_text: “Call her to mind—think of her—what goodness there was in her—it showed in every line of her face! Most women, even when but slightly annoyed, show a flicker of evil in some curl of the mouth or some corner of the cheek; but as for her, never in her angriest moments was there anything malicious in her look. She was angered quickly, but she forgave just as readily, and underneath her pride there was the meekness of a child. What came of it?—what cared you? You hated her just as she was learning to love you. O! couldn’t you see what was best for you, but must bring a curse upon me, and agony and death upon her, by doing that cruel deed! What was the fellow’s name who was keeping you company and causing you to add cruelty to her to your wrong to me? Was it Wildeve? Was it poor Thomasin’s husband? Heaven, what wickedness! Lost your voice, have you? It is natural after detection of that most noble trick.... Eustacia, didn’t any tender thought of your own mother lead you to think of being gentle to mine at such a time of weariness? Did not one grain of pity enter your heart as she turned away? Think what a vast opportunity was then lost of beginning a forgiving and honest course. Why did not you kick him out, and let her in, and say I’ll be an honest wife and a noble woman from this hour? Had I told you to go and quench eternally our last flickering chance of happiness here you could have done no worse. Well, she’s asleep now; and have you a hundred gallants, neither they nor you can insult her any more.”

text: Once he left the room, muffled sounds of him moving around the kitchen drifted to her now and then, and she lost track of where she was entirely; for a moment she had to make a conscious effort to work out what those noises even were. She was so wrapped up in her own wandering thoughts that the stretch of time before he returned felt barely any time at all. He stepped back through the door carrying a tray loaded with steaming tea and toast, even though it was almost lunchtime.
source_text: When he had gone, and the dull sounds occasionally reached her ears of his movements in the kitchen, she forgot where she was, and had for a moment to consider by an effort what the sounds meant. After an interval which seemed short to her whose thoughts were elsewhere, he came in with a tray on which steamed tea and toast, though it was nearly lunch-time.

text: It wasn’t long before Venn was told he wasn’t needed for any further duties, so he headed for the door, still barely able to wrap his head around the bizarre, devastating turn that had struck the family he cared so deeply about. Thomasin was almost certainly going to crumble under the weight of this sudden, crushing event. The steady, level-headed Mrs. Yeobright was gone now, no longer there to hold the soft-spoken young woman up through this agonizing trial; and regardless of what a detached onlooker might make of her losing a husband like Wildeve, there was no question that right now she was reeling, horrified by the shock. As for him, he had no special claim to push his way in to sit with her and offer comfort, so he couldn’t see any point in lingering any longer in a house where he was still nothing more than an outsider.
source_text: Venn soon felt himself relieved from further attendance, and went to the door, scarcely able yet to realize the strange catastrophe that had befallen the family in which he took so great an interest. Thomasin surely would be broken down by the sudden and overwhelming nature of this event. No firm and sensible Mrs. Yeobright lived now to support the gentle girl through the ordeal; and, whatever an unimpassioned spectator might think of her loss of such a husband as Wildeve, there could be no doubt that for the moment she was distracted and horrified by the blow. As for himself, not being privileged to go to her and comfort her, he saw no reason for waiting longer in a house where he remained only as a stranger.

text: Seven days slipped by, Elowen never once stepping past her doorstep. Then she wandered out to the walled kitchen yard, and lifted her grandfather’s brass field glass to her eye, a small ritual she’d kept up long before she had wed. One afternoon, she spotted where the main lane cut through the far dale, a creaking, overloaded wagon trundling slowly past, stacked high with domestic furnishings. She held the glass to her eye again and again, heat pricking her throat as she realized every piece belonged to her. As dusk fell, her grandfather stepped inside, passing along the gossip that Kael had moved that same day from the cottage at Cleeve Hollow back to his family’s old homestead at Mossfoot.
source_text: A week passed, Eustacia never going out of the house. Then she walked into the enclosed plot and looked through her grandfather’s spyglass, as she had been in the habit of doing before her marriage. One day she saw, at a place where the highroad crossed the distant valley, a heavily laden wagon passing along. It was piled with household furniture. She looked again and again, and recognized it to be her own. In the evening her grandfather came indoors with a rumour that Yeobright had removed that day from Alderworth to the old house at Blooms-End.

text: The reddleman made his home the open road just like a gypsy, yet he held gypsies in open scorn. His fortunes were roughly on par with those of itinerant basket and mat weavers, but he had no affiliation with their trade whatsoever. He came from a far more respectable upbringing than the cattle drovers who crossed his path again and again on his travels, yet the men never gave him more than a cursory nod as they passed. The goods he carried were worth far more than the wares of common peddlers, but the peddlers refused to acknowledge that, staring straight ahead as they trundled past his cart. His skin was stained such an odd, unearthly shade of red that even carnival roundabout workers and waxwork show operators looked like proper gentlemen next to him, yet he wrote them off as disreputable company and kept his distance. The reddleman moved constantly among these drifters, squatters, and roadside folk, yet he never belonged to their ranks. The nature of his trade fostered this isolation, and more often than not, he was seen for exactly what he was: a man set apart.
source_text: The reddleman lived like a gipsy; but gipsies he scorned. He was about as thriving as travelling basket and mat makers; but he had nothing to do with them. He was more decently born and brought up than the cattledrovers who passed and repassed him in his wanderings; but they merely nodded to him. His stock was more valuable than that of pedlars; but they did not think so, and passed his cart with eyes straight ahead. He was such an unnatural colour to look at that the men of roundabouts and waxwork shows seemed gentlemen beside him; but he considered them low company, and remained aloof. Among all these squatters and folks of the road the reddleman continually found himself; yet he was not of them. His occupation tended to isolate him, and isolated he was mostly seen to be.

text: Aunt and niece stood side by side in the bedroom where the bride was getting ready. Wherever sunlight caught Thomasin’s hair— which she had always worn braided—it turned the strands to a glinting, mirror-like surface. Her braiding followed a small, personal rule tied to the day’s importance: the more meaningful the occasion, the more strands she wove into the plait. On regular workdays, she used three strands; on typical Sundays, four. For maypole festivals, gypsy outings, and other similar celebrations, she wove five strands together. Years earlier, she had said she would braid her hair with seven strands on her wedding day. That was exactly what she’d done today.
source_text: Aunt and niece stood together in the bedroom where the bride was dressing. The sun, where it could catch it, made a mirror of Thomasin’s hair, which she always wore braided. It was braided according to a calendar system—the more important the day the more numerous the strands in the braid. On ordinary working-days she braided it in threes; on ordinary Sundays in fours; at Maypolings, gipsyings, and the like, she braided it in fives. Years ago she had said that when she married she would braid it in sevens. She had braided it in sevens today.

text: He shook his head gently at her. “I know you too well, my Eustacia, far too well. There isn’t a note of your nature that I cannot place; that hot, impulsive little bosom of yours could never play such a cold, unfeeling trick to save its own life. I spotted a woman on Rainbarrow at dusk, looking down toward my house. I think I drew the truth of who you were out before you could draw whatever you wanted out of me.”
source_text: He shook his head at her. “I know you too well, my Eustacia; I know you too well. There isn’t a note in you which I don’t know; and that hot little bosom couldn’t play such a cold-blooded trick to save its life. I saw a woman on Rainbarrow at dusk looking down towards my house. I think I drew out you before you drew out me.”

text: “I’ll walk a short stretch with you,” said Clym. Amid the din of lively merriment drifting up from below, they made their way down. The path leading to the front entrance brought them close beside a small side window, where warm candlelight streamed out over the leaves of the surrounding shrubs. Screened from general view by the thick bushes, the window had been left unblinded, so anyone tucked into that secluded little nook could see almost everything unfolding in the room where the wedding guests were gathered, save for what was obscured by the panes’ aged, green-tinted glass.
source_text: “I will go a little way with you,” said Clym. And amid the noise of merriment from below they descended. Their path to the front led them close to a little side window, whence the rays of candles streamed across the shrubs. The window, being screened from general observation by the bushes, had been left unblinded, so that a person in this private nook could see all that was going on within the room which contained the wedding guests, except in so far as vision was hindered by the green antiquity of the panes.

text: All the hauled bundles were stacked atop one another, forming a furze pyramid thirty feet in circumference that sat perched on the crown of the tumulus, a landmark called Rainbarrow known to locals for miles in every direction. A few of the group fumbled with matches and picked out the driest sprigs of furze to use as tinder, while others worked to pry loose the bramble ties holding the faggots tightly bound. Still others, as this prep work unfolded, lifted their gazes to scan the wide sweep of countryside laid out before their elevated vantage point, most of it already swallowed by deepening shade. Down in the heath’s hollows, nothing save its own rugged, untamed face was ever visible no matter the hour of day, but this high point looked out over a horizon enclosing a vast stretch of land that reached well past the bounds of the heath itself in many directions. None of those far-off features were discernible now, but the entire expanse made its presence felt as a dim, vague stretch of endless remoteness.
source_text: The loads were all laid together, and a pyramid of furze thirty feet in circumference now occupied the crown of the tumulus, which was known as Rainbarrow for many miles round. Some made themselves busy with matches, and in selecting the driest tufts of furze, others in loosening the bramble bonds which held the faggots together. Others, again, while this was in progress, lifted their eyes and swept the vast expanse of country commanded by their position, now lying nearly obliterated by shade. In the valleys of the heath nothing save its own wild face was visible at any time of day; but this spot commanded a horizon enclosing a tract of far extent, and in many cases lying beyond the heath country. None of its features could be seen now, but the whole made itself felt as a vague stretch of remoteness.

text: Until this very moment, he’d held fast to his decision not to ask her to come back. At the same time, the harshness he’d shown her had blunted the sharp edge of his grief over his mother, and stirred up the old, soft concern he used to feel for the woman who’d taken his mother’s place in his life. Cruel feelings breed cruel treatment, and that treatment in turn snuffs out the very sentiments that sparked it to begin with. The longer he turned the whole mess over in his mind, the more his hard edge softened. Still, he couldn’t bring himself to see his wife as some blameless victim in distress, even as he found himself wondering if he’d given her nearly enough time—if he hadn’t caught her far too abruptly that gray, heavy morning.
source_text: Up to this hour he had persevered in his resolve not to invite her back. At the same time the severity with which he had treated her lulled the sharpness of his regret for his mother, and awoke some of his old solicitude for his mother’s supplanter. Harsh feelings produce harsh usage, and this by reaction quenches the sentiments that gave it birth. The more he reflected the more he softened. But to look upon his wife as innocence in distress was impossible, though he could ask himself whether he had given her quite time enough—if he had not come a little too suddenly upon her on that sombre morning.

text: Crimson-dye peddlers of the old stamp are scarcely ever spotted now. Ever since rail lines spread across the Cotswolds, local farmers have found ways to get by without these devilish, red-stained wayfarers; the vivid ochre dye shepherds once relied on heavily to mark sheep ahead of market days now reaches them through entirely different supply chains. Even the few who still cling to the trade have shed the romantic edge that once defined their way of life, back when plying their craft meant regular treks to the quarry where the raw pigment was dug, months of steady camping out on the roads save for the harshest midwinter freezes, and wanderings that touched no fewer than a hundred separate farmsteads across the region. For all their nomadic, gypsy-like roaming back then, they’d always maintained a quiet respectability, guaranteed by the fat, reliably full purse their steady trade never failed to provide.
source_text: Reddlemen of the old school are now but seldom seen. Since the introduction of railways Wessex farmers have managed to do without these Mephistophelian visitants, and the bright pigment so largely used by shepherds in preparing sheep for the fair is obtained by other routes. Even those who yet survive are losing the poetry of existence which characterized them when the pursuit of the trade meant periodical journeys to the pit whence the material was dug, a regular camping out from month to month, except in the depth of winter, a peregrination among farms which could be counted by the hundred, and in spite of this Arab existence the preservation of that respectability which is insured by the never-failing production of a well-lined purse.

text: Why she stood so perfectly motionless, the fixed center of this wide expanse of heathland, was no less mysterious. Her uncanny stillness, her stark, unmissable solitude, the way she paid no heed to the creeping dark, signaled among other things that she felt not a flicker of fear. This stretch of country had not changed at all from its foreboding ancient state, the same condition that made Caesar eager every year to flee its oppressive gloom before the autumnal equinox arrived. It was the sort of landscape and weather that leads travelers from the South to describe our island as Homer’s fabled Cimmerian land, a place that, on its face, was hardly welcoming to women.
source_text: Her reason for standing so dead still as the pivot of this circle of heath-country was just as obscure. Her extraordinary fixity, her conspicuous loneliness, her heedlessness of night, betokened among other things an utter absence of fear. A tract of country unaltered from that sinister condition which made Cæsar anxious every year to get clear of its glooms before the autumnal equinox, a kind of landscape and weather which leads travellers from the South to describe our island as Homer’s Cimmerian land, was not, on the face of it, friendly to women.

text: Any onlooker lingering at the spot would have felt their heart twist for her, not first for the lash of the cold rain on her skin or her utter solitude, broken only by the decayed remains tucked inside the ancient barrow at her back; but for the slow, unsteady sway of her frame, a physical echo of the agony churning beneath her skin. A suffocating weight of despair hung off her, impossible to miss even through the fog of falling rain. Rain pattered off the edge of her oilskin umbrella to her thick wool cloak, dripped from her cloak to the gorse and heather at her feet, seeped from the heather down into the dark loam below; and between those soft, pattering beats came the faint, hitching sound of her sobs, the damp grief of the storm mirrored in the streaks of tears carving tracks through the rain on her cheeks. The very wings of her spirit had been snapped by the cold, unyielding cruelty of every turn her life had taken lately; even if she’d suddenly found herself on the clear road to Brineport, stepping onto a steam packet bound for some distant shore across the channel, she would not have felt a scrap lighter, so heavy and unforgiving was the weight of the wrongs that had been done to her. She spoke fragments of sentences out loud to the empty air. When a woman in such straits, who is neither elderly, hard of hearing, unwell in the mind, nor given to fanciful displays, weeps and murmurs to herself in the open wild, you know her suffering is beyond bearing.
source_text: Anyone who had stood by now would have pitied her, not so much on account of her exposure to weather, and isolation from all of humanity except the mouldered remains inside the tumulus; but for that other form of misery which was denoted by the slightly rocking movement that her feelings imparted to her person. Extreme unhappiness weighed visibly upon her. Between the drippings of the rain from her umbrella to her mantle, from her mantle to the heather, from the heather to the earth, very similar sounds could be heard coming from her lips; and the tearfulness of the outer scene was repeated upon her face. The wings of her soul were broken by the cruel obstructiveness of all about her; and even had she seen herself in a promising way of getting to Budmouth, entering a steamer, and sailing to some opposite port, she would have been but little more buoyant, so fearfully malignant were other things. She uttered words aloud. When a woman in such a situation, neither old, deaf, crazed, nor whimsical, takes upon herself to sob and soliloquize aloud there is something grievous the matter.

text: Ever since Yeobright had reappeared, he’d carried himself so subdued and spoken so few words that Venn assumed he’d come to terms with the situation. The real state of his mind only revealed itself once they’d stepped out of the room and were standing on the landing. He tilted his head toward the chamber where Eustacia lay, a wild, unsteady smile tugging at his mouth, and said, “She’s the second woman I’ve killed this year. I was largely responsible for my mother’s death, and I’m the main cause of hers.”
source_text: Yeobright’s manner had been so quiet, he had uttered so few syllables since his reappearance, that Venn imagined him resigned. It was only when they had left the room and stood upon the landing that the true state of his mind was apparent. Here he said, with a wild smile, inclining his head towards the chamber in which Eustacia lay, “She is the second woman I have killed this year. I was a great cause of my mother’s death, and I am the chief cause of hers.”

text: Mrs. Yeobright was nearly felled by the toll of her physical efforts and raw emotional strain, yet she pressed on, shuffling forward in tiny bursts, pausing for long stretches between each small advance. The sun had drifted far southwest by now, hanging directly in her line of sight like a ruthless arsonist, a flaming brand clutched in one hand, poised to burn her to ash. When the boy had vanished, every last glimmer of visible life drained from the surrounding landscape, though the rough, stuttering chirps of male grasshoppers rising from every furze tuft made clear that even as all larger creatures lay listless and drained, a hidden world of insects hummed with unflagging, full-bodied vitality.
source_text: Mrs. Yeobright’s exertions, physical and emotional, had well-nigh prostrated her; but she continued to creep along in short stages with long breaks between. The sun had now got far to the west of south and stood directly in her face, like some merciless incendiary, brand in hand, waiting to consume her. With the departure of the boy all visible animation disappeared from the landscape, though the intermittent husky notes of the male grasshoppers from every tuft of furze were enough to show that amid the prostration of the larger animal species an unseen insect world was busy in all the fullness of life.

text: Once she had vanished entirely from view, Charley, his eyes heavy with unease, moved slowly away from the stable doorway, then picked a different spot along the bank to peer over its edge. Eustacia was leaning against the bank’s outer face, her face buried in her hands, her head pressed into the dewy heather that bristled along the slope’s outer edge. She seemed completely unconcerned that her bonnet, hair, and clothing were growing damp and rumpled from the moisture seeping out of that cold, scratchy makeshift pillow. It was obvious something was deeply amiss.
source_text: When she had disappeared Charley, with misgiving in his eyes, slowly came from the stable door, and going to another point in the bank he looked over. Eustacia was leaning against it on the outside, her face covered with her hands, and her head pressing the dewy heather which bearded the bank’s outer side. She appeared to be utterly indifferent to the circumstance that her bonnet, hair, and garments were becoming wet and disarranged by the moisture of her cold, harsh pillow. Clearly something was wrong.

text: Elowen shifted where she stood beneath the gnarled oak bough, her gaze settling on her aunt across the grass. “Now, listen closely to what I say,” she told her, her usually soft, wispy tone hardening with a quiet resolve that had nothing to do with brute physical force. “Don’t breathe a word of this to Kael. If he works out on his own that I’m no longer fit to call myself his cousin, so be it. But because he cared for me once, we won’t pain him by laying all my trouble on him before we have to. I know every idle tongue in the parish is chattering about this already, but none of those gossips will dare bring it up to his face for the first few days. The fact that he’s always been so close to me is exactly what will keep the story from reaching him any sooner. If I haven’t found a way to shield myself from all the sneers and whispers in a week or two, I’ll tell him myself.”
source_text: Thomasin turned and regarded her aunt from the tree. “Now, hearken to me,” she said, her delicate voice expanding into firmness by a force which was other than physical. “Tell him nothing. If he finds out that I am not worthy to be his cousin, let him. But, since he loved me once, we will not pain him by telling him my trouble too soon. The air is full of the story, I know; but gossips will not dare to speak of it to him for the first few days. His closeness to me is the very thing that will hinder the tale from reaching him early. If I am not made safe from sneers in a week or two I will tell him myself.”

text: A couple of days after the funds arrived, Lila had sent a short note to her Aunt Elspeth to thank her for the gift. She’d been startled by the size of the sum, but since no specific amount had ever been discussed between them, she chalked the excess up to her late uncle’s habitual generosity. Her aunt had strictly charged her to say nothing of the gift to her husband, and Kael, as was only natural, had never mustered the courage to mention a single detail of the strange midnight encounter on the moor to his wife. Toby’s own fear, in the same vein, had tied his tongue regarding the part he’d played in that whole affair; clinging to the hope that the money had somehow made its way to its proper destination, he only asserted as much when asked, never offering any further details.
source_text: A day or two after the receipt of the money Thomasin had sent a note to her aunt to thank her. She had been surprised at the largeness of the amount; but as no sum had ever been mentioned she set that down to her late uncle’s generosity. She had been strictly charged by her aunt to say nothing to her husband of this gift; and Wildeve, as was natural enough, had not brought himself to mention to his wife a single particular of the midnight scene in the heath. Christian’s terror, in like manner, had tied his tongue on the share he took in that proceeding; and hoping that by some means or other the money had gone to its proper destination, he simply asserted as much, without giving details.

text: The comment greatly amused Mr. Sowerberry, as it naturally would, and he laughed for a long, unbroken stretch. “There, there, Mr. Bumble,” he said once he’d recovered his breath, “there’s no getting around the fact that ever since the new feeding regimen went into place, coffins do run a fair bit narrower and shallower than they did in years past; but a man has to turn a profit, Mr. Bumble. Properly cured lumber is terribly costly these days, sir, and all the iron handles have to be shipped by canal all the way from Birmingham.”
source_text: Mr. Sowerberry was much tickled at this: as of course he ought to be; and laughed a long time without cessation. “Well, well, Mr. Bumble,” he said at length, “there’s no denying that, since the new system of feeding has come in, the coffins are something narrower and more shallow than they used to be; but we must have some profit, Mr. Bumble. Well-seasoned timber is an expensive article, sir; and all the iron handles come, by canal, from Birmingham.”

text: Ever after that day, no matter what the sky threw down, Elara lingered at the edge of the old market square for exactly two hours each afternoon. The moment the town steeple’s bell chimed two, she’d already settled on the weathered stone ledge, and when the fourth chime rang out hours later, she’d turn toward home, shoulders hunched with quiet acceptance. On days when the rain held off and the wind didn’t bite sharp enough to sting her young daughter’s cheeks, the girl came with her, tucking cold fingers into Elara’s coat pocket as they waited. On harsher, more unforgiving days, she made the trip alone. Not once, through frost or drizzle or sweltering sun, did she ever miss a single visit.
source_text: From that time, in all weathers, she waited there two hours. As the clock struck two, she was there, and at four she turned resignedly away. When it was not too wet or inclement for her child to be with her, they went together; at other times she was alone; but, she never missed a single day.

text: The joy of the English home he had chosen for himself, the unrelenting need to stay constantly active, the dizzying cascade of the era’s upheavals and crises that followed one another so quickly that this week’s events tore up the half-formed plans of the week prior, and the next week’s shifts wiped the slate clean all over again; he knew full well he had bowed to the weight of these combined circumstances: not without real unease, to be sure, but still without putting up any steady, accumulating resistance. He had watched the turn of the tides for a moment to act, only for those tides to churn and lurch until that window had closed entirely: nobles were streaming out of France along every main highway and hidden backroad, their property was being seized and destroyed, even their very names were being erased from public record. He was as aware of every last detail of this as any new governing authority in France that might impeach him for his inaction could possibly be.
source_text: The happiness of his own chosen English home, the necessity of being always actively employed, the swift changes and troubles of the time which had followed on one another so fast, that the events of this week annihilated the immature plans of last week, and the events of the week following made all new again; he knew very well, that to the force of these circumstances he had yielded:--not without disquiet, but still without continuous and accumulating resistance. That he had watched the times for a time of action, and that they had shifted and struggled until the time had gone by, and the nobility were trooping from France by every highway and byway, and their property was in course of confiscation and destruction, and their very names were blotting out, was as well known to himself as it could be to any new authority in France that might impeach him for it.

text: The words slipped out half under his breath, yet every person lingering on the porch caught them clear as a bell. Elias Thorne made no reply after that, and the faint, muffled din of chatter from inside the house drifted back to their ears, until a few minutes had passed and the soft rattle of light carriage wheels rose above it, winding down the oak-dusted slope above the farm. The clatter grew steadily louder, then cut off sharp right at the white picket gate. Elias hurried back to the front door and tugged it open; warm golden lamplight spilled out across the gravel walk, gilding Elowen Voss as she climbed the path toward them.
source_text: He said this to himself, and they all distinctly heard it. Boldwood remained silent after that, and the noise from indoors was again just audible, until, a few minutes later, light wheels could be distinguished coming down the hill. They drew nearer, and ceased at the gate. Boldwood hastened back to the door, and opened it; and the light shone upon Bathsheba coming up the path.

text: “Nope,” Tall said as he stepped over to join the huddled group. “And honestly, I think we’d be better off keeping our mouths shut. If this rumor isn’t actually true, repeating it will just rattle her and hurt her for no good reason. Even if it is true, there’s no point in forcing her to face this trouble before she has to, rushing her into distress before her time. I’m praying to God it’s all a lie, too. Henery Fray and a few of the others might run their mouths trashing her, but she’s never been anything but fair to me. She’s short-tempered and quick to snap, sure, but she’s a brave girl who’d never lie even when telling the truth would hurt her badly. I’ve got no cause to wish her any harm at all.”
source_text: “No,” said Tall, joining the group. “And I’m inclined to think we’d better keep quiet. If so be ’tis not true, ’twill flurry her, and do her much harm to repeat it; and if so be ’tis true, ’twill do no good to forestall her time o’ trouble. God send that it mid be a lie, for though Henery Fray and some of ’em do speak against her, she’s never been anything but fair to me. She’s hot and hasty, but she’s a brave girl who’ll never tell a lie however much the truth may harm her, and I’ve no cause to wish her evil.”

text: “Just a joke?” she asked, her voice thick with equal parts sorrow and disbelief. “How can you joke around when I’m this miserable and every word out of my mouth is dead serious? Be honest with me, Frank. I’m not a fool, you know that. I’m a woman, sure, and I have my silly little feminine moods sometimes, but that doesn’t mean I can’t see what’s right in front of me. Come on, treat me fairly,” she said, holding his gaze steady, open and unflinching. “I don’t want much—just basic fairness, that’s all. God, there was a time I thought I’d never settle for anything less than full, unreserved devotion from the husband I chose for myself. Now? I’d be happy with anything that stops short of outright cruelty. That’s right—the independent, spirited Bathsheba everyone knew? She’s sunk all the way to this.”
source_text: “A mere jest!” she said, in mournful astonishment. “Can you jest when I am so wretchedly in earnest? Tell me the truth, Frank. I am not a fool, you know, although I am a woman, and have my woman’s moments. Come! treat me fairly,” she said, looking honestly and fearlessly into his face. “I don’t want much; bare justice—that’s all! Ah! once I felt I could be content with nothing less than the highest homage from the husband I should choose. Now, anything short of cruelty will content me. Yes! the independent and spirited Bathsheba is come to this!”

text: “I can lay my hands on all the boy’s clothing I’ll need, at least everything besides the mummer’s costume. What would I have to pay you to lend me your things, let me take your place for an hour or two come Monday night, and never breathe a word to anyone about who I really am? Naturally, you’d have to excuse yourself from performing that night, and tell everyone that a cousin of Miss Vye’s will be stepping in to act your part. None of the other mummers have ever spoken to me in their lives, so the whole thing will be perfectly safe; even if it wasn’t, I wouldn’t care. Now, what do I need to give you to agree to this? Would half a crown be enough?”
source_text: “I can get boy’s clothes—at least all that would be wanted besides the mumming dress. What should I have to give you to lend me your things, to let me take your place for an hour or two on Monday night, and on no account to say a word about who or what I am? You would, of course, have to excuse yourself from playing that night, and to say that somebody—a cousin of Miss Vye’s—would act for you. The other mummers have never spoken to me in their lives so that it would be safe enough; and if it were not, I should not mind. Now, what must I give you to agree to this? Half a crown?”

text: Rather than staying home that night, he decided to go pay a visit to Thomasin and her husband. If the right opportunity presented itself, he would bring up the reason he and Eustacia had separated, though he intended to stay quiet about the fact that a third person had been inside his house the day his mother was turned away. If Wildeve had been there for entirely innocent reasons, he would no doubt mention that fact openly without prompting. If he’d been there with underhanded intentions, though, Wildeve—being a man of sharp, quick emotions—might very well say something that revealed just how compromised Eustacia had been by the whole ordeal.
source_text: Instead of remaining at home that night he determined to go and see Thomasin and her husband. If he found opportunity he would allude to the cause of the separation between Eustacia and himself, keeping silence, however, on the fact that there was a third person in his house when his mother was turned away. If it proved that Wildeve was innocently there he would doubtless openly mention it. If he were there with unjust intentions Wildeve, being a man of quick feeling, might possibly say something to reveal the extent to which Eustacia was compromised.

text: That night seared itself into Eustacia’s mind so deeply she never truly shook it off, packed as it was with more vivid, unshakable happenings than most nights she’d ever lived through. She had a dream that night, one more extraordinary than nearly any dream ever dreamed by anyone, from the ancient king Nebuchadnezzar all the way down to the humble Swaffham tinker of folk lore. No girl living the kind of constrained, quiet life Eustacia led had ever had a dream so layered, disorienting, and pulse-pounding as this one. It held as many winding, tangled offshoots as the Cretan labyrinth, shifted and flickered as unpredictably as the northern lights, blazed with as much rich, bright hue as a formal June parterre, and teemed with as many distinct, bustling characters as a royal coronation. To someone like Scheherazade, who’d spun a thousand and one tales of magic and intrigue, the dream would have felt almost ordinary; to a young woman fresh off touring every royal court across Europe, it might have registered as little more than mildly diverting. But for Eustacia, bounded by the small, unchanging rhythms of her daily existence, it was as wondrous a dream as could ever exist for someone in her position.
source_text: That night was an eventful one to Eustacia’s brain, and one which she hardly ever forgot. She dreamt a dream; and few human beings, from Nebuchadnezzar to the Swaffham tinker, ever dreamt a more remarkable one. Such an elaborately developed, perplexing, exciting dream was certainly never dreamed by a girl in Eustacia’s situation before. It had as many ramifications as the Cretan labyrinth, as many fluctuations as the northern lights, as much colour as a parterre in June, and was as crowded with figures as a coronation. To Queen Scheherazade the dream might have seemed not far removed from commonplace; and to a girl just returned from all the courts of Europe it might have seemed not more than interesting. But amid the circumstances of Eustacia’s life it was as wonderful as a dream could be.

text: He climbed into the van, where a cast-iron stove held a glowing, steady fire. He did not bother lighting the candle he carried, sitting straightaway on the three-legged stool to turn over every sight and sound he had encountered that related to the person he still loved dearly. A noise slipped past his lips, neither a sigh nor a sob, yet it laid bare the troubled disarray of his mind far more plainly than either ever could.
source_text: He entered the van, where there was a fire in a stove. Without lighting his candle he sat down at once on the three-legged stool, and pondered on what he had seen and heard touching that still-loved one of his. He uttered a sound which was neither sigh nor sob, but was even more indicative than either of a troubled mind.

text: He came of age with small, unlooked-for favors that smoothed his path through life. That same mischievous quirk of fate that launched Clive first as a lowly scribe, Gay as a linen draper’s assistant, Keats as a surgeon’s apprentice, and thousands of other souls down equally mismatched early paths, dispatched this wild, austere heath-born boy to a trade whose entire purpose revolved around the most unmistakeable trappings of self-indulgence and empty vainglory.
source_text: He grew up and was helped out in life. That waggery of fate which started Clive as a writing clerk, Gay as a linen-draper, Keats as a surgeon, and a thousand others in a thousand other odd ways, banished the wild and ascetic heath lad to a trade whose sole concern was with the especial symbols of self-indulgence and vainglory.

text: “The day I’m talking about,” Yeobright said, his voice rising and growing rougher with rage, “was the day you shut the door in my mother’s face and killed her. Oh, this is too much—too unforgivable!” He leaned over the bed’s footboard for a few long seconds, his back turned to her, then pushed himself upright again. “Tell me, answer me! Answer me—do you hear me?” he shouted, surging forward to fist the loose, hanging fold of her sleeve.
source_text: “The day I mean,” said Yeobright, his voice growing louder and harsher, “was the day you shut the door against my mother and killed her. O, it is too much—too bad!” He leant over the footpiece of the bedstead for a few moments, with his back towards her; then rising again—“Tell me, tell me! tell me—do you hear?” he cried, rushing up to her and seizing her by the loose folds of her sleeve.

text: Wildeve’s choice to say nothing at all about what had befallen him that day was exactly the sort of conduct guaranteed to leave a mark on a woman of Eustacia’s temperament. Such quiet, well-judged displays of tact were, after all, among his greatest strengths when interacting with women. Wildeve’s peculiarity lay in his mercurial treatment of the women he pursued: one moment he could be fiery, scolding, and bitter towards them, the next he would behave with such unmatchable, gentle poise that prior coldness seemed no rudeness at all, past slights felt like no offense, unwanted meddling came off as thoughtful care, and even harm to a woman’s reputation could be framed as an overabundance of chivalrous devotion. This was the very man whose admiration Eustacia had brushed off only that day, whose kind overtures she had barely bothered to acknowledge, and whom she had ushered out of her home through the back entrance. He held a fortune of eleven thousand pounds, had a solid professional education, and had completed his apprenticeship with a practicing civil engineer.
source_text: Wildeve’s silence that day on what had happened to him was just the kind of behaviour calculated to make an impression on such a woman. Those delicate touches of good taste were, in fact, one of the strong points in his demeanour towards the other sex. The peculiarity of Wildeve was that, while at one time passionate, upbraiding, and resentful towards a woman, at another he would treat her with such unparalleled grace as to make previous neglect appear as no discourtesy, injury as no insult, interference as a delicate attention, and the ruin of her honour as excess of chivalry. This man, whose admiration today Eustacia had disregarded, whose good wishes she had scarcely taken the trouble to accept, whom she had shown out of the house by the back door, was the possessor of eleven thousand pounds—a man of fair professional education, and one who had served his articles with a civil engineer.

text: Mrs. Halewood turned her back, the muscles of her face jumping as she fought to stifle the raw emotion bubbling up inside her. Then she cast aside all pretense of self-control, sobbing as she gasped, “Oh Lila, do you suppose he loathes me? How can he stand to wound me so deeply, when every year of my life these past decades has been lived for him alone?”
source_text: Mrs. Yeobright turned away, and her features worked in her attempt to conceal her feelings. Then she ceased to make any attempt, and said, weeping, “O Thomasin, do you think he hates me? How can he bear to grieve me so, when I have lived only for him through all these years?”

text: After what felt like an endless trudge, Eustacia finally arrived at Rainbarrow, and paused stock-still to gather her whirling thoughts. The tangled turmoil of her own mind matched the roiling disarray of the storm-wracked world around her so perfectly they might have been crafted for one another. A sharp, cold realization struck her all at once: she did not have nearly enough coin to fund the long escape she had planned. Caught up in the wild, shifting moods of the day, her unworldly, unpractical mind had never paused to consider the basic necessity of being well-supplied for such a journey. Now that the full weight of her predicament settled over her, she let out a bitter, shuddering sigh, her posture slumping until she was slowly huddling low beneath her umbrella, as if some invisible hand reaching up from inside the barrow was pulling her down into the earth. Was she fated to remain a prisoner here, after all? She had never grasped the true value of money until this very moment. Even to vanish entirely from this stretch of countryside, she needed resources. Begging Wildeve for financial aid without allowing him to accompany her was unthinkable for a woman who held even a scrap of pride in her bones; to run away as his mistress—she knew full well he loved her—would be nothing short of utter humiliation.
source_text: Eustacia at length reached Rainbarrow, and stood still there to think. Never was harmony more perfect than that between the chaos of her mind and the chaos of the world without. A sudden recollection had flashed on her this moment—she had not money enough for undertaking a long journey. Amid the fluctuating sentiments of the day her unpractical mind had not dwelt on the necessity of being well-provided, and now that she thoroughly realized the conditions she sighed bitterly and ceased to stand erect, gradually crouching down under the umbrella as if she were drawn into the Barrow by a hand from beneath. Could it be that she was to remain a captive still? Money—she had never felt its value before. Even to efface herself from the country means were required. To ask Wildeve for pecuniary aid without allowing him to accompany her was impossible to a woman with a shadow of pride left in her; to fly as his mistress—and she knew that he loved her—was of the nature of humiliation.

text: The next small, fateful nudge that would shape the course of Clym’s life came just a few days later. An old burial barrow was being excavated out on the heath, and Yeobright went to observe the work, staying away from his studies for several hours straight. Later that afternoon, Christian returned from an errand that had taken him that same direction across the moor, and Mrs. Yeobright stopped him to question him.
source_text: The next slight touch in the shaping of Clym’s destiny occurred a few days after. A barrow was opened on the heath, and Yeobright attended the operation, remaining away from his study during several hours. In the afternoon Christian returned from a journey in the same direction, and Mrs. Yeobright questioned him.

text: “Well,” said Timothy Fairway, keenly aware he owed the man some sort of complimentary remark, “getting married is a fine, proper thing, Mr. Wildeve, and the woman you’ve tied the knot with is a total gem, that’s my take on it. Aye,” he continued, raising his voice loud enough to carry through the partition to Grandfer Cantle, tilting his head toward the inner room as he spoke, “her father was as decent a man as ever walked this earth. He never hesitated to fly into a proper fury at the first hint of anything sneaky or underhanded.”
source_text: “Well,” said Timothy Fairway, feeling demands upon his praise in some form or other, “’tis a worthy thing to be married, Mr. Wildeve; and the woman you’ve got is a dimant, so says I. Yes,” he continued, to Grandfer Cantle, raising his voice so as to be heard through the partition, “her father (inclining his head towards the inner room) was as good a feller as ever lived. He always had his great indignation ready against anything underhand.”

text: It felt as though these grown men and lads had plunged straight back through millennia, dragging up an hour and a ritual that had belonged to this very stretch of earth long before any of them were born. The cold, crumbly ash of the first ancient pyres that had roared atop this crest still lay unmolested, sealed in the earthen barrow pressed beneath their boots. The glow of funeral bonfires lit here untold generations before had spilled over the rolling lowlands exactly as these flames did that very night. Ritual bonfires honoring Thunor and Woden had blazed on the same patch of ground in their turn, burning bright before sputtering out when their eras passed. In truth, most local folk know full well that the bright bonfires the moor dwellers were celebrating with that night trace an unbroken line back to the tangled rites of the Druids and old Saxon customs, rather than springing from the widespread annual tradition marking the foiling of the Gunpowder Treason.
source_text: It was as if these men and boys had suddenly dived into past ages, and fetched therefrom an hour and deed which had before been familiar with this spot. The ashes of the original British pyre which blazed from that summit lay fresh and undisturbed in the barrow beneath their tread. The flames from funeral piles long ago kindled there had shone down upon the lowlands as these were shining now. Festival fires to Thor and Woden had followed on the same ground and duly had their day. Indeed, it is pretty well known that such blazes as this the heathmen were now enjoying are rather the lineal descendants from jumbled Druidical rites and Saxon ceremonies than the invention of popular feeling about Gunpowder Plot.

text: Venn gave a respectful bow of farewell, then trundled back to the spot he’d been stationed earlier, where the side road leading from Mistover connected to the main highway. He stood rooted there for almost half an hour, until a second set of lights drifted into view, winding down the hill. It was the captain’s odd, old-fashioned wheeled rig that no one could quite put a name to; Thomasin was the only passenger inside, and Charley was at the wheel.
source_text: Venn made a farewell obeisance, and walked back to his former position, where the byroad from Mistover joined the highway. Here he remained fixed for nearly half an hour, and then another pair of lights came down the hill. It was the old-fashioned wheeled nondescript belonging to the captain, and Thomasin sat in it alone, driven by Charley.

text: He made no detour to the small cottage tucked off the path, instead pressing onward to a grassy knoll where he could look out across the entire stretch of countryside that had once been Liora’s home. As he lingered there, gazing out at the landscape bleeding into twilight, a figure drifted up the slope behind him. Kael could only make out his hazy outline in the fading glow, and would have let him pass without a word, if the passerby—who was Joren—had not spotted the younger man first and called out a greeting.
source_text: He did not turn aside to the cottage, but pushed on to an eminence, whence he could see over the whole quarter that had once been Eustacia’s home. While he stood observing the darkening scene somebody came up. Clym, seeing him but dimly, would have let him pass silently, had not the pedestrian, who was Charley, recognized the young man and spoken to him.

text: One of those mild, sun-warmed afternoons found Eustacia walking out alone, making her way toward the site where Yeobright was working. He was busy chopping steadily at furze, a long row of bound faggots stretching downhill from where he stood a clear record of his day’s labor so far. He didn’t notice her approaching, so she came to a stop right next to him, catching the low, steady undercurrent of a song he was humming under his breath. It jolted her. The first sight of him there, a worn, struggling man earning every cent by the sweat of his brow, had brought tears to her eyes; but to hear him singing, not even pushing back against work that, however satisfying it was to him, felt deeply degrading to her as his educated, well-bred wife, cut her right to the quick. Still completely unaware she was there, he kept right on singing:
source_text: On one of these warm afternoons Eustacia walked out alone in the direction of Yeobright’s place of work. He was busily chopping away at the furze, a long row of faggots which stretched downward from his position representing the labour of the day. He did not observe her approach, and she stood close to him, and heard his undercurrent of song. It shocked her. To see him there, a poor afflicted man, earning money by the sweat of his brow, had at first moved her to tears; but to hear him sing and not at all rebel against an occupation which, however satisfactory to himself, was degrading to her, as an educated lady-wife, wounded her through. Unconscious of her presence, he still went on singing:—

text: Kaelen lingered here, taking what little shelter he could from the lashing rain beneath a tall earthen berm heaped up years prior at the edge of the lane. Where lantern glow gilded the lane’s packed surface, loose grit and tiny pebbles skittered and clacked ahead of the gale, which piled them in jagged drifts before surging out over the gorse-heath, booming through thick underbrush until it faded into the inky blackness. Only one noise cut through the racket of the storm: the deep, throaty roar of a ten-slated weir to the south, fed by a river winding through water meadows that marked the heath’s far edge in that direction.
source_text: Here Wildeve waited, slightly sheltered from the driving rain by a high bank that had been cast up at this place. Along the surface of the road where lit by the lamps the loosened gravel and small stones scudded and clicked together before the wind, which, leaving them in heaps, plunged into the heath and boomed across the bushes into darkness. Only one sound rose above this din of weather, and that was the roaring of a ten-hatch weir to the southward, from a river in the meads which formed the boundary of the heath in this direction.

“He told′ her that she′ was the joy′ of his life′, And if′ she’d con-sent′ he would make her his wife′; She could′ not refuse′ him; to church′ so they went′, Young Will was forgot′, and young Sue′ was content′; And then′ was she kiss’d′ and set down′ on his knee′, No man′ in the world′ was so lov′-ing as he′!”

text: Week after week dragged past, and no glimmer of relief cut through the heavy shadow hanging over the young married pair. Elowen was haunted by terrible, unspoken what-ifs, which she tucked tight behind her soft smiles whenever she spoke to Thorne. What if he lost his sight entirely? Or, even if he regained some vision, what if it never grew strong enough for him to take up the kind of work that would align with the life she longed for, and let them leave this isolated cottage tucked between the windswept hills? That soft, vivid dream of glittering Florence, the one they had whispered about over late-night fires, felt thinner than smoke now, unlikely to ever solidify into real life under the weight of this disaster. As each sun rose and set without any sign of his condition improving, her thoughts circled tighter and tighter around that same bitter groove. She would slip away from his side when he napped, out into the overgrown garden, and let silent, despairing sobs shake her shoulders where he could not see.
source_text: One week and another week wore on, and nothing seemed to lighten the gloom of the young couple. Dreadful imaginings occurred to Eustacia, but she carefully refrained from uttering them to her husband. Suppose he should become blind, or, at all events, never recover sufficient strength of sight to engage in an occupation which would be congenial to her feelings, and conduce to her removal from this lonely dwelling among the hills? That dream of beautiful Paris was not likely to cohere into substance in the presence of this misfortune. As day after day passed by, and he got no better, her mind ran more and more in this mournful groove, and she would go away from him into the garden and weep despairing tears.

text: Humphrey bustled through the door. “Hey, have you all heard the news? Wait, I can tell you already do. It’s such a strange quirk, isn’t it? Any time someone from Egdon steps foot in church, there’s always some weird, unforeseen mess unfolding. The last time any of us went was when our neighbor Fairway popped in that fall, remember? That was the very same day you forbade the wedding banns from being called, Mrs. Yeobright.”
source_text: In came Humphrey. “Well, have ye heard the news? But I see you have. ’Tis a very strange thing that whenever one of Egdon folk goes to church some rum job or other is sure to be doing. The last time one of us was there was when neighbour Fairway went in the fall; and that was the day you forbad the banns, Mrs. Yeobright.”

text: Down, deeper down they went, and still further below—each step’s downward drop seemed to outpace any forward progress they made. Their skirts snagged noisily on the thick furze, their shoulders brushed against the ferns, which, though dead and dried crisp, stood as upright as they had in life, since winter hadn’t yet turned harsh enough to beat them flat to the earth. Some would have called this hellish, isolated spot a foolishly reckless place for two unchaperoned women to be. But these wild, tucked-away hollows were familiar surroundings to Olly and Mrs. Yeobright no matter the time of year; and when you know something as well as you know a friend, adding darkness to the picture doesn’t make it feel the least bit frightening.
source_text: Down, downward they went, and yet further down—their descent at each step seeming to outmeasure their advance. Their skirts were scratched noisily by the furze, their shoulders brushed by the ferns, which, though dead and dry, stood erect as when alive, no sufficient winter weather having as yet arrived to beat them down. Their Tartarean situation might by some have been called an imprudent one for two unattended women. But these shaggy recesses were at all seasons a familiar surrounding to Olly and Mrs. Yeobright; and the addition of darkness lends no frightfulness to the face of a friend.

text: They fastened the rope around his waist as planned, and the work pressed forward. When they hauled in the line next, the drag felt unnaturally light, and they soon realized they had only hooked a loose coil of rope that had broken free from the bucket. The snarled, knotted mass was tossed off to the side out of the way. Humphrey stepped into Yeobright’s position, and they lowered the grapnel back down a second time.
source_text: The rope was accordingly tied round his waist, and the work proceeded. At the next haul the weight was not heavy, and it was discovered that they had only secured a coil of the rope detached from the bucket. The tangled mass was thrown into the background. Humphrey took Yeobright’s place, and the grapnel was lowered again.

text: Never, Mother. They’ll never catch on to it, because their teachers never work directly with the group of students that actually needs this kind of system—meaning, the ones who’ve had no prior foundational training at all. My approach is built to impart advanced knowledge to these empty, untaught minds, without first cramming them full of material they’ll only have to unlearn later, before genuine, deep study can even begin.
source_text: “Never, Mother. They cannot find it out, because their teachers don’t come in contact with the class which demands such a system—that is, those who have had no preliminary training. My plan is one for instilling high knowledge into empty minds without first cramming them with what has to be uncrammed again before true study begins.”

text: By mid-afternoon, groups of people had begun to assemble on the village green, and Yeobright was sufficiently intrigued to lean out the open window of his upstairs room to watch them. Not long after, Thomasin stepped through the door directly beneath his window, then tilted her head up to meet her cousin’s gaze. She was dressed far more vibrantly and thoughtfully than Yeobright had ever seen her since Wildeve’s death eighteen months earlier; even dating back to her own wedding day, she had never appeared quite so polished and radiant.
source_text: When afternoon came people began to gather on the green, and Yeobright was interested enough to look out upon them from the open window of his room. Soon after this Thomasin walked out from the door immediately below and turned her eyes up to her cousin’s face. She was dressed more gaily than Yeobright had ever seen her dressed since the time of Wildeve’s death, eighteen months before; since the day of her marriage even she had not exhibited herself to such advantage.

text: His usual approach to waiting was to circle the overgrown fenced enclosure and dawdle at the moss-edged pool, for Kael Rainer had never once been invited across the household threshold by Elara Voss, its proud, faintly condescending mistress. She gave no sign of intending to emerge in any hurry. Minutes stretched thin, and a quiet restlessness began to curdle into impatience in his chest. After nearly twenty minutes had slipped by, she rounded the side corner of the house, stepping forward as if she had merely stepped out for a casual stroll to stretch her legs.
source_text: He waited in his customary manner by walking round the enclosure and idling by the pool, for Wildeve was never asked into the house by his proud though condescending mistress. She showed no sign of coming out in a hurry. The time wore on, and he began to grow impatient. In the course of twenty minutes she appeared from round the corner, and advanced as if merely taking an airing.

text: After that gray, unremarkable Tuesday, no matter how brutal the weather grew, Elara kept her two-hour vigil at the splintered oak fence marking the edge of the depot lot. When the town clock struck two, she was already leaning against its weathered slats, and when it chimed four, she turned for home with a quiet, resigned slump to her shoulders. On days when the rain stayed light and the wind did not bite hard enough to risk her young daughter Mia falling ill, the girl came with her, kicking at loose gravel along the path as they went; on harsher days she made the trip alone, but she never once missed a single visit.
source_text: From that time, in all weathers, she waited there two hours. As the clock struck two, she was there, and at four she turned resignedly away. When it was not too wet or inclement for her child to be with her, they went together; at other times she was alone; but, she never missed a single day.

text: “No,” Tall said as he stepped over to join the group. “And honestly, I think we’re better off keeping this to ourselves. If it isn’t true, repeating it will just rattle her, do her real unnecessary harm. And if it is true, there’s no use in forcing her to face that trouble before she has to. I hope to God it’s all a lie, too. Even if Henery Fray and half the others around here run their mouths about her, she’s never been anything but fair to me. Sure, she’s fiery and quick to snap, but she’s got more backbone than most, and she’d never tell a lie no matter how much telling the truth might hurt her. I’ve got no reason at all to wish her any ill will.”
