source_text: “No,” said Tall, joining the group. “And I’m inclined to think we’d better keep quiet. If so be ’tis not true, ’twill flurry her, and do her much harm to repeat it; and if so be ’tis true, ’twill do no good to forestall her time o’ trouble. God send that it mid be a lie, for though Henery Fray and some of ’em do speak against her, she’s never been anything but fair to me. She’s hot and hasty, but she’s a brave girl who’ll never tell a lie however much the truth may harm her, and I’ve no cause to wish her evil.”

text: The quiet joy of the self-chosen English home he had made for himself, the constant need to stay actively occupied, the swift, roiling upheavals and troubles of the era that tumbled over one another so quickly that this week’s events entirely swept away the half-baked plans of the week prior, only for the next week’s developments to rewrite the slate entirely: he knew full well he had yielded to the weight of these circumstances. Not without quiet unease, to be sure, but also without putting up any sustained, mounting resistance. He had watched the tides of the times, waiting for a suitable moment to act, only for those tides to churn and shift until that window had slipped shut entirely. Nobles were pouring out of France by every main road and hidden back path, their estates and possessions were being seized and destroyed, even their very family names were being wiped from the record, and he was as aware of all this as any of the new French authorities that might choose to level charges against him for it.
source_text: The happiness of his own chosen English home, the necessity of being always actively employed, the swift changes and troubles of the time which had followed on one another so fast, that the events of this week annihilated the immature plans of last week, and the events of the week following made all new again; he knew very well, that to the force of these circumstances he had yielded:--not without disquiet, but still without continuous and accumulating resistance. That he had watched the times for a time of action, and that they had shifted and struggled until the time had gone by, and the nobility were trooping from France by every highway and byway, and their property was in course of confiscation and destruction, and their very names were blotting out, was as well known to himself as it could be to any new authority in France that might impeach him for it.

text: “You’re only joking?” she said, shocked and heavy with sorrow. “Can you make light of things right now when I’m this wretchedly serious? Tell me the truth, Frank. I’m not an idiot, you know that—even if I am a woman, and I have my soft, overemotional feminine moments now and then. Come on, be fair with me,” she said, looking him dead in the face, open and unflinching. “I don’t ask for much, just plain, basic decency, that’s all. God, once I was sure I’d never settle for anything less than the highest, most devoted respect from the husband I picked for myself. Now? I’d take anything that stops short of outright cruelty and call it enough. Yeah! That bold, self-reliant, spirited Bathsheba everyone knew? She’s sunk this far.”
source_text: “A mere jest!” she said, in mournful astonishment. “Can you jest when I am so wretchedly in earnest? Tell me the truth, Frank. I am not a fool, you know, although I am a woman, and have my woman’s moments. Come! treat me fairly,” she said, looking honestly and fearlessly into his face. “I don’t want much; bare justice—that’s all! Ah! once I felt I could be content with nothing less than the highest homage from the husband I should choose. Now, anything short of cruelty will content me. Yes! the independent and spirited Bathsheba is come to this!”

text: Anxious to stay out of the house’s direct line of sight, she’d veered off the shortest path home. As she scanned the area trying to find her way back to that route, she came across a small boy gathering whortleberries in a sunken hollow. The boy was Johnny Nunsuch, the same child who’d tended Eustacia’s bonfire as her stoker, and, just as small objects naturally drift toward larger masses, he started hovering near Mrs. Yeobright the moment she appeared, trotting along beside her without any conscious awareness that he was doing so.
source_text: In her anxiety to get out of the direct view of the house she had diverged from the straightest path homeward, and while looking about to regain it she came upon a little boy gathering whortleberries in a hollow. The boy was Johnny Nunsuch, who had been Eustacia’s stoker at the bonfire, and, with the tendency of a minute body to gravitate towards a greater, he began hovering round Mrs. Yeobright as soon as she appeared, and trotted on beside her without perceptible consciousness of his act.

text: At the base of Shadwater Weir sat a wide, perfectly round pool, fifty feet across, fed by water spilling through ten massive sluice hatches that operated the standard way, cranked up and down via a winch and gear system. The pool was lined entirely with stone masonry, built to stop the rushing water from eroding the surrounding earth; even so, the sheer force of the winter current was occasionally strong enough to eat away at the base of the retaining wall and send chunks of it tumbling straight into the pool below. When Clym made it to the hatches, he could feel the entire frame rattling down to its very supports, jostled by the brutal speed of the oncoming water. The pool beneath was so thoroughly roiled that he could make out nothing but the bright white froth of breaking waves across its surface. He stepped onto the narrow plank bridge spanning the sluice race, gripping the railing tight to keep the howling wind from yanking him off his feet as he picked his way across to the opposite bank of the river. Once there, he leaned out over the stone wall and held his lamp down toward the water, and all he could see was the tight, spinning vortex that coiled right where the rebounding current curled back on itself.
source_text: Shadwater Weir had at its foot a large circular pool, fifty feet in diameter, into which the water flowed through ten huge hatches, raised and lowered by a winch and cogs in the ordinary manner. The sides of the pool were of masonry, to prevent the water from washing away the bank; but the force of the stream in winter was sometimes such as to undermine the retaining wall and precipitate it into the hole. Clym reached the hatches, the framework of which was shaken to its foundations by the velocity of the current. Nothing but the froth of the waves could be discerned in the pool below. He got upon the plank bridge over the race, and holding to the rail, that the wind might not blow him off, crossed to the other side of the river. There he leant over the wall and lowered the lamp, only to behold the vortex formed at the curl of the returning current.

text: “You’re wildly overstating things,” she said, her voice faint and worn ragged with exhaustion, “but I don’t have the energy to defend myself—there’s no point to it anyway. From now on, you mean absolutely nothing to me, and the full backstory of what happened between us doesn’t need to be aired at all. You cost me everything I had, and I never once complained. Your mistakes and string of bad luck might have only been a source of grief for you, but they were a genuine wrong inflicted on me. Every person of good breeding I used to know has run for the hills ever since I sank into the mire of this marriage. Is this what you call caring for me? Shoving me into a ratty little hut like this, treating me no better than a farmhand’s wife? You deceived me—you didn’t even have to lie out loud, you just put on a false front that’s even harder to see through than any spoken lie would be. I suppose this place works just as well as any other, though—somewhere to wait out the time before I go to my grave.” Her words got caught and choked off in her throat, and her head drooped forward heavily.
source_text: “You exaggerate fearfully,” she said in a faint, weary voice; “but I cannot enter into my defence—it is not worth doing. You are nothing to me in future, and the past side of the story may as well remain untold. I have lost all through you, but I have not complained. Your blunders and misfortunes may have been a sorrow to you, but they have been a wrong to me. All persons of refinement have been scared away from me since I sank into the mire of marriage. Is this your cherishing—to put me into a hut like this, and keep me like the wife of a hind? You deceived me—not by words, but by appearances, which are less seen through than words. But the place will serve as well as any other—as somewhere to pass from—into my grave.” Her words were smothered in her throat, and her head drooped down.

text: The first place she came to was what locals called Wildeve’s Patch: a parcel of land reclaimed from the surrounding heath, converted into workable farmland only after years of grueling, unrelenting effort. The first man who figured out the soil could be tilled worked himself to death trying to do just that; the next person who took ownership of the plot ruined himself trying to enrich the barren earth enough to support crops. Wildeve turned up later, just like Amerigo Vespucci, and claimed all the praise and recognition that rightfully belonged to the people who’d poured all the work into the land before him.
source_text: She first reached Wildeve’s Patch, as it was called, a plot of land redeemed from the heath, and after long and laborious years brought into cultivation. The man who had discovered that it could be tilled died of the labour; the man who succeeded him in possession ruined himself in fertilizing it. Wildeve came like Amerigo Vespucci, and received the honours due to those who had gone before.

text:
MY DEAR EUSTACIA,
I have to follow what my heart demands, without overthinking what my rational side might urge. Will you come back to me? If you do, I will never speak of our past quarrel again. I was far too harsh, but oh, Eustacia, how provoked I was! You cannot know, you never will know, how much it cost me to speak those bitter, angry words to you that day, even if you had drawn them upon yourself. I give you every promise an honest man can offer: you will never suffer any hurt from me over this matter again. For all the solemn vows we have exchanged, Eustacia, I believe we owe it to each other to spend the rest of our lives trying to keep them.

So come to me, even if you first have harsh reproaches for me. I have turned over the memory of your suffering the morning we parted again and again; I know that pain was genuine, and you have already borne more than enough for what passed between us. Our love must not end here. Hearts like ours were never given to us to care for anyone but each other.

I could not ask you to return at first, Eustacia, because I could not shake the belief that the man I saw with you was there as your lover. But if you will come and explain those confusing, upsetting circumstances, I have no doubt you can prove your faithfulness to me. Why have you not come to me before now? Do you think I would refuse to listen to you? You cannot believe that, not when you recall the kisses and vows we shared beneath the soft summer moon. Return then, and you will be greeted with all the warmth you deserve. I can no longer hold anything against you; all I want now is to find every reason to justify you in my own mind.
Your husband as ever,
source_text: MY DEAR EUSTACIA,—I must obey my heart without consulting my reason too closely. Will you come back to me? Do so, and the past shall never be mentioned. I was too severe; but O, Eustacia, the provocation! You don’t know, you never will know, what those words of anger cost me which you drew down upon yourself. All that an honest man can promise you I promise now, which is that from me you shall never suffer anything on this score again. After all the vows we have made, Eustacia, I think we had better pass the remainder of our lives in trying to keep them. Come to me, then, even if you reproach me. I have thought of your sufferings that morning on which I parted from you; I know they were genuine, and they are as much as you ought to bear. Our love must still continue. Such hearts as ours would never have been given us but to be concerned with each other. I could not ask you back at first, Eustacia, for I was unable to persuade myself that he who was with you was not there as a lover. But if you will come and explain distracting appearances I do not question that you can show your honesty to me. Why have you not come before? Do you think I will not listen to you? Surely not, when you remember the kisses and vows we exchanged under the summer moon. Return then, and you shall be warmly welcomed. I can no longer think of you to your prejudice—I am but too much absorbed in justifying you.—Your husband as ever,

text: The only way to carry the unmistakeable air of a queen when you have no realms to govern and no hearts to hold sway over is to move as if you once had both, and grieved their loss; and Elowen pulled this off to utter perfection. In the old commander’s stone cottage, she could conjure the weight of stately manors she had never once entered. Perhaps that was because she frequented a far vaster estate than any such manor could ever be: the wide, unbroken hills that rolled unimpeded to the horizon. Like the summer state of the land around her, she was a living embodiment of the phrase “a teeming solitude” — apparently so listless, hollow, and still, she was in truth brimming with unspoken, restless activity.
source_text: The only way to look queenly without realms or hearts to queen it over is to look as if you had lost them; and Eustacia did that to a triumph. In the captain’s cottage she could suggest mansions she had never seen. Perhaps that was because she frequented a vaster mansion than any of them, the open hills. Like the summer condition of the place around her, she was an embodiment of the phrase “a populous solitude”—apparently so listless, void, and quiet, she was really busy and full.

text: The uneven ridges and dips of the path were lost to the dim light, so Wildeve stumbled every so often as they pressed forward; Eustacia, for her part, had to execute little feats of graceful balancing whenever a tuft of heather or knotted furze root jutted up through the grass of the narrow track and snagged at her feet. Each time these small hindrances interrupted her stride, a hand unfailingly stretched out to steady her, holding her firmly until they stepped onto smooth ground again, before pulling back once more to a respectful, unpresuming distance.
source_text: On this account the irregularities of the path were not visible, and Wildeve occasionally stumbled; whilst Eustacia found it necessary to perform some graceful feats of balancing whenever a small tuft of heather or root of furze protruded itself through the grass of the narrow track and entangled her feet. At these junctures in her progress a hand was invariably stretched forward to steady her, holding her firmly until smooth ground was again reached, when the hand was again withdrawn to a respectful distance.

text: “You really didn’t marry her!” she murmured, bright with unmistakeable triumph. “I always knew that was why—you loved me more than anyone else, and you just couldn’t go through with it… Damon, you were so cruel to me, leaving the way you did. I told myself over and over I’d never forgive you for it. I don’t think I can even fully forgive you right now, honestly—any woman with a shred of spirit and self-respect couldn’t just overlook something that hurts that badly, not completely.”
source_text: “That you did not marry her!” she murmured exultingly. “And I knew it was because you loved me best, and couldn’t do it.... Damon, you have been cruel to me to go away, and I have said I would never forgive you. I do not think I can forgive you entirely, even now—it is too much for a woman of any spirit to quite overlook.”

text: “Let me just talk to you for the short time I’m here, all right?” Thomasin said in a gentle, soothing tone. “Think about how lopsided your take on this whole thing is, Clym. When she said that to the little boy, you hadn’t found her yet and pulled her into your arms, right? She could’ve just spit the words out in a split second of deep bitterness. That’s exactly the kind of hasty, unthinking remark Aunt used to make, anyway. She’d snap at me the exact same way sometimes. Even if she never ended up coming over, I’m fully convinced she thought about coming to see you. Do you honestly believe a man’s own mother could go two or three months without a single forgiving thought crossing her mind? She forgave me, after all—why wouldn’t she have forgiven you too?”
source_text: “Let me talk to you instead for the little time I shall be here,” Thomasin said soothingly. “Consider what a one-sided way you have of looking at the matter, Clym. When she said that to the little boy you had not found her and taken her into your arms; and it might have been uttered in a moment of bitterness. It was rather like Aunt to say things in haste. She sometimes used to speak so to me. Though she did not come I am convinced that she thought of coming to see you. Do you suppose a man’s mother could live two or three months without one forgiving thought? She forgave me; and why should she not have forgiven you?”

text: “Oh, my very existence!” said Liora, with a low trill of a laugh that parted her soft lips so wide the slanting afternoon sun slipped into her mouth as it might the folded petals of a crimson peony, gilding it with the same vivid, burning ruby glow. “You far overrate my sway over men, charcoal burner. If I held the kind of power you imagine I do, I would walk a straight path and use it for the good of any soul that had ever treated me with kindness—and Elspeth Thorne has never done so in any notable way, not as far as I have noticed.”
source_text: “Ah, my life!” said Eustacia, with a laugh which unclosed her lips so that the sun shone into her mouth as into a tulip, and lent it a similar scarlet fire. “You think too much of my influence over menfolk indeed, reddleman. If I had such a power as you imagine I would go straight and use it for the good of anybody who has been kind to me—which Thomasin Yeobright has not particularly, to my knowledge.”

text: After miles of sodden tramping, Elowen finally stepped down into a wind-scoured hollow, and caught sight of a faint, blotted glow seeping through the driving rain. Bit by bit it sharpened into the oblong outline of an open, unlatched door. She knew no dwelling stood anywhere near this patch of heath, and quickly guessed the structure’s nature from how high its sill hovered above the sodden ground below.
source_text: At length Thomasin reached a hollow and began to discern through the rain a faint blotted radiance, which presently assumed the oblong form of an open door. She knew that no house stood hereabouts, and was soon aware of the nature of the door by its height above the ground.

text: She was speaking so fast she could barely catch her breath: “So they’re actually running off together! He came inside around eight tonight, all casual like it was nothing, and said, ‘Tamsie, I just found out I have to take a trip.’ I asked him when, and he said tonight. When I pressed him where he was going, he just said, ‘I can’t tell you right now, I’ll be back tomorrow.’ Then he turned away and started rummaging for his things, completely ignoring me. I waited around figuring I’d see him leave, but he never did. By ten o’clock he turned to me and said, ‘You should head to bed.’ I had no clue what to make of it, so I did as he said. I’m pretty sure he thought I’d fallen asleep, because half an hour later he crept upstairs and unlocked that oak chest we use to stash cash when we’ve got a lot lying around the house. He pulled out a thick roll of something I’m almost certain was banknotes— I had no idea he’d even stashed those there. He must have withdrawn them when he went to the bank a couple days back. Why would he need that much cash if he’s only gone for a single day? After he went back downstairs, I thought of Eustacia, and how he’d met up with her the night before. I know for a fact he met her, Clym, I followed him part of the way, but I didn’t want to say anything when you stopped by earlier. I didn’t want you to think badly of him when I didn’t know how bad this really was. I couldn’t stand lying in bed after that, so I got up and got dressed. When I heard him out in the stable, I knew I had to come tell you. I snuck downstairs as quiet as I could and slipped out the door.”
source_text: She went on breathlessly: “Then they are going to run off together! He came indoors tonight about eight o’clock and said in an off-hand way, ‘Tamsie, I have just found that I must go a journey.’ ‘When?’ I said. ‘Tonight,’ he said. ‘Where?’ I asked him. ‘I cannot tell you at present,’ he said; ‘I shall be back again tomorrow.’ He then went and busied himself in looking up his things, and took no notice of me at all. I expected to see him start, but he did not, and then it came to be ten o’clock, when he said, ‘You had better go to bed.’ I didn’t know what to do, and I went to bed. I believe he thought I fell asleep, for half an hour after that he came up and unlocked the oak chest we keep money in when we have much in the house and took out a roll of something which I believe was banknotes, though I was not aware that he had ’em there. These he must have got from the bank when he went there the other day. What does he want banknotes for, if he is only going off for a day? When he had gone down I thought of Eustacia, and how he had met her the night before—I know he did meet her, Clym, for I followed him part of the way; but I did not like to tell you when you called, and so make you think ill of him, as I did not think it was so serious. Then I could not stay in bed; I got up and dressed myself, and when I heard him out in the stable I thought I would come and tell you. So I came downstairs without any noise and slipped out.”

text: “I didn’t just happen to be there,” Fairway said, laying extra weight on each word for emphasis, “I was sitting in the exact same pew as Miz Yeobright. You might not think that would land so hard, but listening to her honestly made my blood run cold. Yeah, it’s a strange thing to say, but it really did chill me to the bone— I was sitting right at her elbow, after all.” He glanced around at the bystanders, who were now pressing in closer to hear every detail, his lips drawn tighter than ever as he held fast to his strict, no-frills way of telling the story.
source_text: “I not only happened to be there,” said Fairway, with a fresh collection of emphasis, “but I was sitting in the same pew as Mis’ess Yeobright. And though you may not see it as such, it fairly made my blood run cold to hear her. Yes, it is a curious thing; but it made my blood run cold, for I was close at her elbow.” The speaker looked round upon the bystanders, now drawing closer to hear him, with his lips gathered tighter than ever in the rigorousness of his descriptive moderation.

text: By that point, the man was making his way back from where he’d left the horses. Terrified of being spotted, the boy all but guaranteed he’d be caught when he made a sharp, nervous movement. Thick matted layers of heather and peat jutted out over the pit’s upper rim, completely obscuring where the solid ground actually ended. The boy had already stepped past the stable stretch of earth; the heather gave way right then, and he went tumbling down the steep scarp of grey sand, landing directly at the man’s feet.
source_text: The man was by this time coming back from the horses. In his fear of being seen the boy rendered detection certain by nervous motion. The heather and peat stratum overhung the brow of the pit in mats, hiding the actual verge. The boy had stepped beyond the solid ground; the heather now gave way, and down he rolled over the scarp of grey sand to the very foot of the man.

text: The brindle tabby was curled fast asleep on the path’s bare crushed stone, as if cushioned beds, woven rugs, and plush carpets were too stifling to bear. Hollyhock fronds drooped like umbrellas left half-furled after a storm, sap throbbed so hot in their stalks it might have been simmering, and every waxy leaf glinted as bright as polished tin mirrors. A tiny Earlyblush apple tree, the only fruit tree that thrived in the whole yard thanks to the loose, sandy soil, grew just inside the picket gate; beneath it, among the windfall apples scattered across the dirt, wasps stumbled woozy with fermented juice, or picked their way through the tiny hollowed-out cavities they’d gnawed into each fruit before the sugar rush left them sluggish. Propped against the back door lay Luka’s gorse-cutting sickle and the last bundle of kindling ties she’d watched him collect earlier; they’d clearly been dropped in a hurry the second he stepped across the threshold.
source_text: There lay the cat asleep on the bare gravel of the path, as if beds, rugs, and carpets were unendurable. The leaves of the hollyhocks hung like half-closed umbrellas, the sap almost simmered in the stems, and foliage with a smooth surface glared like metallic mirrors. A small apple tree, of the sort called Ratheripe, grew just inside the gate, the only one which throve in the garden, by reason of the lightness of the soil; and among the fallen apples on the ground beneath were wasps rolling drunk with the juice, or creeping about the little caves in each fruit which they had eaten out before stupefied by its sweetness. By the door lay Clym’s furze-hook and the last handful of faggot-bonds she had seen him gather; they had plainly been thrown down there as he entered the house.

text: For a young child, catching their first glimpse of a reddleman was a defining, unforgettable milestone in their early life. That rich blood-red figure felt like the living distillation of every terrifying nightmare that had plagued young minds ever since they first developed the capacity for imagination. “The reddleman’s coming to get you!” was the standard threat Wessex mothers had relied on to discipline their children for countless generations. At the start of the current century, he was briefly pushed out of this role by Buonaparte; but as time wore on, Buonaparte grew stale and lost his punch as a figure of fear, and the old warning about the reddleman rose back to its former widespread popularity. Now, the reddleman has in turn followed Buonaparte into the realm of discarded, out-of-date bogeymen, his place filled by more modern inventions tailored for the current era.
source_text: A child’s first sight of a reddleman was an epoch in his life. That blood-coloured figure was a sublimation of all the horrid dreams which had afflicted the juvenile spirit since imagination began. “The reddleman is coming for you!” had been the formulated threat of Wessex mothers for many generations. He was successfully supplanted for a while, at the beginning of the present century, by Buonaparte; but as process of time rendered the latter personage stale and ineffective the older phrase resumed its early prominence. And now the reddleman has in his turn followed Buonaparte to the land of worn-out bogeys, and his place is filled by modern inventions.

text: “I could’ve sworn I heard a faint stir just outside the lean-to,” said Tobin. “I wish all troubles showed up in the daylight, when a man can stand firm and show his mettle, and would never have to beg mercy even of the scruffiest broom-brandishing old crone he stumbled across, so long as he was bold enough, and quick enough to dart out of her line of sight.”
source_text: “I fancy I heard something outside the shed,” said Christian. “I wish troubles would come in the daytime, for then a man could show his courage, and hardly beg for mercy of the most broomstick old woman he should see, if he was a brave man, and able to run out of her sight!”

text: He stepped inside only moments later. The tea was already prepared, and he took the seat opposite his mother. She barely spoke, and for his part, the events and words exchanged on the hill only minutes earlier kept him from striking up any meandering, casual conversation. There was an unmistakeable ominous edge to his mother’s silence, but he gave no sign of noticing it. He knew exactly why she had so little to say, but there was nothing he could do to undo the cause of her distant bearing toward him. These half-silent sittings had grown quite common between the two of them as of late. Finally, Yeobright began to speak, intent on addressing the very root of the whole matter between them.
source_text: But he entered soon after. The tea was ready, and he sat down opposite his mother. She did not speak many words; and as for him, something had been just done and some words had been just said on the hill which prevented him from beginning a desultory chat. His mother’s taciturnity was not without ominousness, but he appeared not to care. He knew why she said so little, but he could not remove the cause of her bearing towards him. These half-silent sittings were far from uncommon with them now. At last Yeobright made a beginning of what was intended to strike at the whole root of the matter.

text: That’s how Eustacia ended up with the strangest mishmash of old and new thoughts sitting side by side in her head. Her worldview held no soft middle ground at all: romantic, glowing memories of sunny esplanade afternoons, where military bands played and dashing officers and eligible suitors drifted all around her, stood out like gilded lettering pressed into the dark, heavy slate of the Egdon heath that surrounded her on all sides. Every odd, contradictory effect you could get from haphazardly weaving the glitter of a seaside resort together with the vast, quiet solemnity of the moors took shape in her. With almost no exposure to the kind of bustling social life she once knew these days, she clung to the scenes she’d already lived, reimagining them in far richer detail than she’d ever noticed at the time.
source_text: Thus it happened that in Eustacia’s brain were juxtaposed the strangest assortment of ideas, from old time and from new. There was no middle distance in her perspective—romantic recollections of sunny afternoons on an esplanade, with military bands, officers, and gallants around, stood like gilded letters upon the dark tablet of surrounding Egdon. Every bizarre effect that could result from the random intertwining of watering-place glitter with the grand solemnity of a heath, was to be found in her. Seeing nothing of human life now, she imagined all the more of what she had seen.

text: “Mine?” asked Elias, a vacant haze over his unblinking, far-focused eyes. “I—I ain’t got no maid, wife, nor widowed kin to my name at all, and I’m afeared folks will laugh me to scorn if I take it, Master Wayfarer. I was so caught up in the eagerness to join in I never stopped to think of that! What on earth would I do with a woman’s garments in my own bedroom, without losing every shred of my decency?”
source_text: “Mine?” asked Christian, with a vacant stare from his target eyes. “I—I haven’t got neither maid, wife, nor widder belonging to me at all, and I’m afeard it will make me laughed at to ha’e it, Master Traveller. What with being curious to join in I never thought of that! What shall I do wi’ a woman’s clothes in _my_ bedroom, and not lose my decency!”

text: Elowen carried a faint, uncharacteristic reserve in her bearing toward her cousin these days, a quiet remove that had not colored their exchanges before. Marriage holds that odd double effect: it sweeps away every trace of bashfulness between the two people who wed, only to sprout small, unforeseen cautions in nearly every other connection the newlywed nurtures. “Your mother told me of it,” she said, her tone low and even. “She stopped by my cottage directly after she’d finished visiting Marwenna.”
source_text: There was a little more reticence now than formerly in Thomasin’s manner towards her cousin. It is the effect of marriage to engender in several directions some of the reserve it annihilates in one. “Your mother told me,” she said quietly. “She came back to my house after seeing Eustacia.”

text: He couldn’t make out a single thing at first, his gaze straining against the churning, roiling water. Then, between the glinting swirl of eddies and clumps of frothy white foam, he spotted a woman’s bonnet bobbing unaccompanied on the surface. He was searching the stretch running along the left stone wall when something broke the surface barely an arm’s length away. It wasn’t the woman he’d expected to find, though—it was a man. Venn the reddleman clamped the lantern’s metal ring between his teeth, grabbed the floating man by the back of his collar, hooked his free arm over the wooden hatch he’d been holding, and kicked out into the fastest part of the current. The unconscious man, the hatch, and Venn himself were all swept downstream in one headlong rush. As soon as he felt his feet scuff against the pebbled riverbed in the shallower stretch further down, he secured his footing and waded toward the bank. By the time he reached water that only came up to his waist, he tossed the hatch aside and tried to haul the man fully out of the current. It was far more difficult than he’d anticipated, and he quickly learned the reason: the unfortunate stranger’s legs were held tight in the grip of another man’s arms, a second person who’d been entirely submerged beneath the surface this whole time.
source_text: At first he could see nothing. Then amidst the glistening of the whirlpools and the white clots of foam he distinguished a woman’s bonnet floating alone. His search was now under the left wall, when something came to the surface almost close beside him. It was not, as he had expected, a woman, but a man. The reddleman put the ring of the lantern between his teeth, seized the floating man by the collar, and, holding on to the hatch with his remaining arm, struck out into the strongest race, by which the unconscious man, the hatch, and himself were carried down the stream. As soon as Venn found his feet dragging over the pebbles of the shallower part below he secured his footing and waded towards the brink. There, where the water stood at about the height of his waist, he flung away the hatch, and attempted to drag forth the man. This was a matter of great difficulty, and he found as the reason that the legs of the unfortunate stranger were tightly embraced by the arms of another man, who had hitherto been entirely beneath the surface.

text: There was once a young roustabout in New Orleans who gambled until he’d lost every last cent to his name. He put up his brass pocket watch and fob chain, and lost just as he had before; staked his oilcloth umbrella, lost again; wagered his wool felt hat, fell short once more; bet his heavy canvas work coat and was left standing in his threadbare undershirt, only to lose that too. He’d just started undoing the ties of his work trousers when an onlooker lingering by the table slipped him a small coin for his stubborn grit. With that tiny stake, he began to win. He won back his coat, won back his hat, won back his umbrella, his watch, every last dollar he’d lost, and stepped out of the waterfront saloon that evening a wealthy man.
source_text: “And once there was a young man in America who gambled till he had lost his last dollar. He staked his watch and chain, and lost as before; staked his umbrella, lost again; staked his hat, lost again; staked his coat and stood in his shirt-sleeves, lost again. Began taking off his breeches, and then a looker-on gave him a trifle for his pluck. With this he won. Won back his coat, won back his hat, won back his umbrella, his watch, his money, and went out of the door a rich man.”

text: And so strangers who first glanced idly at Kael ended up poring over every line of his face, as if decoding a half-faded handwritten manuscript. Every plane of his countenance bore unmistakeable, readable layers of lived experience. Though he did not yet carry the haggard creases of a life spent entirely in rumination, faint, persistent marks scored his skin, left by years of watching and parsing the world around him—marks often seen on those four or five years out from the soft, uncomplicated bubble of their formal schooling, when they have spent every waking hour striving to carve out a place for themselves. He already embodied the quiet truth that thinking is a slow erosion of the flesh, and bore unspoken witness to the fact that unmarred, ideal physical beauty cannot coexist with deep emotional maturation, nor with the full, heavy recognition of how tangled and knotted the web of human life truly is. The bright glow of a sharp, curious mind must be fed with the same finite store of life oil that fuels the body’s most basic needs; here, the quiet, heart-wrenching sight of those two competing claims drawing from the same limited supply was already impossible to overlook.
source_text: Hence people who began by beholding him ended by perusing him. His countenance was overlaid with legible meanings. Without being thought-worn he yet had certain marks derived from a perception of his surroundings, such as are not unfrequently found on men at the end of the four or five years of endeavour which follow the close of placid pupilage. He already showed that thought is a disease of flesh, and indirectly bore evidence that ideal physical beauty is incompatible with emotional development and a full recognition of the coil of things. Mental luminousness must be fed with the oil of life, even though there is already a physical need for it; and the pitiful sight of two demands on one supply was just showing itself here.

text: He got no response, as everyone was fully caught up in the moment, rapt in admiration for the performance they’d just heard described. Much like Farinelli’s legendary recitals before royal princesses, Sheridan’s famed Begum Speech, and other such storied cultural touchstones, the simple fact that the late Mr. Yeobright’s tour de force from that unforgettable afternoon was permanently lost to the world wrapped it in a steadily accumulating glory that comparative critical analysis, had that ever been feasible, would almost certainly have pared down considerably.
source_text: He received no answer, all for the moment sitting rapt in admiration of the performance described. As with Farinelli’s singing before the princesses, Sheridan’s renowned Begum Speech, and other such examples, the fortunate condition of its being for ever lost to the world invested the deceased Mr. Yeobright’s tour de force on that memorable afternoon with a cumulative glory which comparative criticism, had that been possible, might considerably have shorn down.

text: “Oh, do let’s go already—Damon, look at what you’ve forced me to say!” She buried her face in her handkerchief. “Here I am, the one asking you to marry me, when you ought to be on your knees imploring me, your merciless little mistress, not to turn you away, swearing your heart would break if I refused. I always thought that moment would be so tender and sweet, but this is nothing like the pretty fantasy I used to hold!”
source_text: “Then do let us go!—O Damon, what you make me say!” She hid her face in her handkerchief. “Here am I asking you to marry me, when by rights you ought to be on your knees imploring me, your cruel mistress, not to refuse you, and saying it would break your heart if I did. I used to think it would be pretty and sweet like that; but how different!”

text: Waiting until the following morning to seek out Thomasin and lay out his plan in full was entirely out of the question for him. He hurried through washing and changing, hauling a crisp wool suit from a storage chest, and within twenty minutes stood in the warm glow of his van’s lantern: the only mark of his trade as a reddleman that remained was the deep vermilion stain across his face, a tint that would not fade or wash away for a full day at the earliest. He pulled the van door shut, latched it tight with a heavy padlock, and set off down the road toward Blooms-End.
source_text: He could not afford to wait till the next day before seeing Thomasin and detailing his plan. He speedily plunged himself into toilet operations, pulled a suit of cloth clothes from a box, and in about twenty minutes stood before the van-lantern as a reddleman in nothing but his face, the vermilion shades of which were not to be removed in a day. Closing the door and fastening it with a padlock, Venn set off towards Blooms-End.

text: “So be it, then. There’s no sense picking apart that tangled mess at this point. Sons will be willfully blind if that’s what they choose, I suppose. How is it a woman can spot the plain truth from half a mile off, when a man can’t see the very same thing staring him right in the face? Kael can do as he pleases—he is nothing to me any longer. And this is the sum of motherhood, is it? To give your finest years and deepest love just to guarantee you end up scorned and cast aside!”
source_text: “Very well; we will let it be so. There is little use in unravelling that now. Sons must be blind if they will. Why is it that a woman can see from a distance what a man cannot see close? Clym must do as he will—he is nothing more to me. And this is maternity—to give one’s best years and best love to ensure the fate of being despised!”

text: Thomasin was barely present in the house at all, really; now that she was at ease about the little girl asleep upstairs, her whole mind was tracing every step of Clym’s journey. After she’d been lost in this imagined trek for what felt like a long stretch, she was suddenly struck by how agonizingly slow time was dragging. Still, she stayed seated. When she finally hit the point where she could barely sit still a second longer, it felt like a cruel mockery of her patience to remind herself that Clym probably hadn’t even reached the inn yet. Eventually she got up to check on the baby by its crib. The little one was sleeping soundly, but her mind was spinning with all the potentially disastrous things that might be unfolding at her home, the unseen what-ifs completely overriding the calm, tangible scene in front of her, until she was too agitated to bear it. She couldn’t stop herself from heading downstairs and pulling open the front door. The rain was still coming down hard; candlelight from inside caught the closest drops, turning them into glinting little darts as they fell, cutting through the dense throng of invisible raindrops behind them. Stepping out into that downpour would be like plunging into water thinned out just slightly with air. But the fact that heading home right now would be so difficult only made her want to do it more—literally anything was better than this endless suspense. “I got here safely enough,” she said, “so why shouldn’t I be able to go back? I never should have left in the first place.”
source_text: But the least part of Thomasin was in the house, for her heart being at ease about the little girl upstairs she was mentally following Clym on his journey. Having indulged in this imaginary peregrination for some considerable interval, she became impressed with a sense of the intolerable slowness of time. But she sat on. The moment then came when she could scarcely sit longer, and it was like a satire on her patience to remember that Clym could hardly have reached the inn as yet. At last she went to the baby’s bedside. The child was sleeping soundly; but her imagination of possibly disastrous events at her home, the predominance within her of the unseen over the seen, agitated her beyond endurance. She could not refrain from going down and opening the door. The rain still continued, the candlelight falling upon the nearest drops and making glistening darts of them as they descended across the throng of invisible ones behind. To plunge into that medium was to plunge into water slightly diluted with air. But the difficulty of returning to her house at this moment made her all the more desirous of doing so—anything was better than suspense. “I have come here well enough,” she said, “and why shouldn’t I go back again? It is a mistake for me to be away.”

text: A taut, watchful stillness hung thick over the whole expanse now; while every other thing around slipped into brooding, heavy sleep, the heath seemed to rouse slowly, perking up to listen closely. Each night, its massive, timeless form appeared to hold its breath waiting for something; but it had waited exactly like this, unshaken, across so many centuries, through the upheavals and turning points of so many eras and lives, that one could only assume it was holding out for one last cataclysm—the final collapse of all things.
source_text: The place became full of a watchful intentness now; for when other things sank brooding to sleep the heath appeared slowly to awake and listen. Every night its Titanic form seemed to await something; but it had waited thus, unmoved, during so many centuries, through the crises of so many things, that it could only be imagined to await one last crisis—the final overthrow.

text: Mrs. Yeobright shook her head. “Thomasin believes, and I am fully of her mind, that she ought to become Wildeve’s wife, if she means to step out in public without a blot on her name. If they wed soon, everyone will accept that a genuine accident truly did keep their first wedding from going forward. If they do not, it may cast a shadow over her character—at the very least, make her an object of ridicule. In short, if it is at all possible, they must marry now.”
source_text: Mrs. Yeobright shook her head. “Thomasin thinks, and I think with her, that she ought to be Wildeve’s wife, if she means to appear before the world without a slur upon her name. If they marry soon, everybody will believe that an accident did really prevent the wedding. If not, it may cast a shade upon her character—at any rate make her ridiculous. In short, if it is anyhow possible they must marry now.”

text: The traits that come to be embodied in the ideals shaped by this new understanding will likely bear a close resemblance to Yeobright’s. Anyone observing him had their gaze caught not by his face as a static, self-contained work of art, but by his face as an open page: what drew notice wasn’t the face in and of itself, but the history it recorded. His features were appealing in their role as symbols, much the way inherently ordinary sounds become charming when woven into language, and inherently simple shapes grow compelling when arranged into written text.
source_text: The lineaments which will get embodied in ideals based upon this new recognition will probably be akin to those of Yeobright. The observer’s eye was arrested, not by his face as a picture, but by his face as a page; not by what it was, but by what it recorded. His features were attractive in the light of symbols, as sounds intrinsically common become attractive in language, and as shapes intrinsically simple become interesting in writing.

text: By then she’d led him to the back door, which stood propped open, revealing a rutted dirt path that cut through the flower beds down to the far end of the garden. “Just one word first, Elias,” she said as he stepped out onto the threshold. “This is your first visit to this house, and let it be your last. We were wild, eager lovers once, but that life is behind us now, and there’s no room for it here. Goodbye.”
source_text: By this time she had taken him to the back door, which was open, disclosing a path leading down the garden. “Now, one word, Damon,” she remarked as he stepped forth. “This is your first visit here; let it be your last. We have been hot lovers in our time, but it won’t do now. Good-bye.”

text: “We have to make certain he remembers this. You know I keep a small box full of spade guineas, the same ones your uncle placed in my care to split between you and Clym whenever I saw fit. I suspect the time to do so has finally arrived. Those coins can be exchanged for sovereigns at a moment’s notice, no trouble whatsoever.”
source_text: “He must be made to remember. You are aware that I have a little box full of spade-guineas, which your uncle put into my hands to divide between yourself and Clym whenever I chose. Perhaps the time has come when it should be done. They can be turned into sovereigns at any moment.”

text: “That marriage is no misfortune in and of itself,” she retorted, a faint note of petulance sharp in her voice. “It is only the unlooked-for events that unfolded after it that have been the cause of my ruin. In worldly terms, I have surely gotten thistles where I hoped for figs, but how could I have guessed what the passage of time would bring to pass?”
source_text: “The marriage is no misfortune in itself,” she retorted with some little petulance. “It is simply the accident which has happened since that has been the cause of my ruin. I have certainly got thistles for figs in a worldly sense, but how could I tell what time would bring forth?”

text: “Alright, let’s set that whole subject aside for now,” Yeobright said. “But either way, God’s put a mark on me that would stick out like a sore thumb in any kind of romantic courtship moment. I’ve only got two plans locked in my head, no more. I’m going to run a night school, and I’m going to become a preacher. What do you make of that, Humphrey?”
source_text: “Well, we’ll leave that out,” said Yeobright. “But anyhow God has set a mark upon me which wouldn’t look well in a love-making scene. I have two ideas in my head, and no others. I am going to keep a night-school; and I am going to turn preacher. What have you got to say to that, Humphrey?”

text: “Nicely done, Charley!” called Captain Vye from his spot in the chimney corner. “Though I do hope that’s not my firewood he’s burning, now… Ah, it was exactly this time last year I crossed paths with that man Venn, bringing Thomasin Yeobright home—of course it was! Well, who would have guessed that girl’s troubles would end so well? You made such a fool of yourself over that whole affair, Eustacia! Has your husband written to you yet?”
source_text: “Well done, Charley!” said Captain Vye from the chimney-corner. “But I hope it is not my wood that he’s burning.... Ah, it was this time last year that I met with that man Venn, bringing home Thomasin Yeobright—to be sure it was! Well, who would have thought that girl’s troubles would have ended so well? What a snipe you were in that matter, Eustacia! Has your husband written to you yet?”

text: “We have no small cause for alarm,” came the grave, measured reply, and Elowen recognized the tone instantly as belonging to the hamlet’s only surgeon. “The adder’s bite has caused her real distress, but it is utter exhaustion that has overpowered her. My sense is that she must have been walking for an extraordinarily long distance before she collapsed.”
source_text: “There is a great deal to fear,” was gravely answered, in a voice which Eustacia recognized as that of the only surgeon in the district. “She has suffered somewhat from the bite of the adder; but it is exhaustion which has overpowered her. My impression is that her walk must have been exceptionally long.”

text: The woad peddler tracked the traveler’s silhouette as it shrank to a faint dot on the rutted lane, swallowed whole by the creeping, gauzy layers of dusk. He pulled a fistful of dried clover from a bale slung beneath his painted cart, tossed a portion of it in front of his two shaggy draft ponies, and twisted the rest into a thick pad which he laid on the packed dirt beside his vehicle. He lowered himself onto it, leaning his weight back against the wide wooden wheel. A low, steady breath drifted out to him from the cart’s covered interior. The sound seemed to quiet the last of his quiet worries, and he scanned the dim, hushing landscape slowly, as if weighing exactly what step he ought to take next.
source_text: The reddleman watched his form as it diminished to a speck on the road and became absorbed in the thickening films of night. He then took some hay from a truss which was slung up under the van, and, throwing a portion of it in front of the horses, made a pad of the rest, which he laid on the ground beside his vehicle. Upon this he sat down, leaning his back against the wheel. From the interior a low soft breathing came to his ear. It appeared to satisfy him, and he musingly surveyed the scene, as if considering the next step that he should take.

text: He strolled west through warm, gilded sunlight, picking his way into the stretches of Bramble Moor he knew best, the swathes of heath that lay closest to the cottage where he’d spent his childhood. Down in a hollow ahead, he caught a glint of sharpened steel, and as he drew closer, he made out that the light bounced off the blade of a man hacking at gorse. The laborer looked up and called out his name, and Finn recognized the rough, burring tone immediately as Joss’s.
source_text: He walked through the warm sun westward into those tracts of Egdon with which he was best acquainted, being those lying nearer to his old home. He saw before him in one of the valleys the gleaming of whetted iron, and advancing, dimly perceived that the shine came from the tool of a man who was cutting furze. The worker recognized Clym, and Yeobright learnt from the voice that the speaker was Humphrey.

text: That settled it for her: the reddleman was nothing but a last-ditch fallback in Mrs. Yeobright’s mind, and what’s more, he had no clue he’d even been slotted into that unflattering, low-rung position. “It was just a random thought I had,” she said evenly, and was about to step past him without another word when she glanced off to her right and spotted a gut-wrenchingly familiar figure zigzagging up one of the narrow trails that climbed to the spot where she stood. The path forced him to wind back and forth as he ascended, so at the moment his back was fully turned to the two of them. She cast a quick, frantic look around; there was only one possible way to avoid running into that man. Turning to Venn, she said, “Would you mind if I rested in your van for a few minutes? The grassy banks are far too damp to sit on right now.”
source_text: She was thus convinced that the reddleman was a mere _pis aller_ in Mrs. Yeobright’s mind; one, moreover, who had not even been informed of his promotion to that lowly standing. “It was a mere notion of mine,” she said quietly; and was about to pass by without further speech, when, looking round to the right, she saw a painfully well-known figure serpentining upwards by one of the little paths which led to the top where she stood. Owing to the necessary windings of his course his back was at present towards them. She glanced quickly round; to escape that man there was only one way. Turning to Venn, she said, “Would you allow me to rest a few minutes in your van? The banks are damp for sitting on.”

text: Naturally, he’d shown up, just as she’d known he would. Who was the woman curled in his arms as they spun across the floor? Maybe some unremarkable stranger, with none of her own refinement, was weaving the quietest of spells to bind his future to hers this exact moment. Sharing a dance with a man is the same as pouring a full year’s worth of slow, steady warmth over him in the space of a few short minutes. Leaping straight to courtship without so much as a proper introduction, or to marriage without even a season of wooing, is a shortcut afforded only to those who walk this gilded path of ballroom flirtation. She would study every shift of their movements, every flicker of their expressions, until she knew exactly where his affections rested.
source_text: He was there, of course. Who was she that he danced with? Perhaps some unknown woman, far beneath herself in culture, was by the most subtle of lures sealing his fate this very instant. To dance with a man is to concentrate a twelvemonth’s regulation fire upon him in the fragment of an hour. To pass to courtship without acquaintance, to pass to marriage without courtship, is a skipping of terms reserved for those alone who tread this royal road. She would see how his heart lay by keen observation of them all.

text: The pair circled around and climbed the wobbly winding staircase in total darkness until they reached Clym’s sitting room on the upper floor, where he lit a candle while Charley stepped in gently behind him. Yeobright rummaged through his desk, pulled out a sheet of tissue paper, and unfolded it to reveal two or three wavy locks of jet-black hair that spilled across the paper like dark trickling streams. He picked one lock from the group, wrapped it up neatly, and handed it to the boy, whose eyes had already welled up with tears. Charley kissed the small wrapped packet, tucked it into his pocket, and said in a thick, emotional voice, “Oh, Mr. Clym, how good you are to me!”
source_text: The two went round and ascended the crooked stair in darkness till Clym’s sitting-room on the upper floor was reached, where he lit a candle, Charley entering gently behind. Yeobright searched his desk, and taking out a sheet of tissue-paper unfolded from it two or three undulating locks of raven hair, which fell over the paper like black streams. From these he selected one, wrapped it up, and gave it to the lad, whose eyes had filled with tears. He kissed the packet, put it in his pocket, and said in a voice of emotion, “O, Mr. Clym, how good you are to me!”

text: The youths and grown men made ready to leave the property, and Elowen wandered back to her seat beside the hearth. She had sunk deep into quiet reverie, but the spell did not hold long. Barely a few minutes had passed when Tobin, the teenage boy who’d come earlier to ask permission to use the outbuilding, returned with its key to the back kitchen. Elowen caught the soft scuff of his boots on the flagstones, pulled open the door leading to the entry passage, and called, “Tobin, come here.”
source_text: The lads and men prepared to leave the premises, and Eustacia returned to her fireside. She was immersed in thought, but not for long. In a few minutes the lad Charley, who had come to ask permission to use the place, returned with the key to the kitchen. Eustacia heard him, and opening the door into the passage said, “Charley, come here.”

text: If any spark of satisfaction could pierce the taut, frayed mood she was tangled in, it flickered to life when she spotted a matching lamp glow bobbing near Kael Thorne’s cottage barely two minutes later. They’d struck a bargain that he would hold vigil at this exact time each evening, on call if she needed emergency aid, and this quick response was irrefutable proof he’d clung to their arrangement unwaveringly. Four hours out from that moment, at the stroke of midnight, he would be waiting with his cart to carry her to Brineport, just as they’d planned days prior.
source_text: She was gratified, if gratification were possible to such a mood, by seeing a similar light in the vicinity of Wildeve’s residence a minute or two later. Having agreed to keep watch at this hour every night, in case she should require assistance, this promptness proved how strictly he had held to his word. Four hours after the present time, that is, at midnight, he was to be ready to drive her to Budmouth, as prearranged.

text: There was no such thing as a single, official time of day across Egdon. What counted as the correct time at any given second depended on which of the conflicting local systems the nearby hamlet followed: some of these systems had split off from a single shared origin years prior, while others had always operated entirely independently. West Egdon went by the clock at Blooms-End, and East Egdon stuck to the time kept at the Quiet Woman Inn. Grandfer Cantle’s pocket watch had been the trusted standard for a lot of folks back in the day, but as he’d grown older, people stopped putting as much stock in its accuracy. As a result, when the mummers converged here from scattered points all across the heath, every one of them carried their own set of rules for what counted as early or late, so they all agreed to wait just a bit longer as a compromise.
source_text: On Egdon there was no absolute hour of the day. The time at any moment was a number of varying doctrines professed by the different hamlets, some of them having originally grown up from a common root, and then become divided by secession, some having been alien from the beginning. West Egdon believed in Blooms-End time, East Egdon in the time of the Quiet Woman Inn. Grandfer Cantle’s watch had numbered many followers in years gone by, but since he had grown older faiths were shaken. Thus, the mummers having gathered hither from scattered points each came with his own tenets on early and late; and they waited a little longer as a compromise.

text: He walked her through every detail of Clym’s debilitating condition and the sparse, isolated rhythm his daily life had fallen into, before shifting the conversation to Thomasin and gently noting the quiet, unmissable sadness that seemed to hang over all her days lately. “Look, ma’am, you can take my word for it,” he said, “there’s no kinder, more useful thing you could do for either of them than to drop by their homes and make yourself comfortable like you belong there, even if you get a small, cold rebuff the first time you show up.”
source_text: He gave her a full account of Clym’s affliction, and of the state in which he was living; then, referring to Thomasin, touched gently upon the apparent sadness of her days. “Now, ma’am, depend upon it,” he said, “you couldn’t do a better thing for either of ’em than to make yourself at home in their houses, even if there should be a little rebuff at first.”

text: Anyone watching Elara could tell that, for all the road she’d traveled, her inner, contemplative life felt strikingly full and cohesive. The quiet logic of her temperament, and the ways unyielding circumstance had bounded its scope, was almost legible in every small movement she made. There was a quiet grandeur underpinning every tilt of her wrist or shift of her weight, though none of her actions read as openly majestic; a core of unshakable self-possession humming beneath the surface, even when she seemed tentative and unsure. Just as the light, springy stride she’d carried in her youth had grown heavy and muted by the passing years, the bright, unapologetic pride in living that had once been her birthright had been stunted before it could fully bloom, worn thin by the endless, unrelenting demands of getting by.
source_text: One could see that, as far as it had gone, her life was very complete on its reflective side. The philosophy of her nature, and its limitation by circumstances, was almost written in her movements. They had a majestic foundation, though they were far from being majestic; and they had a ground-work of assurance, but they were not assured. As her once elastic walk had become deadened by time, so had her natural pride of life been hindered in its blooming by her necessities.

text: The woman reached into the workbasket beside her on the window seat and pulled out a paper packet of pins— the old-fashioned, long, yellowed kind whose heads almost always popped right off the first time you used them. She began shoving these pins into the small effigy from every direction, with a vicious, straining energy that looked almost excruciating to expend. She likely inserted as many as fifty that way: some into the wax model’s head, some into its shoulders, some into its torso, others pushed upward right through the soles of its feet, until the entire little figure was thoroughly riddled with pins.
source_text: From her workbasket in the window-seat the woman took a paper of pins, of the old long and yellow sort, whose heads were disposed to come off at their first usage. These she began to thrust into the image in all directions, with apparently excruciating energy. Probably as many as fifty were thus inserted, some into the head of the wax model, some into the shoulders, some into the trunk, some upwards through the soles of the feet, till the figure was completely permeated with pins.

text: “You mustn’t shrink back from me, dear Clym,” Thomasin said earnestly, that soft, sweet voice of hers wrapping around a hurting soul like fresh air drifting into the suffocating dark of a Black Hole. “Nothing about you could ever shock me, or drive me away. I’ve come here before, you see—you just don’t remember it.”
source_text: “You must not shrink from me, dear Clym,” said Thomasin earnestly, in that sweet voice of hers which came to a sufferer like fresh air into a Black Hole. “Nothing in you can ever shock me or drive me away. I have been here before, but you don’t remember it.”

text: To Kael, Elowen was no longer the untouchable goddess of his quiet fantasies, but a flesh-and-blood woman: someone he would fight for, stand by, lift up, even endure public slander to protect. Now that the heady rush of his infatuation had faded to a calmer, clearer frame of mind, he would have preferred a far less hasty march to the altar; but the die was cast, and he was determined to see the wager through to its end. Whether Elowen would add her name to the long list of souls who love too fiercely to love enduringly and well, their impending nuptials were certain to provide a swift, unvarnished answer.
source_text: Eustacia was now no longer the goddess but the woman to him, a being to fight for, support, help, be maligned for. Now that he had reached a cooler moment he would have preferred a less hasty marriage; but the card was laid, and he determined to abide by the game. Whether Eustacia was to add one other to the list of those who love too hotly to love long and well, the forthcoming event was certainly a ready way of proving.

text: Mrs. Yeobright spoke in a steady, calm tone, yet the fierce feeling thrumming beneath her words was perfectly obvious to someone who knew her as deeply as her own son did. He made no reply. Written across his face was that quiet despair of ever being understood, the kind that arises when the person opposing you is inherently incapable of grasping your line of reasoning—reasoning that, even under the most favorable conditions, is far too blunt a vessel to carry the delicate, fine-grained nuance of the point you are trying to make.
source_text: Mrs. Yeobright spoke calmly, but the force of feeling behind the words was but too apparent to one who knew her as well as her son did. He did not answer. There was in his face that hopelessness of being understood which comes when the objector is constitutionally beyond the reach of a logic that, even under favouring conditions, is almost too coarse a vehicle for the subtlety of the argument.

text: “I really do have to head to Blooms-End soon,” he went on, “and I’m convinced it’s best I go by myself.” He picked up his leggings and gloves, only to drop them again a moment later, adding, “Since dinner’s going to be so late today, there’s no sense trekking back to the heath now. I’ll work in the garden until evening, when the air’s cooler, then walk over to Blooms-End. I’m quite sure if I make just a small first gesture, Mother will be happy to let the whole past quarrel fade. It’ll be fairly late by the time I get home, after all—I can’t make the round trip in under an hour and a half. But you won’t mind that for one evening, will you, dear? Why do you look so far away? What’s occupying your thoughts right now?”
source_text: “I must certainly go to Blooms-End soon,” he continued, “and I think I had better go alone.” He picked up his leggings and gloves, threw them down again, and added, “As dinner will be so late today I will not go back to the heath, but work in the garden till the evening, and then, when it will be cooler, I will walk to Blooms-End. I am quite sure that if I make a little advance Mother will be willing to forget all. It will be rather late before I can get home, as I shall not be able to do the distance either way in less than an hour and a half. But you will not mind for one evening, dear? What are you thinking of to make you look so abstracted?”

text: “Ah, now, I did once, yeah,” said Timothy Fairway, planting one leg a little more firmly under his weight. “I did know a man just like that. But only the one time, you hear?” He raked a hand thoroughly around his throat, clearing it as if he wanted to make sure no one would mistake his words on account of his thick, gravelly voice. “Yep, I knew a man just like that,” he said.
source_text: “Well, now, I did once,” said Timothy Fairway, adding more firmness to one of his legs. “I did know of such a man. But only once, mind.” He gave his throat a thorough rake round, as if it were the duty of every person not to be mistaken through thickness of voice. “Yes, I knew of such a man,” he said.

text: For young men and women in small rural communities, church attendance is the standard social gambit in such circumstances. In any ordinary village or country town, you can reliably count on every native returned home for the holidays who hasn’t lost the taste for seeing and being seen—whether from old age or world-weariness—to appear in some pew on either Christmas Day or the nearest adjacent Sunday, glowing with quiet hope, faint self-consciousness, and the crisp sheen of new clothing. As a result, the Christmas morning congregation is little more than a Madame Tussaud’s exhibition of the neighbourhood’s most notable native sons and daughters. This is where the young woman left neglected at home all year can slip in quietly to observe how the former lover who forgot her has changed, peering at him over the edge of her prayer book and clinging to the hope that he will feel a flicker of renewed fidelity once the allure of new, unfamiliar diversions has faded. It is also where a relative newcomer like Eustacia might come to study the face of a local man who left the village before she arrived, weighing whether it would be worth cultivating a friendship with his parents during his next absence, so that she might make his acquaintance when he returns home again.
source_text: The customary expedient of provincial girls and men in such circumstances is churchgoing. In an ordinary village or country town one can safely calculate that, either on Christmas day or the Sunday contiguous, any native home for the holidays, who has not through age or ennui lost the appetite for seeing and being seen, will turn up in some pew or other, shining with hope, self-consciousness, and new clothes. Thus the congregation on Christmas morning is mostly a Tussaud collection of celebrities who have been born in the neighbourhood. Hither the mistress, left neglected at home all the year, can steal and observe the development of the returned lover who has forgotten her, and think as she watches him over her prayer book that he may throb with a renewed fidelity when novelties have lost their charm. And hither a comparatively recent settler like Eustacia may betake herself to scrutinize the person of a native son who left home before her advent upon the scene, and consider if the friendship of his parents be worth cultivating during his next absence in order to secure a knowledge of him on his next return.

text: As the mummers neared the front of the house, they could tell that music and dancing were unfolding with bright, bustling energy inside. Now and then a long, low note from the serpent—the lead wind instrument for gatherings of this sort—carried farther out over the heath than the faint, thin treble tones, reaching their ears entirely on its own; next, an uncommonly loud footfall from a dancer would drift across the same stretch of open land to meet them. When they drew closer still, these fragmented bits of sound slotted together, and they recognized the distinctive peaks of the tune known as “Nancy’s Fancy.”
source_text: As they drew nearer to the front of the house the mummers became aware that music and dancing were briskly flourishing within. Every now and then a long low note from the serpent, which was the chief wind instrument played at these times, advanced further into the heath than the thin treble part, and reached their ears alone; and next a more than usual loud tread from a dancer would come the same way. With nearer approach these fragmentary sounds became pieced together, and were found to be the salient points of the tune called “Nancy’s Fancy.”

text: Mr. Venn’s so tall he banged his head right on the low beam when he took a little skip as he passed underneath it. Mrs. Venn rushed over immediately, all flustered and worried, and she’s pressing her palm to his head now, feeling around to check if a lump has formed. And now everyone’s laughing again like absolutely nothing out of the ordinary just went down.
source_text: “Mr. Venn is so tall that he knocked his head against the beam in gieing a skip as he passed under. Mrs. Venn has run up quite frightened and now she’s put her hand to his head to feel if there’s a lump. And now they be all laughing again as if nothing had happened.”

text: One day, mid-way through this structured training routine, she was bent low clearing twigs, fern stalks, and other small bits of debris from the child’s path, so the little one’s unsteady journey wouldn’t be cut short by some supposedly insurmountable barrier barely a quarter of an inch high, when she jolted in alarm to find a mounted man nearly right beside her. The soft, natural ground cover had muffled the horse’s hoofbeats so thoroughly she had not detected his approach at all. The rider, who was Venn, waved his hat aloft and bowed gallantly.
source_text: Once, when engaged in this system of training, and stooping to remove bits of stick, fern-stalks, and other such fragments from the child’s path, that the journey might not be brought to an untimely end by some insuperable barrier a quarter of an inch high, she was alarmed by discovering that a man on horseback was almost close beside her, the soft natural carpet having muffled the horse’s tread. The rider, who was Venn, waved his hat in the air and bowed gallantly.

text: “I always am!” snapped Thorne, sharp with irritation. He flicked the glowworms off the fern frond he’d collected them on, his fingers shaking as he arranged them in a neat ring on the flat granite slab, leaving a bare hollow at the center for the dice cup to land. The thirteen tiny, pulsing lights spilled a faint, eerie phosphorescence over the spot. The game picked back up at once. This was the stretch of the year when glowworms burn at their most vivid, and the glow they cast was more than enough for their purpose; on nights like these, you could easily make out the scrawled handwriting of a letter by the light of only two or three of the insects.
source_text: “I always am!” said Wildeve angrily. And shaking the glowworms from the leaf he ranged them with a trembling hand in a circle on the stone, leaving a space in the middle for the descent of the dice-box, over which the thirteen tiny lamps threw a pale phosphoric shine. The game was again renewed. It happened to be that season of the year at which glowworms put forth their greatest brilliancy, and the light they yielded was more than ample for the purpose, since it is possible on such nights to read the handwriting of a letter by the light of two or three.

text: “Things like this never happen without good reason,” said Aunt Maren, her voice sharp with affront. “It’s a grievous snub to me and my entire household; when word gets out, we’re all in for a deeply unpleasant stretch of time. How can she possibly hold her head up around her friends come tomorrow? It’s a terrible wrong, one I cannot readily forgive. It may even cast a lasting stain on her good character.”
source_text: “Such things don’t happen for nothing,” said the aunt. “It is a great slight to me and my family; and when it gets known there will be a very unpleasant time for us. How can she look her friends in the face tomorrow? It is a very great injury, and one I cannot easily forgive. It may even reflect on her character.”

text: Once his wits returned to him he pushed himself up, clutching his snuffed-out lantern in one hand, and picked his way toward the main thoroughfare. When he reached it he paused. The hush of the night hung thick over all the moor save for a single direction: the path leading up toward Bramble Tor. From that quarter came the faint clatter of light carriage wheels, and moments later he spotted two carriage lanterns bobbing down the slope. Voss ducked behind a gorse bush and held perfectly still, waiting.
source_text: When he had come to himself he also arose, and, with the extinguished lantern in his hand, went towards the highroad. On reaching it he stood still. The silence of night pervaded the whole heath except in one direction; and that was towards Mistover. There he could hear the noise of light wheels, and presently saw two carriagelamps descending the hill. Wildeve screened himself under a bush and waited.

text: As Mrs. Yeobright drew close to the inn and was just about to step through its entrance, she caught sight of a horse-drawn rig some two hundred yards beyond the building, moving steadily toward her, with a man walking at its side carrying a lit lantern. She recognized him almost at once as the reddleman who had previously come inquiring after her. Rather than entering the inn as she’d intended, she walked straight past its doors and headed toward the van.
source_text: When Mrs. Yeobright had drawn near to the inn, and was about to enter, she saw a horse and vehicle some two hundred yards beyond it, coming towards her, a man walking alongside with a lantern in his hand. It was soon evident that this was the reddleman who had inquired for her. Instead of entering the inn at once, she walked by it and towards the van.

text: Anyone who had wandered through Thornhollow Crossing shortly before eleven on the morning fixed for the wedding would have observed that, while Hale’s cottage held a comparative stillness, sounds of brisk, eager activity spilled over from the dwelling of his nearest neighbor, Gideon Rook. The din was composed chiefly of footsteps, crunching sharply back and forth across the sanded floorboards inside the cottage. Only one man was visible out of doors, and he appeared to have missed the time of an expected appointment, for he hurried up the flagstone path to the door, flipped the latch up with a quick flick of his wrist, and stepped inside without so much as a knock to announce himself.
source_text: Anybody who had passed through Blooms-End about eleven o’clock on the morning fixed for the wedding would have found that, while Yeobright’s house was comparatively quiet, sounds denoting great activity came from the dwelling of his nearest neighbour, Timothy Fairway. It was chiefly a noise of feet, briskly crunching hither and thither over the sanded floor within. One man only was visible outside, and he seemed to be later at an appointment than he had intended to be, for he hastened up to the door, lifted the latch, and walked in without ceremony.
