text: “You poor girl,” Wildeve said, openly moved by her distress, until he finally acted on the impulse to take her hand. “It is unbearably cruel, that you have been caught up in this tangled mess when you have done nothing to deserve such hardship. You were never meant for these sorts of sorrowful scenes. The greater fault is mine. If only I could have spared you every last bit of this suffering.”
source_text: “Poor girl!” said Wildeve, visibly affected at her distress, and at last led on so far as to take her hand. “It is hard, when you have done nothing to deserve it, that you should have got involved in such a web as this. You were not made for these sad scenes. I am to blame most. If I could only have saved you from it all!”

text: A soft, guileless, sun-kissed rural face came into view, tucked amid a tangle of honeyed auburn curls. It fell somewhere on the spectrum between winsome and breathtakingly lovely. Though her lids were shut tight, it took no effort to picture the warm, bright spark that must dwell in those eyes, the perfect finishing touch to the gentle, glowing features that framed them. Hope was the very foundation of her expression, yet stretched across it now, thin as an alien veil, was a faint layer of worry and sorrow. The sorrow had settled there so recently it had not yet dimmed the fresh, dewy glow of her skin; for now, it only lent a quiet gravity to features it might one day wear away entirely. The vivid crimson of her lips had not had time to fade, and at this moment it seemed even richer, set against the unusual paleness of her cheeks, which normally held a faint, fleeting rose flush. Every so often her lips would part slightly, a soft mumble of half-formed speech slipping out. She looked as if she had stepped straight out of a pastoral sonnet, as if she ought only to be seen framed by the lilt of verse and the soft swell of gentle melody.
source_text: A fair, sweet, and honest country face was revealed, reposing in a nest of wavy chestnut hair. It was between pretty and beautiful. Though her eyes were closed, one could easily imagine the light necessarily shining in them as the culmination of the luminous workmanship around. The groundwork of the face was hopefulness; but over it now lay like a foreign substance a film of anxiety and grief. The grief had been there so shortly as to have abstracted nothing of the bloom, and had as yet but given a dignity to what it might eventually undermine. The scarlet of her lips had not had time to abate, and just now it appeared still more intense by the absence of the neighbouring and more transient colour of her cheek. The lips frequently parted, with a murmur of words. She seemed to belong rightly to a madrigal—to require viewing through rhyme and harmony.

text: Right then, the faint outline of a man materialized against the low-hanging sky stretched over the valley, just beyond the outer edge of the pool. He circled the water’s perimeter and jumped onto the bank directly at her side. A soft, low laugh slipped out of her—only the third utterance the girl had allowed herself all night. The first, when she’d stood atop Rainbarrow, had thrummed with unspoken anxiety; the second, out on the ridge, had been sharp with impatience; this one bloomed with unmistakeable, triumphant joy. She didn’t say a word, just let her bright, joy-filled gaze linger on him, as if he were some extraordinary, wondrous thing she had conjured whole out of total chaos.
source_text: Thereupon the contour of a man became dimly visible against the low-reaching sky over the valley, beyond the outer margin of the pool. He came round it and leapt upon the bank beside her. A low laugh escaped her—the third utterance which the girl had indulged in tonight. The first, when she stood upon Rainbarrow, had expressed anxiety; the second, on the ridge, had expressed impatience; the present was one of triumphant pleasure. She let her joyous eyes rest upon him without speaking, as upon some wondrous thing she had created out of chaos.

text: Lila had a point, for the most part. Don’t take this to heart as a cruelty, I beg you— I am only speaking what I know to be unvarnished fact. You’ve treated her with such consistent kindness, and none of us have let that slip from memory. But since she has made clear she has no desire to become your wife of her own accord, the matter is settled entirely independent of what I might personally prefer.
source_text: “She was in a measure right. You must not take this unkindly—I merely state it as a truth. You have been good to her, and we do not forget it. But as she was unwilling on her own account to be your wife, that settles the point without my wishes being concerned.”

text: “Blimey, I’m heading over to them, Timothy—the newly married pair!” Granfer Cantle said, voice bright with newly sparked enthusiasm as he spun on his heel to go. “I’ll drop by tonight and sing them a proper wedding song, eh? You know that’s exactly the sort of goofy, warm thing I do, and they’ll take it exactly that way too. My rendition of ‘Down in Cupid’s Gardens’ was a huge hit across four local parishes back in the day, but I’ve got other songs just as good, even better, honestly. What do you say to my
source_text: “Begad, I’ll go to ’em, Timothy—to the married pair!” said Granfer Cantle in an encouraged voice, and starting round briskly. “I’ll go to ’em tonight and sing a wedding song, hey? ’Tis like me to do so, you know; and they’d see it as such. My ‘Down in Cupid’s Gardens’ was well liked in four; still, I’ve got others as good, and even better. What do you say to my

text: Women working in the theater have pulled off plenty of remarkable wins for themselves over the years, all while dressed in stage costume. Even looking past the most high-achieving among them—like the lovely performer who played Polly Peachum early in the previous century, and another who portrayed Lydia Languish at the start of this one, both of whom secured not just devoted love but a duke’s coronet to go with it—scores of other actresses have managed the deeply satisfying base win of earning affection from almost anyone they set their hearts on. But the woman playing the Turkish Knight was barred from even trying for that kind of joy, held back by the fluttering costume ribbons she didn’t dare push aside.
source_text: Women have done much for themselves in histrionic dress. To look far below those who, like a certain fair personator of Polly Peachum early in the last century, and another of Lydia Languish early in this,[1] have won not only love but ducal coronets into the bargain, whole shoals of them have reached to the initial satisfaction of getting love almost whence they would. But the Turkish Knight was denied even the chance of achieving this by the fluttering ribbons which she dared not brush aside.

text: The thread of their conversation had captivated her completely. A sharp, quick-witted young man was bound for that windswept, empty moor from Paris, of all places on earth so starkly unlike the remote uplands. It might as well have been a man stepping down straight from heaven. Stranger still, the moor’s lifelong residents had instinctively paired her and this stranger in their thoughts, convinced the two were made for each other.
source_text: The subject of their discourse had been keenly interesting to her. A young and clever man was coming into that lonely heath from, of all contrasting places in the world, Paris. It was like a man coming from heaven. More singular still, the heathmen had instinctively coupled her and this man together in their minds as a pair born for each other.

text: He ran until he was completely winded, and once he’d caught his breath a little, boldness crept in. He ambled along at an unhurried pace, crooning in a rough, worn old voice a ditty about a sailor lad, a pretty lass, and the hoard of bright gold waiting for them down the line. Halfway through the tune, the child came to an abrupt halt. From a pit dug into the base of the hill ahead, a light glowed out; a cloud of swirling fine dust billowed from the spot, accompanied by a sharp, wet smacking noise.
source_text: He ran until he was out of breath, and then, becoming more courageous, walked leisurely along, singing in an old voice a little song about a sailor-boy and a fair one, and bright gold in store. In the middle of this the child stopped—from a pit under the hill ahead of him shone a light, whence proceeded a cloud of floating dust and a smacking noise.

text: On the crisp evening of the twelfth of October, his thoughts of Elowen pressed sharp and unyielding against the edges of his mind. Faint reverberations of those sunlit days when they had traded soft, honeyed nothings from dawn till dusk drifted to him like the low, muffled roar of an ocean shore he’d left hours and miles behind his journey. “Surely,” he muttered to the empty air, “she could have mustered the courage to reach out to me before this, and tell me plainly just what Kael had meant to her.”
source_text: On the evening of the fifth November his thoughts of Eustacia were intense. Echoes from those past times when they had exchanged tender words all the day long came like the diffused murmur of a seashore left miles behind. “Surely,” he said, “she might have brought herself to communicate with me before now, and confess honestly what Wildeve was to her.”

text: Yet Elowen’s attention was not fixed on the huddled group perched on the settle’s slatted seat. One countenance stood out with sharp, unmissable clarity against the aged, sun-darkened oak of the settle’s high back. The man leaning his shoulder against the settle’s far outer edge was Thaddeus Hale, or Thad, as everyone in the parish called him; she would have recognized that profile among a thousand. The small two-foot span of space around him looked like a snippet pulled straight from Rembrandt’s most dramatic, shadow-drenched work. There was an odd, magnetic pull to the loitering man’s presence: even though his full form was lit plainly enough for onlookers to make out every detail, the eye could not help but fix solely on his face.
source_text: It was, however, not with those who sat in the settle that Eustacia was concerned. A face showed itself with marked distinctness against the dark-tanned wood of the upper part. The owner, who was leaning against the settle’s outer end, was Clement Yeobright, or Clym, as he was called here; she knew it could be nobody else. The spectacle constituted an area of two feet in Rembrandt’s intensest manner. A strange power in the lounger’s appearance lay in the fact that, though his whole figure was visible, the observer’s eye was only aware of his face.

text: Now that the initial hot surge of his anger had faded, he was unwilling to assume her bond with Wildeve was anything worse than an imprudent, overly casual friendship, since she had never shown any hint of disloyalty in her bearing or conduct. Once he accepted that reality, he no longer felt forced to interpret her actions toward his mother through the grimmest, most unforgiving possible lens.
source_text: Now that the first flush of his anger had paled he was disinclined to ascribe to her more than an indiscreet friendship with Wildeve, for there had not appeared in her manner the signs of dishonour. And this once admitted, an absolutely dark interpretation of her act towards his mother was no longer forced upon him.

text: It stands to reason Thomasin had been fully convinced; just a few days later, when Clym wandered into a patch of the heath he hadn’t set foot in recently, Humphrey, who was working the spot at the time, said to him, “I’m glad to see Mrs. Wildeve and Venn have patched things up again, from the look of it.”
source_text: It is to be supposed that Thomasin was convinced; for a few days after this, when Clym strayed into a part of the heath that he had not lately visited, Humphrey, who was at work there, said to him, “I am glad to see that Mrs. Wildeve and Venn have made it up again, seemingly.”

text: “Be that as it may,” her aunt chimed in, her cheer forced just enough to catch at the edges, “I shall call on you this afternoon, and bring the almond layer cake I set to cool last night. If Kael has returned from his overland trip by then, he will likely come along too. I want to show Mr. Hale that I bear him no hard feelings. Let what’s past stay buried. Well, heaven bless you, dear! Oh, I don’t put any stock in dusty old superstitions, but I may as well follow through.” She tossed a soft linen slipper at the girl’s retreating back; the girl turned, smiled, and continued on her way down the path.
source_text: “At any rate,” added her aunt with forced cheerfulness, “I shall call upon you this afternoon, and bring the cake with me. If Clym has returned by that time he will perhaps come too. I wish to show Mr. Wildeve that I bear him no ill-will. Let the past be forgotten. Well, God bless you! There, I don’t believe in old superstitions, but I’ll do it.” She threw a slipper at the retreating figure of the girl, who turned, smiled, and went on again.

text: “There’s no point in saying that,” his mother replied, her face tight with weary, impatient grief. “You have no idea how wretched these past weeks have been for us, Clym. You can’t fathom how deeply humiliating a scandal like that is for a woman. You haven’t lived through the sleepless nights under this roof, the sharp, almost bitter words we’ve exchanged ever since that Fifth of November. I pray I never have to endure seven weeks like that a second time. Tamsin hasn’t set foot past the threshold this whole time, and I’ve been too ashamed to meet a single person’s eye. And now you blame me for letting her take the only step that can set this whole ugly trouble right?”
source_text: “It is useless to say that,” his mother answered with an impatient look of sorrow. “You don’t know how bad it has been here with us all these weeks, Clym. You don’t know what a mortification anything of that sort is to a woman. You don’t know the sleepless nights we’ve had in this house, and the almost bitter words that have passed between us since that Fifth of November. I hope never to pass seven such weeks again. Tamsin has not gone outside the door, and I have been ashamed to look anybody in the face; and now you blame me for letting her do the only thing that can be done to set that trouble straight.”

text: I only lit that fire because I was dead bored, and figured I’d get a little rush by summoning you here so I could gloat over you, just like the Witch of Endor called up Samuel. I’d decided for certain you’d show up, and here you are! I’ve proven I’ve got the power to make that happen. A mile and a half to get here to me, and another mile and a half back to your house once you go—three whole miles trekked in the dark, all on my account. Doesn’t that prove how much sway I hold?
source_text: “I merely lit that fire because I was dull, and thought I would get a little excitement by calling you up and triumphing over you as the Witch of Endor called up Samuel. I determined you should come; and you have come! I have shown my power. A mile and half hither, and a mile and half back again to your home—three miles in the dark for me. Have I not shown my power?”

text: She turned toward the fire, which had been fed on turf. The tall heap of ash that turf fires always leave looked dark and lifeless on its surface, but when she raked through the mass with a shovel, the inner core glowed with deep, smoldering red heat. She fetched a few lumps of fresh turf from the chimney nook and stacked them carefully over the glow, and the fire immediately flared brighter. Gripping the small effigy of Eustacia she had fashioned with a pair of tongs, she held it directly in the rising heat, watching as it began to slowly char and waste away. As she stood there focused on the task, a quiet, continuous murmur slipped from between her lips.
source_text: She turned to the fire. It had been of turf; and though the high heap of ashes which turf fires produce was somewhat dark and dead on the outside, upon raking it abroad with the shovel the inside of the mass showed a glow of red heat. She took a few pieces of fresh turf from the chimney-corner and built them together over the glow, upon which the fire brightened. Seizing with the tongs the image that she had made of Eustacia, she held it in the heat, and watched it as it began to waste slowly away. And while she stood thus engaged there came from between her lips a murmur of words.

text: “I couldn’t bring myself to do it back then, and I still can’t now. There are moments when five short minutes plant more deep, lingering bitterness than a person can shake off over their entire lifetime, and that might well be what’s happened here.” She paused for a few seconds, then added, “If you’d never come back to your hometown, Clym, what a blessing that would’ve been for you… It’s altered the fates of—”
source_text: “I could not do it then, nor can I now. Sometimes more bitterness is sown in five minutes than can be got rid of in a whole life; and that may be the case here.” She paused a few moments, and added, “If you had never returned to your native place, Clym, what a blessing it would have been for you!... It has altered the destinies of——”

text: Once her grandfather had vanished around the lane’s mossy bend, Elowen continued down the moor path on autopilot, her thoughts no longer tangled up in lingering frustrations with Kael and his sharp-tongued mother. Thorne, for all his endless grumbling about the cruelty of his lot, had been plucked straight out of the mire by fate and set down in golden, unlooked-for good fortune. Eleven thousand pounds! To every soul that called Bramble Moor home, that sum made him a man of substantial, enviable wealth. To Elowen, too, it was more than enough: enough to afford all those small, soft comforts Kael had scorned as frivolous and indulgent whenever his rigid, austere moods took hold. She’d never cared for coin for its own sake, but she ached for the doors it could unlock, and the elegant, unharried life she could picture wrapping around Thorne made him feel newly, sharply fascinating to her. She found herself replaying their chance meeting that morning, how sharp and well-tailored his coat had looked, likely his very best, worn without a care for snags from the moor’s gorse and brambles. And after that, her mind drifted to the soft, attentive way he’d held himself around her, the quiet warmth in his gaze when he’d thought no onlooker could see.
source_text: When her grandfather was gone Eustacia went on her way mechanically; but her thoughts were no longer concerning her mother-in-law and Clym. Wildeve, notwithstanding his complaints against his fate, had been seized upon by destiny and placed in the sunshine once more. Eleven thousand pounds! From every Egdon point of view he was a rich man. In Eustacia’s eyes, too, it was an ample sum—one sufficient to supply those wants of hers which had been stigmatized by Clym in his more austere moods as vain and luxurious. Though she was no lover of money she loved what money could bring; and the new accessories she imagined around him clothed Wildeve with a great deal of interest. She recollected now how quietly well-dressed he had been that morning—he had probably put on his newest suit, regardless of damage by briars and thorns. And then she thought of his manner towards herself.

text: If that stone had struck Eustacia square in the chest, her heart could not have jolted harder than it did just then. She’d considered it possible that such a signal might come in reply to the one Charley had sent unwittingly, but she’d never dreamed it would arrive this soon. Wildeve was so unnervingly quick to respond! But how could he possibly imagine she would want to deliberately pick up their old trysts again right now? Two conflicting urges warred in her: the impulse to flee the spot entirely, and the pull to stay where she was. The latter won out, but only just; she did not even dare climb the earthen bank to peek over its edge. She stayed perfectly still, not twitching a single facial muscle or lifting her eyes an inch. If she tilted her face upward, the fire burning atop the bank would illuminate her features, and Wildeve might be watching down from the other side, ready to recognize her.
source_text: Had Eustacia received the stone full in the bosom her heart could not have given a more decided thump. She had thought of the possibility of such a signal in answer to that which had been unwittingly given by Charley; but she had not expected it yet. How prompt Wildeve was! Yet how could he think her capable of deliberately wishing to renew their assignations now? An impulse to leave the spot, a desire to stay, struggled within her; and the desire held its own. More than that it did not do, for she refrained even from ascending the bank and looking over. She remained motionless, not disturbing a muscle of her face or raising her eyes; for were she to turn up her face the fire on the bank would shine upon it, and Wildeve might be looking down.

text: An undercurrent of unease laced his voice, a quiet uncertainty about his right to claim the role of devoted suitor, a nagging worry he was being unfair to someone whose preferences aligned with his own so seldom, and only on the smallest of things. She caught the unspoken fear right away, and spoke in a soft, warm murmur thick with urgent certainty: “Don’t get me wrong, Elias—even though I’ve always dreamed of Bordeaux, I love you for who you are, nothing more. Being your wife and settling there would feel like pure bliss to me, but I’d sooner share a tiny, isolated cottage here with you than have anyone else as my partner, no matter the life they could give me. Either path is a gift, a better fortune than I ever thought I deserved. That’s the whole honest truth, messy and unfiltered as it is.”
source_text: There was that in his tone which implied distrust of his position as a solicitous lover, a doubt if he were acting fairly towards one whose tastes touched his own only at rare and infrequent points. She saw his meaning, and whispered, in a low, full accent of eager assurance, “Don’t mistake me, Clym—though I should like Paris, I love you for yourself alone. To be your wife and live in Paris would be heaven to me; but I would rather live with you in a hermitage here than not be yours at all. It is gain to me either way, and very great gain. There’s my too candid confession.”

text: Yet the gnawing terror of the unknowable had lifted. The faint, persistent misgivings she’d carried about her future as an abandoned wife had faded entirely. The worst fate she’d once trembled to guess at was now nothing more than a cold, practical fact, a bounded hardship with no hidden shocks left to offer. Her dearest priority, her small daughter Lila, still remained at the center of her world. Her grief held no spark of defiance, no sharp edge of fury at her circumstances; when a sorrow wears that quiet, unresisting shape, a frayed, shaken spirit will almost always settle into gentle stillness.
source_text: But the horrors of the unknown had passed. Vague misgivings about her future as a deserted wife were at an end. The worst had once been matter of trembling conjecture; it was now matter of reason only, a limited badness. Her chief interest, the little Eustacia, still remained. There was humility in her grief, no defiance in her attitude; and when this is the case a shaken spirit is apt to be stilled.

text: Kael and Elowen, in their cozy croft at Bramble Hollow, just past West Thorne Moor, were settling into a quiet routine that felt like pure joy to the pair of them. For now, the wide moor and its shifting weather might as well have been blotted entirely from their view. They were wrapped in a sort of glowing haze that softened every harsh edge of the world around them, gilding even the plainest little things with a warm, soft radiance. On rainy days they were giddy, because the weather gave them the perfect excuse to linger indoors side by side the whole day long; when the sun shone, they were just as delighted, able to perch together on the grassy slopes above their cottage for hours on end. They were like those paired binary stars that spin endlessly around one another, so closely intertwined that from far off they look like a single point of light. The utter seclusion of their life together only deepened the attention they paid to one another; even so, some onlookers might have noted it had a downside, burning through the soft warmth of their mutual fondness at a startlingly fast, wasteful pace. Kael felt no worry on his own account; but the memory of Elowen’s old remark about how quickly love could flicker out, a line she seemed to have entirely forgotten now, made him turn the thought over in his mind now and then, and he flinched away from the quiet truth that even paradise was never built to last forever.
source_text: Clym and Eustacia, in their little house at Alderworth, beyond East Egdon, were living on with a monotony which was delightful to them. The heath and changes of weather were quite blotted out from their eyes for the present. They were enclosed in a sort of luminous mist, which hid from them surroundings of any inharmonious colour, and gave to all things the character of light. When it rained they were charmed, because they could remain indoors together all day with such a show of reason; when it was fine they were charmed, because they could sit together on the hills. They were like those double stars which revolve round and round each other, and from a distance appear to be one. The absolute solitude in which they lived intensified their reciprocal thoughts; yet some might have said that it had the disadvantage of consuming their mutual affections at a fearfully prodigal rate. Yeobright did not fear for his own part; but recollection of Eustacia’s old speech about the evanescence of love, now apparently forgotten by her, sometimes caused him to ask himself a question; and he recoiled at the thought that the quality of finiteness was not foreign to Eden.

text: Left to herself at last, Elowen peeled off her sopping outer garments, lifted the drowsy infant up the creaking stairs to tuck it into Kael’s bed, then trundled back down to the ground-floor parlor, where she stoked the hearth into a brighter blaze and knelt to dry her chilled, damp skin. Flames soon roared up the flue, wrapping the small room in soft golden warmth that felt twice as welcoming against the drumming rage of the gale beyond the walls, which nipped and rattled at the windowpanes and sent guttural, huffing sighs down the chimney that sounded like the low, menacing prologue to some grim unwritten tragedy.
source_text: Thomasin, being left alone, took off some of her wet garments, carried the baby upstairs to Clym’s bed, and then came down to the sitting-room again, where she made a larger fire, and began drying herself. The fire soon flared up the chimney, giving the room an appearance of comfort that was doubled by contrast with the drumming of the storm without, which snapped at the windowpanes and breathed into the chimney strange low utterances that seemed to be the prologue to some tragedy.

text: When the carriage rolled past the cluster of people who’d rushed out from the homestead, they cheered “Hurrah!” and waved wildly, bits of feather and down drifting from their hair, their sleeves, and the creases of their clothes with every gesture, while Grandfer Cantle’s watch fob seals danced gaily in the sunlight as he twirled in place. The carriage driver cast a haughty, dismissive stare over the crowd; he even treated the newly wedded pair themselves with a faint air of condescension, for what state but unrefined heathenry could people of any class, rich or poor, fall into when they were fated to live in such a remote, forgotten corner of the world as Egdon? Thomasin held no such air of superiority toward the group gathered at the door, her hand fluttering as quickly as a sparrow’s wing as she waved back, eyes glistening with tears as she asked Diggory if they ought to alight and speak to these kind neighbors. Venn, however, pointed out that every last one of them was coming to their house later that evening, so the stop was hardly necessary.
source_text: As the fly passed the group which had run out from the homestead they shouted “Hurrah!” and waved their hands; feathers and down floating from their hair, their sleeves, and the folds of their garments at every motion, and Grandfer Cantle’s seals dancing merrily in the sunlight as he twirled himself about. The driver of the fly turned a supercilious gaze upon them; he even treated the wedded pair themselves with something like condescension; for in what other state than heathen could people, rich or poor, exist who were doomed to abide in such a world’s end as Egdon? Thomasin showed no such superiority to the group at the door, fluttering her hand as quickly as a bird’s wing towards them, and asking Diggory, with tears in her eyes, if they ought not to alight and speak to these kind neighbours. Venn, however, suggested that, as they were all coming to the house in the evening, this was hardly necessary.

text: He often wandered the heath by himself, and on those walks the past would reach out with its cold, shadowed grip to hold him fast, forcing him to attend to its long-unspoken stories. His imagination would populate the stretch of moor with its earliest residents: long-forgotten Celtic clans moved along their old paths all around him, and he could nearly step into their world, meet their gaze, and watch them linger beside the rounded burial mounds that rose across the landscape, as unmarred and whole as the day they were first raised. The woad-stained tribespeople who had settled fertile, tilled lowlands were, next to the groups who had left their mark on this heath, like scribes writing on flimsy paper compared to those inscribing their words on durable parchment. The traces of the lowland settlers had been erased centuries ago by the plow, while the monuments of the heath dwellers still stood unbroken. Yet every last one of them had lived and died with no inkling of the wildly differing fates that awaited the things they left behind. The quiet truth of it struck him again: unforeseen forces shape how immortality is won and lost across the span of human history.
source_text: He frequently walked the heath alone, when the past seized upon him with its shadowy hand, and held him there to listen to its tale. His imagination would then people the spot with its ancient inhabitants—forgotten Celtic tribes trod their tracks about him, and he could almost live among them, look in their faces, and see them standing beside the barrows which swelled around, untouched and perfect as at the time of their erection. Those of the dyed barbarians who had chosen the cultivable tracts were, in comparison with those who had left their marks here, as writers on paper beside writers on parchment. Their records had perished long ago by the plough, while the works of these remained. Yet they all had lived and died unconscious of the different fates awaiting their relics. It reminded him that unforeseen factors operate in the evolution of immortality.

text: Her lips then parted, stirred first by a faint spark of anticipation, then more distinctly by a tremor of doubt. Every half-formed thought and fleeting flicker of feeling that crossed her features was picked out with perfect clarity by the light, not the smallest shift left unilluminated. It laid bare her guileless, transparent nature, as if the very current of her existence could be seen flowing soft and steady beneath her skin. In an instant, she grasped the full meaning of the scene before her.
source_text: The lips then parted with something of anticipation, something more of doubt; and her several thoughts and fractions of thoughts, as signalled by the changes on her face, were exhibited by the light to the utmost nicety. An ingenuous, transparent life was disclosed, as if the flow of her existence could be seen passing within her. She understood the scene in a moment.

text: All day long Kael had carried himself as though his mind was so heavy with unvoiced preoccupations that he scarcely noticed the small goings-on around him, and the words he spoke now laid bare exactly what had been turning over in his thoughts. He raised the topic mere seconds after that strange, unaccountable knock at the door had faded. “While I was away earlier today, Liora, I’ve been thinking that something has to be done to mend this terrible rift between my dear mother and me. It weighs on me constantly.”
source_text: All the day Clym had borne himself as if his mind were too full of its own matter to allow him to care about outward things, and his words now showed what had occupied his thoughts. It was just after the mysterious knocking that he began the theme. “Since I have been away today, Eustacia, I have considered that something must be done to heal up this ghastly breach between my dear mother and myself. It troubles me.”

text: The woad peddler stepped out of the cottage and lingered on the grassy rise abutting the homestead. A long stretch of time slipped past, and no word came to bid him return inside. He had just begun to conclude his gambit had failed entirely, when he caught sight of Liora’s distinct figure drifting leisurely in his direction. The novelty of granting an audience to that strange, roving figure had been enough to draw her out of her rooms.
source_text: The reddleman left the house and loitered on the hill adjoining. A considerable time elapsed, and no request for his presence was brought. He was beginning to think that his scheme had failed, when he beheld the form of Eustacia herself coming leisurely towards him. A sense of novelty in giving audience to that singular figure had been sufficient to draw her forth.

text: “They’ve dug out a hollow up there, Mis’ess Yeobright, and unearthed things that look just like upside-down flowerpots, with actual old burial bones tucked inside them. The men have carted the lot off to keep in their cottages, but I’d never rest easy sleeping anywhere those bones are stored. Everyone knows the dead come wandering back to claim what’s rightfully theirs. Mr. Yeobright had picked up one of those pots full of bones—proper skeleton bones, no less—and was all set to bring it home with him, but plans shifted at the last minute. You’ll be right relieved to hear he thought twice and gave the whole pot and its contents away. That’s a blessed bit of luck for you, Mis’ess Yeobright, what with how the wind moans round the eaves on cold nights.”
source_text: “They have dug a hole, and they have found things like flowerpots upside down, Mis’ess Yeobright; and inside these be real charnel bones. They have carried ’em off to men’s houses; but I shouldn’t like to sleep where they will bide. Dead folks have been known to come and claim their own. Mr. Yeobright had got one pot of the bones, and was going to bring ’em home—real skellington bones—but ’twas ordered otherwise. You’ll be relieved to hear that he gave away his pot and all, on second thoughts; and a blessed thing for ye, Mis’ess Yeobright, considering the wind o’ nights.”

text: Dusk crept steadily lower, and the hum of activity from the lower levels of the house grew more distinct, the wicket gate set in the wooden palings clicking open and shut almost without pause. The celebration had been scheduled to run early, so every guest had assembled long before the last sliver of sunlight slipped below the heath horizon. Yeobright descended the back staircase and stepped out onto the moor via a side path rather than the main front track, intending to wander the open air until the festivities ended, at which point he would return to bid Thomasin and her husband farewell as they left. Without his conscious direction, his steps turned toward Mistover, tracing the exact route he had followed that terrible morning when Susan’s boy had brought him that bizarre, unforeseen news.
source_text: Evening drew on, and sounds of life and movement in the lower part of the domicile became more pronounced, the gate in the palings clicking incessantly. The party was to be an early one, and all the guests were assembled long before it was dark. Yeobright went down the back staircase and into the heath by another path than that in front, intending to walk in the open air till the party was over, when he would return to wish Thomasin and her husband good-bye as they departed. His steps were insensibly bent towards Mistover by the path that he had followed on that terrible morning when he learnt the strange news from Susan’s boy.

text: “Rest assured I have no intention of singing your praises,” she said drily. “If this strikes you as underhanded scheming, you would do well to remember how precarious her position is, and how cruelly she has been wronged. I will not be working to secure this match alone, either: her own fierce desire to escape the humiliation of her current circumstances will aid me, and a woman’s pride in situations like these will carry her a very long way. A small amount of careful steering may be needed to bring her around, but I am more than capable of that, provided you agree to the one indispensable condition: you must make it explicitly clear to her that she is to abandon any thought of you as a possible husband. That slight to her pride will be enough to goad her into accepting him.”
source_text: “Well, you may depend upon my not praising you,” she said drily. “And if this seems like manœuvring, you must remember that her position is peculiar, and that she has been hardly used. I shall also be helped in making the match by her own desire to escape from the humiliation of her present state; and a woman’s pride in these cases will lead her a very great way. A little managing may be required to bring her round; but I am equal to that, provided that you agree to the one thing indispensable; that is, to make a distinct declaration that she is to think no more of you as a possible husband. That will pique her into accepting him.”

text: Kael uttered not a single further syllable. He twisted around with his hands clasped behind his back, so she would not catch sight of the crushing, unresigned dejection etched across his features. The sharp intelligence and unshakable resolve he had observed in this isolated young woman had pricked at him with unease almost from the first minutes they had been in close company. Her tender age and remote life on the moor had led him to anticipate a pliable naivety that would bend easily to the tactics he had honed. Yet the cascade of coaxing and small enticements that would have swept more soft-willed country girls off their feet had only pushed Liora further away. To most folk who called Bramble Moor home, the very name Brineport carried a nearly magical allure. That royal harbor and seaside retreat, as it lived in the fantasies of moor-dwellers, merged in some ineffably lovely way the fevered construction bustle of a rising mercantile power, the sun-warmed indulgence of a famed Mediterranean pleasure city, and the crisp, health-giving beauty of a coastal sanctuary. Liora was hardly less enchanted by the fabled town than her neighbors; but she refused to set aside her autonomy and self-respect for the chance to relocate there.
source_text: Venn spoke not another word. With his hands behind him he turned away, that she might not see the hopeless disappointment in his face. The mental clearness and power he had found in this lonely girl had indeed filled his manner with misgiving even from the first few minutes of close quarters with her. Her youth and situation had led him to expect a simplicity quite at the beck of his method. But a system of inducement which might have carried weaker country lasses along with it had merely repelled Eustacia. As a rule, the word Budmouth meant fascination on Egdon. That Royal port and watering place, if truly mirrored in the minds of the heathfolk, must have combined, in a charming and indescribable manner a Carthaginian bustle of building with Tarentine luxuriousness and Baian health and beauty. Eustacia felt little less extravagantly about the place; but she would not sink her independence to get there.

text: A faint flush rose to Thomasin’s cheeks, and it was not sparked by love. Whatever fleeting emotion had pulled that warm tinge to her face, it faded as quickly as it had appeared, and she spoke in a soft, humble tone: “I have no intention of being that, if I can possibly avoid it. I only mean that at long last, you hold a certain measure of sway over my aunt.”
source_text: Thomasin coloured a little, and not with love. But whatever the momentary feeling which caused that flush in her, it went as it came, and she humbly said, “I never mean to be, if I can help it. I merely feel that you have my aunt to some extent in your power at last.”

text: The two of them had lingered for a quiet beat in the front vestibule. She turned the deadbolt on the exterior door just as she always did, then hauled open the door to the adjacent parlor and gestured for him to step inside. Elias crossed the threshold, and the space looked entirely unoccupied at first; but he’d only taken three shuffling steps forward when he froze mid-stride. Sprawled across the thick wool hearthrug was Kael, fast asleep. Piled next to his slouched form were the canvas gaiters, heavy work boots, scuffed hide gloves, and sleeved work waistcoat he wore for his daily outdoor labor.
source_text: They had been standing in the entry. Closing the front door and turning the key, as before, she threw open the door of the adjoining room and asked him to walk in. Wildeve entered, the room appearing to be empty; but as soon as he had advanced a few steps he started. On the hearthrug lay Clym asleep. Beside him were the leggings, thick boots, leather gloves, and sleeve-waistcoat in which he worked.

text: The faint distant light Eustacia had glanced at as she departed her home came, just as she had surmised, from the cottage window of Susan Nunsuch. What Eustacia did not suspect, however, was what the woman inside was occupied with at that very moment. Earlier that evening, no more than five minutes after her sick young son had exclaimed, “Mother, I do feel so terrible!” Susan had spotted Eustacia’s passing form, and the tight timing convinced the matron beyond doubt that an evil influence was certainly being exerted by Eustacia’s close proximity.
source_text: The distant light which Eustacia had cursorily observed in leaving the house came, as she had divined, from the cottage window of Susan Nunsuch. What Eustacia did not divine was the occupation of the woman within at that moment. Susan’s sight of her passing figure earlier in the evening, not five minutes after the sick boy’s exclamation, “Mother, I do feel so bad!” persuaded the matron that an evil influence was certainly exercised by Eustacia’s propinquity.

text: “That’s right, he has,” Clym said in a flat, uninflected tone. “I wish with all my heart I could tell you to marry him, truly. But I cannot shake the memory of my mother’s feelings on this matter, and it goes against my conscience to dismiss her judgment. We have more than enough cause now to do whatever small things we can to respect her wishes.”
source_text: “Yes, he has,” said Clym in a neutral tone. “Well, I wish with all my heart that I could say, marry him. But I cannot forget what my mother thought on that matter, and it goes rather against me not to respect her opinion. There is too much reason why we should do the little we can to respect it now.”

text: Yeobright held a deep, sincere love for his fellow people. He was convinced that what most folks lacked was a type of knowledge that builds wisdom rather than material riches. He wanted to uplift the entire class even if that required small sacrifices from individual members, instead of advancing individual people at the expense of the broader group. What’s more, he was fully ready to be the first person to make that sacrifice, no hesitation whatsoever.
source_text: Yeobright loved his kind. He had a conviction that the want of most men was knowledge of a sort which brings wisdom rather than affluence. He wished to raise the class at the expense of individuals rather than individuals at the expense of the class. What was more, he was ready at once to be the first unit sacrificed.

text: No one caught a single glimpse of him for four full hours. He returned along the exact same trail as dusk settled, and fine dew already sheathed every patch of green growing thing. The loud, raucous May party music had died down completely, but since he came onto the property from the rear, he had no way of telling if all the guests had left until he cut through Thomasin’s side of the house to reach the front entryway. Thomasin was standing just inside the porch, entirely alone.
source_text: Nothing was seen of him for four hours. When he came back by the same path it was dusk, and the dews were coating every green thing. The boisterous music had ceased; but, entering the premises as he did from behind, he could not see if the May party had all gone till he had passed through Thomasin’s division of the house to the front door. Thomasin was standing within the porch alone.

text: All the rich, sensory feeling stitched through the entire village, scattered thin across every ordinary day of the year, swelled into a single thrumming, concentrated focus here for one short hour. The forty hearts of those swaying, dancing pairs beat faster than they had at any point since they’d gathered for the same giddy, raucous celebration twelve full months prior. For that fleeting stretch, unbridled pagan warmth bloomed again in their chests, the unapologetic joy of being alive was the only thing that mattered, and the only thing they worshipped was the bright, vivid pulse of their own selves.
source_text: A whole village-full of sensuous emotion, scattered abroad all the year long, surged here in a focus for an hour. The forty hearts of those waving couples were beating as they had not done since, twelve months before, they had come together in similar jollity. For the time paganism was revived in their hearts, the pride of life was all in all, and they adored none other than themselves.

text: “That happens more often than not,” said Olly, the besom-maker. “And yet folks chase that skill so hard and end up mastering it, too! There’s whole swathes of people who once couldn’t even draw a simple round O to save themselves from being tossed down the mine pit. Now they can sign their own names without the pen stuttering once, more often than not without a single blot—hell, what am I even saying? They can pull it off almost without even a desk to lean their elbows and torso against while they do it.”
source_text: “Very often the case,” said Olly, the besom-maker. “And yet how people do strive after it and get it! The class of folk that couldn’t use to make a round O to save their bones from the pit can write their names now without a sputter of the pen, oftentimes without a single blot—what do I say?—why, almost without a desk to lean their stomachs and elbows upon.”

text: For a lean twenty-four-year-old, the reddleman’s trudge across the first valley and over the rise to the next where his cart was parked was anything but lively. His mind was so troubled that the distress throbbed like a physical ache. The soft wind brushing his lips as he walked carried off the harsh, muttered curses he let spill under his breath.
source_text: The reddleman’s walk across the vale, and over into the next where his cart lay, was not sprightly for a slim young fellow of twenty-four. His spirit was perturbed to aching. The breezes that blew around his mouth in that walk carried off upon them the accents of a commination.

text: “Aye, that’s exactly what we’ll do!” said Old Gideon Rook, turning so briskly that the brass fobs on his worn watch chain swung wildly against his frayed wool vest. “I’m as parched as a dried bullrush from hunkering up here in this cutting wind, and I haven’t so much as glimpsed the glint of an ale mug since my midday bite. Word has it the latest brew tapped at the Giddy Maid goes down as sweet and smooth as can be. And listen here, neighbors, even if we run a little late wrapping up tonight, what’s the harm? Tomorrow’s Sunday, after all—we can laze in bed and sleep every last bit of it off, can’t we?”
source_text: “Hey? And so we will!” said Grandfer Cantle, turning so briskly that his copper seals swung extravagantly. “I’m as dry as a kex with biding up here in the wind, and I haven’t seen the colour of drink since nammet-time today. ’Tis said that the last brew at the Woman is very pretty drinking. And, neighbours, if we should be a little late in the finishing, why, tomorrow’s Sunday, and we can sleep it off?”

text: The lone figure treading this familiar stretch paid no heed to the thin, warbling tune the wind still drew through the dead heathbells. She did not turn her head to glance at the cluster of dark shapes further ahead, which fled at her presence as she skirted the edge of the ravine where they had been grazing. They numbered roughly twenty of the small, wild ponies known as heath-croppers. They roamed freely across the rolling swells of Egdon, but their numbers were far too sparse to meaningfully detract from the prevailing, vast solitude.
source_text: The solitary figure who walked this beat took no notice of the windy tune still played on the dead heathbells. She did not turn her head to look at a group of dark creatures further on, who fled from her presence as she skirted a ravine where they fed. They were about a score of the small wild ponies known as heath-croppers. They roamed at large on the undulations of Egdon, but in numbers too few to detract much from the solitude.

text: “Yep, yep, that’s exactly it. But hold on a second, Timothy, listen close,” Grandfer said earnestly. “Everybody knows I’m nothing but a silly joker most days, but I’ve got good sense when I’m being serious, and I’m dead serious right now. I can tell you all there is to know about that newly married pair. They headed up county at six this morning to get the whole deed done, and there’s been hide nor hair of either of them ever since. Though I’d wager they’re already back home this afternoon, properly paired off as man and wife—husband and wife, that is. Doesn’t that sound like straight, honest talk from a man who means what he says, Timothy? Mrs. Yeobright had me pegged all wrong, didn’t she?”
source_text: “Yes, yes—that’s it. But, Timothy, hearken to me,” said the Grandfer earnestly. “Though known as such a joker, I be an understanding man if you catch me serious, and I am serious now. I can tell ’ee lots about the married couple. Yes, this morning at six o’clock they went up the country to do the job, and neither vell nor mark have been seen of ’em since, though I reckon that this afternoon has brought ’em home again man and woman—wife, that is. Isn’t it spoke like a man, Timothy, and wasn’t Mis’ess Yeobright wrong about me?”

text: To give him his fair due, he’s a sharp, well-learned fellow in his own line—nearly as quick-witted as Lorne Hale used to be back in his heyday. He was raised for far finer things than tending the Resting Hare. A civil engineer, that’s what the man was, as all of us around here know; but he squandered his chance, so he took on running a public house to make his living. All his years of learning never served him a single bit of good at all.
source_text: “To give him his due he’s a clever, learned fellow in his way—a’most as clever as Clym Yeobright used to be. He was brought up to better things than keeping the Quiet Woman. An engineer—that’s what the man was, as we know; but he threw away his chance, and so ’a took a public house to live. His learning was no use to him at all.”

text: Venn signaled his agreement by pushing himself to his feet, and alongside Charley he followed Clym to the base of the staircase, where he tugged off his boots; Charley did the exact same. The pair trailed Yeobright up the stairs to the landing, where a single candle sat burning. Yeobright picked up the candle in his hand, and carried it ahead of them to lead the way into the adjoining room. Once they were inside, he crossed to the bedside and folded back the sheet.
source_text: Venn assented by rising to his feet, and with Charley he followed Clym to the foot of the staircase, where he took off his boots; Charley did the same. They followed Yeobright upstairs to the landing, where there was a candle burning, which Yeobright took in his hand, and with it led the way into an adjoining room. Here he went to the bedside and folded back the sheet.

text: Before long she was climbing the rise of Thistle-Glen vale, picking her way across the rolling slopes at the hill’s flank. The keening wind across the moor cut sharp, as if trilling in delight at having found a night so perfectly suited to its wild temper. Every so often the path dipped her into hollows walled by thick stands of tall, sodden bracken, browned by frost but still standing stiff, closing around her like the dark walls of a sunken pond. When the fronds grew taller than usual, she hoisted the swaddled toddler up to rest on the crown of her head, to keep him clear of their soaking, lashing edges. On the higher stretches, where the wind blew steady and biting, the rain drove horizontally with no perceptible downward arc, so that it was impossible to fathom how high up in the roiling gray cloud mass it had first formed. There was no shielding herself here, each individual raindrop driving into her skin like one of the arrows piercing Saint Sebastian. She managed to steer clear of puddles by the faint, milky paleness that marked their position, though set against any surface less inky than the moor, those same stretches of standing water would have looked pitch-black.
source_text: She was soon ascending Blooms-End valley and traversing the undulations on the side of the hill. The noise of the wind over the heath was shrill, and as if it whistled for joy at finding a night so congenial as this. Sometimes the path led her to hollows between thickets of tall and dripping bracken, dead, though not yet prostrate, which enclosed her like a pool. When they were more than usually tall she lifted the baby to the top of her head, that it might be out of the reach of their drenching fronds. On higher ground, where the wind was brisk and sustained, the rain flew in a level flight without sensible descent, so that it was beyond all power to imagine the remoteness of the point at which it left the bosoms of the clouds. Here self-defence was impossible, and individual drops stuck into her like the arrows into Saint Sebastian. She was enabled to avoid puddles by the nebulous paleness which signified their presence, though beside anything less dark than the heath they themselves would have appeared as blackness.

text: The moor lay utterly unpeopled that night; Kaelan Voss leaned over Liora Thorne’s garden gate for a long quiet stretch, a clove cigarette perched between his lips, the reckless thrill of hidden, forbidden feeling drawing him forward toward her front window, left slightly ajar, its linen shade pulled only halfway down. He could see clearly into the cottage parlor, where Liora sat completely alone. Kaelan studied her for a minute, then slipped back into the moor’s thick bracken, brushing the fronds lightly until startled moths burst from the foliage. He cupped one in his palm, returned to the window, held the moth to the narrow crack in the frame, and opened his hand. The moth drifted toward the beeswax candle on Liora’s table, circled its flickering flame two or three times, then flew straight into the fire.
source_text: The heath tonight appeared to be totally deserted; and Wildeve, after looking over Eustacia’s garden gate for some little time, with a cigar in his mouth, was tempted by the fascination that emotional smuggling had for his nature to advance towards the window, which was not quite closed, the blind being only partly drawn down. He could see into the room, and Eustacia was sitting there alone. Wildeve contemplated her for a minute, and then retreating into the heath beat the ferns lightly, whereupon moths flew out alarmed. Securing one, he returned to the window, and holding the moth to the chink, opened his hand. The moth made towards the candle upon Eustacia’s table, hovered round it two or three times, and flew into the flame.

text: Her eyes held the wild, unruly magic of old pagan lore, brimming with secrets that belonged only to the deep of night. The soft glow shifting within them—fading, flaring, dimming then brightening once more—was partially muted and obscured by the heavy weight of her lids and thick, dark lashes, and her lower lid was far plumper than what was typical for English women. That extra fullness let her sink into deep reverie without anyone noticing she’d drifted away; you could easily have believed she was capable of sleeping without ever shutting her eyes at all. If you imagined that the souls of men and women were visible, glowing essences, you’d picture Eustacia’s soul as the vivid, roiling hue of an open flame. The tiny, flickering sparks that rose from that inner fire to catch in her dark pupils conveyed that exact same impression of smoldering, fiery life.
source_text: She had pagan eyes, full of nocturnal mysteries, and their light, as it came and went, and came again, was partially hampered by their oppressive lids and lashes; and of these the under lid was much fuller than it usually is with English women. This enabled her to indulge in reverie without seeming to do so—she might have been believed capable of sleeping without closing them up. Assuming that the souls of men and women were visible essences, you could fancy the colour of Eustacia’s soul to be flamelike. The sparks from it that rose into her dark pupils gave the same impression.

text: That mouth looked shaped not so much for speaking as for trembling, not so much for trembling as for kissing. Some would have added that it was made less for kissing than to curl softly at the corners. Seen in profile, the seam where her lips pressed together traced, with nearly mathematical exactness, the curve familiar to decorative artists as the cima-recta, or ogee. To catch sight of such a soft, supple curve on harsh, unforgiving Egdon Heath was little short of a ghostly apparition. You knew immediately that this mouth had not crossed the sea from Sleswig alongside a band of Saxon raiders, whose lips fit together as plain and unshapely as the two halves of a cut muffin. Most would have assumed such delicate lip curves only existed half-buried in southern soil, broken fragments of long-forgotten marble statues. The lines of her lips were so exquisitely shaped that, even at their fullest, each corner of her mouth was chiseled as sharply as the tip of a spear. That sharp definition at the corners softened only when she was seized by sudden bouts of melancholy, one facet of the darker, more brooding side of feeling that she knew far too intimately for someone so young.
source_text: The mouth seemed formed less to speak than to quiver, less to quiver than to kiss. Some might have added, less to kiss than to curl. Viewed sideways, the closing-line of her lips formed, with almost geometric precision, the curve so well known in the arts of design as the cima-recta, or ogee. The sight of such a flexible bend as that on grim Egdon was quite an apparition. It was felt at once that the mouth did not come over from Sleswig with a band of Saxon pirates whose lips met like the two halves of a muffin. One had fancied that such lip-curves were mostly lurking underground in the South as fragments of forgotten marbles. So fine were the lines of her lips that, though full, each corner of her mouth was as clearly cut as the point of a spear. This keenness of corner was only blunted when she was given over to sudden fits of gloom, one of the phases of the night-side of sentiment which she knew too well for her years.

text: Never going to happen! I’ll keep my mouth clamped shut as tight as the grave I don’t even fear facing, even though speaking up right now would clear me of half the garbage you buy into about me. That’s right, I mean it. What person with an ounce of dignity would bother scraping the cobwebs out of some unreasonable brute’s head after he’s spewed language like that? No. Let him keep right on, cling to his narrow, small-minded thoughts, and run his own head straight into the mire for all I care. I’ve got far more important things to tend to.
source_text: “Never! I’ll hold my tongue like the very death that I don’t mind meeting, even though I can clear myself of half you believe by speaking. Yes. I will! Who of any dignity would take the trouble to clear cobwebs from a wild man’s mind after such language as this? No; let him go on, and think his narrow thoughts, and run his head into the mire. I have other cares.”

text: As the effigy modeled after Eustacia burned down to nothing at all, the beautiful young woman herself stood atop Rainbarrow, her spirit sunk in a well of desolation so deep few people her age ever touched such depths, while Yeobright sat alone at Blooms-End. He had kept his promise to Thomasin by sending Fairway off with the letter addressed to his wife, and now he waited with growing restlessness for any sound or sign that she would return. If Eustacia was still staying at Mistover, the very least he expected was that she would send a reply back with Fairway that same night; still, to let the choice be entirely hers, he had warned Fairway not to ask her for an answer at all. If she did hand over a response, Fairway was to bring it to him immediately; if not, he was to head straight home, no need to make a detour to Blooms-End again that night.
source_text: While the effigy of Eustacia was melting to nothing, and the fair woman herself was standing on Rainbarrow, her soul in an abyss of desolation seldom plumbed by one so young, Yeobright sat lonely at Blooms-End. He had fulfilled his word to Thomasin by sending off Fairway with the letter to his wife, and now waited with increased impatience for some sound or signal of her return. Were Eustacia still at Mistover the very least he expected was that she would send him back a reply tonight by the same hand; though, to leave all to her inclination, he had cautioned Fairway not to ask for an answer. If one were handed to him he was to bring it immediately; if not, he was to go straight home without troubling to come round to Blooms-End again that night.

text: “Alright, dear. I suppose that means I need to go pay her a visit.” His tone stayed soft and tender as he went on: “I’m not too proud to do that, not even a little bit. The only thing that’s kept me away this long is worrying I might irritate her. But I have to take action now. Letting this whole situation drag on like this is wrong of me.”
source_text: “Very well, dear. Then I think I must go and call upon her.” ...He went on with tender feeling: “It is a thing I am not at all too proud to do, and only a fear that I might irritate her has kept me away so long. But I must do something. It is wrong in me to allow this sort of thing to go on.”

text: Venn sat perfectly still as his companion wandered here and there through the dim grass, until he had gathered thirteen glowworms—every last one he could find over the course of four or five minutes—resting them on a foxglove leaf he’d plucked expressly for the task. When the reddleman spotted his opponent returning with this small glowing haul, he let out a low, good-humored laugh. “So you’re determined to keep this going, then?” he asked, his tone dry and unruffled.
source_text: Venn sat still, and his companion went hither and thither till he had gathered thirteen glowworms—as many as he could find in a space of four or five minutes—upon a fox-glove leaf which he pulled for the purpose. The reddleman vented a low humorous laugh when he saw his adversary return with these. “Determined to go on, then?” he said drily.

text: For the mummers, the first five minutes of hanging around listening were plenty engaging on their own. Those five minutes stretched into ten, then dragged on to a full quarter of an hour, but there wasn’t a single audible hint that the lively performance of “Dream” was anywhere close to wrapping up. The thuds against the door, peals of laughter, and sharp stamping of feet were all just as vigorous as they had been from the start, and the group’s enjoyment of lingering outside had dwindled considerably.
source_text: The first five minutes of listening was interesting enough to the mummers. The five minutes extended to ten minutes, and these to a quarter of an hour; but no signs of ceasing were audible in the lively “Dream.” The bumping against the door, the laughter, the stamping, were all as vigorous as ever, and the pleasure in being outside lessened considerably.

text: He cut across the heath toward home, not bothering to stick to any marked footpath. Nobody knew this stretch of moorland better than Clym, that much was certain. Every sweeping vista, every crumb of its peaty soil, every sharp, earthy scent had seeped into his very bones. You could fairly call him the heath’s own creation. He’d first opened his eyes to its skyline; every earliest memory he held was tangled up with its rough contours, and it had dyed every belief he held about life its own earthy hue. As a small boy, his playthings were the flint blades and arrowheads he dug up in its turf, marveling that stones could “grow” into such strange, sharp shapes; his favorite flowers were its purple heather bells and bright yellow gorse; his idea of the entire animal kingdom was its slithering snakes and plump game birds; his first sense of community came from the other drifters and wanderers who haunted its expanse. If you took every layered, bitter hatred Eustacia Vye held for the heath and flipped every last one into fierce, abiding affection, you’d have a perfect blueprint of Clym’s heart. He kept his gaze fixed on the wide, open spread of land stretching out ahead as he walked, and felt a quiet, unshakable gladness settle over him.
source_text: He walked along towards home without attending to paths. If anyone knew the heath well it was Clym. He was permeated with its scenes, with its substance, and with its odours. He might be said to be its product. His eyes had first opened thereon; with its appearance all the first images of his memory were mingled, his estimate of life had been coloured by it: his toys had been the flint knives and arrow-heads which he found there, wondering why stones should “grow” to such odd shapes; his flowers, the purple bells and yellow furze: his animal kingdom, the snakes and croppers; his society, its human haunters. Take all the varying hates felt by Eustacia Vye towards the heath, and translate them into loves, and you have the heart of Clym. He gazed upon the wide prospect as he walked, and was glad.

text: Lack of breath made continuing the song impossible, and the ragged break in the tune caught the attention of a sturdy, straight-standing middle-aged man. He held both corners of his crescent-shaped mouth pulled rigidly back into his cheeks, as if actively working to dispel any mistaken impression that he might be amused.
source_text: Want of breath prevented a continuance of the song; and the breakdown attracted the attention of a firm-standing man of middle age, who kept each corner of his crescent-shaped mouth rigorously drawn back into his cheek, as if to do away with any suspicion of mirthfulness which might erroneously have attached to him.

text: The truth was, Yeobright had found his true calling working as a traveling open-air preacher and lecturer focusing on unassailably ethical topics. From that day forward, he threw himself into the work without pause: he spoke in plain, straightforward language at Rainbarrow and in the small surrounding hamlets, and switched to a more refined, educated tone for other engagements all across neighboring Wessex towns and villages—from the steps and porticos of town halls, at market crosses and public conduits, on seaside esplanades and dock wharves, from the low stone parapets of bridges, inside empty barns and outbuildings, and every other similar public spot he could access. He chose not to wade into debates over religious creeds or abstract philosophical systems, figuring there was more than enough material to fill his speeches in the shared values and everyday choices common to all people of good character. Audiences were split on his work: some took his words to heart, others dismissed them entirely; some wrote off his messaging as unoriginal, others grumbled that he neglected to include traditional theological doctrine, and still others remarked that preaching was a fine enough pursuit for a man who wasn’t capable of holding down any other line of work. Even so, he was greeted warmly everywhere he went, as the full story of his life had spread widely across the region.
source_text: Yeobright had, in fact, found his vocation in the career of an itinerant open-air preacher and lecturer on morally unimpeachable subjects; and from this day he laboured incessantly in that office, speaking not only in simple language on Rainbarrow and in the hamlets round, but in a more cultivated strain elsewhere—from the steps and porticoes of town halls, from market-crosses, from conduits, on esplanades and on wharves, from the parapets of bridges, in barns and outhouses, and all other such places in the neighbouring Wessex towns and villages. He left alone creeds and systems of philosophy, finding enough and more than enough to occupy his tongue in the opinions and actions common to all good men. Some believed him, and some believed not; some said that his words were commonplace, others complained of his want of theological doctrine; while others again remarked that it was well enough for a man to take to preaching who could not see to do anything else. But everywhere he was kindly received, for the story of his life had become generally known.

text: Then the day’s unforeseen incident unfolded: the reddleman, who still cared for her deeply, had stepped in to help her by pure chance at a make-or-break moment, and the experience spurred him to pledge open, active support for whatever she wanted, rather than lingering on the sidelines pining as he’d done up until that point. After everything he’d just witnessed, he couldn’t possibly doubt that Wildeve’s motives were less than honorable. Still, it was clear all her hopes were pinned on Wildeve, so Venn pushed aside his own misgivings and resolved to help her find happiness on the path she’d picked for herself. The fact that this particular path was the one that would cause him the most heartache was a bitter pill to swallow, but the reddleman’s affection for her was fundamentally generous and selfless.
source_text: Then came the incident of that day, and the reddleman, still loving her well, was excited by this accidental service to her at a critical juncture to vow an active devotion to her cause, instead of, as hitherto, sighing and holding aloof. After what had happened it was impossible that he should not doubt the honesty of Wildeve’s intentions. But her hope was apparently centred upon him; and dismissing his regrets Venn determined to aid her to be happy in her own chosen way. That this way was, of all others, the most distressing to himself, was awkward enough; but the reddleman’s love was generous.

text: “That was me!” Thomasin sobbed once more. “When I realized we wouldn’t be able to get married after all, I couldn’t bring myself to come back with him, and I was really sick on top of it. Then I ran into Diggory Venn, and I was so grateful he agreed to take me home. I can’t explain it any plainer than that—you can be angry with me if that’s what you want.”
source_text: “That was me!” again sobbed Thomasin. “When I found we could not be married I didn’t like to come back with him, and I was very ill. Then I saw Diggory Venn, and was glad to get him to take me home. I cannot explain it any better, and you must be angry with me if you will.”

text: A handful of days before this exact moment, Kael Voss leaned against the doorframe of The Rested Hare. Beside the steep footpath cutting up through the gorse-dusted moor to Thorn Barrow and Frostveil Hall, a narrower lane split off from the main post road just a stone’s throw downhill from the tavern, winding upward to Frostveil along a gentle, meandering slope. That lane was the only passable route for carts and carriages to reach the old sea captain’s remote homestead from that side of the moor. A small dray coming from the closest market town trundled down that very lane, and the teenaged driver hauled on the reins to stop directly outside the tavern, calling out for a drink to slake his dust-parched throat.
source_text: One day just before this time Wildeve was standing at the door of the Quiet Woman. In addition to the upward path through the heath to Rainbarrow and Mistover, there was a road which branched from the highway a short distance below the inn, and ascended to Mistover by a circuitous and easy incline. This was the only route on that side for vehicles to the captain’s retreat. A light cart from the nearest town descended the road, and the lad who was driving pulled up in front of the inn for something to drink.

text: “Clym,” she answered slowly, “do you truly believe there is anything you could do to me that I would not dare endure? But before you raise your hand to strike, listen well. You will get nothing from me with a blow, even if it kills me, as it most likely will. But perhaps you have no wish to hear me speak—could killing me be all you intend?”
source_text: “Clym,” she answered slowly, “do you think you dare do anything to me that I dare not bear? But before you strike me listen. You will get nothing from me by a blow, even though it should kill me, as it probably will. But perhaps you do not wish me to speak—killing may be all you mean?”

text: “I don’t think I’ll be asking you to come,” Thomasin said, her tone reluctant yet unyielding. “I’m almost certain it would only turn out unpleasant for all involved. It’s better if only strangers are present, not a single one of my relatives. That’s how I’d prefer things to go. I have no wish to do anything that might harm your reputation, and I know I’d be deeply uncomfortable the whole time if you were there, given everything that’s happened between us lately. I’m only your niece, after all—there’s no need for you to concern yourself with my affairs any more than you already have.”
source_text: “I don’t think I will ask you to come,” said Thomasin reluctantly, but with decision. “It would be unpleasant, I am almost sure. Better let there be only strangers present, and none of my relations at all. I would rather have it so. I do not wish to do anything which may touch your credit, and I feel that I should be uncomfortable if you were there, after what has passed. I am only your niece, and there is no necessity why you should concern yourself more about me.”

text: The quiet, isolated surroundings settled her nerves. She walked over to the wooden picket fence, leaned over its slats, and gazed up at the moon. She’d only been standing that way for a few moments when the door swung open again. Eustacia turned, expecting to see the rest of the group coming out, but she was wrong—Clym Yeobright stepped outside just as quietly as she had when she’d slipped out earlier, and pulled the door shut gently behind him.
source_text: The calm, lone scene reassured her. She went forward to the palings and leant over them, looking at the moon. She had stood thus but a little time when the door again opened. Expecting to see the remainder of the band Eustacia turned; but no—Clym Yeobright came out as softly as she had done, and closed the door behind him.

text: Wildeve’s shout drew no response, only the mournful, thin whine of herons nesting further down the valley drifting up to meet them. Neither man moved to stand, just casting blank, unfocused looks over their surroundings. Once their eyes adjusted to the thick darkness, they picked out faint greenish pinpricks of light tucked between clumps of grass and fern fronds. These tiny glows dotted the hillside, no brighter than the dimmest stars hanging far overhead.
source_text: But there was no reply to Wildeve’s shout, save a mournful whining from the herons which were nesting lower down the vale. Both men looked blankly round without rising. As their eyes grew accustomed to the darkness they perceived faint greenish points of light among the grass and fern. These lights dotted the hillside like stars of a low magnitude.

text: The reddleman’s warning that gossip could paint her in an unflattering light never troubled Eustacia for long. She cared as little about that possibility as a goddess would fret over a shortage of fine linens. This indifference did not stem from any innate lack of modesty, but from how far removed her life was from the wider world, making the weight of public judgment feel entirely irrelevant. Even Zenobia, stranded in the desert, could hardly have cared less what was said of her in Rome. When it came to unwritten social ethics, Eustacia verged on the untamed, though her inner emotional life was that of a dedicated epicure. She had wandered into the most secret recesses of sensual delight, yet had barely stepped across the threshold of accepted social convention.
source_text: The reddleman’s hint that rumour might show her to disadvantage had no permanent terror for Eustacia. She was as unconcerned at that contingency as a goddess at a lack of linen. This did not originate in inherent shamelessness, but in her living too far from the world to feel the impact of public opinion. Zenobia in the desert could hardly have cared what was said about her at Rome. As far as social ethics were concerned Eustacia approached the savage state, though in emotion she was all the while an epicure. She had advanced to the secret recesses of sensuousness, yet had hardly crossed the threshold of conventionality.

text: The first concrete action he took to safeguard Thomasin’s interests came around seven o’clock the following evening, spurred entirely by the account he had heard from the grieving young boy. The moment Venn learned of Eustacia and Wildeve’s clandestine meeting, he jumped immediately to the conclusion that Eustacia was, in some manner, responsible for Wildeve’s careless inaction around their upcoming marriage. It never crossed his mind that the tender romantic overture Eustacia had sent to Wildeve was simply the soft, vulnerable reaction of the neglected young beauty to the upsetting news her grandfather had brought home earlier. His instinct led him to cast her as a calculating conspirator against Thomasin’s happiness, rather than a preexisting, unwitting obstacle to it.
source_text: His first active step in watching over Thomasin’s interests was taken about seven o’clock the next evening and was dictated by the news which he had learnt from the sad boy.
