That Eustacia was somehow the cause of Wildeve’s carelessness in relation to the marriage had at once been Venn’s conclusion on hearing of the secret meeting between them. It did not occur to his mind that Eustacia’s love signal to Wildeve was the tender effect upon the deserted beauty of the intelligence which her grandfather had brought home. His instinct was to regard her as a conspirator against rather than as an antecedent obstacle to Thomasin’s happiness.

text: Yeobright passed along specific details of her illness and eventual death to him, then continued speaking: “After all this, no sort of pain will ever register as anything worse than a minor ailment to me. I started out meaning to ask you a question, didn’t I? I wander off subject as easily as a man addled by drink. What I’m eager to know is what my mother said to you the last time you saw each other. You spent a good long while talking with her, if I recall correctly?”
source_text: Yeobright gave him some particulars of her illness and death, and continued: “After this no kind of pain will ever seem more than an indisposition to me. I began saying that I wanted to ask you something, but I stray from subjects like a drunken man. I am anxious to know what my mother said to you when she last saw you. You talked with her a long time, I think?”

text: The preacher pressed on, running through a range of technological advancements before landing finally on the invention of daguerreotypes and modern photography. He drew his audience’s attention to this nearly miraculous craft, which could capture and preserve the exact expression of a person’s face permanently, and wove in a moral lesson about the lasting impression our own everyday expressions and demeanor leave on everyone we live and interact with regularly. He spent considerable time outlining the enormous volume of joy the innovation had sparked by putting portraits of beloved friends and family within nearly everyone’s reach, and noted how much deeper familial bonds and broader communal goodwill must have grown as a direct result of the technology. He even floated the idea that the cultures of older, long-established nations might shift over time, as immigrants who’d found success in their adopted countries regularly sent home snapshots of themselves and their new households, and touched on how this trend had almost certainly had a measurable impact on immigration rates.
source_text: So the preacher continued, speaking of different improvements, and lastly of the invention of daguerreotypes and photographs. He called the attention of his hearers to this almost miraculous art of indelibly fixing the expression of a countenance, and drew a lesson as to the permanent effect of our daily looks and expression on those among whom we live. He considered at length the vast amount of happiness which had been caused by bringing pictures of loved ones within the reach of all; the increase of family affection and general good feeling which must have resulted from the invention; he suggested a possible change in the civilization of the older nations through the constant sending home, by prosperous adopted citizens, of photographs of themselves and of their homes, and alluded to the effect which this must have had upon immigration.

text: “And now, as we prepare to lower his long-worn frame into the cool dark soil, let us not let our senses grow numb to this moment, jaded though we may be by the world’s endless cycle of loss and interment. Right now, our old neighbor Elias is no longer frail or weathered; the deep lines scored across his brow, the shake in his knotted hands, the frost of his silver hair he has shed entirely, and we hold faith that this day he understands more than the sharpest living scholar walking the earth. We might spend every year of our mortal lives poring over texts, debating in halls, trying to pin down the true essence of being, or what shape existence takes once we leave this flesh behind; but in that single swift, sacred instant of crossing, the good and gentle soul grasps every last piece of that puzzle. Here, the gulf between our intellectual capacities can feel uncrossable. A tiny blockage in a single brain vessel, a faint scarring of neural tissue, might have turned a Dante into a scribe copying tax records for a local magistrate. But in that luminous realm that awaits us, I truly believe no such flaws stand in the way of clear sight and true speech; the chasms between minds that seem to stretch across continents here may dissolve entirely when we stand on that shore. When the soul bursts free of its clay cage and rises to meet that light, who can say how radiant and unshackled even the quietest, most overlooked among us might become? There is a deeper, more vivid truth than most of us ever dare to imagine in the old promise: ‘The last shall be first.’”
source_text: “And now, as we are about to lay his deserted body in the earth, let not our perceptions be dulled by the constant repetition in this world of death and burial. At this hour our friend is no longer aged; wrinkles and furrows, trembling limbs and snowy locks he has left behind him, and he knows, we believe, to-day, more than the wisest philosopher on earth. We may study and argue, all our lives, to discover the nature of life, or the form it takes beyond the grave; but in one moment of swift transition the righteous man may learn it all. We differ widely one from another, here, in mental power. A slight hardening of some tissue of the brain might have left a Shakspeare an attorney's clerk. But, in the brighter world, no such impediments prevent, I believe, clear vision and clear expression; and differences of mind that seem world-wide here, may vanish there. When the spirit breaks its earthly prison and flies away, who can tell how bright and free the humblest of us may come to be! There may be a more varied truth than we commonly think, in the words,--'The last shall be first.'

text: That evening, Miss Maria and her brothers sat in formal reception in their small, weathered old house, and welcomed dozens of callers throughout the night. The talk nearly all revolved around a single subject—not the funeral sermon, though everyone agreed it was a forthright, unpretentious biographical address well worth commending, but the larger-than-life moments that had accompanied Uncle Capen’s entire journey through life, events almost as grand as the deeds Horace wrote of in his Ode to Augustus.
source_text: That evening Miss Maria and her brothers, sitting in state in the little old house, received many a caller; and the conversation was chiefly upon one theme,--not the funeral sermon, although that was commended as a frank and simple biographical discourse, but the great events which had accompanied Uncle Capen's progress through this world, almost like those which Horace records in his Ode to Augustus.

text: “Why,” said Mr. Noyes, “you only have to open the pages of your Bible, pick a text to build from, then run through the standard set of questions: When did this happen? Why did this happen? Who did this happen to? Keep going down that line, and your entire sermon is already done. I’ve heard that exact lazy formula a hundred times if I’ve heard it once.”
source_text: “Why,” said Mr. Noyes, “you 've only to open the leds of your Bible, and choose a text, and then: When did this happen? Why did this happen? To who did this happen? and so forth and so on; and there's your sermon. I 've heard 'em so a hunderd times.”

text: The Willowwood Charitable Trust is dedicated to adhering to all regulations governing nonprofit organizations and charitable giving across every U.S. state. These regulatory mandates vary widely from state to state, and satisfying and maintaining adherence demands significant staff time, extensive administrative documentation, and substantial annual filing fees. We never request charitable contributions in jurisdictions where we have not yet received formal written verification that we meet all local regulatory standards. If you wish to make a donation or confirm our compliance status for a given state, please visit our official website at http://pglaf.org.
source_text: The Foundation is committed to complying with the laws regulating charities and charitable donations in all 50 states of the United States. Compliance requirements are not uniform and it takes a considerable effort, much paperwork and many fees to meet and keep up with these requirements. We do not solicit donations in locations where we have not received written confirmation of compliance. To SEND DONATIONS or determine the status of compliance for any particular state visit http://pglaf.org

text: After we had cast off the cruel yoke of tyranny, both in our own nation and in France, Lafayette—whose very name brings tears to the eyes of every true-hearted American—came to visit the America he loved so deeply, the country that cherished him right back. The man whose cold, rigid face I gaze down upon this very moment was part of one of those wildly enthusiastic throngs that turned that historic visit into a celebration as grand and jubilant as an ancient Roman Triumph.
source_text: “And when, the yoke of tyranny thrown off, in our country and in France, Lafayette, the mere mention of whose name brings tears to the eyes of every true American, came to see the America that he loved and that loved him, he on whose cold, rigid face I now look down, joined in one of those enthusiastic throngs that made the visit like a Roman Triumph.

text: It showed all over him. Unstoppable, never pausing, scrapping for every extra second and minute all week, working around hold-ups and bulldozing through every obstacle, a wellspring of unflagging energy, a revved-to-the-max human engine, a total work obsessive—now that he’d knocked out the full week’s tasks, he was completely undone. He looked frayed and gaunt, his handsome face slack with sharp, gnawing exhaustion. He dragged on his cigarette with no spark at all, and his voice was strangely flat and lifeless when he spoke. Every bit of his usual bite and fire had seeped right out of him. The victory he’d chased so hard felt like a sad, empty thing.
source_text: He looked it. Indomitable, never resting, fighting for seconds and minutes all week, circumventing delays and crushing down obstacles, a fount of resistless energy, a high-driven human motor, a demon for work, now that he had accomplished the week’s task he was in a state of collapse. He was worn and haggard, and his handsome face drooped in lean exhaustion. He pulled his cigarette spiritlessly, and his voice was peculiarly dead and monotonous. All the snap and fire had gone out of him. His triumph seemed a sorry one.

text: But Mr. Morse was far from content. He hated the laziness and total disinterest in steady, honest, proper work from this would-be son-in-law of his, whose views he held in complete contempt and whose whole nature he couldn’t begin to understand. So he redirected the conversation to Herbert Spencer. Judge Blount supported him skillfully, and Martin, who’d pricked up his ears the instant the philosopher’s name was first uttered, listened as the judge delivered a grave, smug diatribe attacking Spencer. Every so often, Mr. Morse glanced at Martin, as much as to say, “There you go, my boy. See that?”
source_text: But Mr. Morse was not content. He did not like the laziness and the disinclination for sober, legitimate work of this prospective son-in-law of his, for whose ideas he had no respect and of whose nature he had no understanding. So he turned the conversation to Herbert Spencer. Judge Blount ably seconded him, and Martin, whose ears had pricked at the first mention of the philosopher’s name, listened to the judge enunciate a grave and complacent diatribe against Spencer. From time to time Mr. Morse glanced at Martin, as much as to say, “There, my boy, you see.”

text: “Smart enough, I’ll give you that,” came the sharp retort, “but it’s perfectly fair, too. The only people who actually know their Latin are pharmacists, barristers, and classics department lecturers. And if Elias wants to end up as any of those, I’ve badly misjudged him. But what on earth does any of that have to do with Herbert Spencer, anyway? Elias just stumbled across Spencer’s work a little while back, and he’s utterly wild for it. Why? Because Spencer gives him a path to follow, somewhere to go. Spencer would never lead me anywhere, and he wouldn’t do a thing for you either. We don’t have anyplace we need to reach in the first place. You’ll get married and settle down eventually, and all I’ll ever have to occupy myself with is checking in on the solicitors and financial managers who handle the fortune my father’s set to leave me.”
source_text: “It’s clever all right,” was the retort, “but it’s fair, too. The only men who know their Latin are the apothecaries, the lawyers, and the Latin professors. And if Martin wants to be one of them, I miss my guess. But what’s all that got to do with Herbert Spencer anyway? Martin’s just discovered Spencer, and he’s wild over him. Why? Because Spencer is taking him somewhere. Spencer couldn’t take me anywhere, nor you. We haven’t got anywhere to go. You’ll get married some day, and I’ll have nothing to do but keep track of the lawyers and business agents who will take care of the money my father’s going to leave me.”

text: Half a second later Jax spun on his heel, and the tram riders watched his fist crack square into the jaw of a sprinting man who’d been lunging for the open door. But fists were connecting with flushed, angry faces up and down the entire length of the tram, right then. Jax and his crew, lined up along the tram’s low, extended running boards, were facing off against the rival gang that had come to ambush them. The tram lurched forward with a sharp, clanging peal of its bell, and just as Jax’s crew had shoved off the final would-be attackers, they hopped down to the asphalt to settle the score properly. The tram sped onward, leaving the swirling scrum of combat far in its wake, and its stunned riders never guessed that the soft-spoken young man and the sharp, lovely factory girl perched on the outer corner seat had been the whole reason for the brawl.
source_text: The next moment Jimmy whirled about, and the passengers saw him land his fist on the face of a running man who was trying to board the car. But fists were landing on faces the whole length of the car. Thus, Jimmy and his gang, strung out on the long, lower steps, met the attacking gang. The car started with a great clanging of its gong, and, as Jimmy’s gang drove off the last assailants, they, too, jumped off to finish the job. The car dashed on, leaving the flurry of combat far behind, and its dumfounded passengers never dreamed that the quiet young man and the pretty working-girl sitting in the corner on the outside seat had been the cause of the row.

text: “And what of it?” Elias shot back. “The thirteen original colonies cast off their distant overlords and founded what they called a Republic. The formerly subjugated were meant to be their own masters, with no more warlords wielding power through brute force of arms. But no society can function without some ruling class, and soon a new cohort of masters rose to fill the gap—not the bold, fierce, honorable fighters of old, but the cunning, skulking merchants and moneylenders that skittered like spiders through the halls of power. They bound you into servitude all over again, though not openly, the way honest, unyielding men would, by the strength of their own arms. Instead, they operated in the shadows, spinning tangled webs of manipulation, coaxing and flattering and lying their way into total control. They’ve bought off your servile judges, corrupted your complicit legislatures, and condemned your sons and daughters to fates far more cruel than the chattel bondage of old. Two million of your children labor even now under this merchant oligarchy that calls itself the United States. Ten million of you trapped in this system lack decent roofs over your heads and enough food to fill your bellies.”
source_text: “And how about it?” Martin retorted. “The thirteen colonies threw off their rulers and formed the Republic so-called. The slaves were their own masters. There were no more masters of the sword. But you couldn’t get along without masters of some sort, and there arose a new set of masters—not the great, virile, noble men, but the shrewd and spidery traders and money-lenders. And they enslaved you over again—but not frankly, as the true, noble men would do with weight of their own right arms, but secretly, by spidery machinations and by wheedling and cajolery and lies. They have purchased your slave judges, they have debauched your slave legislatures, and they have forced to worse horrors than chattel slavery your slave boys and girls. Two million of your children are toiling to-day in this trader-oligarchy of the United States. Ten millions of you slaves are not properly sheltered nor properly fed.

text: The dented tin alarm clock on the table ticked steadily on, but Martin Eden, his face buried in the crook of his folded arms, did not register its rhythm at all. He picked up no sound whatsoever. His mind held no coherent thought. The life he had just relived in his head was so vivid, so visceral, that he had blacked out entirely, just as he had fainted years earlier on the Eighth Street Bridge. The thick, unbroken blank darkness stretched on for a full minute. Then, as if roused straight from the grave, he snapped upright, his eyes blazing, cold sweat streaming down his face, and cried out:
source_text: The tin alarm-clock on the table ticked on, but Martin Eden, his face buried on his arms, did not hear it. He heard nothing. He did not think. So absolutely had he relived life that he had fainted just as he fainted years before on the Eighth Street Bridge. For a full minute the blackness and the blankness endured. Then, like one from the dead, he sprang upright, eyes flaming, sweat pouring down his face, shouting:-

text: “Rang the Halsteads earlier. Miss Halstead picked up the receiver, so now I’m here.” He fished in the depths of his overcoat pocket and tossed a slim volume onto the scuffed tabletop. “That’s a collection, by a little-known poet. Read through it, you can keep it.” Then, cutting off Elias’s fumbling protest: “What use would I have for books anyway? I had another bleeding spell this morning. You got any whiskey stashed somewhere? No, of course you don’t. Hang on a minute.”
source_text: “Called up the Morses. Miss Morse answered the ’phone. And here I am.” He tugged at his coat pocket and flung a thin volume on the table. “There’s a book, by a poet. Read it and keep it.” And then, in reply to Martin’s protest: “What have I to do with books? I had another hemorrhage this morning. Got any whiskey? No, of course not. Wait a minute.”

text: “And yet a man like Provost Fenton of Cambridge—a man who holds an even more esteemed public station than you do, Judge Thorne—has declared that Spencer will be written off by posterity as a poet and fanciful visionary rather than a rigorous thinker. Chattering fools and empty windbags, the whole miserable lot of them! ‘*First Principles* is not wholly lacking in a certain faint literary appeal,’ one of them sneered. And others among them have claimed he was a diligent drudge rather than an original, incisive mind. Chattering fools and empty windbags! Chattering fools and empty windbags!”
source_text: “And yet a man like Principal Fairbanks of Oxford—a man who sits in an even higher place than you, Judge Blount—has said that Spencer will be dismissed by posterity as a poet and dreamer rather than a thinker. Yappers and blatherskites, the whole brood of them! ‘“First Principles” is not wholly destitute of a certain literary power,’ said one of them. And others of them have said that he was an industrious plodder rather than an original thinker. Yappers and blatherskites! Yappers and blatherskites!”

text: In those first days, Martin did little else but rest. He slept for hours on end, and whiled away whole stretches of time lost in quiet, aimless thought, never stirring to take on meaningful work. He might have been a man slowly pulling himself back together after a brutal, draining bout of hardship. The first glimmer of reawakening arrived when he found himself giving more than lazy, passing attention to the daily newspaper. Next he took up reading again, starting with breezy novels and collections of poetry, and a handful of days later he was completely absorbed in his long-neglected copy of Fiske. His robust frame and naturally strong constitution flooded him with fresh vitality, and he carried all the springy, quick-to-rebound resilience of youth.
source_text: At first, Martin had done nothing but rest. He had slept a great deal, and spent long hours musing and thinking and doing nothing. He was like one recovering from some terrible bout of hardship. The first signs of reawakening came when he discovered more than languid interest in the daily paper. Then he began to read again—light novels, and poetry; and after several days more he was head over heels in his long-neglected Fiske. His splendid body and health made new vitality, and he possessed all the resiliency and rebound of youth.

text: Martin cut off mid-sentence sharply, the room falling completely still. Every member of Ruth’s family held Judge Blount in high regard as a formidable, highly accomplished man, so they were all appalled by Martin’s sudden outburst. The rest of the dinner dragged on with the stifling, somber energy of a funeral; the judge and Mr. Morse kept their conversation exclusively between themselves, and any other scraps of chat were scattered, half-hearted, and entirely aimless. Later, once the meal ended and Ruth and Martin were left alone together, a tense, explosive scene unfolded.
source_text: Martin ceased abruptly, in a dead silence. Everybody in Ruth’s family looked up to Judge Blount as a man of power and achievement, and they were horrified at Martin’s outbreak. The remainder of the dinner passed like a funeral, the judge and Mr. Morse confining their talk to each other, and the rest of the conversation being extremely desultory. Then afterward, when Ruth and Martin were alone, there was a scene.

text: Long before he’d grown proficient at crafting pocket tales, Elias had drafted half a dozen reusable structural templates he referred to every time he sat down to piece a new story together. These schematics worked much like the clever computational tables mathematicians relied on, navigable from top or bottom, left or right, their grids made up of dozens of rows and scores of columns, yielding thousands of distinct, unassailably accurate results without requiring any complex reasoning or deliberate thought from the user. Working from his templates for just half an hour, Elias could rough out the skeletons of a dozen or more pocket tales, which he set aside to flesh out whenever he had spare time. He discovered that even after a full day of demanding labor, he could finish drafting one of these pre-framed stories in the hour before he turned in for the night. As he later admitted to Mara, he could practically pull the whole task off half-asleep. The only substantial work lay in building the initial templates, and that process was purely mechanical.
source_text: Before he got very far along in the art of the storiette, Martin worked out half a dozen stock forms, which he always consulted when constructing storiettes. These forms were like the cunning tables used by mathematicians, which may be entered from top, bottom, right, and left, which entrances consist of scores of lines and dozens of columns, and from which may be drawn, without reasoning or thinking, thousands of different conclusions, all unchallengably precise and true. Thus, in the course of half an hour with his forms, Martin could frame up a dozen or so storiettes, which he put aside and filled in at his convenience. He found that he could fill one in, after a day of serious work, in the hour before going to bed. As he later confessed to Ruth, he could almost do it in his sleep. The real work was in constructing the frames, and that was merely mechanical.

text: I’d guess there aren’t many people like me floating around this university bubble, honestly. Some days I’m dead certain I’m out of my depth here, that I really belong in Paris, or Grub Street, or a hermit’s quiet cave, or running with some ragged, unruly bohemian crew—sipping claret, what they call dago-red back in San Francisco, eating cheap, hearty meals in Latin Quarter hole-in-the-wall spots, yelling my head off about radical takes on every single topic under the sun. Truth be told, more often than not I’m half-convinced I was born to be a radical. But then there are so many questions I just don’t have solid answers for. I shrink back when I’m forced to confront how fallible I am, how that inherent human weakness always stops me from grasping every single variable at play in any real issue—those messy, urgent, human problems, you know what I mean?
source_text: “Few like me, I imagine, in the university pond. Sometimes I am fairly sure I am out of water, and that I should belong in Paris, in Grub Street, in a hermit’s cave, or in some sadly wild Bohemian crowd, drinking claret,—dago-red they call it in San Francisco,—dining in cheap restaurants in the Latin Quarter, and expressing vociferously radical views upon all creation. Really, I am frequently almost sure that I was cut out to be a radical. But then, there are so many questions on which I am not sure. I grow timid when I am face to face with my human frailty, which ever prevents me from grasping all the factors in any problem—human, vital problems, you know.”

text: “That story was totally fantastic,” she said, pushing herself upright from the laundry tub with a weary sigh, swiping beaded sweat off her forehead with one red, steamy hand still damp from the hot soapy suds; “but it makes me feel so down. I could burst into tears right now. There’s already way too much sad stuff in the world as it is. I’m so much happier when I’m thinking about nice, upbeat things. If he’d just ended up marrying her, y’know, and—you don’t mind me saying that, right, Mart?” she asked, a little anxious she’d rambled out of turn. “I’m probably just reacting like this ‘cause I’m dead tired today, is all. But the story really was amazing, don’t get me wrong, totally great. Where are you planning to sell it, anyway?”
source_text: “That story was perfectly grand,” she announced, straightening up from the wash-tub with a tired sigh and wiping the sweat from her forehead with a red, steamy hand; “but it makes me sad. I want to cry. There is too many sad things in the world anyway. It makes me happy to think about happy things. Now if he’d married her, and—You don’t mind, Mart?” she queried apprehensively. “I just happen to feel that way, because I’m tired, I guess. But the story was grand just the same, perfectly grand. Where are you goin’ to sell it?”

text: “There’s not a single laundry outfit around that pulls this off, save for ours. And I’ve got to stick to the process exactly if I want to wrap up by three o’clock Saturday afternoon. But I’ve got the knack for it, that’s what makes all the difference. You need the exact heat, just the right pressure, and run each piece through three full passes. Look at that!” He held a crisp dress cuff aloft. “You couldn’t get a better finish doing it by hand, or even on a tilting press.”
source_text: “Something no laundry ever does, except this one. An’ I got to do it if I’m goin’ to get done Saturday afternoon at three o’clock. But I know how, an’ that’s the difference. Got to have right heat, right pressure, and run ’em through three times. Look at that!” He held a cuff aloft. “Couldn’t do it better by hand or on a tiler.”

text: Ruth had no patience to wait ten days, let alone two full weeks. On top of that, the man she loved was sick. The very next afternoon, she arrived with Arthur in the Morse family carriage, to the unreserved delight of the entire Silva clan and every scruffy street urchin loitering on the block—and the total consternation of Maria. She swatted the ears of the Silva kids crowding around the visitors on the tiny front porch, then fumbled through an apology for her appearance in even more mangled, broken English than usual. Her sleeves were pushed up past soap-flecked forearms, and a damp burlap sack tied around her waist made it obvious she’d been pulled right in the middle of a chore. She was so flustered that two such well-off, polished young people had come asking for her lodger that she completely forgot to invite them to take a seat in the small front parlor. To get to Martin’s room, they had to cut through the kitchen, which was warm, damp, and thick with steam from the huge load of laundry she’d been working through. In her excitement, Maria accidentally wedged the bedroom door and the bedroom closet door stuck together, and for five whole minutes, great clouds of steam reeking of soap suds and grime poured through the half-open gap straight into Martin’s sickroom.
source_text: But Ruth did not care to wait ten days or two weeks. Besides, her lover was sick. The next afternoon, accompanied by Arthur, she arrived in the Morse carriage, to the unqualified delight of the Silva tribe and of all the urchins on the street, and to the consternation of Maria. She boxed the ears of the Silvas who crowded about the visitors on the tiny front porch, and in more than usual atrocious English tried to apologize for her appearance. Sleeves rolled up from soap-flecked arms and a wet gunny-sack around her waist told of the task at which she had been caught. So flustered was she by two such grand young people asking for her lodger, that she forgot to invite them to sit down in the little parlor. To enter Martin’s room, they passed through the kitchen, warm and moist and steamy from the big washing in progress. Maria, in her excitement, jammed the bedroom and bedroom-closet doors together, and for five minutes, through the partly open door, clouds of steam, smelling of soap-suds and dirt, poured into the sick chamber.

text: She arrived at the house gasping and winded, left short of breath by how frantically she’d rushed to get there. Dreading she would walk straight into some kind of trouble, she had crammed the small handful of dollars she owned into her fabric hand-satchel before setting out; she was so certain disaster had struck her brother that she stumbled forward, sobbing, right into his arms, all while silently shoving the satchel into his hands.
source_text: She arrived at the house panting and short of breath from the haste she had made. Apprehensive of trouble, she had stuffed the few dollars she possessed into her hand-satchel; and so sure was she that disaster had overtaken her brother, that she stumbled forward, sobbing, into his arms, at the same time thrusting the satchel mutely at him.

text: Ruth and Martin barely spoke as they walked side by side. She was too stunned to string together casual remarks, while he was sunk in dull, unresponsive apathy. Only two lines passed between them the entire way: once, he noted offhand that he would soon be leaving to return to the South Seas, and once, she asked him to forgive her for having sought him out at all. That was the full extent of their conversation. Their parting on her doorstep was stiffly conventional. They shook hands, exchanged quiet good nights, and he lifted his hat in a gesture of polite courtesy. The door swung shut behind her, and he lit a cigarette before turning to head back to his hotel. When he reached the very doorway he had watched Norman shrink into earlier, he paused and peered inside with a quiet, speculative curiosity.
source_text: During the walk Ruth and Martin held little conversation. She was stunned. He was apathetic. Once, he mentioned that he was going away, back to the South Seas, and, once, she asked him to forgive her having come to him. And that was all. The parting at her door was conventional. They shook hands, said good night, and he lifted his hat. The door swung shut, and he lighted a cigarette and turned back for his hotel. When he came to the doorway into which he had seen Norman shrink, he stopped and looked in in a speculative humor.

text: “It is.” Her voice was low, unyielding, each word delivered with deliberate care. “This is entirely of my own free will. You have disgraced me so thoroughly I am too ashamed to show my face to my friends. I know all of them are talking about me behind my back. That is all I have to tell you. You have made me deeply unhappy, and I never wish to see you again.”
source_text: “It is.” She spoke in a low, firm voice and with deliberation. “It is of my own free will. You have disgraced me so that I am ashamed to meet my friends. They are all talking about me, I know. That is all I can tell you. You have made me very unhappy, and I never wish to see you again.”

text: Days drifted by one after another, and he stuck to a steady routine of getting eight full hours of sleep each night. Even though, for now, while he waited for more checks to come in, he only ate at cheap Japanese diners where full meals cost just ten cents, his once gaunt frame filled back out, and the sunken hollows under his cheekbones smoothed over too. He’d stopped running himself ragged with too little sleep, nonstop work, and cramming more study into his days than he could handle. He didn’t write a single word, and all his books stayed shut on their shelves. He spent hours hiking out in the rolling hills, and lazed away whole afternoons in the quiet, tree-shaded parks around town. He had no friends or casual acquaintances right now, and he had zero interest in seeking any out. He was holding out for some unnameable spark, coming from somewhere he couldn’t even guess at, to kick his stalled life back into gear. Until that spark arrived, though, his days stayed worn thin, unplanned, hollow, and completely unoccupied.
source_text: The days slipped along, and he slept eight hours regularly every night. Though now, while waiting for more checks, he ate in the Japanese restaurants where meals were served for ten cents, his wasted body filled out, as did the hollows in his cheeks. He no longer abused himself with short sleep, overwork, and overstudy. He wrote nothing, and the books were closed. He walked much, out in the hills, and loafed long hours in the quiet parks. He had no friends nor acquaintances, nor did he make any. He had no inclination. He was waiting for some impulse, from he knew not where, to put his stopped life into motion again. In the meantime his life remained run down, planless, and empty and idle.

text:
Thus he lingered at Lila’s side, gaze fixed on her profile, his throat tight with the love he ached to voice but dared not set loose. He feared jolting her soft, sheltered view of the world, and he still doubted he was enough for someone as bright and gentle as she was. If he’d only realized it, he was already taking exactly the path that would win her. Love existed long before humans learned to shape sounds into words, and in those first raw eons it forged quiet, wordless tricks it has never set aside. It was by this ancient, primal language that Elias courted Lila. He had no idea he was doing so at first, though the truth dawned on him slowly, months down the line.

The brush of his calloused palm against hers carried far more weight than any carefully strung line he might have stammered out, and the quiet, unshakable heft of his strength that wrapped around her when they walked close was far more intoxicating than the printed verses and shouted declarations of ten thousand generations of smitten suitors. Anything he might have said out loud would have landed first on the scales of her deliberate judgment, to be weighed and poked and questioned; but those soft, fleeting brushes of skin slipped straight past all that, straight to the quiet instincts thrumming under her skin. Her critical sense was as fresh and untested as she was at nineteen, but those instincts were as ancient as the human line, and older still. They had been new when love itself was new, and they were far wiser than social rules, other people’s opinions, and every other flimsy, recently invented thing. So her judgment never stirred. It was never called upon, and she had no idea how powerfully Elias was tugging, every second they spent together, at the part of her that was made to love.

That he adored her, by contrast, was as obvious as the sun hanging high in the summer sky, and she took quiet, intentional joy in noticing the little tells of his affection: the honey-gold fire that lit his hazel eyes when he looked at her, the faint tremor in his fingers when he passed her a drink, the deep, dusky flush that always crept up under the bronze of his sun-kissed cheeks when she laughed at his jokes. She even went a step further, prodding him on in shy, small ways, so subtly that he never caught on, and so half-unconscious that she barely registered her own game. She shivered with quiet pride at these little signs of her power, the proof that she was grown, a woman who could bend a man’s whole attention to her, and she took a soft, Eve-like glee in teasing him, in plucking at the threads of his longing just to see him flush and stammer.
source_text: So he waited with Ruth and watched, desiring to speak his love but not daring. He was afraid of shocking her, and he was not sure of himself. Had he but known it, he was following the right course with her. Love came into the world before articulate speech, and in its own early youth it had learned ways and means that it had never forgotten. It was in this old, primitive way that Martin wooed Ruth. He did not know he was doing it at first, though later he divined it. The touch of his hand on hers was vastly more potent than any word he could utter, the impact of his strength on her imagination was more alluring than the printed poems and spoken passions of a thousand generations of lovers. Whatever his tongue could express would have appealed, in part, to her judgment; but the touch of hand, the fleeting contact, made its way directly to her instinct. Her judgment was as young as she, but her instincts were as old as the race and older. They had been young when love was young, and they were wiser than convention and opinion and all the new-born things. So her judgment did not act. There was no call upon it, and she did not realize the strength of the appeal Martin made from moment to moment to her love-nature. That he loved her, on the other hand, was as clear as day, and she consciously delighted in beholding his love-manifestations—the glowing eyes with their tender lights, the trembling hands, and the never failing swarthy flush that flooded darkly under his sunburn. She even went farther, in a timid way inciting him, but doing it so delicately that he never suspected, and doing it half-consciously, so that she scarcely suspected herself. She thrilled with these proofs of her power that proclaimed her a woman, and she took an Eve-like delight in tormenting him and playing upon him.

text: The entire layered vision unfurled faster than a lightning strike, not so much as a lull marring their conversation, nor a flicker disrupting his steady line of thinking. Across the canvas of his mind’s eye, he watched himself and this warm, luminous young woman face one another, trading easy, well-spoken words in a sunlit room lined with leather-bound books, framed oil paintings, and the soft hum of cultivated ease, every corner washed in the steady, golden glow of unwavering light. Fringing this central image, fading to the far blurs of his mental screen, were opposing vignettes, each a frozen snippet of memory, and he was the unhurried observer, free to linger on whatever he chose. These other scenes swam into view through wispy veils of drifting, acrid fog, melting away in bursts of garish, blood-red neon. He saw ranch hands leaning against a sawdust-floored saloon bar, knocking back fiery rye, the air thick with crude jokes and unprintable insults, and he was right there among them, swilling liquor and shouting curses as loud as the rowdiest of the bunch, or hunched over a scarred wood table under sputtering, soot-streaked kerosene lamps, poker chips clinking and skittering as the dealer fanned cards around the circle. He saw himself bare-chested, knuckles bared, trading brutal blows with Belfast Mick in the cramped forecastle of the *Wanderer’s Wake*; then the blood-sticky deck of the *Hollow Star*, that pale gray dawn when the crew tried to mutiny, the first mate convulsing in his death throes against the main hatch, the captain’s revolver spitting flame and acrid smoke, the men’s faces twisted into feral snarls, screaming vulgar blasphemies as they crumpled to the planks around him. And then he snapped back to the central image, soft, quiet, and immaculate in its steady glow, where Elara sat chatting with him among shelves of books and hung canvases; he spotted the glossy grand piano in the corner where she would soon play lilting pieces just for him, and he caught the echo of his own carefully chosen, perfectly articulated words, “But then, might I not be built in a way that makes me especially suited to write?”
source_text: The whole composite vision was achieved with the speed of light, producing no pause in the conversation, nor interrupting his calm train of thought. On the screen of his imagination he saw himself and this sweet and beautiful girl, facing each other and conversing in good English, in a room of books and paintings and tone and culture, and all illuminated by a bright light of steadfast brilliance; while ranged about and fading away to the remote edges of the screen were antithetical scenes, each scene a picture, and he the onlooker, free to look at will upon what he wished. He saw these other scenes through drifting vapors and swirls of sullen fog dissolving before shafts of red and garish light. He saw cowboys at the bar, drinking fierce whiskey, the air filled with obscenity and ribald language, and he saw himself with them drinking and cursing with the wildest, or sitting at table with them, under smoking kerosene lamps, while the chips clicked and clattered and the cards were dealt around. He saw himself, stripped to the waist, with naked fists, fighting his great fight with Liverpool Red in the forecastle of the _Susquehanna_; and he saw the bloody deck of the _John Rogers_, that gray morning of attempted mutiny, the mate kicking in death-throes on the main-hatch, the revolver in the old man’s hand spitting fire and smoke, the men with passion-wrenched faces, of brutes screaming vile blasphemies and falling about him—and then he returned to the central scene, calm and clean in the steadfast light, where Ruth sat and talked with him amid books and paintings; and he saw the grand piano upon which she would later play to him; and he heard the echoes of his own selected and correct words, “But then, may I not be peculiarly constituted to write?”

text: Martin trudged into the kitchen, his heart heavy as lead, the sight of her flushed cheeks and unkempt, worn frame burning into his mind like corrosive acid. If she just had a moment to breathe, she might still care for him, he reasoned. But she was being run ragged. Bernard Higginbotham was a monster for driving her this relentlessly. Even so, he could not shake the quiet disappointment that the kiss had held no warmth, no beauty. It had been an uncommon gesture, to be sure: for years, she had only kissed him when he came home from long voyages or left to set sail again. But this one had carried the faint, bitter tang of soapsuds on her lips, and he had felt how slack and worn those lips were against his cheek. There was none of the sharp, eager press that should come with a kiss given out of affection. It was the kiss of a woman so worn down by years of unrelenting toil that she had forgotten how to kiss with any feeling at all. He could still picture her as a young unmarried girl, who would dance all night with the liveliest crowd after a full grueling day slaving at the laundry, and never even complain when she left the dance floor to head straight into another long day of work. Then his mind drifted to Ruth, to the cool, soft sweetness that clung to every part of her, and must dwell in her lips too. Her kiss, he thought, would be just like her handshake, or the direct, warm way she looked at a person: steady, sincere, unapologetic. He let himself dare to picture those lips pressed to his, and the fantasy was so bright and vivid that his head spun, as if he was drifting through clouds of soft rose petals, their heady scent flooding every corner of his mind.
source_text: Martin went into the kitchen with a sinking heart, the image of her red face and slatternly form eating its way like acid into his brain. She might love him if she only had some time, he concluded. But she was worked to death. Bernard Higginbotham was a brute to work her so hard. But he could not help but feel, on the other hand, that there had not been anything beautiful in that kiss. It was true, it was an unusual kiss. For years she had kissed him only when he returned from voyages or departed on voyages. But this kiss had tasted of soapsuds, and the lips, he had noticed, were flabby. There had been no quick, vigorous lip-pressure such as should accompany any kiss. Hers was the kiss of a tired woman who had been tired so long that she had forgotten how to kiss. He remembered her as a girl, before her marriage, when she would dance with the best, all night, after a hard day’s work at the laundry, and think nothing of leaving the dance to go to another day’s hard work. And then he thought of Ruth and the cool sweetness that must reside in her lips as it resided in all about her. Her kiss would be like her hand-shake or the way she looked at one, firm and frank. In imagination he dared to think of her lips on his, and so vividly did he imagine that he went dizzy at the thought and seemed to rift through clouds of rose-petals, filling his brain with their perfume.

text: Every last thread of that experience, and a hundred unspoken half-feelings he’d woven in alongside it, made up the tale he was telling, and he was convinced it was the raw power of that narrative that was making her glow as she leaned in to listen. Her eyes were round and unblinking, pink had flushed up through the usual waxy pallor of her cheeks, and long before he reached the final line of the tale, he could have sworn her chest was rising and falling in quick, shallow little gasps. She was thrumming with heat, that much was true, but the fire had nothing to do with the story he told. She’d barely registered the plot at all; all she felt was Elias’s searing intensity, that wild, unbridled force of being that seemed to roll off his frame in waves and wrap itself tight around her, down to her bones. The odd, unspoken contradiction lay in the fact that the story itself was the vessel holding all that crackling energy, the temporary conduit through which his strength flowed straight to her. She couldn’t see the medium at all, only the power thrumming through it, so that when she looked as if she was utterly swept away by the twists of his tale, she was actually reeling from something entirely unconnected to it: a sharp, dangerous thought that had bloomed unbidden in the back of her mind. She’d caught herself daydreaming about what it might feel like to be married to him, and the moment she recognized how reckless and hungry that thought was, a jolt of fear shot through her. It was unladylike, improper, nothing like the quiet, demure girl she’d always been. She’d never felt the restless pull of her own womanhood before, having spent her whole life adrift in the soft, chivalric dreamscapes of Tennyson’s verse, so sheltered she’d never even parsed the full weight of the poet’s gentle allusions to the messy, carnal urges that sometimes disrupted the stately bonds between knights and their ladies. She’d been half-asleep to the world her whole life, and now existence was pounding, loud and unignorable, on every door of her heart. Half of her was frantic to slide all the bolts home and bar the entrance tight against the chaos, while a wild, unruly instinct screamed at her to fling every door wide open and invite that delicious, unknowable stranger across the threshold.
source_text: It was this, all of it, and more, that he had put into his story, and it was this, he believed, that warmed her as she sat and listened. Her eyes were wide, color was in her pale cheeks, and before he finished it seemed to him that she was almost panting. Truly, she was warmed; but she was warmed, not by the story, but by him. She did not think much of the story; it was Martin’s intensity of power, the old excess of strength that seemed to pour from his body and on and over her. The paradox of it was that it was the story itself that was freighted with his power, that was the channel, for the time being, through which his strength poured out to her. She was aware only of the strength, and not of the medium, and when she seemed most carried away by what he had written, in reality she had been carried away by something quite foreign to it—by a thought, terrible and perilous, that had formed itself unsummoned in her brain. She had caught herself wondering what marriage was like, and the becoming conscious of the waywardness and ardor of the thought had terrified her. It was unmaidenly. It was not like her. She had never been tormented by womanhood, and she had lived in a dreamland of Tennysonian poesy, dense even to the full significance of that delicate master’s delicate allusions to the grossnesses that intrude upon the relations of queens and knights. She had been asleep, always, and now life was thundering imperatively at all her doors. Mentally she was in a panic to shoot the bolts and drop the bars into place, while wanton instincts urged her to throw wide her portals and bid the deliciously strange visitor to enter in.

text: Even as he moped through the tedious days aboard the steamer, a fresh, sharp misery settled over him. What would he do when the vessel docked in Tahiti? He would have to go ashore, for a start, then place orders for his trade goods, secure passage on a schooner bound for the Marquesas, and tend to a thousand small, draining tasks that made his chest tight just to contemplate. Every time he forced himself to confront his situation deliberately, he could see exactly how desperate his peril was. He was truly in the Valley of the Shadow, and the core of his danger lay in the fact that he felt no fear at all. If only he could feel frightened, he would reach toward life, fight to stay afloat. But since he was unafraid, he drifted ever deeper into the shadow. None of the old, familiar small joys of his life held any delight for him anymore. The *Mariposa* was now cutting through the northeast trade winds, and that same crisp, wine-rich gust that had once thrilled him now only irritated him. He had his deck chair moved out of the wind’s path, eager to escape the embrace of what had been a boisterous, loyal comrade of his old days and nights at sea.
source_text: Miserable as he was on the steamer, a new misery came upon him. What when the steamer reached Tahiti? He would have to go ashore. He would have to order his trade-goods, to find a passage on a schooner to the Marquesas, to do a thousand and one things that were awful to contemplate. Whenever he steeled himself deliberately to think, he could see the desperate peril in which he stood. In all truth, he was in the Valley of the Shadow, and his danger lay in that he was not afraid. If he were only afraid, he would make toward life. Being unafraid, he was drifting deeper into the shadow. He found no delight in the old familiar things of life. The _Mariposa_ was now in the northeast trades, and this wine of wind, surging against him, irritated him. He had his chair moved to escape the embrace of this lusty comrade of old days and nights.

text: A split second later, Martin’s right hand flew up to cinch a choking grip around his throat, and he shook him so hard his teeth clattered together. But when Martin stared straight into his eyes, he found no fear there at all—nothing but a strange, taunting devilishness staring back. Martin caught himself before he went too far, hauled Brissenden by the neck to fling him sideways onto the bed, and released his hold in the same motion.
source_text: The next instant Martin’s right hand had shot to a throttling clutch on his throat, and he was being shaken till his teeth rattled. But Martin, looking into his eyes, saw no fear there,—naught but a curious and mocking devil. Martin remembered himself, and flung Brissenden, by the neck, sidelong upon the bed, at the same moment releasing his hold.

text: The opening stretches of conversation drifted, aimless and scattered at first. Even so, Elias could not help but marvel at the sharp, darting agility of their intellects. These were men of firm, often clashing convictions, quick with a joke and clever with a retort, yet never hollow or superficial. He spotted immediately that no matter what topic they landed on, every man wove in threads of his full breadth of knowledge to make his case, each holding a deep, cohesive understanding of both human society and the wider cosmos. Their beliefs were never spoon-fed to them; every last one was a dissident in his own right, and stale, overused platitudes never passed their lips. Never in all his visits to the Hathaway household had Elias heard such a dizzying, expansive range of topics up for discussion. It seemed the only boundary to what they cared to unpack was the number of hours left in the evening. Chat drifted from Zadie Smith’s newly released novel to Jeremy O. Harris’ most recent stage work, meandered through the future of live theater to fond recollections of Lorraine Hansberry. They praised or tore apart the morning’s paper editorials, jumped from labor rights reforms in Norway to James Baldwin and Harold Bloom, moved on to Chinese trade ambitions in the Global South and the economic ramifications of rising Indo-Pacific tensions, argued over recent French parliamentary elections and Jean-Luc Mélenchon’s latest fiery address, then settled into debates over local politics, the latest backroom deals and scandals roiling the regional labor party leadership, and the quiet lobbying that had sparked the longshoremen’s strike up the coast. Elias was stunned by how much insider context they carried; they knew all the details never printed in the papers—the backroom favors, the hidden strings, the unseen hands pulling the levers that made public figures dance to unheard tunes. To Elias’ astonishment, the young woman at the end of the table, Lila, jumped into the fray too, showcasing a sharpness of mind he had never encountered in the small circle of women he had known growing up. The two of them fell into a side discussion of Swinburne and Rossetti first, after which she steered him into niche corners of 19th century French symbolist writing that he had never explored, leaving him fumbling for coherent responses. His chance to turn the tables came when she defended the work of Maurice Maeterlinck, and he pulled out the carefully constructed argument from his long-worked-on essay “The Blight of Unthinking Idealism.”
source_text: At first the conversation was desultory. Nevertheless Martin could not fail to appreciate the keen play of their minds. They were men with opinions, though the opinions often clashed, and, though they were witty and clever, they were not superficial. He swiftly saw, no matter upon what they talked, that each man applied the correlation of knowledge and had also a deep-seated and unified conception of society and the Cosmos. Nobody manufactured their opinions for them; they were all rebels of one variety or another, and their lips were strangers to platitudes. Never had Martin, at the Morses’, heard so amazing a range of topics discussed. There seemed no limit save time to the things they were alive to. The talk wandered from Mrs. Humphry Ward’s new book to Shaw’s latest play, through the future of the drama to reminiscences of Mansfield. They appreciated or sneered at the morning editorials, jumped from labor conditions in New Zealand to Henry James and Brander Matthews, passed on to the German designs in the Far East and the economic aspect of the Yellow Peril, wrangled over the German elections and Bebel’s last speech, and settled down to local politics, the latest plans and scandals in the union labor party administration, and the wires that were pulled to bring about the Coast Seamen’s strike. Martin was struck by the inside knowledge they possessed. They knew what was never printed in the newspapers—the wires and strings and the hidden hands that made the puppets dance. To Martin’s surprise, the girl, Mary, joined in the conversation, displaying an intelligence he had never encountered in the few women he had met. They talked together on Swinburne and Rossetti, after which she led him beyond his depth into the by-paths of French literature. His revenge came when she defended Maeterlinck and he brought into action the carefully-thought-out thesis of “The Shame of the Sun.”

text: So the little one kept growing. He was healthy and perfectly ordinary, ate his meals on a steady schedule, and slept for long, unbroken hours, yet that growing small boy had settled into his mind as a persistent, unshakable obsession. *Work performed*. The phrase haunted every corner of his thoughts. He sat across from Bernard Higginbotham at a rich, heavy Sunday dinner on the floor above Higginbotham’s Cash Store, and it took every last bit of his willpower to hold back the shout rising in his throat.
source_text: So the little thing grew bigger. He was healthy and normal, ate regularly, slept long hours, and yet the growing little thing was becoming an obsession. _Work performed_. The phrase haunted his brain. He sat opposite Bernard Higginbotham at a heavy Sunday dinner over Higginbotham’s Cash Store, and it was all he could do to restrain himself from shouting out:-

text: Joe had locked his arms around Martin and tried to heave him off balance, and Martin was twisting and squirming to wriggle free of the advantage Joe’s grip afforded him. They stumbled back and forth across the room, bodies tangled tight in each other’s hold, before crashing down hard atop the splintered wreckage of a wicker chair. Joe landed underneath, his arms splayed out and pinned, Martin’s knee pressed firm to his chest. He was heaving and gasping for ragged breath by the time Martin finally let him go.
source_text: Joe had clinched and attempted to throw him, and he was twisting and writhing out of the advantage of the other’s hold. They reeled about the room, locked in each other’s arms, and came down with a crash across the splintered wreckage of a wicker chair. Joe was underneath, with arms spread out and held and with Martin’s knee on his chest. He was panting and gasping for breath when Martin released him.

text: It was glorious, he mused, his gaze lingering on her. She had made up for every shortcoming she’d ever carried, finally stepping fully into her power as the unapologetic woman she was, unshackled from the suffocating strictures of upper-middle class decorum. It was glorious, awe-inspiring, almost reckless in its courage. Still, what had gone hollow in him? He felt no thrill, no sharp, warm stir at the bold choice she’d just made. Its grandeur and radical bravery registered only in the cool, logical corner of his mind. In what ought to have been a blazing, electric moment, he studied her with detached calm. His heart remained cold, untouched. He could not summon even a flicker of longing for her. Once more, the words Elspeth had spoken to him drifted to the front of his thoughts.
source_text: It was splendid, he thought, looking at her. She had redeemed herself for all that she had lacked, rising up at last, true woman, superior to the iron rule of bourgeois convention. It was splendid, magnificent, desperate. And yet, what was the matter with him? He was not thrilled nor stirred by what she had done. It was splendid and magnificent only intellectually. In what should have been a moment of fire, he coldly appraised her. His heart was untouched. He was unaware of any desire for her. Again he remembered Lizzie’s words.

text: “I hope Norton’s there,” he panted a moment later, shrugging off Martin’s attempt to take the two demijohns off his hands. “Norton’s a genuine idealist, a Harvard man with an exceptional memory. His idealism led him to embrace philosophical anarchy, and his family cast him out entirely over it. His father is a railroad president worth many millions, but the young man is half-starving in San Francisco, editing a small anarchist paper for twenty-five dollars a month.”
source_text: “Hope Norton’s there,” he panted a little later, resisting Martin’s effort to relieve him of the two demijohns. “Norton’s an idealist—a Harvard man. Prodigious memory. Idealism led him to philosophic anarchy, and his family threw him off. Father’s a railroad president and many times millionnaire, but the son’s starving in ’Frisco, editing an anarchist sheet for twenty-five a month.”

text: “Step foot in my shop,” came the sharp, snapping reply, “an’ I’ll send for the cops straight away. An’ I’ll make sure they throw the book at you, too. Oh, I know exactly who you are, and you won’t start no ruckus with me. I want nothing to do with the likes of you. You’re a good-for-nothing loafer, that’s what you are, an’ I ain’t born yesterday. You ain’t gonna sponge off me just ‘cause I’m marrying your sister. Why don’t you go get a job an’ earn an honest living, eh? You got any answer to that?”
source_text: “You come to my shop,” came the reply, “an’ I’ll send for the police. An’ I’ll put you through, too. Oh, I know you, but you can’t make no rough-house with me. I don’t want nothin’ to do with the likes of you. You’re a loafer, that’s what, an’ I ain’t asleep. You ain’t goin’ to do no spongin’ off me just because I’m marryin’ your sister. Why don’t you go to work an’ earn an honest livin’, eh? Answer me that.”

text: “No, those two scenarios aren’t remotely comparable. Those flash pulp tales I cranked out, bleary and worn thin, came only at the tail end of full days spent honing my prose voice. But a newspaper reporter’s grind is unrelenting hackwork from sunrise to last edition, the only obligation that takes up every corner of your waking life. It’s a gale-force existence, living only for the next breaking update, no room for past reflection or future ambition, and certainly no consideration for any prose style outside the rigid, stilted reportorial template—nothing that counts as actual art, let alone literature. Taking a reporter post right now, when my own voice is finally solidifying, taking clear, sharp shape, would be nothing short of artistic self-sabotage. As it was, every last pulp tale, every clumsy line I forced out for those cheap markets, felt like a betrayal of who I am, of my self-respect, of every reverence I hold for beautiful writing. It made my skin crawl. I knew I was compromising everything I cared about. I was quietly, shamefully thrilled when those publishers stopped buying my work, even if it meant half my wardrobe ended up at the pawn shop to cover rent. But the pure rush of writing that ‘Hearth and Horizon’ love sequence? That unfiltered, exalted creative euphoria in its most noble form? It made every last hardship worth it, and then some.”
source_text: “No, the cases are different. The storiettes were ground out, jaded, at the end of a long day of application to style. But a reporter’s work is all hack from morning till night, is the one paramount thing of life. And it is a whirlwind life, the life of the moment, with neither past nor future, and certainly without thought of any style but reportorial style, and that certainly is not literature. To become a reporter now, just as my style is taking form, crystallizing, would be to commit literary suicide. As it is, every storiette, every word of every storiette, was a violation of myself, of my self-respect, of my respect for beauty. I tell you it was sickening. I was guilty of sin. And I was secretly glad when the markets failed, even if my clothes did go into pawn. But the joy of writing the ‘Love-cycle’! The creative joy in its noblest form! That was compensation for everything.”

text: He was at the head of this bizarre, chattering procession, with Maria trotting right beside him, as the whole group filed into a confectioner’s shop in search of the largest candy cane ever made, when he ran straight into Ruth and her mother. Mrs. Morse was visibly stunned. Even Ruth felt wounded: she cared a great deal about keeping up proper appearances, and seeing her lover pressed shoulder to shoulder with Maria, leading that ragtag army of scruffy Portuguese street kids, was far from a flattering sight. But what hurt far more than the spectacle itself was what she took as his complete lack of pride and self-respect. Worse, sharpest of all her grievances, she read the entire incident as proof that he would never outrun the stain of his working-class origins. The mere fact of those roots carried enough stigma on its own, but to shamelessly flaunt them in full view of polite society—her society—was crossing a line she could not forgive. Though her engagement to Martin had been kept private, their long, close familiarity had already sparked no small amount of gossip; and as she stood in the shop, stealing covert glances at her lover and his rowdy following, she spotted several of her own acquaintances watching the scene unfold. She did not possess Martin’s easy, unselfconscious generosity of spirit, and could not rise above the unspoken judgments of her social circle. The shame cut her to the quick, her sensitive nature thrumming with the humiliation of the moment. That was why, when Martin stopped by her home later that same day, he tucked the gift he’d brought for her deep in his breast pocket, putting off giving it to her until a more favorable moment arose. The sight of Ruth in tears—hot, passionate, angry tears—was a total revelation to him. Her obvious suffering convinced him he had behaved like a thoughtless brute, even if deep in his soul he could not grasp exactly what he had done wrong, or why it mattered so much. It never once crossed his mind to feel ashamed of the people he cared for, and treating the Silva children to a special Christmas outing seemed to him in no way a sign of disregard for Ruth. All the same, once Ruth laid out her perspective for him, he did understand where she was coming from; he wrote the whole reaction off as a silly feminine foible, the kind that afflicted every woman, even the very best of them.
source_text: It was with this extraordinary procession trooping at his and Maria’s heels into a confectioner’s in quest of the biggest candy-cane ever made, that he encountered Ruth and her mother. Mrs. Morse was shocked. Even Ruth was hurt, for she had some regard for appearances, and her lover, cheek by jowl with Maria, at the head of that army of Portuguese ragamuffins, was not a pretty sight. But it was not that which hurt so much as what she took to be his lack of pride and self-respect. Further, and keenest of all, she read into the incident the impossibility of his living down his working-class origin. There was stigma enough in the fact of it, but shamelessly to flaunt it in the face of the world—her world—was going too far. Though her engagement to Martin had been kept secret, their long intimacy had not been unproductive of gossip; and in the shop, glancing covertly at her lover and his following, had been several of her acquaintances. She lacked the easy largeness of Martin and could not rise superior to her environment. She had been hurt to the quick, and her sensitive nature was quivering with the shame of it. So it was, when Martin arrived later in the day, that he kept her present in his breast-pocket, deferring the giving of it to a more propitious occasion. Ruth in tears—passionate, angry tears—was a revelation to him. The spectacle of her suffering convinced him that he had been a brute, yet in the soul of him he could not see how nor why. It never entered his head to be ashamed of those he knew, and to take the Silvas out to a Christmas treat could in no way, so it seemed to him, show lack of consideration for Ruth. On the other hand, he did see Ruth’s point of view, after she had explained it; and he looked upon it as a feminine weakness, such as afflicted all women and the best of women.

text: He turned to soft clay in her grip right away, as desperate to be remade by her as she was eager to sculpt him into the living embodiment of the perfect man she’d long held as her ideal. When she flagged how perfect the timing was, noting that high school entrance exams were starting the very next Monday, he didn’t waste a second offering to sit for them.
source_text: He was clay in her hands immediately, as passionately desirous of being moulded by her as she was desirous of shaping him into the image of her ideal of man. And when she pointed out the opportuneness of the time, that the entrance examinations to high school began on the following Monday, he promptly volunteered that he would take them.

text: That’s how the moment wrapped up, with soft, lingering touches and warm, affectionate laughter. Still, she’d gotten her way on only one issue, and she knew better than to hope for more than a single victory at a time. A quiet, feminine pride hummed in her chest at the fact that she’d successfully convinced him to quit smoking. Next time, she would talk him into accepting that formal job position he’d been brushing off—after all, hadn’t he explicitly said he’d do anything she asked of him?
source_text: And so it ended in caresses and love-laughter. But she had made one point, and she could not expect to make more than one at a time. She felt a woman’s pride in that she had made him stop smoking. Another time she would persuade him to take a position, for had he not said he would do anything she asked?

text: We’re heading to his place right now. He used to be a professor, got canned from the university, same old run-of-the-mill story. He’s got a mind as sharp as a steel trap, never lets a single detail slip past him. He scrapes by doing whatever odd work he can scare up, and I know for a fact he worked as a cheap street con artist when he hit rock bottom. The man’s completely unscrupulous—would steal the burial shroud right off a corpse if it suited him, no questions asked, no guilt whatsoever. The only real difference between him and your typical bourgeois type is that he doesn’t hide behind self-serving illusions when he takes what he wants. He’ll hold forth for hours about Nietzsche, Schopenhauer, Kant, any philosopher you bring up, but the only thing in the entire world he actually cares about—not even Mary is an exception—is his monist philosophy. Haeckel is his personal little tin god, the one figure he reveres without question. The only surefire way to insult him is to talk trash about Haeckel.
source_text: “His rooms we’re going to. One time professor—fired from university—usual story. A mind like a steel trap. Makes his living any old way. I know he’s been a street fakir when he was down. Unscrupulous. Rob a corpse of a shroud—anything. Difference between him and the bourgeoisie is that he robs without illusion. He’ll talk Nietzsche, or Schopenhauer, or Kant, or anything, but the only thing in this world, not excepting Mary, that he really cares for, is his monism. Haeckel is his little tin god. The only way to insult him is to take a slap at Haeckel.”

text: Yet even his first fumbling attempts at writing bore none of the awkward, plodding clumsiness of unremarkable talent. What defined them instead was the ungainliness of raw, unregulated power—the sort of misstep a novice makes when he swats moths with a sledgehammer, or chases delicate line drawings into shape with a stone axe. That was why Elias was perfectly happy to sell those early pieces for pocket change. He saw exactly how flawed they were, and it had taken him barely any time at all to arrive at that understanding. All his hope was pinned to the work he produced after those early missteps. He had worked to be far more than a hack churning out disposable pulp for monthly periodicals. He had put in the hours to master the precise, delicate tools of craft, all while refusing to dilute the fiery core of strength that anchored his writing. His deliberate goal had been to hone that strength, reining in its excesses to make it sharper rather than dulling it. Nor had he turned his back on his devotion to capturing unvarnished truth. His work was rooted firmly in realism, though he had labored to weave into it the glow of whimsy and the soft, sharp edge of imaginative beauty. What he chased was a fervent, breathing realism, thrumming with the quiet hopes and stubborn faith of ordinary people. He wanted to put life on the page exactly as it was, leaving in every fumbling reach for meaning, every quiet cry of the soul striving for something more.
source_text: But even his earliest efforts were not marked with the clumsiness of mediocrity. What characterized them was the clumsiness of too great strength—the clumsiness which the tyro betrays when he crushes butterflies with battering rams and hammers out vignettes with a war-club. So it was that Martin was glad to sell his early efforts for songs. He knew them for what they were, and it had not taken him long to acquire this knowledge. What he pinned his faith to was his later work. He had striven to be something more than a mere writer of magazine fiction. He had sought to equip himself with the tools of artistry. On the other hand, he had not sacrificed strength. His conscious aim had been to increase his strength by avoiding excess of strength. Nor had he departed from his love of reality. His work was realism, though he had endeavored to fuse with it the fancies and beauties of imagination. What he sought was an impassioned realism, shot through with human aspiration and faith. What he wanted was life as it was, with all its spirit-groping and soul-reaching left in.

text: Looking back from his current elevated vantage point, the old world he’d once known so intimately—one of shorelines and open seas, creaking wooden ships, gruff sailors and sharp, predatory women—felt shockingly small at first glance. Even so, it wove seamlessly into the new world he was only just discovering, stretching out to fit the broader space. He was inherently driven to seek out coherence, so the first time he spotted unexpected overlaps between those two wildly disparate spheres, he was caught off guard. He also felt lifted, almost ennobled, by the soaring ideas and quiet, resonant beauty he encountered in the books he was reading now. That discovery made him surer than ever that everyone in the rarefied social strata Ruth and her family occupied lived and breathed exactly those kinds of graceful, thoughtful ideas. The working-class world he still inhabited down below, by contrast, felt crass and unworthy. He ached to scrub away every trace of that coarseness that had stained every year of his life so far, to climb up to that purified, elevated sphere where the upper class made their home. For his entire childhood and young adulthood, a vague, nagging restlessness had hung over him. He’d never been able to name what he was craving, but he’d chased that unidentifiable something fruitlessly for years, right up until the day he met Ruth. Now, that quiet restlessness had turned sharp, almost agonizing, and he finally knew exactly, without a shred of doubt, what he was meant to pursue: he had to have beauty, intellect, and love, all three, to feel complete.
source_text: When he looked back now from his vantage-ground, the old world he had known, the world of land and sea and ships, of sailor-men and harpy-women, seemed a very small world; and yet it blended in with this new world and expanded. His mind made for unity, and he was surprised when at first he began to see points of contact between the two worlds. And he was ennobled, as well, by the loftiness of thought and beauty he found in the books. This led him to believe more firmly than ever that up above him, in society like Ruth and her family, all men and women thought these thoughts and lived them. Down below where he lived was the ignoble, and he wanted to purge himself of the ignoble that had soiled all his days, and to rise to that sublimated realm where dwelt the upper classes. All his childhood and youth had been troubled by a vague unrest; he had never known what he wanted, but he had wanted something that he had hunted vainly for until he met Ruth. And now his unrest had become sharp and painful, and he knew at last, clearly and definitely, that it was beauty, and intellect, and love that he must have.

text: Half a beat later, he was thrown off-kilter by how unhesitatingly he’d said yes. Back at his boarding house, three hours of drudge-work for the trade presses loomed before he could even crawl between the sheets, and once he did, a dog-eared copy of Haeckel’s evolutionary essays waited on his nightstand, not to mention John Stuart Mill’s memoir, which he found as rich with quiet adventure as any pulpy detective thriller. Why throw away precious hours on a stranger he’d taken an immediate dislike to? he wondered. Yet it was neither the man nor the whiskey that pulled at him, but everything tied to the act of buying a round: the gilded overhead lights, mirrored walls glinting with rows of cut crystal, the flush of heated cheeks and the low, rolling thrum of men laughing and talking over one another. That was the core of it, the sound of other men: unflappable, sure of themselves, men who carried good fortune in their bones and bought rounds for strangers like it was the most natural thing in the world. He was lonely, that was the whole mess of it; that was why he’d leapt at the invitation the way a mackerel snaps at a scrap of white cloth strung on a fishing hook. He hadn’t had a drink in a public taproom since he’d gone away with Jude to Pine Ridge Thermal Pools, save for a single glass of rough red he’d shared with the Greek provision merchant down the street from his lodgings. Mental burnout didn’t stoke the same thirst for spirits as backbreaking physical work did, and he’d felt no urge to seek it out these past months. But right now, he ached for that drink, or more accurately, for the warm, loud air where drinks were poured and raised in toasts without a second thought. The Cavern was exactly that kind of spot, where he and Thorne sank into deep, worn leather armchairs and ordered scotch and seltzer to share.
source_text: The next moment he was nonplussed by the readiness of his acceptance.
