At home was several hours’ hack-work waiting for him before he went to bed, and after he went to bed there was a volume of Weismann waiting for him, to say nothing of Herbert Spencer’s Autobiography, which was as replete for him with romance as any thrilling novel. Why should he waste any time with this man he did not like? was his thought. And yet, it was not so much the man nor the drink as was it what was associated with the drink—the bright lights, the mirrors and dazzling array of glasses, the warm and glowing faces and the resonant hum of the voices of men. That was it, it was the voices of men, optimistic men, men who breathed success and spent their money for drinks like men. He was lonely, that was what was the matter with him; that was why he had snapped at the invitation as a bonita strikes at a white rag on a hook. Not since with Joe, at Shelly Hot Springs, with the one exception of the wine he took with the Portuguese grocer, had Martin had a drink at a public bar. Mental exhaustion did not produce a craving for liquor such as physical exhaustion did, and he had felt no need for it. But just now he felt desire for the drink, or, rather, for the atmosphere wherein drinks were dispensed and disposed of. Such a place was the Grotto, where Brissenden and he lounged in capacious leather chairs and drank Scotch and soda.

text: “Quite the opposite,” Martin mused, “it’s because the whole incident isn’t worth bothering over.” He burst into a loud, warm, genuine laugh. “I’ll admit you absolutely made a fool of me, Brissenden. That I’m half-starving and you’ve noticed it is just a plain, unremarkable fact, there’s no shame in it. You see, I spend all my time scoffing at the petty, conventional moralities the herd lives by; then you drift along, say one sharp, unvarnished truth, and right away I’m bending to the exact same silly little rules I claim to despise.”
source_text: “On the contrary,” Martin considered, “because the incident is not worthy.” He broke into a laugh, hearty and wholesome. “I confess you made a fool of me, Brissenden. That I am hungry and you are aware of it are only ordinary phenomena, and there’s no disgrace. You see, I laugh at the conventional little moralities of the herd; then you drift by, say a sharp, true word, and immediately I am the slave of the same little moralities.”

text: “See here—” Brissenden took a slow sip of his hot toddy, a satisfied smile playing across his lips as he savored the warm drink. “It’s the women, that’s why. They’ll nag and fret at you till the day you draw your last breath, same as they’ve already been getting under your skin lately, unless I’m a bumbling fool who hasn’t a clue what he’s talking about. And don’t even think about throttling me to shut me up, I’m getting this off my chest first. This is obviously just your silly first infatuation, but for the love of all that’s beautiful, have better judgment next time. What in the world do you want with some spoiled bourgeois rich girl anyway? Steer clear of that whole lot. Go for some bold, untamed wildfire of a woman instead, the kind who laughs at life’s trivialities, sneers at the thought of death, and loves fiercely for as long as the moment lasts. Women like that exist, and they’ll fall for you just as readily as any mealy-mouthed, coddled little thing raised behind the sheltered walls of bourgeois privilege.”
source_text: “Because—” Brissenden sipped his toddy and smiled appreciation of it. “Because of the women. They will worry you until you die, as they have already worried you, or else I was born yesterday. Now there’s no use in your choking me; I’m going to have my say. This is undoubtedly your calf love; but for Beauty’s sake show better taste next time. What under heaven do you want with a daughter of the bourgeoisie? Leave them alone. Pick out some great, wanton flame of a woman, who laughs at life and jeers at death and loves one while she may. There are such women, and they will love you just as readily as any pusillanimous product of bourgeois sheltered life.”

text: The produce shop where Martin bought his vegetables was run by an American shopkeeper with surprisingly lax business standards: he let Martin rack up a five-dollar tab before finally cutting off his charge privileges. The baker had drawn the line at two dollars, and the butcher at four. When Martin tallied up all his outstanding debts, he realized the total credit extended to him anywhere in the world added up to exactly fourteen dollars and eighty-five cents. He was already fully caught up on his typewriter rental payments, but he estimated he could swing two more months of credit on that, which would amount to eight dollars. Once he used that up, every last possible line of credit available to him would be completely exhausted.
source_text: The fruit store, where Martin had bought his vegetables, was run by an American whose business principles were so weak that he let Martin run a bill of five dollars before stopping his credit. The baker stopped at two dollars, and the butcher at four dollars. Martin added his debts and found that he was possessed of a total credit in all the world of fourteen dollars and eighty-five cents. He was up with his type-writer rent, but he estimated that he could get two months’ credit on that, which would be eight dollars. When that occurred, he would have exhausted all possible credit.

text:
Tuesday brought another stretch of relentless, unbroken drudgery. Joe’s blistering work pace immediately drew Martin’s admiration. The man worked like a whole horde of demons possessed, strung tight as an instrument tuned to concert pitch, and not a single second of the long shift slipped by without him scrambling to wring extra productivity out of each moment. His entire focus was split between the task at hand and finding ways to cut wasted effort, pointing out to Martin where he was taking five movements to complete a step that could be done in three, or three that could be trimmed to two.
“Eliminating wasted motion,” Martin labeled the practice as he observed and patterned his own work after Joe’s example. A skilled, nimble worker in his own right, Martin had long taken pride in the fact that no man could either do his job for him or outperform him at a task. Accordingly, he poured the same single-minded focus into his work, eagerly snatching up every tip and suggestion his coworker offered. He “rubbed out” collars and cuffs, working the excess starch out from between the double layers of linen to prevent unsightly blisters during the ironing step, and moved so quickly through the task that even Joe offered him open praise for his speed.
source_text: Tuesday was a day of similar unremitting toil. The speed with which Joe worked won Martin’s admiration. Joe was a dozen of demons for work. He was keyed up to concert pitch, and there was never a moment in the long day when he was not fighting for moments. He concentrated himself upon his work and upon how to save time, pointing out to Martin where he did in five motions what could be done in three, or in three motions what could be done in two. “Elimination of waste motion,” Martin phrased it as he watched and patterned after. He was a good workman himself, quick and deft, and it had always been a point of pride with him that no man should do any of his work for him or outwork him. As a result, he concentrated with a similar singleness of purpose, greedily snapping up the hints and suggestions thrown out by his working mate. He “rubbed out” collars and cuffs, rubbing the starch out from between the double thicknesses of linen so that there would be no blisters when it came to the ironing, and doing it at a pace that elicited Joe’s praise.

text: “That was entirely my fault,” Elias admitted contritely. “But I’d already riled him up, and he was so absorbing that I didn’t stop to think it through. Can you believe he’s the sharpest, most intellectually vibrant person I’ve ever conversed with? And I’ll tell you another thing. I used to think everyone who attended university, or held those lofty positions in upper society, was just as brilliant and sharp-minded as he is.”
source_text: “My mistake,” Martin admitted contritely. “But I’d got him stirred up, and he was so interesting that I did not think. Do you know, he is the brightest, the most intellectual, man I have ever talked with. And I’ll tell you something else. I once thought that everybody who went to universities, or who sat in the high places in society, was just as brilliant and intelligent as he.”

text: Her gaze drifted over the broad line of his throat, barely visible in the faint starlight, then trailed up to his steady, held-high head, and that familiar old urge to rest her palms against the nape of his neck bubbled up again in her. That raw, unyielding strength she claimed to loathe drew her in anyway. Her loneliness swelled sharper, heavier, and a bone-deep weariness settled over her. Perched on the tilting sailboat felt awkward and uncomfortable, and she thought back to the throbbing headache he’d once eased for her, how just being near him wrapped her in a quiet, calming rest. He was right next to her, so close their arms almost brushed, and every small lurch of the boat seemed to tip her straight into his space. Then a faint, half-formed impulse flickered to life in her chest: to slump against him, to let his steady strength hold her weight. She’d barely even registered the thought before it took over, pulling her slowly in his direction. Or was that just the boat shifting under them? She couldn’t tell. She never could, later, when she thought back to the moment. All she was aware of in that second was the press of her body against his, and how good that soft, uncomplicated relief felt, how the quiet calm she’d remembered wrapped around her at once. Maybe it really had just been the boat’s unsteady tilt, but she didn’t make any move to right herself, to pull back. Her weight rested light on his shoulder, but it was there, solid, and she didn’t shift away even when he adjusted his posture to make the spot she was leaning against softer, more comfortable for her.
source_text: Her eyes wandered along the strong throat, dimly seen in the starlight, and over the firm-poised head, and the old desire to lay her hands upon his neck came back to her. The strength she abhorred attracted her. Her feeling of loneliness became more pronounced, and she felt tired. Her position on the heeling boat irked her, and she remembered the headache he had cured and the soothing rest that resided in him. He was sitting beside her, quite beside her, and the boat seemed to tilt her toward him. Then arose in her the impulse to lean against him, to rest herself against his strength—a vague, half-formed impulse, which, even as she considered it, mastered her and made her lean toward him. Or was it the heeling of the boat? She did not know. She never knew. She knew only that she was leaning against him and that the easement and soothing rest were very good. Perhaps it had been the boat’s fault, but she made no effort to retrieve it. She leaned lightly against his shoulder, but she leaned, and she continued to lean when he shifted his position to make it more comfortable for her.

text: Kreis launched straight into a debate with Martin right off the bat, but Norton cut in little by little, until before anyone knew it, he and Kreis were off in their own heated, personal back-and-forth. Martin listened on, so stunned he almost wanted to rub his eyes to make sure he wasn’t dreaming. This whole thing felt impossible—even more so that it was unfolding in the working-class enclave south of Market, of all places. The ideas from the books he’d studied weren’t just static words on a page to these men; they were vivid, breathing, alive. They spoke with such fire and enthusiasm, that intellectual spark riling them up the same way he’d watched alcohol or rage rile up other men he’d known. What he was hearing wasn’t the dry, dusty philosophy of printed text he’d pored over before, written by half-legendary, larger-than-life thinkers like Kant and Spencer. This was living philosophy, thrumming with warm, red blood, embodied so fully in these two men that even their expressions twisted and glowed with excitement. Every so often other men in the room chimed in, all of them following the discussion so closely that their cigarettes burned down unheeded in their hands, their faces sharp and rapt with attention.
source_text: Kreis began directly on Martin, but step by step Norton interfered, until he and Kreis were off and away in a personal battle. Martin listened and fain would have rubbed his eyes. It was impossible that this should be, much less in the labor ghetto south of Market. The books were alive in these men. They talked with fire and enthusiasm, the intellectual stimulant stirring them as he had seen drink and anger stir other men. What he heard was no longer the philosophy of the dry, printed word, written by half-mythical demigods like Kant and Spencer. It was living philosophy, with warm, red blood, incarnated in these two men till its very features worked with excitement. Now and again other men joined in, and all followed the discussion with cigarettes going out in their hands and with alert, intent faces.

text: Still no reply arrived the following day, nor the one after that. *Delinquent* lay undisturbed on the scuffed kitchen table, and with each passing sunset the stack of rejected manuscripts tucked under the table leg swelled higher. For the first time in years, Elias’s usual deep, unbroken sleep was fractured by insomnia; he tossed and turned through endless, jittery nights. Three separate times he walked to the Halewood family’s front door, only to be sent away by the crisply uniformed house servant who answered the bell. Thaddeus was laid up with a wracking fever in his downtown hotel room, too frail to step outside, and though Elias stopped by to sit with him nearly every afternoon, he never brought up his own mounting worries to add to his friend’s strain.
source_text: But no answer came the next day, nor the next. “Overdue” lay untouched upon the table, and each day the heap of returned manuscripts under the table grew larger. For the first time Martin’s glorious sleep was interrupted by insomnia, and he tossed through long, restless nights. Three times he called at the Morse home, but was turned away by the servant who answered the bell. Brissenden lay sick in his hotel, too feeble to stir out, and, though Martin was with him often, he did not worry him with his troubles.

text: He longed to be tender with his sister, who was good-hearted, and who he knew loved him in her own particular way. But as the years slipped by, she somehow seemed less and less like the person he’d once known, growing ever more baffling to him. He chalked the change up to backbreaking work, the constant demands of her many children, and her husband’s endless nagging. Then, in a sudden, vivid flicker of fancy, it struck him that her very nature seemed to have taken on the qualities of stale, wilted vegetables, acrid, stinking soapsuds, and the greasy dimes, nickels, and quarters she took in over the store’s counter each day.
source_text: He wanted to be affectionate to this sister, who was good, and who, in her way, he knew, loved him. But, somehow, she grew less herself as the years went by, and more and more baffling. It was the hard work, the many children, and the nagging of her husband, he decided, that had changed her. It came to him, in a flash of fancy, that her nature seemed taking on the attributes of stale vegetables, smelly soapsuds, and of the greasy dimes, nickels, and quarters she took in over the counter of the store.

text: “Guess what I’ve sorted out—hope you approve of the choice I made!” he told Ruth a few days later. “Boarding at my sister’s had grown far too expensive, so I’ve decided to keep house for myself instead. I rented a small room out in North Oakland, it’s a quiet, sleepy retired neighborhood just like I wanted, and I picked up an oil burner to cook all my meals on.”
source_text: “I wonder if you’ll like what I have done!” he said to Ruth several days later. “I’ve decided that boarding with my sister is too expensive, and I am going to board myself. I’ve rented a little room out in North Oakland, retired neighborhood and all the rest, you know, and I’ve bought an oil-burner on which to cook.”

text: “What reason could there possibly be?” he wondered uselessly. What wrong had he ever committed against Cedric Hollingsworth? The whole affair was so senseless, so petty and unprovoked. There was no logic to unpack from it. Over the next seven days, twelve nearly identical missives were passed along to Elias by the editorial teams of a dozen different East Coast literary journals. The editors were acting with remarkable decency, Elias decided. He was a complete stranger to all of them, and a handful had even included kind, apologetic notes alongside the forwarded slander. It was clear they loathed the cowardice of unsigned attacks. He realized the petty plot to sabotage his writing career had fallen flat immediately. If anything, the whole mess was likely to work in his favor, if it had any impact at all—at the very least, his name had now crossed the desks of dozens of editors who’d never heard of him before. Down the line, when they picked up one of his submitted stories to read, they might recall he was the writer someone had tried to smear with a cowardly unsigned note. And who could say that faint memory wouldn’t tip their judgment just a hair in his direction, out of distaste for the petty attack?
source_text: But why? he vainly questioned. What injury had he done Bernard Higginbotham? The thing was so unreasonable, so wanton. There was no explaining it. In the course of the week a dozen similar letters were forwarded to Martin by the editors of various Eastern magazines. The editors were behaving handsomely, Martin concluded. He was wholly unknown to them, yet some of them had even been sympathetic. It was evident that they detested anonymity. He saw that the malicious attempt to hurt him had failed. In fact, if anything came of it, it was bound to be good, for at least his name had been called to the attention of a number of editors. Sometime, perhaps, reading a submitted manuscript of his, they might remember him as the fellow about whom they had received an anonymous letter. And who was to say that such a remembrance might not sway the balance of their judgment just a trifle in his favor?

text: Lila never stopped to parse Elias’s words, nor measure the merit of his claims against the substance of what he said. Her certainty that his take was flawed landed, unacknowledged even to herself, from a quick tally of surface facts. The tenured literature faculty could not be wrong about their own field, after all—they had built thriving, respected careers out of those very judgments. Elias’s hot takes on poetry and fiction were automatically wrong, for he could not get a single editor to buy a single line of his own work. As he’d put it himself once, they’d landed on their feet, and he was still tripping over the starting line. What’s more, it felt utterly ludicrous to imagine he could have the upper hand here: only a few months prior, he’d stood in that very parlor, pink-cheeked and fumbling, as their host made their introduction, darting nervous glances at the shelves of delicate figurines his broad, swinging shoulders nearly brushed as he turned, asking when Algernon Swinburne had passed, then puffing out his chest to announce he’d read both *Excelsior* and *A Psalm of Life* in full.
source_text: She did not weigh Martin’s words nor judge his argument by them. Her conclusion that his argument was wrong was reached—unconsciously, it is true—by a comparison of externals. They, the professors, were right in their literary judgments because they were successes. Martin’s literary judgments were wrong because he could not sell his wares. To use his own phrase, they made good, and he did not make good. And besides, it did not seem reasonable that he should be right—he who had stood, so short a time before, in that same living room, blushing and awkward, acknowledging his introduction, looking fearfully about him at the bric-a-brac his swinging shoulders threatened to break, asking how long since Swinburne died, and boastfully announcing that he had read “Excelsior” and the “Psalm of Life.”

text: “His hands shake so bad,” Lila admitted, her cheek still pressed to her folded arms to hide her flush of embarrassment. “It’s so silly, almost comical, but my chest aches a little for him at the same time. When his hands are trembling too hard to hold his glass steady, and his eyes are all shiny and overbright, I end up lecturing him about how he’s living, all the wrong turns he’s taking instead of fixing what’s broken in his life. But he adores me, I’m sure of it. His hands don’t lie, and neither do those wide, earnest eyes of his. Just thinking about it, even the smallest flicker of the thought, makes me feel like a proper grown-up. Like I finally have something that’s mine by right, something that makes me just like all the other girls, the other young women I pass on the street. And I know I wasn’t like them before, too. I knew it worried you, even if you tried so hard to keep that gentle little anxiety hidden from me. I noticed it anyway, and I wanted to—‘to make good,’ as Martin Eden says.”
source_text: “His hand trembles,” Ruth was confessing, her face, for shame’s sake, still buried. “It is most amusing and ridiculous, but I feel sorry for him, too. And when his hands are too trembly, and his eyes too shiny, why, I lecture him about his life and the wrong way he is going about it to mend it. But he worships me, I know. His eyes and his hands do not lie. And it makes me feel grown-up, the thought of it, the very thought of it; and I feel that I am possessed of something that is by rights my own—that makes me like the other girls—and—and young women. And, then, too, I knew that I was not like them before, and I knew that it worried you. You thought you did not let me know that dear worry of yours, but I did, and I wanted to—‘to make good,’ as Martin Eden says.”

text: Martin had countless occasions to be grateful Brissenden was dead. He had hated the public rabble so fiercely, and now the finest, most sacred parts of his life’s work had been tossed carelessly to that very crowd. Beauty was vivisected for mass consumption every single day. Every empty-headed fool in the country scrambled to get their work published for free, floating their shrunken, petty little egos into the public eye on the surging tide of Brissenden’s posthumous greatness. One newspaper ran the line: “We have received a letter from a gentleman who wrote a poem nearly identical to it, only of higher quality, some years prior.” Another paper, speaking with deadly, misplaced solemnity as it reproved Helen Della Delmar for her parody, wrote: “But there is no question Miss Delmar wrote the piece in a moment of lighthearted jest, and not quite with the respect that one great poet ought to show to another, and perhaps to the greatest of all. Still, whether Miss Delmar is jealous of the man who created *Ephemera* or not, it is certain she is as fascinated by his work as thousands of others are, and the day may come when she will attempt to write lines in his style.”
source_text: Martin had many times to be glad that Brissenden was dead. He had hated the crowd so, and here all that was finest and most sacred of him had been thrown to the crowd. Daily the vivisection of Beauty went on. Every nincompoop in the land rushed into free print, floating their wizened little egos into the public eye on the surge of Brissenden’s greatness. Quoth one paper: “We have received a letter from a gentleman who wrote a poem just like it, only better, some time ago.” Another paper, in deadly seriousness, reproving Helen Della Delmar for her parody, said: “But unquestionably Miss Delmar wrote it in a moment of badinage and not quite with the respect that one great poet should show to another and perhaps to the greatest. However, whether Miss Delmar be jealous or not of the man who invented ‘Ephemera,’ it is certain that she, like thousands of others, is fascinated by his work, and that the day may come when she will try to write lines like his.”

text: Yet “Adventure,” along with every piece he considered his finest work, still languished unwanted on editors’ desks. He saw no value in his early output beyond the paychecks it earned, and the two horror tales he’d managed to sell struck him as neither significant work nor representative of his full capability. He acknowledged they were unapologetically fanciful and surreal, yet layered with such vivid, lifelike texture that this very quality made them compelling. This practice of dressing the grotesque and the utterly impossible in the trappings of everyday reality, he wrote off as a gimmick—an impressively skillful gimmick, to be fair, but nothing more. True great literature, he was convinced, could never grow from such a foundation. The craft of those horror stories was sharp, but he saw no merit in technical artistry stripped of genuine human substance. The gimmick had been to drape a thin mask of human authenticity over the surface of that technical craft, a trick he’d employed for the half-dozen or so horror stories he’d penned before he climbed to the creative high points of “Adventure,” “Joy,” “The Pot,” and “The Wine of Life.”
source_text: But “Adventure,” and all that he deemed his best work, still went begging among the editors. His early work counted for nothing in his eyes except for the money it brought, and his horror stories, two of which he had sold, he did not consider high work nor his best work. To him they were frankly imaginative and fantastic, though invested with all the glamour of the real, wherein lay their power. This investiture of the grotesque and impossible with reality, he looked upon as a trick—a skilful trick at best. Great literature could not reside in such a field. Their artistry was high, but he denied the worthwhileness of artistry when divorced from humanness. The trick had been to fling over the face of his artistry a mask of humanness, and this he had done in the half-dozen or so stories of the horror brand he had written before he emerged upon the high peaks of “Adventure,” “Joy,” “The Pot,” and “The Wine of Life.”

text: When Lila pressed him for an answer, Jax blurted the truth out recklessly, no pretty excuses to soften it: he’d stayed away from her this whole time because his only set of nice clothes was sitting at the pawnshop. He told her he’d been laid low by illness, but was almost fully recovered now, and in ten days or two weeks at the very longest—just as long as it took a letter to travel to New York City and back—he’d have his clothes out of hock and show up on her doorstep at last.
source_text: And when he answered, he told her recklessly that he had not been to see her because his best clothes were in pawn. He told her that he had been sick, but was once more nearly well, and that inside ten days or two weeks (as soon as a letter could travel to New York City and return) he would redeem his clothes and be with her.

text: “I’ve been turning something over in my head.” Ruth paused for a long while, letting the thought coalesce into words she could speak clearly. “And it’s awful. Just thinking about it makes my stomach twist. I told you before that falling for him was a terrible, unplanned accident, but I can’t fight how I feel. Could you have ever forced yourself to stop loving Dad? It’s exactly the same for me. There’s this pull between us, something in me, something in him I never even knew existed before today, but it’s real, and it’s why I love him. I never thought I’d care for him this way, but… well, I do,” she finished, a faint, quiet note of triumph lacing her voice.
source_text: “I have thought.” Ruth waited a long time for the thought to frame itself. “And it is terrible. It sickens me to think of it. I told you it was a dreadful accident, my loving him; but I can’t help myself. Could you help loving father? Then it is the same with me. There is something in me, in him—I never knew it was there until to-day—but it is there, and it makes me love him. I never thought to love him, but, you see, I do,” she concluded, a certain faint triumph in her voice.

text: A narrow built-in wardrobe held all Elias’s clothing and the tattered volumes he’d amassed over years, none of which could fit on the rickety dining table or the cramped floor beneath it. Alongside his obsession with reading, he’d picked up a relentless note-taking habit, so prolific that he would have had nowhere to breathe in the shoebox apartment if he hadn’t strung three lengths of cotton washing line taut from wall to wall, every inch strung with folded slips of scribbled paper. Even with that workaround, the space was so packed that moving through it required careful choreography. He couldn’t yank open the front door without first shutting the wardrobe door, and the reverse was just as true. There was no spot in the whole unit where he could walk a straight line. The path from the entry to the head of his cot was a jagged zigzag he still hadn’t mastered to navigate in the dark without knocking a hip or elbow against something hard. Once he’d sorted out the tangle of the two conflicting doors, he had to veer sharply right to dodge the tiny kitchen nook. Next, he’d cut left to miss the foot of his cot, but if that swerve was too wide, he’d crack his shin against the sharp corner of his table. With a quick, practiced jerk and shift of his weight, he’d end that left swerve and veer right down a narrow gap like a tiny canal, one edge formed by the cot frame, the other by the table legs. When the only chair in the unit sat in its usual spot pulled up to the table, that gap was entirely impassable. When he wasn’t using the chair, he propped it on top of the cot, though sometimes he dragged it into the kitchen nook to sit while he cooked, flipping pages while his kettle whistled, and even growing deft enough to get through a full paragraph or two while a cut of sirloin sizzled in the pan. The little nook that served as his kitchen was so tiny that from a seated position, he could reach every pot, spice, and utensil he owned. It was actually far more practical to cook sitting down; when he stood, he kept bumping into counters and tripping over his own feet in the cramped space.
source_text: A small closet contained his clothes and the books he had accumulated and for which there was no room on the table or under the table. Hand in hand with reading, he had developed the habit of making notes, and so copiously did he make them that there would have been no existence for him in the confined quarters had he not rigged several clothes-lines across the room on which the notes were hung. Even so, he was crowded until navigating the room was a difficult task. He could not open the door without first closing the closet door, and _vice versa_. It was impossible for him anywhere to traverse the room in a straight line. To go from the door to the head of the bed was a zigzag course that he was never quite able to accomplish in the dark without collisions. Having settled the difficulty of the conflicting doors, he had to steer sharply to the right to avoid the kitchen. Next, he sheered to the left, to escape the foot of the bed; but this sheer, if too generous, brought him against the corner of the table. With a sudden twitch and lurch, he terminated the sheer and bore off to the right along a sort of canal, one bank of which was the bed, the other the table. When the one chair in the room was at its usual place before the table, the canal was unnavigable. When the chair was not in use, it reposed on top of the bed, though sometimes he sat on the chair when cooking, reading a book while the water boiled, and even becoming skilful enough to manage a paragraph or two while steak was frying. Also, so small was the little corner that constituted the kitchen, he was able, sitting down, to reach anything he needed. In fact, it was expedient to cook sitting down; standing up, he was too often in his own way.

text: The query was entirely routine, and Lila had known exactly what his answer would be before he even parted his lips. She then sank into drowsy, warm wonder at the soothing, salve-like comfort of his steady strength: it seemed to her that soft vitality streamed from the tips of his fingers, chasing every flicker of pain before it, until the ache faded entirely, she drifted off to sleep, and he slipped away without a sound.
source_text: The question was perfunctory, and she knew what the answer would be. Then she lost herself in drowsy contemplation of the soothing balm of his strength: Life poured from the ends of his fingers, driving the pain before it, or so it seemed to her, until with the easement of pain, she fell asleep and he stole away.

text: Before long, Lila’s quiet dread was fully vindicated. Elias and Professor Hale had clustered in a highly conspicuous corner of the room, and though Elias no longer gestured broadly enough to weave patterns in the air between them, to Lila’s discerning, critical eye he still let his gaze spark and glitter far too often, spoke too quickly and fervently, tipped into unseemly intensity, and let the rush of his roused blood flush his cheeks far too deeply. He lacked all easy decorum and self-control, a stark, obvious contrast to the young English professor he was conversing with.
source_text: Later, Ruth’s anxiety found justification. Martin and Professor Caldwell had got together in a conspicuous corner, and though Martin no longer wove the air with his hands, to Ruth’s critical eye he permitted his own eyes to flash and glitter too frequently, talked too rapidly and warmly, grew too intense, and allowed his aroused blood to redden his cheeks too much. He lacked decorum and control, and was in decided contrast to the young professor of English with whom he talked.

text: Elias pushed his inquiry far deeper than casual curiosity. Working his way through the collected works of lauded, established authors, he jotted down every effect they managed to pull off, then reverse-engineered the mechanisms that made those effects land: the tight craft of plot pacing, of explanatory framing, of tonal texture, the choices of narrative lens, the deliberate juxtaposition, the sharp, memorable turns of phrase; he compiled all these into neat, annotated lists to pore over. He never copied blindly. What he chased were underlying rules. He cataloged vivid, successful stylistic tics pulled from dozens of creators, until he could distill from that hodgepodge a core framework for what made those tics work, and with that foundation laid, he could brainstorm fresh, unique flourishes of his own, testing, assessing, and refining each one with deliberate care. Using the same method, he amassed lists of searing, memorable turns of phrase pulled from real, spoken language: lines that bit like undiluted vinegar and burned like dry tinder, or that hummed soft, rich, and honey-sweet amid the flat, dusty expanse of everyday conversation. He was always hunting for the root logic that hid beneath every effect. He needed to understand exactly how a piece of writing worked; once he had that knowledge, he could replicate and reimagine it on his own terms. He refused to settle for only admiring beauty’s polished surface. He picked beauty apart in his cramped, cluttered bedroom workshop, where the sharp tang of fried plantains mingled with the constant racket of the boisterous Marquez clan next door; once he had picked apart its inner workings and memorized every part of its structure, he was one step closer to building beautiful things entirely from scratch.
source_text: He went farther in the matter. Reading the works of men who had arrived, he noted every result achieved by them, and worked out the tricks by which they had been achieved—the tricks of narrative, of exposition, of style, the points of view, the contrasts, the epigrams; and of all these he made lists for study. He did not ape. He sought principles. He drew up lists of effective and fetching mannerisms, till out of many such, culled from many writers, he was able to induce the general principle of mannerism, and, thus equipped, to cast about for new and original ones of his own, and to weigh and measure and appraise them properly. In similar manner he collected lists of strong phrases, the phrases of living language, phrases that bit like acid and scorched like flame, or that glowed and were mellow and luscious in the midst of the arid desert of common speech. He sought always for the principle that lay behind and beneath. He wanted to know how the thing was done; after that he could do it for himself. He was not content with the fair face of beauty. He dissected beauty in his crowded little bedroom laboratory, where cooking smells alternated with the outer bedlam of the Silva tribe; and, having dissected and learned the anatomy of beauty, he was nearer being able to create beauty itself.

text: Martin chatted with him for a full fifteen minutes, and Ruth couldn’t have asked for better behavior from her boyfriend. He never once let his eyes flash with irritation or his cheeks flush with frustration, and the steady, unflappable poise he carried through the whole conversation caught her completely off guard. But as far as Martin was concerned, the entire profession of bank cashiers plummeted a good few hundred points in his esteem, and for the rest of the evening he couldn’t shake the belief that “bank cashier” was just another term for someone who spouts empty, overused platitudes. The army officer, by contrast, struck him as good-natured and unpretentious, a healthy, well-adjusted young man who was perfectly content occupying the spot in life that birth and luck had tossed him into. When he learned the officer had completed two years of university, Martin was puzzled to figure out where he’d stored all that education, since he gave no obvious sign of it in casual interaction. Even so, Martin still liked him far better than the trite, cliché-ridden bank cashier.
source_text: Martin talked for fifteen minutes with him, nor could Ruth have wished better behavior on her lover’s part. Not once did his eyes flash nor his cheeks flush, while the calmness and poise with which he talked surprised her. But in Martin’s estimation the whole tribe of bank cashiers fell a few hundred per cent, and for the rest of the evening he labored under the impression that bank cashiers and talkers of platitudes were synonymous phrases. The army officer he found good-natured and simple, a healthy, wholesome young fellow, content to occupy the place in life into which birth and luck had flung him. On learning that he had completed two years in the university, Martin was puzzled to know where he had stored it away. Nevertheless Martin liked him better than the platitudinous bank cashier.

text: From the day they first met right up to that moment, the gulf between them had never felt so wide. The unvarnished confession he’d laid bare, sharp with the fire of rebellion running under every word, had made her recoil. Yet what startled her more than the reason for that reaction was the reaction itself. That jolt of distaste drove home just how close she’d grown to him, and once she let herself acknowledge that truth, it cleared a path for them to grow even closer. It also stirred up pity in her, along with soft, idealistic fantasies of helping him turn his life around. She would rescue this rough, unrefined young man who’d already come such a long way on his own. She would pull him free of the heavy curse of his troubled upbringing, and she would save him from his own worst impulses, even if he fought her every step of the way. To her, this entire impulse felt like the height of selfless, noble virtue; it never once crossed her mind that tangled beneath it all were the sharp, unacknowledged pangs of jealousy and the hungry want of romantic love.
source_text: Never, since the first meeting, had they seemed so far apart as that day. His confession, told in frankness, with the spirit of revolt behind, had repelled her. But she was more shocked by the repulsion itself than by the cause of it. It pointed out to her how near she had drawn to him, and once accepted, it paved the way for greater intimacy. Pity, too, was aroused, and innocent, idealistic thoughts of reform. She would save this raw young man who had come so far. She would save him from the curse of his early environment, and she would save him from himself in spite of himself. And all this affected her as a very noble state of consciousness; nor did she dream that behind it and underlying it were the jealousy and desire of love.

text: When *Holloway’s Monthly* ran “The Line Reader,” highlighting the piece with decorative flourishes by Leroi and two full illustrations by Voss, Arthur Hale forgot he’d once dismissed the verses as unforgivably vulgar. He declared his wife Clara had been the poem’s quiet muse, made sure the tip landed with a local reporter, and sat for a full interview with a staff writer who brought along a staff photographer and in-house sketch artist. The final spread took up an entire page of the Sunday paper’s leisure supplement, packed with candid snapshots and romanticized drawings of Clara, countless cozy anecdotes about Jesse Thorne and his blue-collar family, and the full text of “The Line Reader” in bold, large font, reprinted with special approval from *Holloway’s Monthly*. It set the whole block buzzing; proper housewives who already knew the celebrated author’s sister preened over the connection, while those who didn’t scrambled to strike up an acquaintance. Arthur chuckled to himself in his tiny engine repair shop and put in an order for a brand-new lathe that afternoon. “Beats any paid ad I could ever buy,” he told Clara that evening, “and I didn’t spend a cent on it.”
source_text: When _Mackintosh’s Magazine_ published “The Palmist,” featuring it with decorations by Berthier and with two pictures by Wenn, Hermann von Schmidt forgot that he had called the verses obscene. He announced that his wife had inspired the poem, saw to it that the news reached the ears of a reporter, and submitted to an interview by a staff writer who was accompanied by a staff photographer and a staff artist. The result was a full page in a Sunday supplement, filled with photographs and idealized drawings of Marian, with many intimate details of Martin Eden and his family, and with the full text of “The Palmist” in large type, and republished by special permission of _Mackintosh’s Magazine_. It caused quite a stir in the neighborhood, and good housewives were proud to have the acquaintances of the great writer’s sister, while those who had not made haste to cultivate it. Hermann von Schmidt chuckled in his little repair shop and decided to order a new lathe. “Better than advertising,” he told Marian, “and it costs nothing.”

text: For five straight days he plugged away at *Overdue*, never stepping foot outside, not seeing a single person, and getting by on skimpy, plain meals. On the sixth morning, the postman dropped off a slim letter from the editor of *The Parthenon*. One quick glance was all he needed to see that “Ephemera” had been accepted. “We sent the poem to Mr. Cartwright Bruce for his assessment,” the editor’s note went on, “and his feedback was so glowing that there’s no way we could pass on publishing it. As a token of how thrilled we are to run the piece, I wanted to let you know we’ve slotted it for the August issue—our July issue is already fully assembled and ready to go to press. Please pass along both our excitement and our thanks to Mr. Brissenden. We’d also appreciate it if you could send his photograph and short biographical information by return mail. If the honorarium we’re offering doesn’t feel fair to you, please telegraph us right away and let us know what rate you think is appropriate.”
source_text: For five days he toiled on at “Overdue,” going nowhere, seeing nobody, and eating meagrely. On the morning of the sixth day the postman brought him a thin letter from the editor of _The Parthenon_. A glance told him that “Ephemera” was accepted. “We have submitted the poem to Mr. Cartwright Bruce,” the editor went on to say, “and he has reported so favorably upon it that we cannot let it go. As an earnest of our pleasure in publishing the poem, let me tell you that we have set it for the August number, our July number being already made up. Kindly extend our pleasure and our thanks to Mr. Brissenden. Please send by return mail his photograph and biographical data. If our honorarium is unsatisfactory, kindly telegraph us at once and state what you consider a fair price.”

text:
One evening he headed to the theater, clinging to the faint, unplanned hope he might spot her there—and sure enough, from his seat in the second balcony, he caught sight of her. He watched her make her way down the aisle, alongside Arthur and an unfamiliar young guy with a thick, unruly mop of hair and wire-rimmed glasses, and a sharp jolt of unease and jealousy hit him immediately at the sight of the stranger. He tracked her to her seat in the orchestra section, and he barely noticed anything else for the rest of the night: just the curve of her slim, pale shoulders and the cloud of soft gold hair, blurred a little by the distance between them.

Other people were watching him, though. Every so often when he glanced around at the people nearby, he caught two young women a dozen seats down in the row ahead staring back, grinning at him with unapologetically forward looks. He’d always been laid-back, the kind of person who hated snubbing anyone. A few years earlier, he would have grinned right back, even leaned into it a little to keep the flirtation going. But things were different now. He gave them a small smile back, then turned his gaze away and made a point not to look over again. But more than once, when he’d forgotten the two women were even there, his line of sight drifted back to their waiting grins.

He couldn’t rewrite his entire personality overnight, and he’d never go against the inherent kindness that was part of who he was, so in those moments he smiled back at them with gentle, harmless friendliness. This wasn’t out of the ordinary for him; he knew they were making romantic overtures his way. But none of that landed the way it used to. Far below him in the orchestra seats was the only woman that mattered to him in the whole world, so far removed, so impossibly far above the two women from his own social circle, that all he could feel for them was soft pity. He found himself quietly wishing they could have even a fraction of her warmth and grace.

He would never have dreamed of hurting them just for being interested in him, either. He didn’t take their attention as a compliment; if anything, he felt a faint twinge of shame that his lower social standing made this kind of advance acceptable to them. He knew that if he moved in Ruth’s circles, these two women would never have dared to flirt with him at all, and every time they glanced his way, he could almost feel the weight of his own background yanking at him, trying to keep him tied to the life he’d known before.
source_text: One night he went to the theatre, on the blind chance that he might see her there, and from the second balcony he did see her. He saw her come down the aisle, with Arthur and a strange young man with a football mop of hair and eyeglasses, the sight of whom spurred him to instant apprehension and jealousy. He saw her take her seat in the orchestra circle, and little else than her did he see that night—a pair of slender white shoulders and a mass of pale gold hair, dim with distance. But there were others who saw, and now and again, glancing at those about him, he noted two young girls who looked back from the row in front, a dozen seats along, and who smiled at him with bold eyes. He had always been easy-going. It was not in his nature to give rebuff. In the old days he would have smiled back, and gone further and encouraged smiling. But now it was different. He did smile back, then looked away, and looked no more deliberately. But several times, forgetting the existence of the two girls, his eyes caught their smiles. He could not re-thumb himself in a day, nor could he violate the intrinsic kindliness of his nature; so, at such moments, he smiled at the girls in warm human friendliness. It was nothing new to him. He knew they were reaching out their woman’s hands to him. But it was different now. Far down there in the orchestra circle was the one woman in all the world, so different, so terrifically different, from these two girls of his class, that he could feel for them only pity and sorrow. He had it in his heart to wish that they could possess, in some small measure, her goodness and glory. And not for the world could he hurt them because of their outreaching. He was not flattered by it; he even felt a slight shame at his lowliness that permitted it. He knew, did he belong in Ruth’s class, that there would be no overtures from these girls; and with each glance of theirs he felt the fingers of his own class clutching at him to hold him down.

text: Every so often he flipped through magazines and local dailies to track how badly “Transience” was being torn apart. It had blown up in the public consciousness. But what a bizarre, bruising kind of hit it was! Everyone had read it, and every social circle and street corner hummed with debates over whether it qualified as real poetry at all. Regional papers had latched onto the conversation, and each day brought columns of stuffy academic criticism, snarky facetious editorials, and earnest, lengthy letters from subscribed readers. Elara Voss—proclaimed with blaring trumpets and rolling drum fanfare to be the greatest woman poet in the United States—refused to grant Hale a seat beside her on Pegasus, and wrote reams of open letters to the public, laying out exhaustive proof that he was no true poet.
source_text: Sometimes he glanced over the magazines and newspapers to see how “Ephemera” was being maltreated. It had made a hit. But what a hit! Everybody had read it, and everybody was discussing whether or not it was really poetry. The local papers had taken it up, and daily there appeared columns of learned criticisms, facetious editorials, and serious letters from subscribers. Helen Della Delmar (proclaimed with a flourish of trumpets and rolling of tomtoms to be the greatest woman poet in the United States) denied Brissenden a seat beside her on Pegasus and wrote voluminous letters to the public, proving that he was no poet.

text: “There we have it,” Brissenden laughed. “Clever little title, isn’t it? ‘Ephemera’—it’s the only word that fits perfectly. And you’re the one who inspired it, all thanks to that idea of yours about humankind: that upright, animated mass of otherwise inert matter, the most recent of all short-lived ephemera, a creature bound to narrow temperature ranges, strutting about his tiny sliver of space on the thermometer scale. The thought burrowed right into my head and wouldn’t let go, so I had to write the whole piece just to shake it loose. Tell me what you make of it.”
source_text: “Yes, that’s it,” Brissenden laughed. “Pretty good title, eh? ‘Ephemera’—it is the one word. And you’re responsible for it, what of your _man_, who is always the erected, the vitalized inorganic, the latest of the ephemera, the creature of temperature strutting his little space on the thermometer. It got into my head and I had to write it to get rid of it. Tell me what you think of it.”

text: So he hauled himself to the Herald back alley, aching in every bone and raw at the core, but slowly learning that heavy, unyielding patience, to face his lifelong rival Crust-Nose, who was just as beaten down as he was, and half-ready to throw in the towel if not for the huddle of newsboys loitering to watch, turning pride into a sharp, unavoidable obligation. One afternoon, after twenty minutes of desperate, flailing efforts to overpower each other under long-held rules that forbade kicking, striking below the belt, or landing a blow on someone who’d fallen, Crust-Nose, chest heaving so hard his shoulders shook, swaying unsteadily on scuffed canvas shoes, yelled that he wanted to call the fight even. Jimmie, forehead resting on his crossed arms where he leaned against a chipped brick wall, felt a sharp, thrumming jolt of that old fire as he recalled that distant late afternoon: he was swaying too, breath coming in ragged, burning gasps, copper-tasting blood pouring from a split upper lip into his mouth and down his windpipe, making him choke so hard his ribs ached. He stumbled a wobbly step toward Crust-Nose, spitting a mouthful of thick, warm blood onto the dusty alley gravel so he could speak, shouting that he would never surrender, even if Crust-Nose wanted to tap out right then. And Crust-Nose refused to back down either, and the fight dragged on.
source_text: And so he dragged himself to the _Enquirer_ alley, sick in body and soul, but learning the long patience, to confront his eternal enemy, Cheese-Face, who was just as sick as he, and just a bit willing to quit if it were not for the gang of newsboys that looked on and made pride painful and necessary. One afternoon, after twenty minutes of desperate efforts to annihilate each other according to set rules that did not permit kicking, striking below the belt, nor hitting when one was down, Cheese-Face, panting for breath and reeling, offered to call it quits. And Martin, head on arms, thrilled at the picture he caught of himself, at that moment in the afternoon of long ago, when he reeled and panted and choked with the blood that ran into his mouth and down his throat from his cut lips; when he tottered toward Cheese-Face, spitting out a mouthful of blood so that he could speak, crying out that he would never quit, though Cheese-Face could give in if he wanted to. And Cheese-Face did not give in, and the fight went on.

text: The washing machine rumbled to life on Friday morning. Twice a week, they had to work through the hotel’s linen stock: bed sheets, pillowcases, bedspreads, tablecloths, and cloth napkins. Once that task was complete, they buckled down to “fancy starch” work. It was slow, meticulous, and delicate labor, and Martin did not pick up the skill easily. On top of that, he could not afford to take risks; any misstep would be catastrophic.
source_text: On Friday morning the washer ran. Twice a week they had to put through hotel linen,—the sheets, pillow-slips, spreads, table-cloths, and napkins. This finished, they buckled down to “fancy starch.” It was slow work, fastidious and delicate, and Martin did not learn it so readily. Besides, he could not take chances. Mistakes were disastrous.

text: He sent a letter to Hawthorne, Pruitt & Sons, offering to sign over all rights to his manuscript *The Blight of the Noon Sky* for a flat hundred dollars, but the firm declined to take the gamble on the untested work. For the moment, though, he had no pressing need for extra cash: a handful of his newer short stories had been accepted for publication and already paid out. He even opened his first proper bank account, and with no outstanding debts to his name, he carried a balance of several hundred dollars. His piece *Days Past Due*, which had been turned down by more than a dozen periodicals, finally found a home at Carter & Hale Press. Elias recalled the five-dollar bill Maren had pressed into his palm when he had nothing to his name, and the quiet vow he’d made to repay her a hundredfold, so he sent a note to his new publisher requesting a five-hundred dollar advance against future royalties. To his shock, a check for the full amount, tucked inside a printed contract, arrived in the very next post. He cashed the check entirely in five-dollar gold pieces, then telephoned Maren that he wanted to see her right away.
source_text: He wrote to Singletree, Darnley & Co., offering to sell out his rights in “The Shame of the Sun” for a hundred dollars, but they did not care to take the risk. In the meantime he was not in need of money, for several of his later stories had been accepted and paid for. He actually opened a bank account, where, without a debt in the world, he had several hundred dollars to his credit. “Overdue,” after having been declined by a number of magazines, came to rest at the Meredith-Lowell Company. Martin remembered the five dollars Gertrude had given him, and his resolve to return it to her a hundred times over; so he wrote for an advance on royalties of five hundred dollars. To his surprise a check for that amount, accompanied by a contract, came by return mail. He cashed the check into five-dollar gold pieces and telephoned Gertrude that he wanted to see her.

text: Beauty carries great meaning, but I never understood what that meaning was until now. I’d always written beauty off as something hollow, nothing more than a pretty thing with no rhyme or reason behind it. I truly knew nothing of what beauty actually is. But now I know—well, more accurately, I am only just starting to learn. This grass feels far more lovely to me now that I grasp what makes it grass, all the unseen chemical dance of sunlight, rain, and soil that coaxes it into being. Why, there is real romance in the life story of any blade of grass, yes, and adventure too. Just the thought of it sends a thrill through me. When I consider the interplay of force and matter, and all the staggering, immense struggle wrapped up in that process, I feel as if I could write an entire epic about grass alone.
source_text: “Beauty has significance, but I never knew its significance before. I just accepted beauty as something meaningless, as something that was just beautiful without rhyme or reason. I did not know anything about beauty. But now I know, or, rather, am just beginning to know. This grass is more beautiful to me now that I know why it is grass, and all the hidden chemistry of sun and rain and earth that makes it become grass. Why, there is romance in the life-history of any grass, yes, and adventure, too. The very thought of it stirs me. When I think of the play of force and matter, and all the tremendous struggle of it, I feel as if I could write an epic on the grass.

text: Kierkegaard had it dead to rights. I won’t waste breath explaining who Kierkegaard was, but every word he said on this holds true. The world belongs to the unyielding—to the strong who carry nobility in their bones, who never grovel in the muck of haggling and transactional smallness. It belongs to the true aristocrat of the spirit, to the wild golden predator, to those who never bend, to the men who say “yes” to every hard truth life hands them. And they’ll chew you up and spit you out, you so-called socialists—you lot who quake at the very idea of the socialism you claim to champion, who fancy yourselves rugged individualists. Your slave ethic of meekness and bowing to the lowest common denominator will never keep you safe.—Hell, I know this is all over your heads, I won’t pester you with it any longer. But mark this one thing. There are barely half a dozen genuine individualists in all of Astoria, and Rafe Thorne is one of them.
source_text: “Nietzsche was right. I won’t take the time to tell you who Nietzsche was, but he was right. The world belongs to the strong—to the strong who are noble as well and who do not wallow in the swine-trough of trade and exchange. The world belongs to the true nobleman, to the great blond beasts, to the noncompromisers, to the ‘yes-sayers.’ And they will eat you up, you socialists—who are afraid of socialism and who think yourselves individualists. Your slave-morality of the meek and lowly will never save you.—Oh, it’s all Greek, I know, and I won’t bother you any more with it. But remember one thing. There aren’t half a dozen individualists in Oakland, but Martin Eden is one of them.”

text: “Look, you just pass the word along to them tomorrow, that’s all for now,” he said. “And before I slip my mind and forget, I need to let you know you ought to send for Marian tomorrow to look after the kids. Now that Tom’s quit, I’ll be tied up out driving the wagon all day, so you might as well make up your mind to be downstairs manning the counter waiting on customers.”
source_text: “Well, you tell ’m to-morrow, that’s all,” he said. “An’ I just want to tell you, before I forget it, that you’d better send for Marian to-morrow to take care of the children. With Tom quit, I’ll have to be out on the wagon, an’ you can make up your mind to it to be down below waitin’ on the counter.”

text: “Hey, let me read this to you,” he exclaimed, prying apart the stacked carbon duplicates and tapping the sheaf of manuscript pages against his palm to line them up. “It’s my newest work, and nothing like anything I’ve ever written. It’s such a sharp departure from my usual fare that I’m almost jittery about it, but I’ve got a secret hunch it’s actually decent. You be the judge. It’s a story set in Hawaii, I titled it *Wiki-wiki*.”
source_text: “Here, let me read you this,” he cried, separating the carbon copies and running the pages of manuscript into shape. “It’s my latest, and different from anything I’ve done. It is so altogether different that I am almost afraid of it, and yet I’ve a sneaking idea it is good. You be judge. It’s an Hawaiian story. I’ve called it ‘Wiki-wiki.’”

text: Deep in his most private thoughts, Martin understood the full situation perfectly, as he leaned back and stared unblinkingly at Von Schmidt’s head, fantasizing about pummeling it nearly clean off his shoulders, landing every blow crisp and dead on target—what a chuckleheaded Dutch oaf the man was. There was one quality of his, though, that Martin genuinely liked. For all his own poverty, and all his fierce determination to climb the social and economic ladder, Von Schmidt had still hired a servant to lift all the heaviest, most backbreaking work from Marian’s hands. Martin had already spoken at length with the superintendent of the Asa agency chain, and after dinner ended he drew the man aside alongside Hermann, offering full financial backing to launch the best-outfitted, highest-end bicycle shop in all of Oakland. He went even further, pulling Hermann aside for a private conversation to tell him to keep his eyes peeled for an available automobile agency and garage space, explaining there was no reason he could not run both establishments with consistent success.
source_text: And in his heart of hearts Martin understood the situation only too well, as he leaned back and gloated at Von Schmidt’s head, in fancy punching it well-nigh off of him, sending blow after blow home just right—the chuckle-headed Dutchman! One thing he did like about him, however. Poor as he was, and determined to rise as he was, he nevertheless hired one servant to take the heavy work off of Marian’s hands. Martin talked with the superintendent of the Asa agencies, and after dinner he drew him aside with Hermann, whom he backed financially for the best bicycle store with fittings in Oakland. He went further, and in a private talk with Hermann told him to keep his eyes open for an automobile agency and garage, for there was no reason that he should not be able to run both establishments successfully.

text: “Blame the cheap tobacco for that harsh bite—it’s the only kind I can scrounge up enough cash for right now. But just you wait until that forty-dollar check clears. I’m springing for a brand so smooth it wouldn’t even rub the angels the wrong way. Not a bad run, huh? Two acceptances in three days? That forty-five dollars total will wipe out nearly every debt I’ve got hanging over my head.”
source_text: “That’s the fault of the tobacco. I can afford only the cheapest. But wait until I get that forty-dollar check. I’ll use a brand that is not offensive even to the angels. But that wasn’t so bad, was it, two acceptances in three days? That forty-five dollars will pay about all my debts.”

text: “Oh, of course. Your practice is entirely corporate. You don’t take on working-class clients or criminal cases, so you never rely on wife-beaters or small-time pickpockets for your income. Your entire livelihood comes straight from the ruling masters of society, and everyone knows the hand that feeds a man is the hand that commands him. That makes you a hired henchman, plain and simple. Your only priority is advancing the interests of the massive capital conglomerates you work for.”
source_text: “Why, yes. You do corporation work. You have no working-class nor criminal practice. You don’t depend upon wife-beaters and pickpockets for your income. You get your livelihood from the masters of society, and whoever feeds a man is that man’s master. Yes, you are a henchman. You are interested in advancing the interests of the aggregations of capital you serve.”

text: Then, without a single premonition, the uncrossable chasm between them narrowed for one fleeting heartbeat, and though the distance never vanished entirely, it stayed far smaller ever after. They’d spent the hour eating plump, luscious deep-purple cherries, their juice so rich and dark it looked like aged port when it beaded on their fingertips. Later, as she read aloud to him from *Goblin Market*, he chanced to catch the faint purplish cherry stain smudged along the curve of her lower lip. In that instant, the unearthly divinity he’d draped around her shattered. She was flesh and blood, after all, mere soft, fallible flesh, subject to the same small, messy laws of being human as his own body was, or anybody else’s. Her lips were made of the same warm skin as his, and cherry juice dyed them the exact same way it dyed his. If that was true of her lips, then it had to be true of every part of her. She was a woman, entirely a woman, just like any other. The realization crashed over him abruptly. It was a revelation that left him stunned, as though he had watched the sun plummet straight out of the sky, or seen a sacred, venerated icon smudged and marked by ordinary grime.
source_text: And then, one day, without warning, the gulf between them was bridged for a moment, and thereafter, though the gulf remained, it was ever narrower. They had been eating cherries—great, luscious, black cherries with a juice of the color of dark wine. And later, as she read aloud to him from “The Princess,” he chanced to notice the stain of the cherries on her lips. For the moment her divinity was shattered. She was clay, after all, mere clay, subject to the common law of clay as his clay was subject, or anybody’s clay. Her lips were flesh like his, and cherries dyed them as cherries dyed his. And if so with her lips, then was it so with all of her. She was woman, all woman, just like any woman. It came upon him abruptly. It was a revelation that stunned him. It was as if he had seen the sun fall out of the sky, or had seen worshipped purity polluted.

text: He shook off his lingering grogginess and started sorting through his stack of mail. There were a dozen autograph requests—he could spot those instantly, no need to tear the envelopes open; there was the usual batch of professional begging letters; and there were messages from oddballs, ranging from the man who’d built a working perpetual motion prototype, the one who’d drafted a full proof that the Earth’s surface was actually the inner wall of a hollow sphere, to the man asking for financial support to buy the entire Lower California Peninsula to set up a communist colony there. There were also notes from women hoping to get to know him, and he smiled faintly at one of these: tucked inside the envelope was her receipt for church pew rent, sent as evidence of her good faith and as proof of her respectable standing.
source_text: He roused himself and began glancing through his mail. There were a dozen requests for autographs—he knew them at sight; there were professional begging letters; and there were letters from cranks, ranging from the man with a working model of perpetual motion, and the man who demonstrated that the surface of the earth was the inside of a hollow sphere, to the man seeking financial aid to purchase the Peninsula of Lower California for the purpose of communist colonization. There were letters from women seeking to know him, and over one such he smiled, for enclosed was her receipt for pew-rent, sent as evidence of her good faith and as proof of her respectability.

text: “Yeah, I follow that,” Martin replied. “Every art form has its own set of conventions.” The word struck Ruth as unexpected. It made him sound like he’d completed formal university studies himself, rather than cobbling together a patchy education by randomly browsing whatever books he could find in the library. “But even those conventions have to feel genuine, right? Flat cardboard cutouts of trees propped up on either side of the stage? We accept those as a whole forest, no problem. That’s a convention that holds up, that feels real enough. But we’d never accept a set built to look like the ocean as a forest, though. We just can’t. It goes against every one of our senses. And by that same logic, you wouldn’t—no, you shouldn’t—accept the raving, thrashing, agonized twisted poses those two unhinged performers put on tonight as a convincing depiction of love.”
source_text: “Yes, I understood that,” Martin answered. “All the arts have their conventions.” (Ruth was surprised at his use of the word. It was as if he had studied at the university himself, instead of being ill-equipped from browsing at haphazard through the books in the library.) “But even the conventions must be real. Trees, painted on flat cardboard and stuck up on each side of the stage, we accept as a forest. It is a real enough convention. But, on the other hand, we would not accept a sea scene as a forest. We can’t do it. It violates our senses. Nor would you, or, rather, should you, accept the ravings and writhings and agonized contortions of those two lunatics to-night as a convincing portrayal of love.”

text: Norton was sensitive and easily stirred, though he never lost his cool, while Kreis and Hamilton were a pair of cold, ruthless brutes, always hunting for vulnerable spots to nudge and prod for sport. As the evening stretched late, smarting under their repeated accusations that he was a mere metaphysician, Norton clamped his hands to the arms of his chair to stop himself from springing to his feet, his gray eyes snapping bright, his typically soft, girlish face turned harsh and unyielding as he launched a full, fierce counterattack on their core position.
source_text: Norton was sensitive and excitable, though he never lost his head, while Kreis and Hamilton were like a pair of cold-blooded savages, seeking out tender places to prod and poke. As the evening grew late, Norton, smarting under the repeated charges of being a metaphysician, clutching his chair to keep from jumping to his feet, his gray eyes snapping and his girlish face grown harsh and sure, made a grand attack upon their position.

text: Ruth never picked up on the hunger written across Martin’s face, even as it grew gaunt, the faint hollows under his cheekbones deepening noticeably. If anything, she looked on this shift in his features with satisfaction. It seemed to refine him, stripping away the coarse, unrefined fullness of his frame and that overly animal vitality that tempted her even as she despised it. Now and then when they were together, she spotted an uncommon sharp brightness in his gaze, and she admired it—it made him look far more like the poet and scholar he ached to become, the exact version of him she’d been hoping for. But Maria Silva saw an entirely different truth in those hollowed cheeks and burning eyes. She tracked their small shifts each passing day, using them to trace the ebb and flow of his fortunes. She’d watch him head out wearing his overcoat and return without it, even on grey, bone-chilling days, and immediately afterward she’d notice his cheeks fill out just a touch, that ravenous, burning edge fade from his gaze. She’d seen his bicycle and his watch vanish the exact same way, and every time one of those items was gone, his strength and energy would bloom again right after.
source_text: Ruth never read hunger in Martin’s face, which had grown lean and had enlarged the slight hollows in the cheeks. In fact, she marked the change in his face with satisfaction. It seemed to refine him, to remove from him much of the dross of flesh and the too animal-like vigor that lured her while she detested it. Sometimes, when with her, she noted an unusual brightness in his eyes, and she admired it, for it made him appear more the poet and the scholar—the things he would have liked to be and which she would have liked him to be. But Maria Silva read a different tale in the hollow cheeks and the burning eyes, and she noted the changes in them from day to day, by them following the ebb and flow of his fortunes. She saw him leave the house with his overcoat and return without it, though the day was chill and raw, and promptly she saw his cheeks fill out slightly and the fire of hunger leave his eyes. In the same way she had seen his wheel and watch go, and after each event she had seen his vigor bloom again.

text: “You really are an excellent speaker,” she insisted. “Just look at how much you’ve improved in the short time we’ve known each other. Mr. Butler’s a well-recognized public speaker, after all—the state committee always calls on him to deliver stump speeches every campaign cycle. But the other night at dinner, you spoke every bit as well as he did. The only difference is that he’s far more calm and measured when he talks. You get a little too fired up right now, but that’ll fade with enough practice. Honestly, you could be a fantastic public speaker. You could go really far with it, if that’s something you want to pursue. You’re so sharp at this kind of work. I’m positive you can lead other people easily, and there’s no reason you can’t nail anything you set your mind to, just like you mastered grammar. You’d make a great lawyer, you’d stand out so much in politics. There’s absolutely nothing stopping you from building a career just as successful as Mr. Butler’s. And you wouldn’t even end up with his chronic indigestion,” she added with a smile.
source_text: “But you do talk well,” she insisted. “Just think how you have improved in the short time I have known you. Mr. Butler is a noted public speaker. He is always asked by the State Committee to go out on stump during campaign. Yet you talked just as well as he the other night at dinner. Only he was more controlled. You get too excited; but you will get over that with practice. Why, you would make a good public speaker. You can go far—if you want to. You are masterly. You can lead men, I am sure, and there is no reason why you should not succeed at anything you set your hand to, just as you have succeeded with grammar. You would make a good lawyer. You should shine in politics. There is nothing to prevent you from making as great a success as Mr. Butler has made. And minus the dyspepsia,” she added with a smile.

text: She turned up promptly at the arranged time, and the only clue her mind had been able to work out for the mysterious errand was that they were buying shoes. So she felt a sharp, clear stab of disappointment when Martin walked straight past the shoe store and ducked into a real estate office instead. Everything that happened next lived in her memory forever after as a hazy, wonderful dream. Well-dressed gentlemen smiled kindly at her as they conversed with Martin and one another; a typewriter clicked away in the background; signatures were added to a grand, formal document; her own landlord was present too, and put his name to the paper. When the whole process was finished and they were back out on the sidewalk, her landlord addressed her, saying, “Well, Maria, you won’t have to pay me that seven dollars and a half rent this month.”
source_text: At the appointed time she was there; but _shoes_ was the only clew to the mystery her mind had been capable of evolving, and she suffered a distinct shock of disappointment when Martin walked her right by a shoe-store and dived into a real estate office. What happened thereupon resided forever after in her memory as a dream. Fine gentlemen smiled at her benevolently as they talked with Martin and one another; a type-writer clicked; signatures were affixed to an imposing document; her own landlord was there, too, and affixed his signature; and when all was over and she was outside on the sidewalk, her landlord spoke to her, saying, “Well, Maria, you won’t have to pay me no seven dollars and a half this month.”

text: Five dollars for five thousand words, ten words to a cent—this specific calculation had wedged itself firmly in his skull, and he could no more shake it loose than he could dismiss the glowing “$3.85” burned into the backs of his eyelids. The figure shifted abruptly, and he watched it through heavy lids, curious, until “$2.00” blazed in its place. Ah, he thought, that was what he owed the baker. Next, “$2.50” flickered into view. It stumped him, and he turned the number over and over as if life itself hung on working out who it was for. He definitely owed someone two dollars and fifty cents, that much was certain, but who? The cold, imperious world seemed to demand he find the answer at once, so he wandered the endless winding halls of his memory, prying open every cluttered storage closet and dusty cranny stuffed with half-forgotten facts and random fragments of the past, turning up empty no matter how hard he looked. It felt like centuries passed before the answer hit him out of nowhere, no effort at all: that was Maria. He exhaled in sharp relief, ready to let his frayed mind rest now that he’d cracked the puzzle. But no sooner had he relaxed than the “$2.50” dimmed, and “$8.00” burned bright in its spot. Who did he owe eight dollars to? He’d have to drag himself through that same tedious, exhausting sweep of his memories all over again to find out.
source_text: Five dollars for five thousand words, ten words for a cent—that particular thought took up its residence in his brain, and he could no more escape it than he could the “$3.85” under his eyelids. A change seemed to come over the latter, and he watched curiously, till “$2.00” burned in its stead. Ah, he thought, that was the baker. The next sum that appeared was “$2.50.” It puzzled him, and he pondered it as if life and death hung on the solution. He owed somebody two dollars and a half, that was certain, but who was it? To find it was the task set him by an imperious and malignant universe, and he wandered through the endless corridors of his mind, opening all manner of lumber rooms and chambers stored with odds and ends of memories and knowledge as he vainly sought the answer. After several centuries it came to him, easily, without effort, that it was Maria. With a great relief he turned his soul to the screen of torment under his lids.
