He had solved the problem; now he could rest. But no, the “$2.50” faded away, and in its place burned “$8.00.” Who was that? He must go the dreary round of his mind again and find out.

text: Elias swiveled toward the thick stack of press cuttings delivered that morning from his media monitoring service, skimming pieces about his sudden fame that had swelled from a quiet vogue to a full-blown cultural frenzy. Every last piece of his creative work had been released to the public in one sweeping, high-profile drop, which seemed to explain the mania. He had swept audiences completely off their feet, much as had happened to W.B. Yeats once, when a near-fatal fever left him bedridden, and the collective hive mind of the general public suddenly latched onto his work en masse. Elias recalled how that same global mob, after devouring the poet’s work, heaping praise on him, and grasping almost nothing of what he’d written, had turned on him without warning a few months later, tearing his reputation to shreds. Elias huffed a dry, amused grin at the memory. Who was he to think he would escape the same treatment a few months down the line? Well, he would fool the mob. He would be long gone, off in the South Pacific islands, building his own thatched grass hut, bartering for pearls and copra, navigating sharp reefs in wobbly outrigger canoes, reeling in sharks and plump bonito, chasing feral goats across the craggy cliff edges of the valley that sat just beside the valley of Hatiheu.
source_text: He turned to the huge bundle of clippings which had come in from his press bureau, and read about himself and his vogue, which had become a furore. All his creative output had been flung to the public in one magnificent sweep. That seemed to account for it. He had taken the public off its feet, the way Kipling had, that time when he lay near to death and all the mob, animated by a mob-mind thought, began suddenly to read him. Martin remembered how that same world-mob, having read him and acclaimed him and not understood him in the least, had, abruptly, a few months later, flung itself upon him and torn him to pieces. Martin grinned at the thought. Who was he that he should not be similarly treated in a few more months? Well, he would fool the mob. He would be away, in the South Seas, building his grass house, trading for pearls and copra, jumping reefs in frail outriggers, catching sharks and bonitas, hunting wild goats among the cliffs of the valley that lay next to the valley of Taiohae.

text: It was like Martin snapped out of a daze all at once. He popped open his gear bag, lubed his bike wheel, rubbed graphite onto the chain, and tweaked the bearings until they sat just right. Joe was already halfway to the bar when Martin zoomed past him, hunched low over the handlebars, legs pumping the 96-gear with steady, powerful rhythm, his jaw set for the full seventy miles of rough pavement, steep grades, and swirling dust ahead. He crashed for the night in Oakland, then turned around and covered the same seventy-mile trip back on Sunday. Come Monday morning, he showed up to start the new workweek exhausted, but he had stayed completely sober the whole time.
source_text: Martin seemed suddenly to wake up. He opened the kit bag and oiled his wheel, putting graphite on the chain and adjusting the bearings. Joe was halfway down to the saloon when Martin passed by, bending low over the handle-bars, his legs driving the ninety-six gear with rhythmic strength, his face set for seventy miles of road and grade and dust. He slept in Oakland that night, and on Sunday covered the seventy miles back. And on Monday morning, weary, he began the new week’s work, but he had kept sober.

text: Day after endless day, the same brutal afternoon fight played out on repeat. Every time he lifted his guard to start the bout, his arms throbbed with sharp, unforgiving pain, and the first handful of blows—whether he landed them or took them—felt like they were tearing his spirit apart. Once the numbness set in, though, he kept swinging blindly, only making out Cheese-Face’s broad features and blazing, feral eyes through a dreamlike, shifting haze. He fixated entirely on that face; everything else around him dissolved into a spinning, empty void. Nothing else existed in the whole world but that face, and he would never know a moment of blessed, longed-for rest until he’d bashed that face to a pulp with his own bleeding knuckles, or until Cheese-Face’s own split, bloodied knuckles had pounded him into the same wrecked state. Either way, finally, he would get to rest. But walking away? For Martin, for him to call it quits? That was completely out of the question.
source_text: The next day and the next, days without end, witnessed the afternoon fight. When he put up his arms, each day, to begin, they pained exquisitely, and the first few blows, struck and received, racked his soul; after that things grew numb, and he fought on blindly, seeing as in a dream, dancing and wavering, the large features and burning, animal-like eyes of Cheese-Face. He concentrated upon that face; all else about him was a whirling void. There was nothing else in the world but that face, and he would never know rest, blessed rest, until he had beaten that face into a pulp with his bleeding knuckles, or until the bleeding knuckles that somehow belonged to that face had beaten him into a pulp. And then, one way or the other, he would have rest. But to quit,—for him, Martin, to quit,—that was impossible!

text: The force of his presence rolled over her, unrelenting wave after unrelenting wave, and the fiercer his will clashed with hers, the more powerfully she felt herself pulled toward him. The steady strength he had always extended to her was blazing to full bloom now, vivid in the heat of his impassioned voice, the sharp fire in his flashing eyes, and the thrumming surge of raw life and sharp intellect rolling off his frame. Right then, for that single fleeting instant, a crack split open the unshakable certainty she’d always clung to, and through that gap she caught a glimpse of the real Martin Eden: dazzling, unbeatable, larger than life. And just as animal trainers sometimes feel a flash of unbidden doubt in their own abilities, for that split second, she found herself questioning if she really had the power to tame this man’s wild, untamed spirit.
source_text: His power beat against her, wave upon wave; and in the moment his will opposed hers most she felt herself most strongly drawn toward him. The strength that had always poured out from him to her was now flowering in his impassioned voice, his flashing eyes, and the vigor of life and intellect surging in him. And in that moment, and for the moment, she was aware of a rift that showed in her certitude—a rift through which she caught sight of the real Martin Eden, splendid and invincible; and as animal-trainers have their moments of doubt, so she, for the instant, seemed to doubt her power to tame this wild spirit of a man.

text: As he grew into his own as a thinker, Kael found himself staring down the doctrine of economic morality, otherwise called the ethics of the ruling class; it soon curdled in his view into a grisly, gnawing fiend. He was by conviction an intellectually rigorous moralist, and far more repulsive to him than trite, puffed-up posturing was the unspoken ethical code of those in his orbit: a bizarre, lopsided hodgepodge of transactional financial logic, unmoored metaphysical speculation, saccharine performative sentiment, and unthinking copycat conformity.
source_text: Thus, in his development, Martin found himself face to face with economic morality, or the morality of class; and soon it became to him a grisly monster. Personally, he was an intellectual moralist, and more offending to him than platitudinous pomposity was the morality of those about him, which was a curious hotchpotch of the economic, the metaphysical, the sentimental, and the imitative.

text: “Let me stop you right there, I’ll say this plain,” he cut in, sharp. “Ninety-nine percent of all editors have one core credential, and it’s abject failure. They flopped as writers first, every last one of that lot. Don’t buy the lie that they’d rather grind away at a desk, chained to circulation numbers and their business manager’s whims, than feel the thrill of crafting a story that sings. They tried to write, and they couldn’t cut it. That’s the damned, backwards paradox of the whole industry, right there. Every door that leads to literary success is guarded by these same curs, the ones who couldn’t make it as writers themselves. Editors, assistant editors, associate editors, most of the lot, plus the manuscript readers for magazines and publishing houses—nearly all of them, every last one, are people who dreamed of writing and fell flat. And yet these poor sods, the least qualified people under heaven to judge writing, are the exact ones who get to decide what sees print and what rots in a slush pile. They’ve already proven they have no original spark, no sacred, burning drive to create, and yet they get to sit in judgment of original work and actual genius. And after them come the reviewers, just another batch of failed writers. Don’t try to tell me they never chased that same dream, never tried to pen a poem or a novel of their own; they did, and they crashed and burned. Hell, the average book review is more sickly cloying than cod liver oil forced down a squirming kid’s throat. You already know how I feel about reviewers and those so-called critics, anyway. There are a handful of great critics out there, sure, but they’re as rare as a bright comet passing overhead. If I tank as a writer myself? I’ll have the perfect resume for an editor job. Steady bread and butter with a dollop of jam, at the very least, no worrying if my latest story’s good enough to sell.”
source_text: “And I’ll tell you,” he interrupted. “The chief qualification of ninety-nine per cent of all editors is failure. They have failed as writers. Don’t think they prefer the drudgery of the desk and the slavery to their circulation and to the business manager to the joy of writing. They have tried to write, and they have failed. And right there is the cursed paradox of it. Every portal to success in literature is guarded by those watch-dogs, the failures in literature. The editors, sub-editors, associate editors, most of them, and the manuscript-readers for the magazines and book-publishers, most of them, nearly all of them, are men who wanted to write and who have failed. And yet they, of all creatures under the sun the most unfit, are the very creatures who decide what shall and what shall not find its way into print—they, who have proved themselves not original, who have demonstrated that they lack the divine fire, sit in judgment upon originality and genius. And after them come the reviewers, just so many more failures. Don’t tell me that they have not dreamed the dream and attempted to write poetry or fiction; for they have, and they have failed. Why, the average review is more nauseating than cod-liver oil. But you know my opinion on the reviewers and the alleged critics. There are great critics, but they are as rare as comets. If I fail as a writer, I shall have proved for the career of editorship. There’s bread and butter and jam, at any rate.”

text:
Navigating his way to the dining room was a total nightmare for him. Between stopping short every few steps, tripping over his own feet, and lurching awkwardly forward, there were moments he thought he’d never make it there at all. But somehow he pulled it off, and ended up sitting right next to Her.

The spread of forks and knives laid out before him made his stomach twist. They looked sharp, alien, bristling with hidden perils he couldn’t name; he stared at them, transfixed, until their glinting silver surface blurred into a backdrop for a string of memories from the ship’s forecastle. In those flashes, he and his crewmates huddled together gnawing on salt-cured beef with pocket knives and their bare hands, or dipping dented iron spoons into metal camp bowls full of thick, lumpy pea soup.

He could practically smell the rank rot of spoiled beef in his nostrils now, and in his ears he heard the loud, smacking chewing sounds of the other men, layered over the creak of wooden beams and the low groan of the ship’s hull. He’d watched them eat back then, and thought they ate no better than pigs. Not him, not here. He’d be deliberate, careful. He wouldn’t make a single sloppy sound. He’d fix every last bit of his attention on doing this right.
source_text: The process of getting into the dining room was a nightmare to him. Between halts and stumbles, jerks and lurches, locomotion had at times seemed impossible. But at last he had made it, and was seated alongside of Her. The array of knives and forks frightened him. They bristled with unknown perils, and he gazed at them, fascinated, till their dazzle became a background across which moved a succession of forecastle pictures, wherein he and his mates sat eating salt beef with sheath-knives and fingers, or scooping thick pea-soup out of pannikins by means of battered iron spoons. The stench of bad beef was in his nostrils, while in his ears, to the accompaniment of creaking timbers and groaning bulkheads, echoed the loud mouth-noises of the eaters. He watched them eating, and decided that they ate like pigs. Well, he would be careful here. He would make no noise. He would keep his mind upon it all the time.

text: By the end of his second day at sea, once the worst of his motion sickness had faded, every last traveler aboard the steamer had emerged from their cabins, and the longer Elias observed his fellow passengers, the deeper his distaste for them grew. He was aware, even as the feeling settled, that he was judging them unfairly. They were decent, gentle folk for the most part, he made himself concede, and even as the thought crossed his mind he amended it: decent and gentle the way all middle-class folks are, saddled with the same cramped inner lives and hollow intellectual posturing that defined their caste. Their small, surface-level thoughts were so stuffed with trivial nothingness that every conversation with them felt like a tedious chore; the raucous, overcharged exuberance of the younger passengers, meanwhile, made his skin crawl. They never stayed still for a minute, endlessly tossing deck quoits, hurling ring-toss hoops, pacing the full length of the promenade, or sprinting whooping to the railing to gawk at leaping harbor porpoises and the first flocks of flying fish skittering across the wave tops.
source_text: After the second day, recovered from seasickness, the full passenger list was in evidence, and the more he saw of the passengers the more he disliked them. Yet he knew that he did them injustice. They were good and kindly people, he forced himself to acknowledge, and in the moment of acknowledgment he qualified—good and kindly like all the bourgeoisie, with all the psychological cramp and intellectual futility of their kind, they bored him when they talked with him, their little superficial minds were so filled with emptiness; while the boisterous high spirits and the excessive energy of the younger people shocked him. They were never quiet, ceaselessly playing deck-quoits, tossing rings, promenading, or rushing to the rail with loud cries to watch the leaping porpoises and the first schools of flying fish.

text: She answered the door herself, and her keen, feminine gaze immediately picked out the creased line of his trousers, alongside a faint, unnameable shift in him that read as an all-around improvement. What struck her most, though, was his face. There was an almost unruly intensity to his good health, as if it rolled off him in tangible, thrumming waves that washed straight toward her. She felt that familiar, sharp urge again, the impulse to lean into him for warmth, and marveled anew at how thoroughly his mere presence could affect her. For his part, that floating, dizzying rush of bliss flooded him the second her hand brushed his in greeting. The difference between their responses was plain: she remained cool and poised, fully in control of herself, while his face flushed bright red all the way to the roots of his hair. He stumbled after her with that same old awkwardness, his shoulders swinging and lurching perilously as he walked.
source_text: She met him at the door herself, and her woman’s eyes took in immediately the creased trousers and the certain slight but indefinable change in him for the better. Also, she was struck by his face. It was almost violent, this health of his, and it seemed to rush out of him and at her in waves of force. She felt the urge again of the desire to lean toward him for warmth, and marvelled again at the effect his presence produced upon her. And he, in turn, knew again the swimming sensation of bliss when he felt the contact of her hand in greeting. The difference between them lay in that she was cool and self-possessed while his face flushed to the roots of the hair. He stumbled with his old awkwardness after her, and his shoulders swung and lurched perilously.

text: The work was brutally draining, pushed along at breakneck pace for hour after unrelenting hour. Out on the hotel’s wide, shaded verandas, men and women clad in crisp, cool white clothing sipped frosty drinks to keep their bodies from overheating. But inside the laundry room, the air was so scorching it practically sizzled. The massive heating stove roared, glowing first dull red then searing white, and every time the irons glided over damp fabric, thick plumes of steam billowed upward. These irons ran far hotter than the ones ordinary housewives used at home: if an iron passed the standard test of a flicked wet finger hissing on its surface, it was still far too cool for Joe and Martin to use, making that old trick completely useless here. Instead, they relied entirely on holding the irons just a hair away from their cheeks, judging the temperature through some quiet, instinctive calculation that Martin found impressive even though he couldn’t wrap his head around how it worked. When the newly heated irons turned out to be too hot, they slung them onto metal hooks attached to iron rods and dipped them briefly in cold water. This step, too, demanded an incredibly precise, nuanced sense of timing. Even a split second too long submerged, and that perfect, delicate sweet spot of ideal heat was gone entirely. Martin still found himself awed sometimes at the precision he’d built up over time—an automatic, unthinking accuracy, rooted in standards as consistent and flawless as any well-oiled machine.
source_text: It was exhausting work, carried on, hour after hour, at top speed. Out on the broad verandas of the hotel, men and women, in cool white, sipped iced drinks and kept their circulation down. But in the laundry the air was sizzling. The huge stove roared red hot and white hot, while the irons, moving over the damp cloth, sent up clouds of steam. The heat of these irons was different from that used by housewives. An iron that stood the ordinary test of a wet finger was too cold for Joe and Martin, and such test was useless. They went wholly by holding the irons close to their cheeks, gauging the heat by some secret mental process that Martin admired but could not understand. When the fresh irons proved too hot, they hooked them on iron rods and dipped them into cold water. This again required a precise and subtle judgment. A fraction of a second too long in the water and the fine and silken edge of the proper heat was lost, and Martin found time to marvel at the accuracy he developed—an automatic accuracy, founded upon criteria that were machine-like and unerring.

text: Later that same afternoon, Martin was pleasantly surprised when a small boy dropped off the wheel he’d sent off for repairs. He took the unusual gesture as a sign that Von Schmidt was ready to be friendly again, since fixed wheels were almost never delivered to your door—you always had to go pick them up yourself. But when he gave the wheel a thorough once-over, he found that no repair work had been done at all. A little while later that day, he called his sister’s fiancé, and learned the man wanted absolutely nothing to do with him in “any shape, manner, or form.”
source_text: The afternoon of the same day Martin was pleased by the wheel being delivered by a small boy. Von Schmidt was also inclined to be friendly, was Martin’s conclusion from this unusual favor. Repaired wheels usually had to be called for. But when he examined the wheel, he discovered no repairs had been made. A little later in the day he telephoned his sister’s betrothed, and learned that that person didn’t want anything to do with him in “any shape, manner, or form.”

text: Five whole dollars for *The Ring of Bells*—five bucks for five thousand words total! Forget the standard two cents per word he’d expected; this worked out to ten words for a single penny. Worse, the editor had even gushed about how good the story was, for crying out loud. He wouldn’t even get the check until the issue carrying the story was published, too. So all that talk about a two-cent per word minimum and payment upon acceptance was total garbage, then. It was all a lie, and it had led him completely astray. He never would have tried his hand at writing in the first place if he’d known the truth. He would’ve gotten a regular job instead, the kind that would let him provide for Ruth. His mind drifted back to the first day he’d ever sat down to draft a story, and he was appalled at how much time he’d thrown away—all for a rate of ten words per lousy cent. All those accounts he’d read of writers earning generous, reliable rewards had to be lies as well. Every secondhand idea he’d held about what being a professional author was like was dead wrong, and this measly offer was all the proof he needed.
source_text: Five dollars for “The Ring of Bells”—five dollars for five thousand words! Instead of two cents a word, ten words for a cent! And the editor had praised it, too. And he would receive the check when the story was published. Then it was all poppycock, two cents a word for minimum rate and payment upon acceptance. It was a lie, and it had led him astray. He would never have attempted to write had he known that. He would have gone to work—to work for Ruth. He went back to the day he first attempted to write, and was appalled at the enormous waste of time—and all for ten words for a cent. And the other high rewards of writers, that he had read about, must be lies, too. His second-hand ideas of authorship were wrong, for here was the proof of it.

text: Alongside an iron stomach that could handle absolutely anything, he’d built up an exhaustive knowledge of affordable, nutrient-packed food options to get the most value for his money. Pea soup was a regular staple for him, alongside potatoes and large, earthy brown beans prepared the traditional Mexican way. He served rice at his table at least once a day, cooked in a method most American home cooks never master and never quite learn to pull off, no matter how hard they try. Dried fruit cost less than fresh, so he almost always kept a pot of stewed dried fruit on hand to slather on his bread in place of butter. Every now and then he’d splurge to grace his table with a cut of round steak or a meaty soup bone. He drank black coffee, no cream or milk, twice a day, swapping it out for tea in the evenings; both his coffee and tea were brewed excellently every single time.
source_text: In conjunction with a perfect stomach that could digest anything, he possessed knowledge of the various foods that were at the same time nutritious and cheap. Pea-soup was a common article in his diet, as well as potatoes and beans, the latter large and brown and cooked in Mexican style. Rice, cooked as American housewives never cook it and can never learn to cook it, appeared on Martin’s table at least once a day. Dried fruits were less expensive than fresh, and he had usually a pot of them, cooked and ready at hand, for they took the place of butter on his bread. Occasionally he graced his table with a piece of round-steak, or with a soup-bone. Coffee, without cream or milk, he had twice a day, in the evening substituting tea; but both coffee and tea were excellently cooked.

text: To him, though, her laugh carried a sharp, metallic edge, and the light in her eyes felt unmistakeably cold. Out of nowhere, the feeling jolted a memory loose of a gale he’d once lived through in the North Pacific. For a split second, the whole storm loomed right in front of his eyes: a midnight gale under a clear sky and fat full moon, the massive, rolling waves glinting coldly in the pale silver light. The image shifted then, and he saw the girl from the leper refuge, suddenly remembering she’d let him go entirely out of love for him.
source_text: But it seemed to him there was something metallic in her laughter, and that the light in her eyes was cold. On the spur of the moment it reminded him of a gale he had once experienced in the North Pacific. And for the moment the apparition of the gale rose before his eyes—a gale at night, with a clear sky and under a full moon, the huge seas glinting coldly in the moonlight. Next, he saw the girl in the leper refuge and remembered it was for love of him that she had let him go.

text: “Look—” His voice faltered, the whole sweeping breadth of literary canon and centuries of publishing history surging to the front of his mind, making plain how fruitless it would be to try to explain the logic behind his belief to her. “Well, it’s just better than all but a sliver of what runs in mainstream magazines, that’s why.”
source_text: “Because—” He faltered as the whole vast field of literature and the history of literature stirred in his brain and pointed the futility of his attempting to convey to her the reasons for his faith. “Well, because it’s better than ninety-nine per cent of what is published in the magazines.”

text: Martin’s whole body hummed with elation. This was the old days come back in full force, all their raucous dancing, scrappy brawls, and unhinged good cheer intact. Even as he kept a wary, sharp gaze locked on his opponent, he snuck a quick glance at Lizzie. Most girls shrieked when the men started throwing punches, but she had not made a sound. She watched with bated breath, leaning slightly forward in her fierce interest, one hand pressed to her chest, her cheeks flushed hot, and her eyes bright with enormous, stunned admiration.
source_text: Martin was thrilling all over. This was the old days with a vengeance, with their dancing, and their fighting, and their fun. While he kept a wary eye on his antagonist, he glanced at Lizzie. Usually the girls screamed when the fellows got to scrapping, but she had not screamed. She was looking on with bated breath, leaning slightly forward, so keen was her interest, one hand pressed to her breast, her cheek flushed, and in her eyes a great and amazed admiration.

text: She stepped away from his side to wander the room, craning first to study the crisscrossed clotheslines strung overhead, strung thick with scribbled notes, then puzzling out the clever pulley rig he’d used to hang his bicycle from the ceiling. Her chest tightened faintly at the stack of crumpled manuscripts piled under the table; to her, every page represented so much wasted time. The small oil stove caught her eye and won her instant admiration, but when she leaned in to check the food shelves, she found them entirely bare.
source_text: She left his side to explore the room, examining the clothes-lines of notes overhead, learning the mystery of the tackle used for suspending his wheel under the ceiling, and being saddened by the heap of manuscripts under the table which represented to her just so much wasted time. The oil-stove won her admiration, but on investigating the food shelves she found them empty.

text: “From here on out, all my free hours will go to deep study and the work that actually matters to me. When I’m not slogging through the day-to-day grind, I’ll draft early attempts at the kind of lasting work I want to create, and keep honing my craft to prepare myself to write those pieces well. I still can’t believe how much ground I’ve covered already. When I first tried my hand at writing, the only material I had was a handful of trivial, unexamined life moments I couldn’t make sense of, let alone value properly. I had no real ideas to speak of, back then. Truly, I didn’t. I didn’t even have the right vocabulary to frame my own thoughts. All my lived moments felt like a jumble of unconnected, meaningless snapshots. But as I built up my knowledge base and expanded my command of language, I started to see layers beneath those snapshots I’d stored away. I held onto all those old images, and finally figured out what they were trying to tell me. That’s when I started turning out work I was proud of: *Drift*, *Threshold*, *The Chipped Bowl*, *Cider from Wild Orchards*, *Crowded Sidewalks*, the *Hymn to Longing* sequence, and the *Shoreline Ballads*. I’ll write more pieces like those, and far better ones too, but I’ll carve out time for them around my other commitments. I’ve got my feet planted firmly on solid ground now. Rote, paying work and steady income come first, the work that’ll outlive me comes after. Just to show you how well the system works, last night I cranked out six one-liners for the comedy weeklies, and right as I was about to turn in, I got the idea to draft a humorous triolet; in less than an hour I had four finished. Those should fetch a dollar apiece. Four whole dollars right there, just for a little whimsy that struck me on my way to bed.”
source_text: “Then, I’ll have my spare time for study and for real work. In between the grind I’ll try my hand at masterpieces, and I’ll study and prepare myself for the writing of masterpieces. Why, I am amazed at the distance I have come already. When I first tried to write, I had nothing to write about except a few paltry experiences which I neither understood nor appreciated. But I had no thoughts. I really didn’t. I didn’t even have the words with which to think. My experiences were so many meaningless pictures. But as I began to add to my knowledge, and to my vocabulary, I saw something more in my experiences than mere pictures. I retained the pictures and I found their interpretation. That was when I began to do good work, when I wrote ‘Adventure,’ ‘Joy,’ ‘The Pot,’ ‘The Wine of Life,’ ‘The Jostling Street,’ the ‘Love-cycle,’ and the ‘Sea Lyrics.’ I shall write more like them, and better; but I shall do it in my spare time. My feet are on the solid earth, now. Hack-work and income first, masterpieces afterward. Just to show you, I wrote half a dozen jokes last night for the comic weeklies; and just as I was going to bed, the thought struck me to try my hand at a triolet—a humorous one; and inside an hour I had written four. They ought to be worth a dollar apiece. Four dollars right there for a few afterthoughts on the way to bed.”

text: “Every last bit of that work was already finished! Now you press food on me, when back then you let me go hungry for days, barred me from your doorway, and cursed me for refusing to take whatever dead-end mill job you thought fit for me. The work was done, every last piece of it, long before any of you bothered to glance my way. Now, when I open my mouth to speak, you bite back whatever unspoken retort you had, lean in hanging on every word, and treat every stray thought I voice as if it’s gospel. I tell you your political party is rotten to the core, stuffed full of thieves and grafters, and instead of screaming at me like you would’ve a decade back, you hem and haw and admit I’ve got more than a little right. And why’s that? Because I’m a household name now, because my bank account’s fat enough to drown in. Not because I’m Elias Thorne, the same decent fellow who was never half as stupid as you thought he was. I could stand here and tell you the moon is spun from moldy green cheese, and you’d nod along and subscribe to the nonsense, or at least you’d never dare say I’m wrong, all because I’ve got stacks of cash, piles of them high as your roof. And all of that work, every last bit of it, was done years ago— I’m telling you, it was finished back when you’d spit on the ground I walked on like I was nothing more than gutter muck.”
source_text: “It was work performed! And now you feed me, when then you let me starve, forbade me your house, and damned me because I wouldn’t get a job. And the work was already done, all done. And now, when I speak, you check the thought unuttered on your lips and hang on my lips and pay respectful attention to whatever I choose to say. I tell you your party is rotten and filled with grafters, and instead of flying into a rage you hum and haw and admit there is a great deal in what I say. And why? Because I’m famous; because I’ve a lot of money. Not because I’m Martin Eden, a pretty good fellow and not particularly a fool. I could tell you the moon is made of green cheese and you would subscribe to the notion, at least you would not repudiate it, because I’ve got dollars, mountains of them. And it was all done long ago; it was work performed, I tell you, when you spat upon me as the dirt under your feet.”

text: There’s one fellow, Stevens, a theosophist, who rambles into the most tangled, convoluted tangents once he gets going on a topic. Just now he’s working as a dishwasher in a local restaurant, and he’s deeply partial to a high-quality cigar. I once saw him eat his fill at a grubby ten-cent hash house, then pay fifty cents for the cigar he lit up and smoked right after finishing his meal. I’ve got a couple of nice ones tucked in my pocket for him, if he happens to show up.
source_text: “There’s one fellow—Stevens—a theosophist. Makes a pretty tangle when he gets going. Just now he’s dish-washer in a restaurant. Likes a good cigar. I’ve seen him eat in a ten-cent hash-house and pay fifty cents for the cigar he smoked afterward. I’ve got a couple in my pocket for him, if he shows up.”

text: “Ruth, you leave Martin be. He knows what’s right for his own life better than anyone. Just look at everything he’s already pulled off. Sometimes he makes me sick to my stomach, so sick I feel ashamed of my own choices and limitations. Right now he understands more about how the world works, what life actually amounts to, where people fit in the grand scheme of things, and every other big question that matters, than Arthur, or Norman, or me, or even you, for that matter. And that’s all in spite of all our Latin, French, Saxon coursework, and the fancy privileged culture the rest of us were raised with.”
source_text: “You leave Martin alone, Ruth. He knows what’s best for himself. Look at what he’s done already. He makes me sick sometimes, sick and ashamed of myself. He knows more now about the world, and life, and man’s place, and all the rest, than Arthur, or Norman, or I, or you, too, for that matter, and in spite of all our Latin, and French, and Saxon, and culture.”

text: “She spun me nothing but lies,” he said, voice rough enough to scrape the cold street air. “She acted like she was throwing her whole life away to be with me, daring every rule her family ever set, when the whole time she knew the brother who’d driven her here was idling three blocks over, waiting to ferry her right back the second things got real.” A sharp, bitter laugh burst from his chest, rough and unhinged. “Oh, these rich petty-bourgeois fools! When I was penniless, crashing on friends’ floors and picking up odd construction shifts to eat, I wasn’t fit to so much as breathe the same air as his precious little sister. Now that I’ve got a padded bank account and a lease on a downtown loft? He practically shoves her into my arms himself.”
source_text: “She lied,” he said aloud. “She made believe to me that she had dared greatly, and all the while she knew the brother that brought her was waiting to take her back.” He burst into laughter. “Oh, these bourgeois! When I was broke, I was not fit to be seen with his sister. When I have a bank account, he brings her to me.”

text: “You know,” he said at last, “I’ve received that exact same criticism once before, from Joseph Le Conte, the eminent evolutionary scientist. He’s deceased now, and I’d assumed my shortcoming would never be brought to light again—until you came along and uncovered it, that is. On a more serious note, though, this is a genuine confession: I believe there’s real merit to your argument, a great deal of it, in fact. I’m far too steeped in classical scholarship, not nearly up to speed on the interpretive branches of modern science. All I can offer in my defense are the gaps in my early education, and an inherent slothfulness that stops me from putting in the work to remedy the issue. I wonder if you’ll believe I’ve never once stepped inside a physics or chemistry laboratory? It’s the truth, regardless. Le Conte was correct, and so are you, Mr. Eden, at least to a certain degree—just how far that degree goes, I couldn’t say.”
source_text: “Do you know,” he said at last, “I’ve had that same criticism passed on me once before—by a very great man, a scientist and evolutionist, Joseph Le Conte. But he is dead, and I thought to remain undetected; and now you come along and expose me. Seriously, though—and this is confession—I think there is something in your contention—a great deal, in fact. I am too classical, not enough up-to-date in the interpretative branches of science, and I can only plead the disadvantages of my education and a temperamental slothfulness that prevents me from doing the work. I wonder if you’ll believe that I’ve never been inside a physics or chemistry laboratory? It is true, nevertheless. Le Conte was right, and so are you, Mr. Eden, at least to an extent—how much I do not know.”

text: Kael Rainer made no move to hunt for work when dawn broke. It was well past mid-afternoon when he surfaced from his fevered delirium, squinting around the cramped room through stinging, aching eyes. Lila, the eight-year-old youngest of the Valez clan who’d been perched beside his bed keeping watch, let out a sharp, shrill cry the moment she registered he was conscious again. Marisol hurried into the room from the adjacent kitchen at the sound. She pressed her work-calloused palm to his scorching forehead, then rested her fingers against the curve of his jaw to feel his thready pulse.
source_text: Martin Eden did not go out to hunt for a job in the morning. It was late afternoon before he came out of his delirium and gazed with aching eyes about the room. Mary, one of the tribe of Silva, eight years old, keeping watch, raised a screech at sight of his returning consciousness. Maria hurried into the room from the kitchen. She put her work-calloused hand upon his hot forehead and felt his pulse.

text: Martin was absolutely overjoyed—so thrilled, in fact, that the moment he recalled *The Hornet* owed him fifteen dollars for his piece “The Peri and the Pearl,” he decided on the spot to head over and collect the debt. But *The Hornet* was run by a crew of clean-shaven, strapping young men, unapologetic grifters who robbed every person and every asset they came across, not even sparing their own colleagues. A handful of office furnishings ended up broken in the ensuing scuffle, and the editor, a former college athlete, had capable backup from the business manager, an advertising agent, and the porter to haul Martin out of the office; the hard initial shove they gave him only sped up his trip down the first flight of stairs.
source_text: Martin was elated—so elated that when he recollected that _The Hornet_ owed him fifteen dollars for “The Peri and the Pearl,” he decided forthwith to go and collect it. But _The Hornet_ was run by a set of clean-shaven, strapping young men, frank buccaneers who robbed everything and everybody, not excepting one another. After some breakage of the office furniture, the editor (an ex-college athlete), ably assisted by the business manager, an advertising agent, and the porter, succeeded in removing Martin from the office and in accelerating, by initial impulse, his descent of the first flight of stairs.

text: If Elowen had been jittery and on edge when she first crossed the threshold, she was now consumed by blind, thrumming terror. That fear was no vague, fleeting unease—it was unshakable, unarguable certitude. She did not harbor quiet, unproven suspicions. She knew. She stared at Silas with wide, frozen horror, and her leaden limbs shrank inward beneath the slanting golden lamplight as if the glow itself was searing her flesh.
source_text: If Gertrude had been frightened when she arrived, she was now in a panic of fear. Her fear was such that it was certitude. She was not suspicious. She was convinced. She looked at Martin in horror, and her heavy limbs shrank under the golden stream as though it were burning her.

text: “A philosopher for the barely educated,” one stuffy professional philosopher sneeringly called him—though that academic was hardly fit to even share the same air as the man he derided. I doubt you’ve read even ten pages of Spencer’s work, and you are far from unique in that: there are critics, presumably sharper than you, who have read no more of his output than you have, yet publicly dared his supporters to name a single original idea from across his entire body of writing. This is Herbert Spencer, after all: the man whose genius left an indelible mark on every corner of scientific inquiry and modern intellectual life, the father of modern psychology, the thinker who upended the entire field of education so that even the child of a poor French farmer learns to read, write, and do arithmetic using the principles he first laid out. These tiny, gnat-like men snipe at his legacy even as they earn their entire livelihoods from the practical applications of his ideas. The small amount of worthwhile thought they carry in their heads exists almost entirely because of his work. There is no question that if he had never been born, nearly all the valid parts of the rote, parroted knowledge they spout would never have existed at all.
source_text: “‘The philosopher of the half-educated,’ he was called by an academic Philosopher who was not worthy to pollute the atmosphere he breathed. I don’t think you have read ten pages of Spencer, but there have been critics, assumably more intelligent than you, who have read no more than you of Spencer, who publicly challenged his followers to adduce one single idea from all his writings—from Herbert Spencer’s writings, the man who has impressed the stamp of his genius over the whole field of scientific research and modern thought; the father of psychology; the man who revolutionized pedagogy, so that to-day the child of the French peasant is taught the three R’s according to principles laid down by him. And the little gnats of men sting his memory when they get their very bread and butter from the technical application of his ideas. What little of worth resides in their brains is largely due to him. It is certain that had he never lived, most of what is correct in their parrot-learned knowledge would be absent.

text: The formulaic, churned-out short tales he cranked out may have filled him with loathing and scorn, but they sold reliably enough. With the earnings, he made good on every outstanding promise, settled nearly all his debts, and picked up a fresh set of rubber tires for his scuffed secondhand bicycle. These throwaway stories at least kept food on the table and freed up hours for the serious, ambitious writing he cared about; the single bright spot holding his resolve together was the forty-dollar check he’d gotten from *The Copper Sparrow* a few weeks prior. He pinned all his hope to that small win, certain that top-tier literary journals would pay an untested writer at least that much per word, if not a far higher rate. The problem, of course, was breaking into those elite publications. His finest short fiction, personal essays, and verse circulated through their slush piles unbidden and unwanted, even as he pored through their issues each month and found page after page of flat, lifeless, unoriginal work filling their pages. If just one editor, he often mused, would climb down from their lofty, condescending perch to send him even a single line of encouragement! Even if his work was odd, even if it didn’t fit their editorial guidelines for practical, risk-averse reasons, there had to be a flicker of something worthwhile in it somewhere, enough to spark at least a glimmer of recognition from them. In those moments he’d yank one of his carefully drafted manuscripts off his shelf, *Wanderer’s Fall* among them, and reread it again and again, fruitlessly trying to work out why the editors had left him hanging without a word.
source_text: His machine-made storiettes, though he hated them and derided them, were successful. By means of them he redeemed all his pledges, paid most of his bills, and bought a new set of tires for his wheel. The storiettes at least kept the pot a-boiling and gave him time for ambitious work; while the one thing that upheld him was the forty dollars he had received from _The White Mouse_. He anchored his faith to that, and was confident that the really first-class magazines would pay an unknown writer at least an equal rate, if not a better one. But the thing was, how to get into the first-class magazines. His best stories, essays, and poems went begging among them, and yet, each month, he read reams of dull, prosy, inartistic stuff between all their various covers. If only one editor, he sometimes thought, would descend from his high seat of pride to write me one cheering line! No matter if my work is unusual, no matter if it is unfit, for prudential reasons, for their pages, surely there must be some sparks in it, somewhere, a few, to warm them to some sort of appreciation. And thereupon he would get out one or another of his manuscripts, such as “Adventure,” and read it over and over in a vain attempt to vindicate the editorial silence.

text: He’d been on the cusp of suggesting *The Lady of Shalott* for their next shared reading, and would have spoken the words aloud, had a sudden, unmooring reverie not seized him, leaving him staring unblinking at her, the woman of his own species, who’d risen from the roiling primordial ferment, creeping and clambering up the vast ladder of life across a million millennia, to emerge at the very top rung, forged into Elowen: pure, luminous, divine, with the power to teach him what love was, to stir in him a hunger for goodness, to make him crave a taste of that same sanctity—him, Leo Voss, who’d also clawed his way up, in some astounding, unforeseen fashion, from the grime and the mire, the countless missteps and false starts of unending creation. There was the romance, the wonder, the glory of it all. There was the stuff to write, if only he could find the right words. Saints in heaven! They were only saints, and had no choice in their holiness. But he was a man.
source_text: He was impelled to suggest “Locksley Hall,” and would have done so, had not his vision gripped him again and left him staring at her, the female of his kind, who, out of the primordial ferment, creeping and crawling up the vast ladder of life for a thousand thousand centuries, had emerged on the topmost rung, having become one Ruth, pure, and fair, and divine, and with power to make him know love, and to aspire toward purity, and to desire to taste divinity—him, Martin Eden, who, too, had come up in some amazing fashion from out of the ruck and the mire and the countless mistakes and abortions of unending creation. There was the romance, and the wonder, and the glory. There was the stuff to write, if he could only find speech. Saints in heaven!—They were only saints and could not help themselves. But he was a man.

text:
A sharp, soul-deep queasiness hit him out of nowhere. The jump from thinking about Ruth to the situation right in front of him had been far too jarring. As he stared at the brash, unapologetic gaze of the girl across from him, Ruth’s soft, glowing saint-like eyes superimposed themselves over the sight, looking back at him from fathomless wells of untainted goodness.

Some quiet, thrumming strength stirred in his chest all at once. He was better than this mess. Life held so much more weight for him than it did for these two girls, whose whole world never extended past ice cream dates and a well-dressed suitor. He’d always carried a whole secret, internal life inside his head, he recalled. He’d tried to open up about those thoughts before, but he’d never met a woman who could grasp what he was talking about, and never a man either. Every time he’d made the attempt, he’d only left the people listening confused.

If his mind worked on a level they could never reach, he reasoned, then he himself must be operating on a higher plane than they were. That thrumming strength swelled again, and he curled his fingers tight into fists. If life had more to give him, then he had every right to ask for more from it—but he’d never get anything worth having from the kind of company he was stuck with right now. Those sharp, unblinking dark eyes had nothing worthwhile to give him. He knew exactly what ran through the head behind them: ice cream, and cheap, fleeting thrills after that. But Ruth’s saint-like eyes, the ones he carried in his head? They offered everything he already valued, and a thousand things he hadn’t even dared to imagine yet. They held the promise of books and paintings, quiet beauty and peace, all the gentle, refined grace of a life lived on a higher, more intentional plane.

He could map every single thought that passed behind those dark eyes, plain as day. They ran like a well-oiled clock, no surprises, no hidden parts. What they promised was cheap, hollow satisfaction, as narrow and empty as a casket, that would fade fast before leading straight to that same casket in the end. But what Ruth’s saintly eyes offered was mystery, unnameable wonder, and a kind of life that didn’t end. He’d caught tiny flashes of her soul in them before, and in those flashes, he’d caught sight of his own, too.
source_text: He was afflicted by a sudden spiritual nausea. The transition from Ruth to this had been too abrupt. Ranged side by side with the bold, defiant eyes of the girl before him, he saw Ruth’s clear, luminous eyes, like a saint’s, gazing at him out of unplumbed depths of purity. And, somehow, he felt within him a stir of power. He was better than this. Life meant more to him than it meant to these two girls whose thoughts did not go beyond ice-cream and a gentleman friend. He remembered that he had led always a secret life in his thoughts. These thoughts he had tried to share, but never had he found a woman capable of understanding—nor a man. He had tried, at times, but had only puzzled his listeners. And as his thoughts had been beyond them, so, he argued now, he must be beyond them. He felt power move in him, and clenched his fists. If life meant more to him, then it was for him to demand more from life, but he could not demand it from such companionship as this. Those bold black eyes had nothing to offer. He knew the thoughts behind them—of ice-cream and of something else. But those saint’s eyes alongside—they offered all he knew and more than he could guess. They offered books and painting, beauty and repose, and all the fine elegance of higher existence. Behind those black eyes he knew every thought process. It was like clockwork. He could watch every wheel go around. Their bid was low pleasure, narrow as the grave, that palled, and the grave was at the end of it. But the bid of the saint’s eyes was mystery, and wonder unthinkable, and eternal life. He had caught glimpses of the soul in them, and glimpses of his own soul, too.

text: He’d once signed on for an Arctic whaling voyage scheduled to last three years, only for the journey to end in shipwreck a mere six months after departure. Though his imagination was whimsical, even wildly fantastical at times, he held a core devotion to grounding his work in reality that compelled him to write only of things he knew firsthand. Whaling was a world he understood intimately, so he drew directly from that lived, concrete knowledge to craft the fictional adventures of the two boys he’d chosen as his story’s joint heroes. The work felt surprisingly easy, he concluded that Saturday evening; he’d finished the first three-thousand-word installment of the tale that very day. The accomplishment amused Jim greatly, while Mr. Higginbotham met it with open derision, sneering nonstop throughout the meal about the “litery” person they had suddenly discovered in their family.
source_text: He had been on a whaling voyage in the Arctic, once—a voyage that was to have been for three years and which had terminated in shipwreck at the end of six months. While his imagination was fanciful, even fantastic at times, he had a basic love of reality that compelled him to write about the things he knew. He knew whaling, and out of the real materials of his knowledge he proceeded to manufacture the fictitious adventures of the two boys he intended to use as joint heroes. It was easy work, he decided on Saturday evening. He had completed on that day the first instalment of three thousand words—much to the amusement of Jim, and to the open derision of Mr. Higginbotham, who sneered throughout meal-time at the “litery” person they had discovered in the family.

text: “But I’m so terribly drawn to him,” she went on. “He’s my protégé, in a sense. He’s also the first boy I’ve been close to, though “friend” doesn’t quite fit—he’s equal parts protégé and companion, really. Sometimes, when he frightens me, he feels like a bulldog I picked up as a plaything, the way some of the frat girls do. He tugs so hard, bares his teeth, and looks like he’s about to break free any moment.”
source_text: “But I am interested in him dreadfully,” she continued. “In a way he is my protégé. Then, too, he is my first boy friend—but not exactly friend; rather protégé and friend combined. Sometimes, too, when he frightens me, it seems that he is a bulldog I have taken for a plaything, like some of the ‘frat’ girls, and he is tugging hard, and showing his teeth, and threatening to break loose.”

text: Ruth didn’t even realize she was proving his point perfectly: she unreservedly revered all the trappings of the established order. Martin tracked the quiet workings of her mind in that moment, but held back from pressing the conversation any further. His affection for her had never been tied to the opinions she held of Praps, Vanderwater, or the college’s English professors, and he was growing more and more certain that there were entire uncharted corners of his mind and vast swathes of his knowledge that she would never be able to understand, or even suspect existed at all.
source_text: Unwittingly, Ruth herself proved his point that she worshipped the established. Martin followed the processes of her thoughts, but forbore to go farther. He did not love her for what she thought of Praps and Vanderwater and English professors, and he was coming to realize, with increasing conviction, that he possessed brain-areas and stretches of knowledge which she could never comprehend nor know existed.

text: “There’s so much that’s non-negotiable when it comes to this.” She gazed at him softly, and he felt like an absolute brute for pushing back against her. “Physics and chemistry—you can’t get a real grasp on either without hands-on lab work; you’ll also find algebra and geometry nearly impossible to work through without proper guidance. You need skilled, knowledgeable teachers, specialists who know exactly how to convey that kind of complex material effectively to learners.”
source_text: “There is so much that is necessary.” She looked at him gently, and he was a brute to oppose her. “Physics and chemistry—you can’t do them without laboratory study; and you’ll find algebra and geometry almost hopeless without instruction. You need the skilled teachers, the specialists in the art of imparting knowledge.”

text: He slept more often than not. After finishing breakfast, he’d grab a magazine he’d never managed to get all the way through and head for his deck chair. Staring at the printed words drained him quickly. He couldn’t wrap his head around how people found that much worth putting down on paper, and as he turned that thought over in his head, he drifted off right in his seat. When the gong jolted him awake to call everyone to lunch, he was frustrated at being pulled out of his sleep. There was nothing pleasant or rewarding about being conscious.
source_text: He slept much. After breakfast he sought his deck-chair with a magazine he never finished. The printed pages tired him. He puzzled that men found so much to write about, and, puzzling, dozed in his chair. When the gong awoke him for luncheon, he was irritated that he must awaken. There was no satisfaction in being awake.

text: When he was not with Ruth, he dedicated all his free hours to his “Love-cycle” project, reading at home, or visiting the public reading rooms, where he grew more familiar with contemporary magazines, their editorial policies, and the sort of content they favored. The hours he spent in Ruth’s company were equally maddening for their bright, unfulfilled promise and their total lack of forward progress. A week after he had eased her headache, Norman proposed a moonlight sail on Lake Merritt, a plan Arthur and Olney were quick to support. Martin was the only one of the group who could steer a boat, so he was pressed into taking charge of the voyage. Ruth sat close beside him in the stern, while the three other young men lounged amidships, wrapped up in a loud, meandering argument about fraternity affairs.
source_text: The many hours he was not with Ruth he devoted to the “Love-cycle,” to reading at home, or to the public reading-rooms, where he got more closely in touch with the magazines of the day and the nature of their policy and content. The hours he spent with Ruth were maddening alike in promise and in inconclusiveness. It was a week after he cured her headache that a moonlight sail on Lake Merritt was proposed by Norman and seconded by Arthur and Olney. Martin was the only one capable of handling a boat, and he was pressed into service. Ruth sat near him in the stern, while the three young fellows lounged amidships, deep in a wordy wrangle over “frat” affairs.

text: “It’s too late.” Martin shook his head. “That night was the only one that ever mattered for me. I was in pure paradise. I know moments like that are run of the mill for you, I’m well aware. But they were nothing short of extraordinary to me. I’ll never exist at that same sharp, glowing pitch of feeling ever again. I’m finished with philosophy entirely. I never want to hear another word of it.”
source_text: “Too late.” Martin shook his head. “That night was the one night for me. I was in paradise. It’s commonplace with you, I know. But it wasn’t to me. I shall never live at such a pitch again. I’m done with philosophy. I want never to hear another word of it.”

text: He froze, mouth hanging slack, teetering on the edge of the gaping chasm of his own moral failings, convinced he didn’t even deserve to breathe the same air as her. As Arthur launched into the same story he’d told at least twenty times already—about the run-in he’d had with drunk thugs on the ferry, and how Martin Eden had stepped in out of nowhere to save him—Martin himself sat scowling, turning over how ridiculous he’d made himself look, and grappled more fiercely than ever with the question of how he was supposed to act around these people. He’d definitely messed it up up to this point, he thought. He wasn’t part of their world, and he didn’t speak their slang, that was the long and short of it. He couldn’t just pretend he fit in with them. That kind of act would fall apart sooner or later, anyway, and performing wasn’t in his bones to begin with. He had no patience for pretense or manipulation. No matter what went down, he had to stay true to himself. He couldn’t match their way of speaking right now, but he was dead set on learning it eventually. In the meantime, though, he still had to speak up, and he’d do it in his own voice—softened a little, obviously, so they could follow what he was saying and wouldn’t be too put off by his rough edges. What’s more, he wouldn’t pretend to know something he didn’t, not even by letting a reference he didn’t get slide without comment. Sticking to that resolve, when the two brothers threw the word “trig” around a few times while chatting about university matters, Martin Eden cut in to ask:
source_text: He paused, open-mouthed, on the verge of the pit of his own depravity and utter worthlessness to breathe the same air she did. And while Arthur took up the tale, for the twentieth time, of his adventure with the drunken hoodlums on the ferry-boat and of how Martin Eden had rushed in and rescued him, that individual, with frowning brows, meditated upon the fool he had made of himself, and wrestled more determinedly with the problem of how he should conduct himself toward these people. He certainly had not succeeded so far. He wasn’t of their tribe, and he couldn’t talk their lingo, was the way he put it to himself. He couldn’t fake being their kind. The masquerade would fail, and besides, masquerade was foreign to his nature. There was no room in him for sham or artifice. Whatever happened, he must be real. He couldn’t talk their talk just yet, though in time he would. Upon that he was resolved. But in the meantime, talk he must, and it must be his own talk, toned down, of course, so as to be comprehensible to them and so as not to shock them too much. And furthermore, he wouldn’t claim, not even by tacit acceptance, to be familiar with anything that was unfamiliar. In pursuance of this decision, when the two brothers, talking university shop, had used “trig” several times, Martin Eden demanded:-

text: The rookie reporter was a little thrown off by how everyone kept talking over his head, around him, and even directly at him without ever bothering to address him as part of the conversation. He had just been praised for his sharp, vivid write-up of the socialist gathering, and he had further been assigned specifically to secure a personal interview with Martin Eden, the leader of the organized group widely labeled a menace to society.
source_text: The cub reporter was a trifle perplexed by this talking over him and around him and at him. But he had been commended for his brilliant description of the socialist meeting and had further been detailed to get a personal interview with Martin Eden, the leader of the organized menace to society.

text: The first thing Elias did when he woke the next morning was act directly counter to both Caspar Voss’s casual advice and his firm, explicit command. *The Weight of the Daylight*, his full-length manuscript, he wrapped carefully and posted off to *The High Citadel*. He was confident he could secure magazine serialization for the work, and suspected that formal endorsement from a reputable periodical would make hard-to-impress book publishers far more receptive to his pitches. He also wrapped *Mayflies*, his sprawling long poem, and dropped it in the post to a different prominent literary magazine. For all Caspar’s rabid, near-obsessive loathing of mainstream periodicals, Elias was determined that the celebrated verse piece should see print eventually. He had no intention of running the poem without his friend’s permission, though. His gambit was to get it accepted by one of the most prestigious magazines first, and with that proof of its merit in hand, argue Caspar around to giving his consent.
source_text: The first thing Martin did next morning was to go counter both to Brissenden’s advice and command. “The Shame of the Sun” he wrapped and mailed to _The Acropolis_. He believed he could find magazine publication for it, and he felt that recognition by the magazines would commend him to the book-publishing houses. “Ephemera” he likewise wrapped and mailed to a magazine. Despite Brissenden’s prejudice against the magazines, which was a pronounced mania with him, Martin decided that the great poem should see print. He did not intend, however, to publish it without the other’s permission. His plan was to get it accepted by one of the high magazines, and, thus armed, again to wrestle with Brissenden for consent.

text: “Elowen.” He’d never imagined a single syllable could hold such sweetness. It curled warm through his ears, and he grew giddy, saying it over and over under his breath. “Elowen.” It was a charm, a spell woven just for him to speak. Every time he whispered the name, her face glowed behind his eyelids, drenching the scuffed, grimy tenement wall in gilded light. That light didn’t end at the plaster, either. It stretched out endless into the dark, and along its honeyed threads his soul reached, straining to brush against hers. Every good part of him swelled and poured forth unhindered. The mere idea of her lifted him, scrubbed the grit from his edges, made him more than he’d ever been, and hungry to be more still. This was uncharted ground. No woman he’d ever known had ever made him want to rise; they’d always dragged him downward, pulled the worst, most animal parts of him to the surface. He didn’t stop to consider that most of those women had tried their hardest, flawed as those efforts were. He’d never spent any time examining his own nature, so he had no idea there was something in him that drew affection, that had made those women cling to the raw, bright warmth of his youth. They’d often been a nuisance to him, and he’d never spared them a second thought; it would never have crossed his mind that some of those women had grown softer, better, for having known him. He’d drifted through life in a haze of selfish unconcern until this moment, and now all those past encounters felt like they’d been grabbing at him with grubby, greedy hands, trying to pull him down into the mire. It was an unfair judgment, both to them and to the boy he’d been then. But he, who was seeing his own past clearly for the very first time, was in no state to be fair. His face burned with hot shame as he stared through the glow of Elowen’s face at the ugly, careless mess of his own history.
source_text: “Ruth.” He had not thought a simple sound could be so beautiful. It delighted his ear, and he grew intoxicated with the repetition of it. “Ruth.” It was a talisman, a magic word to conjure with. Each time he murmured it, her face shimmered before him, suffusing the foul wall with a golden radiance. This radiance did not stop at the wall. It extended on into infinity, and through its golden depths his soul went questing after hers. The best that was in him was out in splendid flood. The very thought of her ennobled and purified him, made him better, and made him want to be better. This was new to him. He had never known women who had made him better. They had always had the counter effect of making him beastly. He did not know that many of them had done their best, bad as it was. Never having been conscious of himself, he did not know that he had that in his being that drew love from women and which had been the cause of their reaching out for his youth. Though they had often bothered him, he had never bothered about them; and he would never have dreamed that there were women who had been better because of him. Always in sublime carelessness had he lived, till now, and now it seemed to him that they had always reached out and dragged at him with vile hands. This was not just to them, nor to himself. But he, who for the first time was becoming conscious of himself, was in no condition to judge, and he burned with shame as he stared at the vision of his infamy.

text: To her utter amazement—and it was a tale she would recount at every opportunity for the rest of her days—Maria watched Martin Eden yank a hot iron straight from the stove and lay an ornate shirtwaist flat across the ironing board. That was Kate Flanagan’s finest Sunday waist, and there was no woman in all of Maria’s social circle more exacting and particular about her clothing than Kate. What’s more, Miss Flanagan had left explicit orders that the garment be finished and delivered that very evening. Everyone in the neighborhood knew she was courting John Collins, the local blacksmith, and Maria was privy to the secret that the pair planned to take a trip to Golden Gate Park the following day. Maria tried to snatch the waist away before damage could be done, but her effort was useless: Martin guided her unsteady, tottering steps to a nearby chair, where she sat, eyes bulging, as she observed his work. He finished ironing the shirtwaist in a quarter of the time it would have taken Maria, and even she had to concede the job was every bit as neat and well-done as anything she could have produced herself.
source_text: To her astonishment (and it was something that she never ceased from relating to her dying day), she saw Martin Eden seize an iron from the stove and throw a fancy shirt-waist on the ironing-board. It was Kate Flanagan’s best Sunday waist, than whom there was no more exacting and fastidiously dressed woman in Maria’s world. Also, Miss Flanagan had sent special instruction that said waist must be delivered by that night. As every one knew, she was keeping company with John Collins, the blacksmith, and, as Maria knew privily, Miss Flanagan and Mr. Collins were going next day to Golden Gate Park. Vain was Maria’s attempt to rescue the garment. Martin guided her tottering footsteps to a chair, from where she watched him with bulging eyes. In a quarter of the time it would have taken her she saw the shirt-waist safely ironed, and ironed as well as she could have done it, as Martin made her grant.

text: Harthaven, Hale & Rowe had tentatively pressed a first run of fifteen hundred copies, but glowing early notices sent a second edition of double that size whirring through the presses before the first had even fully shipped; before the second run could hit bookstalls, a third printing of five thousand had already been commissioned. A London publishing house struck a deal via transatlantic cable to secure UK distribution rights, and hard on the heels of that announcement came word that French, German, and Norwegian translation efforts were already underway. The volume’s takedown of the neo-symbolist school of continental thought could not have landed at a more serendipitous cultural moment. A roaring public dispute erupted almost overnight. Biologists Julian Hargrove and Ernst Voss stepped forward to endorse and defend *The Blight of the Noon Sky*, for the first time in their decades-long public rivalry finding themselves aligned on an intellectual question. Physicists Arthur Croft and Alfred Waller lined up in the opposing camp, while Sir Alistair Loddon tried to craft a middle ground that aligned neatly with his own idiosyncratic theories of cosmic order. The neo-symbolist faithful rallied behind the banner of transcendental mysticism, penning fiery open letters and essays in popular weeklies. Humorist and essayist Gilbert K. Pembroke sent the entire reading public cackling with a string of supposedly neutral takes on the fracas, and the entire debate, along with nearly every public figure tangled up in it, was almost blown clean off the cultural stage by a scathing, thunderous takedown from playwright George Seaborne. Needless to say, the arena was packed with throngs of lesser-known commentators, and the dust, sweat, and roar of clashing opinions grew deafening.
source_text: Singletree, Darnley & Co. had cautiously brought out an edition of fifteen hundred copies, but the first reviews had started a second edition of twice the size through the presses; and ere this was delivered a third edition of five thousand had been ordered. A London firm made arrangements by cable for an English edition, and hot-footed upon this came the news of French, German, and Scandinavian translations in progress. The attack upon the Maeterlinck school could not have been made at a more opportune moment. A fierce controversy was precipitated. Saleeby and Haeckel indorsed and defended “The Shame of the Sun,” for once finding themselves on the same side of a question. Crookes and Wallace ranged up on the opposing side, while Sir Oliver Lodge attempted to formulate a compromise that would jibe with his particular cosmic theories. Maeterlinck’s followers rallied around the standard of mysticism. Chesterton set the whole world laughing with a series of alleged non-partisan essays on the subject, and the whole affair, controversy and controversialists, was well-nigh swept into the pit by a thundering broadside from George Bernard Shaw. Needless to say the arena was crowded with hosts of lesser lights, and the dust and sweat and din became terrific.

text: “You’d have to call that a raw deal for a kid just starting out,” he grumbled. “Four dollars a week? How in the world was he supposed to get by on that? No way he had any little luxuries, that’s for certain. Shoot, I fork over five dollars a week just for room and board right now, and there’s absolutely nothing fancy about it, take my word for it. He must’ve scraped by worse than a stray. The slop he had to call food—”
source_text: “I’d say they was pretty hard lines for a young fellow,” he remarked. “Four dollars a week! How could he live on it? You can bet he didn’t have any frills. Why, I pay five dollars a week for board now, an’ there’s nothin’ excitin’ about it, you can lay to that. He must have lived like a dog. The food he ate—”

text: I’d still be stuck there to this day, if it hadn’t been for a young woman I met on site: half Chinese, a quarter white, a quarter Native Hawaiian. She was gorgeous, poor thing, and really well educated. Her mom lived in Honolulu, and was worth roughly a million dollars or more, at the time. Anyway, she finally helped me get away. Her mother covered all the settlement costs, you see, so she didn’t have to fear getting in trouble for letting me escape. First, though, she made me swear I’d never reveal where the hiding spot was. I never have, either—this is the first time I’ve ever even mentioned it to anyone. She was just showing the earliest signs of leprosy back then. The fingers on her right hand were slightly twisted, and there was a tiny discolored spot on her arm, that was all. I imagine she’s passed away by now, anyway.
source_text: “I’d have been there yet, if it hadn’t been for a girl there, a half-Chinese, quarter-white, and quarter-Hawaiian. She was a beauty, poor thing, and well educated. Her mother, in Honolulu, was worth a million or so. Well, this girl got me away at last. Her mother financed the settlement, you see, so the girl wasn’t afraid of being punished for letting me go. But she made me swear, first, never to reveal the hiding-place; and I never have. This is the first time I have even mentioned it. The girl had just the first signs of leprosy. The fingers of her right hand were slightly twisted, and there was a small spot on her arm. That was all. I guess she is dead, now.”

text: “No, Maria,” he went on, “Nick and Joe won’t ever have to hawk milk door to door, and all the kids will be able to go to school and wear shoes all twelve months of the year. It’ll be a top-tier dairy ranch, fully outfitted with every amenity we could need. We’ll have a comfortable house to live in, a stable for the horses, and of course, plenty of cow barns. There will be chickens, pigs, vegetable beds, fruit trees, and all the rest, plus enough cows to cover the cost of hiring one or two farmhands. Then you won’t have to lift a finger for any of the farm work, just look after the children. For that matter, if you ever meet a decent, hardworking man, you can get married and take it easy while he runs the entire ranch for you.”
source_text: “No, Maria,” he went on; “Nick and Joe won’t have to peddle milk, and all the kids can go to school and wear shoes the whole year round. It will be a first-class milk ranch—everything complete. There will be a house to live in and a stable for the horses, and cow-barns, of course. There will be chickens, pigs, vegetables, fruit trees, and everything like that; and there will be enough cows to pay for a hired man or two. Then you won’t have anything to do but take care of the children. For that matter, if you find a good man, you can marry and take it easy while he runs the ranch.”

text: He bent the arm closest to her—just a tiny, tentative curve, done so discreetly he hoped it would read as unplanned. It was no overt invitation, only a lazy, offhand gesture, as if he always strolled with his arm held just so. Then the most wonderful thing happened. He felt her hand settle lightly on his forearm. Delicious, shivery thrills raced through him at the contact, and for a few giddy, perfect seconds, he swore he’d left the solid ground entirely, drifting through the sky right alongside her. But he crashed back to earth almost immediately, flustered by a sudden, trivial crisis. They were crossing the street, which would leave him walking on the inner curb side. Proper custom dictated he ought to take the outer edge, closest to passing traffic. Should he let go of her arm to swap positions? And if he did, would he have to repeat the whole awkward maneuver every single time they crossed a road from then on? The whole thing felt clumsy no matter the choice, so he resolved not to dart around like a bumbling fool. Still, he couldn’t shake the nagging feeling he’d picked the wrong option, so when they reached the other side and he was indeed stuck on the inside, he chattered quickly and intently, putting on a show of being completely swept up in what he was saying, so that if he was wrong to have stayed put, his obvious enthusiasm would read as the cause of his carelessness.
source_text: He crooked the arm next to her—crooked it very slightly and with secret tentativeness, not invitingly, but just casually, as though he was accustomed to walk that way. And then the wonderful thing happened. He felt her hand upon his arm. Delicious thrills ran through him at the contact, and for a few sweet moments it seemed that he had left the solid earth and was flying with her through the air. But he was soon back again, perturbed by a new complication. They were crossing the street. This would put him on the inside. He should be on the outside. Should he therefore drop her arm and change over? And if he did so, would he have to repeat the manoeuvre the next time? And the next? There was something wrong about it, and he resolved not to caper about and play the fool. Yet he was not satisfied with his conclusion, and when he found himself on the inside, he talked quickly and earnestly, making a show of being carried away by what he was saying, so that, in case he was wrong in not changing sides, his enthusiasm would seem the cause for his carelessness.
