text: He had no way of tracking how long he’d drifted adrift in that ruminative haze, but after what felt like an endless stretch of unmoored minutes, a sharp rap at the door jolted him back to his body, followed by Lila’s soft voice calling through the wood to ask if he was unwell. He answered in a thick, unrecognizable murmur, fumbling to explain he’d only been dozing off for a moment. A jolt of shock ran through him when he glanced up and saw that full, inky night had settled over the room. The envelope had landed in his hands just after two that afternoon, and the truth of it hit him all at once: he was far sicker than he’d wanted to admit.
source_text: How long he was gone on this quest he did not know, but after what seemed an enormous lapse of time, he was called back to himself by a knock at the door, and by Maria’s asking if he was sick. He replied in a muffled voice he did not recognize, saying that he was merely taking a nap. He was surprised when he noted the darkness of night in the room. He had received the letter at two in the afternoon, and he realized that he was sick.

text: “This next piece I’ve titled ‘The Pot’,” he said, unfolding his creased manuscript. “Four or five magazines have already turned it down, but I still believe it’s strong work. If I’m honest, I’m not quite sure what to make of it myself, other than that I managed to capture something genuine there. It might not land for you the way it does for me, mind. It’s a short piece—only two thousand words long.”
source_text: “This next thing I’ve called ‘The Pot’,” he said, unfolding the manuscript. “It has been refused by four or five magazines now, but still I think it is good. In fact, I don’t know what to think of it, except that I’ve caught something there. Maybe it won’t affect you as it does me. It’s a short thing—only two thousand words.”

text: “Mr. Butler’s fine,” he cut in. “The only thing I take issue with is his lousy indigestion. But honest to goodness, I can’t tell a bit of difference between writing jokes or comic verse and tapping at a typewriter, taking dictation, or balancing ledgers. All of it’s just a means to an end. Your idea is that I should start out doing bookkeeping to work my way up to a successful lawyer or businessman. Mine is that I start with grunt work hack writing and grow into a capable, accomplished author.”
source_text: “Mr. Butler’s all right,” he interrupted. “It’s only his indigestion I find fault with. But to save me I can’t see any difference between writing jokes or comic verse and running a type-writer, taking dictation, or keeping sets of books. It is all a means to an end. Your theory is for me to begin with keeping books in order to become a successful lawyer or man of business. Mine is to begin with hack-work and develop into an able author.”

text: It was precisely while turning these very thoughts over in his mind that he drafted the essay he called “Star-dust,” a piece that took playful but pointed aim not at the core tenets of criticism itself, but at the field’s most prominent critics. The work was sharp, layered, philosophically rigorous, and laced with a warm, delightful wit. It also got turned down immediately by every magazine he sent it to, no matter how many times he submitted it. But since he had already gotten the ideas out of his system, he carried on with his days unperturbed. He had long since fallen into this routine: he would let his thoughts on a given topic marinate and fully form, then dash them off at the typewriter as soon as they were ready. Whether the piece ever saw publication was of barely any importance to him. The act of writing itself was the final step of a long internal process, the moment he wove together all the scattered threads of his thinking and drew cohesive conclusions from the mass of information he had been carrying in his head. Drafting such an essay was the intentional practice he used to clear mental space, leaving his mind open to new subject matter and new puzzles to solve. In its way, it was not unlike the common urge felt by people weighed down by real or perceived slights, who periodically break their long held silence to pour out every last thought they have been bottling up, not stopping until they have said every single thing they needed to say.
source_text: In fact, it was when filled with these thoughts that he wrote his essay entitled “Star-dust,” in which he had his fling, not at the principles of criticism, but at the principal critics. It was brilliant, deep, philosophical, and deliciously touched with laughter. Also it was promptly rejected by the magazines as often as it was submitted. But having cleared his mind of it, he went serenely on his way. It was a habit he developed, of incubating and maturing his thought upon a subject, and of then rushing into the type-writer with it. That it did not see print was a matter of small moment with him. The writing of it was the culminating act of a long mental process, the drawing together of scattered threads of thought and the final generalizing upon all the data with which his mind was burdened. To write such an article was the conscious effort by which he freed his mind and made it ready for fresh material and problems. It was in a way akin to that common habit of men and women troubled by real or fancied grievances, who periodically and volubly break their long-suffering silence and “have their say” till the last word is said.

text: The days never stretched long enough for him. There was an endless list of subjects he ached to dig into. He trimmed his sleep down to five hours first, and found he could get by perfectly well on that amount of rest. When he tested four and a half, though, he had to grudgingly bump it back up to five. He would have gladly devoted every single waking minute to any one of his pursuits, if he could. It always stung to set his writing aside to hit the textbooks, to step away from his studies to head to the library, to tear himself away from that vault of reference materials or the reading room magazines crammed with trade secrets from writers who’d cracked the code of selling their work. When he was with Ruth, standing up to leave felt like yanking his own heartstrings out by the root; he’d race home down the dark streets as fast as he could, to cut down the time he lost away from his books. The hardest part of all, though, was shutting his algebra or physics textbooks, tucking his notebook and pencil to the side, and letting his heavy, tired eyes slip shut for sleep. He hated the thought of pausing his life even for that short stretch, and his only comfort was knowing his alarm was set to go off five hours later. He was only losing five hours, after all, and then that harsh jangling bell would yank him out of his unconscious haze, and he’d have a whole other glorious nineteen-hour day stretched out ahead of him.
source_text: The days were too short. There was so much he wanted to study. He cut his sleep down to five hours and found that he could get along upon it. He tried four hours and a half, and regretfully came back to five. He could joyfully have spent all his waking hours upon any one of his pursuits. It was with regret that he ceased from writing to study, that he ceased from study to go to the library, that he tore himself away from that chart-room of knowledge or from the magazines in the reading-room that were filled with the secrets of writers who succeeded in selling their wares. It was like severing heart strings, when he was with Ruth, to stand up and go; and he scorched through the dark streets so as to get home to his books at the least possible expense of time. And hardest of all was it to shut up the algebra or physics, put note-book and pencil aside, and close his tired eyes in sleep. He hated the thought of ceasing to live, even for so short a time, and his sole consolation was that the alarm clock was set five hours ahead. He would lose only five hours anyway, and then the jangling bell would jerk him out of unconsciousness and he would have before him another glorious day of nineteen hours.

text: Martin got no reading done that night. He hadn’t glanced at a daily paper all week, and strange as it felt to him, he had no desire to seek one out. The news held no appeal for him whatsoever. He was too tired and burnt out to care about much of anything, though he’d mapped out a small plan: if they wrapped up work at three on Saturday afternoon, he’d ride his bicycle seventy miles out to Oakland. Making the same seventy-mile return trip on Sunday afternoon would leave him far from rested for the second week of his shift, of course. Taking the train would have been far easier, but a round-trip ticket cost two dollars and fifty cents, and he was dead set on saving every penny he could.
source_text: And Martin did no reading that night. He had seen no daily paper all week, and, strangely to him, felt no desire to see one. He was not interested in the news. He was too tired and jaded to be interested in anything, though he planned to leave Saturday afternoon, if they finished at three, and ride on his wheel to Oakland. It was seventy miles, and the same distance back on Sunday afternoon would leave him anything but rested for the second week’s work. It would have been easier to go on the train, but the round trip was two dollars and a half, and he was intent on saving money.

text: He flipped through every magazine that wrote about him, staring so long at the portraits of himself printed in their pages that he could no longer connect those faces to his own sense of who he was. He was the guy who’d lived through every giddy thrill and soft, searing love affair, who’d always been laid-back, quick to extend grace to the messy flaws inherent to being alive. He’d worked as a common sailor in ship forecastles, rambled through far-flung, unrecognizable lands, and led his crew through all the rough, scrappy fights of his younger years. He was the same man who’d walked into the free public library for the first time and felt stunned by the thousands of books lining its shelves, who later learned to navigate every stack and work his way through their contents, who’d stayed up writing until the small hours of the morning, pushed himself relentlessly to improve, and eventually turned out books of his own. But the one thing he knew he absolutely was not was that larger-than-life, insatiable persona the roaring crowd was so dead set on showering with attention and praise.
source_text: He read the magazines about himself, and pored over portraits of himself published therein until he was unable to associate his identity with those portraits. He was the fellow who had lived and thrilled and loved; who had been easy-going and tolerant of the frailties of life; who had served in the forecastle, wandered in strange lands, and led his gang in the old fighting days. He was the fellow who had been stunned at first by the thousands of books in the free library, and who had afterward learned his way among them and mastered them; he was the fellow who had burned the midnight oil and bedded with a spur and written books himself. But the one thing he was not was that colossal appetite that all the mob was bent upon feeding.

text: He tramped at the other man’s heels, shoulders swinging loosely, his legs unconsciously splayed as if the flat floorboards were rolling and dipping with the ocean’s heave and surge. The spacious rooms felt far too cramped for his rolling gait, and he was privately terrified his broad shoulders would bash against doorframes or send the bric-a-brac clattering off the low mantelpiece. He flinched left and right around assorted furnishings, inflating risks that existed entirely in his own head. The gap between a grand piano and a central table stacked high with books was wide enough for half a dozen people to walk side by side, yet he edged through it with skittish dread. His heavy arms hung limp at his sides; he had no idea what to do with those gangly limbs, and when his overwrought gaze spotted his arm brushing perilously close to the volumes stacked on the table, he lurched away like a spooked horse, barely clearing the piano stool. He watched the easy, unselfconscious stride of the man ahead, and for the first time it hit him that his walk was nothing like other men’s. A sharp, fleeting stab of shame pricked him at how uncouth his gait was. Tiny beads of sweat burst across his forehead, and he stopped short, dabbing at his bronzed face with his handkerchief.
source_text: He walked at the other’s heels with a swing to his shoulders, and his legs spread unwittingly, as if the level floors were tilting up and sinking down to the heave and lunge of the sea. The wide rooms seemed too narrow for his rolling gait, and to himself he was in terror lest his broad shoulders should collide with the doorways or sweep the bric-a-brac from the low mantel. He recoiled from side to side between the various objects and multiplied the hazards that in reality lodged only in his mind. Between a grand piano and a centre-table piled high with books was space for a half a dozen to walk abreast, yet he essayed it with trepidation. His heavy arms hung loosely at his sides. He did not know what to do with those arms and hands, and when, to his excited vision, one arm seemed liable to brush against the books on the table, he lurched away like a frightened horse, barely missing the piano stool. He watched the easy walk of the other in front of him, and for the first time realized that his walk was different from that of other men. He experienced a momentary pang of shame that he should walk so uncouthly. The sweat burst through the skin of his forehead in tiny beads, and he paused and mopped his bronzed face with his handkerchief.

text: When the first silver glow of the cresting moon spilled over the sail, gilding the small fishing skiff in milky luminescence, Lila pulled back from his side. Even as she shifted, she sensed him shrinking back too. The urge to pretend nothing had happened was shared between them, wordless. That small, unspoken moment had held a secret, thrumming closeness neither dared name. She perched on the opposite bench, cheeks flaming, as the weight of what they’d done settled over her. She had acted in a way she would never let her older brothers catch her doing, nor Jesse, her childhood playmate who tagged along on all their outings. Why had she let that happen? She’d never behaved so recklessly in all her nineteen years, even though she’d gone on dozens of moonlit sails with other boys from the village before. She’d never once felt the urge to lean that close, to let her shoulder brush theirs like she’d just done with him. Shame washed over her, tangled up with the strange, unknowable stirrings of her own growing womanhood. She snuck a quick look at Kael, who was occupied heeling the boat over onto the opposite tack, and she almost resented him for drawing her into something so unseemly, so mortifying. Of all people, it had to be him. Maybe her mother had been right when she’d said Lila was spending far too much time in his company. She’d never let something like that happen again, she vowed, and she’d keep her distance from him going forward. A silly, frantic thought crossed her mind: the next time they were alone, she’d make an excuse, lie and say she’d been hit by a sudden wave of lightheadedness right before the moon broke over the horizon. Then she remembered how both of them had pulled back of their own accord the second the light hit them, and she knew he would see straight through the falsehood.
source_text: When the first light of the rising moon touched the sail, illuminating the boat with pearly radiance, Ruth moved away from him. And, even as she moved, she felt him move away. The impulse to avoid detection was mutual. The episode was tacitly and secretly intimate. She sat apart from him with burning cheeks, while the full force of it came home to her. She had been guilty of something she would not have her brothers see, nor Olney see. Why had she done it? She had never done anything like it in her life, and yet she had been moonlight-sailing with young men before. She had never desired to do anything like it. She was overcome with shame and with the mystery of her own burgeoning womanhood. She stole a glance at Martin, who was busy putting the boat about on the other tack, and she could have hated him for having made her do an immodest and shameful thing. And he, of all men! Perhaps her mother was right, and she was seeing too much of him. It would never happen again, she resolved, and she would see less of him in the future. She entertained a wild idea of explaining to him the first time they were alone together, of lying to him, of mentioning casually the attack of faintness that had overpowered her just before the moon came up. Then she remembered how they had drawn mutually away before the revealing moon, and she knew he would know it for a lie.

text: Still, none of those wandering thoughts and daydreams stopped him from keeping pace right at Professor Caldwell’s side. As he walked along, engaging with every word both thoughtfully and skeptically, he couldn’t help but notice how seamlessly the professor’s expertise spanned every topic they touched on. For his own part, the discussion kept turning up gaps in his own learning, vast blank swathes of entire subject areas he’d never even dabbled in. Even so, his deep dive into Spencer’s work had given him a clear sketch of the full landscape of all there was to know, he realized. Filling in every empty spot on that sketch was just a matter of putting in the time. When that day came, everyone better watch their step, he smirked to himself—shallow waters ahead for anyone who thought they could outmatch him. For a while he felt like he could kneel right there at the professor’s feet, eager to soak up every last word, reverent of the older man’s smarts. But the longer he listened, the more he picked up on a faint flaw in the professor’s reasoning, a quirk so scattered and hard to pin down he never would have spotted it if it didn’t pop up again and again. The second he pinned that flaw down, he felt himself standing on even ground with the man immediately.
source_text: But such thoughts and visions did not prevent him from following Professor Caldwell closely. And as he followed, comprehendingly and critically, he noted the unbroken field of the other’s knowledge. As for himself, from moment to moment the conversation showed him gaps and open stretches, whole subjects with which he was unfamiliar. Nevertheless, thanks to his Spencer, he saw that he possessed the outlines of the field of knowledge. It was a matter only of time, when he would fill in the outline. Then watch out, he thought—’ware shoal, everybody! He felt like sitting at the feet of the professor, worshipful and absorbent; but, as he listened, he began to discern a weakness in the other’s judgments—a weakness so stray and elusive that he might not have caught it had it not been ever present. And when he did catch it, he leapt to equality at once.

text: “You bow only at the altars of the canonized,” he told her once, mid-argument about the relative merits of Hale and Thorne. “I’ll concede they’re unimpeachable as name-dropped authorities—the two leading critical voices in the whole of the United States, no question. Every high school English teacher across the nation reveres Thorne as the unofficial dean of American literary critique. But every time I pick up his essays, all I see is flawless, pretty phrasing wrapped around absolutely nothing of substance. He’s nothing but an overstuffed walking platitude, to borrow a turn from Gelett Burgess. And Hale’s just as bad, for that matter. Take his collection *Cedar and Alpine Moss*, for example: it’s immaculately crafted. Not a single comma is misplaced, and the tone—oh—so grand, so impossibly elevated. He’s the highest-compensated critic working in the US today, too. Though God help us, he’s not actually a critic in any meaningful sense of the word. They do criticism far better over in England, anyway.”
source_text: “You worship at the shrine of the established,” he told her once, in a discussion they had over Praps and Vanderwater. “I grant that as authorities to quote they are most excellent—the two foremost literary critics in the United States. Every school teacher in the land looks up to Vanderwater as the Dean of American criticism. Yet I read his stuff, and it seems to me the perfection of the felicitous expression of the inane. Why, he is no more than a ponderous bromide, thanks to Gelett Burgess. And Praps is no better. His ‘Hemlock Mosses,’ for instance is beautifully written. Not a comma is out of place; and the tone—ah!—is lofty, so lofty. He is the best-paid critic in the United States. Though, Heaven forbid! he’s not a critic at all. They do criticism better in England.

text: Warm-cheeked, fumbling over his words as he responded to her, he was dizzy with happiness, and the story of Browning and his frail, ailing Elizabeth Barrett danced through his head for the entire duration of their phone call. What had been done once could be done again, and he, Martin Eden, could make it happen, would make it happen, for Ruth Morse. He trundled back to his room, where Spencer’s *Sociology* lay splayed open across his bed. But he couldn’t focus on the text no matter how hard he tried. Love gnawed at him, overriding every shred of his willpower, so that despite all his determination to read, he found himself drifting over to his small, ink-stained desk before he even realized what he was doing. The sonnet he composed that night was the first in a love sequence of fifty sonnets that he finished within two months. He had *Sonnets from the Portuguese* in mind the whole time he wrote, and he was working under the perfect conditions for producing great work: at a fever pitch of his life, in the throes of his own giddy, sweet love-madness.
source_text: He was warm, and bungling of speech, and very happy, as he replied to her, and there was dancing in his mind, throughout the telephone conversation, the memory of Browning and of sickly Elizabeth Barrett. What had been done could be done again, and he, Martin Eden, could do it and would do it for Ruth Morse. He went back to his room and to the volume of Spencer’s “Sociology” lying open on the bed. But he could not read. Love tormented him and overrode his will, so that, despite all determination, he found himself at the little ink-stained table. The sonnet he composed that night was the first of a love-cycle of fifty sonnets which was completed within two months. He had the “Love-sonnets from the Portuguese” in mind as he wrote, and he wrote under the best conditions for great work, at a climacteric of living, in the throes of his own sweet love-madness.

text: So they swapped old memories, letting the sharp, unaged sour wine take the edge off their hunger. For Martin, the future no longer looked so dim and unknowable. Success hovered just within reach, trembling so close he was on the cusp of closing his fingers around it. Then his gaze drifted to the deeply lined, work-worn face of the woman across from him, and he thought of her rich homemade soups and loaves still warm from the oven, a bright, warm swell of gratitude and openhearted generosity rising unbidden in his chest.
source_text: And so they reminiscenced and drowned their hunger in the raw, sour wine. To Martin the future did not seem so dim. Success trembled just before him. He was on the verge of clasping it. Then he studied the deep-lined face of the toil-worn woman before him, remembered her soups and loaves of new baking, and felt spring up in him the warmest gratitude and philanthropy.

text: He tried to put most of these convictions into words for Elara, only to startle her, making it plain that further remaking of his outlook would be required. She carried that all-too-common narrowness of perspective that leads people to assume their own ethnic background, religious beliefs, and partisan values are the only correct and superior ones, and that everyone else spread across the globe lives in far less fortunate circumstances than their own. It was that very same blinkered worldview that led the ancient Hebrew to offer thanks to his deity that he had not been born female, and that spurs contemporary missionaries to carry their substitute for divine truth to the farthest reaches of the planet; and it was that same narrowness that made Elara long to remold this man, who came from far-flung corners of existence, into the exact image of the men who inhabited her own small, familiar corner of the world.
source_text: Much of this he strove to express to Ruth, and shocked her and made it clear that more remodelling was necessary. Hers was that common insularity of mind that makes human creatures believe that their color, creed, and politics are best and right and that other human creatures scattered over the world are less fortunately placed than they. It was the same insularity of mind that made the ancient Jew thank God he was not born a woman, and sent the modern missionary god-substituting to the ends of the earth; and it made Ruth desire to shape this man from other crannies of life into the likeness of the men who lived in her particular cranny of life.

text: “Yep, that’s exactly what did the whole thing in,” Jim burst out. “All you did was dance with her and glance at her that way, and that killed my shot entirely. Sure, you didn’t mean nothing by it, but I was done for good after that. She wouldn’t even look at me again. All she ever did was ask about you. She would’ve locked down as many dates with you as you wanted, if you’d been interested at all.”
source_text: “Yes, an’ that’s just what did it,” Jim cried out. “You just danced with her an’ looked at her, an’ it was all off. Of course you didn’t mean nothin’ by it, but it settled me for keeps. Wouldn’t look at me again. Always askin’ about you. She’d have made fast dates enough with you if you’d wanted to.”

text: He received letters from editors that often read like this: “Roughly a year ago, we made the unfortunate call of rejecting your collection of love poems. We were really impressed by the work when we first reviewed it, but we already had pre-existing publishing commitments locked in that kept us from being able to take it on. If you still have the manuscript on hand and would be kind enough to send it our way, we’ll gladly publish the full collection entirely on whatever terms you set. We’re also prepared to offer you a very favorable deal to release it as a formal printed book.”
source_text: He received letters from editors like the following: “About a year ago we were unfortunate enough to refuse your collection of love-poems. We were greatly impressed by them at the time, but certain arrangements already entered into prevented our taking them. If you still have them, and if you will be kind enough to forward them, we shall be glad to publish the entire collection on your own terms. We are also prepared to make a most advantageous offer for bringing them out in book-form.”

text: He turned the whole recent incident over in his head once she’d left, and barked out bitter laughter more than once as he watched his sister and her fiancé, every member of his own social circle and every one of Ruth’s too, navigating their tiny, constrained lives by tiny, inflexible unwritten rules. They were nothing but pack animals, clustering together and shaping every choice around what the rest of the group thought, too cowed to be real individuals or actually live fully, chained down by the childish, unexamined scripts they’d all let enslave them. He called them up in his mind as a ghostly procession: Bernard Higginbotham arm in arm with Mr. Butler, Hermann von Schmidt pressed shoulder to shoulder with Charley Hapgood. One by one, then in pairs, he judged them against the intellectual and moral standards he’d learned from his books, then dismissed each of them outright. He asked over and over with no answer: Where are the great souls, the truly extraordinary men and women? He found not a single one among the careless, coarse, small-minded figures that paraded through his cramped little room in this vision. The revulsion he felt for them was sharp, the exact sort of disgust Circe must have felt staring at the men she’d turned to swine. When he’d dismissed the last of them and thought he was alone in his head, one last figure slipped in uninvited, unplanned, out of nowhere. Martin stared at him, taking in the stiff-brimmed hat, the boxy square-cut double-breasted coat, the swaggering broad shoulders of the young street hoodlum he had once been.
source_text: He mused over the incident after she had gone, and once or twice broke out into laughter that was bitter as he saw his sister and her betrothed, all the members of his own class and the members of Ruth’s class, directing their narrow little lives by narrow little formulas—herd-creatures, flocking together and patterning their lives by one another’s opinions, failing of being individuals and of really living life because of the childlike formulas by which they were enslaved. He summoned them before him in apparitional procession: Bernard Higginbotham arm in arm with Mr. Butler, Hermann von Schmidt cheek by jowl with Charley Hapgood, and one by one and in pairs he judged them and dismissed them—judged them by the standards of intellect and morality he had learned from the books. Vainly he asked: Where are the great souls, the great men and women? He found them not among the careless, gross, and stupid intelligences that answered the call of vision to his narrow room. He felt a loathing for them such as Circe must have felt for her swine. When he had dismissed the last one and thought himself alone, a late-comer entered, unexpected and unsummoned. Martin watched him and saw the stiff-rim, the square-cut, double-breasted coat and the swaggering shoulders, of the youthful hoodlum who had once been he.

text: He had no clue what the bulk of the night’s conversation had actually been about. It didn’t matter, anyway. Terms like “revolution” were all he needed to find his angle. Just as a paleontologist can reconstruct an entire skeleton from a single shard of fossil bone, he could piece together a full, fiery address from that one word alone. He pulled it off that night, and pulled it off exceptionally well; since Martin had made the biggest splash among the crowd, he stuck every line of that invented speech in Martin’s mouth, casting him as the lead anarchist of the entire event, twisting his reactionary individualist beliefs into the most lurid, red-shirted socialist rant imaginable. The cub reporter was a genuine artist, and he daubed on local color with a broad, bold brush: wild-eyed, long-haired men, jittery, degenerate fringe types, voices shaking with unbridled passion, clenched fists held aloft, all set against a backdrop of shouted curses, raucous yells, and the low, guttural rumble of angry men.
source_text: He did not know what all the talk was about. It was not necessary. Words like _revolution_ gave him his cue. Like a paleontologist, able to reconstruct an entire skeleton from one fossil bone, he was able to reconstruct a whole speech from the one word _revolution_. He did it that night, and he did it well; and since Martin had made the biggest stir, he put it all into his mouth and made him the arch-anarch of the show, transforming his reactionary individualism into the most lurid, red-shirt socialist utterance. The cub reporter was an artist, and it was a large brush with which he laid on the local color—wild-eyed long-haired men, neurasthenic and degenerate types of men, voices shaken with passion, clenched fists raised on high, and all projected against a background of oaths, yells, and the throaty rumbling of angry men.

text: “Oh, no, no, I really do like it. It reads wonderfully smoothly. But I’m afraid it’ll go straight over most of your readers’ heads. It’s certainly over mine, at any rate. It sounds so beautifully crafted, but I can’t make head or tail of half of it. All that specialized scientific jargon is totally lost on me. You’re such a zealot for your work, you know that, dear? What makes perfect, obvious sense to you might be entirely indecipherable to the rest of us.”
source_text: “No, no; I do like it. It reads well. But I am afraid it’s over the heads of your readers. At least it is over mine. It sounds beautiful, but I don’t understand it. Your scientific slang is beyond me. You are an extremist, you know, dear, and what may be intelligible to you may not be intelligible to the rest of us.”

text: Twelve months ago I begged you to give me two years total. I still have a full year left on that timeline. And I swear on my honor, on my very soul, that I’ll have pulled this off before that year runs out. You remember what you told me ages ago, that I had to serve my full apprenticeship to learn the craft of writing? Well, I’ve done that. I crammed every bit of required work in, compressed the whole timeline to move as fast as possible. Knowing you were waiting for me at the end of all this, I never once shirked a task or cut a corner. Do you know I can’t even remember what it feels like to fall asleep peacefully anymore? It feels like a million lifetimes ago that I was able to sleep as long as I wanted, wake up naturally when my body was fully rested, no obligations yanking me out of bed. Now I’m always jolted awake by an alarm clock. No matter if I pass out early or stay up half the night, I set that alarm to the right time first; that, and turning off the lamp, are the last conscious things I do every single night.
source_text: “A year ago I begged for two years. One of those years is yet to run. And I do believe, upon my honor and my soul, that before that year is run I shall have succeeded. You remember what you told me long ago, that I must serve my apprenticeship to writing. Well, I have served it. I have crammed it and telescoped it. With you at the end awaiting me, I have never shirked. Do you know, I have forgotten what it is to fall peacefully asleep. A few million years ago I knew what it was to sleep my fill and to awake naturally from very glut of sleep. I am awakened always now by an alarm clock. If I fall asleep early or late, I set the alarm accordingly; and this, and the putting out of the lamp, are my last conscious actions.

text: “I’d love it if you’d type this up for me,” he said. “You’re a thousand times more skilled at this than any professional stenographer, you know that. And first, I want to offer you a bit of advice.” He pulled a thick, unwieldy manuscript from the outer pocket of his coat. “This is your *The Shame of the Sun*. I didn’t just read it once— I went through it two, even three times, which is the highest compliment I can give any piece of writing. After what you said about *Ephemera*, I don’t have any right to push back on your thoughts there. But I will say this: when *The Shame of the Sun* gets published, it’s going to be a massive hit. It’ll kick up such a heated public controversy that the free advertising alone will be worth thousands of dollars to you.”
source_text: “I wish you’d type it for me,” he said. “You know how a thousand times better than any stenographer. And now I want to give you some advice.” He drew a bulky manuscript from his outside coat pocket. “Here’s your ‘Shame of the Sun.’ I’ve read it not once, but twice and three times—the highest compliment I can pay you. After what you’ve said about ‘Ephemera’ I must be silent. But this I will say: when ‘The Shame of the Sun’ is published, it will make a hit. It will start a controversy that will be worth thousands to you just in advertising.”

text: It hit him just how much time he spent asleep these days, and how badly he craved even more of it. Once, he’d loathed sleep, seen it as a thief snatching precious hours he could have spent fully alive. Four hours of rest out of every twenty-four had felt like four full hours of his life ripped away; he’d begrudged every single second he spent unconscious. Now, though, it was waking life he resented. Nothing about being alive felt good anymore; the very taste of existence on his tongue was flat, no spark to it at all, just a sharp, lingering bitterness. That was the danger he was in. When a life had no drive to keep going, it was already halfway to fading out entirely. Some faint, distant survival instinct tugged at the back of his mind, and he knew he had to get out of there. He scanned the room around him, and the thought of packing up all his things felt so heavy and draining he could barely stomach it. Maybe it made more sense to leave that task for the very end. In the meanwhile, he could go pick up the travel outfit and supplies he’d need first.
source_text: He realized how much he slept, and how much he desired to sleep. Of old, he had hated sleep. It had robbed him of precious moments of living. Four hours of sleep in the twenty-four had meant being robbed of four hours of life. How he had grudged sleep! Now it was life he grudged. Life was not good; its taste in his mouth was without tang, and bitter. This was his peril. Life that did not yearn toward life was in fair way toward ceasing. Some remote instinct for preservation stirred in him, and he knew he must get away. He glanced about the room, and the thought of packing was burdensome. Perhaps it would be better to leave that to the last. In the meantime he might be getting an outfit.

text: Elias worked through the entire day, only remembering at the eleventh hour that he’d promised to attend dinner at the Halewoods’. Thanks to his old friend Jude, his formal black suit was out of hock, so he was once again presentable for formal social gatherings. He cut his trip downtown short enough to duck into the public library and hunt up Thorne’s published works. He checked out *The Rhythm of Existence*, and once he was seated on the streetcar, flipped straight to the essay Owen had referenced about the philosopher Spencer. As Elias read, a hot fury bubbled up in him. His cheeks burned, his jaw clamped tight, and without noticing he fisted his hand, released it, then fisted it again, as if he was getting a firmer hold on some loathsome creature he was wringing the life out of. When he stepped off the streetcar, he tramped down the sidewalk the way any seething man would, and he jabbed the Halewoods’ doorbell so hard that the harsh buzz jolted him back to awareness of how worked up he was. He smoothed his expression by the time the door opened, chuckling softly at his own ridiculous temper. The second he crossed the threshold, though, a heavy, cold gloom settled over him. He dropped straight down from the high he’d ridden all day, buoyed by the wings of his own creative drive. “Middle-class philistines,” “merchant’s hovel”—Jude’s snide labels for the household looped over and over in his head. But so what? he snapped silently to himself. He was marrying Lila, not her whole tedious family.
source_text: He toiled on all day, recollecting, at the last moment, that he was to have dinner at the Morses’. Thanks to Brissenden, his black suit was out of pawn and he was again eligible for dinner parties. Down town he stopped off long enough to run into the library and search for Saleeby’s books. He drew out “The Cycle of Life,” and on the car turned to the essay Norton had mentioned on Spencer. As Martin read, he grew angry. His face flushed, his jaw set, and unconsciously his hand clenched, unclenched, and clenched again as if he were taking fresh grips upon some hateful thing out of which he was squeezing the life. When he left the car, he strode along the sidewalk as a wrathful man will stride, and he rang the Morse bell with such viciousness that it roused him to consciousness of his condition, so that he entered in good nature, smiling with amusement at himself. No sooner, however, was he inside than a great depression descended upon him. He fell from the height where he had been up-borne all day on the wings of inspiration. “Bourgeois,” “trader’s den”—Brissenden’s epithets repeated themselves in his mind. But what of that? he demanded angrily. He was marrying Ruth, not her family.

text: She stumbled off, sobbing loud enough for the whole porch to carry the sound, and a sharp, twisting grief lanced through Elias as he tracked her lumbering frame and awkward, shuffling step. As he stared after her retreating back, the whole Nietzschean framework of beliefs he’d spent three years building up in his head seemed to wobble, teeter on its carefully laid foundation. The exploited slave class, as an abstract academic concept, had always made perfect sense to him, but the theory felt hollow and unsatisfying the second it landed right in his own household. And yet, if there had ever been a person ground under the boot of the powerful, that person was his older sister Marnie. He bared his teeth in a harsh, bitter grin at the ridiculous contradiction. Some paragon of Nietzschean mastery he was, letting his carefully reasoned intellectual positions fall apart the second the first stray twinge of feeling crossed his path—ay, letting them be upended by slave morality itself, because that’s exactly what this soft, pitying ache for his sister was. The truly noble, strong-willed men he admired were meant to rise above pity and soft-hearted compassion. Those impulses had been born in the damp, underground holding pens of the enslaved, nothing more than the raw agony and clammy sweat of hordes of desperate, powerless wretches.
source_text: She went away weeping audibly, and he felt a pang of sorrow shoot through him at sight of her heavy body and uncouth gait. As he watched her go, the Nietzschean edifice seemed to shake and totter. The slave-class in the abstract was all very well, but it was not wholly satisfactory when it was brought home to his own family. And yet, if there was ever a slave trampled by the strong, that slave was his sister Gertrude. He grinned savagely at the paradox. A fine Nietzsche-man he was, to allow his intellectual concepts to be shaken by the first sentiment or emotion that strayed along—ay, to be shaken by the slave-morality itself, for that was what his pity for his sister really was. The true noble men were above pity and compassion. Pity and compassion had been generated in the subterranean barracoons of the slaves and were no more than the agony and sweat of the crowded miserables and weaklings.

text: Good God, Martin seethed internally. You get to ride around in a luxury Pullman car while I’m going hungry over the lousy five bucks you owe me. Hot, roiling anger washed over him all at once. The injustice the *Transcontinental* had dealt him felt impossibly massive, sharpened by every dismal month of unmet hope, hunger, and grinding hardship he’d endured. His current hunger reared up sharp and gnawing, a painful reminder he hadn’t eaten a bite since the previous day, and what he’d eaten then was barely enough to count. For a second he was blind with rage. These people weren’t even common robbers—they were low, cowardly sneak-thieves. They’d cheated him out of his story with empty lies and broken promises. Fine, he’d show them. A fierce, unshakable resolve locked into place in his mind: he wasn’t leaving that office until he got every cent he was owed. He knew perfectly well if he walked away empty-handed, he had no way to get back home to Oakland at all. He forced himself to calm down with great effort, but not before the feral, wolfish set of his face had already left them visibly shaken and unsettled.
source_text: My God! Martin thought; you can travel in a Pullman while I starve for the paltry five dollars you owe me. A wave of anger rushed over him. The wrong done him by the _Transcontinental_ loomed colossal, for strong upon him were all the dreary months of vain yearning, of hunger and privation, and his present hunger awoke and gnawed at him, reminding him that he had eaten nothing since the day before, and little enough then. For the moment he saw red. These creatures were not even robbers. They were sneak-thieves. By lies and broken promises they had tricked him out of his story. Well, he would show them. And a great resolve surged into his will to the effect that he would not leave the office until he got his money. He remembered, if he did not get it, that there was no way for him to go back to Oakland. He controlled himself with an effort, but not before the wolfish expression of his face had awed and perturbed them.

text: “Why didn’t you have the nerve to do this before?” he bit out sharply. “Back when I had no work? When I was going hungry? When I was exactly the man I am now, the same Martin Eden, same person, same artist? That’s the question I’ve been turning over in my head for months now—not just about you, but about everyone I know. I haven’t changed, you know—though the sudden way everyone seems to think I’m worth something makes me check that fact for myself all the time. I carry the same flesh on my bones, the same ten fingers, ten toes. I’m exactly the same person. I haven’t gained any new strengths or virtues. My mind works the exact same way it always did. I haven’t come up with a single new insight about literature or philosophy, not one. I’m worth the exact same as I was back when no one had any use for me. What I can’t figure out is why everyone wants me now. They can’t possibly want me for who I am, because I’m the exact same person they turned away from before. So they must want me for something else, something outside of me, something that isn’t even part of who I am! Want me to tell you what that thing is? It’s the acclaim I’ve gotten. That acclaim isn’t me. It only exists in other people’s heads. And then there’s the money I’ve made and keep making. But that money isn’t me either. It sits in bank accounts, or in the pockets of random strangers. Is that really what this is? You want me now only for that fame and that money?”
source_text: “Why didn’t you dare it before?” he asked harshly. “When I hadn’t a job? When I was starving? When I was just as I am now, as a man, as an artist, the same Martin Eden? That’s the question I’ve been propounding to myself for many a day—not concerning you merely, but concerning everybody. You see I have not changed, though my sudden apparent appreciation in value compels me constantly to reassure myself on that point. I’ve got the same flesh on my bones, the same ten fingers and toes. I am the same. I have not developed any new strength nor virtue. My brain is the same old brain. I haven’t made even one new generalization on literature or philosophy. I am personally of the same value that I was when nobody wanted me. And what is puzzling me is why they want me now. Surely they don’t want me for myself, for myself is the same old self they did not want. Then they must want me for something else, for something that is outside of me, for something that is not I! Shall I tell you what that something is? It is for the recognition I have received. That recognition is not I. It resides in the minds of others. Then again for the money I have earned and am earning. But that money is not I. It resides in banks and in the pockets of Tom, Dick, and Harry. And is it for that, for the recognition and the money, that you now want me?”

text: “Not a single swing of the pick,” he announced, jaw set firm. “Not one. They can fire me if that’s the game they want to play, but if they do, I’m walking out before the words even leave their mouths. No more slogging down in that mine, thank you very much. I’m for hopping boxcars and lazing in the cool shade under the cottonwoods. Keep at it, you lot of slaves! That’s right. Toil and sweat! Toil and sweat! And when you’re cold in the ground, you’ll rot just the same as me, so what’s the point of how you scrape by?—eh? You tell me that—what’s any of it matter, when you look at the big picture?”
source_text: “Not a tap,” he announced. “Not a tap. They can fire me if they want to, but if they do, I’ll quit. No more work in mine, thank you kindly. Me for the freight cars an’ the shade under the trees. Go to it, you slaves! That’s right. Slave an’ sweat! Slave an’ sweat! An’ when you’re dead, you’ll rot the same as me, an’ what’s it matter how you live?—eh? Tell me that—what’s it matter in the long run?”

text: Where their romantic feelings were concerned, Lila and Elias might as well have been small children, every bit as unworldly and clumsy when it came to showing their affection for one another as a pair of gawky grade-schoolers with their first crush. That wide-eyed innocence held even though Lila had completed a full four years of university study, and Elias’s mind was crammed full of rigorous scientific philosophy and the unvarnished, hard-won truths of adult life.
source_text: In truth, they were children together, so far as love was concerned, and they were as naive and immature in the expression of their love as a pair of children, and this despite the fact that she was crammed with a university education and that his head was full of scientific philosophy and the hard facts of life.

text: That was how she came to view Martin Eden as a curiosity, an odd, uncategorizable man, and she chalked up every strange feeling he roused in her to nothing more than a reaction to his novelty and strangeness. It made perfect sense, after all. She’d had the same kind of unplaceable thrills before, when she’d stared at caged wild animals at the menagerie, watched a howling gale tear through the landscape, or shuddered at the jagged, glowing forks of lightning splitting the sky. All those things felt vast, almost unknowable, touched by the scope of the universe, and there was that same cosmic weight to him. When he was near her, she could practically smell the wide open skies and endless, uncharted spaces he’d lived in. The glow of tropical suns lingered on his face, and the thick, springy muscle of his frame hummed with the raw, ancient energy of life itself. He was marked with nicks and scars from that shadowy world of hard men and even harsher acts, a world whose edges started far past the limits of everything she’d ever known. He was unbroken, feral in his own quiet way, and a secret part of her preened at the thought that he softened so easily for her, of all people. She also felt that familiar, universal urge to gentle something that had never been tamed before. It was a totally unconscious urge, too; it never once crossed her mind that what she really wanted was to reshape him like soft clay, to mold him into a copy of her father, who she thought was the finest example of a man alive. And with how little life experience she had, she had no way of recognizing that that vast, unnameable feeling he stirred in her was the most universal force of all: love. It was the same power that pulled men and women to each other across entire continents, drove stags to fight to the death during mating season, and even pulled the raw elements of the universe together with unstoppable force.
source_text: So it was that she looked upon Martin Eden as a novelty, a strange individual, and she identified with novelty and strangeness the effects he produced upon her. It was only natural. In similar ways she had experienced unusual feelings when she looked at wild animals in the menagerie, or when she witnessed a storm of wind, or shuddered at the bright-ribbed lightning. There was something cosmic in such things, and there was something cosmic in him. He came to her breathing of large airs and great spaces. The blaze of tropic suns was in his face, and in his swelling, resilient muscles was the primordial vigor of life. He was marred and scarred by that mysterious world of rough men and rougher deeds, the outposts of which began beyond her horizon. He was untamed, wild, and in secret ways her vanity was touched by the fact that he came so mildly to her hand. Likewise she was stirred by the common impulse to tame the wild thing. It was an unconscious impulse, and farthest from her thoughts that her desire was to re-thumb the clay of him into a likeness of her father’s image, which image she believed to be the finest in the world. Nor was there any way, out of her inexperience, for her to know that the cosmic feel she caught of him was that most cosmic of things, love, which with equal power drew men and women together across the world, compelled stags to kill each other in the rutting season, and drove even the elements irresistibly to unite.

text: Frugality was non-negotiable for him right now. His recent vacation had eaten through nearly every cent he’d earned working at the laundry, and he was so far removed from any consistent payout for his freelance writing that weeks would pass before he could expect the first check for his submitted pieces. Save for the times he visited Ruth, or popped in to check on his sister Gertrude, he lived as a near recluse, cramming at least three days of an average worker’s output into every single one of his own. He slept barely five hours a night; only someone with an ironclad constitution could have chained themselves to nineteen straight hours of grueling labor day after day the way Martin did, and he never let a moment go to waste. Taped to his bathroom mirror were lists of word definitions and proper pronunciations, which he studied while shaving, getting dressed, or combing his hair. Matching lists hung on the wall above his oil stove, memorized in snatches while he cooked meals or washed dishes. Old lists were constantly swapped out for new ones: any odd or half-familiar word he came across in his reading was jotted down immediately, and once he had gathered enough to make a full set, he typed them up and pinned them to the walls or mirror. He even slipped slips of these lists into his pockets, to review during lulls while walking down the street, or while waiting in line at the butcher shop or grocery store to be helped.
source_text: There was need for him to be economical. His vacation had consumed nearly all he had earned in the laundry, and he was so far from his market that weeks must elapse before he could hope for the first returns from his hack-work. Except at such times as he saw Ruth, or dropped in to see his sister Gertude, he lived a recluse, in each day accomplishing at least three days’ labor of ordinary men. He slept a scant five hours, and only one with a constitution of iron could have held himself down, as Martin did, day after day, to nineteen consecutive hours of toil. He never lost a moment. On the looking-glass were lists of definitions and pronunciations; when shaving, or dressing, or combing his hair, he conned these lists over. Similar lists were on the wall over the oil-stove, and they were similarly conned while he was engaged in cooking or in washing the dishes. New lists continually displaced the old ones. Every strange or partly familiar word encountered in his reading was immediately jotted down, and later, when a sufficient number had been accumulated, were typed and pinned to the wall or looking-glass. He even carried them in his pockets, and reviewed them at odd moments on the street, or while waiting in butcher shop or grocery to be served.

text:
He drifted to the mangle almost without thinking, feeding crisp starched shirt cuffs through its rollers. As he worked, he spotted faint printed numbers stamped across the fabric. He assumed it was some new method of labeling laundry, until he leaned in and made out “$3.85” printed across one cuff's edge. All at once it clicked: those were his unpaid bills, swirling around on the mangle's turning drum, the grocer's charge among them. A clever, scheming thought popped into his head. If he tossed these bill-cuffs on the floor, he wouldn't have to pay what he owed. He acted on the impulse instantly, crumpling each cuff viciously before hurling it onto the grimy, unwashed floor below.

The pile swelled higher and higher, and even though every other bill repeated a thousand times over, he only spotted one for two dollars and fifty cents, the exact sum he owed Maria. He took that as a sign Maria would never hound him for the money, and made the generous decision that hers would be the only debt he settled. He began digging through the heap of discarded cuffs to find it. He rummaged frantically, what felt like endless hours passing, when the hotel's portly Dutch manager stepped through the door. His face was bright with fury, and he roared in a booming voice that seemed to shake the whole world: “I’m taking the cost of every last one of these cuffs out of your pay!”

The mound of cuffs swelled into a towering mountain right before his eyes, and Martin knew he was fated to work a thousand years to settle the debt. There was only one solution left, he thought: kill the manager and burn the whole laundry to the ground. But the burly Dutchman cut that plan short, grabbing him by the scruff of the neck and bouncing him up and down like a rag doll. He jostled him over ironing boards, the hot stove, and the line of mangles, through to the washroom, over the wringer and the washing tubs. Martin was bounced so hard his teeth chattered and his skull throbbed, and he could barely believe how inhumanly strong the Dutchman was.
source_text: Quite naturally he found himself at a mangle, feeding starched cuffs. But as he fed he noticed figures printed in the cuffs. It was a new way of marking linen, he thought, until, looking closer, he saw “$3.85” on one of the cuffs. Then it came to him that it was the grocer’s bill, and that these were his bills flying around on the drum of the mangle. A crafty idea came to him. He would throw the bills on the floor and so escape paying them. No sooner thought than done, and he crumpled the cuffs spitefully as he flung them upon an unusually dirty floor. Ever the heap grew, and though each bill was duplicated a thousand times, he found only one for two dollars and a half, which was what he owed Maria. That meant that Maria would not press for payment, and he resolved generously that it would be the only one he would pay; so he began searching through the cast-out heap for hers. He sought it desperately, for ages, and was still searching when the manager of the hotel entered, the fat Dutchman. His face blazed with wrath, and he shouted in stentorian tones that echoed down the universe, “I shall deduct the cost of those cuffs from your wages!” The pile of cuffs grew into a mountain, and Martin knew that he was doomed to toil for a thousand years to pay for them. Well, there was nothing left to do but kill the manager and burn down the laundry. But the big Dutchman frustrated him, seizing him by the nape of the neck and dancing him up and down. He danced him over the ironing tables, the stove, and the mangles, and out into the wash-room and over the wringer and washer. Martin was danced until his teeth rattled and his head ached, and he marvelled that the Dutchman was so strong.

text: At the peak of his widespread popularity, the Meredith-Lowell Company rushed *Overdue* to market, and as a work of fiction, it notched even stronger sales than his earlier hit *The Shame of the Sun*. For weeks on end, he claimed the unprecedented distinction of holding the top two spots on the national bestseller lists simultaneously. The sea story did not just land well with casual fiction readers; those who’d devoured *The Shame of the Sun* with great enthusiasm were equally drawn to it, captivated by the sweeping, masterful command he brought to its narrative. He had first targeted the body of mystical literature, dismantling its premises with exceptional skill; next, he had successfully produced exactly the kind of literature he had laid out in his critique, proving himself to be that rare genius: both a sharp, perceptive critic and a gifted creator.
source_text: “Overdue” was rushed upon the market by the Meredith-Lowell Company in the height of his popularity, and being fiction, in point of sales it made even a bigger strike than “The Shame of the Sun.” Week after week his was the credit of the unprecedented performance of having two books at the head of the list of best-sellers. Not only did the story take with the fiction-readers, but those who read “The Shame of the Sun” with avidity were likewise attracted to the sea-story by the cosmic grasp of mastery with which he had handled it. First he had attacked the literature of mysticism, and had done it exceeding well; and, next, he had successfully supplied the very literature he had exposited, thus proving himself to be that rare genius, a critic and a creator in one.

text: This judgment might have been unduly harsh toward the professors, but to Ruth, it bordered on outright blasphemy. She could not stop herself from measuring the professors—neat, scholarly, dressed in perfectly fitted clothing, speaking in even, well-modulated tones, every breath redolent of culture and refinement—against this nearly uncategorizable young man she had somehow fallen in love with. His clothes never hung right on his frame, his heavy, corded muscles spoke of backbreaking, unforgiving toil, and he grew heated the moment he spoke, trading calm, measured statements for sharp, angry denunciations and fiery, impassioned outbursts for cool, collected self-possession. They at least drew steady, generous salaries and were—yes, she compelled herself to face the unvarnished truth—gentlemen; he, by contrast, could not earn a single cent to his name, and he was nothing like them at all.
source_text: Which was rather severe on the professors, but which to Ruth was blasphemy. She could not help but measure the professors, neat, scholarly, in fitting clothes, speaking in well-modulated voices, breathing of culture and refinement, with this almost indescribable young fellow whom somehow she loved, whose clothes never would fit him, whose heavy muscles told of damning toil, who grew excited when he talked, substituting abuse for calm statement and passionate utterance for cool self-possession. They at least earned good salaries and were—yes, she compelled herself to face it—were gentlemen; while he could not earn a penny, and he was not as they.

text: He fumbled onward through the darkness, unguided, with no one to cheer him on, pushing stubbornly forward even as discouragement closed in on all sides. Even Gertrude had started side-eyeing him lately. At first, she’d brushed off what she saw as his silly little flight of fancy with warm sisterly fondness, letting it slide without comment; now, that same protective care for her brother had her twisted up with worry. It looked to her like that harmless foolishness of his was curdling into something close to madness. Martin picked up on her shift in attitude immediately, and it stung far worse than the constant, loud, nagging disdain Bernard Higginbotham threw his way at every opportunity. Martin held unshakable faith in his own path, but he carried that faith entirely alone; no one else shared it. Not even Ruth believed in him. She’d always pushed for him to throw his energy into his formal studies, and while she’d never openly come out against his writing, she’d never once offered a word of approval for it, either.
source_text: He struggled in the dark, without advice, without encouragement, and in the teeth of discouragement. Even Gertrude was beginning to look askance. At first she had tolerated with sisterly fondness what she conceived to be his foolishness; but now, out of sisterly solicitude, she grew anxious. To her it seemed that his foolishness was becoming a madness. Martin knew this and suffered more keenly from it than from the open and nagging contempt of Bernard Higginbotham. Martin had faith in himself, but he was alone in this faith. Not even Ruth had faith. She had wanted him to devote himself to study, and, though she had not openly disapproved of his writing, she had never approved.

text: He received dozens of dinner invitations, and said yes to a handful of them. People went out of their way to get formally introduced to him just so they could extend a dinner invite. All the while, he kept turning over the same small, swelling puzzle in his mind. When Bernard Higginbotham invited him to dinner, that confusion only deepened. He thought back to the days when he was half-dead with starvation, when no one had ever asked him out to eat. That was when he’d needed those dinners most: he’d grown weak and lightheaded for lack of food, shedding pounds from sheer, unrelenting hunger. It was such a cruel paradox. When he’d been desperate for a hot meal, no one had offered him one. Now, when he could afford to buy a hundred thousand dinners on a whim and had even started losing his appetite for them, invites were being pressed on him from every direction. What was the reason? There was no fairness to it, no credit he had newly earned. He was exactly the same man he’d always been. Every line of the work he’d produced had already been written back in those hungry days. Mr. and Mrs. Morse had dismissed him as a lazy idler and a shirker, and had pushed Ruth to convince him to take a clerk’s position at an office. They’d even seen all that work of his, too: Ruth had handed them manuscript after manuscript, and they’d read each one. It was that exact same work that had gotten his name printed in every newspaper, and it was that very newspaper notoriety, not the work itself, that was leading them to invite him now.
source_text: He had many invitations to dinner, some of which he accepted. Persons got themselves introduced to him in order to invite him to dinner. And he went on puzzling over the little thing that was becoming a great thing. Bernard Higginbotham invited him to dinner. He puzzled the harder. He remembered the days of his desperate starvation when no one invited him to dinner. That was the time he needed dinners, and went weak and faint for lack of them and lost weight from sheer famine. That was the paradox of it. When he wanted dinners, no one gave them to him, and now that he could buy a hundred thousand dinners and was losing his appetite, dinners were thrust upon him right and left. But why? There was no justice in it, no merit on his part. He was no different. All the work he had done was even at that time work performed. Mr. and Mrs. Morse had condemned him for an idler and a shirk and through Ruth had urged that he take a clerk’s position in an office. Furthermore, they had been aware of his work performed. Manuscript after manuscript of his had been turned over to them by Ruth. They had read them. It was the very same work that had put his name in all the papers, and, it was his name being in all the papers that led them to invite him.

text: He found himself surrounded by the familiar old crew—faces he’d known for half his life, dotted with empty spots where old companions used to be, and the occasional unfamiliar newcomer. None of them were stonemasons, but just like they’d done back in their younger years, they showed up to every Sunday riverside picnic for the foot-stomping dancing, the scrappy backlot fights, and the rowdy, unplanned joy of it all. Leo drank round after round with them, and for the first time in months, he felt like a real, flesh-and-blood person instead of a ghost haunting library stacks. He’d been an idiot to ever walk away, he thought; he was certain his total share of happiness would have been far greater if he’d stayed right here, never touching the stuffy leather-bound books or simpering at the pretentious wealthy folks who spent their nights in gilded, high-up drawing rooms. Still, the cheap draft beer didn’t hit the way it used to. It was flat, strangely bitter, nothing like the crisp cold pints he remembered from years back. Elias had ruined him for low-grade bar beer, he concluded, and a cold little niggling thought wondered if all those books had ruined him for the company of the friends he’d grown up with. He shook the thought off, determined not to let that kind of distance take root, and wandered over to the open-air dance pavilion. He ran into Mickey the pipefitter there, accompanied by a lanky, ash-blonde girl who immediately left Mickey’s side to make her way straight over to Leo.
source_text: It was the old crowd in which he found himself—the old crowd, with here and there a gap, and here and there a new face. The fellows were not bricklayers, but, as in the old days, they attended all Sunday picnics for the dancing, and the fighting, and the fun. Martin drank with them, and began to feel really human once more. He was a fool to have ever left them, he thought; and he was very certain that his sum of happiness would have been greater had he remained with them and let alone the books and the people who sat in the high places. Yet the beer seemed not so good as of yore. It didn’t taste as it used to taste. Brissenden had spoiled him for steam beer, he concluded, and wondered if, after all, the books had spoiled him for companionship with these friends of his youth. He resolved that he would not be so spoiled, and he went on to the dancing pavilion. Jimmy, the plumber, he met there, in the company of a tall, blond girl who promptly forsook him for Martin.

text: “And so,” he concluded, in a sharp, rapid summary, “no state built of people with a slave’s mindset can ever endure. The ancient law of development still holds unshaken. In the struggle for existence, as I have demonstrated, the strong and their descendants are disposed to survive, while the weak and their children are crushed and fated to perish. The outcome is that the strong and their line persist, and as long as that struggle remains in place, each generation grows more capable than the last. That is the very essence of development. But you slaves— I will grant that it is a miserable thing to be a slave— but you cling to a fantasy of a society where the law of development is struck down, where no weak, unfit person ever perishes, where every incompetent person gets all the food they crave whenever they want it, and where everyone may marry and have children—the weak just as freely as the strong. What will come of this? The strength and inherent vitality of each generation will no longer rise. On the contrary, it will dwindle. That is the nemesis of your slave philosophy. Your society of slaves, by slaves, and for slaves, will inevitably weaken and crumble just as the lives that form it weaken and are snuffed out.”
source_text: “And so,” he concluded, in a swift résumé, “no state composed of the slave-types can endure. The old law of development still holds. In the struggle for existence, as I have shown, the strong and the progeny of the strong tend to survive, while the weak and the progeny of the weak are crushed and tend to perish. The result is that the strong and the progeny of the strong survive, and, so long as the struggle obtains, the strength of each generation increases. That is development. But you slaves—it is too bad to be slaves, I grant—but you slaves dream of a society where the law of development will be annulled, where no weaklings and inefficients will perish, where every inefficient will have as much as he wants to eat as many times a day as he desires, and where all will marry and have progeny—the weak as well as the strong. What will be the result? No longer will the strength and life-value of each generation increase. On the contrary, it will diminish. There is the Nemesis of your slave philosophy. Your society of slaves—of, by, and for, slaves—must inevitably weaken and go to pieces as the life which composes it weakens and goes to pieces.

text: He shifted until he was upright in the water. He tilted his head to glance up at the quiet, unmoving stars, pushing every last bit of air out of his lungs as he did. Kicking his feet and pulling hard with his hands in sharp, forceful motions, he hoisted his shoulders and half his chest above the waterline to build momentum for the plunge downward. Then he went completely limp, sinking without a twitch like a pale white statue straight into the cold sea. He drew the water deep into his lungs slowly and intentionally, the way someone might breathe in anesthetic before a medical procedure. When his body started to choke and spasm, his arms and legs scrabbled at the water entirely against his will, pushing him back up to the surface until he broke through, face-to-face once more with the bright, unobscured stars.
source_text: He changed his position to a vertical one. He glanced up at the quiet stars, at the same time emptying his lungs of air. With swift, vigorous propulsion of hands and feet, he lifted his shoulders and half his chest out of water. This was to gain impetus for the descent. Then he let himself go and sank without movement, a white statue, into the sea. He breathed in the water deeply, deliberately, after the manner of a man taking an anaesthetic. When he strangled, quite involuntarily his arms and legs clawed the water and drove him up to the surface and into the clear sight of the stars.

text: The unfeeling publishing submission mill churned on as efficiently as it always had. Elias slid the self-addressed stamped envelope inside the manila folder holding his novella, tucked the whole packet shut, and dropped it into the blue corner mailbox. Three or four weeks would pass, then the mail carrier would trudge up his porch steps and press the very same manila folder back into his hands. There was no way living, breathing editors sat on the receiving end of these submissions, he thought. The whole operation was nothing but gears, levers, and lubricated bearings, a fancy contraption run by mindless drones. His discouragement grew so deep sometimes that he wondered if editors were even real people. He’d never gotten so much as a scribbled note to prove any of them existed, and the total lack of discernment in every form rejection he received made it easy to believe editors were nothing but a fairy tale, invented and propped up by front desk interns, layout specialists, and printing press operators.
source_text: The inhuman editorial machine ran smoothly as ever. He folded the stamps in with his manuscript, dropped it into the letter-box, and from three weeks to a month afterward the postman came up the steps and handed him the manuscript. Surely there were no live, warm editors at the other end. It was all wheels and cogs and oil-cups—a clever mechanism operated by automatons. He reached stages of despair wherein he doubted if editors existed at all. He had never received a sign of the existence of one, and from absence of judgment in rejecting all he wrote it seemed plausible that editors were myths, manufactured and maintained by office boys, typesetters, and pressmen.

text: A second check arrived in the same day’s post, sent from a Boston satirical weekly as payment for a set of silly rhymes he’d submitted and gotten accepted half a year prior. It was worth ten dollars. A thought drifted into his head, which he turned over slowly, unrushed. He had no clue what long-term move he’d make next, and felt no impulse to rush into any choice. All that mattered right now was getting by. He also owed small, nagging debts to half the shopkeepers on his block. Wouldn’t it be a smart use of cash to buy stamps for the towering stack of unsold stories and poems tucked under his desk, and send them back out to editors across the country? Maybe one or two would land an acceptance. That would keep him afloat for a few more weeks. He settled on the plan, and after cashing both checks at the branch bank downtown in Portland, he spent the full ten dollars on postage stamps. The idea of trundling back to his cramped, airless garret to fry up his own breakfast made his skin crawl. For the first time, he pushed all thoughts of his debts entirely out of his mind. He knew he could throw together a filling meal at home for no more than fifteen or twenty cents. But instead, he ducked into the Atlas Diner and ordered a spread that came out to two dollars even. He left the waiter a quarter tip, then dropped fifty cents on a pack of Turkish cigarettes. It was the first time he’d touched tobacco since Elara had begged him to quit months earlier. He couldn’t think of a single reason to hold back now, anyway, and he craved the burn of smoke in his lungs. What did the cost even matter? For five cents, he could have bought a pouch of rolling tobacco and thin brown papers, enough to make forty smokes of his own—but who cared? Money held no value for him anymore beyond what it could get him in the exact present moment. He had no map, no anchor, no harbor he was steering toward; drifting required the least effort at existing, and existing was the one thing that cut deepest.
source_text: Another check was in the same mail, sent from a New York weekly in payment for some humorous verse which had been accepted months before. It was for ten dollars. An idea came to him, which he calmly considered. He did not know what he was going to do, and he felt in no hurry to do anything. In the meantime he must live. Also he owed numerous debts. Would it not be a paying investment to put stamps on the huge pile of manuscripts under the table and start them on their travels again? One or two of them might be accepted. That would help him to live. He decided on the investment, and, after he had cashed the checks at the bank down in Oakland, he bought ten dollars’ worth of postage stamps. The thought of going home to cook breakfast in his stuffy little room was repulsive to him. For the first time he refused to consider his debts. He knew that in his room he could manufacture a substantial breakfast at a cost of from fifteen to twenty cents. But, instead, he went into the Forum Café and ordered a breakfast that cost two dollars. He tipped the waiter a quarter, and spent fifty cents for a package of Egyptian cigarettes. It was the first time he had smoked since Ruth had asked him to stop. But he could see now no reason why he should not, and besides, he wanted to smoke. And what did the money matter? For five cents he could have bought a package of Durham and brown papers and rolled forty cigarettes—but what of it? Money had no meaning to him now except what it would immediately buy. He was chartless and rudderless, and he had no port to make, while drifting involved the least living, and it was living that hurt.

text:
As he spoke, the girl stared at him, her eyes wide with unanticipated shock. The raw, vivid heat coming off him seeped into her bones, and she found herself wondering if she’d been chilled, unknowing, her entire life up to this point. She ached to lean into this searing, vibrant man, who felt like an active volcano pouring out unfiltered strength, rugged vitality, and unshakable good health. The pull to tip closer to him felt almost involuntary, and she had to brace herself to fight it. But at the exact same time, a conflicting urge flared up to recoil from him entirely.

His scraped, raw hands turned her stomach—caked with years of hard labor, the grit of hard living worked so deep into the skin it would never wash out—just as the raw red collar chafing around his neck and the thick, bulging lines of his muscles did. His unpolished edge scared her; every rough turn of phrase felt like a slight against her sensitive ears, every jagged, unrefined chapter of his past felt like a personal offense to everything she held sacred in her core.
