Still, that magnetic pull kept yanking her back toward him, again and again, until she decided he must be some kind of demonic force to hold that much sway over her without even trying. Every belief she’d held as unshakable her whole life was wobbling on its foundation. His stories of wild romance and reckless adventure were slamming full force against all the stuffy, unspoken rules she’d always lived by.

Next to his easy willingness to run headlong into danger and his constant, easy laugh, life stopped feeling like a grind of constant discipline and self-denial, and started feeling like a plaything to mess with, flip upside down, live recklessly for every thrill, then toss aside without a second thought. “So why not play?” the voice echoed loud in her head. “Lean into him, if that’s what you want—wrap both your hands right around his neck!” She wanted to gasp aloud at how reckless that impulse was, and she futilely clung to her own sense of polish, her upbringing and good breeding, weighing every part of who she was against every rough, unrefined part he lacked. She darted a quick look around the room, and saw everyone else hanging on his every word, completely transfixed; she would have felt completely unmoored if she hadn’t caught the look of horror on her mother’s face—fascinated horror, sure, but horror all the same.

This man who’d crawled out of some rough, unknowable corner of the world was dangerous, wrong. Her mother saw that, and her mother was always right. She’d trust her mom’s call here, just like she’d trusted every other call she’d ever made her whole life. The heat rolling off him didn’t feel warm anymore, and the sharp, thrumming fear he’d sparked in her softened to something dull and distant.
source_text: And while he talked, the girl looked at him with startled eyes. His fire warmed her. She wondered if she had been cold all her days. She wanted to lean toward this burning, blazing man that was like a volcano spouting forth strength, robustness, and health. She felt that she must lean toward him, and resisted by an effort. Then, too, there was the counter impulse to shrink away from him. She was repelled by those lacerated hands, grimed by toil so that the very dirt of life was ingrained in the flesh itself, by that red chafe of the collar and those bulging muscles. His roughness frightened her; each roughness of speech was an insult to her ear, each rough phase of his life an insult to her soul. And ever and again would come the draw of him, till she thought he must be evil to have such power over her. All that was most firmly established in her mind was rocking. His romance and adventure were battering at the conventions. Before his facile perils and ready laugh, life was no longer an affair of serious effort and restraint, but a toy, to be played with and turned topsy-turvy, carelessly to be lived and pleasured in, and carelessly to be flung aside. “Therefore, play!” was the cry that rang through her. “Lean toward him, if so you will, and place your two hands upon his neck!” She wanted to cry out at the recklessness of the thought, and in vain she appraised her own cleanness and culture and balanced all that she was against what he was not. She glanced about her and saw the others gazing at him with rapt attention; and she would have despaired had not she seen horror in her mother’s eyes—fascinated horror, it was true, but none the less horror. This man from outer darkness was evil. Her mother saw it, and her mother was right. She would trust her mother’s judgment in this as she had always trusted it in all things. The fire of him was no longer warm, and the fear of him was no longer poignant.

text: “Real fine example he’s setting for the children,” Mr. Higginbotham snorted abruptly, piercing the quiet his wife had let fall, a silence that chafed at him. Now and then he almost wished she would push back against him a little more. “If he pulls that stunt again, he’s out on his ear. You hear me? I won’t stand for his nonsense—debotchin’ innocent children with his drunken antics.” Mr. Higginbotham was rather pleased with the word, a new addition to his vocabulary he’d recently gleaned from a newspaper column. “That’s exactly what it is, debotchin’—ain’t no other name for it.”
source_text: “Settin’ a fine example to the children,” Mr. Higginbotham snorted, suddenly, in the silence for which his wife was responsible and which he resented. Sometimes he almost wished she would oppose him more. “If he does it again, he’s got to get out. Understand! I won’t put up with his shinanigan—debotchin’ innocent children with his boozing.” Mr. Higginbotham liked the word, which was a new one in his vocabulary, recently gleaned from a newspaper column. “That’s what it is, debotchin’—there ain’t no other name for it.”

text: “And then there’s another bloke—Kael, an Australian, a statistician and walking almanac of all things sports. Ask him the maize yield of Argentina for 1911, or British cotton yarn exports to Japan in 1897, what weight Joe Gans fought Battling Jim Johnson at, or who took the U.S. middleweight champion title in 1872, and you’ll get the right answer with the automatic quickness of a gumball machine spitting out a treat. Then there’s Javi, a stonemason who has opinions on every subject under the sun, and a wicked good chess player to boot; and another guy, Moe, a baker, fiery committed socialist and staunch union man. Oh, by the way, you remember that restaurant workers’ strike a few years back? Elias was the fellow who built that union from scratch and kicked off the strike, mapped every last step of it out beforehand, right here in Rainer’s rooms. He did it just for the rush of pulling it off, but was too lazy to stick with the union once it was up and running. He could have gone far in the labor movement if he’d ever wanted to put in the effort. There’s no end to what that man could do—if he weren’t so insurmountably, unshakably lazy.”
source_text: “And there’s another fellow—Parry—an Australian, a statistician and a sporting encyclopaedia. Ask him the grain output of Paraguay for 1903, or the English importation of sheetings into China for 1890, or at what weight Jimmy Britt fought Battling Nelson, or who was welter-weight champion of the United States in ’68, and you’ll get the correct answer with the automatic celerity of a slot-machine. And there’s Andy, a stone-mason, has ideas on everything, a good chess-player; and another fellow, Harry, a baker, red hot socialist and strong union man. By the way, you remember Cooks’ and Waiters’ strike—Hamilton was the chap who organized that union and precipitated the strike—planned it all out in advance, right here in Kreis’s rooms. Did it just for the fun of it, but was too lazy to stay by the union. Yet he could have risen high if he wanted to. There’s no end to the possibilities in that man—if he weren’t so insuperably lazy.”

text: Elias’s part in the weekend plan was already settled for him, even as he, egged on by Finn and Jasper, was plotting a little splurge of his own. The two boys had planned a Sunday morning bicycle ride out into the foothills, a plan that had not piqued Elias’s interest at all until he heard Lila would be joining them, and that she rode a bike herself. Elias had never learned to ride, nor did he own a bicycle, but if Lila rode, he decided, there was no question he would learn too. He said his goodnights to the group, then detoured to a bike shop on his walk home and dropped forty dollars on a new model. That sum was more than he earned in a full month of backbreaking work, and it cut his available cash sharply, but when he tallied the hundred dollars coming to him from the _Herald_ alongside the minimum four hundred twenty he would get from _Young Folks' Quarterly_, he was relieved to whittle down the awkward surplus of money that had been weighing on him as of late. He didn’t even care that he tore and stained his good suit terribly while fumbling to learn to ride the bike the rest of the way home. That same night, he called his tailor from the pay phone at Mr. Hollister’s general store and put in an order for a new suit. After that, he hauled the bicycle up the narrow staircase bolted like a fire escape to the back of his apartment building, and once he shifted his bed a few inches away from the wall, he found his tiny room had just enough floor space left for both him and his new purchase.
source_text: And so Martin’s rôle was arranged for him, while he, led on by Arthur and Norman, was meditating an extravagance. They were going out for a ride into the hills Sunday morning on their wheels, which did not interest Martin until he learned that Ruth, too, rode a wheel and was going along. He did not ride, nor own a wheel, but if Ruth rode, it was up to him to begin, was his decision; and when he said good night, he stopped in at a cyclery on his way home and spent forty dollars for a wheel. It was more than a month’s hard-earned wages, and it reduced his stock of money amazingly; but when he added the hundred dollars he was to receive from the _Examiner_ to the four hundred and twenty dollars that was the least _The Youth’s Companion_ could pay him, he felt that he had reduced the perplexity the unwonted amount of money had caused him. Nor did he mind, in the course of learning to ride the wheel home, the fact that he ruined his suit of clothes. He caught the tailor by telephone that night from Mr. Higginbotham’s store and ordered another suit. Then he carried the wheel up the narrow stairway that clung like a fire-escape to the rear wall of the building, and when he had moved his bed out from the wall, found there was just space enough in the small room for himself and the wheel.

text: Noon drifted past, then the first stretch of the afternoon. He’d forgotten entirely to stop for a meal, combing the stacks for books on etiquette; on top of his anxieties about his career, one simple, very specific problem was eating at him. He’d framed it clearly to himself: When you meet a young woman and she tells you to call on her, how soon is acceptable to actually do so? But even when he located the right shelf, he hunted for the answer in vain. He was stunned by the sheer, massive structure of formal etiquette, and got lost in the tangled maze of rules around visiting card conduct for people in polite society. He gave up his search entirely. He hadn’t found the answer he’d come looking for, though he had learned that being properly polite would take up every minute of a person’s time, and that you’d have to live through a whole preliminary phase of life just dedicated to learning how to be polite in the first place.
source_text: Noon came, and afternoon. He forgot to eat, and sought on for the books on etiquette; for, in addition to career, his mind was vexed by a simple and very concrete problem: _When you meet a young lady and she asks you to call, how soon can you call_? was the way he worded it to himself. But when he found the right shelf, he sought vainly for the answer. He was appalled at the vast edifice of etiquette, and lost himself in the mazes of visiting-card conduct between persons in polite society. He abandoned his search. He had not found what he wanted, though he had found that it would take all of a man’s time to be polite, and that he would have to live a preliminary life in which to learn how to be polite.

text: It was the rejection slips that drove home just how horribly, unfeelingly mechanical the entire submission process was. These slips were all printed from generic, unchanging templates, and he had received hundreds of them—for each of his earliest manuscripts, as many as a dozen or more rejections apiece. If even a single one of all those rejections had come with one handwritten line, one small personal note, it would have cheered him deeply. But not a single editor had ever extended that small proof that a real person had engaged with his work. He could only conclude there were no warm, living human beings on the other end of the process, only mere cogs, well-oiled and turning flawlessly as part of the impersonal machine.
source_text: It was the rejection slips that completed the horrible machinelikeness of the process. These slips were printed in stereotyped forms and he had received hundreds of them—as many as a dozen or more on each of his earlier manuscripts. If he had received one line, one personal line, along with one rejection of all his rejections, he would have been cheered. But not one editor had given that proof of existence. And he could conclude only that there were no warm human men at the other end, only mere cogs, well oiled and running beautifully in the machine.

text: “You can’t put together a coherent refutation of Berkeley, even if you’re convinced you’ve completely dismantled Kant’s entire body of work. All the same, you automatically take Berkeley for granted as wrong when you insist science definitively proves God doesn’t exist, or, just as relevantly, that matter has an objective, independent existence. You know full well I only conceded the reality of matter in the first place to make my arguments accessible to your frame of reference. Stick to empirical, hard science if that’s the lane you want to stay in, but ontology has zero place in positive scientific inquiry, so quit trying to drag it in. Spencer’s agnostic position is entirely correct, but if Spencer—”
source_text: “You cannot answer Berkeley, even if you have annihilated Kant, and yet, perforce, you assume that Berkeley is wrong when you affirm that science proves the non-existence of God, or, as much to the point, the existence of matter.—You know I granted the reality of matter only in order to make myself intelligible to your understanding. Be positive scientists, if you please; but ontology has no place in positive science, so leave it alone. Spencer is right in his agnosticism, but if Spencer—”

text: Martin had no end of troubles to contend with. The fallout from the cub reporter’s stunt spread far wider than Martin had ever anticipated. The Portuguese grocer cut off his line of credit entirely, while the greengrocer, a proudly outspoken American, had labeled him a traitor to his country and refused to do any further business with him. He carried his patriotism so far that he wiped Martin’s outstanding account balance entirely and forbade him from ever attempting to pay what he owed. Chatter across the neighborhood reflected that exact same sentiment, and fury against Martin was running hot. No one wanted anything to do with a socialist turncoat. Poor Maria was confused and frightened by the whole uproar, but she stayed firmly loyal to him. The neighborhood children had long gotten over the awe they’d felt when that grand carriage had once visited Martin’s home, and now they jeered insults like “hobo” and “bum” at him from a safe distance. The Silva crew, though, defended him staunchly, getting into more than one full-on pitched brawl to protect his honor. Black eyes and bloody noses became such a regular, everyday occurrence that they barely drew notice, and they only added to Maria’s growing pile of worries and burdens.
source_text: For Martin’s troubles were many. The aftermath of the cub reporter’s deed was even wider than Martin had anticipated. The Portuguese grocer refused him further credit, while the greengrocer, who was an American and proud of it, had called him a traitor to his country and refused further dealings with him—carrying his patriotism to such a degree that he cancelled Martin’s account and forbade him ever to attempt to pay it. The talk in the neighborhood reflected the same feeling, and indignation against Martin ran high. No one would have anything to do with a socialist traitor. Poor Maria was dubious and frightened, but she remained loyal. The children of the neighborhood recovered from the awe of the grand carriage which once had visited Martin, and from safe distances they called him “hobo” and “bum.” The Silva tribe, however, stanchly defended him, fighting more than one pitched battle for his honor, and black eyes and bloody noses became quite the order of the day and added to Maria’s perplexities and troubles.

text: “It was nothing but an accident,” he said, pressing his palm to his cheek. “One night, dead calm, but the swells were rolling heavy as anything. First the main boom lift snapped clean through, then the tackle right after it. That lift was braided wire, so it was thrashing every which way like a riled-up snake. The whole watch was diving to try and snag it, I rushed in to help and got clobbered.”
source_text: “It was just an accident,” he said, putting his hand to his cheek. “One night, in a calm, with a heavy sea running, the main-boom-lift carried away, an’ next the tackle. The lift was wire, an’ it was threshin’ around like a snake. The whole watch was tryin’ to grab it, an’ I rushed in an’ got swatted.”

text: Elias’s long-held personal creed rose unbidden, melting the sharp edge of his fury, and he set the landline handset back in its cradle with a low, drawn-out whistle of bewildered, disbelieving amusement. The giddy spark of that amusement faded fast, replaced by a heavy, sinking lurch, and a crushing weight of loneliness settled deep in his bones. No one saw him for who he really was, no one appeared to have any place for him in their lives, save for Jude, and Jude had vanished off the face of the earth, with no one but God aware of his whereabouts.
source_text: Martin’s philosophy asserted itself, dissipating his anger, and he hung up the receiver with a long whistle of incredulous amusement. But after the amusement came the reaction, and he was oppressed by his loneliness. Nobody understood him, nobody seemed to have any use for him, except Brissenden, and Brissenden had disappeared, God alone knew where.

text: Martin had climbed rung after rung of intellectual elevation, and now he stood at a height he had never reached before. Every long-concealed truth was unfurling its hidden core to him. He was giddy, drunk on the sheer force of his new understanding. When he slept at night, he wandered beside gods in vast, thrumming nightmares; waking by day, he moved through the world like a sleepwalker, his gaze distant and unmoored, fixed on the startling new version of reality he had only just uncovered. Sitting at the dinner table, he did not register the chatter of small, mean concerns around him, his restless mind racing to map the lines of cause and effect woven into every object in his line of sight. Looking at the cut of meat on his plate, he could see the bright glow of the sun, tracing its energy backward through every step of transformation to its source a hundred million miles out in space, or following it forward to the flexing muscles in his arm that let him slice the meat, to the brain that sent the command for those muscles to move—until, with his mind’s eye, he could see that very same sun glowing inside his own skull. He was so lost in this flood of revelation that he did not catch Jim’s quiet, sneering whisper of “Bughouse,” nor spot the worried furrow of his sister’s brow, nor catch Bernard Higginbotham twirling a finger next to his temple, wordlessly insinuating that his brother-in-law’s wits had come unmoored.
source_text: Martin had ascended from pitch to pitch of intellectual living, and here he was at a higher pitch than ever. All the hidden things were laying their secrets bare. He was drunken with comprehension. At night, asleep, he lived with the gods in colossal nightmare; and awake, in the day, he went around like a somnambulist, with absent stare, gazing upon the world he had just discovered. At table he failed to hear the conversation about petty and ignoble things, his eager mind seeking out and following cause and effect in everything before him. In the meat on the platter he saw the shining sun and traced its energy back through all its transformations to its source a hundred million miles away, or traced its energy ahead to the moving muscles in his arms that enabled him to cut the meat, and to the brain wherewith he willed the muscles to move to cut the meat, until, with inward gaze, he saw the same sun shining in his brain. He was entranced by illumination, and did not hear the “Bughouse,” whispered by Jim, nor see the anxiety on his sister’s face, nor notice the rotary motion of Bernard Higginbotham’s finger, whereby he imparted the suggestion of wheels revolving in his brother-in-law’s head.

text: You might have mistaken his mouth for a cherub’s, if not for the way those full, velvety lips pressed hard against his teeth whenever he was under strain. Sometimes they drew so taut, the line of his mouth turned severe, unyielding, almost austere. They were the lips of both a brawler and a romantic: they could savor every honeyed drop of life with unapologetic glee, and just as easily set that sweetness aside to bend life to his will. His chin and jaw, solid and edged with the faint, sharp promise of square-jawed tenacity, backed up that quiet authority his lips carried. His hard-won grit balanced out his soft, hungry appetite for pleasure, grounding him so he craved only bright, unspoiled beauty and thrumming, untainted sensation. Beneath those lips sat teeth that had never so much as seen the inside of a dentist’s office, let alone needed work. He studied them in the smudged mirror propped on his windowsill: white, sturdy, perfectly even, he noted with a flicker of pride. But that pride faded fast, curling into a sharp, flustered worry. Some half-forgotten memory, tucked in the crannies of his mind, surfaced: the folk who lived in the fine neighborhoods on the hill, the people from the world Elara came from, brushed their teeth every single day. Elara must do the same, surely. What would she think if she found out he had never once brushed his teeth in his whole life? He made up his mind right then: he’d buy a toothbrush, build the habit, start first thing tomorrow. He knew empty grand gestures wouldn’t be enough to earn a place at her side. He’d have to remake every small part of himself, even down to tooth-brushing and the stiff collars he’d always hated, even if those starched bands felt like little chains around his throat, robbing him of every scrap of the freedom he’d always held dear.
source_text: His might have been a cherub’s mouth, had not the full, sensuous lips a trick, under stress, of drawing firmly across the teeth. At times, so tightly did they draw, the mouth became stern and harsh, even ascetic. They were the lips of a fighter and of a lover. They could taste the sweetness of life with relish, and they could put the sweetness aside and command life. The chin and jaw, strong and just hinting of square aggressiveness, helped the lips to command life. Strength balanced sensuousness and had upon it a tonic effect, compelling him to love beauty that was healthy and making him vibrate to sensations that were wholesome. And between the lips were teeth that had never known nor needed the dentist’s care. They were white and strong and regular, he decided, as he looked at them. But as he looked, he began to be troubled. Somewhere, stored away in the recesses of his mind and vaguely remembered, was the impression that there were people who washed their teeth every day. They were the people from up above—people in her class. She must wash her teeth every day, too. What would she think if she learned that he had never washed his teeth in all the days of his life? He resolved to get a tooth-brush and form the habit. He would begin at once, to-morrow. It was not by mere achievement that he could hope to win to her. He must make a personal reform in all things, even to tooth-washing and neck-gear, though a starched collar affected him as a renunciation of freedom.

text: “We’re way behind on orders,” he grunted. “Have to put in hours after supper.” Once the meal was cleared away, they labored straight through to ten o’clock beneath the searing glow of overhead electric bulbs, not pausing until the final piece of underclothing had been pressed crisp and tucked away in the distribution room. It was a scorching California night, and though every window had been flung wide to the dark, the small workroom, heated by the red-glowing ironing stove, was nothing short of a furnace. Leo and Rafe, stripped down to thin cotton undershirts, bare-armed, dripped with sweat and panted for even the smallest wisp of cool air.
source_text: “Way behind,” he said. “Got to work after supper.” And after supper they worked until ten o’clock, under the blazing electric lights, until the last piece of under-clothing was ironed and folded away in the distributing room. It was a hot California night, and though the windows were thrown wide, the room, with its red-hot ironing-stove, was a furnace. Martin and Joe, down to undershirts, bare armed, sweated and panted for air.

text: But *The Midland Journal*, a Cleveland-based monthly, dealt Elias the harshest blow of all his publishing misfortunes. He had hoarded his *Shore Verse* collection for months, refusing to send it out until crippling hunger left him no other choice. Rejected by twelve different publications first, the manuscript finally wound up on the slush pile at *The Midland Journal* offices. There were thirty poems total, and the editors offered him one dollar per piece for the right to run them. The first month, four were printed, and a four-dollar check arrived promptly in his mailbox—but when he flipped open the issue, he froze in horror at the butchery he found. Some titles had been altered without warning: “Endnote,” for example, had been retitled “The Last Line,” and “Hymn of the Open Shoal” changed to “Hymn of the Sandbar.” In one particularly galling case, a completely unrelated, ill-fitting title had been swapped in entirely: in place of his original “Glimmer of the Jellyfish Drift,” the editors had printed “The Road Back Home.” The vandalism done to the poems themselves was even more stomach-churning. Elias groaned, cold sweat beading on his forehead as he raked his fingers roughly through his curls. Whole phrases, lines, even stanzas had been cut out, rearranged, or twisted beyond recognition in ways that made no sense at all. Occasionally, lines and stanzas he had never written were inserted in place of his own work. He could not fathom that a reasonable, professional editor would stoop to such mistreatment, and his most convincing explanation was that the poems must have been mangled by the office intern or the typing pool clerk. Elias sat down to write a letter the same hour, begging the editor to halt any further publication of the verses and mail the full manuscript back to him at once.
source_text: But _The Globe_, a Chicago magazine, gave Martin the most cruel treatment of all. He had refrained from offering his “Sea Lyrics” for publication, until driven to it by starvation. After having been rejected by a dozen magazines, they had come to rest in _The Globe_ office. There were thirty poems in the collection, and he was to receive a dollar apiece for them. The first month four were published, and he promptly received a check for four dollars; but when he looked over the magazine, he was appalled at the slaughter. In some cases the titles had been altered: “Finis,” for instance, being changed to “The Finish,” and “The Song of the Outer Reef” to “The Song of the Coral Reef.” In one case, an absolutely different title, a misappropriate title, was substituted. In place of his own, “Medusa Lights,” the editor had printed, “The Backward Track.” But the slaughter in the body of the poems was terrifying. Martin groaned and sweated and thrust his hands through his hair. Phrases, lines, and stanzas were cut out, interchanged, or juggled about in the most incomprehensible manner. Sometimes lines and stanzas not his own were substituted for his. He could not believe that a sane editor could be guilty of such maltreatment, and his favorite hypothesis was that his poems must have been doctored by the office boy or the stenographer. Martin wrote immediately, begging the editor to cease publishing the lyrics and to return them to him.

text: “Let her spend as much time with that boy as she pleases,” was Mr. Hale’s blunt counsel. “The more she learns who he really is, the faster that silly infatuation will fizzle, I’d put good money on it. And make sure she’s got plenty of points of comparison, too. Go out of your way to host young folks at the house—girls her age, and all manner of young men: sharp, driven men who’ve already built something of themselves, or are well on their way to doing so, men of her own social station, proper gentlemen. She’ll hold him up next to them, and they’ll lay bare exactly how unfit he is. He’s barely twenty-one, after all, still little more than a boy himself. Lila’s hardly more than a child either. This is nothing but puppy love on both their parts, and they’ll grow out of it soon enough.”
source_text: “Let her see all she wants of him,” was Mr. Morse’s advice. “The more she knows him, the less she’ll love him, I wager. And give her plenty of contrast. Make a point of having young people at the house. Young women and young men, all sorts of young men, clever men, men who have done something or who are doing things, men of her own class, gentlemen. She can gauge him by them. They will show him up for what he is. And after all, he is a mere boy of twenty-one. Ruth is no more than a child. It is calf love with the pair of them, and they will grow out of it.”

text: In this quiet, unforeseen way, Lila found herself both a property holder and the sole proprietor of a small creamery, with two hired hands tending to the heavy labor and a bank balance that swelled month after month, even as every last one of her children owned sturdy shoes and attended the local grade school without interruption. Most people never cross paths with the fairy-tale princes they daydream of as girls; but Lila, who toiled from dawn to dusk and had no patience for silly, fanciful wishes, received her own quiet stroke of luck in the form of a former dry-cleaner who’d won her heart years prior.
source_text: And so it was that Maria became a landlord and the sole owner of a dairy, with two hired men to do the work for her and a bank account that steadily increased despite the fact that her whole brood wore shoes and went to school. Few persons ever meet the fairy princes they dream about; but Maria, who worked hard and whose head was hard, never dreaming about fairy princes, entertained hers in the guise of an ex-laundryman.

text: Would he ever! He fought to tamp down the giddy eagerness threatening to leak into his voice. It felt almost unreal. Up until that point, he had only ever encountered her inside the warm, curtained parlor of her family’s home, and he’d never mustered the courage to invite her anywhere at all. Still holding the telephone receiver to his ear, mid-conversation with her, an unrelated, overpowering urge to lay down his life for her washed over him. Vivid flashes of heroic, selfless sacrifice bloomed and faded fast in his reeling, spinning mind. He loved her so fiercely, so achingly, so hopelessly out of his depth. In that split second of wild, unmooring joy that she’d said yes, that she’d go to the downtown public lecture with him—with him, Elias Voss—she rose so far above every ordinary part of his world that the only thing that felt remotely equal to the feeling was giving his life for her. It was the only fitting way he could express the huge, reverent emotion swelling so big it felt like it might split his ribs. That sublime, self-abnegating instinct that touches every person swept up in true love hit him right there, propped against the wall by the telephone, in a whirlwind of fire and soft, searing glory; to die for her, he thought, would mean he had lived and loved fully, without regret. He was only twenty-one, and he had never been in love before.
source_text: Would he! He fought to suppress the eagerness in his voice. It was amazing. He had always seen her in her own house. And he had never dared to ask her to go anywhere with him. Quite irrelevantly, still at the telephone and talking with her, he felt an overpowering desire to die for her, and visions of heroic sacrifice shaped and dissolved in his whirling brain. He loved her so much, so terribly, so hopelessly. In that moment of mad happiness that she should go out with him, go to a lecture with him—with him, Martin Eden—she soared so far above him that there seemed nothing else for him to do than die for her. It was the only fit way in which he could express the tremendous and lofty emotion he felt for her. It was the sublime abnegation of true love that comes to all lovers, and it came to him there, at the telephone, in a whirlwind of fire and glory; and to die for her, he felt, was to have lived and loved well. And he was only twenty-one, and he had never been in love before.

text: Here’s the thing, though: they hit exactly the note the public wants, and they deliver it with such polished flair, performative virtue, and smug contentment. Reading their criticism feels exactly like a stuffy, unchanging British Sunday. They’re the mainstream’s go-to mouthpieces, locked in a mutual backscratching loop with every tenured English professor out there—each backs the other up, no questions asked. Not a single one of them has an original thought bouncing around their skulls. They only recognize what’s already been sanctioned by the status quo; in fact, they are the status quo. Their minds are so pliable, the dictates of the established order stamp themselves onto them as effortlessly as a brewery’s logo is pressed into the side of a beer bottle. Their whole function is to sink their hooks into every young student attending university, drive out even the faintest glimmer of originality that might happen to take root there, and imprint them with the official, pre-approved stamp of the status quo.
source_text: “But the point is, they sound the popular note, and they sound it so beautifully and morally and contentedly. Their reviews remind me of a British Sunday. They are the popular mouthpieces. They back up your professors of English, and your professors of English back them up. And there isn’t an original idea in any of their skulls. They know only the established,—in fact, they are the established. They are weak minded, and the established impresses itself upon them as easily as the name of the brewery is impressed on a beer bottle. And their function is to catch all the young fellows attending the university, to drive out of their minds any glimmering originality that may chance to be there, and to put upon them the stamp of the established.”

text: Even with all these random misfortunes piling up, the memory of that $40 check from *The White Mouse* kept him going, even as he was forced to take on more and more uncreative, low-paying hack writing gigs to get by. He figured out that agricultural weeklies and trade journals were a reliable, steady source of work, but pitching pieces to religious weeklies was basically a surefire way to go hungry. When he hit rock bottom, his only nice black suit sitting in the pawn shop, he landed what felt like a huge win in a prize contest run by the Republican Party’s County Committee. The contest had three separate categories, so he submitted work to every single one, laughing bitterly at himself the whole time—he couldn’t believe he’d been pushed to such desperate lengths just to scrape by. His poem took first place and its $10 prize, his original campaign song landed second place for $5, and his essay laying out the core principles of the Republican Party won first place and its $25 award. He was thrilled with the haul, right up until he tried to collect the money. Some kind of administrative snafu had thrown things off at the County Committee, and even though the group counted a wealthy banker and a sitting state senator among its members, no payout was forthcoming. While that whole mess was still tied up in limbo, he proved he understood Democratic Party principles just as thoroughly, winning first place and $25 in an identical contest put on by the local Democrats. This time, he actually got the cash as promised. The $40 he’d earned from the first contest, though, he never saw a cent of.
source_text: Despite these various misadventures, the memory of the _White Mouse_ forty-dollar check sustained him, though he was driven more and more to hack-work. He discovered a bread-and-butter field in the agricultural weeklies and trade journals, though among the religious weeklies he found he could easily starve. At his lowest ebb, when his black suit was in pawn, he made a ten-strike—or so it seemed to him—in a prize contest arranged by the County Committee of the Republican Party. There were three branches of the contest, and he entered them all, laughing at himself bitterly the while in that he was driven to such straits to live. His poem won the first prize of ten dollars, his campaign song the second prize of five dollars, his essay on the principles of the Republican Party the first prize of twenty-five dollars. Which was very gratifying to him until he tried to collect. Something had gone wrong in the County Committee, and, though a rich banker and a state senator were members of it, the money was not forthcoming. While this affair was hanging fire, he proved that he understood the principles of the Democratic Party by winning the first prize for his essay in a similar contest. And, moreover, he received the money, twenty-five dollars. But the forty dollars won in the first contest he never received.

text: Neither of them had uttered a single syllable, and the silence stretched on for a long, unbroken span. He leaned down to kiss her twice, and each time her lips met his softly, tentative, her whole body shifting to curl closer to him in quiet, happy contentment. She held fast to him, unable to make herself pull away, and he sat with her half propped in his arms, his gaze fixed unfocused on the hazy, glowing sprawl of the massive city on the other side of the bay. For once, there were no whirring thoughts or imagined scenarios playing out in his head. All that pulsed through his mind were soft streaks of color and flickering light, as warm as the mild day around them and as tender as the love thrumming in his chest. He leaned further over her. She was starting to speak.
source_text: Not a word had been spoken, and not a word was spoken for a long time. Twice he bent and kissed her, and each time her lips met his shyly and her body made its happy, nestling movement. She clung to him, unable to release herself, and he sat, half supporting her in his arms, as he gazed with unseeing eyes at the blur of the great city across the bay. For once there were no visions in his brain. Only colors and lights and glows pulsed there, warm as the day and warm as his love. He bent over her. She was speaking.

text: “You’d better believe he was,” Martin declared. “Three dollars one week, four the next, just a young kid cooking all his own meals on an old oil burner, stashing every cent he made. He worked all day long, then studied straight through every night—nothing but constant grind, no time to play, no opportunities to have a good time, not even a clue how to enjoy himself even if he’d had the chance. Of course that thirty thousand dollars landed in his lap far too late.”
source_text: “You can bet he was,” Martin proclaimed. “Three dollars a week, an’ four dollars a week, an’ a young boy cookin’ for himself on an oil-burner an’ layin’ up money, workin’ all day an’ studyin’ all night, just workin’ an’ never playin’, never havin’ a good time, an’ never learnin’ how to have a good time—of course his thirty thousand came along too late.”

text: “I have had a mighty vision,” he said, and when his own words reached his ears, his heart gave a sharp, surprised leap. Where had that turn of phrase come from? It perfectly captured the quiet lull his revelation had dropped into their conversation. It felt like a small miracle. Never before had he wrapped a lofty, soaring thought in such fittingly elevated language. But then, he had never attempted to put lofty thoughts into words before. That was the answer, that explained everything. He had never tried. But Swinburne had, and Tennyson, and Kipling, and all the other great poets. His mind flew at once to his own work in progress, “Pearl-diving.” He had never dared reach for the grand, sweeping things before—the core of that beauty that burned like fire inside him. That piece would be entirely different when he finished reworking it now. He was almost staggered by the vastness of the beauty that rightfully belonged within its pages, and again a flash of daring seized his mind, as he demanded of himself why he could not sing that beauty in noble, rolling verse just as the great poets did. And then there was all the quiet, unnameable delight and sacred, breathless wonder of his love for Ruth. Why could he not sing of that, too, as the poets had? They had written their odes to love for centuries. So would he. By God!—
source_text: “I have had a great visioning,” he said, and at the sound of his words in his own ears his heart gave a leap. Where had those words come from? They had adequately expressed the pause his vision had put in the conversation. It was a miracle. Never had he so loftily framed a lofty thought. But never had he attempted to frame lofty thoughts in words. That was it. That explained it. He had never tried. But Swinburne had, and Tennyson, and Kipling, and all the other poets. His mind flashed on to his “Pearl-diving.” He had never dared the big things, the spirit of the beauty that was a fire in him. That article would be a different thing when he was done with it. He was appalled by the vastness of the beauty that rightfully belonged in it, and again his mind flashed and dared, and he demanded of himself why he could not chant that beauty in noble verse as the great poets did. And there was all the mysterious delight and spiritual wonder of his love for Ruth. Why could he not chant that, too, as the poets did? They had sung of love. So would he. By God!—

text: But Kael spun on his heel and walked off, leaving the stranger to ramble his story to the barkeep, until that gruff, apron-clad man was pulled away to pour rounds for two ranchers who’d just stepped through the door, nodding in acceptance of Kael’s open offer. Kael doled out his generosity with unapologetic grandeur, buying a round for every soul in the place: the ranch hands loitering by the door, a livery worker who’d popped in for a quick sip, the under-gardener from the grand hotel down the block, the barkeep himself, and the skittish unhoused man who’d slipped in quiet as a wraith, lingering like one too at the far edge of the bar.
source_text: But Martin turned away, leaving him to tell it to the barkeeper, until that worthy was called away to furnish drinks to two farmers who, coming in, accepted Martin’s invitation. Martin dispensed royal largess, inviting everybody up, farm-hands, a stableman, and the gardener’s assistant from the hotel, the barkeeper, and the furtive hobo who slid in like a shadow and like a shadow hovered at the end of the bar.

text: Little by little, her trust seeped back in, and she pored over the letter again, reading every line with deliberate care. He’d taken ages walking her through the exact nature of the transaction that had put the money in his possession, and even longer to get her to accept that the funds truly belonged to her, and that he had no use for them at all.
source_text: Slowly faith came back to her, and she reread the letter carefully. It took him long to explain to her the nature of the transaction which had put the money into his possession, and longer still to get her to understand that the money was really hers and that he did not need it.

text: Martin nodded, but a sharp, grating prickle of irritation rose in his chest. The comment had been painfully obvious, entirely unnecessary. They never spoke while they worked, for good reason. Conversation always threw them off their rhythm, just as it had this time: Martin missed a pass with his iron, and had to make two extra, awkward adjustments before he settled back into his usual steady pace.
source_text: Martin nodded, but felt a rasp of irritation. The statement had been obvious and unnecessary. They did not talk while they worked. Conversation threw them out of their stride, as it did this time, compelling Martin to miss a stroke of his iron and to make two extra motions before he caught his stride again.

text: One evening, as she was walking to night school with Lizzie, she caught an attractive, expensively dressed upper-middle-class woman glancing over at Martin. The look dragged on just a beat too long, held a hint of overly deliberate scrutiny. Lizzie recognized exactly what that glance meant, and her entire body tensed up with sharp anger. Martin noticed her reaction right away, worked out what had prompted it, and told her he was growing so accustomed to that sort of attention that he didn’t care about it at all.
source_text: Once, walking with Lizzie toward night school, she caught a glance directed toward him by a well-gowned, handsome woman of the bourgeoisie. The glance was a trifle too long, a shade too considerative. Lizzie knew it for what it was, and her body tensed angrily. Martin noticed, noticed the cause of it, told her how used he was becoming to it and that he did not care anyway.

text: “I hope you won’t take this as boasting,” he began, earnest. “That’s not what I’m aiming for at all, I swear. But I really think I’m what you might call a natural learner. I can study perfectly well on my own; it comes to me as easily as swimming comes to a duck. You’ve seen firsthand what I managed with grammar, after all. And I’ve picked up so much else besides—you’d never even guess how much. This is just the start, too. Wait until I get—” He paused, running the word over silently in his head to confirm the pronunciation before he spoke it clearly: “momentum. I’m finally getting my first real feel for how all this works now, starting to get a clear read on the whole situation—”
source_text: “Please don’t think I’m bragging,” he began. “I don’t intend it that way at all. But I have a feeling that I am what I may call a natural student. I can study by myself. I take to it kindly, like a duck to water. You see yourself what I did with grammar. And I’ve learned much of other things—you would never dream how much. And I’m only getting started. Wait till I get—” He hesitated and assured himself of the pronunciation before he said “momentum. I’m getting my first real feel of things now. I’m beginning to size up the situation—”

text: It felt to him like he’d never seen Ruth look more beautiful—more softly spiritual, more unearthly and ethereal, yet somehow more vibrantly healthy and alive all at once. A warm flush colored her cheekbones, and her gaze kept yanking his own back again and again: those were the exact eyes where he’d first caught a glimmer of immortality, once upon a time. He’d all but forgotten the very concept of immortality lately; every scientific text he’d pored over in recent months had pushed the idea further and further out of his frame of reference. But here, staring into Ruth’s eyes, he found a wordless argument that outstripped every carefully constructed, verbose debate he’d ever encountered. All his carefully considered intellectual positions dissolved the second he met that look, because he could see plain, unmistakeable love staring back at him. His own eyes were brimming with the same feeling, after all; and love was the one truth no one could argue against. That was his fierce, passionate creed, in that moment.
source_text: It seemed to him that he had never seen Ruth more beautiful, more spiritual and ethereal and at the same time more healthy. There was color in her cheeks, and her eyes drew him again and again—the eyes in which he had first read immortality. He had forgotten immortality of late, and the trend of his scientific reading had been away from it; but here, in Ruth’s eyes, he read an argument without words that transcended all worded arguments. He saw that in her eyes before which all discussion fled away, for he saw love there. And in his own eyes was love; and love was unanswerable. Such was his passionate doctrine.

text: Once they’d settled into the faded velvet armchairs of her front parlor, the conversation unfolded far more easily than he’d dared to anticipate. She met him halfway at every turn, the unforced warmth of her manner stoking his adoration to a giddy, dizzying pitch he’d never thought possible. They first chatted about the stack of books he’d borrowed the week prior: the wild, throbbing verse of Dowson he’d memorized half of by heart, and the layered, allusive poems of Rossetti he still couldn’t parse. She steered the talk gently from topic to topic, all while turning over the question of how she might lift him up, a problem she’d mulled constantly since their first meeting by the town’s frozen mill pond. She ached to help him. He tugged at a vein of pity and tenderness no other person had ever reached in her, and that softness was not belittling to him so much as it was gently maternal, rooted in care, not condescension. It could never be the ordinary sort of pity, not when the man who inspired it was so unapologetically vividly male that he roused fluttering, girlish alarms in her, sent her thoughts spinning and her pulse thrumming with odd, unrecognizable feelings. She was still drawn to the strong, sun-browned slope of his neck, and the thought of pressing her palms flat to it hummed with quiet sweetness. It still felt like a bold, unruly impulse, but she’d grown far more comfortable with the thought of it lately. She never guessed that new, fledgling love so often dressed itself in exactly these small, unnameable, half-wanton urges. She never guessed that the warm, thrumming feeling he roused in her was love at all, instead convincing herself she was merely curious about him, an unusual, rough-edged figure brimming with untapped potential, and she even framed the whole interest as a sort of quiet, philanthropic project.
source_text: Once they were seated in the living-room, he began to get on easily—more easily by far than he had expected. She made it easy for him; and the gracious spirit with which she did it made him love her more madly than ever. They talked first of the borrowed books, of the Swinburne he was devoted to, and of the Browning he did not understand; and she led the conversation on from subject to subject, while she pondered the problem of how she could be of help to him. She had thought of this often since their first meeting. She wanted to help him. He made a call upon her pity and tenderness that no one had ever made before, and the pity was not so much derogatory of him as maternal in her. Her pity could not be of the common sort, when the man who drew it was so much man as to shock her with maidenly fears and set her mind and pulse thrilling with strange thoughts and feelings. The old fascination of his neck was there, and there was sweetness in the thought of laying her hands upon it. It seemed still a wanton impulse, but she had grown more used to it. She did not dream that in such guise new-born love would epitomize itself. Nor did she dream that the feeling he excited in her was love. She thought she was merely interested in him as an unusual type possessing various potential excellencies, and she even felt philanthropic about it.

text: Elspeth Thorne offered no reply. The sharp spark of her defiance guttered out at once, her spirit wilting into the heavy, overworn fatigue of her flesh. Her husband Gideon was triumphant. He had her right where he wanted her. His eyes glinted with petty, unkind satisfaction, while his ears drank in the hitching sniffles she could not suppress. He drew enormous, unwholesome joy from crushing her will, and she caved far more easily these days than she once had—back in the first years of their marriage, before their brood of small children and his ceaseless, niggling nagging had leached every last spark of stubborn energy from her.
source_text: Mrs. Higginbotham made no reply. Her revolt faded away, her spirit wilting down into her tired flesh. Her husband was triumphant. He had her. His eyes snapped vindictively, while his ears joyed in the sniffles she emitted. He extracted great happiness from squelching her, and she squelched easily these days, though it had been different in the first years of their married life, before the brood of children and his incessant nagging had sapped her energy.

text: Martin grinned as he accepted the invitation, quietly marveling at his own unshakable complacency the whole time. The dinner gathered half a dozen of the community’s most prominent men, each accompanied by their wives and female companions, and Martin quickly realized he was the guest of the hour, the undisputed center of everyone’s attention. Judge Blount pulled him aside for a private chat, with Judge Hanwell warmly seconding every point he made, to urge Martin to let them put his name forward for membership to the Styx—an ultra-exclusive club whose ranks were made up not just of wealthy men, but of men who’d earned significant, widely respected achievements in their fields. Martin turned the offer down, and left the conversation more baffled than he’d ever been before.
source_text: Martin grinned and accepted the invitation, marvelling the while at his complacence. And at the dinner, where, with their womankind, were half a dozen of those that sat in high places, and where Martin found himself quite the lion, Judge Blount, warmly seconded by Judge Hanwell, urged privately that Martin should permit his name to be put up for the Styx—the ultra-select club to which belonged, not the mere men of wealth, but the men of attainment. And Martin declined, and was more puzzled than ever.

text: “Darling,” she cut in softly, “just listen. I know that whole hour was nothing but frustration and strain for you. I did it for her—the girl I spoke to on the coach, remember? I was so happy, Jim—so unshakably happy with you that I couldn’t bear to deny that same joy to someone else. Jim, those two were married only this morning, the pair of them; I needed him to get away safe. While you were tangled up dealing with them, I watched him slip out from behind the tree he’d been hiding behind and hurry across the park. That’s the whole of it, dear—I had no choice but to do it.”
source_text: “Dear,” she interrupted, “listen. It was an hour’s pain and trial to you. I did it for her—I mean the girl who spoke to me on the coach. I was so happy, Jim—so happy with you that I didn’t dare to refuse that happiness to another. Jim, they were married only this morning—those two; and I wanted him to get away. While they were struggling with you I saw him slip from behind his tree and hurry across the park. That’s all of it, dear—I had to do it.”

text: Weeks slipped by in the interim, his savings dwindled steadily, and not a cent of new income came his way. A full month after he’d mailed off his boys’ adventure serial, *The Youth’s Companion* returned the manuscript to him. Its rejection note was phrased with such gentle consideration that he bore the editor no ill will at all. He felt far less charitable toward the editor of the *San Francisco Examiner*, though. After waiting two entire weeks for a response, Martin wrote to inquire after his submission. A week later, he followed up with a second letter. When the month drew to a close, he traveled to San Francisco to call on the editor in person. He never got to meet that lofty figure, though—barred at the door by a redheaded, barely teenaged office boy who guarded the entrance like a surly Cerberus. At the end of the fifth week, his manuscript arrived back in the post with no comment whatsoever: no rejection slip, no explanation, not a single line of context. His other submissions to San Francisco’s leading papers were stuck in the same unresponsive limbo. Once he finally retrieved those manuscripts, he forwarded them to Eastern magazines, which sent them back even more quickly, every last one paired with a generic printed rejection slip.
source_text: In the meantime the weeks were passing, his money was ebbing low, and there was no money coming in. A month after he had mailed it, the adventure serial for boys was returned to him by _The Youth’s Companion_. The rejection slip was so tactfully worded that he felt kindly toward the editor. But he did not feel so kindly toward the editor of the _San Francisco Examiner_. After waiting two whole weeks, Martin had written to him. A week later he wrote again. At the end of the month, he went over to San Francisco and personally called upon the editor. But he did not meet that exalted personage, thanks to a Cerberus of an office boy, of tender years and red hair, who guarded the portals. At the end of the fifth week the manuscript came back to him, by mail, without comment. There was no rejection slip, no explanation, nothing. In the same way his other articles were tied up with the other leading San Francisco papers. When he recovered them, he sent them to the magazines in the East, from which they were returned more promptly, accompanied always by the printed rejection slips.

text: One dollar and eighty-seven cents. That was every last cent she had. Sixty cents of that sum was in pennies, scraped together one or two at a time by haggling relentlessly with the grocer, the vegetable seller, and the butcher until her cheeks burned with the unspoken accusation of stinginess that such tightfisted bargaining implied. Della counted the pile three separate times. It still came to one dollar and eighty-seven cents. And Christmas was the very next day.
source_text: One dollar and eighty-seven cents. That was all. And sixty cents of it was in pennies. Pennies saved one and two at a time by bulldozing the grocer and the vegetable man and the butcher until one’s cheeks burned with the silent imputation of parsimony that such close dealing implied. Three times Della counted it. One dollar and eighty-seven cents. And the next day would be Christmas.

text: The second she stepped into the hall, Mr. Hoover seized his opening. He asked her to marry him, his heavy, fleshy frame hovering over her like a looming avalanche. She dodged out of his path, grabbing the balustrade to stay upright. When he reached for her hand, she lifted her arm and struck him weakly across the face. She pulled herself up the stairs one step at a time, clinging to the railing for purchase. As she passed Mr. Skidder’s door, he was inside scrawling a red-ink stage direction for Myrtle Delorme (Miss Leeson herself) in his unaccepted comedy, noting that she should “pirouette across the stage from left to the Count’s side.” At last, she crawled up the narrow carpeted ladder and pushed open the door to the skylight room.
source_text: As she stepped into the hall Mr. Hoover met her and seized his chance. He asked her to marry him, and his fatness hovered above her like an avalanche. She dodged, and caught the balustrade. He tried for her hand, and she raised it and smote him weakly in the face. Step by step she went up, dragging herself by the railing. She passed Mr. Skidder’s door as he was red-inking a stage direction for Myrtle Delorme (Miss Leeson) in his (unaccepted) comedy, to “pirouette across stage from L to the side of the Count.” Up the carpeted ladder she crawled at last and opened the door of the skylight room.

text: I sat mulling over the unfeigned, far-roaming cosmopolitan I’d crossed paths with, wondering how generations of poets had never stumbled on his type to immortalize. He was my own unshared discovery, and I held full belief in his singular character. How had he slipped through the cracks? “The men born of that stock drift back and forth trading across borders, yet cling to the frayed edge of their home city’s skirt as a small child clutches at the fabric of its mother’s gown.”
source_text: I sat reflecting upon my evident cosmopolite and wondering how the poet had managed to miss him. He was my discovery and I believed in him. How was it? “The men that breed from them they traffic up and down, but cling to their cities’ hem as a child to the mother’s gown.”

text: Testing door handles as he paced his route, spinning his heavy hickory baton in all manner of deft, clever flourishes, pausing every few steps to sweep his alert gaze down the quiet, dim thoroughfare, the patrolman—broad-shouldered, with a faint, easy swagger to his step—cut a fine, reassuring figure as the neighborhood’s guardian of order. This stretch of the city kept strictly early hours. Every so often you might catch the glimmer of lights from a tobacco shop or an around-the-clock hash house, but the vast majority of the storefronts belonged to businesses that had been shuttered and locked for hours already.
source_text: Trying doors as he went, twirling his club with many intricate and artful movements, turning now and then to cast his watchful eye adown the pacific thoroughfare, the officer, with his stalwart form and slight swagger, made a fine picture of a guardian of the peace. The vicinity was one that kept early hours. Now and then you might see the lights of a cigar store or of an all-night lunch counter; but the majority of the doors belonged to business places that had long since been closed.

text: Up in the front second-floor apartment, Mr. and Mrs. McCaskey moved to the window to catch their breath. Mr. McCaskey was scraping bits of turnip out of his vest with a crooked index finger, while his wife dabbed at an eye stinging from the salt of their roast pork. They heard shouting rise from the street below, and stuck their heads out the window to see what was happening.
source_text: In the second floor front Mr. and Mrs. McCaskey came to the window to recover their second wind. Mr. McCaskey was scooping turnips out of his vest with a crooked forefinger, and his lady was wiping an eye that the salt of the roast pork had not benefited. They heard the outcry below, and thrust their heads out of the window.

text: The young woman had served as Maxwell’s stenographer for a full year. Her beauty was of a sort entirely uncharacteristic of what one expected from someone in her line of work. She forewent the fuss of the alluring, fashionable pompadour popular among other young office women. She wore no chains, bracelets, or lockets of any kind. She carried none of the air of someone on the verge of accepting an invitation to a fancy luncheon. Her dress was plain grey, but it fit her figure with quiet, exacting fidelity. Tucked into her neat black turban hat was the glossy gold-green wing feather of a macaw. On this particular morning, she glowed with a soft, shy radiance. Her eyes were bright with a dreamy, faraway light, her cheeks the soft, true hue of peach bloom, her expression warm with happiness, tinged with the faint sweetness of half-recollected joy.
source_text: The young lady had been Maxwell’s stenographer for a year. She was beautiful in a way that was decidedly unstenographic. She forewent the pomp of the alluring pompadour. She wore no chains, bracelets or lockets. She had not the air of being about to accept an invitation to luncheon. Her dress was grey and plain, but it fitted her figure with fidelity and discretion. In her neat black turban hat was the gold-green wing of a macaw. On this morning she was softly and shyly radiant. Her eyes were dreamily bright, her cheeks genuine peachblow, her expression a happy one, tinged with reminiscence.

text: A tall young man strode along the park path that ran right near the spot where she was seated. Trailing a few paces behind him was a boy toting a suitcase. The instant he caught sight of the young woman, his face flushed deep scarlet before draining immediately to a pale, ashen hue. As he drew closer, he kept his eyes fixed on her expression, his own features knitted with a tangled mix of hope and thrumming anxiety. He passed no more than a few yards away from her, but there was no indication whatsoever that she was aware of his presence, or that he even existed at all.
source_text: A tall young man came striding through the park along the path near which she sat. Behind him tagged a boy carrying a suit-case. At sight of the young lady, the man’s face changed to red and back to pale again. He watched her countenance as he drew nearer, with hope and anxiety mingled on his own. He passed within a few yards of her, but he saw no evidence that she was aware of his presence or existence.

text: “Miss Leslie,” he started, words tumbling out in a rush, “I’ve only got a split second to spare, so I need to get this out right now. Will you be my wife? I haven’t had time to romance you the way most people do, but I really do love you. Hurry up and answer, please—those guys are pummeling Union Pacific stock into the dirt right now.”
source_text: “Miss Leslie,” he began hurriedly, “I have but a moment to spare. I want to say something in that moment. Will you be my wife? I haven’t had time to make love to you in the ordinary way, but I really do love you. Talk quick, please—those fellows are clubbing the stuffing out of Union Pacific.”

text: “I’m glad you brought this topic up. I’ve felt the weight of this after-hours blight hanging over our city for a while, but it never occurred to me to break it down before. It’s clear now that this ‘Man About Town’ figure you mention should have been properly categorized ages ago. Wherever he passes, wine sales reps and high-end cloak models crop up in his wake, and the house orchestra scraps Handel to play “Let’s All Go Up to Maud’s” for him by special request. He hits all his regular haunts every single evening, while you and I only get to go out looking for a good time once a week if we’re lucky. When the unlicensed cigar shop gets raided, he just winks at the officer who knows his face and his usual spots, then walks away scot-free, while you and I are left scrambling to dig up fake names from lists of former presidents and fake addresses pulled from celebrity star maps to hand the desk sergeant.”
source_text: “I’m glad you brought up the subject; I’ve felt the influence of this nocturnal blight upon our city, but I never thought to analyse it before. I can see now that your ‘Man About Town’ should have been classified long ago. In his wake spring up wine agents and cloak models; and the orchestra plays ‘Let’s All Go Up to Maud’s’ for him, by request, instead of Händel. He makes his rounds every evening; while you and I see the elephant once a week. When the cigar store is raided, he winks at the officer, familiar with his ground, and walks away immune, while you and I search among the Presidents for names, and among the stars for addresses to give the desk sergeant.”

text: A thin, biting drizzle was falling now, and the wind had picked up from its earlier fitful, uncertain puffs into a steady, cutting blow. The few pedestrians out in that part of the neighborhood hurried along in glum silence, their coat collars tugged high to their cheeks and their hands stuffed deep in their pockets. Leaning in the doorway of the hardware store, the man who had traveled a thousand miles to keep an appointment so uncertain it bordered on absurdity, arranged with the friend he had known since his youth, smoked his cigar and waited.
source_text: There was now a fine, cold drizzle falling, and the wind had risen from its uncertain puffs into a steady blow. The few foot passengers astir in that quarter hurried dismally and silently along with coat collars turned high and pocketed hands. And in the door of the hardware store the man who had come a thousand miles to fill an appointment, uncertain almost to absurdity, with the friend of his youth, smoked his cigar and waited.

text: Elias had never once run behind schedule. Maren folded the watch fob chain over twice in her palm, then perched on the edge of the side table right by the front door he always used to come inside. A moment later, she caught the sound of his boots on the lowest flight of stairs down the landing, and her face drained of color for a split second. She’d long held a habit of whispering small, quiet prayers over even the most trivial everyday moments, and now she breathed the words soft enough that only the walls could hear: “Please God, let him still think I’m lovely.”
source_text: Jim was never late. Della doubled the fob chain in her hand and sat on the corner of the table near the door that he always entered. Then she heard his step on the stair away down on the first flight, and she turned white for just a moment. She had a habit for saying little silent prayers about the simplest everyday things, and now she whispered: “Please God, make him think I am still pretty.”

text: It was nine thirty when Dulcie cast one final glance at the portraits propped on her dresser, flipped off the lamp, and hopped under the covers. There’s something deeply unsettling about saying goodnight to the faces of General Kitchener, William Muldoon, the Duchess of Marlborough, and Benvenuto Cellini right before you fall asleep. Truth be told, this little tale isn’t going anywhere right now. The rest of it will unfold later, though—sometime when Piggy invites Dulcie out for dinner again, when she’s more lonesome than she normally is, and General Kitchener’s portrait happens to be turned just enough that he’s not watching; and then—
source_text: At half-past nine Dulcie took a last look at the pictures on the dresser, turned out the light, and skipped into bed. It’s an awful thing to go to bed with a good-night look at General Kitchener, William Muldoon, the Duchess of Marlborough, and Benvenuto Cellini. This story really doesn’t get anywhere at all. The rest of it comes later—sometime when Piggy asks Dulcie again to dine with him, and she is feeling lonelier than usual, and General Kitchener happens to be looking the other way; and then—

text: The tour vehicle glided smoothly along Golden Way. Perched at the front of the massive cruiser-style bus, the guide stood, booming the big city’s landmarks into a megaphone for his passengers. Eyes wide and ears pricked, they strained to match the sights of the metropolis he roared out to what lay right before their eyes. Giddy, overstimulated, and brimming with small-town wonder, they fumbled to point at the correct spots as part of this whole megaphone-led song and dance. They mistook the stately spires of sprawling cathedrals for the Vanderbilt family mansion, and stared awestruck at the hulking, bustling mass of Grand Central Terminal, convinced it was the modest home of Russell Sage. When he told them to look out for the Hudson Highlands, they gaped unknowingly at the great heaped mounds of dirt from a freshly dug sewer line. Plenty of them thought the elevated train line was the famous Rialto theater district, and figured the uniformed staff posted at its stations were there to mangle tickets beyond all recognition. Even now, folks in outlying rural areas swear that Chuck Connors leads all local reform efforts with his hand pressed over his heart, and that if it hadn’t been for the upstanding city work of district attorney Parkhurst, the infamous “Bishop” Potter gang would have wiped out every last bit of law and order from the Bowery all the way up to the Harlem River.
source_text: The car glided up the Golden Way. On the bridge of the great cruiser the captain stood, trumpeting the sights of the big city to his passengers. Wide-mouthed and open-eared, they heard the sights of the metropolis thundered forth to their eyes. Confused, delirious with excitement and provincial longings, they tried to make ocular responses to the megaphonic ritual. In the solemn spires of spreading cathedrals they saw the home of the Vanderbilts; in the busy bulk of the Grand Central depot they viewed, wonderingly, the frugal cot of Russell Sage. Bidden to observe the highlands of the Hudson, they gaped, unsuspecting, at the upturned mountains of a new-laid sewer. To many the elevated railroad was the Rialto, on the stations of which uniformed men sat and made chop suey of your tickets. And to this day in the outlying districts many have it that Chuck Connors, with his hand on his heart, leads reform; and that but for the noble municipal efforts of one Parkhurst, a district attorney, the notorious “Bishop” Potter gang would have destroyed law and order from the Bowery to the Harlem River.

text: As he meandered down the pavement, a sharp, chittering rattle of dentures in a glass display case on the curb first caught his notice, sending a faint, uneasy twist through his stomach; he took it for a novelty promotion for the bistro it stood outside, until a second glance revealed the glowing electric lettering of a dentist’s sign strung high over the adjacent doorway. A towering Black man, fancifully clad in a scarlet embroidered coat, sunflower-yellow trousers, and a crisp military cap, was discreetly passing out business cards to those in the passing throng who agreed to take them.
source_text: During his walk a violent chattering of teeth in a glass case on the sidewalk seemed at first to draw his attention (with a qualm), to a restaurant before which it was set; but a second glance revealed the electric letters of a dentist’s sign high above the next door. A giant negro, fantastically dressed in a red embroidered coat, yellow trousers and a military cap, discreetly distributed cards to those of the passing crowd who consented to take them.

text: Late one overcast afternoon Clara huddled in her elegant hall bedroom, shivering even as she parroted the landlady’s well-worn sales pitch under her breath: “Furnaces stoked, every corner scrubbed spotless, all modern conveniences—folks notice, and they come back.” The only work waiting for her was lettering the weekly dinner menus for Herr Holzapfel, the bistro owner two blocks west. Clara sank into her creaky willow rocking chair and let her gaze drift to the street outside. The paper calendar tacked to the wall beside her seemed to call out, soft and teasing, right into the hush of the room: “Spring is here, Clara—spring is here, I swear it. Look at my dates, Clara, my numbers don’t lie. You’ve got such a neat, sweet figure yourself, Clara—such a lovely springtime figure—why are you staring out that window so blue?”
source_text: One afternoon Sarah shivered in her elegant hall bedroom; “house heated; scrupulously clean; conveniences; seen to be appreciated.” She had no work to do except Schulenberg’s menu cards. Sarah sat in her squeaky willow rocker, and looked out the window. The calendar on the wall kept crying to her: “Springtime is here, Sarah—springtime is here, I tell you. Look at me, Sarah, my figures show it. You’ve got a neat figure yourself, Sarah—a—nice springtime figure—why do you look out the window so sadly?”

text: “I think I could fall for a man with soft, deep blue eyes, the sort who treats scullery maids and street flower sellers with quiet courtesy, who’s warm and decent and never leans into cheap, unthinking flirtation. But that fondness would only take root if he had a drive, a purpose, some work he cared enough to tend to in the world. I wouldn’t mind a bit how little he had, as long as I could stand at his side and help him carve out a place for himself. But dearest sister, the kind of men we always run into—the idle fellows who drift between society soirees and their gentlemen’s clubs with nothing to fill their days but trivialities—I could never love a man like that, even if his eyes were exactly the soft blue I like, even if he were endlessly kind to every poor girl he passed on the pavement.”
source_text: “I could love a man with dark and kind blue eyes, who is gentle and respectful to poor girls, who is handsome and good and does not try to flirt. But I could love him only if he had an ambition, an object, some work to do in the world. I would not care how poor he was if I could help him build his way up. But, sister dear, the kind of man we always meet—the man who lives an idle life between society and his clubs—I could not love a man like that, even if his eyes were blue and he were ever so kind to poor girls whom he met in the street.”

text: The guy who boasted the world was an oyster he’d pry open with his sword got way more acclaim than he ever deserved. Cracking open an oyster with a blade isn’t some impressive, unheard-of feat, after all. But have you ever watched someone try to work that same globe-sized bivalve open with a typewriter? Do you feel like hanging around waiting for a dozen raw oysters shucked that way?
source_text: The gentleman who announced that the world was an oyster which he with his sword would open made a larger hit than he deserved. It is not difficult to open an oyster with a sword. But did you ever notice any one try to open the terrestrial bivalve with a typewriter? Like to wait for a dozen raw opened that way?

text: Late one evening, raucous party noises drifted out of the large brick tenement two doors down from McGary’s Family Café. The commotion seemed to be coming straight from the Walsh family’s apartment. The sidewalk out front was crammed with curious neighbors, who’d part to clear a narrow path every so often for rushed messengers hauling party supplies and refreshments from McGary’s for the festivities. The cluster of onlookers loitering on the pavement chattered back and forth, making no effort to hide that the celebration was for Norah Walsh’s wedding.
source_text: One night there were sounds of revelry in the big brick tenement-house next door but one to McGary’s Family Café. The sounds seemed to emanate from the apartments of the Walsh family. The sidewalk was obstructed by an assortment of interested neighbours, who opened a lane from time to time for a hurrying messenger bearing from McGary’s goods pertinent to festivity and diversion. The sidewalk contingent was engaged in comment and discussion from which it made no effort to eliminate the news that Norah Walsh was being married.

text: Dreamy, meandering thoughts drifted through Prince Michael’s mind as he smiled, the expression half-obscured beneath the stubble of his multihued beard. Lolling in the park dressed no better than the scruffiest of the beggars who wandered the grounds, he took great delight in observing the ordinary people around him. He derived far more pleasure from acts of unselfish kindness than he ever had from his vast wealth, his royal rank, or any of the coarse, indulgent pleasures his high station had ever offered him. His greatest solace and satisfaction came from easing individual hardship, extending aid to deserving souls in need of support, and stunning the unfortunate with unanticipated, dazzling gifts of truly royal magnificence—though he always bestowed such generosity with careful wisdom and sound discretion.
source_text: These thoughts strayed dreamily through the mind of Prince Michael, as he smiled under the stubble of his polychromatic beard. Lounging thus, clad as the poorest of mendicants in the parks, he loved to study humanity. He found in altruism more pleasure than his riches, his station and all the grosser sweets of life had given him.
