It was his chief solace and satisfaction to alleviate individual distress, to confer favours upon worthy ones who had need of succour, to dazzle unfortunates by unexpected and bewildering gifts of truly royal magnificence, bestowed, however, with wisdom and judiciousness.

text: Once he’d finished his property check, Elias mounted the steep flight of weathered stone steps briskly, stepping through the front door into the townhouse. He kept climbing past the ground floor, ascending two more flights of threadbare, carpeted stairs before coming to an abrupt stop at the landing. The corridor stretched before him in faint shadow, lit only by two guttering gas jets: one sputtered far down the hall to his right, the other glowed soft and close just to his left. His gaze drifted to the closer lamp, and within its thin, washed-out circle of light he spotted a door painted a deep, mossy green. He hesitated for a heartbeat; then the contumelious sneer of the North African street card juggler he’d encountered earlier flickered across his mind, and he walked straight to the green door and rapped his knuckles against its surface.
source_text: After concluding his survey Rudolf walked briskly up the high flight of stone steps into the house. Up two flights of the carpeted stairway he continued; and at its top paused. The hallway there was dimly lighted by two pale jets of gas—one far to his right, the other nearer, to his left. He looked toward the nearer light and saw, within its wan halo, a green door. For one moment he hesitated; then he seemed to see the contumelious sneer of the African juggler of cards; and then he walked straight to the green door and knocked against it.

text: When Prince Elias’s gaze settled on the glowing face of the tall tower’s grand clock, his usual warm, generous smile curved into a faint edge of contempt. The Prince’s mind was always turning over vast, world-spanning thoughts, and he would inevitably shake his head as he considered how all of humanity had been subjugated to the arbitrary, unyielding dictates of Time. The way people hurried to and fro, harried and anxious, their every move controlled by the tiny ticking metal hands of a clock, always settled a quiet, heavy sadness over him.
source_text: And as Prince Michael’s eye rested upon the glowing face of the great clock in the tower, his smile, altruistic as it was, became slightly tinged with contempt. Big thoughts were the Prince’s; and it was always with a shake of his head that he considered the subjugation of the world to the arbitrary measures of Time. The comings and goings of people in hurry and dread, controlled by the little metal moving hands of a clock, always made him sad.

text: Dulcie paid two dollars a week for her small room. On workdays, her breakfast cost ten cents total; she brewed coffee and fried a single egg over the gas lamp while she got dressed for the day. On Sunday mornings, she splurged on a lavish spread of veal chops and pineapple fritters at Billy’s restaurant, dropping twenty-five cents for the meal plus a ten-cent tip for the waitress. New York is stuffed with so many little temptations that make it far too easy to slip into overspending before you even notice. She ate all her lunches at the department store’s in-house diner, adding up to sixty cents total for the workweek; dinners ran her a dollar and five cents across the whole week. Evening newspapers, too—seriously, name one New Yorker who goes without their daily paper? Those cost six cents a week, and she picked up two Sunday papers: one for the personal ads column, the other just to read for leisure, which set her back another ten cents. All told, that adds up to four dollars and seventy-six cents a week. Then, of course, you have to buy clothes, and—
source_text: For the room, Dulcie paid two dollars per week. On week-days her breakfast cost ten cents; she made coffee and cooked an egg over the gaslight while she was dressing. On Sunday mornings she feasted royally on veal chops and pineapple fritters at “Billy’s” restaurant, at a cost of twenty-five cents—and tipped the waitress ten cents. New York presents so many temptations for one to run into extravagance. She had her lunches in the department-store restaurant at a cost of sixty cents for the week; dinners were $1.05. The evening papers—show me a New Yorker going without his daily paper!—came to six cents; and two Sunday papers—one for the personal column and the other to read—were ten cents. The total amounts to $4.76. Now, one has to buy clothes, and—

text: Ikey stepped behind the prescription counter. Once there, he ground two dissolvable tablets—each holding a quarter grain of morphine—into a fine powder. He mixed in a small pinch of milk sugar to bulk the dose up slightly, then folded the combined mixture tidily into a slip of white paper. For a full-grown adult, this powder would guarantee several hours of deep, uninterrupted sleep with no risk of harm to the person taking it. He handed the folded packet to Chunk McGowan, advised him to mix it into a drink if at all possible when giving it out, and got an effusive, heartfelt thank-you from that scrappy backyard Lochinvar.
source_text: Ikey went behind the prescription desk. There he crushed to a powder two soluble tablets, each containing a quarter of a grain of morphia. To them he added a little sugar of milk to increase the bulk, and folded the mixture neatly in a white paper. Taken by an adult this powder would insure several hours of heavy slumber without danger to the sleeper. This he handed to Chunk McGowan, telling him to administer it in a liquid if possible, and received the hearty thanks of the backyard Lochinvar.

text: But Maggie was already gone, cutting a zigzag path through the tangled throng of dancers. She burst through the back door into the shadowy hallway, then drove her sturdy shoulder against the door of the private fighting chamber. It gave under her weight, and the second she crossed the threshold she took in the entire scene: the Board members clustered around, open stopwatches in their hands; Dempsey Donovan in just his shirtsleeves, shifting light on his feet with the wary grace of a modern prizefighter, well within striking distance of his opponent; Terry O’Sullivan standing stock-still with his arms folded, a murderous glint in his dark eyes. Not slowing her momentum for an instant as she surged into the room, she flew forward with a sharp scream—fast enough to catch and cling to O’Sullivan’s abruptly raised arm, and yank the long, glinting stiletto he had drawn from his breast right out of his grasp.
source_text: But Maggie was off, darting her zig-zag way through the maze of dancers. She burst through the rear door into the dark hall and then threw her solid shoulder against the door of the room of single combat. It gave way, and in the instant that she entered her eye caught the scene—the Board standing about with open watches; Dempsey Donovan in his shirt sleeves dancing, light-footed, with the wary grace of the modern pugilist, within easy reach of his adversary; Terry O’Sullivan standing with arms folded and a murderous look in his dark eyes. And without slacking the speed of her entrance she leaped forward with a scream—leaped in time to catch and hang upon the arm of O’Sullivan that was suddenly uplifted, and to whisk from it the long, bright stiletto that he had drawn from his bosom.

text: “I wish you could lay eyes on the carved oak wainscoting in that front parlor, Jude! And those thick, curled Karakul rug draperies strung across each doorway. And Elowen has such a soft, quaint little cough. I do hope she’s sturdier than her delicate appearance suggests. Oh, I really am growing quite attached to her—she’s so gentle and thoroughly well-bred. General Harlow’s brother once served as envoy to Peru, you know.”
source_text: “I wish you could see the wainscoting in that drawing-room, Joe! And those Astrakhan rug portières. And Clementina has such a funny little cough. I hope she is stronger than she looks. Oh, I really am getting attached to her, she is so gentle and high bred. Gen. Pinkney’s brother was once Minister to Bolivia.”

text: He was rough-cut, full-bearded, and looked as if constant wind had scoured every last trace of softness from his skin. He told us he’d just come off the long northern trail, stepping ashore only moments before at one of the North River ferry docks. I could have sworn fine, powdery snow from Chilcoot Pass still flecked the slope of his shoulders. Next, he spread the classic haul of a returning Klondike prospector all across the table: glinting gold nuggets, taxidermied ptarmigans, delicate hand-beaded craftwork, and plush seal pelts, before launching into a giddy, boastful rant about the multi-million dollar fortune he’d staked up in the Yukon.
source_text: He was rugged and bearded and wind-dried. He had just come off the “trail,” he said, at one of the North River ferries. I fancied I could see the snow dust of Chilcoot yet powdering his shoulders. And then he strewed the table with the nuggets, stuffed ptarmigans, bead work and seal pelts of the returned Klondiker, and began to prate to us of his millions.

text: “No trouble here, officer,” he said, in a calm, reassuring tone. “I’m just hanging around waiting for a friend, that’s all. We actually set this meetup up twenty whole years ago. That probably sounds a little weird to you, right? Look, I can explain the whole thing if you want to make sure everything’s on the up and up. Around that same time, there used to be a restaurant right where this shop now stands—Big Joe Brady’s place.”
source_text: “It’s all right, officer,” he said, reassuringly. “I’m just waiting for a friend. It’s an appointment made twenty years ago. Sounds a little funny to you, doesn’t it? Well, I’ll explain if you’d like to make certain it’s all straight. About that long ago there used to be a restaurant where this store stands—‘Big Joe’ Brady’s restaurant.”

text: That would be unfiltered, heady adventure. Would you say yes to it? Not you. Heat would flood your cheeks with sheepish embarrassment; you’d fumble the roll, drop it clumsily, and keep moving down State Street, patting your coat front weakly as you hunt for the missing button. That’s exactly what you’d do, unless you are one of the rare, fortunate few in whom the unspoiled spirit of adventure has not yet fizzled out.
source_text: That would be pure adventure. Would you accept it? Not you. You would flush with embarrassment; you would sheepishly drop the roll and continue down Broadway, fumbling feebly for the missing button. This you would do unless you are one of the blessed few in whom the pure spirit of adventure is not dead.

text: “That sort of thing can turn a perfectly good man bad sometimes,” the tall man said. “You’ve been under arrest for ten minutes now, ‘Silky’ Bob. Chicago figured you might’ve swung through our neck of the woods, so they wired us saying they want a word with you. You’re coming without a fuss, right? That’s the smart move. Now, before we head to the station, I’ve got a note I was told to give you. You can read it right here by the window. It’s from Patrolman Wells.”
source_text: “It sometimes changes a good man into a bad one,” said the tall man. “You’ve been under arrest for ten minutes, ‘Silky’ Bob. Chicago thinks you may have dropped over our way and wires us she wants to have a chat with you. Going quietly, are you? That’s sensible. Now, before we go on to the station here’s a note I was asked to hand you. You may read it here at the window. It’s from Patrolman Wells.”

text: My self-proclaimed citizen of the world went by K. Thorne Hartwell, and you’d hear his name everywhere come next summer on the Atlantic City boardwalk. He was set to launch a new “spectacle” there, he told me, offering entertainment fit for royalty. From there, his conversation bounded across every parallel and meridian on the globe. He held the whole round world in his palm, so to speak, casual and dismissive, and it seemed no bigger than the pit of a maraschino cherry tucked inside a prix-fixe ruby grapefruit. He scoffed at the equator, hopped from continent to continent like they were cobblestones in a lane, jeered at the temperate and tropical zones, blotted at the high seas with the edge of his linen napkin. With a flick of his wrist he’d mention a hidden spice bazaar in Ahmedabad. Whoosh! Next you were carving cross-country skis through Arctic Norway’s snow drifts. Snap! You were riding frothy breakers with native Hawaiian surfers at Honolua Bay. Quick as a wink, he dragged you through a tupelo swamp in southern Mississippi, let you dry off for a minute on the wind-scoured alkali flats of his Wyoming ranch, then spun you straight into the drawing rooms of Austrian countesses. A moment later he’d be recounting the hacking cold he caught off a Lake Michigan gust in Milwaukee, and how old Doña Marisol had cured it in Lima with a steaming steep of the rare quechua herb. You could have addressed an envelope to “K. Thorne Hartwell, Esq., The Earth, Solar System, The Entire Universe,” dropped it in any post box, and felt fully confident it would be delivered straight to him.
source_text: My cosmopolite was named E. Rushmore Coglan, and he will be heard from next summer at Coney Island. He is to establish a new “attraction” there, he informed me, offering kingly diversion. And then his conversation rang along parallels of latitude and longitude. He took the great, round world in his hand, so to speak, familiarly, contemptuously, and it seemed no larger than the seed of a Maraschino cherry in a _table d’hôte_ grape fruit. He spoke disrespectfully of the equator, he skipped from continent to continent, he derided the zones, he mopped up the high seas with his napkin. With a wave of his hand he would speak of a certain bazaar in Hyderabad. Whiff! He would have you on skis in Lapland. Zip! Now you rode the breakers with the Kanakas at Kealaikahiki. Presto! He dragged you through an Arkansas post-oak swamp, let you dry for a moment on the alkali plains of his Idaho ranch, then whirled you into the society of Viennese archdukes. Anon he would be telling you of a cold he acquired in a Chicago lake breeze and how old Escamila cured it in Buenos Ayres with a hot infusion of the _chuchula_ weed. You would have addressed a letter to “E. Rushmore Coglan, Esq., the Earth, Solar System, the Universe,” and have mailed it, feeling confident that it would be delivered to him.

text: Right as the megaphone blares outside that iconic hotel, let me murmur into the low-tuned personal earpiece to tell you to stay put: something big is about to unfold right now, and the sprawling metropolis will swallow every trace of it afterward the same way it absorbs a stray scrap of ticker tape drifting down from the lair of a Broad Street market bear.
source_text: While the megaphone barks at a famous hostelry, let me whisper you through the low-tuned cardiaphone to sit tight; for now things are about to happen, and the great city will close over them again as over a scrap of ticker tape floating down from the den of a Broad street bear.

text: While out-of-town passersby were utterly captivated by the enchanting Aileen, the establishment’s regular patrons were outright adorers. Fierce rivalry simmered among many of the long-time steady customers. Aileen could have had a social engagement lined up for every single evening if she’d wished. At least twice a week, one of her suitors took her to a theater performance or a local dance. One portly gentleman, whom she and Tildy had privately nicknamed “The Hog,” gifted her a delicate turquoise ring. Another regular known as “Freshy,” who drove a repair wagon for the Traction Company, promised to give her a fluffy poodle as soon as his brother secured the Ninth District hauling contract. And the man who always ordered spareribs and spinach and claimed he was a stockbroker asked her to attend a production of *Parsifal* with him.
source_text: If the transients were entranced by the fascinating Aileen, the regulars were her adorers. There was much rivalry among many of the steady customers. Aileen could have had an engagement every evening. At least twice a week some one took her to a theatre or to a dance. One stout gentleman whom she and Tildy had privately christened “The Hog” presented her with a turquoise ring. Another one known as “Freshy,” who rode on the Traction Company’s repair wagon, was going to give her a poodle as soon as his brother got the hauling contract in the Ninth. And the man who always ate spareribs and spinach and said he was a stock broker asked her to go to “Parsifal” with him.

text: A fold-out couch-bed, dresser, plain table, washstand, and single hard chair: that was the full extent of the furnishings the landlady provided, and not a thing more. Every other belonging in the small room was Dulcie’s alone. Arranged across the top of her dresser were her most treasured odds and ends: a gilded china vase given to her by Sadie, a promotional calendar issued by a pickle company, a slim volume on interpreting dreams, a glass dish holding loose rice powder, and a cluster of artificial cherries tied with a soft pink ribbon.
source_text: Couch-bed, dresser, table, washstand, chair—of this much the landlady was guilty. The rest was Dulcie’s. On the dresser were her treasures—a gilt china vase presented to her by Sadie, a calendar issued by a pickle works, a book on the divination of dreams, some rice powder in a glass dish, and a cluster of artificial cherries tied with a pink ribbon.

text: I stepped out of my hotel and wandered down Broadway. Hunting for the sort of person I sought lent a bright, lively tang to every breath of air I drew. I was delighted to find myself in a metropolis so vast, so layered and richly varied. I sauntered at a leisurely pace, carrying myself with a quiet, pleased swagger, my heart swelling as I remembered I was a citizen of great Gotham: entitled to share in its sweeping magnificence and small, vivid pleasures, part of all its quiet glory and unmistakable prestige.
source_text: I left the hotel and walked down Broadway. The pursuit of my type gave a pleasant savour of life and interest to the air I breathed. I was glad to be in a city so great, so complex and diversified. Leisurely and with something of an air I strolled along with my heart expanding at the thought that I was a citizen of great Gotham, a sharer in its magnificence and pleasures, a partaker in its glory and prestige.

text: That single exquisite evening sifted from every string of seventy dreary workaday days was for Elias a wellspring of reviving, unspoiled joy. A young society hopeful has only one coming-out to her name; it lingers soft and unrivaled in her recollections long after her tresses have gone silver; but for Elias, every ten weeks unfurled a delight as sharp, as heady, as untouched by familiarity as that very first taste had been. To settle into a seat alongside fellow lovers of good cheer beneath fronded palm boughs, wrapped in the warm, curling drift of hidden orchestral music, to gaze at the regulars who called this gilded paradise their own and meet their curious, approving glances in return—what is a young girl’s first formal dance and flimsy cap-sleeved tulle gown when set beside such a triumph?
source_text: This one delectable evening culled from each dull seventy was to Chandler a source of renascent bliss. To the society bud comes but one début; it stands alone sweet in her memory when her hair has whitened; but to Chandler each ten weeks brought a joy as keen, as thrilling, as new as the first had been. To sit among _bon vivants_ under palms in the swirl of concealed music, to look upon the _habitués_ of such a paradise and to be looked upon by them—what is a girl’s first dance and short-sleeved tulle compared with this?

text: When Chandler’s gaze landed on the girl, he realized he was instantly, sharply curious about her. Her prettiness had a soft, refined edge to it, and her eyes held both gentle warmth and a playful spark. She wore a simple, affordable black dress that looked like the standard uniform most retail workers wear. Her glossy deep brown hair coiled neatly under a low-cost black straw hat, adorned with nothing but a single velvet ribbon tied into a bow. She could easily have posed as the face of the most upstanding, self-possessed sort of young working woman.
source_text: Chandler looked at the girl and found her swiftly drawing his interest. She was pretty in a refined way; and her eye was both merry and kind. She was inexpensively clothed in a plain black dress that suggested a sort of uniform such as shop girls wear. Her glossy dark-brown hair showed its coils beneath a cheap hat of black straw whose only ornament was a velvet ribbon and bow. She could have posed as a model for the self-respecting working girl of the best type.

text: Kraft was the first to make a move. He shot up from his seat and thumped the Klondiker squarely on the back. “Come on out with us for a drink,” he yelled. “Drinks first, we can get something to eat later.” Judkins latched onto one of his arms, and I snagged the other. Loud, giddy, completely impossible to push off, leaning hard into that silly, warm good-pal energy, we hauled him out of the restaurant and toward a nearby café, shoving his preserved bird specimens and those totally indigestible gold nuggets into his pockets as we went.
source_text: Kraft was the first to act. He leaped up and pounded the Klondiker’s back. “Come out and drink,” he shouted. “Drink first and eat afterward.” Judkins seized one arm and I the other. Gaily, roaringly, irresistibly, in jolly-good-fellow style, we dragged him from the restaurant to a café, stuffing his pockets with his embalmed birds and indigestible nuggets.

text: Genuine adventurers have never been all that common. Most of the people labeled as such in printed accounts are basically just enterprising people with clever, newly devised tactics. They set out chasing specific prizes they’ve set their hearts on: golden fleeces, holy grails, the affection of beloved women, hoards of treasure, royal crowns, and widespread fame. A real adventurer, by contrast, heads out aimlessly and without pre-planned calculations, ready to meet and embrace whatever unforeseen fate has waiting for them. One perfect example of this is the Prodigal Son, specifically the moment he began his journey back home.
source_text: True adventurers have never been plentiful. They who are set down in print as such have been mostly business men with newly invented methods. They have been out after the things they wanted—golden fleeces, holy grails, lady loves, treasure, crowns and fame. The true adventurer goes forth aimless and uncalculating to meet and greet unknown fate. A fine example was the Prodigal Son—when he started back home.

text: Dulcie worked at a neighborhood department store. She sold everything from Hamburg edging to stuffed peppers to automobiles, along with all the other small trinkets and odds and ends that line department store shelves. Out of all the value she brought in, Dulcie only received six dollars per week in pay. The remainder was nominally credited to her name, but debited straight to another party’s account in the ledger kept by G––– Oh, you refer to that as primal energy, Reverend Doctor? Very well then, in the Ledger of Primal Energy.
source_text: Dulcie worked in a department store. She sold Hamburg edging, or stuffed peppers, or automobiles, or other little trinkets such as they keep in department stores. Of what she earned, Dulcie received six dollars per week. The remainder was credited to her and debited to somebody else’s account in the ledger kept by G–––– Oh, primal energy, you say, Reverend Doctor—Well then, in the Ledger of Primal Energy.

text: He secured the small back room, paying a full week’s rent upfront before raising any other requests. Bone-tired, he explained, and wanted to claim the space immediately. He fished crumpled bills from his coat pocket and counted them out one by one. The room was already fully prepared, the landlady told him, right down to folded cotton towels and a pitcher of fresh well water. As she turned to step down the hall and leave him to settle in, he asked, for what felt like the thousandth time, the question that perpetually hovered at the tip of his tongue.
source_text: He engaged the room, paying for a week in advance. He was tired, he said, and would take possession at once. He counted out the money. The room had been made ready, she said, even to towels and water. As the housekeeper moved away he put, for the thousandth time, the question that he carried at the end of his tongue.

text: He stared at her for a split second, the memory of the cards flashing through his mind, and a sudden stabbing pang of jealousy shot through him. What if they’d fallen into the hands of someone else just as bold and curious as he was? He decided right away that she could never learn the truth. He would never let her know that he was aware of the odd, desperate workaround she’d been pushed to resort to when she was crippled by overwhelming hardship.
source_text: He looked at her for a moment, remembering the cards, and felt a sudden jealous pain. What if they had fallen into other hands as adventurous as his? Quickly he decided that she must never know the truth. He would never let her know that he was aware of the strange expedient to which she had been driven by her great distress.

text: “Every now and then,” she said, with a soft note of weariness lacing her tone, “Clementina really tries my patience. I’m afraid she never puts in nearly enough practice, so I end up going over the exact same corrections with her time and time again. What’s more, she wears nothing but head-to-toe white, which does get dreadfully monotonous after a while. But General Pinkney is the dearest old soul! I so wish you could know him, Joe. He stops by sometimes when I’m sitting with Clementina at the piano—he’s a widower, you know—and stands there plucking at his neat white goatee. ‘And how are the semiquavers and demisemiquavers coming along?’ he always asks.”
source_text: “Sometimes,” she said, a little wearily, “Clementina tries me. I’m afraid she doesn’t practise enough, and I have to tell her the same things so often. And then she always dresses entirely in white, and that does get monotonous. But Gen. Pinkney is the dearest old man! I wish you could know him, Joe. He comes in sometimes when I am with Clementina at the piano—he is a widower, you know—and stands there pulling his white goatee. ‘And how are the semiquavers and the demisemiquavers progressing?’ he always asks.

text: Dulcie climbed the stairs to her room, the rear unit on the third floor of a West Side brownstone. She turned on the gas. Any scientist will tell you diamond is the hardest substance known to humankind. They’re wrong. Landladies are familiar with a compound next to which diamond is as soft as putty. They pack this stuff right into the tips of gas burners; you can stand on a chair and dig at it fruitlessly until your fingers are pink and bruised. Not even a hairpin can pry it loose, so we might as well call it entirely immovable.
source_text: Dulcie went up to her room—the third floor back in a West Side brownstone-front. She lit the gas. Scientists tell us that the diamond is the hardest substance known. Their mistake. Landladies know of a compound beside which the diamond is as putty. They pack it in the tips of gas-burners; and one may stand on a chair and dig at it in vain until one’s fingers are pink and bruised. A hairpin will not remove it; therefore let us call it immovable.

text: “Old Man Riddle can’t stand me, not even a little,” the anxious suitor went on, dead set on laying out every point he had to make his case. “He hasn’t let Rosy so much as step out the front door with me for a full week. If they weren’t afraid of losing a paying boarder, they would’ve kicked me out ages ago. I pull in 20 bucks a week, and she’ll never regret ditching this place and running off with Chunk McGowan.”
source_text: “Old man Riddle don’t like me a little bit,” went on the uneasy suitor, bent upon marshalling his arguments. “For a week he hasn’t let Rosy step outside the door with me. If it wasn’t for losin’ a boarder they’d have bounced me long ago. I’m makin’ $20 a week and she’ll never regret flyin’ the coop with Chunk McGowan.”

text: “I was thinking,” Chunk went on, his tone bright with hope, “that if I could get my hands on one of those powders to give Rosy when I meet her for supper tonight, it might shore up her nerve and keep her from backing out of our plan to run off. I reckon she don’t need a whole mule team to drag her away, but women are always better at calling plays from the sidelines than they are at running the bases themselves. If the stuff works even just for a couple of hours, it’ll do the trick.”
source_text: “I thought,” went on Chunk hopefully, “that if I had one of them powders to give Rosy when I see her at supper to-night it might brace her up and keep her from reneging on the proposition to skip. I guess she don’t need a mule team to drag her away, but women are better at coaching than they are at running bases. If the stuff’ll work just for a couple of hours it’ll do the trick.”

text: “There’s a specific sort of ‘Metropolitan Roamer’ native to San Francisco,” he replied. “I know the label well enough, though no one’s ever asked me to pin down exactly what makes one before. Pointing you to a perfect example would be tricky. Off the top of my head, I’d describe him as someone incurable with that unique San Francisco fever of needing to witness and learn every little thing the city has to offer. His day doesn’t start until six in the evening. He sticks unflinchingly to all the unwritten rules of tailored dress and polished social graces, but when it comes to sticking his nose where he’s not invited, he could out-snoop a raccoon or a magpie without half trying. He’s the soul who’s chased bohemian haunts all across the city, from basement beer halls to sky-high cocktail lounges, from the Mission District out to the Richmond, until there’s not a corner left where folks don’t hack their pasta with a butter knife to save time. That’s the Metropolitan Roamer’s doing, plain and simple. He’s forever hot on the trail of some fresh novelty. He is pure nosiness, unapologetic cheek, and impossible to escape. Horse-drawn cabs were built for him, and gold-foil wrapped cigars, and that bothersome fad of hiring string quartets to play over supper. There are precious few of these men scattered across the city, but every odd little preference they pick up is adopted by everyone else before the month is out.”
source_text: “There is a type of ‘Man About Town’ in New York,” he answered. “The term is quite familiar to me, but I don’t think I was ever called upon to define the character before. It would be difficult to point you out an exact specimen. I would say, offhand, that it is a man who had a hopeless case of the peculiar New York disease of wanting to see and know. At 6 o’clock each day life begins with him. He follows rigidly the conventions of dress and manners; but in the business of poking his nose into places where he does not belong he could give pointers to a civet cat or a jackdaw. He is the man who has chased Bohemia about the town from rathskeller to roof garden and from Hester street to Harlem until you can’t find a place in the city where they don’t cut their spaghetti with a knife. Your ‘Man About Town’ has done that. He is always on the scent of something new. He is curiosity, impudence and omnipresence. Hansoms were made for him, and gold-banded cigars; and the curse of music at dinner. There are not so many of him; but his minority report is adopted everywhere.

text: If you’ve never stepped foot in Mabel’s Hearth Grill and Family Diner, you’re missing out. Because if you count yourself among the fortunate few who regularly splurge on high-end meals, you ought to be curious about how the other half eats their fill. And if you belong to the group for whom every line on a server’s check feels like a pressing concern, you need to seek out Mabel’s: there, you get every bit of what you pay for, in sheer volume at the very least.
source_text: If you do not know Bogle’s Chop House and Family Restaurant it is your loss. For if you are one of the fortunate ones who dine expensively you should be interested to know how the other half consumes provisions. And if you belong to the half to whom waiters’ checks are things of moment, you should know Bogle’s, for there you get your money’s worth—in quantity, at least.

text: The man with the megaphone hefted his clunky instrument of torment, and the inside of the massive tour bus immediately started thumping and throbbing like the heartbeat of someone who’d chugged far too much black coffee. Passengers on the upper deck white-knuckled the edges of their seats, while the elderly woman from Valparaiso, Indiana, shrieked to be let off right away. But before a single wheel even begins to turn, pause for a quick announcement over that loudspeaker: it will point out a notable point of interest on the great sightseeing tour that is life.
source_text: The megaphone man raised his instrument of torture; the inside of the great automobile began to thump and throb like the heart of a coffee drinker. The top-riders nervously clung to the seats; the old lady from Valparaiso, Indiana, shrieked to be put ashore. But, before a wheel turns, listen to a brief preamble through the cardiaphone, which shall point out to you an object of interest on life’s sightseeing tour.

text: “Goodbye, Billy,” she murmured, her voice so thin it almost dissolved before it could leave her lips. “You’re millions of miles out of reach, and you won’t even flicker once for me, will you? But you stayed right where I could catch sight of you most of the time, up there when there was nothing else to stare at but endless dark, weren’t you?… Millions of miles apart… Goodbye, Billy Jackson.”
source_text: “Good-bye, Billy,” she murmured faintly. “You’re millions of miles away and you won’t even twinkle once. But you kept where I could see you most of the time up there when there wasn’t anything else but darkness to look at, didn’t you? . . . Millions of miles . . . Good-bye, Billy Jackson.”

text: Amid the surging, clamoring, thrumming throng, one could catch fleeting glimpses of Ollie’s tall top hat, scuffed and dented by years of gale winds and driving rain; of his carrot-shaped nose, bashed by the rowdy, sportive offspring of millionaire magnates and by snappish, unruly fares; of his brass-buttoned forest green coat, widely admired by folks who frequented the blocks around O’Malley’s Taphouse. It was plain to see that Ollie had usurped his cab’s less dignified purpose, and was toting a very obvious “load.” Indeed, the figure can be extended to liken him to a baked goods delivery wagon, if we credit the testimony of a young bystander, who was heard to remark, “Ollie’s got a proper bun on.”
source_text: Among the shifting, sonorous, pulsing crowd glimpses could be had of Jerry’s high hat, battered by the winds and rains of many years; of his nose like a carrot, battered by the frolicsome, athletic progeny of millionaires and by contumacious fares; of his brass-buttoned green coat, admired in the vicinity of McGary’s. It was plain that Jerry had usurped the functions of his cab, and was carrying a “load.” Indeed, the figure may be extended and he be likened to a bread-waggon if we admit the testimony of a youthful spectator, who was heard to remark “Jerry has got a bun.”

text: And oh, brother Elias, never again boast of how all-conquering money is. A tiny token of genuine devotion—a thin little ring that symbolized endless, unmercenary affection—was the very reason our Jude found his lasting happiness. He dropped it onto the cobblestones mid-ride, and climbed out of the cab to retrieve it. Before they could nudge back into the street’s flow, the gridlock settled in. He spoke to the woman he’d loved from afar for ages, and won her over entirely while their cab was hemmed tight between wagons and pedestrians. All the gold in the world is dross compared to true love, Elias.
source_text: “And oh, brother Anthony, don’t ever boast of the power of money again. A little emblem of true love—a little ring that symbolised unending and unmercenary affection—was the cause of our Richard finding his happiness. He dropped it in the street, and got out to recover it. And before they could continue the blockade occurred. He spoke to his love and won her there while the cab was hemmed in. Money is dross compared with true love, Anthony.”

text: Right then, Della’s beautiful hair tumbled loose around her, rippling and glinting like a cascading fall of chestnut brown water. It hung all the way below her knees, so long it practically functioned as a second garment for her. A second later, she twisted it back up again, jittery and moving in quick, fumbling bursts. For a full minute she wavered, standing stock still, as one or two tears splattered onto the well-worn red carpet beneath her feet.
source_text: So now Della’s beautiful hair fell about her, rippling and shining like a cascade of brown waters. It reached below her knee and made itself almost a garment for her. And then she did it up again nervously and quickly. Once she faltered for a minute and stood still while a tear or two splashed on the worn red carpet.

text: In the space of a single breath, his chest thrumming with a strange, unaccustomed lightness, his whole being rose to meet this unforeseen shift in spirit. A sharp, unignorable urge seized him, hot and urgent, to fight back against the ruin he’d let creep over his life. He would haul himself out of the gutter he’d been wallowing in; he would carve out a worthy life for himself again; he would beat back the vice that had clamped its jaws around him for so long. There was still time, after all; he was barely past thirty, far from too far gone; he would dig up the bright, hungry ambitions he’d buried years prior and chase them down, no hesitation, no looking back. The low, warm peal of the chapel bells drifting down the street had sparked a full upheaval in his core. Tomorrow he would head straight for the bustling warehouse district along the river and lock down a job. A leather goods wholesaler had once offered him a spot as a delivery runner, back when he still showed up to meetings sober. He would track the man down first thing tomorrow and ask for the position, no pride, no excuses. He would amount to something in the world. He would—
source_text: And also in a moment his heart responded thrillingly to this novel mood. An instantaneous and strong impulse moved him to battle with his desperate fate. He would pull himself out of the mire; he would make a man of himself again; he would conquer the evil that had taken possession of him. There was time; he was comparatively young yet; he would resurrect his old eager ambitions and pursue them without faltering. Those solemn but sweet organ notes had set up a revolution in him. To-morrow he would go into the roaring downtown district and find work. A fur importer had once offered him a place as driver. He would find him to-morrow and ask for the position. He would be somebody in the world. He would—

text: At 7:45, the couple in the next apartment began to bicker loudly; the man on the floor above was straining to hit an E flat on his scuffed clarinet; the gas lamp dimmed to a faint gold glow; three gravel drays pulled up to unload, their rough clatter of shifting stone the only sound the hall phonograph ever seemed to envy; stray cats on the back fence crept slowly toward Hearthside Row. From all these small signs, Lila knew it was time to read. She pulled out *The Bell and the Thatch*, the month’s top non-selling title, propped her feet on her dented steamer trunk, and set off wandering through 14th century village lanes at Elias’s side.
source_text: At 7:30 the couple in the next room began to quarrel: the man in the room above sought for A on his flute; the gas went a little lower; three coal wagons started to unload—the only sound of which the phonograph is jealous; cats on the back fences slowly retreated toward Mukden. By these signs Sarah knew that it was time for her to read. She got out “The Cloister and the Hearth,” the best non-selling book of the month, settled her feet on her trunk, and began to wander with Gerard.

text: Only two subjects allow one to discourse with unfettered imagination, without the faintest possibility of being contradicted. You may hold forth at length about your own nocturnal dreams, and you may recount the strange, disjointed phrases you have heard a pet cockatoo squawk. Both the lord of the oneiric realm and that chattering feathered creature are entirely unfit to serve as corroborating witnesses, so no listener would dare challenge the veracity of your account. The unrooted tapestry of a half-remembered vision, then, shall furnish my theme—selected with small apologies and gentle regret, in lieu of the far more limited sphere of that garrulous bird’s trivial small talk.
source_text: There are but two subjects upon which one may discourse with a free imagination, and without the possibility of being controverted. You may talk of your dreams; and you may tell what you heard a parrot say. Both Morpheus and the bird are incompetent witnesses; and your listener dare not attack your recital. The baseless fabric of a vision, then, shall furnish my theme—chosen with apologies and regrets instead of the more limited field of pretty Polly’s small talk.

text: Today, of all days, was Harvey Maxwell’s nonstop, chaotic workday. The stock ticker stuttered as it jerked out spools of unsteady, fitful tape, and the desk landline seemed to have come down with a permanent case of the buzzes, droning on without pause. Crowds of people poured into the office, calling out to him over the railing in every possible tone: cheery, sharp, snappish, breathlessly excited. Courier boys darted in and out nonstop, clutching scribbled notes and urgent telegrams. The office clerks scrambled every which way, just like sailors fighting to keep their vessel steady in the middle of a raging storm. Even Pitcher’s usually impassive face softened into something that looked almost like real liveliness.
source_text: And this day was Harvey Maxwell’s busy day. The ticker began to reel out jerkily its fitful coils of tape, the desk telephone had a chronic attack of buzzing. Men began to throng into the office and call at him over the railing, jovially, sharply, viciously, excitedly. Messenger boys ran in and out with messages and telegrams. The clerks in the office jumped about like sailors during a storm. Even Pitcher’s face relaxed into something resembling animation.

text: Every Saturday night, the Clover Leaf Social Club hosted a hop in the East Side hall belonging to the Give and Take Athletic Association. To gain entry to one of these dances, you either had to be a member of the Give and Take—or, if you were part of the set that leads waltzes with the right foot, you had to hold a job at Rhinegold’s paper-box factory. There was one small exception: any Clover Leaf member was allowed to escort an outside guest for a single dance, or to be escorted by an outsider for that one number. But for the most part, every Give and Take member brought the paper-box factory girl he was sweet on, and barely any strangers could boast of having stepped foot on the dance floor at those regular weekly hops.
source_text: Every Saturday night the Clover Leaf Social Club gave a hop in the hall of the Give and Take Athletic Association on the East Side. In order to attend one of these dances you must be a member of the Give and Take—or, if you belong to the division that starts off with the right foot in waltzing, you must work in Rhinegold’s paper-box factory. Still, any Clover Leaf was privileged to escort or be escorted by an outsider to a single dance. But mostly each Give and Take brought the paper-box girl that he affected; and few strangers could boast of having shaken a foot at the regular hops.

text: We called her our Goddess of Grub, and she cut such a majestically imposing figure that you could only take her in with quiet awe. Her shirtsleeves were always rolled up past her elbows, no matter the time of day. She was strong enough to scoop all three of us self-styled musketeers up in one go, one under each arm, and toss us straight out the window if she’d felt like it. She was younger than every last one of us, but she carried that warm, uncomplicated, earthy maternal energy—like the very first mother of all humanity—that made her look out for us like we were her own from the very first day we wandered in. She doled out every snack and meal stocked in Cypher’s for us like a queen pouring offerings from an endless cornucopia, with zero care for how much it cost or how much inventory she was going through. Her voice rang out bright and clear as a massive silver bell; she smiled all the time, wide, toothy, and unreserved, like that golden first glow of sunrise spilling over mountain peaks. Every time I laid eyes on her, my mind immediately drifted to Yosemite, all that vast, unforced grandeur. Even so, I could never picture her existing anywhere outside of Cypher’s. It was like nature had placed her there exactly where she belonged, and she’d sunk deep roots and grown into the space beautifully. She always seemed genuinely happy there, and when she collected her small pile of pay dollars on Saturday nights, her cheeks flushed with the giddy delight of a kid who just got an unexpected, wonderful gift.
source_text: Our Goddess of Grub was built on lines so majestic that they could be followed only with awe. Her sleeves were always rolled above her elbows. She could have taken us three musketeers in her two hands and dropped us out of the window. She had seen fewer years than any of us, but she was of such superb Evehood and simplicity that she mothered us from the beginning. Cypher’s store of eatables she poured out upon us with royal indifference to price and quantity, as from a cornucopia that knew no exhaustion. Her voice rang like a great silver bell; her smile was many-toothed and frequent; she seemed like a yellow sunrise on mountain tops. I never saw her but I thought of the Yosemite. And yet, somehow, I could never think of her as existing outside of Cypher’s. There nature had placed her, and she had taken root and grown mightily. She seemed happy, and took her few poor dollars on Saturday nights with the flushed pleasure of a child that receives an unexpected donation.

text: A sudden thought struck the young architect unbidden: he would invite this girl to dine with him. It was exactly the missing piece of his lavish, if lonely, recurring feasts. The brief spell of elegant luxury he carved out for himself would feel twice as joyful if he could share it with a lady’s company. He had no doubt she was a gentlewoman—her bearing and the way she spoke settled that beyond question. And even though her attire was remarkably plain, he knew he would be thoroughly pleased to sit across a table from her.
source_text: A sudden idea came into the head of the young architect. He would ask this girl to dine with him. Here was the element that his splendid but solitary periodic feasts had lacked. His brief season of elegant luxury would be doubly enjoyable if he could add to it a lady’s society. This girl was a lady, he was sure—her manner and speech settled that. And in spite of her extremely plain attire he felt that he would be pleased to sit at table with her.

text: Hushed, unyielding, sprawling, the great metropolis has always stood its ground against its critics. They sneer it is as unforgiving as cold steel; they claim no thrum of compassion beats beneath its concrete skin; they liken its winding thoroughfares to barren pine barrens and fields of sun-scorched ash. But underneath the jagged, calcified shell of the Dungeness crab hides sweet, buttery, melt-in-your-mouth meat. Perhaps a less clumsy comparison would have been more prudent. Even so, no one need take umbrage. We would never label anyone a crab without first confirming they possess the requisite sharp, pinching pincers.
source_text: Silent, grim, colossal, the big city has ever stood against its revilers. They call it hard as iron; they say that no pulse of pity beats in its bosom; they compare its streets with lonely forests and deserts of lava. But beneath the hard crust of the lobster is found a delectable and luscious food. Perhaps a different simile would have been wiser. Still, nobody should take offence. We would call no one a lobster without good and sufficient claws.

text: “This here’s my plain little cottage,” he rumbled, “and I can tell by the dark in the upstairs windows that my missus has already turned in for the night. So I feel bold enough to offer what small hospitality I can, no fuss attached. I’d be right pleased if you’d step down to the lower cellar room we use for family meals, and share a bit of simple sustenance with me. I’ve got a lovely cut of cold roast fowl, a wedge of aged tangy cheese, and a bottle or two of hoppy ale tucked away down there. You’re more than welcome to come in and eat, for I’m in your debt for the fine distraction you gave me earlier this evening.”
source_text: “’Tis me humble dwelling,” says he, “and I begin to perceive by the signs that me wife has retired to slumber. Therefore I will venture a bit in the way of hospitality. ’Tis me wish that ye enter the basement room, where we dine, and partake of a reasonable refreshment. There will be some fine cold fowl and cheese and a bottle or two of ale. Ye will be welcome to enter and eat, for I am indebted to ye for diversions.”

text: This is how one bearer of the simple gold wedding band recognizes another standing in that spellbound glow that alights only once, fleetingly, for each person it touches. Ordinary men only register that a wedding has occurred when they spot tossed rice and satin ribbon bows strung around the space. But a bride knows another bride at a single unspoken glance. Between them flits instant comfort and shared understanding, in a language no man or widow has ever learned to parse.
source_text: Thus does one sister of the plain gold band know another who stands in the enchanted light that shines but once and briefly for each one. By rice and satin bows does mere man become aware of weddings. But bride knoweth bride at the glance of an eye. And between them swiftly passes comfort and meaning in a language that man and widows wot not of.

text: Joe Larrabee hailed from the post-oak flatlands of the Midwest, bursting with innate genius for visual art from the moment he left those rural stretches. At six years old, he drew a sketch of the town pump, with one of the community’s most prominent residents hurrying past it in a rush. The piece was framed and hung in the local drugstore window, right alongside the ear of corn that had an odd, uneven number of rows. When he turned twenty, he set off for New York, wearing a loose, flowing necktie and carrying a small pool of savings he’d stowed away far more tightly than that casual neckwear was knotted.
source_text: Joe Larrabee came out of the post-oak flats of the Middle West pulsing with a genius for pictorial art. At six he drew a picture of the town pump with a prominent citizen passing it hastily. This effort was framed and hung in the drug store window by the side of the ear of corn with an uneven number of rows. At twenty he left for New York with a flowing necktie and a capital tied up somewhat closer.

text: Bob: I showed up at the spot we agreed on right on time. The second you struck a match to light your cigar, I got a clear look at your face and recognized you as the man wanted in Chicago. I just couldn’t bring myself to carry out the arrest on my own, so I tracked down a plainclothes officer to handle the task for me.
source_text: _Bob: I was at the appointed place on time. When you struck the match to light your cigar I saw it was the face of the man wanted in Chicago. Somehow I couldn’t do it myself, so I went around and got a plain clothes man to do the job.

text: Before long, Sarah choked back her tears. The stack of cards had to be typed up, no exceptions. Still drifting in the soft, gilded afterglow of her dandelion-tinged daydream, she brushed her fingers lightly over the typewriter keys absentmindedly for a few moments, her mind and heart entirely off wandering the grassy meadow lane at the side of her young farmer. Soon enough, though, she snapped sharply back to the hard, stone-lined streets of Manhattan, and the typewriter immediately began to clatter and jolt like a strikebreaker’s speeding automobile.
source_text: By and by Sarah forced back her tears. The cards must be written. But, still in a faint, golden glow from her dandeleonine dream, she fingered the typewriter keys absently for a little while, with her mind and heart in the meadow lane with her young farmer. But soon she came swiftly back to the rock-bound lanes of Manhattan, and the typewriter began to rattle and jump like a strike-breaker’s motor car.

text: Every week, Chandler tucked one dollar away from his earnings. Once every ten weeks, with that small stockpile of savings, he bought himself a proper gentleman’s evening from the discount shelf of thrifty old Father Time. He dressed himself in the kind of fine garments worn by millionaires and heads of state, then headed for the city’s most glittering, ostentatious neighborhood, where he ate a meal curated for elegance and indulgence. Ten dollars is more than enough for a man to pull off the role of a carefree, wealthy idler flawlessly for a few hours. The sum covers a thoughtfully chosen meal, a bottle of reputable wine, appropriately generous tips, a smoke, cab fare, and all the usual small incidental costs that come with such an excursion.
source_text: Out of each week’s earnings Chandler set aside $1. At the end of each ten weeks with the extra capital thus accumulated, he purchased one gentleman’s evening from the bargain counter of stingy old Father Time. He arrayed himself in the regalia of millionaires and presidents; he took himself to the quarter where life is brightest and showiest, and there dined with taste and luxury. With ten dollars a man may, for a few hours, play the wealthy idler to perfection. The sum is ample for a well-considered meal, a bottle bearing a respectable label, commensurate tips, a smoke, cab fare and the ordinary etceteras.

text: Dear Dr. Arnold: I am writing to extend my sincerest gratitude for your deeply kind, perfectly timed aid to my daughter last Friday evening. She suffered a sudden flare-up of her longstanding heart condition while in the conservatory at Mrs. Waldron’s reception, and had you not been nearby to catch her as she collapsed and administer proper care immediately, we might very well have lost her. I would be greatly obliged if you would visit us soon to take charge of her ongoing treatment.
source_text: Dear Dr. Arnold: I want to thank you for your most kind and opportune aid to my daughter last Friday evening, when she was overcome by an attack of her old heart-trouble in the conservatory at Mrs. Waldron’s reception. Had you not been near to catch her as she fell and to render proper attention, we might have lost her. I would be glad if you would call and undertake the treatment of her case.

text: A hansom cab driver holds his own distinct worldview, one arguably more single-minded than that of people in any other line of work. Perched on the high, swaying seat of his vehicle, he regards all other people as wandering, trivial specks, unworthy of his attention unless they signal a wish to travel. He is Jehu, and you are merely goods in transit. Whether you are a president or a penniless vagabond, to the cabby you are nothing but a Fare. He stops to pick you up, cracks his whip, jolts every vertebra in your back as he trundles along, and sets you down at your stop.
source_text: The cabby has his point of view. It is more single-minded, perhaps, than that of a follower of any other calling. From the high, swaying seat of his hansom he looks upon his fellow-men as nomadic particles, of no account except when possessed of migratory desires. He is Jehu, and you are goods in transit. Be you President or vagabond, to cabby you are only a Fare, he takes you up, cracks his whip, joggles your vertebrae and sets you down.

text: Dearest gentle fairy, could you cross all those run-of-the-mill wishes off the list first? No piles of extra cash, no 40-horsepower touring car, no widespread public acclaim, no thick new head of hair, no election as president of the local boat club. Skip every last one of those, and instead—oh, please, just turn back the clock a little for us? We don’t ask for much, just the teeniest, tiniest sliver of our old honeymoon to relive. Just a single hour, sweet fairy, that’s all we need. It’s long enough to call back to mind how the soft grass and tall poplars looked that day, how the neat little bow of her bonnet strings sat tied right under her chin—even if we both know the hidden hat pins were what actually held the whole hat in place. You can’t swing that? Alright, fair enough. Hurry up with that touring car and the oil stock instead, then.
source_text: Dear kind fairy, please cut out those orders for money and 40 H. P. touring cars and fame and a new growth of hair and the presidency of the boat club. Instead of any of them turn backward—oh, turn backward and give us just a teeny-weeny bit of our wedding trip over again. Just an hour, dear fairy, so we can remember how the grass and poplar trees looked, and the bow of those bonnet strings tied beneath her chin—even if it was the hatpins that did the work. Can’t do it? Very well; hurry up with that touring car and the oil stock, then.

text: “Jim, my love,” she cried out, “please don’t look at me like that. I cut all my hair off and sold it—there was no way I could have gotten through Christmas without giving you a present. It’ll grow right back again, you won’t mind, will you? I just had to do it, honest. My hair grows terribly fast anyway. Say ‘Merry Christmas!’ Jim, and let’s be happy. You don’t know what a lovely—what a perfectly beautiful, fine gift I’ve got for you.”
source_text: “Jim, darling,” she cried, “don’t look at me that way. I had my hair cut off and sold because I couldn’t have lived through Christmas without giving you a present. It’ll grow out again—you won’t mind, will you? I just had to do it. My hair grows awfully fast. Say ‘Merry Christmas!’ Jim, and let’s be happy. You don’t know what a nice—what a beautiful, nice gift I’ve got for you.”

text: He pulled the second empty chair across the scuffed linoleum and settled into it. The steaming chamomile tea gilded the young woman’s irises and painted faint rose back into her sallow, hollowed cheeks. She picked at the bread and jam first, then tore into it with a delicate, ravenous hunger, sharp as a starved wren that’s stumbled across a forgotten windowsill feeder. She treated his presence and the kindness he’d extended as something unremarkable, expected even—not that she dismissed ordinary social niceties; but when one has been stretched thin past breaking point, the unspoken rules of politeness fall away to make room for raw, unvarnished need. But as warmth seeped back into her frozen fingertips and the tight knot of hunger in her stomach unclenched, the weight of those small, unwritten social rules crept back in, and she began to haltingly share what had led her there. It was the kind of tale the great gray city swallowed by the dozen each day, no more notable than a raindrop hitting the sidewalk: a sales clerk’s account of wages too meager to cover rent, whittled even thinner by arbitrary “infraction fees” that padded the department store’s annual profits; weeks missed from work after a bout of winter flu that left her too weak to stand; then a pink termination slip, no savings left, no prospects, and finally, the desperate rap of someone with nowhere else to turn on his forest-green apartment door.
source_text: He drew up the other chair. The tea brightened the girl’s eyes and brought back some of her colour. She began to eat with a sort of dainty ferocity like some starved wild animal. She seemed to regard the young man’s presence and the aid he had rendered her as a natural thing—not as though she undervalued the conventions; but as one whose great stress gave her the right to put aside the artificial for the human. But gradually, with the return of strength and comfort, came also a sense of the little conventions that belong; and she began to tell him her little story. It was one of a thousand such as the city yawns at every day—the shop girl’s story of insufficient wages, further reduced by “fines” that go to swell the store’s profits; of time lost through illness; and then of lost positions, lost hope, and—the knock of the adventurer upon the green door.

text: A kaleidoscopic throw rug, dotted with vivid blooms like some sun-drenched rectangular tropical cay, rested adrift in a billowing, grime-streaked sea of frayed floor matting. The gingham-patterned wall held the exact set of kitsch prints that hound every transient renter from one sublet to the next: The Cavalier’s Secret Tryst, The First Spat, The Bridal Supper, Cupid at the Well. The mantel’s crisp, unadorned silhouette was unceremoniously hidden behind a pert tasseled drapery, slung rakishly askew like the sashes worn by dancers in a vintage burlesque revue. Across its surface lay forlorn flotsam cast aside by the room’s previously marooned occupants when a lucky break had ferried them to a new home: a couple of cheap, chipped vases, headshots of stage actresses, a half-used medicine bottle, a handful of stray playing cards missing from their set.
source_text: A polychromatic rug like some brilliant-flowered rectangular, tropical islet lay surrounded by a billowy sea of soiled matting. Upon the gay-papered wall were those pictures that pursue the homeless one from house to house—The Huguenot Lovers, The First Quarrel, The Wedding Breakfast, Psyche at the Fountain. The mantel’s chastely severe outline was ingloriously veiled behind some pert drapery drawn rakishly askew like the sashes of the Amazonian ballet. Upon it was some desolate flotsam cast aside by the room’s marooned when a lucky sail had borne them to a fresh port—a trifling vase or two, pictures of actresses, a medicine bottle, some stray cards out of a deck.

text: “Can’t you see what I’m trying to say?” Maxwell asked, shifting restlessly in place. “I’m asking you to be my wife. I love you, Miss Leslie. I’ve been dying to tell you this, so I snatched the first spare minute when the office rush let up even a little. They’re paging me for a call right now. Tell them to hold a minute, Pitcher. Won’t you give me your answer, Miss Leslie?”
source_text: “Don’t you understand?” said Maxwell, restively. “I want you to marry me. I love you, Miss Leslie. I wanted to tell you, and I snatched a minute when things had slackened up a bit. They’re calling me for the ’phone now. Tell ’em to wait a minute, Pitcher. Won’t you, Miss Leslie?”

text: “Clementina insisted on having Welsh rabbit straight after her lesson,” she explained to Joe. “She’s such an odd little thing, craving Welsh rabbit at five in the afternoon, of all times. General Pinkney was there that day, too—you ought to have seen him sprint for the chafing dish, acting like there wasn’t a single servant in the whole house. I know Clementina’s in poor health; she’s so terribly nervous all the time. When she was serving the rabbit, she spilled a huge amount of it, boiling hot, right across my hand and wrist. It hurt like anything at first, Joe. That dear girl was so distraught over it! But General Pinkney, Joe—that old man almost came undone, he was so upset. He rushed downstairs and sent someone—they said it was the furnace man or another worker from the basement—off to the drugstore for some healing oil and supplies to bind the burn up. It doesn’t hurt nearly as much now, honestly.”
source_text: “Clementina,” she explained, “insisted upon a Welsh rabbit after her lesson. She is such a queer girl. Welsh rabbits at 5 in the afternoon. The General was there. You should have seen him run for the chafing dish, Joe, just as if there wasn’t a servant in the house. I know Clementina isn’t in good health; she is so nervous. In serving the rabbit she spilled a great lot of it, boiling hot, over my hand and wrist. It hurt awfully, Joe. And the dear girl was so sorry! But Gen. Pinkney!—Joe, that old man nearly went distracted. He rushed downstairs and sent somebody—they said the furnace man or somebody in the basement—out to a drug store for some oil and things to bind it up with. It doesn’t hurt so much now.”

text: The police officer was still lingering with his gaze fixed on the pair. All the harried young woman had to do was lift a single finger, and Soapy would effectively be on his way to the small, isolated haven he’d been chasing. He was already so caught up in imagining the cozy, warm air of the precinct that he swore he could feel it seeping into his bones. The young woman turned to face him fully, extended a hand, and curled her fingers around the sleeve of Soapy’s coat.
source_text: The policeman was still looking. The persecuted young woman had but to beckon a finger and Soapy would be practically en route for his insular haven. Already he imagined he could feel the cozy warmth of the station-house. The young woman faced him and, stretching out a hand, caught Soapy’s coat sleeve.

text: “That’s all well and good for you, Dele,” said Joe, prying at a can of peas with a carving knife and a hatchet like he was waging war on the tin, “but what about me? Do you think I’d ever let you hustle for pay while I dally in the world of high art? Not by the bones of Benvenuto Cellini, I wouldn’t! I’m more than able to sell newspapers or lay cobblestones, and bring in a dollar or two to boot.”
source_text: “That’s all right for you, Dele,” said Joe, attacking a can of peas with a carving knife and a hatchet, “but how about me? Do you think I’m going to let you hustle for wages while I philander in the regions of high art? Not by the bones of Benvenuto Cellini! I guess I can sell papers or lay cobblestones, and bring in a dollar or two.”

text: My cosmopolitan friend offered an elaborate farewell and departed, certain he’d spotted someone he knew through the swirl of smoke and the hum of chattering crowds. I was left then with the would-be periwinkle, who’d been reduced to nursing Würzburger wine, too far gone to expound any further on his dream of perching melodiously atop a valley’s highest point.
source_text: My cosmopolite made a large adieu and left me, for he thought he saw some one through the chatter and smoke whom he knew. So I was left with the would-be periwinkle, who was reduced to Würzburger without further ability to voice his aspirations to perch, melodious, upon the summit of a valley.

text: “I’d trade a million dollars before I’d let this tangled dual bind get off to a bungled start tonight,” he said. “I’ve got a tiny studio up in Crown Heights all prepared, with marigolds arranged on the table and a tea kettle set to boil the second we walk in. I already locked in a fire-and-brimstone preacher to wait for us at his parsonage at half past nine. This has to go through. That is, if Lila doesn’t switch up her mind again!” Mr. Hale fell silent, caught squarely in the grip of his own nagging doubts.
source_text: “I wouldn’t have this double handicap make a false start to-night for a million,” he said. “I’ve got a little flat up in Harlem all ready, with chrysanthemums on the table and a kettle ready to boil. And I’ve engaged a pulpit pounder to be ready at his house for us at 9.30. It’s got to come off. And if Rosy don’t change her mind again!”—Mr. McGowan ceased, a prey to his doubts.

text: “Exactly twenty years ago tonight,” the man said, “I ate dinner right here at Big Joe Brady’s with Jimmy Wells, my dearest friend and the finest fellow alive. He and I grew up together here in New York, as close as two brothers. I was eighteen back then, and Jimmy was twenty. The next morning I was set to leave for the West to make my fortune. You could never have dragged Jimmy out of New York; he was convinced it was the only place worth living on the entire earth. That night, we agreed we would meet back here exactly twenty years to the date and time, no matter what our circumstances were, no matter how far we’d have to travel to get here. We figured that in twenty years’ time, each of us would have worked out our fate and built our fortunes, whatever those turned out to be.”
source_text: “Twenty years ago to-night,” said the man, “I dined here at ‘Big Joe’ Brady’s with Jimmy Wells, my best chum, and the finest chap in the world. He and I were raised here in New York, just like two brothers, together. I was eighteen and Jimmy was twenty. The next morning I was to start for the West to make my fortune. You couldn’t have dragged Jimmy out of New York; he thought it was the only place on earth. Well, we agreed that night that we would meet here again exactly twenty years from that date and time, no matter what our conditions might be or from what distance we might have to come. We figured that in twenty years each of us ought to have our destiny worked out and our fortunes made, whatever they were going to be.”

text: The bistro wasn’t quite as ostentatious or overdone as the spot three blocks further down Michigan Avenue that Elias usually favored, but it came remarkably close. Every table was occupied by well-heeled patrons, a small chamber ensemble played low enough that easy, unstrained conversation never felt like a chore, and both the menu and the waitstaff’s attentiveness were unimpeachable. His dining companion, even in her thrifted wool hat and simple, unadorned cotton dress, carried herself with a quiet grace that lent an extra edge of elegance to her already striking features and willowy frame. And there was no mistaking the warm, near-admiring glint in her own soft, expressive face as she watched Elias, his manner lively but never unsteady, his bright, honest blue eyes sparkling as he spoke.
source_text: The restaurant was not so showy or pretentious as the one further down Broadway, which he always preferred, but it was nearly so. The tables were well filled with prosperous-looking diners, there was a good orchestra, playing softly enough to make conversation a possible pleasure, and the cuisine and service were beyond criticism. His companion, even in her cheap hat and dress, held herself with an air that added distinction to the natural beauty of her face and figure. And it is certain that she looked at Chandler, with his animated but self-possessed manner and his kindling and frank blue eyes, with something not far from admiration in her own charming face.

text: No. The answer was always no. Five straight months of endless asking around, and every single inquiry ended in the same inevitable dead end. Days he wore out his shoes questioning venue managers, talent agents, performing arts schools, and choral troupes; nights he lingered in theater audiences, from glittering marquee houses with all-star lineups all the way down to seedy, run-down music halls where the thought of stumbling on the very thing he ached to find made his throat go tight with dread. No one had loved her more deeply than he had, and he’d spared no effort to track her down. He was sure she was somewhere in this sprawling, water-ringed city, ever since she’d disappeared from home—but the city felt like some colossal, churning quicksand, its particles shifting constantly with no solid foundation, the tiny grains visible on its surface one day swallowed up by ooze and slimy muck the next.
source_text: No. Always no. Five months of ceaseless interrogation and the inevitable negative. So much time spent by day in questioning managers, agents, schools and choruses; by night among the audiences of theatres from all-star casts down to music halls so low that he dreaded to find what he most hoped for. He who had loved her best had tried to find her. He was sure that since her disappearance from home this great, water-girt city held her somewhere, but it was like a monstrous quicksand, shifting its particles constantly, with no foundation, its upper granules of to-day buried to-morrow in ooze and slime.

text: For Leo Marlow was shining down on her, calm, bright, unwavering, through the slanted glass skylight. The rest of the world had vanished entirely. She was sunk in a hollow of thick, unbroken blackness, with only that small square of pallid light framing the star she’d named on such a whimsical, and oh, so futile impulse. Ms. Hale must be right; it was Gamma, of the constellation Cassiopeia, and not Leo Marlow. And yet she could not bear to let it be Gamma.
source_text: For Billy Jackson was shining down on her, calm and bright and constant through the skylight. There was no world about her. She was sunk in a pit of blackness, with but that small square of pallid light framing the star that she had so whimsically and oh, so ineffectually named. Miss Longnecker must be right; it was Gamma, of the constellation Cassiopeia, and not Billy Jackson. And yet she could not let it be Gamma.

text: “I’ve circled the globe a full dozen times,” he said. “I know an Esquimau in Upernavik who orders all his neckties from Cincinnati, and I once came across a goat herder in Uruguay who won a prize in a puzzle contest run by Battle Creek breakfast food makers. I keep year-round rent on a room in Cairo, Egypt, and another in Yokohama. I have a pair of slippers set aside just for me at a Shanghai teahouse, and I never have to give instructions for how I like my eggs cooked, whether I’m in Rio de Janeiro or Seattle. This old world is awfully small, when you get right down to it. What’s the point of bragging about hailing from the North, or the South, or some grand old manor house tucked in a dale, or Cleveland’s Euclid Avenue, or Pike’s Peak, or Fairfax County, Virginia, or Hooligan’s Flats, or any other spot? The world will be a far better place when we stop acting like fools over some mildewed little town or ten acres of swampland, just because we happened to be born there.”
source_text: “I’ve been around the world twelve times,” said he. “I know an Esquimau in Upernavik who sends to Cincinnati for his neckties, and I saw a goat-herder in Uruguay who won a prize in a Battle Creek breakfast food puzzle competition. I pay rent on a room in Cairo, Egypt, and another in Yokohama all the year around. I’ve got slippers waiting for me in a tea-house in Shanghai, and I don’t have to tell ’em how to cook my eggs in Rio de Janeiro or Seattle. It’s a mighty little old world. What’s the use of bragging about being from the North, or the South, or the old manor house in the dale, or Euclid avenue, Cleveland, or Pike’s Peak, or Fairfax County, Va., or Hooligan’s Flats or any place? It’ll be a better world when we quit being fools about some mildewed town or ten acres of swampland just because we happened to be born there.”

text: Perched behind the cashier’s counter sits Bogle: cold, grubby, slow-moving, simmering with quiet ill-temper, as he takes your payment. Half-hidden behind a towering mountain of toothpicks, he counts out your change, files your receipt, and croaks a single offhand comment about the weather at you, like a toad puffing out a stray, uninvited ribbit. You’d be wise not to venture any response beyond a quick agreement with his meteorological observation. You are no friend of Bogle’s; you are just a sated, passing customer, and the two of you will likely not meet again until Gabriel blows his final dinner horn. Take your change and go—straight to the devil, for all he cares. That, in short, is Bogle’s entire stance toward you.
source_text: At the cashier’s desk sits Bogle, cold, sordid, slow, smouldering, and takes your money. Behind a mountain of toothpicks he makes your change, files your check, and ejects at you, like a toad, a word about the weather. Beyond a corroboration of his meteorological statement you would better not venture. You are not Bogle’s friend; you are a fed, transient customer, and you and he may not meet again until the blowing of Gabriel’s dinner horn. So take your change and go—to the devil if you like. There you have Bogle’s sentiments.

text: The apartment directly across the landing from ours was home to a woman who kept a stocky black-and-rust terrier. Her husband clipped a leash to its collar and took it out every evening, always returning bright and good-humored, a lilting whistle drifting down the hall ahead of him. One day I bumped noses with the terrier by the stairwell, and I prodded him as if to wring an explanation out of him.
source_text: In the flat across the hall from us lived a lady with a black-and-tan terrier. Her husband strung it and took it out every evening, but he always came home cheerful and whistling. One day I touched noses with the black-and-tan in the hall, and I struck him for an elucidation.

text: Schulenberg received his naturalized citizenship that very day. Before Sarah left his side, she got him to willingly agree to a formal arrangement. She would supply typed menus for all twenty-one tables in the restaurant: a fresh menu for each day’s dinner service, plus updated breakfast and lunch menus any time the food offerings shifted, or whenever the existing copies grew too worn to meet neatness standards.
source_text: Schulenberg became a naturalised citizen on the spot. Before Sarah left him she had him willingly committed to an agreement. She was to furnish typewritten bills of fare for the twenty-one tables in the restaurant—a new bill for each day’s dinner, and new ones for breakfast and lunch as often as changes occurred in the food or as neatness required.

text: Soapy tramped five whole blocks before he worked up enough nerve to make another attempt at getting himself arrested. This time, the setup that landed in front of him was what he foolishly deemed a total cinch in his own head. A modest, pleasant-looking young woman stood just before a store display window, gazing with bright, eager interest at its selection of shaving mugs and inkstands. Only two yards away from the window, a large, stern-faced policeman leaned against a fire hydrant.
source_text: Five blocks Soapy travelled before his courage permitted him to woo capture again. This time the opportunity presented what he fatuously termed to himself a “cinch.” A young woman of a modest and pleasing guise was standing before a show window gazing with sprightly interest at its display of shaving mugs and inkstands, and two yards from the window a large policeman of severe demeanour leaned against a water plug.

text: Either from the thick press of people crowding the sidewalk or the sparse trickle of pedestrians crossing the street, a young woman stumbled slightly before coming to a halt right beside the cab. Jerry, whose years on the job had given him a hawk’s razor-sharp gaze, spotted the motion instantly.
