He lunged toward the cab, knocking three or four gawking bystanders off balance and nearly going down himself—no, he hooked a hand around the cap of a fire hydrant and steadied himself, staying upright. Moving like a deckhand scrambling up rigging in the middle of a gale, Jerry hauled himself up into his driver’s perch. The second he was settled in place, the sloshing booze he’d knocked back at McGary’s stopped threatening to throw him off balance. He rocked back and forth atop the cab’s equivalent of a ship’s mizzenmast as steady as a steeplejack harnessed to the flagpole at the top of a downtown skyscraper.
source_text: From somewhere among the throng in the street or else out of the thin stream of pedestrians a young woman tripped and stood by the cab. The professional hawk’s eye of Jerry caught the movement. He made a lurch for the cab, overturning three or four onlookers and himself—no! he caught the cap of a water-plug and kept his feet. Like a sailor shinning up the ratlins during a squall Jerry mounted to his professional seat. Once he was there McGary’s liquids were baffled. He seesawed on the mizzenmast of his craft as safe as a Steeple Jack rigged to the flagpole of a skyscraper.

text: The trading floor of the Exchange roiled with upheavals as violent as hurricanes, landslides, blizzards, glacial shifts, and volcanic eruptions, and all those raw elemental disturbances played out in miniature, concentrated form inside the broker’s offices. Maxwell shoved his chair hard against the wall and worked through his transactions with the light, precise step of a toe dancer. He darted from stock ticker to telephone, from desk to doorway, with the honed, effortless agility of a trained harlequin.
source_text: On the Exchange there were hurricanes and landslides and snowstorms and glaciers and volcanoes, and those elemental disturbances were reproduced in miniature in the broker’s offices. Maxwell shoved his chair against the wall and transacted business after the manner of a toe dancer. He jumped from ticker to ’phone, from desk to door with the trained agility of a harlequin.

text: Lila spent a full two weeks lodging at Bramblewood Homestead. It was there that she found herself falling for Elias, the only son of the weathered old homesteader Mr. Hale. Plenty of farm folks have been courted, wed, and settled into quiet, self-sufficient lives on their own plots in less time than that. But young Elias was far from a traditional yeoman; he was a thoroughly modern agricultural innovator. He’d run a telephone line straight out to his dairy barn, and he could calculate down to the last bushel exactly how the following year’s prairie wheat yields would impact the harvest of potatoes he planted during the new moon phase.
source_text: Sarah stayed two weeks at Sunnybrook Farm. There she learned to love old Farmer Franklin’s son Walter. Farmers have been loved and wedded and turned out to grass in less time. But young Walter Franklin was a modern agriculturist. He had a telephone in his cow house, and he could figure up exactly what effect next year’s Canada wheat crop would have on potatoes planted in the dark of the moon.

text: “Oh,” wailed Mrs. Murphy, “’twas yesterday, or maybe only four hours ago! I don’t rightly know. But me little boy Mike is lost, gone he is. He was playin’ on the sidewalk just this mornin’—or was that Wednesday? I’m so run off my feet with work, it’s hard to keep track of dates. I’ve searched the whole house top to cellar, every last corner, and he’s nowhere to be found. Oh, for the love of Heaven—”
source_text: “Oh,” wailed Mrs. Murphy, “’twas yisterday, or maybe four hours ago! I dunno. But it’s lost he is, me little boy Mike. He was playin’ on the sidewalk only this mornin’—or was it Wednesday? I’m that busy with work, ’tis hard to keep up with dates. But I’ve looked the house over from top to cellar, and it’s gone he is. Oh, for the love av Hiven—”

text: Dempsey was the head of the association. He dressed in a crisp formal suit, and could pull off two one-handed chin-ups on a bar without effort. One of Big Mike O’Sullivan’s most trusted lieutenants, he never had to worry about consequences for his actions. No cop dared take him into custody. Whenever he smashed a pushcart vendor’s skull or shot a member of the Heinrick B. Sweeney Outing and Literary Association in the kneecap, an officer would stop by and say:
source_text: Dempsey was the leader of the association. He wore a dress suit, and could chin the bar twice with one hand. He was one of “Big Mike” O’Sullivan’s lieutenants, and was never troubled by trouble. No cop dared to arrest him. Whenever he broke a pushcart man’s head or shot a member of the Heinrick B. Sweeney Outing and Literary Association in the kneecap, an officer would drop around and say:

text: The bright May moon spilled silver light over Mrs. Murphy’s private boarding house. A quick glance at any almanac would confirm its glow stretched across a far vaster swath of territory beyond that small building, too. Spring was at its giddy peak, with hay fever hot on its heels. The parks had burst to vivid green with tender new leaves, and were dotted with trade buyers scouting stock for Western and Southern markets. Blossoms unfurled on every curb, and summer resort agents swarmed the sidewalks hawking their getaways; the air grew soft and balmy, and even the answers Lawson received had turned far less brusque. Every block hummed with the trill of hand organs, the gurgle of fountains, and the quiet clink of pinochle tiles mid-game.
source_text: The May moon shone bright upon the private boarding-house of Mrs. Murphy. By reference to the almanac a large amount of territory will be discovered upon which its rays also fell. Spring was in its heydey, with hay fever soon to follow. The parks were green with new leaves and buyers for the Western and Southern trade. Flowers and summer-resort agents were blowing; the air and answers to Lawson were growing milder; hand-organs, fountains and pinochle were playing everywhere.

text: “Mind yourself now,” the palm reader continues, her gnarled finger still pressed to the creases of my palm, “keep clear of a swarthy man and a fair-haired woman, for both will bring ye nothing but trouble. Ye’ll take a trip across open water very soon, and suffer a loss of coin along the way. There’s one line here that bodes excellent luck, though. A man is coming into your life who will fetch ye great fortune. Ye’ll know him the moment ye spot him, by his crooked nose.”
source_text: “Look out,” goes on the palmist, “for a dark man and a light woman; for they’ll both bring ye trouble. Ye’ll make a voyage upon the water very soon, and have a financial loss. I see one line that brings good luck. There’s a man coming into your life who will fetch ye good fortune. Ye’ll know him when ye see him by his crooked nose.”

text: Soapy had unshakable confidence in every part of himself from the lowest button of his vest upward. He was clean-shaven, his coat was presentable, and his crisp pre-tied black four-in-hand tie had been a gift from a lady missionary on Thanksgiving Day. If he could make his way to a table in the restaurant without raising suspicion, his gambit would be a success. Every inch of him visible above the table would give the waitstaff no cause for doubt. A roasted mallard duck, Soapy mused, would be just the thing—paired with a bottle of Chablis, followed by Camembert cheese, a demi-tasse, and a cigar. One dollar would be more than enough for the cigar. The total bill would not be steep enough to provoke any excessive retaliation from the café management, yet the rich meat would leave him sated and happy for the journey to his winter refuge.
source_text: Soapy had confidence in himself from the lowest button of his vest upward. He was shaven, and his coat was decent and his neat black, ready-tied four-in-hand had been presented to him by a lady missionary on Thanksgiving Day. If he could reach a table in the restaurant unsuspected success would be his. The portion of him that would show above the table would raise no doubt in the waiter’s mind. A roasted mallard duck, thought Soapy, would be about the thing—with a bottle of Chablis, and then Camembert, a demi-tasse and a cigar. One dollar for the cigar would be enough. The total would not be so high as to call forth any supreme manifestation of revenge from the café management; and yet the meat would leave him filled and happy for the journey to his winter refuge.

text: Sarah recognized the first card she’d typed up that afternoon immediately. The rayed, sunburst smudge in its upper right corner, left by a teardrop that had fallen on the page earlier, was still plainly visible. But in the space that was supposed to bear the name of the meadow wildflower, her fingers had struck entirely odd keys, guided by the clinging, vivid memory of their bright golden blossoms.
source_text: Sarah recognised the first card she had typewritten that afternoon. There was still the rayed splotch in the upper right-hand corner where a tear had fallen. But over the spot where one should have read the name of the meadow plant, the clinging memory of their golden blossoms had allowed her fingers to strike strange keys.

text: Mr. Seeders did not return for two whole days. In those intervening days, Tildy fully embraced her new identity as a woman worthy of being courted. She purchased new ribbons, styled her hair to match Aileen’s, and cinched her waist a full two inches tighter than usual. A giddy, delicious little fear lingered in her mind that Mr. Seeders might burst through the door without warning and shoot her with a pistol. He had to be desperately in love with her, after all; impulsive, smitten lovers were always given to blind, unthinking jealousy.
source_text: For two days Mr. Seeders came not again. During that time Tildy established herself firmly as a woman to be wooed. She bought ribbons, and arranged her hair like Aileen’s, and tightened her waist two inches. She had a thrilling but delightful fear that Mr. Seeders would rush in suddenly and shoot her with a pistol. He must have loved her desperately; and impulsive lovers are always blindly jealous.

text: Chandler drifted north up Broadway alongside the evening parade of well-dressed strollers. Tonight, he was as much a display for passersby to admire as he was a spectator drinking in the sights around him. For the next sixty-nine evenings, he’d be eating in his plain wool work clothes at questionable prix-fixe diners, grabbing hasty meals at fast-paced lunch counters, or making do with sandwiches and beer in his tiny, uninsulated bedroom off a shared apartment hallway. He bore that routine happily, for he was a true child of this glittering, raucous metropolis, and to him, one single night spent in the spotlight more than compensated for dozens of dull, unglamorous ones.
source_text: Up Broadway Chandler moved with the vespertine dress parade. For this evening he was an exhibit as well as a gazer. For the next sixty-nine evenings he would be dining in cheviot and worsted at dubious _table d’hôtes_, at whirlwind lunch counters, on sandwiches and beer in his hall-bedroom. He was willing to do that, for he was a true son of the great city of razzle-dazzle, and to him one evening in the limelight made up for many dark ones.

text: Elspeth, what words could I possibly offer to mark the cruel snuffing out of this radiant, fiery soul? What phrasing could ever capture the raw, hollow weight of my grief so you might grasp even a fraction of its depth? Every line I fumble to voice feels thin, feeble, utterly unequal to the ache in my chest. Tears stream down my cheeks; a heavy fog of thwarted hope hangs over every corner of my mind. Yet I am bound for England still, and perhaps there I will find some small scrap of consolation to ease this hurt.
source_text: Margaret, what comment can I make on the untimely extinction of this glorious spirit? What can I say that will enable you to understand the depth of my sorrow? All that I should express would be inadequate and feeble. My tears flow; my mind is overshadowed by a cloud of disappointment. But I journey towards England, and I may there find consolation.

text: Amid the mounting, unforgiving crush of peak midday trading, the broker suddenly caught sight of a tumble of honey-gold curls peeking beneath a nodding velvet hat trimmed with glossy ostrich tips, a faux sealskin short jacket, and a strand of beads as wide as hickory nuts that ended just above the floor with a dangling silver heart. These adornments belonged to a calm, self-assured young woman, and Carter, the office lead clerk, was waiting nearby to explain her purpose.
source_text: In the midst of this growing and important stress the broker became suddenly aware of a high-rolled fringe of golden hair under a nodding canopy of velvet and ostrich tips, an imitation sealskin sacque and a string of beads as large as hickory nuts, ending near the floor with a silver heart. There was a self-possessed young lady connected with these accessories; and Pitcher was there to construe her.

text: Putting all these words to page has lifted my mood considerably, dearest cousin, but that familiar sharp anxiety creeps right back as I wrap this note up. Please, Victor—write to us. Even a single line, even one solitary word, would feel like an absolute blessing to all of us here. We owe Henry a thousand thanks for his unfailing kindness, his steady affection, and the many letters he’s sent our way; we’re truly, deeply grateful. Farewell for now, cousin. Take good care of yourself, and I’m begging you—write back soon.
source_text: “I have written myself into better spirits, dear cousin; but my anxiety returns upon me as I conclude. Write, dearest Victor,—one line—one word will be a blessing to us. Ten thousand thanks to Henry for his kindness, his affection, and his many letters; we are sincerely grateful. Adieu! my cousin; take care of yourself; and, I entreat you, write!

text: Elizabeth had been so cheerful of late, and my calm, unflappable bearing had gone a long way toward keeping her mind at peace. But on the very day that was supposed to fulfill my dearest wishes and seal my destiny, she was sunk in quiet melancholy, hounded by a sharp premonition that something awful was about to happen. She was likely also fixated on the dreadful secret I’d promised to reveal to her the next day. My father, meanwhile, was completely overjoyed, so caught up in the chaotic bustle of last-minute preparations that he mistook his niece’s low spirits for the usual shy diffidence of a bride-to-be.
source_text: Elizabeth seemed happy; my tranquil demeanour contributed greatly to calm her mind. But on the day that was to fulfil my wishes and my destiny, she was melancholy, and a presentiment of evil pervaded her; and perhaps also she thought of the dreadful secret which I had promised to reveal to her on the following day. My father was in the meantime overjoyed, and, in the bustle of preparation, only recognised in the melancholy of his niece the diffidence of a bride.

text: But as I neared home, grief and fear overwhelmed me all over again. Night was closing in fast, too; by the time I could barely make out the dark, hulking shapes of the mountains, my mood had grown even grimmer. The whole landscape felt like some vast, murky vista of ill omen, and I had a vague, heavy premonition that I was fated to be the most wretched person alive. I wish I’d been wrong. That premonition was entirely correct, save for one single detail: of all the misery I’d pictured and dreaded, I hadn’t even grasped one hundredth of the agony I was actually destined to endure.
source_text: Yet, as I drew nearer home, grief and fear again overcame me. Night also closed around; and when I could hardly see the dark mountains, I felt still more gloomily. The picture appeared a vast and dim scene of evil, and I foresaw obscurely that I was destined to become the most wretched of human beings. Alas! I prophesied truly, and failed only in one single circumstance, that in all the misery I imagined and dreaded, I did not conceive the hundredth part of the anguish I was destined to endure.

text: “Calm yourself,” said Clerval, who had spotted the color draining from my face, “I won’t bring the topic up again if it disturbs you so much. But your father and cousin would be overjoyed to get a handwritten letter straight from you. They have no idea how unwell you’ve been lately, and they’re growing deeply anxious over your long silence.”
source_text: “Compose yourself,” said Clerval, who observed my change of colour, “I will not mention it if it agitates you; but your father and cousin would be very happy if they received a letter from you in your own handwriting. They hardly know how ill you have been and are uneasy at your long silence.”

text: The wind that had died away to the south now blew up with brutal force out of the west. The moon had reached its highest arc across the sky and was just starting to sink lower; clouds scudded past its face faster than a vulture in flight, dimming its soft glow, while the lake below mirrored all the restless commotion of the heavens above, its rippling surface made even more chaotic by the first unsteady waves starting to swell. All at once, a heavy, driving rainstorm crashed down.
source_text: The wind, which had fallen in the south, now rose with great violence in the west. The moon had reached her summit in the heavens and was beginning to descend; the clouds swept across it swifter than the flight of the vulture and dimmed her rays, while the lake reflected the scene of the busy heavens, rendered still busier by the restless waves that were beginning to rise. Suddenly a heavy storm of rain descended.

text: I intend to depart for that more distant town in a fortnight to three weeks’ time. Once I arrive, I plan to charter a ship—a simple enough arrangement, as I only need to cover the owner’s insurance premium—and hire as many crew members as I see fit from the ranks of sailors well accustomed to whaling expeditions. I will not set sail until the month of June. But when will I return? Ah, dearest sister, how can I answer that? If my journey succeeds, many months, perhaps even years, will pass before we meet again. If I fail, you will either see me again very shortly, or never see me at all.
source_text: I shall depart for the latter town in a fortnight or three weeks; and my intention is to hire a ship there, which can easily be done by paying the insurance for the owner, and to engage as many sailors as I think necessary among those who are accustomed to the whale-fishing. I do not intend to sail until the month of June; and when shall I return? Ah, dear sister, how can I answer this question? If I succeed, many, many months, perhaps years, will pass before you and I may meet. If I fail, you will see me again soon, or never.

text: Well, my dearest Elias, I suspect you are eager to be indulged in a little gossip concerning the good people of Avignon. The lovely Miss Elara Voss has already received a steady stream of congratulatory visits on her approaching marriage to a young English gentleman, Edward Hale, Esquire. Her plain, unassuming sister Margot wed Monsieur Thibault, the wealthy local banker, last autumn. Your favorite boyhood schoolmate, Julien Rousseau, has suffered a string of small misfortunes since the departure of Florian from Avignon. But he has already recovered his usual good cheer, and word has it he is on the point of marrying a quick-witted, charming Frenchwoman, Madame Leroy. She is a widow, and significantly older than Rousseau; but she is very much admired, and a universal favorite with everyone in town.
source_text: “Now, dear Victor, I dare say you wish to be indulged in a little gossip concerning the good people of Geneva. The pretty Miss Mansfield has already received the congratulatory visits on her approaching marriage with a young Englishman, John Melbourne, Esq. Her ugly sister, Manon, married M. Duvillard, the rich banker, last autumn. Your favourite schoolfellow, Louis Manoir, has suffered several misfortunes since the departure of Clerval from Geneva. But he has already recovered his spirits, and is reported to be on the point of marrying a lively pretty Frenchwoman, Madame Tavernier. She is a widow, and much older than Manoir; but she is very much admired, and a favourite with everybody.

text:
I passed the whole of the next day wandering the length of the Veyra valley. I paused at the headwaters of the Lyrath burn, whose waters well up from beneath a glacier that inches, unhurried, down from the mountain summits to dam the narrow valley floor. Sheer faces of colossal peaks rose before me; the glacier’s frozen, blue-tinged wall jutted out above my head; a handful of stunted, wind-scoured pines dotted the rocky ground around me. The hush of this grand throne room of wild, sovereign Nature was shattered only by the rush of churning rapids, the crash of a falling boulder, the low, thunderous rumble of an avalanche, or the sharp crack of compacted ice that echoed for miles along the ridges—ice that, by the quiet, unchanging work of nature’s unbreakable laws, was split and shattered time and again, as though it were nothing more than a fragile trinket in those laws’ unyielding grip.

These awe-striking, overwhelming views offered me the deepest comfort I could have hoped for in that state. They lifted me above every small, gnawing worry; though they could not erase the grief clinging to my bones, they softened it, lulled it to quiet. To some extent, too, they pulled my mind away from the dark, looping thoughts I had turned over and over for the past month. I retreated to rest when night fell; my slumbers, it seemed, were tended by all the towering, grand forms I had gazed upon that day. They clustered close around me: the unmarked, snow-dusted mountain summits, the glinting, sharp granite spires, the thick pine stands, the jagged, empty ravines, the golden eagle circling high among the wispy clouds—all of them drew near, and bid me be at peace.
source_text: I spent the following day roaming through the valley. I stood beside the sources of the Arveiron, which take their rise in a glacier, that with slow pace is advancing down from the summit of the hills to barricade the valley. The abrupt sides of vast mountains were before me; the icy wall of the glacier overhung me; a few shattered pines were scattered around; and the solemn silence of this glorious presence-chamber of imperial Nature was broken only by the brawling waves or the fall of some vast fragment, the thunder sound of the avalanche or the cracking, reverberated along the mountains, of the accumulated ice, which, through the silent working of immutable laws, was ever and anon rent and torn, as if it had been but a plaything in their hands. These sublime and magnificent scenes afforded me the greatest consolation that I was capable of receiving. They elevated me from all littleness of feeling, and although they did not remove my grief, they subdued and tranquillised it. In some degree, also, they diverted my mind from the thoughts over which it had brooded for the last month. I retired to rest at night; my slumbers, as it were, waited on and ministered to by the assemblance of grand shapes which I had contemplated during the day. They congregated round me; the unstained snowy mountain-top, the glittering pinnacle, the pine woods, and ragged bare ravine, the eagle, soaring amidst the clouds—they all gathered round me and bade me be at peace.

text: The days drag so sluggishly here, hemmed in on all sides by crusted frost and banks of unmelting snow! Even so, I have taken the second major step toward my long-planned undertaking. I have secured a sturdy seagoing vessel, and am occupied with recruiting my crew; the men I have already brought on board seem perfectly trustworthy, and are certainly possessed of unflinching, unshakable courage.
source_text: How slowly the time passes here, encompassed as I am by frost and snow! Yet a second step is taken towards my enterprise. I have hired a vessel and am occupied in collecting my sailors; those whom I have already engaged appear to be men on whom I can depend and are certainly possessed of dauntless courage.

text: Days and nights cycled by a handful of times, and the moon had shrunk to a thin sliver by the time I started telling my different sensations apart. Slowly, I could make out crisp details: the bright, running stream that gave me water to drink, and the trees that draped their leaves over me for shade. A thrill of joy shot through me when I first realized the soft, pretty sounds that kept drifting to my ears came from the small winged creatures that had so often darted between my eyes and the sun, cutting off the light. I also started noticing the shapes around me more clearly, and could pick out the edges of the glowing, bright ceiling of sky that arched overhead. Every now and then I tried to copy the birds’ sweet songs, but I couldn’t get the notes right. Other times I wanted to put the things I was feeling into my own kind of expression, but the rough, garbled noises that burst out of my mouth scared me so badly I went quiet all over again.
source_text: “Several changes of day and night passed, and the orb of night had greatly lessened, when I began to distinguish my sensations from each other. I gradually saw plainly the clear stream that supplied me with drink and the trees that shaded me with their foliage. I was delighted when I first discovered that a pleasant sound, which often saluted my ears, proceeded from the throats of the little winged animals who had often intercepted the light from my eyes. I began also to observe, with greater accuracy, the forms that surrounded me and to perceive the boundaries of the radiant roof of light which canopied me. Sometimes I tried to imitate the pleasant songs of the birds but was unable. Sometimes I wished to express my sensations in my own mode, but the uncouth and inarticulate sounds which broke from me frightened me into silence again.

text: As we walked side by side, Clerval tried to offer words of solace, though he could only give voice to his profound, heartfelt sympathy. “Poor William!” he said. “That dear, lovely child, he sleeps now beside his angel mother. Who that ever saw him bright and laughing, glowing with the beauty of youth, would not weep for his untimely, cruel loss? To die so wretchedly, to feel the cold grip of a murderer! What sort of wretch could snuff out that radiant innocence? Poor little fellow. We have only one consolation: his friends mourn and weep for him, but he is at rest. The sharp pang is done, his suffering is ended forever. Turf covers his gentle small form, and he knows no pain anymore. He is no longer the one deserving of our pity; we must reserve that for the miserable, broken loved ones he left behind.”
source_text: During our walk, Clerval endeavoured to say a few words of consolation; he could only express his heartfelt sympathy. “Poor William!” said he, “dear lovely child, he now sleeps with his angel mother! Who that had seen him bright and joyous in his young beauty, but must weep over his untimely loss! To die so miserably; to feel the murderer’s grasp! How much more a murdered that could destroy radiant innocence! Poor little fellow! one only consolation have we; his friends mourn and weep, but he is at rest. The pang is over, his sufferings are at an end for ever. A sod covers his gentle form, and he knows no pain. He can no longer be a subject for pity; we must reserve that for his miserable survivors.”

text: It fell on one of those soft afternoons when the small cottage household took their weekly rest from field work and fence mending—the gray-bearded patriarch plucked gently at his mandolin, the two young grandchildren perched cross-legged on the hearthrug hanging on every note—that I noticed Elias’s face held a quiet melancholy too heavy to mask. He huffed frequent, ragged sighs, and once his grandfather stilled his fingers on the instrument’s strings; I guessed from the soft tilt of his head that he was asking what weighed on his grandson’s mind. Elias answered in a bright, forced tone, and the old man had just lifted his hands to resume playing when a sharp rap sounded on the cottage door.
source_text: “It was on one of these days, when my cottagers periodically rested from labour—the old man played on his guitar, and the children listened to him—that I observed the countenance of Felix was melancholy beyond expression; he sighed frequently, and once his father paused in his music, and I conjectured by his manner that he inquired the cause of his son’s sorrow. Felix replied in a cheerful accent, and the old man was recommencing his music when someone tapped at the door.

text: The searing sorrow that first wracked me at the sight of the carnage curdled fast into white-hot rage and bottomless despair. They lay cold and gone, while I still breathed; their killer walked free too, and to tear him apart I would drag my heavy, hollow bones through every last weary day left to me. I sank to my knees in the cool grass, pressed my lips to the loam, and cried out through trembling teeth: “By the holy soil under my knees, by the ghosts of the slain that hover all around me, by the endless, fathomless grief gnawing through my ribs, I swear it. And by you, O endless Night, and all the shadowed spirits that tend your domains, I will hunt the fiend who wrought this suffering until one of us falls cold in a fight to the death. For this cause alone I will cling to life; to carry out this sacred vengeance I will let the sun still touch my face and walk the green wilds of this world, which I would otherwise have turned from forever the moment I saw their bodies. I call to you now, spirits of the beloved dead, and you, roving messengers of retribution, guide and strengthen me in this task. Let that accursed, hell-spawned brute choke on his own suffering; let him know the full weight of the despair that is eating me alive right now.”
source_text: The deep grief which this scene had at first excited quickly gave way to rage and despair. They were dead, and I lived; their murderer also lived, and to destroy him I must drag out my weary existence. I knelt on the grass and kissed the earth and with quivering lips exclaimed, “By the sacred earth on which I kneel, by the shades that wander near me, by the deep and eternal grief that I feel, I swear; and by thee, O Night, and the spirits that preside over thee, to pursue the dæmon who caused this misery, until he or I shall perish in mortal conflict. For this purpose I will preserve my life; to execute this dear revenge will I again behold the sun and tread the green herbage of earth, which otherwise should vanish from my eyes for ever. And I call on you, spirits of the dead, and on you, wandering ministers of vengeance, to aid and conduct me in my work. Let the cursed and hellish monster drink deep of agony; let him feel the despair that now torments me.”

text: Once I’d settled on that plan, I made my way across the northern highlands, and picked one of the most isolated islets in the Orkney archipelago as the spot where I’d carry out my work. It was perfectly suited to my purpose: little more than a bare crag, its steep cliffs pounded nonstop by churning sea waves. Its soil was almost entirely infertile, barely producing enough rough grass to feed a handful of scrawny cows, and just enough oats to grind into meal for the five people who called the rock home. Their lean, gaunt frames made obvious how meager their daily meals were. Any vegetables or bread, when they could splurge on those rare luxuries, and even their supply of fresh drinking water, had to be ferried over from the mainland roughly five miles away.
source_text: With this resolution I traversed the northern highlands and fixed on one of the remotest of the Orkneys as the scene of my labours. It was a place fitted for such a work, being hardly more than a rock whose high sides were continually beaten upon by the waves. The soil was barren, scarcely affording pasture for a few miserable cows, and oatmeal for its inhabitants, which consisted of five persons, whose gaunt and scraggy limbs gave tokens of their miserable fare. Vegetables and bread, when they indulged in such luxuries, and even fresh water, was to be procured from the mainland, which was about five miles distant.

text: Once I’d tidied my makeshift hovel and lined its floor with fresh, dry rushes, I drew back into the shadows—there was the silhouette of a man on the horizon, and the brutality I’d suffered at the hands of strangers the previous night was too sharp in my memory to risk letting him spot me. Before hiding, though, I’d secured my day’s sustenance: a loaf of rough, gritty bread I’d pilfered from a cottage doorstep, and a chipped clay cup that let me sip from the clear stream trickling past my refuge far easier than cupping my palms. The plank floor of my shelter sat a few inches above the ground, so it stayed completely free of damp, and its position right up against the cottage’s chimney stack kept it decently warm even in the cool late autumn air.
source_text: “Having thus arranged my dwelling and carpeted it with clean straw, I retired, for I saw the figure of a man at a distance, and I remembered too well my treatment the night before to trust myself in his power. I had first, however, provided for my sustenance for that day by a loaf of coarse bread, which I purloined, and a cup with which I could drink more conveniently than from my hand of the pure water which flowed by my retreat. The floor was a little raised, so that it was kept perfectly dry, and by its vicinity to the chimney of the cottage it was tolerably warm.

text: I stepped into the chamber where the corpse lay, and was guided straight to the coffin. How can I ever capture what I felt when I laid eyes on it? Even now I burn dry with horror, and I cannot call that terrible moment to mind without shuddering and sharp, searing agony. The official questioning, the presence of the magistrate and gathered witnesses, all faded from my memory like a wisp of dream the second I saw Henry Clerval’s motionless form stretched out before me. I gasped for air, and throwing myself across the body, I cried out, “Have my own murderous schemes robbed you too of life, my dearest Henry? I have already destroyed two; other victims wait to meet their fated end—but you, Clerval, my friend, my benefactor—”
source_text: I entered the room where the corpse lay and was led up to the coffin. How can I describe my sensations on beholding it? I feel yet parched with horror, nor can I reflect on that terrible moment without shuddering and agony. The examination, the presence of the magistrate and witnesses, passed like a dream from my memory when I saw the lifeless form of Henry Clerval stretched before me. I gasped for breath, and throwing myself on the body, I exclaimed, “Have my murderous machinations deprived you also, my dearest Henry, of life? Two I have already destroyed; other victims await their destiny; but you, Clerval, my friend, my benefactor—”

text: This tale left us badly shaken, and we kept scouring the thickets and overgrown paths for him until dusk settled cold over the hills, when Mara guessed he might have wandered back to the cottage on his own. He was nowhere to be found inside. We headed out again, pine torches flaring and guttering in the damp dark; I could not bear to sit still, not when I imagined my sweet little boy lost and alone, exposed to the chill mist and heavy dew of the autumn night, and Mara too was wracked with searing, unconsolable anguish. Shortly after five o’clock in the morning, I found my precious child—only the evening before I had watched him run and laugh, rosy and brimming with health—sprawled on the dewy clover, pale and motionless, the dark bruise of a killer’s fingerprint pressed deep into the soft skin of his throat.
source_text: “This account rather alarmed us, and we continued to search for him until night fell, when Elizabeth conjectured that he might have returned to the house. He was not there. We returned again, with torches; for I could not rest, when I thought that my sweet boy had lost himself, and was exposed to all the damps and dews of night; Elizabeth also suffered extreme anguish. About five in the morning I discovered my lovely boy, whom the night before I had seen blooming and active in health, stretched on the grass livid and motionless; the print of the murder’s finger was on his neck.

text: Among the first tasks I faced once I was well enough to leave my rooms was introducing Elias, my oldest and dearest companion, to the various department faculty at the college. In the process, I was subjected to a quiet, unspoken agony entirely unfit for the raw, still-festering wounds my mind carried. Ever since that cursed night when my years of toil reached their terrible conclusion, and my ruin began, I’d harbored a fierce, visceral revulsion even to the mention of the physical sciences. Even when my body had fully mended, the glimpse of a single glass beaker or brass distiller would send my nerves spiraling back into the same wrenching torment I’d suffered in the aftermath of that night. Elias had noticed, of course. He’d cleared every last piece of my lab equipment out of my sight, even arranged for me to switch to a new set of rooms, knowing I hated the old space that had once housed my workbench and shelves of reagents. But all his careful thought was for nothing when we visited the professors. Dr. Hale’s kind, warm praise of the extraordinary strides I’d made in my research felt like a blade twisted slow in my ribs. He caught on quickly that I hated talking about the subject, but he never guessed the real reason, chalking my quiet withdrawal up to shyness. He shifted the topic from my own work to the latest advances in the field, clearly hoping to rouse my interest and draw me out. What was I supposed to do? He meant only to be kind, but every word was torture. It felt as though he was laying out, one by one, the very tools that would later be used to drag out my death slow and cruel. I squirmed under his remarks, but dared not let a flicker of the pain I felt show on my face. Elias, who always had a sharp eye for others’ unspoken feelings, cut in gracefully, saying he knew nothing at all of the field and would far rather hear of lighter matters. The conversation shifted to far more general, harmless topics, and I thanked my friend with every fiber of my being, though I said nothing out loud. I could tell he was curious about my strange reaction, but he never pressed me to explain. Even though I loved him with a depth of devotion and respect I could never put into words, I could never bring myself to tell him of that event that haunted every waking and sleeping moment of my life. I feared that speaking it aloud, laying all the terrible details out for another person, would only etch it deeper into both our minds.
source_text: One of my first duties on my recovery was to introduce Clerval to the several professors of the university. In doing this, I underwent a kind of rough usage, ill befitting the wounds that my mind had sustained. Ever since the fatal night, the end of my labours, and the beginning of my misfortunes, I had conceived a violent antipathy even to the name of natural philosophy. When I was otherwise quite restored to health, the sight of a chemical instrument would renew all the agony of my nervous symptoms. Henry saw this, and had removed all my apparatus from my view. He had also changed my apartment; for he perceived that I had acquired a dislike for the room which had previously been my laboratory. But these cares of Clerval were made of no avail when I visited the professors. M. Waldman inflicted torture when he praised, with kindness and warmth, the astonishing progress I had made in the sciences. He soon perceived that I disliked the subject; but not guessing the real cause, he attributed my feelings to modesty, and changed the subject from my improvement, to the science itself, with a desire, as I evidently saw, of drawing me out. What could I do? He meant to please, and he tormented me. I felt as if he had placed carefully, one by one, in my view those instruments which were to be afterwards used in putting me to a slow and cruel death. I writhed under his words, yet dared not exhibit the pain I felt. Clerval, whose eyes and feelings were always quick in discerning the sensations of others, declined the subject, alleging, in excuse, his total ignorance; and the conversation took a more general turn. I thanked my friend from my heart, but I did not speak. I saw plainly that he was surprised, but he never attempted to draw my secret from me; and although I loved him with a mixture of affection and reverence that knew no bounds, yet I could never persuade myself to confide in him that event which was so often present to my recollection, but which I feared the detail to another would only impress more deeply.

text: When word reached the port of Trieste that Elias had been stripped of his family fortune and noble rank, the wealthy spice merchant commanded his daughter to banish all thoughts of her lover from her mind, and prepare at once to return to their home country. Lila’s generous, unbowed nature rebelled fiercely at this order; she tried to reason and plead with her father, but he stormed away from her in a rage, repeating his harsh, non-negotiable mandate as he went.
source_text: “When the news reached Leghorn that Felix was deprived of his wealth and rank, the merchant commanded his daughter to think no more of her lover, but to prepare to return to her native country. The generous nature of Safie was outraged by this command; she attempted to expostulate with her father, but he left her angrily, reiterating his tyrannical mandate.

text: Those thoughts settled me, and by mid-afternoon I drifted off into a deep, heavy sleep—but the fever thrumming through my blood kept any soft, peaceful dreams at bay. The horrific scene from the day before played on a nonstop loop right in front of my eyes: the women fleeing in terror, Felix wild with rage ripping me away from where I’d knelt at his father’s feet. I woke up completely drained, and realized night had already fallen. I crept silently out of my hiding spot, and slipped off to look for something to eat.
source_text: “These thoughts calmed me, and in the afternoon I sank into a profound sleep; but the fever of my blood did not allow me to be visited by peaceful dreams. The horrible scene of the preceding day was for ever acting before my eyes; the females were flying and the enraged Felix tearing me from his father’s feet. I awoke exhausted, and finding that it was already night, I crept forth from my hiding-place, and went in search of food.

text: It was a grim, dreary November night when I finally stood witness to the fruit of all my grueling toil. I was twisted up with an anxiety so sharp it edged close to outright agony as I arranged my life-giving instruments around me, ready to breathe a spark of being into the lifeless form stretched out on the floor at my feet. It was already one in the morning; rain pattered miserably against the windowpanes, and my candle was guttering down to its final dregs, when in the faint, wavering glow of the half-extinguished light, I saw the creature’s murky yellow eye flutter open. It pulled in a harsh, ragged breath, and a sharp convulsive tremor shook through all of its limbs.
source_text: It was on a dreary night of November that I beheld the accomplishment of my toils. With an anxiety that almost amounted to agony, I collected the instruments of life around me, that I might infuse a spark of being into the lifeless thing that lay at my feet. It was already one in the morning; the rain pattered dismally against the panes, and my candle was nearly burnt out, when, by the glimmer of the half-extinguished light, I saw the dull yellow eye of the creature open; it breathed hard, and a convulsive motion agitated its limbs.

text: Driven half by idle restlessness and half by plain curiosity, I slipped into the lecture hall, and M. Waldman walked in not long after I’d settled into a seat. This professor was nothing like his colleague. He looked to be roughly fifty years old, but his face radiated the deepest, most genuine kindness; faint streaks of gray dusted his temples, though the hair at the back of his head was still nearly all dark. He was short in stature but carried himself with remarkable, unbowed poise, and his voice was the softest, most melodic I had ever heard. He opened his lecture by walking through the full history of chemistry, touching on the wide range of advances made by generations of scholars, speaking with clear fervor as he named the field’s most distinguished trailblazers. Next, he offered a quick survey of the science’s current state, breaking down many of its most basic foundational terms for the room. After running through a small set of introductory preparatory experiments, he closed his talk with a glowing tribute to modern chemistry, phrasing that I have never once been able to forget:
source_text: Partly from curiosity and partly from idleness, I went into the lecturing room, which M. Waldman entered shortly after. This professor was very unlike his colleague. He appeared about fifty years of age, but with an aspect expressive of the greatest benevolence; a few grey hairs covered his temples, but those at the back of his head were nearly black. His person was short but remarkably erect and his voice the sweetest I had ever heard. He began his lecture by a recapitulation of the history of chemistry and the various improvements made by different men of learning, pronouncing with fervour the names of the most distinguished discoverers. He then took a cursory view of the present state of the science and explained many of its elementary terms. After having made a few preparatory experiments, he concluded with a panegyric upon modern chemistry, the terms of which I shall never forget:

text: Once my hunger had been fully sated, I turned onto the well-worn path leading to the cottage. The whole place lay still and peaceful. I crept into my small hovel, waiting silently for the usual hour when the family would rouse for the day. That hour came and went, the sun climbing high overhead, yet none of the cottagers stepped outside. I trembled violently, seized by dread that some horrible misfortune had occurred. The cottage interior was dark, and not a single sound of movement drifted out to me; there are no words to convey the gut-wrenching agony of that torturous suspense.
source_text: “When my hunger was appeased, I directed my steps towards the well-known path that conducted to the cottage. All there was at peace. I crept into my hovel and remained in silent expectation of the accustomed hour when the family arose. That hour passed, the sun mounted high in the heavens, but the cottagers did not appear. I trembled violently, apprehending some dreadful misfortune. The inside of the cottage was dark, and I heard no motion; I cannot describe the agony of this suspense.

text: But let me turn back to the matters closest to my heart. Will I ever see you again, after crossing thousands of miles of churning open ocean, sailing home around the frigid southern capes of either the African or South American continents? I am foolhardy to hope for so lucky an outcome, yet I cannot bring myself to dwell on the grimmer alternative. For now, send me a letter whenever you have the chance: your words may find me at my lowest, when I need that small, familiar comfort most to hold my spirits up. I love you more softly and deeply than I can put to words. If no word of me ever reaches you again, hold me in your affectionate thoughts always.
source_text: But to return to dearer considerations. Shall I meet you again, after having traversed immense seas, and returned by the most southern cape of Africa or America? I dare not expect such success, yet I cannot bear to look on the reverse of the picture. Continue for the present to write to me by every opportunity: I may receive your letters on some occasions when I need them most to support my spirits. I love you very tenderly. Remember me with affection, should you never hear from me again.

text: She went on to explain that, with Elizabeth’s permission, she had spent the evening of the murder at her aunt’s home in Chêne, a small village roughly one league outside Geneva. On her return journey at around nine o’clock, a man stopped her to ask if she had seen any trace of the missing child. Alarmed by his account, she spent several hours searching for the boy, only to find the gates of Geneva already locked for the night. Unwilling to rouse the residents of a nearby cottage who knew her well, she took shelter in their attached barn for the remainder of the night. She stayed awake for most of her time there, only dozing for a few minutes as dawn neared, when the sound of approaching footsteps jolted her awake. As the sky lightened, she left her makeshift shelter to resume her search for my brother. If she had wandered anywhere near the spot where his body was later discovered, she had no awareness of it. Her disorientation when the market woman questioned her was perfectly unsurprising: she had passed the entire night without proper rest, and poor William’s fate was still unknown. She could offer no explanation whatsoever for the picture in question.
source_text: She then related that, by the permission of Elizabeth, she had passed the evening of the night on which the murder had been committed at the house of an aunt at Chêne, a village situated at about a league from Geneva. On her return, at about nine o’clock, she met a man who asked her if she had seen anything of the child who was lost. She was alarmed by this account and passed several hours in looking for him, when the gates of Geneva were shut, and she was forced to remain several hours of the night in a barn belonging to a cottage, being unwilling to call up the inhabitants, to whom she was well known. Most of the night she spent here watching; towards morning she believed that she slept for a few minutes; some steps disturbed her, and she awoke. It was dawn, and she quitted her asylum, that she might again endeavour to find my brother. If she had gone near the spot where his body lay, it was without her knowledge. That she had been bewildered when questioned by the market-woman was not surprising, since she had passed a sleepless night and the fate of poor William was yet uncertain. Concerning the picture she could give no account.

text: My copy of *Plutarch’s Lives* held the life stories of the earliest founders of the ancient world’s republics. Its effect on me could not have been more distinct from that of *The Sorrows of Werter*. Werter’s brooding introspections had taught me only despair and quiet gloom, but Plutarch filled my mind with lofty ideals; he lifted me out of the miserable, narrow circle of my own bitter ruminations, so that I might revere and cherish the heroic figures of bygone eras. Much of what I read lay far beyond my own comprehension and lived experience. I held only a muddled, fuzzy understanding of kingdoms, vast stretches of land, powerful rivers, and endless oceans. Of cities and great throngs of people, however, I was entirely ignorant. The cottage of my protectors had been my only classroom for learning the workings of human nature, but this book unfolded grand, never-before-imagined landscapes of action. I read of men engaged in public affairs, ruling over their fellow humans or slaughtering them en masse. A fierce passion for virtue swelled in my chest, alongside a deep revulsion for vice, as much as I could grasp the meaning of those terms; I defined them only in relation to the pleasure and pain I had known, after all. Driven by these impulses, I naturally came to admire peaceful lawgivers Numa, Solon, and Lycurgus far more than figures like Romulus and Theseus. The gentle, close-knit domestic lives of my protectors anchored these impressions deep in my mind; had my first introduction to humanity been a young soldier burning with hunger for glory and bloodshed, I might well have absorbed entirely different beliefs and impulses.
source_text: “The volume of _Plutarch’s Lives_ which I possessed contained the histories of the first founders of the ancient republics. This book had a far different effect upon me from the _Sorrows of Werter_. I learned from Werter’s imaginations despondency and gloom, but Plutarch taught me high thoughts; he elevated me above the wretched sphere of my own reflections, to admire and love the heroes of past ages. Many things I read surpassed my understanding and experience. I had a very confused knowledge of kingdoms, wide extents of country, mighty rivers, and boundless seas. But I was perfectly unacquainted with towns and large assemblages of men. The cottage of my protectors had been the only school in which I had studied human nature, but this book developed new and mightier scenes of action. I read of men concerned in public affairs, governing or massacring their species. I felt the greatest ardour for virtue rise within me, and abhorrence for vice, as far as I understood the signification of those terms, relative as they were, as I applied them, to pleasure and pain alone. Induced by these feelings, I was of course led to admire peaceable lawgivers, Numa, Solon, and Lycurgus, in preference to Romulus and Theseus. The patriarchal lives of my protectors caused these impressions to take a firm hold on my mind; perhaps, if my first introduction to humanity had been made by a young soldier, burning for glory and slaughter, I should have been imbued with different sensations.

text: Still, there was one obligation I couldn’t shirk, and the thought of it finally won out over the wallowing, self-centered despair that had swallowed me whole. I had to return to Geneva without delay, to stand guard over the lives of the people I loved so dearly, and lie in wait for the murderer. If some stroke of chance led me to his hiding spot, or if he dared show his face to torment me again, I would strike with unerring aim, and put an end to the existence of that monstrous form I’d brought to life—this thing clad in a grotesque mockery of a body, which held a soul even more hideous beneath its surface. My father still wanted to push back our departure, terrified I couldn’t withstand the exhaustion of a journey, for I was a shattered wreck, a faint shadow of a whole human being. All my strength had vanished. I was little more than a skeleton, and a constant fever gnawed at my wasted frame night and day.
source_text: Yet one duty remained to me, the recollection of which finally triumphed over my selfish despair. It was necessary that I should return without delay to Geneva, there to watch over the lives of those I so fondly loved and to lie in wait for the murderer, that if any chance led me to the place of his concealment, or if he dared again to blast me by his presence, I might, with unfailing aim, put an end to the existence of the monstrous image which I had endued with the mockery of a soul still more monstrous. My father still desired to delay our departure, fearful that I could not sustain the fatigues of a journey, for I was a shattered wreck—the shadow of a human being. My strength was gone. I was a mere skeleton, and fever night and day preyed upon my wasted frame.

text: His voice grew fainter and more threadbare as he spoke, until at last, drained entirely by the effort, he fell into silence. Roughly half an hour later, he tried once more to form words, but no sound left his throat; he squeezed my hand feebly, his eyelids drifted shut for the final time, and the soft, luminous glow of a gentle smile faded slowly from his lips.
source_text: His voice became fainter as he spoke, and at length, exhausted by his effort, he sank into silence. About half an hour afterwards he attempted again to speak but was unable; he pressed my hand feebly, and his eyes closed for ever, while the irradiation of a gentle smile passed away from his lips.

text: So fell Dr. Corvin’s words—no, better to call them the edicts of fate, spoken expressly to unmake me. As he continued speaking, I felt my soul locked in grapple with a foe I could almost touch; each turn of his lecture plucked at the hidden wires strung through the core of my being, one after another, chord after resonant chord, until my whole mind narrowed to a single thought, a single vision, a single unshakable purpose. So much has already been achieved, the soul of Elias Thorne exulted—more, far more, will I accomplish; treading the faint paths others have already marked, I will cut an untrodden way forward, probe powers no mortal has ever uncovered, and lay bare the deepest, most closely guarded mysteries of creation for all the world to witness.
source_text: Such were the professor’s words—rather let me say such the words of the fate—enounced to destroy me. As he went on I felt as if my soul were grappling with a palpable enemy; one by one the various keys were touched which formed the mechanism of my being; chord after chord was sounded, and soon my mind was filled with one thought, one conception, one purpose. So much has been done, exclaimed the soul of Frankenstein—more, far more, will I achieve; treading in the steps already marked, I will pioneer a new way, explore unknown powers, and unfold to the world the deepest mysteries of creation.

text: Food grew scarce, all the same, and I would often wander for entire days searching fruitlessly for a handful of acorns to dull the sharp, twisting pangs of hunger. Once I accepted this unyielding state of things, I made up my mind to leave the spot I had inhabited up until that point, to search for somewhere my few simple needs might be met with less struggle. As I prepared to set off, I grieved deeply over the loss of the fire I had stumbled upon by chance, for I had no idea how to make a new one for myself. I spent hours turning the problem over carefully in my mind, but I was eventually forced to abandon any hope of replacing it. Pulling my cloak tight around my shoulders, I struck out through the woods, heading in the direction of the sinking sun. I wandered for three days before I finally came upon open, unfenced countryside. A heavy snowstorm had blown through the night before, painting every stretch of field the same stark, unbroken white. The sight was dreary and desolate, and I felt my feet growing numb, stung by the cold, damp crust that blanketed the ground.
source_text: “Food, however, became scarce, and I often spent the whole day searching in vain for a few acorns to assuage the pangs of hunger. When I found this, I resolved to quit the place that I had hitherto inhabited, to seek for one where the few wants I experienced would be more easily satisfied. In this emigration I exceedingly lamented the loss of the fire which I had obtained through accident and knew not how to reproduce it. I gave several hours to the serious consideration of this difficulty, but I was obliged to relinquish all attempt to supply it, and wrapping myself up in my cloak, I struck across the wood towards the setting sun. I passed three days in these rambles and at length discovered the open country. A great fall of snow had taken place the night before, and the fields were of one uniform white; the appearance was disconsolate, and I found my feet chilled by the cold damp substance that covered the ground.

text: The roaring, crashing waterfall clung to his thoughts like an all-consuming fixation: the jagged, towering rock outcrops, the sweeping mountain slopes, the dense, shadow-choked deep woods—every last one of their hues and contours felt to him like a physical craving, a visceral feeling, a fierce, unthinking devotion that needed no distant, intellectually manufactured allure, no flicker of fondness that didn’t come directly, unfiltered, straight from what his own eyes took in.
source_text: ——The sounding cataract Haunted him like a passion: the tall rock, The mountain, and the deep and gloomy wood, Their colours and their forms, were then to him An appetite; a feeling, and a love, That had no need of a remoter charm, By thought supplied, or any interest Unborrow’d from the eye.

text: We muddled through a handful of gut-wrenching hours until eleven o’clock, when the trial was scheduled to begin. Since my father and every other member of my family were required to appear as witnesses, I went with them to the courthouse. The entire time that farce of a proceeding dragged on, I was in unrelenting, living agony. The court was set to rule on a question that would seal the fates of two people, all because of the reckless, unregulated experiments I’d pursued out of my own selfish curiosity: the first was a grinning baby, full of pure, unspoiled joy, and the second had been killed so brutally, with such layers of cruelty and infamy, that the crime would be remembered for its unthinkable horror for years. Justine, too, was a kind, deserving young woman who had every trait that should have guaranteed her a long, joyful life. Now all of that potential was going to be snuffed out in a shameful, unmarked grave, and it was all my fault. I would have confessed to the crime they were charging Justine with a thousand times over if it would have helped, but I’d been somewhere else entirely when the murder happened. Any claim I made to be the killer would have been written off as the ramblings of a lunatic, and it wouldn’t have done a thing to exonerate the woman who was suffering only because of me.
source_text: We passed a few sad hours until eleven o’clock, when the trial was to commence. My father and the rest of the family being obliged to attend as witnesses, I accompanied them to the court. During the whole of this wretched mockery of justice I suffered living torture. It was to be decided whether the result of my curiosity and lawless devices would cause the death of two of my fellow beings: one a smiling babe full of innocence and joy, the other far more dreadfully murdered, with every aggravation of infamy that could make the murder memorable in horror. Justine also was a girl of merit and possessed qualities which promised to render her life happy; now all was to be obliterated in an ignominious grave, and I the cause! A thousand times rather would I have confessed myself guilty of the crime ascribed to Justine, but I was absent when it was committed, and such a declaration would have been considered as the ravings of a madman and would not have exculpated her who suffered through me.

text: Those words left me deeply unsettled. I had not lost all hope, nor had I even begun to consider the idea of returning home if I was set free. Even so, could I in good conscience, or even realistically, refuse this demand? I held back from responding for a long beat, when Frankenstein—who had been silent up to that point, looking so worn thin he could barely pay attention to what was being said—suddenly roused himself. His eyes sparked with light, and his cheeks flushed with a fleeting burst of energy. Turning to face the men, he said,
source_text: This speech troubled me. I had not despaired, nor had I yet conceived the idea of returning if set free. Yet could I, in justice, or even in possibility, refuse this demand? I hesitated before I answered, when Frankenstein, who had at first been silent, and indeed appeared hardly to have force enough to attend, now roused himself; his eyes sparkled, and his cheeks flushed with momentary vigour. Turning towards the men, he said,

text: I’m scribbling these few quick lines to let you know I’m unharmed, and making steady progress on my journey. This note will reach England aboard a merchant vessel currently sailing home from Archangel—far luckier than I am, since I likely won’t lay eyes on my native country for many years, if not longer. Even so, I’m in high spirits: my crew is fearless, and they seem fully locked in on our mission. The drifting slabs of ice that glide past us nonstop, a clear sign of how dangerous the territory we’re pushing into is, don’t appear to rattle them in the slightest. We’ve already reached an extremely high northern latitude, but it’s the height of summer right now. Though it’s not as warm as it would be back in England, the southern gales that are speeding us toward the shores I’ve so deeply longed to reach carry a soft, revitalizing warmth I never would have expected to find here.
source_text: I write a few lines in haste to say that I am safe—and well advanced on my voyage. This letter will reach England by a merchantman now on its homeward voyage from Archangel; more fortunate than I, who may not see my native land, perhaps, for many years. I am, however, in good spirits: my men are bold and apparently firm of purpose, nor do the floating sheets of ice that continually pass us, indicating the dangers of the region towards which we are advancing, appear to dismay them. We have already reached a very high latitude; but it is the height of summer, and although not so warm as in England, the southern gales, which blow us speedily towards those shores which I so ardently desire to attain, breathe a degree of renovating warmth which I had not expected.

text: Those were the first thoughts that ran through my head, but it didn’t take long for me to realize how exceptionally kind Mr. Kirwin had been to me. He’d arranged for the best room in the prison to be set up for me—though even the nicest option the facility had to offer was still utterly wretched—and he was the one who’d secured a doctor and a nurse to care for me. To be fair, he rarely stopped by to see me in person: for all he was deeply committed to easing the suffering of every human being he could, he had no desire to sit through the agony and disoriented, miserable ravings of a murderer. So he only popped in every now and then to make sure I wasn’t being neglected, but those visits were brief, with long gaps of time between them.
source_text: These were my first reflections, but I soon learned that Mr. Kirwin had shown me extreme kindness. He had caused the best room in the prison to be prepared for me (wretched indeed was the best); and it was he who had provided a physician and a nurse. It is true, he seldom came to see me, for although he ardently desired to relieve the sufferings of every human creature, he did not wish to be present at the agonies and miserable ravings of a murderer. He came, therefore, sometimes to see that I was not neglected, but his visits were short and with long intervals.

text: After leaving Derby, we continued traveling north, and spent two full months exploring Cumberland and Westmorland. By that point, I could almost fool myself into believing I was standing amid the Swiss mountains. The small patches of snow still clinging to the mountains’ northern slopes, the glassy lakes, and the frothy, crashing rush of the rocky streams were all familiar, beloved sights to me. We met a handful of new acquaintances here, too, who very nearly managed to draw me out of my quiet sorrow and make me feel genuine happiness, if only for a little while. Clerval’s joy was far greater than mine, however; being in the company of talented, thoughtful people made his mind expand, and he discovered far more capacity and creative resource in his own nature than he’d ever imagined he held back when he’d only spent time with people less curious or driven than himself. “I could spend my whole life here,” he said to me. “Surrounded by these mountains, I’d barely even regret leaving Switzerland and the Rhine behind.”
source_text: From Derby, still journeying northwards, we passed two months in Cumberland and Westmorland. I could now almost fancy myself among the Swiss mountains. The little patches of snow which yet lingered on the northern sides of the mountains, the lakes, and the dashing of the rocky streams were all familiar and dear sights to me. Here also we made some acquaintances, who almost contrived to cheat me into happiness. The delight of Clerval was proportionably greater than mine; his mind expanded in the company of men of talent, and he found in his own nature greater capacities and resources than he could have imagined himself to have possessed while he associated with his inferiors. “I could pass my life here,” said he to me; “and among these mountains I should scarcely regret Switzerland and the Rhine.”

text: Elara pasted on a bright, unhurried demeanor, even as she fought desperately to hold back the bitter, stinging tears that pricked the corners of her eyes. She pulled Liora into a tight, trembling embrace, her voice thick with half-swallowed emotion as she spoke: “Farewell, my sweet girl, dearest Liora, my one and only beloved friend. May the heavens in their boundless generosity bless and keep you; may this be the last sorrow you ever have to bear. Live, be full of joy, and share that happiness with everyone you meet.”
source_text: Justine assumed an air of cheerfulness, while she with difficulty repressed her bitter tears. She embraced Elizabeth and said in a voice of half-suppressed emotion, “Farewell, sweet lady, dearest Elizabeth, my beloved and only friend; may heaven, in its bounty, bless and preserve you; may this be the last misfortune that you will ever suffer! Live, and be happy, and make others so.”

text: Prior to this moment, I was already familiar with the most straightforward, well-documented laws of electricity. That day, we were joined by a prominent researcher in the natural sciences, and fired up by the strange destructive event we’d just witnessed, he walked us through a theory he’d developed around electricity and galvanism, ideas that felt entirely groundbreaking and mind-blowing to me at the time. Everything he laid out completely overshadowed the work of Cornelius Agrippa, Albertus Magnus, and Paracelsus, the three thinkers who’d long reigned over my intellectual fantasies. But for some perverse reason, seeing these idols of mine discredited made me lose all interest in the studies I’d spent so much time on up to that point. I was suddenly convinced that there was no real truth out there waiting to be uncovered, that no lasting, reliable knowledge was even possible. Every topic that had held my fascination for years felt cheap and worthless all at once. Caught up in one of those impulsive, irrational mental shifts that hit most of us hardest when we’re young, I abandoned all my old pursuits overnight. I wrote off natural history and every related field as a stunted, failed experiment, and felt nothing but scorn for this supposed area of study that couldn’t even cross the first threshold of genuine, verifiable knowledge. In that frame of mind, I turned my full attention to mathematics and all its related subfields, since they were built on solid, unshakable foundations, and seemed the only disciplines truly worth my time and effort.
source_text: Before this I was not unacquainted with the more obvious laws of electricity. On this occasion a man of great research in natural philosophy was with us, and excited by this catastrophe, he entered on the explanation of a theory which he had formed on the subject of electricity and galvanism, which was at once new and astonishing to me. All that he said threw greatly into the shade Cornelius Agrippa, Albertus Magnus, and Paracelsus, the lords of my imagination; but by some fatality the overthrow of these men disinclined me to pursue my accustomed studies. It seemed to me as if nothing would or could ever be known. All that had so long engaged my attention suddenly grew despicable. By one of those caprices of the mind which we are perhaps most subject to in early youth, I at once gave up my former occupations, set down natural history and all its progeny as a deformed and abortive creation, and entertained the greatest disdain for a would-be science which could never even step within the threshold of real knowledge. In this mood of mind I betook myself to the mathematics and the branches of study appertaining to that science as being built upon secure foundations, and so worthy of my consideration.

text: Once I’d reached the end of my account, I leaned forward slightly and spoke sharply, “This is the creature I hold accountable for every atrocity I’ve just recounted, and I demand you marshal every resource at your disposal to seize it and see it punished. You are bound to this by your oath as a justice, and I trust, even hope, that your own moral compass as a private person will not rebel against fulfilling that office where this matter is concerned.”
source_text: When I had concluded my narration, I said, “This is the being whom I accuse and for whose seizure and punishment I call upon you to exert your whole power. It is your duty as a magistrate, and I believe and hope that your feelings as a man will not revolt from the execution of those functions on this occasion.”

text: I huddled in my drafty, overgrown shed for the rest of the day, sunk in dull, unthinking despair. The small family that had been my only lifeline to the rest of humanity was gone, snapping the single thread that had kept me anchored to anything good in the world. For the first time, hot, coiling vengeance and hatred bloomed in my chest, and I made no effort to rein them in. I let the current of rage sweep me away, my thoughts turning only to harm and destruction. When my mind drifted back to the people I’d grown to love, to Elias’s soft, weathered voice, Lila’s kind, dark eyes, and Zara’s luminous, otherworldly grace, the rage ebbed for a moment, and a flood of tears blunted the sharp edge of my pain. But the second I remembered how they had cast me out, how they’d turned their backs on me without a second thought, the fury roared back, white-hot and ungovernable. Unable to lash out at any living person, I turned my wild anger on the lifeless things around their home. As the darkness deepened, I stacked dry kindling and tinder all around the edges of the cottage, trampled every last sprig of planted herb and vegetable in the garden until no trace of their careful tending remained, and waited with a gnawing, restless impatience for the moon to dip below the horizon so I could set my plan in motion.
source_text: “I continued for the remainder of the day in my hovel in a state of utter and stupid despair. My protectors had departed and had broken the only link that held me to the world. For the first time the feelings of revenge and hatred filled my bosom, and I did not strive to control them, but allowing myself to be borne away by the stream, I bent my mind towards injury and death. When I thought of my friends, of the mild voice of De Lacey, the gentle eyes of Agatha, and the exquisite beauty of the Arabian, these thoughts vanished and a gush of tears somewhat soothed me. But again when I reflected that they had spurned and deserted me, anger returned, a rage of anger, and unable to injure anything human, I turned my fury towards inanimate objects. As night advanced, I placed a variety of combustibles around the cottage, and after having destroyed every vestige of cultivation in the garden, I waited with forced impatience until the moon had sunk to commence my operations.

text: As the sun grew milder and daylight stretched longer each week, the last of the frost and snow melted away, leaving only gnarled, leafless boughs and dark, loamy soil spread out before me. From that point onward, Elias was busier than he had been all winter, and the gut-wrenching little signs of coming starvation that I had watched for so long faded entirely. Their meals, I learned later, were still plain and rough, but nourishing, and they never went short of what they needed to get by. A handful of new, bright green shoots pushed up through the garden beds, which they tended carefully each afternoon; these small markers of ease and security multiplied every day, as spring settled more firmly over the valley.
source_text: “As the sun became warmer and the light of day longer, the snow vanished, and I beheld the bare trees and the black earth. From this time Felix was more employed, and the heart-moving indications of impending famine disappeared. Their food, as I afterwards found, was coarse, but it was wholesome; and they procured a sufficiency of it. Several new kinds of plants sprang up in the garden, which they dressed; and these signs of comfort increased daily as the season advanced.

text: We’d arrived in England in early October, and by now the month was February. We therefore decided we would set off northward once another month had elapsed. We did not plan to take the main thoroughfare to Edinburgh for this expedition; instead, we would visit Windsor, Oxford, Matlock, and the Cumberland lakes, aiming to finish the entire tour around the end of July. I packed up my chemical instruments and all the materials I had collected, resolved to complete my work in some remote, little-known corner of the northern Scottish highlands.
source_text: We had arrived in England at the beginning of October, and it was now February. We accordingly determined to commence our journey towards the north at the expiration of another month. In this expedition we did not intend to follow the great road to Edinburgh, but to visit Windsor, Oxford, Matlock, and the Cumberland lakes, resolving to arrive at the completion of this tour about the end of July. I packed up my chemical instruments and the materials I had collected, resolving to finish my labours in some obscure nook in the northern highlands of Scotland.

text: All this while, the dark earth had grown thick with lush herbage, and the green banks were stippled with countless wildflowers, sweet to both the nose and the eye, tiny points of pale glow scattered like stars through the moonlit woods. The sun grew warmer as the season shifted, the nights turning clear and soft; my nightly wanderings brought me immense pleasure, though they were now considerably shorter, for the sun set late and rose early. I never dared venture out in daylight, terrified I would suffer the same harsh treatment I had endured when I first entered a human village.
source_text: “In the meanwhile also the black ground was covered with herbage, and the green banks interspersed with innumerable flowers, sweet to the scent and the eyes, stars of pale radiance among the moonlight woods; the sun became warmer, the nights clear and balmy; and my nocturnal rambles were an extreme pleasure to me, although they were considerably shortened by the late setting and early rising of the sun, for I never ventured abroad during daylight, fearful of meeting with the same treatment I had formerly endured in the first village which I entered.

text: “There’s no use trying to discuss it further,” Felix replied. “We can never live in your cottage again. My father’s life is in mortal danger because of the awful ordeal I described to you. My wife and sister will never recover from the horror they endured. I beg you not to press me on this any longer. Take back your home, and let me flee this place at once.”
source_text: “‘It is utterly useless,’ replied Felix; ‘we can never again inhabit your cottage.
