The life of my father is in the greatest danger, owing to the dreadful circumstance that I have related. My wife and my sister will never recover from their horror. I entreat you not to reason with me any more. Take possession of your tenement and let me fly from this place.’

text:
I was perched on a stool in my lab one evening; the sun had dipped fully below the horizon, and the moon was just peeking up over the surface of the sea. There wasn’t enough light to tend to the fine, delicate work my project required, so I sat idle, stuck weighing whether I should call it quits for the night, or hunker down and push through nonstop to bring the work to a faster close.

As I lingered there, a whole cascade of thoughts unspooled in my head, forcing me to confront exactly what the consequences of my current labor could be. Three years prior, I’d been buried in the exact same kind of work, and I’d brought a demon into the world—one whose unspeakable cruelty had gutted me, leaving me trapped in unending, gnawing remorse. Now I was on the cusp of building a second creature, and I knew just as little about what her core disposition would be. She could end up a thousand times more vicious than the monster I’d already made, taking active, unprompted joy in inflicting murder and misery on anyone she crossed.

He’d promised to leave all human settlements behind and seclude himself in uninhabited desert wastes, but she’d never agreed to any such bargain. She would almost certainly be a fully conscious, rational being, after all—why would she honor a deal that was struck long before she even existed? The two of them might even hate each other outright. The creature that was already alive despised his own grotesque appearance; wouldn’t he hate that deformity even more when he saw it mirrored in a female counterpart? She might also recoil from him in revulsion, drawn instead to the far more familiar beauty of human men. If she left him, he’d be alone all over again, his rage stoked to brutal new heights by the fresh humiliation of being abandoned by one of his own species.
source_text: I sat one evening in my laboratory; the sun had set, and the moon was just rising from the sea; I had not sufficient light for my employment, and I remained idle, in a pause of consideration of whether I should leave my labour for the night or hasten its conclusion by an unremitting attention to it. As I sat, a train of reflection occurred to me which led me to consider the effects of what I was now doing. Three years before, I was engaged in the same manner and had created a fiend whose unparalleled barbarity had desolated my heart and filled it for ever with the bitterest remorse. I was now about to form another being of whose dispositions I was alike ignorant; she might become ten thousand times more malignant than her mate and delight, for its own sake, in murder and wretchedness. He had sworn to quit the neighbourhood of man and hide himself in deserts, but she had not; and she, who in all probability was to become a thinking and reasoning animal, might refuse to comply with a compact made before her creation. They might even hate each other; the creature who already lived loathed his own deformity, and might he not conceive a greater abhorrence for it when it came before his eyes in the female form? She also might turn with disgust from him to the superior beauty of man; she might quit him, and he be again alone, exasperated by the fresh provocation of being deserted by one of his own species.

text: I woke to bright morning light, and the first thing I hurried to check on was my fire. I pulled the covering off the embers, and a soft passing breeze caught them right away, stoking them up into a steady flame. I noticed how that worked, so I made a simple fan out of twigs and leafy boughs, perfect for stirring dying embers back to life when they were on the verge of going out. When night fell once more, I was delighted to realize the fire didn’t only give off warmth—it cast light too. I also found this new element was a huge help for preparing food: some of the scraps travelers had left behind had been roasted, and they tasted far richer and more flavorful than the wild berries I picked from the surrounding trees. So I decided to try preparing my own food the same way, setting it right on top of the glowing live embers. I quickly learned the berries got ruined by the heat, but the nuts and roots I’d foraged tasted far better cooked this way.
source_text: “It was morning when I awoke, and my first care was to visit the fire. I uncovered it, and a gentle breeze quickly fanned it into a flame. I observed this also and contrived a fan of branches, which roused the embers when they were nearly extinguished. When night came again I found, with pleasure, that the fire gave light as well as heat and that the discovery of this element was useful to me in my food, for I found some of the offals that the travellers had left had been roasted, and tasted much more savoury than the berries I gathered from the trees. I tried, therefore, to dress my food in the same manner, placing it on the live embers. I found that the berries were spoiled by this operation, and the nuts and roots much improved.

text: “I’m not insane,” I shouted, every word laced with fierce urgency. “The sun and the skies above have watched every single thing I’ve done—they can testify that I’m telling the truth. I’m the one who murdered those wholly innocent people; their deaths came directly from my own schemes. I would have spilled my own blood, drop by drop, a thousand times over to save their lives, but I couldn’t, Father. I truly could not bring myself to sacrifice the entire human race.”
source_text: “I am not mad,” I cried energetically; “the sun and the heavens, who have viewed my operations, can bear witness of my truth. I am the assassin of those most innocent victims; they died by my machinations. A thousand times would I have shed my own blood, drop by drop, to have saved their lives; but I could not, my father, indeed I could not sacrifice the whole human race.”

text: The son corroborated every part of his father’s story, but when Daniel Nugent was called to give his statement, he swore unreservedly that moments before his companion fell, he spotted a boat carrying just one man a short distance off the shore. As best as he could make out under the faint glow of the few scattered stars, that craft was the exact same one I had just stepped out of onto the bank.
source_text: The son confirmed his father’s account, but when Daniel Nugent was called he swore positively that just before the fall of his companion, he saw a boat, with a single man in it, at a short distance from the shore; and as far as he could judge by the light of a few stars, it was the same boat in which I had just landed.

text: The text Elias used to tutor Lila was Aris Volkov’s *Fallen Dominions*. I would never have grasped the book’s core meaning if Elias had not paused constantly while reading to offer exhaustive, granular context. He’d selected the volume, he explained, because its soaring, rhetorical cadence was deliberately crafted to echo the prose of Near Eastern writers. From its pages I picked up a rough, sweeping grasp of world history and a sense of the various empires that spanned the globe in that era; it offered me a window into the customs, governing systems, and spiritual beliefs of peoples across every continent. I learned of the languid societies of East Asia, of the extraordinary intellectual fire and creative verve of the ancient Greeks, of the brutal conquests and remarkable civic virtue of the early Romans—their slow moral decay, the collapse of that colossal empire, the rise of chivalric codes, Christian doctrine, and royal dynasties. When the text turned to the European arrival in the Americas, I cried alongside Lila for the tragic, unmerited suffering of its Indigenous populations.
source_text: “The book from which Felix instructed Safie was Volney’s _Ruins of Empires_. I should not have understood the purport of this book had not Felix, in reading it, given very minute explanations. He had chosen this work, he said, because the declamatory style was framed in imitation of the Eastern authors. Through this work I obtained a cursory knowledge of history and a view of the several empires at present existing in the world; it gave me an insight into the manners, governments, and religions of the different nations of the earth. I heard of the slothful Asiatics, of the stupendous genius and mental activity of the Grecians, of the wars and wonderful virtue of the early Romans—of their subsequent degenerating—of the decline of that mighty empire, of chivalry, Christianity, and kings. I heard of the discovery of the American hemisphere and wept with Safie over the hapless fate of its original inhabitants.

text: I made it to Geneva. Both my father and Ernest were still alive when I arrived, but the terrible news I carried shattered my father completely. I can see him even now, that kind, venerable old man. His gaze drifted vacant and unfocused, stripped of their usual spark and joy—all because he had lost Elizabeth, the girl he loved more than his own biological daughter, doted on with every last bit of tenderness a man holds in the twilight of his life, when he has so few loved ones left that he clings all the more fiercely to the ones that remain. Cursed, cursed be that fiend that heaped misery on his grey head and condemned him to waste away in utter wretchedness! He could not bear the weight of all the horrors stacking up around him; the very core of his will to live gave out all at once. He could not lift himself from his bed, and just a few days later, he breathed his last in my arms.
source_text: I arrived at Geneva. My father and Ernest yet lived, but the former sunk under the tidings that I bore. I see him now, excellent and venerable old man! His eyes wandered in vacancy, for they had lost their charm and their delight—his Elizabeth, his more than daughter, whom he doted on with all that affection which a man feels, who in the decline of life, having few affections, clings more earnestly to those that remain. Cursed, cursed be the fiend that brought misery on his grey hairs and doomed him to waste in wretchedness! He could not live under the horrors that were accumulated around him; the springs of existence suddenly gave way; he was unable to rise from his bed, and in a few days he died in my arms.

text: I cannot feign the words to describe what I felt in that instant. I have known crippling horror before, have strived to render those unmooring sensations in fitting language, but no turn of phrase could ever capture the heart-choking despair that gnawed at me then. The court clerk I had been begging for answers added that Lila had already confessed to the crime. “That proof was hardly necessary for a case so glaringly obvious,” he noted, “but I am relieved to have it all the same—none of our judges care to condemn an accused person on circumstantial evidence, even if that evidence appears entirely decisive.”
source_text: I cannot pretend to describe what I then felt. I had before experienced sensations of horror, and I have endeavoured to bestow upon them adequate expressions, but words cannot convey an idea of the heart-sickening despair that I then endured. The person to whom I addressed myself added that Justine had already confessed her guilt. “That evidence,” he observed, “was hardly required in so glaring a case, but I am glad of it, and, indeed, none of our judges like to condemn a criminal upon circumstantial evidence, be it ever so decisive.”

text: I picked my way through the twisting tracks of the silver birch woodland until I reached its far edge, where a churning, deep-banked river curled past, its banks overhung with boughs just furred with the tender pale green buds of early spring. I stood still there, uncertain which track to take next, when the murmur of approaching voices made me duck into the dappled shadow of a towering juniper. I’d barely tucked myself out of sight when a young girl came sprinting toward my hiding spot, giggling like she was playing tag with someone just out of view. She kept running along the sharp, crumbling edge of the water, when her boot caught on a loose root and she pitched headfirst into the frothing current. I bolted from my hiding place, fighting hard against the river’s brutal pull to yank her free and haul her limp body onto the muddy bank. Her eyes were closed and her breathing was shallow, so I fumbled to rub warmth back into her limbs and clear water from her airways, only to be jolted by the sound of hasty footsteps—probably the playmate she’d been running from, a farmhand by the look of his rough, mud-streaked clothes. The second he spotted me, he lunged forward, ripped the girl out of my grasp, and hurried off toward the denser, darker part of the woodland. I followed fast, not fully thinking through why I was chasing them, but the moment he noticed me closing in, he lifted the hunting rifle slung over his shoulder, aimed straight at my chest, and pulled the trigger. I crumpled to the damp earth, and my attacker, moving even faster now, vanished into the trees.
source_text: “I continued to wind among the paths of the wood, until I came to its boundary, which was skirted by a deep and rapid river, into which many of the trees bent their branches, now budding with the fresh spring. Here I paused, not exactly knowing what path to pursue, when I heard the sound of voices, that induced me to conceal myself under the shade of a cypress. I was scarcely hid when a young girl came running towards the spot where I was concealed, laughing, as if she ran from someone in sport. She continued her course along the precipitous sides of the river, when suddenly her foot slipped, and she fell into the rapid stream. I rushed from my hiding-place and with extreme labour, from the force of the current, saved her and dragged her to shore. She was senseless, and I endeavoured by every means in my power to restore animation, when I was suddenly interrupted by the approach of a rustic, who was probably the person from whom she had playfully fled. On seeing me, he darted towards me, and tearing the girl from my arms, hastened towards the deeper parts of the wood. I followed speedily, I hardly knew why; but when the man saw me draw near, he aimed a gun, which he carried, at my body and fired. I sank to the ground, and my injurer, with increased swiftness, escaped into the wood.

text: Why haven’t I died? More wretched than any person who has ever walked this earth, why didn’t I slip into total forgetfulness and finally rest? Death snatches countless thriving children from the world, the only hope their doting parents live for; how many brides and young lovers are bursting with health and bright possibility one day, only to become fodder for worms and the slow rot of the grave the next? What even am I made of, that I can withstand so many blows, each one spinning back around like a turning wheel to heap fresh torture on me again and again?
source_text: Why did I not die? More miserable than man ever was before, why did I not sink into forgetfulness and rest? Death snatches away many blooming children, the only hopes of their doting parents; how many brides and youthful lovers have been one day in the bloom of health and hope, and the next a prey for worms and the decay of the tomb! Of what materials was I made that I could thus resist so many shocks, which, like the turning of the wheel, continually renewed the torture?

text: Little by little, I stumbled on a far more important revelation. I realized the people I was watching had a way to share their experiences and emotions with each other using clear, spoken sounds. I noticed that the words they said could spark joy or hurt, grins or sorrow, both in the listeners’ faces and their inner thoughts. This felt like a skill almost divine in its power, and I ached to master it for myself. But every effort I made to learn it left me totally stumped at first. They spoke so fast, and most of the words they said had no obvious link to anything I could see around me, so I couldn’t find a single thread to pull that would untangle what they were referring to. Slowly, though, through sheer relentless effort, after spending several full lunar cycles hidden away in my small shelter, I figured out the labels they used for some of the most common items they talked about. I memorized and started using the words for fire, milk, bread, and wood. I also picked up what the cottage dwellers called each other. The young man and the woman he lived with went by multiple names, but the elderly man only had one: father. The young woman was called either sister or Agatha, and the young man Felix, brother, or son. I can’t put into words how overjoyed I was when I understood what each of those sounds meant, and could say them out loud myself. I picked out a handful of other words too, though I couldn’t yet figure out their meaning or use them correctly—words like good, dearest, and unhappy.
source_text: “By degrees I made a discovery of still greater moment. I found that these people possessed a method of communicating their experience and feelings to one another by articulate sounds. I perceived that the words they spoke sometimes produced pleasure or pain, smiles or sadness, in the minds and countenances of the hearers. This was indeed a godlike science, and I ardently desired to become acquainted with it. But I was baffled in every attempt I made for this purpose. Their pronunciation was quick, and the words they uttered, not having any apparent connection with visible objects, I was unable to discover any clue by which I could unravel the mystery of their reference. By great application, however, and after having remained during the space of several revolutions of the moon in my hovel, I discovered the names that were given to some of the most familiar objects of discourse; I learned and applied the words, _fire, milk, bread,_ and _wood._ I learned also the names of the cottagers themselves. The youth and his companion had each of them several names, but the old man had only one, which was _father._ The girl was called _sister_ or _Agatha,_ and the youth _Felix, brother,_ or _son_. I cannot describe the delight I felt when I learned the ideas appropriated to each of these sounds and was able to pronounce them. I distinguished several other words without being able as yet to understand or apply them, such as _good, dearest, unhappy._

text: But this place had books, and men who’d dug far deeper into those subjects and understood far more than anyone I’d ever encountered. I accepted every claim they made at face value, and threw myself entirely into being their student. It might seem odd that someone with my interests would turn up in the eighteenth century, but even as I went through the standard required schooling at Geneva’s academies, I was almost entirely self-taught when it came to the subjects I cared about most. My father had no background in the sciences, so I was left to fumble along, hamstrung by the naivety of a child paired with the ravenous, unguided curiosity of a budding scholar. With my new teachers leading the way, I threw every ounce of effort I had into chasing both the philosopher’s stone and the elixir of life, though the latter quickly became my sole focus. Wealth was a trivial goal next to it—think of the glory that would come with that discovery, if I could wipe all disease from the human body and make people impervious to every kind of death except those caused by violence!
source_text: But here were books, and here were men who had penetrated deeper and knew more. I took their word for all that they averred, and I became their disciple. It may appear strange that such should arise in the eighteenth century; but while I followed the routine of education in the schools of Geneva, I was, to a great degree, self-taught with regard to my favourite studies. My father was not scientific, and I was left to struggle with a child’s blindness, added to a student’s thirst for knowledge. Under the guidance of my new preceptors I entered with the greatest diligence into the search of the philosopher’s stone and the elixir of life; but the latter soon obtained my undivided attention. Wealth was an inferior object, but what glory would attend the discovery if I could banish disease from the human frame and render man invulnerable to any but a violent death!

text: I turned away in revulsion from the woman who could utter such a cold, unfeeling remark to someone who’d just been pulled back from the very brink of death; but I was so weak and listless I could barely parse the full weight of all that had unfolded. The entire arc of my life felt like a hazy, unmoored dream; sometimes I doubted if any of it had actually been real, for none of it ever settled in my mind with the sharp, tangible force of lived truth.
source_text: I turned with loathing from the woman who could utter so unfeeling a speech to a person just saved, on the very edge of death; but I felt languid and unable to reflect on all that had passed. The whole series of my life appeared to me as a dream; I sometimes doubted if indeed it were all true, for it never presented itself to my mind with the force of reality.

text: Safie was determined to stay at her father’s side right up until the day of his departure, and before that time came, the Turk reaffirmed his promise that she would be married to the man who’d rescued him. Felix stayed on with the pair waiting for that promised union to take place, and in the intervening days, he cherished every moment spent in the Arabian woman’s company; she showed him the most unguarded, gentle affection imaginable. They spoke to one another with the help of an interpreter, and sometimes simply by reading the emotion in each other’s faces, and Safie sang him the beautiful, soul-stirring melodies native to her home country.
source_text: “Safie resolved to remain with her father until the moment of his departure, before which time the Turk renewed his promise that she should be united to his deliverer; and Felix remained with them in expectation of that event; and in the meantime he enjoyed the society of the Arabian, who exhibited towards him the simplest and tenderest affection. They conversed with one another through the means of an interpreter, and sometimes with the interpretation of looks; and Safie sang to him the divine airs of her native country.

text: His fate was sealed, and I had no choice but to bear my grief quietly and hold fast to patience. I sat at his bedside keeping watch; his eyes were shut, and I assumed he slept, until he called to me in a thin, wavering voice, bidding me lean closer. “Alas,” he said, “the strength I once relied on has vanished entirely. I know I will die soon, and he, my enemy and lifelong persecutor, may still draw breath. Do not think, Walton, that in these final moments I carry the same searing hatred and ravenous hunger for revenge I once spoke of. Even so, I cannot call my desire to see my adversary dead unjust. These past days I have spent turning over every choice of my past, and I find no fault in how I acted. In a fever of reckless, all-consuming passion I brought a thinking, rational being into the world, and I owed him every effort I could make to secure his joy and well-being. That was my obligation, but I had another duty that outweighed it entirely. My responsibilities to the rest of my own species deserved greater care, for the harm or good I did them touched far more lives, for better or worse. Guided by that truth, I refused to make a companion for my first creation, and that refusal was the right choice. He acted with unmatchable cruelty and cold self-interest in every wicked deed; he murdered my friends, he snuffed out the lives of people brimming with tender feeling, joy, and wisdom, and I have no way of knowing where his ravenous thirst for vengeance will stop. Miserable as he is, he deserves death so he can bring no more suffering to others. It was my task to destroy him, and I have failed. When I first asked you to take up this unfinished work, I was driven by selfish, bitter impulses—but I renew that request now, moved only by reason and virtue.”
source_text: His sentence was pronounced, and I could only grieve and be patient. I sat by his bed, watching him; his eyes were closed, and I thought he slept; but presently he called to me in a feeble voice, and bidding me come near, said, “Alas! The strength I relied on is gone; I feel that I shall soon die, and he, my enemy and persecutor, may still be in being. Think not, Walton, that in the last moments of my existence I feel that burning hatred and ardent desire of revenge I once expressed; but I feel myself justified in desiring the death of my adversary. During these last days I have been occupied in examining my past conduct; nor do I find it blamable. In a fit of enthusiastic madness I created a rational creature and was bound towards him to assure, as far as was in my power, his happiness and well-being. This was my duty, but there was another still paramount to that. My duties towards the beings of my own species had greater claims to my attention because they included a greater proportion of happiness or misery. Urged by this view, I refused, and I did right in refusing, to create a companion for the first creature. He showed unparalleled malignity and selfishness in evil; he destroyed my friends; he devoted to destruction beings who possessed exquisite sensations, happiness, and wisdom; nor do I know where this thirst for vengeance may end. Miserable himself that he may render no other wretched, he ought to die. The task of his destruction was mine, but I have failed. When actuated by selfish and vicious motives, I asked you to undertake my unfinished work, and I renew this request now, when I am only induced by reason and virtue.

text: That was the final stretch of my life where I knew uncomplicated, unshadowed joy. We skimmed fast across the water; the midday sun blazed fierce, but a woven canvas canopy strung over our small craft kept its searing rays off our skin as we drank in the wild, sweeping beauty around us. When we drifted near the western shore, we spotted the rolling, pine-dusted slopes of Bramble Ridge, the sun-warmed, cottage-dotted banks of Harthaven, and far off, towering over every other peak, the glinting, snow-capped crown of Mount Veyra, surrounded by a cluster of frosty lesser summits that could never hope to match its quiet grandeur. When we hugged the eastern shore instead, the massive, craggy range of the Ironspires rose before us, its shadowed, rugged face a quiet rebuke to any who would abandon their home soil, and a near-unpassable barrier for any conqueror foolish enough to try to subdue its people.
source_text: Those were the last moments of my life during which I enjoyed the feeling of happiness. We passed rapidly along; the sun was hot, but we were sheltered from its rays by a kind of canopy while we enjoyed the beauty of the scene, sometimes on one side of the lake, where we saw Mont Salêve, the pleasant banks of Montalègre, and at a distance, surmounting all, the beautiful Mont Blanc, and the assemblage of snowy mountains that in vain endeavour to emulate her; sometimes coasting the opposite banks, we saw the mighty Jura opposing its dark side to the ambition that would quit its native country, and an almost insurmountable barrier to the invader who should wish to enslave it.

text:
Our sailing master, Elias Voss, has a famously even temper, known across every northern wharf and vessel for his quiet kindness and the leniency of his crew discipline. That reputation, paired with his well-documented honesty and unshakable nerve in even the worst gales, made me desperate to sign him on for my expedition. I grew up in near isolation, my formative years shaped by your soft, tender care as my guardian, so my nature is too refined to stomach the casual cruelty that is standard on most ships. I have never thought such brutality necessary, so when I heard of a seaman as well-loved for his decency as he was for the effortless loyalty his crew showed him, I counted myself extraordinarily lucky to lock in his employment before another captain could snap him up.

I first learned of his character through a strangely whimsical tale, told to me by a woman who credits him with every bit of joy in her adult life. This is the short version of his story. Years back, he fell in love with a young Finnish woman of small, comfortable means; he had saved a small fortune from privateer plunder during the last continental war, so her father agreed to their betrothal. He met his fiancée only once before the planned wedding, and she was soaked through with tears, throwing herself to her knees before him to beg him to release her. She admitted she loved another man, who was penniless, and that her father would never let her marry him for his poverty.

My generous friend calmed the distraught girl, and once she told him the name of her beloved, he gave up any claim to her immediately. He had already purchased a small farmstead with his savings, where he planned to spend the rest of his days, but he signed the entire property over to his rival, along with the rest of his plunder to buy livestock and seed, then personally pleaded with the girl’s father to approve her marriage to the man she loved. The old man refused outright, convinced he was honor-bound to give his daughter to Elias. When Elias realized the father would not bend, he left the country entirely, and did not return until he heard the girl had married the man she chose, as she had wished.

“What a noble fellow!” you will surely exclaim. He is, without question. But he is entirely unschooled, as quiet as a sealed oak cask adrift at sea, and carries a sort of unthinking, unknowing roughness about him. That roughness makes his acts of generosity all the more startling, but it also dims the interest and sympathy one might otherwise feel for him, if he were more open and able to communicate the feelings that drive his fine deeds.
source_text: The master is a person of an excellent disposition and is remarkable in the ship for his gentleness and the mildness of his discipline. This circumstance, added to his well-known integrity and dauntless courage, made me very desirous to engage him. A youth passed in solitude, my best years spent under your gentle and feminine fosterage, has so refined the groundwork of my character that I cannot overcome an intense distaste to the usual brutality exercised on board ship: I have never believed it to be necessary, and when I heard of a mariner equally noted for his kindliness of heart and the respect and obedience paid to him by his crew, I felt myself peculiarly fortunate in being able to secure his services. I heard of him first in rather a romantic manner, from a lady who owes to him the happiness of her life. This, briefly, is his story. Some years ago he loved a young Russian lady of moderate fortune, and having amassed a considerable sum in prize-money, the father of the girl consented to the match. He saw his mistress once before the destined ceremony; but she was bathed in tears, and throwing herself at his feet, entreated him to spare her, confessing at the same time that she loved another, but that he was poor, and that her father would never consent to the union. My generous friend reassured the suppliant, and on being informed of the name of her lover, instantly abandoned his pursuit. He had already bought a farm with his money, on which he had designed to pass the remainder of his life; but he bestowed the whole on his rival, together with the remains of his prize-money to purchase stock, and then himself solicited the young woman’s father to consent to her marriage with her lover. But the old man decidedly refused, thinking himself bound in honour to my friend, who, when he found the father inexorable, quitted his country, nor returned until he heard that his former mistress was married according to her inclinations. “What a noble fellow!” you will exclaim. He is so; but then he is wholly uneducated: he is as silent as a Turk, and a kind of ignorant carelessness attends him, which, while it renders his conduct the more astonishing, detracts from the interest and sympathy which otherwise he would command.

text: When Felix caught sight of her, he looked utterly transported with delight; every trace of grief wiped clean from his face, which lit up at once with a level of giddy rapture I would never have thought it capable of holding. His eyes glowed bright, his cheeks flushed warm with pleasure, and in that instant he seemed just as beautiful to me as the unknown woman. She, by contrast, was caught up in a tangle of softer, more complicated feeling; she brushed a few stray tears from her lovely features, then held one hand out to Felix. He kissed it rapturously, and as far as I could discern, addressed her as his sweet Arabian. She did not appear to understand his words, but offered him a small, soft smile in return. He helped her climb down from her horse, dismissed her attendant, and guided her through the door of the cottage. After a short exchange between him and his father, the young stranger dropped to her knees at the old man’s feet, as if to press a kiss to his hand, but he lifted her gently and pulled her into a warm, affectionate embrace.
source_text: “Felix seemed ravished with delight when he saw her, every trait of sorrow vanished from his face, and it instantly expressed a degree of ecstatic joy, of which I could hardly have believed it capable; his eyes sparkled, as his cheek flushed with pleasure; and at that moment I thought him as beautiful as the stranger. She appeared affected by different feelings; wiping a few tears from her lovely eyes, she held out her hand to Felix, who kissed it rapturously and called her, as well as I could distinguish, his sweet Arabian. She did not appear to understand him, but smiled. He assisted her to dismount, and dismissing her guide, conducted her into the cottage. Some conversation took place between him and his father, and the young stranger knelt at the old man’s feet and would have kissed his hand, but he raised her and embraced her affectionately.

text: The white-bearded wanderer stilled for a beat before he went on, “If you are willing to confide every unvarnished detail of your story to me, I may be able to set the record straight with those who have misjudged you. I lost my sight long ago and cannot read the expression on your face, but there is a quiet honesty in the cadence of your voice that tells me you speak true. I have no wealth to my name, and I am a displaced outsider in this land, but it would bring me deep, unfeigned joy to be of whatever small help I can to a fellow human being.”
source_text: “The old man paused and then continued, ‘If you will unreservedly confide to me the particulars of your tale, I perhaps may be of use in undeceiving them. I am blind and cannot judge of your countenance, but there is something in your words which persuades me that you are sincere. I am poor and an exile, but it will afford me true pleasure to be in any way serviceable to a human creature.’

text: For years I stood as the sole focus of my parents’ devotion. My mother had long ached for a little girl to raise alongside me, but I remained their only child for nearly a decade. When I was about five years old, while we were traveling through the borderlands of southern France, my parents spent a week camped along the shores of Lac d’Annecy. Their generous natures often drew them to stop at the humble cottages of local peasant families. For my mother, these visits were far more than an obligation; they were a compulsion, a deep calling, for she remembered her own childhood of hardship, and the strangers who had lifted her out of it, and she longed to pay that kindness forward as a guardian to those suffering misfortune. On one of their afternoon rambles, a ragged little cottage tucked into the crook of a valley caught their eye, looking unusually forlorn, the cluster of shivering, half-dressed children loitering on its threshold making plain the family was crippled by dire poverty. One day, when my father had traveled alone to Grenoble on business, my mother brought me with her to call on the household. Inside, she found a farmer and his wife, both hardworking but bowed low by constant worry and backbreaking toil, doling out a meager crust of bread and thin broth to their five ravenous small children. Of all the children, one caught my mother’s gaze instantly, holding it far longer than the rest. She seemed to come from an entirely different line. The four other children had dark, flashing eyes and the rough, sturdy bearing of little urchins used to running wild in the hills; this girl was slight, and pale as a lily blossom. Her hair was the color of spun sunlight, and even tangled and unkempt beneath her tattered shawl, it looked like a gilded crown marking her apart. Her forehead was smooth and broad, her blue eyes as clear as a summer sky, and the curve of her lips and the soft shape of her face held such gentle warmth and quiet sensitivity that no one could look at her without thinking she was something other than ordinary, a creature sent down from the heavens, every line of her face bearing the mark of something divine.
source_text: For a long time I was their only care. My mother had much desired to have a daughter, but I continued their single offspring. When I was about five years old, while making an excursion beyond the frontiers of Italy, they passed a week on the shores of the Lake of Como. Their benevolent disposition often made them enter the cottages of the poor. This, to my mother, was more than a duty; it was a necessity, a passion—remembering what she had suffered, and how she had been relieved—for her to act in her turn the guardian angel to the afflicted. During one of their walks a poor cot in the foldings of a vale attracted their notice as being singularly disconsolate, while the number of half-clothed children gathered about it spoke of penury in its worst shape. One day, when my father had gone by himself to Milan, my mother, accompanied by me, visited this abode. She found a peasant and his wife, hard working, bent down by care and labour, distributing a scanty meal to five hungry babes. Among these there was one which attracted my mother far above all the rest. She appeared of a different stock. The four others were dark-eyed, hardy little vagrants; this child was thin and very fair. Her hair was the brightest living gold, and despite the poverty of her clothing, seemed to set a crown of distinction on her head. Her brow was clear and ample, her blue eyes cloudless, and her lips and the moulding of her face so expressive of sensibility and sweetness that none could behold her without looking on her as of a distinct species, a being heaven-sent, and bearing a celestial stamp in all her features.

text:
Hardly anything has changed since you went away, save for how much our beloved children have grown. The blue stretch of the lake and the snow-capped mountains never shift, and I truly believe our quiet home and our own contented hearts follow those same unchanging, gentle laws. My small, daily tasks fill my days and bring me pleasure, and every bit of effort I put in is repaid tenfold when I look around to see nothing but cheerful, kind faces greeting me.

There is only one small shift that has happened in our little household since your departure. Do you recall when Justine Moritz first came to live with us? You likely do not, so I will tell you her story briefly. Her mother, Madame Moritz, was a widow with four children, and Justine was the third of the brood. She had always been her father’s favorite, but for some unaccountable, cruel perversity, her mother could not bear the girl, and after M. Moritz passed away, she treated her very harshly. My aunt noticed this ill treatment, and when Justine was twelve years old, convinced her mother to let the girl come stay in our home.

The republican structure of our country fosters far simpler, happier customs than those common in the great monarchies that border us. Because of this, there is far less division between the different classes of people who live here; the working classes are neither so destitute nor so scorned, so their manners are more refined and morally grounded. The role of a servant in Geneva means something entirely different than it does in France or England. When Justine joined our household, she learned the responsibilities of a servant, a station that, in our fortunate nation, never carries the implication of ignorance or the demand to sacrifice one’s inherent human dignity.
source_text: “Little alteration, except the growth of our dear children, has taken place since you left us. The blue lake and snow-clad mountains—they never change; and I think our placid home and our contented hearts are regulated by the same immutable laws. My trifling occupations take up my time and amuse me, and I am rewarded for any exertions by seeing none but happy, kind faces around me. Since you left us, but one change has taken place in our little household. Do you remember on what occasion Justine Moritz entered our family? Probably you do not; I will relate her history, therefore in a few words. Madame Moritz, her mother, was a widow with four children, of whom Justine was the third. This girl had always been the favourite of her father, but through a strange perversity, her mother could not endure her, and after the death of M. Moritz, treated her very ill. My aunt observed this, and when Justine was twelve years of age, prevailed on her mother to allow her to live at our house. The republican institutions of our country have produced simpler and happier manners than those which prevail in the great monarchies that surround it. Hence there is less distinction between the several classes of its inhabitants; and the lower orders, being neither so poor nor so despised, their manners are more refined and moral. A servant in Geneva does not mean the same thing as a servant in France and England. Justine, thus received in our family, learned the duties of a servant, a condition which, in our fortunate country, does not include the idea of ignorance and a sacrifice of the dignity of a human being.

text: My thoughts quickened now, thrumming with a restless urge to unpick the quiet hopes and sorrows of these gentle, dear people; I burned to understand why Elias carried such a heavy, hunched grief in his shoulders, and why Lila’s smiles so often faded into distant, watery sighs. What a silly, deluded wretch I was, to think I might hold the power to stitch joy back into the lives of these good, worthy folk. Even when I drifted off to sleep or ventured far from their cottage, the faces of the kind, sightless old patriarch, soft-spoken Lila, and steadfast Elias danced at the edge of my vision. I held them as beings far finer than I, fated to decide the shape of all the days ahead of me. A thousand little fantasies unspooled in my head: how I might step before them at last, and how they might greet me. I pictured their first recoil, their sharp disgust, until my soft, careful manners and gentle, appeasing words won first their favour, then their affection, then their wholehearted love.
source_text: “My thoughts now became more active, and I longed to discover the motives and feelings of these lovely creatures; I was inquisitive to know why Felix appeared so miserable and Agatha so sad. I thought (foolish wretch!) that it might be in my power to restore happiness to these deserving people. When I slept or was absent, the forms of the venerable blind father, the gentle Agatha, and the excellent Felix flitted before me. I looked upon them as superior beings who would be the arbiters of my future destiny. I formed in my imagination a thousand pictures of presenting myself to them, and their reception of me. I imagined that they would be disgusted, until, by my gentle demeanour and conciliating words, I should first win their favour and afterwards their love.

text: A woman giving sworn testimony recounted that she lived close to the beach, and had been standing at her cottage doorstep waiting for the local fishermen to return to shore, when roughly an hour before she received word a body had been discovered, she spotted a small boat carrying only one man push off from the exact stretch of coast where the corpse was later recovered.
source_text: A woman deposed that she lived near the beach and was standing at the door of her cottage, waiting for the return of the fishermen, about an hour before she heard of the discovery of the body, when she saw a boat with only one man in it push off from that part of the shore where the corpse was afterwards found.

text: I all but collapsed into the carriage meant to carry me off, with no clear sense of my destination and no attention to anything unfolding around me. The only thing I did recall, the thought of which twisted through me with sharp, bitter agony, was to give the order that my chemical lab instruments be packed to come along. My head swarming with bleak, heavy thoughts, I traveled past countless sweeping, stunning landscapes, but my gaze stayed glazed and unseeing. I could fix my mind on nothing but the final stop of my journey, and the grim task that would fill every moment of the trip until I reached it.
source_text: I threw myself into the carriage that was to convey me away, hardly knowing whither I was going, and careless of what was passing around. I remembered only, and it was with a bitter anguish that I reflected on it, to order that my chemical instruments should be packed to go with me. Filled with dreary imaginations, I passed through many beautiful and majestic scenes, but my eyes were fixed and unobserving. I could only think of the bourne of my travels and the work which was to occupy me whilst they endured.

text: It’s impossible to put into words the tangled mess of emotions that swept me forward like a raging storm in the first giddy rush of my breakthrough. Life and death felt like arbitrary, made-up lines I was meant to shatter first, sending a flood of illumination spilling over our shadowed, ignorant world. An entirely new kind of being would revere me as its maker and origin; scores of good, joyful lives would owe their very existence to my work. No biological father could ever lay claim to a child’s gratitude as fully, as rightfully, as I would deserve theirs. Chasing these daydreams, I reasoned that if I could breathe life into inert, nonliving material, I might eventually – even if that goal felt out of reach right now – bring back life to bodies death had already consigned to rot and decay.
source_text: No one can conceive the variety of feelings which bore me onwards, like a hurricane, in the first enthusiasm of success. Life and death appeared to me ideal bounds, which I should first break through, and pour a torrent of light into our dark world. A new species would bless me as its creator and source; many happy and excellent natures would owe their being to me. No father could claim the gratitude of his child so completely as I should deserve theirs. Pursuing these reflections, I thought that if I could bestow animation upon lifeless matter, I might in process of time (although I now found it impossible) renew life where death had apparently devoted the body to corruption.

text: My days of indecision are over, and the era of your control ends now. Your threats won’t coerce me into committing an act of pure evil—if anything, they’ve only cemented my resolve to never create a partner for you to share your cruelty with. Do you really think I’d cold-bloodedly unleash another demon on the world, one that revels in death and human suffering? Leave! My mind is made up, and every word you speak only stokes my fury.
source_text: “The hour of my irresolution is past, and the period of your power is arrived. Your threats cannot move me to do an act of wickedness; but they confirm me in a determination of not creating you a companion in vice. Shall I, in cool blood, set loose upon the earth a dæmon whose delight is in death and wretchedness? Begone! I am firm, and your words will only exasperate my rage.”

text: He went on to say he’d start telling his full story the following day, once I had free time. I thanked him more profusely than I could say for that vow. I’ve made up my mind to write down everything he tells me each day, as close to his exact phrasing as I can get, every night when my urgent work responsibilities don’t take up all my time. If I’m swamped with other tasks, I’ll jot down rough notes at the very least. This written account will no doubt give you immense pleasure when you read it, but for me, who knows him well and has heard every line directly from his own lips, I can already tell I’ll revisit it someday with a depth of care and investment no one else could match. Even right now, as I start this work, his rich, warm voice rings clear in my ears; I can picture his bright, shining eyes fixed on me, soft with that gentle sadness he carries, his slim hand lifted as he speaks with passion, every feature of his face lit up from within by the intensity of what he’s sharing. His story has to be as strange as it is gut-wrenching, the storm that caught that sturdy ship mid-voyage and tore it apart must have been unspeakably terrifying to leave us here, like this.
source_text: He then told me that he would commence his narrative the next day when I should be at leisure. This promise drew from me the warmest thanks. I have resolved every night, when I am not imperatively occupied by my duties, to record, as nearly as possible in his own words, what he has related during the day. If I should be engaged, I will at least make notes. This manuscript will doubtless afford you the greatest pleasure; but to me, who know him, and who hear it from his own lips—with what interest and sympathy shall I read it in some future day! Even now, as I commence my task, his full-toned voice swells in my ears; his lustrous eyes dwell on me with all their melancholy sweetness; I see his thin hand raised in animation, while the lineaments of his face are irradiated by the soul within. Strange and harrowing must be his story, frightful the storm which embraced the gallant vessel on its course and wrecked it—thus!

text: No horses could be secured for the journey, so I had to make my return along the lake route; the wind was blowing directly against us, and rain poured down in thick, unrelenting sheets. It was barely dawn, however, so I still had reasonable cause to hope I would reach my destination by nightfall. I hired a pair of men to man the oars, and took one for myself as well; physical exertion had always softened the edge of my mental anguish in the past. But the overwhelming grief that gnawed at me now, the sharp, thrumming restlessness that wracked every nerve, left me unable to summon even the smallest effort. I dropped my oar, rested my forehead against my clasped hands, and surrendered entirely to the grim, swirling thoughts that rose unbidden in my mind. When I lifted my gaze, I was surrounded by landscapes I’d known in far brighter days, spots I’d wandered only the previous afternoon in the company of the woman who was now little more than a ghost, a faint, fading recollection. Tears poured freely down my cheeks. The rain had let up for a brief moment, and I watched fish dart and leap just beneath the surface, exactly as they had done only a few hours earlier; Elizabeth had been the one to notice them then. There is no sharper agony for the human spirit than a sudden, shattering reversal of fortune. The sun might break through the clouds, or the sky might remain heavy and overcast, but nothing in the world looked as it had just twenty-four hours prior. A demon had torn every last spark of future joy from my grasp; no living being had ever suffered the depths of misery I felt now, no horror so unthinkable had ever been recorded in the whole of human experience.
source_text: There were no horses to be procured, and I must return by the lake; but the wind was unfavourable, and the rain fell in torrents. However, it was hardly morning, and I might reasonably hope to arrive by night. I hired men to row and took an oar myself, for I had always experienced relief from mental torment in bodily exercise. But the overflowing misery I now felt, and the excess of agitation that I endured rendered me incapable of any exertion. I threw down the oar, and leaning my head upon my hands, gave way to every gloomy idea that arose. If I looked up, I saw scenes which were familiar to me in my happier time and which I had contemplated but the day before in the company of her who was now but a shadow and a recollection. Tears streamed from my eyes. The rain had ceased for a moment, and I saw the fish play in the waters as they had done a few hours before; they had then been observed by Elizabeth. Nothing is so painful to the human mind as a great and sudden change. The sun might shine or the clouds might lower, but nothing could appear to me as it had done the day before. A fiend had snatched from me every hope of future happiness; no creature had ever been so miserable as I was; so frightful an event is single in the history of man.

text: Am I fated to lose this extraordinary soul, then? For years I ached for a kindred spirit, scouring every path my life has taken for someone who would meet my heart with equal understanding and devotion. And look—here, adrift on these barren, wind-scoured waves, I found exactly that person, only to dread I won him merely to grasp how infinitely precious he is, right before he slips away forever. I would coax him to cling to the warmth of living, but he rejects the very thought out of hand.
source_text: Must I then lose this admirable being? I have longed for a friend; I have sought one who would sympathise with and love me. Behold, on these desert seas I have found such a one, but I fear I have gained him only to know his value and lose him. I would reconcile him to life, but he repulses the idea.

text: Theron! Dearest companion! Even now it fills my chest with soft, quiet joy to set your words down on the page, and to linger on the tributes you have more than earned. He was a creature shaped of nature’s own purest verse. His fierce, vivid, unruly fancies were softened and tempered by the gentle, responsive kindness that beat at the very core of him. His whole being brimmed with bright, burning affection, and his loyalty was of that rare, unshakable, wondrous kind that cynical, world-weary folk insist exists nowhere but in the daydreams of idealists. But even the rich comfort of connection to other people was not enough to sate his curious, hungry spirit. The wild, sweeping landscapes of the natural world that others only glance at with passing admiration, he cherished with a fierce, all-consuming ardour:—
source_text: Clerval! Beloved friend! Even now it delights me to record your words and to dwell on the praise of which you are so eminently deserving. He was a being formed in the “very poetry of nature.” His wild and enthusiastic imagination was chastened by the sensibility of his heart. His soul overflowed with ardent affections, and his friendship was of that devoted and wondrous nature that the worldly-minded teach us to look for only in the imagination. But even human sympathies were not sufficient to satisfy his eager mind. The scenery of external nature, which others regard only with admiration, he loved with ardour:—

text: A pall of mourning hung heavy over our home. The terror of the recent horrors had left my father’s health deeply, precariously shaken. Elizabeth was sunk in quiet, unshakable despair; she found no joy anymore in the small daily tasks that once occupied her, convinced that any lightness of heart would be a sacrilege against the dead. She believed endless grief and tears were the only fitting tribute she could offer to such blameless, senselessly destroyed innocence. Gone was the bright, carefree girl who had wandered the lake shores with me in our younger days, chattering giddily about all the soft hopes we held for our future. The first of those quiet, unyielding sorrows meant to turn our hearts away from earthly delights had found her, its dimming shadow snuffing out the warm, dear smiles that used to light up her face.
source_text: Our house was the house of mourning. My father’s health was deeply shaken by the horror of the recent events. Elizabeth was sad and desponding; she no longer took delight in her ordinary occupations; all pleasure seemed to her sacrilege toward the dead; eternal woe and tears she then thought was the just tribute she should pay to innocence so blasted and destroyed. She was no longer that happy creature who in earlier youth wandered with me on the banks of the lake and talked with ecstasy of our future prospects. The first of those sorrows which are sent to wean us from the earth had visited her, and its dimming influence quenched her dearest smiles.

text: I wandered the island like an unquiet specter, severed from every soul I loved, wretched in the loneliness of that separation. When noon arrived and the sun climbed higher in the sky, I stretched out on the grass and was overcome by a deep, unshakable sleep. I had stayed awake the entirety of the previous night, my nerves raw and jangled, my eyes burning from ceaseless vigil and misery. The sleep I slipped into now restored me somewhat; when I woke, I felt once more as if I belonged among the rest of humankind, and I began to turn over the events that had unfolded with far greater composure. Even so, the fiend’s words still echoed in my ears like a funeral toll; they felt like the fading fragments of a dream, yet sharp and stifling enough to be unmistakeable reality.
source_text: I walked about the isle like a restless spectre, separated from all it loved and miserable in the separation. When it became noon, and the sun rose higher, I lay down on the grass and was overpowered by a deep sleep. I had been awake the whole of the preceding night, my nerves were agitated, and my eyes inflamed by watching and misery. The sleep into which I now sank refreshed me; and when I awoke, I again felt as if I belonged to a race of human beings like myself, and I began to reflect upon what had passed with greater composure; yet still the words of the fiend rang in my ears like a death-knell; they appeared like a dream, yet distinct and oppressive as a reality.

text: As evening shaded over into full night, Agatha and the Arab visitor turned in for bed far earlier than usual. As the two women parted from the rest of the household, Felix pressed a gentle kiss to the stranger’s hand and murmured, “Good night, dearest Safie.” He stayed awake for hours long after they’d left, talking quietly with his father, and the frequent repetition of her name led me to guess that their lovely houseguest was the sole topic of their conversation. I ached desperately to make out what they were saying, and strained every last sense and faculty I had toward that goal, only to find it completely impossible.
source_text: “As night came on, Agatha and the Arabian retired early. When they separated Felix kissed the hand of the stranger and said, ‘Good night sweet Safie.’ He sat up much longer, conversing with his father, and by the frequent repetition of her name I conjectured that their lovely guest was the subject of their conversation. I ardently desired to understand them, and bent every faculty towards that purpose, but found it utterly impossible.

text: No soul could fathom the searing agony that wracked me through the rest of that night, which I passed, chilled through and soaked to the bone, huddled unprotected beneath the open sky. But I scarcely registered the harshness of the elements; my mind spun unceasingly through visions of ruin and hopelessness. I thought of the creature I had unleashed upon the world, gifted with the volition and strength to carry out acts of unspeakable terror—like the atrocity he had just committed—and saw him as something close to my own personal wraith, a fragment of my own soul loosed from the dark of the tomb, compelled to tear apart every last thing I held precious.
source_text: No one can conceive the anguish I suffered during the remainder of the night, which I spent, cold and wet, in the open air. But I did not feel the inconvenience of the weather; my imagination was busy in scenes of evil and despair. I considered the being whom I had cast among mankind, and endowed with the will and power to effect purposes of horror, such as the deed which he had now done, nearly in the light of my own vampire, my own spirit let loose from the grave, and forced to destroy all that was dear to me.

text: I kept walking like that for a good while, trying to use the steady motion of my body to lighten the crushing burden weighing on my mind. I drifted through block after block of streets with no real sense of where I’d ended up or what I was even doing. My heart hammered hard in my chest, tight with that sick, twisting kind of fear, and I hurried onward with unsteady, lurching steps, too scared to glance at anything around me:
source_text: I continued walking in this manner for some time, endeavouring by bodily exercise to ease the load that weighed upon my mind. I traversed the streets without any clear conception of where I was or what I was doing. My heart palpitated in the sickness of fear, and I hurried on with irregular steps, not daring to look about me:

text: My father watched the obvious shift in my temperament and daily habits with a deep, quiet pain, and drew on lessons forged from his own clear conscience and unblemished life to urge me toward greater fortitude, to rouse the courage I needed to chase away the heavy dark cloud that hung perpetually over me. “Do you honestly think, Victor,” he said, “that I’m not hurting too? No one could have loved a child more than I loved your brother” — tears welled in his eyes as he spoke — “but don’t we owe it to the people who are still here to refrain from piling more unhappiness on them by displaying grief so unbridled it overwhelms everyone around us? You owe that duty to yourself, too. Excessive, all-consuming sorrow keeps you from growing, from finding any small joy, even from showing up for the basic, necessary work of daily life. No one is fit to be part of their community if they can’t carry out those small, essential obligations.”
source_text: My father observed with pain the alteration perceptible in my disposition and habits and endeavoured by arguments deduced from the feelings of his serene conscience and guiltless life to inspire me with fortitude and awaken in me the courage to dispel the dark cloud which brooded over me. “Do you think, Victor,” said he, “that I do not suffer also? No one could love a child more than I loved your brother”—tears came into his eyes as he spoke—“but is it not a duty to the survivors that we should refrain from augmenting their unhappiness by an appearance of immoderate grief? It is also a duty owed to yourself, for excessive sorrow prevents improvement or enjoyment, or even the discharge of daily usefulness, without which no man is fit for society.”

text: A couple of minutes later, I caught the faint creak of my door, as though someone was trying to push it open as quietly as possible. I shook uncontrollably from head to toe; I knew in my bones who it was, and I wanted to call out to rouse one of the peasants living in the small cottage just a short distance from my home. But I was swallowed up by that same helpless, heavy feeling you get in terrifying dreams, when you fight with everything you have to run from a looming danger and can’t make your body cooperate no matter how hard you try. I was rooted straight to the spot.
source_text: In a few minutes after, I heard the creaking of my door, as if some one endeavoured to open it softly. I trembled from head to foot; I felt a presentiment of who it was and wished to rouse one of the peasants who dwelt in a cottage not far from mine; but I was overcome by the sensation of helplessness, so often felt in frightful dreams, when you in vain endeavour to fly from an impending danger, and was rooted to the spot.

text: Fury blazed bright in my eyes as I spoke, and the local justice shrank back, visibly unnerved. “You misunderstand me,” he said, his voice softening into a conciliatory hush. “I will spare no effort in this matter, and if it lies within my power to apprehend that fiend, rest assured he will be punished in exact measure to the atrocities he has committed. But given the uncanny traits you have ascribed to him, I fear this will prove impossible. All due steps will be taken, of course, but you ought to steel yourself for the very real likelihood of failure.”
source_text: As I spoke, rage sparkled in my eyes; the magistrate was intimidated. “You are mistaken,” said he. “I will exert myself, and if it is in my power to seize the monster, be assured that he shall suffer punishment proportionate to his crimes. But I fear, from what you have yourself described to be his properties, that this will prove impracticable; and thus, while every proper measure is pursued, you should make up your mind to disappointment.”

text: Take care of yourself, get better soon, and come back home to us. When you do, you’ll walk into a warm, bright house full of friends who care about you more than anything. Your dad’s still in excellent health, and all he asks for is to see you face to face, to know you’re doing well, so no worry will ever cast a shadow over that kind, gentle face of his. You’d be so delighted to see how much Ernest has grown, too. He’s sixteen now, buzzing with endless energy and lively spirit. He’s dead set on being a proper Swiss and joining the foreign military, but we can’t bear to let him leave, not until his older brother comes back to us at the very least. Uncle doesn’t care for the idea of him chasing a military career in a far-off country, but Ernest never had the same ability to stick to focused, consistent work that you do. He sees studying as a horrible, suffocating shackle holding him back; almost all his time is spent outside, scrambling up hill trails or rowing out on the lake. I’m worried he’ll end up drifting aimlessly with no direction if we don’t give in and let him pursue the career he’s already set his heart on.
source_text: “Get well—and return to us. You will find a happy, cheerful home and friends who love you dearly. Your father’s health is vigorous, and he asks but to see you, but to be assured that you are well; and not a care will ever cloud his benevolent countenance. How pleased you would be to remark the improvement of our Ernest! He is now sixteen and full of activity and spirit. He is desirous to be a true Swiss and to enter into foreign service, but we cannot part with him, at least until his elder brother returns to us. My uncle is not pleased with the idea of a military career in a distant country, but Ernest never had your powers of application. He looks upon study as an odious fetter; his time is spent in the open air, climbing the hills or rowing on the lake. I fear that he will become an idler unless we yield the point and permit him to enter on the profession which he has selected.

text: Lira’s father had been the root of her family’s downfall. A Persian trader who had resided in Amsterdam for nearly two decades, he fell out of favor with the city’s ruling council for reasons I never managed to unearth. He was arrested and locked in a mildewed jail cell the very same afternoon Lira arrived from Isfahan to reunite with him. His trial was rushed, and the verdict handed down was a death sentence. The unfairness of the ruling was impossibly flagrant; the entire city seethed with indignation, and it was widely agreed that his faith and sizable fortune, not any crime he was alleged to have committed, were the real drivers of his condemnation.
source_text: “The father of Safie had been the cause of their ruin. He was a Turkish merchant and had inhabited Paris for many years, when, for some reason which I could not learn, he became obnoxious to the government. He was seized and cast into prison the very day that Safie arrived from Constantinople to join him. He was tried and condemned to death. The injustice of his sentence was very flagrant; all Paris was indignant; and it was judged that his religion and wealth rather than the crime alleged against him had been the cause of his condemnation.

text: Even as I read, though, I couldn’t help but map most of what I encountered directly onto my own emotions and circumstances. I recognized parts of myself in the people I read about, the ones whose conversations I’d been listening in on, but I was also bizarrely different from them in ways I couldn’t quite name. I felt for them, and even grasped bits of their perspective, but my own mind was still half-formed; I had no ties to anyone, no one to rely on, no kin at all. I could walk away and never return whenever I pleased, and no one would mourn if I ceased to exist entirely. I was hideous to look at, and far taller than any ordinary human being. What did any of this add up to? Who was I? What even was I? Where had I come from? Where was I meant to end up? These questions circled through my head on a constant loop, but I had no way to work out the answers to any of them.
source_text: “As I read, however, I applied much personally to my own feelings and condition. I found myself similar yet at the same time strangely unlike to the beings concerning whom I read and to whose conversation I was a listener. I sympathised with and partly understood them, but I was unformed in mind; I was dependent on none and related to none. ‘The path of my departure was free,’ and there was none to lament my annihilation. My person was hideous and my stature gigantic. What did this mean? Who was I? What was I? Whence did I come? What was my destination? These questions continually recurred, but I was unable to solve them.

text: I owe him my genuine thanks. As my final moments draw close, I feel the deepest gratitude for anyone who holds me in their kind thoughts. How sweet it is to receive care from others when I’m as much of a wretch as I am right now! It takes more than half the weight of all my suffering away, and I truly feel I can pass peacefully now that both you, dear lady, and your cousin have acknowledged my innocence.
source_text: “I truly thank him. In these last moments I feel the sincerest gratitude towards those who think of me with kindness. How sweet is the affection of others to such a wretch as I am! It removes more than half my misfortune, and I feel as if I could die in peace now that my innocence is acknowledged by you, dear lady, and your cousin.”

text: I could scarce put into words how those volumes seared themselves into my mind. They unfurled an endless cascade of new impressions and emotions, lifting me at times to giddy rapture, but far more often dragging me down into the dregs of crushing misery. In *The Lament of Elias Voss*, beyond the quiet, gut-wrenching pull of its unadorned narrative, so many perspectives were unpacked, so many sharp, clear beams cast over topics that had been nothing but murky shadow to me before, that it felt like an unending well of curiosity and wonder. The soft, small joys of household life it laid out, paired with grand, unselfish passions and convictions oriented toward something larger than one’s own petty wants, aligned perfectly with what I had observed in the small family that sheltered me, and with the longings that had burned unquenched in my chest for as long as I could remember. But Elias himself struck me as a more holy, perfect creature than any I had ever laid eyes on or even dreamed of; there was not a shred of pretense in his nature, yet every line of it settled into my bones. Its meditations on death and the choice to end one’s own life were crafted to leave me reeling with awe. I made no claim to grasp the full weight of the moral question at hand, yet I leaned into the beliefs of the story’s hero, and I cried over his end even when I could not fully name why the loss cut so deep.
source_text: “I can hardly describe to you the effect of these books. They produced in me an infinity of new images and feelings, that sometimes raised me to ecstasy, but more frequently sunk me into the lowest dejection. In the _Sorrows of Werter_, besides the interest of its simple and affecting story, so many opinions are canvassed and so many lights thrown upon what had hitherto been to me obscure subjects that I found in it a never-ending source of speculation and astonishment. The gentle and domestic manners it described, combined with lofty sentiments and feelings, which had for their object something out of self, accorded well with my experience among my protectors and with the wants which were for ever alive in my own bosom. But I thought Werter himself a more divine being than I had ever beheld or imagined; his character contained no pretension, but it sank deep. The disquisitions upon death and suicide were calculated to fill me with wonder. I did not pretend to enter into the merits of the case, yet I inclined towards the opinions of the hero, whose extinction I wept, without precisely understanding it.

text: “They’re kind, really—some of the best people you could ever come across. But unfortunately, they’re dead set against me for no reason other than blind prejudice. I’ve always had a gentle, generous nature; I’ve never hurt a soul up to this point, and I’ve even done my share of good for others when I had the chance. But that terrible, unshakable bias clouds their vision entirely. Where they ought to recognize a warm, caring friend, all they can make out is a loathsome, repulsive monster.”
source_text: “‘They are kind—they are the most excellent creatures in the world; but, unfortunately, they are prejudiced against me. I have good dispositions; my life has been hitherto harmless and in some degree beneficial; but a fatal prejudice clouds their eyes, and where they ought to see a feeling and kind friend, they behold only a detestable monster.’

text: The following morning, I delivered my letters of introduction and called on several of the university’s senior professors. Chance, or rather the malicious, omnipotent Angel of Destruction that had ruled every choice I made from the moment I turned my reluctant feet from my father’s doorstep, steered me first to M. Krempe, professor of natural philosophy. He was a coarse, unrefined man, but steeped in the most intimate secrets of his discipline. He asked me a series of questions about my progress across the different branches of study connected to natural philosophy. I replied casually, with a faint undercurrent of disdain, and named the alchemists I had pored over as the primary authors I had studied. The professor stared, aghast. “Have you,” he said, “truly spent your time studying such utter nonsense?”
source_text: The next morning I delivered my letters of introduction and paid a visit to some of the principal professors. Chance—or rather the evil influence, the Angel of Destruction, which asserted omnipotent sway over me from the moment I turned my reluctant steps from my father’s door—led me first to M. Krempe, professor of natural philosophy. He was an uncouth man, but deeply imbued in the secrets of his science. He asked me several questions concerning my progress in the different branches of science appertaining to natural philosophy. I replied carelessly, and partly in contempt, mentioned the names of my alchemists as the principal authors I had studied. The professor stared. “Have you,” he said, “really spent your time in studying such nonsense?”

text: Whenever I could, I stuck to routes that followed the rivers’ bends, but the demon I was pursuing usually avoided these areas, since this was where most of the region’s population was concentrated. Away from the riverbanks, I rarely encountered other people, and I mostly sustained myself on the wild game that crossed my path. I had cash on me, and would earn the goodwill of local villagers by passing it out; if I was carrying meat from an animal I’d hunted, I’d set aside a small serving for myself and give the rest to anyone who let me use their fire and cookware to prepare food.
source_text: I followed, when I could, the courses of the rivers; but the dæmon generally avoided these, as it was here that the population of the country chiefly collected. In other places human beings were seldom seen, and I generally subsisted on the wild animals that crossed my path. I had money with me and gained the friendship of the villagers by distributing it; or I brought with me some food that I had killed, which, after taking a small part, I always presented to those who had provided me with fire and utensils for cooking.

text: I gave him a nod of confirmation. “Every minute,” M. Voss went on, hot with frustration, “every single moment you have squandered on those volumes is entirely and irretrievably lost. You have weighed your memory down with debunked systems and worthless names that hold no modern merit. Good God! What isolated backwater have you inhabited, where no soul was generous enough to tell you that the fantasies you have so greedily absorbed are a thousand years out of date, as moldy as they are ancient? I scarcely expected, in this enlightened, scientific era, to find a disciple of Albertus Magnus and Paracelsus. My dear fellow, you must begin your studies completely from the ground up.”
source_text: I replied in the affirmative. “Every minute,” continued M. Krempe with warmth, “every instant that you have wasted on those books is utterly and entirely lost. You have burdened your memory with exploded systems and useless names. Good God! In what desert land have you lived, where no one was kind enough to inform you that these fancies which you have so greedily imbibed are a thousand years old and as musty as they are ancient? I little expected, in this enlightened and scientific age, to find a disciple of Albertus Magnus and Paracelsus. My dear sir, you must begin your studies entirely anew.”

text: I reached the crest of the climb right around midday. I perched for a while on the boulder that juts out above the sprawling glacier field. Thick fog hung over the ice and all the nearby peaks. When a gust of wind finally blew the mist clear, I picked my way down onto the glacier’s surface. Its terrain was wildly uneven, swelling like the crests of a storm-churned ocean, dropping into sharp hollows, and crisscrossed with deep, gaping crevasses. The ice field was nearly a league across, but it took me almost two full hours to pick my way from one side to the other. The mountain on the far side was a sheer, bare slab of rock. From where I stood, Montanvert sat directly across from me, another league away, and Mont Blanc loomed high above it, breathtaking in its unforgiving grandeur. I hunkered down in a sheltered hollow in the rock, staring out at this staggering, otherworldly view.
