The ice field—more a slow-moving frozen river, really—curved between the cluster of lower mountains around it, their sharp, cloud-touched peaks hanging over the glacier’s sunken recesses. Their glittering, ice-capped summits glowed bright in the sun high above the cloud layer. My chest, tight with grief only minutes earlier, now ached with a feeling close to joy; I cried out loud, “Wandering spirits, if you truly do roam instead of resting in your narrow beds, let me hold onto this small, fragile happiness, or take me as your companion and carry me far from the small joys of this living world.”
source_text: It was nearly noon when I arrived at the top of the ascent. For some time I sat upon the rock that overlooks the sea of ice. A mist covered both that and the surrounding mountains. Presently a breeze dissipated the cloud, and I descended upon the glacier. The surface is very uneven, rising like the waves of a troubled sea, descending low, and interspersed by rifts that sink deep. The field of ice is almost a league in width, but I spent nearly two hours in crossing it. The opposite mountain is a bare perpendicular rock. From the side where I now stood Montanvert was exactly opposite, at the distance of a league; and above it rose Mont Blanc, in awful majesty. I remained in a recess of the rock, gazing on this wonderful and stupendous scene. The sea, or rather the vast river of ice, wound among its dependent mountains, whose aerial summits hung over its recesses. Their icy and glittering peaks shone in the sunlight over the clouds. My heart, which was before sorrowful, now swelled with something like joy; I exclaimed, “Wandering spirits, if indeed ye wander, and do not rest in your narrow beds, allow me this faint happiness, or take me, as your companion, away from the joys of life.”

text: “Demon,” I snarled, “have you the gall to step within my reach? Have you no dread of the brutal wrath my hands will rain down on your wretched, hollow skull? Flee, loathsome grub! Or no—stay, hold your ground, so I may crush you beneath my heel until there is nothing left but fine dust! And oh, if only snuffing out your pathetic, cursed existence could bring back every innocent soul you have butchered in your unspeakable, devilish cruelty!”
source_text: “Devil,” I exclaimed, “do you dare approach me? And do not you fear the fierce vengeance of my arm wreaked on your miserable head? Begone, vile insect! Or rather, stay, that I may trample you to dust! And, oh! That I could, with the extinction of your miserable existence, restore those victims whom you have so diabolically murdered!”

text:
I didn’t sleep a wink all that night. My whole mind was roiling with chaos and unrest, even as I sensed some kind of clarity would eventually settle out of it—yet I had no power to force that order to come. Slowly, once dawn had broken across the sky, I finally drifted off. When I woke, all the frantic, tangled thoughts I’d had the night before felt like a faded, half-remembered dream. All that remained was a firm resolution to return to my long-held course of study, and devote myself fully to the science I’d always believed I had a natural talent for.

I went to visit Professor Waldman that very same day. He was far gentler and more charming in private than he was in public; the quiet, stately dignity he carried during his lectures softened entirely in his own home, replaced by open, warm affability and kindness. I told him almost the exact same account of my past pursuits that I’d shared with his fellow professor. He listened closely as I walked him through my earlier studies, and smiled when I mentioned the names of Cornelius Agrippa and Paracelsus, with none of the sneering contempt Professor Krempe had shown at those very same names.

“These were men whose relentless, unflagging zeal laid most of the foundation for the knowledge modern scientists build their work on,” he said. “They did the hard work of bringing so many key facts to light; our far simpler task is to give those facts new, standardized names and sort them into connected, logical systems. Even the work of brilliant men, no matter how misdirected it might seem at the time, almost always ends up serving the lasting good of humanity in the end.”

I listened to his words, which he delivered without any trace of arrogance or performative pretension, then told him his lecture had erased all the prejudices I’d held against modern chemists. I spoke in careful, measured terms, with the modesty and deference a young student owes their instructor, careful not to let any of the fiery enthusiasm spurring my planned work slip out; my inexperience with the world would have left me mortified to show that level of unguarded eagerness. I ended by asking him for advice on which books I ought to purchase for my studies.
source_text: I closed not my eyes that night. My internal being was in a state of insurrection and turmoil; I felt that order would thence arise, but I had no power to produce it. By degrees, after the morning’s dawn, sleep came. I awoke, and my yesternight’s thoughts were as a dream. There only remained a resolution to return to my ancient studies and to devote myself to a science for which I believed myself to possess a natural talent. On the same day I paid M. Waldman a visit. His manners in private were even more mild and attractive than in public, for there was a certain dignity in his mien during his lecture which in his own house was replaced by the greatest affability and kindness. I gave him pretty nearly the same account of my former pursuits as I had given to his fellow professor. He heard with attention the little narration concerning my studies and smiled at the names of Cornelius Agrippa and Paracelsus, but without the contempt that M. Krempe had exhibited. He said that “These were men to whose indefatigable zeal modern philosophers were indebted for most of the foundations of their knowledge. They had left to us, as an easier task, to give new names and arrange in connected classifications the facts which they in a great degree had been the instruments of bringing to light. The labours of men of genius, however erroneously directed, scarcely ever fail in ultimately turning to the solid advantage of mankind.” I listened to his statement, which was delivered without any presumption or affectation, and then added that his lecture had removed my prejudices against modern chemists; I expressed myself in measured terms, with the modesty and deference due from a youth to his instructor, without letting escape (inexperience in life would have made me ashamed) any of the enthusiasm which stimulated my intended labours. I requested his advice concerning the books I ought to procure.

text: Yet what reason have I to linger over the incidents that followed that final, world-shattering blow? Every word I have spoken so far has been stitched from dread and bloodshed; I have already scaled the highest, most ghoulish peak of this nightmare, and the scraps left to tell will almost certainly feel hollow and wearisome to you. Understand this, if nothing else: my dearest companions were torn from my side one after the next, until no one remained but me, adrift in utter ruin. I have no more strength left to spin out the story at length, so I will give you the last of this ghastly account as briefly as I am able.
source_text: But why should I dwell upon the incidents that followed this last overwhelming event? Mine has been a tale of horrors; I have reached their _acme_, and what I must now relate can but be tedious to you. Know that, one by one, my friends were snatched away; I was left desolate. My own strength is exhausted, and I must tell, in a few words, what remains of my hideous narration.

text: Unchecked, hot tears spilled down my brother’s cheeks; a searing, all-consuming agony coiled through every inch of my frame. Until that moment, I had only conjured mental images of the crippling wretchedness of our empty, gutted home; the harsh reality struck me as a fresh, no less devastating, catastrophe. I fumbled to soothe Kael, pressing for more precise details about our father, then spoke my cousin’s name aloud to ask after her as well.
source_text: Tears, unrestrained, fell from my brother’s eyes; a sense of mortal agony crept over my frame. Before, I had only imagined the wretchedness of my desolated home; the reality came on me as a new, and a not less terrible, disaster. I tried to calm Ernest; I enquired more minutely concerning my father, and here I named my cousin.

text: Truly, there are no more miserable, gut-wrenching turns of fate than the bizarre string of events that unfolded just recently. You washed up on this shore, famous for its generous welcome to strangers, after a totally unforeseen accident, only to be seized immediately and charged with murder. The very first sight that met your eyes was your friend’s corpse, killed in such an incomprehensible manner, left there almost as if some cruel fiend had deliberately placed it directly across your path.
source_text: “Nothing indeed could be more unfortunate and agonising than the strange chances that have lately occurred. You were thrown, by some surprising accident, on this shore, renowned for its hospitality, seized immediately, and charged with murder. The first sight that was presented to your eyes was the body of your friend, murdered in so unaccountable a manner and placed, as it were, by some fiend across your path.”

text: I stepped out of the room, turned the key in the lock behind me, and swore a solemn, private vow to myself that I would never resume my work again; then, with trembling, unsteady steps, I made my way to my own chambers. I was completely alone; no one was nearby to chase away the oppressive gloom, or lift the sick, suffocating weight of the most terrifying reveries looping through my mind.
source_text: I left the room, and locking the door, made a solemn vow in my own heart never to resume my labours; and then, with trembling steps, I sought my own apartment. I was alone; none were near me to dissipate the gloom and relieve me from the sickening oppression of the most terrible reveries.

text: I wandered through Edinburgh dull-eyed and listless, even though the city held enough charm to captivate even the most miserable soul. Clerval liked it less than Oxford, truth be told—he was far fonder of the deep, ancient character of the latter city. Still, Edinburgh’s sleek, orderly New Town, its atmospheric old castle, and the surrounding landscapes, easily some of the most delightful in the world, from Arthur’s Seat and St. Bernard’s Well to the rolling Pentland Hills, made up for the change of scene, leaving him bright with cheer and quiet admiration. I, however, could barely tamp down my restlessness, eager only to reach the final stop of our journey.
source_text: I visited Edinburgh with languid eyes and mind; and yet that city might have interested the most unfortunate being. Clerval did not like it so well as Oxford, for the antiquity of the latter city was more pleasing to him. But the beauty and regularity of the new town of Edinburgh, its romantic castle and its environs, the most delightful in the world, Arthur’s Seat, St. Bernard’s Well, and the Pentland Hills, compensated him for the change and filled him with cheerfulness and admiration. But I was impatient to arrive at the termination of my journey.

text: These were far from the only strange fancies that held me in their grip. The authors I adored freely promised that any dedicated practitioner could summon ghosts or demons, a feat I chased with ravenous eagerness; when my incantations never once succeeded, I laid the failure entirely at my own door, chalking it up to inexperience and clumsy error rather than any lack of skill or fidelity in the works that guided me. For a stretch of time, I poured myself over these long-discredited schools of thought, fumbling like an utter novice as I tangled a thousand conflicting theories together and flailed desperately through a morass of disjointed, wide-ranging learning, led only by my burning imagination and childish reasoning, until a chance occurrence once again redirected the entire current of my ideas.
source_text: Nor were these my only visions. The raising of ghosts or devils was a promise liberally accorded by my favourite authors, the fulfilment of which I most eagerly sought; and if my incantations were always unsuccessful, I attributed the failure rather to my own inexperience and mistake than to a want of skill or fidelity in my instructors. And thus for a time I was occupied by exploded systems, mingling, like an unadept, a thousand contradictory theories and floundering desperately in a very slough of multifarious knowledge, guided by an ardent imagination and childish reasoning, till an accident again changed the current of my ideas.

text: I leave my entire case in the fair hands of my judges, even as I can’t spot a single glimmer of hope ahead of me. I’m begging for permission to call a small handful of witnesses to testify to my character, and if their statements don’t outweigh the accusations of guilt against me, I will accept my sentence, even though I’d stake my eternal salvation on the truth of my innocence.
source_text: “I commit my cause to the justice of my judges, yet I see no room for hope. I beg permission to have a few witnesses examined concerning my character, and if their testimony shall not overweigh my supposed guilt, I must be condemned, although I would pledge my salvation on my innocence.”

text: This streak of genuine kindness touched me deeply. I had grown used to sneaking out under cover of night to steal small portions of their stored food for my own meals, but once I realized my actions inflicted real hardship on the cottagers, I stopped entirely. I satisfied my hunger instead with wild berries, nuts, and roots that I foraged from the nearby woods.
source_text: “This trait of kindness moved me sensibly. I had been accustomed, during the night, to steal a part of their store for my own consumption, but when I found that in doing this I inflicted pain on the cottagers, I abstained and satisfied myself with berries, nuts, and roots which I gathered from a neighbouring wood.

text: I know you’ve no doubt been waiting restlessly for a letter from us locking in the date you can come home; at first, I almost just sent a short note, only naming the day I wanted you back. But that would be a kindness that cuts deeper than cruelty, and I couldn’t bring myself to do it. Can you imagine how shocked you’d be, my son, when you’re expecting a warm, joyful welcome home, only to walk in on nothing but tears and crushing misery instead? And Victor, how do I even put what’s happened to our family into words? Being gone all this time hasn’t made you numb to our happiness or our heartache, I know that—so how am I supposed to hurt the son I haven’t seen for so long? I want to soften the blow of this terrible news first, but I know there’s no way to do that; even right now, you’re probably skimming these lines, hunting for the words that will tell you exactly what awful thing has occurred.
source_text: “You have probably waited impatiently for a letter to fix the date of your return to us; and I was at first tempted to write only a few lines, merely mentioning the day on which I should expect you. But that would be a cruel kindness, and I dare not do it. What would be your surprise, my son, when you expected a happy and glad welcome, to behold, on the contrary, tears and wretchedness? And how, Victor, can I relate our misfortune? Absence cannot have rendered you callous to our joys and griefs; and how shall I inflict pain on my long absent son? I wish to prepare you for the woeful news, but I know it is impossible; even now your eye skims over the page to seek the words which are to convey to you the horrible tidings.

text: Her brothers and sister passed away one after the other, leaving her mother with no children besides the daughter she’d long brushed off. The woman’s conscience started gnawing at her; she began to believe her favorite kids’ deaths were a divine punishment for how unfairly she’d played favorites over the years. She was a Roman Catholic, and I’m fairly certain her confessor confirmed the belief she’d settled on. So, a few months after you left for Ingolstadt, Justine was called home by her now repentant mother. Poor girl, she was weeping when she left our house. She’d changed so much since my aunt’s death; grief had softened her demeanor, giving her a warm, winning gentleness that replaced the bright, bubbly vivacity she’d been known for before. Staying at her mother’s house did nothing to bring back her old cheer, either. That poor woman was so inconsistent in her remorse. Sometimes she’d beg Justine to forgive her for years of unkindness, but far more often she’d accuse her of being the cause of her brothers’ and sister’s deaths. All that perpetual fretting eventually pushed Madame Moritz into a slow wasting decline, which at first only made her more irritable, but she’s at peace forever now. She died when the first cold weather hit, right at the start of this past winter. Justine just returned to stay with us, and I assure you I love her dearly. She’s very clever, gentle, and extraordinarily pretty; as I mentioned before, her bearing and her little everyday expressions constantly remind me of my dear aunt.
source_text: “One by one, her brothers and sister died; and her mother, with the exception of her neglected daughter, was left childless. The conscience of the woman was troubled; she began to think that the deaths of her favourites was a judgement from heaven to chastise her partiality. She was a Roman Catholic; and I believe her confessor confirmed the idea which she had conceived. Accordingly, a few months after your departure for Ingolstadt, Justine was called home by her repentant mother. Poor girl! She wept when she quitted our house; she was much altered since the death of my aunt; grief had given softness and a winning mildness to her manners, which had before been remarkable for vivacity. Nor was her residence at her mother’s house of a nature to restore her gaiety. The poor woman was very vacillating in her repentance. She sometimes begged Justine to forgive her unkindness, but much oftener accused her of having caused the deaths of her brothers and sister. Perpetual fretting at length threw Madame Moritz into a decline, which at first increased her irritability, but she is now at peace for ever. She died on the first approach of cold weather, at the beginning of this last winter. Justine has just returned to us; and I assure you I love her tenderly. She is very clever and gentle, and extremely pretty; as I mentioned before, her mien and her expression continually remind me of my dear aunt.

text: “I owe you my gratitude,” he returned, “for the sympathy you extend, yet it is of no avail; my fate has nearly run its course. I wait only for one final occurrence, and then I shall repose in undisturbed peace. I read your impulse to interrupt,” he continued, catching the way I leaned forward to speak, “but you are mistaken, my friend—if you will suffer me to name you so. No power on earth can alter my destiny; listen to my story, and you will perceive how irrevocably it has been determined.”
source_text: “I thank you,” he replied, “for your sympathy, but it is useless; my fate is nearly fulfilled. I wait but for one event, and then I shall repose in peace. I understand your feeling,” continued he, perceiving that I wished to interrupt him; “but you are mistaken, my friend, if thus you will allow me to name you; nothing can alter my destiny; listen to my history, and you will perceive how irrevocably it is determined.”

text: What he said settled over me in an odd, unshakable haze. I ached for him, sometimes caught myself longing to soothe the raw, jagged edges of his grief, but the second my eyes landed on him, on that lurching, reeking hulk of flesh that moved and spoke, my stomach roiled, and every soft urge curdled straight into revulsion and loathing. I fought to stifle those ugly, roiling sensations; I told myself that even if I could never truly meet him with full sympathy, I had no right to deny him the small scrap of happiness it was still within my power to grant.
source_text: His words had a strange effect upon me. I compassionated him and sometimes felt a wish to console him, but when I looked upon him, when I saw the filthy mass that moved and talked, my heart sickened and my feelings were altered to those of horror and hatred. I tried to stifle these sensations; I thought that as I could not sympathise with him, I had no right to withhold from him the small portion of happiness which was yet in my power to bestow.

text:
We journeyed during the grape harvest, and the bright, lilting songs of field workers drifted to us as we glided gently down the river. Even I, whose mind hung heavy with despair and whose spirits were endlessly roiled by dark, restless thoughts, found myself unexpectedly cheered. I stretched out on the floor of the boat, and as I stared up at the unblemished cerulean sky, I felt myself breathing in a quiet calm I had not known for years. If such was my state, who could possibly capture what Henry felt? He might have been spirited away to some enchanted realm, savoring a joy so rare few mortals ever knew it.
“I have laid eyes on the most stunning vistas my homeland has to offer,” he said. “I have wandered the shores of Lakes Lucerne and Uri, where snow-capped peaks drop nearly straight down to the water, throwing deep, impenetrable shadows that would lend the whole scene a somber, mournful air if not for the bright, lush islands that catch the eye and soften the view with their vivid warmth. I have watched that same lake roiled by storm, when the wind ripped great columns of water skyward, giving you a perfect sense of what a waterspout must look like out on the open ocean; the waves crashed with unbridled fury against the mountain’s base, the very spot where a priest and his lover were buried by an avalanche, and where local legend says their final cries still drift through the air during lulls in the midnight wind. I have trekked the peaks of La Valais and the rolling hills of the Pays de Vaud, but this land, Victor, delights me more than all those marvels combined. Switzerland’s mountains are grander, more otherworldly, but there is a gentle magic along the banks of this sacred river I have never encountered anywhere else. Look at that castle perched right on the edge of that crag, and the other one on the island, nearly hidden beneath the leaves of those beautiful trees; and see that group of workers making their way home from the vineyards, and that little village tucked away in the mountain’s hollow. Oh, I am certain the spirit that dwells here and watches over this place has a heart far more aligned with humanity than the cold spirits that stack glaciers and hide away in the unreachable, frozen peaks of our home mountains.”
source_text: We travelled at the time of the vintage and heard the song of the labourers as we glided down the stream. Even I, depressed in mind, and my spirits continually agitated by gloomy feelings, even I was pleased. I lay at the bottom of the boat, and as I gazed on the cloudless blue sky, I seemed to drink in a tranquillity to which I had long been a stranger. And if these were my sensations, who can describe those of Henry? He felt as if he had been transported to Fairy-land and enjoyed a happiness seldom tasted by man. “I have seen,” he said, “the most beautiful scenes of my own country; I have visited the lakes of Lucerne and Uri, where the snowy mountains descend almost perpendicularly to the water, casting black and impenetrable shades, which would cause a gloomy and mournful appearance were it not for the most verdant islands that relieve the eye by their gay appearance; I have seen this lake agitated by a tempest, when the wind tore up whirlwinds of water and gave you an idea of what the water-spout must be on the great ocean; and the waves dash with fury the base of the mountain, where the priest and his mistress were overwhelmed by an avalanche and where their dying voices are still said to be heard amid the pauses of the nightly wind; I have seen the mountains of La Valais, and the Pays de Vaud; but this country, Victor, pleases me more than all those wonders. The mountains of Switzerland are more majestic and strange, but there is a charm in the banks of this divine river that I never before saw equalled. Look at that castle which overhangs yon precipice; and that also on the island, almost concealed amongst the foliage of those lovely trees; and now that group of labourers coming from among their vines; and that village half hid in the recess of the mountain. Oh, surely the spirit that inhabits and guards this place has a soul more in harmony with man than those who pile the glacier or retire to the inaccessible peaks of the mountains of our own country.”

text: We had barely finished roaming the glassy tarns of Northumberland and the moorland meres of Durham, and grown warm with fondness for a handful of the small village folk there, when the date of our planned meeting with our Highland contact drew near, and we bade the locals farewell to continue our journey. I, for one, felt no pang at the departure. I had put off the vow I’d made for weeks now, and dreaded the wrath the wraith I’d bound myself to would unleash when his patience frayed entirely. He could linger in the Bavarian highlands and turn his fury on my family, all of whom still resided there. The thought nipped at my heels constantly, gnawing at me even in the rare quiet moments when I might otherwise have stolen a few hours of rest and calm. I waited for post with a fevered, gnawing restlessness; if a delivery was late, I was unmoored, beset by a thousand screaming, hideous fears, and when letters did arrive and I spotted the familiar looping scrawl of Elspeth or my father’s sharp, slanted hand, I could barely summon the nerve to break the seal and learn what ill had befallen my loved ones. Now and then I was certain the fiend dogged my steps, and would punish my slowness to keep my vow by killing my travel companion, Kael. When these thoughts took hold of my mind, I would not leave Kael’s side for an instant, trailing after him like a second shadow to shield him from the imagined fury of the creature that would mark him for death to wound me. I carried the weight of some unspoken, terrible transgression as I walked, the knowledge of it clinging to my bones and hounding every waking step. I had done no wrong by the laws of men, but I had called down a hideous, unshakable curse on my own head, one as damning and deadly as if I’d spilled innocent blood with my own two hands.
source_text: We had scarcely visited the various lakes of Cumberland and Westmorland and conceived an affection for some of the inhabitants when the period of our appointment with our Scotch friend approached, and we left them to travel on. For my own part I was not sorry. I had now neglected my promise for some time, and I feared the effects of the dæmon’s disappointment. He might remain in Switzerland and wreak his vengeance on my relatives. This idea pursued me and tormented me at every moment from which I might otherwise have snatched repose and peace. I waited for my letters with feverish impatience; if they were delayed I was miserable and overcome by a thousand fears; and when they arrived and I saw the superscription of Elizabeth or my father, I hardly dared to read and ascertain my fate. Sometimes I thought that the fiend followed me and might expedite my remissness by murdering my companion. When these thoughts possessed me, I would not quit Henry for a moment, but followed him as his shadow, to protect him from the fancied rage of his destroyer. I felt as if I had committed some great crime, the consciousness of which haunted me. I was guiltless, but I had indeed drawn down a horrible curse upon my head, as mortal as that of crime.

text: Once we’d spent several months in London, we got a letter from an acquaintance in Scotland who’d previously come to visit us back in Geneva. He wrote at length about the breathtaking beauty of his home country, and asked if those charms would be enough to tempt us to stretch our journey all the way north to Perth, where he made his home. Clerval was desperate to accept the invitation, and while I loathed being around large groups of people, I longed to revisit the mountains, rivers, and all the extraordinary wild creations that Nature uses to adorn her most cherished, unspoiled corners.
source_text: After passing some months in London, we received a letter from a person in Scotland who had formerly been our visitor at Geneva. He mentioned the beauties of his native country and asked us if those were not sufficient allurements to induce us to prolong our journey as far north as Perth, where he resided. Clerval eagerly desired to accept this invitation, and I, although I abhorred society, wished to view again mountains and streams and all the wondrous works with which Nature adorns her chosen dwelling-places.

text: I had a fiery temper at times, and my passions ran intensely hot, but some innate quirk of my disposition channeled that energy away from frivolous childish pursuits and toward a ravenous hunger to learn—though not to learn any random subject indiscriminately. I’ll admit I never found the structure of languages, the legal frameworks of governing bodies, or the political machinations of various states the least bit appealing. What I ached to uncover were the hidden secrets of heaven and earth; whether I was preoccupied with the outward tangible substance of things, the inner animating spirit of the natural world, or the mysterious, unknowable core of the human soul, all my inquiries were pointed either toward the metaphysical, or, in its highest, most literal sense, the unplumbed physical secrets of the world.
source_text: My temper was sometimes violent, and my passions vehement; but by some law in my temperature they were turned not towards childish pursuits but to an eager desire to learn, and not to learn all things indiscriminately. I confess that neither the structure of languages, nor the code of governments, nor the politics of various states possessed attractions for me. It was the secrets of heaven and earth that I desired to learn; and whether it was the outward substance of things or the inner spirit of nature and the mysterious soul of man that occupied me, still my inquiries were directed to the metaphysical, or in its highest sense, the physical secrets of the world.

text: Do not let this note unsettle you; there is no need to reply tomorrow, the day after, or even before you arrive at all, if doing so would cause you the smallest ache. My godfather will send word of how you are faring, and if I spot but one soft smile on your lips when we meet, kindled by this or any other small exertion of mine, I shall have no need of any other happiness.
source_text: “Do not let this letter disturb you; do not answer tomorrow, or the next day, or even until you come, if it will give you pain. My uncle will send me news of your health, and if I see but one smile on your lips when we meet, occasioned by this or any other exertion of mine, I shall need no other happiness.

text: Six years have slipped by since I first committed to the venture I now pursue. Even now, I can recall the exact moment I consecrated myself to this grand pursuit. I began by conditioning my body to bear unforgiving hardship. I joined whaling crews on multiple expeditions to the North Sea, willfully enduring biting cold, gnawing hunger, crippling thirst, and prolonged sleep deprivation. I frequently labored longer and harder than the average sailor by day, while my nights were spent studying mathematics, medical theory, and those fields of physical science that offer the most practical benefit to a seafaring explorer. On two separate occasions, I signed on as under-mate aboard a Greenland whaling vessel, and performed my duties so well I earned widespread admiration. I will admit I felt a quiet swell of pride when my captain offered me the ship’s second-highest post and entreated me to stay with the utmost sincerity, so highly did he value my services.
source_text: Six years have passed since I resolved on my present undertaking. I can, even now, remember the hour from which I dedicated myself to this great enterprise. I commenced by inuring my body to hardship. I accompanied the whale-fishers on several expeditions to the North Sea; I voluntarily endured cold, famine, thirst, and want of sleep; I often worked harder than the common sailors during the day and devoted my nights to the study of mathematics, the theory of medicine, and those branches of physical science from which a naval adventurer might derive the greatest practical advantage. Twice I actually hired myself as an under-mate in a Greenland whaler, and acquitted myself to admiration. I must own I felt a little proud when my captain offered me the second dignity in the vessel and entreated me to remain with the greatest earnestness, so valuable did he consider my services.

text: Mark my words: I do not set down the ravings of a fevered, unhinged mind. What I affirm here is no less certain than the sun’s unchanging path across the vault of the sky. One might dismiss it as a miracle, but every stage of my discovery was deliberate, documented, and fully consistent with the rules of the natural world. After days and nights of grueling, unbroken labor, of pushing through a fatigue so deep it blurred my vision at the workbench, I succeeded in unlocking the core secret of generation and life. More than that: I myself gained the power to bestow the spark of animation upon matter that had never known life.
source_text: Remember, I am not recording the vision of a madman. The sun does not more certainly shine in the heavens than that which I now affirm is true. Some miracle might have produced it, yet the stages of the discovery were distinct and probable. After days and nights of incredible labour and fatigue, I succeeded in discovering the cause of generation and life; nay, more, I became myself capable of bestowing animation upon lifeless matter.

text: And that was the moment my lifelong wandering began, a journey that will end only when my life does. I’ve crossed vast stretches of the globe, enduring every hardship typical of people traveling through desolate deserts and ungoverned, wild lands. I barely know how I’ve managed to survive at all; countless times I’ve collapsed on sun-scorched sandy plains, my limbs failing me, and begged for death to take me. But revenge kept me clinging to life. I could not dare to die and leave my adversary still living in the world.
source_text: And now my wanderings began which are to cease but with life. I have traversed a vast portion of the earth and have endured all the hardships which travellers in deserts and barbarous countries are wont to meet. How I have lived I hardly know; many times have I stretched my failing limbs upon the sandy plain and prayed for death. But revenge kept me alive; I dared not die and leave my adversary in being.

text: Next he told me that the very morning the murder of young Liam was first discovered, Elara had fallen violently ill, confined to her small bedroom for several days. In that interval, one of the household maids, sorting through the gown she’d worn the night of the killing, stumbled on the miniature portrait of my mother tucked into its side pocket—the same heirloom all agreed had been the murderer’s core motive. The maid instantly showed the find to a fellow servant, who said nothing of the discovery to any member of the family before going straight to a local magistrate; on the strength of their sworn statement, Elara was taken into custody at once. When formally charged with the crime, the terrified girl’s wild, disoriented manner and stumbling answers did a great deal to confirm everyone’s worst suspicions of her guilt.
source_text: He then related that, the morning on which the murder of poor William had been discovered, Justine had been taken ill, and confined to her bed for several days. During this interval, one of the servants, happening to examine the apparel she had worn on the night of the murder, had discovered in her pocket the picture of my mother, which had been judged to be the temptation of the murderer. The servant instantly showed it to one of the others, who, without saying a word to any of the family, went to a magistrate; and, upon their deposition, Justine was apprehended. On being charged with the fact, the poor girl confirmed the suspicion in a great measure by her extreme confusion of manner.

text: That roiling tangle of unspoken feelings guided what I said to my father when he asked after my wishes that spring. I told him I longed to travel through the Scottish Highlands, but hid the true motive for the request, wrapping my aim in a vague, innocent story of wanting to sketch misty glens and storm-scoured coastal cliffs that raised no suspicion. I pressed the request so earnestly that he complied almost at once. He’d watched me sink into a consuming, fevered low for months, one that bordered on lunacy in its sharp, unshakable hold, so he was relieved to see me show even a flicker of excitement at the thought of the trip. He was certain the shift in scenery, the small new joys of wandering unfamiliar roads and conversing with wayfarers, would knit me back entirely to my old self by the time I came home.
source_text: These feelings dictated my answer to my father. I expressed a wish to visit England, but concealing the true reasons of this request, I clothed my desires under a guise which excited no suspicion, while I urged my desire with an earnestness that easily induced my father to comply. After so long a period of an absorbing melancholy that resembled madness in its intensity and effects, he was glad to find that I was capable of taking pleasure in the idea of such a journey, and he hoped that change of scene and varied amusement would, before my return, have restored me entirely to myself.

text: I’ll explain where all these feelings are leading me soon enough, but let me turn back now to the cottage residents first. Their story roused such a jumble of conflicting emotions in me: sharp indignation, bright delight, and stunned wonder, all of which ultimately coalesced into even deeper love and reverence for my protectors. That’s what I liked to call them, after all, clinging to a guileless, half-aching little act of self-deception.
source_text: “I will soon explain to what these feelings tended, but allow me now to return to the cottagers, whose story excited in me such various feelings of indignation, delight, and wonder, but which all terminated in additional love and reverence for my protectors (for so I loved, in an innocent, half-painful self-deceit, to call them).”

text: Somewhere between two and three o’clock in the pre-dawn dark, the moon finally lifted above the tree-lined shore; I slung my wicker crate over the gunwale of my dented small dinghy then, and drifted and sculled until I was nearly four miles out from land. The whole expanse of water around me felt utterly empty; a handful of fishing cutters were heading back toward the docks, but I veered deliberately away from their wake. A cold dread coiled in my gut, as though I was poised to carry out some unforgivable wrong, and I shrank from even the distant glint of another person’s lantern, my shoulders hunching with jittery unease. The moon, which had burned sharp and bright only minutes before, was suddenly swallowed by a bank of thick, roiling cloud, and I seized that stretch of total darkness to heave my wicker crate over the side; I held my breath, listening to the bubbling, gurgling rattle as it slipped below the waves, then pushed off hard from the spot with my oar. The sky stayed streaked and heavy with cloud, but the air was crisp and clean, sharpened by the northeast wind that had just begun to pick up off the water. It bit at my cheeks and cleared the fog from my head, filling me with a quiet, unexpected ease, so I decided to linger out on the waves longer than I’d planned; I wedged the rudder in place to hold a steady course, then stretched out flat along the planked floor of the dinghy. Clouds kept the moon entirely hidden, every outline around me blurred into shadow, and the only sound I could pick out was the soft, steady slice of the dinghy’s keel through the small, rolling swells; that low, rhythmic murmur wrapped around me like a blanket, and before long I had drifted off into a deep, dreamless sleep.
source_text: Between two and three in the morning the moon rose; and I then, putting my basket aboard a little skiff, sailed out about four miles from the shore. The scene was perfectly solitary; a few boats were returning towards land, but I sailed away from them. I felt as if I was about the commission of a dreadful crime and avoided with shuddering anxiety any encounter with my fellow creatures. At one time the moon, which had before been clear, was suddenly overspread by a thick cloud, and I took advantage of the moment of darkness and cast my basket into the sea; I listened to the gurgling sound as it sank and then sailed away from the spot. The sky became clouded, but the air was pure, although chilled by the northeast breeze that was then rising. But it refreshed me and filled me with such agreeable sensations that I resolved to prolong my stay on the water, and fixing the rudder in a direct position, stretched myself at the bottom of the boat. Clouds hid the moon, everything was obscure, and I heard only the sound of the boat as its keel cut through the waves; the murmur lulled me, and in a short time I slept soundly.

text: I can scarce put into words the feelings that overtake me as my expedition draws close at hand. There is no way to convey to you the flutter of emotion I carry as I ready to leave—equal parts bright thrill and sharp unease. I head for uncharted territories, for that “land of mist and snow,” but I will bring no harm to any albatross, so you need not fear for my safety, nor fret if I return to you as haggard and heavy-hearted as the Ancient Mariner of the poem. You may smile at this reference, but I will share a secret with you. I have long traced my deep, fervent obsession with the ocean’s dangerous, hidden wonders back to that very work by the most visionary of modern poets. There is a force at work in my core I cannot fully explain. I am, by nature, methodical and diligent—meticulous, a laborer who sees tasks through with steady grit and effort—but tangled up in every one of my plans is a fierce love of the extraordinary, a wholehearted belief in its existence, that pushes me beyond the well-worn paths of ordinary men, all the way to the wild, unvisited seas I am soon to traverse.
source_text: I cannot describe to you my sensations on the near prospect of my undertaking. It is impossible to communicate to you a conception of the trembling sensation, half pleasurable and half fearful, with which I am preparing to depart. I am going to unexplored regions, to “the land of mist and snow,” but I shall kill no albatross; therefore do not be alarmed for my safety or if I should come back to you as worn and woeful as the “Ancient Mariner.” You will smile at my allusion, but I will disclose a secret. I have often attributed my attachment to, my passionate enthusiasm for, the dangerous mysteries of ocean to that production of the most imaginative of modern poets. There is something at work in my soul which I do not understand. I am practically industrious—painstaking, a workman to execute with perseverance and labour—but besides this there is a love for the marvellous, a belief in the marvellous, intertwined in all my projects, which hurries me out of the common pathways of men, even to the wild sea and unvisited regions I am about to explore.

text: The sharp crack of the pistol drew a throng flooding into the room. I pointed to the very spot where he had vanished, and we set out in boats to follow his trail; we cast our nets repeatedly, but all efforts came to nothing. After several hours had slipped by, we headed back despondent, most of my companions convinced the figure had been nothing more than a hallucination conjured by my own imagination. Once we landed, they set about searching the surrounding countryside, small parties splitting off in different directions through the dense woods and tangled vines.
source_text: The report of the pistol brought a crowd into the room. I pointed to the spot where he had disappeared, and we followed the track with boats; nets were cast, but in vain. After passing several hours, we returned hopeless, most of my companions believing it to have been a form conjured up by my fancy. After having landed, they proceeded to search the country, parties going in different directions among the woods and vines.

text: He was brought back to the house, and the raw, unmistakeable agony written across my face gave the terrible secret away to Elizabeth immediately. She begged insistently to be allowed to see the body. I tried to stop her at first, but she refused to back down; she stepped into the room where he lay, darted a frantic, hasty look at the victim’s neck, then clasped her hands tight to her chest and cried out, “Oh God! I’ve murdered my darling child!”
source_text: “He was conveyed home, and the anguish that was visible in my countenance betrayed the secret to Elizabeth. She was very earnest to see the corpse. At first I attempted to prevent her but she persisted, and entering the room where it lay, hastily examined the neck of the victim, and clasping her hands exclaimed, ‘O God! I have murdered my darling child!’

text: After nearly a week of drifting, unmoored and listless, as I tramped nearly thirty miles across rolling hill country, I stumbled into Rothenburg, where I lingered two days waiting for Thaddeus. When he finally turned up, the gulf between our states of mind might as well have stretched a thousand miles wide. Every new vista made his eyes light up; he glowed watching the sunset bleed pink over the ridgeline, and fairly bubbled over with delight when he roused at dawn to watch the sun paint the world awake again. He kept tugging my sleeve to point out the kaleidoscopic shift of the valley’s hues at twilight, or the wispy, gilded clouds streaked across the midday sky. “This is what being alive feels like!” he exclaimed, breathless with glee. “I’m savoring every single second of it! But you, my dearest Elias, why do you look so weighed down, so full of grief?” The truth was, I was so swallowed by dark, circling thoughts that I never noticed the evening star peek over the horizon, nor the honeyed glow of dawn spilling across the waters of the Neckar below the town walls. Any reader, I think, would take far more pleasure in paging through Thaddeus’s journal, where he recorded every sight with warmth and unbridled delight, than in lingering on my own dreary musings. I was nothing but a miserable, broken thing, hounded by a curse that bolted shut every possible path to joy.
source_text: After some days spent in listless indolence, during which I traversed many leagues, I arrived at Strasburgh, where I waited two days for Clerval. He came. Alas, how great was the contrast between us! He was alive to every new scene, joyful when he saw the beauties of the setting sun, and more happy when he beheld it rise and recommence a new day. He pointed out to me the shifting colours of the landscape and the appearances of the sky. “This is what it is to live,” he cried; “now I enjoy existence! But you, my dear Frankenstein, wherefore are you desponding and sorrowful!” In truth, I was occupied by gloomy thoughts and neither saw the descent of the evening star nor the golden sunrise reflected in the Rhine. And you, my friend, would be far more amused with the journal of Clerval, who observed the scenery with an eye of feeling and delight, than in listening to my reflections. I, a miserable wretch, haunted by a curse that shut up every avenue to enjoyment.

text: A little while later I pulled myself to my feet, and almost on pure instinct, dragged myself into the chamber where my dearest one’s body rested. Groups of weeping women huddled around the bier; I leaned over the still form and let my own bitter tears fall alongside theirs, and for the longest stretch I couldn’t hold a single clear thought in my head. My mind jumped wildly from one disjointed topic to the next as I tried and failed to make sense of my unending misfortunes and where they had all started. I felt completely unmoored, adrift in a fog of stunned disbelief and gut-churning terror. William’s death, Justine’s unfair execution, Clerval’s brutal murder, and now my wife’s end too; even in that foggy state I knew I had no guarantee the last of my loved ones were out of the monster’s cruel reach. My father could be suffering under his cruel hold right that second, and Ernest might already be lifeless where he’d fallen at the creature’s feet. That thought sent a violent shudder down my spine and jolted me out of my dazed stupor. I snapped fully alert, my mind made up to get back to Geneva as fast as I possibly could.
source_text: After an interval I arose, and as if by instinct, crawled into the room where the corpse of my beloved lay. There were women weeping around; I hung over it and joined my sad tears to theirs; all this time no distinct idea presented itself to my mind, but my thoughts rambled to various subjects, reflecting confusedly on my misfortunes and their cause. I was bewildered, in a cloud of wonder and horror. The death of William, the execution of Justine, the murder of Clerval, and lastly of my wife; even at that moment I knew not that my only remaining friends were safe from the malignity of the fiend; my father even now might be writhing under his grasp, and Ernest might be dead at his feet. This idea made me shudder and recalled me to action. I started up and resolved to return to Geneva with all possible speed.

text: Clerval had never shared my fervor for natural science; his own literary pursuits were entirely disconnected from the work that had long occupied my days. He had come to the university with the explicit aim of mastering the principal oriental languages, so he might open a path for the life he had carefully charted for himself. Determined to pursue no unworthy, obscure career, he turned his gaze to the East, which he saw as fertile ground for his enterprising, adventurous spirit. The Persian, Arabic, and Sanskrit languages soon claimed his full attention, and I needed little convincing to join him in these studies. Idleness had always been intolerable to me, and in this period I was desperate to escape my own ruminations, as well as deeply repulsed by the scientific work I had once devoted myself to. Being a fellow student alongside my friend brought me enormous relief; I found not just instruction, but profound consolation in the works of oriental writers. Unlike Clerval, I made no attempt to gain a critical, thorough mastery of their dialects, since I had no intention of using them for anything beyond temporary amusement. I read only to grasp their plain meaning, and my efforts were richly rewarded. Their quiet melancholy was soothing, their joy uplifting, to a degree I had never experienced in the writing of authors from any other nation. When you lose yourself in their works, life seems to consist of nothing but warm sunlight and a garden of roses, the smiles and frowns of a beautiful, teasing adversary, and the fire that consumes your own heart. It could not be more different from the stoic, heroic poetry of Greece and Rome.
source_text: Clerval had never sympathised in my tastes for natural science; and his literary pursuits differed wholly from those which had occupied me. He came to the university with the design of making himself complete master of the oriental languages, and thus he should open a field for the plan of life he had marked out for himself. Resolved to pursue no inglorious career, he turned his eyes toward the East, as affording scope for his spirit of enterprise. The Persian, Arabic, and Sanskrit languages engaged his attention, and I was easily induced to enter on the same studies. Idleness had ever been irksome to me, and now that I wished to fly from reflection, and hated my former studies, I felt great relief in being the fellow-pupil with my friend, and found not only instruction but consolation in the works of the orientalists. I did not, like him, attempt a critical knowledge of their dialects, for I did not contemplate making any other use of them than temporary amusement. I read merely to understand their meaning, and they well repaid my labours. Their melancholy is soothing, and their joy elevating, to a degree I never experienced in studying the authors of any other country. When you read their writings, life appears to consist in a warm sun and a garden of roses,—in the smiles and frowns of a fair enemy, and the fire that consumes your own heart. How different from the manly and heroical poetry of Greece and Rome!

text: The unschooled homesteader observed the natural forces surrounding his small plot and knew their practical functions intimately. The most erudite natural scholar understood barely anything beyond that. He had pulled back a corner of the wild world’s veil, but its unchanging, ageless features remained as much a marvel and enigma as they were to the farmer. He could cut apart specimens, map their internal structures, and assign taxonomical labels to every piece; yet even setting aside the question of ultimate purpose, the second and third order forces driving those systems were entirely beyond his grasp. I had stared for years at the ramparts and barriers that seemed to bar mortal minds from stepping into nature’s innermost stronghold, and foolhardy and unknowing as I was, I had railed against them.
source_text: The untaught peasant beheld the elements around him and was acquainted with their practical uses. The most learned philosopher knew little more. He had partially unveiled the face of Nature, but her immortal lineaments were still a wonder and a mystery. He might dissect, anatomise, and give names; but, not to speak of a final cause, causes in their secondary and tertiary grades were utterly unknown to him. I had gazed upon the fortifications and impediments that seemed to keep human beings from entering the citadel of nature, and rashly and ignorantly I had repined.

text: We don’t always limit our conversations to his personal history and the misfortunes he’s endured. On every subject within general literature, he showcases a staggering breadth of knowledge and a sharp, perceptive grasp of nuance. He speaks with such compelling, heartfelt eloquence that I never manage to hold back tears when he recounts a devastating story, or tries to rouse feelings of pity or tenderness in those listening. If he carries himself with this much dignity and almost divine grace even in ruin, I can only fathom how extraordinary he must have been in the height of his success. He seems fully conscious of both his own inherent worth, and the sheer magnitude of how far he has fallen.
source_text: Our conversations are not always confined to his own history and misfortunes. On every point of general literature he displays unbounded knowledge and a quick and piercing apprehension. His eloquence is forcible and touching; nor can I hear him, when he relates a pathetic incident or endeavours to move the passions of pity or love, without tears. What a glorious creature must he have been in the days of his prosperity, when he is thus noble and godlike in ruin! He seems to feel his own worth and the greatness of his fall.

text: Not long after, the dark-haired lad trundled back, a heavy stack of split kindling balanced across his broad shoulders. The fair young woman met him on the cottage threshold, slipping one edge of the bundle off to lighten his load, then carried an armful of the dry wood straight to the hearth and stacked it beneath the flickering flames. The pair drifted off to a shadowed alcove off the main room next, where he pulled a crusty round loaf and a thick wedge of herbed cheese from the lining of his wool coat. Her face lit up at the sight, and she hurried out to the kitchen garden to tug up a handful of tender turnips and leafy wild herbs, which she soaked briefly in a pail of cold well water before setting them to simmer in a dented iron pot over the fire. She turned back to her half-mended shirt after that, while the lad headed out to the same garden plot, his movements quick and focused as he turned over loamy soil and pulled up straggling root vegetables. Roughly an hour passed before she set her needlework aside and joined him, and the two walked back through the cottage door side by side, their hands brushing as they stepped over the threshold.
source_text: “Soon after this the young man returned, bearing on his shoulders a load of wood. The girl met him at the door, helped to relieve him of his burden, and taking some of the fuel into the cottage, placed it on the fire; then she and the youth went apart into a nook of the cottage, and he showed her a large loaf and a piece of cheese. She seemed pleased and went into the garden for some roots and plants, which she placed in water, and then upon the fire. She afterwards continued her work, whilst the young man went into the garden and appeared busily employed in digging and pulling up roots. After he had been employed thus about an hour, the young woman joined him and they entered the cottage together.

text: “I’ll be frank with you, my son: I have long held out hope that your marriage to our dear Elizabeth would be the thread that knits our household’s comfort tight, and a steady support for me as I enter my final years. The two of you were fond of one another from earliest babyhood, learned side by side, and seemed perfectly matched in every turn of temperament and every personal taste. But human foresight is so painfully blind that the very details I thought would ensure this plan’s success may have undone it completely. You may see Elizabeth as nothing more than your sister, with no desire to ever make her your wife. What’s more, you might have met another woman you love, and believing you are honor-bound to Elizabeth, that internal struggle could be the cause of the sharp, gnawing misery you’ve been carrying so plainly of late.”
source_text: “I confess, my son, that I have always looked forward to your marriage with our dear Elizabeth as the tie of our domestic comfort and the stay of my declining years. You were attached to each other from your earliest infancy; you studied together, and appeared, in dispositions and tastes, entirely suited to one another. But so blind is the experience of man that what I conceived to be the best assistants to my plan may have entirely destroyed it. You, perhaps, regard her as your sister, without any wish that she might become your wife. Nay, you may have met with another whom you may love; and considering yourself as bound in honour to Elizabeth, this struggle may occasion the poignant misery which you appear to feel.”

text: The ruling council of Veyra seethed with unbridled fury when their high-profile political prisoner slipped their grasp, and poured every resource at their disposal into hunting down the person who’d orchestrated his breakout. Elias’s role in the plot was uncovered within days, and his blind, elderly father Thibault and soft-spoken younger sister Elodie were seized and thrown into a squalid, vermin-ridden city jail. Word of the arrests found Elias while he was still drifting in the giddy haze of his new romance, and the shock ripped him violently out of his sunlit reverie. The thought that his infirm father and gentle sister were rotting in a reeking, lightless cell while he wandered free in the southern countryside, doted on by the woman he adored, felt like a hot knife twisted steadily between his ribs. He struck a hasty deal with Kadir, the freed Levantine prisoner: if the man found a safe chance to flee the region before Elias could return from his journey, Lira, Kadir’s daughter and Elias’s beloved, would take up temporary residence as a boarder in the coastal convent at San Luca until he could send for her. Leaving the dark-eyed young woman he loved behind, he raced straight to the Veyraussian capital, turning himself over to the courts without resistance, hoping his voluntary surrender would convince the authorities to release his father and sister from their captivity.
source_text: “The government of France were greatly enraged at the escape of their victim and spared no pains to detect and punish his deliverer. The plot of Felix was quickly discovered, and De Lacey and Agatha were thrown into prison. The news reached Felix and roused him from his dream of pleasure. His blind and aged father and his gentle sister lay in a noisome dungeon while he enjoyed the free air and the society of her whom he loved. This idea was torture to him. He quickly arranged with the Turk that if the latter should find a favourable opportunity for escape before Felix could return to Italy, Safie should remain as a boarder at a convent at Leghorn; and then, quitting the lovely Arabian, he hastened to Paris and delivered himself up to the vengeance of the law, hoping to free De Lacey and Agatha by this proceeding.

text: I wrote to you in my last correspondence of the gnawing dread I held that the crew might rise in mutiny. This very dawn, as I sat hunched beside my dearest companion, tracing the gaunt, ashen cast of his face—his lids heavy, half-shuttered, his limbs draped slack and unresponsive across the cot—I was jolted from my reverie by the sharp, insistent rap of half a dozen seamen, demanding to be let into the captain’s quarters. They filed in, their thick wool coats crusted with frost, and their spokesperson stepped forward to address me. He explained that the rest of the crew had selected him and his mates as a delegation to lay a demand before me, one I could not in good conscience turn down. We were locked fast in the pack ice, he said, and would likely never claw our way free at all—but if by some miracle the floes broke apart and cleared a path back to open water, they feared I would be foolhardy enough to press onward with our expedition, dragging them headlong into new perils the moment they had barely survived this one. They insisted, therefore, that I bind myself with a solemn promise: the second our vessel broke free of the ice, I would turn her prow due south and set course for warmer waters at once.
source_text: I mentioned in my last letter the fears I entertained of a mutiny. This morning, as I sat watching the wan countenance of my friend—his eyes half closed and his limbs hanging listlessly—I was roused by half a dozen of the sailors, who demanded admission into the cabin. They entered, and their leader addressed me. He told me that he and his companions had been chosen by the other sailors to come in deputation to me to make me a requisition which, in justice, I could not refuse. We were immured in ice and should probably never escape, but they feared that if, as was possible, the ice should dissipate and a free passage be opened, I should be rash enough to continue my voyage and lead them into fresh dangers, after they might happily have surmounted this. They insisted, therefore, that I should engage with a solemn promise that if the vessel should be freed I would instantly direct my course southwards.

text: My grandfather never wrote a word of censure in his correspondence, and only remarked on my long unresponsiveness by pressing for details of my daily work far more insistently than he ever had before. Frost, bloom, and swelter slipped by while I bent over my project; I did not pause to mark the cherry blossoms unfurling or the new oak leaves stretching toward the sun—sights that had once filled me with unrivaled joy—so wholly was I consumed by the task at hand. That year’s foliage had already curled brown and crumbled underfoot before my work edged close to completion, and each passing hour laid bare how thoroughly my efforts were paying off. Yet any spark of excitement was smothered under a constant, gnawing dread, and I moved more like a convict condemned to life laboring in fume-choked quarries or some other noxious line of work than a crafter chasing a long-held dream. Each night I was wracked by a low, persistent fever, and my nerves frayed to an almost unbearable pitch; the rustle of a dead leaf against my window made me flinch, and I avoided all contact with other people as though I carried the weight of some unspoken, terrible sin. Now and then I stared at my own reflection and recoiled at the gaunt, hollow-eyed wreck I had turned into; only the single-minded drive of my goal kept me on my feet: my toil would be over soon, I told myself, and long walks and lighthearted company would chase off the first stirrings of illness before they could take root; I swore I would grant myself both of these comforts the moment my creation was finished.
source_text: My father made no reproach in his letters and only took notice of my silence by inquiring into my occupations more particularly than before. Winter, spring, and summer passed away during my labours; but I did not watch the blossom or the expanding leaves—sights which before always yielded me supreme delight—so deeply was I engrossed in my occupation. The leaves of that year had withered before my work drew near to a close, and now every day showed me more plainly how well I had succeeded. But my enthusiasm was checked by my anxiety, and I appeared rather like one doomed by slavery to toil in the mines, or any other unwholesome trade than an artist occupied by his favourite employment. Every night I was oppressed by a slow fever, and I became nervous to a most painful degree; the fall of a leaf startled me, and I shunned my fellow creatures as if I had been guilty of a crime. Sometimes I grew alarmed at the wreck I perceived that I had become; the energy of my purpose alone sustained me: my labours would soon end, and I believed that exercise and amusement would then drive away incipient disease; and I promised myself both of these when my creation should be complete.

text: “You have the wrong of it,” the outcast answered; “I do not come to snarl threats, but to speak fairly with you. I lash out because I am wretched to my core. Is there a single soul alive that does not flinch from my shadow, that does not spit my name with loathing? You, who shaped me from dust and spark, would rend me limb from limb and cheer the sight. Keep that in mind, and tell me why I ought to spare humanity a shred of mercy it has never once offered me? You would not name it killing if you could hurl me down one of those frosty crevasses, and smash to shards the body you yourself stitched and breathed life into. Why should I hold any reverence for a species that condemns me unheard? If even one person would dwell beside me, exchanging small kindnesses, I would do them no harm at all—instead, I would heap every good I could offer at their feet, weeping with gratitude that they deigned to see me as more than a monster. But that will never come to pass. The revulsion written into human eyes is a wall I can never climb over. But I will not bow and scrape like a beaten dog to beg for crumbs of kindness either. So I will pay back every wrong I have suffered. If I cannot make anyone love me, I will make them fear me—and most of all you, my bitterest foe, who made me only to cast me out. I swear to you a hatred that will never burn out, never fade. Watch yourself. I will spend every day of my endless life tearing your world apart, and I will not stop until your heart is a hollow, frozen waste, and you curse the very moment you ever drew breath to bring me into being.”
source_text: “You are in the wrong,” replied the fiend; “and instead of threatening, I am content to reason with you. I am malicious because I am miserable. Am I not shunned and hated by all mankind? You, my creator, would tear me to pieces and triumph; remember that, and tell me why I should pity man more than he pities me? You would not call it murder if you could precipitate me into one of those ice-rifts and destroy my frame, the work of your own hands. Shall I respect man when he condemns me? Let him live with me in the interchange of kindness, and instead of injury I would bestow every benefit upon him with tears of gratitude at his acceptance. But that cannot be; the human senses are insurmountable barriers to our union. Yet mine shall not be the submission of abject slavery. I will revenge my injuries; if I cannot inspire love, I will cause fear, and chiefly towards you my arch-enemy, because my creator, do I swear inextinguishable hatred. Have a care; I will work at your destruction, nor finish until I desolate your heart, so that you shall curse the hour of your birth.”

text: As the night wore on, a brutal, howling wind gusted up from the deep woods, scattering the slow-loitering clouds that had hung heavy across the sky in seconds. The gale tore through the air like a crashing avalanche, tipping my mind into a rabid frenzy that shattered every last boundary of reason and careful thought. I lit a dry, gnarled tree branch and danced in a wild rage around the cottage I’d marked for destruction, my eyes locked tight to the western horizon where the moon hovered just a hair’s breadth above the edge. When the first sliver of its disc dipped out of view, I swung my burning torch high; the moment it sank fully out of sight, I let out a deafening, ragged scream and touched the flame to the pile of straw, heath, and dry brush I’d stashed beforehand. The wind fanned the fire to a roar, and the cottage was quickly wrapped in roiling flames, which coiled tight to its walls and lapped at every inch of it with their sharp, forked, ravenous tongues.
source_text: “As the night advanced, a fierce wind arose from the woods and quickly dispersed the clouds that had loitered in the heavens; the blast tore along like a mighty avalanche and produced a kind of insanity in my spirits that burst all bounds of reason and reflection. I lighted the dry branch of a tree and danced with fury around the devoted cottage, my eyes still fixed on the western horizon, the edge of which the moon nearly touched. A part of its orb was at length hid, and I waved my brand; it sank, and with a loud scream I fired the straw, and heath, and bushes, which I had collected. The wind fanned the fire, and the cottage was quickly enveloped by the flames, which clung to it and licked it with their forked and destroying tongues.

text: Right now is the ideal window for traveling across Russia. People zip over the crisp, packed snow in their sleds, and the motion of the ride is genuinely pleasant—far more enjoyable, in my book, than jostling along in an English stagecoach. The cold isn’t even unmanageably harsh, as long as you’re wrapped snugly in furs, a garment I’ve already taken to wearing. There’s a world of difference between pacing back and forth on a ship’s deck and sitting perfectly motionless for hours at a stretch, after all; with no physical activity to keep your circulation going, your blood could practically freeze in your veins otherwise. I have zero interest in winding up dead on the postal road between Saint Petersburg and Arkhangelsk.
source_text: This is the most favourable period for travelling in Russia. They fly quickly over the snow in their sledges; the motion is pleasant, and, in my opinion, far more agreeable than that of an English stagecoach. The cold is not excessive, if you are wrapped in furs—a dress which I have already adopted, for there is a great difference between walking the deck and remaining seated motionless for hours, when no exercise prevents the blood from actually freezing in your veins. I have no ambition to lose my life on the post-road between St. Petersburgh and Archangel.

text: Since the particulars of his union lay bare the core of his nature, I cannot hold myself back from recounting them. One of his dearest confidants was a trader who, once at the height of commercial success, had been reduced to penury by a string of unforeseen calamities. This man, Theron by name, carried a stubborn, unyielding streak, and refused to scrape by in obscurity and want in the very nation where he had once been celebrated for his status and lavish generosity. After settling every last one of his debts with scrupulous integrity, he retreated with his only daughter to the city of Innsbruck, where he passed his days unrecognized and mired in hardship. My father cherished Theron with the most sincere of bonds, and was profoundly aggrieved that his friend would flee in the face of such misfortune. He railed against the misplaced pride that had driven Theron to act in a manner so unworthy of the deep affection that bound the two men together. He wasted no time in trying to track him down, hoping to convince him to rebuild his livelihood with the support of his connections and financial backing.
source_text: As the circumstances of his marriage illustrate his character, I cannot refrain from relating them. One of his most intimate friends was a merchant who, from a flourishing state, fell, through numerous mischances, into poverty. This man, whose name was Beaufort, was of a proud and unbending disposition and could not bear to live in poverty and oblivion in the same country where he had formerly been distinguished for his rank and magnificence. Having paid his debts, therefore, in the most honourable manner, he retreated with his daughter to the town of Lucerne, where he lived unknown and in wretchedness. My father loved Beaufort with the truest friendship and was deeply grieved by his retreat in these unfortunate circumstances. He bitterly deplored the false pride which led his friend to a conduct so little worthy of the affection that united them. He lost no time in endeavouring to seek him out, with the hope of persuading him to begin the world again through his credit and assistance.

text: With the knapsack slung over his back, he was fully kitted out now, and looked less like a monarch than any man I’d ever laid eyes on. But those shoulders of his were stubborn to a fault; they never quite picked up the knack of hunching with any sort of convincingly natural, unforced ease. The drill carried on, with me prompting and correcting him as we went:
source_text: He was complete now with that knapsack on, and looked as little like a king as any man I had ever seen. But it was an obstinate pair of shoulders; they could not seem to learn the trick of stooping with any sort of deceptive naturalness. The drill went on, I prompting and correcting:

text: Elspeth and I chatted about the old chivalric tale she’d been recounting as we jogged along the heath, and she told me Sir Kaelen’s string of misfortunes had kicked off the very instant that quest began: the court’s fool-knight had unhorsed him on the first day, and by all the rules of the time, the lady accompanying a defeated knight was supposed to switch her loyalties to the victor, but Liora never did. Even after every subsequent crushing loss, she kept right on staying by his side, stubborn as a bramble. But, I cut in, what if the winning knight turned down that particular prize? She shook her head firmly, said that wouldn’t fly—he had to take her. There was no refusing; it went against every custom of tourney and quest alike. I tucked that little fact away in my head. If Elspeth’s endless folk tales and off-key hiking songs ever got too unbearable down the line, I could just let some wandering knight beat me in a joust, on the off chance she’d decide to follow him instead.
source_text: Sandy and I discussed his story, as we rode along, and she said that La Cote's bad luck had begun with the very beginning of that trip; for the king's fool had overthrown him on the first day, and in such cases it was customary for the girl to desert to the conqueror, but Maledisant didn't do it; and also persisted afterward in sticking to him, after all his defeats. But, said I, suppose the victor should decline to accept his spoil? She said that that wouldn't answer--he must. He couldn't decline; it wouldn't be regular. I made a note of that. If Sandy's music got to be too burdensome, some time, I would let a knight defeat me, on the chance that she would desert to him.

text: I did not laugh—this is a mercy I have always been deeply thankful for—but the effort of holding back my reaction shredded every cartilage in my body, and for weeks afterward I could hear my bones clack against one another with every step I took. She never learned of her mistake at all. The first time she heard that particular form of salute used when answering the telephone, she was taken aback, and not at all pleased; but I told her I had issued explicit orders for it: that from then on, for all time, the telephone must always be addressed with that reverent formality, as a perpetual tribute to and remembrance of my lost friend and her small namesake. This was not the truth. But it served its purpose perfectly.
source_text: I didn't laugh--I am always thankful for that--but the strain ruptured every cartilage in me, and for weeks afterward I could hear my bones clack when I walked. She never found out her mistake. The first time she heard that form of salute used at the telephone she was surprised, and not pleased; but I told her I had given order for it: that henceforth and forever the telephone must always be invoked with that reverent formality, in perpetual honor and remembrance of my lost friend and her small namesake. This was not true. But it answered.

text: Dusk had fully fallen by now, so I called my outposted pickets back in. The man assigned to the northern watch reported he’d spotted an enemy camp, though it was only visible through a spyglass. He also noted that a small party of knights had been creeping cautiously toward our position, and had driven a few head of cattle across our perimeter, though the knights themselves never ventured particularly close. That was exactly what I’d been expecting. They were probing us, you see—trying to suss out if we were going to unleash that brutal red terror on them again. They’d probably get far bolder once full night set in, too. I was confident I knew what gambit they’d attempt next, because it was plainly the exact move I’d make myself if I were in their position and as ignorant of our strengths as they were. I ran the whole theory by Clarence right then.
source_text: It was nightfall now, and I withdrew my pickets. The one who had had the northern outlook reported a camp in sight, but visible with the glass only. He also reported that a few knights had been feeling their way toward us, and had driven some cattle across our lines, but that the knights themselves had not come very near. That was what I had been expecting. They were feeling us, you see; they wanted to know if we were going to play that red terror on them again. They would grow bolder in the night, perhaps.
