I believed I knew what project they would attempt, because it was plainly the thing I would attempt myself if I were in their places and as ignorant as they were. I mentioned it to Clarence.

text: “I am struck utterly dumb by the sheer frivolity of this wretch’s words. Let all present mark this, if perchance any among you have not yet learned it: sorcerers of my rank do not deign to occupy themselves with the affairs of any save kings, crown princes, emperors, those born to the crimson of royalty and no others. Had you asked me what High King Kaelen the Farsighted is occupied with, it would be an altogether different matter, and I would have answered you without hesitation; the doings of a lowly subject, though, hold not a shred of interest for me.”
source_text: “It hath struck me speechless, the frivolity of this person's speech. Let all know, if perchance there be any who know it not, that enchanters of my degree deign not to concern themselves with the doings of any but kings, princes, emperors, them that be born in the purple and them only. Had ye asked me what Arthur the great king is doing, it were another matter, and I had told ye; but the doings of a subject interest me not.”

text: For the most part, the realm of Vorath looked much the same as it had the day I first crossed its misty northern border three years prior. I’d implemented tweaks here and there, of course, but all were deliberately small, unobtrusive enough that most common folk never marked them. Up to this point, I’d not dared touch the broad, kingdom-wide tax codes at all, save for the specific levies that fed the crown’s treasury. I’d overhauled those levy systems entirely, rooting out embezzlement among collectors and rewriting collection rules to run efficiently and equitably for all who owed payment. The end result was that crown income had already quadrupled, even as the weight of those levies was spread far more fairly across the population than it ever had been under the old royal regime. The entire realm breathed easier for it, and warm, widespread acclaim for my governance rolled in from every corner of Vorath.
source_text: As for the general condition of the country, it was as it had been when I arrived in it, to all intents and purposes. I had made changes, but they were necessarily slight, and they were not noticeable. Thus far, I had not even meddled with taxation, outside of the taxes which provided the royal revenues. I had systematized those, and put the service on an effective and righteous basis. As a result, these revenues were already quadrupled, and yet the burden was so much more equably distributed than before, that all the kingdom felt a sense of relief, and the praises of my administration were hearty and general.

text: He dragged the tale out at length, but the only details that held immediate urgency for me were these: I was Sir Kay’s prisoner, he said, and per longstanding custom I would be thrown into a dungeon, kept on meager rations until my people paid my ransom—if I did not waste away and rot there first. I knew that second outcome was far more likely, but I did not waste breath fretting over it; every moment was too precious to squander. The page went on to explain that dinner in the great hall was nearly finished by now, and once the merrymaking and heavy drinking got underway, Sir Kay would bring me in to parade before King Arthur and his famed knights gathered at the Round Table. He would boast of his exploit in capturing me, likely embroidering the facts a fair bit, and it would be neither proper nor particularly safe for me to correct him. Once the showing off was done, it would be straight to the dungeon—but he, Clarence, would find a way to slip in to see me now and then, lift my spirits, and help me send word back to my friends.
source_text: He made a long story of it; but the part that had immediate interest for me was this: He said I was Sir Kay's prisoner, and that in the due course of custom I would be flung into a dungeon and left there on scant commons until my friends ransomed me--unless I chanced to rot, first. I saw that the last chance had the best show, but I didn't waste any bother about that; time was too precious. The page said, further, that dinner was about ended in the great hall by this time, and that as soon as the sociability and the heavy drinking should begin, Sir Kay would have me in and exhibit me before King Arthur and his illustrious knights seated at the Table Round, and would brag about his exploit in capturing me, and would probably exaggerate the facts a little, but it wouldn't be good form for me to correct him, and not over safe, either; and when I was done being exhibited, then ho for the dungeon; but he, Clarence, would find a way to come and see me every now and then, and cheer me up, and help me get word to my friends.

text: Poor girl, going that long without chattering had been absolute torture for her, and I knew I was about to deal with the consequences. I wasn’t wrong, either. But she’d stood by me so reliably back at the castle, backing me up and bailing me out with those huge, ridiculous stunts that turned out to be way more useful in the moment than twice as much carefully considered good sense could ever have been. So I figured she’d more than earned the right to run her mouth as much as she wanted for a while, and I didn’t feel even the smallest twinge of irritation when she finally got started:
source_text: Poor girl, her jaws had had a wearisome rest now for a while, and I was expecting to get the consequences. I was right; but she had stood by me most helpfully in the castle, and had mightily supported and reinforced me with gigantic foolishnesses which were worth more for the occasion than wisdoms double their size; so I thought she had earned a right to work her mill for a while, if she wanted to, and I felt not a pang when she started it up:

text: I always reach for that lofty, heightened register when I’m building to the peak of a dramatic effect. It was a noble sight, watching Launcelot and his crew surge up onto that scaffold and hurl sheriffs and other such officials right over the rails. And it was deeply satisfying to watch that stunned, gaping crowd drop to their knees, begging for their lives from the very king they had been jeering and insulting only moments prior. As he stood apart from the fray, clad in rags while receiving their groveling homage, I thought to myself that really, there is something uniquely grand about a king’s gait and bearing, after all.
source_text: I always use that high style when I'm climaxing an effect. Well, it was noble to see Launcelot and the boys swarm up onto that scaffold and heave sheriffs and such overboard. And it was fine to see that astonished multitude go down on their knees and beg their lives of the king they had just been deriding and insulting. And as he stood apart there, receiving this homage in rags, I thought to myself, well, really there is something peculiarly grand about the gait and bearing of a king, after all.

text: That phrase was definitely a powerful one to wield—you could tell just by how those sniveling little rats were wriggling where they stood. The queen’s guards snapped into formation, and she departed at their head, torch carriers trailing behind the group. The steady thud of their receding footsteps bounced off the walls of the vaulted tunnels, rousing the echoes that lingered in the dark space. I ordered the prisoner cut loose from the rack, laid gently on his cot, given healing salves for his wounds, and a cup of strong wine to sip. A woman crept close to the cot, watching him with a mix of eagerness, quiet devotion, and obvious skittishness, like she was terrified of being turned away. She even tried to brush his forehead on the sly, but yelped and scrambled back, the very picture of terror, when I turned toward her without meaning to. The whole sight was utterly heartbreaking.
source_text: It was certainly a good word to conjure with: you could see it by the squirming of these rats. The queen's guards fell into line, and she and they marched away, with their torch-bearers, and woke the echoes of the cavernous tunnels with the measured beat of their retreating footfalls. I had the prisoner taken from the rack and placed upon his bed, and medicaments applied to his hurts, and wine given him to drink. The woman crept near and looked on, eagerly, lovingly, but timorously,--like one who fears a repulse; indeed, she tried furtively to touch the man's forehead, and jumped back, the picture of fright, when I turned unconsciously toward her. It was pitiful to see.

text: We had no choice but to offer our thanks and accept the proposal. The six of us trotted along at a steady, comfortable pace, and over conversation learned that Lord Grip was an immensely prominent figure in his home domain, which lay a full day’s journey beyond Cambenet. We moved so slowly and stopped so often that it was nearly mid-forenoon by the time we entered the town’s market square. We dismounted, passed along another round of gratitude to Lord Grip, then headed toward a crowd clustered in the center of the square to see what had drawn everyone’s attention. It was the tattered remains of that old roving slave gang! They had been dragging their heavy chains from place to place this entire, weary stretch of time. That poor husband we’d encountered before was gone, along with many others from the group, though a small number of newly purchased enslaved people had been added to the gang. The king had no interest in the sight and wanted to press on, but I was transfixed, flooded with pity. I could not tear my eyes from these worn, emaciated wrecks of humanity. They sat huddled on the hard dirt, silent, uncomplaining, heads bowed low, a gut-wrenchingly pathetic sight. And in a hideous, jarring contrast, a long-winded orator was addressing another gathering not thirty paces away, gushing with over-the-top praise for “our glorious British liberties!”
source_text: We could do nothing less than express our thanks and accept the offer. We jogged along, six in the party, at a moderate and comfortable gait, and in conversation learned that my lord Grip was a very great personage in his own region, which lay a day's journey beyond Cambenet. We loitered to such a degree that it was near the middle of the forenoon when we entered the market square of the town. We dismounted, and left our thanks once more for my lord, and then approached a crowd assembled in the center of the square, to see what might be the object of interest. It was the remnant of that old peregrinating band of slaves! So they had been dragging their chains about, all this weary time. That poor husband was gone, and also many others; and some few purchases had been added to the gang. The king was not interested, and wanted to move along, but I was absorbed, and full of pity. I could not take my eyes away from these worn and wasted wrecks of humanity. There they sat, grounded upon the ground, silent, uncomplaining, with bowed heads, a pathetic sight. And by hideous contrast, a redundant orator was making a speech to another gathering not thirty steps away, in fulsome laudation of “our glorious British liberties!”

text: A sharp, distressing shock jolted through me. I began to reason that my situation was grave in the extreme, dream or no dream; I knew from past experience how intensely lifelike dreams could be, that being burned to death even in a dream was far from a trivial jest, and was a fate I would avoid by any means I could contrive, fair or foul. So I spoke, my tone thick with pleading:
source_text: The shock that went through me was distressing. I now began to reason that my situation was in the last degree serious, dream or no dream; for I knew by past experience of the lifelike intensity of dreams, that to be burned to death, even in a dream, would be very far from being a jest, and was a thing to be avoided, by any means, fair or foul, that I could contrive. So I said beseechingly:

text: We arrived at the monastery just as dusk began to fall; the men among us were given quarters on the grounds, while the women were escorted across to the adjacent nunnery. The monastery bells hung so close at hand now that their deep, reverberating tolls struck sharp against the ear, each low peal heavy as a message of impending doom. A crippling, superstitious despair had taken root in every monk’s chest, and it showed unmistakeably on their gaunt, ghastly faces. All around us, these figures in coarse black robes, soft-soled sandals, and sallow, wax-pale faces drifted into view, flitted across shadowed corners, and vanished again, as silent and uncanny as the phantoms that haunt a troubled, fitful dream.
source_text: We reached the monastery before dark, and there the males were given lodging, but the women were sent over to the nunnery. The bells were close at hand now, and their solemn booming smote upon the ear like a message of doom. A superstitious despair possessed the heart of every monk and published itself in his ghastly face. Everywhere, these black-robed, soft-sandaled, tallow-visaged specters appeared, flitted about and disappeared, noiseless as the creatures of a troubled dream, and as uncanny.

text: Naturally, we got a searing, unbroken sunny day—nearly the first entirely cloudless one in three whole weeks; the universe always lines up that way, doesn’t it? I stayed out of sight, perched by a narrow slit window to track every shift in the sky. Thorne stopped by every hour or so to report that public fervor was climbing higher by the minute, and the plains beyond the fortress walls were crammed with throngs of people, visible all the way to the horizon from the upper ramparts. Finally a sharp gust picked up, and a dark smudge of cloud bloomed on the horizon—exactly where I’d hoped it would, right as the sun dipped below the treeline. I tracked that distant cloud for a few minutes as it swelled and deepened to inky gray, then decided the moment had come for me to step forward. I gave the order to light the iron fire baskets strung along the walls, and for Mordryn to be released from his cell and brought to my quarters. Fifteen minutes later I climbed the outer parapet, and found the king and his entire court gathered there, all staring out into the gathering dark toward Mordryn’s Spire. The dark had grown thick enough to obscure anything more than a few yards away; the gathered nobles and the weathered stone turrets, half swallowed by deep shadow and half gilded by the fiery red glow of the fire baskets high above, made for a strikingly dramatic scene.
source_text: Of course, we had a blazing sunny day--almost the first one without a cloud for three weeks; things always happen so. I kept secluded, and watched the weather. Clarence dropped in from time to time and said the public excitement was growing and growing all the time, and the whole country filling up with human masses as far as one could see from the battlements. At last the wind sprang up and a cloud appeared--in the right quarter, too, and just at nightfall. For a little while I watched that distant cloud spread and blacken, then I judged it was time for me to appear. I ordered the torch-baskets to be lit, and Merlin liberated and sent to me. A quarter of an hour later I ascended the parapet and there found the king and the court assembled and gazing off in the darkness toward Merlin's Tower. Already the darkness was so heavy that one could not see far; these people and the old turrets, being partly in deep shadow and partly in the red glow from the great torch-baskets overhead, made a good deal of a picture.

text: Still, it would have been terrible political sense to let the king show up with zero pomp and circumstance at all, so I headed down to rustle up a procession of pilgrims, drag a whole batch of hermits out of their isolated retreats, and send the whole group off at two o’clock to greet him. That was exactly the chaotic state of things when he neared the grounds. When I led the abbot out onto a balcony to point out the royal procession marching toward us, with not a single monk on hand to welcome him, no sign of activity or peal of celebratory bells to lift his spirits, the man was paralyzed by a mix of rage and humiliation. He took one look and bolted to rouse his people. Seconds later, bells were clanging furiously, every building on the compound was spewing monks and nuns who swarmed in a frantic rush toward the approaching procession; right in their midst was that supposed magician, too—perched on a rail by the abbot’s direct order, his reputation thoroughly in the gutter while mine was soaring higher than ever. Yeah, you can keep your good name intact in a country like this, but you can’t laze around and expect it to happen on its own. You’ve got to be on the ground, tending to business nonstop.
source_text: However, it was not good politics to let the king come without any fuss and feathers at all, so I went down and drummed up a procession of pilgrims and smoked out a batch of hermits and started them out at two o'clock to meet him. And that was the sort of state he arrived in. The abbot was helpless with rage and humiliation when I brought him out on a balcony and showed him the head of the state marching in and never a monk on hand to offer him welcome, and no stir of life or clang of joy-bell to glad his spirit. He took one look and then flew to rouse out his forces. The next minute the bells were dinning furiously, and the various buildings were vomiting monks and nuns, who went swarming in a rush toward the coming procession; and with them went that magician --and he was on a rail, too, by the abbot's order; and his reputation was in the mud, and mine was in the sky again. Yes, a man can keep his trademark current in such a country, but he can't sit around and do it; he has got to be on deck and attending to business right along.

text: On the fourth day’s morning, just as the sun broke over the horizon, we’d already been tramping for an hour through the crisp chill of the dawn when I settled on a firm resolution: the king simply had to be drilled. Things could not keep going this way; I had to take him in hand and train him deliberately, thoroughly, with real care, or we would never dare set foot inside any dwelling at all. Even the house cats would see right through this masquerader, tell he was a fraud and no genuine peasant. So I called our march to a halt and said:
source_text: On the morning of the fourth day, when it was just sunrise, and we had been tramping an hour in the chill dawn, I came to a resolution: the king _must_ be drilled; things could not go on so, he must be taken in hand and deliberately and conscientiously drilled, or we couldn't ever venture to enter a dwelling; the very cats would know this masquerader for a humbug and no peasant. So I called a halt and said:

text: Goodness, will you just listen to this man? These people come from every single corner of the globe! Every last one has to get back to their own home; do you really think we could squeeze all that travel into the short, fleeting lifespan God set for us when he created life, and brought death into the world too, all thanks to Adam? He sinned because his wife talked him into it, right? And she’d been tricked and led astray by the lies of mankind’s great enemy, that serpent we call Satan. That devil was dead set on that wicked work ages before, all driven by that rabid, all-consuming spite and jealousy that took root in his chest from his ugly, power-hungry ambitions. Those ambitions corrupted and rotted a nature that was once so bright and pure, back when he drifted alongside the rest of his shining, angelic siblings in the open meadows and quiet nooks of that perfect heaven, where every being born to that blessed, glorious status and--
source_text: “La, but list to him! They be of all the regions of the earth! Each must hie to her own home; wend you we might do all these journeys in one so brief life as He hath appointed that created life, and thereto death likewise with help of Adam, who by sin done through persuasion of his helpmeet, she being wrought upon and bewrayed by the beguilements of the great enemy of man, that serpent hight Satan, aforetime consecrated and set apart unto that evil work by overmastering spite and envy begotten in his heart through fell ambitions that did blight and mildew a nature erst so white and pure whenso it hove with the shining multitudes its brethren-born in glade and shade of that fair heaven wherein all such as native be to that rich estate and--”

text: Chattel slavery had been fully abolished; every person stood equal under the nation’s legal code; tax burdens had been standardized fairly across all ranks of society. Wire telegraphs, domestic telephones, wax cylinder phonographs, office typewriters, motorized sewing machines, and thousands of other nimble, obedient innovations powered by steam and electricity were steadily growing in popularity across households and workplaces. We already operated a pair of steam ferries up and down the Severn, boasted a small fleet of steam-powered naval warships, and were laying the first foundations of a transoceanic steam merchant fleet; I was just putting the final touches on plans to launch an exploratory expedition to discover the Americas.
source_text: Slavery was dead and gone; all men were equal before the law; taxation had been equalized. The telegraph, the telephone, the phonograph, the typewriter, the sewing-machine, and all the thousand willing and handy servants of steam and electricity were working their way into favor. We had a steamboat or two on the Thames, we had steam warships, and the beginnings of a steam commercial marine; I was getting ready to send out an expedition to discover America.

text: Fairness demands even the devil his share of credit, I suppose. For one act alone, she was owed a kind word; I tried to force it out, but the syllables clotted behind my teeth. She’d been within her rights to end the boy’s life, after all, and no rule on earth bound her to make reparations for his death. That kind of accountability applied to folks far below her station, never to someone like her. She was fully aware that covering the costs of that boy’s burial and restitution to his family was a grand, uncommonly magnanimous gesture, and that basic decency required me to gush my approval aloud—but I couldn’t. My jaw stayed clamped shut. I couldn’t banish the visions rising unbidden behind my eyes: his haggard old grandmother crumpled under the weight of her grief, that soft, bright boy laid out cold and mangled, the frills of his fine little silk shirt stiff and crusted with the bright glow of his spilled blood. What good would her money do, anyway? Who on earth could ever be paid to make that loss whole? And so, even though I knew full well that a woman raised to her station of power and privilege was owed fulsome praise, even adulation, for such a choice, my own upbringing and conscience made it impossible to speak. The most I could manage was to scrape up a platitude from somewhere far from my true feelings, as it were—and the cruel, ironic thing was, the words were technically true:
source_text: Well, we must give even Satan his due. She deserved a compliment for one thing; and I tried to pay it, but the words stuck in my throat. She had a right to kill the boy, but she was in no wise obliged to pay for him. That was law for some other people, but not for her. She knew quite well that she was doing a large and generous thing to pay for that lad, and that I ought in common fairness to come out with something handsome about it, but I couldn't--my mouth refused. I couldn't help seeing, in my fancy, that poor old grandma with the broken heart, and that fair young creature lying butchered, his little silken pomps and vanities laced with his golden blood. How could she _pay_ for him! _Whom_ could she pay? And so, well knowing that this woman, trained as she had been, deserved praise, even adulation, I was yet not able to utter it, trained as I had been. The best I could do was to fish up a compliment from outside, so to speak--and the pity of it was, that it was true:

text: A single sweeping glance confirmed the remaining enemy troops—roughly ten thousand strong—were wedged between our position and the encircling defensive ditch, surging forward to mount their assault. That meant we had every last one of them completely trapped, with no possibility of outside rescue. It was time for the final act of the tragedy. I fired the three prearranged revolver shots—which meant:
source_text: A glance showed that the rest of the enemy--perhaps ten thousand strong--were between us and the encircling ditch, and pressing forward to the assault. Consequently we had them _all!_ and had them past help. Time for the last act of the tragedy. I fired the three appointed revolver shots--which meant:

text: We were drawing close to a castle set on high ground: a huge, sturdy, timeworn structure, its gray stone towers and battlements draped charmingly with thick, trailing ivy, the whole of its imposing bulk flooded with the brilliant, spilling glow of the setting sun. It was the largest castle we had come across so far, so I guessed it might be the one we were looking for, but Sandy said it wasn’t. She had no idea who owned the estate; she told me she’d passed right by it without stopping to pay a visit when she traveled down to Camelot.
source_text: We were approaching a castle which stood on high ground; a huge, strong, venerable structure, whose gray towers and battlements were charmingly draped with ivy, and whose whole majestic mass was drenched with splendors flung from the sinking sun. It was the largest castle we had seen, and so I thought it might be the one we were after, but Sandy said no. She did not know who owned it; she said she had passed it without calling, when she went down to Camelot.

text: It called to mind a tale I’d come across in my youth, about the clever ruse London’s aldermen used to raise the funds to build the city’s Mansion House. Per the laws of the era, no person who had not received the Sacrament according to Anglican rite was eligible to stand as a candidate for London sheriff. This automatically barred all Dissenters: they could not run even if nominated, and could not take office even if they won the election. The aldermen, who were without a doubt Yankees in disguise, hit upon this neat little scheme: they passed a municipal bylaw imposing a £400 fine on anyone who refused to stand as a sheriff candidate, and a £600 fine on any person who, after being elected sheriff, refused to take up the post. They then set to work electing Dissenters to the position one after another, keeping at it until they had collected a full £15,000 in fines. The stately Mansion House still stands to this day, a reminder to blushing local citizens of that long-past, lamented era when a band of Yankees slipped into London and pulled the sort of tricks that have earned their race a unique, unsavory reputation among every truly good and godly people on the face of the earth.
source_text: It reminded me of something I had read in my youth about the ingenious way in which the aldermen of London raised the money that built the Mansion House. A person who had not taken the Sacrament according to the Anglican rite could not stand as a candidate for sheriff of London. Thus Dissenters were ineligible; they could not run if asked, they could not serve if elected. The aldermen, who without any question were Yankees in disguise, hit upon this neat device: they passed a by-law imposing a fine of L400 upon any one who should refuse to be a candidate for sheriff, and a fine of L600 upon any person who, after being elected sheriff, refused to serve. Then they went to work and elected a lot of Dissenters, one after another, and kept it up until they had collected L15,000 in fines; and there stands the stately Mansion House to this day, to keep the blushing citizen in mind of a long past and lamented day when a band of Yankees slipped into London and played games of the sort that has given their race a unique and shady reputation among all truly good and holy peoples that be in the earth.

text: I’d never encountered a group so clumsy around machinery, mind you—they had absolutely no experience working with it at all. The so-called miller-gun was a small double-barreled tube made of tempered glass, fitted with a clever little spring mechanism that released a single pellet when you squeezed it. Those pellets couldn’t hurt a soul, either—they’d just fall right into your palm when you fired it. The gun held two sizes of pellet: tiny ones no bigger than mustard seeds, and another variety several times their size. They were currency, actually. The mustard-seed sized ones stood in for milrays, and the larger ones were mills. That made the gun effectively a wallet, and a surprisingly convenient one at that. You could dispense exact change even in total darkness, tuck it in your mouth for safekeeping, or slip it into your vest pocket if you happened to be wearing one. I manufactured these gun-purses in a range of sizes, too—one large enough to hold the equivalent of a full dollar’s worth of currency. Using pellets as currency was a huge win for the government, too. The base metal was practically free, and there was no way to counterfeit the coins—since I was the only person in the entire kingdom who knew how to operate a shot tower. “Paying the shot” quickly became a common turn of phrase around the kingdom. What’s more, I knew it would still be rolling off people’s tongues all the way in the nineteenth century, and not a single one of them would have any clue where or how the phrase first originated.
source_text: I never saw such an awkward people, with machinery; you see, they were totally unused to it. The miller-gun was a little double-barreled tube of toughened glass, with a neat little trick of a spring to it, which upon pressure would let a shot escape. But the shot wouldn't hurt anybody, it would only drop into your hand. In the gun were two sizes--wee mustard-seed shot, and another sort that were several times larger. They were money. The mustard-seed shot represented milrays, the larger ones mills. So the gun was a purse; and very handy, too; you could pay out money in the dark with it, with accuracy; and you could carry it in your mouth; or in your vest pocket, if you had one. I made them of several sizes --one size so large that it would carry the equivalent of a dollar. Using shot for money was a good thing for the government; the metal cost nothing, and the money couldn't be counterfeited, for I was the only person in the kingdom who knew how to manage a shot tower. “Paying the shot” soon came to be a common phrase. Yes, and I knew it would still be passing men's lips, away down in the nineteenth century, yet none would suspect how and when it originated.

text: You ought to have seen those acres of people hurl themselves down into that water and press their lips to it; kiss its surface, cup it gently in their palms, caress it, and murmur to it as if it were a living, breathing thing, greeting its return with the soft, dear pet names they saved for their own darlings, just as if it were a long-lost, deeply beloved friend finally coming home at last. It was such a sweet, lovely sight to witness, and it made me think far more fondly of them than I ever had before.
source_text: You should have seen those acres of people throw themselves down in that water and kiss it; kiss it, and pet it, and fondle it, and talk to it as if it were alive, and welcome it back with the dear names they gave their darlings, just as if it had been a friend who was long gone away and lost, and was come home again. Yes, it was pretty to see, and made me think more of them than I had done before.

text: And that’s exactly what she did. She was the exact sort of person you wanted at your side on a raid, no question about it. Left to my own devices, I’d have written the whole errand off as a shaky proposition from the start. Before long I spotted the knights galloping off down the road, and Sandy heading back toward me. That took a heavy weight right off my shoulders. I figured she’d somehow failed to get the upper hand right out the gate—first innings of the conversation, that is; otherwise the whole exchange never would’ve wrapped up so quickly. As it turned out, though, she’d managed the whole affair well, admirably so, in fact. She told me that as soon as she let those men know I was The Boss, it hit them exactly where it counted: “smote them sore with fear and dread” was how she phrased it, and after that they were willing to comply with any demand she made. She bound them by oath to appear at Arthur’s court within two days to surrender themselves, their horses, and all their armor and gear, to serve as my knights henceforth and answer only to my command. I never could’ve pulled that off half as smoothly on my own, not in a million years. She was a real treasure.
source_text: And she did. She was a handy person to have along on a raid. I would have considered this a doubtful errand, myself. I presently saw the knights riding away, and Sandy coming back. That was a relief. I judged she had somehow failed to get the first innings --I mean in the conversation; otherwise the interview wouldn't have been so short. But it turned out that she had managed the business well; in fact, admirably. She said that when she told those people I was The Boss, it hit them where they lived: “smote them sore with fear and dread” was her word; and then they were ready to put up with anything she might require. So she swore them to appear at Arthur's court within two days and yield them, with horse and harness, and be my knights henceforth, and subject to my command. How much better she managed that thing than I should have done it myself! She was a daisy.

text: Grown men and women, lads and lasses, scurried alongside or behind the rickety cart, jeering, yelling crude and lewd taunts, belting snippets of vulgar drinking songs, hopping, capering—nothing short of a feast for miscreants, a sight enough to turn the stomach. We’d reached a slum fringe of old London, just beyond the weathered city ramparts, and this rabid crowd was a perfect snapshot of one unvarnished slice of the capital’s underclass. Our keeper wrangled us a spot right up close to the hanging frame. A chaplain waited on the platform, and he helped the young woman step up onto the planks, murmured gentle reassurances to her, and badgered the under-sheriff into fetching her a small wooden stool to stand on. He took his place beside her on the scaffold, and for a long beat his gaze drifted first over the crush of tilted, expectant faces clustered at the base of the gallows, then out across the unbroken expanse of bobbing heads that spilled into every alley and empty yard for blocks in every direction, before he began to speak the facts of her case aloud. There was raw compassion in his tone—a sound so rarely heard in that brutal, uneducated corner of the world! I can call to mind every small beat of that speech, though the precise phrasing has slipped my memory over the years; so I have rendered it here, in my own turn of phrase:
source_text: Men and women, boys and girls, trotted along beside or after the cart, hooting, shouting profane and ribald remarks, singing snatches of foul song, skipping, dancing--a very holiday of hellions, a sickening sight. We had struck a suburb of London, outside the walls, and this was a sample of one sort of London society. Our master secured a good place for us near the gallows. A priest was in attendance, and he helped the girl climb up, and said comforting words to her, and made the under-sheriff provide a stool for her. Then he stood there by her on the gallows, and for a moment looked down upon the mass of upturned faces at his feet, then out over the solid pavement of heads that stretched away on every side occupying the vacancies far and near, and then began to tell the story of the case. And there was pity in his voice --how seldom a sound that was in that ignorant and savage land! I remember every detail of what he said, except the words he said it in; and so I change it into my own words:

text: Truly there is no murkiness to this case, for it is as uncomplicated a matter as a toddler could sort out. Had the newly wedded wife sent word, as her sworn duty demanded, to her liege and rightful guardian, the archdeacon of Bramblewell, she would have faced no loss whatsoever: the aforementioned archdeacon could easily have secured a temporary dispensation waiving his customary claim for that occasion, and she would have retained every scrap of what was hers. But because she neglected that foremost obligation, all else has crumbled in her grasp. Whoever clutches at a rope only to slice it through above their own fingers is bound to plummet, and it will do them no good to protest that the length of rope below their hands is still whole, nor will that spare them the bruise of the fall, as they will soon learn. By my faith, this woman’s cause is spoiled from its very root. The court rules that she forfeit every last possession she holds to the aforementioned lord archdeacon, down to the tiniest copper farthing in her purse, and that she further pay all costs of these proceedings. Next matter!
source_text: “Truly I find small difficulty here, the matter being even a child's affair for simpleness. An the young bride had conveyed notice, as in duty bound, to her feudal lord and proper master and protector the bishop, she had suffered no loss, for the said bishop could have got a dispensation making him, for temporary conveniency, eligible to the exercise of his said right, and thus would she have kept all she had. Whereas, failing in her first duty, she hath by that failure failed in all; for whoso, clinging to a rope, severeth it above his hands, must fall; it being no defense to claim that the rest of the rope is sound, neither any deliverance from his peril, as he shall find. Pardy, the woman's case is rotten at the source. It is the decree of the court that she forfeit to the said lord bishop all her goods, even to the last farthing that she doth possess, and be thereto mulcted in the costs. Next!”

text: Aye, that is how it unfolded. Sir Mordred set to work at once to cement his hold on the throne, determined to make his kingship unassailable. His first planned move was to marry Queen Guenever, but she evaded his grasp, fled, and locked herself inside the Tower of London. When Mordred moved to attack the stronghold, the Bishop of Canterbury intervened, punishing him with a full ecclesiastical interdict. King Arthur returned shortly thereafter to reclaim his crown, and Mordred met his forces in battle three times: at Dover, at Canterbury, and finally at Barham Down. Talks of a peace treaty and formal compromise soon followed. Per the terms, Mordred would hold Cornwall and Kent for the duration of Arthur’s life, and inherit the entire kingdom once Arthur passed.
source_text: “Yes. Sir Mordred set himself at once to work to make his kingship permanent. He was going to marry Guenever, as a first move; but she fled and shut herself up in the Tower of London. Mordred attacked; the Bishop of Canterbury dropped down on him with the Interdict. The king returned; Mordred fought him at Dover, at Canterbury, and again at Barham Down. Then there was talk of peace and a composition. Terms, Mordred to have Cornwall and Kent during Arthur's life, and the whole kingdom afterward.”

text: I found myself trusting the scruffy young boy, for no logical reason I could name. Some quiet, unshakable part of me accepted his story outright—call it my core awareness, if you like—even as my rational mind pushed back immediately. My logical side raised a raucous, entirely justified protest. I had no idea how to verify his claim, because I knew any eyewitness accounts from other people would never satisfy my skepticism; my reason would just dismiss them all as deranged, tossing their testimony out entirely. Then out of nowhere, a perfect test occurred to me entirely by luck. I’d memorized years prior that the only total solar eclipse in the first half of the sixth century took place on June 21, 528 A.D. by the old calendar, starting precisely three minutes past noon. I also knew beyond doubt that no total solar eclipse was forecast for what I understood to be the current year, 1879. That meant, if I could keep my roiling anxiety and ravenous curiosity from gnawing me raw for forty-eight hours, I would soon have irrefutable proof of whether the boy had been telling the truth or not.
source_text: I seemed to believe the boy, I didn't know why. _Something_ in me seemed to believe him--my consciousness, as you may say; but my reason didn't. My reason straightway began to clamor; that was natural. I didn't know how to go about satisfying it, because I knew that the testimony of men wouldn't serve--my reason would say they were lunatics, and throw out their evidence. But all of a sudden I stumbled on the very thing, just by luck. I knew that the only total eclipse of the sun in the first half of the sixth century occurred on the 21st of June, A.D. 528, O.S., and began at 3 minutes after 12 noon. I also knew that no total eclipse of the sun was due in what to _me_ was the present year--i.e., 1879. So, if I could keep my anxiety and curiosity from eating the heart out of me for forty-eight hours, I should then find out for certain whether this boy was telling me the truth or not.

text: Aye, we went under the gavel at auction, same as scruffy barnyard hogs. In a proper bustling city with a brisk trade, we’d have fetched a handsome sum; but this backwater was totally dead, so we went for a price that makes my cheeks burn every time it crosses my mind. The Emperor of Gaul pulled seven dollars, his chief chancellor nine, even though the emperor was easily worth twelve at a minimum, and I myself no less than fifteen. But that’s just how the world works; push a sale in a sluggish market, I don’t care what the goods are, you’re going to get shortchanged, you can count on that. If the viscount had had the wit to--
source_text: Yes, we were sold at auction, like swine. In a big town and an active market we should have brought a good price; but this place was utterly stagnant and so we sold at a figure which makes me ashamed, every time I think of it. The King of England brought seven dollars, and his prime minister nine; whereas the king was easily worth twelve dollars and I as easily worth fifteen. But that is the way things always go; if you force a sale on a dull market, I don't care what the property is, you are going to make a poor business of it, and you can make up your mind to it. If the earl had had wit enough to--

text: This volume does not resolve the long-debated question of whether monarchs hold a true divine right to rule. The puzzle proved far too thorny to untangle within these pages. It was self-evident, beyond any possible argument, that a country’s ruling head ought to be someone of unimpeachable moral fiber and rare, exceptional talent; it was equally obvious that no power but the divine could choose such a leader without error; it followed just as plainly that the divine ought to make that selection; the only logical conclusion, then, was that God does indeed appoint rulers as proponents of divine right claim. That line of reasoning held up perfectly, that is, until this book’s author stumbled into studying the reign of Madame de Valois, the infamous royal favorite who pulled all the strings of 17th-century French statecraft, and Lady Hartwell, the corrupt power behind the English throne a generation later, along with half a dozen other unelected de facto rulers cut from the same shoddy cloth. Fitting those figures into the earlier logical framework proved so intractable that it was decided the wiser course was to pivot to a different argument in this volume, which is already slated for publication this coming autumn, then put in the work to resolve the divine right question properly in a later work. The question does deserve a definitive answer, after all, and I have no pressing commitments lined up to fill my time next winter in any case.
source_text: The question as to whether there is such a thing as divine right of kings is not settled in this book. It was found too difficult. That the executive head of a nation should be a person of lofty character and extraordinary ability, was manifest and indisputable; that none but the Deity could select that head unerringly, was also manifest and indisputable; that the Deity ought to make that selection, then, was likewise manifest and indisputable; consequently, that He does make it, as claimed, was an unavoidable deduction. I mean, until the author of this book encountered the Pompadour, and Lady Castlemaine, and some other executive heads of that kind; these were found so difficult to work into the scheme, that it was judged better to take the other tack in this book (which must be issued this fall), and then go into training and settle the question in another book. It is, of course, a thing which ought to be settled, and I am not going to have anything particular to do next winter anyway.

text:
Look, anyone who’s sweated through plate armor in rivulets knows the point where you just—you just start itching, all over, no escape. Your torso’s locked inside the cuirass, your arms poking out through the gaps, and that’s all there is to it: just cold, unyielding steel between you and any relief. Don’t let how silly it sounds fool you, it’s no small misery. First it nips at your shoulder blade, then your ribcage, then the small of your back, creeping and crawling wider and wider until every inch of skin under the metal is prickling, and there’s not a soul alive who hasn’t been there that can grasp how wretched it feels.

Right when I thought I couldn’t possibly endure another second of it, a horsefly slipped through the visor slats and landed right on the tip of my nose. The catch on the visor had jammed shut hours earlier, I couldn’t yank it up no matter how I wiggled, all I could do was jerk my head back and forth, which was already roasting so bad I thought my brains might boil. And that fly—you know how stubborn those things get when they’ve found a soft mark? The shaking only made it hop from my nose to my upper lip, then to the shell of my ear, droning loud as a mill wheel right next to my skull, landing and nipping every few seconds, and for someone already half out of their mind with discomfort, it was too much to bear.

So I caved, and yelled for Elowen to pry the helmet off my head for me. She dumped out the little bits of lint and crumpled dried herb I kept tucked in the lining, then filled the whole helmet full of cold well water. I drank half of it in one go, then stood up straight so she could pour the rest down the neck of my armor. You can’t imagine how heavenly that cold rush felt. She kept going back to the well with the helmet, pouring load after load down my plates until I was soaked through to the skin and perfectly, blissfully comfortable.
source_text: Well, you know, when you perspire that way, in rivers, there comes a time when you--when you--well, when you itch. You are inside, your hands are outside; so there you are; nothing but iron between. It is not a light thing, let it sound as it may. First it is one place; then another; then some more; and it goes on spreading and spreading, and at last the territory is all occupied, and nobody can imagine what you feel like, nor how unpleasant it is. And when it had got to the worst, and it seemed to me that I could not stand anything more, a fly got in through the bars and settled on my nose, and the bars were stuck and wouldn't work, and I couldn't get the visor up; and I could only shake my head, which was baking hot by this time, and the fly--well, you know how a fly acts when he has got a certainty--he only minded the shaking enough to change from nose to lip, and lip to ear, and buzz and buzz all around in there, and keep on lighting and biting, in a way that a person, already so distressed as I was, simply could not stand. So I gave in, and got Alisande to unship the helmet and relieve me of it. Then she emptied the conveniences out of it and fetched it full of water, and I drank and then stood up, and she poured the rest down inside the armor. One cannot think how refreshing it was. She continued to fetch and pour until I was well soaked and thoroughly comfortable.

text: But my consternation ebbed right then, and my sharp panic melted away entirely; for the queen, gone weak and reeling, made no move to resist, only flicked a countermanding gesture before slumping heavily into her seat. By the time she settled against the cushions, she was completely clear-headed. So too were a great many of the onlookers. The entire assembly surged to its feet, cast all formal ceremony to the wind, and stampeded for the door like a raucous mob: they overturned chairs, shattered crockery, tugged, jostled, shouldered past one another, crammed tight in the throng—all desperate to escape before I changed my mind and blew the entire castle into the endless, shadowed emptiness of space. Good grief, they were such a hopelessly superstitious lot. It’s all a person can do to even wrap their head around it.
source_text: But my consternation subsided there, and my panic passed off; for the queen, all in a collapse, made no show of resistance but gave a countermanding sign and sunk into her seat. When she reached it she was sober. So were many of the others. The assemblage rose, whiffed ceremony to the winds, and rushed for the door like a mob; overturning chairs, smashing crockery, tugging, struggling, shouldering, crowding--anything to get out before I should change my mind and puff the castle into the measureless dim vacancies of space. Well, well, well, they _were_ a superstitious lot. It is all a body can do to conceive of it.

text: “C’mon, friend, I’ll walk a short stretch with you, and lay out which direction I figure they’re most likely to head. If they’d only been pushing back against the gabelle or some other similarly absurd, unjust rule, I’d do whatever I could to keep them from getting caught; but when people kill a high-ranking noble and burn his home to the ground on top of that? That’s an entirely different matter.”
source_text: “Come, friend, I will walk a little way with you, and explain which direction I think they would try to take. If they were merely resisters of the gabelle or some kindred absurdity I would try to protect them from capture; but when men murder a person of high degree and likewise burn his house, that is another matter.”

text: The gathered onlookers were treated to a brief, unforeseen burst of amusement from the ragged royal claimant. The second our restraints were cut loose, he shot to his feet, clad in his wild, tattered scraps of clothing, his face so bruised and swollen it was nearly unrecognizable, and declared himself Kael, rightful King of the Northern Isles, roaring that every soul in attendance would face the gravest penalties for treason if so much as a strand of his consecrated head was harmed. He was stunned and bewildered when they exploded into a thunderous, echoing wave of guffaws. The slight to his regal pride cut deep, and he clamped his jaw shut, retreating into tight, unyielding silence. Even then, the crowd badgered him to keep speaking, goading him on with shrill catcalls, cutting jeers, and raucous shouts of:
source_text: The crowd got a brief and unexpected dash of diversion out of the king. The moment we were freed of our bonds he sprang up, in his fantastic rags, with face bruised out of all recognition, and proclaimed himself Arthur, King of Britain, and denounced the awful penalties of treason upon every soul there present if hair of his sacred head were touched. It startled and surprised him to hear them break into a vast roar of laughter. It wounded his dignity, and he locked himself up in silence. Then, although the crowd begged him to go on, and tried to provoke him to it by catcalls, jeers, and shouts of:

text: Yeah, but don’t ask me to explain, okay? She’s a whole new kind of girl, the type you never run into around here. You might snap at them sharply out of nowhere when they haven’t done a single thing wrong, and even thirteen hundred years later you still can’t shake how sorry and ashamed you are for it. It’s such shoddy, petty behavior, totally unprovoked. Truth is, no decent gentleman would ever act that way—though I… well, if I’ve got to own up to it, I myself…
source_text: “Yes, but don't you ask me to explain; it's a new kind of a girl; they don't have them here; one often speaks sharply to them when they are not the least in fault, and he can't get over feeling sorry for it and ashamed of himself in thirteen hundred years, it's such shabby mean conduct and so unprovoked; the fact is, no gentleman ever does it--though I--well, I myself, if I've got to confess--”

text: Our venture through the Hollow of Sacred Pines had gone off as well as anyone could have hoped, and I was thoroughly satisfied, already primed to pack our gear and move on, when a frustrating setback stopped me short. I came down with a heavy, rattling cold that stirred up a long-lurking case of rheumatism I’d carried for years. Of course the aching stiffness tracked directly to my most tender, vulnerable spot to settle in. That was the exact spot where the hollow’s head hermit had wrapped his arms around me and squeezed so hard he left me bruised, back when he’d been moved to show his gratitude to me with a giddy, crushing embrace.
source_text: It was a good campaign that we made in that Valley of Holiness, and I was very well satisfied, and ready to move on now, but I struck a disappointment. I caught a heavy cold, and it started up an old lurking rheumatism of mine. Of course the rheumatism hunted up my weakest place and located itself there. This was the place where the abbot put his arms about me and mashed me, what time he was moved to testify his gratitude to me with an embrace.

text: Then he let out a sigh so thick with sorrow that I resolved to get at least one layer of his burdensome obligations lifted, even if it meant expending every last scrap of my influence and burning through every favor I’d accumulated. So I went to the abbot and put in a request for a special dispensation for this young novice. He flinched at the idea—not that you could have seen the flinch, of course, for you’d have had to scrape off decades of cultivated stoicism to catch a hint of it, and I didn’t care enough to put in that kind of effort, but I knew the flinch was there all the same, sitting no deeper than the thickness of a hymnal’s cover beneath his stony face—flinched, and trembled. He said:
source_text: And then he sighed in such a sorrowful way that I was resolved he should have at least one layer of his real estate removed, if it sized up my whole influence and bankrupted the pile. So I went to the abbot and asked for a permit for this Brother. He blenched at the idea--I don't mean that you could see him blench, for of course you couldn't see it without you scraped him, and I didn't care enough about it to scrape him, but I knew the blench was there, just the same, and within a book-cover's thickness of the surface, too--blenched, and trembled. He said:

text: After that, I dispatched a senior construction specialist and forty laborers to a spot just beyond our southern defensive lines, to redirect the small mountain stream that cut through that stretch, channeling it inside our perimeter and under our full control, with its routing calibrated so I could deploy its flow immediately if an emergency arose. The forty workers were split into two alternating shifts of twenty apiece, scheduled to relieve one another every two hours to keep progress steady. Ten hours after the first shovel hit dirt, the entire project was complete.
source_text: Next, I sent an engineer and forty men to a point just beyond our lines on the south, to turn a mountain brook that was there, and bring it within our lines and under our command, arranging it in such a way that I could make instant use of it in an emergency. The forty men were divided into two shifts of twenty each, and were to relieve each other every two hours. In ten hours the work was accomplished.

text: Four cycles of seasons slipped past—and then? Why, you could never in a thousand years guess what came to pass. Unfettered authority is the ideal of governance, so long as it rests in unshakably responsible hands. The absolute rule of the celestial realm is the only perfectly flawless system of governance in existence. An earthly autocracy would be the perfect form of mortal government, too, if it could satisfy the same exact conditions: first, that the ruler is the most morally and intellectually perfect human being to walk the earth, and second, that his hold on power lasts for all eternity. But even the most impeccable mortal is still perishable, bound to die eventually, and leave his absolute dominion in the hands of a deeply flawed successor. That is why earthly autocracy is not merely a bad system of government—it is the single worst form that could ever be inflicted on a population.
source_text: Four years rolled by--and then! Well, you would never imagine it in the world. Unlimited power is the ideal thing when it is in safe hands. The despotism of heaven is the one absolutely perfect government. An earthly despotism would be the absolutely perfect earthly government, if the conditions were the same, namely, the despot the perfectest individual of the human race, and his lease of life perpetual. But as a perishable perfect man must die, and leave his despotism in the hands of an imperfect successor, an earthly despotism is not merely a bad form of government, it is the worst form that is possible.

text: Everything I’d built proved just how much an absolute ruler could pull off when he had an entire kingdom’s resources at his disposal. This benighted, unsuspecting nation had no clue that full-fledged 19th-century civilization was booming right under its very nose! It was all walled off from public view, but it was there: an enormous, unassailable reality, and it would make its presence known soon enough, if I stayed alive and got a few lucky breaks. It was as tangible and unshakable a truth as any quiet volcano, its smoke-free peak sitting unassuming against the blue sky, no hint of the churning fury building in its core. Four years earlier, my schools and churches were barely fledglings; now they were fully mature, thriving institutions. The small workshops I’d operated back then had expanded into massive factories. Where I’d once had a dozen trained workers on payroll, I now had a thousand. Where I’d employed a single brilliant expert before, I had fifty of them now. I was, so to speak, standing with my hand on the main valve, ready to turn it at any second and flood this dark, unenlightened world with light. But I had no intention of making that move so abruptly. That was never my policy. The general population would never have been able to tolerate such a sudden upheaval, for one thing. What’s more, the state-established Roman Catholic Church would have come down on me hard the second I made a move like that.
source_text: My works showed what a despot could do with the resources of a kingdom at his command. Unsuspected by this dark land, I had the civilization of the nineteenth century booming under its very nose! It was fenced away from the public view, but there it was, a gigantic and unassailable fact--and to be heard from, yet, if I lived and had luck. There it was, as sure a fact and as substantial a fact as any serene volcano, standing innocent with its smokeless summit in the blue sky and giving no sign of the rising hell in its bowels. My schools and churches were children four years before; they were grown-up now; my shops of that day were vast factories now; where I had a dozen trained men then, I had a thousand now; where I had one brilliant expert then, I had fifty now. I stood with my hand on the cock, so to speak, ready to turn it on and flood the midnight world with light at any moment. But I was not going to do the thing in that sudden way. It was not my policy. The people could not have stood it; and, moreover, I should have had the Established Roman Catholic Church on my back in a minute.

text: The entryway hung just slightly unlatched. We crept toward it on tiptoe, our breath caught halfway in our throats—for that is the quiet instinct that takes over in moments thrumming with unspoken tension, no thought needed. The earl rapped gently against the wood. We waited. No response came. He rapped again, a touch louder. Still no answer. I eased the door open soundlessly and peered inside. I could just make out a few faint, shadowed shapes before a woman jolted upright from where she’d been huddled on the floor, staring at me like someone dragged suddenly from the depths of sleep. After a long, disoriented beat, she found her voice:
source_text: The door stood a trifle ajar. We approached it stealthily--on tiptoe and at half-breath--for that is the way one's feeling makes him do, at such a time. The king knocked. We waited. No answer. Knocked again. No answer. I pushed the door softly open and looked in. I made out some dim forms, and a woman started up from the ground and stared at me, as one does who is wakened from sleep. Presently she found her voice:

text: You’re not answering, brother. Just a while back you were ready to sing the pillory’s praises, even pitying future generations that will never get to use it. I say the pillory ought to be abolished entirely. What usually happens when some poor fellow is clamped in the pillory for some petty offense that barely amounts to anything at all? The crowd torments him for their own amusement, don’t they?
source_text: “You don't answer, brother. You were about to glorify the pillory a while ago, and shed some pity on a future age that isn't going to use it. I think the pillory ought to be abolished. What usually happens when a poor fellow is put in the pillory for some little offense that didn't amount to anything in the world? The mob try to have some fun with him, don't they?”

text: I was fond of the king, and honored him in his royal role—respected the crown, at least as much as I could ever bring myself to respect any unearned authority. But as ordinary men? I thought little of him and his nobles, and kept that judgment strictly to myself. For their part, they liked me well enough, and respected the post I held. But as a man of no noble birth or counterfeit title, they looked down on me openly, with no pretense of secrecy. I never hid what I thought of them, and they never hid what they thought of me: the scales were even, the accounts tallied perfectly, and no one had cause to complain.
source_text: Well, I liked the king, and as king I respected him--respected the office; at least respected it as much as I was capable of respecting any unearned supremacy; but as MEN I looked down upon him and his nobles--privately. And he and they liked me, and respected my office; but as an animal, without birth or sham title, they looked down upon me--and were not particularly private about it, either. I didn't charge for my opinion about them, and they didn't charge for their opinion about me: the account was square, the books balanced, everybody was satisfied.

text: At the center of this groin-vaulted public square stood a solid oak table everyone knew as the Round Table. It was as wide across as a full circus ring, and gathered all along its edge was a large company of men clad in such a wildly varied, glittering array of bright hues that staring at them for too long made your eyes sting. They kept their feathered hats firmly perched on their heads the entire time, save for when one of them spoke directly to the king: he would tip his hat just a small fraction the very moment he opened his mouth to talk.
source_text: In the middle of this groined and vaulted public square was an oaken table which they called the Table Round. It was as large as a circus ring; and around it sat a great company of men dressed in such various and splendid colors that it hurt one's eyes to look at them. They wore their plumed hats, right along, except that whenever one addressed himself directly to the king, he lifted his hat a trifle just as he was beginning his remark.

text: I was utterly disoriented. There was no rescue coming for me. My head throbbed, my senses felt thick and unmoored; I had no mastery over my own body, only stumbled aimlessly back and forth as if robbed of my reason, until the guards seized me by the shoulders and dragged me along with them, out of the cold, narrow cell, through the winding labyrinth of underground corridors, and finally into the brutal, blinding glare of daylight and the world above. The second we stepped into the fortress’s vast walled courtyard, a jolt of terror shot through me; the first thing I laid eyes on was the wooden post driven into the earth at its center, piled stacks of dry kindling heaped beside it, and a robed friar standing a few paces off. On all four sides of the courtyard, seated crowds rose in tier after tier, forming sloping terraces stippled with the rich, bright colors of their garments. The sovereign and his consort sat on their carved thrones, naturally the most striking figures in the entire assembly.
source_text: I was lost. There was no help for me. I was dazed, stupefied; I had no command over myself, I only wandered purposely about, like one out of his mind; so the soldiers took hold of me, and pulled me along with them, out of the cell and along the maze of underground corridors, and finally into the fierce glare of daylight and the upper world. As we stepped into the vast enclosed court of the castle I got a shock; for the first thing I saw was the stake, standing in the center, and near it the piled fagots and a monk. On all four sides of the court the seated multitudes rose rank above rank, forming sloping terraces that were rich with color. The king and the queen sat in their thrones, the most conspicuous figures there, of course.

text: Marnie was as soft-hearted and obliging as they come, but her stream of chatter ran as constant as a gristmill’s wheel, and left your skull throbbing the way the endless rumble of cart and carriage traffic does after a day downtown. If you could’ve plugged her up like a jar of preserves, she’d have been perfect company. But folks like her can’t be silenced; they’d wilt right away if you tried. Her tongue ran nonstop from dawn to dusk, and you’d swear her internal gears would seize up sooner or later, but they never did, never even stuttered. She never had to pause to fumble for the right phrase, either. She’d rattle, hum, natter, and prattle for seven days straight without needing to pause to catch her breath or rest her jaw, and every word out of her mouth was just empty air. She had no more actual thoughts to share than a wisp of mist has shape. She was a first-class chatterbox, all yammer, yammer, yammer, prate, prate, prate, gibber, gibber, gibber, even though she meant nothing but kindness the whole time. I’d tuned out her endless droning that morning, what with my own mess of problems buzzing around my head like an angry wasp nest, but by mid-afternoon I’d snapped enough that I had to speak up, more than once:
source_text: She was a quite biddable creature and good-hearted, but she had a flow of talk that was as steady as a mill, and made your head sore like the drays and wagons in a city. If she had had a cork she would have been a comfort. But you can't cork that kind; they would die. Her clack was going all day, and you would think something would surely happen to her works, by and by; but no, they never got out of order; and she never had to slack up for words. She could grind, and pump, and churn, and buzz by the week, and never stop to oil up or blow out. And yet the result was just nothing but wind. She never had any ideas, any more than a fog has. She was a perfect blatherskite; I mean for jaw, jaw, jaw, talk, talk, talk, jabber, jabber, jabber; but just as good as she could be. I hadn't minded her mill that morning, on account of having that hornets' nest of other troubles; but more than once in the afternoon I had to say:

text: The monks drew a lot of hope from my being there, and it lifted their moods dramatically; so much so that they tucked into a hearty, full dinner that night, the first proper meal they’d had in ten whole days. Once they’d had enough food to shore up their energy and settle their empty stomachs, their spirits started soaring right away; when the jugs of mead started getting passed around the table, their moods climbed even quicker. By the time everyone was halfway to drunk, the whole community of holy men were more than ready to make a full night of the celebration; so we stayed planted at the table and kept that raucous energy going full tilt. Things got incredibly merry. They swapped all those classic, slightly off-color old stories that had everyone crying with laughter, their sunken jaws hanging wide and their round bellies shaking so hard they could barely stay seated; then they roared through a whole set of equally dubious drinking songs in thunderous unison, loud enough to drown out the deep, booming chime of the tolling bells.
source_text: My presence gave the monks hope, and cheered them up a good deal; insomuch that they ate a square meal that night for the first time in ten days. As soon as their stomachs had been properly reinforced with food, their spirits began to rise fast; when the mead began to go round they rose faster. By the time everybody was half-seas over, the holy community was in good shape to make a night of it; so we stayed by the board and put it through on that line. Matters got to be very jolly. Good old questionable stories were told that made the tears run down and cavernous mouths stand wide and the round bellies shake with laughter; and questionable songs were bellowed out in a mighty chorus that drowned the boom of the tolling bells.

text: Oh, if it please your worship, sir, there’s no fixed bearing to it from this spot; for the road does not run true, but twists and doubles without end. So the heading to that place never stays the same, now pointing beneath one stretch of sky, now another entirely. If you reckon it lies east and set off that way, you’ll find the path curls right back on itself in a full half-circle before you’ve gone far at all. This oddity repeats, again and again and again, till you’ll grieve that you let the idle fancies of your mind try to defy and undo the will of the Lord, who only grants a castle a clear direction from any spot if it fits His purpose. If it does not please Him, He would sooner wipe every castle and every path leading to them clean off the earth, leaving the ground they stood on desolate and empty, to warn all His creatures that what He wills comes to pass, and what He does not will He--
source_text: “Ah, please you sir, it hath no direction from here; by reason that the road lieth not straight, but turneth evermore; wherefore the direction of its place abideth not, but is some time under the one sky and anon under another, whereso if ye be minded that it is in the east, and wend thitherward, ye shall observe that the way of the road doth yet again turn upon itself by the space of half a circle, and this marvel happing again and yet again and still again, it will grieve you that you had thought by vanities of the mind to thwart and bring to naught the will of Him that giveth not a castle a direction from a place except it pleaseth Him, and if it please Him not, will the rather that even all castles and all directions thereunto vanish out of the earth, leaving the places wherein they tarried desolate and vacant, so warning His creatures that where He will He will, and where He will not He--”

text: The device started addressing the young man, so I slipped away quickly to run my calculations. Half an hour from now, it would be nine o’clock. Fully armored knights and their heavy warhorses couldn’t move at much of a pace, but this party would push for the best time possible. With the ground firm and clear of snow or mud, they could likely hold a seven-mile per hour clip; they’d need to swap mounts twice along the route, so they would arrive around six, or a little after. There would still be plenty of daylight left, they’d spot the white cloth I planned to tie around my right arm, and I would take command of the group. We’d surround that prison and free the king in no time at all. All things considered, the whole affair would be showy and vivid enough, though I would have preferred staging it at midday for the far more theatrical effect the bright full sun would bring to the scene.
source_text: The instrument began to talk to the youth and I hurried away. I fell to ciphering. In half an hour it would be nine o'clock. Knights and horses in heavy armor couldn't travel very fast. These would make the best time they could, and now that the ground was in good condition, and no snow or mud, they would probably make a seven-mile gait; they would have to change horses a couple of times; they would arrive about six, or a little after; it would still be plenty light enough; they would see the white cloth which I should tie around my right arm, and I would take command. We would surround that prison and have the king out in no time. It would be showy and picturesque enough, all things considered, though I would have preferred noonday, on account of the more theatrical aspect the thing would have.

text: At long last, I thought I’d spotted my perfect opportunity. I would build this elite crack regiment entirely from commissioned officers—no rank-and-file privates whatsoever. Half its ranks would be made up of nobles, who would hold every post up to Major-General, serve without pay, and cover all their own costs; I knew they would be eager to do so once they learned the other half of the regiment would be reserved exclusively for princes of the blood. Those royal princes would hold ranks ranging from Lieutenant General all the way up to Field Marshal, with lavish state-funded salaries, uniforms, equipment, and rations. What’s more, this was my master stroke: I would issue a decree that these princely grandees would always be addressed by a dazzling, awe-inspiring custom title I planned to invent shortly, and they would be the only people in all of England permitted to receive that honorific. Finally, every prince of the blood would get a free choice: join the regiment, claim the prestigious title, and renounce their regular royal grant, or stay out of the corps and receive the grant as usual. My cleverest little touch of all? Even unborn, soon-to-arrive princes of the blood could be born directly into the regiment, so they would enter the world with a guaranteed well-paying, permanent post, as long as their parents submitted proper advance notice.
source_text: But I believed I saw my chance at last. I would form this crack regiment out of officers alone--not a single private. Half of it should consist of nobles, who should fill all the places up to Major-General, and serve gratis and pay their own expenses; and they would be glad to do this when they should learn that the rest of the regiment would consist exclusively of princes of the blood. These princes of the blood should range in rank from Lieutenant-General up to Field Marshal, and be gorgeously salaried and equipped and fed by the state. Moreover--and this was the master stroke --it should be decreed that these princely grandees should be always addressed by a stunningly gaudy and awe-compelling title (which I would presently invent), and they and they only in all England should be so addressed. Finally, all princes of the blood should have free choice; join that regiment, get that great title, and renounce the royal grant, or stay out and receive a grant. Neatest touch of all: unborn but imminent princes of the blood could be _born_ into the regiment, and start fair, with good wages and a permanent situation, upon due notice from the parents.

text: Good grief, you ought to have seen the king’s eyes drift slowly open, like he was lifting the entire weight of the earth’s atmosphere a full inch off the ground just by waking up! That shut Brer Merlin down right then and there. You never had to jump through hoops to prove your facts with these folks, after all; all you needed to do was say the thing out loud. It never even crossed anyone’s mind to doubt a single word of it.
source_text: My land, you should have seen the king's eyes spread slowly open, and lift the earth's entire atmosphere as much as an inch! That settled Brer Merlin. One never had any occasion to prove his facts, with these people; all he had to do was to state them. It never occurred to anybody to doubt the statement.

text: Oh, it was a fine sight, remarkably sharp and charming to take in. It looked exactly like a canal barge detonation on the Severn; and for the full quarter-hour after, we huddled under a steady fine drizzle of minuscule scraps of jousters, plate steel, and horse flesh. I say we, because the king joined the crowd of onlookers, of course, the second he’d caught his breath again. The crater left there would give every soul in that region steady work for years on end—trying to explain how it came to be, that is; as for filling it back in, that job would be over comparatively quickly, and would fall to a small, handpicked lot: the serfs tied to that lord’s estate. And they wouldn’t earn a farthing for the work, either.
source_text: Yes, it was a neat thing, very neat and pretty to see. It resembled a steamboat explosion on the Mississippi; and during the next fifteen minutes we stood under a steady drizzle of microscopic fragments of knights and hardware and horse-flesh. I say we, for the king joined the audience, of course, as soon as he had got his breath again. There was a hole there which would afford steady work for all the people in that region for some years to come --in trying to explain it, I mean; as for filling it up, that service would be comparatively prompt, and would fall to the lot of a select few--peasants of that seignory; and they wouldn't get anything for it, either.

text: Try as I might, I can’t fathom how you’ll ever truly grasp the weight of the rank these lords hold. Let’s cut past unnecessary preamble to spare us both wasted time and fuss. Treat me as the commanding officer of the company of knights posted just over there. That settled, you are the bearer of the flag of truce. Step forward, deliver your message, and I will give you my reply without delay.
source_text: “Somehow it seems impossible for you to ever fully realize what these nobilities are. Now let us save a little time and trouble. Consider me the commander of the knights yonder. Now, then, you are the flag of truce; approach and deliver me your message, and I will give you your answer.”

text: It was all a ruse, you see.
