When you’re stuck in a tight spot like that, the only sound call is to hold your head high and play your hand for a hundred times its actual worth; forty-nine times out of fifty, no one has the grit to call your bet, and you walk away with the whole pot. But this one time? Well, things were looking very bad, very fast. Barely two heartbeats had passed before five hundred mounted riders were scrambling into their stirrups, and before I could blink, a ragged, spreading charge was under way, thundering straight toward me. I wrenched both revolvers from their leather holsters, my head already spinning to measure the distance between us and weigh my slim odds.
source_text: It was a “bluff” you know. At such a time it is sound judgment to put on a bold face and play your hand for a hundred times what it is worth; forty-nine times out of fifty nobody dares to “call,” and you rake in the chips. But just this once--well, things looked squally! In just no time, five hundred knights were scrambling into their saddles, and before you could wink a widely scattering drove were under way and clattering down upon me. I snatched both revolvers from the holsters and began to measure distances and calculate chances.

text: The king seethed with white-hot rage, hurling insults and challenges with all the blustering vigor of his royal station. The knights had already ridden a fair distance away when they stopped, thoroughly taken aback. They twisted in their saddles to glance back, plainly debating if miscreants like us were even worth the trouble of engaging. Then they spun their mounts around and charged straight for our position. There was not a second to spare. I ran headlong toward them instead. I darted past their galloping line at a breakneck clip, and as I went I loosed a blistering, blood-boiling, thirteen-part insult so vicious it made the king’s earlier outburst look tame and amateurish by comparison. I’d picked it up back in the nineteenth century, where folks knew how to craft a proper jeer. They had so much forward momentum that they nearly barrelled straight into the king before they could rein their steeds in. Mad with fury, they hauled their horses up onto their hind legs, spun them sharp around, and came thundering after me, their armored shoulders nearly brushing as they galloped side by side. By that point I was seventy yards away, scrambling up the side of a huge boulder perched at the edge of the road. When they drew within thirty yards of me, they lowered their long lances to a horizontal position, ducked their helmeted heads, and with their horsehair plumes streaming straight back in the wind—looking every bit the picture of daring knightly valor—this ironclad lightning bolt came tearing straight for my position! The second they were fifteen yards out, I hurled the bomb with perfect aim, and it struck the dirt just inches in front of their horses’ snorting nostrils.
source_text: The king was in a flaming fury, and launched out his challenge and epithets with a most royal vigor. The knights were some little distance by now. They halted, greatly surprised, and turned in their saddles and looked back, as if wondering if it might be worth while to bother with such scum as we. Then they wheeled and started for us. Not a moment must be lost. I started for _them_. I passed them at a rattling gait, and as I went by I flung out a hair-lifting soul-scorching thirteen-jointed insult which made the king's effort poor and cheap by comparison. I got it out of the nineteenth century where they know how. They had such headway that they were nearly to the king before they could check up; then, frantic with rage, they stood up their horses on their hind hoofs and whirled them around, and the next moment here they came, breast to breast. I was seventy yards off, then, and scrambling up a great bowlder at the roadside. When they were within thirty yards of me they let their long lances droop to a level, depressed their mailed heads, and so, with their horse-hair plumes streaming straight out behind, most gallant to see, this lightning express came tearing for me! When they were within fifteen yards, I sent that bomb with a sure aim, and it struck the ground just under the horses' noses.

text: Hand me that bag, then. I want to know what it feels like to carry a burden that comes with no honor. I’m fairly sure it’s the shame of the load that hunches your shoulders over, not the actual weight—armor is plenty heavy, after all, but it’s a burden you take pride in, so a man stands straight and tall under it. And don’t give me any buts, don’t bother trying to talk me out of this. I’m taking it, no arguments. Strap it right onto my back.
source_text: “Give me, then, the bag. I will learn the spirit that goeth with burdens that have not honor. It is the spirit that stoopeth the shoulders, I ween, and not the weight; for armor is heavy, yet it is a proud burden, and a man standeth straight in it.... Nay, but me no buts, offer me no objections. I will have the thing. Strap it upon my back.”

text: I was standing on the platform, fully prepped to carry out my ceremonial duties, when the abbot’s somber procession finally came into view—it only became visible when it was almost at the rope barrier, since the night was pitch-black and starless, and torches had been strictly forbidden. Merlin was traveling with the group, and he claimed a front spot on the platform; for once, he’d followed through on his promise exactly as he’d said he would. It was impossible to make out the massive throngs packed tight beyond the barrier, but I knew they were out there, thick as could be, even if they were hidden in the dark. The second the bells fell silent, that dense huddled crowd surged forward, spilling over the barrier like a huge, roiling black tide. The steady stream of people kept coming for a full half hour before it settled into a still, tightly packed mass. You could have walked for literal miles across a flat surface made entirely of their heads, if you’d been inclined to try.
source_text: I was at the platform and all ready to do the honors when the abbot's solemn procession hove in sight--which it did not do till it was nearly to the rope fence, because it was a starless black night and no torches permitted. With it came Merlin, and took a front seat on the platform; he was as good as his word for once. One could not see the multitudes banked together beyond the ban, but they were there, just the same. The moment the bells stopped, those banked masses broke and poured over the line like a vast black wave, and for as much as a half hour it continued to flow, and then it solidified itself, and you could have walked upon a pavement of human heads to--well, miles.

text: My hunch had been spot-on. He launched right in, in that guilelessly manipulative, obvious, bumbling way of his, to steer the conversation straight toward farming. Ice-cold sweat prickled across every inch of my skin. I ached to lean in and murmur in his ear, “Man, we’re in mortal peril! Every second is worth a noble’s fortune until we win these people’s trust back—don’t waste a single moment of this precious window.” But of course I couldn’t do it. Whisper to him? It would look for all the world like we were conspiring. So I had to sit there, holding a calm, pleasant expression, while the king loomed over that powder keg rambling on about his damned turnips and other trivial crops. At first the tumult of my own thoughts, jolted awake by the blaring danger signal and swarming to the rescue from every corner of my skull, kicked up such a racket of cheering, clatter, fifing and drumming that I couldn’t take in a single word he said; but presently when that mob of half-formed plans began to crystallize, slot into position, and form a line of battle, a sort of quiet order fell over my mind, and I caught the low rumble of the king’s droning, as if it were drifting to me from some far-off distance.
source_text: I was right. He began, straight off, in the most innocently artful, and transparent, and lubberly way, to lead up to the subject of agriculture. The cold sweat broke out all over me. I wanted to whisper in his ear, “Man, we are in awful danger! every moment is worth a principality till we get back these men's confidence; _don't_ waste any of this golden time.” But of course I couldn't do it. Whisper to him? It would look as if we were conspiring. So I had to sit there and look calm and pleasant while the king stood over that dynamite mine and mooned along about his damned onions and things. At first the tumult of my own thoughts, summoned by the danger-signal and swarming to the rescue from every quarter of my skull, kept up such a hurrah and confusion and fifing and drumming that I couldn't take in a word; but presently when my mob of gathering plans began to crystallize and fall into position and form line of battle, a sort of order and quiet ensued and I caught the boom of the king's batteries, as if out of remote distance:

text: No, I had been proceeding with great caution every step of the way. For some time, I’d had trusted confidential agents moving quietly throughout the countryside, tasked with eroding the authority of the knighthood by nearly imperceptible degrees, nibbling away at each and every outmoded superstition they came across, and in doing so gradually lay the groundwork for a far better state of affairs. I was increasing the glow of my light only one candlepower at a time, and I meant to carry on with that careful, incremental approach indefinitely.
source_text: No, I had been going cautiously all the while. I had had confidential agents trickling through the country some time, whose office was to undermine knighthood by imperceptible degrees, and to gnaw a little at this and that and the other superstition, and so prepare the way gradually for a better order of things. I was turning on my light one-candle-power at a time, and meant to continue to do so.

text: Merlin’s woven a cruel spell all around this dungeon out of pure malice. There isn’t a single person in any of these kingdoms reckless enough to even attempt crossing its perimeter with you, no matter how desperate they might be. God, have pity on me, I shouldn’t have let that slip! Please, be gentle with me, show mercy to a poor kid who only wants the best for you. If you betray me to anyone, I’m completely finished.
source_text: “Merlin, in his malice, has woven a spell about this dungeon, and there bides not the man in these kingdoms that would be desperate enough to essay to cross its lines with you! Now God pity me, I have told it! Ah, be kind to me, be merciful to a poor boy who means thee well; for an thou betray me I am lost!”

text: Let me be honest with you, Alisande: these ancient archaic texts are just a touch too simplistic, their vocabularies so narrow that their descriptions end up sorely lacking in variety. They read far too much like endless, flat Saharas of plain fact, with barely any vivid, picturesque flourishes, which wraps the whole work in a thick air of monotony. Truth be told, every battle scene is identical: two people charge at each other totally at random—random’s a fine word, come to think of it, same as exegesis, holocaust, defalcation, usufruct, and a hundred more fancy terms I could name, but goodness gracious! A person ought to know when to use what, right? They charge at each other haphazardly, a spear shatters, one man’s shield splits, the other goes tumbling over his horse’s rump, horse and all, and breaks his neck. Then the next contender comes lurching in randomly, his spear shatters, the other man’s shield splits, he goes tumbling over his horse’s rump, horse and all, and breaks his neck. Then another takes a turn, then another, then another, on and on until there’s no one left to fight. By the time you tally up the outcome, you can’t tell one battle from the next, or even who won. As a portrait of a real, seething, roaring, living fight? Pfft! It’s pale and lifeless, no sound at all—nothing but ghosts tussling in a thick fog. Good grief, what would this sad, stunted vocabulary even make of the grandest spectacle imaginable? Take the burning of Rome in Nero’s time, for example. It would just say, ‘Town burned down; no insurance; boy broke a window, fireman broke his neck!’ Why, that’s no picture at all!
source_text: “The truth is, Alisande, these archaics are a little _too_ simple; the vocabulary is too limited, and so, by consequence, descriptions suffer in the matter of variety; they run too much to level Saharas of fact, and not enough to picturesque detail; this throws about them a certain air of the monotonous; in fact the fights are all alike: a couple of people come together with great random --random is a good word, and so is exegesis, for that matter, and so is holocaust, and defalcation, and usufruct and a hundred others, but land! a body ought to discriminate--they come together with great random, and a spear is brast, and one party brake his shield and the other one goes down, horse and man, over his horse-tail and brake his neck, and then the next candidate comes randoming in, and brast _his_ spear, and the other man brast his shield, and down _he_ goes, horse and man, over his horse-tail, and brake _his_ neck, and then there's another elected, and another and another and still another, till the material is all used up; and when you come to figure up results, you can't tell one fight from another, nor who whipped; and as a _picture_, of living, raging, roaring battle, sho! why, it's pale and noiseless--just ghosts scuffling in a fog. Dear me, what would this barren vocabulary get out of the mightiest spectacle?--the burning of Rome in Nero's time, for instance? Why, it would merely say, 'Town burned down; no insurance; boy brast a window, fireman brake his neck!' Why, _that_ ain't a picture!”

text: All at once, right as the priest lifted his hands and every attentive person in the hall had bowed their head in reverent anticipation of the coming blessing, a hunched, white-haired elderly woman appeared beneath the arch of the distant door at the far lower end of the room, leaning heavily on a wooden crutch. She lifted the crutch, pointed it directly at the queen, and cried out:
source_text: Suddenly, even while the priest was lifting his hands, and all conscious heads were bowed in reverent expectation of the coming blessing, there appeared under the arch of the far-off door at the bottom of the hall an old and bent and white-haired lady, leaning upon a crutch-stick; and she lifted the stick and pointed it toward the queen and cried out:

text: It ended up being one of those lush, uncommon autumn days—exactly the kind of sun-kissed June day softened just enough that being outside feels like pure bliss. By midday, the guests started showing up, and we all gathered beneath a massive old tree, chatting easily with one another like we’d been close friends for years. Even the king’s usual standoffishness thawed a little, though he stumbled at first when he had to get used to answering to the plain, ordinary name of Jones. I’d told him to do his best to remember he was supposed to be a regular farmer, but I’d also thought it was smart to warn him not to add any extra details beyond that basic backstory. He was exactly the sort of person who’d absolutely ruin a small, simple setup like that if you didn’t lay down ground rules first: he talked too quickly, was too eager to jump into any conversation, and half the facts he spouted were unreliable at best.
source_text: It turned out to be one of those rich and rare fall days which is just a June day toned down to a degree where it is heaven to be out of doors. Toward noon the guests arrived, and we assembled under a great tree and were soon as sociable as old acquaintances. Even the king's reserve melted a little, though it was some little trouble to him to adjust himself to the name of Jones along at first. I had asked him to try to not forget that he was a farmer; but I had also considered it prudent to ask him to let the thing stand at that, and not elaborate it any. Because he was just the kind of person you could depend on to spoil a little thing like that if you didn't warn him, his tongue was so handy, and his spirit so willing, and his information so uncertain.

text: “Oh, honestly, that’s exactly right. There’s no place that fits the description better. Back in ancient times, an abbot and his community of monks lived on that site. There was hardly anyone more holy anywhere in the world, as far as anyone knew. They dedicated all their time to studying devotional texts, never spoke to one another, or really to any other person at all. They only ate withered, rotting herbs, with no other food to go with them, slept on unyielding hard surfaces, spent hours in prayer, never washed themselves, and wore the same single piece of clothing until it literally fell apart and dropped off their bodies from age and decay. Those severe, devout practices are exactly what made them known across the entire world. Rich and poor people alike traveled to visit them, and everyone regarded them with the deepest respect.”
source_text: “Oh, of a truth, yes. There be none more so. Of old time there lived there an abbot and his monks. Belike were none in the world more holy than these; for they gave themselves to study of pious books, and spoke not the one to the other, or indeed to any, and ate decayed herbs and naught thereto, and slept hard, and prayed much, and washed never; also they wore the same garment until it fell from their bodies through age and decay. Right so came they to be known of all the world by reason of these holy austerities, and visited by rich and poor, and reverenced.”

text: When I led my shuffling procession of cave-dwelling men, who’d dwelled so long in total dark they’d grown as sensitive to light as bats, up into the open air and the searing burn of the late afternoon sun, I’d bound their eyes tight first, out of mercy for soft organs that hadn’t known the sting of bright light in decades. The sight of them was almost unthinkable. They were walking skeletons, straw-stuffed scarecrows, lurching goblins, every last one a heartbreakingly wretched thing; the most unmistakeable offspring of the Crown by Anointed Right and the official State Church. I mumbled half to myself:
source_text: When I brought my procession of human bats up into the open world and the glare of the afternoon sun--previously blindfolding them, in charity for eyes so long untortured by light--they were a spectacle to look at. Skeletons, scarecrows, goblins, pathetic frights, every one; legitimatest possible children of Monarchy by the Grace of God and the Established Church. I muttered absently:

text: “Not bad, really not bad at all. Tuck your chin just a little lower, please—there, that’s perfect. Your eyes are tilted too far up, c’mon, don’t stare off at the horizon. Fix your gaze on the ground ten steps ahead of you, yeah? Ah, that’s way better, that works great. Hold on a second, though—you’re coming off too energized, too sure of yourself. You need to shamble more, drag your feet a little. Watch me, okay? This is what I’m talking about… Oh, you’re getting the hang of it now, that’s exactly the vibe—well, it’s close enough, anyway… Yeah, that’s pretty decent. But! There’s still something big missing, I can’t quite put my finger on it yet. Walk thirty yards for me, would you? So I can get a proper perspective on how it reads from a distance… Okay, let’s break this down—your head position’s right, pace is right, shoulders are right, eyes are right, chin is right, your gait, your posture, the whole general energy checks out—every last little detail is correct! But the fact remains, the full package is off. It doesn’t add up at all. Do it again, please… Now I think I finally see what the issue is. Yeah, I figured it out. Look, you don’t have that real, bone-deep lack of spirit, that’s what’s wrong. This is all total amateur hour—all the mechanical details are almost perfect, down to the smallest tiny thing, every part of the act is flawless, except for the fact that it doesn’t fool anyone for a second.”
source_text: “Pretty fair--pretty fair. Chin a little lower, please--there, very good. Eyes too high; pray don't look at the horizon, look at the ground, ten steps in front of you. Ah--that is better, that is very good. Wait, please; you betray too much vigor, too much decision; you want more of a shamble. Look at me, please--this is what I mean.... Now you are getting it; that is the idea--at least, it sort of approaches it.... Yes, that is pretty fair. _But!_ There is a great big something wanting, I don't quite know what it is. Please walk thirty yards, so that I can get a perspective on the thing.... Now, then--your head's right, speed's right, shoulders right, eyes right, chin right, gait, carriage, general style right--everything's right! And yet the fact remains, the aggregate's wrong. The account don't balance. Do it again, please.... _Now_ I think I begin to see what it is. Yes, I've struck it. You see, the genuine spiritlessness is wanting; that's what's the trouble. It's all _amateur_--mechanical details all right, almost to a hair; everything about the delusion perfect, except that it don't delude.”

text: Solid ground, what a staggering sight! We were boxed in on three sides by ramparts of corpses! Every remaining stretch of barricade was crammed nearly full of living soldiers, picking their way furtively through the strung wire toward our position. The abrupt burst of floodlight froze that throng cold, turned them to stone, you might say, with sheer shock; I had a single split second to make use of their frozen state, and I did not waste it. I knew full well that half a heartbeat later they would have shaken off their daze, roared a battle cry, and surged forward, and my wire barriers would have crumpled under the weight of that charge; but that single missed sliver of time cost them their chance for good; before that tiny window could snap shut, I sent a lethal current surging through every length of fence, and dropped the entire throng where they stood! That was a groan you could feel in your bones! It carried the dying agony of eleven thousand men, rolling out across the dark with a gut-wrenching, terrible pathos.
source_text: Land, what a sight! We were enclosed in three walls of dead men! All the other fences were pretty nearly filled with the living, who were stealthily working their way forward through the wires. The sudden glare paralyzed this host, petrified them, you may say, with astonishment; there was just one instant for me to utilize their immobility in, and I didn't lose the chance. You see, in another instant they would have recovered their faculties, then they'd have burst into a cheer and made a rush, and my wires would have gone down before it; but that lost instant lost them their opportunity forever; while even that slight fragment of time was still unspent, I shot the current through all the fences and struck the whole host dead in their tracks! _There_ was a groan you could _hear_! It voiced the death-pang of eleven thousand men. It swelled out on the night with awful pathos.

text: The poor boy was so shaken he could barely get a word out in reply to me. It was gut-wrenching to look at a person so frozen with terror, so frayed of nerve, so thoroughly demoralized. He promised to do anything I asked of him, but first he made me vow over and over that I would remain his friend, never turn against him, never cast any enchantments to harm him. After that he picked his way out of the room, steadying himself with one hand along the wall every step of the way, as if he were gravely ill.
source_text: The poor boy was in such a state that he could hardly answer me. It was pitiful to see a creature so terrified, so unnerved, so demoralized. But he promised everything; and on my side he made me promise over and over again that I would remain his friend, and never turn against him or cast any enchantments upon him. Then he worked his way out, staying himself with his hand along the wall, like a sick person.

text: He performed exceedingly well in that latter role. A thoughtful, compassionate magistrate, he strove openly to rule with complete honesty and evenhandedness—so far as his own frame of reference allowed. That caveat carries great weight. His frame of reference, shaped entirely by his blue-blooded upbringing, tinted every ruling he handed down. Any dispute that pitted a lord or landed gentry member against a laborer or peasant found King Theron’s instincts and loyalties tilting invariably toward the privileged party, even when he believed he was being perfectly impartial. There was no way around this skew. The dulling effect of chattel slavery on an enslaver’s sense of right and wrong is a widely acknowledged truth across the globe; and a ruling elite, an aristocracy, is nothing more than a cohort of enslavers operating under a different label. That phrasing might sound harsh, but it ought not to offend anyone—even a peer of the realm—unless the reality it describes is itself offensive: it is merely a plain articulation of observable fact. The ugly core of slavery is the system itself, not what we choose to call it. One has only to listen to an aristocrat dismiss the ranks beneath him to pick out—only barely softened, at that—the exact haughty cadence and attitude of an actual enslaver; behind that tone lies the same sense of entitled dominance, the same stunted capacity for empathy. Both stem from the exact same root: the powerful party’s lifelong, ingrained habit of seeing themselves as inherently superior to those below them. Theron’s rulings often resulted in deep unfairness, but the flaw lay not in his character, but his conditioning, his innate, unshakable allegiances. He was as poorly suited to sit in judgment as the average mother would be to hand out milk rations to starving children during a famine; her own little ones would always get a crumb more than the rest.
source_text: He shone very well in this latter office. He was a wise and humane judge, and he clearly did his honest best and fairest,--according to his lights. That is a large reservation. His lights--I mean his rearing--often colored his decisions. Whenever there was a dispute between a noble or gentleman and a person of lower degree, the king's leanings and sympathies were for the former class always, whether he suspected it or not. It was impossible that this should be otherwise. The blunting effects of slavery upon the slaveholder's moral perceptions are known and conceded, the world over; and a privileged class, an aristocracy, is but a band of slaveholders under another name. This has a harsh sound, and yet should not be offensive to any--even to the noble himself--unless the fact itself be an offense: for the statement simply formulates a fact. The repulsive feature of slavery is the _thing_, not its name. One needs but to hear an aristocrat speak of the classes that are below him to recognize--and in but indifferently modified measure --the very air and tone of the actual slaveholder; and behind these are the slaveholder's spirit, the slaveholder's blunted feeling. They are the result of the same cause in both cases: the possessor's old and inbred custom of regarding himself as a superior being. The king's judgments wrought frequent injustices, but it was merely the fault of his training, his natural and unalterable sympathies. He was as unfitted for a judgeship as would be the average mother for the position of milk-distributor to starving children in famine-time; her own children would fare a shade better than the rest.

text: Oh, Morgan le Fay was undoubtedly one of the oddest souls I’ve ever known, no two ways about it. I’ve crossed paths with every sort of woman over the years, but none could hold a candle to her for sheer, unmatchable eccentricity. This little incident summed her up so sharply it might as well have had her name carved right into it. She didn’t have the faintest clue how to photograph a public procession, not a scrap more sense for it than a plow horse would; but when she was stuck for a way forward, grabbing an axe to get the job done was exactly the harebrained, signature move she’d make.
source_text: Well, she certainly was a curious one, was Morgan le Fay. I have seen a good many kinds of women in my time, but she laid over them all for variety. And how sharply characteristic of her this episode was. She had no more idea than a horse of how to photograph a procession; but being in doubt, it was just like her to try to do it with an axe.

text: Right around that same time, I noticed the driving force behind the whole operation had started only putting weight on one leg, and it turned out the other was seriously impaired. So I got all the business’s holdings in order and offloaded the entire thing, sticking Sir Bors de Ganis and a handful of his friends with the full financial liability in the process. The whole venture ground to a complete halt within a year, and that good, dedicated soul finally got to take his permanent rest. He’d more than earned that peace, too. I’ll say that much for him, no question.
source_text: But about that time I noticed that the motive power had taken to standing on one leg, and I found that there was something the matter with the other one; so I stocked the business and unloaded, taking Sir Bors de Ganis into camp financially along with certain of his friends; for the works stopped within a year, and the good saint got him to his rest. But he had earned it. I can say that for him.

text: Thus I did not speak of rebellion and spilled blood to the man huddled over a hunk of dark rye, eating alongside that exploited, misled herd of cowed, downtrodden folk. Instead, I led him a short distance apart from the rest and spoke to him of an entirely different sort of errand. Once we’d finished talking, I persuaded him to give me a small drop of blood from his finger to use as ink; with that, and a sharp splinter of pine for a stylus, I etched my note onto a flat strip of tree bark--
source_text: So I did not talk blood and insurrection to that man there who sat munching black bread with that abused and mistaught herd of human sheep, but took him aside and talked matter of another sort to him. After I had finished, I got him to lend me a little ink from his veins; and with this and a sliver I wrote on a piece of bark--

text: I couldn’t help patting myself on the back for being so sharp as to see this coming ahead of time and switching trees to dodge the whole mess. But here’s the thing, right? There are some forces that outsmart even the sharpest planning and most careful foresight. Clumsiness and sheer, unapologetic stupidity, for example. The greatest fencer alive doesn’t lose sleep over the second-ranked fencer in the world, not at all. The person he ought to be scared of is some totally clueless opponent who’s never even held a sword in his life. He doesn’t make the moves he’s supposed to make, so the expert has no way to prepare for him; he pulls all the stunts he should never pull, and more often than not he catches the pro completely off guard and takes him out right then and there. So how was I, for all my quick wits, supposed to make any sort of useful plan to outmaneuver a near-sighted, cross-eyed, thick-skulled oaf who’d line himself up for the wrong tree and end up hitting the correct one by accident? That’s exactly what he did, too. He made a beeline for the wrong tree, which naturally ended up being the right one entirely by mistake, and hauled himself right up after me.
source_text: I was obliged to admire my cuteness in foreseeing this very thing and swapping trees to beat it. But, don't you know, there are some things that can beat smartness and foresight? Awkwardness and stupidity can. The best swordsman in the world doesn't need to fear the second best swordsman in the world; no, the person for him to be afraid of is some ignorant antagonist who has never had a sword in his hand before; he doesn't do the thing he ought to do, and so the expert isn't prepared for him; he does the thing he ought not to do; and often it catches the expert out and ends him on the spot. Well, how could I, with all my gifts, make any valuable preparation against a near-sighted, cross-eyed, pudding-headed clown who would aim himself at the wrong tree and hit the right one? And that is what he did. He went for the wrong tree, which was, of course, the right one by mistake, and up he started.

text: I stumbled on the shallow spring almost three hundred yards from our hiding spot, and had been catching my breath for nearly twenty minutes when I caught the faint murmur of voices. No harm in that, I reckoned—farmhands heading out to tend their fields; no one else would be up and moving at this gray pre-dawn hour. But just a heartbeat later, the group rounded the sharp curve of the dirt lane, their harnesses jingling loud enough to cut through the quiet—finely dressed nobles, their retinue of footmen and luggage-laden pack mules trailing close behind! I bolted like a startled hare, crashing through the underbrush on the most direct path back. For a few panicky seconds, I was certain that party would reach the sleeping king before I could get to him; but desperation lends you speed you never knew you had, after all. I leaned so far forward my chest nearly grazed the ferns, filled my lungs as full as they would go, held tight to my breath, and ran like the wind. I made it. With more than enough time to spare, too.
source_text: I found the water some three hundred yards away, and had been resting about twenty minutes, when I heard voices. That is all right, I thought--peasants going to work; nobody else likely to be stirring this early. But the next moment these comers jingled into sight around a turn of the road--smartly clad people of quality, with luggage-mules and servants in their train! I was off like a shot, through the bushes, by the shortest cut. For a while it did seem that these people would pass the king before I could get to him; but desperation gives you wings, you know, and I canted my body forward, inflated my breast, and held my breath and flew. I arrived. And in plenty good enough time, too.

text: Sir Gawaine rose to his feet easily, drew his sword, and squared up to face Sir Marhaus on foot. The two men charged at each other fiercely, their swords clashing so hard their shields shattered into splinters, their helmets and chainmail dented deeply, and both left bleeding wounds on one another. But once nine o’clock had passed, Sir Gawaine grew steadily stronger for three straight hours, his strength tripling as the time dragged on. Sir Marhaus picked up on this shift, and was stunned by how his opponent’s power kept swelling. The pair continued to inflict increasingly grave injuries on each other, until the hour ticked over to noon--
source_text: --“and lightly Sir Gawaine rose upon his feet and pulled out his sword, and dressed him toward Sir Marhaus on foot, and therewith either came unto other eagerly, and smote together with their swords, that their shields flew in cantels, and they bruised their helms and their hauberks, and wounded either other. But Sir Gawaine, fro it passed nine of the clock, waxed by the space of three hours ever stronger and stronger and thrice his might was increased. All this espied Sir Marhaus, and had great wonder how his might increased, and so they wounded other passing sore; and then when it was come noon--”

text: We hosted one tournament that stretched on daily for well over a week, with fully five hundred knights taking part across its entire run. It took weeks for all the attendees to assemble: they rode in on horseback from every corner, from the farthest reaches of the kingdom, and even from lands across the sea. Many brought noble ladies in their retinue, and every last traveler brought squires and entire contingents of household staff. The whole gathering was dazzlingly, garishly grand in its finery, and perfectly emblematic of the place and era: brimming over with unruly, boisterous energy, peppered with playful, unselfconscious vulgarity of speech, and suffused with a cheerful, unapologetic disregard for strict social mores. Every single day was filled with either jousting or spectating, and half of every night was given over to singing, gambling, dancing, and raucous feasting. Everyone had the grandest possible time; there was no crowd quite like them, anywhere. The rows of lovely ladies, resplendent in their wild, glittering finery, would watch a knight go tumbling from his mount in the lists, a lance-shaft as thick as a man’s ankle run clear through his body and blood gushing from the wound, and rather than swooning they would clap and jostle one another for a clearer vantage point. Only now and then would one press a handkerchief to her face, putting on a dramatic show of utter devastation—and more often than not, you could bet two to one she was hiding some secret scandal, and fretting that the rest of the crowd hadn’t yet caught wind of it.
source_text: We had one tournament which was continued from day to day during more than a week, and as many as five hundred knights took part in it, from first to last. They were weeks gathering. They came on horseback from everywhere; from the very ends of the country, and even from beyond the sea; and many brought ladies, and all brought squires and troops of servants. It was a most gaudy and gorgeous crowd, as to costumery, and very characteristic of the country and the time, in the way of high animal spirits, innocent indecencies of language, and happy-hearted indifference to morals. It was fight or look on, all day and every day; and sing, gamble, dance, carouse half the night every night. They had a most noble good time. You never saw such people. Those banks of beautiful ladies, shining in their barbaric splendors, would see a knight sprawl from his horse in the lists with a lanceshaft the thickness of your ankle clean through him and the blood spouting, and instead of fainting they would clap their hands and crowd each other for a better view; only sometimes one would dive into her handkerchief, and look ostentatiously broken-hearted, and then you could lay two to one that there was a scandal there somewhere and she was afraid the public hadn't found it out.

text: Yeah, yeah, I’m not denying that whatsoever. But that’s totally beside the point; the raw number of coins you get as pay, with meaningless little labels attached just to tell them apart, has nothing to do with what actually matters. The real question is, how much stuff can you buy with those wages? That’s the whole crux of this. Even if it’s true that a skilled mechanic where you’re from makes about three and a half dollars a year, while our equivalent only gets roughly a dollar seventy-five—
source_text: “Yes-yes, I don't deny that at all. But that's got nothing to do with it; the _amount_ of the wages in mere coins, with meaningless names attached to them to know them by, has got nothing to do with it. The thing is, how much can you _buy_ with your wages? --that's the idea. While it is true that with you a good mechanic is allowed about three dollars and a half a year, and with us only about a dollar and seventy-five--”

text: Expedition No. 3 is set to depart around the first of next month on a mission to locate Sir Sagramour le Desirous. It will be led by the renowned Knight of the Red Lawns, assisted first by Sir Persant of Inde—competent, sharp-witted, courteous, and an all-around reliable, stand-up fellow—and further supported by Sir Palamides the Saracen, who is no slouch in his own right. This is no casual, leisurely outing; these men mean serious business.
source_text: Expedition No. 3 will start adout the first of mext month on a search f8r Sir Sagramour le Desirous. It is in com- and of the renowned Knight of the Red Lawns, assissted by Sir Persant of Inde, who is compete9t. intelligent, courte- ous, and in every way a brick, and fur- tHer assisted by Sir Palamides the Sara- cen, who is no huckleberry hinself. This is no pic-nic, these boys mean busine&s.

text: “Good gracious, who on earth could’ve seen this coming? One full-fledged earl and six squabbling little earl-lets? Why, Meg, that was a prime catch, no two ways about it. Wandering hero work is the daftest racket there is, backbreaking and dull as ditchwater most days, but I’m starting to think there really is coin in it, after all, if the fates smile on you. Not that I’d ever take it up as a proper trade, mind you—no honest, steady business ever builds itself on a foundation of pure speculation. A big winning streak in the wandering hero line—what is it, when you blow off all the flowery chivalry nonsense and get down to cold, hard facts? It’s just a temporary corner on the pork market, that’s all, and you can’t spin it into anything more. You’re rich—sure, suddenly flush with cash—for maybe a day, maybe a week; then someone else corners the market on you, and your little fly-by-night operation crumbles to dust. Ain’t that right, Meg?”
source_text: “Well, well, well,--now who would ever have thought it? One whole duke and six dukelets; why, Sandy, it was an elegant haul. Knight-errantry is a most chuckle-headed trade, and it is tedious hard work, too, but I begin to see that there _is_ money in it, after all, if you have luck. Not that I would ever engage in it as a business, for I wouldn't. No sound and legitimate business can be established on a basis of speculation. A successful whirl in the knight-errantry line--now what is it when you blow away the nonsense and come down to the cold facts? It's just a corner in pork, that's all, and you can't make anything else out of it. You're rich--yes,--suddenly rich--for about a day, maybe a week; then somebody corners the market on _you_, and down goes your bucket-shop; ain't that so, Sandy?”

text: He brought only the one because it was the only one in existence to bring—there is no other specimen of it anywhere. It rightfully belongs to the King of the Sea Demons. This man is an ignorant pretender, else he would have known the weapon can be used for no more than eight bouts before it dissipates and returns to its home beneath the waves.
source_text: “He brought but the one because there was but the one to bring. There exists none other but that one. It belongeth to the king of the Demons of the Sea. This man is a pretender, and ignorant, else he had known that that weapon can be used in but eight bouts only, and then it vanisheth away to its home under the sea.”

text: This cut deep, coming as it did to someone who’d nurtured a dream of a just, representative republic for as long as he could remember. It pulled me back to a chapter of history nearly thirteen centuries prior, when the landless white farmers of the American South, scorned and routinely insulted by the wealthy enslaver barons who ruled their communities, their entire squalid, lowly existence a direct consequence of the slave system that warped every corner of their world, still cowered and aligned themselves with those same barons at every political turn to defend and entrench human bondage. In the end, they even hefted their rifles and spilled their own blood fighting to save the very system that had ground them into the dirt. The single redeeming thread woven through that miserable, twisted piece of history was this: deep down, those landless farmers hated the enslaver lords, and felt the full weight of their own shame. That resentment never bubbled up into open action, but the simple truth that it lived inside them, waiting for the right, favorable conditions to break free, was something—in fact, it was enough. It proved that at their core, every person is still fully human, even if that truth never shows on the surface.
source_text: This was depressing--to a man with the dream of a republic in his head. It reminded me of a time thirteen centuries away, when the “poor whites” of our South who were always despised and frequently insulted by the slave-lords around them, and who owed their base condition simply to the presence of slavery in their midst, were yet pusillanimously ready to side with the slave-lords in all political moves for the upholding and perpetuating of slavery, and did also finally shoulder their muskets and pour out their lives in an effort to prevent the destruction of that very institution which degraded them. And there was only one redeeming feature connected with that pitiful piece of history; and that was, that secretly the “poor white” did detest the slave-lord, and did feel his own shame. That feeling was not brought to the surface, but the fact that it was there and could have been brought out, under favoring circumstances, was something--in fact, it was enough; for it showed that a man is at bottom a man, after all, even if it doesn't show on the outside.

text: Sure enough, he followed through on his call. Those small-time traders couldn’t deliver the shares. Lord, he had them dead to rights—just settled his grip and squeezed every last one of them dry. They’d been snickering behind their hands for ages, smug as cats about their clever trick of dumping stock on him at 15, 16, and right around there, when the stuff wasn’t worth even 10 a share. Well, once they’d laughed long enough out of one side of their mouths, they had to rest that half of their face by switching the hollow, panicked grin to the other side. That was when they cut their losses and settled with the Unassailable Corporation at 283 a share!
source_text: “Very well, he did call. The boys couldn't deliver. Oh, he had them--and he just settled his grip and squeezed them. They were laughing in their sleeves over their smartness in selling stock to him at 15 and 16 and along there that wasn't worth 10. Well, when they had laughed long enough on that side of their mouths, they rested-up that side by shifting the laugh to the other side. That was when they compromised with the Invincible at 283!”

text: The chieftain finished stowing his freight on board, and since none of the conversation drifted to raids, territorial seizure, or trial by steel, his energy ebbed into drowsiness and he retired to his cabin for a short sleep. Mistress Elspeth cleared the trestle table, set the cask of ale within easy reach, and slipped off to eat her meal of scraps in quiet, unassuming privacy. The rest of us quickly fell to discussing the topics that hit closest to home for folks like us: work and pay, naturally. At a casual glance, the whole of this small vassal realm—ruled over by King Thrain—seemed far more prosperous than the territory I called home. Their protectionist trade laws were fully enacted across the kingdom, while our own lands were moving gradually toward open trade, and had already made roughly half that transition. It wasn’t long before Kael and I were carrying the whole conversation, the rest of the group hanging on every word. Kael grew bolder as he spoke, scented an opening to get the better of me, and started firing off questions he thought would catch me off guard—and to his credit, they did seem poised to do just that:
source_text: The king got his cargo aboard, and then, the talk not turning upon battle, conquest, or iron-clad duel, he dulled down to drowsiness and went off to take a nap. Mrs. Marco cleared the table, placed the beer keg handy, and went away to eat her dinner of leavings in humble privacy, and the rest of us soon drifted into matters near and dear to the hearts of our sort--business and wages, of course. At a first glance, things appeared to be exceeding prosperous in this little tributary kingdom--whose lord was King Bagdemagus--as compared with the state of things in my own region. They had the “protection” system in full force here, whereas we were working along down toward free trade, by easy stages, and were now about half way. Before long, Dowley and I were doing all the talking, the others hungrily listening. Dowley warmed to his work, snuffed an advantage in the air, and began to put questions which he considered pretty awkward ones for me, and they did have something of that look:

text: But of course, my point didn’t land. I had to abandon the whole argument entirely. All these people cared about was the raw number of their high wages; whether that money could actually stretch to cover their needs didn’t seem to register with them at all. They championed “protectionist” policies and swore by them, which made a sad sort of sense—parties with a vested interest had tricked them into believing those very policies were the source of their high pay in the first place. I laid out clear evidence for them: over a quarter century, their wages had risen only 30 percent, while the cost of living had doubled. Where my group came from, over an even shorter period, pay had climbed 40 percent while living costs had fallen steadily. But none of it did any good. Nothing could dislodge their odd, deeply held convictions.
source_text: But, alas! it didn't crush. No, I had to give it up. What those people valued was _high wages_; it didn't seem to be a matter of any consequence to them whether the high wages would buy anything or not. They stood for “protection,” and swore by it, which was reasonable enough, because interested parties had gulled them into the notion that it was protection which had created their high wages. I proved to them that in a quarter of a century their wages had advanced but 30 per cent., while the cost of living had gone up 100; and that with us, in a shorter time, wages had advanced 40 per cent. while the cost of living had gone steadily down. But it didn't do any good. Nothing could unseat their strange beliefs.

text: There’s no way to put what’s happening into words. The spring has been bone dry for nine full days now. The prayers we started the moment it went dry, the public lamentations in sackcloth and ash, the sacred processions—none of them have stopped for a single minute, day or night. All that work has left the monks, nuns, and foundling children completely spent, so they’ve started hanging prayers written on parchment around the grounds instead, since no one has enough strength left to call their prayers out loud. Finally, they sent for you, Sir Boss, to see if your magic and enchantments can fix this. If you hadn’t been able to come, the messenger was supposed to fetch Merlin next. As it is, Merlin’s already been here three days, swearing he’ll get that water flowing even if he has to shatter the entire globe and tear every kingdom on it apart to do it. He’s been working his spells as fiercely as he can, summoning his demonic underlings to hurry over and help, but he hasn’t pulled even the smallest hint of moisture out of the ground yet—nothing that would even count as faint mist on a polished copper mirror, that is, if you don’t count the buckets of sweat he pours out from sunrise to sunset straining over this brutal, unforgiving task. And if you--
source_text: “None may describe it in words. The fount is these nine days dry. The prayers that did begin then, and the lamentations in sackcloth and ashes, and the holy processions, none of these have ceased nor night nor day; and so the monks and the nuns and the foundlings be all exhausted, and do hang up prayers writ upon parchment, sith that no strength is left in man to lift up voice. And at last they sent for thee, Sir Boss, to try magic and enchantment; and if you could not come, then was the messenger to fetch Merlin, and he is there these three days now, and saith he will fetch that water though he burst the globe and wreck its kingdoms to accomplish it; and right bravely doth he work his magic and call upon his hellions to hie them hither and help, but not a whiff of moisture hath he started yet, even so much as might qualify as mist upon a copper mirror an ye count not the barrel of sweat he sweateth betwixt sun and sun over the dire labors of his task; and if ye--”

text: My train of thought frayed and slipped away entirely there, and I drifted off into a doze, turning over the sharp, sad waste of it all in my foggy mind: men with that kind of staggering, unbreakable strength—strength enough to stay upright cased in punishing, unwieldy iron plate, soaked through with stinging sweat, swinging and bashing and clashing against one another for six unbroken hours at a time—had never been born into an era where they could put that power to some genuinely useful purpose. Take a work mule, for instance: a mule has that same brute, uncomplaining strength, puts it to use hauling loads and tilling fields, and is worth its place in the world precisely because it is a mule; but a titled lord is worth nothing at all when he acts no better than a mule. It is a sorry, useless mismatch, one that never should have been thought up in the first place. And yet, the second you let a mistake take root, the harm is already done, and you never can tell what sort of unforeseen mess it will spiral into later.
source_text: But I lost the thread there, and dozed off to slumber, thinking about what a pity it was that men with such superb strength --strength enabling them to stand up cased in cruelly burdensome iron and drenched with perspiration, and hack and batter and bang each other for six hours on a stretch--should not have been born at a time when they could put it to some useful purpose. Take a jackass, for instance: a jackass has that kind of strength, and puts it to a useful purpose, and is valuable to this world because he is a jackass; but a nobleman is not valuable because he is a jackass. It is a mixture that is always ineffectual, and should never have been attempted in the first place. And yet, once you start a mistake, the trouble is done and you never know what is going to come of it.

text: Look, the loyalty I believe in is devotion to the nation itself, not to its governing institutions or the public officials that run them. The nation is the real, tangible, enduring thing: it is what we ought to guard, tend to, and give our full faithfulness to. Governing systems are incidental, no more than the outer garments it wears. Garments wear thin, grow tattered, stop feeling comfortable, stop shielding the body from frost, sickness, and ruin. To pledge loyalty to rags, to shout for rags, to worship rags, to die for rags—that is senseless, unthinking loyalty, little more than brute animal impulse. It was invented by monarchies, belongs only to monarchies; let the crowns hoard that sorry brand of devotion for themselves. I was born and raised in Vermont, whose state constitution plainly states “that all political power is inherent in the people, and all free governments are founded on their authority and created for their collective good; and that they hold at all times an undeniable, indefeasible right to alter their form of government in whatever manner they judge most fitting.”
source_text: You see my kind of loyalty was loyalty to one's country, not to its institutions or its office-holders. The country is the real thing, the substantial thing, the eternal thing; it is the thing to watch over, and care for, and be loyal to; institutions are extraneous, they are its mere clothing, and clothing can wear out, become ragged, cease to be comfortable, cease to protect the body from winter, disease, and death. To be loyal to rags, to shout for rags, to worship rags, to die for rags--that is a loyalty of unreason, it is pure animal; it belongs to monarchy, was invented by monarchy; let monarchy keep it. I was from Connecticut, whose Constitution declares “that all political power is inherent in the people, and all free governments are founded on their authority and instituted for their benefit; and that they have _at all times_ an undeniable and indefeasible right to _alter their form of government_ in such a manner as they may think expedient.”

text: “Now, mark the contrast: you pay eight cents and four mills, we hand over only four.” I was primed to land my zinger right then. I said, “Hold on, good friend, whatever became of those fat high wages you were strutting and boasting about just a few minutes prior?” I swept a glance over the gathered crowd with calm, quiet satisfaction, because I’d edged up on him bit by bit and tied him hand and foot, you see, without him ever realizing he was being bound at all. “What’s happened to those lofty high wages of yours? Looks to me like I’ve knocked every last bit of wind out of that brag, if I’m being honest.”
source_text: “Well, observe the difference: you pay eight cents and four mills, we pay only four cents.” I prepared now to sock it to him. I said: “Look here, dear friend, _what's become of your high wages you were bragging so about a few minutes ago?_”--and I looked around on the company with placid satisfaction, for I had slipped up on him gradually and tied him hand and foot, you see, without his ever noticing that he was being tied at all. “What's become of those noble high wages of yours?--I seem to have knocked the stuffing all out of them, it appears to me.”

text: How laughably simple and low-effort it was to pass as a master magician by this man’s shoddy standards. His signature act was claiming he could reveal what any person anywhere on Earth was doing at that very moment, what they had done at any point in the past, and what they would do at any point in the future. He posed a question to the gathered crowd: would anyone care to learn what the Emperor of the East was occupied with right then? Glittering, eager eyes and delighted, impatient hand-rubbing were answer enough: this pious, enthralled crowd very much wanted to know exactly what that sovereign was up to at that exact instant. The con man performed a few more rounds of overwrought, meaningless ritual, then delivered his solemn proclamation:
source_text: How easy and cheap it was to be a great magician on this fellow's terms. His specialty was to tell you what any individual on the face of the globe was doing at the moment; and what he had done at any time in the past, and what he would do at any time in the future. He asked if any would like to know what the Emperor of the East was doing now? The sparkling eyes and the delighted rubbing of hands made eloquent answer--this reverend crowd _would_ like to know what that monarch was at, just as this moment. The fraud went through some more mummery, and then made grave announcement:

text: “That’s the gist of it. I stationed four of my men there on watch—tucked out of sight inside the entryway. No one was supposed to get harmed so long as they stayed on the other side of the threshold, but if anyone tried to force their way in? We were more than ready to meet them. After that, I hiked out into the highlands to dig up and snip the covert line that ran straight from your sleeping quarters to the blasting charges buried beneath every last one of our sprawling factories, textile mills, assembly shops, weapons stockpiles, and the rest of the industrial complex. Right around midnight, my crew and I got to work rerouting that line to the cave system, and no one besides the two of us has any clue where the far end terminates. We buried the whole length under sod and rock, of course, and the whole job was wrapped up in two hours give or take. Now we don’t even have to step foot outside our stronghold if we decide to bring the whole rotten edifice of our so-called civilization crashing down.”
source_text: “I think so. I placed four of my boys there as a guard--inside, and out of sight. Nobody was to be hurt--while outside; but any attempt to enter--well, we said just let anybody try it! Then I went out into the hills and uncovered and cut the secret wires which connected your bedroom with the wires that go to the dynamite deposits under all our vast factories, mills, workshops, magazines, etc., and about midnight I and my boys turned out and connected that wire with the cave, and nobody but you and I suspects where the other end of it goes to. We laid it under ground, of course, and it was all finished in a couple of hours or so. We sha'n't have to leave our fortress now when we want to blow up our civilization.”

text: Kael wavered, torn between a sharp, tugging longing on one hand and icy, coiling dread on the other; then he slipped soundlessly to the door, pressed an eye to the gap in its scuffed wooden frame, and held his breath to listen. At last he crept huddled to my side, set his mouth tight to the shell of my ear, and breathed his horrifying news out in a thin, trembling whisper, every line of his frame taut with the cringing dread of someone stepping onto unhallowed ground, speaking of things whose very mention might be heavy with the weight of immediate death.
source_text: He hesitated, pulled one way by desire, the other way by fear; then he stole to the door and peeped out, listening; and finally crept close to me and put his mouth to my ear and told me his fearful news in a whisper, and with all the cowering apprehension of one who was venturing upon awful ground and speaking of things whose very mention might be freighted with death.

text: I was dying to see what this thing was, and I also wanted to demonstrate just how much more impressive the one I planned to present to the Examining Board would be. I dropped a subtle hint about this to the king, and it sparked his curiosity right away. Once the full Board had gathered, I followed him into the room, and the line of candidates filed in right behind us. Among those candidates was a sharp young West Point cadet of mine, and he was accompanied by a pair of my West Point professors.
source_text: I was impatient to see what this was; and to show, too, how much more admirable was the one which I should display to the Examining Board. I intimated this, gently, to the king, and it fired his curiosity. When the Board was assembled, I followed him in; and behind us came the candidates. One of these candidates was a bright young West Pointer of mine, and with him were a couple of my West Point professors.

text:
I didn’t push the matter further. Then I read aloud softly, pitched to carry only to the cluster of people who’d pressed in close enough to fit their shaved heads within earshot, a section of the account of the well’s miraculous restoration. The entire reading was punctuated by shocked, reverent exclamations: “Ohhhh!” “That’s exactly how it went!” “Incredible, just incredible!” “These are the actual events, word for word, perfectly spot-on!”

When they asked if they could hold this strange, precious object in their own hands to feel and examine it—promising they would be as careful as could be—I said yes. They passed it between them, handling it as cautiously and devoutly as if it were a sacred relic sent down from some otherworldly realm. They ran their fingers lightly over its texture, lingered to stroke its warm, smooth surface, and stared at the mysterious printed characters with rapt, fascinated eyes.

All those huddled, bowed heads, those entranced faces, those bright, expressive eyes—they struck me as so deeply beautiful. Wasn’t this little volume my most cherished darling, after all? And wasn’t all that quiet awe, eager curiosity, and unprompted respect the most eloquent, sincere tribute and unforced compliment it could ever receive?

In that moment, I understood exactly how a mother feels when other women, be they strangers or friends, crowd around to hold her newborn baby, leaning in all at once out of pure, urgent affection, their heads bowed over the infant in a rapt, adoring haze that wipes the entire rest of the universe from their consciousness, like nothing else exists for those few seconds. I knew that quiet, soaring joy, and I knew no other fulfilled ambition—whether that of a king, a conqueror, or a celebrated poet—ever comes even halfway to that serene, distant high, or delivers half as much divine, unshakable contentment.
source_text: I let it go at that. Then I read in a low voice, to as many as could crowd their shaven heads within hearing distance, part of the account of the miracle of the restoration of the well, and was accompanied by astonished and reverent ejaculations all through: “Ah-h-h!” “How true!” “Amazing, amazing!” “These be the very haps as they happened, in marvelous exactness!” And might they take this strange thing in their hands, and feel of it and examine it?--they would be very careful. Yes. So they took it, handling it as cautiously and devoutly as if it had been some holy thing come from some supernatural region; and gently felt of its texture, caressed its pleasant smooth surface with lingering touch, and scanned the mysterious characters with fascinated eyes. These grouped bent heads, these charmed faces, these speaking eyes --how beautiful to me! For was not this my darling, and was not all this mute wonder and interest and homage a most eloquent tribute and unforced compliment to it? I knew, then, how a mother feels when women, whether strangers or friends, take her new baby, and close themselves about it with one eager impulse, and bend their heads over it in a tranced adoration that makes all the rest of the universe vanish out of their consciousness and be as if it were not, for that time. I knew how she feels, and that there is no other satisfied ambition, whether of king, conqueror, or poet, that ever reaches half-way to that serene far summit or yields half so divine a contentment.

text: They surged to their feet and lunged straight for him! Roaring furiously, “One’s a traitor, the other’s a raving lunatic! Kill them both! Kill them!” they threw themselves at us. Pure delight blazed in the king’s eyes! He might have been hopeless at farming and tending the fields, but this sort of scuffle was right up his alley. He’d gone long without a scrap, and he was ravenous for a brawl. He landed a sharp crack to the blacksmith’s jaw that sent the man flying straight off his feet and splayed flat on his back. “St. George for Britain!” he yelled as he took the wheelwright down too. The mason was a hulking brute, but I dropped him without breaking a sweat. The three of them hauled themselves back up and charged again; went down hard again; staggered back up once more; repeating the cycle over and over with that stubborn native British grit, until they were beaten to a pulp, swaying with fatigue, so dazed they couldn’t tell us apart from each other, yet they kept swinging, throwing every last ounce of strength they had left into their blows. Mostly, they were hitting each other—we’d stepped back to watch while they rolled around, grappled, gouged, punched, and bit, focused on their fight as silently and single-mindedly as a pack of bulldogs. We watched without a hint of worry; they were quickly losing the strength to go running for help, and the clearing we were in was far enough from the main road that no random passerby would stumble on the scene.
source_text: They rose and went for him! With a fierce shout, “The one would betray us, the other is mad! Kill them! Kill them!” they flung themselves upon us. What joy flamed up in the king's eye! He might be lame in agriculture, but this kind of thing was just in his line. He had been fasting long, he was hungry for a fight. He hit the blacksmith a crack under the jaw that lifted him clear off his feet and stretched him flat on his back. “St. George for Britain!” and he downed the wheelwright. The mason was big, but I laid him out like nothing. The three gathered themselves up and came again; went down again; came again; and kept on repeating this, with native British pluck, until they were battered to jelly, reeling with exhaustion, and so blind that they couldn't tell us from each other; and yet they kept right on, hammering away with what might was left in them. Hammering each other--for we stepped aside and looked on while they rolled, and struggled, and gouged, and pounded, and bit, with the strict and wordless attention to business of so many bulldogs. We looked on without apprehension, for they were fast getting past ability to go for help against us, and the arena was far enough from the public road to be safe from intrusion.

text: “Thine ears are keen, but that sharp sense will do thee no good this day!” He tapped the hilt of his massive broadsword, its steel thrumming faintly where his calloused fingers brushed it. “If thou canst not make it out for the veil’s dimming magic that clouds thy sight, know this: it is no unwieldy spear, but a blade—and I doubt thou shalt slip its strike.”
source_text: “Thou'rt quick of ear, but it will not save thee from this!” and he touched the hilt of his great sword. “An ye are not able to see it, because of the influence of the veil, know that it is no cumbrous lance, but a sword--and I ween ye will not be able to avoid it.”

text: Confound that ridiculous sum! Just hold your tongue for a minute, would you? You chatter so nonstop a person can’t get a single word in edgewise. You need to break that habit, Marco—it’s poor form, and it’ll only get worse if you don’t check it now. Right, we’ll step in here now to ask what this man’s stock costs—and don’t you dare let on to Jones that you know he had any part in this whole affair. You have no idea how oddly sensitive and proud he is. He’s a farmer, a fairly well-to-do one at that, and I’m his bailiff—but you wouldn’t believe the delusions of grandeur that man has. Sometimes when he forgets himself and starts blowing his own horn, you’d think he was one of the wealthiest, most important people on the planet. You could listen to him for a hundred years and never guess he was a farmer—especially if he’s talking about agriculture. He’s convinced he’s the absolute best farmer alive, some kind of legendary old master from back in the day, but just between you and me, he knows about as much about farming as he knows about running an entire kingdom. Still, no matter what he’s going on about, you just let your jaw drop and listen like you’ve never heard such incredible wisdom in your whole life, like you’re terrified you’ll drop dead before you can soak up every last bit of it. That’s guaranteed to please Jones.
source_text: “Hang the vastness of the sum! Try to keep quiet for a moment, and see how it would seem; a body can't get in a word edgeways, you talk so much. You ought to cure that, Marco; it isn't good form, you know, and it will grow on you if you don't check it. Yes, we'll step in here now and price this man's stuff--and don't forget to remember to not let on to Jones that you know he had anything to do with it. You can't think how curiously sensitive and proud he is. He's a farmer--pretty fairly well-to-do farmer --and I'm his bailiff; _but_--the imagination of that man! Why, sometimes when he forgets himself and gets to blowing off, you'd think he was one of the swells of the earth; and you might listen to him a hundred years and never take him for a farmer--especially if he talked agriculture. He _thinks_ he's a Sheol of a farmer; thinks he's old Grayback from Wayback; but between you and me privately he don't know as much about farming as he does about running a kingdom--still, whatever he talks about, you want to drop your underjaw and listen, the same as if you had never heard such incredible wisdom in all your life before, and were afraid you might die before you got enough of it. That will please Jones.”

text: Our war against the English as a unified nation is at an end. The entire populace has withdrawn from the battlefield and stepped away from the conflict. Long before they could ever be persuaded to return to fighting, all hostilities will have ceased entirely. This is the only remaining campaign we will wage. It will be brief—the shortest in recorded history, in fact. It will also be the most devastating to human life, when measured by the ratio of casualties to the total number of troops engaged. We have no further business with the English nation at large; from now on, we contend only with their knights. English knights can be killed, but they can never be conquered. We know full well what lies before us. As long as even one of these men remains alive, our task is unfinished, and the war is not over. We will kill every last one of them. [Loud, long-sustained applause erupted.]
source_text: “The war with the English nation, as a nation, is at an end. The nation has retired from the field and the war. Before it can be persuaded to return, war will have ceased. This campaign is the only one that is going to be fought. It will be brief --the briefest in history. Also the most destructive to life, considered from the standpoint of proportion of casualties to numbers engaged. We are done with the nation; henceforth we deal only with the knights. English knights can be killed, but they cannot be conquered. We know what is before us. While one of these men remains alive, our task is not finished, the war is not ended. We will kill them all.” [Loud and long continued applause.]

text: The slave trader bought both of us, clipped us to the end of his long, clinking chain line, and we formed the very rear of his procession. We set off on our march and cleared the edges of Cambenet at midday; I couldn’t wrap my head around how absurdly strange it was that the King of England and his chief minister, tramping along manacled, chained, and yoked into a slave convoy, could pass right by crowds of loitering men and women, under windows where soft, lovely people sat, and not draw a single curious glance, not spark one offhand comment. Good lord, that just goes to show there’s nothing inherently more sacred about a king than there is about a random wandering tramp, when it comes down to it. He’s just a cheap, hollow performative fake when no one has any idea he’s royalty. But let slip his true status, and hell, you’d be holding your breath just looking at him, acting like he’s some kind of divine figure. I reckon we’re all a bunch of fools. Born that way, no doubt about it.
source_text: The slave-dealer bought us both, and hitched us onto that long chain of his, and we constituted the rear of his procession. We took up our line of march and passed out of Cambenet at noon; and it seemed to me unaccountably strange and odd that the King of England and his chief minister, marching manacled and fettered and yoked, in a slave convoy, could move by all manner of idle men and women, and under windows where sat the sweet and the lovely, and yet never attract a curious eye, never provoke a single remark. Dear, dear, it only shows that there is nothing diviner about a king than there is about a tramp, after all. He is just a cheap and hollow artificiality when you don't know he is a king. But reveal his quality, and dear me it takes your very breath away to look at him. I reckon we are all fools. Born so, no doubt.

text: For another thing, these missionaries would slowly introduce basic standards of cleanliness among the nobility, no suspicion or alarm roused in the process, and the practice would seep down to the general population if the priests could be kept from interfering. That would undermine the Church’s foundations— or rather, it would be a critical first step toward that end. Next would come widespread education, then liberty, and at that point the institution would start to crack and crumble. I hold the unshakable conviction that any state-established Church is state-sanctioned crime, an official, institutionalized slave pen, so I had no moral scruples about the work; I was ready to assail it by any method, with any weapon that promised to do real damage. Why, in my own former lifetime—those distant centuries that have not yet stirred in the womb of unwritten time—there were old Englishmen who genuinely believed they had been born in a free country: a “free” nation where the Corporation Act and Test Act remained fully in force, heavy timbers propped against men’s liberties and dishonored consciences, all to shore up that state-endorsed anachronism.
source_text: Secondly, these missionaries would gradually, and without creating suspicion or exciting alarm, introduce a rudimentary cleanliness among the nobility, and from them it would work down to the people, if the priests could be kept quiet. This would undermine the Church. I mean would be a step toward that. Next, education--next, freedom --and then she would begin to crumble. It being my conviction that any Established Church is an established crime, an established slave-pen, I had no scruples, but was willing to assail it in any way or with any weapon that promised to hurt it. Why, in my own former day--in remote centuries not yet stirring in the womb of time--there were old Englishmen who imagined that they had been born in a free country: a “free” country with the Corporation Act and the Test still in force in it--timbers propped against men's liberties and dishonored consciences to shore up an Established Anachronism with.

text: I cautioned the king that this spot posed far too much danger to any outsider. Our best course was to pull back out of the open light, and lay low until the unrest calmed. We retreated a short distance, tucking ourselves out of sight at the treeline. From that concealed vantage point, we watched as the mob hunted down men and women alike. That terrible, brutal work dragged on almost until dawn. By then the fire had guttered out and the storm had spent its fury; shouting and the thud of flying footsteps faded quickly, leaving darkness and stillness to reign over the land once more.
source_text: I warned the king that this was not a safe place for strangers. We would better get away from the light, until matters should improve. We stepped back a little, and hid in the edge of the wood. From this hiding-place we saw both men and women hunted by the mob. The fearful work went on until nearly dawn. Then, the fire being out and the storm spent, the voices and flying footsteps presently ceased, and darkness and stillness reigned again.

text: Every gaze was fixed sharply, thick with skeptical inquiry, on Sir Kay. But he rose to the moment flawlessly. He stood and played his gambit like a seasoned master, sweeping every point before him. He announced he would lay out the matter exactly as it had unfolded, telling the plain, unvarnished tale with no personal commentary of his own. “And when you have heard it,” he said, “if any glory or honor is due, you will bestow it on the man with the mightiest arm who ever carried a shield or struck a sword blow in the ranks of Christian war—he who sits right there!” He pointed straight to Sir Launcelot. Oh, he had them in the palm of his hand; it was a brilliant, rousing move. Then he went on to recount how Sir Launcelot, out questing for adventures not long prior, had cut down seven giants with a single sweep of his blade, freeing a hundred and forty-two captive maidens. Pressing onward still in search of noble deeds, he had come upon Kay himself locked in a desperate, brutal fight against nine foreign knights, and had immediately stepped in to take on the entire battle alone, defeating all nine handily. That night, Launcelot had slipped out quietly, donned Kay’s armor, taken his horse, and ridden off to distant lands, where he vanquished sixteen knights in one pitched fight, and thirty-four in another.
