All those men, plus the nine he had defeated to save Kay, he made swear that around Whitsuntide they would ride to Arthur’s court and surrender themselves to Queen Guenever as captives of Sir Kay the Seneschal, trophies of his knightly prowess. Now the first half dozen of them had arrived, and the rest would follow as soon as they recovered from their grave battle wounds.
source_text: Every eye was fastened with severe inquiry upon Sir Kay. But he was equal to the occasion. He got up and played his hand like a major--and took every trick. He said he would state the case exactly according to the facts; he would tell the simple straightforward tale, without comment of his own; “and then,” said he, “if ye find glory and honor due, ye will give it unto him who is the mightiest man of his hands that ever bare shield or strake with sword in the ranks of Christian battle--even him that sitteth there!” and he pointed to Sir Launcelot. Ah, he fetched them; it was a rattling good stroke. Then he went on and told how Sir Launcelot, seeking adventures, some brief time gone by, killed seven giants at one sweep of his sword, and set a hundred and forty-two captive maidens free; and then went further, still seeking adventures, and found him (Sir Kay) fighting a desperate fight against nine foreign knights, and straightway took the battle solely into his own hands, and conquered the nine; and that night Sir Launcelot rose quietly, and dressed him in Sir Kay's armor and took Sir Kay's horse and gat him away into distant lands, and vanquished sixteen knights in one pitched battle and thirty-four in another; and all these and the former nine he made to swear that about Whitsuntide they would ride to Arthur's court and yield them to Queen Guenever's hands as captives of Sir Kay the Seneschal, spoil of his knightly prowess; and now here were these half dozen, and the rest would be along as soon as they might be healed of their desperate wounds.

text: I realized right away I’d pushed the stunt a little too far. I’d set out to give them a good scare, not frighten them half to death—but that was exactly how close they were to breaking entirely. You have to understand, they’d spent their entire lives learning just how brutal the pillory could be. To have that fate staring them directly in the face, every last one of them completely at my mercy—me, a total stranger—if I chose to turn them in? It was unthinkable, and they couldn’t snap out of the shock, couldn’t pull themselves together no matter how hard they tried. Pale, trembling, speechless, pitiable? They might as well have been so many corpses already. The whole situation felt deeply uncomfortable. I’d assumed they’d beg me to keep quiet, then we’d shake hands, pass around a round of drinks, laugh the whole mess off, and that would be the end of it. But that’s not what happened. You see, I was an unknown outsider among a brutally oppressed, deeply distrustful people, folks who’d spent their whole lives having their helplessness exploited, who never expected fair or kind treatment from anyone outside their immediate families and innermost circle of confidants. Beg me to be gentle, to be fair, to show them generosity? They ached to, of course—but they didn’t dare.
source_text: But I saw in a moment that I had overdone the thing a little. I was expecting to scare them, but I wasn't expecting to scare them to death. They were mighty near it, though. You see they had been a whole lifetime learning to appreciate the pillory; and to have that thing staring them in the face, and every one of them distinctly at the mercy of me, a stranger, if I chose to go and report--well, it was awful, and they couldn't seem to recover from the shock, they couldn't seem to pull themselves together. Pale, shaky, dumb, pitiful? Why, they weren't any better than so many dead men. It was very uncomfortable. Of course, I thought they would appeal to me to keep mum, and then we would shake hands, and take a drink all round, and laugh it off, and there an end. But no; you see I was an unknown person, among a cruelly oppressed and suspicious people, a people always accustomed to having advantage taken of their helplessness, and never expecting just or kind treatment from any but their own families and very closest intimates. Appeal to _me_ to be gentle, to be fair, to be generous? Of course, they wanted to, but they couldn't dare.

text: Dusk was settling fast, and a storm was rolling right in behind it. The dark swallowed the landscape quicker than I expected. There was no question we had to hunker down and camp right where we were. I tracked down a decent, sheltered nook under an overhanging rock for the demoiselle Alisande, then wandered off to find a separate spot for myself. The catch was, I had to stay fully suited in my armor the whole night. I couldn’t get the damn thing off by myself, but asking Alisande for help was completely off the table—it would have felt way too much like undressing in front of another person. Logically, I knew it wasn’t actually that big a deal; I had layers of regular clothes on underneath all the metal, after all. But the social norms you’re raised to internalize don’t just disappear in an instant, and I knew I’d be bright red with awkwardness the second I started yanking off that bob-tailed iron petticoat part of the suit.
source_text: Night approached, and with it a storm. The darkness came on fast. We must camp, of course. I found a good shelter for the demoiselle under a rock, and went off and found another for myself. But I was obliged to remain in my armor, because I could not get it off by myself and yet could not allow Alisande to help, because it would have seemed so like undressing before folk. It would not have amounted to that in reality, because I had clothes on underneath; but the prejudices of one's breeding are not gotten rid of just at a jump, and I knew that when it came to stripping off that bob-tailed iron petticoat I should be embarrassed.

text: Milo and I crawled forward on our palms and scuffed knees, stopping when we were no more than ten paces off, and craned our necks upward. There was no mistaking it: a tall, shadowed figure clad in full plate, held upright by both fists clamped around the top strand of the perimeter wire, and the sharp, acrid tang of seared flesh hung thick in the cold air around him. Poor bastard, he was long gone, had never even felt the jolt that killed him. He stood as still as a carved monument, no twitch or shift to his frame, save for the ragged feather plumes fixed to his helmet that fluttered faint and restless in the night breeze. We pulled ourselves to our feet and peered through the slits of his visor, but we couldn’t tell if we’d ever met him before—his features were swallowed entirely by the deep, shifting gloom.
source_text: We crept along on our hands and knees until we were pretty close, and then looked up. Yes, it was a man--a dim great figure in armor, standing erect, with both hands on the upper wire--and, of course, there was a smell of burning flesh. Poor fellow, dead as a door-nail, and never knew what hurt him. He stood there like a statue--no motion about him, except that his plumes swished about a little in the night wind. We rose up and looked in through the bars of his visor, but couldn't make out whether we knew him or not--features too dim and shadowed.

text: Jasper’s snappish reply was just as cutting as it had been every time prior, but it fell like a perfect folk tune on my ears right then; I could never have wished for a more lovely sound, even if the profanity woven through it was clumsily cobbled together, the harshest, most explosive curse word stuck nearly halfway through the line instead of at the very end, where such terms obviously belonged.
source_text: His answer was as sharp as before, but it was music this time; I shouldn't ever wish to hear pleasanter, though the profanity was not good, being awkwardly put together, and with the crash-word almost in the middle instead of at the end, where, of course, it ought to have been.

text: I banked three times overhead, cutting through the cool night air, before a thunderous crash split the dark. The weathered old tower blew apart in jagged chunks, hurled skyward alongside a roiling volcanic geyser of flame that turned midnight as bright as full noon, illuminating a thousand acres of huddled, crumpled people all undone by overwhelming, paralyzing terror. Mortar and chipped masonry rained down on the surrounding land for the rest of the week, or so the account claims; the actual facts of the incident would almost certainly have tempered those outlandish details.
source_text: I made about three passes in the air, and then there was an awful crash and that old tower leaped into the sky in chunks, along with a vast volcanic fountain of fire that turned night to noonday, and showed a thousand acres of human beings groveling on the ground in a general collapse of consternation. Well, it rained mortar and masonry the rest of the week. This was the report; but probably the facts would have modified it.

text:
Yeah, I sorted all that out, and had the man sent back to his home. I was dead set on punishing that executioner; not because he was a competent, thorough official good at doling out pain—doing his job well wasn’t something to hold against him, after all—but to get even with him for slapping that young woman around for no reason and hurting her just because he could. The priests were the ones who told me what he’d done, and they were properly furious, pushing hard to see him held accountable.

Annoying little episodes like this kept cropping up every so often. What I mean is, these little moments made it clear not every priest was a greedy fraud out only for himself. A lot of them—even the majority, the ones who worked right alongside regular folks on the ground—were genuine, good-hearted people, fully dedicated to easing other people’s troubles and suffering when they could.

Look, it was something I couldn’t do anything about, so I rarely wasted energy stressing over it, never more than a few minutes at a time anyway. I’ve never been the type to fixate on problems you can’t fix. But I still didn’t like it, because it was exactly the kind of thing that made people okay with keeping a state-sponsored official church around. Obviously we have to have some kind of religion, that goes without saying—but my take was, we should split it into forty independent, unaffiliated sects, so they’d keep an eye on each other and call each other out on their garbage, the way it worked back in the United States when I was there.

Concentrating power in any political machine is a bad idea, and a state church is nothing but a political machine at the end of the day. That’s what it was invented for, that’s what it’s coddled, nurtured, and protected for all this time. It’s an enemy to human liberty, and any good it does would be done far better if it was split up into small, scattered groups. That wasn’t some written law, wasn’t some divine gospel truth—it was just an opinion, my opinion, and I was just some guy, one ordinary person. So it wasn’t worth any more than the Pope’s take, for what it’s worth… and not any less, either.
source_text: Well, I arranged all that; and I had the man sent to his home. I had a great desire to rack the executioner; not because he was a good, painstaking and paingiving official,--for surely it was not to his discredit that he performed his functions well--but to pay him back for wantonly cuffing and otherwise distressing that young woman. The priests told me about this, and were generously hot to have him punished. Something of this disagreeable sort was turning up every now and then. I mean, episodes that showed that not all priests were frauds and self-seekers, but that many, even the great majority, of these that were down on the ground among the common people, were sincere and right-hearted, and devoted to the alleviation of human troubles and sufferings. Well, it was a thing which could not be helped, so I seldom fretted about it, and never many minutes at a time; it has never been my way to bother much about things which you can't cure. But I did not like it, for it was just the sort of thing to keep people reconciled to an Established Church. We _must_ have a religion --it goes without saying--but my idea is, to have it cut up into forty free sects, so that they will police each other, as had been the case in the United States in my time. Concentration of power in a political machine is bad; and an Established Church is only a political machine; it was invented for that; it is nursed, cradled, preserved for that; it is an enemy to human liberty, and does no good which it could not better do in a split-up and scattered condition. That wasn't law; it wasn't gospel: it was only an opinion--my opinion, and I was only a man, one man: so it wasn't worth any more than the pope's--or any less, for that matter.

text: I’d quietly planted a string of small auxiliary schools across the kingdom, and all of them were running smoothly, even exceeding my projections. I intended to grow this little side operation more and more as the years passed, so long as no unexpected upheaval spooked me into shutting it down. One of my most closely held secrets was my Ironspire Academy, my own private military training institute for future officers. I guarded its existence jealously, hiding it from every prying eye, and took the same care with my naval academy, which I’d founded in an isolated, little-visited coastal port. Both were thriving, a source of constant quiet satisfaction for me.
source_text: I had scattered some branch schools secretly about the kingdom, and they were doing very well. I meant to work this racket more and more, as time wore on, if nothing occurred to frighten me. One of my deepest secrets was my West Point--my military academy. I kept that most jealously out of sight; and I did the same with my naval academy which I had established at a remote seaport. Both were prospering to my satisfaction.

text: From all I’ve observed, boys are identical across every era. They respect nothing, care for no one and nothing at all. They hurl “Go up, baldhead” at the unoffending prophet making his way through the gray mists of antiquity; they sass me in the hushed holy dimness of the Middle Ages; and I watched them pull the exact same nonsense during Buchanan’s presidency— I’d know, I was there and joined right in. That prophet had his bears to settle the score with his lot of brats; I wanted to climb down and square up with mine too, but it would never work, because I’d never have been able to haul myself back up afterwards. There’s nothing I despise more than a place with no derrick to hand.
source_text: In my experience boys are the same in all ages. They don't respect anything, they don't care for anything or anybody. They say “Go up, baldhead” to the prophet going his unoffending way in the gray of antiquity; they sass me in the holy gloom of the Middle Ages; and I had seen them act the same way in Buchanan's administration; I remember, because I was there and helped. The prophet had his bears and settled with his boys; and I wanted to get down and settle with mine, but it wouldn't answer, because I couldn't have got up again. I hate a country without a derrick.

text: I never got to finish what I was saying. Their waves of gleeful, jeering mockery swallowed my words entirely. Then silence fell all at once: the Sheriffs of London, robed in their formal official garb and flanked by their subordinates, had begun stirring through the space, their purposeful commotion a clear signal the scheduled proceedings were about to start. As the hush stretched over the gathered crowd, the details of our crime were recited aloud, followed by the reading of our death warrant. Every person in attendance uncovered their heads, as a priest spoke a solemn prayer.
source_text: I got no further. They drowned me out with joyous derision. But presently there was silence; for the sheriffs of London, in their official robes, with their subordinates, began to make a stir which indicated that business was about to begin. In the hush which followed, our crime was recited, the death warrant read, then everybody uncovered while a priest uttered a prayer.

text: All right, then—that’s what’s coming for wages, plain and simple. In that far-off day, a worker will earn enough in one single week to buy that same batch of goods that costs you more than fifty weeks of work to afford right now. There are a good handful of other pretty startling changes coming, too. Brother Hale, who is it that settles, each spring, the exact wage for every type of craftsman, manual laborer, and household servant for the coming year?
source_text: “Now, then, that is what is going to happen as regards wages. In that remote day, that man will earn, with _one_ week's work, that bill of goods which it takes you upwards of _fifty_ weeks to earn now. Some other pretty surprising things are going to happen, too. Brother Dowley, who is it that determines, every spring, what the particular wage of each kind of mechanic, laborer, and servant shall be for that year?”

text: I was happy to see him show up, because just from that small cue I recognized him right away as one of my knights. That was Sir Madok de la Montaine, a huge, stocky guy whose biggest claim to fame was that he’d once come inches away from knocking Sir Launcelot right off the back of his horse. He couldn’t spend five minutes around a stranger without digging up some excuse to bring up that brag-worthy near-victory. But there was a second, equally notable detail he never volunteered out of nowhere, though he never hid it if someone asked: the reason he never actually managed to unseat Launcelot was that he got interrupted mid-strike and thrown off his own horse first, tumbling right over the hind end. This big, good-natured oaf didn’t see any real difference between the two stories, honestly. I was fond of him, though—he took his duties seriously, and he was incredibly useful to have around. He cut such an impressive figure, too, with his wide, armor-plated shoulders, that regal, lion-like tilt to his feather-crested helmet, and his huge shield emblazoned with the quirky crest I’d designed: a gauntleted fist clutching a preventive toothbrush, under the motto “Try Noyoudont.” That was the brand name of the mouthwash I was rolling out to the court at the time.
source_text: I was glad of his coming, for even by this token I knew him for knight of mine. It was Sir Madok de la Montaine, a burly great fellow whose chief distinction was that he had come within an ace of sending Sir Launcelot down over his horse-tail once. He was never long in a stranger's presence without finding some pretext or other to let out that great fact. But there was another fact of nearly the same size, which he never pushed upon anybody unasked, and yet never withheld when asked: that was, that the reason he didn't quite succeed was, that he was interrupted and sent down over horse-tail himself. This innocent vast lubber did not see any particular difference between the two facts. I liked him, for he was earnest in his work, and very valuable. And he was so fine to look at, with his broad mailed shoulders, and the grand leonine set of his plumed head, and his big shield with its quaint device of a gauntleted hand clutching a prophylactic tooth-brush, with motto: “Try Noyoudont.” This was a tooth-wash that I was introducing.

text: I ordered the pair rounded up and sent back to their kin. The queen took great displeasure at the call. Not that she held any personal stake in the matter, but she considered it a deliberate slight to Sir Rian the Merciless. All the same, I assured her that if he found the arrangement unbearable, I’d tend to him well enough that he’d learn to stomach it.
source_text: I had them taken out and sent to their friends. The queen did not like it much. Not that she felt any personal interest in the matter, but she thought it disrespectful to Sir Breuse Sance Pite. However, I assured her that if he found he couldn't stand it I would fix him so that he could.

text: Once prayers concluded, we sat down to dinner in a vast banqueting hall lit by hundreds of tallow wicks, every detail as grand, opulent, and unpolishedly magnificent as befitted the royal station of our hosts. At the hall’s upper end, raised on a dais, stood the table reserved for the king, queen, and their son, Prince Uwaine. Stretching the full length of the hall down from this platform was the common table, set directly on the floor. At its upper end, above the salt cellar, sat visiting nobles and their adult family members of both sexes—effectively the full resident court, sixty-one people total. Below the salt were seated minor household officers and their senior subordinates: all told, one hundred and eighteen seated diners, with roughly the same number of liveried servants standing behind their chairs or tending to various serving duties. It was a thoroughly impressive sight. Up in a gallery, a band armed with cymbals, horns, harps, and other cacophonous nuisances kicked off the proceedings with what sounded like a rough first draft, or the original agonized wail, of the tune later centuries would know as “In the Sweet Bye and Bye.” The piece was brand new, and clearly ought to have been rehearsed a few more times. For some unstated reason, the queen had the composer hanged directly after dinner.
source_text: After prayers we had dinner in a great banqueting hall which was lighted by hundreds of grease-jets, and everything was as fine and lavish and rudely splendid as might become the royal degree of the hosts. At the head of the hall, on a dais, was the table of the king, queen, and their son, Prince Uwaine. Stretching down the hall from this, was the general table, on the floor. At this, above the salt, sat the visiting nobles and the grown members of their families, of both sexes,--the resident Court, in effect--sixty-one persons; below the salt sat minor officers of the household, with their principal subordinates: altogether a hundred and eighteen persons sitting, and about as many liveried servants standing behind their chairs, or serving in one capacity or another. It was a very fine show. In a gallery a band with cymbals, horns, harps, and other horrors, opened the proceedings with what seemed to be the crude first-draft or original agony of the wail known to later centuries as “In the Sweet Bye and Bye.” It was new, and ought to have been rehearsed a little more. For some reason or other the queen had the composer hanged, after dinner.

text: I shooed the three farmhands off my property first, then lifted the rusted latch of the pigpen gate and beckoned Lila over. She came at once, no slow meander, but surging forward fast as a grassfire tearing across sunbaked prairie. When I watched her fling herself at the hogs, bright tears of joy streaming down her freckled cheeks, press them tight to her breast, kiss their fuzzy snouts, stroke their bristled sides, and murmur lofty, royal names to them as if they were long-lost princes, I burned with shame for her, and for the whole of the human race.
source_text: I sent the three men away, and then opened the sty gate and beckoned Sandy to come--which she did; and not leisurely, but with the rush of a prairie fire. And when I saw her fling herself upon those hogs, with tears of joy running down her cheeks, and strain them to her heart, and kiss them, and caress them, and call them reverently by grand princely names, I was ashamed of her, ashamed of the human race.

text: I felt a deep, quiet pride in the work I’d built up so far. Tucked away in dozens of unmarked, out-of-the-way hollows and farmsteads across the countryside, I’d seeded the first shoots of every trade I planned to scale—tiny seedlings that would one day bloom into sprawling manufacturing complexes, the steel and smelted backbone of the new civilization I intended to raise. I’d gathered the sharpest, most curious young thinkers I could locate in these spaces, and kept roving scouts crisscrossing the region constantly to dig up more promising talent. I was molding throngs of uneducated, eager villagers into specialists—masters of every craft, every field of applied and theoretical science I could teach them. These small, growing workshops ran smooth and unimpeded in their remote rural hiding spots, for no one was allowed to cross their thresholds without a signed, personal pass from me—I’d long known the established Church would crush the whole project if it caught even a whiff of what we were building.
source_text: I was pretty well satisfied with what I had already accomplished. In various quiet nooks and corners I had the beginnings of all sorts of industries under way--nuclei of future vast factories, the iron and steel missionaries of my future civilization. In these were gathered together the brightest young minds I could find, and I kept agents out raking the country for more, all the time. I was training a crowd of ignorant folk into experts--experts in every sort of handiwork and scientific calling. These nurseries of mine went smoothly and privately along undisturbed in their obscure country retreats, for nobody was allowed to come into their precincts without a special permit--for I was afraid of the Church.

text: I showed up at the weathered stone well bright and early the next morning. Kaelen was already there, chanting incantations as busily as a beaver piling dam sticks, but not a single drop of moisture rose from the dry basin. His mood was thoroughly sour; every time I dropped a gentle hint that the contract he’d signed on for might be a little too hefty for an inexperienced spellcaster, he let loose a volley of curses coarse enough to match a bishop – a French Regency bishop, that is, the sort far fonder of wine and debauchery than pious prayer.
source_text: I was at the well next day betimes. Merlin was there, enchanting away like a beaver, but not raising the moisture. He was not in a pleasant humor; and every time I hinted that perhaps this contract was a shade too hefty for a novice he unlimbered his tongue and cursed like a bishop--French bishop of the Regency days, I mean.

text: I had Thalyn carried home on a splintered oak shutter. He’d crumpled and collapsed like a mudslide the second I spoke that dread name, and hadn’t regained his senses once in the hours after. He’d never heard that name before—nor had I, for that matter—but to him it was unmistakeably correct. Any random string of guttural syllables would have struck him as just as authentic. He confessed later that the spirit’s own birth mother couldn’t have spoken the name with more trueness than I’d managed that day. He never could fathom how I’d walked away unharmed from uttering it, and I never gave him the answer. Only foolhardy fledgling mages give away a trade secret that valuable. Thalyn poured three full months of work into weaving enchantments to unearth the hidden trick of speaking that name and living through it. But he never cracked it.
source_text: I sent Merlin home on a shutter. He had caved in and gone down like a landslide when I pronounced that fearful name, and had never come to since. He never had heard that name before,--neither had I--but to him it was the right one. Any jumble would have been the right one. He admitted, afterward, that that spirit's own mother could not have pronounced that name better than I did. He never could understand how I survived it, and I didn't tell him. It is only young magicians that give away a secret like that. Merlin spent three months working enchantments to try to find out the deep trick of how to pronounce that name and outlive it. But he didn't arrive.

text: The smoke he raised this time blotted out light across the entire region, and must have left the local hermits thoroughly uncomfortable— the wind was blowing straight toward their dwellings, rolling over their cave dens in thick, billowing banks of fog. He matched the churning smoke with a nonstop torrent of incantations, twisting his body into sharp, unnatural shapes and sawing at the empty air with his hands in a truly outlandish display. After twenty minutes had passed, he crumpled to the ground panting, all but drained of energy. Just then the abbot arrived, accompanied by several hundred monks and nuns, followed by a huge crowd of pilgrims and what seemed like acres of foundlings, all lured by the enormous, unearthly smoke, all thrumming with giddy excitement. The abbot pressed him anxiously for news of how the working had gone. Merlin said:
source_text: He raised a smoke this time that darkened all the region, and must have made matters uncomfortable for the hermits, for the wind was their way, and it rolled down over their dens in a dense and billowy fog. He poured out volumes of speech to match, and contorted his body and sawed the air with his hands in a most extraordinary way. At the end of twenty minutes he dropped down panting, and about exhausted. Now arrived the abbot and several hundred monks and nuns, and behind them a multitude of pilgrims and a couple of acres of foundlings, all drawn by the prodigious smoke, and all in a grand state of excitement. The abbot inquired anxiously for results. Merlin said:

text: Go on, guess what our so-called master did next? We’d huddled around that terrified, helpless woman to shield her, and that’s when he spotted his chance. He told the mob they could either burn her right there on the spot, or they wouldn’t get their hands on her at all. Can you believe that? The crowd agreed immediately. They chained her to a rough wooden post, hauled over armfuls of dry kindling and stacked it high around her feet, then lit the flame even as she screamed, begged for mercy, and squeezed her two small daughters as tight as she could against her chest. And that monster who owned us, who cared about nothing but turning a profit, herded us all into a circle around the burning stake and used that very same fire that killed that innocent, defenseless mother to thaw us out, get us back on our feet, and make sure we still held commercial value. That’s the kind of man we answered to. I made a note of exactly who he really was, that day. Not long after, a brutal snowstorm wiped out nine of his flock. For weeks afterward, he was even more vicious to the rest of us, spitting mad over the money he’d lost.
source_text: Well, now, what do you suppose our master did? When we closed around this poor creature to shelter her, he saw his chance. He said, burn her here, or they shouldn't have her at all. Imagine that! They were willing. They fastened her to a post; they brought wood and piled it about her; they applied the torch while she shrieked and pleaded and strained her two young daughters to her breast; and our brute, with a heart solely for business, lashed us into position about the stake and warmed us into life and commercial value by the same fire which took away the innocent life of that poor harmless mother. That was the sort of master we had. I took _his_ number. That snow-storm cost him nine of his flock; and he was more brutal to us than ever, after that, for many days together, he was so enraged over his loss.

text: Our side of the field held only two tents: one for my own use, the other for my household servants. When the prearranged hour arrived, the king gave a small, deliberate signal, and the heralds clad in their bright embroidered tabards stepped forward to issue the official proclamation, calling out the names of the combatants and laying out the root of their dispute. A heavy, expectant hush hung for a beat, before a clear, ringing bugle blast split the air—the prearranged signal for us to step out into view. The entire assembled multitude drew a collective sharp breath, and bright, eager curiosity sparked across every onlooker’s face.
source_text: Down at our end there were but two tents; one for me, and another for my servants. At the appointed hour the king made a sign, and the heralds, in their tabards, appeared and made proclamation, naming the combatants and stating the cause of quarrel. There was a pause, then a ringing bugle-blast, which was the signal for us to come forth. All the multitude caught their breath, and an eager curiosity flashed into every face.

text: A sodden, gut-wrenching trek. A desolate hush hung over every stretch of ground, and it seeped even into the heart of Veyra, the capital that had once thrummed with noise at all hours. All wheeled traffic had ground to a halt; no one laughed or called out greetings, or clustered in groups, or even walked two abreast. They drifted listlessly through the streets, each person entirely alone, gaze fixed on the dirt at their feet, grief and cold dread coiled tight in their chests. The city’s ancient Grey Spire bore fresh shrapnel gouges and smoke streaks across its weathered stone face. Truly, unthinkable upheaval had unfolded in the days past.
source_text: A miserable journey. A desolate silence everywhere. Even in London itself. Traffic had ceased; men did not talk or laugh, or go in groups, or even in couples; they moved aimlessly about, each man by himself, with his head down, and woe and terror at his heart. The Tower showed recent war-scars. Verily, much had been happening.

text: I’d made the acquaintance of a number of skilled master mechanics, but far and away the most compelling of the bunch was the local blacksmith, Dowley. He was sharp, energetic, and a fast, witty conversationalist, with two fully qualified journeymen and three apprentices on his payroll, and his shop was swamped with nonstop work. Truth be told, he was raking in money faster than he could count it, and the whole town held him in high regard. Marco was incredibly proud to count someone of Dowley’s standing as a friend. He’d brought me to the smithy on the pretense of showing me the large operation that purchased such a huge share of his charcoal, but his real goal was to show off how casual, almost buddy-buddy, he was with this prominent local figure. Dowley and I hit it off immediately; I’d supervised plenty of sharp, stand-up guys just like him back when I worked at the Colt Arms Factory. I knew I wanted to spend more time with him, so I invited him to come over to Marco’s place for Sunday dinner with the two of us. Marco looked horrified, and froze holding his breath the second I extended the invite; when the important man actually said yes, Marco was so overwhelmed with gratitude that he nearly forgot to be shocked that someone as high-up as Dowley would deign to join us.
source_text: I got to know several master mechanics, but about the most interesting fellow among them was the blacksmith, Dowley. He was a live man and a brisk talker, and had two journeymen and three apprentices, and was doing a raging business. In fact, he was getting rich, hand over fist, and was vastly respected. Marco was very proud of having such a man for a friend. He had taken me there ostensibly to let me see the big establishment which bought so much of his charcoal, but really to let me see what easy and almost familiar terms he was on with this great man. Dowley and I fraternized at once; I had had just such picked men, splendid fellows, under me in the Colt Arms Factory. I was bound to see more of him, so I invited him to come out to Marco's Sunday, and dine with us. Marco was appalled, and held his breath; and when the grandee accepted, he was so grateful that he almost forgot to be astonished at the condescension.

text: Good gracious, what trivial, insignificant offenses landed most of those forty-seven men and women locked up in that place! In fact, several were held on no concrete charges at all, locked away solely to satisfy someone’s petty spite—and far more often than not, it was not even the queen’s spite, but that of a personal acquaintance with a grudge. The most recently arrived inmate’s only “crime” was an offhand comment he’d made. He’d argued that all people were fundamentally the same, and that every person was just as worthy as the next, if you set their fine clothing aside. If you stripped every person in the kingdom bare and sent a total stranger walking through the crowd, he’d claimed, that stranger wouldn’t be able to tell the king apart from a fake traveling physician, or a duke from a common hotel desk clerk. It was clear right away this was a man whose mind hadn’t been turned to useless, compliant slop by all the foolish, hierarchical conditioning everyone else was subjected to. I had him released immediately and assigned him to work at the Factory.
source_text: Dear me, for what trifling offenses the most of those forty-seven men and women were shut up there! Indeed, some were there for no distinct offense at all, but only to gratify somebody's spite; and not always the queen's by any means, but a friend's. The newest prisoner's crime was a mere remark which he had made. He said he believed that men were about all alike, and one man as good as another, barring clothes. He said he believed that if you were to strip the nation naked and send a stranger through the crowd, he couldn't tell the king from a quack doctor, nor a duke from a hotel clerk. Apparently here was a man whose brains had not been reduced to an ineffectual mush by idiotic training. I set him loose and sent him to the Factory.

text: The settlement held a scattering of thick, windowless stone longhouses half-buried in a tangle of thatch-roofed huts; its streets were little more than rutted, unpaved winding lanes, where packs of scruffy dogs and unclothed toddlers darted through the sunlight raising raucous shouts. Swine wandered freely snuffling at the dirt, and one fat sow wallowed in a stinking mud pit right in the center of the main thoroughfare, grunting softly as her litter nursed at her sides. A distant blare of brass military fanfare cut through the din first, growing louder and clearer by the second, until a stately procession of riders coiled into view, resplendent with feathered helm crests, glinting steel plate, rippling pennants, richly embroidered tunic hems, beaded horse trappings, and gilded lance tips. It picked its proud path through muck and swine, barefoot brats, yapping dogs, and rickety huts, and we fell in step right behind its trailing edge. We trailed after it down one snaking lane then the next, climbing steadily, ever upward, until we reached the wind-scoured hilltop where the sprawling fortress loomed. A volley of answering bugle calls rang out, followed by a shouted parley from the battlements, where guards in chainmail and steel helmets paced back and forth with halberds slung over their shoulders, beneath snapping banners emblazoned with the rough, jagged silhouette of a dragon. Then the massive iron-bound gates swung wide, the drawbridge rumbled down to span the moat, and the front of the cavalcade trotted forward beneath the shadowed, scowling stone arches. We followed close behind, and moments later stepped into a vast flagstone courtyard, its four sides lined with towers and turrets that jutted high into the crisp blue sky. All around us, riders were dismounting, calling out warm greetings and going through formal welcomes, servants darting to and fro, a bright kaleidoscope of shifting, overlapping colors, and a bright, thrumming buzz of chatter, clatter, and joyful, lively chaos.
source_text: In the town were some substantial windowless houses of stone scattered among a wilderness of thatched cabins; the streets were mere crooked alleys, and unpaved; troops of dogs and nude children played in the sun and made life and noise; hogs roamed and rooted contentedly about, and one of them lay in a reeking wallow in the middle of the main thoroughfare and suckled her family. Presently there was a distant blare of military music; it came nearer, still nearer, and soon a noble cavalcade wound into view, glorious with plumed helmets and flashing mail and flaunting banners and rich doublets and horse-cloths and gilded spearheads; and through the muck and swine, and naked brats, and joyous dogs, and shabby huts, it took its gallant way, and in its wake we followed. Followed through one winding alley and then another,--and climbing, always climbing--till at last we gained the breezy height where the huge castle stood. There was an exchange of bugle blasts; then a parley from the walls, where men-at-arms, in hauberk and morion, marched back and forth with halberd at shoulder under flapping banners with the rude figure of a dragon displayed upon them; and then the great gates were flung open, the drawbridge was lowered, and the head of the cavalcade swept forward under the frowning arches; and we, following, soon found ourselves in a great paved court, with towers and turrets stretching up into the blue air on all the four sides; and all about us the dismount was going on, and much greeting and ceremony, and running to and fro, and a gay display of moving and intermingling colors, and an altogether pleasant stir and noise and confusion.

text: We tramped across the corral and settled side by side between the two inner fence lines. The cave’s faint, hazy glow had left our vision slightly muddled, but our focus immediately began to recalibrate, quickly adjusting to the dim conditions around us. Moments before we’d had to feel our way forward blindly, but now we could just make out the stark outline of each fence post. We’d been conversing in quiet whispers when Clarence abruptly broke off mid-thought and said:
source_text: We crossed the corral and lay down together between the two inside fences. Even the dim light of the cave had disordered our eyesight somewhat, but the focus straightway began to regulate itself and soon it was adjusted for present circumstances. We had had to feel our way before, but we could make out to see the fence posts now. We started a whispered conversation, but suddenly Clarence broke off and said:

text: He set the girl down gently beside her mother, who flooded her with soft, adoring words and tender touches, her heart so brimming with affection it could barely be contained. The only sign the child was taking any of it in was a faint, flickering glimmer of response in her eyes, nothing more. The mother hovered over her, kissing her cheeks, stroking her hair, pleading with her to say even a single word, but the girl’s lips only trembled, no sound slipping past them. I snatched my liquor flask out of my knapsack, but the woman stopped me short, saying:
source_text: He laid the girl down by her mother, who poured out endearments and caresses from an overflowing heart, and one could detect a flickering faint light of response in the child's eyes, but that was all. The mother hung over her, kissing her, petting her, and imploring her to speak, but the lips only moved and no sound came. I snatched my liquor flask from my knapsack, but the woman forbade me, and said:

text: Things played out almost exactly how I’d guessed they would. The so-called “fountain” was just a regular, unexceptional well, dug the same mundane way any well is, lined with stone the same standard, unremarkable way. There was nothing even slightly miraculous about it. Even the tall tale that had built up its famous reputation wasn’t some clever, impossible fabrication; I could’ve spun the exact same lie myself, with one hand tied behind my back. The well was tucked in a dark, enclosed chamber at the very center of a cut-stone chapel, its walls strung with pious religious paintings so shoddily executed even a cheap mass-produced chromolithograph would think it looked high-quality next to them. The paintings claimed to commemorate historical healing miracles the well’s waters had performed when no human was around to see them. That is, no human but angels, of course; those beings are always on hand the second a miracle’s about to go down, probably just so they can get included in the artwork afterward. Angels are as eager for that kind of spotlight as a local fire department showing up for a photo op at a small fire—just look through any collection of old master works and you’ll see what I’m talking about.
source_text: Matters were about as I expected to find them. The “fountain” was an ordinary well, it had been dug in the ordinary way, and stoned up in the ordinary way. There was no miracle about it. Even the lie that had created its reputation was not miraculous; I could have told it myself, with one hand tied behind me. The well was in a dark chamber which stood in the center of a cut-stone chapel, whose walls were hung with pious pictures of a workmanship that would have made a chromo feel good; pictures historically commemorative of curative miracles which had been achieved by the waters when nobody was looking. That is, nobody but angels; they are always on deck when there is a miracle to the fore--so as to get put in the picture, perhaps. Angels are as fond of that as a fire company; look at the old masters.

text: I found myself wondering where they’d picked up that particular term, but I had no time to puzzle over linguistic trivialities right then— the whole hive of wandering knights was abuzz with energy, and my prospects for new work could not have been rosier. The instant my lasso was freed and Sir Sagramor had been escorted back to his tent, I reeled in the slack line, took my position, and began swinging the loop over my head once more. I knew I would have cause to use it the second they selected a successor to Sir Sagramor, and with so many eager candidates clamoring for the spot, that choice would not take long. Sure enough, they named their next challenger right away: Sir Hervis de Revel.
source_text: I wondered where they got the word, but there was no time to cipher on philological matters, because the whole knight-errantry hive was just humming now, and my prospect for trade couldn't have been better. The moment my lasso was released and Sir Sagramor had been assisted to his tent, I hauled in the slack, took my station and began to swing my loop around my head again. I was sure to have use for it as soon as they could elect a successor for Sir Sagramor, and that couldn't take long where there were so many hungry candidates. Indeed, they elected one straight off --Sir Hervis de Revel.

text: Can you believe the massive coup I just pulled off? I couldn’t stop turning it over in my head, obsessing over every last detail the way someone who’s just struck a gushing oil well does. Nothing I’d ever accomplished before came even close to measuring up, unless you counted that old story of Joseph’s; even his feat only edged near mine, it never fully matched it, not really. It only makes sense, after all: all Joseph’s brilliant financial maneuvering only benefited the Pharaoh he served, so the general public must have viewed him with a whole lot of resentment. I, by contrast, had done every single member of my community a massive favor by sparing the sun, so everyone loved me for it.
source_text: What a jump I had made! I couldn't keep from thinking about it, and contemplating it, just as one does who has struck oil. There was nothing back of me that could approach it, unless it might be Joseph's case; and Joseph's only approached it, it didn't equal it, quite. For it stands to reason that as Joseph's splendid financial ingenuities advantaged nobody but the king, the general public must have regarded him with a good deal of disfavor, whereas I had done my entire public a kindness in sparing the sun, and was popular by reason of it.

text: Readers of the *Crestwood Courier* will be sorry to learn that the handsome, widely beloved Lord Kaelan of Ayrshire, who during his four-week stay at the Stag and Trout right here in town has won every heart with his refined bearing and sharp, charming conversation, sets off for his family estate today. Do drop in again soon, Kael!
source_text: The readers of the Hosannah will re- gret to learn that the hadndsome and popular Sir Charolais of Gaul, who dur- ing his four weeks' stay at the Bull and Halibut, this city, has won every heart by his polished manners and elegant cPnversation, will pUll out to-day for home. Give us another call, Charley!

text: I’d been curious to lay eyes on the man ever since I’d heard the stories of what had befallen him. His real age was only thirty-four, but his worn, gaunt face made him look twice that, every hard year etched deep into the lines of his skin. He was perched on a roughly hewn block of limestone, chin tucked to his chest, forearms slung loose over his bent knees, matted gray-streaked hair falling in a ragged curtain across his features as he mumbled half-audible words to no one but himself. After a moment he lifted his chin, dragging his gaze across our little group slow and empty, squinting in pain as the guttering torchlight seared his sun-starved eyes, then let his head fall forward again, resuming his quiet rambling as if we’d never been there at all. A handful of silent, heartbreakingly telling artifacts sat scattered near his feet. Old, silvery smooth scar tissue crisscrossed his wrists and ankles, and bolted tight to the limestone block he sat on was a heavy iron chain strung with cuffs and shackles; the whole contraption lay unused on the dirt, its surface crusted through with decades of rust. You don’t need iron to hold a man when every last scrap of his fight and will has already been drained away.
source_text: I wanted to see the man, after hearing all this. He was thirty-four years old, and looked sixty. He sat upon a squared block of stone, with his head bent down, his forearms resting on his knees, his long hair hanging like a fringe before his face, and he was muttering to himself. He raised his chin and looked us slowly over, in a listless dull way, blinking with the distress of the torchlight, then dropped his head and fell to muttering again and took no further notice of us. There were some pathetically suggestive dumb witnesses present. On his wrists and ankles were cicatrices, old smooth scars, and fastened to the stone on which he sat was a chain with manacles and fetters attached; but this apparatus lay idle on the ground, and was thick with rust. Chains cease to be needed after the spirit has gone out of a prisoner.

text: I could neither subject the executioner to torture nor brush off the priests’ fully justified grievances. The man had to be punished one way or another, so I stripped him of his official post and named him leader of the new musical band we were preparing to form. He begged profusely, insisting he did not know how to play—a plausible enough excuse at first glance, but far too thin to hold water; there was not a single musician in the whole country who could, anyway.
source_text: Well, I couldn't rack the executioner, neither would I overlook the just complaint of the priests. The man must be punished somehow or other, so I degraded him from his office and made him leader of the band--the new one that was to be started. He begged hard, and said he couldn't play--a plausible excuse, but too thin; there wasn't a musician in the country that could.

text: “No, no, Dad, that won’t work, just like the locals around here say. If we forced him to go along against his will, he’d lace that well with a nasty, malicious enchantment that’d block me from using it until I worked out its secret. That could easily take a full month. I could rig up one of my own small spells, the one I call the telephone, and he’d never figure out how it works even if he had a hundred years to try. You get what I’m saying, right? He could hold me up for an entire month. Do you really want to risk wasting a whole month when we’re stuck in a brutal dry spell like this?”
source_text: “No-no, Father, it skills not, as these people say. If he were persuaded against his will, he would load that well with a malicious enchantment which would balk me until I found out its secret. It might take a month. I could set up a little enchantment of mine which I call the telephone, and he could not find out its secret in a hundred years. Yes, you perceive, he might block me for a month. Would you like to risk a month in a dry time like this?”

text: Hmm, what was my best move here? Rushing into anything was definitely out of the question. I needed to conjure up some small distraction, something to occupy my hands while I sorted through my options, and give these dazed lads time to shake off their stupor. Luka was perched on the rough wooden stool beside me, frozen mid-motion as he’d been trying to figure out his wind-up pellet shooter—locked stiff in the exact pose he’d struck the second my heavy iron post driver slammed into the earth, the little toy still clamped in his unresponding fingers. So I gently pried it from his grip and offered to walk him through how it worked. As if it was some great secret! It was nothing more than a cheap, clunky trinket, really—but it might as well have been magic, to folks from that corner of the world, in that far simpler time.
source_text: Well, what had I better do? Nothing in a hurry, sure. I must get up a diversion; anything to employ me while I could think, and while these poor fellows could have a chance to come to life again. There sat Marco, petrified in the act of trying to get the hang of his miller-gun--turned to stone, just in the attitude he was in when my pile-driver fell, the toy still gripped in his unconscious fingers. So I took it from him and proposed to explain its mystery. Mystery! a simple little thing like that; and yet it was mysterious enough, for that race and that age.

text: “No—she feels no pain; this is the kinder state. To rouse her would drag her back to a life of misery, and no one as good and gentle as you would ever inflict that cruel harm on her. Think on it—what does she have left to live for? Her brothers are dead, her father is gone, her mother is dying, the Church’s curse hangs heavy over her, and not a soul would shelter her or show her kindness even if she lay perishing by the roadside. She is utterly desolate. I never asked you, kind heart, if her sister still lives upstairs, you see; I had no need. If she were alive, you would have gone back to her long ago, and never left this poor, forsaken wretch abandoned to her fate—”
source_text: “No--she does not suffer; it is better so. It might bring her back to life. None that be so good and kind as ye are would do her that cruel hurt. For look you--what is left to live for? Her brothers are gone, her father is gone, her mother goeth, the Church's curse is upon her, and none may shelter or befriend her even though she lay perishing in the road. She is desolate. I have not asked you, good heart, if her sister be still on live, here overhead; I had no need; ye had gone back, else, and not left the poor thing forsaken--”

text: I fumbled my way back to the corral by this point; I’d seen all I needed to see. I headed for the control platform and gave the signal to run current through the two inner perimeter fences. After that I stepped inside the cave, and found everything was as it should be there—no one was up except the on-duty watch team. I shook Clarence awake and told him the big trench was packed full of men, and I was sure every last knight was mobilizing to come for us all at once. My guess was that the minute dawn drew near, those thousands of hidden fighters tucked in the trench would pour over the embankment to launch their attack, with the rest of their army right on their heels.
source_text: I groped my way back to the corral now; I had seen enough. I went to the platform and signaled to turn the current on to the two inner fences. Then I went into the cave, and found everything satisfactory there--nobody awake but the working-watch. I woke Clarence and told him the great ditch was filling up with men, and that I believed all the knights were coming for us in a body. It was my notion that as soon as dawn approached we could expect the ditch's ambuscaded thousands to swarm up over the embankment and make an assault, and be followed immediately by the rest of their army.

text: Aye, that was exactly how it unfolded. The king had sentenced the queen to the stake, intending to purify her with flame. Launcelot and his knights came to her rescue, but in the fray they slew a number of our old, cherished friends—truly, some of the finest men we ever knew: namely, Sir Belias le Orgulous, Sir Segwarides, Sir Griflet le Fils de Dieu, Sir Brandiles, Sir Aglovale—
source_text: “Yes--that was the way of it. The king sent the queen to the stake, proposing to purify her with fire. Launcelot and his knights rescued her, and in doing it slew certain good old friends of yours and mine--in fact, some of the best we ever had; to wit, Sir Belias le Orgulous, Sir Segwarides, Sir Griflet le Fils de Dieu, Sir Brandiles, Sir Aglovale--”

text:
Let us now turn to Sir Marhaus, who traveled southward in the company of the thirty-year-old damsel. By chance, dusk overtook them as they ventured into a thick, ancient forest, and they pressed on along a shadowed, rutted track until at last they came upon the manor holding of the Duke of the South Marches, where they begged lodging for the night. At dawn the next day, the duke sent a messenger to Sir Marhaus, bidding him prepare for combat. Sir Marhaus rose, donned his full armor, heard mass said for his protection, ate his morning meal, and mounted his steed in the castle courtyard, the prearranged site of the fight.

The duke was already mounted, fully clad in armor, with his six sons at his side, each gripping a heavy lance in his hand. The first charge came from the duke and his two eldest sons, all three of whom shattered their lances against Sir Marhaus’s shield and armor; Sir Marhaus, for his part, kept his own lance raised, landing no blow against any of them. Next the four younger sons advanced two at a time, each pair shattering their lances against him just as their elders had. Still Sir Marhaus did not move to strike any of them.

At last Sir Marhaus lowered his lance and spurred his horse toward the duke, striking him so hard that man and mount crashed to the ground. He dealt the same blow to each of the six sons in turn. Sir Marhaus dismounted, and bade the duke yield or face immediate death. A handful of the sons had regained their feet, and moved to rush Sir Marhaus. Sir Marhaus turned to the fallen duke. “Call your sons off,” he said, “or I will strike down every last one of you.” When the duke saw there was no way to avoid death if they fought on, he called out to his sons, ordering them to surrender to Sir Marhaus. All six knelt and held out the pommels of their swords to the knight in token of surrender, which he accepted. They helped their father to his feet, and all of them swore jointly that they would never again take up arms against King Arthur, and that both the duke and his sons would appear at the coming Whitsun court to place themselves at the king’s grace.
source_text: “Now turn we unto Sir Marhaus that rode with the damsel of thirty winter of age southward. And so they came into a deep forest, and by fortune they were nighted, and rode along in a deep way, and at the last they came into a courtelage where abode the duke of South Marches, and there they asked harbour. And on the morn the duke sent unto Sir Marhaus, and bad him make him ready. And so Sir Marhaus arose and armed him, and there was a mass sung afore him, and he brake his fast, and so mounted on horseback in the court of the castle, there they should do the battle. So there was the duke already on horseback, clean armed, and his six sons by him, and every each had a spear in his hand, and so they encountered, whereas the duke and his two sons brake their spears upon him, but Sir Marhaus held up his spear and touched none of them. Then came the four sons by couples, and two of them brake their spears, and so did the other two. And all this while Sir Marhaus touched them not. Then Sir Marhaus ran to the duke, and smote him with his spear that horse and man fell to the earth. And so he served his sons. And then Sir Marhaus alight down, and bad the duke yield him or else he would slay him. And then some of his sons recovered, and would have set upon Sir Marhaus. Then Sir Marhaus said to the duke, Cease thy sons, or else I will do the uttermost to you all. When the duke saw he might not escape the death, he cried to his sons, and charged them to yield them to Sir Marhaus. And they kneeled all down and put the pommels of their swords to the knight, and so he received them. And then they holp up their father, and so by their common assent promised unto Sir Marhaus never to be foes unto King Arthur, and thereupon at Whitsuntide after, to come he and his sons, and put them in the king's grace.*

text: Good grief, I was reeling; half from the sheer unanticipated idiocy of his take, half from the way his entire cohort plainly lined up behind him, subscribed to that nonsense – if you could even dignify it as a coherent thought. My stance had been straightforward, uncomplicated from the start; how was there any way to make it clearer than I already had? Even so, I had to give it another go:
source_text: Well, I was stunned; partly with this unlooked-for stupidity on his part, and partly because his fellows so manifestly sided with him and were of his mind--if you might call it mind. My position was simple enough, plain enough; how could it ever be simplified more? However, I must try:

text: All told, there’s no wiser move than pressing a gained advantage without delay. The ironclad realm knights were routed for the moment, but if I wanted to keep them down, I’d need to cripple them entirely—nothing less would suffice. You have to understand, I’d been bluffing through that last skirmish in the field; they’d certainly piece that truth together if I gave them even the smallest opening. So I couldn’t afford to grant them a single spare second—and I didn’t.
source_text: Well, it is always a good plan to follow up an advantage promptly. The knights were temporarily down, but if I would keep them so I must just simply paralyze them--nothing short of that would answer. You see, I was “bluffing” that last time in the field; it would be natural for them to work around to that conclusion, if I gave them a chance. So I must not give them time; and I didn't.

text: I’d landed a new gig, and the work was pouring in nonstop. The king was ravenous to learn every single thing that would play out over the next thirteen hundred years, acting like he’d be around to see all of it personally. From that point on, I cranked out so many predictions I practically gave myself premature baldness just trying to keep up with his demands. I’ve pulled my share of stupid, impulsive stunts in my time, but passing myself off as a prophet was easily the dumbest call I ever made. That said, it wasn’t all bad—there were a few upsides to the gig. You don’t need a single working brain cell to be a prophet. Don’t get me wrong, smarts are handy for regular day-to-day stuff, but they’re totally useless when it comes to the job itself. It’s literally the most low-effort, relaxing profession on the planet. When that so-called prophetic spirit hits you, all you have to do is tuck your intellect somewhere cool to take a break, slacken your jaw and leave it to its own devices; it’ll run on autopilot, and whatever comes out the other end is prophecy.
source_text: I had a new trade now, and plenty of business in it. The king was as hungry to find out everything that was going to happen during the next thirteen centuries as if he were expecting to live in them. From that time out, I prophesied myself bald-headed trying to supply the demand. I have done some indiscreet things in my day, but this thing of playing myself for a prophet was the worst. Still, it had its ameliorations. A prophet doesn't have to have any brains. They are good to have, of course, for the ordinary exigencies of life, but they are no use in professional work. It is the restfulest vocation there is. When the spirit of prophecy comes upon you, you merely take your intellect and lay it off in a cool place for a rest, and unship your jaw and leave it alone; it will work itself: the result is prophecy.

text: Before the midday liturgy had drawn to a close, we were already huddled at the old wellhead; there was still a great deal of work left to finish, and I was dead set on pulling off the miracle before midnight for strictly commercial reasons: while a wonder performed for the parish on a regular weekday brings in a respectable haul, it nets six times that sum if you time it to land right on a Sunday. In nine hours, the water had climbed back to its customary resting height–that is to say, it sat a full twenty-three feet below the well’s chipped stone rim. We lowered in a compact cast-iron pump, one of the first units rolled off the line at my factory outside the provincial capital; we drilled through the thick granite catch basin built against the outer wall of the well’s stone vault, and fed in a length of lead piping long enough to run all the way to the chapel entrance and stick a few inches past the doorsill, where the gushing stream would be visible to the two hundred and fifty acres of people I planned to have gathered on the broad flat meadow below this small sacred knoll when the right moment arrived.
source_text: Before the noon mass was over, we were at the well again; for there was a deal to do yet, and I was determined to spring the miracle before midnight, for business reasons: for whereas a miracle worked for the Church on a week-day is worth a good deal, it is worth six times as much if you get it in on a Sunday. In nine hours the water had risen to its customary level--that is to say, it was within twenty-three feet of the top. We put in a little iron pump, one of the first turned out by my works near the capital; we bored into a stone reservoir which stood against the outer wall of the well-chamber and inserted a section of lead pipe that was long enough to reach to the door of the chapel and project beyond the threshold, where the gushing water would be visible to the two hundred and fifty acres of people I was intending should be present on the flat plain in front of this little holy hillock at the proper time.

text: All told, there’s no profit in clinging to sour what-ifs, or frittering hours on empty, grinding anxiety—one ought to buckle down to the work at hand and suss out what options are left. Every lie holds a grain of truth tucked beneath its layers of nonsense, and my job right now was to pick that grain free from all the chaff here. I sent for the maid, and she arrived a few minutes later. She was a handsome enough young thing, mild-mannered and shy, but if small tells counted for anything, she had barely more wits about her than a lady’s pendant watch that’s run down. I leaned forward and said:
source_text: Well, one must make the best of things, and not waste time with useless fretting, but get down to business and see what can be done. In all lies there is wheat among the chaff; I must get at the wheat in this case: so I sent for the girl and she came. She was a comely enough creature, and soft and modest, but, if signs went for anything, she didn't know as much as a lady's watch. I said:

text: Breakfast was served and waiting. The moment we finished the meal, I held up Sir Ozana’s hat to show him the note I had scrawled on its inner lining: “Chemical Department, Laboratory Extension, Section G. Pxxp. Send two of the first size, two of No. 3, and six of No. 4, together with the appropriate complementary components—and two of my trained assistants.” And I said:
source_text: Breakfast was ready. As soon as it was over I showed to Sir Ozana these words which I had written on the inside of his hat: “Chemical Department, Laboratory extension, Section G. Pxxp. Send two of first size, two of No. 3, and six of No. 4, together with the proper complementary details--and two of my trained assistants.” And I said:

text: Right at this spot stood a roadside blacksmith’s shop, and just then a local landowner pulled up—he’d purchased this young woman a few miles prior, with the agreement that she’d be handed over here so her shackles could be removed first. Once the irons were struck off, the landowner and the slave trader got into a petty argument over who owed the blacksmith for the work. The second the shackles came off her, the young woman threw herself, sobbing hysterically and soaked with tears, into the arms of the enslaved man who’d looked away when she was beaten earlier. He pulled her tight against his chest, pressing kiss after kiss to her face and the face of the baby she held, his own tears streaming down to soak both their skin. I had a hunch. I asked around. Sure enough, I was correct: they were husband and wife. Men had to physically yank them apart. She was dragged off, kicking, fighting, and screaming like someone driven out of her mind, until a bend in the road took her out of view. Even then, we could still hear the thin, wailing edge of her screams growing fainter as they carried her further away. As for the husband and father, left standing there with his wife and child gone, never to cross his path again for the rest of his life? The expression on his face was too agonizing to bear, so I looked away. But I knew right then I’d never be able to scrub the image of him from my memory, and it’s still there even now, twisting my heart into knots every time it crosses my mind.
source_text: Just here was the wayside shop of a smith; and now arrived a landed proprietor who had bought this girl a few miles back, deliverable here where her irons could be taken off. They were removed; then there was a squabble between the gentleman and the dealer as to which should pay the blacksmith. The moment the girl was delivered from her irons, she flung herself, all tears and frantic sobbings, into the arms of the slave who had turned away his face when she was whipped. He strained her to his breast, and smothered her face and the child's with kisses, and washed them with the rain of his tears. I suspected. I inquired. Yes, I was right; it was husband and wife. They had to be torn apart by force; the girl had to be dragged away, and she struggled and fought and shrieked like one gone mad till a turn of the road hid her from sight; and even after that, we could still make out the fading plaint of those receding shrieks. And the husband and father, with his wife and child gone, never to be seen by him again in life?--well, the look of him one might not bear at all, and so I turned away; but I knew I should never get his picture out of my mind again, and there it is to this day, to wring my heartstrings whenever I think of it.

text: His face blazed over the assembled company like a sudden sunburst. I wasn’t frightened in the slightest, though. I readied my pile-driver maneuver, and gave myself fifteen minutes to drive him straight into the earth—every last inch of him, so deep not even the curve of his skull would peek above the dirt. This was how I opened my assault on him: I asked:
source_text: And his face shone upon the company like a sunburst. But I didn't scare at all. I rigged up my pile-driver, and allowed myself fifteen minutes to drive him into the earth--drive him _all_ in --drive him in till not even the curve of his skull should show above ground. Here is the way I started in on him. I asked:

text: Every last lad would sign on, I had no doubt of it; every active existing allotment would therefore be formally surrendered, and it was just as certain that every infant born going forward would always choose to join, too. In less than two months, that odd, absurd holdover, the Crown Land Allotment, would cease to be a living, functioning part of daily life, and take its place among the quirky forgotten relics of bygone eras.
source_text: All the boys would join, I was sure of that; so, all existing grants would be relinquished; that the newly born would always join was equally certain. Within sixty days that quaint and bizarre anomaly, the Royal Grant, would cease to be a living fact, and take its place among the curiosities of the past.

text: Little by little, I’d developed an uncanny, awestruck regard for this young woman. These days, the second she pulled out of the station and got her verbal train chugging full speed down the tracks of one of those endless, cross-continental sentences she favored, it struck me with full force that I stood in the dread, hallowed presence of the very Mother of the German language. I was so thoroughly struck by the thought that sometimes when she started pouring one of these sprawling sentences out in my direction, I slipped unthinkingly into a pose of deep reverence, baring my head without even noticing; and if her words had been water, I would have drowned for certain, no question about it. She had that quintessentially German knack for speech: no matter what she intended to say, whether it was a passing offhand comment, a full sermon, a whole encyclopedia entry, or a complete war history, she would cram the whole thing into one single sentence or perish trying. When a literary German speaker dives headfirst into a sentence, that’s the last you’ll catch sight of him until he pops up on the far side of his personal Atlantic Ocean, his verb clamped firmly between his teeth.
source_text: I was gradually coming to have a mysterious and shuddery reverence for this girl; nowadays whenever she pulled out from the station and got her train fairly started on one of those horizonless transcontinental sentences of hers, it was borne in upon me that I was standing in the awful presence of the Mother of the German Language. I was so impressed with this, that sometimes when she began to empty one of these sentences on me I unconsciously took the very attitude of reverence, and stood uncovered; and if words had been water, I had been drowned, sure. She had exactly the German way; whatever was in her mind to be delivered, whether a mere remark, or a sermon, or a cyclopedia, or the history of a war, she would get it into a single sentence or die. Whenever the literary German dives into a sentence, that is the last you are going to see of him till he emerges on the other side of his Atlantic with his verb in his mouth.

text: As we drew closer to the town, faint traces of human habitation started to come into view. Every so often we passed a ramshackle thatch-roofed cabin, ringed by tiny fields and vegetable plots tended with only the most careless, half-hearted effort. We spotted locals too: thick-muscled men with long, unwashed, matted hair falling forward over their faces, giving them a brutish, almost feral appearance. Most of these men and the women alike wore scratchy, rough-spun linen robes falling well past the knee, paired with crude, roughly made sandals, and a great number of them had iron collars fastened around their necks. The young boys and girls ran around completely unclothed, though no one in the community appeared to pay the fact any mind at all. Every single one of these people fixed their eyes on me, murmured comments among themselves about me, and even darted back into their cabins to drag out their whole households to stare openly at me. No one paid the other man with me any mind at all, save to offer him a quiet, deferential greeting, which he never bothered to acknowledge.
source_text: As we approached the town, signs of life began to appear. At intervals we passed a wretched cabin, with a thatched roof, and about it small fields and garden patches in an indifferent state of cultivation. There were people, too; brawny men, with long, coarse, uncombed hair that hung down over their faces and made them look like animals. They and the women, as a rule, wore a coarse tow-linen robe that came well below the knee, and a rude sort of sandal, and many wore an iron collar. The small boys and girls were always naked; but nobody seemed to know it. All of these people stared at me, talked about me, ran into the huts and fetched out their families to gape at me; but nobody ever noticed that other fellow, except to make him humble salutation and get no response for their pains.

text: The clock had just struck twelve when the last breath faded, and we huddled numb across the small cottage from four still, cold bodies. We draped whatever tattered scraps of bedding and old clothing we could scrounge up over their faces, then slipped out into the crisp dark, sliding the rusted iron latch shut tight behind us. This hovel would have to serve as their final resting place, for the church would never grant them a blessed funeral, nor would they be allowed to lie in the hallowed soil of the village graveyard. They were treated no better than feral hounds, rabid vermin, untouchable lepers; no devout soul who prized their shot at salvation would dare risk damning themselves by so much as meddling in the smallest way with these shunned, cursed outcasts.
source_text: At midnight all was over, and we sat in the presence of four corpses. We covered them with such rags as we could find, and started away, fastening the door behind us. Their home must be these people's grave, for they could not have Christian burial, or be admitted to consecrated ground. They were as dogs, wild beasts, lepers, and no soul that valued its hope of eternal life would throw it away by meddling in any sort with these rebuked and smitten outcasts.

text: Two days later, as noon drew near, Sandy began to show glimmers of restless, fevered anticipation. She said we were drawing close to the ogre’s castle, and the news jolted me into an uncomfortable rush of shock. The whole point of our quest had slowly slipped entirely from my mind, so this abrupt resurrection of it made it feel startlingly, tangibly real for a moment, and sparked a sharp, eager interest in me. Sandy’s excitement swelled with every passing second, and mine rose right alongside it— that kind of energy is contagious. My heart started thudding hard against my ribs. You can’t reason with a heart, after all; it abides by its own rules, pounding for things the rational intellect would dismiss out of hand. Presently, when Sandy slid down from her horse, gestured for me to stop, and crept stealthily, head hunched nearly to her knees, toward a row of bushes edging a steep declivity, that thudding grew even stronger and faster. It kept up its wild pace as she settled into her hiding spot and peeked over the edge of the slope, and as I crawled on my knees to huddle beside her. Her eyes were blazing now, as she pointed with one finger and spoke in a gasping, breathless whisper:
source_text: Two days later, toward noon, Sandy began to show signs of excitement and feverish expectancy. She said we were approaching the ogre's castle. I was surprised into an uncomfortable shock. The object of our quest had gradually dropped out of my mind; this sudden resurrection of it made it seem quite a real and startling thing for a moment, and roused up in me a smart interest. Sandy's excitement increased every moment; and so did mine, for that sort of thing is catching. My heart got to thumping. You can't reason with your heart; it has its own laws, and thumps about things which the intellect scorns.
