Presently, when Sandy slid from the horse, motioned me to stop, and went creeping stealthily, with her head bent nearly to her knees, toward a row of bushes that bordered a declivity, the thumpings grew stronger and quicker. And they kept it up while she was gaining her ambush and getting her glimpse over the declivity; and also while I was creeping to her side on my knees. Her eyes were burning now, as she pointed with her finger, and said in a panting whisper:

text: Good heavens, Lila, this is the most absurd stunt I’ve ever witnessed. The sheer nerve of it is almost too staggering to praise. We sauntered uninvited into some stranger’s house, stuffed every corner of it with the only truly precious, sun-kissed treasure this earth has ever produced, and only now are we realizing we don’t even know the man’s name. How on earth did you dare take such a wild, unprompted liberty? I’d naturally assumed this was your own home. What in the world is he going to say when he finds us here?
source_text: “Why, woman, this is a most extraordinary performance. The effrontery of it is beyond admiration. We blandly march into a man's house, and cram it full of the only really valuable nobility the sun has yet discovered in the earth, and then it turns out that we don't even know the man's name. How did you ever venture to take this extravagant liberty? I supposed, of course, it was your home. What will the man say?”

text: Flickering lamps cast a dim, wavering glow over the well-chamber. When the well held water to draw, monks hauled it up with a windlass and iron chain, pouring the flow into troughs that carried it to stone reservoirs in the chapel, just outside the well-chamber’s walls. No one but the monastery’s monks was permitted to cross the chamber’s threshold, but I gained entry on temporary authority, courtesy of a junior colleague who shared my profession. He himself had never set foot inside, though: he handled every problem with muttered incantations, never bothering to engage his intellect. If he had stepped across that threshold and used his own two eyes instead of trusting his addled, disordered wits, he could have repaired the well through perfectly ordinary means, then framed the fix as a miracle according to longstanding custom. But no—he was a bumbling old numskull, a magician who actually believed the tricks he peddled, and no magician can hope to thrive weighed down by that kind of self-deluding superstition.
source_text: The well-chamber was dimly lighted by lamps; the water was drawn with a windlass and chain by monks, and poured into troughs which delivered it into stone reservoirs outside in the chapel--when there was water to draw, I mean--and none but monks could enter the well-chamber. I entered it, for I had temporary authority to do so, by courtesy of my professional brother and subordinate. But he hadn't entered it himself. He did everything by incantations; he never worked his intellect. If he had stepped in there and used his eyes, instead of his disordered mind, he could have cured the well by natural means, and then turned it into a miracle in the customary way; but no, he was an old numskull, a magician who believed in his own magic; and no magician can thrive who is handicapped with a superstition like that.

text: The scrawny urchin curled into the crook of my shoulder, feigning drowsiness as the gray-haired storyteller cleared his throat to begin his far-fetched saga. Within minutes the boy had drifted off for real, as had the pair of shaggy hunting hounds at our feet, the clustered courtiers, the harried footmen, and the rigid ranks of royal guards lining the hall. The storyteller’s low, rumbling drawl droned unbroken, and soft snores rose from every corner to underscore it, deep and muted as the thrum of distant organ pipes. Some attendees had bowed their heads onto their crossed forearms, others lolled back with slack jaws spilling unselfconscious, rumbling breaths; flies buzzed and nipped at exposed skin entirely unhindered, plump grain rats slipped out of dozens of wall cracks to patter across the stone floors, making themselves comfortable on benches, sills, and even the raised dais. One bold little rat perched upright like a chipmunk atop the dozing sovereign’s crown, clutching a crumb of aged cheddar in its tiny paws to nibble, letting crumbs tumble down onto the ruler’s cheek with a guileless, cheeky disregard for all royal decorum. It was a perfectly peaceful scene, soft and soothing to fatigued eyes and frayed, overtaxed souls.
source_text: The boy nestled himself upon my shoulder and pretended to go to sleep. The old man began his tale; and presently the lad was asleep in reality; so also were the dogs, and the court, the lackeys, and the files of men-at-arms. The droning voice droned on; a soft snoring arose on all sides and supported it like a deep and subdued accompaniment of wind instruments. Some heads were bowed upon folded arms, some lay back with open mouths that issued unconscious music; the flies buzzed and bit, unmolested, the rats swarmed softly out from a hundred holes, and pattered about, and made themselves at home everywhere; and one of them sat up like a squirrel on the king's head and held a bit of cheese in its hands and nibbled it, and dribbled the crumbs in the king's face with naive and impudent irreverence. It was a tranquil scene, and restful to the weary eye and the jaded spirit.

text: Before long, the guards posted on the castle ramparts called out to halt us, and we were let inside only after a short exchange. I don’t have a single nice thing to say about that visit, but that didn’t come as a letdown. I’d heard all about Mrs. le Fay’s reputation long before we arrived, so I’d never gone in anticipating a pleasant experience anyway. The entire kingdom feared her, because she’d convinced every last person she was a powerful sorceress. Every choice she made was cruel, every instinct she had was twisted. She brimmed with unfeeling, spiteful malice from head to toe. Her entire past was stained with cruelty, and murder was far from an uncommon offense for her. I’d been desperate to meet her, as curious about her as I would have been to lay eyes on the devil himself. I was shocked to find she was stunningly beautiful: the darkness of her mind hadn’t twisted her face into something ugly, and the years hadn’t etched a single wrinkle into her smooth, silken skin, or dimmed its soft, dewy glow. She could easily have passed for old Uriens’ granddaughter, and anyone would have mistaken her for her own son’s sister.
source_text: In due time we were challenged by the warders, from the castle walls, and after a parley admitted. I have nothing pleasant to tell about that visit. But it was not a disappointment, for I knew Mrs. le Fay by reputation, and was not expecting anything pleasant. She was held in awe by the whole realm, for she had made everybody believe she was a great sorceress. All her ways were wicked, all her instincts devilish. She was loaded to the eyelids with cold malice. All her history was black with crime; and among her crimes murder was common. I was most curious to see her; as curious as I could have been to see Satan. To my surprise she was beautiful; black thoughts had failed to make her expression repulsive, age had failed to wrinkle her satin skin or mar its bloomy freshness. She could have passed for old Uriens' granddaughter, she could have been mistaken for sister to her own son.

text: Yet he never drew near. He paused, staring distractedly at the dim, huddled shape of us for a long beat, clearly wrapped up in some totally separate train of thought; then he set down his lamp, drifted thoughtfully toward the door, and before anyone could even fathom what he intended to do, he’d slipped outside and pulled the door shut behind him.
source_text: But he didn't approach. He stopped, gazed absently toward our dusky mass a minute, evidently thinking about something else; then set down his light, moved musingly toward the door, and before a body could imagine what he was going to do, he was out of the door and had closed it behind him.

text: When I finally came to next, it felt like I’d been asleep for ages. My first bleary thought was, Man, that was such an insane dream! I must’ve woken up right at the last second too, or I’d have ended up hanged, drowned, burned alive, or some other terrible fate… I’ll just doze a little longer until the whistle blows, then head down to the arms factory and hash the whole thing out with Hercules face to face.
source_text: When I next came to myself, I seemed to have been asleep a very long time. My first thought was, “Well, what an astonishing dream I've had! I reckon I've waked only just in time to keep from being hanged or drowned or burned or something.... I'll nap again till the whistle blows, and then I'll go down to the arms factory and have it out with Hercules.”

text: “There’s no point dancing around the issue or trying to dodge the truth that way, Sandy. It’s exactly how I’m telling you it is. I know for a fact it’s true. And when you get right down to the core of it, knight-errantry is actually worse than pork. No matter what goes down, the pork is still there, so someone always gets something out of it regardless. But when the whole scheme crashes in the middle of some knight-errant spree, every knight involved goes bust, what do you have left as actual assets? Just a heap of bashed-up corpses and a couple barrels of broken metal gear. Are you really gonna call that anything of value? I’ll take pork every single time. Am I right?”
source_text: “There's no use in beating about the bush and trying to get around it that way, Sandy, it's _so_, just as I say. I _know_ it's so. And, moreover, when you come right down to the bedrock, knight-errantry is _worse_ than pork; for whatever happens, the pork's left, and so somebody's benefited anyway; but when the market breaks, in a knight-errantry whirl, and every knight in the pool passes in his checks, what have you got for assets? Just a rubbish-pile of battered corpses and a barrel or two of busted hardware. Can you call _those_ assets? Give me pork, every time. Am I right?”

text: Ugh, there was no point wasting good sense on her. Training—training is everything; it’s the sum total of every part of what makes a person who they are. We prattle on about inherent nature, and it’s all foolishness. There’s no such thing as that at all; the term is a total misnomer, and what we’re really referencing when we use that misleading name is nothing more than heredity and the training we receive over a lifetime. None of us have truly original thoughts, or opinions that are uniquely our own; every last one is passed down to us, drilled into us from the day we’re born. The only sliver of us that is actually original, the only part we can rightfully take credit or blame for, is so tiny it could be entirely covered by the tip of a fine cambric needle. Everything else is just atoms contributed by, and inherited from, an unbroken procession of ancestors stretching back a billion years, to that first Adam-clam or grasshopper or monkey our species evolved from in this tedious, showy, completely useless process of development. As for me, the only thing I fix my mind on in this slow, sad trudge of a pilgrimage, this pathetic drift between two eternities, is to stay mindful, live humbly, purely, honorably, and blamelessly, and hold onto that one microscopic atom in me that is truly *me*. The rest can land in Sheol and be as welcome there as it likes, for all I care.
source_text: Oh, it was no use to waste sense on her. Training--training is everything; training is all there is _to_ a person. We speak of nature; it is folly; there is no such thing as nature; what we call by that misleading name is merely heredity and training. We have no thoughts of our own, no opinions of our own; they are transmitted to us, trained into us. All that is original in us, and therefore fairly creditable or discreditable to us, can be covered up and hidden by the point of a cambric needle, all the rest being atoms contributed by, and inherited from, a procession of ancestors that stretches back a billion years to the Adam-clam or grasshopper or monkey from whom our race has been so tediously and ostentatiously and unprofitably developed. And as for me, all that I think about in this plodding sad pilgrimage, this pathetic drift between the eternities, is to look out and humbly live a pure and high and blameless life, and save that one microscopic atom in me that is truly _me_: the rest may land in Sheol and welcome for all I care.

text: We wandered the cobblestone lanes at a leisurely clip, ducking into storefronts on a whim, haggling over the cost of dry goods and trinkets, swapping tales of the recent uprising with the shopkeepers who ran the stalls. Every so often we stumbled on heart-wrenching traces of the violence: huddled, ostracized clusters of homeless family survivors, red-eyed and trembling, their houses seized, their mothers and fathers either slaughtered in the streets or strung up from the gallows at the square. Kael’s tunic was stitched from rough tow linen, Elara’s dress from scratchy linsey-woolsey, and both garments looked less like clothing than hand-drawn parish maps, crammed end to end with swatches of mismatched fabric added one by one as they’d moved from parish to parish over half a dozen years, so little of the original cloth remained that you could scarcely find a scrap big enough to cover the palm of your hand. I’d been meaning to buy the pair a proper set of new clothes for the upcoming gathering of high-ranking folks, but I couldn’t figure out how to offer without making them feel ashamed, until a thought struck me all at once: I’d already spent days spinning lies about the king’s deep gratitude for their help, so what better way to make those lies feel real than to pair them with a tangible reward? So I said:
source_text: And so we went gadding along, dropping in here and there, pricing things, and gossiping with the shopkeepers about the riot, and now and then running across pathetic reminders of it, in the persons of shunned and tearful and houseless remnants of families whose homes had been taken from them and their parents butchered or hanged. The raiment of Marco and his wife was of coarse tow-linen and linsey-woolsey respectively, and resembled township maps, it being made up pretty exclusively of patches which had been added, township by township, in the course of five or six years, until hardly a hand's-breadth of the original garments was surviving and present. Now I wanted to fit these people out with new suits, on account of that swell company, and I didn't know just how to get at it --with delicacy, until at last it struck me that as I had already been liberal in inventing wordy gratitude for the king, it would be just the thing to back it up with evidence of a substantial sort; so I said:

text: Word of the challenge soon reached every member of the Round Table, and of course it was endlessly discussed, for exploits of that sort never failed to capture the knights’ interest. The king argued that I ought to set out in search of adventures right away, so I could earn renown and prove myself more worthy to face Sir Sagramor once the allotted years had elapsed. I made my excuses for the moment: I told him I still needed three or four years to get all my initiatives properly set up and running smoothly before I would be ready. I noted that all signs pointed to Sir Sagramor still being off on his Grail quest by that point anyway, so no valuable time would be lost to the delay. By then I would have held my post for six or seven years, I explained, and I was confident my systems and governing infrastructure would be so well established that I could take a leave of absence without risking any disruption to their function.
source_text: The Round Table soon heard of the challenge, and of course it was a good deal discussed, for such things interested the boys. The king thought I ought now to set forth in quest of adventures, so that I might gain renown and be the more worthy to meet Sir Sagramor when the several years should have rolled away. I excused myself for the present; I said it would take me three or four years yet to get things well fixed up and going smoothly; then I should be ready; all the chances were that at the end of that time Sir Sagramor would still be out grailing, so no valuable time would be lost by the postponement; I should then have been in office six or seven years, and I believed my system and machinery would be so well developed that I could take a holiday without its working any harm.

text: Fifteen? God, my heart’s shattering right in two—oh, my lost darling, my baby! She was exactly that age, so gentle, so warm, the entire world to me, and I’ll never get to look at her face again. The thought of her yanks me right across that vast, rolling sea of memory to a hazy, distant, happy stretch of time that feels like dozens of lifetimes ago, when I’d wake up on soft summer mornings, still reeling from sweet dreams of her, and call out “Hello, Central?” just to hear that dear voice of hers drift warm back to me with a “Hi, Hank!” That sound was sweeter than any heavenly music to my lovesick ears. She only made three dollars a week back then, but she was worth every last cent, and then some.
source_text: Fifteen! Break--my heart! oh, my lost darling! Just her age who was so gentle, and lovely, and all the world to me, and whom I shall never see again! How the thought of her carries me back over wide seas of memory to a vague dim time, a happy time, so many, many centuries hence, when I used to wake in the soft summer mornings, out of sweet dreams of her, and say “Hello, Central!” just to hear her dear voice come melting back to me with a “Hello, Hank!” that was music of the spheres to my enchanted ear. She got three dollars a week, but she was worth it.

text: We also had another major new initiative in the works. This one was our first foray into telegraph and telephone technology. For the time being, these lines were meant exclusively for private use, and their existence had to stay under wraps until we were ready to unveil them publicly. We had a crew of men out in the field, working almost entirely after dark. They were running ground wires rather than stringing lines on poles, which we feared would draw too much curious attention. Ground wires worked perfectly well for both systems, anyway; I’d invented a custom insulation coating that was completely fail-safe, so no disruption could compromise the signal. My crew had explicit orders to cut cross-country rather than follow established roads, connect their lines to any sizable town whose glimmering lights they spotted on the horizon, and leave a trained specialist on site to maintain each connection. No one in the kingdom could give you reliable directions to any given settlement, after all—no one ever traveled to a place on purpose, only stumbled across them by chance while wandering, and almost always left again without bothering to ask what the settlement was called. We’d dispatched topographical survey expeditions multiple times to map the whole kingdom, but the priests had stepped in every time to stir up unrest and shut our work down. We’d tabled the mapping project for the moment; picking a fight with the Church would be foolish and counterproductive.
source_text: We had another large departure on hand, too. This was a telegraph and a telephone; our first venture in this line. These wires were for private service only, as yet, and must be kept private until a riper day should come. We had a gang of men on the road, working mainly by night. They were stringing ground wires; we were afraid to put up poles, for they would attract too much inquiry. Ground wires were good enough, in both instances, for my wires were protected by an insulation of my own invention which was perfect. My men had orders to strike across country, avoiding roads, and establishing connection with any considerable towns whose lights betrayed their presence, and leaving experts in charge. Nobody could tell you how to find any place in the kingdom, for nobody ever went intentionally to any place, but only struck it by accident in his wanderings, and then generally left it without thinking to inquire what its name was. At one time and another we had sent out topographical expeditions to survey and map the kingdom, but the priests had always interfered and raised trouble. So we had given the thing up, for the present; it would be poor wisdom to antagonize the Church.

text: Unlike most people he knew, he had never fully bought into the claim that the modern financial system was reliably stable. He could not wrap his head around how this moratorium functioned, or what specific benefit was meant to come from extending the bank holidays. As far as he could tell, the policy only amounted to a universal halt on all payouts and a full cutoff of access to ready cash. And according to Mrs. Faber, Hickson the grocer was already eyeing cheques with clear suspicion.
source_text: He had never felt quite so sure as most people about the stability of the modern financial system. He did not, he felt, understand the working of this moratorium, or the peculiar advantage of prolonging the bank holidays. It meant, he supposed, a stoppage of payment all round, and a cutting off of the supply of ready money. And Hickson the grocer, according to Mrs. Faber, was already looking askance at cheques.

text: Mrs. Britling didn’t find out about the catastrophic blow that had hit their household until after dinner wrapped up that night. She was so used to tuning out his impossible-to-read moods that she didn’t realize anything was truly, terribly wrong with him until they’d both settled into their seats at the dining table. He seemed weighed down, irritable, and eager to avoid any interaction with her, but that kind of sullen fit was nothing new to her. She knew that things that struck her as totally insignificant—poring over political speeches in *The Times*, throwaway little comments about daily life made without a second thought, even small, meaningless gestures—could push him to withdraw completely. She’d deliberately trained herself to brush off those sudden, unpredictable bouts of gloom. But when she glanced up mid-meal, she was stabbed straight through the heart to catch sight of his haggard, pale face, his eyes heavy with searing, deep despair, fixed on her in a way she couldn’t make sense of.
source_text: Mrs. Britling did not learn of the blow that had struck them until after dinner that night. She was so accustomed to ignore his incomprehensible moods that she did not perceive that there was anything tragic about him until they sat at table together. He seemed heavy and sulky and disposed to avoid her, but that sort of moodiness was nothing very strange to her. She knew that things that seemed to her utterly trivial, the reading of political speeches in _The Times_, little comments on life made in the most casual way, mere movements, could so avert him. She had cultivated a certain disregard of such fitful darknesses. But at the dinner-table she looked up, and was stabbed to the heart to see a haggard white face and eyes of deep despair regarding her ambiguously.

text: But he couldn’t settle. He was more on edge in his own home than he’d ever been before, and he had no idea why. Every last thing around him was exactly as it had always been, yet none of it felt right; it all seemed far more flimsy and unmoored than anything had ever felt to him before. The tedious, drawn-out back and forth of the Irish dispute bored him to tears; the smoldering, unspoken threat hanging over the Balkans got under his skin; he was annoyed by the suffragettes, by the endless string of senseless, small-scale strikes, by the general sense that there was no overarching throughline, so to speak, holding all these bickering matches and trivial messes together. When he was at the Dower House, the worst kinds of intrusive thoughts would pop up unbidden. He even found himself wondering if he’d more or less accidentally plagiarized Henry James’s *The Great Good Place* when he wrote *The Silent Places*, lifting bits without even being aware he was doing it.
source_text: But he was restless. He was more restless in his house than he had ever been. He could not understand it. Everything about him was just as it had always been, and yet it was unsatisfactory, and it seemed more unstable than anything had ever seemed before. He was bored by the solemn development of the Irish dispute; he was irritated by the smouldering threat of the Balkans; he was irritated by the suffragettes and by a string of irrational little strikes; by the general absence of any main plot as it were to hold all these wranglings and trivialities together.... At the Dower House the most unpleasant thoughts would come to him. He even had doubts whether in "The Silent Places," he had been plagiarising, more or less unconsciously, from Henry James's "Great Good Place."...

text: Yet I took my plea straight to the emperor. I begged him to let my protégé be formally assessed for the command post. The emperor was amenable, but the Royal Military Commission, every last one of them old blue-blooded aristocrats, begged him to spare them the insult of having to evaluate a cooper’s son. I knew full well they didn’t have the wit to test him anyway, so I added my own entreaties to theirs, and the emperor passed the task over to my handpicked academic panel. I’d had a slate chalkboard erected beforehand, and it was wheeled out now, the whole show underway. It was a sheer delight to hear the lad break down the full art of warfare, wade through fine points of skirmish and siege, logistics, convoy routes, sapping and counter-sapping, grand tactical maneuvers, large-scale and small-unit strategy, flag signaling, foot soldiers, mounted troops, artillery corps, every detail of siege cannons, field howitzers, rapid-fire repeaters, rifled barrels, smoothbores, long rifle drills, sidearm target practice—and not a single syllable of any of it could those overstuffed carp make heads or tails of, mind you—and it was a marvel to watch him scrawl mind-bending mathematical formulas on the board that would leave angels themselves stumped, knocking them out like they were child’s play, too—all about planetary eclipses, comet trajectories, solstice calculations, constellation mapping, standard time, sidereal time, mess hour, guard rotation, every last thing above the sky or below the ground you could use to harry or confound an enemy until he regretted ever crossing your border—and when the boy snapped a crisp military salute and stepped back at last, I was so proud I wanted to squeeze him tight, and all those stuffy nobles were so bewildered they looked half turned to stone, half addled with bad mead, and entirely caught flat-footed and buried under a mountain of knowledge they couldn’t fathom. I figured we’d won the prize, and by a mile at that.
source_text: But I appealed to the king. I begged that my candidate might be examined. The king was willing, but the Board, who were all well-born folk, implored the king to spare them the indignity of examining the weaver's son. I knew they didn't know enough to examine him anyway, so I joined my prayers to theirs and the king turned the duty over to my professors. I had had a blackboard prepared, and it was put up now, and the circus began. It was beautiful to hear the lad lay out the science of war, and wallow in details of battle and siege, of supply, transportation, mining and countermining, grand tactics, big strategy and little strategy, signal service, infantry, cavalry, artillery, and all about siege guns, field guns, gatling guns, rifled guns, smooth bores, musket practice, revolver practice--and not a solitary word of it all could these catfish make head or tail of, you understand--and it was handsome to see him chalk off mathematical nightmares on the blackboard that would stump the angels themselves, and do it like nothing, too--all about eclipses, and comets, and solstices, and constellations, and mean time, and sidereal time, and dinner time, and bedtime, and every other imaginable thing above the clouds or under them that you could harry or bullyrag an enemy with and make him wish he hadn't come--and when the boy made his military salute and stood aside at last, I was proud enough to hug him, and all those other people were so dazed they looked partly petrified, partly drunk, and wholly caught out and snowed under. I judged that the cake was ours, and by a large majority.

text: I don’t believe you people of the German Commonwealth fully grasp how methodically you were winning global preeminence in the years before this conflict erupted. If you’d poured even half the drive and ingenuity you’ve sunk into this bloodshed into winning over the hearts and minds of people across the globe peacefully, I am convinced you could have claimed world leadership without a single shot fired, no soul left to contest your claim. Your scientific advances ran a full five years ahead of ours, while your social welfare frameworks and economic governance were a full twenty-five years more sophisticated than any system we had built to date. There has never been a moment where a great people held more power to steer all of humanity toward a global commonwealth and universal, enduring peace. All it would have required was a small measure of generous, far-seeing imagination.
source_text: _I do not think you Germans realise how steadily you were conquering the world before this war began. Had you given half the energy and intelligence you have spent upon this war to the peaceful conquest of men's minds and spirits, I believe that you would have taken the leadership of the world tranquilly--no man disputing. Your science was five years, your social and economic organisation was a quarter of a century in front of ours.... Never has it so lain in the power of a great people to lead and direct mankind towards the world republic and universal peace. It needed but a certain generosity of the imagination...._

text: She leaned across the table, prodding the air with her knitting needles to punctuate each point of her argument, her voice steady with gentle, reasoned protest. “You see,” she said, “if people as good as Teddy are meant to be killed off, then every last belief we hold about what life is for—honesty, softness, joy—are all wrong. This world would be nothing but a gathering place for devils, nothing more than a filthy, cruel hell. Being born would be the same as being damned from the first breath. That’s why we can’t let ourselves cave to that thought, no matter how likely it seems that he’s already gone…"
source_text: She leant across the table and pointed her remarks with her knitting needles, speaking in a tone of reasonable remonstrance. "You see," she said, "if people like Teddy are to be killed, then all our ideas that life is meant for, honesty and sweetness and happiness, are wrong, and this world is just a place of devils; just a dirty cruel hell. Getting born would be getting damned. And so one must not give way to that idea, however much it may seem likely that he is dead....

text: More often than not, Mr. Britling did his best to push thoughts of Germany entirely out of his head. By daylight, he acted as though Germany held no significance for England at all. He had no patience for alarmists, and hated lingering on any unpleasant, unpalatable possibilities. He insisted the idea that an entire vast nation was lying in wait to attack them was utterly preposterous. What reason would they have for such a thing? Seventy million people could not possibly all be raving lunatics, after all. But when the dark of night settled in, those easy, offhand dismissals no longer held weight. What if, for all his arguments, their army was more than just a ceremonial parade force, their navy more than a performative, empty protest?
source_text: Usually Mr. Britling kept his mind off Germany. In the daytime he pretended Germany meant nothing to England. He hated alarmists. He hated disagreeable possibilities. He declared the idea of a whole vast nation waiting to strike at us incredible. Why should they? You cannot have seventy million lunatics.... But in the darkness of the night one cannot dismiss things in this way. Suppose, after all, their army was more than a parade, their navy more than a protest?

text: Reason held fast to her argument, a steady lamp hanging directly over Mr. Britling’s head that he stubbornly refused to glance at, as he imagined hacking enemy airmen to trembling, tattered chunks with a spade he’d sharpened himself, and choking German nobles to death with their own toxic gas, doled out slowly to inflict the maximum possible agony. “And what of the towns our own ships have bombarded?” Reason asked, her voice falling on deaf ears. “What of the Tasmanians our people completely swept from existence?”
source_text: So reason maintained her thesis, like a light above the head of Mr. Britling at which he would not look, while he hewed airmen to quivering rags with a spade that he had sharpened, and stifled German princes with their own poison gas, given slowly and as painfully as possible. "And what of the towns _our_ ships have bombarded?" asked reason unheeded. "What of those Tasmanians _our_ people utterly swept away?"

text: He set out first to explain to Mr. Van der Pant that the matters under discussion were of no real consequence whatsoever, that the nation’s attention and collective interest were fixed on entirely separate concerns; second, that the very qualities he was questioning were, by and large, simply virtues wearing a mild disguise. He framed a charming argument that England was never meant to be the arena where English people strove and achieved, but rather their cradle, their place of quiet reprieve: a space valued not for crisp efficiency, but for easy, warm good cheer. If Mr. Van der Pant made a point of asking around, he would discover that nearly every household in Matching’s Easy had sent some driven, enterprising member out of the country to join the ranks of English people building lives across the globe. English verve and drive were never expended on home soil, he claimed. These clipped hedgerows, these meandering, unhurried country lanes were steeped in layers of shared memory. There was one lane that curved sharply away just outside Market Saffron to bypass Turk’s Wood, a stretch of forest first given that name back in the fourteenth century for a local man of the same moniker. He even cited a handful of G.K. Chesterton’s light, joyful verses to make the case for these charmingly crooked country thoroughfares.
source_text: He set himself to explain to Mr. Van der Pant firstly that these things did not matter in the slightest degree, the national attention, the national interest ran in other directions; and secondly that they were, as a matter of fact and on the whole, merits slightly disguised. He produced a pleasant theory that England is really not the Englishman's field, it is his breeding place, his resting place, a place not for efficiency but good humour. If Mr. Van der Pant were to make inquiries he would find there was scarcely a home in Matching's Easy that had not sent some energetic representative out of England to become one of the English of the world. England was the last place in which English energy was spent. These hedges, these dilatory roads were full of associations. There was a road that turned aside near Market Saffron to avoid Turk's wood; it had been called Turk's wood first in the fourteenth century after a man of that name. He quoted Chesterton's happy verses to justify these winding lanes.

text: Winding along the path between shadow-thick hedgerows, Mr. Britling soon pulled his mind away from his half-hearted attempts to recall the curious inquiries he’d pursued in his youth, those deep dives into humanity’s oldest philosophical speculations, and turned instead to the timeless, unanswerable riddles that have outlasted every last one of those thousands of competing theories. Was hatred ever a necessary force? Is it still necessary now? Will it always be necessary for as long as humans exist? Is all of life nothing but an eternal, unending war? The rabbit is fast, sharp, fully engaged with its own existence, kept from devolving into some sick, sluggish, crawling herbivore by the constant, unrelenting threat of the ferret. If you stripped away the ferret that is war, what would life become?… There’s no getting around the fact that war is outright murder, of course—but isn’t peace just slow, unremarkable decay?
source_text: Mr. Britling, between the black hedges, came back presently from his attempts to recall his youthful inquiries into man's ancient speculations, to the enduring riddles that have outlasted a thousand speculations. Has hate been necessary, and is it still necessary, and will it always be necessary? Is all life a war forever? The rabbit is nimble, lives keenly, is prevented from degenerating into a diseased crawling eater of herbs by the incessant ferret. Without the ferret of war, what would life become?... War is murder truly, but is not Peace decay?

text: Faith is the first pillar and the final anchor of everything that matters, and until a person has sought out God and been claimed by Him in turn, they drift with no true starting point, laboring toward no real finish line. They might have close friendships, half-formed loyalties, small pockets of hard-won decency. But none of these pieces fit, none of life clicks into coherent shape, unless God is at the center. Unless God is at the center. God, who fights through human hands to push back against unthinking brute force, darkness, and the void that would erase all good; He is the final purpose, the core of every true meaning. He is the only rightful ruler…. Of course I have to write about Him. I have to share who He is with every corner of the world I know. And long before the arrival of that true, unassailable King, the inevitable ruler whose presence is felt every time just people gather together, all the blood-soaked detritus of our old, broken world orders will crumble away: those petty, small-time kings and gaudy, hollow emperors, those scheming politicians and slippery, self-serving lawyers, all the people who lay claim, grab what isn’t theirs, lie, and coerce others to their will, all the warmongers and oppressors who grind the vulnerable under their heel—they will all shrink up and vanish in an instant, the same way a scrap of paper vanishes the second it’s thrust into an open flame….
source_text: "Religion is the first thing and the last thing, and until a man has found God and been found by God, he begins at no beginning, he works to no end. He may have his friendships, his partial loyalties, his scraps of honour. But all these things fall into place and life falls into place only with God. Only with God. God, who fights through men against Blind Force and Night and Non-Existence; who is the end, who is the meaning. He is the only King.... Of course I must write about Him. I must tell all my world of Him. And before the coming of the true King, the inevitable King, the King who is present whenever just men foregather, this blood-stained rubbish of the ancient world, these puny kings and tawdry emperors, these wily politicians and artful lawyers, these men who claim and grab and trick and compel, these war makers and oppressors, will presently shrivel and pass--like paper thrust into a flame...."

text: She kept harping at him and jabbing at him with sharp little comments, refusing to let him get a single word in about his side of the argument. He was desperate to lay out his full reasoning, to talk through every layer of his position. The knot of conscience tied to the munitions work felt especially subtle, full of messy, hard-to-unpack gray areas he couldn’t untangle alone. He kept hoping she’d stop cutting him off, lean in, and try to engage with the complexities alongside him. But she was operating on that sharp, immediate English instinct, the kind that reads less as deliberate, step-by-step reasoning than unmediated gut feeling. Even as he was fully convinced this quick, reactive take was unfair to him, he couldn’t help adoring that fiery, lightning-fast spark in her.
source_text: So she nagged him and taunted him, and would hear no word of his case. And he wanted dreadfully to discuss his case. He felt that the point of conscience about the munitions was particularly fine and difficult. He wished she would listen and enter into it more. But she thought with that more rapid English flash which is not so much thinking as feeling. He loved that flash in her in spite of his persuasion of its injustice.

text: Yet spring and summer slipped by without a single Allied victory. The much-touted May “great offensive” ground to a halt and was called off entirely after a string of fruitless, staggeringly costly assaults launched along the line between Ypres and Soissons. The German army had engineered a rigorously scientific defensive system that swapped rifles for machine guns, designed to deal the maximum possible damage to attacking troops while suffering the bare minimum of casualties on their own side. The British War Office had dismissed machine guns as largely unimportant prior to this point, but they now devoted considerable attention to the weapons. On top of that, more and more of Britain’s wartime resources and focus were being redirected to the campaign at the Dardanelles.
source_text: But through the spring and summer there was no victory. The "great offensive" of May was checked and abandoned after a series of ineffective and very costly attacks between Ypres and Soissons. The Germans had developed a highly scientific defensive in which machine-guns replaced rifles and a maximum of punishment was inflicted upon an assaulting force with a minimum of human loss. The War Office had never thought much of machine-guns before, but now it thought a good deal. Moreover, the energies of Britain were being turned more and more towards the Dardanelles.

text: Mr. Hale’s conviction about Elias was that the boy was no ordinary child, marked by an uncommon goodness, and that the extra care he required stemmed from a fragility of nerve and temperament that was, at its core, a rare strength. Elias was sharp: quick to catch a stray sound, quick to dart into motion, startlingly precise in all his fast, impulsive actions. He spoke far earlier than most children, and took up drawing before he was even school-aged, his work marked by a stubborn, unpolished roughness and a striking, unteachable knack for conveying feeling. That he was occasionally clumsy, frequently unkempt, so lost in his own thoughts he could come off as rude to those around him, and prone to catching every passing ailment, all fit into this same pattern of delicate, overactive inner life. The quiet, unvoiced criticism from Mrs. Hale, the unspoken comparisons she drew between Elias and her own much cheerier, thoroughly unremarkable younger children, kept Mr. Hale constantly hungry for small signs and demonstrations of Elias’s unique gifts. This eagerness did not always turn out well; it sparked a great deal of mutual irritation, but never enough to snuff out the genuine, growing response Elias held for his father in return for his steady solicitude. The boy knew with unshakable certainty that his father was his father, just as he knew without question that Mrs. Hale would never be his mother. He brought every small success to his father, and it was his father he turned to with every appeal for help or understanding.
source_text: It was Mr. Britling's case for Hugh that he was something exceptional, something exceptionally good, and that the peculiar need there was to take care of him was due to a delicacy of nerve and fibre that was ultimately a virtue. The boy was quick, quick to hear, quick to move, very accurate in his swift way, he talked unusually soon, he began to sketch at an early age with an incurable roughness and a remarkable expressiveness. That he was sometimes ungainly, often untidy, that he would become so mentally preoccupied as to be uncivil to people about him, that he caught any malaise that was going, was all a part of that. The sense of Mrs. Britling's unexpressed criticisms, the implied contrasts with the very jolly, very uninspired younger family, kept up a nervous desire in Mr. Britling for evidences and manifestations of Hugh's quality. Not always with happy results; it caused much mutual irritation, but not enough to prevent the growth of a real response on Hugh's part to his father's solicitude. The youngster knew and felt that his father was his father just as certainly as he felt that Mrs. Britling was not his mother. To his father he brought his successes and to his father he appealed.

text: For the full span of his Atlantic crossing, Mr. Thorne had fought to scrub from his mind the image of that final meeting: her chin tilted high even as her gaze darted down, and every other sharp fragment of that same crushing humiliation. All the years he’d lavished on her! All the wasted hours! Every moment he’d taken her at her word that she held a special, singular fondness for him above all others. He tried to frame his agony as the soft, romantic pangs of unrequited love, refusing to admit to himself how thoroughly his pride and vanity had been gutted by her final, unnegotiable rejection. Once, not so long ago, she’d given him every cause to snicker privately at Jude Fairchild’s hapless suit.
source_text: All the way across the Atlantic Mr. Direck had been trying to forget her downward glance with the chin up, during that last encounter--and other aspects of the same humiliation. The years he had spent upon her! The time! Always relying upon her assurance of a special preference for him. He tried to think he was suffering from the pangs of unrequited love, and to conceal from himself just how bitterly his pride and vanity had been rent by her ultimate rejection. There had been a time when she had given him reason to laugh in his sleeve at Booth Wilmington.

text: Mr. Britling carried nearly all of the dinner table conversation, and every part of it that held any real weight came from him. Teddy only ever spoke up to agree with whatever was being said, Hugh stayed quiet and seemed more than a little unfocused the whole time. Mrs. Britling’s mind was split between tracking the upcoming courses, checking on the serving staff, and keeping an eye on the boys, so she only gave her husband half her attention at best. Captain Carmine barely said a word, looking as if he was caught up in some unpleasant, nagging worry he couldn’t set aside. Every so often he’d chime in to confirm a point Mr. Britling made, or add a small extra detail to it. Three separate times, he made the same comment: “People still haven’t even begun to understand.”
source_text: He did most of the table talk, and all that mattered. Teddy was an assenting voice, Hugh was silent and apparently a little inattentive, Mrs. Britling was thinking of the courses and the servants and the boys, and giving her husband only half an ear, Captain Carmine said little and seemed to be troubled by some disagreeable preoccupation. Now and then he would endorse or supplement the things Mr. Britling was saying. Thrice he remarked: "People still do not begin to understand."...

text: The first moment Mr. Britling was fully, unshakably convinced that English and German attitudes toward the war were fundamentally at odds was when he came across a stack of German satirical comic papers in a friend’s London study. Their pages were stuffed full of caricatures mocking the Allies, and the English most of all, radiating a raw, staggering intensity of fury that felt almost impossible to wrap his head around. Both their unhinged, rabid hatred and their repulsive, crude vulgarity hit Mr. Britling like a physical blow. There was no trace of quiet national pride or measured national dignity anywhere in their pages, only a bellowing, chest-thumping performative patriotism and an unbounded urge to inflict pain and humiliation on their targets. They were practically foaming at the mouth, faces flushed crimson with rage, spitting venom across every printed line. He sat there clutching those violent, vitriolic sheets in his hands, and all he could feel was a deep, burning shame.
source_text: The first thing that really pierced Mr. Britling with the conviction that there was something essentially different in the English and the German attitude towards the war was the sight of a bale of German comic papers in the study of a friend in London. They were filled with caricatures of the Allies and more particularly of the English, and they displayed a force and quality of passion--an incredible force and quality of passion. Their amazing hate and their amazing filthiness alike overwhelmed Mr. Britling. There was no appearance of national pride or national dignity, but a bellowing patriotism and a limitless desire to hurt and humiliate. They spat. They were red in the face and they spat. He sat with these violent sheets in his hands--_ashamed_.

text: One letter held nothing but a drawing, a playful parody of that ubiquitous wartime illustration: a ragged bivouac takes up the lower half of the frame, while a sleeping soldier’s tender dream of returning home to his sweetheart fills the upper space. But the dozing man in this version was Master Elias, who clung tight to an enormous glass retort, while a conveniently positioned voltmeter tracked the intensity of his affection, and tiny brass tripods skipped and danced in a ring around him.
source_text: And one letter was just a picture, a parody of the well-known picture of the bivouac below and the soldier's dream of return to his beloved above. But Master Hugh in the dream was embracing an enormous retort, while a convenient galvanometer registered his emotion and little tripods danced around him.

text: His initial idea of a unified global push among nations to secure lasting world peace and dismantle militaristic Germany was being overshadowed by this second, totally contradictory mental image of a world thrown into utter chaos and disarray. Thousands of ordinary people just like Mrs. Fabers stockpiling food and household essentials, rowdy rioting crowds smashing through shop fronts, payment moratoriums, locked bank doors, and endless lines of waiting people. Could the entire global system really break down entirely just because public confidence in it had shattered? He didn’t register any clash between the two wildly different visions; the grand, noble project of pacifying the entire planet had been fully replaced in his mind by these far more immediate, personal possible outcomes. He heard a soft rustle behind him, and turned to face his wife.
source_text: His first conception of a great convergent movement of the nations to make a world peace and an end to militant Germany was being obscured by this second, entirely incompatible, vision of a world confused and disorganised. Mrs. Fabers in great multitudes hoarding provisions, riotous crowds attacking shops, moratorium, shut banks and waiting queues. Was it possible for the whole system to break down through a shock to its confidence? Without any sense of incongruity the dignified pacification of the planet had given place in his mind to these more intimate possibilities. He heard a rustle behind him, and turned to face his wife.

text: When Mr. Thorne stepped into his usual gentlemen’s club, the air hummed with heated conversation and a bristling, martial energy. In the front hall, Crowther the essayist was holding up a dummy rifle forged from bent iron bar for a small circle of fascinated fellow members. The crudely made weapon was meant for recruit drill exercises until proper firearms could be sourced, and each one cost only a shilling and sixpence to manufacture. That was the first inkling Mr. Thorne had that the War Office’s staggering lack of foresight extended far beyond its abject failure to prepare for the sudden flood of new volunteers. Conversation flowed uncommonly freely across the club’s common rooms; one curious transient effect of the war’s opening weeks had been to partially melt the ingrained, habitual reserve of British men. Men who had traded nothing but stony, unsmiling glares across the lunch tables with Mr. Thorne for a decade or more were now stopping to chat with him unprompted.
source_text: At his club Mr. Britling found much talk and belligerent stir. In the hall Wilkins the author was displaying a dummy rifle of bent iron rod to several interested members. It was to be used for drilling until rifles could be got, and it could be made for eighteen pence. This was the first intimation Mr. Britling got that the want of foresight of the War Office only began with its unpreparedness for recruits. Men were talking very freely in the club; one of the temporary effects of the war in its earlier stages was to produce a partial thaw in the constitutional British shyness; and men who had glowered at Mr. Britling over their lunches and had been glowered at by Mr. Britling in silence for years now started conversations with him.

text: Look, I don’t buy a single thing Cissie’s ever said about America—not one word. You don’t get what kind of project America’s working toward, right? It’s the New World, where all those old lines between races and nations don’t count anymore. It’s the Melting Pot, the place we’re gonna forge a whole better kind of society out of. I’ve always believed that—even with a thousand tiny frustrations that make you second-guess for a split second, I still believe it now. I’m not backing down on any of that. And I’m not heading off to fight as an American, either. I’m doing this just as me… And for the record, I’m not fighting for England, either. Don’t even start thinking that. I don’t think I’m even that fond of a whole lot of British customs to go that far for them. I can’t imagine anyone being a huge fan of your whole Empire setup, with its half-dead stuffy royal court, its sleazy backstabbing politicians, all its lords and ladies and obnoxious snobs, the way you brutalize the Irish and exploit India, everyone passing the buck and lying through their teeth about your working class people. I’m not fighting for England. I’m fighting for Cissie—and justice, and Belgium, and all that other high-minded stuff—but mostly for Cissie. Plus, I can’t look Pa Britling in the eye anymore, to be honest… And I wanna see those trenches, up close. I figure they’re the kind of thing you’ll have a hell of a good time telling stories about later, right?… So I’m going,” Mr. Direck said. “But the main reason? It’s Cissie. Got it?
source_text: "Mind you, I don't admit a thing that Cissie has ever said about America--not one thing. You don't understand the sort of proposition America is up against. America is the New World, where there are no races and nations any more; she is the Melting Pot, from which we will cast the better state. I've believed that always--in spite of a thousand little things I believe it now. I go back on nothing. I'm not fighting as an American either. I'm fighting simply as myself.... I'm not going fighting for England, mind you. Don't you fancy that. I don't know I'm so particularly in love with a lot of English ways as to do that. I don't see how any one can be very much in love with your Empire, with its dead-alive Court, its artful politicians, its lords and ladies and snobs, its way with the Irish and its way with India, and everybody shifting responsibility and telling lies about your common people. I'm not going fighting for England. I'm going fighting for Cissie--and justice and Belgium and all that--but more particularly for Cissie. And anyhow I can't look Pa Britling in the face any more.... And I want to see those trenches--close. I reckon they're a thing it will be interesting to talk about some day.... So I'm going," said Mr. Direck. "But chiefly--it's Cissie. See?"

text: The dominant conversation topic in the upstairs smoking room was how thoroughly the War Office was failing to manage the flood of new recruits pouring in, and how aggressively it opposed the kind of volunteer initiative Mr. Britling was pitching. A good number of the club members were eager to volunteer themselves, and there was plenty of discussion about their own physical fitness for service. “I’m fifty-four,” one man remarked, “and I could march twenty-five miles in full kit far better than half those nineteen-year-old lads signing up.” Another, who was thirty-eight, spoke up next. “I have to keep my business running, of course,” he said, “but why on earth shouldn’t I learn how to shoot and use a bayonet anyway?” The personal frustration of men who’d already had their enlistment attempts rejected lent extra bite to their criticisms of the recruitment process and broader military organization. “The War Office has one unfixable, recurring flaw,” a large, broad-shouldered mine owner said. “During peacetime, they staff their entire home administration with men who are guaranteed to be called up to the front the second a war breaks out. As soon as war is declared, then, everyone has to shuffle roles to fill gaps, and some brand new, untested person—usually some decrepit retired officer dug out of obscurity—gets thrust into the job. Chaos follows automatically. The War Office has always operated this way, and as far as anyone can tell, it always will. It seems completely incapable of anticipating that it will need a replacement for a staff member until that person is already gone. It doesn’t even have the basic foresight to plan for that.”
source_text: The prevailing topic in the smoking-room upstairs was the inability of the War Office to deal with the flood of recruits that was pouring in, and its hostility to any such volunteering as Mr. Britling had in mind. Quite a number of members wanted to volunteer; there was much talk of their fitness; "I'm fifty-four," said one, "and I could do my twenty-five miles in marching kit far better than half those boys of nineteen." Another was thirty-eight. "I must hold the business together," he said; "but why anyhow shouldn't I learn to shoot and use a bayonet?" The personal pique of the rejected lent force to their criticisms of the recruiting and general organisation. "The War Office has one incurable system," said a big mine-owner. "During peace time it runs all its home administration with men who will certainly be wanted at the front directly there is a war. Directly war comes, therefore, there is a shift all round, and a new untried man--usually a dug-out in an advanced state of decay--is stuck into the job. Chaos follows automatically. The War Office always has done this, and so far as one can see it always will. It seems incapable of realising that another man will be wanted until the first is taken away. Its imagination doesn't even run to that."

text: Finn, fresh out of his workday wear and back in his faded check flannels, grabbed hold of the vintage pram the moment he spotted it. Maeve fussed over tucking the swaddled infant safely into its padded basket, every small adjustment brimming with soft, unmistakeable maternal care. Lila had slipped off to change out of her smudged house dress, and when she returned she was breezy, calling quick, cheerful farewells and paying the napping baby no mind whatsoever. Finn headed out in that charmingly roguish, playfully bigamous way he had, pushing the pram between the two sisters as they wandered into the soft silver haze of the moonlit lane, a chorus of overlapping goodnights trailing after them. Mr. Hale’s quiet curiosity, which had been simmering all evening, sharpened to a fine, burning point of focus.
source_text: Teddy, who was back in his flannels, seized upon the perambulator. There was much careful baby stowing on the part of Cecily; she displayed an infinitely maternal solicitude. Letty was away changing; she reappeared jauntily taking leave, disregarding the baby absolutely, and Teddy departed bigamously, wheeling the perambulator between the two sisters into the hazes of the moonlight. There was much crying of good nights. Mr. Direck's curiosities narrowed down to a point of great intensity....

text: We finally have rifles! We’re actual properly armed soldiers now, every last one of us with our own gun. They’re made in Japan, too! The stock is this pale, fine-grained wood that looks just like fresh-cut oak, the kind used for high-end arts and crafts furniture, so much so it feels like we belong to some silly First Garden Suburb Regiment rather than a real fighting unit. I reckon linseed oil will fix that dainty polished look soon enough, though. These are proper working rifles, make no mistake—they fire just like they’re supposed to, loud bang and all. We’re all a little giddy with excitement over them right now, honestly. If it wasn’t for all the training and discipline we’ve drilled into us, we’d probably be taking potshots at any random passersby dumb enough to show their heads on the ridgeline. I spotted some bloke half a mile away yesterday, and I couldn’t shake the stupid, itching thought that I could nail him dead center, no trouble at all. Ortheris, that little pest, got himself a motorbike he swears is his “b----y oto” — no one has a clue where that ridiculous nickname came from. And from what I’ve heard, the only way I’m getting out of being promoted to corporal in the next month is if I either kick the bucket or do something so dishonorable I get drummed straight out of the unit.
source_text: "We've got rifles! We're real armed soldiers at last. Every blessed man has got a rifle. And they come from Japan! They are of a sort of light wood that is like new oak and art furniture, and makes one feel that one belongs to the First Garden Suburb Regiment; but I believe much can be done with linseed oil. And they are real rifles, they go bang. We are a little light-headed about them. Only our training and discipline prevent our letting fly at incautious spectators on the skyline. I saw a man yesterday about half a mile off. I was possessed by the idea that I could get him--right in the middle.... Ortheris, the little beast, has got a motor-bicycle, which he calls his 'b----y oto'--no one knows why--and only death or dishonourable conduct will save me, I gather, from becoming a corporal in the course of the next month...."

text: The second he finished speaking, his gaze clashed with hers, and he was suddenly dead certain she knew exactly what bad news the telegram had brought, and that she was horrified he was carrying on with the light, festive mood as if the terrible update meant nothing at all. He faltered, keenly aware he ought to say something more, that he was blowing this unspoken social expectation entirely, before resolving that no matter what, he had to yank his face out of the line of her unwavering stare. She didn’t so much as twitch to look away. It felt like she was drinking him in, committing every flicker of his expression to memory to revisit later, greedily, every fiber of her being fixed entirely on cataloging every small, telling detail of him.
source_text: As he said this he met her eyes, and had a sudden persuasion that she knew exactly what it was the telegram had told him, and that she was shocked at this gala-like treatment of such terrible news. He hesitated, feeling that he had to say something else, that he was socially inadequate, and then he decided that at any cost he must get his face away from her staring eyes. She made no movement to turn away. She seemed to be taking him in, recording him, for repetition, greedily, with every fibre of her being.

text: Strange, sharp threads would run through the tale whenever it finally came to be spoken aloud. There was the grizzled smallholder who’d forced Rafe to labor in his turnip rows even when his arm was crumpled and throbbing, who’d jerked his chin at a passing Wehrmacht patrol the moment Rafe tried to protest; there were the shadowy, unnamed figures everyone only referred to as “them,” who’d spent those months smuggling stranded Allied stragglers across the border into the Netherlands. There were the endless, frigid night watches, one after the other, stretching out in the lead-up to the desperate dash for freedom. But Elspeth’s concern was fixed entirely on his hand. Beneath the worn canvas sling there was something that turned the imagination cold, something swathed in discolored bandages, a stump. She could not bear to dwell on it. She could not shake the thought of it from her head.
source_text: There would be queer things in the story when it came to be told. There was an old peasant who had made Teddy work in his fields in spite of his smashed and aching arm, and who had pointed to a passing German when Teddy demurred; there were the people called "they" who had at that time organised the escape of stragglers into Holland. There was the night watch, those long nights in succession before the dash for liberty. But Letty's concern was all with the hand. Inside the sling there was something that hurt the imagination, something bandaged, a stump. She could not think of it. She could not get away from the thought of it.

text: I ought to have prefaced this with a word about these young people. They’re the type we churn out in enormous numbers over here these days, our homegrown counterpart to the old Russian intelligentsia: a feckless, idea-addled segment of the middle class. Leo, you’ll remember, is my secretary. I believe he’s the son of a Walthamstow solicitor, recommended to me by Hale of *The Guardian*. He moved out here and stayed in a set of lodgings for a while, and then, all at once, the young woman turned up.
source_text: "I should have explained these young people. They're the sort of young people we are producing over here now in quite enormous quantity. They are the sort of equivalent of the Russian Intelligentsia, an irresponsible middle class with ideas. Teddy, you know, is my secretary. He's the son, I believe, of a Kilburn solicitor. He was recommended to me by Datcher of _The Times_. He came down here and lived in lodgings for a time. Then suddenly appeared the young lady."

text: With the head of the household out of the way, Mr. Direck expanded his acquaintance with the rest of the Britling family. Mrs. Britling escorted him on a stroll through the grounds, showing off the full array of roses, and just beyond the rose garden, tucked into a small shaded arbour, they came upon Miss Corner deep in a book. She looked exceptionally pretty and grave, absorbed in her reading. Mr. Direck came to a halt directly in front of her, and Mrs. Britling stopped at his side. The young lady lifted her gaze and smiled.
source_text: While he was out of the way Mr. Direck extended his acquaintance with the Britling household. He was taken round the garden and shown the roses by Mrs. Britling, and beyond the rose garden in a little arbour they came upon Miss Corner reading a book. She looked very grave and pretty reading a book. Mr. Direck came to a pause in front of her, and Mrs. Britling stopped beside him. The young lady looked up and smiled.

text: I crashed for thirty straight hours the second I got back here. Right now I’m still slowly processing everything I went through out there, and most of the other fellows in my unit are doing the same. My mind and nerves are pretty frayed and rattled from all the shelling, though not half as bad as you’d probably guess. I get the sense I’ll barely even think much of the whole ordeal before too long. Plenty of our men took the shock of the barrage far worse than I did, though. It hits the older fellows hardest, everyone says that. Men over thirty-five don’t bounce back from a stretch of shelling for weeks on end. They move around… sort of halting, tentative, like they’re not sure what’s coming next.
source_text: "I slept thirty hours after I got back here. And now I am slowly digesting these experiences. Most of our fellows are. My mind and nerves have been rather bumped and bruised by the shelling, but not so much as you might think. I feel as though I'd presently not think very much of it. Some of our men have got the stun of it a lot more than I have. It gets at the older men more. Everybody says that. The men of over thirty-five don't recover from a shelling for weeks. They go about--sort of hesitatingly....

text:
Another thing that strikes me as worth pointing out is how grown adults, for lack of a better term, still seem to prioritize enjoying themselves instead of hanging on the sidelines cheering on young people as they have fun. And the young folks here don’t even seem like they’re trying to have a good time in the first place.

Take someone as lovely as Miss Corner, for instance: back in the States, she’d be so much more conscious of her own energy and appeal than she is here, no question. That little sideways glance of hers, if I can be so forward as to mention it, would’ve been pointed out to her ages ago. It’s such a gorgeous expression, the kind a master painter would jump at the chance to capture forever. I don’t think I’ll ever be able to get it out of my head, honestly.

But she has no idea she even does it, not a clue. In the US, she’d know. She’d know full well. People would’ve made sure she knew. Someone would’ve mentioned it to her outright. Everyone would be waiting for that look, and she’d know they were waiting. She’d serve it up on purpose, the same way a singer trots out her most beloved hit for an adoring crowd. Take Mamie Nelson, for example: she had this little habit of tilting her chin back and laughing a very specific way. Everyone talked about it. People would turn up to events just to see her do it.
source_text: "And another thing I find noteworthy is the way in which what I might call mature people seem to go on having a good time instead of standing by and applauding the young people having a good time.... And the young people do not seem to have set out to have a good time at all.... Now in America, a charming girl like Miss Corner would be distinctly more aware of herself and her vitality than she is here, distinctly more. Her peculiarly charming sidelong look, if I might make so free with her--would have been called attention to. It's a perfectly beautiful look, the sort of look some great artist would have loved to make immortal. It's a look I shall find it hard to forget.... But she doesn't seem to be aware in the least of it. In America she would be aware of it. She would be distinctly aware of it. She would have been _made_ aware of it. She would have been advised of it. It would be looked for and she would know it was looked for. She would _give_ it as a singer gives her most popular song. Mamie Nelson, for example, used to give a peculiar little throw back of the chin and a laugh.... It was talked about. People came to see it....

text: This was Washington Irving’s England, no two ways about it. He couldn’t stay settled in his seat sneaking furtive glances at the view; he had to haul himself upright in the tiny train compartment and press his broad, sharp-featured, warm face out the window, as if he was reaching out to greet the countryside itself. Bathed in golden June sunlight, the landscape was as crisp and luminous as a vintage storybook garden: small cornfields ringed with thick dog rose hedges, stretches of quiet woodland, and tiny rush-fringed pastures so immaculately kept they looked deliberately arranged. He’d already spotted a proper deer park, its rickety iron gates slung between stone pillars topped with carved heraldic shields, and there in the far distance, impossible to miss, was Bracebridge Hall tucked snugly in the shade of towering ancient trees. He’d passed half-timbered cottages with thatched roofs, and six or seven roadside inns with wooden signs that creaked and swayed in the soft breeze. He’d even caught sight of a stout, rosy-cheeked vicar steering his own governess cart down a grass-edged lane, pulled by a plump, shaggy grey pony. None of this felt like the everyday reality he’d known his whole life. It was as if he was traveling directly through the pages of a beloved old book.
source_text: Washington Irving's England! Indeed it was. He couldn't sit still and just peep at it, he had to stand up in the little compartment and stick his large, firm-featured, kindly countenance out of the window as if he greeted it. The country under the June sunshine was neat and bright as an old-world garden, with little fields of corn surrounded by dog-rose hedges, and woods and small rushy pastures of an infinite tidiness. He had seen a real deer park, it had rather tumbledown iron gates between its shield-surmounted pillars, and in the distance, beyond all question, was Bracebridge Hall nestling among great trees. He had seen thatched and timbered cottages, and half-a-dozen inns with creaking signs. He had seen a fat vicar driving himself along a grassy lane in a governess cart drawn by a fat grey pony. It wasn't like any reality he had ever known. It was like travelling in literature.

text: The notion that she believed he ought to enlist in the war made him burn with the self-conscious shame of a renegade; yet her assertion that he was still, at his core, English held him fast, even when his own reason argued against the idea. Adrift amid these tangled, conflicting doubts, he was relieved to stumble on one uncomplicated, neutral task where he could throw himself entirely into standing with and for Cissie.
source_text: Her thought that he ought to go to the war made him feel like a renegade; but her claim that he was somehow still English held him in spite of his reason. In the midst of such perplexities he was glad to find one neutral task wherein he could find himself whole-heartedly with and for Cissie.

text: He clung stubbornly, unwilling to release his dream that German imperialism would crumble catastrophically: a massive, definitive demonstration of the fundamental rot at the core of militarist monarchy, to be followed by a global conference of humbled but hopeful nations, and finally—the dawn of the Millennium. He tried now to convince himself that Mr. Direck had observed poorly and misconstrued everything he had seen. An American, with no experience of military events of any kind, could easily mistake tens of thousands for millions, and the rowdy excitement of a few traveling merchants for the ardent fervor of an entire united people. But the newspapers, sharing that same deep reluctance, were beginning to walk back, bit by bit, their initial portrayal of the German invasion through Belgium as a sprawling, already half-thwarted spectacle of incompetence. He gathered that the Germans were supposedly being beaten again and again across Belgium; yet just as consistently, they pushed forward. Every new town name he looked up on his map to match the latest dispatches marked the slow creep of an oncoming tide. Alost—Charleroi. Further east, the French were retreating from the Saales Pass. Surely the British, who had been deployed in France for two full weeks already, would make their presence known soon, holding back the surging advance; perhaps somewhere in Brabant or East Flanders. Mr. Britling passed a deeply unpleasant night after hearing at Claverings that the French were woefully underequipped; they had no effective modern artillery at either Lille or Maubeuge, were short on boots and all standard supplies, and were already rather demoralized by how the campaign was unfolding. Mr. Britling dismissed this as nothing but pessimistic gossip, and anchored all his hopes to the still unseen British army, hovering out of sight somewhere—
source_text: He was parting very reluctantly from his dream of a disastrous collapse of German imperialism, of a tremendous, decisive demonstration of the inherent unsoundness of militarist monarchy, to be followed by a world conference of chastened but hopeful nations, and--the Millennium. He tried now to think that Mr. Direck had observed badly and misconceived what he saw. An American, unused to any sort of military occurrences, might easily mistake tens of thousands for millions, and the excitement of a few commercial travellers for the enthusiasm of a united people. But the newspapers now, with a kindred reluctance, were beginning to qualify, bit by bit, their first representation of the German attack through Belgium as a vast and already partly thwarted parade of incompetence. The Germans, he gathered, were being continually beaten in Belgium; but just as continually they advanced. Each fresh newspaper name he looked up on the map marked an oncoming tide. Alost--Charleroi. Farther east the French were retreating from the Saales Pass. Surely the British, who had now been in France for a fortnight, would presently be manifest, stemming the onrush; somewhere perhaps in Brabant or East Flanders. It gave Mr. Britling an unpleasant night to hear at Claverings that the French were very ill-equipped; had no good modern guns either at Lille or Maubeuge, were short of boots and equipment generally, and rather depressed already at the trend of things. Mr. Britling dismissed this as pessimistic talk, and built his hopes on the still invisible British army, hovering somewhere--

text: For nearly a week the wider, less self-absorbed reaches of Mr. Thorne’s mind, separate from the sharp, self-preoccupied edge of his daily personal concerns, had been turning over with growing clarity and coherence the sight of global civilisation setting aside the thousand small, scattered routines of peace, gripping its weapons and gritting its jaw for a supreme, all-consuming fight against expansionist military tyranny. From the vantage point of Bramble End, that massive, slow congealing of long-simmering hostilities read for a while as nothing more than a jumble of bold headlines and shuffled column layouts in the sun-bleached newspaper display windows, the panes through which people sheltered by the quiet stability of rural England peered out at the rest of the globe. It was a drama playing out entirely in the realm of newsprint and public debate, impossibly far removed from the soft, living green grass under one’s boots, from the warm baritone of Reverend Vale and the church bells that draped the village in their rich, rolling comfort, from the crack of stags’ antlers as they began rubbing the velvet from their points in the manor park, or the rattle of the butcher’s cart and the polite, cheerful greeting of his apprentice down the lane. That far-off global upheaval felt less tangible to most villagers even than the fictional worlds of the novels they read or the touring plays that came to the town hall each summer. When people chatted about the brewing conflict, there was always a quiet undercurrent of awareness that they were spinning tales and debating abstractions, not confronting something that could touch their own quiet lives.
