source_text: For days the broader side of Mr. Britling's mind, as distinguished from its egotistical edge, had been reflecting more and more vividly and coherently the spectacle of civilisation casting aside the thousand dispersed activities of peace, clutching its weapons and setting its teeth, for a supreme struggle against militarist imperialism. From the point of view of Matching's Easy that colossal crystallising of accumulated antagonisms was for a time no more than a confusion of headlines and a rearrangement of columns in the white windows of the newspapers through which those who lived in the securities of England looked out upon the world. It was a display in the sphere of thought and print immeasurably remote from the real green turf on which one walked, from the voice and the church-bells of Mr. Dimple that sounded their ample caresses in one's ears, from the clashing of the stags who were beginning to knock the velvet from their horns in the park, or the clatter of the butcher's cart and the respectful greeting of the butcher boy down the lane. It was the spectacle of the world less real even to most imaginations than the world of novels or plays. People talked of these things always with an underlying feeling that they romanced and intellectualised.

text: No amount of mental contortion could make him view the two men as sworn enemies. There was no conceivable point of friction between them at all. They shared nearly identical scientific temperaments, after all: Hugh brought more daring, spontaneous inspiration to his work, while Karl leaned into patient compliance and systematic, rigorous method. Until war tore through their lives and hurled them violently against one another…
source_text: By no conceivable mental gymnastics could he think of the two as antagonists. Between them there was no imaginable issue. They had both very much the same scientific disposition; with perhaps more dash and inspiration in the quality of Hugh; more docility and method in the case of Karl. Until war had smashed them one against the other....

text: One grey Saturday afternoon, Mr. Haleworth arrived at Hawthorn Grange beside his wife, Elspeth. Lady Fallowfield, the estate’s owner, was in deep mourning for her two great-nephews, the Merrivale boys, both recently killed—one on the Western Front in Flanders, the other at Suvla Bay during the Gallipoli campaign. Mr. Thwaite, the local antiquarian, was also present, hunched and hollow-eyed with fatigue and quiet despair. Three young men in army khaki lingered near the fireplace, one marked with the scarlet tabs of a staff officer; there were two or three women Mr. Haleworth had never been introduced to, and Miss Elowen Voss, the popular novelist, just returned from three months tending convalescent soldiers in the south of France. But Haleworth’s mood curdled when he saw the gathering was entirely dominated by his long-time adversary, Lady Kershaw: unbowed, unchanged, lording over every conversation, sharp-tongued, arrogant, deliberately cutting to anyone who dared contradict her. She too wore mourning, draped in the most lavish jet-black sable furs Haleworth had ever seen; her broad, triumphant profile jutted from the thick fur collar like the head of a golden eagle from its ruff of neck feathers. Her elder brother was a wounded prisoner of war in Germany, her second brother had been killed at Ypres the year prior, and she carried herself as if her losses were the only meaningful sacrifices the war had exacted from anyone in the whole of the country. She spoke as though her grief granted her the sole, unassailable right to critique the war’s conduct or lay claim to compensation for its harms.
source_text: One Saturday afternoon Mr. Britling found himself with Mrs. Britling at Claverings. Lady Homartyn was in mourning for her two nephews, the Glassington boys, who had both been killed, one in Flanders, the other in Gallipoli. Raeburn was there too, despondent and tired-looking. There were three young men in khaki, one with the red of a staff officer; there were two or three women whom Mr. Britling had not met before, and Miss Sharsper the novelist, fresh from nursing experience among the convalescents in the south of France. But he was disgusted to find that the gathering was dominated by his old antagonist, Lady Frensham, unsubdued, unaltered, rampant over them all, arrogant, impudent, insulting. She was in mourning, she had the most splendid black furs Mr. Britling had ever seen; her large triumphant profile came out of them like the head of a vulture out of its ruff; her elder brother was a wounded prisoner in Germany, her second was dead; it would seem that hers were the only sacrifices the war had yet extorted from any one. She spoke as though it gave her the sole right to criticise the war or claim compensation for the war.

text: For the life of me I can’t bring this up to Clara. I know how silly that sounds. But there’s some block, I just can’t… It wouldn’t be right, not to her. If there was any way to make it easier, I’d try… Not long after we married, I stopped really talking to her. The honest, unfiltered kind of talking, the way I’m speaking to you right now. And that openness never came back. I still can’t put my finger on why… And above all else, I can’t mention Elias to her… Just these small, unspoken shadows of criticism hanging between us, tiny as they are, they make the whole conversation impossible… Half the time I’m walking around turning Elias over in my head, wondering what’s happened to him lately… and it feels like something’s clamped tight around my chest, like I’m stifling.
source_text: "Somehow I can't talk about this to Edith. It is ridiculous, I know. But in some way I can't.... It isn't fair to her. If I could, I would.... Quite soon after we were married I ceased to talk to her. I mean talking really and simply--as I do to you. And it's never come back. I don't know why.... And particularly I can't talk to her of Hugh.... Little things, little shadows of criticism, but enough to make it impossible.... And I go about thinking about Hugh, and what has happened to him sometimes... as though I was stifling."

text: Letty tramped through the small patch of woods and clambered over the stile, and the cottage popped immediately into her line of sight. Tucked under the thick, overhanging bough of the massive walnut tree, the cottage windows blazed bright, lit up golden by the full force of the sun. The crimson rambler twining over the front porch—the one she and Teddy had planted together—was still heavy with open, blushing roses. The door stood wide open, and she could see people moving to and fro just inside the entryway.
source_text: Letty came through the little wood and over the stile that brought her into sight of the cottage. The windows of the cottage as she saw it under the bough of the big walnut tree, were afire from the sun. The crimson rambler over the porch that she and Teddy had planted was still bearing roses. The door was open and people were moving in the porch.

text: You know, no one wants to use words that have been stretched into a thousand different meanings unless they’re being perfectly unambiguous about their intent, and at the same time no one wants to come off as sanctimonious, or pitch any thought a note or two higher than it honestly deserves to land. But all the same, it seems to me that this intangible something Mr. Britling is always calling for in his essays and all his writing, the thing you’re seeking just as fiercely—so important to you that even love feels secondary until you can align the two—is nothing short of Religion. I don’t mean this creed or that denomination, just Religion itself: a vast, reverent, all-encompassing idea that binds you, me, and every person on earth to one single, magnificent universal order. And though it’s not exactly the kind of approach to romance that’s been popular for ages in places like Carrierville, it’s the right one; we have to live by it if we don’t want courtship to turn into some lazy, aggravating game of small treats and empty flattery that’s all but guaranteed to lead straight to disappointment, thoughtless foolishness, infidelity, and—plain and simple, hell. From what I can tell, what you’re getting at is that marriage has to be a religious union, or else you’re tearing your life apart at the seams. Religion and love make up most of life, and all of its real power, and they cannot afford to be harnessed in two separate directions. I never held any of these ideas before I came here and met you, but now they rise up in my mind as though they’ve been rooted there my whole life. That’s why you don’t want to rush into getting married. That’s why I’m almost glad you don’t want to rush into getting married, too.
source_text: "You see one doesn't want to use terms that have been used in a thousand different senses in any way that isn't a perfectly unambiguous sense, and at the same time one doesn't want to seem to be canting about things or pitching anything a note or two higher than it ought legitimately to go, but it seems to me that this sort of something that Mr. Britling is always asking for in his essays and writings and things, and what you are looking for just as much and which seems so important to you that even love itself is a secondary kind of thing until you can square the two together, is nothing more nor less than Religion--I don't mean this Religion or that Religion but just Religion itself, a Big, Solemn, Comprehensive Idea that holds you and me and all the world together in one great, grand universal scheme. And though it isn't quite the sort of idea of love-making that's been popular--well, in places like Carrierville--for some time, it's the right idea; it's got to be followed out if we don't want love-making to be a sort of idle, troublesome game of treats and flatteries that is sure as anything to lead right away to disappointments and foolishness and unfaithfulness and--just Hell. What you are driving at, according to my interpretation, is that marriage has got to be a religious marriage or else you are splitting up life, that religion and love are most of life and all the power there is in it, and that they can't afford to be harnessed in two different directions.... I never had these ideas until I came here and met you, but they come up now in my mind as though they had always been there.... And that's why you don't want to marry in a hurry. And that's why I'm glad almost that you don't want to marry in a hurry."

text: There was no question she belonged in the frame, so she stepped into it like she was its entire focal point, like she was the living embodiment of quintessential Englishness. There she stood, carrying all the hallmarks, the lineage, the familiar bearing you’d spot in countless longstanding Massachusetts families—cut from exactly the same stock, by all appearances, and yet she was still unmistakably different.
source_text: She had to be in the picture, and so she came in as though she were the central figure, as though she were the quintessential England. There she was, the type, the blood, the likeness, of no end of Massachusetts families, the very same stuff indeed, and yet she was different....

text: He walked to the farthest corner of the hockey field, wandered aimlessly there for a few minutes before coming to a stop, both hands curled around the fence slats as he stared unseeing into the thick darkness for a long stretch. When he finally turned to leave, he stumbled clumsily in the direction of the rose garden, tripping over uneven ground as he went. A trailing vine caught at his cheek, leaving a sharp, stinging scrape that made him flinch in irritation; he swiped it away roughly, only for its tiny thorns to catch the soft skin of his palm. He picked his way to the bench tucked inside the arbour, sank down onto its slats, mumbled a few half-coherent words to himself, then went completely still, one arm slung over the bench’s back and his head resting heavy on his forearm.
source_text: He went into the far corner of the hockey paddock, and there he moved about for a while and then stood for a long time holding the fence with both hands and staring blankly into the darkness. At last he turned away, and went stumbling and blundering towards the rose garden. A spray of creeper tore his face and distressed him. He thrust it aside fretfully, and it scratched his hand. He made his way to the seat in the arbour, and sat down and whispered a little to himself, and then became very still with his arm upon the back of the seat and his head upon his arm.

text: He pulled his car into the garage, then circled around the side of the house under the deep, inky shadow cast by his cedar trees to get to the front door. He didn’t have his key on him, and for what felt like ages, he couldn’t rouse his wife no matter how many pebbles and bits of loose gravel he tossed at her half-open bedroom window. Finally, though, he caught the faint sound of her stirring inside, and called up to her.
source_text: He put his automobile in the garage, and then went round under the black shadow of his cedars to the front door. He had no key, and for a long time he failed to rouse his wife by flinging pebbles and gravel at her half-open window. But at last he heard her stirring and called out to her.

text: Why—he dragged back up that searing, bitter question that had gnawed at him through a thousand and one miserable, sleepless nights—why was he such a fool? Such a rash, impulsive fool? Why could he never pause to look before he threw himself headlong into things? Why did he court unnecessary risks at every turn? Why was he always so quick to act on the untested assumption that everything was certain to go his way? (He might as well have asked why he had those quick, warm brown eyes, for all the answer he would ever get.)
source_text: Why--he revived again that bitter question of a thousand and one unhappy nights--why was he such a fool? Such a hasty fool? Why couldn't he look before he leapt? Why did he take risks? Why was he always so ready to act upon the supposition that all was bound to go well? (He might as well have asked why he had quick brown eyes.)

text: Mr. Britling’s gaze drifted far out over the hills, and he seemed to turn a thought over slowly in his head. “Yeah,” he said. “Maybe not today—maybe not anytime soon, not in any steady, consistent way. But kings fall and empires crumble; great ideas, once they take root in the world, can never be snuffed out entirely. At the end of it all, this world republic, this sensible, rational system of governing the whole globe, is as guaranteed as the sun setting each night. Only….”
source_text: Mr. Britling, with his eyes far away over the hills, seemed to think. "Yes," he said. "Not perhaps to-day--not steadily. But kings and empires die; great ideas, once they are born, can never die again. In the end this world-republic, this sane government of the world, is as certain as the sunset. Only...."

text: White-hot fury gnawed at him at the thought of the hostile dirigible slipping away, high and remote in the ink-dark sky, shrinking to a faint pinprick before it vanished among the scattered stars, those cold-blooded killers onboard slipping clean out of his reach. Again and again he stopped short, shaking a clenched fist at Ursa Major as it crept steadily upward across the wide arc of the night sky.
source_text: He was maddened by the thought of the Zeppelin making off, high and far in the sky, a thing dwindling to nothing among the stars, and the thought of those murderers escaping him. Time after time he stood still and shook his fist at Boötes, slowly sweeping up the sky....

text: “That’s right. Manning is a London-based journalist, with a tiny cottage roughly a mile off in that direction,” Mr. Britling said, gesturing vaguely into the distance. “He comes down here for weekends, see. Now Rendezvous has decided he’s woefully out of shape—and for Rendezvous, being fit is the entire sum and purpose of life. Fitness, that’s his whole creed: an almost rock-hard, unyielding quality, relentless physical restlessness, total uncomplicated clarity of mind. So he takes poor old Manning in tow and makes him trot those full fourteen miles, steady at four miles an hour. The first eight to ten miles are pure torture for Manning, every bit as bad as the torments of hell—he’s half melted with sweat, gasping, dragging his feet like lead. But I’ll admit Rendezvous’ theory sort of proves out by the end. Come afternoon, you’ll find him laid up in a hammock with blistered feet, but otherwise unusually bright and well. Still, he dodges these forced marches whenever he can. He even hides to get out of them.”
source_text: "Yes. Manning is a London journalist. He has a little cottage about a mile over there"--Mr. Britling pointed vaguely--"and he comes down for the week-ends. And Rendezvous has found out he isn't fit. And everybody ought to be fit. That is the beginning and end of life for Rendezvous. Fitness. An almost mineral quality, an insatiable activity of body, great mental simplicity. So he takes possession of poor old Manning and trots him for that fourteen miles--at four miles an hour. Manning goes through all the agonies of death and damnation, he half dissolves, he pants and drags for the first eight or ten miles, and then I must admit he rather justifies Rendezvous' theory. He is to be found in the afternoon in a hammock suffering from blistered feet, but otherwise unusually well. But if he can escape it, he does. He hides."

text: He stirred awake to find his morning tea set beside his bed, the housemaid just slipping out through the bedroom door. Some quiet part of him registered that a world-altering event had occurred, but for a few disoriented seconds he could not call it to mind. Then it flooded back: Germany had invaded France, Russia had crossed into German territory, and England was all but guaranteed to enter the fray. In the pale morning light, the truth felt raw and unthinkable: it would demand crippling strain, it promised unavoidable ruin, stripped entirely of the somber, stately gravity it had carried when he’d heard the news the night before. He recognized this same sharp, gnawing dread of the unknown from fifteen years earlier, in the lull right after the Boer War was declared, before any frontline dispatches had arrived. The first report from that conflict had been of a British armoured train wrecked near Kimberley. What equally devastating, unforeseen tidings might the overdue morning paper hold when it finally arrived?
source_text: He woke to find his morning tea beside him and the housemaid going out of the room. He knew that something stupendous had happened to the world, but for a few moments he could not remember what it was. Then he remembered that France was invaded by Germany and Germany by Russia, and that almost certainly England was going to war. It seemed a harsh and terrible fact in the morning light, a demand for stresses, a certainty of destruction; it appeared now robbed of all the dark and dignified beauty of the night. He remembered just the same feeling of unpleasant, anxious expectation as he now felt when the Boer War had begun fifteen years ago, before the first news came. The first news of the Boer War had been the wrecking of a British armoured train near Kimberley. What similar story might not the overdue paper tell when presently it came?

text: It will strike the gentler, more fortunate readers of a coming era as nearly unbelievable—if this tattered account of lived experience ever finds its way to anyone in those far-off days—that so much of Elias Halewood’s inner life at this moment was consumed by the raw, unvarnished atrocity of the conflict raging around him. There is no use speculating now how those future audiences will frame this war; it may harden into sweeping, heroic dramatic contours, it may come to be regarded as a just, logical, unavoidable step, the purging of a hundred old barriers, the shattering of long-standing obstacles to human progress. But Halewood was far too near the muck, the agony, the searing chaos of it all to take any solace from those distant, lofty interpretations. Each fresh day brought some new small, harrowing detail that burrowed deep into his thoughts. Sometimes it was the unvarnished account from a Belgian refugee who had settled in the village. There was a nineteen-year-old girl from Dendermonde staying with the local baker, for instance, who had heard the crackle of rifle fire that marked the execution of townsfolk, people she had grown up next door to and played with as a child. She had seen the still damp, blood-darkened stone wall where her two murdered uncles had been shot, the scuffed, rust-streaked mud where their bodies had been hauled away by soldiers. Three German enlisted men had been billeted in her home with her and her bedridden father, and had spoken openly of the mass killings they had been ordered to carry out. One of them, a former primary school teacher from Hamburg, had admitted he had been forced to shoot a young mother and her infant son. The girl had stared at him, disbelieving. Yes, he had done it, he had insisted. What choice had he had? His commanding officer had stood right over him, pistol aimed at his head if he refused. He had no option but to follow orders. But in the months since, he had not slept a full night through, had never been able to scrub the memory from behind his eyes.
source_text: It will be an incredible thing to the happier reader of a coming age--if ever this poor record of experience reaches a reader in the days to come--to learn how much of the mental life of Mr. Britling was occupied at this time with the mere horror and atrocity of warfare. It is idle and hopeless to speculate now how that future reader will envisage this war; it may take on broad dramatic outlines, it may seem a thing, just, logical, necessary, the burning of many barriers, the destruction of many obstacles. Mr. Britling was too near to the dirt and pain and heat for any such broad landscape consolations. Every day some new detail of evil beat into his mind. Now it would be the artless story of some Belgian refugee. There was a girl from Alost in the village for example, who had heard the fusillade that meant the shooting of citizens, the shooting of people she had known, she had seen the still blood-stained wall against which two murdered cousins had died, the streaked sand along which their bodies had been dragged; three German soldiers had been quartered in her house with her and her invalid mother, and had talked freely of the massacres in which they had been employed. One of them was in civil life a young schoolmaster, and he had had, he said, to kill a woman and a baby. The girl had been incredulous. Yes, he had done so! Of course he had done so! His officer had made him do it, had stood over him. He could do nothing but obey. But since then he had been unable to sleep, unable to forget.

text: The government hadn’t issued any formal call to action, nor set up any framework for widespread public participation in the war effort. People spoke without reserve, it seemed like absolutely everyone had something to say on the matter; they waved flags and showed plenty of vague, eager willingness to pitch in however they could. Every opening to serve was snapped up immediately, no questions asked. Word had it Lord Kitchener had requested half a million volunteers; the War Office’s recruitment plans, which had been drawn up for a far smaller turnout, were almost instantly swamped by a flood of eager young men signing up. Officials had to stem the tide by hiking the physical fitness requirements well above the typical national baseline, and eventually recruitment numbers slowed to a level they could handle. A great many people honestly held the view that it was all too easy to fixate entirely on the war; for the vast majority of the public, it felt like more of an upsetting, disruptive distraction than a core, personal priority. The slogan “Business as Usual” spread like wildfire across the country and beyond, and newspapers were full of pieces arguing that carrying on with daily life exactly as if no conflict was unfolding was the most genuine expression of patriotism possible. “Leave it all to Kitchener” was another popular catchphrase, one that resonated deeply with the prevailing national temperament. One upbeat local barber even put up an advertisement reading “Business as usual during alterations to the map of Europe”, and the line was shared and quoted far and wide.
source_text: There was no invitation from the government and no organisation for any general participation in war. People talked unrestrictedly; every one seemed to be talking; they waved flags and displayed much vague willingness to do something. Any opportunity of service was taken very eagerly. Lord Kitchener was understood to have demanded five hundred thousand men; the War Office arrangements for recruiting, arrangements conceived on a scale altogether too small, were speedily overwhelmed by a rush of willing young men. The flow had to be checked by raising the physical standard far above the national average, and recruiting died down to manageable proportions. There was a quite genuine belief that the war might easily be too exclusively considered; that for the great mass of people it was a disturbing and distracting rather than a vital interest. The phase "Business as Usual" ran about the world, and the papers abounded in articles in which going on as though there was no war at all was demonstrated to be the truest form of patriotism. "Leave things to Kitchener" was another watchword with a strong appeal to the national quality. "Business as usual during Alterations to the Map of Europe" was the advertisement of one cheerful barber, widely quoted....

text: Mr. Britling pressed for concrete details about the late Lord Chancellor’s supposed acts of treason. No such specifics existed at all. The whole story was nothing more than a notion the well-meaning lady had latched onto, an idea that had worked its way into the minds of a great many easily influenced people. Lady Frensham insisted there was only one strong, steadfast man left in the entire country right now, and that man was Sir Edward Carson.
source_text: Mr. Britling pleaded for particulars of the late Lord Chancellor's treasons. There were no particulars. It was just an idea the good lady had got into her head, that had got into a number of accessible heads. There was only one strong man in all the country now, Lady Frensham insisted. That was Sir Edward Carson.

text: Be it drawn out or swift, this conflict will end in Germany’s total collapse. Yet I cannot see it dragging on for years. I don’t share Hale’s estimation on that front. Even now, I refuse to accept that an entire, proud nation could be wholly consumed by bloodlust and fever for conquest. This conflict, to my mind, is the doing of Germany’s arms manufacturing lobby and the monarchist court faction. They are the ones who shoved this war down the German people’s throats. Make no mistake—they have to keep notching victories, one after the other. For as long as they come out on top, Germany will stay unified; as long as their ground forces suffer no unambiguous rout, and their fleet remains intact. But the moment their advance is halted, their momentum broken, and their forces routed, I am convinced Germany’s national mood will shift overnight, just as it did in the wake of Jena....
source_text: "Long or short, it will end in the downfall of Germany. But I do not believe it will be long. I do not agree with Manning. Even now I cannot believe that a whole great people can be possessed by war madness. I think the war is the work of the German armaments party and of the Court party. They have forced this war on Germany. Well--they must win and go on winning. So long as they win, Germany will hold together, so long as their armies are not clearly defeated nor their navy destroyed. But once check them and stay them and beat them, then I believe that suddenly the spirit of Germany will change even as it changed after Jena...."

text: “I’m here drafting the true, living map of the whole globe,” he remarked. “Every region with its own distinct cultural character gets to set its own local rules, and the grand global federated republic will uphold shared, impartial peace between every one of them. That’s the simple, unvarnished truth of how life ought to work: a unified world commonwealth. Why waste our loyalty on any smaller, pettier governing body when that’s the only cause worth standing for? All it takes is enough people standing together to demand it, and that republic would be ours tomorrow. Why have we dragged our feet for so long, letting these cruel, avoidable tragedies strike us first? We just need to carve the globe into its natural self-governing states, lay out the structure of its shared governance, and write the terms that protect mutual tolerance across every border.”
source_text: "Here I am planning the real map of the world," he said. "Every sort of district that has a character of its own must have its own rule; and the great republic of the united states of the world must keep the federal peace between them all. That's the plain sense of life; the federal world-republic. Why do we bother ourselves with loyalties to any other government but that? It needs only that sufficient men should say it, and that republic would be here now. Why have we loitered so long--until these tragic punishments come? We have to map the world out into its states, and plan its government and the way of its tolerations."

text: For all his visceral loathing of armed conflict, Mr. Hale could never bring himself to argue that every state of peace was inherently preferable to a state of war. If war spawned unspeakable cruelty and razed whole communities to ash, unchallenged stagnant peace could breed listless apathy, moral perversity, predatory hoarding of wealth, and self-serving excess that ate away at societies from the inside out. War forces people to confront their worst impulses through harsh, unforgiving discipline, but long unexamined peace can let even the most virtuous grow complacent and slip away from their better nature. The laborer scraping by on meager wages knows as much hunger and despair in a time of supposed peace as he does when bombs fall on neighboring towns. The festering injustices of a corrupt peace and the senseless brutality and waste of war are nothing more than two faces of the same flawed coin, minted from the deep misalignment of how human beings treat one another. Might there not be a truer, Greater Peace waiting to be won? Not merely a recuperative breath between rounds of slaughter and ruin, but an era of noble, purposeful creation: a time of building homes and communities, of bold discovery, of cultivated beauty and relentless, curious inquiry. He recalled, with the soft, heavy tenderness of someone grieving a lost loved one, the old dreams he’d once held: of glittering, equitable cities, of radical, sweeping freedoms for all, of a coming age where human potential would unfurl in unthinkable ways, where science would be a lantern for the lost and art would be a force to heal and lift up whole peoples.
source_text: In spite of his detestation of war Mr. Britling found it impossible to maintain that any sort of peace state was better than a state of war. If wars produced destructions and cruelties, peace could produce indolence, perversity, greedy accumulation and selfish indulgences. War is discipline for evil, but peace may be relaxation from good. The poor man may be as wretched in peace time as in war time. The gathering forces of an evil peace, the malignity and waste of war, are but obverse and reverse of the medal of ill-adjusted human relationships. Was there no Greater Peace possible; not a mere recuperative pause in killing and destruction, but a phase of noble and creative living, a phase of building, of discovery, of beauty and research? He remembered, as one remembers the dead, dreams he had once dreamt of the great cities, the splendid freedoms, of a coming age, of marvellous enlargements of human faculty, of a coming science that would be light and of art that could be power....

text: Gladys had been left stranded in a ditch, and the pair of them had gotten a kind ride home from a passing motorist. Cecily was at the Dower House when they showed up, looking thoroughly rueful about the whole messy ordeal. She witnessed firsthand how unflappably an American can bear their injuries. She offered them all the sympathy and practical help they needed, and wove in open, genuine admiration that made her kindness feel even more warm and delightful.
source_text: Gladys had been abandoned in the ditch, and they had been very kindly brought home by a passing automobile. Cecily had been at the Dower House at the moment of the rueful arrival. She had seen how an American can carry injuries. She had made sympathy and helpfulness more delightful by expressed admiration.

text: “Every one of us has to tend to the work we’re capable of,” he mused, and for a stretch the work of guiding his sedan along a twisting downhill country road occupied him so fully he could not spare a thought for anything beyond the next curve. He swung the car around, climbed back over the hill’s crest, and coasted down long sloping stretches further inland, the engine purring so softly and smoothly he could barely hear it, his headlights gobbling up the dark pavement ahead and raking over the earthen banks and brambled hedges flanking the lane. As he drifted down a small rise through a huddled hamlet, he caught the jumbled clatter and tinkle of moving traffic up ahead, and spotted the fluorescent danger triangle posted to warn of an upcoming crossroads. He eased off the accelerator, then pulled to a sharp, sudden stop.
source_text: "We must all do the thing we can," he thought, and for a time the course of his automobile along a winding down-hill road held his attention so that he could not get beyond it. He turned about and ran up over the hill again and down long slopes inland, running very softly and smoothly with his lights devouring the road ahead and sweeping the banks and hedges beside him, and as he came down a little hill through a village he heard a confused clatter and jingle of traffic ahead, and saw the danger triangle that warns of cross-roads. He slowed down and then pulled up abruptly.

text: At the start, you think the only thing standing between us and them is a language barrier. Pretty soon, though, you realize language is the smallest of the gaps between us. The folks here operate on a set of core beliefs totally unlike our own, beliefs that are far more rigid, far more fully formed than anything we hold to. You’d picture our home back in Antwerp as something far more self-contained, far more shut off from the outside world—its own little closed space, a proper tight-knit social unit, nothing at all like this loose, open gathering spot we’re in now. Our boys will happily play with anyone who turns up, but little André has never even learned to play with kids who aren’t part of his immediate inner circle. To the Van der Pant family, we must come off as ridiculously unguarded, like a house with no walls at all… Last Sunday I never even learned the names of the two girls who arrived on bicycles and joined the game so well. They’d come with Kitty Westropp, and Van der Pant kept pressing to know how they were related to us, or what explanation there was for them being there in the first place.
source_text: "At first you imagine there is nothing separating us but language. Presently you find that language is the least of our separations. These people are people living upon fundamentally different ideas from ours, ideas far more definite and complete than ours. You imagine that home in Antwerp as something much more rounded off, much more closed in, a cell, a real social unit, a different thing altogether from this place of meeting. Our boys play cheerfully with all comers; little André hasn't learnt to play with any outside children at all. We must seem incredibly _open_ to these Van der Pants. A house without sides.... Last Sunday I could not find out the names of the two girls who came on bicycles and played so well. They came with Kitty Westropp. And Van der Pant wanted to know how they were related to us. Or how was it they came?...

text: “No,” she said, “it will only bring Hugh and the way he died right back to the front of his mind. Don’t go out there, Teddy. Not right now. What does he want with your company, anyway? Let him rest from those sharp, painful thoughts for a while. Leave him to pore over his atlas, lost in his own quiet daydreams. He isn’t half as desolate as he looks, if you only knew. I’ll explain everything to you, Teddy… when I can.”
source_text: "No," she said; "it will only make him think again of Hugh--and how he died. Don't go out, Teddy. Not now. What does he care for _you_?... Let him rest from such things.... Leave him to dream over his atlas.... He isn't so desolate--if you knew.... I will tell you, Teddy--when I can....

text: At the outset, Mrs. Britling had been giving nearly her full attention to Mr. Direck as he spoke, but just as he was getting into the core of his opening remarks, the meal laid out on the blue linen table moved into a new phase that demanded more and more of her active direction. The two little boys appeared out of nowhere right at her elbows. “Can we take the plates to get the strawberries, Mummy?” they asked in perfect unison. Next, she had to call over one of the crisply put-together maids lingering in the background and murmur quiet instructions to her, and Mr. Direck caught on that for the time being, she was not going to be available to listen to Robinson’s illuminating anecdote. A little thrown off, but fully understanding how these domestic interruptions go, he turned to the guest seated to his left....
source_text: At first Mrs. Britling had listened to Mr. Direck with an almost undivided attention, but as he had developed his opening the feast upon the blue linen table had passed on to a fresh phase that demanded more and more of her directive intelligence. The two little boys appeared suddenly at her elbows. "Shall we take the plates and get the strawberries, Mummy?" they asked simultaneously. Then one of the neat maids in the background had to be called up and instructed in undertones, and Mr. Direck saw that for the present Robinson's illuminating experience was not for her ears. A little baffled, but quite understanding how things were, he turned to his neighbour on his left....

text:
Mr. Thorne bore no resemblance at all to the figure Mr. Hale had pictured ahead of their meeting. He’d envisioned a proper English country gentleman clad in golfing tweeds, the exact sort that populated the bucolic panels of American serialized story magazines. Beneath those tweeds he’d imagined the softly melancholic, brooding face, down to its sagging moustache, that Mr. Thorne’s publisher had for some baffling, ill-advised reason circulated widely to readers across the Atlantic. Instead, Mr. Thorne wore a hodgepodge of garments, and mildness was the furthest trait anyone might ascribe to him. His moustache, hair, and thick eyebrows all stood on end; his ruddy, freckled cheeks looked as if they might bristle too. His small hazel eyes snapped forward, fixing Mr. Hale with a sharp, almost playful ping of focus.

Mr. Thorne belonged to a large but singular group of people who seem to rearrange their hair, their attire, even their entire persona the moment a camera is pointed at them. No photographer had ever managed to capture a glimmer of his true, bristling Thorne energy. Only the lens could convince him to smooth his unruly hair, and only for the camera did he reserve that meek, long-suffering expression Mr. Hale recognized from the publicity shots. Mr. Hale was also wholly unprepared for the unstudied sloppiness of dress that sometimes overtook the writer. He wore a faded, much-worn blue flannel blazer, no cap, and a pair of tweed knickerbockers far baggier than standard riding breeches, woven from one of those earnest, socialist-adjacent homespun fabrics that pulls into fuzzy knots and loose threads anywhere it rubs against another surface. His thick wool stockings were bunched and creased around his ankles, and on his feet were those odd, brightly colored woven slippers made of a bast-like fiber sold in small towns across northern France. These were a deep purple streaked with flecks of moss green.

He’d remembered he needed to meet Mr. Hale at the station with barely a minute to spare, and had rushed straight from his home office in the rumpled clothes he’d pulled on right after waking that morning. His face held the eager, affable expression of a wirehaired terrier inclined to greet a stranger warmly, and it startled Mr. Hale to realize that for a man of such renowned intellectual stature, Mr. Thorne was surprisingly short.
source_text: Mr. Britling did not look at all as Mr. Direck had expected him to look. He had expected an Englishman in a country costume of golfing tweeds, like the Englishman in country costume one sees in American illustrated stories. Drooping out of the country costume of golfing tweeds he had expected to see the mildly unhappy face, pensive even to its drooping moustache, with which Mr. Britling's publisher had for some faulty and unfortunate reason familiarised the American public. Instead of this, Mr. Britling was in a miscellaneous costume, and mildness was the last quality one could attribute to him. His moustache, his hair, his eyebrows bristled; his flaming freckled face seemed about to bristle too. His little hazel eyes came out with a "ping" and looked at Mr. Direck. Mr. Britling was one of a large but still remarkable class of people who seem at the mere approach of photography to change their hair, their clothes, their moral natures. No photographer had ever caught a hint of his essential Britlingness and bristlingness. Only the camera could ever induce Mr. Britling to brush his hair, and for the camera alone did he reserve that expression of submissive martyrdom Mr. Direck knew. And Mr. Direck was altogether unprepared for a certain casualness of costume that sometimes overtook Mr. Britling. He was wearing now a very old blue flannel blazer, no hat, and a pair of knickerbockers, not tweed breeches but tweed knickerbockers of a remarkable bagginess, and made of one of those virtuous socialistic homespun tweeds that drag out into woolly knots and strings wherever there is attrition. His stockings were worsted and wrinkled, and on his feet were those extraordinary slippers of bright-coloured bast-like interwoven material one buys in the north of France. These were purple with a touch of green. He had, in fact, thought of the necessity of meeting Mr. Direck at the station at the very last moment, and had come away from his study in the clothes that had happened to him when he got up. His face wore the amiable expression of a wire-haired terrier disposed to be friendly, and it struck Mr. Direck that for a man of his real intellectual distinction Mr. Britling was unusually short.

text: Mr. Britling’s rush of relief didn’t make it through the whole week. The Germans, despite their earlier losses, continued to advance. Across seven days of deepening disillusionment, the main tide of the battle rolled steadily back toward Paris. Lille fell without a single fight, lost with a strange, unaccountable ease. The next place name that jolted Mr. Britling, as he sat poring over his newspaper and atlas to track these massive unfolding events, was Compiègne. “Right here.” It was plain as day that the British were still in retreat. Then came word that the Germans had seized Laon and Rheims, and were still pressing southward. Maubeuge, which had been surrounded and cut off for several days already, had apparently fallen as well.
source_text: Mr. Britling's mood of relief did not last through the week. The defeated Germans continued to advance. Through a week of deepening disillusionment the main tide of battle rolled back steadily towards Paris. Lille was lost without a struggle. It was lost with mysterious ease.... The next name to startle Mr. Britling as he sat with newspaper and atlas following these great events was Compiègne. "Here!" Manifestly the British were still in retreat. Then the Germans were in possession of Laon and Rheims and still pressing south. Maubeuge surrounded and cut off for some days, had apparently fallen....

text: Soon Mr. Thorne could no longer gauge the time remaining to him at the Hawthorn Dower House in days, only in dwindling, slipping hours. His trunks sat half-closed by his bedroom door, most of his effects already stowed inside, and his tickets for Rotterdam, Cologne, Munich, Dresden, and Vienna were tucked neatly in his overcoat’s inner pocket, all in perfect order. Yet the matter between him and Elowen was still painfully unwritten, no clear resolution to the quiet pull that had tangled between them for weeks. But the Almighty had not fashioned Americans to be sharp-jawed and clean-shaven for no end, and he resolved he would bring their unspoken feelings to some clear conclusion before he embarked on a trip that was rapidly losing all its appeal, now that every corner of his attention was fixed on this one unresolved thing.
source_text: Soon Mr. Direck could measure the time that was left to him at the Dower House no longer by days but by hours. His luggage was mostly packed, his tickets to Rotterdam, Cologne, Munich, Dresden, Vienna, were all in order. And things were still very indefinite between him and Cecily. But God has not made Americans clean-shaven and firm-featured for nothing, and he determined that matters must be brought to some sort of definition before he embarked upon travels that were rapidly losing their attractiveness in this concentration of his attention....

text: There was no cause for despair, for he counted himself among the world’s feeble, unassuming souls. Truly, God abided with him, and he abided in God. The King was coming to claim what was rightfully his. Amid the darkness and disarray, the nightmarish cruelties and grotesque, senseless folly of the great war, God, Captain of the World Republic, fought his way to rightful empire. As long as one gave their full, unreserved effort to such a mighty cause, did the smallness and plainness of the task they carried out really hold any weight?
source_text: There was no need to despair because he himself was one of the feeble folk. God was with him indeed, and he was with God. The King was coming to his own. Amidst the darknesses and confusions, the nightmare cruelties and the hideous stupidities of the great war, God, the Captain of the World Republic, fought his way to empire. So long as one did one's best and utmost in a cause so mighty, did it matter though the thing one did was little and poor?

text:
All the boys were whooping so loud you could barely hear the wind over the noise. Hanging steady against the pale, streaked sky was one of our aircraft, so solid and sure you itched to stretch up and give its fuselage a fond thump. It climbed higher then, veering straight for the German lines, as if it was sniffing out another enemy craft to chase. It moved almost lazily, like it had all the time in the world. For a minute we weren’t even sure it was ours; our fighters are painted in the same red-white-blue tricolor as the French ones, easy to mix up from the ground. But everyone agreed after a minute it was British, one of our top ace squadrons’ craft, with seven confirmed German downings to its name since it first deployed.

That was the sum of the action that day, though. There had been fierce fighting here, a full month prior. Maybe twelve German dead still lay out in no-man’s-land, crumpled small and twisted, like fallen scarecrows tossed aside by a storm, only a hundred yards beyond our trench parapet. That was all there was to it.
source_text: "But all the chaps were cheering.... And there was our machine hanging in the sky. You wanted to reach up and pat it on the back. It went up higher and away towards the German lines, as though it was looking for another German. It seemed to go now quite slowly. It was an English machine, though for a time we weren't sure; our machines are done in tri-colour just as though they were French. But everybody says it was English. It was one of our crack fighting machines, and from first to last it has put down seven Germans.... And that's really all the fighting there was. There has been fighting here; a month ago. There are perhaps a dozen dead Germans lying out still in front of the lines. Little twisted figures, like overthrown scarecrows, about a hundred yards away. But that is all.

text: Afterward she apologized with such tender, abashed earnestness, saying that if only he would still love her, she would suffer a thousand Elspeths without a word of protest. He set aside the faint, humorous disrespect implicit in framing the sentiment as suffering Elspeths, and they shared a gentle, perfect reconciliation, speaking for hours of lofty, meaningful things. That evening he worked with rare focus on *The Hushed Thresholds*, turning up half a dozen startlingly lovely lines that felt almost too good to be his own. By the middle of the next day, he had returned to Grange Manor, to Mr. Hale, to tottering stacks of unaddressed correspondence, and to the final completion of his Highland land reform pamphlet.
source_text: Afterwards she repented very touchingly, and said that if only he would love her she would swallow a thousand Ediths. He waived a certain disrespect in the idea of her swallowing Edith, and they had a beautiful reconciliation and talked of exalted things, and in the evening he worked quite well upon "The Silent Places" and thought of half-a-dozen quite wonderful lines, and in the course of the next day he returned to Dower House and Mr. Direck and considerable piles of correspondence and the completion of the Irish pamphlet.

text: Most importantly, I’m sending along his violin—the very instrument he asked me three separate times to get to you. It’s either a gift you gave him in the past, or it held such deep meaning for him as a symbol of everything he tied to home and to you. I’ll pack it with extraordinary care, and I’ll do everything I can to guarantee it arrives safely and intact.
source_text: _Especially I am sending his violin, which he had asked me thrice to convey to you. Either it is a gift from you or it symbolised many things for him that he connected with home and you. I will have it packed with particular care, and I will do all in my power to ensure its safe arrival._

text: Sometimes I’m terrified by how little I can parse what goes on inside my son’s head. I have no clue what his spiritual beliefs even are. He’s my flesh and blood, so I know he must hold some kind of faith, even if I never see it. I don’t know what he thinks about sex, or how much he’s figured out about that whole messy, confusing side of being alive. I can’t even name the small, ordinary things that make his chest feel light, the things he considers beautiful. Every now and then he lets a small detail slip, and I can make a guess, but that’s all I ever get—fleeting hints, nothing solid. Look, you don’t actually understand what love is until you have a kid. That romantic love for women? It’s not the same thing, not even close. It’s all give and take, a transaction at the end of the day. You can have these huge, roaring thrills, big hopes for the future, all-consuming want, and that’s fine, it has its place. But the love you feel for your child? It’s this soft, sharp ache that wraps around your whole chest, so tender it feels like it could split you open. It’s something holy, straight from God. Some nights I lie awake, reaching out into the dark for him, this boy of mine—he’ll never get how much I care, not really, not until he has sons of his own, years from now.
source_text: "I am frightened at times at all I don't know about in that boy's mind. I know nothing of his religiosities. He's my son and he must have religiosities. I know nothing of his ideas or of his knowledge about sex and all that side of life. I do not know of the things he finds beautiful. I can guess at times; that's all; when he betrays himself.... You see, you don't know really what love is until you have children. One doesn't love women. Indeed you don't! One gives and gets; it's a trade. One may have tremendous excitements and expectations and overwhelming desires. That's all very well in its way. But the love of children is an exquisite tenderness: it rends the heart. It's a thing of God. And I lie awake at nights and stretch out my hands in the darkness to this lad--who will never know--until his sons come in their time...."

text: On top of everything else, he’d long been curious to visit the old country his maternal grandmother had hailed from. Didn’t he still keep a watercolor of Market Saffron church propped on a shelf in his New York bedroom, the very church where that dear old woman had been confirmed as a young girl? More broadly, he was just eager to explore Europe at large, full stop. As a low-stakes, enjoyable side piece of the excursion, he hoped to connect with Mr. Britling, who lived in Matching’s Easy, to talk through a few promising potential opportunities in his capacity as secretary of the Massachusetts Society for the Study of Contemporary Thought— a position that left him drastically underworked and noticeably overpaid, if anyone bothered to ask.
source_text: And also, he wanted to see the old country from which his maternal grandmother had sprung. Wasn't there even now in his bedroom in New York a water-colour of Market Saffron church, where the dear old lady had been confirmed? And generally he wanted to see Europe. As an interesting side show to the excursion he hoped, in his capacity of the rather underworked and rather over-salaried secretary of the Massachusetts Society for the Study of Contemporary Thought, to discuss certain agreeable possibilities with Mr. Britling, who lived at Matching's Easy.

text: It’s possible those nagging worries threw off his performance. Either way, he didn’t pull off a single move he was proud of the entire second half. Cecily wove and darted circles all around and straight through his defense, leaving him totally flustered, and over the span of just ten minutes, her team notched the two remaining goals they needed to bring the final score to five to one. By Matching’s Easy’s local rules, hitting five goals counts as an immediate, decisive game win.
source_text: Perhaps these apprehensions affected his game. At any rate he did nothing that pleased him in the second half, Cecily danced all over him and round and about him, and in the course of ten minutes her side had won the two remaining goals with a score of Five-One; and five goals is "game" by the standards of Matching's Easy.

text: Life out on this sector is stripped to its rawest edges—not that we’re brushing up against any great universal truths, just shrinking down to the small, unthinking tasks that fill every waking minute. Every day blurs into hauling rations, carting jerry cans of well water, tramping up to the forward trenches before dawn and stumbling back after dark. We grind like that for weeks on end, until some random, unremarkable morning you catch yourself yelling into the empty wind, “What’s the point of any of this? How much longer can it drag on?” Before this conflict swallowed everything, I had a whole life mapped out ahead of me: university degrees, marine biology research, field work, breakthrough papers, a dozen different paths to chase; out here, though, it’s almost impossible to imagine there’s any kind of future waiting on the other side of the shelling.
source_text: "Life is very primitive here--which doesn't mean that one is getting down to anything fundamental, but only going back to something immediate and simple. It's fetching and carrying and getting water and getting food and going up to the firing line and coming back. One goes on for weeks, and then one day one finds oneself crying out, 'What is all this for? When is it to end?' I seemed to have something ahead of me before this war began, education, science, work, discoveries; all sorts of things; but it is hard to feel that there is anything ahead of us here....

text: From this point, Mr. Britling veered off to add yet another entry to his already extensive catalog of contrasts between England and Germany. Germany, he argued, was a nation that had been entirely engulfed and subsumed by its army and governing bureaucracy; the Prussian military framework had absorbed every facet of German public and private life. It was a state gorged to bursting, a state that had devoured all its own citizens. Britain, by contrast, was no such formal, centralized state. It was a loose, unincorporated collection of people. The British army, the British War Office, the entire British administrative apparatus had absorbed nothing of the nation they served; they were small, outdated, partial institutions, and the full British nation existed entirely outside their bounds, beyond their limited understanding and staid traditions: a formless, newly emergent force, but a formidable one. And now that British nation, that real, unacknowledged nation of “outsiders,” was being forced to take up arms. All the unspoken guiding principles of that larger, outer English life that existed beyond politics, beyond the formal state services were suddenly under threat: its easy, tolerant good humor, its hard-won freedom, its casual unconcern with overbearing state control. It was not merely English life that hung in the balance; it was every breadth of democratic possibility, every liberal ideal and every individual liberty. Civilization itself was in peril. The unmapped, unregimented liberal order had been seized by the throat, and it would either prove its worth or be destroyed entirely.
source_text: From this Mr. Britling broke away into a fresh addition to his already large collection of contrasts between England and Germany. Germany was a nation which has been swallowed up and incorporated by an army and an administration; the Prussian military system had assimilated to itself the whole German life. It was a State in a state of repletion, a State that had swallowed all its people. Britain was not a State. It was an unincorporated people. The British army, the British War Office, and the British administration had assimilated nothing; they were little old partial things; the British nation lay outside them, beyond their understanding and tradition; a formless new thing, but a great thing; and now this British nation, this real nation, the "outsiders," had to take up arms. Suddenly all the underlying ideas of that outer, greater English life beyond politics, beyond the services, were challenged, its tolerant good humour, its freedom, and its irresponsibility. It was not simply English life that was threatened; it was all the latitudes of democracy, it was every liberal idea and every liberty. It was civilisation in danger. The uncharted liberal system had been taken by the throat; it had to "make good" or perish....

text: Believing this is as absurd as a camel holding out hope that it will one day stumble on a spring so perfectly replenishing that it never feels thirsty again. Most of the time, Mr. Britling pushed that foolish conviction out of his awareness, and pushed back against every urge it sparked. But every now and then, most often in the drowsy afternoons, mid-journey, or in the lulls between finishing one book and starting the next, Mr. Britling gave in fully to that odd, lingering expectation that some extraordinary surprise was waiting just around the corner. He cast loose the moorings of his usual dry wit and sharp self-criticism, and fell in with the vast, roving fraternity of the Pilgrims of Love....
source_text: This persuasion is as foolish as though a camel hoped that some day it would drink from such a spring that it would never thirst again. For the most part Mr. Britling ignored its presence in his mind, and resisted the impulses it started. But at odd times, and more particularly in the afternoon and while travelling and in between books, Mr. Britling so far succumbed to this strange expectation of a wonder round the corner that he slipped the anchors of his humour and self-contempt and joined the great cruising brotherhood of the Pilgrims of Love....

text: Day after day, he couldn’t turn his mind to anything else at all, no matter how hard he tried. His thoughts were trapped like a galley slave chained to his bench, utterly unable to stop straining against that same heavy oar. Every part of his world functioned like a magnetic field, tugging every last thing in his orbit toward that one sharp, polar question, whether he wanted it to or not.
source_text: He could think continuously day by day of nothing else. His mind was as caught as a galley slave, as unable to escape from tugging at this oar. All his universe was a magnetic field which oriented everything, whether he would have it so or not, to this one polar question.

text: Restlessness curdled into white-hot fury in his bones. He could not settle back into the crumpled sheets of his bed for even a minute; he hauled himself upright and stalked the perimeter of his chamber, knocking his shins against the edges of armchairs and sideboards in the pitch black. We were far too thick-headed to see what was staring us right in the face; we were shuttling all these boys headlong into grinding hardship and unforgiving peril, sending them off with threadbare kits, no proper backup, feeding our own flesh and blood to the insatiable war god, because at our core we Britons were a nation run by lazy, overindulged, performatively genial old and middle-aged men. That was how he parceled out the unbearable weight of his own guilt to keep from drowning in it. Why was he sitting idly by instead of fighting to fix this rot? What use was the false modesty he clung to, the patience he extended to these sodden old barristers, these blowhard stump speakers, these rigid, slow-witted commanders and inflexible bureaucratic cogs? They were slaughtering Britain’s young men by the thousands. Grey-haired men sat far from the front lines, plotting death for the boys huddled in mud-filled trenches. That was the unvarnished truth of it. “My boy!” he gasped, the words torn out of him in the dark. His awareness of every flaw in our national leadership sharpened to a vicious, almost delirious edge. It felt like every fond, naive belief he’d ever held about our country had crumbled to dust at his feet. What was the point of pretending the men in command upstairs were drafting some brilliant counter-offensive that would win the war for good? It was as obvious as the sun at noon that they had neither the creative vision nor the shared sense to even dream up such a plan. Any slow, lumbering herd can hunker down and defend itself, but it takes bold imagination to go on the attack. Imagination! We’d never summon the nerve to strike by the end of it all. We could send bombers through the sky. We could land troops across the Channel. We could have hit the beaches at the Dardanelles with overwhelming force instead of trickling half-strength battalions there the way our grandfathers dribbled reinforcements to the Siege of Sevastopol. But the old men would sit at their oak desks, bellies full of port and half-asleep, and wag their crafty, wrinkled heads. The papers would prattle on, spitting contradictory nonsense like a cargo hold full of chattering macaques caught in a gale. The vultures of Westminster would hand posts to the least qualified men, tear down every competent would-be leader with lies, smears, and petty vitriol.
source_text: He began to fret and rage. He could not lie in peace in his bed; he got up and prowled about his room, blundering against chairs and tables in the darkness.... We were too stupid to do the most obvious things; we were sending all these boys into hardship and pitiless danger; we were sending them ill-equipped, insufficiently supported, we were sending our children through the fires to Moloch, because essentially we English were a world of indolent, pampered, sham good-humoured, old and middle-aged men. (So he distributed the intolerable load of self-accusation.) Why was he doing nothing to change things, to get them better? What was the good of an assumed modesty, an effort at tolerance for and confidence in these boozy old lawyers, these ranting platform men, these stiff-witted officers and hide-bound officials? They were butchering the youth of England. Old men sat out of danger contriving death for the lads in the trenches. That was the reality of the thing. "My son!" he cried sharply in the darkness. His sense of our national deficiencies became tormentingly, fantastically acute. It was as if all his cherished delusions had fallen from the scheme of things.... What was the good of making believe that up there they were planning some great counter-stroke that would end in victory? It was as plain as daylight that they had neither the power of imagination nor the collective intelligence even to conceive of a counter-stroke. Any dull mass may resist, but only imagination can strike. Imagination! To the end we should not strike. We might strike through the air. We might strike across the sea. We might strike hard at Gallipoli instead of dribbling inadequate armies thither as our fathers dribbled men at the Redan.... But the old men would sit at their tables, replete and sleepy, and shake their cunning old heads. The press would chatter and make odd ambiguous sounds like a shipload of monkeys in a storm. The political harridans would get the wrong men appointed, would attack every possible leader with scandal and abuse and falsehood....

text: A little while ago, Mr. Britling, you were kind enough to ask me what my first impression of England was. Well, Mr. Britling, the only first impression of the country that feels even remotely worth mentioning is this: it looks and feels far more like that iconic traditional Old England than anyone could ever have believed, but in reality, it’s less like that long-held vision of Old England than anyone would possibly be able to imagine.
source_text: "You were so good as to ask me, Mr. Britling, a little while ago, what my first impression of England was. Well, Mr. Britling, my first impression of England that seems to me to matter in the least is this: that it looks and feels more like the traditional Old England than any one could possibly have believed, and that in reality it is less like the traditional Old England than any one would ever possibly have imagined."

text: Mr. Britling was still buzzing off the heady rush of liberal optimism he’d been stewing on upstairs. “I don’t regret having lived long enough to see this war,” he said. “On one hand, it might be an unimaginable catastrophe, but on the other, it’s a massive leap forward for human existence. It wraps up forty years of poisonous, hanging uncertainty. It’s both the crisis and the resolution.”
source_text: Mr. Britling was full of the heady draught of liberal optimism he had been brewing upstairs. "I am not sorry I have lived to see this war," he said. "It may be a tremendous catastrophe in one sense, but in another it is a huge step forward in human life. It is the end of forty years of evil suspense. It is crisis and solution."

text: For weeks, the thought had been tumbling around the edges of his mind. He’d even brought the idea up with Letty: this Finite God, the figure who presides over all of humanity’s bold, meandering adventures across space and time. But up until that point, God had only ever been an intellectual exercise for him: a theory, a secondhand account, something other people spoke of that he’d never truly felt or recognized in his own life. Mr. Britling’s prior thoughts of God had felt exactly like stumbling across an empty, beautiful house, warm and welcoming, thrumming with the quiet promise that its owner was a deeply kind, vivid personality. And just as he pokes through the rooms alone, curiosity guiding his slow, quiet exploration, he hears the dear, friendly lilt of the Master’s voice drifting up from the floor below, soft and present as the owner steps through the front door.
source_text: For weeks his mind had been playing about this idea. He had talked to Letty of this Finite God, who is the king of man's adventure in space and time. But hitherto God had been for him a thing of the intelligence, a theory, a report, something told about but not realised.... Mr. Britling's thinking about God hitherto had been like some one who has found an empty house, very beautiful and pleasant, full of the promise of a fine personality. And then as the discoverer makes his lonely, curious explorations, he hears downstairs, dear and friendly, the voice of the Master coming in....

text: More than almost any other concern weighing on him lately was the slow, creeping sense that he no longer held a firm grasp on what the war was actually, fundamentally about. At the outset, Germany’s naked aggression and rabid militarism had struck him as so unforgivably wrong that he’d been unable to view the actions of Britain and its allies as anything short of entirely morally justified. He’d boiled the entire conflict down to a single, uncomplicated mantra: “This militaristic rot has to end.” He’d framed Germany as a monolithic system: a toxic fusion of imperialism and Junker aristocratic tyranny, cold, greed-fueled violent aggression, the very embodied impulse that drives nations to wage war. The Allies, by contrast, he’d seen as the united voice of all humankind pushing back against every last one of these cruel, destructive forces.
source_text: What worried his mind perhaps more than anything else was his gradual loss of touch with the essential issues of the war. At first the militarism, the aggression of Germany, had seemed so bad that he could not see the action of Britain and her allies as anything but entirely righteous. He had seen the war plainly and simply in the phrase, "Now this militarism must end." He had seen Germany as a system, as imperialism and junkerism, as a callous materialist aggression, as the spirit that makes war, and the Allies as the protest of humanity against all these evil things.

text: The group clustered around a small table beneath the cedar boughs, passing the time with their little experiments. Once that wrapped up, Cecily, Mr. Britling, and the two young boys amused themselves by sketching pigs with their eyes closed, before Mr. Britling and Teddy launched into a fierce game of badminton that lasted right up until tea was due to be served. Cecily sat beside Mr. Direck in the meantime, asking after the details of his injury, and he told her of summer holidays spent in the Adirondacks, and how much he loved to wander the world. She admitted she ached to travel too. He explained that as soon as he recovered fully, he planned to head first to Paris, then on into Germany. He was intensely curious about the country, and its pervasive, overwhelming militarism; he’d far rather explore that than Italy, which he dismissed as little more than a landscape of art and ancient history. His interest lay squarely in modern issues, though he did not intend to skip Italy entirely on his trip, of course. Their conversation was soon broken off, though, as a flurry of excitement swept the gathering: Herr Heinrich’s pet squirrel had gone missing.
source_text: They sat round a little table under the cedar trees amusing themselves with these experiments, and after that Cecily and Mr. Britling and the two small boys entertained themselves by drawing pigs with their eyes shut, and then Mr. Britling and Teddy played hard at Badminton until it was time for tea. And Cecily sat by Mr. Direck and took an interest in his accident, and he told her about summer holidays in the Adirondacks and how he loved to travel. She said she would love to travel. He said that so soon as he was better he would go on to Paris and then into Germany. He was extraordinarily curious about this Germany and its tremendous militarism. He'd far rather see it than Italy, which was, he thought, just all art and ancient history. His turn was for modern problems. Though of course he didn't intend to leave out Italy while he was at it. And then their talk was scattered, and there was great excitement because Herr Heinrich had lost his squirrel.

text: Mr. Britling was propped in his front doorway, where he’d been studying the late October foliage gilded by the warm glow of the afternoon sun, when the telegram messenger rounded the corner. He ripped open the envelope, clinging to the same quiet, fragile hope he’d carried with every telegram he’d opened since Hugh had left for the front: that it wouldn’t hold the exact terrible words he’d been dreading, that it might say he was wounded, that at the absolute worst it would only read “missing,” that it might even bring some small, joyful surprise—news of a short trip home, the kind his most recent letter had teased might be coming soon. He scanned the page and landed on the final, unmitigated confirmation, the crisp, impersonal note of regret. He stood perfectly still for several long moments, his gaze locked unseeingly on the words beneath his fingers....
source_text: Mr. Britling was standing at the front door; he had been surveying the late October foliage, touched by the warm light of the afternoon, when the messenger appeared. He opened the telegram, hoping as he had hoped when he opened any telegram since Hugh had gone to the front that it would not contain the exact words he read; that it would say wounded, that at the worst it would say "missing," that perhaps it might even tell of some pleasant surprise, a brief return to home such as the last letter had foreshadowed. He read the final, unqualified statement, the terse regrets. He stood quite still for a moment or so, staring at the words....

text: The esteemed contemporary ethicist undressed unhurriedly, snuffed out his candle, and climbed into bed. Still turning the nature of the Kabirian divine over in his thoughts, he slipped his right hand beneath his pillow per his usual custom, and brushed against something soft, warm, and wriggling. He convulsively launched himself out of bed, relit his candle, and lifted his pillow with deliberate caution.
source_text: The worthy modern thinker undressed slowly, blew out his candle and got into bed. Still meditating deeply upon the God of the Tagores, he thrust his right hand under his pillow according to his usual practice, and encountered something soft and warm and active. He shot out of bed convulsively, lit his candle, and lifted his pillow discreetly.

text: Elias’s letters had grown to shape a great deal of Mr. Halewood’s daily thinking. Elias had always had a knack for correspondence, and what might have been an inherited urge to commit every stray thought to paper was coming through clearly now. Back in his boarding school days, he’d liven up his notes with daft little doodles, sometimes sending nothing but drawings across the post, and now he hopped between scrawled paragraphs and quick, lopsided sketches mid-letter in a way that delighted his father endlessly. The older man adored this odd knack for caricature; he had never been able to pull off anything of the sort himself, so to him it was the most dazzling of all Elias’s small, charming talents. Mr. Halewood would tuck these letters into his coat pocket and tote them around until their corners were smudged and frayed; he’d pass them around to anyone he sensed would pick up on their bright, boyish freshness, and pull lines from them as unassailable proof to win whatever small debate he was wrapped up in. It never crossed his mind that thousands of other mothers and fathers were tucking exactly the same sort of mementos away with equal care. In his eyes, every other young man his son’s age was a dull, unremarkable thing next to Elias.
source_text: Hugh's letters were becoming a very important influence upon Mr. Britling's thought. Hugh had always been something of a letter-writer, and now what was perhaps an inherited desire to set things down was manifest. He had been accustomed to decorate his letters from school with absurd little sketches--sometimes his letters had been all sketches--and now he broke from drawing to writing and back to drawing in a way that pleased his father mightily. The father loved this queer trick of caricature; he did not possess it himself, and so it seemed to him the most wonderful of all Hugh's little equipment of gifts. Mr. Britling used to carry these letters about until their edges got grimy; he would show them to any one he felt capable of appreciating their youthful freshness; he would quote them as final and conclusive evidence to establish this or that. He did not dream how many thousands of mothers and fathers were treasuring such documents. He thought other sons were dull young men by comparison with Hugh.

text: “There’s such a pleasure to working through the hush of night, and just as much to rousing with the first pale wash of morning,” Mr. Hale said, chafing his palms briskly against one another. “I reckon I put down nearly two thousand words last night. No noise to pull you off course, so sharp, so fully anchored to the work. And the moment I finish my breakfast, I’m going straight back to it.”
source_text: "It's good to work in the night, and it's good to wake in the morning," said Mr. Britling, rubbing his hands together. "I suppose I wrote nearly two thousand words. So quiet one is, so concentrated. And as soon as I have had my breakfast I shall go on with it again."

text: He appeared to sense he owed Mr. Direck an explanation. “You see, it’s perfectly simple, really—first you steer around a corner, then you fail to straighten the wheels afterward, so you just keep turning far more than you’d planned. It’s the bicycle habit, you know; a bike rights itself on its own, so I expected the car to do the same thing. This was entirely my fault. The guidebook breaks this whole issue down clearly, but in that exact moment, it slipped my mind entirely.”
source_text: He seemed to feel some explanation was due to Mr. Direck. "You see, at first--it's perfectly simple--one steers _round_ a corner and then one doesn't put the wheels straight again, and so one keeps on going round--more than one meant to. It's the bicycle habit; the bicycle rights itself. One expects a car to do the same thing. It was my fault. The book explains all this question clearly, but just at the moment I forgot."

text: “I’m leaving—any place but here.” She spun on her heel, wool coat slung over one arm. “What, I can’t step out whenever I feel like it?” she snapped. “The sun’s shining, for Christ’s sake… Am I not allowed to breathe fresh air?… I guess you expect me to swallow everything you just dumped on me all at once, right? Just bat my eyes and chirp ‘How fascinating!’ Deserted his post! Left his troops to fall back alone! Isn’t that just darling?
