And then I’m supposed to wipe it from my mind like it never happened… And anyway, I need to get out. You two want the place to yourselves. Want to make goo-goo eyes at each other. Go ahead, enjoy it while it lasts!”
source_text: "I'm going out--anywhere." She turned, coat in hand. "Can't I go out if I like?" she asked. "It's a beautiful day.... Mustn't I go out?... I suppose you think I ought to take in what you have told me in a moment. Just smile and say '_Indeed!_' ... Abandoned!--while his men retreated! How jolly! And then not think of it any more.... Besides, I must go out. You two want to be left together. You want to canoodle. Do it while you can!"

text: She got it right away. In the stretch of time since Teddy vanished, she’d already watched this decent man approach her four separate times, every time visibly set on doing what he believed was his obligation, every time carrying the same unsteady, hesitant doubt he wore on his face right now. Back when she and Teddy were still happy and building their life together, they’d brought him up constantly, teasing him, mocking his stuffy manner, even laughing fondly at his harmless absurdities. They’d both agreed he was a perfect match for Jane Austen’s Mr. Collins, if Mr. Collins had put on a few extra pounds around the middle. Right now, under her sharp, defensive stare, he might as well have been made of glass. She knew he was pushed by the long-held customs he abided by, his rigid sense of what was appropriate, his devotion to his role, to offer her his official support and condolences, to recite his bland, overblown pleasantries for her benefit and pat her arm in that soft, well-meaning way of his. She also knew he was afraid of her. That sweet old fraud was fully aware deep in his bones that he was nothing but a sad, out-of-touch phony, and that she was completely wise to his whole act. Deep down, he was too honest at his core to foist his tired, empty platitudes on anyone who clearly had no interest in hearing them. If she’d seemed glad for his words, he would have delivered them with no guilt or hesitation at all. He was a man torn apart by his own conflicting impulses, too flustered to pull off his usual over-the-top performative kindness, left standing there uneasy and defeated.
source_text: Instantly she understood. Already on four different occasions since Teddy's disappearance she had seen the good man coming towards her, always with a manifest decision, always with the same faltering doubt as now. Often in their happy days had she and Teddy discussed him and derided him and rejoiced over him. They had agreed he was as good as Jane Austen's Mr. Collins. He really was very like Mr. Collins, except that he was plumper. And now, it was as if he was transparent to her hard defensive scrutiny. She knew he was impelled by his tradition, by his sense of fitness, by his respect for his calling, to offer her his ministrations and consolations, to say his large flat amiabilities over her and pat her kindly with his hands. And she knew too that he dreaded her. She knew that the dear old humbug knew at the bottom of his heart quite certainly that he was a poor old humbug, and that she was in his secret. And at the bottom of his heart he found himself too honest to force his poor platitudes upon any who would not be glad of them. If she could have been glad of them he would have had no compunction. He was a man divided against himself; failing to carry through his rich pretences, dismayed.

text: It’s the way we operate here, stitched into the very marrow of both your people and ours. For the English and Irish, at the very least, distrust of authority and willingness to gamble on untested, unpredictable paths is second nature… And anyone who’s ever cracked open a Russian novel can see their people carry that exact same contrary streak deep down too… Take this young Prussian we’ve got with us now, for example: he’s a constant source of amazement. He genuinely believes in the inherent virtue of the law, *likes* to follow rules. To the rest of us, that reads like some kind of absurd punchline. It’s striking how alien Germans feel to every other corner of the globe, all because of that instinctive docility. Scratch a Russian, and you’ll find the Tartar underneath. Give a Russian, an American, an Englishman, an Irishman, a Frenchman, or any true northern European bar a German an education, and what you get is an anarchist: that is to say, the man who dreams of order unshackled from rigid organisation, of a way of being that rises above bureaucratic structure entirely…
source_text: "As we do here. It's in your blood and our blood. It's the instinct of the English and the Irish anyhow to suspect government and take the risks of the chancy way.... And manifestly the Russians, if you read their novelists, have the same twist in them.... When we get this young Prussian here, he's a marvel to us. He really believes in Law. He _likes_ to obey. That seems a sort of joke to us. It's curious how foreign these Germans are--to all the rest of the world. Because of their docility. Scratch the Russian and you get the Tartar. Educate the Russian or the American or the Englishman or the Irishman or Frenchman or any real northern European except the German, and you get the Anarchist, that is to say the man who dreams of order without organisation--of something beyond organisation....

text: The only shortcomings Herr Heinrich would acknowledge in the English way of life were their habit of serving meager portions and a distinct absence of tender feeling. He truly found the English unemotional to the point of coldness; his heart felt even more unfulfilled than his stomach. He was a man of deep, demonstrative affection, craving abiding friendships, intimate unspoken bonds, and all-consuming love. He did his utmost to admire, understand, and share that quiet, unvoiced mutual understanding with Mr. Britling; he sought out long rambles and earnest conversations with Hugh and the younger boys; he tried to fill the empty space in his heart with devotion to Cissie; at last, he discovered a rare, beautiful innocence and sweetness in the Hickson girl. When they first met, she wore her hair in a single plait, and it made her look almost exactly like his vision of Marguerite. This young man had cried out for love—warm, enveloping, satisfying, the same way he ached for the hearty midday meals he had always taken for granted back home. But none of these Essex residents could meet that need. They were quiet, guarded, difficult to pin down, slipping right through the hungry, earnest grip of his heart, until he finally retreated to his own company, his letters to German correspondents, his idealized image of Maud Hickson, and the work of teaching little Billy right from wrong. Billy. The memory came back to Mr. Britling at last, of young Heinrich with a squirrel perched on his shoulder, the image that had so often held him back from unreservedly condemning Germany. Seen clearly, that boy was no brutal Prussian thug. What possible quarrel could we ever have had with someone like him?
source_text: Their disposition towards under-feeding and a certain lack of fine sentiment were the only flaws in the English scheme that Herr Heinrich admitted. He certainly found the English unfeeling. His heart went even less satisfied than his Magen. He was a being of expressive affections; he wanted great friendships, mysterious relationships, love. He tried very bravely to revere and to understand and be occultly understood by Mr. Britling; he sought long walks and deep talks with Hugh and the small boys; he tried to fill his heart with Cissie; he found at last marvels of innocence and sweetness in the Hickson girl. She wore her hair in a pigtail when first he met her, and it made her almost Marguerite. This young man had cried aloud for love, warm and filling, like the Mittagsessen that was implicit in their understanding. And all these Essex people failed to satisfy him; they were silent, they were subtle, they slipped through the fat yet eager fingers of his heart, so that he fell back at last upon himself and his German correspondents and the idealisation of Maud Hickson and the moral education of Billy. Billy. Mr. Britling's memories came back at last to the figure of young Heinrich with the squirrel on his shoulder, that had so often stood in the way of the utter condemnation of Germany. That, seen closely, was the stuff of one brutal Prussian. What quarrel had we with him?...

text: She lurched into a run, legs that felt like they didn’t quite belong to her carrying her forward, her hands flailing wildly as she moved. With every pounding stride, she grew surer and surer that the injured man clad in odd, unrecognizable clothing was Teddy. She pushed herself faster and faster still, stumbling over ruts in the ground and nearly pitching to the dirt a half-dozen times. If she didn’t reach his side right away, she was convinced the entire world would burst apart at the seams.
source_text: And she began to run forward, with legs that seemed misfits, waving her hands about, and as she ran she saw more and more certainly that this wounded man in strange clothing was Teddy. She ran faster and still faster, stumbling and nearly falling. If she did not get to him speedily the world would burst.

text: What was all this bloodshed ever for, anyway? What are we still spilling it for right now? You tell me—does anyone on this scorched, grieving earth actually have an answer? Why am I burning through the last of my savings and my failing strength, and you frittering away the last of yours, just to keep tearing at each other’s throats in this senseless war? What do either of us stand to gain by wounding and breaking one another more than we already have? Why should we remain puppets a minute longer, jerked around by empty-headed royals and bumbling, out-of-touch diplomats? Even if we held our tongues and meekly went along with all their demands before, don’t the cold graves of our boys scream loud enough to tell us this farce has to end? We stood by and let these people march our sons straight to the slaughter.
source_text: _What have we been fighting for? What are we fighting for? Do you know? Does any one know? Why am I spending what is left of my substance and you what is left of yours to keep on this war against each other? What have we to gain from hurting one another still further? Why should we be puppets any longer in the hands of crowned fools and witless diplomatists? Even if we were dumb and acquiescent before, does not the blood of our sons now cry out to us that this foolery should cease? We have let these people send our sons to death._

text: For what felt like hours, Mr. Hale could not drift off. His mind was far too overstimulated to settle, fired up by that unending, crowded day packed with unforeseen new experiences and constant little nudges that invited comparison to his ordinary routine. The whole tangled, chaotic spectacle of the day clustered itself entirely around Lila Corwin, with an unforced ease and total absence of logic that anyone who has ever been young will recognize immediately.
source_text: For a time Mr. Direck could not sleep. His mind had been too excited by this incessant day with all its novelties and all its provocations to comparison. The whole complicated spectacle grouped itself, with a naturalness and a complete want of logic that all who have been young will understand, about Cecily Corner.

text: Mr. Van der Pant had overseen the dynamos at the Antwerp Power Station, where he kept the electrified barbed wire entanglements charged right up until German high-explosive shells shredded his wiring and rendered the dynamos completely useless. He shared sharp, vivid little anecdotes about the roar of the bombardment, the dead left splayed uncollected in open public spaces, and the way German gunners repeatedly missed the floating boat bridge that the vast majority of defending troops and fleeing civilians used to escape the city. He pulled out a tiny tourist map of Antwerp and dotted spots across it with a pencil he fished from his carrying case. “The—what do you call it?—*obus*, ah, shells! They fell right here, and here, and all along this stretch.” He’d fled across this section on his *bécane*, he explained, cutting along these side streets and then down this long road. He’d brought his rifle with him, and hid it alongside weapons belonging to a dozen other Belgian men in the gap between the floorboards and ceiling joists of a house in Zeebrugge. He’d stumbled on a shady unregulated steamer in the harbor, whose captain was dead set on squeezing every last possible penny out of every refugee he ferried to London. Once everyone was onboard and the ship had pulled away from the docks, they realized the only food aboard was the hard ration biscuits carried by a handful of Belgian soldiers. They split the small stock evenly between everyone, rationing as carefully as castaways stranded on a life raft. The *mer* had been *calme*, he said, thank Heaven. They’d spent the entire night bailing water out of the leaking hull, and he’d pitched in with the pumping the whole time. Even now, Mr. Van der Pant still hoped he’d get a chance to settle the score with that ship’s captain one day.
source_text: Mr. Van der Pant had been in charge of the dynamos at the Antwerp Power Station, he had been keeping the electrified wires in the entanglements "alive," and he had stuck to his post until the German high explosives had shattered his wires and rendered his dynamos useless. He gave vivid little pictures of the noises of the bombardment, of the dead lying casually in the open spaces, of the failure of the German guns to hit the bridge of boats across which the bulk of the defenders and refugees escaped. He produced a little tourist's map of the city of Antwerp, and dotted at it with a pencil-case. "The--what do you call?--_obus_, ah, shells! fell, so and so and so." Across here he had fled on his _bécane_, and along here and here. He had carried off his rifle, and hid it with the rifles of various other Belgians between floor and ceiling of a house in Zeebrugge. He had found the pirate steamer in the harbour, its captain resolved to extract the uttermost fare out of every refugee he took to London. When they were all aboard and started they found there was no food except the hard ration biscuits of some Belgian soldiers. They had portioned this out like shipwrecked people on a raft.... The _mer_ had been _calme_; thank Heaven! All night they had been pumping. He had helped with the pumps. But Mr. Van der Pant hoped still to get a reckoning with the captain of that ship.

text: Even so, he shared his successes far more readily than he did his hardships. For his own part, he was perfectly happy to laugh off the mishaps and half-baked plans that fell flat for him, but when those same blunders were framed as a “Tremendous Set-Down for the Proud Parent,” there was no way to treat them with any lighthearted humor.
source_text: But he brought his successes more readily than he brought his troubles. So far as he himself was concerned he was disposed to take a humorous view of the things that went wrong and didn't come off with him, but as a "Tremendous Set-Down for the Proud Parent" they resisted humorous treatment....

text: The Thorne boy loathed Sonny Skyler. He considered the perky comic-strip character an entirely unnecessary little upstart. He despised the blunt, too-neat cut of his hair, the stiff, old-fashioned cut of his knickerbockers, and—him, full stop. He thought Sonny Skyler was the single drop of turpentine spoiling the otherwise lovely spread of the Sunday funnies section. But he was a tactful, shrewd child.
source_text: The Britling child hated Buster Brown. He regarded Buster Brown as a totally unnecessary infant. He detested the way he wore his hair and the peculiar cut of his knickerbockers and--him. He thought Buster Brown the one drop of paraffin in the otherwise delicious feast of the Sunday Supplement. But he was a diplomatic child.

text: How unblemished and unswerving the spirit of youth is. It glows like a freshly forged, gleaming spear, like a sword tempered to a flawless, resilient edge. The figure of his young son took hold of his thoughts: his boy, who surveyed the world through his mother’s dark, deep eyes, the slender child born of that earnest, all-consuming first love. He was a being both exquisitely fine and uncomplicated, a beloved, intimate mystery. Would he, too, have to bear the dents, the worn wrinkles, the dulled tarnish of life in his own turn?
source_text: What a clean, straight thing the spirit of youth is. It is like a bright new spear. It is like a finely tempered sword. The figure of his boy took possession of his mind, his boy who looked out on the world with his mother's dark eyes, the slender son of that whole-hearted first love. He was a being at once fine and simple, an intimate mystery. Must he in his turn get dented and wrinkled and tarnished?

text: Hugh was set to spend the night. The evening dragged on for him and everyone else present, long, awkward, and full of stilted, unfulfilling small talk. Only the young boys acted like their usual unselfconscious, lively selves. They were thoroughly impressed and giddy about his upcoming departure, bursting at the seams to ask a hundred different questions about what daily life in the trenches was really like. Hugh had to promise most of them he’d answer every single one once he arrived, when he’d be able to see exactly how things operated firsthand. Mrs. Britling was warm, motherly, and thoughtful as she went through his packed outfit with him. “Will you need winter clothes while you’re over there?” she asked...
source_text: Hugh was to stop the night. He spent what seemed to him and every one a long, shy, inexpressive evening. Only the small boys were really natural and animated. They were much impressed and excited by his departure, and wanted to ask a hundred questions about the life in the trenches. Many of them Hugh had to promise to answer when he got there. Then he would see just exactly how things were. Mrs. Britling was motherly and intelligent about his outfit. "Will you want winter things?" she asked....

text: The plain truth is that almost no one has ever fully internalized the simple fact that the Earth is round. Round, that is, shaped exactly like a common orange. We’re taught this fact in school, tossed to us like any tired, secondhand rumor, and we promptly set it aside for every practical matter of daily life. For all intents and purposes, we occupy a world as flat as a pancake, one where time stretches on endlessly and nothing ever shifts. Who among us truly accepts that there is any real world beyond the curve of our own local horizon? Here we stand, with no obvious change unfolding before our eyes, so we carry on under the assumption that nothing ever will change, that everything will simply drift on, unaltered, across space and time. If we could only grasp the reality of that full, rounded world lying past what we can see, we would move through our days with far more care and caution. If the globe functioned like a vast whispering gallery, what quiet murmurs might we catch even now: voices from India, from Africa, from Germany, warnings carried over from the past, soft hints of what the future holds.
source_text: "The truth of the matter is that hardly any one has ever yet mastered the fact that the world is round. The world is round--like an orange. The thing is told us--like any old scandal--at school. For all practical purposes we forget it. Practically we all live in a world as flat as a pancake. Where time never ends and nothing changes. Who really believes in any world outside the circle of the horizon? Here we are and visibly nothing is changing. And so we go on to--nothing will ever change. It just goes on--in space, in time. If we could realise that round world beyond, then indeed we should go circumspectly.... If the world were like a whispering gallery, what whispers might we not hear now--from India, from Africa, from Germany, warnings from the past, intimations of the future....

text: The amiable Norwegian intermediary had written to inform Mr. Britling that Herr Heinrich was also dead; he had passed away months prior while held as a wounded prisoner of war in Russia. He had sustained injuries and been captured, after enduring severe hardship, during the massive early spring Russian offensive against the Carpathian mountain passes, and his wound had become gangrenous. He rallied partially for a time, only to be beaten and harmed again during a scuffle between German and Croatian prisoners, after which he grew gravely ill and died. Before his death, he had written to his parents, and once more asked that the fiddle he had left in Mr. Britling’s keeping be returned to them if at all possible. It was evident that, for both him and his family by that point, the instrument had become a symbol weighted with countless layered associations.
source_text: The friendly go-between in Norway had written to tell Mr. Britling that Herr Heinrich also was dead; he had died a wounded prisoner in Russia some months ago. He had been wounded and captured, after undergoing great hardships, during the great Russian attack upon the passes of the Carpathians in the early spring, and his wound had mortified. He had recovered partially for a time, and then he had been beaten and injured again in some struggle between German and Croatian prisoners, and he had sickened and died. Before he died he had written to his parents, and once again he had asked that the fiddle he had left in Mr. Britling's care should if possible be returned to them. It was manifest that both for him and them now it had become a symbol with many associations.

text: Halfway through drafting his note, Mr. Hale’s thoughts snagged abruptly on the stack of snapshots he kept tucked away, and he pulled open the narrow desk drawer where he always stashed them when he didn’t want to look too long. He sifted out the shots of Erik, the quiet German student who’d boarded with them the summer before, but the rest of the stack, sun-dappled and glowing, hummed now with unforeseen, weighty meaning: two frames caught Milo, Jasper, Poppy, and Lila clowning through a silly marching drill in the back garden, and another showed Mr. Voorhees grinning on the front porch, wearing Erik’s forgotten scuffed slippers. There were dozens of shots of Milo, too, his wide, gap-toothed grin splitting every frame he popped into. There’s a gentle, unspoken bias to casual film photography, a refusal to capture the gray, heavy days, so any roll of snapshots from a life strings together only its softest, most sunlit moments. In the drawer just above this jumble of prints, Jasper’s old letters were stacked next to a hodgepodge of small, trivial mementos tied to the stretch of his life he’d spent living under their roof.
source_text: While writing this Mr. Britling had been struck by the thought of the photographs, and he had taken them out of the little drawer into which he was accustomed to thrust them. He picked out the ones that showed the young German, but there were others, bright with sunshine, that were now charged with acquired significances; there were two showing the children and Teddy and Hugh and Cissie and Letty doing the goose step, and there was one of Mr. Van der Pant, smiling at the front door, in Heinrich's abandoned slippers. There were endless pictures of Teddy also. It is the happy instinct of the Kodak to refuse those days that are overcast, and the photographic record of a life is a chain of all its kindlier aspects. In the drawer above these snapshots there were Hugh's letters and a miscellany of trivial documents touching on his life.

text: He stood in bright sunlight poring over the *Observer*, which the gardener’s boy had just brought over from the May Tree. He’d spread the paper open across a garden table under the blue cedar, and father and son were both scanning its pages, each craning to catch as much as the other’s position would allow. Fresh reports from France had arrived: accounts of further German advances, fighting at Senlis—“But that’s so close to Paris!”—and sightings of German forces at Nogent-sur-Seine. “Sur Seine!” Mr. Britling exclaimed. “Wherever can that be? South of the Marne? Or perhaps below Paris, somewhere?”
source_text: He stood in the sunshine reading the _Observer_ which the gardener's boy had just brought from the May Tree. He had spread it open on a garden table under the blue cedar, and father and son were both reading it, each as much as the other would let him. There was fresh news from France, a story of further German advances, fighting at Senlis--"But that is quite close to Paris!"--and the appearance of German forces at Nogent-sur-Seine. "Sur Seine!" cried Mr. Britling. "But where can that be? South of the Marne? Or below Paris perhaps?"

text: Herr Heinrich had two core ambitions he spoke of openly. While the two goals were vastly different in scope, he held them both as equally critical to his life’s purpose. The first, which he planned to pursue immediately after earning his philology doctorate, was to develop a flawless universal language that merged the strongest features of both Esperanto and Ido. “Once that exists,” Herr Heinrich would say, “there won’t be any more wars ever again.” His second goal ranked equally high for two reasons: he found tinkering with the concept endlessly enjoyable, and he was convinced it would earn him enough fortune and influence to spread his universal language far and wide. The project in question was a precision system of notched page edges for every kind of dictionary and alphabetized reference work. The system would be so precise that anyone looking up information could simply stand over the volume, run a finger along its fore-edge while glancing at the notches, and flip the book open straight to the exact page they needed. He intended to tackle the project with characteristic German meticulousness. “Sooner or later,” he noted, “I’ll have to learn how the machines that trim book edges work. I might even need to design entirely new equipment for this.” That two-pronged plan formed the entire backbone of Herr Heinrich’s professional future. He intended to follow it to the letter, and in the process, he would also get the chance to nurture the big, unformed softness at his core that was so clearly unfulfilled right now.
source_text: He confessed two principal ambitions. They varied perhaps in their absolute dimensions, but they were of equal importance in his mind. The first of these was, so soon as he had taken his doctorate in philology, to give himself to the perfecting of an International Language; it was to combine all the virtues of Esperanto and Ido. "And then," said Herr Heinrich, "I do not think there will be any more wars--ever." The second ambition, which was important first because Herr Heinrich found much delight in working at it, and secondly because he thought it would give him great wealth and opportunity for propagating the perfect speech, was the elaboration of his system of marginal indentations for dictionaries and alphabetical books of reference of all sorts. It was to be so complete that one would just stand over the book to be consulted, run hand and eye over its edges and open the book--"at the very exact spot." He proposed to follow this business up with a quite Germanic thoroughness. "Presently," he said, "I must study the machinery by which the edges of books are cut. It is possible I may have to invent these also." This was the double-barrelled scheme of Herr Heinrich's career. And along it he was to go, and incidentally develop his large vague heart that was at present so manifestly unsatisfied....

text: Nearly every last one of our officers is a total stand-up guy. That’s exactly what makes this whole situation so baffling. If only they weren’t such decent blokes! If they treated their men the way Prussian officers treat theirs, we could stage a revolution right alongside fighting the war, and sort every last thing out all in one go. But they’re kind, they’re endlessly charming… they just don’t put any real work into thinking things through, and they don’t grasp that doing a job properly means tackling it as intentionally and deliberately as you possibly can. They refuse to fuss over the important details. If they had worse tempers, their work might actually be far stronger. They won’t bother with maps, timetables, or reference books. When we move to a new posting, they only pick up whatever information they can secondhand, from gossip and casual chatter; not a single one of them has the good sense to carry a topographical map or a Michelin guide. They operate entirely on hearsay. They’re nitpicky, small-minded, and wasteful—and when it comes to actually getting tasks completed, they’re full of empty posturing. By their own internal code, they’re perfect paragons of honor. Courage? They’ve got more than enough of that, no argument there. Honesty, trustworthiness, all those values? They rank incredibly high on all of them. They also have that sort of equestrian-adjacent standard of sharpness and pluck that’s not half bad, and they’re properly horrified by unkempt facial hair and walking around with their uniforms half-unbuttoned. But the massive mistake they make is lumping critical thinking in with scraggly beards, writing it off as some fussy, unnecessary side thing, instead of grouping it with being slovenly and unbuttoned, something that should be avoided at all costs. They loathe being economical. And they hate advance planning even more.
source_text: "Our officers are beastly good chaps, nearly all of them. That's where the perplexity of the whole thing comes in. If only they weren't such good chaps! If only they were like the Prussian officers to their men, then we'd just take on a revolution as well as the war, and make everything tidy at once. But they are decent, they are charming.... Only they do not think hard, and they do not understand that doing a job properly means doing it as directly and thought-outly as you possibly can. They won't worry about things. If their tempers were worse perhaps their work might be better. They won't use maps or timetables or books of reference. When we move to a new place they pick up what they can about it by hearsay; not one of our lot has the gumption to possess a contoured map or a Michelin guide. They have hearsay minds. They are fussy and petty and wasteful--and, in the way of getting things done, pretentious. By their code they're paragons of honour. Courage--they're all right about that; no end of it; honesty, truthfulness, and so on--high. They have a kind of horsey standard of smartness and pluck, too, that isn't bad, and they have a fine horror of whiskers and being unbuttoned. But the mistake they make is to class thinking with whiskers, as a sort of fussy sidegrowth. Instead of classing it with unbuttonedupness. They hate economy. And preparation....

text: I don’t think that’s the case at all. I’m writing to you now to say I truly do believe our two sons didn’t die for nothing, not entirely. The searing pain and grief we’re carrying might not be wasted—it might even be necessary. If I’m being honest, I think they almost certainly are. I’m here, hollowed out by loss and miserable as anything, and still I hold out hope. I won’t deny that the whole ugly fabric of war has never been so unrelentingly dark, that much I’ll admit. But that same dark, heavy fabric of war has also never been so thin and frayed, worn almost threadbare all over. Light is seeping through it in a thousand different places, bright and impossible to snuff out.
source_text: _I do not think it is. I can write to you and tell you that I do indeed believe that our two sons have died not altogether in vain. Our pain and anguish may not be wasted--may be necessary. Indeed they may be necessary. Here am I bereaved and wretched--and I hope. Never was the fabric of war so black; that I admit. But never was the black fabric of war so threadbare. At a thousand points the light is shining through._

text: All the while, the rolling English countryside shifted slowly from its soft, unhurried, peaceful charm into the rough, half-disorganized semblance of a slatternly military encampment; long-standing property lines blurred and dissolved, and a vast, irreversible drain on the world’s finite resources gained unstopping momentum. Mr. Hale fulfilled his duties as a volunteer reserve constable, sent his eldest son to enlist in the County Yeomanry, hosted Belgian refugee families in his unused guest wing, fussed over the infantrymen billeted in his old hay barn, helped his godson Toby secure an officer’s commission, donated generously to war relief charities, sold off chunks of his securities portfolio at a steep loss to subscribe to the War Loan, and turned the grim, unspooling realities of the conflict over and over in his mind, never able to set the thought of it aside for long.
source_text: And while the countryside of England changed steadily from its lax pacific amenity to the likeness of a rather slovenly armed camp, while long-fixed boundaries shifted and dissolved and a great irreparable wasting of the world's resources gathered way, Mr. Britling did his duty as a special constable, gave his eldest son to the Territorials, entertained Belgians, petted his soldiers in the barn, helped Teddy to his commission, contributed to war charities, sold out securities at a loss and subscribed to the War Loan, and thought, thought endlessly about the war.

text: After the young man left, he mailed two quick greeting postcards directly addressed to the “Familie Britling,” and one letter passed along by Mr. Britling’s friendly American publisher. He also sent a short update once through a mutual acquaintance based in Norway. The postcards did little more than mark each leg of the frazzled pacifist’s journey across Holland as he made his way to enlist. The letter routed through America arrived two months after his departure. He’d been transitioned into a combat role extremely quickly. He’d completed three weeks of training, spent a full fortnight in the hospital with a severe cold, then been deployed to Belgium as a transport driver—his father had worked as a horse dealer, so he knew his way around horses very well. “If anything happens to me,” he wrote, “please at least send my violin to my mother, packed as carefully as you possibly can.” It was perfectly in keeping with his character that he reported staying in very comfortable lodgings in Courtrai with “really nice people.” That niceness came with unspoken constraints, though. “The one thing we never do,” he added, “is talk about the war. It’s better not to bring it up.” He mentioned the violin again in the later communication sent via Norway. In that note, he mourned the easy, pleasant comforts he’d left behind in Courtrai. He’d been stationed in Posen after that, and now he was in the Carpathians, wading through snow up to his knees and “very uncomfortable….”
source_text: Since the young man's departure he had sent two postcards of greeting directly to the "Familie Britling," and one letter through the friendly intervention of Mr. Britling's American publisher. Once also he sent a message through a friend in Norway. The postcards simply recorded stages in the passage of a distraught pacifist across Holland to his enrolment. The letter by way of America came two months later. He had been converted into a combatant with extreme rapidity. He had been trained for three weeks, had spent a fortnight in hospital with a severe cold, and had then gone to Belgium as a transport driver--his father had been a horse-dealer and he was familiar with horses. "If anything happens to me," he wrote, "please send my violin at least very carefully to my mother." It was characteristic that he reported himself as very comfortably quartered in Courtrai with "very nice people." The niceness involved restraints. "Only never," he added, "do we talk about the war. It is better not to do so." He mentioned the violin also in the later communication through Norway. Therein he lamented the lost fleshpots of Courtrai. He had been in Posen, and now he was in the Carpathians, up to his knees in snow and "very uncomfortable...."

text: Yet the notion that a unique, inherent malice in the German character lay at the root of the entire war struck Mr. Britling as even more flimsy and short-lived an explanation than his initial, unrefined framing that pitted militarism against peaceful humanity, with the Central Powers embodying the former and the Russo-Western alliance the latter. That line of thinking logically led to the conclusion that eradicating the German people entirely was the only guarantee of global goodwill, a conclusion that barely appealed to his fundamentally gentle nature. When it came down to it, none of the Germans he had met and observed in person were cruel or driven by hatred. He circled back to that fact stubbornly, again and again. Turning away from the harsh, vile rhetoric of printed propaganda and its grotesque, dehumanizing depictions, he retreated to the memory of real flesh-and-blood people, their inherent humanity and solid, decent qualities—epitomized, for him, by the young man Heinrich.
source_text: But the idea of a peculiar malignity in the German quality as a key to the broad issue of the war was even less satisfactory and less permanent in Mr. Britling's mind than his first crude opposition of militarism and a peaceful humanity as embodied respectively in the Central Powers and the Russo-Western alliance. It led logically to the conclusion that the extermination of the German peoples was the only security for the general amiability of the world, a conclusion that appealed but weakly to his essential kindliness. After all, the Germans he had met and seen were neither cruel nor hate-inspired. He came back to that obstinately. From the harshness and vileness of the printed word and the unclean picture, he fell back upon the flesh and blood, the humanity and sterling worth, of--as a sample--young Heinrich.

text: “Good gracious,” said Mr. Hale, “but this is one staggering war. It plows forward heedless of every last scrap of common decency. As an American citizen, I’d naturally counted on being shown a baseline of respect, war or no war. That hope has gone entirely unfulfilled…. Europe is coming apart at the seams…. You folks here still have no notion just how entirely Europe is coming apart at the seams….”
source_text: "My word," said Mr. Direck, "but this is _some_ war. It is going on regardless of every decent consideration. As an American citizen I naturally expected to be treated with some respect, war or no war. That expectation has not been realised.... Europe is dislocated.... You have no idea here yet how completely Europe is dislocated....

text: When chatting with Mr. Direck, Mr. Britling could paint England as a wonderful, charming portrait of easy, laid-back leisure, but was that picture really as pleasant as he made it out to be? The comment Mr. Direck had made about his barn kept niggling at the back of his mind. His barn was no longer a functional farm building, his yard held no livestock at all; he was just coasting lazily in structures erected for long-ago practical needs, surviving on the stacked-up prosperity of past eras, the wasteful, privileged heir to generations of backbreaking labor. It wasn’t just that he was a coddled, unmoored sort of person himself; he lived in a coddled, unmoored sort of community too. The two went hand in hand… That infuriating mess with Ireland, you could joke about it in the light of day, but was it actually something to laugh off? We were drifting sluggishly straight toward a very real disaster. Our government seemed to run entirely on Mr. Micawber’s whole philosophy, with “Wait and see” as its official guiding slogan. For months now, this conflict had grown more and more threatening. What if civil war broke out in Ireland before we knew it? What if those frustrated, mistreated suffragettes pulled some desperate, unforgivable stunt, an assassination for instance? The bomb that went off in Westminster Abbey a few days prior could easily have killed a dozen people… What if the slow-burning resentment of British rule in India and Egypt got stoked into an all-out blaze by thoughtless, fumbling administrative choices?
source_text: In his talks with Mr. Direck, Mr. Britling could present England as a great and amiable spectacle of carelessness and relaxation, but was it indeed an amiable spectacle? The point that Mr. Direck had made about the barn rankled in his thoughts. His barn was a barn no longer, his farmyard held no cattle; he was just living laxly in the buildings that ancient needs had made, he was living on the accumulated prosperity of former times, the spendthrift heir of toiling generations. Not only was he a pampered, undisciplined sort of human being; he was living in a pampered, undisciplined sort of community. The two things went together.... This confounded Irish business, one could laugh at it in the daylight, but was it indeed a thing to laugh at? We were drifting lazily towards a real disaster. We had a government that seemed guided by the principles of Mr. Micawber, and adopted for its watchword "Wait and see." For months now this trouble had grown more threatening. Suppose presently that civil war broke out in Ireland! Suppose presently that these irritated, mishandled suffragettes did some desperate irreconcilable thing, assassinated for example! The bomb in Westminster Abbey the other day might have killed a dozen people.... Suppose the smouldering criticism of British rule in India and Egypt were fanned by administrative indiscretions into a flame....

text: She clung wholeheartedly to that version of events. There was no real evidence to back it up, but she refused to entertain even the smallest doubt of its truth. Before long, she started assembling care parcels to send to him. “They’re short of nearly every basic necessity,” she told anyone who would listen. “The conditions they’re kept in are appalling. He simply hasn’t had the chance to write to me yet, and I see no reason to wait until he specifically reaches out to ask for help.”
source_text: She adopted that story. She had no justification for it, but she would hear no doubts upon it. She presently began to prepare parcels to send him. "They want almost everything," she told people. "They are treated abominably. He has not been able to write to me yet, but I do not think I ought to wait until he asks me."

text: The most iconic image of all was of Mr. Hale bent over his pages in the small warm circle of amber lamp glow, the room’s blinds drawn up to let in the silver of the moon, but the casement latched tight to bar the moths that battered softly at its panes. Beyond the glass stretched the moon-swept expanse of high summer sky, the weathered stone church tower half a mile off blurring faint above the crowns of shadowed oaks, its clock—whose chimes Mr. Hale registered automatically in the small hours but never so much as glanced at in daylight—ticking its steady path through the long, slow arc of the nocturnal hours. He had always loathed strife and ruin, convinced that war between modern, civilized nations was the purest distillation of human folly: a senseless blight that choked progress and every unfettered, idiosyncratic expression of human life, and he had insisted a thousand times over that such a catastrophe was impossible. Even now, with his own nation locked in combat, he could barely bring himself to accept that this crisis could ever reach him beyond the realm of abstract thought. He did not truly believe, not in the marrow of his bones, not in the press of his fingertips against the page or the hush of the room around him, that murder, ruin, and suffering of a scale so monstrous it had no parallel was unfolding in the very same world that lay sleeping outside, framed by the glossy dark ivy and the moon-glazed window ledge that bordered his quiet, unshaken view.
source_text: Most typical picture of all would be Mr. Britling writing in a little circle of orange lamplight, with the blinds of his room open for the sake of the moonlight, but the window shut to keep out the moths that beat against it. Outside would be the moon and the high summer sky and the old church tower dim above the black trees half a mile away, with its clock--which Mr. Britling heard at night but never noted by day--beating its way round the slow semicircle of the nocturnal hours. He had always hated conflict and destruction, and felt that war between civilised states was the quintessential expression of human failure, it was a stupidity that stopped progress and all the free variation of humanity, a thousand times he had declared it impossible, but even now with his country fighting he was still far from realising that this was a thing that could possibly touch him more than intellectually. He did not really believe with his eyes and finger-tips and backbone that murder, destruction, and agony on a scale monstrous beyond precedent was going on in the same world as that which slumbered outside the black ivy and silver shining window-sill that framed his peaceful view.

text: Thursday, July 23rd, the Austro-Hungarian minister stationed in Belgrade delivered a completely unworkable ultimatum to the Serbian government, and demanded an official response within forty-eight hours. With the clarity of hindsight, we now know exactly what that move meant. The Sarajevo assassination was going to be dragged back into the spotlight and used as a pretext for declaring war. But at the time, nine hundred and ninety-nine out of every thousand Europeans had not the faintest inkling of what was unfolding for them. The ultimatum was featured prominently in the Friday morning papers that arrived at Matching’s Easy, but it never came close to overshadowing the rest of the day’s news. Sir Edward Carson’s rejection of the government’s Ulster proposals was given top billing, and the updates from Serbia got almost exactly the same amount of coverage as the ongoing Caillaux trial and reports of Cossacks storming barricades in St. Petersburg. The questions Herr Heinrich raised about the situation over lunch all got reassuring, unalarmed answers.
source_text: On Thursday, July 23rd, the Austro-Hungarian minister at Belgrade presented his impossible ultimatum to the Serbian government, and demanded a reply within forty-eight hours. With the wisdom of retrospect we know now clearly enough what that meant. The Sarajevo crime was to be resuscitated and made an excuse for war. But nine hundred and ninety-nine Europeans out of a thousand had still no suspicion of what was happening to them. The ultimatum figured prominently in the morning papers that came to Matching's Easy on Friday, but it by no means dominated the rest of the news; Sir Edward Carson's rejection of the government proposals for Ulster was given the pride of place, and almost equally conspicuous with the Serbian news were the Caillaux trial and the storming of the St. Petersburg barricades by Cossacks. Herr Heinrich's questions at lunch time received reassuring replies.

text: True to the habit of our still woefully unripe world, Mr. Haleworth and Mrs. Elowen Voss set about navigating these thoroughly unromantic affairs using the cadences of that guileless, earnest, boyish ardor that remains the only vocabulary for such things, and now and then Haleworth almost convinced himself he felt for her the same blazing devotion he’d once carried for his late Eleanor, that the thought of losing her had nothing at all to do with Kedrick’s convenience or any prudent social judgment alive. In truth, though, the single thread in the whole tangled web of possible feeling that retained any of its bright, unjaded youthfulness in his mind was the fierce, unshakable distaste he harbored at the idea of passing her off to any other person on earth. On top of that, he carried a faint paternal softness no younger suitor could have mustered, which pricked him with a sharp need to keep her from making a fool of herself by marrying a man purely out of petty resentment. He’d reasoned that a stubborn, spurned suitor tends to make a demanding, unforgiving husband, and that eventually Elias’s overbearing hold on her would draw far truer, sharper tears than any she’d ever shed over him. But that flash of selfless care was little more than the gilded edge of a core made almost entirely of possessive, burning jealousy.
source_text: And, after the fashion of our still too adolescent world, Mr. Britling and Mrs. Harrowdean proceeded to negotiate these extremely unromantic matters in the phrases of that simple, honest and youthful passionateness which is still the only language available, and at times Mr. Britling came very near persuading himself that he had something of the passionate love for her that he had once had for his Mary, and that the possible loss of her had nothing to do with the convenience of Pyecrafts or any discretion in the world. Though indeed the only thing in the whole plexus of emotional possibility that still kept anything of its youthful freshness in his mind was the very strong objection indeed he felt to handing her over to anybody else in the world. And in addition he had just a touch of fatherly feeling that a younger man would not have had, and it made him feel very anxious to prevent her making a fool of herself by marrying a man out of spite. He felt that since an obstinate lover is apt to be an exacting husband, in the end the heavy predominance of Oliver might wring much sincerer tears from her than she had ever shed for himself. But that generosity was but the bright edge to a mainly possessive jealousy.

text: Or is this entire grand mess just senseless, cruel by accident maybe, but not actively malicious? Or is it fundamentally wise, just choosing not to coddle us? Is there, somewhere out in those endless vastnesses, a flicker of receptive kindness, some slim hope of forbearance and help, something that’s actually in our corner when we’re up against death and cold, unfeeling brutality? If that’s the case, though, it’s clearly dead set against coddling us… But if the whole setup is cruel, maybe it’s also senseless and devoid of any driving will of its own? It’s impossible to picture the force that runs everything as some kind of devil—that’s just absurd. So even if the worst case is true, and it’s nothing more than a cold, unthinking system, we still have our ragged, flawed wills and our faltering wits to pit against it. It follows, then, that the whole point of living, the meaning of any life that’s not just passive drift, rests in a will that’s been rigorously shaped and focused, and a mind that’s been honed and strengthened. And what had he actually done over the last twenty years, for all his lectures and published work, to shore up that will and steel that mind, the only weapons he had against the dark? He was always so quick to point fingers at others—dons, politicians, the general public’s apathy—but what had he actually been doing?
source_text: Or is the whole thing just witless, accidentally cruel perhaps, but not malignant? Or is it wise, and merely refusing to pamper us? Is there somewhere in the immensities some responsive kindliness, some faint hope of toleration and assistance, something sensibly on our side against death and mechanical cruelty? If so, it certainly refuses to pamper us.... But if the whole thing is cruel, perhaps also it is witless and will-less? One cannot imagine the ruler of everything a devil--that would be silly. So if at the worst it is inanimate then anyhow we have our poor wills and our poor wits to pit against it. And manifestly then, the good of life, the significance of any life that is not mere receptivity, lies in the disciplined and clarified will and the sharpened and tempered mind. And what for the last twenty years--for all his lectures and writings--had he been doing to marshal the will and harden the mind which were his weapons against the Dark? He was ready enough to blame others--dons, politicians, public apathy, but what was he himself doing?

text: Three hours had passed, and Mr. Britling was working by broad daylight now, though the lamp on his study desk still glowed softly. The letter he’d been trying to draft to old Heinrich was still little more than a jumble of loose notes and half-formed fragments, not a coherent finished message by any stretch. But those scattered bits of material were starting to settle into a rough outline, and Mr. Britling was finally clarifying what he actually intended to say. It was perfectly clear to him now that he was no longer writing as his own narrow, individual self, reaching out to two specific grieving people who lived in that sprawling, old, high-walled home with steep sloped roofs tucked among pine forests—the very house Heinrich had once shown him a photograph of. He didn’t know them nearly well enough to pull off that kind of intimate, personal address. He realized he wasn’t writing as Mr. Britling at all, but as an Englishman; that was the only identity he could possibly hold for them, just as he could only grasp them as Germans, nothing more specific. He was simply a bereaved voice from England speaking to a bereaved family in Germany, nothing more and nothing less....
source_text: Three hours later Mr. Britling was working by daylight, though his study lamp was still burning, and his letter to old Heinrich was still no better than a collection of material for a letter. But the material was falling roughly into shape, and Mr. Britling's intentions were finding themselves. It was clear to him now that he was no longer writing as his limited personal self to those two personal selves grieving, in the old, large, high-walled, steep-roofed household amidst pine woods, of which Heinrich had once shown him a picture. He knew them too little for any such personal address. He was writing, he perceived, not as Mr. Britling but as an Englishman--that was all he could be to them--and he was writing to them as Germans; he could apprehend them as nothing more. He was just England bereaved to Germany bereaved....

text: War’s a thrilling game—one I never wanted any part of. You get that rush of excitement once every couple of months, if you’re lucky. The rest of it is just filth, chaos, and soul-sucking boredom: crumbled houses, rutted, ruined roads, mud-soaked countryside and boredom, supply convoys lumbering slowly up to the lines, then lumbering back loaded down with the wounded and worn-out troops—and boredom. You spend all your time making vague, half-baked guesses about how it’ll all end, and boredom, and more boredom, and even more boredom. You think about the work you’d planned to take on, the trips you’d been meaning to take, all the lives wasted for nothing, all the days tossed in the trash, and boredom. There are splintered poplars everywhere, and stench—stink everywhere, grime and filth and boredom… And it’s all because those goddamned Prussians were too stupid to see how much endless, mind-numbing boredom they were setting the world up for when they pranced around puffed up with pride, earning all that praise from Thomas Carlyle… _Gott strafe Deutschland_… So send me some books, okay? Books of dreams, books about China, about willow-pattern plates, the golden age, fairyland. Send them quick, and make sure you write the address really carefully…
source_text: "War is an exciting game--that I never wanted to play. It excites once in a couple of months. And the rest of it is dirt and muddle and boredom, and smashed houses and spoilt roads and muddy scenery and boredom, and the lumbering along of supplies and the lumbering back of the wounded and weary--and boredom, and continual vague guessing of how it will end and boredom and boredom and boredom, and thinking of the work you were going to do and the travel you were going to have, and the waste of life and the waste of days and boredom, and splintered poplars and stink, everywhere stink and dirt and boredom.... And all because these accursed Prussians were too stupid to understand what a boredom they were getting ready when they pranced and stuck their chests out and earnt the praises of Mr. Thomas Carlyle.... _Gott strafe Deutschland_.... So send me some books, books of dreams, books about China and the willow-pattern plate and the golden age and fairyland. And send them soon and address them very carefully...."

text: “I have to go,” he said, sharp with unspoken urgency. “I need to get up to London today, to a listed address in Bloomsbury. The people waiting there will tell me exactly how to travel on to Germany. I still have to pack, then grab a taxi from the junction to make the main line connection—why on earth don’t the branch line run any trains on Sundays, right when I need one?”
source_text: "I have to go," he said. "I must go right up to London to-day. To an address in Bloomsbury. Then they will tell me how to go to Germany. I must pack and I must get the taxi-cab from the junction and I must go. Why are there no trains on the branch line on Sundays for me to go by it?"

text: She wandered through the ramshackle, straggling village, cut across the open fields to the sloping hillside that looks out toward Mertonsome, its sharp church steeple pricking the distant horizon. Where the incline first began to dip downward, she dropped heavily onto the earth beneath the thick hedge lining the footpath, just a short distance from the wooden stile leading into the narrow lane, and lay perfectly still. She did not properly think so much as rest in a soft, blank stillness, waiting for the first faint impressions to register at the edge of her awareness.
source_text: She went through the straggling village, and across the fields to the hillside that looks away towards Mertonsome and its steeple. And where the hill begins to fall away she threw herself down under the hedge by the path, near by the stile into the lane, and lay still. She did not so much think as remain blank, waiting for the beginning of impressions....

text: The door opened onto a spacious, cool entryway lined with mounted hippopotamus and rhinoceros heads, plus a taxidermied lion posed against one wall. Most of the room’s furniture was taken up by an enormous table, its surface strewn haphazardly with hats, walking sticks, and crumpled newspapers. A manservant, his demeanor quiet and almost conspiratorial, informed Mr. Britling that her ladyship was out on the terrace. He accepted the hats and walking sticks the group handed over, then guided them through the interior of the house. They stepped out onto a wide, open terrace shaded by towering cedar trees, which looked out over formal flower beds, carved stone urns, manicured tennis lawns, and yew hedges cut low in spots to frame views of rolling hills far off. Roughly a dozen people were gathered on the terrace, most clutching teacups as they lounged in deck chairs and foldable seats clustered around a small table loaded with tea service items. Lady Homartyn stepped forward to welcome the newly arrived guests.
source_text: It opened into a large cool hall adorned with the heads of hippopotami and rhinoceroses and a stuffed lion, and furnished chiefly with a vast table on which hats and sticks and newspapers were littered. A manservant with a subdued, semi-confidential manner, conveyed to Mr. Britling that her ladyship was on the terrace, and took the hats and sticks that were handed to him and led the way through the house. They emerged upon a broad terrace looking out under great cedar trees upon flower beds and stone urns and tennis lawns and yew hedges that dipped to give a view of distant hills. On the terrace were grouped perhaps a dozen people for the most part holding teacups, they sat in deck chairs and folding seats about a little table that bore the tea-things. Lady Homartyn came forward to welcome the newcomers.

text: All the while, the other unspoken riddle he’d been turning over sorted itself out without fanfare. A vague, half-formed thought had lingered at the back of his mind for weeks, unexamined. Then one rainy afternoon he recognized it for what it was: a perfect, unlooked-for inspiration. He could hold fast to his conscientious objection to the United States, and still choose a course that would satisfy Marnie entirely. He took that path without a second thought.
source_text: And in the meanwhile the other riddle resolved itself. He had had a certain idea in his mind for some time. He discovered one day that it was an inspiration. He could keep his conscientious objection about America, and still take a line that would satisfy Cissie. He took it.

text: The entire frame of reference has shifted. It no longer matters one bit if we are left destitute. It makes no difference at all if we have to survive on plain potatoes and fall into arrears on our rent. These are the most trivial of concerns now, when only a week prior they would have been our top, most urgent priorities. Right now, we stand face to face with both the greatest catastrophe and the most extraordinary opportunity in human history. We have to plunge straight through the disaster to reach that opportunity waiting on the other side. There is nothing worth doing across the whole world right now, except making the most of this massive upheaval that is melting down every fixed, settled part of ordinary life. He had gotten exactly what he came for. He left her standing alone on the lawn and hurried back to his desk....
source_text: "The scale has altered. It does not matter now in the least if we are ruined. It does not matter in the least if we have to live upon potatoes and run into debt for our rent. These now are the most incidental of things. A week ago they would have been of the first importance. Here we are face to face with the greatest catastrophe and the greatest opportunity in history. We have to plunge through catastrophe to opportunity. There is nothing to be done now in the whole world except to get the best out of this tremendous fusing up of all the settled things of life." He had got what he wanted. He left her standing upon the lawn and hurried back to his desk....

text: You’ll probably say I’m forever nitpicking, but it truly feels like almost every task around here is carried out as wastefully as possible. We squander hours, we throw hard work down the drain, we fritter away supplies, good grief—most of all, we burn through our nation’s funds like they mean nothing. I promise you this isn’t just some stuck-up young kid spouting off. There’s nothing to feel smug about here, anyway. We’re half dead with boredom just loitering around this godforsaken tiny village. There’s not a single thing to occupy us, other than hanging around the handful of slovenly local girls we can’t stand and actively despise. I don’t do that, Daddy. And I don’t drink either. Why on earth didn’t I inherit a single bad habit to pass the time with? We got into a brawl yesterday, purely out of restlessness. Ortheris has a puffed-up lip, and another private’s got a nasty black eye. We’re already planning a rematch. I’m starting to think the worst, most soul-crushing horror of warfare is the endless, mind-numbing boredom.
source_text: "You will think I am always carping, but it does seem to me that nearly everything is being done here in the most wasteful way possible. We waste time, we waste labour, we waste material, oh Lord! how we waste our country's money. These aren't, I can assure you, the opinions of a conceited young man. It's nothing to be conceited about.... We're bored to death by standing about this infernal little village. There is nothing to do--except trail after a small number of slatternly young women we despise and hate. I _don't_, Daddy. And I don't drink. Why have I inherited no vices? We had a fight here yesterday--sheer boredom. Ortheris has a swollen lip, and another private has a bad black eye. There is to be a return match. I perceive the chief horror of warfare is boredom....

text:
Photographs of the mobilization unfolding in Vienna arrived on Friday, alongside reports that Belgrade was engulfed in flames. The images showed young men in straw hats nearly identical to those worn by their English, French, or Belgian counterparts, parading through Vienna’s streets. They carried flags and banners with heavy, portentous gravity, blowing trumpets, waving their hats aloft, and shouting in fervent chorus. By Saturday, mobilization orders had spread across all of Europe, and Herr Heinrich was tearing around on Teddy’s bicycle, racing to the crossroads junction to grab every evening paper he could find. For the residents of Dower House, two matters soon became the central preoccupations of daily life: the continent-wide mobilization, and Herr Heinrich’s increasingly fraught, volatile emotions.

The two youngest Britling boys launched their own boisterous mobilization right on the playroom floor. The older boy commanded a force of 190 toy soldiers, complete with a full array of cannons and supply wagons; the younger’s corps numbered 123, not counting three railway porters with their full work trucks, a police officer, five civilian figures, and two women. They also cut and colored a stack of paper British and German flags for their campaigns, but since neither would consent to his army representing any real existing foreign nation, they settled on naming their territories Redland and Blueland, matching the dominant hue of their respective soldiers’ uniforms.

All the while, Herr Heinrich was openly, almost unreservedly confessing to an extra layer of personal distress: he had developed a previously unsuspected romantic interest in the village publican’s daughter. She was a calm, receptive young woman named Maud Hickson, though the smitten young man had apparently taken to calling her Marguerite, for a more poetic, romantic flair.
source_text: Friday brought photographs of the mobilisation in Vienna, and the news that Belgrade was burning. Young men in straw hats very like English or French or Belgian young men in straw hats were shown parading the streets of Vienna, carrying flags and banners portentously, blowing trumpets or waving hats and shouting. Saturday saw all Europe mobilising, and Herr Heinrich upon Teddy's bicycle in wild pursuit of evening papers at the junction. Mobilisation and the emotions of Herr Heinrich now became the central facts of the Dower House situation. The two younger Britlings mobilised with great vigour upon the playroom floor. The elder had one hundred and ninety toy soldiers with a considerable equipment of guns and wagons; the younger had a force of a hundred and twenty-three, not counting three railway porters (with trucks complete), a policeman, five civilians and two ladies. Also they made a number of British and German flags out of paper. But as neither would allow his troops to be any existing foreign army, they agreed to be Redland and Blueland, according to the colour of their prevailing uniforms. Meanwhile Herr Heinrich confessed almost promiscuously the complication of his distresses by a hitherto unexpected emotional interest in the daughter of the village publican. She was a placid receptive young woman named Maud Hickson, on whom the young man had, it seemed, imposed the more poetical name of Marguerite.

text: Miss Corner stepped off to fetch two sticks and a ball, then returned to teach Mr. Direck how to play, noting he had a sharp, capable eye for the game. The two small boys, catching wind of play nearby, grabbed their own sticks and darted over to join in. The wife of the overnight guest emerged from the house in cropped skirts, a set of formidable shin guards fastened to her legs. Her thick fair hair was already slipping free of its fastenings, as if itching to throw itself into the fray, and she looked for all the world like a short, stout dismounted Valkyrie, her gaze clear and unshakably steady.
source_text: So Miss Corner went off to get two sticks and a ball and came back to instruct Mr. Direck. She said he had a good eye. The two small boys scenting play in the air got sticks and joined them. The overnight visitor's wife appeared from the house in abbreviated skirts, and wearing formidable shin-guards. With her abundant fair hair, which was already breaking loose, so to speak, to join the fray, she looked like a short stout dismounted Valkyr. Her gaze was clear and firm.

text: So he sat there chatting, cheeks flushed and practically buoyant with the searing vividness of every experience he’d weathered. There was no shadow of tragedy clinging to him, no faint suggestion that he had ever been broken or subjugated. Were it not for his choice of clothing, his precisely trimmed beard, and the cadence of his speech, he might easily have been taken for a Dubliner or a Cockney.
source_text: So he sat and talked, flushed and, as it were, elated by the vividness of all that he had undergone. He had no trace of tragic quality, no hint of subjugation. But for his costume and his trimmed beard and his language he might have been a Dubliner or a Cockney.

text: Mr. Fernsby’s thoughts were faintly addled by the odd, two-sided tenor of their chat, and by the curious habit Mr. Croft had of saying “runabout” when he was clearly talking about a full-sized automobile. He handed his train ticket to the station master without thinking, the man still shouting his rambling anecdote at the top of his lungs, repeating key beats and vouching for the truth of every detail as Mr. Croft settled himself and his guest into the plush seats of the waiting vehicle.
source_text: Mr. Direck's mind was a little confused by the double nature of the conversation and by the fact that Mr. Britling spoke of a car when he meant an automobile. He handed his ticket mechanically to the station-master, who continued to repeat and endorse his anecdote at the top of his voice as Mr. Britling disposed himself and his guest in the automobile.

text: “Naturally,” he said, “we all owe it to ourselves to go the extra mile for plucky little Belgium. I’d never dream of grumbling over the small discomforts that come with doing one’s bit. All the same, I can’t help saying you and dear Mrs. Haverstock are extraordinarily lucky, uncommonly lucky, with the Belgian refugees you’ve taken in. Mine, I’m afraid, are another matter entirely. The man works as some kind of correspondent, and he’s so thoroughly—oh, far too thoroughly—an atheist. Openly, unapologetically so, not just the sort of honest questioning most of us accept as reasonable these days. You and I certainly have no disagreement there. But he’s positively confrontational about it. He makes snide, dismissive comments about miracles, and half the time he doesn’t even bother saying them in French. He mumbles well enough in English for everyone to follow, right there in front of my unmarried sister. Then he heads out, claiming he’s searching for a proper café. He never locates one, of course, but he manages to track down every pub within three miles of the village. He stumbles back reeking of stout, and when I venture a small, delicate hint about his behavior, he blames the beer itself. Says it’s not up to standard—as if our fine Suffolk stout isn’t the finest in the country! He has no grasp of our quiet local customs at all, he’s far too worldly for that. The local girls all pin Belgian flags to their blouses and try out the snippets of French they’ve learned, and he takes that as a blatant invitation. There was a proper commotion only yesterday. Turns out he tried to kiss the Parslow girl who works at the Crown Inn—Ettie, you know the one. And his wife, that big, lumbering woman—she’s so very… ample, in every sense. I know it’s uncharitable to say so, but I do so wish she wouldn’t decide to nurse her infant right there in my little old bachelor drawing room, usually at the most awkward, inappropriate moments. And so openly, too, with no attempt at discretion…”
source_text: "Of course," he said, "we have to do our Utmost for Brave Little Belgium. I would be the last to complain of any little inconvenience one may experience in doing that. Still, I must confess I think you and dear Mrs. Britling are fortunate, exceptionally fortunate, in the Belgians you have got. My guests--it's unfortunate--the man is some sort of journalist and quite--oh! much too much--an Atheist. An open positive one. Not simply Honest Doubt. I'm quite prepared for honest doubt nowadays. You and I have no quarrel over that. But he is aggressive. He makes remarks about miracles, quite derogatory remarks, and not always in French. Sometimes he almost speaks English. And in front of my sister. And he goes out, he says, looking for a Café. He never finds a Café, but he certainly finds every public house within a radius of miles. And he comes back smelling dreadfully of beer. When I drop a Little Hint, he blames the beer. He says it is not good beer--our good Essex beer! He doesn't understand any of our simple ways. He's sophisticated. The girls about here wear Belgian flags--and air their little bits of French. And he takes it as an encouragement. Only yesterday there was a scene. It seems he tried to kiss the Hickson girl at the inn--Maudie.... And his wife; a great big slow woman--in every way she is--Ample; it's dreadful even to seem to criticise, but I do so _wish_ she would not see fit to sit down and nourish her baby in my poor old bachelor drawing-room--often at the most _unseasonable_ times. And--so lavishly...."

text: “We’ve been hashing out plans for the home defense volunteers,” the barrister said. “No matter how you slice it, we ought to be running drills already. But the War Office is just as dead set against us doing anything of that sort as if we were plotting to defect to the German side. It’s completely preposterous. Even if we older men aren’t fit enough to ship out overseas, we could at least fill local posts to free up active troops who are able to deploy.”
source_text: "We've been discussing home defence volunteers," said the barrister. "Anyhow we ought to be drilling. But the War Office sets its face as sternly against our doing anything of the sort as though we were going to join the Germans. It's absurd. Even if we older men aren't fit to go abroad, we could at least release troops who could."

text: We’re also issued exactly three pairs of socks each. We work ourselves to the bone day in and day out, and none of us know the first thing about darning. Once the heel of a sock wears through, blisters pop up right away. Bad enough blisters can put a man completely out of action. There are millions of surplus women referenced earlier (see above), yet the government lacks even the basic sense to assign any of them to mend our socks. So a steady portion of us end up lame, unable to work. And so it goes. And so it always goes.
source_text: "Also we get three pairs of socks. We work pretty hard. We don't know how to darn socks. When the heels wear through, come blisters. Bad blisters disable a man. Of the million of surplus women (see above) the government has not had the intelligence to get any to darn our socks. So a certain percentage of us go lame. And so on. And so on.

text: With every passing month, the war grew harsher and more unforgiving. When the Germans launched their submarine campaign in early 1915, Mr. Britling’s worries for a time centered almost entirely on the sailors and passengers aboard the sunken vessels. He watched with horror as German U-boats grew ever less willing to issue any warning to their targets before striking, and he could not fathom the grim logic that was turning every submarine assault into a reckless, do-or-die gamble with death. For the German submariners had pitted themselves against a naval power far more resourceful, steady, and skilled, far grimmer and more tight-lipped, than their own. It would be many months before Mr. Britling learned the true nature of the submarine blockade. One U-boat after another departed German ports for the North Sea, never to be seen again. No prisoners were ever reported, and the British “fishers of men” who patrolled the waters released no boastful public dispatches; U-boat after U-boat slipped away into a frigid, unbroken mystery. Only much later did Mr. Britling start to catch quiet whispers, and piece together an understanding of the soundless, suffocating grip that combed the deep waters in search of its quarry.
source_text: Each month the war grew bitterer and more cruel. Early in 1915 the Germans began their submarine war, and for a time Mr. Britling's concern was chiefly for the sailors and passengers of the ships destroyed. He noted with horror the increasing indisposition of the German submarines to give any notice to their victims; he did not understand the grim reasons that were turning every submarine attack into a desperate challenge of death. For the Germans under the seas had pitted themselves against a sea power far more resourceful, more steadfast and skilful, sterner and more silent, than their own. It was not for many months that Mr. Britling learnt the realities of the submarine blockade. Submarine after submarine went out of the German harbours into the North Sea, never to return. No prisoners were reported, no boasting was published by the British fishers of men; U boat after U boat vanished into a chilling mystery.... Only later did Mr. Britling begin to hear whispers and form ideas of the noiseless, suffocating grip that sought through the waters for its prey.

text: These inconsistent breaks from his usual distinct sense of self left Mr. Britling a baffling figure to many who judged people solely by long-held, rigid standards of individual character. On some days he came off as an infuriating, unreasonably self-righteous curmudgeon no one could ever manage to please or appease, but the truth was that when he was at his harshest calling out the flaws of his people or his country, he was actually reproaching his own failings too. And when he came across as overly self-absorbed, caught up in his own head and fixated entirely on his inner world, he was actually picking apart every corner of his own psyche to find a thread that would untangle that same messy jumble of conflicting aims that squanders humanity’s collective hope and vitality. And as the tangled, grief-heavy hours of that night dragged on, even an angel observing him would have struggled to draw a clear line between the moments Mr. Britling mourned his own personal losses and humiliations, and the moments he grieved for those universal human flaws he himself carried so much of.
source_text: These fluctuating lapses from individuation made Mr. Britling a perplexity to many who judged only by the old personal standards. At times he seemed a monster of cantankerous self-righteousness, whom nobody could please or satisfy, but indeed when he was most pitiless about the faults of his race or nation he was really reproaching himself, and when he seemed more egotistical and introspective and self-centred he was really ransacking himself for a clue to that same confusion of purposes that waste the hope and strength of humanity.
