And now through the busy distresses of the night it would have perplexed a watching angel to have drawn the line and shown when Mr. Britling, was grieving for his own loss and humiliation and when he was grieving for these common human weaknesses of which he had so large a share.

text: For several quiet moments after she finished speaking, she sat motionless, staring unblinking at the space directly before her. Then she rose sharply, stepping to the peg on the back of the kitchen door to pull down her hat and coat. The coat’s hanging strap was tangled tight, and she wrestled with it in open irritation until she ripped the fabric clean through.
source_text: She sat staring straight before her for a time after saying this. Then suddenly she got up and began taking down her hat and coat from the peg behind the kitchen door. The hanging strap of the coat was twisted and she struggled with it petulantly until she tore it.

text: He paused to reflect for a moment. “People who join in a panic are the very ones that make it worse. After all, there’s just as much food in the world as there was last month. Short of burning it all to waste, the only way to get rid of that supply is to eat it. And this year’s harvests have been strong and bountiful. Why start a frantic scramble for portions at a groaning board already heaped with enough food for everyone?”
source_text: He reflected. "Those who join a panic make a panic. After all, there is just as much food in the world as there was last month. And short of burning it the only way of getting rid of it is to eat it. And the harvests are good. Why begin a scramble at a groaning board?"

text: Mr. Britling was the kind of man who stumbled into things, while Mrs. Britling was the sort who built them up into something lasting. Mr. Britling had leased the Dower House on a random whim, and she’d turned it into a warm, welcoming home. He’d stumbled on the messy, joyful chaos of coed Sunday hockey during what was supposed to be a boring weekend at Pontings, and she’d turned that casual pastime into a beloved local institution. When they’d first gotten together, he’d brought along his young orphaned son, and the raw, lingering grief of losing his first great love. Some days, especially early on, he seemed to have pushed that loss entirely out of his mind; other days, it was impossible to miss how deeply every part of him still ached with it. She’d handled the keepsakes of his soft, darkly beautiful first wife with extreme care—she’d been surprised to find how many of them Mr. Britling had kept scattered around—and she’d done right by her often confusing, hard-to-read stepson. She never allowed herself to unpack exactly what she felt for this boy, and it’s entirely possible she never even thought there was any need to do so.
source_text: It was in the nature of Mr. Britling to incur things; it was in the nature of Mrs. Britling to establish them. Mr. Britling had taken the Dower House on impulse, and she had made it a delightful home. He had discovered the disorderly delights of mixed Sunday hockey one week-end at Pontings that had promised to be dull, and she had made it an institution.... He had come to her with his orphan boy and a memory of a passionate first loss that sometimes, and more particularly at first, he seemed to have forgotten altogether, and at other times was only too evidently lamenting with every fibre of his being. She had taken the utmost care of the relics of her duskily pretty predecessor that she found in unexpected abundance in Mr. Britling's possession, and she had done her duty by her sometimes rather incomprehensible stepson. She never allowed herself to examine the state of her heart towards this youngster; it is possible that she did not perceive the necessity for any such examination....

text: None of this was anything Mr. Direck had ever anticipated finding in England, and the supper arrangement struck him as just as unforeseen: a long table draped in no tablecloth, lit only by tapered candles set down its length. No servants were on hand to wait on guests, but a sideboard was piled high with cold salmon, chilled roasted joints, kalter aufschnitt, kartoffel salat, a wide range of other dishes, and dozens of beer, wine, and whisky bottles. Everyone served themselves, and passed plates or bottles to anyone else within reach, and Mr. Direck did his very best to be attentive to Mrs. Britling and Miss Corner, a task made far simpler by Miss Corner’s ready assistance.
source_text: It was all just exactly what Mr. Direck had never expected in England, and equally unexpected was the supper on a long candle-lit table without a cloth. No servants were present, but on a sideboard stood a cold salmon and cold joints and kalter aufschnitt and kartoffel salat, and a variety of other comestibles, and many bottles of beer and wine and whisky. One helped oneself and anybody else one could, and Mr. Direck did his best to be very attentive to Mrs. Britling and Miss Corner, and was greatly assisted by the latter.

text: He sat ruminating for a long beat, then kept speaking: “Just the reading and writing, for starters! I taught him to read entirely on my own—his first governess, you have to understand, wasn’t all that sharp. She was a good, steady, by-the-book sort, but she had no spark, no real inspiration for teaching. So I put together all kinds of custom methods to walk him through learning to read. But none of that work even ended up being necessary. He seemed to leap right over every possible difficulty as soon as it came up. He’d grasp exactly what I was trying to teach him almost before I finished explaining it. It was as quick and instinctive as the darting movement of some wild animal…
source_text: He sat thinking for an interval, and then went on, "The reading and writing alone! I taught him to read myself--because his first governess, you see, wasn't very clever. She was a very good methodical sort, but she had no inspiration. So I got up all sorts of methods for teaching him to read. But it wasn't necessary. He seemed to leap all sorts of difficulties. He leapt to what one was trying to teach him. It was as quick as the movement of some wild animal....

text:
It was mid-afternoon when Mr. Manning telephoned to buoy his friend’s spirits. A reassuring dispatch from General French had just been made public, and all was, for all practical purposes, well; the British troops had performed superbly. They had been locked in nonstop combat for days in and around Mons, attacked by forces outnumbering them six to one, yet they had repulsed the assault and inflicted devastating losses on the enemy. They had established an undeniable edge in individual fighting prowess over the Germans: enemy troops were cut down in heaps, and British soldiers charged through German cavalry formations as easily as if they were tearing through paper. This was the first full pitched battle between British and German forces, and it had ended in resounding British glory. After the brutal fighting on the 26th near Landrecies, the British had faced barely any harassment, fresh reinforcements numbering twice their recent losses had joined their ranks, and the German advance was definitively halted.

Mr. Britling’s mood swung sharply back to elation. He scribbled down the full dispatch as Mr. Manning dictated it over the line, then hurried out to the garden, where Mrs. Britling was overseeing tea for the usual casual Sunday gathering of guests, a rare expression of anxiety tugging at her features. The dispatch was read aloud twice to the group. After that came a game of hockey and raucous high spirits, before Mr. Britling headed up to his study to reply to a letter from Mrs. Harrowdean. It was the first correspondence he had received from her since their falling out the moment war broke out, and he was far better disposed to answer it warmly now than he had been at any point prior.
source_text: But in the afternoon Mr. Manning telephoned to comfort his friend. A reassuring despatch from General French had been published and--all was well--practically--and the British had been splendid. They had been fighting continuously for several days round and about Mons; they had been attacked at odds of six to one, and they had repulsed and inflicted enormous losses on the enemy. They had established an incontestable personal superiority over the Germans. The Germans had been mown down in heaps; the British had charged through their cavalry like charging through paper. So at last and very gloriously for the British, British and German had met in battle. After the hard fighting of the 26th about Landrecies, the British had been comparatively unmolested, reinforcements covering double the losses had joined them and the German advance was definitely checked ... Mr. Britling's mind swung back to elation. He took down the entire despatch from Mr. Manning's dictation, and ran out with it into the garden where Mrs. Britling, with an unwonted expression of anxiety, was presiding over the teas of the usual casual Sunday gathering.... The despatch was read aloud twice over. After that there was hockey and high spirits, and then Mr. Britling went up to his study to answer a letter from Mrs. Harrowdean, the first letter that had come from her since their breach at the outbreak of the war, and which he was now in a better mood to answer than he had been hitherto.

text: What weighed heaviest on Mrs. Elspeth Voss was the gnawing certainty that she was squandering her own days, and squandering too the days of quiet, long-suffering Tobias—all for the sake of a loyalty that exposed her to the ugliest neighborhood gossip, while he treated her with careless disregard: turned up only when the mood struck him, vanished for stretches without a word of regard, never once stopped to consider she might crave small, thoughtful kindnesses, small gentle gestures of notice, small tokens to prove he’d thought of her. Did he truly imagine she could content herself with her shabby, unfulfilling work, as paltry as it was, while left to isolation and constant neglect? He seemed to forget women were soft, fragile little creatures, meant to be delighted, and kept delighted once the feeling was kindled. Tobias might not be quick-witted or effortlessly charming, but he did at least, in his bumbling, unpolished way, grasp what was expected of him and make an effort to meet his obligations….
source_text: The burthen of Mrs. Harrowdean was that she was wasting her life, that she was wasting the poor, good, patient Oliver's life, that for the sake of friendship she was braving the worst imputations and that he treated her cavalierly, came when he wished to do so, stayed away heartlessly, never thought she needed _little_ treats, _little_ attentions, _little_ presents. Did he think she could settle down to her poor work, such as it was, in neglect and loneliness? He forgot women were dear little tender things, and had to be made happy and _kept_ happy. Oliver might not be clever and attractive but he did at least in his clumsy way understand and try and do his duty....

text: Look, if Mrs. Harrowdean had just left Mrs. Britling well enough alone, everything would have been perfectly manageable. He thought Mrs. Harrowdean was utterly charming, the kind of person he had no business being with, really. Far too good for him, by every measure. She was all easy initiative and warm, quick reciprocity, that sort of effortless, engaging energy. And she was so careful, too—she had her own reputation to safeguard, after all, and one or two of the women he’d been involved with before her (god, let those old, burnt-out affairs stay buried where they belong) hadn’t been nearly as discreet. Even so, he couldn’t stand by and let this sort of thing happen behind Edith’s back. There were all kinds of trivial, silly things he might let slide without Edith ever finding out, but not this—writing and spewing such downright ugly, malicious things about her. There was no getting around the fact that Edith was tied to his core sense of honor, his very self-respect.
source_text: You see, if Mrs. Harrowdean had left Mrs. Britling alone everything would have been quite tolerable. He considered Mrs. Harrowdean a charming human being, and altogether better than he deserved. Ever so much better. She was all initiative and response and that sort of thing. And she was so discreet. She had her own reputation to think about, and one or two of her predecessors--God rest the ashes of those fires!--had not been so discreet. Yet one could not have this sort of thing going on behind Edith's back. All sorts of things one might have going on behind Edith's back, but not this writing and saying of perfectly beastly things about Edith. Nothing could alter the fact that Edith was his honour....

text: Mr. Voss noted that his host was angling to turn the car into an exquisitely carved stone gateway, flanked by matching tile-roofed gatehouses on either side. It quickly became apparent that Mr. Thorne had abandoned this attempt entirely, and the vehicle rolled to a stop a dozen or so yards further down the main thoroughfare. “Missed it,” Mr. Thorne grumbled, dropping his hands from the steering wheel to blow out a sharp, stormy huff, then whistle a handful of bars from a fretful, skittish tune before going perfectly still.
source_text: Mr. Direck perceived that his host was trying to turn the car into a very beautiful gateway, with gate-houses on either side. Then it was manifest that Mr. Britling had abandoned this idea, and then they came to a stop a dozen yards or so along the main road. "Missed it," said Mr. Britling, and took his hands off the steering wheel and blew stormily, and then whistled some bars of a fretful air, and became still.

text: Too wrapped up in the pressing practical realities of their work to track every meandering trend and obscure niche of the era’s intellectual thought, these Massachusetts businessmen had settled on a far more streamlined, direct approach. Rather than seek out promising ideas in their half-formed, germinal stages, they waited until a thinker had risen to reliable, widespread acclaim for their fully realized work. Then, instead of slogging through dense, often recondite texts that might already have been revised or walked back by their author, they offered a generous speaking fee to the established thinker, inviting him to visit, deliver lectures, hold informal discussions, and share the full scope of his work plainly, expertly, and unfiltered straight from the source. The request was, in essence, for him to bring his full self and ideas in a tight, concentrated form. This arrangement had already sponsored pleasant, all-expenses-paid trips to America for a number of notable European thinkers, while allowing the society to form clear, first-hand judgments of each of their teachings. Mr. Britling was one of the prominent thinkers the society had decided they wanted to learn more about. It was to extend this invitation, along with the substantial honorarium the society offered both as a courtesy to its guests and a mark of its own prestige, that Mr. Direck had now traveled to Matching’s Easy. He had already sent Mr. Britling a letter of introduction, which did not lay out his exact errand, only noting that he hoped to make his acquaintance. The note had been so warmly phrased, and Mr. Britling still held such fond memories of Mr. Direck’s gracious hospitality during his own prior trip to New York, that Mr. Direck received not just an invitation to call, but an offer to stay for the entire weekend immediately upon the letter’s arrival.
source_text: Too busily occupied with practical realities to follow the thought of the age through all its divagations and into all its recesses, these Massachusetts business men had had to consider methods of access more quintessential and nuclear. And they had decided not to hunt out the best thought in its merely germinating stages, but to wait until it had emerged and flowered to some trustworthy recognition, and then, rather than toil through recondite and possibly already reconsidered books and writings generally, to offer an impressive fee to the emerged new thinker, and to invite him to come to them and to lecture to them and to have a conference with them, and to tell them simply, competently and completely at first hand just all that he was about. To come, in fact, and be himself--in a highly concentrated form. In this way a number of interesting Europeans had been given very pleasant excursions to America, and the society had been able to form very definite opinions upon their teaching. And Mr. Britling was one of the representative thinkers upon which this society had decided to inform itself. It was to broach this invitation and to offer him the impressive honorarium by which the society honoured not only its guests but itself, that Mr. Direck had now come to Matching's Easy. He had already sent Mr. Britling a letter of introduction, not indeed intimating his precise purpose, but mentioning merely a desire to know him, and the letter had been so happily phrased and its writer had left such a memory of pleasant hospitality on Mr. Britling's mind during Mr. Britling's former visit to New York, that it had immediately produced for Mr. Direck an invitation not merely to come and see him but to come and stay over the week-end.

text: He could not push his mind to take another single step. It had exhausted its allotted run, spent every last ounce of its capacity. The white-hot rage that had roiled through him was gone entirely now, and the despair that had once felt bottomless no longer stretched on into infinity. Still, the world around him remained dark, unbearable, and unforgiving. The faint glimmer of light somewhere at the far end of it all did nothing to make that darkness feel less heavy, less suffocating. That tiny sliver of light was so far removed it lay well past the limits of his own lifespan, even well past the years his son would live. It held no salve for the agony he carried now. Stretched between that distant glow and where he stood were the long, weary generations yet to come: generations full of petty bickering and sharp, unfair accusations, selfish greed and faint-hearted cowardice, half-told truths and hasty, thoughtless acts of violence. All those coming years would be crammed full of gut-wrenching, pitiful horrors: the blackened, crumbled ruins of the town just behind him, the small, grey, lifeless faces of dead children, lives torn apart and wasted for no reason, hopes snuffed out before they could even fully bloom, every spark of gladness snuffed out before it could warm anyone.
source_text: His mind could make no further steps. It had worked for its spell. His rage had ebbed away now altogether. His despair was no longer infinite. But the world was dark and dreadful still. It seemed none the less dark because at the end there was a gleam of light. It was a gleam of light far beyond the limits of his own life, far beyond the life of his son. It had no balm for these sufferings. Between it and himself stretched the weary generations still to come, generations of bickering and accusation, greed and faintheartedness, and half truth and the hasty blow. And all those years would be full of pitiful things, such pitiful things as the blackened ruins in the town behind, the little grey-faced corpses, the lives torn and wasted, the hopes extinguished and the gladness gone....

text: Why on earth did we lack even a scrap of foresight? Why were we so willfully blind to every unpleasant possibility that might lie ahead? Why were we so completely unguarded when that unanticipated crisis struck? The exact things he said about himself, he said just as readily about his country. It was such a strange detail to call to mind. It hit me just how perfectly Dower House functioned as a tiny microcosm of the entire Empire....
source_text: Why had we no foresight? Why had we this wilful blindness to disagreeable possibilities? Why did we lie so open to the unexpected crisis? Just what he said of himself he said also of his country. It was curious to remember that. To realise how closely Dower House could play the microcosm to the whole Empire....

text: “Anyhow,” said Mr. Dimple, “I’ve got it far better than poor dear Mrs. Bynne. She brought on two milliners, was completely insistent on hiring them, too. The clothes they made were genuinely exquisite—even my bumbling, unworldly old eye could tell that much. She figured two milliners would be so useful for a large family like hers. They certainly claimed they were milliners, for whatever that’s worth. But as it turns out— I have no clue what we’re even going to do about the two of them… My dear Mr. Britling, those young women are anything but milliners—anything even remotely close to milliners…”
source_text: "But anyhow," said Mr. Dimple, "I'm better off than poor dear Mrs. Bynne. She secured two milliners. She insisted upon them. And their clothes were certainly beautifully made--even my poor old unworldly eye could tell that. And she thought two milliners would be so useful with a large family like hers. They certainly _said_ they were milliners. But it seems--I don't know what we shall do about them.... My dear Mr. Britling, those young women are anything but milliners--anything but milliners...."

text: I says to ‘im, I says, ‘‘Ave you tried the vibritation of the trains, Mr. Darling?’ I says. ‘That’s what you ‘aven’t gotten around to testing, that’s what you just can’t try,’ I says. ‘But you rest assured that’s the whole secret behind my sweet peas,’ I tells him, ‘nothin’ less, nothin’ more, than the vibritation of the trains.’
source_text: "I says to 'im, I says, ''ave you tried the vibritation of the trains?' I says. 'That's what you 'aven't tried, Mr. Darling. That's what you _can't_ try,' I says. 'But you rest assured that that's the secret of my sweet peas,' I says, 'nothing less and nothing more than the vibritation of the trains.'"

text: “You think they are,” Mr. Britling said, pausing for a beat before he unloaded all his pent-up feelings about America in a rant that grew steadily more bitter with every passing sentence. He started out making measured, reasoned points and walking through his perspective, but the longer he talked, the more he lost his grip on self-control. He turned rigidly dogmatic, then openly condemnatory, then downright abusive. More and more, he conflated Mr. Direck with the entire country he was lashing out at, spitting the sharp, uncivil “you” straight at him with greater force each time he spoke it. His cigar burned out unnoticed while he ranted, and he tossed it into the fireplace with an impatient flick, as if America itself had been the one to snuff out the flame.
source_text: "You think they are," said Mr. Britling, and paused, and then began to deliver his soul about America in a discourse of accumulating bitterness. At first he reasoned and explained, but as he went on he lost self-control; he became dogmatic, he became denunciatory, he became abusive. He identified Mr. Direck more and more with his subject; he thrust the uncivil "You" more and more directly at him. He let his cigar go out, and flung it impatiently into the fire. As though America was responsible for its going out....

text: I headed over to Europe with zero hostile intent, honestly, so what’s unfolded since has completely blindsided me. I’ve basically been tossed out on my ear, no two ways about it. Every last piece of my luggage is gone, stuck at some rinky-dink train stop close to the Dutch border that no one will even tell me the name of. I bet some German household is thrilled to bits with my dress shirts, too—those were really nice, high-quality ones. This tweed suit I’m wearing right now is literally the only clothing I own on the entire planet. All my cash, legitimate US banknotes? They just laughed when I tried to use those. Then I pulled out English gold coins, and they decided I must be British, so they locked me up. Let me tell you, English people are wildly unpopular in Germany right now, like, seriously hated. You’d think for a group of people who’re getting exactly what they asked for, the Germans would be a lot less ticked off, honestly. I ended up having to get the American consul to spot me some emergency cash to get by. Since this whole mess started, I’ve spent more time loitering around, skipping meals on and off, traveling on a totally empty stomach, and staring at whatever I was allowed to see from rail yard sidelines—they usually made us yank the window blinds down any time there was something sensitive on the tracks—than any steer that ever got shipped to Chicago. I was treated like cargo, bottom-of-the-barrel cargo at that. It’s not even worth thinking back on.
source_text: "I came to Europe in a perfectly friendly spirit--and I must say I am surprised. Practically I have been thrown out, neck and crop. All my luggage is lost. Away at some one-horse junction near the Dutch frontier that I can't even learn the name of. There's joy in some German home, I guess, over my shirts; they were real good shirts. This tweed suit I have is all the wardrobe I've got in the world. All my money--good American notes--well, they laughed at them. And when I produced English gold they suspected me of being English and put me under arrest.... I can assure you that the English are most unpopular in Germany at the present time, thoroughly unpopular.... Considering that they are getting exactly what they were asking for, these Germans are really remarkably annoyed.... Well, I had to get the American consul to advance me money, and I've done more waiting about and irregular fasting and travelling on an empty stomach and viewing the world, so far as it was permitted, from railway sidings--for usually they made us pull the blinds down when anything important was on the track--than any cow that ever came to Chicago.... I was handed as freight--low grade freight.... It doesn't bear recalling."

text: If I’m to stay put right here just on account of that little shake-up—maybe for a couple of weeks, maybe three—and if you’ll come around to chat with me every now and again, I’ll tell you I don’t call this a misfortune. It isn’t any sort of bad luck. It’s straight, sheer good fortune, plain and simple.
source_text: "If I'm to stay right here just as a consequence of that little shake up, may be for a couple of weeks, may be three, and if you're coming to do a bit of a talk to me ever and again, then I tell you I don't call this a misfortune. It isn't a misfortune. It's right down sheer good luck...."

text: That fateful night, when the first gunshots rang out and the first lives were lost, Mr. Britling did not sleep until the first streaks of daylight broke over the horizon. He sat hunched at his desk working on his pamphlet, the piece he’d drafted to herald the final blast and the permanent end of all war. He wrote with urgent, unbroken energy for two hours or so, before his drive began to ebb. He fell into long stretches where he sat completely still, not putting a single word to the page. He yawned over and over, rubbing at his heavy, bleary eyes. By the time he undressed slowly, dropping his clothes wherever they landed on the floor and slipping into bed, the sun had fully risen and the birds outside were chattering noisily.
source_text: On that eventful night of the first shots and the first deaths Mr. Britling did not sleep until daylight had come. He sat writing at this pamphlet of his, which was to hail the last explosion and the ending of war. For a couple of hours he wrote with energy, and then his energy flagged. There came intervals when he sat still and did not write. He yawned and yawned again and rubbed his eyes. The day had come and the birds were noisy when he undressed slowly, dropping his clothes anyhow upon the floor, and got into bed....

text: It also became clear that Mr. Van der Pant had no way of reaching any of his family members at all. All at once, he grew sharply critical of how the British handled just about everything. His wife and young child had traveled to England ahead of him, making the crossing from Ostend to Folkestone two weeks earlier; his in-laws had arrived back in August. Both groups had been picked up by ad-hoc British relief organizations, and had vanished entirely without a trace. He’d written to the Belgian Embassy for help, who passed his case along to a London committee. The committee’s first move was to track down a Madame Van der Pant living in Camberwell, a woman he’d never heard of in his life, and they reacted with clear suspicion and irritation when he told them that wasn’t his wife. A handful of useless telegrams had been exchanged to no effect. “What,” asked Mr. Van der Pant, “is a person even supposed to do?”
source_text: Also it became manifest that Mr. Van der Pant was cut off from all his family. And suddenly he became briskly critical of the English way of doing things. His wife and child had preceded him to England, crossing by Ostend and Folkestone a fortnight ago; her parents had come in August; both groups had been seized upon by improvised British organisations and very thoroughly and completely lost. He had written to the Belgian Embassy and they had referred him to a committee in London, and the committee had begun its services by discovering a Madame Van der Pant hitherto unknown to him at Camberwell, and displaying a certain suspicion and hostility when he said she would not do. There had been some futile telegrams. "What," asked Mr. Van der Pant, "ought one to do?"

text: “You yelled, ‘What the hell are we even doing at this hockey match? We should be out drilling or taking shots at those blasted Germans!’… I’ve never been able to get that line out of my head… I should’ve done this ages ago. I used to be a scoutmaster, after all. Before long, they’re going to be crying out for more officers. From what I’ve heard, there are tons of officer training corps in London rushing men through their required drills as fast as possible… If only I could go…”
source_text: "You said, 'What the devil are we doing at this hockey? We ought to be drilling or shooting against those confounded Germans!' ... I've never forgotten it.... I ought to have done it before. I've been a scout-master. In a little while they will want officers. In London, I'm told, there are a lot of officers' training corps putting men through the work as quickly as possible.... If I could go...."

text: I’m not sure what to make of this “missing” business. Nothing’s unfolded on our stretch of line that would leave anyone unaccounted for—every last man in our company is logged present and safe. But conditions shift every couple of miles along the whole front. His unit might have been posted right up on the forward edge, where there’s constant raiding parties and scrapping over half-rotted snippets of trench no one even cares about half the time. In some sectors, the German trenches are less than thirty paces off, so there’s nonstop tunneling, grenade volleys, and quiet little patrol skirmishes that swap a few feet of mud back and forth daily. Our sector’s actually pushed a little ways forward these past weeks. I’ll fill you in on all that later, though. Either way, I still can’t wrap my head around this “missing” label. Hardly anyone’s taken prisoner these days, for one thing. But don’t breathe a word of that to Lila. I keep trying to picture old Edmund stuck in that kind of chaos…
source_text: "I don't know about 'missing.' We've had nothing going on here that has led to any missing. All our men have been accounted for. But every few miles along the front conditions alter. His lot may have been closer up to the enemy, and there may have been a rush and a fight for a bit of trench either way. In some parts the German trenches are not thirty yards away, and there is mining, bomb throwing, and perpetual creeping up and give and take. Here we've been getting a bit forward. But I'll tell you about that presently. And, anyhow, I don't understand about 'missing.' There's very few prisoners taken now. But don't tell Letty that. I try to imagine old Teddy in it....

text: For several days, Mr. Britling could not turn his mind to anything but Hugh, and every thought of his son came paired with a dull, heavy ache rooted deep in his chest. The feeling was identical to the dread he’d carried years earlier, when he’d waited on the floor below while Hugh lay upstairs on an operating table, under a surgeon’s blade. Only this time, the procedure dragged on and on, with no end in sight. Even at the very worst, his boy had only a one-in-five chance of dying or suffering serious, lasting harm, but for a stretch he could fixate on nothing besides that single terrible possibility. He felt it pressing against every edge of his thoughts, bearing down on him until he could barely lift his shoulders.
source_text: For some days Mr. Britling could think of nothing but Hugh, and always with a dull pain at his heart. He felt as he had felt long ago while he had waited downstairs and Hugh upstairs had been under the knife of a surgeon. But this time the operation went on and still went on. At the worst his boy had but one chance in five of death or serious injury, but for a time he could think of nothing but that one chance. He felt it pressing upon his mind, pressing him down....

text: It was deep into the night when Mrs. Britling finally went to her husband, after what had felt like an eternity of weighing resolutions, nursing doubts, and wrestling with indecision. He was huddled close to the fire, chin resting on his hands, waiting for her; he had known she would come, and in the interim his thoughts had moved slowly, circling nowhere, fixed entirely on Hugh. A small, subtle shift of his posture signaled he’d heard her enter the room, but he did not turn to look at her. He shrank back just slightly as she approached.
source_text: It was late that night and after an eternity of resolutions and doubts and indecisions that Mrs. Britling went to her husband. He was sitting close up to the fire with his chin upon his hands, waiting for her; he felt that she would come to him, and he was thinking meanwhile of Hugh with a slow unprogressive movement of the mind. He showed by a movement that he heard her enter the room, but he did not turn to look at her. He shrank a little from her approach.

text: She could not say if five minutes or nearly forty minutes had drifted past before she registered that someone was watching her. She turned with a sharp, involuntary flinch, and found Pastor Thorne with one foot propped on the weathered stile, a look of confused alarm stretched across his plump, ruddy face.
source_text: She did not know if it was five minutes or half an hour later that she became aware that some one was looking at her. She turned with a start, and discovered the Reverend Dimple with one foot on the stile, and an expression of perplexity and consternation upon his chubby visage.

text: She went on holding forth to everyone within earshot about exactly how she’d deal with striking laborers. She’d give them “no quarter whatsoever”, she said, administer a dose of the Prussian method as a kind of homeopathic corrective. “But of course that old vote-grubbing Gresham daren’t—he simply hasn’t the nerve!” Mr. Thorne opened his mouth to interject, then closed it again without a sound, thoroughly silenced. The men in khaki uniform listened respectfully but gave no clear sign of their own thoughts; one of the younger women in the group was fully aligned with Lady Harlow’s stance, and keen to make that alignment obvious. The well-meaning matriarch had now turned her sights to the Cabinet, and set about dressing down all twenty-two of its members thoroughly. Edmund Kittredge had overruled Lord Pembroke on the question of the Dardanelles campaign, and shouldered terrible blame for the resulting fallout. Lord Cray—she referred to him as “Pudgy Cray”—was a convicted traitor in her eyes. “The man’s a German through and through. Oh, what if he doesn’t have a single drop of German blood in his veins? He’s a German by choice—and that’s a far worse sin.”
source_text: She proceeded to tell everybody what she would do with strikers. She would give them "short shrift." She would give them a taste of the Prussian way--homoeopathic treatment. "But of course old vote-catching Asquith daren't--he daren't!" Mr. Britling opened his mouth and said nothing; he was silenced. The men in khaki listened respectfully but ambiguously; one of the younger ladies it seemed was entirely of Lady Frensham's way of thinking, and anxious to show it. The good lady having now got her hands upon the Cabinet proceeded to deal faithfully with its two-and-twenty members. Winston Churchill had overridden Lord Fisher upon the question of Gallipoli, and incurred terrible responsibilities. Lord Haldane--she called him "Tubby Haldane"--was a convicted traitor. "The man's a German out and out. Oh! what if he hasn't a drop of German blood in his veins? He's a German by choice--which is worse."

text: It was immediately clear that Lady Frensham belonged to the fiercely energetic cohort of aristocratic women who had dug in their heels for an uncompromising stance against Home Rule, “in any shape or form,” in that particular era. These women were quickly warping British politics into a web of vicious personal grudges, where any consideration for the empire’s collective good had been entirely cast aside. A reckless urge to outdo even the most militant suffragettes appeared to have taken hold of the group entirely. They hurled insults, publicly denounced their opponents, turned down every social invitation that might put them in the same room as the man they dismissed as the “traitor” Prime Minister, and mimicked the rabid partisan loathing of far more violent historical eras. Even the mild, politically shrewd Philbert was being completely blanked by them at that very moment. Their cause was backed by the most hardline wing of the Conservative press, which trotted out shockingly unhinged writers to rant and rave like madmen, branding the entire government “traitors” who had “insulted the King.” The *Morning Post*, along with the more flippant, less thoughtful end of the Unionist media ecosystem, spewed a constant flood of partisan garbage so over the top it’s almost impossible to believe looking back. Lady Frensham, all puffed up with indignation as she made her way through Lady Homartyn’s gathering, had stepped away from Mr. Britling for the moment to rush off and recount the latest twists in this massive political grudge match. She had an incredibly vivid story about Lady Londonderry sitting directly across from “that old scoundrel the Prime Minister” at a recent performance of Mozart’s *Zauberflöte*.
source_text: Lady Frensham, it was manifest, was one of that energetic body of aristocratic ladies who were taking up an irreconcilable attitude against Home Rule "in any shape or form" at that time. They were rapidly turning British politics into a system of bitter personal feuds in which all sense of imperial welfare was lost. A wild ambition to emulate the extremest suffragettes seems to have seized upon them. They insulted, they denounced, they refused every invitation lest they should meet that "traitor" the Prime Minister, they imitated the party hatreds of a fiercer age, and even now the moderate and politic Philbert found himself treated as an invisible object. They were supported by the extremer section of the Tory press, and the most extraordinary writers were set up to froth like lunatics against the government as "traitors," as men who "insulted the King"; the _Morning Post_ and the lighter-witted side of the Unionist press generally poured out a torrent of partisan nonsense it is now almost incredible to recall. Lady Frensham, bridling over Lady Homartyn's party, and for a time leaving Mr. Britling, hurried on to tell of the newest developments of the great feud. She had a wonderful description of Lady Londonderry sitting opposite "that old rascal, the Prime Minister," at a performance of Mozart's _Zauberflöte_.

text: Truly, the very instant Mr. Hawthorne uttered those words, the hour had already ticked later in Sarajevo, Bosnia, where clusters of men huddled together, speaking in low, urgent whispers. One man clutched a matte black parcel pressed into his hands earlier, his knuckles white with taut nerves, nodding sharply as his companions repeated his assigned instructions back to him. Tucked inside that unassuming black bundle were volatile chemical compounds and a carefully calibrated arrangement of detonators, a package fated to eventually shatter almost every core tenet of the orderly, unchallenged worldview Mr. Hawthorne and Lady Wetherby had always taken as unshakable fact.
source_text: And indeed at the very moment when Mr. Britling was saying these words, in Sarajevo in Bosnia, where the hour was somewhat later, men whispered together, and one held nervously to a black parcel that had been given him and nodded as they repeated his instructions, a black parcel with certain unstable chemicals and a curious arrangement of detonators therein, a black parcel destined ultimately to shatter nearly every landmark of Mr. Britling's and Lady Frensham's cosmogony....

text: Good lord, how lazy we English have gotten these days! We cling so easily to those cozy, self-serving lies that let us off the hook for putting in any real effort. For three whole weeks now, I’ve willfully clung to the belief that our small British army—word is it’s barely a hundred thousand men strong—would somehow manage to halt this tidal wave of millions of advancing troops. But they’ve been pushed back, just as anyone who isn’t addicted to convenient fantasies would have predicted they’d be—first at this spot, then that one, then another, all up the line. They’ve fought around the clock, night and day. It’s been the most brilliant, heroic stand imaginable—and also the most completely futile one.… You can see the German force swinging like a massive, crushing flail across Belgium, can’t you? And all this while, we’ve just been milling about chattering about all the grand things we’d do once we secured our victory.…
source_text: "How lazy we English are nowadays! How readily we grasp the comforting delusion that excuses us from exertion. For the last three weeks I have been deliberately believing that a little British army--they say it is scarcely a hundred thousand men--would somehow break this rush of millions. But it has been driven back, as any one not in love with easy dreams might have known it would be driven back--here and then here and then here. It has been fighting night and day. It has made the most splendid fight--and the most ineffectual fight.... You see the vast swing of the German flail through Belgium. And meanwhile we have been standing about talking of the use we would make of our victory....

text: “It’s—what do they call that governing body, again? The Essex County Council.” But no one paid that offhand remark any attention whatsoever. When Mr. Philbert, who held a government ministerial post, joined them for lunch, he carried himself exactly like any other guest in attendance. It was only after he had already departed that Herr Heinrich learned entirely by chance that the man was a senior minister, and entitled to the formal honorific “Right Honourable…”
source_text: "It is--what do they call it? the Essex County Council." But nobody took any notice of that. And when Mr. Philbert, who was a minister in the government, came to lunch he was just like any one else. It was only after he had gone that Herr Heinrich had learnt by chance that he was a minister and "Right Honourable...."

text: “That’s a clumsy turn of phrase,” said Mr. Halewright. “I’ll retract it. Let me lay out precisely what I’m getting at. I’m talking about a shift taking root across the U.S., right here in Britain, throughout the Nordic nations, and in Russia—an emerging cultural framework, a break from the Levantine faiths and the Catholic cultural lineage that flowed to us from the Mediterranean basin. Let’s set aside the term ‘Neo-European’; I’ll call it Northern instead. We are Northern peoples, after all. The beating heart, the unshakable core of any cultural order is how it frames the relationship between men and women; and this rising new order works to erode the rigid specialization of women purely on the basis of their gender, unlocking them from the confined sphere of the domestic home to stand as equal citizens alongside men. It’s a still-evolving cultural project, one that will shape men to be more communal and collaborative, and women to be more daring, quick-thinking, accountable, and far less confined to sheltered, separate lives. It downplays, rather than amplifies, the outsized emphasis placed on sex as a defining category....
source_text: "It's a bad phrase," said Mr. Britling. "I'll withdraw it. Let me try and state exactly what I have in mind. I mean something that is coming up in America and here and the Scandinavian countries and Russia, a new culture, an escape from the Levantine religion and the Catholic culture that came to us from the Mediterranean. Let me drop Neo-European; let me say Northern. We are Northerners. The key, the heart, the nucleus and essence of every culture is its conception of the relations of men and women; and this new culture tends to diminish the specialisation of women as women, to let them out from the cell of the home into common citizenship with men. It's a new culture, still in process of development, which will make men more social and co-operative and women bolder, swifter, more responsible and less cloistered. It minimises instead of exaggerating the importance of sex....

text:
The notion of an assault on the Dardanelles held a timeless, almost mythic appeal for the British collective consciousness. Generations had grown up with “forcing the Dardanelles” as a familiar, almost comforting turn of phrase; it carried none of the flighty novelty and tacky sensationalism that made an “aerial offensive” feel like such an unprincipled, reckless gambit. Forcing the Dardanelles was steeped in British military history, representing no break with long-held strategic tradition.

Barely a week after Turkey entered the war, British submarines slipped into the Sea of Marmora, and by February a systematic bombardment of the Dardanelles coast had commenced; it continued on and off for a month, with Ottoman defenders leveraging lulls in the shelling and stretches of harsh weather to reinforce their fortifications. This first phase of the attack culminated in the sinking of the *Irresistible*, *Ocean*, and *Bouvet*, when on March 17 the attacking fleet closed in full force on the Narrows.

A six-week lull followed, which the now fully alerted defenders used to rush further preparations, before Allied armies amassed in the region and pulled off a brutal, high-cost landing at two points on the Gallipoli Peninsula. That landing marked the start of a slow, blood-soaked siege of the Dardanelles defences, which dragged on until the surprise landing of a fresh British force at Suvla Bay in August. That operation collapsed in failure at the Battle of Anafarta, felled by incompetent commanding officers and widespread sloppiness across the senior leadership, leaving the force unable to cut off and seize Maidos and the Narrows fortifications.

Meanwhile, the Russian military, which had hit its high-water mark with the capture of Przemysl, was being pushed back first on the southern front, then the northern. German forces retook Lemberg, marched into Warsaw, and pushed onward to seize Brest Litowsk. The Russian lines rolled backward in a devastating cascade of defeat, as German troops thrust toward Riga and Petrograd, taking Vilna around mid-September.
source_text: The idea of an attack upon the Dardanelles had a traditional attractiveness for the British mind. Old men had been brought up from childhood with "forcing the Dardanelles" as a familiar phrase; it had none of the flighty novelty and vulgarity about it that made an "aerial offensive" seem so unwarrantable a proceeding. Forcing the Dardanelles was historically British. It made no break with tradition. Soon after Turkey entered the war British submarines appeared in the Sea of Marmora, and in February a systematic bombardment of the Dardanelles began; this was continued intermittently for a month, the defenders profiting by their experiences and by spells of bad weather to strengthen their works. This first phase of the attack culminated in the loss of the _Irresistible_, _Ocean_, and _Bouvet_, when on the 17th of March the attacking fleet closed in upon the Narrows. After an interlude of six weeks to allow of further preparations on the part of the defenders, who were now thoroughly alive to what was coming, the Allied armies gathered upon the scene, and a difficult and costly landing was achieved at two points upon the peninsula of Gallipoli. With that began a slow and bloody siege of the defences of the Dardanelles, clambering up to the surprise landing of a fresh British army in Suvla Bay in August, and its failure in the battle of Anafarta, through incompetent commanders and a general sloppiness of leading, to cut off and capture Maidos and the Narrows defences.... Meanwhile the Russian hosts, which had reached their high-water mark in the capture of Przemysl, were being forced back first in the south and then in the north. The Germans recaptured Lemberg, entered Warsaw, and pressed on to take Brest Litowsk. The Russian lines rolled back with an impressive effect of defeat, and the Germans thrust towards Riga and Petrograd, reaching Vilna about the middle of September....

text: He no longer wrote off the Germans as diabolical fiends. They were only human, after all, with a cause they believed they were right to defend. That cause was foolish, he conceded, but the cause his own side fought for was every bit as foolish. How utterly senseless all these supposed justifications were! What crucial thing were all of them overlooking? It was something, he sensed, so close it was almost touching them, yet maddeningly slippery. Something that could unravel a hundred tangled, warring divides…
source_text: He was no longer thinking of the Germans as diabolical. They were human; they had a case. It was a stupid case, but our case, too, was a stupid case. How stupid were all our cases! What was it we missed? Something, he felt, very close to us, and very elusive. Something that would resolve a hundred tangled oppositions....

text: Yet this vision of America as a dispassionate judicial presence, vocal in protest but deliberately aloof from the fray, led him to a conclusion that left him deeply scandalized. If America will not, and indeed ought not, wield military force in pursuit of just ends, he argued, then it has no right to manufacture and export weapons of war. It cannot trade in the very violence it publicly disavows. He held a wildly inflated sense of how much munitions America was sending to the Allies, even leaning into the belief that the Allies were entirely dependent on those transatlantic supplies to keep fighting. As a result, he grew convinced that an Allied victory and America’s national honor were fundamentally incompatible. And for all his dedication to America’s stated ethical distance from the war, he loved the Allies deeply. He wanted desperately to see them win, even as he believed America ought to abandon the course of action he was certain was critical to that victory. It was a torturous intellectual dilemma. He hid this glaring self-contradiction from the community at Matching’s Easy with the same skittish, ashamed urgency a curate might bring to hiding a poisoned dagger in his coat at a polite afternoon tea party.
source_text: But this idea of an America judicial, remonstrating, and aloof, led him to a conclusion that scandalised him. If America will not, and should not use force in the ends of justice, he argued, then America has no right to make and export munitions of war. She must not trade in what she disavows. He had a quite exaggerated idea of the amount of munitions that America was sending to the Allies, he was inclined to believe that they were entirely dependent upon their transatlantic supplies, and so he found himself persuaded that the victory of the Allies and the honour of America were incompatible things. And--in spite of his ethical aloofness--he loved the Allies. He wanted them to win, and he wanted America to abandon a course that he believed was vitally necessary to their victory. It was an intellectual dilemma. He hid this self-contradiction from Matching's Easy with much the same feelings that a curate might hide a poisoned dagger at a tea-party....

text: Germany’s military buildup, its openly hostile posture, its unwavering ideological and territorial demands, and the constant global friction between British and German national interests had been unavoidable realities in the British public consciousness for more than 25 years. An entire generation had been born and raised with the looming threat of war with Germany hanging over them. Any threat that lingers long enough stops feeling like an urgent risk, and this overhanging possibility had turned into a steady, barely disruptive feature of British life. It kept the Royal Navy diligent and Colonel Rendezvous perpetually on edge; it drove a tiny, largely uninfluential faction of the press to churn out repetitive warnings that irritated Mr. Britling intensely, provided a justification for the activist campaigning that made mandatory national service look absurd, and seeped into his subconscious to shape his opinions on dozens of unrelated issues. Case in point: it was a key factor in his sharp indignation at the Conservative Party’s careless, dismissive handling of Irish affairs, and his disgust with the many policies that riled up or alienated Indian public sentiment. The whole situation bored him; he couldn’t deny it was a genuine danger, yet he firmly believed it was a landmine that would never be triggered, an avalanche that would never come tumbling down. It was a pointless, frustrating nuisance that had all of Europe running military drills and pouring enormous sums into unavoidable defense preparations; it put every forward-facing initiative on hold, like a loud, petty argument derailing an otherwise pleasant gathering in a sitting room, but he could not imagine that human pettiness and folly would ever actually let that landmine detonate. He had been in France in 1911, and had seen firsthand how close the continent had come to open conflict back then. The fact that war never broke out had only vastly reinforced his natural inclination to believe that, at its core, Germany was run by reasonable actors, and its aggressive militarism was nothing more than empty bluff.
source_text: The armament of Germany, the hostility of Germany, the consistent assertion of Germany, the world-wide clash of British and German interests, had been facts in the consciousness of Englishmen for more than a quarter of a century. A whole generation had been born and brought up in the threat of this German war. A threat that goes on for too long ceases to have the effect of a threat, and this overhanging possibility had become a fixed and scarcely disturbing feature of the British situation. It kept the navy sedulous and Colonel Rendezvous uneasy; it stimulated a small and not very influential section of the press to a series of reminders that bored Mr. Britling acutely, it was the excuse for an agitation that made national service ridiculous, and quite subconsciously it affected his attitude to a hundred things. For example, it was a factor in his very keen indignation at the Tory levity in Ireland, in his disgust with many things that irritated or estranged Indian feeling. It bored him; there it was, a danger, and there was no denying it, and yet he believed firmly that it was a mine that would never be fired, an avalanche that would never fall. It was a nuisance, a stupidity, that kept Europe drilling and wasted enormous sums on unavoidable preparations; it hung up everything like a noisy argument in a drawing-room, but that human weakness and folly would ever let the mine actually explode he did not believe. He had been in France in 1911, he had seen how close things had come then to a conflict, and the fact that they had not come to a conflict had enormously strengthened his natural disposition to believe that at bottom Germany was sane and her militarism a bluff.

text: “I stroked him once today, and he bit me three times,” Herr Heinrich reported. “Before long I’ll pet him three times, and he won’t bite me at all… And yesterday, he climbed right up my body, settled on my shoulder, and bit my ear out of the blue. The bite wasn’t hard, just totally unexpected.”
source_text: "To-day I have stroked him once and he has bitten me three times," Herr Heinrich reported. "Soon I will stroke him three times and he shall not bite me at all.... Also yesterday he climbed up me and sat on my shoulder, and suddenly bit my ear. It was not hard he bit, but sudden.

text: Hugh’s letters split almost evenly between two recurring preoccupations: first, the gripping allure of military artistry, and second, a deep, visceral rejection of warfare itself. “Once you’ve moved past the initial gut shock of it,” he wrote, “once your mind has adjusted to and even tuned out the unending horror and senseless waste of it all, I start to realize war is quite simply the greatest game in the world. That’s its real, unspoken power, I think. Not what you argued in that early pamphlet of yours—ambition, cruelty, all those base impulses. Those just give people an excuse for war, they push cowardly, small-minded folks into backing it, but the core of its hold is how it seizes an active, restless imagination. It’s such a vast, unconstrained game. Instead of being fenced into a small field and stuck with a fixed set of tools like you are in nearly every other pastime, you’ve got the whole world as your playing field, and you can use whatever resource you can get your hands on. You get to put every last scrap of creativity and ingenuity you have to work. It’s astonishing…. But real, career soldiers aren’t cruel. And war isn’t cruel at its core. Only in its aftereffects, its consequences. Over here I catch snippets of conversation that make all of this make sense. Almost all the reported atrocities, it’s perfectly clear, were committed by overwrought, civilian-style men, people caught up in a kind of inflamed, feverish state. The vast majority of the German army in the early months of the war was really a force of unmoored civilians. Trained to fight, no doubt, but civilian at their very core. They were kind, orderly, clean, law-abiding men suddenly ripped from their regular lives and tossed into utterly unthinkable, horrific conditions. They thought they were charging straight toward death, that all ordinary decency was done for. They suspected every Belgian had a gun hidden behind the garden hedge and a knife tucked in their pant leg. They watched villages burn to the ground, saw dead bodies and men blown to unrecognizable scraps. They lived in a sort of waking nightmare. They had no idea what they were doing. They did those horrible things the same way you or I might do unthinkable things sometimes, when we’re lost in a bad dream.”
source_text: Hugh's letters divided themselves pretty fairly between two main topics; the first was the interest of the art of war, the second the reaction against warfare. "After one has got over the emotion of it," he wrote, "and when one's mind has just accepted and forgotten (as it does) the horrors and waste of it all, then I begin to perceive that war is absolutely the best game in the world. That is the real strength of war, I submit. Not as you put it in that early pamphlet of yours; ambition, cruelty, and all those things. Those things give an excuse for war, they rush timid and base people into war, but the essential matter is the hold of the thing itself upon an active imagination. It's such a big game. Instead of being fenced into a field and tied down to one set of tools as you are in almost every other game, you have all the world to play and you may use whatever you can use. You can use every scrap of imagination and invention that is in you. And it's wonderful.... But real soldiers aren't cruel. And war isn't cruel in its essence. Only in its consequences. Over here one gets hold of scraps of talk that light up things. Most of the barbarities were done--it is quite clear--by an excited civilian sort of men, men in a kind of inflamed state. The great part of the German army in the early stage of the war was really an army of demented civilians. Trained civilians no doubt, but civilians in soul. They were nice orderly clean law-abiding men suddenly torn up by the roots and flung into quite shocking conditions. They felt they were rushing at death, and that decency was at an end. They thought every Belgian had a gun behind the hedge and a knife in his trouser leg. They saw villages burning and dead people, and men smashed to bits. They lived in a kind of nightmare. They didn't know what they were doing. They did horrible things just as one does them sometimes in dreams...."

text: Ignoring Aunt Wilshire was a deliberate habit Mr. Britling had cultivated after years of exasperating encounters with her, a routine he adhered to reliably. She was not, strictly speaking, his aunt at all; she was one of those distant cousins who seem to have already been woven into the fabric of our lives by the time we reach an age of adult responsibility. She had been a steady presence in his father’s household when Mr. Britling was a boy, back when she went simply by “Jane,” “Cousin Jane,” or occasionally “your cousin Wilshire.” Elevating her to the formal rank of Aunt had been a gentle, playful whim of Mr. Britling’s, later on.
source_text: It was Mr. Britling's habit, a habit he had set himself to acquire after much irritating experience, to disregard Aunt Wilshire. She was not, strictly speaking, his aunt; she was one of those distant cousins we find already woven into our lives when we attain to years of responsibility. She had been a presence in his father's household when Mr. Britling was a boy. Then she had been called "Jane," or "Cousin Jane," or "Your cousin Wilshire." It had been a kindly freak of Mr. Britling's to promote her to Aunty rank.

text: Elias pulled himself together to recount his recent experiences in detail, along with the unmoored, roiling unrest that had settled over his mind. He lingered especially on his futile attempts to take in the landmarks of Bruges while the streets thrummed with the chaos of military mobilization. After a while the steady stream of his story faltered and lost momentum, and a quiet, unspoken consensus settled over the room that he ought to be left alone with Elara. Finn remembered a letter he had to post at once; Maeve scooped up the toddler to let him crawl across the moss-dusted flagstones beneath the gnarled old apple tree. Mr. Hale propped his shoulder against the window ledge, and fell still for several long moments once the door had clicked shut behind Maeve.
source_text: He roused himself to describe his experiences at some length, and the extraordinary disturbance of his mind. He related more particularly his attempts to see the sights of Cologne during the stir of mobilisation. After a time his narrative flow lost force, and there was a general feeling that he ought to be left alone with Cissie. Teddy had a letter that must be posted; Letty took the infant to crawl on the mossy stones under the pear tree. Mr. Direck leant against the window-sill and became silent for some moments after the door had closed on Letty.

text: Other times, Van der Pant would recount brutal stories of death and disfigurement unfolding in Antwerp. German forces occupying Belgium shot women constantly, not just for serious espionage charges, but for petty, insignificant infractions. Then came the German battleship raid on Whitby and Scarborough, which killed, among many other victims, a group of children making their way to school. This news hit Mr. Britling far harder than the atrocities reported in Belgium ever had, a reaction he almost felt was irrational. Those were *English* children, killed on their own home soil! When a ship packed with Flemish refugees was torpedoed, drowning hundreds of passengers, he couldn’t stop turning over piteous, unshakable images of that loss in his head for days afterward. Before 1914 drew to a close, Zeppelin raids started too, building slowly in a crescendo of senseless, blood-soaked destruction. It brought Mr. Britling precious little comfort to remind himself that British homes, women, and children were only suffering the exact same kind of violence British naval ships had inflicted scores of times in the past, on innocent villagers in small settlements across Africa and Polynesia.
source_text: Then again it would be some incident of death and mutilation in Antwerp, that Van der Pant described to him. The Germans in Belgium were shooting women frequently, not simply for grave spying but for trivial offences.... Then came the battleship raid on Whitby and Scarborough, and the killing among other victims of a number of children on their way to school. This shocked Mr. Britling absurdly, much more than the Belgian crimes had done. They were _English_ children. At home!... The drowning of a great number of people on a torpedoed ship full of refugees from Flanders filled his mind with pitiful imaginings for days. The Zeppelin raids, with their slow crescendo of blood-stained futility, began before the end of 1914.... It was small consolation for Mr. Britling to reflect that English homes and women and children were, after all, undergoing only the same kind of experience that our ships have inflicted scores of times in the past upon innocent people in the villages of Africa and Polynesia....

text: “Our boys,” he wrote, “have died, turned against one another in battle. They have fought over a cause so murky that your own German press is still debating what it ever was. For us, the issue was unambiguous: Belgium had been invaded, and France teetered on the brink of total destruction. Nothing else could have roused the English to take up arms against you. But why you chose to invade Belgium and France, and whether that incursion could ever have been averted, we do not know to this day. Still the war drags on, still more boys perish, while the men who never face combat—those in newspaper offices and government ministries—devise campaigns, offensives and countermoves that follow no conceivable, coherent strategy at all. Except for one truth, now: there is something more terrifying to them than war itself. And that is the day they must face reckoning with their own people.”
source_text: _Our boys_, he wrote, _have died, fighting one against the other. They have been fighting upon an issue so obscure that your German press is still busy discussing what it was. For us it was that Belgium was invaded and France in danger of destruction. Nothing else could have brought the English into the field against you. But why you invaded Belgium and France and whether that might have been averted we do not know to this day. And still this war goes on and still more boys die, and these men who do not fight, these men in the newspaper offices and in the ministries plan campaigns and strokes and counter-strokes that belong to no conceivable plan at all. Except that now for them there is something more terrible than war. And that is the day of reckoning with their own people._

text: Back when Elowen was still a wide-eyed, story-besotted romantic, every daydream of the fated lover the universe had earmarked for her cast him as unfailingly _flawless_: the two of them would always be unequivocally in the right, and if the arc of their tale demanded it, the entire rest of the world would be in the wrong. She had never once imagined she would end up yoked by her own tender affections to a man whose views clashed constantly with hers, and who flouted every one of her quiet, long-held standards, so much so that it often felt as if she’d been strapped to the back of a gentle, good-natured elephant that flinches faintly at the prick of her goad but never, ever obeys the direction she tries to steer it.
source_text: When Cissie had dreamt of the lover that fate had in store for her in her old romantic days, he was to be _perfect_ always, he and she were always to be absolutely in the right (and, if the story needed it, the world in the wrong). She had never expected to find herself tied by her affections to a man with whom she disagreed, and who went contrary to her standards, very much as if she was lashed on the back of a very nice elephant that would wince to but not obey the goad....

text: A lively, squirming tussle played out for several seconds before Billy was finally gripped tight. He chattered in a fury and bit Mr. Britling twice before he could be contained. Then Mr. Britling was out in the hallway, holding the wriggling warm mass of fur in one hand, and picking his way through the dark to Herr Heinrich’s door. He pushed it open gently, making no noise.
source_text: For some moments there was a lively struggle before Billy was gripped. He chattered furiously and bit Mr. Britling twice. Then Mr. Britling was out in the passage with the wriggling lump of warm fur in his hand, and paddling along in the darkness to the door of Herr Heinrich. He opened it softly.

text: Mr. Direck was fully settled in the belief that bringing up what he needed to say before they passed through the park gate would be entirely out of line. The winding lane, the open road, the wooden stile, and the entry gate all felt like nothing more than formalities to work through before he could get to the actual point. Once they stepped past that small white gate, though, the path would clear, the whole park would open up around them, and they could move forward without having to fuss over navigating every tricky little turn. And Mr. Direck felt he’d already spent far too long tiptoeing around diplomatic niceties, stuck in those meandering, roundabout lead-up moments.
source_text: The idea took hold of Mr. Direck's mind that until they got through the park gate it would be quite out of order to say anything. The lane and the road and the stile and the gate were all so much preliminary stuff to be got through before one could get to business. But after the little white gate the way was clear, the park opened out and one could get ahead without bothering about the steering. And Mr. Direck had, he felt, been diplomatically involved in lanes and by-ways long enough.

text: He’d reached the last cutting barb of his argument. “You lot prattle on about your Modern Principles of Pacifism, claim you’re far too noble to take up arms. But your merchant class in Chicago give the whole game away. Tiny printed placards hang in half the office lobbies across Chicago right now, laying bare what passes for American pacifism these days. They’re swamped, you see, commerce has never been more profitable. So to keep their business from being disrupted, they tack up those signs reading: ‘Count us out of the war!’ Can you fathom that?—‘Count us out of the war!’ The entire fate of the human race hangs in the balance, and all America has offered is a half-hearted murmur of anger when the Germans torpedoed the _Caledonia_, followed by endless, bitter complaining when we delay a handful of their vessels or some bumbling port master slaps them with an excessive fee. Otherwise—‘Count us out of the war!’…”
source_text: He came to his last shaft. "You talk of your New Ideals of Peace. You say that you are too proud to fight. But your business men in New York give the show away. There's a little printed card now in half the offices in New York that tells of the real pacificism of America. They're busy, you know. Trade's real good. And so as not to interrupt it they stick up this card: 'Nix on the war!' Think of it!--'Nix on the war!' Here is the whole fate of mankind at stake, and America's contribution is a little grumbling when the Germans sank the _Lusitania_, and no end of grumbling when we hold up a ship or two and some fool of a harbour-master makes an overcharge. Otherwise--'Nix on the war!'...

text: He paused to weigh his phrasing carefully. “It isn’t,” he said, “as if you’re venturing to another corner of the same world, or turning over a familiar face of the planet you’ve always known. It’s far more like you’ve spent all your time living in a single room, and someone has just asked you to step outside the door… It makes me think of a strange little incident that happened when I was in London last winter. I fell in with an odd crowd, I don’t believe I ever told you about it. I went to have supper with a group of students in Chelsea. I’d never been to the spot before, but they seemed harmless enough—just ordinary people like me, same as anyone else. After supper, they brought me along to visit some people they didn’t know very well, folks affiliated with a local School of Dramatic Art. There were two or three young actresses there, a singer, and others of that ilk, lounging around smoking cigarettes, and we were chatting about plays, books, films, all that sort of thing, when suddenly there was a sharp rapping at the door. Someone stepped out to answer, and found a policeman holding a warrant on the landing. They took our host’s son away… it was connected to a murder case.”
source_text: He considered his phrasing. "It's not," he said, "as though one was going into another part of the same world, or turning up another side of the world one was used to. It is just as if one had been living in a room and one had been asked to step outside.... It makes me think of a queer little thing that happened when I was in London last winter. I got into Queer Company. I don't think I told you. I went to have supper with some students in Chelsea. I hadn't been to the place before, but they seemed all right--just people like me--and everybody. And after supper they took me on to some people _they_ didn't know very well; people who had to do with some School of Dramatic Art. There were two or three young actresses there and a singer and people of that sort, sitting about smoking cigarettes, and we began talking plays and books and picture shows and all that stuff; and suddenly there was a knocking at the door and some one went out and found a policeman with a warrant on the landing. They took off our host's son.... It had to do with a murder...."

text: We must put a stop to the folly and empty vanity of kings, and do away with any system where one people rules over another instead of every community governing itself. There is no practical benefit, no shred of justice, in one group holding power over another that does not rule itself; when people are governed by outsiders who share none of their creeds, their languages, their unexamined ignorances and their particular prejudices, that is the foundational foolishness that killed Teddy and Hugh—and all those millions of other lives too. Ending that senseless wrong is as much our core duty and personal business as speaking the truth or earning a living....
source_text: "We have to put an end to the folly and vanity of kings, and to any people ruling any people but themselves. There is no convenience, there is no justice in any people ruling any people but themselves; the ruling of men by others, who have not their creeds and their languages and their ignorances and prejudices, that is the fundamental folly that has killed Teddy and Hugh--and these millions. To end that folly is as much our duty and business as telling the truth or earning a living...."

text: Before long, Elspeth Hale had grown entirely accustomed to the sight of half a dozen young men in khaki field shirts and breeches tending to their ablutions in and around her back scullery, or cobbling together an unapproved game of football between the weathered hockey posts at the edge of her lawn. These were not the random, untested recruits of the newly raised conscript armies; they belonged to an older Territorial battalion, unassuming and free of performative bravado, mostly hailing from mill towns across the Midlands. Their two middle-aged corporals ran the detachment tightly, but with the easy, firm care of men tending to a band of younger brothers. Still, they had a stubbornly unruly streak, one that unfolded in small, creative ways that had Thomas Hale spinning quiet theories about ingrained working-class folk wisdom. They poached as if it were second nature, as unthinking and joyful as children chasing fireflies or humming street ballads. They kept a scruffy, unclaimed white dog that trotted at their heels everywhere, and soon unaccountable rabbits began appearing on their nightly supper trays. One evening the whole lower floor reeked of hot butter and fried fish, and the cook came upstairs to report the men were cooking trout. “Trout?” Thomas said later, leaning against the scullery doorframe to address one of the corporals, “where on earth did you lot get hold of trout?”
source_text: In quite a little time Mrs. Britling's mind had adapted itself to the spectacle of half-a-dozen young men in khaki breeches and shirts performing their toilets in and about her scullery, or improvising an unsanctioned game of football between the hockey goals. These men were not the miscellaneous men of the new armies; they were the earlier Territorial type with no heroics about them; they came from the midlands; and their two middle-aged corporals kept them well in hand and ruled them like a band of brothers. But they had an illegal side, that developed in directions that set Mr. Britling theorising. They seemed, for example, to poach by nature, as children play and sing. They possessed a promiscuous white dog. They began to add rabbits to their supper menu, unaccountable rabbits. One night there was a mighty smell of frying fish from the kitchen, and the cook reported trout. "Trout!" said Mr. Britling to one of the corporals; "now where did you chaps get trout?"

text: Mr. Haleworth set out on his moonlit drive a little after half past nine that evening. He’d told the household he could neither settle to rest nor focus on his work, the looming war had left him thrumming with a restless fever; taking the motor out on the open lanes was the only distraction that might quiet his fraying nerves. He added offhand that he might not be back for a day or two, that he would return only once he felt steady enough to pick up his tasks again. He topped off his petrol tank by the glow of a pocket electric torch, then sat behind the wheel in the shadow of the garage poring over his tattered map of the surrounding countryside. His thoughts drifted from the winding backroads to the village of Bramblethwaite to the jagged eastern coast, and then to the likely path a hostile raiding party might take. What if the enemy struck before an official declaration of war was even issued? They could push through this stretch first, and this one....
source_text: Mr. Britling started for his moonlight ride about half-past nine that night.
