He announced that he could neither rest nor work, the war had thrown him into a fever; the driving of the automobile was just the distraction he needed; he might not, he added casually, return for a day or so. When he felt he could work again he would come back. He filled up his petrol tank by the light of an electric torch, and sat in his car in the garage and studied his map of the district. His thoughts wandered from the road to Pyecrafts to the coast, and to the possible route of a raider. Suppose the enemy anticipated a declaration of war! Here he might come, and here....

text: Mr. Britling couldn’t shake the heavy, gnawing weight of those letters as they lingered in his thoughts. He knew full well his boy—sharp, thoughtful, unnervingly wise far beyond his years—was now hovering right on the edge of harm and death, moving back and forth across that threshold constantly, trapped in a state of never-ending, shifting risk. Day or night, there was no telling when that cruel, unbidden strike could find him out of nowhere. If Mr. Britling had been the sort of man who prayed, he would have begged for Hugh’s safety. He stumbled through half-formed, useless pleas a handful of times, but never could bring himself to finish a single one.
source_text: These letters weighed heavily upon Mr. Britling's mind. He perceived that this precociously wise, subtle youngster of his was now close up to the line of injury and death, going to and fro from it, in a perpetual, fluctuating danger. At any time now in the day or night the evil thing might wing its way to him. If Mr. Britling could have prayed, he would have prayed for Hugh. He began and never finished some ineffectual prayers.

text: We get through a fair bit of reading, naturally. But no collection of books shipped out here could ever be large enough to meet our constant hunger for them. We’ll make do with nearly any volume that comes our way, but the pulpy thrillers most of the lads devoured back home don’t hold half their old appeal anymore. A few of them have taken to weighty nonfiction in the most unexpected turns. Old Pritchard, for instance, says he only wants books you can sink your teeth into; he’s working his way through a dog-eared paperback of *On the Origin of Species*. Once he thought the frothy detective potboilers of E. Phillips Oppenheim and Edgar Wallace were the height of written entertainment. I’d be so grateful if you could send him Metchnikoff’s *The Nature of Man* or Pearson’s *Ethics of Freethought*. I like to think I’m nurturing his still-developing intellectual side. That’s not what I’m after, though, Daddy. Nothing that heavy for me. This sort of work hits everyone differently, after all. What I’m craving out here is literary laudanum. I want stories of fauns and nymphs wandering sun-dappled, wide valley clearings. I’ve been longing to dip into Spenser’s *Faerie Queene*. I don’t believe I’ve ever actually read it, but I carry such a vivid mental image of its knights and dragons, enchanters and scheming sorceresses, moving across a scene stitched like a Pre-Raphaelite tapestry, only gilded with soft, warm light. I’d also happily take something in the vein of Maurice Hewlett’s *The Forest Lovers*, or Joseph Conrad writing in that soft, lush register he uses when describing spaces like the Kew Palm House. There’s another book I flicked through once, in a friend’s flat in London; I can’t recall its title, but it was by Richard Garnett, all about old gods fallen on hard times, wandering through bright, postcard-perfect countryside. Scenery with no barbed wire, no metal stakes, no strung communication lines. Something in the same vein as Heine’s *Florentine Nights*. Any story about the Greek gods would make me happy, anything featuring ivory-hued stone temples, violet stretches of sea, scarlet headscarves, coffers crammed with jewels, and little lizards basking on sun-warmed rock. I’d give anything for another book as lush as Anatole France’s *Thais*. The lads have been getting these thin little pamphlets of literary snippets put out by *The Times*, called Broadsheets. Just tiny extracts, most of them from excellent work, but they’re only long enough to whet your hunger, never fill it. They’re more of a tease, really, and the last thing anyone needs out here is more frustration… I used to think reading was supposed to rouse you, to energize you. Out here, it has to be a salve.
source_text: "We read, of course. But there never could be a library here big enough to keep us going. We can do with all sorts of books, but I don't think the ordinary sensational novel is quite the catch it was for a lot of them in peace time. Some break towards serious reading in the oddest fashion. Old Park, for example, says he wants books you can chew; he is reading a cheap edition of 'The Origin of Species.' He used to regard Florence Warden and William le Queux as the supreme delights of print. I wish you could send him Metchnikoff's 'Nature of Man' or Pearson's 'Ethics of Freethought.' I feel I am building up his tender mind. Not for me though, Daddy. Nothing of that sort for me. These things take people differently. What I want here is literary opium. I want something about fauns and nymphs in broad low glades. I would like to read Spenser's 'Faerie Queen.' I don't think I have read it, and yet I have a very distinct impression of knights and dragons and sorcerers and wicked magic ladies moving through a sort of Pre-Raphaelite tapestry scenery--only with a light on them. I could do with some Hewlett of the 'Forest Lovers' kind. Or with Joseph Conrad in his Kew Palm-house mood. And there is a book, I once looked into it at a man's room in London; I don't know the title, but it was by Richard Garnett, and it was all about gods who were in reduced circumstances but amidst sunny picturesque scenery. Scenery without steel or poles or wire. A thing after the manner of Heine's 'Florentine Nights.' Any book about Greek gods would be welcome, anything about temples of ivory-coloured stone and purple seas, red caps, chests of jewels, and lizards in the sun. I wish there was another 'Thais.' The men here are getting a kind of newspaper sheet of literature scraps called _The Times_ Broadsheets. Snippets, but mostly from good stuff. They're small enough to stir the appetite, but not to satisfy it. Rather an irritant--and one wants no irritant.... I used to imagine reading was meant to be a stimulant. Out here it has to be an anodyne....

text: For all the courage Mr. Britling projected on the surface, there was a second, unspoken thought gnawing at the back of his mind: fear. He was ready to puff himself up like a valiant wild turkey, every feather fluffed to its full extent, to stand against the aggressor. He was willing to head to the front, brandish a bayonet, march until his feet burned, dig trenches until his hands cracked, fire his rifle, even die in a ditch if he had to, rather than let German militarism seize control of the entire world. He felt no fear for his own safety. He was prepared to perish on the battlefield, or cut a bold, heroic figure recovering in a military hospital if he was wounded. But the truth he saw plain as day, even as he fought tooth and nail to push it away, was this harsh, disheartening reality: the monstrous entity of war was far less eager to meet him than he was to meet it. Its gaze was fixed on something off to his side, just over his shoulder. It was already only too obvious that it was stretching one long, shadowed arm right past him, reaching straight for Teddy—and for Hugh....
source_text: But behind all this bravery was the other thing, the second thing in the mind of Mr. Britling, a fear. He was prepared now to spread himself like some valiant turkey-gobbler, every feather at its utmost, against the aggressor. He was prepared to go out and flourish bayonets, march and dig to the limit of his power, shoot, die in a ditch if needful, rather than permit German militarism to dominate the world. He had no fear for himself. He was prepared to perish upon the battlefield or cut a valiant figure in the military hospital. But what he perceived very clearly and did his utmost not to perceive was this qualifying and discouraging fact, that the war monster was not nearly so disposed to meet him as he was to meet the war, and that its eyes were fixed on something beside and behind him, that it was already only too evidently stretching out a long and shadowy arm past him towards Teddy--and towards Hugh....

text: July 21st saw Gladys returned, fully repaired and none the worse for her recent misadventure. The very next day he drove her with deliberate care over to the Pyecrafts’ residence, hoping to quiet his nagging unease by leaning into the pretence of a sweeping grand passion and singing the praises of *The Silent Places*—that exquisite work of art entirely free of any taint of practical utility. To his dismay, he found Mrs. Harrowdean in a foul temper. He had been gone from her for ten whole days, after all. No doubt Edith had manoeuvred to keep him away, she accused. He protested she hadn’t. But hadn’t she, really? How was he, poor guileless soul that he was, to know for certain? That back-and-forth was the prelude to a thoroughly stormy afternoon.
source_text: On the twenty-first of July Gladys came back repaired and looking none the worse for her misadventure. Next day he drove her very carefully over to Pyecrafts, hoping to drug his uneasiness with the pretence of a grand passion and the praises of "The Silent Places," that beautiful work of art that was so free from any taint of application, and alas! he found Mrs. Harrowdean in an evil mood. He had been away from her for ten days--ten whole days. No doubt Edith had manoeuvred to keep him. She hadn't! _Hadn't_ she? How was he, poor simple soul! to tell that she hadn't? That was the prelude to a stormy afternoon.

text: He claimed he had dropped by to pay a call on Mr. and Mrs. Britling, and that was true, up to a point; but his gaze kept flicking toward the front door and the windows, straying unmoored from the topic of conversation the whole time he spoke. A soft, unspoken hope of running into Cissie had stepped over the threshold with him, lingering around him as unshakable as the scent of violets clings to its bloom.
source_text: He professed to be calling upon Mr. and Mrs. Britling, and to a certain extent he was; but he had a quick eye for the door or windows; his glance roved irrelevantly as he talked. A faint expectation of Cissie came in with him and hovered about him, as the scent of violets follows the flower.

text: The entire breakfast conversation centered on nothing but the war. Hugh was as jittery and wired as a cat in stormy weather, while the younger boys kept begging for details about different national flags. They were bickering over the Russian and Serbian flags, so they had to look up the flag reference page in Webster’s Dictionary to sort out the disagreement. Both the daily newspapers and the post were running far later than normal, and after growing tired of passing along trivial bits of information to his children, Mr. Britling went out to the garden to smoke cigarettes. He planned to intercept the mail carrier when he passed by. Soon, he caught both the sound and sight of Mrs. Faber’s car approaching. It was a rugged, dependable old red machine, driven by their long-trusted gardener, and there was no mistaking what it was.
source_text: At breakfast there was no talk of anything but war. Hugh was as excited as a cat in thundery weather, and the small boys wanted information about flags. The Russian and the Serbian flag were in dispute, and the flag page of Webster's Dictionary had to be consulted. Newspapers and letters were both abnormally late, and Mr. Britling, tiring of supplying trivial information to his offspring, smoked cigarettes in the garden. He had an idea of intercepting the postman. His eyes and ears informed him of the approach of Mrs. Faber's automobile. It was an old, resolute-looking machine painted red, and driven by a trusted gardener; there was no mistaking it.

text: Mr. Haleworth, his perception sharpened immeasurably by Elias’s frequent letters, crisscrossed the Suffolk flatlands in his roadster, and took two or three longer trips out to Oxfordshire and the western Cotswold fringes, noting how the quiet, long-peaceful countryside was steadily being remade into a sprawling armed encampment. He was inclined to play down Elias’s critical observations. He detected in them the unforgiving edge of youth, sharp enough to cut and far too impatient to abide half-finished work and the messy ways we frail humans dodge the crushing weight of striving for perfection. “The messy ways we frail humans dodge the crushing weight of striving for perfection” was Mr. Haleworth’s own turn of phrase. To Mr. Haleworth, who overlooked small fumbles to focus on the broader sweep of progress, the newly raised force was both a source of fierce pride and an outright marvel.
source_text: Mr. Britling, with an understanding much quickened by Hugh's letters, went about Essex in his automobile, and on one or two journeys into Berkshire and Buckinghamshire, and marked the steady conversion of the old pacific countryside into an armed camp. He was disposed to minimise Hugh's criticisms. He found in them something of the harshness of youth, which is far too keen-edged to be tolerant with half performance and our poor human evasion of perfection's overstrain. "Our poor human evasion of perfection's overstrain"; this phrase was Mr. Britling's. To Mr. Britling, looking less closely and more broadly, the new army was a pride and a marvel.

text: That is the particular magic of casual sport: Mr. Direck held a quiet, humble pride and gratitude for her compliment, and kept pace at the side of this woman who had revealed herself so quick, unshakable, and decisive, his chest brimming with the soft, uncomplicated joy of merely walking beside her. Tea afterwards was a sprawling, noisy, chaotic affair, livened by Mr. Raeburn’s wildly fanciful, entirely untrue anecdotes about the afternoon’s play, and once they finished they returned to the field, this time with far fewer unskilled players, moving with greater precision and speed. Mr. Direck pulled off such fast, clever maneuvers that everyone present swore he was a hockey prodigy sent straight down from the heavens. The dusk that finally blurred the ball beyond recognition, making play impossible, arrived far too early for his liking. He had played six full matches, and knew he would be as rigid as a carved wooden doll come morning. But he was profoundly, unreservedly happy.
source_text: And such is the magic of a game that Mr. Direck was humbly proud and grateful for her praise, and trotted along by the side of this creature who had revealed herself so swift and resolute and decisive, full to overflowing of the mere pleasure of just trotting along by her side. And after tea, which was a large confused affair, enlivened by wonderful and entirely untruthful reminiscences of the afternoon by Mr. Raeburn, they played again, with fewer inefficients and greater skill and swiftness, and Mr. Direck did such quick and intelligent things that everybody declared that he was a hockey player straight from heaven. The dusk, which at last made the position of the ball too speculative for play, came all too soon for him. He had played in six games, and he knew he would be as stiff as a Dutch doll in the morning. But he was very, very happy.

text: She passed far more time at Elias Thorne’s cottage than she did at any other home or lodging. Local gossip held she’d “unlocked” his quiet, introspective side, and that she’d once “gone to bat” for him when his father was heaping harsh criticism on his life choices. It was certainly true that she’d pushed back against dozens of the unkind, reflexive judgments fathers so often level at their grown sons. Though to be fair, she pushed back against practically everything. And Elias could not bear the thought of her drifting from shabby boarding houses to chilly mineral spring retreats, with only random, fleeting opportunities to argue with anyone who crossed her path.
source_text: She spent more time in Mr. Britling's house than in any other. There was a legend that she had "drawn out" his mind, and that she had "stood up" for him against his father. She had certainly contradicted quite a number of those unfavourable comments that fathers are wont to make about their sons. Though certainly she contradicted everything. And Mr. Britling hated to think of her knocking about alone in boarding-houses and hydropathic establishments with only the most casual chances for contradiction.

text: He was thoroughly enjoying the activity, so he tried dancing a second time with one of the visitors’ daughters, who moved with a far more rigid, proper form than his last partner. Then Teddy took over operating the pianola, and Mr. Direck stared in shock at the sight before him: an eminent British intellectual, swirled in black velvet, his dark legs pumping furiously, was performing a rough approximation of an Apache dance while chasing the visitors’ wife across the floor. Into that wild little routine Mr. Lawrence Carmine suddenly cut, unannounced.
source_text: He liked it, and he had another attempt with one of the visitor daughters, who danced rather more formally, and then Teddy took the pianola and Mr. Direck was astonished by the spectacle of an eminent British thinker in a whirl of black velvet and extremely active black legs engaged in a kind of Apache dance in pursuit of the visitor wife. In which Mr. Lawrence Carmine suddenly mingled.

text: Not long after Christmas, rumors started spreading that the South-Eastern Army’s headquarters staff was imminently going to descend on Claverings. Soon after, Mr. Britling ran into Lady Homartyn, just back from France, seething because the headquarters team had ultimately decided to station themselves with Lady Wensleydale at Ladyholt instead. To her, the choice was a blatant snub to Claverings. Her frustration only deepened when the new armies—now cropping up all across England the way floodwaters spread across a low-lying meadow—poured into the parishes surrounding Claverings, amounting to a full battalion and a Territorial battery. Mr. Britling only got wind of their arrival a day or two before they showed up; a bright, energetic young officer turned up with an orderly to arrange billets, clearly stressed because, as he repeated multiple times, he was stuck trying to fit a quart into a pint bottle. He was thrilled when he spotted the barn. He asked for its dimensions, ran some quick calculations, and said he could “fit twenty-five men in there—easy.” That would go a long way to solving all his logistical headaches. He wouldn’t even need to house anyone inside the main house at all. It was an incredible spot, he added, “couldn’t ask for better.”
source_text: Soon after Christmas there were rumours of an impending descent of the Headquarters staff of the South-Eastern army upon Claverings. Then Mr. Britling found Lady Homartyn back from France, and very indignant because after all the Headquarters were to go to Lady Wensleydale at Ladyholt. It was, she felt, a reflection upon Claverings. Lady Homartyn became still more indignant when presently the new armies, which were gathering now all over England like floods in a low-lying meadow, came pouring into the parishes about Claverings to the extent of a battalion and a Territorial battery. Mr. Britling heard of their advent only a day or two before they arrived; there came a bright young officer with an orderly, billeting; he was much exercised to get, as he expressed it several times, a quart into a pint bottle. He was greatly pleased with the barn. He asked the size of it and did calculations. He could "stick twenty-five men into it--easy." It would go far to solve his problems. He could manage without coming into the house at all. It was a ripping place. "No end."

text: Like a great many Britons, Mr. Britling carried that particular strain of affectionate, patriotically rooted regard for America that manifests as sharp, restless criticism. No one in Britain would ever refer to an American as a foreigner. Pointing out flaws in Germany or Spain taps into endless reserves of charitable leniency, but America’s shortcomings gnaw at the English mind nearly as acutely as England’s own failings. Mr. Britling could easily explain away all of England’s faults: our Hanoverian monarchy, the Established Church and its deadening grip on national education, the burden of imperial obligations that stretched our supply of skilled administrative talent thin, all served as perfectly convenient justifications. But America was the same old ancestral stock, free of a crown, a state church, or the weight of messy international duties, and it still fell far short of the glorious standard he had expected for it. His comments to Mr. Direck held the bitter, unvarnished rancor of a family argument. I will share just a few of the sentences that would end up etched permanently in Mr. Direck’s memory.
source_text: Like many Britons Mr. Britling had that touch of patriotic feeling towards America which takes the form of impatient criticism. No one in Britain ever calls an American a foreigner. To see faults in Germany or Spain is to tap boundless fountains of charity; but the faults of America rankle in an English mind almost as much as the faults of England. Mr. Britling could explain away the faults of England readily enough; our Hanoverian monarchy, our Established Church and its deadening effect on education, our imperial obligations and the strain they made upon our supplies of administrative talent were all very serviceable for that purpose. But there in America was the old race, without Crown or Church or international embarrassment, and it was still falling short of splendid. His speech to Mr. Direck had the rancour of a family quarrel. Let me only give a few sentences that were to stick in Mr. Direck's memory.

text: The much-touted “Business as Normal” era was already fading fast, and London was swept up in the full swell of enlistment fervor. That swell crashed against the most shambolic, ill-conceived enlistment infrastructure anyone could conceive of. Overstretched, barely competent recruiting officers, who equated competence exclusively with rejecting civilian aid, moving at a glacial, overly cautious pace, and carrying themselves with stiff, haughty grandeur, hunkered down in grubby, cramped offices and snapped at the unprecedented throngs of Britons crowding doorframes and window sills begging to sign up. Outside every recruiting station, throngs of men and teenage boys loitered, propped against brick walls, perched on curbstones, waiting for hours on end, lingering until sundown only to return at dawn the next day, no cover from the elements, no food, many faint with hunger; they were men who’d rushed up from rural villages, who’d walked away from every sort of steady post, office workers, shop assistants, all driven by a single urge to serve their country and “teach those blasted Germans a lesson.” Between them and that urgent goal stood a baffling, unnecessary obstacle: sheer institutional neglect. As Arthur Thorne cut past St. Martin-in-the-Fields and crossed Trafalgar Square, taking in the tired, huddled masses of this staggeringly patriotic human stock, he got his first glimmer of how woefully unimaginative the War Office was, now that it had been abruptly tasked with orchestrating a national victory. He would get a far fuller picture of that shortcoming once he stepped through the doors of his club.
source_text: The great "Business as Usual" phase was already passing away, and London was in the full tide of recruiting enthusiasm. That tide was breaking against the most miserable arrangements for enlistment it is possible to imagine. Overtaxed and not very competent officers, whose one idea of being very efficient was to refuse civilian help and be very, very slow and circumspect and very dignified and overbearing, sat in dirty little rooms and snarled at this unheard-of England that pressed at door and window for enrolment. Outside every recruiting office crowds of men and youths waited, leaning against walls, sitting upon the pavements, waited for long hours, waiting to the end of the day and returning next morning, without shelter, without food, many sick with hunger; men who had hurried up from the country, men who had thrown up jobs of every kind, clerks, shopmen, anxious only to serve England and "teach those damned Germans a lesson." Between them and this object they had discovered a perplexing barrier; an inattention. As Mr. Britling made his way by St. Martin's Church and across Trafalgar Square and marked the weary accumulation of this magnificently patriotic stuff, he had his first inkling of the imaginative insufficiency of the War Office that had been so suddenly called upon to organise victory. He was to be more fully informed when he reached his club.

text: Even though Mr. Britling no longer felt anxious about his son, a strange, unshakable sense of foreboding stayed with him throughout the whole of July. He carried a quiet certainty that the world was going awry, a feeling he tried and failed to shake off with all manner of trivial diversions. It may well have been that some subtle, subconscious breakdown of the circumstances around him was calculating risks his conscious mind refused to acknowledge. When he eventually bicycled over to Mrs. Harrowdean’s seeking flattery, lighthearted fun, and general comfort, however, he found she was far from the uplifting, approving presence who had validated every one of his beliefs about himself and the wider world back in the giddy early days of their romantic friendship. She still clung to her open hostility toward Edith, and seemed set on making their entire dynamic unworkable. Even so, faint traces of their old, sustaining intimacy still surfaced between them on occasion.
source_text: Though Mr. Britling's anxiety about his son was dispelled, his mind remained curiously apprehensive throughout July. He had a feeling that things were not going well with the world, a feeling he tried in vain to dispel by various distractions. Perhaps some subtler subconscious analysis of the situation was working out probabilities that his conscious self would not face. And when presently he bicycled off to Mrs. Harrowdean for flattery, amusement, and comfort generally, he found her by no means the exalting confirmation of everything he wished to believe about himself and the universe, that had been her delightful rôle in the early stages of their romantic friendship. She maintained her hostility to Edith; she seemed bent on making things impossible. And yet there were one or two phases of the old sustaining intimacies.

text:
These assorted exploits carried him on what felt like a full circuit of the globe, stranding him on sun-scorched tropical shores, leaving him adrift in a mastless, battered craft on empty, desolate seas, and tangling him up in the most shocking last-minute interventions and wildly inconvenient aftereffects. In the lulls between these misadventures, he would sink into stretches of sharp, incisive philosophical reflection.
For years, a quiet doubt had been building in Mr. Britling’s mind: that in searing this all-consuming drive for romance into his core, the unfathomable workings of nature had been, to use the common turn of phrase, pulling his leg for some mere biological end. He had come to suspect there were no perfectly matched, reciprocal connections after all, that to love a woman deeply is a feat one pulls off fully once, maybe twice at most, and every subsequent attempt is little more than a pale, regretful echo of that first feeling, a string of hollow repetitions. He’d grown to think that the whole career of the so-called Pilgrim of Love, once you strip away the starry-eyed, naive gloss people paint over it, is either the most pitiable farce or the cheapest, crassest deviation from how a person ought to conduct their life.
But this doubt had not yet grown large enough to curb his impulses entirely. It was no match for those sudden high tides of longing, the sharp, briny promise of a salt-kissed wind off the water, the quiet call of the seagull circling low overhead. Right now, beneath the energetic, bustling facade of the daily routines Mr. Direck knew so well, he was hiding a vast, tangled web of heartache and trouble, all sprung from his most recent, eighth foray into these romantic digressions.
source_text: Between these various excursions--they took him round and about the world, so to speak, they cast him away on tropical beaches, they left him dismasted on desolate seas, they involved the most startling interventions and the most inconvenient consequences--there were interludes of penetrating philosophy. For some years the suspicion had been growing up in Mr. Britling's mind that in planting this persuasion in his being, the mysterious processes of Nature had been, perhaps for some purely biological purpose, pulling, as people say, his leg, that there were not these perfect responses, that loving a woman is a thing one does thoroughly once for all--or so--and afterwards recalls regrettably in a series of vain repetitions, and that the career of the Pilgrim of Love, so soon as you strip off its credulous glamour, is either the most pitiful or the most vulgar and vile of perversions from the proper conduct of life. But this suspicion had not as yet grown to prohibitive dimensions with him, it was not sufficient to resist the seasons of high tide, the sudden promise of the salt-edged breeze, the invitation of the hovering sea-bird; and he was now concealing beneath the lively surface of activities with which Mr. Direck was now familiar, a very extensive system of distresses arising out of the latest, the eighth of these digressional adventures....

text: But I didn’t check myself into the hospital, because I was terrified they’d hold me for observation, and then I’d never be able to make it back home. I was desperate to get here—so homesick. No one’s ever been as homesick as I was. I’ve thought about this place, the garden, the way you’d peer out the front window at people passing by, a thousand times over. It felt like I could see them all, clear as anything, even when I was miles away. Old Dimple with that warm, gentle smile of his, Mrs. Wolker in the last cottage down the lane, how she’d trudge out to get her beer and wink right at us when she caught us watching her, little Charlie Slobberface sniffling the whole way to feed the pigs, and every last one of the others. And you, Letty. Especially you. I thought about how we’d lean up against the windowsill together, our shoulders pressed tight, your cheek right there in front of my eyes… Back when not a single part of you ached, not even a little.
source_text: "But I didn't go into a hospital because I was afraid they would intern me, and so I wouldn't be able to come home. And I was dying to come home. I was--homesick. No one was ever so homesick. I've thought of this place and the garden, and how one looked out of the window at the passers-by, a thousand times. I seemed always to be seeing them. Old Dimple with his benevolent smile, and Mrs. Wolker at the end cottage, and how she used to fetch her beer and wink when she caught us looking at her, and little Charlie Slobberface sniffing on his way to the pigs and all the rest of them. And you, Letty. Particularly you. And how we used to lean on the window-sill with our shoulders touching, and your cheek just in front of my eyes.... And nothing aching at all in one....

text: His son was spiraling toward ruin, his country was going straight to hell, his home had turned into a makeshift hospital for everyone hurt by his carelessness, country roads were choked with his smashed, uninsured cars, the cows were probably lined up along the property edge right now munching Edith’s carnations, his wallet and dresser were stuffed with venomous insults about her—and he was just lazing in bed!
source_text: His son was drifting to ruin, his country was going to the devil, the house was a hospital of people wounded by his carelessness, the country roads choked with his smashed (and uninsured) automobiles, the cows were probably lined up along the borders and munching Edith's carnations at this very moment, his pocketbook and bureau were stuffed with venomous insults about her--and he was just lying in bed!

text: Humanity has clawed its way, stumbling, bleeding, and raw with agony, out of Time’s churning, primal dark—only for that same dark to swallow and grind it to dust before long. Why not stumble through it all alongside everyone else? Why not eat, drink, brawl, yell, sob, and pray, forget Hugh, stop picking at the memory of Hugh until it festers, toss out all those self-righteous fantasies of “The Better Government of the World” once and for all, and turn instead to the lighter bits of the war: the silly, swashbuckling sides, that hearty Chesterton-style cheer, the cheeky, wisecracking energy you get from *Punch*? Do you really imagine that just because your sons are dead, there’s no more cakes and ale left to enjoy? Let humanity fumble its way out of the muck and carnage the same messy, unthinking way it stumbled into it in the first place....
source_text: Man has come, floundering and wounding and suffering, out of the breeding darknesses of Time, that will presently crush and consume him again. Why not flounder with the rest, why not eat, drink, fight, scream, weep and pray, forget Hugh, stop brooding upon Hugh, banish all these priggish dreams of "The Better Government of the World," and turn to the brighter aspects, the funny and adventurous aspects of the war, the Chestertonian jolliness, _Punch_ side of things? Think you because your sons are dead that there will be no more cakes and ale? Let mankind blunder out of the mud and blood as mankind has blundered in....

text:
All at once the truth settled over him, unshakable: he was not nearly as alone as he had believed. Tens upon hundreds of thousands of men and women scattered across the globe held the same quiet, burning longing he did, every fiber of them aching to speak the gentle, mending word that rested heavy on his own tongue. It was not just his shoulder pushing against the cold, unyielding weight of the barriers before him. Spaniards and Japanese sat in the same heavy hush, staring down the same tangled, unanswerable questions; Nigerians were reaching through the dark to find connection with people just like him, even in the exact second he hunched over his desk scribbling notes.

For the first time, he felt with perfect clarity the Presence he had circled in thought a hundred times the past few months, so close it hummed behind his eyelids, thrumming in his skull and the tips of his calloused fingers. This was no flight of fancy, no over-tired hallucination; it was a sensation as real as the oak of his desk beneath his arm. It was Kael, Kael who he’d grieved as lost to the front lines, it was bright young Luka who was still alive half a world away, it was his own self, it was every one of those people reaching across borders for grace, it was all of them and something far larger too: the Guide, the Shepherd of all weary souls, it was God, sitting right there in the small study with him, and he knew beyond a shadow of a doubt that it was God.

It felt as though he’d been fumbling through pitch-black wilds for years, convinced he was the only soul left among sharp crags, hidden sinkholes, and cruel, unfeeling stone, when all at once a hand, steady and calloused and warm, closed gently around his own. A voice deep in his chest bade him hold fast, to not give in to fear. There was no cheap, flashy miracle in that quiet second; he was still gaunt with exhaustion, a worn-out essayist frayed at the edges, a man with a good heart and far too few tools to do the work he ached to do, but he was no longer isolated and miserable, no longer breathing the same stale air as despair. God walked at his side, lived in his bones, wrapped around every edge of the small room around him.

This was the turning point of Elias Thorne’s life. It was as fleeting and soft as a wisp of cloud drifting across a May midday sky; it was as monumental as the very first spark of light on the first day the world was made. For a handful of seconds he still leaned back in his worn armchair, chin tucked to his chest, hands lax where they’d fallen from the armrests. Then he straightened his shoulders, pulling a long, deep breath into his tight lungs.
source_text: And suddenly it was borne in upon his mind that he was not alone. There were thousands and tens of thousands of men and women like himself, desiring with all their hearts to say, as he desired to say, the reconciling word. It was not only his hand that thrust against the obstacles.... Frenchmen and Russians sat in the same stillness, facing the same perplexities; there were Germans seeking a way through to him. Even as he sat and wrote. And for the first time clearly he felt a Presence of which he had thought very many times in the last few weeks, a Presence so close to him that it was behind his eyes and in his brain and hands. It was no trick of his vision; it was a feeling of immediate reality. And it was Hugh, Hugh that he had thought was dead, it was young Heinrich living also, it was himself, it was those others that sought, it was all these and it was more, it was the Master, the Captain of Mankind, it was God, there present with him, and he knew that it was God. It was as if he had been groping all this time in the darkness, thinking himself alone amidst rocks and pitfalls and pitiless things, and suddenly a hand, a firm strong hand, had touched his own. And a voice within him bade him be of good courage. There was no magic trickery in that moment; he was still weak and weary, a discouraged rhetorician, a good intention ill-equipped; but he was no longer lonely and wretched, no longer in the same world with despair. God was beside him and within him and about him.... It was the crucial moment of Mr. Britling's life. It was a thing as light as the passing of a cloud on an April morning; it was a thing as great as the first day of creation. For some moments he still sat back with his chin upon his chest and his hands dropping from the arms of his chair. Then he sat up and drew a deep breath....

text: Things didn’t go much easier for him dealing with Mr. Britling. Though Mr. Britling spoke in grim, guarded tones, every point he made was unmistakeably aimed straight at America. “There are two kinds of liberalism that pass themselves off as identical,” Mr. Britling said. “One is the liberalism of big, ambitious goals, and the other is the liberalism that comes from a lack of moral drive....”
source_text: He fared little better at Mr. Britling's hands. Mr. Britling talked darkly, but pointed all the time only too plainly at America. "There's two sorts of liberalism," said Mr. Britling, "that pretend to be the same thing; there's the liberalism of great aims and the liberalism of defective moral energy...."

text: The two days he’d spent in London had given him no end of material to unpack, and he was relieved to have a broader circle than just Hugh, Teddy, and Mrs. Britling to hash it all out with. What was unfolding across Great Britain right now, he argued, was *adjustment*. It was an effort from a vast, loosely structured nation—still more instinctual than deliberate at this stage—to refashion its government, and its military organization most of all, to fit the unprecedented scale of warfare Germany had forced on the entire world. For two grueling decades, the British navy had ballooned in size in direct response to German naval build-up, but the country’s land military establishment had seen no corresponding growth. It was true there had been a fruitless, rather haphazardly run campaign pushing for universal military service, but there had been no stockpiling of supplies, no investment in arms manufacturing infrastructure, no advance planning or foundational systems laid to ease the rapid expansion of the nation’s fighting forces when a crisis hit. That kind of forward thinking was fundamentally at odds with the ingrained mindsets of Britain’s military caste. The German model of folding every last bit of the country’s strength and resources into a single, unified national war machine was still entirely alien to British military thinking—even after a full month of war. For Germany, war had become the all-consuming business of the entire nation; for the British, it still read as an incidental, offhand adventure. In Germany, the whole population was militarized; in England, the army was a narrow, specialized institution. For nearly every practical, day-to-day purpose, the country ran perfectly well without it, just as political life had also become a separate, specialized sphere… Now all at once, the public wanted a government that spoke for every citizen, and an army drawn from the whole of the people. How were they ever supposed to build that?
source_text: His two days in London had filled him with matter, and he was glad to have something more than Hugh and Teddy and Mrs. Britling to talk it upon. What was happening now in Great Britain, he declared, was _adjustment_. It was an attempt on the part of a great unorganised nation, an attempt, instinctive at present rather than intelligent, to readjust its government and particularly its military organisation to the new scale of warfare that Germany had imposed upon the world. For two strenuous decades the British navy had been growing enormously under the pressure of German naval preparations, but the British military establishment had experienced no corresponding expansion. It was true there had been a futile, rather foolishly conducted agitation for universal military service, but there had been no accumulation of material, no preparation of armament-making machinery, no planning and no foundations for any sort of organisation that would have facilitated the rapid expansion of the fighting forces of a country in a time of crisis. Such an idea was absolutely antagonistic to the mental habits of the British military caste. The German method of incorporating all the strength and resources of the country into one national fighting machine was quite strange to the British military mind--still. Even after a month of war. War had become the comprehensive business of the German nation; to the British it was an incidental adventure. In Germany the nation was militarised, in England the army was specialised. The nation for nearly every practical purpose got along without it. Just as political life had also become specialised.... Now suddenly we wanted a government to speak for every one, and an army of the whole people. How were we to find it?

text: Mr. Britling carried a fierce, unshakable pride in England, even as he railed against her flaws without pause. He was eager to frame the country for Mr. Direck as a delightfully odd tapestry stitched together from a whole host of absurd shortcomings, a perspective he’d landed on once his comfortable, prosperous middle age softened the sharp, fiery radicalism of his youth. To Mr. Britling, England was simply the ground under his feet, rooted in the Essex countryside he knew like the back of his hand. He led Mr. Direck through a small side door set in his walled garden out into a neatly kept paddock marked by two white goalposts. “We play hockey out here on Sundays,” he remarked, giving Mr. Direck no indication whatsoever that every guest at Matching’s Easy was all but required to take part in the rough, often bruising sport. From there they cut down a thick, hedgerow-lined lane and emerged onto a main road that traced the border of the Claverings estate deer park. “We’ll pop over to Claverings a bit later,” Mr. Britling said. “Lady Homartyn’s got a crowd of guests staying for the weekend, and you ought to get a look at how that set lives and who makes it up. She asked us to come for lunch tomorrow, but I turned her down—we’ve got our afternoon hockey scheduled, after all.”
source_text: He was inordinately proud of England, and he abused her incessantly. He wanted to state England to Mr. Direck as the amiable summation of a grotesque assembly of faults. That was the view into which the comforts and prosperities of his middle age had brought him from a radicalism that had in its earlier stages been angry and bitter. And for Mr. Britling England was "here." Essex was the county he knew. He took Mr. Direck out from his walled garden by a little door into a trim paddock with two white goals. "We play hockey here on Sundays," he said in a way that gave Mr. Direck no hint of the practically compulsory participation of every visitor to Matching's Easy in this violent and dangerous exercise, and thence they passed by a rich deep lane and into a high road that ran along the edge of the deer park of Claverings. "We will call in on Claverings later," said Mr. Britling. "Lady Homartyn has some people there for the week-end, and you ought to see the sort of thing it is and the sort of people they are. She wanted us to lunch there to-morrow, but I didn't accept that because of our afternoon hockey."
